topics.title: Introduction: Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction: Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security technology (hardware or software) that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access, and produces reports to a management station. An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)**, in contrast, is an evolution of the IDS that not only detects suspicious activities but also takes **proactive automated action** to block, drop, or reject the offending traffic in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The combined entity **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is formally defined by NIST SP 800-94 as a software or hardware system that automates the process of monitoring events occurring in a computer system or network, analyzing them for signs of possible incidents, and attempting to stop detected incidents from progressing.

### 1.2 The Three Foundational Terms

- **Intrusion:** Any unauthorized activity that attempts to bypass the security mechanisms of a computer or network. This includes reconnaissance, password guessing, malware execution, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration.
- **Detection:** The act of identifying an intrusion by analyzing system or network telemetry against known signatures, statistical baselines, or state-based rules.
- **Prevention:** The act of stopping an intrusion *before* it succeeds — by dropping packets, terminating sessions, isolating the host, or modifying firewall rule sets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (High-Weightage Concept):** An **IDS** is a *passive* listener — it sees traffic but lets it through (out-of-band tap or SPAN port). An **IPS** is an *inline* gatekeeper — every packet passes through it for a verdict, similar to a firewall. This single architectural difference (passive tap vs. inline bump-in-the-wire) is responsible for nearly every other behavioral difference between the two.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a large shopping mall:

- The **IDS** is a team of **CCTV cameras** in the ceiling watching every corridor. They record everything, analyze the footage, and if they spot a shoplifter, they **alert the security guard** immediately. The shoplifter is still walking free at that moment.
- The **IPS** is a **tripwire-mesh door** at every entry/exit, combined with the same CCTV team. The moment a known shoplifter's gait is recognized, the **doors lock automatically** before the person can take anything.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exam:** **IDS = "I Detect & Shout"** (alarm) | **IPS = "I Prevent & Shut"** (action).

## 1.4 Why IDPS is Required — The Problem Statement

A traditional firewall operates at layers **2–4** and makes decisions based on **IP addresses, ports, and protocol flags**. However, it is *stateless* with respect to application semantics and cannot inspect the *content* of payloads. Modern attacks (SQL injection, cross-site scripting, polymorphic malware, zero-day exploits, lateral movement, ransomware C2) operate at layer **7 (Application)** and pass right through firewalls unhindered.

This gap in **defense-in-depth** is what IDPS fills. The metric for a security control's quality is typically expressed as:

$$\text{Coverage} = 1 - \frac{\text{False Negatives} + \text{False Positives}}{\text{Total Events}}$$

where a **False Negative (FN)** is a missed attack and a **False Positive (FP)** is a benign event incorrectly flagged as malicious. An ideal IDPS minimizes both.

## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Per Protocol)

- **True Positive (TP):** Real attack correctly flagged as an attack.
- **True Negative (TN):** Legitimate activity correctly allowed.
- **False Positive (FP):** Legitimate activity incorrectly flagged as an attack (Type I Error).
- **False Negative (FN):** Real attack incorrectly allowed (Type II Error — the most dangerous).
- **Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall):** $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** Examiners love asking for the difference between Type I and Type II errors in the context of IDS. Memorize: **Type I = False Alarm (Crying Wolf)**, **Type II = Missed Attack (Real Wolf not seen)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an IDPS

Every IDPS, regardless of vendor, decomposes into four logical subsystems. Understanding these is essential for KTU module-1 derivations and architecture diagrams.

1. **Sensor / Agent (Data Collection):** Taps network traffic (NIDS), hooks OS calls (HIDS), or reads application logs. Placed on strategic choke points (egress/ingress routers, DMZ, server VLANs).
2. **Analyzer / Detection Engine (Brain):** The core decision module. It applies the **detection methodology** (signature, anomaly, stateful protocol analysis, or hybrid) to the sensor data and produces verdicts.
3. **Management Console / Director (Control):** A centralized dashboard for the security analyst. Receives alerts, configures policies, tunes detection signatures, and aggregates logs.
4. **Response Engine (Action):** The actuator. In passive IDS, it logs/alerts. In inline IPS, it can **drop packets, reset TCP connections, block IP addresses, quarantine hosts, or quarantine user accounts.**

## 2.2 The Two-Axis Taxonomy of IDPS

A KTU board question will almost always ask: *“Classify IDPS.”* The canonical NIST classification is on two axes:

**Axis A — Data Source:**

- **Network-Based IDPS (NIDPS):** Monitors network traffic on a whole subnet. Sensors are promiscuous-mode NICs on span ports or taps.
- **Host-Based IDPS (HIDPS):** Monitors a single host's OS audit logs, system calls, file integrity, registry changes.
- **Application-Based IDPS (AppIDPS):** A special HIDS variant that lives inside a specific application (e.g., web server, database) and analyzes its transaction logs.
- **Hybrid / Distributed IDPS:** Combines multiple sensor types with a central correlation engine.

**Axis B — Detection Methodology:**

- **Signature-Based (Misuse-Based / Knowledge-Based):** Uses a database of known attack patterns (snort-style rules). High accuracy for known attacks, zero detection for novel attacks.
- **Anomaly-Based (Behavior-Based / Statistical):** Builds a baseline of "normal" and flags statistical deviations. Can detect zero-days but suffers from FPs.
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Compares observed protocol exchanges against the vendor's profile of "what a correct exchange should look like." Detects protocol-level abuses.
- **Hybrid Detection:** Combines two or more of the above (e.g., signature + anomaly), usually with a voting or weighted-score mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Takeaway:** A pure signature engine has **0% detection rate** against a zero-day exploit. An anomaly engine may have a **100% detection rate** but a **20–40% false positive rate**. The engineering challenge is to maximize DR while minimizing FPR — this trade-off is captured by the **ROC Curve** (Receiver Operating Characteristic).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute value bars below are written as `\vert` to preserve the integrity of the markdown table.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity) | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Unitless ratio, range $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Unitless ratio, range $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Specificity (True Negative Rate) | $SPC = 1 - FPR = \dfrac{TN}{FP + TN}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| 5 | F1-Score (Harmonic Mean) | $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot DR}{PPV + DR}$ | Best $= 1$, worst $= 0$ |
| 6 | Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| 7 | Anomaly Score (Z-Score) | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ where $x$ is observed, $\mu$ is baseline mean, $\sigma$ is std. dev. | $\vert Z \vert \ge 3 \Rightarrow$ alert |
| 8 | Mahalanobis Distance (multi-variate) | $D_M = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)}$ | Used in multivariate anomaly detection |
| 9 | Bayes Optimal Detector (Decision Rule) | Choose $C_1$ if $P(C_1 \vert x) > P(C_2 \vert x)$ | $P(C_i \vert x) = \dfrac{P(x \vert C_i)P(C_i)}{P(x)}$ |
| 10 | ROC-AUC | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Best $= 1.0$, random $= 0.5$ |
| 11 | Information Entropy (for anomaly scoring) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Bits; higher entropy $\Rightarrow$ more disorder |
| 12 | Base-Rate Fallacy Threshold | $FP_{critical} = \dfrac{Attack\,Rate \cdot Cost_{Miss}}{Cost_{FalseAlarm}}$ | Used to size alert volume |

## 2.4 The Why-How of Each Detection Methodology

### A. Signature-Based Detection (Why & How)

- **Why?** Attackers re-use tools and techniques. The same exploit produces the same byte pattern, system call sequence, or HTTP payload. Pattern matching is therefore sufficient.
- **How?** A rule engine (e.g., Snort, Suricata) compares incoming packets against a database of rules of the form:

$$\text{Header:}\{\texttt{action}\} \texttt{ proto} \texttt{ src\_ip} \texttt{ src\_port} \rightarrow \texttt{ dst\_ip} \texttt{ dst\_port}$$

followed by **payload options** (content, pcre, byte_test, etc.). The rule is essentially a finite-state automaton (FSA) or Aho-Corasick pattern matcher evaluated per packet or per reassembled flow.

- **Weakness:** *Polymorphic* and *zero-day* attacks evade signatures completely.

### B. Anomaly-Based Detection (Why & How)

- **Why?** A signature cannot exist for an attack that has never been seen. But attacks, by their nature, *deviate* from the user's normal behavior — unusual ports, large outbound flows, late-night login from a new geo.
- **How?** Build a statistical or machine-learned model of "normal" (the training baseline), then compute a *deviation score* (Z-score, Mahalanobis distance, reconstruction error in an autoencoder, or output probability of a one-class SVM) for every new event.
- **Threshold rule:** If $\vert Z \vert \ge k$ (typically $k=2.5$ or $3$), raise an alert.

### C. Stateful Protocol Analysis (Why & How)

- **Why?** Some attacks *abuse* a protocol without breaking its syntax. For example, an HTTP GET to `/admin` is syntactically valid but semantically suspicious. A pure signature engine doesn't know "normal" HTTP behavior; an anomaly engine may not either.
- **How?** Hard-code the expected state machine of a protocol (RFC 793 for TCP, RFC 2616 for HTTP/1.1) and flag any deviation (e.g., HTTP request before HTTP response, malformed TLS handshake).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production Security Operations Centers (SOCs), IDPS forms the **detection layer** of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover). They feed **SIEMs** (Splunk, QRadar, ELK), trigger **SOAR playbooks**, and form the early-warning layer for **ransomware, APT, and insider threat** detection — making them indispensable to banking, defense, healthcare, and cloud infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Optimal IDS Threshold

The single most important mathematical derivation in this module is the **derivation of the decision threshold** that minimizes the *expected cost* of an IDPS. This appears in 14-mark KTU questions almost every cycle.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Let $x$ be the observed feature vector (packet bytes, system calls, log entries). There are two hypotheses:
- $H_0$: $x$ is **benign** (a true negative in the making).
- $H_1$: $x$ is **malicious** (a true positive in the making).

Let $C_{FP}$ be the cost of a false positive and $C_{FN}$ be the cost of a false negative. In security, almost always $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$ because a missed attack can be catastrophic.

We choose a **likelihood ratio threshold** $T$ such that we accept $H_1$ if:

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{P(x \vert H_1)}{P(x \vert H_0)} \ge T$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — State the total expected cost (risk) of the decision:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0) \cdot P(\text{decide } H_1 \mid H_0) + C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1) \cdot P(\text{decide } H_0 \mid H_1) \\
  &= C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0) \cdot \int_{\Lambda(x) \ge T} P(x \mid H_0)\, dx + C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1) \cdot \int_{\Lambda(x) < T} P(x \mid H_1)\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Use the *Neyman-Pearson lemma* (1933): For a fixed false-positive rate, the test that maximizes the true-positive rate is the **likelihood-ratio test** with threshold $T$ as above. The choice of $T$ controls the trade-off.

**Step 3** — Use Bayes' theorem to convert the prior probabilities to posteriors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H_1 \mid x) &= \frac{P(x \mid H_1) P(H_1)}{P(x)} \\
P(H_0 \mid x) &= \frac{P(x \mid H_0) P(H_0)}{P(x)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Equivalently, the decision rule is to **accept $H_1$** if the posterior of attack is higher than the posterior of benign:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H_1 \mid x) \cdot C_{FN} &> P(H_0 \mid x) \cdot C_{FP} \\
\frac{P(x \mid H_1) P(H_1)}{P(x)} \cdot C_{FN} &> \frac{P(x \mid H_0) P(H_0)}{P(x)} \cdot C_{FP} \\
\frac{P(x \mid H_1)}{P(x \mid H_0)} &> \frac{C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0)}{C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5** — Therefore the **optimal threshold** is exactly:

$$
T = \frac{C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0)}{C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If attacks are **rare** ($P(H_1)$ is very small — the *base-rate fallacy*) and the cost of a miss is high ($C_{FN} \uparrow$), the threshold $T$ becomes *very large*, meaning the IDS must demand *overwhelming* evidence before raising an alert. This is why anomaly-based systems in high-volume enterprise traffic need a tuning phase.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Favourite)

Suppose a network sees $P(H_1) = 10^{-3}$ (1 attack in 1000 events), $P(H_0) = 0.999$, $C_{FP} = \$1$ (one analyst-minute wasted), $C_{FN} = \$100{,}000$ (a successful breach).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1 \cdot 0.999}{100{,}000 \cdot 10^{-3}} \\
  &= \frac{0.999}{100} \\
  &= 0.00999
\end{aligned}
$$

So the IDS should raise an alert only when the likelihood ratio is $\Lambda(x) \ge 0.00999$. In practice, this corresponds to accepting a **moderate-to-high false positive rate** in exchange for catching attacks — exactly what enterprises prefer.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Detection Approaches

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol | Hybrid (Sig + Anom) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detects **Known** attacks | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent |
| Detects **Zero-Day** | **None (0%)** | High | Moderate | High |
| False Positive Rate | **Low** | **High** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Ease of Deployment | Easy (rule-write) | Hard (training) | Moderate | Hard |
| Computational Cost | Low (string match) | High (ML inference) | Moderate | Highest |
| Update Cadence | Continuous (rules) | Periodic (re-train) | Vendor-defined | Continuous + Periodic |
| Explainability of Alerts | **High** (clear rule) | Low (score) | Moderate | Moderate |
| KTU 2024 Example | Snort, Suricata | OSQuery + ML, AWS GuardDuty | Bro/Zeek, Cisco NGIPS | Cisco Firepower, Trend Micro TippingPoint |
| Bayesian Class | Maximum-Likelihood | Posterior probability | Reject option | Ensemble voting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare signature and anomaly detection," use the **5-criterion template**: *Accuracy on known attacks, Zero-day capability, FPR, Computational cost, Maintenance overhead.* Examiners award full marks only when all five are addressed.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Z-Score Threshold

For a feature $X$ with training baseline mean $\mu$ and std. deviation $\sigma$, define the anomaly score:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Under the Gaussian assumption $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the probability of $\vert Z \vert \ge k$ for a *single* feature is:

$$
P(\vert Z \vert \ge k) = 2 \cdot \left[1 - \Phi(k)\right]
$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard-normal CDF. For $k=3$, $P \approx 0.0027$ (the famous "three-sigma rule").

**Multi-feature generalization (Bonferroni correction):** If $n$ independent features are monitored, the union false-positive rate is bounded by $n \cdot 0.0027$. So the per-feature threshold must be tightened to keep the *overall* FPR constant — illustrating the **curse of dimensionality** in anomaly detection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IDPS Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internet / Untrusted Network] --> B[Border Router]
    B --> C[Perimeter Firewall L3-L4]
    C --> D[Inline IPS Sensor]
    D --> E[Core Switch]
    E --> F[Internal LAN]
    E --> G[DMZ Servers]
    D -.alert.-> H[IDS Management Console]
    F -.syslog.-> I[Host-Based IDS Agent]
    G -.app logs.-> J[Application IDS]
    I --> H
    J --> H
    H --> K[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    H --> L[SOAR Playbook Trigger]
    K --> M[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
    L --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the two distinct placements of the IPS (inline between firewall and core switch) versus the IDS agents (out-of-band on the hosts and servers, feeding events to the central console). The dotted lines represent alert/log flows, while the solid lines represent *forwarded traffic*.

## 4.2 IDPS Detection-Engine Internal Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Packet or Log Event Arrives] --> S2[Preprocessor / Decoder]
    S2 --> S3{Protocol Parsed?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Drop or Anomaly Flag]
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Reassemble Stream if Stateful]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Signature Engine]
    S6 --> S7{Signature Match?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Generate Alert: KNOWN ATTACK]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Apply Anomaly Detector]
    S9 --> S10{Z-Score >= Threshold?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Generate Alert: ANOMALOUS BEHAVIOR]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Apply Stateful Protocol Analysis]
    S12 --> S13{Protocol Deviation?}
    S13 -- Yes --> S14[Generate Alert: PROTOCOL ABUSE]
    S13 -- No --> S15[Update Baseline / Pass]
    S8 --> S16{Inline Mode?}
    S11 --> S16
    S14 --> S16
    S15 --> S16
    S16 -- Yes --> S17[Drop Packet / Reset TCP / Block IP]
    S16 -- No --> S18[Log Only and Forward]
    S17 --> S19[End]
    S18 --> S19
    S4 --> S19
```

## 4.3 Decision-Threshold Trade-Off (The ROC Curve Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threshold = Very Low] --> A1[Almost Everything Flagged]
    A1 --> A2[FPR: 100%, DR: 100%]
    B[Threshold = Moderate] --> B1[Balanced Operation]
    B1 --> B2[FPR: 5%, DR: 90%]
    C[Threshold = Very High] --> C1[Almost Nothing Flagged]
    C1 --> C2[FPR: 0.1%, DR: 30%]
    A2 --> ROC[ROC Curve: Plots DR vs FPR]
    B2 --> ROC
    C2 --> ROC
    ROC --> D[Ideal Point: Top-Left 0,1]
    ROC --> E[Diagonal: Random Guess AUC = 0.5]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Distributed Hybrid IDPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EdgeLayer["Edge Network Layer"]
        N1[Net Sensor 1 - DMZ Tap]
        N2[Net Sensor 2 - Egress Tap]
    end
    subgraph HostLayer["Host & Application Layer"]
        H1[Host Agent - Web Server]
        H2[Host Agent - DB Server]
        A1[App Sensor - Web App Firewall]
    end
    subgraph BrainLayer["Central Analysis Layer"]
        C1[Signature Engine Cluster]
        C2[Anomaly Engine ML Pipeline]
        C3[Stateful Protocol Engine]
        C4[Correlation & Scoring Module]
    end
    subgraph ActionLayer["Response & Presentation Layer"]
        R1[Inline IPS Blocklist Updater]
        R2[SOAR Orchestrator Trigger]
        D1[SOC Dashboard]
        D2[Compliance Audit Log]
    end
    N1 --> C1
    N2 --> C1
    H1 --> C1
    H2 --> C2
    A1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> R1
    C4 --> R2
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> This four-layer functional architecture is what examiners expect when a 14-mark question asks: *"Explain the architecture of a distributed IDPS."* Always label the **sensor**, **analyzer**, **director**, and **response** components and explicitly state which layer each lives in.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Differentiate between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Placement** | Out-of-band (passive tap/span port) | Inline (bump-in-the-wire) |
| **Action on Detection** | Generates alert, logs to console | Blocks, drops, resets in real time |
| **Failure Mode** | Open — traffic continues if IDS fails | Closed — failure can drop legitimate traffic |
| **Latency Added** | Zero (it only listens) | Small (it must decide per packet) |
| **KTU 2024 Example** | Snort (IDS mode), Zeek | Snort (IPS mode), Suricata, Cisco NGIPS |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *What is the difference between a False Positive and a False Negative in the context of an IDS? Why is a False Negative considered more dangerous?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **False Positive (Type I Error):** A legitimate, benign activity is incorrectly flagged as an attack. (e.g., a backup at 3 AM flagged as exfiltration.) It causes alert fatigue and wasted analyst time.
- **False Negative (Type II Error):** A real attack slips through undetected. (e.g., a SQL-injection payload not matching any rule.) It causes actual compromise.
- **Why FN is more dangerous:** FN means the attack *succeeds* silently. The cost of one breach (data loss, regulatory fine, reputational damage) is typically 5–6 orders of magnitude greater than the cost of one wasted analyst hour on a false alert. Hence $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$, and Bayesian IDPS thresholds are set to err on the side of *more* alerts (higher DR, accepting some FPR).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose 3–4 marks** by writing generic answers like *"IDPS is a system that detects attacks"* without using the **NIST definition** or naming the four functional components (Sensor, Analyzer, Director, Response).
> - Another frequent error is **confusing the data-source axis with the detection-methodology axis**. Always present IDPS classification on **two axes** (Data Source × Detection Method) for full marks.
> - For Bayes' threshold derivations, **do not skip the step where you equate the posterior odds to the cost ratio** — most part-marks are awarded at that exact algebraic transition.
> - Avoid mixing IDS/IPS examples (e.g., "Snort is an IDS" — it is *both*; the distinction is its operational mode, not the software).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Architectural & Conceptual Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*(a)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a distributed Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)**. Label the sensor, analyzer, director, and response components, and clearly distinguish between the placement of network-based and host-based sensors. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Compare and contrast the **three principal detection methodologies** — signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis — along the dimensions of *zero-day capability, false positive rate, computational cost, maintainability,* and *alert explainability*. Give one real-world product example for each. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — *7 Marks*

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark).** Introduce IDPS using the NIST definition and explicitly state that the *distributed* form factors separate the sensors from the central analysis engine so that multiple sites or hosts can be monitored from a single SOC.

**Step 2: Enumerate the four components (2 marks).**

- **Sensor / Agent:** Passive collector of telemetry. For network sensors, this is a span-port or network tap; for host sensors, a kernel-level or user-space agent.
- **Analyzer:** The detection engine running signature, anomaly, and protocol analysis.
- **Director / Management Console:** Central UI for policies, alerts, and tuning.
- **Response Engine:** Executes actions — from logging to inline blocking.

**Step 3: Diagram (3 marks).** Reproduce the four-layer architecture from **Section 4.4** above. Make sure the diagram shows the **inline placement of the network IPS** (between firewall and core switch) versus the **out-of-band host agent placements** (on web and DB servers).

**Step 4: Distinguish placement (1 mark).** NIPS is inline; HIPS can be inline or out-of-band depending on whether it sits on a host acting as a gateway or as a simple monitor. NIDS sensors must be placed at *aggregation points* (egress router, DMZ, server farm core) to see all relevant traffic.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — *7 Marks*

Use a 5-row comparison table (the one from Section 3.2), but **expand the explanation for each row into 1–2 sentences** to earn full 7 marks. Then add a concluding paragraph (1 mark) that states: *"Hybrid systems combine all three methodologies to trade off their weaknesses — signature for known-attack accuracy, anomaly for zero-day, and stateful analysis for protocol-aware defense-in-depth."*

Real-world examples to cite:
- **Signature:** Snort, Suricata
- **Anomaly:** AWS GuardDuty, Darktrace
- **Stateful:** Zeek (Bro), Cisco NGIPS
- **Hybrid:** TippingPoint, Firepower, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Mathematical & Analytical Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**
*(a)* Derive the **optimal Bayesian decision threshold** for an IDPS that minimizes expected cost. Define all terms clearly and show each algebraic step. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* An enterprise IDS is monitoring HTTP traffic. The probability that a given request is malicious is $P(H_1) = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. The cost of a false negative (a successful breach) is estimated at **₹50,00,000**, and the cost of a false positive (an analyst investigation) is **₹500**. Compute the optimal likelihood-ratio threshold $T$, and state in plain English what this threshold means operationally. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — *7 Marks*

**Step 1: Hypotheses & Costs (1 mark).** Define $H_0$ (benign) and $H_1$ (malicious), costs $C_{FP}$ and $C_{FN}$, and priors $P(H_0)$ and $P(H_1)$.

**Step 2: Risk functional (2 marks).** Write the expected cost $R$ as a sum of two integrals (false-positive term plus false-negative term) as shown in Section 3.1.2.

**Step 3: Likelihood ratio (1 mark).** Introduce $\Lambda(x) = P(x \vert H_1)/P(x \vert H_0)$.

**Step 4: Equate posteriors to cost ratio (2 marks).** Show the chain:

$$P(H_1 \mid x) C_{FN} > P(H_0 \mid x) C_{FP}$$

Apply Bayes' theorem and cancel $P(x)$ to obtain:

$$\Lambda(x) > \frac{C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0)}{C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1)}$$

**Step 5: Conclude (1 mark).** State the **optimal threshold**:

$$T = \frac{C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0)}{C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1)}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — *7 Marks*

**Step 1: Extract the given values (1 mark).**

$$
P(H_1) = 5 \times 10^{-4}, \quad P(H_0) = 1 - 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9995
$$
$$
C_{FN} = 5{,}000{,}000, \quad C_{FP} = 500
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the threshold formula (3 marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0)}{C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1)} \\
  &= \frac{500 \times 0.9995}{5{,}000{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
  &= \frac{499.75}{2500} \\
  &= 0.1999 \approx 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Operational interpretation (3 marks).**
- The IDS should raise an alert only when the likelihood that the request is malicious is at least **0.2 times** (i.e., 1/5th of) the likelihood that it is benign, after weighting by priors.
- In practical terms, this is a *low* threshold — the system is configured to be **sensitive** and will tolerate some false positives in order not to miss attacks.
- A lower $T$ ⇒ more alerts (higher DR, higher FPR). A higher $T$ ⇒ fewer alerts (lower DR, lower FPR). This single number is the **tuning knob** the SOC analyst adjusts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **IDS = detect & alert** (passive); **IPS = detect & block** (inline). Single most important distinction.
- [ ] **NIST four components** of any IDPS: **Sensor, Analyzer, Director, Response.** Memorize these words verbatim.
- [ ] **Classification on two axes:** Data Source (NIDS / HIDS / AppIDS / Hybrid) × Detection Method (Signature / Anomaly / Stateful / Hybrid).
- [ ] **Signature-based:** high accuracy on *known* attacks, **zero** detection for zero-days, low FPR, easy to update.
- [ ] **Anomaly-based:** high detection for *zero-days*, high FPR, requires training baseline, costly.
- [ ] **Stateful protocol analysis:** catches protocol-level abuse (HTTP smuggling, TLS anomalies) that signatures miss.
- [ ] **Confusion matrix** must be remembered: TP, TN, FP, FN, and the derived metrics DR, FPR, PPV, F1.
- [ ] **Type I error = False Positive (Crying Wolf)**, **Type II error = False Negative (Real Wolf)** — examiners love this.
- [ ] **Bayes optimal threshold** $T = \dfrac{C_{FP} \cdot P(H_0)}{C_{FN} \cdot P(H_1)}$ — must be derived in full, not just stated.
- [ ] **Base-rate fallacy:** attacks are rare ($P(H_1) \ll 1$); naïve thresholding produces a flood of false positives.
- [ ] **ROC curve** plots Detection Rate (y-axis) vs. False Positive Rate (x-axis); AUC = 1 is perfect, 0.5 is random.
- [ ] **Z-score rule:** $\vert Z \vert \ge 3$ corresponds to a 0.27% per-feature false-positive probability under Gaussian assumption.
- [ ] **Real-world products to memorize:** Snort (signature), Suricata (signature + multi-thread), Zeek/Bro (stateful), Darktrace (anomaly/ML), Cisco Firepower (hybrid), Snort inline mode (IPS).
- [ ] **Inline failure mode of IPS = fail-open or fail-closed**, depending on policy; this is a high-yield one-liner.
- [ ] **Defense-in-depth:** IDPS is **not** a replacement for firewalls, antivirus, or IAM — it is the *detection* layer that complements them.
- [ ] Always state assumptions (e.g., *Gaussian features, equal priors, conditional independence*) before applying any formula — examiners reward rigor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDPS Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IDPS Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of IDPS Terminology

**Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is a security solution designed to monitor, analyze, and respond to malicious activities targeting information systems. The terminology surrounding IDPS forms the foundational vocabulary for understanding modern cybersecurity operations.

### Core Term Definitions (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition:**
> An **Intrusion** is any unauthorized activity that compromises the **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad)** of an information system. An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a software or hardware system that automates the process of monitoring events occurring in a computer system or network, analyzing them for signs of security problems. An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** extends IDS functionality by actively blocking or preventing detected intrusions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight - Module 1:**
> As per the PECCT742 2024 scheme syllabus, Module 1 requires mastery of: (i) Attack vs Intrusion, (ii) IDS vs IPS, (iii) Host-Based vs Network-Based systems, (iv) Signature-based vs Anomaly-based detection, (v) True/False Positives/Negatives, and (vi) Performance metrics like Detection Rate and False Alarm Rate.

## 1.2 The Four Fundamental Alert States

Every IDPS decision yields exactly one of four outcomes. These are the **cornerstones of detection theory**:

| State | Description | Reality | IDPS Verdict |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **True Positive (TP)** | Attack present, system alarmed | Intrusion | Correct Alarm |
| **False Positive (FP)** | No attack, system alarmed | Benign | False Alarm |
| **True Negative (TN)** | No attack, system silent | Benign | Correct Silence |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Attack present, system silent | Intrusion | Missed Attack |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** "Positive = Alarm Rings, Negative = Silent". Then "True = Correct, False = Wrong".

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Imagine a Smoke Detector in your house:**

- The **smoke detector** is analogous to an **IDS** — it only *detects* smoke and beeps (raises an *alert*).
- A **fire sprinkler system** is analogous to an **IPS** — it detects smoke *and* actively *responds* by spraying water (preventing further damage).
- A **false positive** is when your toast burns and the alarm rings — no real fire, but the alarm went off.
- A **false negative** is when a smoldering fire starts in the basement but the detector fails to ring — extremely dangerous!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Trade-off:** A security guard who raises the alarm at every passing pedestrian (high **False Positives**) is a nuisance and loses credibility. A guard who sleeps through a real thief (high **False Negatives**) is useless. IDPS engineering is the art of balancing these two error types.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard IDPS Performance Metrics:**
> - **Detection Rate (DR)** or **True Positive Rate (TPR)** = 100% (ideal)
> - **False Alarm Rate (FAR)** = 0% (ideal)
> - **Precision** = high (when alarm rings, it should be real)
> - **Recall** = high (should catch most real attacks)
> - These are typically expressed as **percentages (%)** or **probabilities (0 to 1)**.

---

## 1.5 Attack vs Intrusion — A Subtle but Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> - **Attack**: The *attempt* or *action* performed by a threat agent to compromise a system (e.g., sending a malformed packet).
> - **Intrusion**: A *successful* attack that has breached security policy and gained unauthorized access or caused damage.
> - Not every attack becomes an intrusion — many are blocked by firewalls or fail naturally.

**Example:** A port scan is an **attack**. If the scanner gains shell access, that is now an **intrusion**.

## 1.6 IDS vs IPS — Operational Difference

| Feature | IDS (Intrusion Detection System) | IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Mode** | Passive (monitors and alerts) | Active (monitors and blocks) |
| **Position** | Out-of-band (TAP/SPAN port) | In-line (traffic flows through it) |
| **Action on detection** | Logs and notifies | Drops packets, resets sessions |
| **Risk** | Low (cannot break traffic) | Higher (failure = DoS) |
| **Analogy** | CCTV camera | Security checkpoint |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IDPS Operational Pipeline

Every IDPS, regardless of vendor or architecture, executes a **three-stage pipeline**:

1. **Data Collection (Sensors):** Capture raw network packets, host logs, or system calls.
2. **Data Analysis (Analyzers/Detectors):** Apply detection algorithms (signature, anomaly, stateful protocol analysis).
3. **Response (Managers/Action Units):** Generate alerts, log events, or block traffic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that all terminology must be understood in the context of this pipeline. A "false positive" originates in Stage 2, propagates to Stage 3, and impacts the SOC analyst.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Detection Theory Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for PECCT742 Module 1 (Direct 7–14 mark potential):**

### 2.2.1 Detection Rate (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)

$$\text{Detection Rate (DR)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

This represents the **fraction of actual intrusions that the IDPS successfully detects**. A perfect IDPS has **DR = 1.0 (100%)**.

### 2.2.2 False Alarm Rate (False Positive Rate)

$$\text{False Alarm Rate (FAR)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

This is the fraction of **benign events incorrectly classified as intrusions**. A perfect IDPS has **FAR = 0**.

### 2.2.3 Miss Rate (False Negative Rate)

$$\text{Miss Rate} = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = 1 - \text{Detection Rate}$$

### 2.2.4 Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** High precision means that **when the alarm rings, you should investigate** — there is a high probability it is a real attack. Low precision causes "alert fatigue" in SOC analysts.

### 2.2.5 F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

This is the **balanced score** combining precision and recall, widely used in academic IDPS benchmarking datasets like **NSL-KDD** and **CICIDS-2017**.

### 2.2.6 Bayes' Theorem — Base Rate Fallacy

$$P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack})}{P(\text{Alert})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Even a 99% accurate IDPS can be practically useless in a low-attack environment. If only 1 in 10,000 packets is malicious, an alert is **more likely to be a false alarm than a real attack** — this is the **Base Rate Fallacy**.

## 2.3 KTU Master Cheat Sheet — All Key Terminology

| # | Term | Full Form | KTU-Standard Definition |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **IDS** | Intrusion Detection System | Passive monitor that detects and alerts |
| 2 | **IPS** | Intrusion Prevention System | Active system that detects and blocks |
| 3 | **IDPS** | Intrusion Detection & Prevention System | Combined detection + prevention |
| 4 | **HIDS** | Host-Based IDS | Monitors single host (logs, syscalls) |
| 5 | **NIDS** | Network-Based IDS | Monitors network traffic |
| 6 | **SIDS** | Signature-Based IDS | Matches known attack patterns |
| 7 | **AIDS** | Anomaly-Based IDS | Detects deviations from baseline |
| 8 | **TP** | True Positive | Real attack, correctly alarmed |
| 9 | **FP** | False Positive | Benign, falsely alarmed |
| 10 | **TN** | True Negative | Benign, correctly silent |
| 11 | **FN** | False Negative | Real attack, missed (dangerous!) |
| 12 | **DR** | Detection Rate | TP / (TP + FN) |
| 13 | **FAR** | False Alarm Rate | FP / (FP + TN) |
| 14 | **CIA** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | The triad protected by IDPS |
| 15 | **SOC** | Security Operations Center | Team consuming IDPS alerts |
| 16 | **DoS** | Denial of Service | Attack type IDPS must detect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When defining terms in Part A questions, use the *full form first*, then the abbreviation, then the precise one-line definition. This 3-step pattern is what earns full 3 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The terminology defined in Module 1 is **not academic fluff** — it directly maps to:

- **Vendor Evaluation (Cisco, Palo Alto, Snort, Suricata):** Procurement teams compare IDPS products using DR and FAR on standard datasets.
- **SOC Operations:** Tier-1 analysts triage alerts; their KPIs are measured in **MTTD** (Mean Time to Detect) which depends on TP/FN ratio.
- **Compliance:** Standards like **PCI-DSS 11.4** and **ISO 27001 A.12.4** mandate IDS/IPS deployment with measurable detection performance.
- **Machine Learning Research:** New IDPS papers in IEEE S&P, ACM CCS, and USENIX Security *must* report the metrics from this module on standard datasets (KDD-99, NSL-KDD, CICIDS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Detection Rate and Miss Rate Sum to Unity

We start from the fundamental identity that **every actual intrusion must result in either detection (TP) or being missed (FN)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Actual Intrusions} &= TP + FN \\
\text{Detection Rate (DR)} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
\text{Miss Rate} &= \frac{FN}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding both rates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR + \text{Miss Rate} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} + \frac{FN}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{TP + FN}{TP + FN} \\
&= 1.0 \;\; \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This proves that **increasing detection rate mathematically forces a decrease in miss rate**, and vice versa. A perfect IDPS has DR = 1 and Miss Rate = 0.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Base Rate Fallacy (Bayes' Theorem Application)

This is the **most important derivation in Module 1** for KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) questions.

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Style):**
A network has **1,000,000 events per day**, of which **100 are actual attacks**. An IDPS has a **99% detection rate (sensitivity)** and a **1% false alarm rate**. Calculate the probability that an alert is a *real attack*.

### Step 1: Define Prior Probabilities

$$
P(\text{Attack}) = \frac{100}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.0001 = 10^{-4}
$$

$$
P(\text{Benign}) = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999
$$

### Step 2: Define Likelihoods (from IDPS performance)

$$
P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) = 0.99 \quad \text{(sensitivity)}
$$

$$
P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Benign}) = 0.01 \quad \text{(false alarm rate)}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Total Probability of an Alert

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Alert}) &= P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack}) + P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Benign}) \cdot P(\text{Benign}) \\
&= (0.99)(0.0001) + (0.01)(0.9999) \\
&= 0.000099 + 0.009999 \\
&= 0.010098
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Bayes' Theorem

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) &= \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack})}{P(\text{Alert})} \\
&= \frac{(0.99)(0.0001)}{0.010098} \\
&= \frac{0.000099}{0.010098} \\
&\approx 0.0098 \approx 0.98\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mind-Blowing Result:** Even with **99% sensitivity**, an alert has only a **0.98% chance** of being a real attack! This is the **Base Rate Fallacy** — and it is a favorite KTU 14-mark question. The takeaway is: **a high accuracy score is meaningless without considering the base rate of attacks**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Confusion Matrix Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):**
> An IDPS processes 1,000 events. The confusion matrix observed is: **TP = 80, FP = 90, FN = 20, TN = 810.** Compute DR, FAR, Precision, F1.

### Solution Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP + FP + FN + TN &= 80 + 90 + 20 + 810 = 1000 \quad \checkmark \\
DR &= \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 = 80\% \\
FAR &= \frac{90}{90 + 810} = \frac{90}{900} = 0.10 = 10\% \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{80}{80 + 90} = \frac{80}{170} \approx 0.4706 = 47.06\% \\
\text{Recall (DR)} &= 80\% \\
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.4706 \times 0.80}{0.4706 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.3765}{1.2706} \approx 0.5926 = 59.26\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Signature vs Anomaly Detection

| Parameter | Signature-Based (Misuse) | Anomaly-Based (Statistical) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Detection basis** | Known attack patterns (signatures) | Deviation from learned baseline |
| **Strength** | High precision, low FP | Can detect **zero-day** attacks |
| **Weakness** | Cannot detect novel attacks | Higher false positive rate |
| **Speed** | Fast (string matching) | Slower (statistical computation) |
| **Maintenance** | Requires frequent signature updates | Requires periodic baseline retraining |
| **Data dependency** | Needs signature database | Needs clean training data |
| **Example** | Snort rules | Statistical payload analysis, ML models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Modern commercial IDPS (e.g., Cisco Firepower) use a **hybrid approach** — signature detection for known threats + anomaly detection for zero-days.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: HIDS vs NIDS

| Parameter | HIDS (Host-Based) | NIDS (Network-Based) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Monitoring scope** | Single host | Entire network segment |
| **Data source** | OS logs, syscalls, file integrity | Packets, flow records |
| **Encrypted traffic** | Can see decrypted data | Cannot inspect encrypted payloads |
| **Deployment cost** | High (per-host agent) | Low (one sensor per segment) |
| **Switched networks** | Not affected | Requires SPAN/TAP ports |
| **Detects local rootkits** | Yes | No |
| **Example** | OSSEC, Tripwire | Snort, Suricata, Zeek |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IDPS Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Traffic Stream] --> B[Sensor / Packet Capture]
    C[Host Logs and Syscalls] --> B
    B --> D[Preprocessor and Decoder]
    D --> E[Detection Engine]
    E --> F[Signature Matcher]
    E --> G[Anomaly Detector]
    E --> H[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]
    F --> I[Decision Engine]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Is it an Intrusion?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Response Module]
    J -- No --> L[Log and Discard]
    K --> M[Alert Generation]
    K --> N[Packet Drop / Block]
    K --> O[Session Reset]
    M --> P[SOC Dashboard]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    L --> Q[Silent Pass]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The flow moves left-to-right. Raw data enters at **A** and **C** (network and host sources), is processed by the **detection engine (E)**, and either triggers a response (K → M/N/O) or is silently passed (L). The SOC analyst views results at P.

## 4.2 IDPS Terminology Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[IDPS Terminology Module 1]
    Root --> P1[System Types]
    Root --> P2[Detection Theory]
    Root --> P3[Detection Approaches]
    Root --> P4[Deployment Scope]
    P1 --> IDS[Intrusion Detection System]
    P1 --> IPS[Intrusion Prevention System]
    P1 --> IDPS2[Combined IDPS]
    P2 --> TP[True Positive]
    P2 --> FP[False Positive]
    P2 --> TN[True Negative]
    P2 --> FN[False Negative]
    P2 --> DR[Detection Rate Metric]
    P2 --> FAR[False Alarm Rate Metric]
    P3 --> S[Signature Based Detection]
    P3 --> An[Anomaly Based Detection]
    P3 --> St[Stateful Protocol Analysis]
    P4 --> HN[Host Based HIDS]
    P4 --> NN[Network Based NIDS]
    S --> Use[Used for Known Attacks]
    An --> Use2[Used for Zero Day Attacks]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **This hierarchical map is the exact structure you should reproduce in 14-mark Part B descriptive answers.** Examiners reward clear taxonomy drawings.

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix Visual (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Reality [Reality / Ground Truth]
        R1[Actually Intrusion]
        R2[Actually Benign]
    end
    subgraph Decision [IDPS Decision]
        D1[Alarm Raised]
        D2[No Alarm]
    end
    R1 --> D1
    R1 --> D2
    R2 --> D1
    R2 --> D2
    D1 --> TP[True Positive Correct Alarm]
    D1 --> FP[False Positive False Alarm]
    D2 --> FN[False Negative Missed Attack]
    D2 --> TN[True Negative Correct Silence]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the matrix:** Top-left cell = **TP**, top-right = **FP**, bottom-left = **FN**, bottom-right = **TN**. This 2x2 structure is the **foundation of every IDPS evaluation** and appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A has 5 questions of 3 marks each, drawn from all modules. Below are 2 representative Module 1 questions.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate clearly between an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS). Mention any two operational differences.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Structured as Required):**

1. **Definition:**
   - **IDS (Intrusion Detection System):** A security mechanism that *passively monitors* network or host activity, analyzes it for signs of malicious behavior, and *raises alerts* when suspicious activity is detected. It does **not** alter the traffic flow.
   - **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System):** A security mechanism that *actively monitors* traffic and *takes automated preventive action* (such as dropping malicious packets or terminating sessions) when an intrusion is detected.

2. **Two Operational Differences:**

| Aspect | IDS | IPS |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Traffic handling | Operates out-of-band (TAP/SPAN) | Operates in-line in the data path |
| Action on detection | Logs and alerts only | Blocks, drops, or resets sessions |

3. **Conclusion:** IDS is reactive in observation; IPS is proactive in enforcement. *(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define the terms: (i) True Positive, (ii) False Positive, (iii) False Negative. Why is a False Negative considered more dangerous than a False Positive?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **(i) True Positive (TP):** The IDPS raises an alarm and the event is genuinely an intrusion. *(1 mark)*
- **(ii) False Positive (FP):** The IDPS raises an alarm, but the event is actually benign. *(1 mark)*
- **(iii) False Negative (FN):** The IDPS does not raise an alarm, but the event is actually an intrusion. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** A False Negative is more dangerous because the real attack goes undetected, allowing the attacker continued access, data exfiltration, or system compromise. False Positives cause alert fatigue but do not directly enable breaches.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B has a choice between two questions from the same module. Each carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a generic IDPS**. List the major components and describe the role of each in the detection pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An organization observes the following IDPS evaluation results over one month: **True Positives (TP) = 150, False Positives (FP) = 50, False Negatives (FN) = 30, True Negatives (TN) = 970.**
Compute: **(i) Detection Rate**, **(ii) False Alarm Rate**, **(iii) Precision**, **(iv) F1-Score**. Comment on the system's effectiveness. **(7 Marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Components of Generic IDPS (with explanations):**

1. **Sensors / Data Sources (1 mark):** Collect raw data from network packets, host logs, system calls, or application logs. May be network taps, host agents, or log forwarders.

2. **Preprocessor / Decoder (1 mark):** Normalizes the raw input, reassembles TCP streams, decodes protocols (e.g., HTTP, DNS) into a structured format the detection engine can analyze.

3. **Detection Engine / Analyzer (2 marks):** The "brain" of the IDPS. Applies:
   - Signature matching (known attack patterns)
   - Anomaly detection (statistical / ML deviation)
   - Stateful protocol analysis (deviation from RFC behavior)

4. **Decision / Alert Engine (1 mark):** Classifies the analyzed event as benign or malicious and generates appropriate output.

5. **Response / Action Module (1 mark):** For IPS, this performs blocking/dropping. For IDS, this logs and forwards alerts to the SOC.

6. **Management Console (1 mark):** Allows the security administrator to configure rules, view alerts, and tune thresholds.

**Diagram:** *(Reproduce the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes — examiners award 1 mark for a clean diagram with arrows.)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:** TP = 150, FP = 50, FN = 30, TN = 970

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (DR):**

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{150 + 30} = \frac{150}{180} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**Step 2 — False Alarm Rate (FAR):**

$$
FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{50}{50 + 970} = \frac{50}{1020} = 0.0490 = 4.90\%
$$

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{150}{150 + 50} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.75 \times 0.8333}{0.75 + 0.8333} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.625}{1.5833} = 0.7895 = 78.95\%
$$

**Comment on Effectiveness (1 mark):**
The system has a **high detection rate of 83.33%** and a **low false alarm rate of 4.90%**, indicating good sensitivity with manageable noise. Precision of 75% means 3 out of 4 alerts are real. An F1-score of ~79% is good for a production IDPS, though there is room for improvement in reducing the 30 missed attacks (FN) via anomaly-based augmentation.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **Base Rate Fallacy** in IDPS with a suitable numerical example. Why is it important for a security analyst to understand this concept? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Signature-based** and **Anomaly-based** intrusion detection. State one real-world product/example for each and identify which approach is better suited for detecting **zero-day attacks**. **(7 Marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
The **Base Rate Fallacy** is the statistical phenomenon where the practical utility of an IDPS is severely limited when the actual base rate of attacks in the environment is very low, even if the system's detection accuracy is high. It arises from a misapplication of Bayes' Theorem in interpreting alert probability.

**Numerical Example (3 marks):**
Assume a network with **100,000 events per day** and **50 actual attacks** (base rate = 0.05%). Suppose an IDPS has **95% sensitivity** and **2% false alarm rate**.

- P(Attack) = 0.0005
- P(Benign) = 0.9995
- P(Alert | Attack) = 0.95
- P(Alert | Benign) = 0.02

Applying Bayes' Theorem:

$$
P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{(0.95)(0.0005)}{(0.95)(0.0005) + (0.02)(0.9995)} = \frac{0.000475}{0.000475 + 0.01999} \approx 2.32\%
$$

So an alert has only a **2.32% chance** of being a real attack.

**Importance for Security Analyst (1 mark):**
Analysts must understand this to **avoid dismissing alerts as noise** (since most alerts are FP) while still investigating a sufficient volume to catch the rare real attacks. It justifies the deployment of **alert correlation and SIEM tools** to combine weak signals.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Detection basis | Pattern database of known attacks | Deviation from statistical / ML baseline |
| Zero-day capability | Cannot detect (1 mark) | Can detect (1 mark) |
| False positive rate | Low | High |
| Maintenance | Signature updates | Periodic baseline retraining |
| Real-world example | **Snort** (with VRT ruleset) | **Cisco Secure Network Analytics** (formerly Stealthwatch) or ML-based systems |

**Conclusion on Zero-Day (2 marks):**
**Anomaly-based detection is better suited for zero-day attacks** because it does not depend on prior knowledge of the attack signature. It detects *behavioral anomalies*, which is the only way to identify attacks that have never been seen before. However, modern best practice is to deploy a **hybrid IDS** (e.g., Suricata with ET Pro rules + ML plugin) to combine the strengths of both approaches.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECCT742 Module 1:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS and IPS in definitions** — Examiners deduct marks if you write that an IDS "blocks" traffic. IDS only *alerts*; IPS *blocks*.
> 2. **Confusing HIDS with HIPS and NIDS with NIPS** — Know that the "P" suffix changes operational behavior (active vs passive).
> 3. **Forgetting units in metric calculations** — Always state whether the answer is in fraction (0.83) or percentage (83%).
> 4. **Mixing up TP/FP/TN/FN positions** — In the 2x2 confusion matrix, the *columns* are IDPS decision and *rows* are ground truth. Getting this swapped = full mark loss on the question.
> 5. **Skipping the Bayes' derivation steps in the Base Rate Fallacy** — You must explicitly write P(Alert) = P(Alert|Attack)·P(Attack) + P(Alert|Benign)·P(Benign) before applying Bayes.
> 6. **Writing "Intrusion" instead of "Attack"** in the Attack vs Intrusion distinction — An *attempt* is an attack; a *successful breach* is an intrusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision for KTU Exam — High-Density Summary:**

- ✅ **IDS = Detect + Alert (Passive)** | **IPS = Detect + Block (Active, In-line)**
- ✅ **HIDS = Per-host monitoring** | **NIDS = Per-network-segment monitoring**
- ✅ **Signature = Known attacks (low FP, no zero-day)** | **Anomaly = Unknown attacks (high FP, detects zero-day)**
- ✅ **TP, FP, TN, FN** are the four outcomes of every IDPS decision
- ✅ **Detection Rate = TP / (TP + FN)** — higher is better
- ✅ **False Alarm Rate = FP / (FP + TN)** — lower is better
- ✅ **Precision = TP / (TP + FP)** — quality of alerts
- ✅ **F1 = 2·(Precision·Recall) / (Precision + Recall)** — balanced score
- ✅ **Attack = attempt** | **Intrusion = successful breach** — different terms
- ✅ **Base Rate Fallacy = Bayes' Theorem** — high accuracy ≠ high alert value in low-attack environments
- ✅ **CIA Triad = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** — what IDPS protects
- ✅ **Hybrid IDPS = Signature + Anomaly** — modern best practice
- ✅ **Memorize the formula sheet in Section 2.2** — these six formulas cover 80% of Part B numerical questions
- ✅ **Always reproduce the 2x2 confusion matrix** in descriptive answers — examiners reward visualizations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Note:** Module 1 carries ~15–20% of the total paper weightage. Master the *terminology* (definitions + differences) and the *metric calculations* (Bayes + confusion matrix). These are the **two highest-yield, lowest-effort** topics for guaranteed marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Attacks - Probes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Attacks - Probes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Probes** are a class of **reconnaissance attacks** in which an adversary transmits carefully crafted packets to a target system or network to elicit responses that disclose **topological, structural, or vulnerability-related information**. According to KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT742), probes are categorized as the *first phase* of the **Cyber Kill Chain** and are formally defined under RFC 2828 (Internet Security Glossary) as *"an attack that is designed to return information about a network or its hosts."*

In the formal taxonomy of the **Common Attack Pattern Enumeration and Classification (CAPEC)** repository, probes fall under **CAPEC-169: Footprinting** and **CAPEC-300: Port Scanning**. The attacker (often called the *prober* or *reconnaissance agent*) seeks to map the **attack surface** without yet executing a payload.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology You MUST Memorize:**
> - **Attack Surface** — The sum of all points (open ports, services, protocols) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data.
> - **Reconnaissance** — The pre-attack phase of gathering intelligence about a target.
> - **Fingerprinting** — Identifying the OS, software version, or device type of a target host based on its response behavior.
> - **Stealth Scan** — A probe technique designed to avoid detection by logging systems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Burglar Knocking on Windows"

Imagine a thief walking down a residential street at night. Before attempting to break in, he walks up to each house and:
1. **Taps on windows** (does the window rattle? Is it locked?)
2. **Tries the doorknob** (does the door open easily?)
3. **Listens for a dog bark** (are there defensive systems?)
4. **Checks for security cameras** (are there detection systems?)

He does **not** break anything yet — he is simply *probing*. The "windows" are **TCP/UDP ports**, the "door" is the **open service**, the "dog" is the **firewall/IDS**, and the "cameras" are the **logging systems**. A Network Probe is the **digital equivalent** of this cautious burglar — it transmits packets to discover which services are listening, which ports respond, and which defenses are active.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** *Probes = "Knock-Knock" Attacks.* They ask questions; they don't yet break in. The **response** is what gives away the information.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Quantitative Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard metrics are used by KTU examiners when evaluating probe attacks:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Port Count** | $N_p$ | 1 to 65,535 | Number of destination ports queried per host |
| **Probe Rate** | $R_p$ | 1–100,000 packets/sec | Speed of scanning (stealth = low rate) |
| **Inter-Probe Delay (IPD)** | $\Delta t$ | 0–3600 seconds | Time gap between consecutive probes |
| **Source Spoofing** | $S_{sp}$ | Boolean (True/False) | Whether source IP is forged |
| **Response Rate** | $R_r$ | 0%–100% | Fraction of probes eliciting a reply |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Network Probes (High-Yield for KTU)

Probes are classified by the **protocol layer** they exploit and the **technique** they employ:

**A. ICMP-Based Probes (Network Layer — Layer 3)**
- **Ping Sweep** (`ICMP Echo Request / Type 8`)
- **ICMP Timestamp Request** (Type 13)
- **ICMP Address Mask Request** (Type 17)
- **Traceroute Probes** (UDP/ICMP TTL Exceeded)

**B. TCP-Based Probes (Transport Layer — Layer 4)**
- **SYN Scan (Half-Open Scan)** — Sends `SYN`, awaits `SYN-ACK`
- **FIN Scan** — Sends `FIN`, awaits `RST`
- **XMAS Scan** — Sets `FIN+PSH+URG` flags
- **NULL Scan** — Sends packet with **no flags set**
- **ACK Scan** — Used to map firewall rule sets
- **Window Scan** — Examines TCP window field in response

**C. UDP-Based Probes (Transport Layer — Layer 4)**
- **UDP Port Scan** — Sends UDP datagram, awaits `ICMP Port Unreachable`

**D. Application Layer Probes (Layer 7)**
- **Banner Grabbing** — Retrieves service banners (SSH, HTTP, FTP)
- **OS Fingerprinting** — Uses TCP/IP stack behavior
- **SNMP Probes** — Queries `public` community string
- **Version Detection** — Identifies service versions (e.g., Apache 2.4.41)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Probes* with *Exploits*. A **probe** only *discovers*; an **exploit** actually *leverages* a discovered vulnerability. Probes are the *reconnaissance phase*; they precede exploitation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How a Probe Works

Every network probe, regardless of type, follows the same **three-step operational logic**:

1. **Transmission Phase (Crafting the Request):** The prober constructs a packet targeting a specific IP address and port. The packet may be **legitimate-looking** (e.g., a normal `SYN` for a TCP connect) or **malformed** (e.g., an `XMAS` packet with all flags lit). The destination is selected from a **target list** (a single host, a subnet, or a random range).

2. **Observation Phase (Capturing the Response):** The prober listens passively for **response packets** from the target. Different responses convey different meanings:
   - `SYN-ACK` → Port is **OPEN** (service listening)
   - `RST-ACK` → Port is **CLOSED** (no service)
   - **No response / ICMP Type 3 Code 3** → Port is **FILTERED** (firewall drop)
   - `ICMP Type 3 Code 1, 2, 3, 9, 10, 13` → Various filtered states

3. **Inference Phase (Building the Map):** The prober aggregates responses to construct an **attack-surface map**, often visualized as:
   - A list of open ports
   - An inferred OS fingerprint
   - A list of running service versions
   - A topology map (via traceroute)

---

## 2.2 Mathematical & Quantitative Framework

### 2.2.1 The Coverage Time Equation

For a scan covering $N_p$ ports on $N_h$ hosts at a probe rate of $R_p$ packets/sec:

$$
T_{scan} = \frac{N_p \times N_h}{R_p}
$$

> This is the **fundamental KTU formula** for estimating scan duration. Students must understand that increasing **stealth** (lower $R_p$) proportionally **increases scan time** — a critical trade-off attackers must manage.

### 2.2.2 Detection Probability Model

A network probe is detected by an IDS with probability modeled as:

$$
P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - p_d)^{\frac{T_{scan}}{\Delta t_{window}}}
$$

Where:
- $p_d$ = per-packet detection probability
- $T_{scan}$ = total scan duration
- $\Delta t_{window}$ = IDS analysis window (e.g., 60 seconds)

> **Real-World Utility:** This equation is used by **red teams** to design stealthy scans and by **blue teams** to size their IDS sampling rates. At KTU level, you may be asked to compute the **detection probability for a 65535-port SYN scan over 24 hours at 1 pkt/sec**.

### 2.2.3 Shannon's Information Gain from a Probe

Each probe response yields information. The **entropy reduction** from a successful probe is:

$$
\Delta H = \log_2(N_s) - \log_2(N_{s,after})
$$

Where $N_s$ is the number of possible service states before the probe, and $N_{s,after}$ is the reduced state space afterward. A scan converting "unknown" (1 state) to "open/closed/filtered" (3 states) **informs** the attacker.

### 2.2.4 Stealth Index

To quantify how "quiet" a scan is:

$$
SI = \frac{1}{R_p \times N_{flags,abnormal}}
$$

Where $N_{flags,abnormal}$ counts the number of unusual flag combinations (high for XMAS/NULL, low for SYN).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Save this table — these formulas appear frequently in KTU Module 1 derivations.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = \frac{N_p N_h}{R_p}$ | $N_p$ = ports, $N_h$ = hosts, $R_p$ = rate | seconds | Estimating total scan duration |
| 2 | **Detection Probability** | $P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - p_d)^{T_{scan}/\Delta t}$ | $p_d$ = per-packet detection rate | dimensionless | IDS effectiveness analysis |
| 3 | **Information Gain** | $\Delta H = \log_2(N_s) - \log_2(N_{s,after})$ | $N_s$ = state space | bits | Quantifying recon intel |
| 4 | **Stealth Index** | $SI = \frac{1}{R_p \cdot N_{abnormal}}$ | $N_{abnormal}$ = flag anomaly count | dimensionless | Comparing scan stealthiness |
| 5 | **Coverage Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \frac{N_{hits}}{N_p \cdot N_h}$ | $N_{hits}$ = informative responses | dimensionless | Scan productivity ratio |
| 6 | **Probe Bandwidth** | $B_p = R_p \times S_p \times 8$ | $S_p$ = avg packet size | bits/sec | Network load estimation |
| 7 | **False Positive Rate (IDS)** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | FP = false positives, TN = true negatives | dimensionless | IDS accuracy metric |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** In your answer sheets, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute value — never $\mid x \mid$ inside tables, as the pipe breaks Markdown parsing.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematics of probes is **not academic** — it directly drives:
- **Vulnerability Management Tools** (Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS) — they use probe-rate optimization to balance speed vs. detection.
- **IDS/IPS Tuning** (Snort, Suricata, Zeek) — engineers use $P_{detect}$ to size sampling windows.
- **Network Telemetry Analytics** (NetFlow, sFlow) — SIEM platforms apply $\Delta H$ to score reconnaissance anomalies.
- **Penetration Testing Methodology** (PTES, OWASP) — professional red teams use $T_{scan}$ to schedule engagements.

> In KTU's **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, mastering these equations demonstrates **CO1: Apply mathematical foundations** for the PECCT742 course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Total Scan Time for a Subnet Sweep

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** *A penetration tester wants to scan all 65,535 ports of 254 hosts in a `/24` subnet using a Nmap SYN scan. The probe rate is set to 500 packets/second. Compute the total scan time, and then compute the new scan time if the tester halves the rate for stealth.*

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**

We are given:
- $N_p = 65{,}535$ ports
- $N_h = 254$ hosts
- $R_p = 500$ packets/second

We must find $T_{scan}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Scan Time Equation**

$$
T_{scan} = \frac{N_p \times N_h}{R_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$
T_{scan} = \frac{65{,}535 \times 254}{500}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Numerator (Multiplication Step-by-Step)**

$$
65{,}535 \times 254 = 65{,}535 \times 250 + 65{,}535 \times 4
$$

$$
65{,}535 \times 250 = 16{,}383{,}750
$$

$$
65{,}535 \times 4 = 262{,}140
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 16{,}383{,}750 + 262{,}140 = 16{,}645{,}890
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Final Division**

$$
T_{scan} = \frac{16{,}645{,}890}{500} = 33{,}291.78 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Readable Units**

$$
T_{scan} \approx \frac{33{,}291.78}{3600} \approx 9.25 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 7 — Stealth Scenario (Halved Rate)**

If $R_p' = 250$ packets/sec, then:

$$
T_{scan}' = \frac{16{,}645{,}890}{250} = 66{,}583.56 \text{ seconds} \approx 18.49 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 8 — Final Conclusion**

> The original scan completes in **~9.25 hours**, while the stealth version requires **~18.49 hours** — exactly double, since halving $R_p$ doubles the time (a linear relationship).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Detection Probability Over a 24-Hour Window

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** *An IDS has a per-packet detection probability of $p_d = 0.001$ for SYN scan packets. A slow scan is conducted at 100 packets/sec for 24 hours. The IDS analysis window is 60 seconds. What is the probability the IDS detects the scan?*

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{scan}$**

$$
T_{scan} = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Number of Analysis Windows**

$$
N_{windows} = \frac{T_{scan}}{\Delta t_{window}} = \frac{86{,}400}{60} = 1{,}440 \text{ windows}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Detection Probability Formula**

$$
P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - p_d)^{N_{windows}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Values**

$$
P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - 0.001)^{1440} = 1 - (0.999)^{1440}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the Approximation for Large Exponents**

For small $p_d$ and large $N$:

$$
(1 - p_d)^N \approx e^{-N \cdot p_d}
$$

$$
(0.999)^{1440} \approx e^{-1440 \times 0.001} = e^{-1.44}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Exponential**

$$
e^{-1.44} \approx 0.2369
$$

**Step 7 — Final Detection Probability**

$$
P_{detect} = 1 - 0.2369 = 0.7631 \approx 76.31\%
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation**

> Despite the scan being **100x slower** than the earlier example, the IDS still has a **~76% chance** of detection over 24 hours. This demonstrates that **slow scans are not undetectable** — they merely shift the odds. In KTU exams, students should always comment on the *operational implication*, not just the number.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Probe Type vs. Response vs. State Inference

| Probe Type | Packet Sent | Expected Response | Inferred State | Stealth Level | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ICMP Echo (Ping)** | `ICMP Type 8` | `ICMP Type 0` (Echo Reply) | Host is **alive** | Low | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TCP SYN Scan** | `SYN` | `SYN-ACK` | Port **OPEN** | Medium | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TCP SYN Scan** | `SYN` | `RST-ACK` | Port **CLOSED** | Medium | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TCP FIN Scan** | `FIN` | `RST` (BSD) / No reply (Win) | **CLOSED** or **FILTERED** | High | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TCP XMAS Scan** | `FIN+PSH+URG` | No reply (open) / RST (closed) | **OPEN** or **CLOSED** | High | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TCP NULL Scan** | No flags | No reply (open) / RST (closed) | **OPEN** or **CLOSED** | High | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TCP ACK Scan** | `ACK` | `RST` | Firewall rule mapping | High | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **UDP Scan** | `UDP` | `ICMP Type 3 Code 3` | Port **CLOSED** | Medium | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Banner Grab** | `TCP connect + payload` | ASCII banner | **Service version** | Low | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **OS Fingerprint** | Crafted SYN + options | Stack-specific reply | **OS family** | Low | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **SYN-ACK = OPEN** and **RST = CLOSED** mapping. This single relationship forms the basis of 60% of probe-related exam questions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Classifying a Captured Probe Packet

**Given:** A packet capture shows: `Flags: FIN, PSH, URG` sent to port 80 with no TCP response from the target.

**Solution Path:**

Step 1 — Identify flag pattern: FIN+PSH+URG = **"XMAS" packet** (all flags lit, like a Christmas tree).

Step 2 — Apply RFC 793 behavior: An XMAS packet sent to a **closed** port triggers an `RST`. Sent to an **open** port, RFC 793 mandates the packet be silently discarded.

Step 3 — Observe: No response → port is likely **OPEN** (or filtered).

Step 4 — Note caveat: Microsoft's Windows stack violates RFC 793 and **always replies with RST** regardless of port state, making XMAS scanning **unreliable on Windows**.

Step 5 — Conclusion: The target is **likely OPEN on port 80**, but the result is **probabilistic**, not deterministic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Probe Attack Lifecycle — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Planning Phase] --> B[Step1: Target Selection]
    B --> C[Step2: Packet Crafting Module]
    C --> D[Step3: Probe Transmission]
    D --> E[Step4: Target Host Receives Packet]
    E --> F{Step5: Port State?}
    F -->|SYN-ACK Received| G[Port OPEN Detected]
    F -->|RST Received| H[Port CLOSED Detected]
    F -->|No Response| I[Port FILTERED Detected]
    G --> J[Step6: Response Aggregation]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step7: Attack Surface Map]
    K --> L[Step8: Service Fingerprinting]
    L --> M[Step9: OS Fingerprinting]
    M --> N[Step10: Vulnerability Correlation]
    N --> O[Phase Complete: Ready for Exploitation]
    D -.->|Parallel| P[StepA: IDS Sniffer Module]
    P --> Q{StepB: Anomaly Threshold}
    Q -->|Exceeded| R[ALERT: Probe Detected]
    Q -->|Not Exceeded| S[Stealth Maintained]
```

## 4.2 Probe Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Probe Initiated] --> B{Protocol Layer?}
    B -->|Layer 3 Network| C[ICMP Probes]
    B -->|Layer 4 Transport| D{TCP or UDP?}
    B -->|Layer 7 Application| E[Banner / Version Probes]
    C --> C1[Ping Sweep]
    C --> C2[Traceroute]
    C --> C3[ICMP Timestamp]
    D -->|TCP| F{Flag Configuration?}
    D -->|UDP| G[UDP Port Scan]
    F -->|SYN only| F1[SYN Scan - Half Open]
    F -->|FIN only| F2[FIN Scan - Stealth]
    F -->|FIN+PSH+URG| F3[XMAS Scan]
    F -->|No Flags| F4[NULL Scan]
    F -->|ACK| F5[ACK Scan - Firewall Mapping]
    E --> E1[HTTP HEAD Request]
    E --> E2[SSH Version Probe]
    E --> E3[SNMP Community Query]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Probe Detection by IDS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AttackerZone
        A1[Prober Engine] --> A2[Packet Generator]
    end
    subgraph NetworkZone
        A2 -->|Probes| FW1[Firewall]
    end
    subgraph DefenseZone
        FW1 --> B1[IDS Packet Capture]
        B1 --> B2[Signature Matcher]
        B1 --> B3[Anomaly Detector]
        B2 --> C1{Signature Hit?}
        B3 --> C2{Anomaly Score High?}
        C1 -->|Yes| D1[ALERT Logged]
        C1 -->|No| D2[No Alert]
        C2 -->|Yes| D1
        C2 -->|No| D2
    end
    subgraph TargetZone
        FW1 -->|Allowed| T1[Target Host]
        T1 -->|Response| B1
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When asked to "draw" or "explain" a probe attack flow in the exam, use **5–7 labeled blocks** and indicate **directional arrows**. Mention both the *attack path* (solid) and the *defensive monitoring path* (dashed) if relevant.

---

## 4.4 Probe Frequency vs. Detection Trade-off (Conceptual Graph)

```
Detection Probability (%)
100 |                                          *
    |                                     *
 75 |                                *
    |                          *
 50 |                    *
    |               *
 25 |          *
    |     *
  0 |*____________________________________
    0     10    100   1000  10000  Probe Rate (pps)
                  (log scale)
    ^ Stealthy                                   ^ Loud
```

> **Reading the Graph:** As probe rate increases (x-axis, log scale), the IDS detection probability rises. A "stealthy" scan at 1–10 pps stays below ~10% detection; an "aggressive" scan at 10,000+ pps approaches **100% detection** by any competent IDS.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define a "Network Probe" in the context of intrusion detection. Differentiate between active and passive probing techniques with one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Scheme):**

> **[Definition — 1 Mark]:** A Network Probe is a reconnaissance attack in which an adversary transmits crafted packets to a target system to elicit responses that reveal information about network topology, open services, or vulnerabilities, without yet attempting exploitation. Probes are the *first phase* of the **Cyber Kill Chain**.

> **[Active Probing — 1 Mark]:** Active probing involves *transmitting* packets to the target. The attacker sends data and observes replies.
> - **Example:** Nmap `SYN scan` — the prober sends a `SYN` packet to port 80 and receives a `SYN-ACK` if the port is open.

> **[Passive Probing — 1 Mark]:** Passive probing involves *observing* traffic that is already in transit (e.g., via a network tap or ARP spoofing) without sending new packets.
> - **Example:** Sniffing broadcast traffic with Wireshark to discover host IPs and running services.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*List and briefly explain THREE types of TCP-based probe scans. State one advantage and one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Scheme):**

> **[1] SYN Scan (Half-Open Scan) — 1 Mark:** Sends a `SYN` packet; on `SYN-ACK` reply, sends `RST` instead of `ACK`, leaving the TCP handshake incomplete. *Advantage:* Fast and reliable. *Limitation:* Detected by modern stateful firewalls and IDS.
