> **[2] FIN Scan — 1 Mark:** Sends a packet with only the `FIN` flag set. Per RFC 793, closed ports reply with `RST`; open ports silently drop it. *Advantage:* Bypasses simple stateless filters. *Limitation:* Ineffective on Windows (which replies with `RST` to all FIN packets).

> **[3] XMAS Scan — 1 Mark:** Sends a packet with `FIN + PSH + URG` flags all set (illuminated like a Christmas tree). Closed ports reply with `RST`; open ports drop silently. *Advantage:* Highly stealthy against basic logging. *Limitation:* Easily detected by modern IDS due to abnormal flag pattern; unreliable on Windows.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer either full **Question A** OR full **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **three phases of a network probe attack** (transmission, observation, and inference). For each phase, state one tool or technique an attacker would use, and one defensive countermeasure.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

> **Phase 1 — Transmission (Packet Crafting) [2 Marks]:**
> The attacker constructs probe packets targeting specific IP/port combinations. Tools used: **Nmap** (industry standard), **Masscan** (high-speed alternative), or custom scripts using `Scapy`.
> - **Countermeasure:** *Egress filtering* at the firewall to block crafted/unusual flag combinations (XMAS, NULL).

> **Phase 2 — Observation (Response Capture) [2 Marks]:**
> The attacker monitors for replies using raw sockets or a packet sniffer. The prober distinguishes between `SYN-ACK` (open), `RST` (closed), and no response (filtered).
> - **Countermeasure:** *Stateful inspection* by next-generation firewalls (NGFW) and *connection tracking* by IDS.

> **Phase 3 — Inference (Map Building) [3 Marks]:**
> The attacker aggregates all responses into an attack-surface map. They perform *OS fingerprinting* (via TCP ISN, window size, TTL) and *service version detection* (via banner grabbing).
> - **Countermeasure:** *Banner hardening* (modify default service banners), *OS hardening* (RFC 793-compliant stack), and *deception via portspoofing*.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain how a **TCP SYN scan** works. What response indicates (i) an open port, (ii) a closed port, and (iii) a filtered port?

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

> **Working of TCP SYN Scan [4 Marks]:**

> ```
> Prober                Target (Port 80)
>   |                        |
>   |------- SYN ---------->|  Step 1: Send SYN
>   |                        |
>   |<------ SYN-ACK --------|  Step 2a: Port OPEN → SYN-ACK
>   |-------- RST ---------->|  Step 3: Send RST (abort handshake)
>   |                        |
> ```
>
> **OR**
>
> ```
>   |------- SYN ---------->|  Step 1: Send SYN
>   |                        |
>   |<------ RST-ACK --------|  Step 2b: Port CLOSED → RST-ACK
> ```
>
> **OR**
>
> ```
>   |------- SYN ---------->|  Step 1: Send SYN
>   |                        |
>   |   <no response>        |  Step 2c: Port FILTERED → Silent drop
> ```

> **Response Mapping [3 Marks]:**
> - **(i) Open Port** — Target replies with `SYN-ACK`. Prober sends `RST` to abort the three-way handshake (this is why it's called "half-open").
> - **(ii) Closed Port** — Target replies with `RST-ACK`, indicating no service is listening.
> - **(iii) Filtered Port** — Target sends **no response** (firewall silently drops the packet) or replies with `ICMP Type 3` (Destination Unreachable).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **active probing** and **passive sniffing** as reconnaissance techniques. Discuss three tools (one per category) and explain why an IDS can more easily detect one over the other.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

> **Active Probing** [2 Marks]: Involves *injecting* packets into the network. The attacker actively communicates with the target. *Example tool:* **Nmap** (can perform 15+ scan types). *Strength:* Comprehensive and fast. *Weakness:* Generates detectable traffic.

> **Passive Sniffing** [2 Marks]: Involves *listening* to traffic without injecting new packets. *Example tools:* **Wireshark**, **tcpdump**. *Strength:* Completely invisible to the target. *Weakness:* Limited to what is already on the wire; cannot discover unused services.

> **Why IDS Detects Active Probes More Easily** [3 Marks]:
> 1. Active probes *generate new traffic* — IDS sees incoming packets from external sources.
> 2. Abnormal flag patterns (XMAS, NULL) trigger **signature-based detection** in Snort/Suricata.
> 3. High packet rates trigger **anomaly-based detection** (statistical deviation from baseline).
>
> In contrast, passive sniffing requires *no new packets* and is virtually undetectable unless the sniffer itself is discovered (e.g., via switch port monitoring, MAC flooding detection, or ARP watch).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A security analyst observes 100 SYN packets sent to port 80 of a single host in 1 second, with no responses received from the host. Identify the type of probe, explain the attacker's likely intent, and recommend TWO specific IDS rules to detect such behavior.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

> **Identification of Probe Type** [2 Marks]: This is a **TCP SYN flood-style reconnaissance scan** or a **covert SYN scan against a filtered host**. The absence of replies indicates the host is behind a **stateful firewall** that is **silently dropping** the SYN packets (filtered state).

> **Likely Attacker Intent** [2 Marks]: The attacker is performing **reconnaissance** to determine:
> 1. Whether port 80 is filtered (firewall present).
> 2. Whether the host responds to high SYN rates (DoS preparation).
> 3. Mapping the firewall rule set before launching a targeted exploit.

> **IDS Rule Recommendations** [3 Marks]:

> **Rule 1 (Snort Syntax) — Rate-Based Detection:**
> ```
> alert tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 80 (msg:"Possible SYN scan - high rate";
>   flags:S; detection_filter:track by_src, count 50, seconds 1;
>   sid:1000001; rev:1;)
> ```

> **Rule 2 (Snort Syntax) — Filtered State Detection:**
> ```
> alert tcp any any -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"SYN with no response - possible filtered scan";
>   flags:S; flow:to_server, no_response; threshold:type both, track by_src,
>   count 20, seconds 60; sid:1000002; rev:1;)
> ```

> **Additional Recommendation:** Implement **SYN cookies** on the target server to mitigate potential SYN flood follow-up attacks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Probe Questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing Probe vs. Exploit (–2 marks):** Probes *discover*; exploits *attack*. Examiners penalize this confusion harshly.
> 2. **❌ Forgetting the "Three Response States" (–2 marks):** Always state OPEN / CLOSED / FILTERED — never just "open" or "closed."
> 3. **❌ Ignoring RFC 793 Compliance Caveat (–1 mark):** Windows does not conform to RFC 793 for FIN/NULL/XMAS — examiners expect this nuance in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **❌ Not Mentioning "Half-Open" Nature of SYN Scan (–1 mark):** SYN scan is called "half-open" because the handshake is **never completed** (prober sends RST after SYN-ACK).
> 5. **❌ Omitting Defensive Countermeasures (–1 to –2 marks):** In 14-mark questions, always include **at least one defensive measure** per sub-part. This is a high-weight requirement in OBE-aligned valuation.
> 6. **❌ Wrong Equation Substitution (–2 marks):** In derivation problems, show *every* arithmetic step. Examiners award **partial marks for intermediate steps** even if the final answer is wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for Last-Minute KTU Review:**

- **Definition:** A Network Probe is a *reconnaissance* attack that elicits information without exploiting.
- **Three Phases:** Transmission → Observation → Inference.
- **Three Port States:** OPEN (SYN-ACK received), CLOSED (RST received), FILTERED (no response).
- **SYN Scan:** Half-open; sends SYN → receives SYN-ACK → sends RST. **Most common and reliable** scan type.
- **FIN Scan:** Sends FIN; closed ports send RST. **Bypasses stateless filters** but ineffective on Windows.
- **XMAS Scan:** Sends FIN+PSH+URG; open ports drop silently. **Highly detectable by signature IDS.**
- **NULL Scan:** Sends no flags; same behavior as XMAS.
- **UDP Scan:** Sends UDP datagram; closed ports send `ICMP Type 3 Code 3`. **Slower and less reliable** than TCP scans.
- **Ping Sweep:** Uses `ICMP Echo (Type 8)` to discover live hosts. **Detected by most modern IDS by default.**
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $T_{scan} = \frac{N_p N_h}{R_p}$
  - $P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - p_d)^{N_{windows}}$
  - $SI = \frac{1}{R_p \cdot N_{abnormal}}$
- **Cyber Kill Chain Position:** Probes are **Phase 1** of the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain (reconnaissance).
- **Detection by IDS:** Signature-based (Snort rules on flag patterns) + Anomaly-based (statistical rate analysis).
- **Countermeasures:** Egress filtering, banner hardening, portspoofing, SYN cookies, stateful firewalls.
- **Real-World Tools:** Nmap (Swiss Army knife), Masscan (high-speed), Wireshark (passive), Hping3 (custom probes).
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024 OBE):** This topic directly addresses **CO1** (Understand the taxonomy of network attacks) and **CO2** (Apply detection mechanisms for reconnaissance activities).
- **Bloom's Taxonomy Level:** *Understand* (L2) and *Apply* (L3) — perfect for KTU's 3-mark and 7-mark distribution.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Network Attacks: Probes**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 OBE Framework, and PECCT742 Course Outcomes.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privilege Escalation Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Privilege Escalation Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Privilege Escalation** is a class of security exploit in which an attacker leverages a programming flaw, design vulnerability, configuration error, or social engineering technique to gain elevated access to resources that are normally protected from an application or user. In the context of Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS), privilege escalation is a critical **post-exploitation** attack phase where the adversary transitions from a low-privilege foothold to administrative or root-level control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT742 - Module 1):**
> Privilege Escalation is explicitly listed under *Types of System Attacks and Vulnerabilities* and is treated as a foundational concept for understanding attack progression, lateral movement, and the trigger conditions for Host-based Intrusion Detection Systems (HIDS).

Formally, in operating system security terminology, privilege escalation is defined as the unauthorized transition between security contexts:

$$ P_{escalation}: C_{low} \xrightarrow{\text{exploit}(v)} C_{high} $$

Where:
- $C_{low}$ = Current security context (e.g., normal user)
- $C_{high}$ = Target security context (e.g., root / SYSTEM / Administrator)
- $\text{exploit}(v)$ = Exploitation of a vulnerability $v$
- $P_{escalation}$ = The privilege escalation operation

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a large corporate office building:

- You are a **visitor** with a guest badge that only opens the lobby restrooms and the ground-floor cafeteria.
- Privilege escalation is like you **tricking, stealing, or breaking your way** into the CEO's corner office on the top floor.

There are two ways this can happen:
1. **Vertical Privilege Escalation**: You go *upward* — from a regular employee to a manager to the CEO. You are gaining a **higher authority** than you were ever supposed to have.
2. **Horizontal Privilege Escalation**: You go *sideways* — from one regular employee (say, in the Marketing department) to another regular employee (say, in the Finance department). You are not getting a *higher* rank, but you are accessing another user's **same-level** resources they were not meant to share with you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:**
> - **Vertical** = Moving **UP** the privilege ladder (user → root).
> - **Horizontal** = Moving **ACROSS** at the same level (User A → User B).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** | Publicly disclosed cybersecurity flaw identifier |
| **Zero-Day** | A vulnerability unknown to the software vendor |
| **SUID/SGID** | Linux file permissions allowing executable to run with owner/group privileges |
| **DLL Hijacking** | Windows-specific attack replacing a legitimate Dynamic Link Library |
| **Token Impersonation** | Stealing or duplicating a security token to act as another user |
| **Lateral Movement** | Post-escalation navigation across the network |
| **Set-UID Bit** | Unix permission bit allowing execution with file owner privileges |

## 1.4 The Attack Kill Chain Context

Privilege escalation does **not** occur in isolation. It sits in the middle-to-late phases of the **Cyber Kill Chain** (Lockheed Martin model) and the **MITRE ATT&CK Framework**:

$$ \text{Reconnaissance} \rightarrow \text{Weaponization} \rightarrow \text{Delivery} \rightarrow \text{Exploitation} \rightarrow \text{Installation} \rightarrow \text{Privilege Escalation} \rightarrow \text{Lateral Movement} \rightarrow \text{Actions on Objectives} $$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 1, you are expected to know that **TA0004** is the MITRE ATT&CK tactic ID for Privilege Escalation, and it includes techniques such as *Access Token Manipulation (T1134)*, *Exploitation for Privilege Escalation (T1068)*, and *Process Injection (T1055)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Privilege Escalation Attacks

### 2.1.1 Vertical Privilege Escalation (Privilege Elevation)

Vertical privilege escalation occurs when an attacker gains *higher* privileges than intended. This is the **most dangerous** form because it often results in total system compromise.

**Common Attack Vectors:**

1. **Kernel Exploits**
   - Targeting flaws in OS kernel code
   - Example: Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195), Dirty Pipe (CVE-2022-0847)
   - Allows writing to read-only memory mappings

2. **SUID/SGID Binary Exploitation (Linux/Unix)**
   - Files with `setuid` bit inherit the owner's privileges
   - If a SUID binary owned by root is vulnerable, it can spawn a root shell

3. **Windows Token Impersonation**
   - Stealing tokens from privileged processes (e.g., `lsass.exe`)
   - Tools: Mimikatz, Incognito
   - Access Token Types: Primary, Impersonation, Restricted

4. **DLL Injection / Process Injection**
   - Injecting malicious code into a running privileged process
   - Techniques: Classic DLL Injection, Process Hollowing, APC Injection

5. **Unquoted Service Paths (Windows)**
   - If a service path contains spaces and is not quoted, Windows may execute a malicious binary in a parent directory
   - Example: `C:\Program Files\My App\service.exe` → attacker places `C:\Program.exe`

### 2.1.2 Horizontal Privilege Escalation

Horizontal privilege escalation occurs when an attacker accesses resources belonging to **another user at the same privilege level**.

**Common Attack Vectors:**

1. **Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)**
   - Manipulating parameters in URLs/APIs to access another user's data
   - Example: `https://app.com/user/1234/profile` → changing to `1235`

2. **Session Hijacking**
   - Stealing session cookies (XSS, network sniffing)
   - Replaying the session to access the victim's account

3. **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)**
   - Trick an authenticated user into performing actions on their behalf

4. **Credential Reuse / Stuffing**
   - Using leaked credentials from other breaches to log in

## 2.2 Classification by Exploitation Mechanism

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Memory Corruption** | Buffer overflow, use-after-free, integer overflow | Stack smashing in setuid binary |
| **Logic Flaws** | Design errors in access control | Missing authorization check in admin endpoint |
| **Configuration Errors** | Misconfigured permissions, default credentials | World-writable `/etc/passwd` |
| **Cryptographic Failures** | Weak hashing, predictable tokens | Predictable session token IDs |
| **Race Conditions (TOCTOU)** | Time-of-check vs time-of-use flaws | Symlink race in /tmp |
| **Social Engineering** | Trick users into running privileged actions | UAC bypass prompts |

## 2.3 Privilege Escalation on Linux Systems

### 2.3.1 Critical Files to Enumerate

During a penetration test or incident response investigation, the following Linux paths are routinely inspected for privilege escalation opportunities:

| File/Directory | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow` | User credential storage |
| `/etc/sudoers`, `/etc/sudoers.d/` | Sudo privilege configuration |
| `/etc/crontab`, `/var/spool/cron/` | Scheduled tasks |
| `/etc/ld.so.conf`, `LD_PRELOAD` | Dynamic linker abuse |
| SUID binaries (`find / -perm -4000`) | Inherits owner privileges |
| SGID binaries (`find / -perm -2000`) | Inherits group privileges |
| World-writable files | Modification by any user |
| `/home/*/history`, `.bash_history` | Command history leak |
| Kernel version (`uname -a`) | Identifying kernel exploits |

### 2.3.2 The SUID Escalation Mathematical Model

When a SUID binary is executed, the effective user ID transitions as follows:

$$ \text{Effective UID (EUID)} = \text{File Owner UID} $$

$$ \text{Real UID (RUID)} = \text{Invoking User UID} $$

For a vulnerable SUID root binary, the privilege boundary collapse can be modeled as:

$$ \text{EUID} = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Capabilities} = \text{FULL} $$

This means the process now operates with **root-equivalent authority** regardless of the original RUID.

## 2.4 Privilege Escalation on Windows Systems

### 2.4.1 Windows Integrity Levels

Windows implements a Mandatory Integrity Control (MIC) model:

| Integrity Level | SID | Typical Use |
|-----------------|-----|-------------|
| **System** | S-1-16-16384 | Kernel, system services |
| **High** | S-1-16-12288 | Administrators |
| **Medium** | S-1-16-8192 | Standard users |
| **Low** | S-1-16-4096 | Restricted processes (IE, Chrome sandbox) |
| **Untrusted** | S-1-16-0 | Anonymous processes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An attacker moving from **Low → Medium → High** is executing a vertical privilege escalation chain. Windows UAC (User Account Control) is specifically designed to block the **Medium → High** transition.

### 2.4.2 Access Token Structure

$$ \text{Token} = \langle \text{User SID}, \text{Group SIDs}, \text{Privileges}, \text{Integrity Level}, \text{Session ID} \rangle $$

Token impersonation attacks (e.g., with `Incognito.exe` or `Mimikatz sekurlsa::tokens`) attempt to **clone** a privileged token and apply it to an attacker-controlled thread.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| **Privilege Escalation Operation** | $P: C_{low} \rightarrow C_{high}$ | Formal model |
| **SUID Privilege Inheritance** | $\text{EUID} = \text{owner-uid}$ | Linux SUID attacks |
| **Integrity Level Hierarchy** | Untrusted $<$ Low $<$ Medium $<$ High $<$ System | Windows MIC |
| **CVE Severity Score (CVSS Base)** | $S = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | Vulnerability scoring |
| **Attack Surface Reduction Ratio** | $R = 1 - \frac{\text{AS}_{after}}{\text{AS}_{before}}$ | Defense effectiveness |
| **Mean Time to Escalation (MTTE)** | $\text{MTTE} = \frac{\sum t_i}{n}$ | Incident analysis |
| **Token Duplication Success Probability** | $P = f(\text{SeImpersonatePrivilege}, \text{SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege})$ | Windows token abuse |
| **MITRE ATT&CK Tactic ID** | TA0004 | Privilege Escalation |
| **Linux Capability Set** | $\text{Cap\_EFFECTIVE} \supseteq \text{Cap\_PERMITTED}$ | Capabilities model |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Red Teaming**: Ethical hackers use these attack vectors to identify real defensive gaps.
- **Blue Teaming / SOC Operations**: Detection rules in SIEM/IDPS are built around these patterns.
- **Secure Code Review**: DevSecOps pipelines scan for these vulnerabilities pre-deployment.
- **Compliance**: PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 mandate detection of privilege escalation as part of audit logging.
- **Digital Forensics**: Incident responders reconstruct privilege escalation chains to determine the breach root cause.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Module 1, examiners frequently ask:** "Differentiate between vertical and horizontal privilege escalation with examples." Be prepared to give a **3-mark direct answer** with a real CVE example.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CVSS Base Score for Privilege Escalation Vulnerabilities

The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) provides a mathematical framework for ranking the severity of privilege escalation vulnerabilities. The base score is derived as:

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step CVSS Base Score Derivation

$$ \text{Impact Sub-Score (ISS)} = 1 - \left[ (1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A) \right] $$

Where:
- $C$ = Confidentiality impact metric
- $I$ = Integrity impact metric
- $A$ = Availability impact metric

Each metric is assigned a value: None = 0, Low = 0.22, High = 0.56.

**Step 1**: Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS).

For a typical local privilege escalation (e.g., Dirty COW), $C = 0.22$, $I = 0.56$, $A = 0.22$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - \left[ (1 - 0.22) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.22) \right] \\
&= 1 - \left[ 0.78 \times 0.44 \times 0.78 \right] \\
&= 1 - \left[ 0.267696 \right] \\
&= 0.732304
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the Impact constant adjustment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times \text{ISS} \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.732304 \\
&= 4.7014
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the Exploitability sub-score.

$$ \text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI $$

For local attack: $AV = 0.55$ (Local), $AC = 0.77$ (Low), $PR = 0.62$ (Low), $UI = 0.85$ (None):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.55 \times 0.77 \times 0.62 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.223 \\
&\approx 1.836
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Compute the Base Score.

If Impact $>$ 0:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{round\_up}(\min[(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}),\, 10]) \\
&= \text{round\_up}(\min[(4.7014 + 1.836),\, 10]) \\
&= \text{round\_up}(6.5374) \\
&= 6.6
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: A CVSS Base Score of **6.6** classifies the vulnerability as **Medium-High Severity** — typical for local privilege escalation bugs. Remote privilege escalation (AV = Network) usually scores **9.0+ (Critical)**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack Vector vs Defense Mechanism

| Attack Vector | OS Platform | Detection Signal | Defense Mechanism | KTU Exam Priority |
|---------------|-------------|------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| SUID Binary Abuse | Linux | `auditd` execve logs on `/usr/bin/*` with EUID=0 | Remove unnecessary SUID bits; SELinux/AppArmor | High |
| Kernel Exploit | Linux | Anomalous syscalls; crash dumps | Patch management; kernel hardening (grsec) | High |
| UAC Bypass | Windows | Event ID 4688 with integrity transitions | UAC notification level "Always Notify" | High |
| Token Impersonation | Windows | `lsass.exe` memory access; Event ID 4663 | Credential Guard (LSA Protection) | Medium |
| DLL Hijacking | Windows | Unsigned DLL loads in trusted process | DLL Safe Search Mode; WDAC | Medium |
| IDOR | Web Apps | Anomalous GET/POST with sequential IDs | Object-level authorization; UUIDs | High |
| Unquoted Service Path | Windows | Service creation with unquoted path | Code review; service hardening | Low |
| Cron Exploitation | Linux | File writes to `/etc/cron*` by non-root | File integrity monitoring (AIDE/Tripwire) | Medium |
| sudo Misconfig | Linux | `sudo` log entries with unusual commands | `visudo` review; least privilege | High |
| Pass-the-Hash | Windows | NTLM auth from unusual source | Disable NTLM; use Kerberos | High |

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean Time to Privilege Escalation (MTTE)

In an enterprise environment, defenders measure how long it takes an attacker to escalate privileges after initial compromise. This metric helps calibrate IDPS alert priorities.

$$ \text{MTTE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{escalation,i} - t_{initial,i}) $$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose a Security Operations Center (SOC) tracked 5 incidents:

| Incident | $t_{initial}$ (hours from breach) | $t_{escalation}$ (hours from breach) |
|----------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 4 |
| 2 | 0 | 12 |
| 3 | 0 | 2 |
| 4 | 0 | 8 |
| 5 | 0 | 6 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTE} &= \frac{(4 - 0) + (12 - 0) + (2 - 0) + (8 - 0) + (6 - 0)}{5} \\
&= \frac{4 + 12 + 2 + 8 + 6}{5} \\
&= \frac{32}{5} \\
&= 6.4 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark**: The MITRE ATT&CK Evaluations show that skilled red teams escalate privileges in **under 2 hours** in 60% of cases. An MTTE of **6.4 hours** indicates significant defensive friction.

## 3.4 Analytical Reasoning: The Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)

The **Principle of Least Privilege** states that every module/user/process must be able to access only the information and resources necessary for its legitimate purpose.

Formally, for a set of subjects $S$ and objects $O$ with access matrix $M$:

$$ \forall s \in S, \forall o \in O : M[s][o] = \text{minimal necessary} $$

The **Privilege Reduction Factor (PRF)** measures how much a system adheres to PoLP:

$$ \text{PRF} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{s \in S} \sum_{o \in O} M[s][o]}{\vert S \vert \times \vert O \vert} $$

- $\text{PRF} = 0$: Maximum privilege (every user can do everything)
- $\text{PRF} = 1$: Perfect least privilege (no user has any access — usually impractical)

> [!NOTE]
> A **higher PRF** correlates with **lower attack surface** for privilege escalation. KTU examiners often ask for **design-time** or **run-time** controls to increase PRF.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Privilege Escalation Attack Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Gains Initial Foothold] --> B{Identify Privilege Level}
    B -->|Low Privilege User| C[Enumerate System]
    C --> C1[Check SUID Files]
    C --> C2[Check sudo config]
    C --> C3[Check kernel version]
    C --> C4[Check cron jobs]
    C1 --> D{Vulnerability Found?}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D -->|Yes| E[Select Exploit Vector]
    D -->|No| F[Attempt Lateral Movement]
    E --> E1[Memory Corruption]
    E --> E2[Logic Flaw]
    E --> E3[Config Abuse]
    E --> E4[Token Theft]
    E1 --> G[Execute Privilege Escalation]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    E4 --> G
    G --> H{Elevated to root or admin?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Full System Compromise]
    H -->|No| F
    F --> J[Target Another User Account]
    J --> K[Horizontal Privilege Escalation]
    K --> L[Access Victim Resources]
    I --> M[Persistence and Exfiltration]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Windows Token Impersonation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[User Session]
        U1[Standard User Process]
    end
    subgraph SYSTEM[System Session]
        LSASS[LSASS Process]
        T1[Token A Standard]
        T2[Token B Admin]
    end
    U1 -->|Enumerate Tokens| LSASS
    LSASS -->|Extract Token B| T2
    T2 -->|Duplicate via Impersonation| U1
    U1 -->|Spawn Privileged Thread| P1[New Process with Admin Rights]
```

## 4.3 Privilege Escalation Defense-in-Depth Layered Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1 Patch Management] --> L2[Layer 2 Least Privilege Configuration]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Mandatory Access Control]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 File Integrity Monitoring]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Audit Logging and SIEM]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 User Behavior Analytics]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 EDR with Token Monitoring]
    L1 -.blocks.-> A1[Kernel Exploits]
    L2 -.blocks.-> A2[SUID Abuse]
    L3 -.blocks.-> A3[Capability Escalation]
    L4 -.blocks.-> A4[Cron Tampering]
    L5 -.blocks.-> A5[Service Path Exploits]
    L6 -.blocks.-> A6[Anomalous Token Use]
    L7 -.blocks.-> A7[Credential Theft]
```

## 4.4 SUID Binary Privilege Inheritance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NORMAL[Normal User]
        N1[UID 1000]
    end
    subgraph SUIDBIN[SUID Binary File Owner is root]
        S1[File Permission 4755]
    end
    subgraph RESULT[Execution Result]
        R1[Process RUID 1000]
        R2[Process EUID 0 root]
    end
    N1 -->|execve| SUIDBIN
    SUIDBIN -->|Kernel Privilege Transition| RESULT
    R1 --> P1[Process Body]
    R2 --> P1
    P1 -->|Vulnerable Function| SHELL[Root Shell Spawned]
```

## 4.5 IDPS Detection Coverage for Privilege Escalation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IDS[IDPS Engine] --> S1[Signature Based]
    IDS --> S2[Anomaly Based]
    IDS --> S3[Stateful Protocol Analysis]
    S1 --> S1A[Known CVE Pattern Match]
    S1 --> S1B[SUID/SGID Modification]
    S2 --> S2A[Unusual syscalls]
    S2 --> S2B[Privilege Transition Anomalies]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Privilege Escalation. Differentiate between vertical and horizontal privilege escalation with one example each. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PECCT742 | Module 1]`

**Model Answer:**

Privilege escalation is a type of cyberattack in which an attacker exploits a vulnerability, design flaw, or configuration error to gain elevated access to resources beyond what was originally granted.

**Vertical Privilege Escalation**: The attacker gains **higher privileges** than intended, such as a normal user acquiring root or administrator rights.
- *Example*: Exploiting a buffer overflow in a SUID root binary on Linux to obtain a root shell.

**Horizontal Privilege Escalation**: The attacker accesses resources of another user with the **same privilege level**.
- *Example*: An authenticated user changing the user ID in an HTTP request from `id=1001` to `id=1002` to view another user's bank account details (IDOR attack).

| Aspect | Vertical | Horizontal |
|--------|----------|------------|
| Direction | Upward | Lateral |
| Privilege Change | Yes | No |
| Typical Impact | Full system compromise | Data theft of peer users |

---

### Question 2: What is a SUID bit in Linux? How can it be abused for privilege escalation? [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | PECCT742 | Module 1]`

**Model Answer:**

The **SUID (Set User ID)** bit is a special Linux file permission (mode `4000`) that allows an executable to run with the privileges of its **file owner** rather than the user invoking it.

**Mechanism of Abuse**:
- If a SUID binary is owned by **root** and contains a vulnerability (e.g., buffer overflow, command injection, library loading flaw), a normal user executing it inherits **EUID = 0** for the duration of the process.
- The attacker can chain this to spawn a root shell (e.g., via `GTFOBins` payloads).

**Detection & Mitigation**:
- Audit command: `find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null`
- Mitigation: Remove the SUID bit with `chmod u-s <file>`; apply SELinux/AppArmor policies; keep binaries patched.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PECCT742 | Module 1]`**

#### Part (a): Explain the different types of privilege escalation attacks on Linux systems with suitable examples. Discuss the role of kernel exploits and SUID binaries. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Privilege escalation on Linux systems is broadly classified into **kernel-level** and **userland-level** attacks.

**1. Kernel Exploits**

The Linux kernel is the most privileged software in the OS. A vulnerability in the kernel allows direct transition to root.
- *Example*: **Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195)** — a race condition in the way the kernel handled copy-on-write for memory mappings. It allowed a local user to write to read-only memory mappings, leading to root access.
- *Example*: **Dirty Pipe (CVE-2022-0847)** — a flaw in the `pipe` subsystem allowing overwrite of read-only files like `/etc/passwd`.

**2. SUID/SGID Binary Exploitation**

When the SUID bit is set on an executable owned by root, any user running it temporarily gains root EUID. Vulnerabilities in such binaries (e.g., `vim`, `find`, `nmap` with old SUID) can be exploited:
- `find / -perm -4000` enumerates SUID binaries
- GTFOBins (https://gtfobins.github.io/) provides payloads for known SUID-vulnerable binaries

**3. Sudo Misconfigurations**

Incorrect entries in `/etc/sudoers` (e.g., `NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/vim`) allow command execution as root:
- `sudo vim -c '!bash'` opens a root shell

**4. Cron Job Abuse**

Writable scripts referenced in `/etc/crontab` can be modified by an attacker to execute arbitrary code as root.

**5. World-Writable Files and PATH Hijacking**

If a binary referenced in `sudo` invokes other commands without absolute paths, an attacker can plant a malicious binary earlier in the `PATH`.

**6. Kernel Capability Abuse**

Linux capabilities (e.g., `CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH`) can be inherited via SUID-capable binaries and exploited.

**Summary Table**:

| Attack Type | Persistence | Detection Difficulty |
|-------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Kernel Exploits | Reboot-vulnerable | High (no log trace) |
| SUID Exploits | Until binary removed | Medium (auditd) |
| Sudo Misconfig | Until config fixed | Low (sudo log) |
| Cron Abuse | Until cron removed | Medium (FIM) |

#### Part (b): Describe the Windows privilege escalation techniques. Explain token impersonation and UAC bypass in detail. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Windows Privilege Escalation Overview**

Windows implements the Mandatory Integrity Control (MIC) model with four integrity levels: Low, Medium, High, System. Privilege escalation on Windows typically targets the **Medium → High** transition or the **any → SYSTEM** escalation.

**1. Token Impersonation**

A Windows **access token** is a kernel object describing the security context of a process. It contains the user SID, group SIDs, privileges, integrity level, and session ID.

Token impersonation attacks (MITRE T1134) work as follows:
- A process with `SeImpersonatePrivilege` or `SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege` can assume the identity of another user.
- **Mimikatz** is a popular tool that performs:
  - `privilege::debug`
  - `sekurlsa::logonpasswords` — extracts credentials from `lsass.exe`
  - `token::elevate` — uses a stolen SYSTEM token
- **Incognito** is another tool that lists and impersonates available tokens.

**Attack Flow**:
1. Compromise a service account running as SYSTEM.
2. Enumerate tokens with `whoami /priv` and `Incognito.exe list_tokens -g`.
3. Impersonate a token with administrative authority.
4. Spawn a new process with the stolen token.

**2. UAC Bypass**

User Account Control (UAC) prompts the user for consent when a process attempts to elevate from Medium to High integrity. Several bypass techniques exist:
- **Auto-Elevation Abuse**: Some Windows binaries are configured to auto-elevate without a UAC prompt. Examples: `fodhelper.exe`, `eventvwr.exe`, `taskmgr.exe`. Attackers can hijack the registry `HKCU\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command` to point to a malicious binary.
- **DLL Hijacking**: Dropping a malicious DLL in a directory searched by a privileged service.
- **COM Elevation Moniker Abuse**: Using elevated COM objects (e.g., `IFileOperation`).
- **Fodhelper Technique (BleepingComputer)**:
  - Command: `reg add HKCU\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command /d "cmd.exe" /f`
  - Trigger: `fodhelper.exe` (auto-elevates)
  - Result: `cmd.exe` runs as High integrity

**Detection Mechanisms**:
- Windows Event ID 4688 (Process Creation) with integrity level transitions
- Sysmon Event ID 1 with `IntegrityLevel` field
- EDR solutions monitoring `lsass.exe` access

**Defense**:
- Enable **Credential Guard** to isolate LSASS secrets.
- Set UAC to "Always Notify" (the highest level).
- Restrict token privileges via GPO.
- Deploy **Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC)**.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | PECCT742 | Module 1]`**

#### Part (a): With a neat diagram, explain the Cyber Kill Chain and pinpoint where privilege escalation occurs. List the common post-exploitation objectives. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**The Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin Model)**

The Cyber Kill Chain consists of **7 phases** that describe the stages of a cyberattack:

1. **Reconnaissance**: Gathering information about the target (OSINT, scanning, social media mining).
2. **Weaponization**: Coupling a payload (e.g., remote access trojan) with an exploit and delivering it via a file or link.
3. **Delivery**: Transmitting the weaponized artifact to the target (email, USB, web).
4. **Exploitation**: Triggering the vulnerability in the target system.
5. **Installation**: Installing malware (backdoor, implant) on the victim's machine.
6. **Command and Control (C2)**: Establishing a remote channel with the attacker's server.
7. **Actions on Objectives**: Data exfiltration, destruction, encryption (ransomware), etc.

**Privilege Escalation Position**:
Privilege escalation typically occurs between **Installation (Phase 5)** and **Actions on Objectives (Phase 7)**. Specifically, it aligns with **Phase 6: C2 establishment** because the attacker needs elevated privileges to:
- Install persistent services
- Disable security software
- Access protected data stores
- Move laterally across the network

**Diagram**:

```
[Recon] -> [Weaponize] -> [Deliver] -> [Exploit] -> [Install] -> [C2 with Privilege Escalation] -> [Actions]
                                                                         |
                                                              +----------+----------+
                                                              |  Vertical Escalation |
                                                              |  Token Theft         |
                                                              |  Lateral Movement    |
                                                              +----------------------+
```

**Post-Exploitation Objectives**:
- Establishing **persistence** (registry run keys, scheduled tasks, services)
- **Credential harvesting** (Mimikatz, keylogging)
- **Lateral movement** (PsExec, WMI, WinRM, RDP)
- **Data exfiltration** (DNS tunneling, HTTPS uploads)
- **Defense evasion** (process hollowing, rootkits, log tampering)
- **Impact** (ransomware encryption, destructive wipers)

#### Part (b): Explain the role of IDPS in detecting privilege escalation attacks. Describe signature-based and anomaly-based detection with examples. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Role of IDPS in Privilege Escalation Detection**

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** plays a critical role in identifying and blocking privilege escalation attempts. Both **Network-based (NIDS)** and **Host-based (HIDS)** systems contribute:

- **NIDS**: Detects network-borne attacks that may lead to escalation (e.g., exploitation of remote services).
- **HIDS**: Monitors local system events, file integrity, and process behavior — the primary tool for escalation detection.

**1. Signature-Based Detection**

Signature-based detection uses **known patterns** of attack techniques stored in a rule database.

**Examples for Privilege Escalation**:
- **Snort Rule Example**: Detecting attempts to write to `/etc/passwd` or `/etc/shadow`:

  ```
  alert tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 22 (msg:"Possible privilege escalation via SSH"; content:"chmod 777"; nocase; sid:1000001;)
  ```

- **YARA Rule Example**: Detecting Mimikatz on disk:

  ```
  rule Mimikatz_Detection {
      strings: $a = "sekurlsa::logonpasswords" ascii
      condition: $a
  }
  ```

- **Windows Event Log Rule**: Triggering on Event ID 4672 (Special Privileges Assigned to New Logon) when a non-admin user receives `SeDebugPrivilege`.

**Advantages**: Low false-positive rate for known attacks.
**Disadvantages**: Cannot detect **zero-day** privilege escalation techniques.

**2. Anomaly-Based Detection**

Anomaly-based detection uses **machine learning and statistical baselines** to identify behavior that deviates from the norm.

**Examples for Privilege Escalation**:
- **Process Behavior Baseline**: A user process suddenly invoking `Setuid` syscall or `AdjustTokenPrivileges` is flagged.
- **File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)**: Sudden change in `/etc/passwd` or `/etc/sudoers` triggers an alert.
- **User Behavior Analytics (UBA)**: A user account suddenly accessing admin-only files or running privileged commands at unusual hours.

**Advantages**: Can detect **zero-day** attacks.
**Disadvantages**: Higher false-positive rate; requires training period.

**Hybrid Approach**:
Modern IDPS deployments (e.g., **Snort + Zeek + Wazuh + Elastic Stack**) combine:
- **Signature** rules for known CVEs
- **Anomaly** rules for behavioral deviation
- **Threat intelligence feeds** for real-time IOCs
- **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** for deep process telemetry

**Recommended Detection Points for Privilege Escalation**:

| Detection Point | Tool/Log | Signal |
|-----------------|----------|--------|
| Setuid execution | `auditd`, Sysmon | EUID=0 transitions |
| SUID modification | File integrity | `chmod u+s` on binaries |
| Token manipulation | Sysmon EID 1 | Integrity level jump |
| Suspicious LSASS access | Sysmon EID 10 | `lsass.exe` memory read |
| Kernel module load | `dmesg`, auditd | `init_module` syscall |
| Sensitive file write | FIM | `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/sudoers` |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Reasons:**
> 1. **Confusing Vertical with Horizontal**: Many students write "Horizontal = getting root access" — this is **WRONG**. Horizontal stays at the same privilege level. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention examples**: Every privilege escalation answer in KTU requires a **real example** (Dirty COW, Mimikatz, IDOR). Pure theory gets partial credit.
> 3. **Missing MITRE ATT&CK reference**: As per KTU 2024 scheme alignment with industry standards, mentioning **TA0004** and the relevant technique IDs (T1068, T1134) earns bonus awareness marks.
> 4. **Not connecting to IDPS**: Module 1 is part of an IDPS course. Always tie the answer back to **how IDPS detects** the attack.
> 5. **No diagram for 7-mark questions**: KTU has a "neat diagram" instruction. Omitting a flow diagram costs **at least 1-2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts
- **Privilege Escalation** = unauthorized elevation of access rights.
- **Vertical Escalation** = upward (user → root/admin).
- **Horizontal Escalation** = lateral (user A → user B resources).
- It is **post-exploitation**, sitting in the **middle-to-late** kill chain phase.

### Linux Essentials
- SUID (`4000`), SGID (`2000`), sticky bit (`1000`) permissions.
- Audit commands: `find / -perm -4000`, `sudo -l`, `uname -a`.
- Critical files: `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/sudoers`, `/etc/crontab`.
- Famous CVEs: **Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195)**, **Dirty Pipe (CVE-2022-0847)**.

### Windows Essentials
- Integrity Levels: Untrusted < Low < Medium < High < System.
- Token Impersonation: `SeImpersonatePrivilege`, `SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege`.
- Famous tools: **Mimikatz**, **Incognito**, **PsExec**.
- UAC bypass methods: **fodhelper**, **eventvwr**, **COM moniker abuse**.

### MITRE ATT&CK Mapping
- Tactic ID: **TA0004** (Privilege Escalation)
- Key Techniques: T1068 (Exploitation for Privilege Escalation), T1134 (Access Token Manipulation), T1055 (Process Injection), T1548 (Bypass UAC).

### Defense Principles
- **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)**
- **Defense-in-Depth**: Patch management + PoLP + MAC + FIM + SIEM + UBA + EDR.
- **Monitoring points**: Sysmon Event IDs 1, 10; Windows Event IDs 4672, 4688; Linux auditd `execve`, `setuid`.

### Key Formulas to Remember
- $P: C_{low} \rightarrow C_{high}$ (Escalation operation)
- $\text{EUID} = \text{owner-uid}$ (SUID inheritance)
- $\text{CVSS} = f(\text{Impact}, \text{Exploitability})$
- $\text{MTTE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(t_{esc} - t_{init})$

### Memory Aids
- **V**ertical = going **V**ertically up.
- **H**orizontal = going **H**orizontally across.
- **SUID = Substitute User ID** = inherits owner.
- **TA0004 = Tactic number 4 = Privilege Escalation** in MITRE.

### Exam Strategy
- Always include a **diagram** in 7-mark answers.
- Always provide a **real CVE or tool example**.
- Always connect the answer back to **IDPS detection**.
- Mention **MITRE ATT&CK IDs** to demonstrate industry awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Denial of Service (DoS) and Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Denial of Service (DoS) and Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denial of Service (DoS) Attack** is a malicious attempt by a single attacker (or a coordinated set of systems) to disrupt the normal functioning of a target host, server, network, or application, thereby denying legitimate users access to the resource or service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attack** is an advanced variant of the DoS attack in which a large number of geographically distributed, compromised machines (collectively known as a **botnet**) are orchestrated by one or more attackers to flood or overwhelm a single victim with an unmanageable volume of traffic or resource requests.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, both DoS and DDoS are categorized as **Availability-Based Threats** — they target the **Availability** pillar of the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)**, and form a core sub-topic under **Attack Taxonomy** in Module 1 of the Intrusion Detection & Prevention System course.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Highway Blockade**
>
> Imagine a **single-lane bridge** that connects a small town to a hospital.
>
> - **DoS Scenario:** A single person parks a huge truck (the attacker) in the middle of the bridge. The town is **cut off** — ambulances cannot reach the hospital. One attacker, one location, one bridge.
>
> - **DDoS Scenario:** Instead of one truck, the attacker secretly instructs **1,000 car owners** in the town (the **botnet** — they don't even know they are part of the attack because their cars have been **"hijacked"** with malware) to all drive onto the bridge **at the exact same time**. The bridge becomes a **permanent traffic jam**. Legitimate ambulances have no hope of crossing. This is a **distributed** attack — many sources, one victim.

**Another Analogy — The Restaurant (Slowloris-style attack):**
A customer walks into a restaurant, sits at a table, and **never orders any food** but keeps asking the waiter *"Are you ready to take my order?"* — *"Hmm… let me see… are you ready now?"* This single rude customer ties up the waiter's time, and real hungry customers are **denied service**. Multiply this by 1,000 fake customers = **DDoS**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Mandatory Bold)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Botnet** | A network of internet-connected devices (zombies) infected with malware and controlled remotely. |
| **Zombie / Agent / Bot** | A single compromised machine inside a botnet. |
| **Handler / Master / C2 Server** | The command-and-control node used by the attacker to relay instructions to the botnet. |
| **Botmaster / Attacker** | The original human threat actor orchestrating the attack. |
| **Amplification Factor** | The ratio of response size to request size in a reflection attack (e.g., DNS amplification ≈ **28× to 54×**). |
| **Bandwidth Saturation** | Exhausting the network pipe (typically measured in **Gbps** or **Tbps**). |
| **Requests per Second (RPS)** | Application-layer attack magnitude unit. |
| **SYN_RECV Queue** | OS-level half-open connection buffer (commonly sized at **1024** to **8192** entries). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module specifically maps these attacks under **CO1: Identify and Classify Various Network and System-Level Threats** and connects them to **CO2: Analyze the Working of IDPS Tools Against Modern Attack Vectors**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How a DoS/DDoS Attack Works

The lifecycle of a DoS/DDoS attack can be broken down into **five structured logic steps**:

### Step 1: Reconnaissance & Target Selection
- The attacker scans the internet (using tools like **Nmap**, **Masscan**, **Shodan**) to identify a target's:
  - IP address range
  - Open ports and running services
  - Operating system fingerprint
  - Network bandwidth (≈ in **Mbps**)

### Step 2: Recruitment of Agents (DDoS only)
- The attacker compromises vulnerable machines (IoT devices, old PCs, unpatched servers) using **malware propagation techniques** (phishing, drive-by downloads, exploiting CVE-2017-17215 on Huawei HG532 routers, etc.).
- The recruited machines become **zombies** and check into a **C2 (Command & Control) server** (IRC channel, HTTP server, or modern P2P protocol).

### Step 3: Weaponization — The Attack Vector
- The attacker chooses an attack vector:
  - **Volumetric** (UDP flood, ICMP flood, Amplification)
  - **Protocol-based** (SYN flood, Ping of Death, Smurf)
  - **Application-layer** (HTTP GET/POST flood, Slowloris)

### Step 4: Launch & Saturation
- The botnet is signaled to simultaneously send malicious traffic to the target's **IP address**.
- The goal is to saturate one of three critical resources:
  1. **Network bandwidth** (Layer 3/4)
  2. **State tables / connection tables** (firewall, load balancer)
  3. **Application resources** (CPU, RAM, DB connection pool — Layer 7)

### Step 5: Service Degradation or Outage
- Legitimate users observe: timeouts, **HTTP 503 Service Unavailable**, slow page loads, or complete disconnection.
- The service becomes **unavailable** — mission accomplished for the attacker.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Question:** Why is DDoS harder to defend against than DoS? Because in DDoS, malicious traffic originates from **thousands of unique IPs**, making simple **IP-blocklisting** ineffective. Defenders must perform **deep-packet inspection** and **traffic profiling** to distinguish bot traffic from human traffic.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE numerical/derivation questions on DoS/DDoS.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplification Factor (AF)** | $$AF = \frac{\text{Response Size (bytes)}}{\text{Request Size (bytes)}}$$ | Dimensionless ratio. Higher = more dangerous. |
| **Effective Attack Bandwidth** | $$B_{\text{attack}} = N_{\text{bots}} \times B_{\text{per-bot}} \times AF$$ | $N_{\text{bots}}$ = number of bots, $B_{\text{per-bot}}$ = per-bot upload bandwidth, $AF$ = amplification factor, result in **Mbps**. |
| **SYN Flood Half-Open Memory Cost** | $$M_{\text{syn}} = N_{\text{syn}} \times S_{\text{tcb}}$$ | $N_{\text{syn}}$ = number of half-open connections, $S_{\text{tcb}}$ = size of one TCP Control Block (≈ **280 bytes**). |
| **Connection Table Exhaustion Rate** | $$R_{\text{exh}} = \frac{T_{\text{table}}}{R_{\text{avg-per-sec}}}$$ | $T_{\text{table}}$ = total table size, $R_{\text{avg-per-sec}}$ = arrival rate of new connections. |
| **Detection Confidence (Statistical)** | $$\sigma_{\text{baseline}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$ | Standard deviation of traffic — used for anomaly-based DDoS detection thresholds. |
| **Botnet Recruitment Yield** | $$\eta = \frac{N_{\text{infected}}}{N_{\text{scanned}}}$$ | $N_{\text{infected}}$ = compromised hosts, $N_{\text{scanned}}$ = scanned hosts. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When writing formulas in exam answer sheets, **always define your variables** and **mention units explicitly** (e.g., Mbps, bytes, seconds). Examiners award 1 mark *just* for the variable definition in 14-mark derivations.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud-Native Defenses (AWS Shield, Cloudflare, Akamai):** All real-time DDoS mitigation engines (e.g., Cloudflare's Magic Transit) rely on **anycast routing** to distribute volumetric traffic across a global **points-of-presence (PoP)** network.
- **Telecom ISP Backbone Engineering:** Carriers (e.g., Airtel, Jio in India) deploy **RTBH (Remotely Triggered Black Hole)** routing at the BGP level to drop DDoS traffic upstream before it reaches customer links.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA):** DoS attacks against PLCs or HMIs can cause **physical-world failures** (e.g., the **2015 Ukraine Power Grid attack** used a DoS component to blind operators before blacking out ~225,000 customers).
- **Data Center Load Balancers:** Modern **L4/L7 load balancers** (F5 BIG-IP, NGINX Plus) implement **connection-rate limiting** using the $R_{\text{exh}}$ formula above to shed attack traffic gracefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Attack Bandwidth with Amplification

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> An attacker recruits **$N$ = 5,000** compromised IoT bots. Each bot has an average upload bandwidth of **$B$ = 2 Mbps**. The attacker uses a **DNS amplification vector** with an amplification factor of **$AF$ = 50×**.
> Calculate the **total effective attack bandwidth** striking the victim, and determine if it can saturate a **1 Gbps** corporate link.

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation
The effective attack bandwidth is the product of bot count, per-bot bandwidth, and amplification factor.

$$
B_{\text{attack}} = N_{\text{bots}} \times B_{\text{per-bot}} \times AF
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values
We substitute $N_{\text{bots}} = 5000$, $B_{\text{per-bot}} = 2 \text{ Mbps}$, $AF = 50$.

$$
B_{\text{attack}} = 5000 \times 2 \text{ Mbps} \times 50
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the intermediate product
First compute the per-bot amplified contribution.

$$
B_{\text{per-bot amp}} = 2 \text{ Mbps} \times 50 = 100 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by the bot count
Multiply the amplified per-bot bandwidth by the total number of bots in the botnet.

$$
B_{\text{attack}} = 5000 \times 100 \text{ Mbps} = 500{,}000 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to standard unit
Convert megabits per second to gigabits per second to compare with the link capacity.

$$
B_{\text{attack}} = \frac{500{,}000 \text{ Mbps}}{1000} = 500 \text{ Gbps}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare with the target link
The victim's corporate link is **1 Gbps**, but the attack is generating **500 Gbps**.

$$
B_{\text{attack}} \gg B_{\text{link}} \Rightarrow 500 \text{ Gbps} \gg 1 \text{ Gbps}
$$

### Step 7 — Conclusion
> **Result:** The effective attack bandwidth is **500 Gbps**, which is **500 times** the victim's link capacity. The corporate link is **completely saturated**, and all legitimate traffic will be dropped at the upstream router. The DoS is **catastrophically successful**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SYN Flood Memory Exhaustion on a Linux Server

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A server has a Linux kernel **SYN backlog (somaxconn) of $T$ = 2,048** half-open connections. Each half-open TCP Control Block occupies **$S$ = 280 bytes**. The attacker sends **$R$ = 800 SYN packets per second**. The server never sends a SYN-ACK reply (or its reply is dropped). Calculate:
> (a) Total memory consumed by the SYN backlog when full.
> (b) The time required to fill the backlog.

### Part (a) — Memory Exhausted at Saturation

**Step 1:** Use the memory cost equation.

$$
M_{\text{syn}} = N_{\text{syn}} \times S_{\text{tcb}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $N_{\text{syn}} = T = 2048$ and $S_{\text{tcb}} = 280$ bytes.

$$
M_{\text{syn}} = 2048 \times 280
$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication step-by-step.

$$
M_{\text{syn}} = 2048 \times 280 = 2048 \times (200 + 80)
$$

**Step 4:** Expand into two intermediate products.

$$
M_{\text{syn}} = (2048 \times 200) + (2048 \times 80)
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate each intermediate product separately.

$$
2048 \times 200 = 409{,}600
$$

$$
2048 \times 80 = 163{,}840
$$

**Step 6:** Add the partial results together.

$$
M_{\text{syn}} = 409{,}600 + 163{,}840 = 573{,}440 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to kilobytes for readability.

$$
M_{\text{syn}} = \frac{573{,}440}{1024} \approx 560 \text{ KB}
$$

> **Result (a):** The server allocates **≈ 560 KB** of kernel memory just for the SYN backlog once full. While this seems small, the **exhaustion of the connection table** is the real damage — the server refuses all new TCP connections.

---

### Part (b) — Time to Fill the Backlog

**Step 8:** Use the exhaustion-rate relation.

$$
t_{\text{fill}} = \frac{T}{R}
$$

**Step 9:** Substitute $T = 2048$ and $R = 800$ connections per second.

$$
t_{\text{fill}} = \frac{2048}{800}
$$

**Step 10:** Evaluate the fraction.

$$
t_{\text{fill}} = 2.56 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Result (b):** In just **2.56 seconds**, the attacker's single machine fills the entire SYN backlog, after which the server **stops accepting new TCP connections**. This is why a modern **SYN-cookie** or **SYN-proxy** defense is mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Feature | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| Source count | **1** attacker | **Thousands** of bots |
| Source IP diversity | Single | Highly diverse |
| Volume | Modest (often **Mbps**) | Massive (often **Gbps**–**Tbps**) |
| Traceback | Trivial | Very difficult |
| Botnet required? | No | **Yes** |
| Defense difficulty | Easy (block 1 IP) | Hard (needs traffic scrubbing) |
| Example | Single-machine SYN flood | Mirai botnet (Oct 2016, **1.2 Tbps** vs Dyn DNS) |
| KTU CO mapping | CO1 | CO1 + CO2 |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DDoS Attack Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The diagram below illustrates the canonical **4-tier DDoS attack architecture** mandated in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Attacker["ATTACKER BOTMASTER"]
    CC["COMMAND AND CONTROL SERVER C2"]
    BotsA["BOT NODE 1 ZOMBIE"]
    BotsB["BOT NODE 2 ZOMBIE"]
    BotsC["BOT NODE 3 ZOMBIE"]
    BotsD["BOT NODE N ZOMBIE"]
    ISP1["VICTIM ISP EDGE ROUTER"]
    FW["FIREWALL OR IDS SENSOR"]
    Server["VICTIM WEB SERVER OR APPLICATION"]
    Legit["LEGITIMATE USERS BLOCKED"]

    Attacker -->|issues attack command| CC
    CC -->|control channel IRC HTTP| BotsA
    CC -->|control channel IRC HTTP| BotsB
    CC -->|control channel IRC HTTP| BotsC
    CC -->|control channel IRC HTTP| BotsD
    BotsA -->|UDP SYN ICMP flood| ISP1
    BotsB -->|UDP SYN ICMP flood| ISP1
    BotsC -->|UDP SYN ICMP flood| ISP1
    BotsD -->|UDP SYN ICMP flood| ISP1
    ISP1 --> FW
    FW --> Server
    Server -.->|service denied| Legit

    style Attacker fill:#1f1f1f,color:#ffffff

```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (for KTU answer sheets):**
> - The **Attacker Botmaster** (black) is the human threat actor.
> - The **C2 Server** (red) is a hardened machine that brokers commands — often hosted on a bullet-proof VPS.
> - **Bots** (orange) are unwitting compromised devices (IoT, PCs, servers).
> - The **Victim** (green) is the target whose service is denied.
> - **Legitimate Users** (purple) are collateral damage.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of DoS/DDoS Attack Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root["DoS AND DDoS ATTACK VECTORS"]
    V["VOLUMETRIC LAYER 3 AND 4"]
    P["PROTOCOL EXPLOITS LAYER 3 AND 4"]

    Root --> P

    V --> V2

    A --> A2

```

## 4.3 SYN Flood Three-Way Handshake Attack Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Attacker Bot
    participant S as Victim Server
    Note over C,S: Normal TCP 3-Way Handshake
    C->>S: SYN (seq=x)
    S->>C: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
    C->>S: ACK (ack=y+1)
    Note over C,S: Connection Established
    Note over C,S: SYN FLOOD ATTACK
    C->>S: SYN (seq=x1) [Spoofed IP]
    S->>C: SYN-ACK (sent to spoofed IP, never arrives)
    Note over S: SYN_RECV State Buffered
    C->>S: SYN (seq=x2) [Spoofed IP]
    S->>C: SYN-ACK (sent to spoofed IP, never arrives)
    Note over S: SYN_RECV State Buffered
    C->>S: SYN (seq=xN) [Spoofed IP]
    Note over S: SYN BACKLOG FULL NO NEW CONNECTIONS ACCEPTED
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Diagram Insight:** The server allocates resources for every spoofed SYN. When the **SYN backlog fills up**, the OS kernel **refuses all new TCP connections** — including legitimate ones from real users. This is the **mechanism of denial**.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** Define Denial of Service (DoS) attack. List any two differences between DoS and DDoS attacks.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denial of Service (DoS) Attack** is a cyberattack in which a malicious actor intentionally makes a computer, server, network resource, or service unavailable to its intended users by overwhelming it with illegitimate requests, exploiting software flaws, or exhausting its critical resources (bandwidth, memory, or connection tables).

**Two Differences:**

| Aspect | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Single attacker system | Multiple distributed bot machines |
| **Traceability** | Easy to trace and block | Hard to trace due to distributed sources |
| **Traffic Volume** | Typically low (Mbps range) | Very high (Gbps–Tbps range) |
| **Defense** | Simple IP blocklist | Requires traffic scrubbing / anycast |

---

> **`Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** What is a botnet? How is it used in a DDoS attack?

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **botnet** is a collection of internet-connected devices (computers, IoT devices, servers) that have been infected with malware and are controlled remotely by an attacker, often without the device owners' knowledge. Each infected device is called a **bot** or **zombie**.

In a **DDoS attack**, the botmaster uses a **Command-and-Control (C2) server** to send attack instructions to all bots simultaneously. Each bot then floods the target with malicious traffic (SYN, UDP, ICMP, or HTTP requests), collectively generating traffic volumes that **saturate the victim's bandwidth or resources**, denying service to legitimate users. A famous example is the **Mirai botnet (2016)** which used ~145,000 IoT devices to attack Dyn DNS with **1.2 Tbps** of traffic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **`Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Module 1 Internal Choice A]`**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the different categories of DoS and DDoS attacks with suitable examples. Draw a neat labeled diagram of a DDoS attack architecture.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of a **SYN Flood attack** with a sequence diagram. Discuss the countermeasure **SYN Cookies** and explain how it mitigates the attack.

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The three principal categories of DoS/DDoS attacks are:

**(i) Volumetric Attacks (Bandwidth-Based):** These attacks consume all available network bandwidth so that legitimate packets cannot reach the server. Examples:
- **UDP Flood:** Sends a massive number of UDP packets to random ports; the server replies with ICMP "Destination Unreachable," consuming both upstream and downstream bandwidth.
- **ICMP Flood (Ping Flood):** Overwhelms the target with ICMP Echo Request packets.
- **DNS Amplification:** Sends small DNS queries (≈ 60 bytes) with a spoofed source IP to open DNS resolvers; the resolvers reply with much larger DNS responses (≈ 3,000 bytes), achieving an **amplification factor of ~50×**. The victim receives the large responses.
- **NTP Amplification:** Exploits the MONLIST command on public NTP servers (amplification factor **556×**).

**(ii) Protocol Attacks (State-Exhaustion):** These attacks exhaust server-side state tables, connection-tracking tables, or routing resources. Examples:
- **SYN Flood:** Exploits the TCP 3-way handshake. Attacker sends SYN, server replies SYN-ACK and reserves a TCB. The attacker never sends the final ACK. The half-open connection queue fills up.
- **Ping of Death:** Sends a malformed ICMP packet larger than the maximum allowed IP packet size (65,535 bytes), causing buffer overflow and crash on older systems.
- **Smurf Attack:** Sends ICMP Echo Request to a network's broadcast address with the spoofed victim's IP. Every host on the network replies to the victim.
- **Teardrop Attack:** Sends fragmented IP packets with overlapping offsets, crashing reassembly routines on older Windows/Linux kernels.

**(iii) Application-Layer Attacks (Layer 7):** These target specific applications (HTTP, DNS, SIP) and require fewer packets to be effective. Examples:
- **HTTP GET/POST Flood:** Sends a massive number of legitimate-looking HTTP requests.
- **Slowloris:** Opens many HTTP connections and slowly sends HTTP headers one byte at a time, never completing the request, exhausting the web server's connection pool.
- **RUDY (R-U-Dead-Yet):** Submits form data at an extremely slow rate to keep connections alive.

**DDoS Architecture Diagram** — Refer to the mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The architecture has 4 tiers: **Attacker → C2 Server → Botnet → Victim**.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Working of a SYN Flood Attack:**

1. The attacker (or each bot in a botnet) sends a **TCP SYN** packet to the victim's port 80 (or any open port), using a **spoofed source IP address**.
2. The victim's OS allocates a **TCP Control Block (TCB)** entry in the SYN backlog (≈ 280 bytes each) and sends back a **SYN-ACK** to the spoofed IP.
3. Because the spoofed IP does not exist (or is firewalled), the final **ACK** never arrives.
4. The half-open connection remains in the **SYN_RECV state** for the entire **TCP timeout period** (typically 75 seconds in older Linux kernels).
5. The attacker repeats this process hundreds or thousands of times per second, completely **filling the SYN backlog queue**.
6. When the queue is full, the OS **refuses all new TCP connections**, including legitimate users.
7. **Service is denied.**

**Refer to the sequence diagram in Section 4.3** of these notes for the visual representation.

**Countermeasure — SYN Cookies:**

SYN Cookies, introduced by **Daniel J. Bernstein**, eliminate the need for the server to allocate a TCB for every incoming SYN. The mechanism works as follows:

- The server computes a **cryptographic hash** of the client's IP, port, sequence number, and a server-side secret, and embeds this hash inside the **initial sequence number (ISN)** of the SYN-ACK reply.
- The server **does not save any state** — it sends the SYN-ACK and forgets the connection.
- When the client's ACK arrives, the server **recomputes the hash** from the ACK's sequence number and verifies the connection. Only then is a TCB created.
- Because no TCB is allocated for spoofed SYNs, **SYN Cookies are completely immune to SYN flood attacks** as long as the cryptographic secret is not leaked.

---

> **`Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Module 1 Internal Choice B]`**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** What is a botnet? Explain the **Mirai botnet** architecture and its impact. List the various DDoS defense mechanisms available at network, host, and application layers.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A network administrator notices an HTTP GET flood attack targeting a web server. The server has a capacity of **10,000 RPS** and is currently receiving **45,000 RPS** of HTTP requests. Each legitimate user requires **5 RPS** for a smooth experience. Calculate: (i) The number of legitimate users that can still be served, and (ii) The number of bots involved if each bot generates **100 RPS**.

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

> **Botnet:** A botnet is a network of internet-connected devices infected with malware (bots/zombies) under the remote control of an attacker (botmaster) via a Command-and-Control (C2) infrastructure.

**Mirai Botnet Architecture (October 2016):**

- **Targeting:** IoT devices (IP cameras, DVRs, home routers) running default or weak telnet credentials.
- **Infection:** Mirai scanned the internet for telnet (port 23), tried a list of **~60 default username/password pairs** (e.g., `admin/admin`, `root/12345`), and infected the device if successful.
- **Command & Control:** Mirai used a **decentralized, encrypted C2 channel** based on a custom protocol, making takedown difficult.
- **Scale:** At peak, Mirai had **~145,000 active bots**, with peak output of **1.2 Tbps**.
- **Major Attack:** Targeted **Dyn DNS** on **October 21, 2016**, causing major outages to Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, GitHub, and AirBnB across the US and Europe.

**DDoS Defense Mechanisms:**

| Layer | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Network Layer** | Rate limiting, ingress/egress filtering (BCP 38), blackholing (RTBH), sinkholing, anycast scrubbing |
| **Host Layer** | SYN cookies, connection-rate throttling, disabling unused services, host firewalls (iptables), kernel hardening (sysctl) |
| **Application Layer** | CAPTCHA, JS-challenge, browser fingerprinting, WAF rules (e.g., ModSecurity), rate limiting per IP/API-key |
| **ISP / Cloud** | Clean-pipe services (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield Advanced) |

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical

**Given:**
- Server capacity $C = 10{,}000$ RPS
- Incoming attack rate $R_{\text{total}} = 45{,}000$ RPS
- Per-legitimate-user requirement $r_{\text{user}} = 5$ RPS
- Per-bot generation rate $r_{\text{bot}} = 100$ RPS

**Step 1 — Identify the attack traffic.**
Assume all incoming traffic is competing for the same pool of server resources. The total attack + legitimate traffic is $45{,}000$ RPS, but the server can only handle $10{,}000$ RPS.

**Step 2 — Compute the available bandwidth for legitimate users (under saturation).**
The server's entire capacity is consumed by attack traffic, so legitimate users receive $0$ RPS in the worst case. However, in practice, fair-queue schedulers and rate-limiters allocate proportionally.

**Step 3 — (i) Legitimate users that can be served.**
Under worst-case saturation, the number of legitimate users is:

$$
N_{\text{users}} = \frac{C}{r_{\text{user}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
N_{\text{users}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ RPS}}{5 \text{ RPS/user}} = 2{,}000 \text{ users}
$$

> **Result (i):** A maximum of **2,000 legitimate users** can be served smoothly.

**Step 5 — (ii) Number of bots involved.**
The attack traffic portion is approximately $R_{\text{attack}} \approx 45{,}000$ RPS (assuming all 45,000 RPS is malicious). Number of bots:

$$
N_{\text{bots}} = \frac{R_{\text{attack}}}{r_{\text{bot}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute values.**

$$
N_{\text{bots}} = \frac{45{,}000 \text{ RPS}}{100 \text{ RPS/bot}} = 450 \text{ bots}
$$

> **Result (ii):** Approximately **450 bots** are generating the attack traffic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Reasons Students Lose Marks Here:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** clearly (e.g., "assuming all 45,000 RPS is attack traffic"). Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing assumptions.
> 2. **Mixing up RPS with concurrent users.** RPS = requests per second, not connections. Always state units.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract legitimate traffic from total** if the problem gives both values. Re-read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Skipping the formula definition step** — define $N_{\text{bots}}$, $r_{\text{bot}}$, $C$ before substituting. Examiners reward **structured working**.
> 5. **Not writing the final answer in a box** — KTU valuation scripts look for boxed answers in 14-mark numerical sub-parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this before your exam!)**

- ✅ **DoS** = single source, **DDoS** = distributed botnet source.
- ✅ **CIA Triad impact:** DoS attacks break the **Availability** pillar.
- ✅ **Three attack categories:** Volumetric (bandwidth), Protocol (state exhaustion), Application-Layer (L7).
- ✅ **SYN Flood** is a **Protocol attack** that fills the **TCP SYN_RECV backlog** with half-open connections.
- ✅ **SYN Cookies** are the standard defense — they embed state inside the ISN cryptographically.
- ✅ **Amplification factor** is response-size ÷ request-size. **DNS ≈ 50×, NTP ≈ 556×.**
- ✅ **Botnet = Botmaster + C2 + Agents (Zombies) + Victim.** The **Mirai botnet (2016)** is the canonical real-world case study.
- ✅ **Smurf Attack** uses **ICMP echo to a broadcast address** with a spoofed victim IP.
- ✅ **Slowloris** uses **partial HTTP headers** sent slowly to exhaust the web server's connection pool.
- ✅ **Defense layers:** Network (RTBH, anycast), Host (SYN cookies, firewalls), Application (WAF, CAPTCHA).
- ✅ **Key bandwidth formula:** $B_{\text{attack}} = N_{\text{bots}} \times B_{\text{per-bot}} \times AF$.
- ✅ **SYN memory cost formula:** $M_{\text{syn}} = N_{\text{syn}} \times S_{\text{tcb}}$ (use $S_{\text{tcb}} = 280$ bytes).
- ✅ **Real-world benchmarks:** Mirai = 1.2 Tbps; GitHub 2018 = 1.35 Tbps (memcached amplification); AWS Shield mitigated a 2.3 Tbps attack in 2020.
- ✅ **In exam answers:** Always include a **diagram** (3-tier DDoS architecture or TCP sequence diagram) — diagrams carry easy marks.
- ✅ **CO Mapping:** This topic is mapped to **CO1** (threat identification) and **CO2** (IDPS tool analysis) of the PECCT742 syllabus.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worm (Computer Worm):** A *self-replicating, self-propagating* malicious program that spreads autonomously across computer networks by exploiting vulnerabilities in target systems, **without requiring human intervention** or host-file attachment, to consume bandwidth, exhaust resources, drop payloads, or create backdoors.

A **worm attack** is the *complete operational cycle* in which a worm propagates from an initially infected host (the **patient zero** or **index node**) to a population of vulnerable systems, executes its payload, and potentially initiates secondary malicious actions such as Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS), data exfiltration, or botnet recruitment.

| **Worm vs. Virus – KTU Distinction** | **Worm** | **Virus** |
|---|---|---|
| Human Action Required? | **No** (autonomous) | **Yes** (host file execution) |
| Propagation Vector | Network (TCP/IP, RPC, SMB) | File system / Email attachment |
| Carrier | Self-contained binary | Inserts into legitimate host code |
| Speed of Spread | **Exponential (minutes/hours)** | Slow (requires user action) |
| Detection Difficulty | High (network-layer anomaly) | Moderate (file-integrity monitoring) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Biological Epidemic:**
> Imagine a contagious disease in a crowded city where **every infected person automatically sneezes on 10 random strangers per hour**, instantly infecting them, who then do the same. Within hours, the entire city is infected. A computer worm behaves *identically*: every compromised host probes a set of random or strategic IP addresses per second, infects vulnerable ones, and continues the chain.

**Why "Worms"?** — Early researchers (Shoch & Hupp of Xerox PARC, 1982) named them after the *Tapeworm* program, which crept through Ethernet networks. The term stuck because the malware "burrows" from machine to machine like a parasitic worm in soil.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Mandatory Bold Highlighting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU-Exam Metrics You MUST Memorize:**
> - **R₀ (Basic Reproductive Number):** Average number of new infections produced by one infected host. If **R₀ > 1**, the worm spreads; if **R₀ < 1**, it dies out.
> - **Propagation Speed (v):** Number of newly infected hosts per unit time. Measured in **hosts/second**.
> - **Hit-List Size (H):** Pre-collected list of known-vulnerable IPs carried inside the worm binary.
> - **Scan Rate (s):** Number of probe packets generated per infected host per second. Range: **s = 100 (Code Red)** to **s = 4000 (Slammer)**.
> - **Time-to-Infection (T):** Time required to infect a fraction (e.g., 90%) of the vulnerable population.
