# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Worm Attack — Structured Operational Phases

A worm attack progresses through **five distinct phases**. Understanding each phase is essential for both KTU theory and intrusion-detection engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 1 — Reconnaissance / War-Seeding:**
> The attacker pre-compiles a *hit-list* of IP addresses belonging to systems known to run vulnerable services (e.g., IIS 4.0 for Code Red). This is called **war-seeding**. Some worms (e.g., **Nmap-based worms**) perform live scanning at runtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 2 — Exploitation / Infection:**
> The worm payload sends a crafted packet to the target's vulnerable port (e.g., TCP/80 for Code Red, UDP/1434 for Slammer). Upon successful exploitation, the target executes a small bootstrap shellcode that connects back to the attacker (or another infected host) to download the full worm binary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 3 — Replication / Propagation:**
> The newly infected host begins scanning and infecting other targets. This is the **exponential growth phase** governed by the propagation model (see Section 3).

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 4 — Payload Execution:**
> After a delay (often randomized to evade detection), the worm activates its malicious payload:
> - **DDoS launch** (e.g., Code Red II attacked www.whitehouse.gov)
> - **Backdoor installation** (e.g., Blaster opened TCP/4444)
> - **Spam/botnet recruitment**
> - **Data destruction** (e.g., payloads targeting SCADA/ICS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 5 — Covering Tracks / Persistence:**
> Advanced worms (e.g., **Stuxnet**) install rootkits, disable antivirus, and modify system binaries to remain undetected across reboots.

## 2.2 Worm Propagation Models — Conceptual Foundation

| **Model** | **Differential Equations** | **Key Assumption** | **Real Worm Fit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Epidemic Model (SEM)** | dI/dt = β·I·(N−I) | Infinite susceptible pool, no recovery | Code Red, early phase |
| **Two-Factor Model** | dI/dt = β(t)·I·(N−I)−R(t) | Includes human countermeasures (patching, blackholing) | Slammer, late phase |
| **Kermack–McKendrick (SIR)** | dS/dt = −β·S·I, dI/dt = β·S·I − δ·I, dR/dt = δ·I | Includes recovery/immunity | Slow worms, post-patching |

Where:
- **I(t)** = number of infected hosts at time *t*
- **S(t)** = number of susceptible (vulnerable, uninfected) hosts
- **R(t)** = number of removed (patched/immunized) hosts
- **N** = total vulnerable population (N = S + I + R)
- **β** = pair-wise contact rate (infection coefficient)
- **δ** = removal rate (counter-defense rate)
- **R₀ = β·N/δ** → threshold condition for epidemic outbreak

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your KTU Exam "lifeline" — every formula below has been asked in previous board papers.**

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **LaTeX Form** | **Units / Meaning** | **Applicability** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Epidemic growth | $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$ | hosts/second | All scanning worms |
| 2 | Logistic solution | $I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + e^{-\beta N (t - T)}}$ | cumulative infected | Slow phase of Code Red |
| 3 | Basic reproductive number | $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\delta}$ | dimensionless | Outbreak threshold |
| 4 | Scan-rate propagation | $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \dfrac{s}{2^{32}} \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$ | hosts/sec (IPv4) | Random-scan worms |
| 5 | Equilibrium (final size) | $I_\infty = N \cdot (1 - e^{-R_0})$ | total infected | SIR model |
| 6 | Slammer scan coefficient | $a = \dfrac{s}{\Omega}$, where $\Omega = 2^{32}$ | dimensionless | UDP worms |
| 7 | Hit-list acceleration | $I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{\beta t}$ initially | exponential | War-seeded phase |
| 8 | Bandwidth saturation time | $t_{sat} = \dfrac{\Omega \cdot N}{s \cdot I(t)^2}$ | seconds | DDoS-onset prediction |
| 9 | Mean inter-scan time | $\bar{\tau} = \dfrac{\Omega}{s}$ | seconds/host | Random scanner |
| 10 | Code Red infection ratio | $I/N \approx 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$ at $t \to \infty$ | fraction | Empirical validation |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by **CERT/CC analysts, network engineers, and IDS rule-set designers** to (a) predict when a worm will saturate a network, (b) compute minimum blackholing rate to suppress R₀ below 1, and (c) validate IDS/IPS alert thresholds during zero-day outbreaks.

## 2.4 Propagation Strategy Taxonomy

| **Strategy** | **Mechanism** | **Pros for Worm** | **Cons for Worm** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Random Scan** | Probe random IPs in 2³² space | Simple, no pre-collection | Wastes probes, slow | Slammer, Blaster |
| **Hit-list Scan** | Carries pre-scanned list | Very fast initial spread | Hit-list can be stale | Code Red II |
| **Topological Scan** | Uses victim info (e.g., email) | Efficient, low bandwidth | Requires info leakage | Email worms |
| **Local-Preference Scan** | Prefers same /16 subnet | Blends with normal traffic | Limited reach | Nimda |
| **Permutation Scan** | Divides 2³² into partitions | Parallelizes, no overlap | Coordination needed | Warhol worm |
| **Sequential Scan** | Scans in numeric order | Maximizes local saturation | Trivially detected | early Morris |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple Epidemic Model (SEM) Logistic Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for $I(t)$ in a worm propagation scenario with no recovery and a finite susceptible population.

### **Step 1: State the Governing ODE**

The infection rate is proportional to the product of (a) the number of infected hosts and (b) the number of uninfected (susceptible) hosts:

$$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$$

**Logic:** Each infected host can contact any of the $(N - I)$ remaining vulnerable hosts. The contact rate per pair is $\beta$. Hence, the total new-infection rate is the product $\beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$.

### **Step 2: Separate Variables**

We move all $I$-dependent terms to the LHS and the $t$-dependent term to the RHS:

$$\dfrac{dI}{I \cdot (N - I)} = \beta \cdot dt$$

**Logic:** Standard separation-of-variables technique from first-order ODE theory.

### **Step 3: Apply Partial-Fraction Decomposition**

The LHS integrand is decomposed as:

$$\dfrac{1}{I \cdot (N - I)} = \dfrac{1}{N} \left( \dfrac{1}{I} + \dfrac{1}{N - I} \right)$$

**Logic:** Verify by recomposition: $\frac{1}{N}\left[\frac{N-I + I}{I(N-I)}\right] = \frac{1}{I(N-I)}$. ✓

### **Step 4: Integrate Both Sides**

Integrating from $t = 0$ (when $I = I_0$) to arbitrary $t$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(t)} \dfrac{1}{N} \left( \dfrac{1}{I} + \dfrac{1}{N - I} \right) dI = \int_0^{t} \beta \cdot d\tau$$

Evaluating both integrals:

$$\dfrac{1}{N} \Big[ \ln I - \ln(N - I) \Big]_{I_0}^{I(t)} = \beta \cdot t$$

$$\dfrac{1}{N} \ln \left( \dfrac{I(t) \cdot (N - I_0)}{I_0 \cdot (N - I(t))} \right) = \beta \cdot t$$

**Logic:** The integral of $1/(N-I)$ requires a sign flip: $\int \frac{dI}{N-I} = -\ln|N-I|$, but the subtraction in the bracket $[f(I(t)) - f(I_0)]$ reverses this and yields the expression above.

### **Step 5: Solve Algebraically for I(t)**

Multiply by $N$ and exponentiate both sides:

$$\dfrac{I(t) \cdot (N - I_0)}{I_0 \cdot (N - I(t))} = e^{\beta N t}$$

Let $C = \dfrac{N - I_0}{I_0} \cdot e^{-\beta N T}$ (a constant absorbing initial conditions and the time-shift $T$). Solving the resulting rational equation:

$$I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + C \cdot e^{-\beta N t}}$$

### **Step 6: Final Canonical Form (Logistic / Sigmoidal)**

For the symmetric case $I_0 = N/2$, the time-shift $T = 0$ and the result simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\, I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + e^{-\beta N (t - T)}} \,}$$

**Logic:** This is the classical **logistic (sigmoidal)** growth curve. It is *S-shaped*: slow start (latency), explosive middle (exponential), and saturation (asymptote at $N$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Basic Reproductive Number (R₀) from SIR Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Determine the threshold condition for worm outbreak.

### **Step 1: SIR Model Equations**

$$\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S \cdot I$$

$$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \delta \cdot I$$

$$\dfrac{dR}{dt} = \delta \cdot I$$

**Logic:** Susceptibles decrease due to infection; infecteds increase by new infection and decrease by recovery (patching/defensive removal); recovereds increase by removal.

### **Step 2: Linear Stability Analysis at Disease-Free Equilibrium (DFE)**

At DFE: $S(0) = N$, $I(0) \approx 0$, $R(0) = 0$. Perturb $I$ by a small amount $\epsilon$:

$$\dfrac{dI}{dt} \approx \beta \cdot N \cdot I - \delta \cdot I = (\beta N - \delta) \cdot I$$

### **Step 3: Solve the Linearized ODE**

$$I(t) = I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta N - \delta) t}$$

**Logic:** The exponent determines growth/decay.

### **Step 4: Identify the Outbreak Threshold**

For *epidemic outbreak*, we need the exponent to be positive:

$$\beta N - \delta > 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dfrac{\beta N}{\delta} > 1$$

Define the basic reproductive number:

$$\boxed{\, R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\delta} \,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

| **Condition** | **R₀ Value** | **Worm Fate** |
|---|---|---|
| $R_0 < 1$ | Subcritical | Dies out exponentially |
| $R_0 = 1$ | Critical | Endemic equilibrium |
| $R_0 > 1$ | Supercritical | **Worm outbreak (epidemic)** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Random-Scan Worm Propagation Coefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Express the propagation coefficient $\beta$ in terms of physical network parameters.

### **Step 1: Define Parameters**

- $s$ = scan rate (probes per second per infected host)
- $\Omega$ = IP address space size; for IPv4, $\Omega = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$
- $V$ = vulnerable subpopulation
- The probability that any single probe hits a vulnerable host is $V / \Omega$

### **Step 2: Construct the Effective Contact Rate**

Each infected host makes $s$ probes per second. The expected number of successful infections per second per infected host is:

$$\beta = \dfrac{s \cdot V}{\Omega}$$

**Logic:** This is the product of scan rate and the per-probe hit probability. For a uniform random distribution of vulnerable hosts.

### **Step 3: Substitute into the SEM Equation**

$$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I) = \left( \dfrac{s V}{\Omega} \right) \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$$

### **Step 4: Initial-Phase Exponential Approximation**

For $t \ll T$ (early phase), $N - I \approx N$:

$$\dfrac{dI}{dt} \approx \left( \dfrac{s V N}{\Omega} \right) \cdot I \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I(t) \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left( \dfrac{s V N}{\Omega} \cdot t \right)$$

**Logic:** This explains the **blistering speed of Slammer**: $s = 4000$ probes/sec gave it the fastest recorded worm outbreak — full Internet saturation in **~10 minutes**.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Worm Attack Lifecycle (Phase Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The flow below models the *operational lifecycle* of a typical Internet worm from attacker deployment to global saturation. It is engineered for KTU examination answers requiring labelled functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["ATTACKER DESIGNS WORM BINARY"] --> A2["PHASE 1: WAR SEEDING"]
    A2 --> A3["PHASE 1B: COLLECT HIT LIST OF VULNERABLE IPS"]
    A3 --> B1["PHASE 2: DEPLOY ON PATIENT ZERO"]
    B1 --> B2["PHASE 2B: EXPLOIT VULNERABILITY BUFFER OVERFLOW"]
    B2 --> C1["PHASE 3A: BEGIN SCANNING NETWORK"]
    C1 --> C2{"RANDOM SCAN STRATEGY"}
    C2 --> C3["PROBE IP ADDRESSES IN IPV4 SPACE"]
    C3 --> D1{"TARGET VULNERABLE"}
    D1 -- YES --> D2["PHASE 3B: SEND SHELLCODE TO TARGET"]
    D1 -- NO --> C3
    D2 --> E1["PHASE 3C: TARGET BECOMES INFECTED HOST"]
    E1 --> F1{"PAYLOAD TRIGGER TIME REACHED"}
    F1 -- NOT YET --> C1
    F1 -- YES --> G1["PHASE 4: EXECUTE PAYLOAD"]
    G1 --> G2["DDOS LAUNCH"]
    G1 --> G3["BACKDOOR INSTALL"]
    G1 --> G4["BOTNET RECRUITMENT"]
    G1 --> G5["DATA EXFILTRATION"]
    E1 --> H1["PHASE 5: COVER TRACKS"]
    H1 --> H2["INSTALL ROOTKIT"]
    H1 --> H3["DISABLE ANTIVIRUS"]
    H1 --> H4["MODIFY SYSTEM BINARIES"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Epidemiological SIR State Transition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S : HOST JOINS NETWORK
    S : SUSCEPTIBLE VULNERABLE HOST
    I : INFECTED ACTIVE WORM CARRIER
    R : REMOVED PATCHED OR IMMUNIZED
    S --> I : BETA TIMES I CONTACT INFECTION
    I --> R : DELTA RATE PATCH OR BLACKHOLE
    R --> [*] : DEFENDED PERMANENTLY
    I --> I : SCAN AND PROPAGATE AUTONOMOUSLY
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Detection & Prevention Counter-Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering context:** This block-level diagram shows how an Intrusion Detection & Prevention System (IDPS) sits *inline* in the network data path to detect and block worm traffic at multiple OSI layers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W["WORM INFECTED HOST"] -->|SCAN PROBES| FW["PERIMETER FIREWALL"]
    FW -->|ALLOWED TRAFFIC| IDS["INTRUSION DETECTION SYSTEM SIGNATURE BASED"]
    IDS -->|ANOMALY DETECTED| IPS["INTRUSION PREVENTION SYSTEM INLINE BLOCKING"]
    IPS -->|CLEAN TRAFFIC| NS["NETWORK SWITCH CORE ROUTER"]
    NS --> VICTIM["VICTIM SERVER UNINFECTED"]
    IDS -->|ALERT| SIEM["SIEM LOG CORRELATION ENGINE"]
    IPS -->|BLOCK RULE PUSH| FW
    SIEM -->|THREAT INTEL FEED| IDS
```

**Layered defence logic:**
- **Firewall:** Stateless filtering of blacklisted IPs/ports
- **IDS (Signature-based):** Matches known worm byte-sequences (Snort rules for Code Red, Slammer)
- **IPS (Inline):** *Drops* packets matching the signature, preventing payload delivery
- **SIEM:** Aggregates alerts across the enterprise and detects slow-propagation worms
- **Threat-Intel Feedback Loop:** Newly identified IOCs (Indicators of Compromise) are pushed to FW and IDS for proactive blocking

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Worm and a Virus. List any two famous worms with their year and exploited vulnerability.**

**Model Answer (Concise — KTU Pattern):**

| **Sl.** | **Worm** | **Virus** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-propagating; does **not** need human action | Needs a host file to be executed by the user |
| 2 | Spreads via network protocols (TCP/IP) | Spreads via file/email attachment |
| 3 | Carries its own execution engine | Inserts code into legitimate programs |

**Two Famous Worms:**

1. **Slammer (Sapphire) Worm — 2003** — Exploited buffer overflow in **Microsoft SQL Server** on UDP port **1434**. Achieved full Internet saturation in ~10 minutes with a scan rate of **4000 probes/second**.

2. **Code Red v2 Worm — 2001** — Exploited buffer overflow in **Microsoft IIS 4.0/5.0** on TCP port **80**. Used random scan with hit-list seeding; infected ~359,000 hosts and launched DDoS on `www.whitehouse.gov`.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**
**Define the Basic Reproductive Number R₀ in worm epidemiology. State the threshold condition for a worm outbreak.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Basic Reproductive Number (R₀)** is defined as the *expected number of secondary infections produced by a single infected host in a completely susceptible population*. Mathematically: $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\delta}$, where $\beta$ is the pair-wise infection rate, $N$ is the vulnerable population, and $\delta$ is the host-removal (defensive) rate.

**Threshold Condition:**
- If $R_0 > 1$ → **worm outbreak** (epidemic spreads)
- If $R_0 < 1$ → worm **dies out** exponentially
- If $R_0 = 1$ → **endemic** (steady state)

> **For a worm to be suppressed, network defenders must reduce $R_0$ below 1 by increasing $\delta$ (e.g., mass patching, sinkholing) or by reducing $\beta$ (firewalling, IPS blocking).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (Strict 2024 Scheme Compliance):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer EITHER the full OR-pair (Q1 OR Q2, Q3 OR Q4, etc.). Below, we provide a complete OR-pair with full model solutions.

---

### **🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Simple Epidemic Model (SEM)** for worm propagation. Derive its solution $I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + e^{-\beta N (t - T)}}$ step-by-step. State one real-world limitation of this model.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The SEM assumes a fixed, finite population of $N$ vulnerable hosts, an infection rate $\beta$ proportional to the product of infected and susceptible hosts, and **no recovery/removal**.

**Governing ODE:**

$$\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I)$$

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Separate variables:** $\dfrac{dI}{I(N - I)} = \beta \cdot dt$

2. **Partial fractions:** $\dfrac{1}{I(N-I)} = \dfrac{1}{N}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{I} + \dfrac{1}{N-I}\right)$

3. **Integrate** from $I_0$ to $I(t)$:

$$\dfrac{1}{N}\Big[\ln I - \ln(N-I)\Big]_{I_0}^{I(t)} = \beta t$$

4. **Rearrange:**

$$\ln \dfrac{I(t)\,(N-I_0)}{I_0\,(N-I(t))} = \beta N t$$

5. **Exponentiate** and solve for $I(t)$:

$$\dfrac{I(t)}{N - I(t)} = \dfrac{I_0}{N - I_0} \cdot e^{\beta N t}$$

6. **Invert** to obtain the closed form:

$$I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + \left(\dfrac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-\beta N t}}$$

Defining $T$ such that $e^{\beta N T} = \dfrac{N - I_0}{I_0}$ yields the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\, I(t) = \dfrac{N}{1 + e^{-\beta N (t - T)}} \,}$$

**Limitation:** SEM ignores **defensive countermeasures** (patching, IDS blocking), so it over-predicts the final infection count. The **Two-Factor Model** by Zou et al. (2002) fixes this by making $\beta$ time-varying.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A network administrator observes a Code-Red-like worm outbreak. The vulnerable population is $N = 100{,}000$ hosts, the scan rate per infected host is $s = 100$ probes/sec, and the address space is $\Omega = 2^{32}$. Compute **(i)** the propagation coefficient $\beta$ (assuming all hosts are vulnerable), **(ii)** the expected number of new infections in the first hour, and **(iii)** recommend **two IPS-level countermeasures** to bring $R_0$ below 1.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Propagation Coefficient:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{s \cdot V}{\Omega} = \dfrac{100 \times 100{,}000}{2^{32}} = \dfrac{10^{7}}{4.294 \times 10^{9}} \approx 2.328 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per host·sec}$$

**(ii) Early-phase infections (using exponential approximation, $I_0 = 1$):**

$$I(t) \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t} = 1 \cdot e^{(2.328 \times 10^{-3})(100{,}000)(3600)}$$

$$I(1\text{ hr}) = e^{838{,}080} \quad \text{(mathematically enormous)}$$

> **Engineering insight:** This shows why Code Red saturated the Internet in hours — the product $\beta N$ is enormous. In reality, the *nonlinear* logistic model caps it at $I \to N = 100{,}000$ as $t \to \infty$.

**(iii) Two IPS-level Countermeasures:**

1. **Sinkholing + Blackhole Routing:** Force DNS/route all malicious traffic (e.g., toward the worm's C2 domain) to a *null route*, artificially increasing the effective removal rate $\delta$ and pushing $R_0$ below 1.
2. **Rate-Limiting Per-Source-IP SYN Packets:** Configure the IPS to throttle outbound scans to ≤10 probes/sec per host, reducing $s$ and thus $\beta$ — directly attacking the $R_0$ numerator.

---

### **🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **five operational phases of a worm attack lifecycle**. For each phase, give one real-world example.

**Model Solution:**

| **Phase** | **Activity** | **Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Reconnaissance / War-Seeding** | Attacker collects a hit-list of IPs running vulnerable services via tools like Nmap | **Code Red II** carried a pre-scanned hit-list of 10,000 IIS servers |
| **2. Exploitation / Infection** | Crafted packet triggers buffer overflow / RCE on victim | **Blaster** exploited MS DCOM RPC vulnerability (MS03-026) on TCP/135 |
| **3. Replication / Propagation** | Newly infected host begins scanning and infecting others | **Slammer** saturated 75,000 hosts in 10 minutes via UDP/1434 |
| **4. Payload Execution** | Worm activates its malicious payload | **Stuxnet** payload reprogrammed Siemens S7-315 PLCs to over-spin centrifuges |
| **5. Covering Tracks / Persistence** | Worm installs rootkit, modifies logs, survives reboot | **Conficker** disabled Windows AutoUpdate and blocked AV vendor domains |

*(Block diagram: see Section 4.1 above; candidates may reproduce a labelled phase-flow.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Random Scan, Hit-list Scan, and Topological Scan** worm propagation strategies. For each, state one advantage, one disadvantage, and one real-world worm that uses it.

**Model Solution:**

| **Strategy** | **Advantage** | **Disadvantage** | **Example Worm** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Random Scan** | Simple; no pre-collection required | Wastes probes on non-vulnerable IPs; slow | **Slammer** (UDP/1434 random scan at 4000 pps) |
| **Hit-list Scan** | Extremely fast initial growth; low wastage | Hit-list becomes stale; large binary size | **Code Red v2** (initial hit-list of 10,000 IIS servers) |
| **Topological Scan** | Bandwidth-efficient; targets trusted neighbours | Requires victim to leak internal info (e.g., email contacts) | **Email worms** like **Nimda** (used email address books to spread) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Modern hybrid worms (e.g., **Conficker**) use a *combination* of all three strategies — random scan for breadth, hit-list for speed, and P2P/topological for redundancy. This is why single-strategy defences (e.g., blocking random scans) are insufficient.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Worm" with "Virus":** Examiners award **zero partial credit** if you use the terms interchangeably. Memorise the distinction (Section 1.1).
> 2. **Skipping Derivation Steps in Part-B:** A 7-mark derivation question demands *all* intermediate steps. Showing only the final $I(t) = N/(1+e^{-\beta N(t-T)})$ earns ≤2 marks. Always show separation of variables, partial fractions, integration, and algebraic isolation.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** "Compute the propagation coefficient" must end with units (per host·sec), not just a number.
> 4. **Missing Countermeasure Specificity:** Saying "install antivirus" is too generic. Examiners expect **technical specificity** — e.g., "deploy an inline IPS with Snort signature `sid:2003:1434` (Slammer-specific) on the perimeter gateway."
> 5. **Not Drawing the Block Diagram:** A 7-mark Phase-Question with *no diagram* loses 2 marks. Always include a labelled phase-flow.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and keep it next to your exam hall revision sheet.**

- ✅ **Worm** = self-replicating, self-propagating, **no human action** needed (vs. virus which needs host execution).
- ✅ **Five-phase lifecycle:** Reconnaissance → Exploitation → Replication → Payload → Cover Tracks.
- ✅ **Six propagation strategies:** Random, Hit-list, Topological, Local-preference, Permutation, Sequential.
- ✅ **Simple Epidemic Model ODE:** $dI/dt = \beta I (N - I)$, solution is the **logistic curve** $I(t) = N / (1 + e^{-\beta N (t - T)})$.
- ✅ **Basic Reproductive Number:** $R_0 = \beta N / \delta$. **Outbreak iff $R_0 > 1$.**
- ✅ **Random-scan coefficient:** $\beta = sV / \Omega$, where $\Omega = 2^{32}$ for IPv4.
- ✅ **Slammer** = fastest worm in history (10 min, 4000 pps, UDP/1434).
- ✅ **Code Red** = TCP/80, IIS exploit, hit-list seeded, 359K hosts in 14 hrs.
- ✅ **Morris (1988)** = first Internet worm, sequential scan, $10M–100M$ damage.
- ✅ **Stuxnet (2010)** = topological + USB + printer; targeted ICS/SCADA.
- ✅ **Two-Factor Model** = improved SEM that incorporates time-varying $\beta(t)$ and removal $R(t)$.
- ✅ **Defence layers:** Firewall → IDS (signature) → IPS (inline block) → SIEM (correlation) → Threat-Intel feedback.
- ✅ **Engineering goal:** Push $R_0 < 1$ via patching (raise $\delta$) or rate-limiting (lower $s$, hence lower $\beta$).
- ✅ **Final-size theorem (SIR):** $I_\infty = N(1 - e^{-R_0})$ — useful for predicting total impact of an outbreak.
- ✅ **Zero-day worms** (e.g., Conficker) require **anomaly-based IDS**, not signature-based, since signatures lag the outbreak.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Routing Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routing Attacks** are a category of network-layer offensive operations in which an adversary deliberately injects, manipulates, suppresses, or fabricates routing control-plane messages (e.g., RIP updates, OSPF LSAs, EIGRP Hellos, or BGP UPDATE/AS_PATH attributes) with the intent to subvert the path-selection process of interior (IGP) or exterior (EGP) gateway protocols.

The objective is to redirect, blackhole, eavesdrop upon, or destabilize legitimate IP traffic flows. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (Course Outcome **CO1**: *Understand the threat landscape of network-layer attacks*), routing attacks are classified as a **control-plane** attack class — they target the *decision-making layer* rather than the data forwarding plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Routing Attacks are studied under the broader umbrella of *Network-Layer Threats* alongside IP Spoofing, ARP Poisoning, and ICMP-based Denial-of-Service. The CO mapping is **CO1 / Bloom Level: Understand (L2) → Analyze (L4)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **postal system** of a large country. Every city has a Sorting Office (a router). Each Sorting Office periodically tells its neighbours: *"The best road to city X passes through me."* This announcement is the *routing update*.

A **Routing Attack** is equivalent to a malicious insider in one Sorting Office shouting:
1. *"City X has been demolished — send all mail for X to my private warehouse"* (Route Hijacking / Blackhole).
2. *"I am the official Sorting Office for the entire country"* (Route Injection / Prefix Hijack).
3. *"There is a secret tunnel that delivers mail 10× faster, trust me"* (Wormhole / Route Falsification).

The honest Sorting Offices believe the lie, update their maps, and start misrouting real mail. The result: lost letters, stolen parcels, and systemic chaos.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as a GPS spoofing attack misleads a driver, a routing attack misleads *every router in an Autonomous System* by corrupting the very maps they use to make forwarding decisions.

## 1.3 Critical Terminology (KTU Board Favourites)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous System (AS)** | A collection of IP networks and routers under a single administrative policy, identified by a unique **16-bit** or **32-bit ASN**. |
| **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)** | The de-facto **path-vector** EGP of the Internet, running over **TCP port 179**. |
| **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** | A **link-state** IGP using **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm**. |
| **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)** | A **distance-vector** IGP using **hop count** as its metric (max **15 hops**). |
| **Route Hijacking** | The unauthorized announcement of IP prefixes belonging to another AS. |
| **Route Leaking** | The intentional or accidental propagation of routes to unintended peer ASes. |
| **RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure)** | A cryptographic validation framework to verify BGP route origin authorization (**ROA**). |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Routing Attacks (Breakdown)

Routing attacks can be classified along **three orthogonal axes** — *protocol class*, *attacker position*, and *objective*. The hierarchical structure below is the de-facto KTU 2024 expectation.

### Axis A — By Protocol Class
- **Distance-Vector Attacks (RIP, IGRP)**
  - *Count-to-Infinity* exploitation
  - *Route Poisoning* reverse engineering
  - *Bellman-Ford* convergence disruption
- **Link-State Attacks (OSPF, IS-IS)**
  - *LSA Injection* / *LSA Storming*
  - *Sequence number wrap-around* (OSPF MaxAge = **3600 seconds**, MaxSequence = **0x7FFFFFFF**)
  - *Disguised LSA* (Type-1 router-LSA spoofing)
- **Path-Vector Attacks (BGP)**
  - *Prefix Hijacking* (more-specific / equally-specific)
  - *AS_PATH manipulation* (prepending shortening, fake hops)
  - *MED / Local-Pref* tampering
  - *BGP Session Reset* via TCP RST injection
  - *Route Flap Dampening* exploitation (threshold = **eBGP default 2000** flaps)
- **Hybrid / Proactive Routing (EIGRP, AODV, DSR)**
  - *Stale-route advertisement*
  - *Successor/Feasible-Successor* poisoning

### Axis B — By Attacker Position
1. **On-Path Attacker** (MITM with full topology visibility).
2. **Off-Path Attacker** (blind spoofing of source IP and TCP sequence numbers — harder, but feasible).
3. **Compromised-Legitimate-Router** (the most devastating — the attacker IS the AS, e.g., the 2008 *Pakistan Telecom – YouTube* incident).

### Axis C — By Objective
- **Blackhole Attack** — drop 100% of targeted traffic.
- **Grayhole Attack** — selectively drop packets (stealthier).
- **Wormhole Attack** — tunnel packets via a low-latency out-of-band link to bypass normal metrics.
- **Sinkhole Attack** — attract traffic by advertising overly attractive routes, then analyze or drop.
- **Sybil Attack** — attacker presents multiple fake identities to subvert distributed routing consensus.

## 2.2 Operational Mechanism — The "Why & How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Vulnerability of Routing Protocols:** They were designed in an era of *mutual trust* (1980s — RIP, 1990s — OSPF/BGP). They possess **no built-in cryptographic authentication of route origin** in their base specifications. MD5/TCP-AO authentication in OSPF/BGP is an *add-on*, not native.

**How a BGP Prefix Hijack Exploits This Trust:**

1. **Attacker** announces a more-specific prefix (e.g., `/24` versus the legitimate `/22`) for victim AS.
2. **BGP best-path selection** favours the *longest-match prefix* — the more-specific always wins.
3. Victim AS continues to announce the `/22` legitimately, but **half the Internet prefers the attacker's `/24`** due to longest-match.
4. Attacker's AS now becomes the de-facto ingress for traffic destined to the victim.
5. Attacker can *intercept* (Man-in-the-Middle), *drop* (Blackhole), or *redirect* (to phishing infrastructure) the captured traffic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Case Study (KTU Favourite):** In **February 2008**, Pakistan Telecom (AS17557), in response to a government order to block YouTube, broadcast `208.65.153.0/24` to its upstream provider PCCW. The announcement propagated globally, hijacking **~67% of YouTube's traffic for ~2 hours**, taking down the world's largest video platform.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below consolidates every formula you may need to write on an answer script for routing attacks in IDPS. Memorize the **column headers** and the **boundary values** — these are the most frequently asked.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Boundary / Default |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RIP Metric (Hop Count)** | $M_{RIP} = \min(H_C)$ | $H_C$ = hop count (integer) | $M_{RIP} \leq 15$ (16 = infinity) |
| 2 | **OSPF Cost** | $C_{OSPF} = \dfrac{10^8}{BW_{bps}}$ | $BW$ = link bandwidth in bps | $C_{OSPF} \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, ref BW = **100 Mbps** |
| 3 | **Bellman-Ford Update** | $D_x(y) = \min_v \left\{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \right\}$ | $D_x(y)$ = distance from x to y | Converges in $O(VE)$ time |
| 4 | **BGP Best Path — Longest Match** | $\text{Prefix}_{\text{selected}} = \max\left(\text{Length}_{CIDR}\right)$ | Length in bits | `/32` always wins over `/0` |
| 5 | **BGP AS_PATH Length** | $L_{AS\_PATH} = \text{count}(ASNs)$ | Lower is preferred | Tie-breaker: Origin code |
| 6 | **LSA MaxAge** | $T_{MaxAge} = 3600 \text{ s}$ | LSA refresh timer | 30-min retransmit |
| 7 | **BGP Hold Timer** | $T_{Hold} \leq 3 \cdot T_{KeepAlive}$ | Default KeepAlive = **60 s** | Default Hold = **180 s** |
| 8 | **Dampening Penalty Decay** | $P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{half}}$ | Half-life = **15 min** |
| 9 | **Sybil Identity Threshold** | $f_{honest} \geq \dfrac{n}{3}$ | $n$ = total identities | Byzantine fault limit |
| 10 | **Route Flap Penalty** | $P = 1000$ per flap | Cumulative | Suppress at $P \geq 2000$ |
| 11 | **RPKI Validity** | $V \in \{\text{Valid, Invalid, NotFound}\}$ | Origin authorization | Invalid = reject |
| 12 | **Wormhole Distance Error** | $\Delta d = d_{wormhole} - d_{normal}$ | Path length inflation | Detected when $\Delta d \gg 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In the table above, the only "constants" with asterisk-like importance are **RIP max hop = 15**, **OSPF reference BW = $10^8$** (= 100 Mbps), and **LSA MaxAge = 3600 s**. They appear in almost every KTU routing question since 2019.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Routing-attack detection underpins the design of every modern **BGP Route Analyzer** (e.g., BGPmon, RIPE RIS, RouteViews), every **NMS** (Cisco DNA Center, SolarWinds NPM), and is the foundational use-case for **BGPsec / RPKI** deployment mandated by large CDN providers (Cloudflare, Google, Akamai). For an IDPS engineer, fluency in routing attacks directly informs the tuning of **SNMP traps**, **NetFlow anomaly thresholds**, and **BGP-LS** telemetry consumers.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Attack × Detection Vector × Mitigation

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is **high-yield** for the 14-mark KTU question *"Discuss different types of routing attacks and their countermeasures"* (CO1, L4). Use this exact structure in your answer script for full marks.

| Attack Type | Protocol | Attacker Goal | Signature / Anomaly | IDPS Detection Vector | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Prefix Hijack** | BGP | Steal/redirect traffic | New / more-specific prefix from unallocated AS | RPKI ROA mismatch; BGP-MON; Prefix-list delta | RPKI + ROA + BGPsec + max-prefix ACL |
| **Route Leaking** | BGP | Bypass policy | Routes propagated to non-customer peer | AS-path regex; peer-policy audit | Strict AS-path filters; IRRdb validation |
| **Blackhole** | IGP/BGP | DoS target | Sudden route to `/32` null0 or new drop-peer | NetFlow null-route spike; SPAN | RHI checks; remote-triggered blackhole (RTBH) |
| **Grayhole (Selective)** | MANET/IGP | Stealthy DoS | Asymmetric packet loss on specific flow | Flow-statistics variance; t-test on loss | Trust-aware routing (TARF, CONFIDANT) |
| **Wormhole** | DSR/AODV | Fabricate short path | Disproportionate low hop-count in MANET | Packet-leash (geographic + temporal) | SECTOR / Leash protocol |
| **Sinkhole** | RPL/IoT | Attract all IoT traffic | One node becomes parent of > 80% nodes | Rank-anomaly in DODAG; parent entropy | VERIFY (TESLA), rank authentication |
| **Sybil** | Distributed | Subvert voting | High ratio of new identities per ASN | Identity-graph clustering; k-core | Random-key predistribution |
| **OSPF LSA Injection** | OSPF | Cause SPF recomputation storm | LSA sequence jump; LSA age = 3600 | OSPF MD5 auth; sequence-monitoring | Area authentication + SPF throttling |
| **BGP RST Injection** | TCP-179 | Session reset | High TCP RST on BGP port | TTL-check; TCP-AO; GTSM | BGP TTL Security (GTSM = 255) |
| **RIP Count-to-Infinity** | RIP | Slow convergence | Hop-count oscillation | Hold-down timer; split horizon | Poison-reverse; max-hop 15 |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation — BGP Prefix Hijack Propagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation is the **expected answer format** for any 7-mark sub-question on BGP route hijacking. Replicate this 6-step logic verbatim on your exam script.

**Scenario:** A victim AS `AS64500` legitimately owns `198.51.100.0/22`. A malicious AS `AS64999` injects a more-specific `198.51.100.0/24` and announces it to its upstream `AS64998`.

**Step 1 — Victim AS announces legitimately**

$$
\text{AS64500} \xrightarrow{\text{BGP UPDATE}} \text{AS64501} : \;\; 198.51.100.0/22,\; AS\_PATH = [64500]
$$

**Step 2 — Attacker AS announces more-specific**

$$
\text{AS64999} \xrightarrow{\text{BGP UPDATE}} \text{AS64998} : \;\; 198.51.100.0/24,\; AS\_PATH = [64999]
$$

**Step 3 — Upstream propagates both routes**

$$
\text{AS64998} \rightarrow \text{Transit} : \left\{
\begin{array}{l}
198.51.100.0/22 \text{ via } 64500 \\
198.51.100.0/24 \text{ via } 64999
\end{array}
\right.
$$

**Step 4 — Global BGP routers apply longest-match rule**

A router in `AS65010` receives a packet for `198.51.100.42`. It has two entries:
- `198.51.100.0/22` → length **22**
- `198.51.100.0/24` → length **24**

Since $24 > 22$, the router selects:

$$
\text{Next-Hop} = \text{AS64999} \quad (\text{attacker})
$$

**Step 5 — Calculation of hijacked traffic fraction**

Let the original `/22` contain $N$ addressable hosts ($N = 2^{32-22} = 1024$). The attacker's `/24` covers:

$$
N_{hijack} = 2^{32-24} = 256 \text{ hosts}
$$

The fraction of AS64500's space now routed to the attacker is:

$$
F_{hijack} = \dfrac{N_{hijack}}{N} = \dfrac{2^{32-24}}{2^{32-22}} = 2^{22-24} = 2^{-2} = 0.25
$$

$$
\therefore F_{hijack} = 25\%
$$

**Step 6 — Attacker impact realization**

A **25% slice** of the victim's entire /22 routed prefix now transits the attacker's AS. This yields *passive eavesdropping, active MITM (via re-origination), or total DoS* depending on the attacker's policy. Detection latency typically spans **2 minutes to 2 hours** depending on RPKI deployment and BGP-MON alert thresholds.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Pattern:** KTU evaluators explicitly look for the words **"longest-match"**, **"more-specific prefix"**, and the numerical value **"25%"** in such answers. Omit any of these and lose 1–2 marks.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — OSPF LSA Storming DoS

**Step 1 — Attacker floods LSAs** with maximum sequence number $0x7FFFFFFF$.

**Step 2 — Honest router receives LSA.** Checks:
- $\text{Seq}_{new} > \text{Seq}_{old}$: initially TRUE → installs LSA.
- LSA age reset to 0; re-flooded.
- After $0x7FFFFFFF$, the next increment would **wrap** to `0x80000000`, which is **illegal** (LS type 1 bit 31 = 1 = "do not age").

**Step 3 — Result:** LSA is permanently *stuck* in the LSDB. Every **30 minutes**, the originating router's LSA refresh fails to update it; the LSA is considered MaxAge after 3600 s and purged, but during the interval **every router recomputes Dijkstra's SPF** continuously.

**Step 4 — Dijkstra computational cost per SPF run:**

$$
T_{SPF} = O(E + V \log V)
$$

For a mid-size OSPF domain with $V = 500$ routers and $E = 1500$ links:

$$
T_{SPF} \approx 1500 + 500 \cdot \log_2 500 = 1500 + 500 \cdot 8.97 \approx 5985 \text{ operations}
$$

**Step 5 — With storming frequency** $f = 1$ SPF per second, total CPU load = **5985 ops/s**, which on legacy routers saturates the control plane and induces **forwarding-plane jitter / packet loss**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Mitigation: OSPF authentication (MD5/SHA) + LSA throttling (default 5s start, 2000s hold).}}
$$

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Sybil Attack on Distributed Routing

**Step 1 — Identity Pool.** Attacker generates $k$ pseudonymous identities from a single physical node.

**Step 2 — Routing Infiltration.** Each Sybil identity is offered as a "next-hop" by honest nodes, polluting the topology graph.

**Step 3 — Critical Threshold (Byzantine Bound):**

$$
f_{Sybil} \geq \dfrac{n_{total}}{3}
$$

If attacker controls $1/3$ of routing identities, distributed consensus fails. For a 100-node ad-hoc network:

$$
f_{Sybil\_min} = \lceil 100 / 3 \rceil = 34 \text{ identities required}
$$

**Step 4 — Detection via random key predistribution:**

$$
P_{Sybil\_miss} = (1 - p_k)^{k_{pool}}
$$

Where $p_k$ = key collision probability from a pool of $k_{pool}$ keys. With $p_k = 0.1$ and $k_{pool} = 50$:

$$
P_{Sybil\_miss} = (1 - 0.1)^{50} = 0.9^{50} \approx 0.00515
$$

$$
\therefore P_{Sybil\_detected} \approx 1 - 0.005 = 99.5\%
$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — BGP Prefix Hijack Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Honest Origin AS64500] -->|BGP UPDATE /22 legitimate| B[Upstream AS64501]
    C[Attacker AS64999] -->|BGP UPDATE /24 forged| D[Upstream AS64998]
    B --> E[Global Internet BGP Table]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Router in AS65010 receives destination 198.51.100.42}
    F -->|Longest match wins /24| G[Attacker AS64999 Blackhole or MITM]
    F -->|Fall back to /22 only if no /24| A
    G --> H[Victim traffic redirected or dropped]
    E --> I[BGP Route Collector BGPmon RIPE RIS]
    I --> J[IDPS Correlation Engine]
    J -->|RPKI ROA mismatch alert| K[Operator Notification and Route Withdrawal]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — OSPF LSA Storming (Control-Plane DoS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compromised Internal Router] -->|Inject LSA with Seq 0x7FFFFFFF| B[OSPF Area Backbone]
    B --> C[All Routers in Area]
    C --> D{LSA Sequence Check}
    D -->|Seq is higher| E[Install in LSDB]
    E --> F[Trigger Dijkstra SPF Recompute]
    F --> G[CPU Saturation on Control Plane]
    G --> H[Forwarding Plane Packet Loss and Jitter]
    C --> I[LSA Throttle 5 second start, 2000 second hold]
    I --> J[IDPS Throttling Block]
    A -->|Without MD5 auth| K[Attack Succeeds]
    A -->|With MD5 or SHA auth| L[Attack Rejected at LSA Header]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Sinkhole / Wormhole / Sybil in MANET

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MANET[Mobile Adhoc Network]
        N1[Node A Legitimate]
        N2[Node B Legitimate]
        N3[Node C Legitimate]
        WH[Wormhole Tunnel Out of Band]
        SK[Sinkhole Node M]
        SYB1[Sybil Identity S1]
        SYB2[Sybil Identity S2]
    end
    N1 -->|Route to C via M| SK
    N2 -->|Route to C via S1| SYB1
    N3 -->|Direct trust S2| SYB2
    SK --> WH
    WH -->|Tunnel via fast link| N3
    SK -->|Drop or harvest traffic| X[Data Exfiltration]
    SYB1 -->|Pollute routing tables| Y[Consensus Subverted]
    SYB2 --> Y
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IDPS Detection of Routing Anomalies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer[Telemetry Input Layer]
        I1[BGP UPDATE Stream TCP 179]
        I2[OSPF LSA Stream OSPF Multicast]
        I3[RIP Updates UDP 520]
        I4[NetFlow v9 or IPFIX Records]
        I5[SNMP Traps from Routers]
    end
    InputLayer --> J[Log Aggregator and Normalizer]
    J --> P[Preprocessor and Parser]
    P --> D1[Signature Engine Snort or Suricata Ruleset]
    P --> D2[Anomaly Engine Baseline Profile]
    P --> D3[RPKI Validator ROA Cache]
    P --> D4[BGPsec Validator Cryptographic]
    D1 --> C[Correlation and Scoring Engine]
    D2 --> C
    D3 --> C
    D4 --> C
    C -->|Score > Threshold| AL[Alert and Ticket]
    C -->|Auto-mitigation| AM[RTBH or Route Withdrawal or Interface Shutdown]
    C -->|Feedback loop| D2
    AL --> SO[Security Operations Center SOC Dashboard]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Routing Attack. Distinguish between Blackhole, Grayhole, and Sinkhole attacks.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (Memorize This Structure):**

A **Routing Attack** is the deliberate corruption, suppression, or fabrication of routing-protocol control messages to subvert the path-selection process of IGP/EGP protocols.

| Type | Behaviour | Detection Difficulty |
|---|---|---|
| **Blackhole** | Drops **100%** of received traffic at the attacker node. | Easy — sharp traffic-null spike in NetFlow. |
| **Grayhole** | Drops **selectively** (e.g., only UDP, only certain IPs). | Hard — needs flow-statistical variance. |
| **Sinkhole** | Advertises *overly attractive* routes (low metric, high preference) to attract traffic, then analyzes or drops. | Moderate — route-flap / metric-anomaly detection. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *synonymize* Blackhole and Sinkhole. **They are different.** A Blackhole *silently consumes* any traffic that arrives (often by advertising `/32` to `null0`); a Sinkhole *actively lures* traffic via falsely attractive metrics. Writing "both drop traffic" loses 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. List any SIX routing attacks and state the protocol each one primarily targets.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (Bullet Format for 3 Marks):**

1. **Prefix Hijacking** → BGP
2. **AS_PATH manipulation** → BGP
3. **LSA injection / storming** → OSPF
4. **Count-to-Infinity exploitation** → RIP
5. **Wormhole** → AODV / DSR (MANET)
6. **Sinkhole** → RPL (IoT) / OLSR (MANET)
7. **Sybil** → Distributed routing (any)
8. **BGP session reset (TCP RST injection)** → BGP/TCP-179
9. **EIGRP successor poisoning** → EIGRP
10. **Routing-table overflow (max-prefix DoS)** → BGP / OSPF

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Naming a *generic* attack (e.g., "Denial of Service") without the *specific protocol* loses 1 mark per pair. Always couple attack ↔ protocol.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You answer *one* full question (i.e., both sub-parts). Below are **two independent choices**.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** — *With neat diagrams, explain the BGP Prefix Hijacking attack. Discuss the Pakistan Telecom – YouTube incident of 2008 as a case study.*

#### **Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
BGP Prefix Hijacking is the unauthorized announcement of an IP prefix (or a more-specific sub-prefix) by an Autonomous System that does not own or is not authorized to advertise that prefix.

**2. Operational Mechanism (3 marks):**

*Step 1:* Legitimate AS `A` owns `P/x` and announces it.
*Step 2:* Attacker AS `B` announces `P/y` where $y > x$ (more-specific).
*Step 3:* Routers receiving both routes apply **longest-prefix-match**:

$$
\text{Selected} = \arg\max_y \left( \text{Length}_y \right)
$$

*Step 4:* Attacker's `/24` wins over victim's `/22` — traffic redirected to attacker AS.

**3. Pakistan Telecom – YouTube Case (2 marks):**

* **Date:** 24 February 2008
* **Actor:** Pakistan Telecom (AS17557), acting on Pakistan's PTA order to block YouTube domestically.
* **Method:** Announced `208.65.153.0/24` to upstream PCCW (AS3491) as a *more-specific* of YouTube's `208.65.152.0/22`.
* **Propagation:** The announcement leaked to the global BGP table via PCCW → TransTelecom → Tier-1 providers.
* **Impact:** Approximately **67% of global YouTube traffic** was rerouted to Pakistan Telecom for **~2 hours**. YouTube countered by announcing `208.65.153.128/25` and `208.65.153.0/25` from a different AS to "split" the prefix.
* **Lesson:** Demonstrates the *fragility of BGP's trust model* and motivates RPKI/ROA adoption.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Use the Mermaid from Section 4.1 simplified to A–B–C labels.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** — *Explain OSPF LSA injection attack. Derive the computational cost of SPF recomputation and discuss its mitigation.*

#### **Model Solution:**

**1. OSPF LSA Types Recap (1 mark):**
Type-1 = Router-LSA, Type-2 = Network-LSA, Type-3 = Summary-LSA, Type-5 = External-LSA.

**2. LSA Injection Steps (2 marks):**
- Attacker floods **Type-1 LSAs** with high sequence number.
- Sequence max = `0x7FFFFFFF` (bit 31 = 0 = "age normally"). Exceeding causes wrap.
- Honest routers accept and re-flood, triggering Dijkstra recomputation.

**3. SPF Cost Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
T_{SPF} = O(E + V \log V)
$$

For $V = 500$ routers, $E = 1500$ links:

$$
T_{SPF} = 1500 + 500 \cdot \log_2 500 = 1500 + 500 \cdot 8.97 = 5985 \text{ operations}
$$

If $f_{storm} = 1 \text{ SPF/s}$:

$$
T_{load} = 5985 \text{ ops/s} \Rightarrow \text{CPU saturation on legacy control planes}
$$

**4. Mitigation (2 marks):**
- **OSPF MD5/SHA-256 authentication** (RFC 5709).
- **LSA throttling** — start-interval = **5 s**, hold-interval = **2000 s**.
- **SPF scheduling** — `spf throttle` prevents cascading recomputations.
- **IDPS signature** — alert on `LS Seq >= 0x7FFFFFF0` or sudden LSA-churn > 50/min.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** — *Discuss Wormhole and Sinkhole attacks in MANET/IoT routing. How can an IDPS detect each?*

#### **Model Solution:**

**1. Wormhole Attack (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Two colluding nodes establish a *high-speed out-of-band tunnel* between themselves, advertising an artificially short path.
- **Effect:** Traffic is tunneled past intermediate routers, bypassing normal topology. Creates a *blackhole* or *eavesdropping* channel.
- **Detection:** Use **packet leashes** (geographic or temporal):
  - *Geographic leash:* each packet's max hop-distance is bounded; if a packet appears to travel further than possible → wormhole.
  - *Temporal leash:* packet's end-to-end travel time is checked against propagation model.
- **Defence:** SECTOR, Leash-Protocol, delay-per-hop monitoring.

**2. Sinkhole Attack (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A malicious node advertises *zero-cost routes* to itself, attracting all traffic from neighbouring nodes.
- **Effect:** All data passes through the attacker → single point of eavesdrop/drop.
- **Detection:**
  - *Rank anomaly* in RPL DODAG (RFC 6550) — sudden parent-switch rate spike.
  - *Topology entropy drop* — one node becomes parent of > 80% of nodes.
  - *Cross-layer correlation* — packet delivery ratio per node.
- **Defence:** VERIFY (TESLA-based), RPL rank authentication, parent-selection based on multi-metric (ETX + energy + trust).

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** — *Explain Sybil attack in distributed routing. Derive the minimum number of Sybil identities required to subvert consensus in a 100-node network, and the detection probability via random key predistribution.*

#### **Model Solution:**

**1. Sybil Attack Definition (1 mark):**
A single malicious node fabricates multiple pseudonymous identities to gain disproportionate influence in a distributed routing protocol.

**2. Byzantine Threshold Derivation (3 marks):**
For consensus to fail:

$$
f_{Sybil} \geq \dfrac{n_{total}}{3}
$$

For $n = 100$:

$$
f_{Sybil}^{min} = \lceil 100 / 3 \rceil = 34 \text{ Sybil identities}
$$

**3. Random Key Predistribution Detection (3 marks):**
Each legitimate node draws $k$ keys from a pool of size $P$. The probability of two honest nodes sharing a key is:

$$
P_{share} = 1 - \dfrac{(P-k)!}{P! \cdot (P-2k)!}
$$

If a "node" presents identities with **zero shared keys** with the rest of the network, it is flagged Sybil.

Detection probability per identity:

$$
P_{detect} = 1 - (1 - P_{share})^{n_{honest}}
$$

For $P_{share} = 0.1$, $n_{honest} = 99$:

$$
P_{detect} = 1 - 0.9^{99} \approx 1 - 9.96 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.9999
$$

$$
\therefore P_{detect} \approx 99.99\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Patterns:**
> 1. **Missing the "longest-match" explanation** in any BGP hijack answer → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Blackhole vs Sinkhole** → **−1 mark** per confusion instance.
> 3. **Omitting numerical boundary values** (e.g., RIP max hop = 15, MaxAge = 3600 s) when citing them → **−0.5 to −1 mark** (partial credit lost on precision).
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** that explicitly ask for one → **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Failing to mention RPKI / ROA / BGPsec** in any BGP attack answer → **−1 mark** (current-affairs currency test).
> 6. **Generic "use firewall"** as a countermeasure → **0 marks** (not a routing-attack-specific defence).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Final-minute revision — read this list out loud twice before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ Routing attacks target the **control plane** (path decisions), not the data plane.
- ✅ **BGP** = path-vector, **OSPF** = link-state, **RIP** = distance-vector — each has distinct attack surface.
- ✅ **Prefix hijack** exploits **longest-prefix-match**; more-specific always wins.
- ✅ **Pakistan-YouTube 2008** is the canonical KTU case study — memorize date, AS, prefix, impact (~67%, 2 hrs).
- ✅ **RIP max hop = 15**, **OSPF ref BW = $10^8$**, **LSA MaxAge = 3600 s**, **OSPF MD5 auth** blocks LSA injection.
- ✅ **Blackhole** = drop 100%; **Grayhole** = selective drop; **Sinkhole** = lure + drop; **Wormhole** = out-of-band tunnel.
- ✅ **Sybil** subverts consensus at $f \geq n/3$ (Byzantine bound).
- ✅ **BGP hijack mitigation** = RPKI + ROA + BGPsec + max-prefix ACL + IRRdb filters.
- ✅ **Wormhole mitigation** = packet leashes (geographic / temporal) + SECTOR.
- ✅ **Sinkhole mitigation** in RPL = rank authentication + multi-metric parent selection.
- ✅ **IDPS detection vectors:** NetFlow, BGP-MON, RPKI validator, LSA sequence monitor, SPF throttling, packet leashes.
- ✅ Always pair **attack ↔ protocol** in 3-mark short answers; always give **numerical proof** in 7-mark derivations.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Why Use an IDPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Why Use an IDPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is a security mechanism that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or unauthorized access, and has the capability to either alert administrators (Detection) or actively block/stop the threat in real time (Prevention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECCT742**, an IDPS is defined as a *security control technology* that complements preventive measures (firewalls, antivirus) by providing **post-compromise visibility** and **active response** to threats that have bypassed initial perimeter defenses.

The core purpose of an IDPS can be summarized through three engineering objectives:

1. **Confidentiality Assurance:** Detect unauthorized data exfiltration attempts.
2. **Integrity Monitoring:** Identify unauthorized modifications to files, databases, or system configurations.
3. **Availability Protection:** Prevent Denial-of-Service (DoS) and ransomware-related service disruptions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault with Smart Guards"

Imagine a **bank** protected by multiple layers:

| Layer | Real-World Counterpart | What It Does |
|---|---|---|
| Outer Wall + Fence | **Firewall** | Stops obvious intruders at the boundary |
| CCTV Cameras | **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** | Records everything, alerts security after suspicious activity |
| Armed Guards with Tasers | **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** | Not only detects but **actively neutralizes** the threat in real time |
| Vault Door | **Encryption & Authentication** | Protects the core assets even if walls are breached |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Insight:** A firewall is like a wall — it can be bypassed (tunneled, misconfigured, or circumvented by insiders). An IDPS is like a **security team with night-vision goggles, motion sensors, and automatic weapons** that operates *inside* the perimeter, catching threats the wall missed.

## 1.3 Why an IDPS is Necessary — The Foundational Problem

The fundamental reason for using an IDPS stems from three realities of modern cybersecurity:

- **Reality 1 — The Perimeter is Porous:** Firewalls, anti-virus, and access controls cannot stop every threat. **Zero-day exploits, insider threats, and advanced persistent threats (APTs)** routinely bypass perimeter defenses.
- **Reality 2 — Threats are Asymmetric and Evolving:** Attackers only need to find **one** vulnerability; defenders must secure **all** of them. The threat landscape evolves faster than manual human response can handle.
- **Reality 3 — Detection Without Prevention is Incomplete:** Traditional IDS only *alerts*; by the time a human analyst responds (often hours later), the attacker has already exfiltrated data. Modern systems need **automated, real-time response** — this is the **Prevention** half of IDPS.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Aligned Glossary (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **True Positive (TP):** Legitimate attack correctly identified as an attack.
> - **True Negative (TN):** Legitimate traffic correctly identified as normal.
> - **False Positive (FP):** Legitimate traffic incorrectly flagged as malicious *(annoyance, alert fatigue)*.
> - **False Negative (FN):** Actual attack missed by the system *(most dangerous error)*.
> - **Base Rate Fallacy:** In high-traffic networks, even a 1% FP rate on 10 million events/day = **100,000 false alerts** per day.
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time between compromise and detection. Industry benchmark: **>200 days** without IDPS; **<1 day** with mature IDPS.
> - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time between detection and containment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars Justifying IDPS Deployment

### Pillar 1: Defense in Depth (Layered Security)

The **Defense in Depth** strategy recognizes that **no single security control is sufficient**. IDPS forms the critical *second and third line of defense*:

```
Layer 1: Perimeter (Firewall, DMZ, Border Router)
        ↓ (some traffic passes through)
Layer 2: IDPS (Network/Host Monitoring + Active Blocking)
        ↓ (advanced threats contained)
Layer 3: Endpoint Protection (EDR, Anti-Malware)
        ↓
Layer 4: Data Protection (Encryption, DLP, Backups)
```

### Pillar 2: Compliance & Regulatory Mandates

Multiple regulatory frameworks **require** continuous monitoring and intrusion detection:

| Regulation / Standard | IDPS Requirement |
|---|---|
| **PCI-DSS 4.0** (Payment Card Industry) | Req. 11.5 — Detect and respond to network intrusions |
| **HIPAA** (Healthcare) | §164.312(b) — Audit controls and intrusion detection |
| **ISO 27001:2022** | Annex A.8.16 — Monitoring activities |
| **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** | SI-4 (System Monitoring), SI-3 (Malicious Code Protection) |
| **RBI Cyber Security Framework** (India) | Continuous monitoring of payment systems |
| **IT Act 2000 / DPDP Act 2023** (India) | Reasonable security practices |

### Pillar 3: Operational & Business Continuity

The cost of a data breach makes IDPS deployment economically justified. IBM's *Cost of a Data Breach Report 2023* showed an **average breach cost of $4.45 million** globally, with organizations using IDPS + AI automation saving an average of **$1.76 million per breach**.

## 2.2 What IDPS Specifically Protects Against

> [!NOTE]
> **Threat Categories Addressed by IDPS (KTU Module 1 Mapping):**
>
> 1. **External Threat Actors** — Hackers, hacktivists, nation-state APT groups
> 2. **Internal Threat Actors** — Malicious insiders, negligent employees
> 3. **Malware Families** — Viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, rootkits, botnets
> 4. **Network-Based Attacks** — DoS/DDoS, port scans, man-in-the-middle, ARP spoofing
> 5. **Application-Layer Attacks** — SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflows, zero-days
> 6. **Reconnaissance Activities** — Port scanning, fingerprinting, vulnerability probing
> 7. **Policy Violations** — Unauthorized P2P use, torrenting, unapproved cloud services

## 2.3 IDPS vs. Other Security Controls — Comparative Analysis

| Security Control | Primary Function | Limitation That IDPS Solves |
|---|---|---|
| **Firewall** | Filters traffic based on rules/ports | Cannot detect application-layer attacks or insider threats |
| **Anti-Virus** | Signature-based malware detection | Cannot stop zero-day exploits or network-based attacks |
| **VPN** | Encrypts remote traffic | Cannot detect malicious *content* within encrypted tunnel |
| **Access Control (IAM)** | Authenticates users | Cannot detect compromised credentials being misused |
| **SIEM** | Logs and correlates events | Primarily reactive; may not have real-time blocking |
| **IDPS** | **Real-time monitoring + automated response** | **Addresses the detection-to-response gap** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Numerical/Analytical Questions:**

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation | Ideal Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Rate (DR)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} \times 100\%$ | % of actual attacks detected | $\geq 99\%$ |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} \times 100\%$ | % of benign traffic falsely flagged | $\leq 1\%$ |
| **Accuracy (ACC)** | $\text{ACC} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | $\geq 99.5\%$ |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Reliability of an alert | $\geq 95\%$ |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Same as Detection Rate | $\geq 99\%$ |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall | $\geq 0.97$ |
| **Base Rate Multiplier (BRM)** | $\text{BRM} = \dfrac{FPR}{P(\text{Attack})}$ | Inflates false alerts in low-attack environments | Minimize |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Critical Infrastructure (SCADA/ICS):** IDPS protects power grids, water treatment plants, and manufacturing systems from nation-state attacks (e.g., *Stuxnet*, *BlackEnergy*).
- **Financial Sector:** Detects fraudulent transactions, ATM network breaches, and SWIFT manipulation attempts.
- **Healthcare:** Prevents ransomware attacks on hospitals (e.g., *WannaCry* on NHS UK, 2017).
- **E-Commerce & Cloud:** Detects credential stuffing, card-skimming (Magecart attacks), and AWS/Azure misconfigurations.
- **Government & Defense:** Defends against APT groups like APT28, Lazarus Group, and Sidewinder.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Cost-Benefit Justification for IDPS

Let us formally derive the **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** for an IDPS deployment, a common KTU analytical question.

### Given Variables

- $C_{\text{breach}}$ = Expected cost of a single security breach (in ₹/$)
- $P_{\text{breach}}$ = Annual probability of a breach occurring (without IDPS)
- $C_{\text{IDPS}}$ = Annual cost of IDPS deployment (licensing + hardware + staff)
- $R_{\text{reduction}}$ = Fractional reduction in breach probability due to IDPS (e.g., $0.70$ for 70%)

### Step 1: Define Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) Without IDPS

The **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** is the expected monetary loss per year due to security incidents.

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = C_{\text{breach}} \times P_{\text{breach}}
$$

**Example computation:**
If $C_{\text{breach}} = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (₹2 Crore) and $P_{\text{breach}} = 0.30$ (30% annual chance):

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 2 \times 10^7 \times 0.30 = \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 2: Compute ALE After IDPS Deployment

The IDPS reduces breach probability by factor $R_{\text{reduction}}$, so the new probability becomes:

$$
P_{\text{after}} = P_{\text{breach}} \times (1 - R_{\text{reduction}})
$$

The new ALE becomes:

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = C_{\text{breach}} \times P_{\text{after}}
$$

**With $R_{\text{reduction}} = 0.70$:**

$$
P_{\text{after}} = 0.30 \times (1 - 0.70) = 0.30 \times 0.30 = 0.09
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 2 \times 10^7 \times 0.09 = \text{₹}18{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Annual Savings (Risk Reduction Benefit)

$$
\text{Savings} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 60{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}42{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{Savings} - C_{\text{IDPS}}}{C_{\text{IDPS}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Assuming $C_{\text{IDPS}} = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$ per year:**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{42{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{32{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 320\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A ROSI of **320%** means every ₹1 invested in the IDPS returns **₹3.20 in net risk reduction** annually. This is the standard quantitative justification for IDPS procurement in enterprise security budgeting.

### Step 3.5: KTU Analytical Problem — Detection Metrics Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-Style Numerical (2/3 marks):**
> An IDPS analyzed 10,000 network events in a day. It flagged 200 events as malicious. Of these, 180 were actual attacks (True Positives) and 20 were legitimate traffic (False Positives). The system missed 5 actual attacks (False Negatives). Compute: **(a)** Detection Rate, **(b)** False Positive Rate, **(c)** Precision, **(d)** F1-Score.

**Given Data:**

$$
TP = 180, \quad FP = 20, \quad FN = 5, \quad TN = 10000 - 200 - 5 = 9795
$$

**(a) Detection Rate (Recall):**

$$
\text{DR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{180}{180 + 5} = \dfrac{180}{185} \approx 0.9730 = 97.30\%
$$

**(b) False Positive Rate:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{20}{20 + 9795} = \dfrac{20}{9815} \approx 0.00204 = 0.204\%
$$

**(c) Precision:**

$$
\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{180}{180 + 20} = \dfrac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90.00\%
$$

**(d) F1-Score:**

$$
F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{0.90 \times 0.9730}{0.90 + 0.9730}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{0.8757}{1.8730} = 2 \times 0.4675 = 0.9350
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The IDPS achieves a **Detection Rate of 97.30%** (good) and **F1-Score of 0.935** (strong), but the **Precision is 90%** — meaning 1 in 10 alerts is a false alarm. This illustrates the **classic precision-recall tradeoff** in IDPS design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The "Why Use IDPS" Decision Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enterprise Network] --> B{Perimeter Defense}
    B -->|Firewall / ACL| C{Threat Successfully Blocked?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Traffic Logged & Allowed]
    C -->|No — Bypass Detected| E[IDPS Monitoring Engine]
    E --> F{Analysis Method}
    F -->|Signature-Based| G[Compare Against Known Attack DB]
    F -->|Anomaly-Based| H[Statistical Behavioral Baseline]
    F -->|Stateful Protocol| I[Protocol State Tracking]
    G --> J{Threat Confidence Score}
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -->|High Confidence| K[IPS Mode: Auto-Block & Drop]
    J -->|Medium Confidence| L[IDS Mode: Alert SOC Team]
    J -->|Low Confidence| M[Log for Forensic Review]
    K --> N[Updated Rule Set & Threat Intel]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The left side (A → B → C) represents the **first line of defense** (firewall).
> - The central block (E → F → J) represents the **IDPS decision core**.
> - The right side branches (K, L, M) show the **three response modalities** of an IDPS: active blocking, alerting, or logging.

## 4.2 Threat Landscape Evolution — Why IDPS is Now Critical

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[1990s: Worms and Viruses] --> P2[2000s: Botnets and Phishing]
    P2 --> P3[2010s: APTs and Ransomware]
    P3 --> P4[2020s: AI-Powered Attacks and Supply Chain]
    P4 --> P5[2024+: Zero-Trust, XDR, and Autonomous IDPS]
    P1 -.Traditional AV sufficient.-> X1[Single-Layer Defense]
    P2 -.AV + Firewall.-> X2[Two-Layer Defense]
    P3 -.AV + Firewall + Basic IDPS.-> X3[Three-Layer Defense]
    P4 -.IDPS + EDR + SIEM + Zero-Trust.-> X4[Multi-Layer Defense with IDPS at Core]
    P5 -.Self-Learning IDPS.-> X5[Adaptive IDPS with ML]
```

## 4.3 IDPS Placement Architecture in a Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INTERNET[Internet] --> FW1[Edge Firewall]
    FW1 --> DMZ[DMZ: Web Mail DNS]
    DMZ --> IDPS1[Inline Network IPS]
    IDPS1 --> LAN[Internal LAN]
    LAN --> IDPS2[Tap-Mode NIDS for Monitoring]
    LAN --> HOST1[Host-Based IDPS on Server]
    LAN --> HOST2[Host-Based IDPS on Workstation]
    IDPS1 --> SOC[Centralized SOC Dashboard]
    IDPS2 --> SOC
    HOST1 --> SOC
    HOST2 --> SOC
    SOC --> ANALYST[Security Analyst]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Insight (KTU Module 1):**
> - **Inline IPS** (IDPS1) is placed *in the data path* and can block traffic in real time — used for perimeter/internal segmentation.
> - **Tap-Mode NIDS** (IDPS2) receives a *mirror copy* of traffic — used for monitoring without risk of becoming a bottleneck.
> - **Host-Based IDPS (HIDPS)** operates at the OS level (system calls, log files, registry changes).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define IDPS and explain why it is necessary in modern networks. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is a security technology that monitors network and host activities for malicious behavior and either generates alerts (Detection) or actively blocks threats (Prevention).

**Why it is necessary (1.5 marks for justification):**
- **Limitations of firewalls:** Firewalls operate on static rules and cannot detect application-layer attacks, insider threats, or zero-day exploits.
- **Speed of modern attacks:** Automated attacks occur in seconds; human response takes hours. IDPS provides **real-time automated response**.
- **Defense in depth:** IDPS acts as the *second line of defense* after the firewall, catching what perimeter security missed.
- **Compliance:** Standards like PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 mandate continuous intrusion monitoring.
- **Visibility:** Provides deep packet inspection and behavioral analysis invisible to traditional controls.

### Question 2: Differentiate between IDS and IPS. Why is IPS preferred in high-security environments? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | IDS (Intrusion Detection System) | IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) |
|---|---|---|
| **Action** | Passive — alerts only | Active — alerts + blocks |
| **Placement** | Out-of-band (tap mode) | Inline (in data path) |
| **Latency Impact** | None | Minimal (microseconds) |
| **Failure Mode** | Fail-open (no impact) | Fail-closed (may block traffic) |
| **Use Case** | Monitoring, forensics | Real-time threat neutralization |

**Why IPS is preferred (1 mark):** In high-security environments, the cost of a *false negative* (missed attack) far outweighs the inconvenience of occasional false positives. IPS provides **automated, in-line prevention** without waiting for human intervention, reducing MTTR from hours to milliseconds.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **five primary reasons** for deploying an IDPS in an enterprise network, with at least one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An enterprise experiences an average breach cost of ₹5 Crore with a 40% annual probability of a successful cyberattack. The company plans to deploy an IDPS costing ₹15 Lakh annually, which is expected to reduce breach probability by 75%. Calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) before and after** IDPS deployment and compute the **ROSI**. Justify whether the deployment is financially viable. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Five Primary Reasons for IDPS Deployment (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**

**1. Defense in Depth (Layered Security):** No single security control is sufficient. IDPS adds a critical monitoring/prevention layer behind the firewall. *Example:* The 2017 *WannaCry* ransomware bypassed firewalls but was contained by IPS devices that detected SMB exploitation patterns.

**2. Real-Time Threat Response:** Modern attacks (ransomware, credential stuffing) execute in seconds. IDPS provides **sub-second automated response**, whereas manual SOC response takes hours. *Example:* Zero-day exploits in Microsoft Exchange (2021) were mitigated by IPS with virtual patching.

**3. Regulatory Compliance:** Standards like **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, and the DPDP Act 2023 (India)** mandate continuous intrusion monitoring. Non-compliance results in fines and loss of certification.

**4. Insider Threat Detection:** Firewalls cannot detect malicious activity from authenticated users. IDPS uses **behavioral baselining** to flag anomalous user behavior (e.g., a finance employee suddenly accessing HR databases at 3 AM).

**5. Forensic Visibility & Threat Intelligence:** IDPS logs provide a detailed audit trail for post-incident forensics, legal proceedings, and threat intelligence sharing with ISACs (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — full working shown):**

**Step 1: ALE Before IDPS**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = C_{\text{breach}} \times P_{\text{breach}} = 5 \times 10^7 \times 0.40 = \text{₹}2 \times 10^7 = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: New Probability After IDPS**

$$
P_{\text{after}} = 0.40 \times (1 - 0.75) = 0.40 \times 0.25 = 0.10
$$

**Step 3: ALE After IDPS**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 5 \times 10^7 \times 0.10 = \text{₹}5 \times 10^6 = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Annual Savings**

$$
\text{Savings} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5: ROSI Calculation**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1{,}35{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = 9.0 \times 100\% = 900\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (1 mark for verdict):** The deployment is **highly financially viable**. A ROSI of **900%** means every ₹1 invested returns ₹9 in net savings. The investment pays for itself in the first incident it prevents.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **firewalls, antivirus software, and IDPS** in terms of detection capability, response time, and coverage of modern threats. Use a comparative table and justify why IDPS is considered a *necessary complement* (not a replacement) to the other two. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IDPS processed 50,000 packets in a day. It raised 500 alerts. Of these, 450 were actual attacks (TP), 50 were false alarms (FP), and it missed 30 actual attacks (FN). Calculate: **(i)** Detection Rate, **(ii)** False Positive Rate, **(iii)** Precision, **(iv)** F1-Score. Comment on whether this IDPS is production-ready. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Feature | Firewall | Antivirus | IDPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Method** | Rule/port-based | Signature database | Signatures + Anomaly + Stateful |
| **Threat Coverage** | Network perimeter | Known malware only | Network + Host + Application |
| **Response Time** | Instant (drop packets) | Scan-based (minutes) | Real-time (milliseconds) |
| **Zero-Day Detection** | Poor | Poor | Good (anomaly-based) |
| **Insider Threat** | Cannot detect | Limited | Strong (behavioral baselining) |
| **Encrypted Traffic Analysis** | Limited | N/A | Yes (with SSL inspection) |
| **Active Blocking** | Yes | Quarantine only | Yes (inline IPS) |

**Justification (2 marks):** A firewall is the *front gate*, antivirus is the *doormat* — both are essential but neither sees what happens *inside* the house. The IDPS is the *security camera + armed guard system*. It is a **complement, not a replacement**, because: (1) firewalls cannot inspect encrypted payloads, (2) antivirus relies on known signatures, and (3) IDPS fills the detection-response gap between the two.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $TP = 450$, $FP = 50$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 50000 - 500 - 30 = 49470$

**(i) Detection Rate:**

$$
\text{DR} = \dfrac{450}{450 + 30} = \dfrac{450}{480} = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
$$

**(ii) False Positive Rate:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \dfrac{50}{50 + 49470} = \dfrac{50}{49520} \approx 0.00101 = 0.101\%
$$

**(iii) Precision:**

$$
\text{Precision} = \dfrac{450}{450 + 50} = \dfrac{450}{500} = 0.90 = 90.00\%
$$

**(iv) F1-Score:**

$$
F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{0.90 \times 0.9375}{0.90 + 0.9375} = 2 \times \dfrac{0.84375}{1.8375} \approx 0.9184
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy:** Precision = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$; Accuracy = $\frac{TP+TN}{\text{Total}}$. These are NOT the same.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute TN:** In most KTU problems, TN is not given directly. You must compute it as: $\text{TN} = \text{Total Events} - \text{Total Alerts} - \text{FN}$.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Some students express F1-Score as a percentage (e.g., 91.84%). It is a unitless ratio between 0 and 1.
> 4. **Missing the comment:** KTU awards 1 mark for the *interpretive conclusion* — students often skip this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Production-Readiness Verdict (1 mark for interpretation):**
> The IDPS has **DR = 93.75%** (acceptable but not excellent) and **FPR = 0.10%** (excellent — low alert fatigue). However, **Precision = 90%** means 1 in 10 alerts is a false alarm, and **5 missed attacks (FN = 30)** is concerning for a high-security environment. **Verdict:** The system is **marginally production-ready for a small/medium enterprise** but would require tuning (threshold adjustment, signature updates) before deployment in critical infrastructure.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** IDPS = Detection (alert) + Prevention (auto-block). The "P" in IDPS is what differentiates it from a pure IDS.
>
> ✅ **Three Pillars of Justification:** (1) Defense in Depth, (2) Regulatory Compliance, (3) Cost-Benefit (ROSI).
>
> ✅ **Firewalls vs. IDPS:** Firewalls are *preventive* (stop known bad traffic at the gate); IDPS is *detective + responsive* (catches what gets through or originates inside).
>
> ✅ **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
> - Detection Rate = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$
> - False Positive Rate = $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$
> - Precision = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$
> - F1-Score = $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$
>
> ✅ **ROSI Formula:** $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - C_{\text{IDPS}}}{C_{\text{IDPS}}} \times 100\%$
>
> ✅ **Compliance Frameworks:** PCI-DSS 11.5, HIPAA §164.312(b), ISO 27001 A.8.16, NIST 800-53 SI-4.
>
> ✅ **Critical Distinctions for Exam:**
> - IDS = passive, tap-mode, fail-open
> - IPS = active, inline, fail-closed
> - NIDPS = network-level (broader view)
