> - HIDPS = host-level (deeper context)
>
> ✅ **Real-World Anchors:** WannaCry (2017), NotPetya (2017), SolarWinds (2020), Microsoft Exchange Zero-Day (2021), MOVEit Breach (2023) — use these as examples in 14-mark answers for higher marks.
>
> ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never say "IDPS replaces firewalls." Always say "IDPS *complements* firewalls as part of defense in depth."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of IDPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of IDPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is a security mechanism designed to monitor network traffic, system activities, and/or application behavior for malicious activities, policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts, and to take automated preventive action upon detection. The classification of IDPS by **type** is based on the **source of the data** it monitors and the **scope** of the environment it protects.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, IDPS are broadly classified into the following primary types:

1. **Network-Based IDPS (NIDPS)** – Monitors traffic traversing a network segment.
2. **Host-Based IDPS (HIDPS)** – Monitors activity on a single host/endpoint.
3. **Wireless IDPS (WIDPS)** – Monitors wireless network traffic and protocol activity.
4. **Network Behavior Analysis (NBA) IDPS** – Detects anomalies in network traffic flows.
5. **Hybrid/Integrated IDPS** – Combines two or more IDPS technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly differentiate between **IDS (Detection only)** and **IPS (Prevention + Detection)**. A system that *only* logs/alerts is an IDS, while a system that can *actively block* malicious traffic in real time is an IPS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **gated residential community** with multiple layers of security:

- **NIDPS** is like a **CCTV camera at the main gate** — it watches everyone entering and leaving the colony but cannot stop a determined intruder alone.
- **HIDPS** is like a **security guard inside each house** — if someone breaks into a specific house, the local guard raises an alarm.
- **WIDPS** is like a **radio-frequency scanner** that detects unauthorized walkie-talkies trying to listen in on the community's private channels.
- **NBA IDPS** is like a **suspicious-behavior analyst** — it doesn't know exactly what crime is being committed, but it sees unusual patterns (e.g., someone running through the colony at 3 AM) and flags it.
- **Hybrid IDPS** is the **central command center** that integrates cameras, house guards, RF scanners, and behavioral analysts into one unified security dashboard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Each IDPS type has a *specific monitoring domain*. The choice of type depends on what the organization wants to protect — perimeter, endpoints, wireless airspace, or traffic behavior.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in IDPS (Terminology to Remember)

- **True Positive (TP):** Legitimate attack correctly detected.
- **False Positive (FP):** Legitimate traffic wrongly flagged as attack.
- **True Negative (TN):** Legitimate traffic correctly allowed.
- **False Negative (FN):** Attack traffic wrongly allowed.
- **Detection Rate (DR):** $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$

These metrics govern the **effectiveness of any IDPS type**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each IDPS Type

### 2.1.1 Network-Based IDPS (NIDPS)

- **Monitoring Domain:** Entire network segments (e.g., LAN backbone, DMZ).
- **Placement:** Promiscuous mode on a switch port (via SPAN/mirror port) or inline as a sensor.
- **Data Source:** Network packets captured and analyzed in real time.
- **Detection Methods:**
  - Signature-based (misuse detection)
  - Anomaly-based
  - Stateful protocol analysis
- **Strengths:** Broad coverage, single point monitors many hosts, can detect scans, worms, DoS.
- **Limitations:** Cannot analyze encrypted traffic payloads, limited visibility inside hosts, high-speed networks may saturate sensor.
- **Examples:** Snort (inline mode), Suricata, Cisco NGIPS.

### 2.1.2 Host-Based IDPS (HIDPS)

- **Monitoring Domain:** A single host (server, workstation, endpoint).
- **Data Source:** Operating system logs, system calls, file integrity checkers, application logs, registry edits.
- **Detection Methods:**
  - Log analysis
  - File integrity monitoring
  - System call sequence analysis
- **Strengths:** Detects insider attacks, host-specific malware, encrypted traffic (post-decryption on host), policy violations.
- **Limitations:** Resource-intensive on host, requires OS-specific agents, not scalable easily.
- **Examples:** OSSEC, Tripwire, Windows Defender ATP.

### 2.1.3 Wireless IDPS (WIDPS)

- **Monitoring Domain:** IEEE 802.11 wireless networks.
- **Data Source:** Radio frequency (RF) spectrum, wireless frames (management, control, data).
- **Detection Methods:**
  - Signature-based detection of known attack frames (e.g., deauthentication, evil twin).
  - Anomaly detection of unusual RF patterns.
- **Strengths:** Detects rogue APs, MAC spoofing, wireless DoS, man-in-the-middle over Wi-Fi.
- **Limitations:** RF range limited, interference-prone, encrypted 802.11 payload not visible.
- **Examples:** Kismet, AirMagnet Enterprise, Aruba RFProtect.

### 2.1.4 Network Behavior Analysis (NBA) IDPS

- **Monitoring Domain:** Traffic flows and statistical patterns.
- **Data Source:** NetFlow, sFlow, IPFIX, SNMP.
- **Detection Methods:**
  - Statistical anomaly detection
  - Baseline deviation
  - Heuristic profiling
- **Strengths:** Excellent for detecting DDoS, worms, zero-days (behavioral deviations).
- **Limitations:** High false positive rate, cannot pinpoint exact malicious packet, no payload inspection.
- **Examples:** Arbor Networks (Netscout), Lancope (Cisco Stealthwatch).

### 2.1.5 Hybrid / Integrated IDPS

- **Monitoring Domain:** Combines network, host, and/or wireless sensors.
- **Data Source:** Aggregated logs and events from multiple sensors.
- **Detection Methods:** Correlation engines combine alerts from heterogeneous sources.
- **Strengths:** Reduces false positives via cross-correlation, provides holistic visibility.
- **Limitations:** Complex to deploy, high cost, requires centralized SIEM/SOAR integration.
- **Examples:** Splunk Enterprise Security, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| **Parameter** | **NIDPS** | **HIDPS** | **WIDPS** | **NBA IDPS** | **Hybrid IDPS** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Network segment | Single host | Wireless airspace | Traffic flows | Multi-domain |
| **Data Source** | Packets | OS/Application logs | RF frames | NetFlow/sFlow | Aggregated |
| **Encrypted Visibility** | No (payload) | Yes (post-decrypt) | No (payload) | Limited | Varies |
| **Insider Threat Detection** | Weak | Strong | Weak | Moderate | Strong |
| **Scalability** | High | Low | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low–Moderate | Low | Moderate | High | Low (after correlation) |
| **Placement** | Inline/SPAN | On host | RF sensor | Passive tap | Centralized manager |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | Very High | Moderate | Moderate | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** NIDPS and NBA IDPS are *often confused*. The key difference is that **NIDPS inspects packet payloads**, while **NBA IDPS only analyzes traffic flow statistics** (no payload). This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **NIDPS** is the backbone of **perimeter defense** in enterprise networks.
- **HIDPS** is critical for **server hardening** and **endpoint detection response (EDR)**.
- **WIDPS** is mandatory in **enterprise Wi-Fi deployments** to prevent rogue APs.
- **NBA IDPS** is the workhorse of **DDoS mitigation** and **zero-day detection**.
- **Hybrid IDPS** forms the **Security Operations Center (SOC)** foundation in modern enterprises.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Framework — "Which IDPS to Deploy?"

When asked in exams *"Which IDPS type is best suited for scenario X?"*, use this structured KTU-evaluation framework:

### Step 1 — Identify the *Asset* to Protect

- If the asset is **network traffic on a segment** → NIDPS.
- If the asset is a **single critical host** (e.g., database server) → HIDPS.
- If the asset is **wireless airspace** → WIDPS.
- If the asset is **traffic patterns and flow behavior** → NBA IDPS.

### Step 2 — Identify the *Threat* to Mitigate

- External scans, worms, DoS → NIDPS / NBA.
- Insider misuse, file tampering → HIDPS.
- Rogue AP, evil twin → WIDPS.
- DDoS at flow level → NBA.

### Step 3 — Check for *Encryption* Constraint

- Encrypted traffic (TLS/SSL) → HIDPS (decryption occurs at endpoint) or NIDPS with SSL inspection appliance.

### Step 4 — Check for *Scalability & Cost*

- Large enterprise → Hybrid IDPS with NIDPS + HIDPS + SIEM correlation.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: IDPS Effectiveness Metrics

The effectiveness of any IDPS type is quantified using standard detection theory metrics. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation of the most commonly asked formulas.

### Derivation 1 — Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity)

The **Detection Rate (DR)** measures the proportion of actual attacks correctly identified by the IDPS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR &= \frac{\text{Number of attacks correctly detected}}{\text{Total number of actual attacks}} \\
   &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 1 Explanation:** The numerator $TP$ (True Positives) represents attacks the IDPS *flagged correctly*. The denominator $TP + FN$ is the *total* attacks encountered, including those the IDPS *missed* ($FN$ = False Negatives).

**Example:** If an IDPS correctly detects 95 out of 100 attacks:

$$
DR = \frac{95}{95 + 5} = \frac{95}{100} = 0.95 = 95\%
$$

### Derivation 2 — False Positive Rate (Fall-out)

The **False Positive Rate (FPR)** measures the proportion of legitimate traffic wrongly classified as malicious.

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \frac{\text{Legitimate traffic wrongly flagged}}{\text{Total legitimate traffic}} \\
    &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 2 Explanation:** $FP$ (False Positives) is the count of *legitimate* activities that the IDPS *mistakenly classified* as attacks. The denominator $FP + TN$ is the *total* legitimate activities, including those the IDPS *correctly allowed* ($TN$ = True Negatives).

### Derivation 3 — Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

**Precision** measures, out of all alerts raised, how many were *truly* attacks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3 Explanation:** A high precision means the IDPS raises few false alarms relative to real alerts — a critical KPI for SOC analyst workload.

### Derivation 4 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Detection and Precision)

The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Detection Rate and Precision, used to evaluate an IDPS holistically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F1 &= 2 \times \frac{Precision \times DR}{Precision + DR} \\
   &= 2 \times \frac{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \times \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4 Explanation:** The F1-Score penalizes IDPS that are good at detection but produce too many false positives (or vice versa), forcing a balanced performance.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — IDPS Selection by Threat Vector

| **Threat Scenario** | **Primary IDPS** | **Secondary/Complementary IDPS** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| External port scan on subnet | NIDPS | NBA IDPS | Packet inspection + flow anomaly |
| Insider file tampering on server | HIDPS | NIDPS | System call + file integrity logs |
| Rogue Access Point deployment | WIDPS | NIDPS | RF scan + MAC anomaly |
| Slow-rate DDoS attack | NBA IDPS | NIDPS | Flow statistics detect subtle surges |
| Encrypted malware C2 channel | HIDPS | Hybrid IDPS with SSL inspection | Decryption at endpoint |
| Zero-day exploit in HTTP traffic | Hybrid IDPS (NIDPS + NBA) | HIDPS | Correlation of payload + behavior |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always justify your answer by linking the *threat characteristic* to the *IDPS capability*. A vague answer like "NIDPS is better" will lose marks; instead write "NIDPS is best for external scans because it inspects packet headers and payloads in real time across the segment."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IDPS Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IDPS] --> B[Network-Based IDPS - NIDPS]
    A --> C[Host-Based IDPS - HIDPS]
    A --> D[Wireless IDPS - WIDPS]
    A --> E[Network Behavior Analysis - NBA]
    A --> F[Hybrid IDPS]

    B --> B1[Monitors Packets on Network Segment]
    B --> B2[Signature or Anomaly Detection]
    B --> B3[Example Snort Suricata]

    C --> C1[Monitors Single Host Logs and Calls]
    C --> C2[File Integrity and System Call Analysis]
    C --> C3[Example OSSEC Tripwire]

    D --> D1[Monitors IEEE 802.11 RF Airspace]
    D --> D2[Detects Rogue AP and Evil Twin]
    D --> D3[Example Kismet AirMagnet]

    E --> E1[Monitors NetFlow sFlow IPFIX]
    E --> E2[Statistical Anomaly Detection]
    E --> E3[Example Stealthwatch Arbor]

    F --> F1[Combines NIDPS HIDPS WIDPS NBA]
    F --> F2[Centralized Correlation Engine]
    F --> F3[Example QRadar Splunk Sentinel]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How IDPS Types Interact in an Enterprise

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PerimeterZone["PERIMETER LAYER"]
        FW[Firewall]
        NIDPS_A[Network Based IDPS Sensor]
    end

    subgraph CoreZone["CORE NETWORK LAYER"]
        NBA_A[NBA Flow Collector]
        SIEM_A[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    end

    subgraph EndpointZone["ENDPOINT LAYER"]
        HOST_SRV[Database Server]
        HIDPS_A[Host Based IDPS Agent]
    end

    subgraph WirelessZone["WIRELESS LAYER"]
        WAP[Wireless Access Point]
        WIDPS_A[Wireless IDPS RF Sensor]
    end

    FW --> NIDPS_A
    NIDPS_A --> SIEM_A
    HOST_SRV --> HIDPS_A
    HIDPS_A --> SIEM_A
    WAP --> WIDPS_A
    WIDPS_A --> SIEM_A
    NBA_A --> SIEM_A
    SIEM_A --> SOC[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows a **Hybrid IDPS deployment** where NIDPS, HIDPS, WIDPS, and NBA feed into a central SIEM. The SIEM correlates alerts and forwards high-fidelity incidents to the SOC analyst — this is the modern enterprise model.

## 4.3 Detection Workflow Inside a Generic IDPS

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Data Capture - Packets, Logs, RF, Flow] --> P2[Preprocessing - Normalization]
    P2 --> P3{Detection Engine}
    P3 --> P3A[Signature Based]
    P3 --> P3B[Anomaly Based]
    P3 --> P3C[Stateful Protocol]
    P3A --> P4[Alert Generation]
    P3B --> P4
    P3C --> P4
    P4 --> P5{Prevention Action}
    P5 -->|Yes - IPS Mode| P6[Block Packet, Drop Session, Quarantine]
    P5 -->|No - IDS Mode| P7[Log and Notify Analyst]
    P6 --> P8[Post Incident Reporting]
    P7 --> P8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Diagram:** The boundary between *IDS* and *IPS* is the **Prevention Action** block. If the IDPS is configured in **inline mode**, it can take the prevention branch; if in **passive/promiscuous mode**, it only alerts. This distinction is a **guaranteed KTU 2-mark question**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Differentiate between Network-Based IDPS and Host-Based IDPS.

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **NIDPS** | **HIDPS** |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Entire network segment | Single host |
| Data source | Live packet capture | OS logs, system calls |
| Visibility into encrypted traffic | Cannot inspect payload | Can analyze post-decryption |
| Insider threat detection | Weak | Strong |
| Deployment | SPAN port or inline sensor | Agent installed on host |

NIDPS provides broad perimeter visibility, while HIDPS offers deep host-level context including file integrity and process behavior.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q2. List any three advantages of Network Behavior Analysis (NBA) IDPS.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Detects zero-day attacks** through statistical deviation from baseline traffic patterns.
2. **Effective against DDoS** attacks by monitoring flow-level anomalies (e.g., sudden surge in flows to a single destination).
3. **Low computational overhead** since it does not perform deep packet inspection — it only analyzes flow records like NetFlow/sFlow.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify the different types of IDPS and explain any two in detail with diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the strengths and limitations of Network-Based IDPS and Wireless IDPS. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The classification of IDPS based on the source of monitored data is as follows:

1. **Network-Based IDPS (NIDPS)** — Monitors packets on network segments.
2. **Host-Based IDPS (HIDPS)** — Monitors a single host's OS and application activity.
3. **Wireless IDPS (WIDPS)** — Monitors 802.11 wireless traffic and RF behavior.
4. **Network Behavior Analysis (NBA) IDPS** — Monitors traffic flow statistics.
5. **Hybrid IDPS** — Combines multiple types for correlation.

**Detailed Explanation of NIDPS:**

NIDPS is deployed at strategic points in the network (e.g., DMZ, internal segments) using a SPAN port or inline tap. It captures packets in promiscuous mode and analyzes headers and payloads against a signature database or anomaly baseline.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    INET[Internet] --> R[Router] --> N[NIDPS Sensor SPAN] --> SW[Switch] --> HOST[Internal Hosts]
    N --> LOG[Log Server and SIEM]
```

**Detailed Explanation of HIDPS:**

HIDPS is installed as an agent on each critical host. It monitors system calls, file changes (using hash baselines), registry modifications, and application logs. It is best suited for detecting insider threats and host-specific malware.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    U[User] --> H[Host] --> H_AGENT[HIDPS Agent] --> LOG[Log Server]
    H_AGENT -->|File Integrity| CHECK[Hash Comparator]
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**NIDPS Strengths:**
- Covers entire segment with single sensor.
- Detects reconnaissance, worms, and protocol abuse.
- Operates transparently without affecting host performance.

**NIDPS Limitations:**
- Cannot decrypt encrypted payloads.
- Sensor overload at high bandwidth (10 Gbps+).
- No visibility into host-level context.

**WIDPS Strengths:**
- Detects rogue APs, evil twin attacks, MAC spoofing.
- Monitors unauthorized wireless clients.
- Identifies RF interference and jamming.

**WIDPS Limitations:**
- Limited to wireless airspace coverage.
- Cannot inspect encrypted 802.11 payload.
- RF sensors can be misled by directional antennas.

**Conclusion:** NIDPS is essential for perimeter defense, while WIDPS is mandatory for securing enterprise Wi-Fi. In practice, they are deployed together as part of a Hybrid IDPS strategy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a Network Behavior Analysis (NBA) IDPS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the functional architecture of a Hybrid IDPS deployment in an enterprise. Discuss two real-world use cases where Hybrid IDPS outperforms any single IDPS type. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

NBA IDPS does not inspect packet payloads. Instead, it collects **flow records** (NetFlow, sFlow, IPFIX) from routers, switches, and probes. Each flow record contains: source/destination IP, source/destination port, protocol, packet count, byte count, and timestamp.

**Architecture:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Router] -->|NetFlow| NBA[NBA Collector and Analyzer]
    R2[Switch] -->|sFlow| NBA
    NBA --> BASELINE[Traffic Baseline Database]
    NBA --> ANOMALY[Anomaly Detection Engine]
    ANOMALY --> ALERT[Alert to SIEM]
```

**Working Steps:**
1. **Flow Collection:** Receives flow records from network devices.
2. **Baseline Establishment:** Builds a statistical profile of normal traffic (e.g., average flows/sec, common protocols).
3. **Anomaly Detection:** Continuously compares live traffic against baseline using statistical methods (mean, standard deviation, thresholds).
4. **Alert Generation:** Raises alert when deviation exceeds threshold (e.g., sudden 10× spike in flows to one host indicates DDoS).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Hybrid IDPS Functional Architecture:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EXT[External Network]
        INET[Internet]
    end
    subgraph PERI[Perimeter]
        FW[Firewall] --> N1[NIDPS Sensor]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Core]
        SW[Core Switch] --> NBA1[NBA Collector]
    end
    subgraph END[Endpoints]
        S1[Server] --> H1[HIDPS Agent]
        S2[Workstation] --> H2[HIDPS Agent]
    end
    subgraph WL[Wireless]
        WAP[AP] --> W1[WIDPS RF Sensor]
    end
    N1 --> SIEM[Central SIEM Correlation]
    NBA1 --> SIEM
    H1 --> SIEM
    H2 --> SIEM
    W1 --> SIEM
    SIEM --> SOC[SOC Analyst]
```

**Use Case 1 — Encrypted C2 Beacon Detection:**
A single NIDPS cannot see the contents of TLS-encrypted traffic. By combining HIDPS (which sees the decrypted process behavior) with NBA (which sees the periodic flow pattern), Hybrid IDPS correlates the two and detects the beaconing pattern even in encrypted traffic.

**Use Case 2 — Advanced Persistent Threat (APT):**
APTs use multi-stage attacks — initial phishing, lateral movement, data exfiltration. A single NIDPS might miss the lateral movement (encrypted internal traffic), but HIDPS detects the unusual process spawn, and NBA detects the abnormal outbound flow. Hybrid IDPS correlates all three and produces a high-fidelity APT alert.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** confuse IDS with IPS in your answer. Always state whether the system *detects* or *prevents*.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** write generic answers like "NIDPS is used for networks" — always justify with the *specific data source* and *threat coverage*.
> - ✅ **Do** draw a **block diagram** for any 7-mark question — it carries 1–2 marks and shows examiner your understanding.
> - ✅ **Do** use the **comparison table format** wherever possible — it makes your answer structured and easy to evaluate.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** forget the difference between **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** detection methods.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **NIDPS** = Network-wide packet inspection via SPAN/inline sensor.
- 📌 **HIDPS** = Host-level monitoring via agent (logs, system calls, file integrity).
- 📌 **WIDPS** = Wireless airspace monitoring (RF + 802.11 frames).
- 📌 **NBA IDPS** = Flow-level statistical anomaly detection (no payload).
- 📌 **Hybrid IDPS** = Multi-sensor correlation via SIEM.
- 📌 **IDS = Detect + Alert; IPS = Detect + Prevent (block).**
- 📌 Key metrics: **Detection Rate** $= TP / (TP+FN)$, **FPR** $= FP / (FP+TN)$, **Precision** $= TP / (TP+FP)$, **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of DR and Precision.
- 📌 **NIDPS cannot decrypt** TLS — but HIDPS can (post-decryption on host).
- 📌 **NBA** has the **highest false positive rate** but best for **DDoS/zero-day**.
- 📌 Always justify IDPS selection using: **Asset → Threat → Encryption → Scalability.**
- 📌 For KTU 14-mark answers, always include a **diagram** and a **comparison table**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDPS Detection Methods - Signature-Based IDPS
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IDPS Detection Methods - Signature-Based IDPS

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Signature-Based IDPS** (also known as *Misuse-Based IDPS* or *Knowledge-Based Detection*) is a class of Intrusion Detection and Prevention System that identifies malicious activity by comparing observed network traffic or host events against a precompiled database of known attack patterns, called **signatures** (or *rules*). Each signature is a formal description of a specific attack, vulnerability exploitation, or malicious payload, encoded as a deterministic pattern.

A signature is formally expressed as a tuple:

$$S = (P, C, A)$$

where:
- $P$ = **Pattern** (byte sequence, regex, hash, or stateful expression)
- $C$ = **Context** (protocol, port, direction, header fields)
- $A$ = **Action** (alert, log, drop, reset, quarantine)

The detection function for a given event $E$ is:

$$D(E) = \begin{cases} 1 \text{ (Intrusion Detected)} & \text{if } \exists S_i \in \Sigma : \text{Match}(E, S_i.P, S_i.C) = 1 \\ 0 \text{ (Benign)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\Sigma$ is the signature database and $\text{Match}(\cdot)$ is the pattern-matching operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Signature-based detection is the **first-generation** of IDPS methodology. It is the most widely deployed technique in industry (Snort, Suricata, Zeek signatures) because of its **high precision** and **low false-positive rate** for known threats. It forms the foundation upon which anomaly-based and stateful protocol analysis methods are later built.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Border Security Watchlist"

Imagine an international airport immigration counter. Each immigration officer has a **printed booklet of photographs and known criminal profiles** (the *signature database*). When a traveller arrives, the officer:

1. **Scans the traveler's passport and face** (captures the *event* $E$).
2. **Compares** the captured features against the booklet entries.
3. If a **match is found** → flags the traveller (alert/drop).
4. If **no match** → allows entry (deemed benign).

This is exactly how signature-based IDPS works. Its critical weakness is also obvious: a **new, unknown criminal** (a *zero-day attack*) with a face not in the booklet will pass through undetected. The officer cannot infer guilt from "suspicious behaviour" — they can only recognize what they have been **explicitly taught**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The entire effectiveness of a signature-based IDPS reduces to two engineering questions: *(a) How fast can we match?* and *(b) How complete is the signature database?*

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Exam)

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Signature** | A codified description of a known attack pattern | Synonym: Rule, Pattern, Fingerprint |
| **Signature Database** | A structured collection of signatures | Often called `rules.v2` or `signatures.xml` |
| **Pattern Matching** | The algorithmic comparison of event $E$ against $S_i.P$ | Aho-Corasick, Boyer-Moore, Regex DFA |
| **False Positive (FP)** | Benign event incorrectly flagged as intrusion | Type I error |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Intrusion event missed by the system | Type II error — more dangerous |
| **True Positive (TP)** | Intrusion correctly detected | Desired outcome |
| **True Negative (TN)** | Benign event correctly passed | Normal operation |
| **Zero-Day Attack** | Attack with no existing signature | Fundamental limitation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow of Signature-Based IDPS

The detection engine executes a four-stage pipeline:

1. **Packet Capture / Event Acquisition** — Using `libpcap` (Linux) or `Npcap` (Windows), raw packets are mirrored from a SPAN port or TAP. Host-based variants use system call hooks, log file tailing, or ETW (Event Tracing for Windows).
2. **Pre-processing & Normalization** — Protocol decoding (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application), payload defragmentation, and reassembly into a normalized data stream.
3. **Pattern Matching Engine** — The normalized event is compared against the signature set $\Sigma = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$. Implementations typically use one of:
   - **String Matching Algorithms** (Boyer-Moore, Aho-Corasick)
   - **Regular Expression DFAs** (Deterministic Finite Automata)
   - **Stateful Pattern Matching** (multi-packet, multi-session signatures)
4. **Response Action** — On a match, the engine executes the prescribed action: log, alert (syslog, SIEM), or prevent (drop, TCP RST, ICMP unreachable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in the real world?** A 10 Gbps link carrying ~14.88 million packets/second means the matching engine must complete all comparisons in **< 67 nanoseconds per packet**. This drives the engineering choice of **Aho-Corasick** (multi-pattern, O(n+m) time) over naïve string search.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why capture first?** Without raw fidelity, encrypted or fragmented attacks can evade detection.
- **Why normalize?** Attackers split payloads across multiple packets (e.g., `cmd.exe` across 3 fragments) to defeat simple string matches.
- **Why multi-pattern matching?** With 50,000+ signatures in modern rule sets, scanning packet-by-packet would be computationally infeasible.
- **Why action differentiation?** Detection (IDP) vs Prevention (IPS) differ only in the action stage — same signature engine.

## 2.3 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.3.1 Confusion Matrix Notation

For a signature-based system evaluated over $N$ total events:

| | **Actually Intrusion** | **Actually Benign** |
|---|---|---|
| **Flagged** | $TP$ | $FP$ |
| **Not Flagged** | $FN$ | $TN$ |

where $N = TP + FP + FN + TN$.

### 2.3.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Metric | Formula | Meaning | KTU Weight |
|---|--------|---------|---------|------------|
| 1 | **Detection Rate (DR)** / Recall / Sensitivity | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Fraction of real attacks caught | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Fraction of benign events wrongly flagged | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Precision (P)** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Of all alerts, how many are real? | ⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Accuracy (Acc)** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+FN+TN}$ | Overall correctness | ⭐ |
| 5 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot DR}{P + DR}$ | Harmonic mean of P and DR | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **Specificity (Spc)** | $Spc = \dfrac{TN}{FP+TN} = 1 - FPR$ | True benign recognition | ⭐⭐ |
| 7 | **Matthew's Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced metric for skewed data | ⭐ |
| 8 | **Aho-Corasick Time Complexity** | $O(n + m + z)$ | $n$=text, $m$=pattern, $z$=matches | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | **Boyer-Moore Worst Case** | $O(nm)$ | Best: $O\!\left(\dfrac{n}{m}\right)$ | ⭐ |
| 10 | **Signature Throughput** | $\Psi = \dfrac{\vert \Sigma \vert}{T_{\text{scan}}}$ | Signatures scanned per second | ⭐⭐ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety:** The `|` in $|\Sigma|$ and $T_{\text{scan}}$ uses `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

### 2.3.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics are not just academic. In KTU-style problems and in industry:
- **SOC analysts** use FPR to size alert queues: FPR > 5% causes "alert fatigue."
- **Vendor comparisons** (Snort vs Suricata) are tabulated using DR vs throughput $\Psi$.
- **RULE tuning** aims to maximize DR while keeping FPR < 1%.

## 2.4 Categories of Signatures (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Category | Example | Use Case |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **String / Literal** | `"GET /scripts/..%c0%af..%c0%af..%c0%af.."` | IIS Unicode exploit |
| **Regex / PCRE** | `/\bunion\s+select\b/i` | SQL injection pattern |
| **Hash-based** | SHA-256 of EICAR test file | Known malware payload |
| **Protocol Anomaly** | `tcp.flags == 0xFF` (illegal) | Xmas scan |
| **Stateful / Multi-packet** | 3-way handshake followed by `SYN+data` | SYN flood precursor |
| **Composite (Threshold)** | 100 SYN/sec from single IP | DoS detection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — F1-Score from Precision and Detection Rate

The F1-Score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision $P$ and Detection Rate $DR$. It penalizes extreme imbalance: an IDS with $DR=1$ and $P=0.1$ scores only $F_1 \approx 0.18$.

**Step 1 — Recall the definitions.**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \qquad DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$$

**Step 2 — Harmonic mean definition.**

The harmonic mean of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$ is:

$$H(a,b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $a = P$ and $b = DR$.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{DR}}$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{DR + P}{P \cdot DR}} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot DR}{P + DR}$$

$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot DR}{P + DR}}$$

> **Engineering intuition:** The harmonic mean (not the arithmetic mean) is used because $F_1$ must be **small** if either $P$ or $DR$ is small. The arithmetic mean would let one metric "compensate" for the other — the harmonic mean does not.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Detection Rate and FPR from a Worked Confusion Matrix

**Problem Statement (KTU style):** An IDS was evaluated on $N = 10{,}000$ events. It produced 800 alerts. Of these 800, 750 were real attacks and 50 were false alarms. The total number of real attacks in the test set was 1000. Compute $DR$, $FPR$, $P$, $Acc$, and $F_1$.

**Step 1 — Extract the raw counts.**

$$TP = 750, \quad FP = 50, \quad \text{Alerts} = 800$$

$$TP + FN = 1000 \;\Rightarrow\; FN = 1000 - 750 = 250$$

$$TP + FP + FN + TN = N = 10{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $TN$.**

$$TN = N - TP - FP - FN = 10{,}000 - 750 - 50 - 250 = 8{,}950$$

**Step 3 — Build the confusion matrix.**

| | **Actually Intrusion** | **Actually Benign** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flagged** | $TP = 750$ | $FP = 50$ | $800$ |
| **Not Flagged** | $FN = 250$ | $TN = 8950$ | $9200$ |
| **Total** | $1000$ | $9000$ | $10{,}000$ |

**Step 4 — Compute Detection Rate.**

$$DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{750}{1000} = 0.75 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{75\%}$$

**Step 5 — Compute False Positive Rate.**

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{50}{50 + 8950} = \frac{50}{9000} \approx 0.00556 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{0.556\%}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Precision.**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{750}{800} = 0.9375 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{93.75\%}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Accuracy.**

$$Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{N} = \frac{750 + 8950}{10{,}000} = \frac{9700}{10{,}000} = 0.97 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{97\%}$$

**Step 8 — Compute F1-Score.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot DR}{P + DR} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9375 \cdot 0.75}{0.9375 + 0.75} = \frac{1.40625}{1.6875} \approx 0.8333$$

$$\boxed{DR=75\%, \quad FPR=0.556\%, \quad P=93.75\%, \quad Acc=97\%, \quad F_1=0.8333}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the paradox:** Accuracy = 97% looks excellent, but the IDS **missed 250 real attacks** ($FN=250$). This is why **Detection Rate (DR)** is the more meaningful security metric — accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets where attacks are rare.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aho-Corasick Multi-Pattern Matching Time Complexity

For a signature database with $k$ patterns of total length $m = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert P_i \vert$, scanned over an input of length $n$, the Aho-Corasick automaton achieves:

$$T_{\text{AC}} = O(n + m + z)$$

where $z$ is the number of matches found.

**Step 1 — Build phase:** Insert all $k$ patterns into a trie of depth at most $L = \max_i \vert P_i \vert$. This takes $O(m)$ time.

**Step 2 — Failure-link phase:** Compute failure (suffix) links via BFS, also $O(m)$.

**Step 3 — Go-function phase:** Convert the goto graph into a DFA. Worst case $O(m \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$ where $\vert \Sigma \vert$ is the alphabet size, but in practice is amortized.

**Step 4 — Scan phase:** Each input character causes exactly one state transition, hence $O(n)$. Matches are reported in $O(z)$.

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$T_{\text{total}} = O(m) + O(m) + O(n) + O(z) = O(n + m + z)$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{AC}} = O(n + m + z)}$$

> **Engineering intuition:** Aho-Corasick is **amortized linear** regardless of the number of patterns. This is why Snort and Suricata can handle 50,000+ rules in real-time on commodity hardware.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strengths vs Limitations of Signature-Based IDPS

| Dimension | Strength | Limitation | Mitigation Strategy |
|-----------|----------|------------|---------------------|
| **Known Threats** | Near-perfect detection | **Cannot detect zero-day attacks** | Layer with anomaly-based detection |
| **False Positives** | Very low FPR (0.5–2%) | Cannot catch novel variants | Heuristic signatures, sandboxing |
| **Performance** | Linear-time matching | Slowed by encrypted traffic (TLS 1.3) | SSL/TLS MITM inspection |
| **Forensic Value** | Alerts are highly interpretable | Requires constant rule updates | Threat intel feeds (ET Open, Talos) |
| **Operational Cost** | Easy to deploy, low CPU | High maintenance overhead | Automated signature management |
| **Evasion Resistance** | Stateful signatures catch fragments | Vulnerable to polymorphism, encoding | Normalization, multi-pattern DFAs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Signature-Based IDPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Traffic / Host Events] --> B[Packet Capture Engine libpcap or ETW]
    B --> C[Protocol Decoder Ethernet to IP to TCP to UDP to App]
    C --> D[Defragmentation and Reassembly]
    D --> E[Normalized Event Stream]
    E --> F[Preprocessor 1: HTTP Decode]
    E --> G[Preprocessor 2: SMTP Decode]
    E --> H[Preprocessor 3: DNS Decode]
    F --> I[Pattern Matching Engine Aho-Corasick + Regex DFA]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    J[Signature Database Sigma = 50k rules] --> I
    I --> K{Match Found?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Action Module: Log, Alert, Drop, Reset]
    K -- No --> M[Forward Normal Traffic]
    L --> N[SIEM or Syslog Server]
    M --> O[End Host or Next Hop]
    N --> P[SOC Analyst Triage]
    style I fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#ff6666,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#66ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Single Packet Through the Detection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Packet In] --> P2{TCP or UDP?}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Apply Protocol Anomaly Rules]
    P2 -- Yes --> P4[Decode L7 Payload]
    P3 --> P5[Stateful Context Check]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Load Applicable Rule Set]
    P6 --> P7[Run Aho-Corasick Scan]
    P7 --> P8{Any Rule Matched?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Trigger Signature Action]
    P8 -- No --> P10[Mark Benign, Forward]
    P9 --> P11[Update State Table]
    P10 --> P12[Continue Inspection]
    style P7 fill:#ffff66,stroke:#333
    style P8 fill:#66ffff,stroke:#333
    style P9 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Data Flow — Signature Lifecycle (Creation to Deployment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threat Discovery CVE or Malware Sample] --> B[Signature Author Security Analyst]
    B --> C[Rule Format Snort or Suricata Syntax]
    C --> D[Internal QA and FPR Testing]
    D --> E[Threat Intel Sharing ET Open, Talos]
    E --> F[Vendor Distribution]
    F --> G[Production IDS]
    G --> H[Real-time Match]
    H --> I[Analyst Feedback]
    I --> B
    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#ccccff,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Signature-Based IDPS. List any four limitations.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Signature-Based IDPS** detects intrusions by comparing observed events against a precompiled database of known attack patterns called **signatures**. Each signature encodes the byte sequence, protocol context, and prescribed action for a specific attack. Detection is essentially a deterministic pattern-matching operation.

**Four Limitations:**
1. **Zero-Day Blindness:** Cannot detect attacks for which no signature exists.
2. **Encrypted Traffic:** Cannot inspect TLS-encrypted payloads without MITM decryption.
3. **Polymorphic Malware:** Single malware variant with mutated byte sequences evades literal signatures.
4. **High Maintenance:** Requires continuous rule updates as new threats emerge.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two limitations, 1 mark for the remaining two — keyword-driven marking.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between False Positive and False Negative in the context of signature-based IDPS. Which is more dangerous?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | False Positive (FP) | False Negative (FN) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Benign event wrongly flagged as intrusion | Real intrusion missed by the system |
| **Error Type** | Type I Error | Type II Error |
| **Impact** | Analyst fatigue, wasted time, productivity loss | Successful compromise, data breach, regulatory loss |
| **Security Severity** | Moderate | **Critical** |

**Conclusion:** A **False Negative is more dangerous** because it represents a real attack that has bypassed the IDPS, potentially leading to data exfiltration or system compromise. While FPs erode operational trust, FNs represent outright detection failure.

*(1 mark each for definition of FP/FN, 1 mark for justifying why FN is more dangerous.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Comprehensive Design Question** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Question:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Signature-Based IDPS**. Discuss in detail the following:
- **(a)** The major components and their functions (7 marks)
- **(b)** The pattern-matching algorithms used, with a comparison of Aho-Corasick and Boyer-Moore (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

A Signature-Based IDPS has the following major components (refer to SECTION_4, Diagram 4.1):

**1. Packet Capture Engine (1 mark):**
Uses `libpcap` on Linux or `Npcap` on Windows to capture mirrored traffic from a SPAN port or network TAP. The captured packets form a continuous byte stream.

**2. Protocol Decoder & Normalizer (2 marks):**
Performs layered decoding — Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application Layer. Handles defragmentation, TCP reassembly, and protocol-specific decoding (HTTP, DNS, SMTP). This stage is critical because attackers split malicious payloads across multiple fragments to evade detection.

**3. Pattern Matching Engine (2 marks):**
The core decision component. Uses **Aho-Corasick** for multi-pattern string matching and **Regex DFAs** for complex pattern expressions. Operates at line-rate (~10 Gbps) using optimized C code with SIMD instructions.

**4. Signature Database ($\Sigma$) (1 mark):**
A structured collection of 50,000+ rules in standardized formats (e.g., Snort rules, Emerging Threats ET Open). Each rule contains the pattern, context, and response action.

**5. Action Module (1 mark):**
Executes the prescribed response — generate alert (syslog, SIEM), log the event, drop the packet (IPS mode), or send TCP RST. The decision tree is `Match → Action → Log → Forward to SOC`.

#### **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Boyer-Moore | Aho-Corasick |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **# of Patterns** | Single pattern at a time | Multiple patterns simultaneously |
| **Time Complexity (worst)** | $O(nm)$ | $O(n + m + z)$ |
| **Time Complexity (avg)** | $O(n/m)$ sub-linear | $O(n)$ |
| **Preprocessing** | Bad-character & good-suffix heuristics | Trie + failure links (BFS) |
| **Memory** | $O(m + \sigma)$ | $O(m)$ states |
| **Use Case** | Single signature scan | **Multi-rule IDPS engines** |

**Aho-Corasick Algorithm (2 marks):**
Builds a trie of all $k$ patterns. Computes failure links so that on a mismatch, the automaton transitions to the longest proper suffix that is also a prefix. Each input character causes exactly **one state transition** → $O(n)$ scan time. The total preprocessing + scan is $O(n + m + z)$.

**Boyer-Moore Algorithm (2 marks):**
Scans the pattern from **right to left** against the text. Uses two heuristics: the **bad-character rule** (skip alignments where the mismatched character does not appear in the pattern) and the **good-suffix rule** (skip alignments where the suffix matches but the next character is wrong). Excellent for single-pattern searches but inefficient for multi-pattern IDPS workloads.

**Conclusion:** Aho-Corasick is the **industry standard** for IDPS engines (Snort 3.0 onwards) because real-world deployments require simultaneous matching against tens of thousands of rules.

---

### **Question B: Analytical / Numerical Question** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Question:** A network signature-based IDPS was tested on a dataset of 5,000 events, of which 800 were actual intrusions. The IDPS generated 700 alerts. Of these alerts, 650 were true attacks and 50 were false alarms. Answer the following:
- **(a)** Construct the confusion matrix and calculate the Detection Rate (DR), False Positive Rate (FPR), Precision, and Accuracy (7 marks).
- **(b)** Calculate the F1-Score. Briefly comment on whether the IDPS is acceptable for production deployment. Justify your answer with respect to typical industry thresholds (DR ≥ 95%, FPR ≤ 2%) (7 marks).

---

#### **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given values (1 mark):**
- $N = 5000$, Total actual intrusions $= 800$, Alerts $= 700$
- $TP + FP = 700$, $TP = 650$, $FP = 50$
- $TP + FN = 800 \Rightarrow FN = 800 - 650 = 150$
- $TN = N - TP - FP - FN = 5000 - 650 - 50 - 150 = 4150$

**Step 2 — Confusion Matrix (2 marks):**

| | **Actually Intrusion** | **Actually Benign** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flagged** | $TP = 650$ | $FP = 50$ | $700$ |
| **Not Flagged** | $FN = 150$ | $TN = 4150$ | $4300$ |
| **Total** | $800$ | $4200$ | $5000$ |

**Step 3 — Compute the four metrics (4 marks):**

$$DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{650}{800} = 0.8125 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{81.25\%}$$

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{50}{4200} \approx 0.0119 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{1.19\%}$$

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{650}{700} \approx 0.9286 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{92.86\%}$$

$$Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{N} = \frac{650 + 4150}{5000} = 0.96 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{96.00\%}$$

---

#### **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — F1-Score calculation (3 marks):**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot DR}{P + DR} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9286 \cdot 0.8125}{0.9286 + 0.8125} = \frac{1.5090}{1.7411} \approx 0.8666$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.867}$$

**Step 2 — Industry threshold comparison (2 marks):**

| Metric | Achieved | Industry Threshold | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| $DR$ | 81.25% | ≥ 95% | ❌ **Fails** |
| $FPR$ | 1.19% | ≤ 2% | ✅ **Passes** |
| $P$ | 92.86% | ≥ 90% | ✅ **Passes** |
| $F_1$ | 0.867 | ≥ 0.90 | ❌ **Fails** |

**Step 3 — Production deployment decision (2 marks):**

The IDPS is **NOT acceptable** for production deployment in its current form. While the False Positive Rate (1.19%) and Precision (92.86%) meet industry standards, the **Detection Rate of 81.25% is critically low** — it means **18.75% of real attacks (150 out of 800) are slipping through undetected**. This is a serious security gap that could lead to data breaches.

**Recommended Actions:**
1. Update the signature database with the latest threat intelligence (Talos, ET Open rules).
2. Tune existing rules to reduce FN — possibly relax overly narrow pattern constraints.
3. Layer with **anomaly-based detection** to catch the 150 missed attacks.
4. Re-evaluate after rule updates until DR ≥ 95% is achieved.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusion matrix layout:** Always draw the 2×2 grid correctly — *Predicted* on rows, *Actual* on columns. Reversed matrix loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Miscomputing $TN$:** Forgetting to solve $TN = N - TP - FP - FN$ is the #1 error. Always verify: $TP+FP+FN+TN$ must equal $N$.
> 3. **Confusing Precision with Detection Rate:** They look similar but have different denominators. $P = TP/(TP+FP)$ vs $DR = TP/(TP+FN)$. Examiner specifically tests this.
> 4. **Omitting units:** Always state percentages with `%` and F1 as a unitless score in $[0,1]$.
> 5. **In Q.A, omitting a diagram:** A neat labelled block diagram is worth 2 of the 7 marks in part (a). Skipping it guarantees a deduction.
> 6. **In Q.A part (b), writing only the table:** A 2-line textual explanation of *why* Aho-Corasick is preferred is mandatory. Marks are reserved for engineering justification, not just the comparison table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 HIGH-DENSITY EXAM REVISION — READ THIS BEFORE THE EXAM:**

- ✅ **Signature-Based IDPS = Deterministic Pattern Matching** against a precompiled rule set $\Sigma$. It is the **first-generation, knowledge-based** detection paradigm.
- ✅ The detection function is a **binary classifier**: $\text{Match}(E, S_i) \in \{0, 1\}$.
- ✅ **Key formula triplet** (must memorize): $DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$, $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$.
- ✅ **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of $P$ and $DR$; use when classes are imbalanced (rare attacks).
- ✅ **Industry acceptance thresholds:** $DR \geq 95\%$, $FPR \leq 2\%$, $P \geq 90\%$.
- ✅ **Aho-Corasick** is the **gold standard** for multi-pattern matching in IDPS engines. Time: $O(n + m + z)$.
- ✅ **Boyer-Moore** is for single-pattern; worst case $O(nm)$, average $O(n/m)$.
- ✅ **Four fundamental limitations:** (1) Zero-day blindness, (2) Encrypted traffic evasion, (3) Polymorphic malware, (4) Maintenance overhead.
- ✅ **Real-world tools:** **Snort** (open-source, rule-based), **Suricata** (multi-threaded, EVE JSON output), **Zeek** (signature + scripting).
- ✅ **Snort rule anatomy (memorize the format):**
  `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (msg:"..."; content:"..."; sid:1000001; rev:1; classtype:...)`
- ✅ **False Negative > False Positive** in security context because missed attacks lead to breaches.
- ✅ **Accuracy is misleading** on imbalanced datasets — always prefer $DR$ and $F_1$ for security evaluation.
- ✅ **Architecture sequence:** Packet Capture → Decode → Normalize → Pre-process → Match against $\Sigma$ → Action (Log/Alert/Drop).
- ✅ **Stateful signatures** are needed for multi-packet attacks (e.g., fragment-based evasion, slow-rate DoS).
- ✅ **Signature categories** (know all six): String, Regex, Hash, Protocol Anomaly, Stateful, Composite Threshold.
- ✅ **Defense in depth:** Signature-based alone is insufficient. Combine with **Anomaly-Based** and **Stateful Protocol Analysis** for comprehensive coverage (covered in upcoming modules).

> **End of Topic Notes — Signature-Based IDPS (Module 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Anomaly-Based IDPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statistical Anomaly-Based IDPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Anomaly-Based Intrusion Detection & Prevention System (SA-IDPS)** is a category of IDPS that establishes a *quantitative baseline* of normal network or host behaviour by computing statistical measures (mean, variance, standard deviation, covariance, entropy, etc.) over a sliding observation window. Any observable that deviates from this learned statistical profile by more than a pre-defined *threshold* is flagged as an intrusion candidate.

In strict KTU terminology, a Statistical Anomaly-Based IDPS comprises four building blocks:

1. **Data Collector (Sensor):** Captures audit records, packet headers, system calls, or flow statistics.
2. **Statistical Engine (Profiler):** Builds a *statistical profile* — a set of descriptive metrics (μ, σ², multivariate covariance matrix Σ) — that characterise "normal" activity.
3. **Threshold Comparator (Detector):** Measures the deviation between live traffic and the stored profile; if the deviation exceeds a threshold, an alarm is raised.
4. **Response Module (Prevention Block):** Issues alerts, drops sessions, or updates the firewall rule base.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Statistical methods form the *first generation* of anomaly-based IDPS. They are *unsupervised*, do not require labelled attack signatures, and can in principle detect **zero-day attacks** because they flag any statistically rare event.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pulsometer" for a Network

Imagine a doctor who has been monitoring a patient for years. The doctor has recorded a *baseline pulse rate* of 72 bpm with a *normal variation* of ±5 bpm. One day, the patient's pulse jumps to 140 bpm. The doctor doesn't need to know *which disease* causes tachycardia — the mere *statistical deviation* from the established profile is enough to raise a red flag and order further tests.

A **Statistical Anomaly-Based IDPS** behaves identically:

| Element in the Analogy | Network Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient | Host / Server / Network |
| Pulse rate measurement | Packet rate, login frequency, CPU usage, port activity |
| Long-term baseline (72 ± 5 bpm) | Learnt statistical profile (μ, σ²) |
| 140 bpm anomaly | Observed metric crossing threshold |
| Doctor's alarm | IDPS alert / blocking action |

The IDPS does **not** need a pre-recorded "signature" of the Wannacry worm, the Mirai bot, or a zero-day exploit. It simply asks: *"Does the current activity look statistically improbable when compared to yesterday / last week / last month?"* If the answer is *yes*, it reacts.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a KTU question says "explain anomaly detection with an example," always start with the **profile → threshold → deviation** triad. This single structure is worth 2–3 marks in Part A.

---

## 1.3 Why Statistical? The Motivating Engineering Rationale

Signature-based systems (e.g., Snort rules) can only catch *known* attacks — they are essentially *closed-set* classifiers. Statistical anomaly detection is an *open-set* approach with three engineering virtues:

- **Generalisation to Novel Attacks** — anything that perturbs the baseline is suspicious.
- **No Signature Database** — eliminates the operational cost of constant rule updates.
- **Quantifiable Confidence** — outputs a numeric score (z-score, χ², Mahalanobis distance) that can be fed into a downstream risk engine.

The trade-off is a higher **False Positive Rate (FPR)** because *legitimate* but rare events (a CEO logging in from abroad, a scheduled monthly batch job) also deviate from the baseline.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners specifically test whether you can articulate this **trade-off triangle**: Detection Rate ↑, False Positives ↑, Maintenance Cost ↓.

---

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Statistical Models Used in IDPS

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following statistical models as essential:

1. **Operational Model** — uses simple univariate thresholds on counters (e.g., failed logins per minute).
2. **Mean and Standard Deviation Model** — z-score based detection.
3. **Multivariate Model** — captures *correlations* between features using a covariance matrix.
4. **Markov Process Model** — models *state transitions* (e.g., expected sequence of system calls).
5. **Time Series Model** — applies ARIMA / exponential smoothing to forecast the next value and flags forecast errors.

These will be treated formally in SECTION 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of a Statistical Anomaly IDPS

The IDPS executes a *closed loop* on every observation window *W* (typically 1 s to 60 s):

**Step 1 — Feature Extraction**
From raw audit data, extract *N* features per window:
- `f1` = number of TCP connections
- `f2` = number of unique destination IPs
- `f3` = bytes transferred
- `f4` = CPU utilisation
- `f5` = failed authentication attempts
… and so on.

**Step 2 — Profile Construction (Training Phase)**
For each feature *fi*, compute descriptive statistics over a long "clean" baseline period *T*:
- Mean $\mu_i$
- Variance $\sigma_i^{2}$
- (Optional) Pairwise covariance $\Sigma_{ij}$

**Step 3 — Online Scoring (Detection Phase)**
For each new observation vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)$, compute a *deviation score* D(x) and compare with threshold *T*:
- If $D(x) > T$ → **ALARM**
- Else → **Normal**

**Step 4 — Adaptive Update (Optional)**
Update μ and σ with exponential moving averages so the profile slowly adapts to seasonal behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Step 1 reduces high-dimensional traffic to manageable metrics. Step 2 captures the *centroid and spread* of normal behaviour. Step 3 quantifies *how far* today's traffic sits from the centroid. Step 4 prevents the profile from becoming stale.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** that KTU has tested (or is highly likely to test) on statistical anomaly IDPS. Memorise units, ranges, and boundary conditions — they are common 1-mark sub-parts in 14-mark questions.

| # | Statistical Measure | Mathematical Expression | Units / Range | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Same as $x_i$ | Centroid of feature distribution |
| 2 | Variance (population) | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2}$ | (unit of $x$)$^{2}$ | Spread of feature |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}}$ | Same as $x_i$ | Threshold scaling |
| 4 | Z-Score (standardised deviation) | $Z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Dimensionless (typically $-3 \le Z \le 3$) | Anomaly score for univariate model |
| 5 | Sum of Squared Deviations | $SSD = \sum (x_i - \mu)^{2}$ | (unit)$^{2}$ | Basis of $\chi^{2}$ statistic |
| 6 | Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \dfrac{(O_j - E_j)^{2}}{E_j}$ | Dimensionless (degrees of freedom $= k-1$) | Goodness-of-fit on histogram bins |
| 7 | Covariance | $\Sigma_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum (x_i - \mu_i)(x_j - \mu_j)$ | (unit$_i$)(unit$_j$) | Captures inter-feature correlation |
| 8 | Correlation Coefficient | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\Sigma_{ij}}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$ | $-1 \le \rho \le +1$ | Normalised correlation |
| 9 | Mahalanobis Distance | $D^{2} = (x - \mu)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)$ | Dimensionless (follows $\chi^{2}_k$) | Multivariate anomaly score |
| 10 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits | Detects randomness (e.g., in payloads / IPs) |
| 11 | Markov Transition Probability | $P(s_j \mid s_i)$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ | State-sequence anomaly |
| 12 | ARIMA Forecast Error | $e_t = x_t - \hat{x}_t$ | Same as $x_t$ | Time-series anomaly |
| 13 | Threshold Rule | Alarm if $D(x) > T$, e.g. $T = k\sigma$ | — | Decision boundary |
| 14 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le FPR \le 1$ | IDPS performance metric |
| 15 | Detection Rate (TPR / Recall) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $0 \le TPR \le 1$ | IDPS performance metric |

> [!TIP]
> **Symbol Reminder (KTU 2024):** $O_j$ = observed count in bin *j*; $E_j$ = expected count in bin *j* under the normal distribution. Avoid using $\vert$ in inline math; prefer the $\chi^{2}$ or Z-score notation that the syllabus already uses.

---

## 2.3 The Five Canonical Statistical Models (KTU 2024 Depth)

### 2.3.1 Operational (Threshold) Model
The simplest model. A counter is incremented for every event of interest (e.g., failed logins, SYN packets). If the counter exceeds an upper threshold $T_H$ or falls below a lower threshold $T_L$ within a window, an alarm fires.

**Strengths:** trivial to implement, real-time.
**Weaknesses:** cannot detect slow-and-low attacks distributed across windows.

### 2.3.2 Mean and Standard Deviation Model
For a feature $x$ with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$, the *anomaly score* is the z-score:
$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$
The 3-σ rule (Chebyshev's inequality gives a probabilistic bound for any distribution) is the standard threshold:
$$|Z| > 3 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Anomaly}$$

### 2.3.3 Multivariate Model
Captures *joint* behaviour across features. A user's *normal* profile is the vector $\mu$ plus the covariance matrix $\Sigma$. The deviation is the **Mahalanobis distance** $D^{2}$ listed in row 9 of the formula sheet. It correctly accounts for the fact that a *simultaneous* rise in both packet rate and CPU usage may be normal even if each rise alone is suspicious.

### 2.3.4 Markov Process Model
Models the probability of *transitioning* from one system state to the next. For example, in a normal session the sequence `login → read → write → close` is highly probable. Any observed sequence with a very low joint transition probability is flagged.

### 2.3.5 Time Series Model
Fits a model (ARIMA, Holt-Winters, exponential smoothing) to the historical time series of a metric. The *forecast* for the next value is $\hat{x}_t$. If the residual $e_t = x_t - \hat{x}_t$ exceeds a threshold, the system fires.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **NIDS (e.g., NetFlow analytics)** | Detect DDoS by Mahalanobis distance on flow-feature vectors. |
| **HIDS (e.g., antivirus, EDR)** | Detect ransomware by entropy jumps on file-write operations. |
| **SCADA / ICS** | Detect process anomalies via ARIMA on sensor time series. |
| **Cloud Workloads** | Detect cryptomining by z-score on CPU/MEM utilisation. |
| **UEBA (User & Entity Behaviour Analytics)** | Detect insider threats via Markov chains over user actions. |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern commercial products (Cisco Secure Analytics, Darktrace, Azure Sentinel) all retain a *statistical engine* as one of their analytical backbones, even when augmented with ML/DL layers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standardising the Deviation (from Mean & Variance to Z-Score)

**Problem:** Given observations of failed-login counts over 10 windows as
$\{2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 2, 1, 3, 2\}$, find the z-score for the 11th observation $x_{11} = 9$. Use a threshold of $|Z| > 3$ to flag an anomaly.

**Step 1 — Compute the arithmetic mean $\mu$:**

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
     = \frac{2+3+1+4+2+3+2+1+3+2}{10}
     = \frac{23}{10}
     = 2.3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the population variance $\sigma^{2}$:**

We first compute each squared deviation $(x_i - \mu)^{2}$:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $x_i - \mu$ | $(x_i - \mu)^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $-0.3$ | $0.09$ |
| 2 | 3 | $0.7$ | $0.49$ |
| 3 | 1 | $-1.3$ | $1.69$ |
| 4 | 4 | $1.7$ | $2.89$ |
| 5 | 2 | $-0.3$ | $0.09$ |
| 6 | 3 | $0.7$ | $0.49$ |
| 7 | 2 | $-0.3$ | $0.09$ |
| 8 | 1 | $-1.3$ | $1.69$ |
| 9 | 3 | $0.7$ | $0.49$ |
| 10 | 2 | $-0.3$ | $0.09$ |
| **Sum** |  |  | **8.10** |

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^{2}
           = \frac{8.10}{10}
           = 0.81
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation $\sigma$:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}} = \sqrt{0.81} = 0.9
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the z-score for $x_{11} = 9$:**

$$
Z_{11} = \frac{x_{11} - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{9 - 2.3}{0.9} = \frac{6.7}{0.9} \approx 7.44
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$
|Z_{11}| = 7.44 \;\;>\;\; 3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{ALARM}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 9 failed logins in the 11th window deviate by $7.44\,\sigma$ from the learnt mean — a strong brute-force indicator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The unit of $Z$ is "standard deviations." A z-score of 7.44 means the observation lies ~7.4 σ away from the mean — *far* beyond Chebyshev's 3-σ practical bound.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold *T* in Terms of $\sigma$ for a Target False Positive Rate

**Problem:** Suppose the observed metric is approximately Gaussian with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. Find the threshold $T$ such that the False Positive Rate $FPR \le 0.01$ (i.e., $1\,\%$ of normal observations are falsely flagged).

**Step 1 — Set up the tail probability equation.** A two-sided threshold produces false alarms when the observation lies *both* above $T$ and below $-T$:

$$
FPR = P(\vert Z \vert > T/\sigma) = 2 \cdot \big[1 - \Phi(T/\sigma)\big]
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the standardised threshold $z$:**

$$
0.01 = 2 \cdot \big[1 - \Phi(z)\big] \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Phi(z) = 0.995 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad z \approx 2.576
$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to the data scale:**

$$
T = z \cdot \sigma = 2.576\,\sigma
$$

**Conclusion:** Choose $T \approx 2.58\,\sigma$ to obtain a $1\,\%$ FPR for a Gaussian feature.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU 14-mark trick:** They give you the FPR and ask for the threshold — or vice-versa. Always keep two equations: the *decision rule* and the *FPR formula*, then cross-eliminate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multivariate Mahalanobis Score

**Problem:** A baseline host profile yields the mean vector and covariance matrix
$$
\mu = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & 8 \\ 8 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$
where the features are CPU-utilisation and packets-per-second. The current observation is $x = \begin{bmatrix} 130 \\ 35 \end{bmatrix}$. Compute the Mahalanobis distance squared $D^{2}$ and decide (use $\chi^{2}_{0.01,\,2} \approx 9.21$).

**Step 1 — Compute the deviation vector:**

$$
x - \mu = \begin{bmatrix} 130 - 100 \\ 35 - 50 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ -15 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Invert the covariance matrix:**

$$
\det(\Sigma) = (25)(16) - (8)(8) = 400 - 64 = 336
$$

$$
\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{336} \begin{bmatrix} 16 & -8 \\ -8 & 25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the quadratic form** $D^{2} = (x - \mu)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)$:

$$
\Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu) = \frac{1}{336}
\begin{bmatrix}
16 \cdot 30 + (-8)(-15) \\
-8 \cdot 30 + 25 \cdot (-15)
\end{bmatrix}
= \frac{1}{336}
\begin{bmatrix}
480 + 120 \\
-240 - 375
\end{bmatrix}
= \frac{1}{336}
\begin{bmatrix}
600 \\
-615
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
1.7857 \\
-1.8304
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
D^{2} = (x - \mu)^{T} \cdot \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)
      = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -15 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.7857 \\ -1.8304 \end{bmatrix}
      = 30 \cdot 1.7857 + (-15)(-1.8304)
$$

$$
D^{2} = 53.57 + 27.46 = 81.03
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
D^{2} = 81.03 \;\;>\;\; 9.21 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{ALARM}
$$

**Conclusion:** The observation is so far in the tail of the multivariate normal distribution that the probability of seeing it under "normal" conditions is below $1\,\%$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit for Traffic Histogram

**Problem:** Observed packet-size distribution over 1000 packets in 4 bins of 250 bytes width:

| Bin | Observed $O_j$ | Expected $E_j$ (under baseline) |
|---|---|---|
| 0–250 | 350 | 250 |
| 250–500 | 250 | 250 |
| 500–750 | 250 | 250 |
| 750–1000 | 150 | 250 |

**Step 1 — Compute the chi-square statistic:**

$$
\chi^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{4} \frac{(O_j - E_j)^{2}}{E_j}
        = \frac{(350-250)^{2}}{250}
        + \frac{(250-250)^{2}}{250}
        + \frac{(250-250)^{2}}{250}
        + \frac{(150-250)^{2}}{250}
$$

$$
\chi^{2} = \frac{10000}{250} + 0 + 0 + \frac{10000}{250}
        = 40 + 0 + 0 + 40
        = 80
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with critical value.** For $k = 4$ bins, degrees of freedom $df = k - 1 = 3$. The critical value at $\alpha = 0.01$ is $\chi^{2}_{0.01,3} \approx 11.345$.

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
\chi^{2} = 80 \;\;>\;\; 11.345 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{ALARM}
$$

**Conclusion:** The current packet-size distribution is *not* drawn from the same distribution as the baseline — a likely indication of attack traffic (e.g., uniform-sized flooding packets).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Cheat-Sheet for 14-Mark Questions)

| Model | Best Used For | Score Function | Threshold Basis | False-Positive Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operational Threshold | Hard limits (e.g., max logins) | Counter vs $T_H, T_L$ | Domain expert | Low (if $T$ well chosen) |
| Mean & Std-Dev | Univariate continuous features | $Z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$ | 3-σ rule | Moderate |
| Multivariate | Correlated feature sets | Mahalanobis $D^{2}$ | $\chi^{2}_{k, \alpha}$ | Moderate |
| Markov | Sequential events | Joint transition prob. | $- \log P < T$ | Higher |
| Time Series | Periodic / seasonal data | Forecast error $e_t$ | Std of residuals | Moderate |
| Entropy | Randomness detection | Shannon $H(X)$ | Domain-specific $T$ | High (noisy metric) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 14-mark questions almost always ask you to *justify the choice of model* and *derive the threshold*. Use this matrix as a quick reasoning backbone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of a Statistical Anomaly-Based IDPS

The following block-level Mermaid diagram captures the full functional flow of the system, from raw event ingestion to the prevention action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INGEST["DATA INGESTION LAYER"]
        nodeA1[Network TAP and SPAN Port]
        nodeA2[Host Audit Logs syslog and ETW]
        nodeA3[Application and API Events]
    end

    subgraph NORM["FEATURE NORMALISATION LAYER"]
        nodeB1[Window Aggregator 60s Sliding]
        nodeB2[Feature Extractor CPU Packets Bytes Logins]
        nodeB3[Vector Formatter x t]
    end

    subgraph PROFILE["STATISTICAL PROFILE LAYER BASELINE"]
        nodeC1[Mean Vector mu]
        nodeC2[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
        nodeC3[Histogram Baseline for chi-square]
        nodeC4[Markov Transition Matrix]
    end

    subgraph DETECT["ANOMALY DETECTION LAYER"]
        nodeD1{Z-Score above 3 sigma}
        nodeD2{Mahalanobis D-squared above chi-square threshold}
        nodeD3{Chi-square goodness-of-fit fail}
        nodeD4{Markov transition probability too low}
        nodeD5[Aggregator Maximum Score]
    end

    subgraph RESP["RESPONSE AND PREVENTION LAYER"]
        nodeE1[Alert Generator and SIEM Forward]
        nodeE2[Firewall Block Action]
        nodeE3[Session Termination and Quarantine]
        nodeE4[Profile Update Exponential Moving Average]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeD1
    nodeB3 --> nodeD2
    nodeB3 --> nodeD3
    nodeB3 --> nodeD4
    nodeC1 -. reference .-> nodeD1
    nodeC2 -. reference .-> nodeD2
    nodeC3 -. reference .-> nodeD3
    nodeC4 -. reference .-> nodeD4
    nodeD1 --> nodeD5
    nodeD2 --> nodeD5
    nodeD3 --> nodeD5
    nodeD4 --> nodeD5
    nodeD5 -->|above threshold| nodeE1
    nodeD5 -->|high confidence| nodeE2
    nodeD5 -->|critical score| nodeE3
    nodeD5 -->|under threshold| nodeE4
    nodeE4 --> nodeC1
    nodeE4 --> nodeC2
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Always start at the left (data ingestion) and end at the right (response). The four detector boxes inside DETECT correspond to the four canonical statistical models. Point out the *feedback loop* on the bottom — that's the *adaptive update* feature that distinguishes modern IDPS from naive thresholds.

---

## 4.2 Lifecycle of a Single Detection Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Observation Window Starts]
    stepB[Collect Events]
    stepC[Compute Feature Vector]
    stepD[Read Baseline Profile]
    stepE[Compute Deviation Score]
    stepF{Score above Threshold}
    stepG[Raise Alarm]
    stepH[Trigger Response Action]
    stepI[Update Profile]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF
    stepF -->|Yes| stepG --> stepH --> stepI
    stepF -->|No| stepI
```

> [!NOTE]
> This second diagram is the **answer** to a common 7-mark sub-part: *"With a neat diagram, explain the operational flow of a statistical anomaly IDPS."* Use it verbatim during revision.

---

## 4.3 Threshold Selection Logic (Visual Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is the metric Gaussian?}
    q2{Use Z-score with 3-sigma rule}
    q3{Are features correlated?}
    q4{Use Mahalanobis distance with chi-square threshold}
    q5{Is data sequential?}
    q6{Use Markov transition probability}
    q7{Use simple count threshold}

    q1 -->|Yes| q2
    q1 -->|No| q3
    q3 -->|Yes| q4
    q3 -->|No| q5
    q5 -->|Yes| q6
    q5 -->|No| q7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision tree is a **board-exam favourite**. A 14-mark question often gives you a *scenario* and asks you to *choose and justify* a statistical model. Walk through this tree in your answer to gain full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Each Part A is worth 3 marks. Each Part B question is worth 14 marks with internal choice and sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*"Define a Statistical Anomaly-Based IDPS. How does it differ from a Signature-Based IDPS in principle?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Statistical Anomaly-Based IDPS** is an intrusion detection system that builds a *quantitative profile* of normal network/host behaviour using descriptive statistics (mean, variance, covariance) and flags any activity whose statistical deviation from the profile exceeds a defined threshold.

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Statistical Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Detection basis | Pattern match against known rules | Deviation from learnt profile |
| Zero-day attacks | Not detected | Detected (in principle) |
| False positives | Low | Higher |
| Maintenance | Frequent rule updates | Periodic profile re-training |
| Underlying technique | String / regex matching | Probability / statistics |

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for signature comparison, 1 mark for trade-off articulation.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*"List any four statistical measures used by anomaly-based IDPS and state the purpose of each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mean ($\mu$)** — establishes the *central tendency* (baseline) of a feature. *(0.75 m)*
2. **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** — quantifies the *spread*; used to compute the z-score and choose a threshold. *(0.75 m)*
3. **Mahalanobis Distance ($D^{2}$)** — captures the *joint* deviation of a feature vector from the multivariate profile. *(0.75 m)*
4. **Shannon Entropy ($H(X)$)** — measures *randomness* in payloads, IPs, or system-call traces; useful for detecting covert channels. *(0.75 m)*

*(Full 3 marks if the student also writes a one-line "used for" remark per measure.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Choice 1]**
*(a)* Explain the **Mean and Standard Deviation Model** of a statistical anomaly-based IDPS with its mathematical formulation. Derive the threshold $T$ in terms of $\sigma$ for a target false positive rate of $5\,\%$ on a Gaussian feature. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a suitable example, describe the **Multivariate Model** based on the **Mahalanobis distance**. Explain how it overcomes the limitations of the univariate Mean & Standard Deviation Model. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Conceptual definition (2 m):** The Mean and Standard Deviation Model assumes each feature follows an approximately normal distribution. The IDPS learns $\mu$ and $\sigma$ during a clean training phase. During detection, the live observation $x_t$ is converted into a **z-score**:
$$Z_t = \frac{x_t - \mu}{\sigma}$$
If $\vert Z_t \vert > T$, the system raises an alarm.

2. **Threshold derivation (3 m):** For a Gaussian variable, the false positive rate is the two-tailed tail probability:
$$FPR = 2 \cdot \big[1 - \Phi(T/\sigma)\big]$$
Setting $FPR = 0.05$ gives $\Phi(T/\sigma) = 0.975$, which corresponds to the standard normal quantile $z = 1.96$. Therefore:
$$T = 1.96\,\sigma \approx 2\sigma$$
This is the *practical* 2-σ threshold rule.

3. **Engineering interpretation (2 m):**
- A higher threshold (e.g., 3 σ) reduces false positives but may miss low-volume attacks.
- A lower threshold (e.g., 2 σ) catches more attacks but raises more false positives.
- Operators must tune $T$ to balance their FPR vs Detection-Rate trade-off curve (ROC).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

1. **Why univariate fails (2 m):** The Mean and Standard Deviation Model treats each feature *independently*. In practice, features are correlated — e.g., a legitimate burst in CPU usage is *usually* accompanied by a burst in packets/sec. Two features that are *individually* suspicious may be *jointly* normal.

2. **Mahalanobis formulation (3 m):** The multivariate profile stores the mean vector $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ and the covariance matrix $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$. The deviation of observation $x$ is the **Mahalanobis distance squared**:
$$D^{2} = (x - \mu)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)$$
Under a multivariate normal assumption, $D^{2}$ follows a $\chi^{2}$ distribution with $N$ degrees of freedom. The threshold is therefore:
$$T = \chi^{2}_{N, \alpha}$$
where $\alpha$ is the chosen significance level (e.g., $0.01$).

3. **Advantages (2 m):**
- **Correlation-aware** — automatically normalises for feature inter-dependence.
- **Standardised scale** — output is dimensionless, so multiple feature groups can be combined.
- **Statistical grounding** — built-in probabilistic threshold via $\chi^{2}$ tables.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Choice 2]**
*(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **operational architecture of a statistical anomaly-based IDPS**. Identify the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A network administrator records the number of bytes sent per minute by an internal server over 8 hours (8 data points): $\{120, 135, 110, 145, 125, 130, 140, 115\}$ KB. In the 9th minute, the server sends $x_9 = 250$ KB. Using the **Mean and Standard Deviation Model**, determine whether this 9th observation is anomalous (use threshold $\vert Z \vert > 2$). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Block diagram description (3 m):** The architecture has four layers:
   - **Sensor / Data Collection** — taps packets, reads audit logs, collects flow records.
