   - **Pre-processor / Feature Extraction** — aggregates events into time-windowed feature vectors.
   - **Statistical Engine** — maintains profile (μ, σ, Σ) and computes deviation score in real time.
   - **Decision & Response** — compares score with threshold, raises alert, and (for IDPS) blocks the source.

2. **Role of each block (2 m):**
   - Sensor → ground-truth data.
   - Pre-processor → dimensionality reduction.
   - Statistical Engine → deviation quantification.
   - Response → enforcement and feedback.

3. **Two operating modes (2 m):**
   - **Training mode** — the engine learns μ and σ from clean traffic.
   - **Detection mode** — the engine scores live traffic and updates the profile via exponential moving averages for *concept drift* resistance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

1. **Compute the mean $\mu$ (2 m):**
$$
\mu = \frac{120+135+110+145+125+130+140+115}{8} = \frac{1020}{8} = 127.5 \text{ KB}
$$

2. **Compute the variance $\sigma^{2}$ (2 m):**
Compute each $(x_i - \mu)^{2}$:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - 127.5$ | $(x_i - 127.5)^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 120 | $-7.5$ | $56.25$ |
| 135 | $7.5$ | $56.25$ |
| 110 | $-17.5$ | $306.25$ |
| 145 | $17.5$ | $306.25$ |
| 125 | $-2.5$ | $6.25$ |
| 130 | $2.5$ | $6.25$ |
| 140 | $12.5$ | $156.25$ |
| 115 | $-12.5$ | $156.25$ |
| **Sum** |  | **1050.00** |

$$
\sigma^{2} = \frac{1050}{8} = 131.25, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{131.25} \approx 11.456 \text{ KB}
$$

3. **Compute the z-score for $x_9 = 250$ KB (1 m):**
$$
Z_9 = \frac{250 - 127.5}{11.456} = \frac{122.5}{11.456} \approx 10.69
$$

4. **Decision and interpretation (2 m):**
$$
\vert Z_9 \vert = 10.69 \;\;>\;\; 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{ALARM}
$$
The 9th minute data transfer of 250 KB is over 10 standard deviations above the mean — a strong candidate for an exfiltration or DDoS amplification event, and must be escalated to the SOC for forensic analysis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on Statistical Anomaly IDPS questions (per KTU valuation key):**
>
> 1. **Confusing σ with σ²** — Marks are deducted if a student uses variance where the question asks for standard deviation (or vice-versa). Always state which one you are using.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3-σ rule is a *guideline*, not a theorem** — Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly mentioning that the 3-σ rule is *empirical* and that Chebyshev's inequality is the formal bound.
> 3. **Mixing up Threshold $T$ and z-score** — $T$ is in the *data scale* (KB, packets), $Z$ is dimensionless. State both clearly.
> 4. **Omitting the "Why" behind a model choice** — If asked *why* a multivariate model is preferred over a univariate one, you must say "to capture inter-feature correlation." Writing only the formula costs 2 marks.
> 5. **No block diagram in 7-mark architectural questions** — A textual-only answer for a "with neat diagram" sub-part loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the FPR / TPR trade-off discussion** — This is the *most-tested* KTU learning outcome. End every anomaly-IDPS answer with one line on the FPR–TPR trade-off.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise this before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] A **Statistical Anomaly IDPS** = quantitative baseline + threshold + deviation scoring.
- [ ] The four canonical steps are: **Collect → Profile → Compare → Respond**.
- [ ] **Z-score** is the bedrock of the univariate model; 3-σ rule is the canonical threshold.
- [ ] **Mahalanobis distance** is the bedrock of the multivariate model; threshold comes from $\chi^{2}_{k, \alpha}$.
- [ ] **Chi-square goodness-of-fit** compares observed and expected distributions; $df = k - 1$.
- [ ] **Markov models** capture *transition* probabilities between system states.
- [ ] **Time-series models** flag large *forecast residuals* $e_t = x_t - \hat{x}_t$.
- [ ] **Entropy** quantifies randomness and is excellent for detecting covert channels and encrypted exfiltration.
- [ ] **Operational model** = simple counter threshold; fastest but weakest.
- [ ] **Threshold $T$ for Gaussian FPR $\alpha$** satisfies $T = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \alpha/2) \cdot \sigma$.
- [ ] The **FPR–TPR trade-off** is controlled by $T$: lower $T$ → higher TPR but higher FPR.
- [ ] **Strengths of statistical IDPS:** zero-day detection, no signature DB, quantifiable confidence.
- [ ] **Weaknesses of statistical IDPS:** high FPR on legitimate rare events (concept drift), cannot classify attack *type*.
- [ ] **Covariance matrix $\Sigma$** is the heart of the multivariate model — its *inverse* is used in Mahalanobis.
- [ ] **Exponential moving average** is the standard mechanism to keep profiles current against concept drift.
- [ ] In any 14-mark answer, include (i) formula, (ii) derivation, (iii) example numbers, (iv) one trade-off line, (v) one diagram — to guarantee full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final mnemonic — "C-P-C-R":** *Collect, Profile, Compare, Respond.* If you remember this loop, you can answer *any* statistical anomaly-IDPS question even if a specific formula slips your mind.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stateful Protocol Analysis (SPA) IDPS** is a sophisticated network-based intrusion detection and prevention technique that observes and tracks the state, context, and expected behavior of network protocols (such as TCP, UDP, ICMP, FTP, HTTP, DNS, SMTP, etc.) across all layers of the OSI model. Unlike stateless detection methods, SPA maintains a **state table** (or connection table) of legitimate protocol transactions and compares each observed packet against the expected state of the conversation to determine whether the packet is legitimate or malicious.

The term *Stateful* refers to the ability of the IDPS to **remember** the prior activity in a network communication session and to **understand** the contextual relationship between successive packets. This is fundamentally different from signature matching (which scans content) and anomaly detection (which scans for statistical outliers).

### Key Terminology
- **State Table**: A dynamic, in-memory database maintained by the IDPS that records every active network connection, including source IP, destination IP, source port, destination port, protocol, sequence numbers, flag bits, and current protocol state.
- **Protocol State Machine**: A formal model describing all permissible states and transitions of a protocol conversation. For example, the TCP three-way handshake has states: `CLOSED → SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT → CLOSED`.
- **Legitimate State Path**: A conversation trajectory that follows the expected, vendor-defined protocol state machine. Any deviation is flagged as a potential intrusion.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Customs Officer at an International Airport Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine a highly trained customs officer at an airport who does **not** just glance at the passport photo (signature matching) or notice someone wearing an "unusual" outfit (anomaly detection). Instead, this officer **remembers the entire journey** of every passenger.
> 
> - If a passenger claims to be arriving from Dubai (state = ARRIVED_FROM_DUBAI), but his boarding pass shows a flight from Singapore → that's a **state violation** (protocol anomaly).
> - If someone is found in the "Baggage Claim" area but never went through "Immigration" → the officer knows the conversation is in an illegal state.
> - The officer maintains a mental ledger (state table) of every traveler's current location, expected next stop, and purpose.
> 
> This is exactly how **Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS** works. It maintains a structured ledger of every network session and validates each packet against the **expected sequence of events** for the protocol in use.

### Why SPA is Crucial
Stateful Protocol Analysis can detect sophisticated attacks that escape signature-based systems, such as:
- **Out-of-order TCP flag combinations** (e.g., ACK packet without prior SYN handshake)
- **Protocol misuse** (e.g., using HTTP tunneling to bypass firewalls)
- **Stateful attacks** like the *SYN flood*, *session hijacking*, and *protocol field manipulation*.

---

## Comparison Snapshot (Mandatory for KTU 2024)

| IDPS Type | What it Tracks | Detection Basis | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature-Based** | Packet content/payload | Known attack patterns | High accuracy for known attacks | Zero-day blind |
| **Anomaly-Based** | Statistical baselines | Deviation from "normal" | Detects novel attacks | High false positives |
| **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Protocol state & context | Violation of expected protocol behavior | Catches state-aware attacks | Protocol-specific |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between **Stateful Protocol Analysis** and **Stateful Inspection Firewalls (SPI Firewalls)**. While SPI firewalls focus on packet-level state tracking (layer 3/4), SPA IDPS performs deep protocol-level state tracking across the entire **application layer state machine** and across multiple correlated sessions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of SPA IDPS

Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS operates through a **multi-stage analytical pipeline**. The following bullet points break down the core "Why" and "How" of each stage.

### Stage 1: Packet Capture & Normalization
- **Why:** Raw packets captured from the network span wire (or mirrored port) contain link-layer, network-layer, and transport-layer headers, plus application-layer payloads. The IDPS must first parse and normalize this data into a structured format.
- **How:** A packet decoder (similar to `libpcap` / `tcpdump` engines) extracts the headers and reassembles fragments.

### Stage 2: Session Identification & State Table Population
- **Why:** To evaluate protocol state, the IDPS must first identify *which* session a packet belongs to.
- **How:** A **5-tuple hash key** is computed for every packet:
$$
\text{SessionKey} = H(\text{SrcIP}, \text{DstIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstPort}, \text{Protocol})
$$
  All packets sharing the same 5-tuple belong to the same session and are stored against a single entry in the state table.

### Stage 3: Protocol Decoding & State Machine Validation
- **Why:** The IDPS must understand the precise semantics of the protocol to validate transitions.
- **How:** Each protocol has a **state machine specification** (often based on RFCs). The IDPS compares the current packet's behavior against the **expected next state** for that session.
- If the packet triggers an **illegal transition** (e.g., a data packet received before `ESTABLISHED` state), the alert is raised.

### Stage 4: Contextual Correlation
- **Why:** Some attacks span multiple sessions or appear legitimate in isolation.
- **How:** The IDPS correlates events across sessions, time windows, and hosts. For example, multiple failed FTP login attempts across different user accounts in a short window may indicate brute force, even if each attempt individually looks "legal."

### Stage 5: Alert Generation & Response
- **Why:** Operators need actionable intelligence, not raw packet data.
- **How:** Alerts are prioritized by severity, enriched with session context, and forwarded to the SIEM or response engine (block, drop, reset, log).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Walkthrough: TCP Three-Way Handshake State Tracking

This is a **high-yield KTU question** topic. The IDPS must validate every TCP flag combination against the expected handshake sequence.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_0 &= \text{CLOSED} \\[4pt]
\text{State}_1 &= \text{SYN\_SENT} \quad (\text{Client} \rightarrow \text{SYN} \rightarrow \text{Server}) \\[4pt]
\text{State}_2 &= \text{SYN\_RECEIVED} \quad (\text{Server} \rightarrow \text{SYN+ACK} \rightarrow \text{Client}) \\[4pt]
\text{State}_3 &= \text{ESTABLISHED} \quad (\text{Client} \rightarrow \text{ACK} \rightarrow \text{Server}) \\[4pt]
\text{State}_4 &= \text{FIN\_WAIT\_1} \quad (\text{One side initiates termination}) \\[4pt]
\text{State}_5 &= \text{CLOSED} \quad (\text{All states cleaned from table})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this a high-yield topic?** Attackers frequently send **crafted TCP packets with abnormal flag combinations** such as SYN+FIN, SYN+RST, or unsolicited FIN packets to evade stateful firewalls, perform OS fingerprinting, or trigger covert channel communication. The SPA IDPS detects these by recognizing that the packet's flag pattern does not match the **expected flag pattern for the session's current state**.

---

## 2.3 The State Table Structure

The state table is the **heart** of any SPA IDPS. The following is a representative schema used in production-grade systems:

| Field | Description | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| Session ID | Unique 5-tuple hash | 0xA1B2C3D4 |
| Source IP | Originator's IP | 192.168.1.10 |
| Destination IP | Target's IP | 10.0.0.50 |
| Source Port | Originator's port | 51234 |
| Destination Port | Target's service port | 80 |
| Protocol | L4 protocol | TCP |
| Current State | State machine state | ESTABLISHED |
| Last Seen | Timestamp of last packet | 1701234567.891 |
| Bytes In | Bytes received from source | 1024 |
| Bytes Out | Bytes sent to source | 4096 |
| Seq/Ack Numbers | TCP sequence tracking | Seq=100, Ack=500 |
| Flags Observed | History of flag bits | [SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK] |
| Timeout | Session expiry time | 3600 seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on KTU Formula Sheet convention:** All vertical bar symbols used in mathematical expressions below are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. The same convention applies to absolute value and set notation.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Session Key (5-tuple) | $K = H(\text{SrcIP}, \text{DstIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstPort}, \text{Proto})$ | hash digest | Used for state table lookup |
| Hash Table Lookup | $T_{\text{lookup}} = O(1)$ | operations | Average case constant time |
| Session Timeout | $T_{\text{timeout}} = T_{\text{idle}} + \epsilon$ | seconds | RFC 793 default = **120 s** (TCP) |
| State Table Size (worst case) | $S = N_{\text{active}} \times \vert \text{sizeof}(\text{entry}) \vert$ | bytes | $N_{\text{active}}$ = concurrent sessions |
| Memory Constraint | $M_{\text{required}} \le M_{\text{available}}$ | MB / GB | Limits $N_{\text{active}}$ |
| Expected Next State | $E[s_{t+1}] = \delta(s_t, a_t)$ | state index | From deterministic FSM $\delta$ |
| Alert Trigger Condition | $\text{Alert} \iff (a_t \notin \Sigma(s_t))$ | boolean | Action $a_t$ not in valid input set for state $s_t$ |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ratio | Lower is better |
| True Positive Rate | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | ratio | Higher is better |
| F1 Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | ratio | Harmonic mean of precision/recall |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS is deployed in the following critical engineering scenarios:

1. **Enterprise Network Perimeter Defense** — Detecting protocol misuse and stateful attacks on internet-facing gateways.
2. **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA)** — Validating industrial protocols like Modbus, DNP3, and IEC 60870-5-104 where state deviations indicate malicious commands.
3. **Cloud Workload Protection** — East-west traffic analysis between virtual machines and microservices using stateful HTTP/gRPC state validation.
4. **VoIP/SIP Security** — Tracking SIP call state machines to prevent toll fraud, call hijacking, and INVITE flooding.
5. **DNS Security** — Detecting DNS tunneling, cache poisoning, and amplification attacks by correlating query-response state pairs.
6. **Database Activity Monitoring** — Tracking SQL session states to detect SQL injection at the protocol level (e.g., abnormally long prepared statements).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: False Positive Rate Optimization for State Table Eviction

This derivation explains how an SPA IDPS must balance memory usage against detection accuracy by tuning the session timeout parameter $T_{\text{timeout}}$.

### Step 1: Define the Memory Constraint
The total memory available to the state table is $M_{\text{available}}$ in bytes. Each state table entry consumes $\vert E \vert$ bytes. Therefore, the maximum number of concurrent sessions is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= \frac{M_{\text{available}}}{\vert E \vert} \\[6pt]
\text{Given: } M_{\text{available}} &= 1 \, \text{GB} = 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \, \text{bytes} \\[4pt]
\vert E \vert &= 256 \, \text{bytes} \quad (\text{typical entry size}) \\[4pt]
\therefore N_{\text{max}} &= \frac{1073741824}{256} = 4194304 \text{ sessions}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Model the Session Arrival Rate
Assume sessions arrive according to a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$ sessions per second, and each session persists for a duration drawn from an exponential distribution with mean $\mu$ seconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected concurrent sessions} &= \lambda \cdot \mu \\[4pt]
\text{Constraint: } \lambda \cdot \mu &\le N_{\text{max}} \\[4pt]
\therefore \lambda_{\text{max}} &= \frac{N_{\text{max}}}{\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Link Timeout to Memory Pressure
The eviction policy uses a timeout $T_{\text{timeout}}$. The probability that a session is still active at time $T_{\text{timeout}}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{active at } T_{\text{timeout}}) &= e^{-T_{\text{timeout}} / \mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $T_{\text{timeout}}$ is too short, legitimate long-lived sessions (e.g., persistent HTTP keep-alive) are prematurely evicted, leading to **false positives** when their subsequent packets appear to be out-of-state.

### Step 4: Compute Optimal Timeout
The optimal timeout minimizes the total cost function combining false positive cost and memory cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(T) &= C_{FP} \cdot P(\text{premature eviction}) + C_{M} \cdot P(\text{table full}) \\[6pt]
C_{FP} \cdot e^{-T/\mu} &+ C_M \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T/\mu}\right) = C_{FP} \cdot e^{-T/\mu} + C_M - C_M \cdot e^{-T/\mu} \\[6pt]
&= C_M + (C_{FP} - C_M) \cdot e^{-T/\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $C_{FP}$ is the cost of a false positive and $C_M$ is the cost of memory exhaustion. Taking the derivative with respect to $T$ and setting to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC}{dT} &= -\frac{1}{\mu}(C_{FP} - C_M) \cdot e^{-T/\mu} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The derivative is non-zero in general. Since $e^{-T/\mu} > 0$ always, the cost function is **monotonically decreasing** in $T$ when $C_{FP} > C_M$. Therefore, the optimal strategy is to set $T_{\text{timeout}} = \mu$ (the mean session duration), which is the conventional industry heuristic used by **Snort**, **Suricata**, and **Zeek**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Detected Attack Patterns

The following matrix maps common attacks to the specific **stateful anomaly** that the SPA IDPS detects.

| Attack | Stateful Anomaly Detected | Protocol State Violated |
|---|---|---|
| SYN Flood | Massive half-open connections, no completion | SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED (never reaches ESTABLISHED) |
| Port Scan (SYN) | Many SYN packets from one source, no completion | Multiple SYN_SENT without handshake |
| FIN Scan | Unsolicited FIN to closed ports | FIN in CLOSED state (illegal) |
| Xmas Scan | FIN+PSH+URG flags | Coherent state never permits all three together |
| NULL Scan | Zero flags | Coherent state expects at least one flag |
| Session Hijack | Sequence number discontinuity | Expected Seq/Ack drift outside window |
| DNS Tunneling | Anomalous TXT record size, state mismatches | DNS response format violation |
| FTP Bounce | PORT command for arbitrary host | FTP PORT state validation fails |
| HTTP Request Smuggling | Conflicting Content-Length vs Transfer-Encoding | HTTP state machine conflict |
| SIP INVITE Flood | Excessive INVITE in non-IDLE state | SIP dialog state overflow |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: How an SPA IDPS Evaluates a Single Packet

This walkthrough demonstrates the complete evaluation logic for an inbound TCP packet.

### Given Input
- Source IP: 203.0.113.45
- Destination IP: 10.0.0.50
- Source Port: 54321
- Destination Port: 22 (SSH)
- Flags: ACK
- Seq: 1000, Ack: 2000
- Payload: 256 bytes

### Evaluation Steps

**Step 1: Hash the 5-tuple**
$$
K = H(203.0.113.45, 10.0.0.50, 54321, 22, TCP)
$$
Lookup $K$ in the state table.

**Step 2: Retrieve current state**
- If $K$ not found → **ALERT: Unsolicited packet to SSH on closed session**.
- If $K$ found with state = `ESTABLISHED` → proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3: Validate flag set**
- In `ESTABLISHED` state, the expected flags are typically `ACK`, `PSH+ACK`, or `FIN+ACK`.
- An `ACK` flag alone is permissible. **State transition: remains `ESTABLISHED`.**

**Step 4: Validate sequence numbers**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected next Seq from client} &= \text{LastSeq}_{\text{client}} + \text{LastWindow} \\[4pt]
\text{Window} &= \text{TCP Window field} \\[4pt]
\text{If } \vert \text{Seq} - \text{ExpectedSeq} \vert &> \text{Window} \Rightarrow \text{ALERT: Out-of-window segment}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Update state table**
- Increment `Bytes In` by payload size.
- Update `Last Seen` timestamp.
- Reset idle timer.

**Step 6: Forward verdict**
- Pass packet to the prevention engine (allow, drop, reset, or log) based on the alert decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Steps 1–6 execute in **microseconds** in production systems using optimized hash tables and lock-free data structures. High-throughput SPA IDPS engines (e.g., Suricata) sustain **multi-gigabit per second** throughput on commodity hardware.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: High-Level SPA IDPS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Traffic Span Port] --> B[Packet Capture Engine]
    B --> C[Protocol Decoder Layer 2 to Layer 7]
    C --> D[Session Identifier 5 Tuple Hash]
    D --> E{State Table Lookup}
    E -->|Match Found| F[Retrieve Current State]
    E -->|No Match| G[Create New State Entry]
    G --> H[State Machine Validator]
    F --> H
    H --> I{Valid State Transition}
    I -->|Yes| J[Update State Table]
    I -->|No| K[Generate Alert]
    J --> L[Forward Packet]
    K --> M[Prevention Engine]
    M --> N[Action Block Drop Reset Log]
    L --> O[SIEM and Logging]
    M --> O
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **Session Identifier** computes a 5-tuple hash for every packet.
- The **State Machine Validator** is the brain of the SPA IDPS, comparing the current packet against the protocol's expected state transition.
- The **Prevention Engine** executes the response policy (block/drop/reset/log).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: TCP Three-Way Handshake State Tracking

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT: Client sends SYN
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RECEIVED: Server replies SYN+ACK
    SYN_RECEIVED --> ESTABLISHED: Client replies ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1: One side initiates close
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2: Other side acknowledges
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT: Other side sends FIN
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED: 2MSL timer expires
    ESTABLISHED --> RST: Abnormal termination
    SYN_SENT --> RST: Connection refused
    note right of SYN_RECEIVED: SPA IDPS tracks this transition\nSYN flood detected if transition\nnot completed within timeout
    note right of RST: Unsolicited RST triggers\nSPA alert
```

**Engineering Note:** The diagram includes **state annotations** showing where SPA IDPS performs its security checks. The two `note` blocks highlight the most common attack detection points.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: End-to-End Detection Workflow for SYN Flood

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Attacker sends 1000 SYN packets per second] --> P2[SPA IDPS state table fills with half open connections]
    P2 --> P3{SYN+ACK sent by server but no ACK from client}
    P3 -->|Yes for many sessions| P4[SPA IDPS flags half open anomaly]
    P4 --> P5[Threshold exceeded 500 half open from same source]
    P5 --> P6[Alert generated and source IP blocked]
    P6 --> P7[Prevention action rate limit or drop]
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram illustrates how a **stateless** signature matcher would miss a SYN flood (it is just a flood of "normal-looking" SYNs). Only a **stateful** analyzer that tracks the absence of the expected follow-up `ACK` can flag this as anomalous.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stateless IDPS
        S1[Packet] --> S2[Pattern Match against Signatures]
        S2 --> S3{Match Found}
        S3 -->|Yes| S4[Alert]
        S3 -->|No| S5[Pass]
    end
    subgraph Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS
        T1[Packet] --> T2[Decode Protocol Headers]
        T2 --> T3[5 Tuple Hash Lookup]
        T3 --> T4{State Table Entry}
        T4 -->|Yes| T5[Validate against FSM]
        T4 -->|No| T6[Create Entry and Validate]
        T5 --> T7{Valid Transition}
        T6 --> T7
        T7 -->|Yes| T8[Update and Pass]
        T7 -->|No| T9[Alert and Prevent]
    end
```

This block diagram visually contrasts the **single-step** logic of stateless IDPS with the **multi-step stateful** logic of SPA IDPS, emphasizing that SPA is fundamentally context-aware.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*Define Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS. Mention two key differences from signature-based IDPS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS is an intrusion detection technique that monitors the state and context of network protocol communications by maintaining a state table of all active sessions and comparing each observed packet against the expected state transitions of the protocol's state machine.

**Two key differences from signature-based IDPS:**
1. **Detection Basis:** SPA detects attacks by recognizing **violations of expected protocol behavior**, whereas signature-based IDPS detects attacks by **matching packet content against known attack patterns**.
2. **Memory Model:** SPA requires **stateful context** (a state table), whereas signature-based IDPS performs **stateless** per-packet inspection and does not track session context.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differences — distribution as per KTU 2024 valuation key.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
*What is a state table in an SPA IDPS? List any four fields stored in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A state table is an in-memory data structure maintained by an SPA IDPS that records the current protocol state, metadata, and history of every active network session. It is indexed by a 5-tuple hash key for O(1) lookup.

**Four fields:**
1. Source IP address
2. Destination IP address
3. Source port
4. Destination port
5. *(Bonus: Current protocol state, sequence numbers, last seen timestamp)*

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for fields.)*

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse Stateful Protocol Analysis with Stateful Inspection Firewalls (SPI firewalls)**. Marks are deducted if the answer mixes the two. SPI firewalls operate mainly at Layer 3/4, while SPA IDPS performs deep protocol state machine validation across all layers including the application layer.
> - Students frequently **forget to mention the state table**, treating SPA as merely "pattern matching with context." Always explicitly mention the **state table** and the **protocol state machine**.
> - In TCP-related questions, students often **miss the flag validation logic**. Always state the expected flag set for each TCP state explicitly.
> - Avoid one-word answers for 14-mark questions. The KTU 2024 valuation scheme awards marks for: (a) correct technical terminology, (b) diagrams/flowcharts, (c) examples, and (d) comparative analysis.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the state table and the protocol state machine in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how SPA IDPS detects a TCP SYN Flood attack. Describe the state transitions observed during the attack and the prevention action triggered. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

The architecture of an SPA IDPS consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Packet Capture Engine:** Captures raw packets from a SPAN port or TAP device on the network.
2. **Protocol Decoder:** Parses packet headers from Layer 2 through Layer 7, extracting IP addresses, ports, flags, and payload.
3. **Session Identifier:** Computes a 5-tuple hash key for each packet to identify the session.
4. **State Table:** A hash-indexed in-memory database storing all active sessions with their current state, metadata, and timeout information.
5. **State Machine Validator:** The core reasoning engine. For each protocol (TCP, UDP, HTTP, DNS, etc.), it maintains a formal finite state machine and validates whether the observed packet triggers a legal transition.
6. **Alert Generator:** Produces alerts when illegal transitions, out-of-window segments, or protocol misuse is detected.
7. **Prevention Engine:** Executes response actions: block, drop, reset connection, rate-limit, or log.

**Role of the State Table:**
The state table is the **persistent memory** of the SPA IDPS. Without it, the system cannot correlate current packets with historical context. The state table enables O(1) session lookup via hashing, supports session aging via timeouts, and stores all metadata required for state machine validation.

**Role of the Protocol State Machine:**
The state machine is the **rulebook** of the SPA IDPS. It encodes the legitimate state transitions of each protocol based on its RFC specification. For example, the TCP state machine has 11 states defined in RFC 793. The validator uses the state machine as a reference: if the observed packet would cause a transition not present in the machine, an alert is raised.

*(Awarded marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for state table/state machine explanation, 2 for example.)*

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

A **TCP SYN Flood** is a Denial-of-Service attack where the attacker sends a huge volume of SYN packets to the target server but never completes the three-way handshake by sending the final ACK.

**Normal State Transitions:**
$$
\text{CLOSED} \rightarrow \text{SYN\_SENT} \rightarrow \text{SYN\_RECEIVED} \rightarrow \text{ESTABLISHED}
$$

**Attack State Observation:**
The SPA IDPS observes the following pattern:
1. The target server receives many SYN packets (state transitions to `SYN_RECEIVED`).
2. The server sends SYN+ACK replies to the attacker.
3. The attacker never responds with the final ACK.
4. The state table fills with many **half-open** connections stuck in `SYN_RECEIVED`.

**Detection Logic:**
- The IDPS maintains a counter of `SYN_RECEIVED` sessions per source IP.
- If this counter exceeds a threshold (e.g., 500 half-open connections from one IP in 60 seconds), the **stateful anomaly** is triggered.
- The state machine validator recognizes that these sessions are **not progressing** to `ESTABLISHED`, indicating a stateful deviation.

**Prevention Action:**
- The prevention engine **drops further SYN packets** from the offending source IP (rate-limit or block).
- The IDPS may also send **TCP RST** to the server to forcibly close the half-open sessions, freeing server resources.
- An alert is forwarded to the SIEM with severity = HIGH.

*(Awarded marks: 2 for SYN flood explanation, 3 for state transition analysis, 2 for detection logic and prevention.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast Signature-based IDPS, Anomaly-based IDPS, and Stateful Protocol Analysis IDPS. Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain in detail how the SPA IDPS validates the TCP three-way handshake. Show all the state transitions and the expected flag combinations. Mention two attacks that can be detected by flag validation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Basis** | Pattern match against known attack signatures | Deviation from statistical baseline | Violation of expected protocol state transitions |
| **Data Model** | Stateless (per-packet) | Statistical profile (e.g., mean, std dev) | Stateful (state table + FSM) |
| **Memory Required** | Low (signature database) | Medium (baseline profile) | High (per-session state) |
| **Zero-Day Detection** | Poor | Good | Moderate |
| **False Positive Rate** | Very Low | High | Low to Moderate |
| **Performance** | Fast (single-pass matching) | Moderate | Moderate (multi-step validation) |
| **Strength** | High accuracy on known attacks | Detects novel/unknown attacks | Detects stateful, protocol-aware attacks |
| **Weakness** | Cannot detect zero-day attacks | High false positive rate | Resource-intensive, protocol-specific |

**Engineering Conclusion:** Modern IDPS deployments (e.g., Snort, Suricata, Zeek) combine all three techniques in a **layered defense** model.

*(Awarded marks: 4 for comparison table, 2 for strengths, 1 for conclusion.)*

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

The **TCP three-way handshake** establishes a reliable connection between a client and a server. The SPA IDPS must validate every packet against the expected state transitions.

**State Transitions and Expected Flags:**

| Step | Direction | Flags | State Transition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client → Server | SYN | CLOSED → SYN_SENT | Client requests connection |
| 2 | Server → Client | SYN + ACK | SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED | Server acknowledges and synchronizes |
| 3 | Client → Server | ACK | SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED | Connection established |

**Validation Logic in SPA IDPS:**

The IDPS rejects packets that violate the expected flag pattern. For example:
- A **packet with FIN flag** in the `CLOSED` state → rejected (illegal state transition).
- A **packet with RST flag** in the `ESTABLISHED` state without prior FIN handshake → flagged as abnormal termination.
- An **ACK packet** received without a prior `SYN` → rejected (no session exists).

**Two Attacks Detected by Flag Validation:**

1. **FIN Scan (Stealth Port Scan):** The attacker sends a FIN packet to a closed port. A normal server responds with RST. The SPA IDPS, however, notes that the FIN flag is being used in a context where no established session exists, indicating reconnaissance activity.

2. **Christmas Tree (Xmas) Scan:** The attacker sends a packet with FIN, PSH, and URG flags all set. The state machine validator recognizes that this flag combination is **never valid** in any TCP state, immediately flagging it as a port scan attempt.

**Bonus Attack — Session Hijacking:** Sequence number validation in the `ESTABLISHED` state detects hijacking attempts when the sequence numbers fall outside the expected TCP window.

*(Awarded marks: 2 for state transition table, 3 for validation logic, 2 for attack examples.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for SPA IDPS:**

- **Definition:** SPA IDPS tracks protocol state, context, and conversation history to detect attacks that violate expected protocol behavior.
- **State Table:** In-memory hash-indexed database of all active sessions, keyed by 5-tuple.
- **Protocol State Machine:** Formal FSM (per RFC) defining all legal state transitions; the validator uses this as the rulebook.
- **Three IDPS Categories:** Signature-based (stateless, content match), Anomaly-based (statistical), Stateful Protocol Analysis (context-aware).
- **TCP States (RFC 793):** CLOSED, LISTEN, SYN_SENT, SYN_RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN_WAIT_1, FIN_WAIT_2, CLOSE_WAIT, CLOSING, LAST_ACK, TIME_WAIT.
- **Key Flag Validation Rules:**
  - SYN alone → only valid in CLOSED → SYN_SENT transition.
  - SYN+ACK → only valid in SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED.
  - ACK → valid from SYN_RECEIVED onwards.
  - FIN/RST → only valid in ESTABLISHED or post-ESTABLISHED states.
- **Major Attacks Detected by SPA:**
  - SYN Flood (half-open connections never reaching ESTABLISHED).
  - FIN Scan, Xmas Scan, NULL Scan (illegal flag combinations).
  - Session Hijacking (sequence number out-of-window).
  - DNS Tunneling (anomalous record size and state mismatches).
  - HTTP Request Smuggling (Content-Length vs Transfer-Encoding conflict).
- **Advantages over Signature-Based:** Can detect novel attacks based on protocol misuse, not just content patterns.
- **Limitations:** Memory-intensive (state table growth), protocol-specific (needs decoder for each protocol), and not effective against pure application-layer semantic attacks.
- **Production Tools Implementing SPA:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (formerly Bro), Cisco Secure IPS.
- **Formula to Remember:** SessionKey = Hash(SrcIP, DstIP, SrcPort, DstPort, Protocol); Lookup time O(1).
- **Key Distinction for KTU 2024:** SPA IDPS ≠ SPI Firewall. SPA performs deep protocol FSM validation; SPI only tracks connection state at Layer 3/4.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the **state transition diagram** in TCP-related questions. KTU examiners award 2 marks for a correct diagram even if the textual explanation is partial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Log File Monitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Log File Monitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Log File Monitor (LFM)** is a specialized class of Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS) component (or a stand-alone network forensics tool) that continuously or periodically inspects log records generated by operating systems, applications, services, and network devices to identify security-relevant events, policy violations, suspicious patterns, and indicators of compromise (IoCs). It functions as a **passive, post-event correlation engine** that uses pattern matching, regular expressions, threshold rules, and statistical heuristics to flag anomalies from otherwise voluminous textual log streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Log File Monitors are categorized under **"Detection Mechanisms / Sensors"** in Module 1. They form the **evidence-collection backbone** of any Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) architecture.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic directly maps to:
- **CO1:** *Understand the fundamental architectures and detection techniques used in modern Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems.*
- **CO2:** *Apply log analysis and correlation techniques to identify security incidents.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **CCTV security control room** in a large shopping mall:
- The **cameras** are constantly recording everything (analogous to the OS and applications generating logs).
- The raw recordings are stored in **massive DVR tapes** (analogous to log files on disk).
- A **security guard** sits in front of the monitors, but cannot watch 24/7 footage of 500 cameras simultaneously.
- Instead, the guard uses a **smart video analytics system** that scans the recordings and shouts out: *"Person loitering near ATM for 30 minutes!"* or *"Same car plate spotted at three different entrances!"*

A **Log File Monitor is exactly that smart analytics system**, but for text-based log files instead of video. It reads millions of log lines so you don't have to, and screams when something suspicious appears.

## 1.3 What Are "Log Files"?

A **log file** is a chronological, append-only data record produced by software to record events, transactions, errors, or informational messages. Standard sources include:

| Log Source | Typical Location (Linux) | Typical Location (Windows) | Content Type |
|------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|--------------|
| **System Log** | `/var/log/syslog` | Event Viewer → System | Kernel, daemon, OS-level events |
| **Authentication Log** | `/var/log/auth.log` | Security Event Log (EVT/EVTX) | Login attempts, sudo, SSH |
| **Web Server Log** | `/var/log/apache2/access.log` | `C:\inetpub\logs\` | HTTP requests, status codes |
| **Application Log** | `/var/log/app/*.log` | App-specific directory | Application errors, transactions |
| **Firewall Log** | `/var/log/ufw.log`, `/var/log/pf.log` | Windows Firewall log | Allowed/blocked connections |
| **Database Log** | `/var/log/mysql/*.log` | SQL Server Error Log | Queries, errors, slow queries |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Log Format (RFC 5424 - Syslog):** Most log file monitors parse logs conforming to **`<PRI>TIMESTAMP HOSTNAME APP-NAME PROCID MSGID MESSAGE>`**. For example: `<34>Oct 11 22:14:15 myserver sshd[1234]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.10`.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Terminology

Key metrics used by log file monitors (commonly appearing in KTU numerical/short-answer questions):

- **EPS (Events Per Second):** Throughput measure; enterprise systems handle **10,000+ EPS**.
- **Log Retention Period:** Typically **30–90 days** for hot storage, **1–7 years** for cold/archive storage.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Ratio of benign events wrongly flagged.
- **Detection Latency:** Time between event occurrence and alert generation (**goal: < 5 seconds** for real-time monitors).
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond):** Average time from alert to remediation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Log File Monitor

A Log File Monitor operates through a structured pipeline. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU Module 1 questions.

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

1. **Log Collection / Ingestion**
   - *Why:* Logs are scattered across files, hosts, and formats.
   - *How:* Agents (e.g., **Syslog-ng, Winlogbeat, NXLog, Fluentd**) ship logs to a central repository over **UDP 514 (legacy syslog)**, **TCP 514**, or **TLS 6514 (secure syslog)**.

2. **Normalization (Parsing)**
   - *Why:* Different devices produce different formats (Cisco, Linux, Apache, Windows).
   - *How:* Each log line is decomposed into fields: `<timestamp, source_ip, username, action, status, raw_message>` using **GROK patterns, regex, or built-in parsers**.

3. **Storage / Indexing**
   - *Why:* Logs must be searchable in real-time.
   - *How:* Stored in **time-series databases** or inverted-index engines like **Elasticsearch, Splunk Index, or OpenSearch**.

4. **Detection / Correlation Engine**
   - *Why:* Single events are noise; patterns are signals.
   - *How:* Applies detection rules (signatures, thresholds, statistical models).

5. **Alerting / Response**
   - *Why:* The defender must be notified (or automated response triggered).
   - *How:* Sends alerts via **email, SMS, Slack, PagerDuty**, or executes automated blocks via firewall APIs.

## 2.2 Detection Techniques Used Inside Log Monitors

| Technique | Mechanism | Example Rule | Limitation |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|
| **Signature / Pattern Matching** | Regex match against known-bad strings | `/Failed password.*for root/` | Zero-day blind |
| **Threshold Detection** | Count of events in a time window exceeds a value | `>5 failed logins in 60s from one IP` | Misses slow attacks |
| **Correlation / Sequencing** | Combine multiple weak events into one strong alert | `Failed login → success from different IP` (credential stuffing) | Complex to author |
| **Anomaly / Statistical Detection** | Deviation from baseline | Login at 3 AM when user normally logs in at 9 AM | High FPR |
| **Whitelisting / Baselining** | Alert on anything NOT in the known-good list | First-time execution of `powershell.exe` | Maintenance heavy |

## 2.3 Classic Log File Monitor Tools (KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically references early Linux-based log monitors. Memorize these:

- **Swatch (Simple WATCHer)** – Perl-based, watches a single log file using regex rules. One of the **earliest open-source LFM** (1998).
- **SEC (Simple Event Correlator)** – Rule-based engine by **Vadim Zaliva**; supports **event correlation**, suppression, and pairing (e.g., pair a login failure with its later success).
- **Logwatch** – Periodic (cron-driven) summary reporter; not real-time.
- **OSSEC (Open Source Security)** – Full-fledged **HIDS with built-in log monitoring**, file integrity, and rootkit detection.
- **Splunk** – Enterprise-grade **SIEM** built around indexed log search.
- **ELK / OpenSearch Stack** – **Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana**, the de-facto open-source SIEM.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since Log File Monitors are more procedural than mathematical, the "formulas" here are detection-rule equations and capacity-planning relations expected in KTU exams.

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---------|-----------------|-------------|------|
| **Events Per Second (EPS)** | $EPS = \dfrac{Total\ Events}{Time\ Window\ (s)}$ | Throughput of a monitor | events/s |
| **Daily Log Volume** | $Volume = EPS \times 86400 \times Avg\ Size$ | Storage planning | MB/day |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Quality of detection rule | ratio (0–1) |
| **Detection Precision** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Rule reliability | ratio (0–1) |
| **Detection Recall** | $Recall = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of true attacks | ratio (0–1) |
| **F1 Score** | $F_1 = 2 \times \dfrac{Precision \times Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Balanced detection metric | ratio (0–1) |
| **Threshold Rule** | $Alert\ if\ count(event,\ \Delta t) > T$ | Time-window thresholding | integer |
| **Syslog Severity** | $Severity \in \{0,1,2,\dots,7\}$ | 0=emergency, 7=debug | integer |
| **Syslog Facility × 8 + Severity** | $PRI = Facility \times 8 + Severity$ | Computes the PRI header value | integer |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Log file monitors are deployed universally:
- **Banks:** PCI-DSS mandates log monitoring of all card-data access.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA requires audit logs for every PHI access.
- **Cloud-native (2024 context):** **AWS CloudWatch**, **Azure Monitor**, **Google Cloud Logging** are all log file monitors at hyperscale.
- **OT/ICS:** SCADA systems log Modbus/DNP3 traffic; monitors detect unauthorized PLC commands.
- **DevSecOps:** CI/CD pipeline logs monitored for secret leaks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Syslog PRI Value Calculation

The Syslog header `<PRI>` is a numerical value derived from the **Facility** and **Severity** codes. KTU frequently tests this conversion.

### Worked Example 1: Compute the PRI value for an "auth.crit" message

**Given:**
- Facility = `auth` (in RFC 5424 numeric mapping, Facility 4)
- Severity = `critical` (numeric level 2)

**Step 1:** Recall the formula.

The Priority Value (PRI) is calculated by multiplying the Facility code by 8 and adding the Severity code.

$$PRI = (Facility \times 8) + Severity$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numeric values into the formula. Facility = 4 and Severity = 2.

$$PRI = (4 \times 8) + 2$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication step.

$$PRI = 32 + 2$$

**Step 4:** Sum the two values to obtain the final result.

$$PRI = 34$$

**Step 5:** Convert the decimal integer into its octal (base-8) equivalent, because RFC 5424 mandates the PRI field be encoded inside angle brackets as an octal number, e.g., `<34>`.

The decimal value 34 in octal representation is computed by successive division by 8.

$$\begin{aligned}
34 \div 8 &= 4 \text{ remainder } 2 \\
4 \div 8 &= 0 \text{ remainder } 4
\end{aligned}$$

Reading remainders from bottom to top gives the octal digits as 4 and 2.

$$34_{10} = 42_{8}$$

Therefore, the formatted syslog PRI header is enclosed in angle brackets as shown below.

$$\text{PRI Header} = \langle 42 \rangle$$

**Final Answer:** An `auth.crit` syslog message starts with `<42>` in its header.

### Worked Example 2: Decode a Given PRI Value

**Given:** A syslog line begins with `<165>`.

**Step 1:** Convert the octal number to decimal. The digits are 1, 6, and 5.

$$165_{8} = (1 \times 8^{2}) + (6 \times 8^{1}) + (5 \times 8^{0})$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate each power of 8 and substitute.

$$165_{8} = (1 \times 64) + (6 \times 8) + (5 \times 1)$$

**Step 3:** Multiply and then sum the three terms together.

$$165_{8} = 64 + 48 + 5$$

$$165_{8} = 117_{10}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by 8 to recover the Facility code as the integer quotient.

$$Facility = \left\lfloor \dfrac{117}{8} \right\rfloor = 14$$

**Step 5:** Take the remainder when divided by 8 to recover the Severity code.

$$Severity = 117 \bmod 8 = 5$$

**Step 6:** Map the numeric codes back to their textual names per RFC 5424. Facility 14 maps to `log audit` and Severity 5 maps to `notice`.

$$Facility = 14 \rightarrow \text{log audit}, \quad Severity = 5 \rightarrow \text{notice}$$

**Final Answer:** The syslog message originates from the `log audit` facility at `notice` severity.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Log Monitor Detection Methods

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU comparison table** often asked as a "Compare and Contrast" 7-mark sub-question.

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Threshold-Based | Anomaly-Based | Correlation-Based |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Detection Basis** | Known patterns | Frequency of events | Deviation from baseline | Multi-event sequences |
| **Example** | Regex for malware string | 5 failed logins / 60 s | Login at unusual hour | Failed log → success (different geo) |
| **False Positive Rate** | Very Low | Low | High | Medium |
| **False Negative Rate** | High (zero-day) | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Resource Usage** | Low | Low | High (ML/statistics) | Medium–High |
| **Setup Complexity** | Low | Low | High | High |
| **Best Suited For** | Known attacks | Brute force, DoS | Insider threats | Multi-stage APTs |
| **Tool Example** | Swatch rules | OSSEC threshold.xml | Splunk ML Toolkit | SEC, Splunk correlation searches |

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Compute Storage Requirements

**Problem:** A web server generates **2 KB per log entry**, and on average sustains **150 EPS**. How much disk space is consumed in **24 hours**?

**Step 1:** Recall the storage formula relating EPS, size per event, and time window.

$$Storage = EPS \times Avg\ Size \times Time$$

**Step 2:** Convert all units to a common base. KB must be converted to MB by dividing by 1024.

$$Avg\ Size = 2\ KB = \dfrac{2}{1024}\ MB \approx 0.001953\ MB$$

**Step 3:** Compute the total time window in seconds for 24 hours.

$$Time = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86400\ s$$

**Step 4:** Substitute all three values into the formula.

$$Storage = 150 \times 0.001953 \times 86400$$

**Step 5:** Perform the multiplication. First, multiply the rate by the size per entry.

$$150 \times 0.001953 = 0.29297\ MB/s$$

**Step 6:** Multiply the result by the total time in seconds.

$$Storage = 0.29297 \times 86400$$

$$Storage \approx 25313\ MB$$

**Step 7:** Convert MB to GB by dividing by 1024 to obtain a more practical storage unit.

$$Storage \approx \dfrac{25313}{1024} \approx 24.72\ GB$$

**Final Answer:** The web server will consume approximately **24.72 GB of disk per day**. Over 30 days, that is **≈ 741 GB** — justifying why enterprises use compressed, indexed storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Log File Monitor System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete data-flow architecture of a typical enterprise Log File Monitor integrated into a SIEM pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[LOG SOURCES]
        A1[Linux Syslog]
        A2[Windows Event Log]
        A3[Apache Access Log]
        A4[Firewall Logs]
        A5[Application Logs]
    end

    subgraph B[COLLECTION LAYER]
        B1[Syslog-ng Agent]
        B2[Winlogbeat Agent]
        B3[Fluentd / NXLog]
    end

    subgraph C[INGESTION AND PARSING]
        C1[Log Receiver TCP 514]
        C2[GROK Pattern Parser]
        C3[Field Normalizer]
    end

    subgraph D[STORAGE AND INDEXING]
        D1[Elasticsearch Index]
        D2[Hot Storage SSD]
        D3[Cold Storage S3]
    end

    subgraph E[DETECTION ENGINE]
        E1[Signature Rules]
        E2[Threshold Counter]
        E3[Correlation Rules]
        E4[ML Anomaly Model]
    end

    subgraph F[ALERTING AND RESPONSE]
        F1[Alert Manager]
        F2[Email / SMS]
        F3[SOAR Playbook Trigger]
        F4[Dashboard Kibana]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B3

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1

    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3

    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E2
    D1 --> E3
    D1 --> E4

    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1

    F1 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F1 --> F4
```

## 4.2 Workflow Diagram: How a Log File Monitor Detects an Attack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Attacker attempts SSH brute force] --> S2[Linux sshd writes to auth.log]
    S2 --> S3[Syslog-ng ships line to central server]
    S3 --> S4[Logstash GROK parses into fields]
    S4 --> S5{Threshold rule: count failures by IP in 60s window}
    S5 -- count <= 5 --> S6[No alert - event stored only]
    S5 -- count > 5 --> S7[Generate alert with severity HIGH]
    S7 --> S8[Trigger SOAR playbook]
    S8 --> S9[Block source IP at firewall via API]
    S8 --> S10[Notify SOC analyst via Slack]
    S8 --> S11[Create ticket in ServiceNow]
```

## 4.3 Component Hierarchy of SEC / Swatch Rule Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Rule Definition File swatchrc or sec.conf]
    R --> R1[Pattern - regex to match]
    R --> R2[Action - what to do on match]

    R --> R5[Correlation - link with other events]

    R1 --> E[Rule Evaluation Engine]
    R2 --> E
    R3 --> E
    R4 --> E
    R5 --> E

    E --> O1[Email Alert]
    E --> O2[Execute Script]
    E --> O3[Pipe to Another Tool]
    E --> O4[Write to Alert Log]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Answer both questions in approximately 60–90 words each.**

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a Log File Monitor. List any two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Log File Monitor (LFM)** is a passive security mechanism that continuously inspects log records produced by operating systems, applications, and devices to detect suspicious activities using pattern matching, thresholds, or correlation rules.

*Advantages:*
1. **Low cost** — leverages already-existing log data without deploying additional network sensors.
2. **High evidentiary value** — provides forensic trail for post-incident investigation.

*Limitation:*
- It is **reactive (post-event)**: damage may already be done by the time an alert is generated.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between Swatch and SEC as log monitoring tools.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Swatch | SEC |
|---------|--------|-----|
| Initial Release | 1998 | 1999 |
| Language | Perl | C / Perl |
| Correlation | No (single-event) | Yes (multi-event) |
| Output | Email / execute command | Multiple actions, can pipe events |
| Configuration | Simple regex rules | Powerful rule DSL with contexts |

**Conclusion:** SEC is more powerful and scalable, while Swatch is suitable for small, single-host deployments.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1]

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Log File Monitor**. List and briefly describe any **four stages** of log processing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Log File Monitor operates as a pipeline that ingests raw textual logs and transforms them into actionable security alerts. The major stages are illustrated below.

**Stage 1 — Log Collection / Ingestion:**
Logs are generated at distributed sources (Linux syslog, Windows Event Logs, Apache access logs, firewall logs). Lightweight **agents** such as **Syslog-ng, NXLog, Winlogbeat, or Fluentd** collect and forward them to a central server typically over **UDP 514, TCP 514, or secure TLS 6514**. Without this stage, logs remain isolated and uncorrelated.

**Stage 2 — Normalization / Parsing:**
Different sources produce heterogeneous formats. A **GROK pattern** or **regex** decomposes each line into structured fields: `<timestamp, source_ip, username, action, status>`. This converts unreadable text into queryable data.

**Stage 3 — Storage and Indexing:**
Parsed events are written into a **time-series or inverted-index database** (e.g., Elasticsearch, Splunk Index). Indexing allows **sub-second search** across billions of historical events.

**Stage 4 — Detection / Correlation Engine:**
Rules are applied to detect threats:
- **Signature rules** match known malicious strings (regex on log content).
- **Threshold rules** trigger when event count exceeds a defined limit in a time window.
- **Correlation rules** link multiple weaker events into a stronger alert (e.g., failed-login followed by success from a different IP).
- **Anomaly / ML models** flag statistical deviations from baseline.

**Stage 5 — Alerting and Response:**
Generated alerts are dispatched to security analysts via email, SMS, Slack, or SOAR playbooks. High-severity alerts may trigger **automated firewall blocks** via API integration.

*(Block diagram already shown in Section 4.1 should be drawn on the answer sheet.)*

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the **different detection techniques** used by log file monitors. Compare **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** detection in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Log File Monitors employ four primary detection techniques:

**1. Signature / Pattern-Based Detection:**
The monitor searches each log line for known malicious strings using **regular expressions**. Example: a rule matching `/Failed password.*for root/` flags brute-force attempts against the root account. This method is **fast, deterministic, and has very low false positives**, but it is **blind to zero-day attacks** that lack a known signature.

**2. Threshold-Based Detection:**
The monitor counts occurrences of an event within a sliding time window and alerts when the count exceeds a threshold. Example: alert if `count(failed_login, IP, 60s) > 5`. Effective against brute force and DoS attacks but **misses slow-and-low attacks** that stay below the threshold.

**3. Correlation-Based Detection:**
Multiple low-severity events are linked temporally to produce a single high-severity alert. Example: a failed login from India followed by a successful login from Russia within 10 minutes → **credential-theft correlation**. Powerful against **multi-stage APTs**, but requires skilled rule authoring.

**4. Anomaly / Statistical Detection:**
A baseline of normal behaviour is built (e.g., user login hour distribution). Events deviating from the baseline are flagged. Detects **zero-day and insider threats** but suffers from **high false-positive rates** during initial baselining.

**Comparison: Signature vs. Anomaly-Based Detection**

| Parameter | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| Detection Model | Known-bad patterns | Deviation from baseline |
| Zero-Day Coverage | Poor | Strong |
| False Positive Rate | Very Low | High |
| Maintenance | Frequent rule updates | Periodic baseline retraining |
| Computational Cost | Low | High (statistical / ML) |
| Example Tool | Swatch regex rules | Splunk ML Toolkit |

**Conclusion:** Modern SIEMs combine both — signatures for known threats and anomaly detection for unknown threats — to maximize coverage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** Describe the **Syslog protocol** as used by log file monitors. Compute the **PRI value** for an `auth.error` message and decode the PRI value `<165>`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Syslog Protocol (RFC 5424):**
Syslog is the de-facto standard protocol for log message transport in Unix/Linux environments. A syslog message has the format:

$$Message = \langle PRI \rangle VERSION\ TIMESTAMP\ HOSTNAME\ APP-NAME\ PROCID\ MSGID\ SD\ MESSAGE$$

The **PRI (Priority)** field is an integer between 0 and 191 that combines two values: the **Facility** (source category) and the **Severity** (urgency).

**Facility Codes (selected):**
`kern=0, user=1, mail=2, daemon=3, auth=4, syslog=5, lpr=6, news=7, uucp=8, cron=9, authpriv=10, ftp=11, …, local0=16, …, local7=23`

**Severity Codes:**
`emerg=0, alert=1, crit=2, err=3, warning=4, notice=5, info=6, debug=7`

**Computation:**
The PRI is calculated as Facility multiplied by 8 plus Severity.

$$PRI = (Facility \times 8) + Severity$$

The resulting integer is then converted to **octal** and wrapped in angle brackets `< >`.

**Compute PRI for `auth.error`:**
- Facility `auth` = 4
- Severity `error` = 3

Substituting into the formula gives the decimal PRI.

$$PRI = (4 \times 8) + 3 = 35$$

Converting decimal 35 to octal by successive division yields octal 43.

$$35_{10} = 43_{8}$$

Therefore, the formatted PRI header is `<43>`.

**Decode PRI `<165>`:**
Converting octal 165 to decimal gives 117.

$$165_{8} = (1 \times 64) + (6 \times 8) + (5 \times 1) = 117_{10}$$

Dividing 117 by 8 gives a quotient of 14 (Facility) and a remainder of 5 (Severity).

$$Facility = 14 \rightarrow \text{log audit}, \quad Severity = 5 \rightarrow \text{notice}$$

**Final Answer:** `<165>` corresponds to facility **log audit** at severity **notice**.

---

**Q.B (b)** A server generates **200 EPS** with an average **log entry size of 1.5 KB**. Compute the **(i)** daily log volume in GB, and **(ii)** the number of entries in a **5-minute window**. Comment on the **storage and indexing strategy** required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Daily Log Volume:**

$$Storage = EPS \times Avg\ Size \times Time$$

Convert 1.5 KB to MB.

$$1.5\ KB = \dfrac{1.5}{1024}\ MB \approx 0.0014648\ MB$$

Compute the time in seconds for 24 hours.

$$Time = 24 \times 3600 = 86400\ s$$

Substitute into the formula.

$$Storage = 200 \times 0.0014648 \times 86400$$

$$Storage \approx 25312.5\ MB$$

Convert to GB by dividing by 1024.

$$Storage \approx \dfrac{25312.5}{1024} \approx 24.72\ GB$$

**(ii) Entries in a 5-minute window:**

$$Entries = EPS \times 5 \times 60$$

$$Entries = 200 \times 300 = 60{,}000\ entries$$

**Storage and Indexing Strategy:**
At ~24.72 GB/day, the organization generates **~740 GB/month** and **~8.8 TB/year**. A naive flat-file strategy will collapse search performance. The recommended strategy is a **two-tier SIEM storage model**:
- **Hot Tier (Elasticsearch on SSD):** Retain **30 days** for real-time search and dashboards.
- **Cold Tier (S3 / HDFS with Parquet compression):** Retain **1–7 years** for compliance and forensic search.
- **Index Sharding:** Time-based indices (e.g., `logs-2024.10.11`) allow efficient retention policies.
- **Compression:** Gzip or Zstandard reduces storage by **60–80 %**.

**Conclusion:** Effective log monitoring demands both high-throughput ingestion and intelligent tiered storage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting octal conversion:** Students frequently write `<34>` instead of `<42>` for the `auth.crit` PRI value — losing **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Facility and Severity:** When decoding `<165>`, students often swap the quotient and the remainder.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in storage problems:** Mixing KB with MB or seconds with hours costs full marks in numericals.
> 4. **Vague answers in 7-mark questions:** Listing only bullet points without *explanation of WHY* results in 50% marks cap.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram:** In Q.A(a), the diagram carries **at least 2 marks**. Always draw it even if rough.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Log File Monitor** = passive sensor that reads logs and raises alerts using pattern/threshold/correlation/anomaly rules.
- Standard sources: **syslog, Windows Event Log, Apache, firewall, application**.
- Classic Linux tools: **Swatch (single-event)**, **SEC (correlating)**, **OSSEC (full HIDS)**, **Logwatch (periodic)**.
- Enterprise SIEMs: **Splunk, ELK/OpenSearch, IBM QRadar, ArcSight, Microsoft Sentinel**.
- Syslog header format: **`<PRI>TIMESTAMP HOSTNAME APP-NAME PROCID MSGID MESSAGE>`**.
- **PRI formula:** $PRI = Facility \times 8 + Severity$, displayed as **octal in angle brackets**.
- Storage planning: $Volume = EPS \times Size \times Time$; enterprise systems need **10,000+ EPS** capacity.
- Detection techniques compared: **Signature (low FP, no zero-day) vs Anomaly (zero-day, high FP) vs Correlation (multi-stage)**.
- 2024 trend: shift from on-prem SIEM to **cloud-native SIEM (Azure Sentinel, AWS Security Lake, Chronicle)**.
- For exam: always include a **block diagram** in architecture questions — it is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDPS Response Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IDPS Response Options

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# IDPS Response Options — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IDPS Response Options** refer to the set of pre-defined, configurable actions an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) executes **after** an analysed event is determined to be malicious or policy-violating. These actions span the spectrum from passive notification (alerts, logs) to active prevention (packet blocking, connection termination).

According to **NIST Special Publication 800-94 (Revision 1)**, the IDPS response set is broadly divided into two functional categories:

1. **Passive Response** — The IDPS only **observes and reports** the suspicious activity. It does not alter the traffic flow.
2. **Active Response (Inline Prevention)** — The IDPS **intervenes in real time** to block, modify, or contain the attack traffic.

A third practical category frequently taught in KTU 2024 scheme syllabi is:

3. **Hybrid / Cooperative Response** — Combines passive logging with selective active intervention (e.g., firewall rule push via API).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of IDPS response options like the security system in a shopping mall.
> - **Passive Response** = A CCTV camera that *records* a thief stealing. The mall operator is notified (alert/email), but the thief is not physically stopped. The footage is evidence.
> - **Active Response** = The same CCTV, but as soon as a thief is spotted, automatic *toughened glass doors* slam shut, blocking the thief's exit (packet drop / TCP reset).
> - **Hybrid Response** = A *panic button* that the security guard presses after seeing the CCTV footage — a human-in-the-loop preventing active damage.

## 1.3 Primary High-Yield Terminology (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **False Positive** | Legitimate traffic wrongly flagged as malicious. |
| **False Negative** | A real attack that the IDPS failed to detect. |
| **Inline IDPS** | Sensor sits directly in the traffic path (promiscuous bridge). |
| **Tap / SPAN Port** | Read-only copy of traffic; cannot block. |
| **TCP Reset (RST)** | Active response technique that injects RST packets. |
| **Shunning** | Firewall rule that blocks source IP for a defined time. |
| **Quarantine** | Isolating an internal host from the rest of the LAN/VLAN. |
| **Honeypot** | Decoy system used for observation (passive response). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to **differentiate between "Detection-only" and "Prevention" functions** and map them to specific response types. This is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Module 1.

## 1.4 Operational Scope of Response

IDPS response options operate across **three distinct timelines**:

$$T_{\text{response}} \in \{T_{\text{pre-attack}},\; T_{\text{during-attack}},\; T_{\text{post-attack}}\}$$

- **Pre-attack:** Hardening, policy tuning, signature updates.
- **During-attack:** Real-time blocking, rate-limiting, session termination.
- **Post-attack:** Forensic logging, alert escalation, countermeasure documentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Response Options (The KTU Master Tree)

IDPS response options are classified along **two axes**:

- **Axis 1 — Automation Level:** Manual vs. Automated
- **Axis 2 — Action Type:** Passive (observe) vs. Active (intervene)

The cross-product yields **four canonical response classes**:

### 2.1.1 Class A — Passive + Manual (Alert-Only, Human Reviews)

- IDPS writes log entry and generates alert.
- Security analyst reviews later.
- **Example:** SNMP trap, syslog message, console pop-up.

### 2.1.2 Class B — Passive + Automated (Auto-Notify)

- IDPS automatically emails/SMS/push-notifies the SOC.
- **Example:** SIEM ingestion via syslog, email alerts via Postfix, webhook to Slack.

### 2.1.3 Class C — Active + Automated (Inline Prevention)

- IDPS **directly** modifies traffic.
- **Example:** Drop packet, send TCP RST, reconfigure ACL.

### 2.1.4 Class D — Active + Manual (Cooperative Response)

- IDPS suggests an action; human approves it.
- **Example:** Ticket auto-generated in JIRA; admin pushes firewall rule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Prevention"** function of an IDPS is realised only through **Class C** and **Class D** responses. Pure NIDS (Class A/B) cannot prevent — they can only **detect**.

## 2.2 Detailed Active Response Techniques

### 2.2.1 Session Termination (TCP Reset Injection)

The IDPS injects spoofed **TCP RST** packets to both endpoints of a malicious flow. Either side receiving a RST aborts the connection.

For a TCP flow defined by the 4-tuple $\{IP_s, IP_d, P_s, P_d\}$:

$$RST_{\text{flag}} = 1 \quad \text{on segment from } IP_x \rightarrow IP_y$$

The injected packet must match the **sequence number window** to be accepted:

$$SEQ_{\text{RST}} \in [SEQ_{\text{expected}} - 2^{31},\; SEQ_{\text{expected}} + 2^{31} - 1]$$

> [!WARNING]
> Modern **TCP Resets are largely ineffective against long-lived HTTP/TLS sessions** (e.g., HTTPS over port 443) because once the session is established, the application continues even if the transport session is reset. This is a frequent KTU Part B discussion point.

### 2.2.2 Traffic Blocking / Shunning

The IDPS adds a **dynamic ACL** or **blacklist entry** for the source IP:

$$\text{ACL}_{\text{new}} = \text{ACL}_{\text{old}} \cup \{(IP_s,\; T_{\text{duration}})\}$$

- Shunning duration is configurable: $\Delta t \in [\Delta t_{\min}, \Delta t_{\max}]$.
- Common values: **5 minutes, 1 hour, 24 hours, permanent**.

### 2.2.3 Quarantine / Containment

The compromised internal host $H_c$ is moved to a **quarantine VLAN**:

$$VLAN(H_c) \rightarrow VLAN_{\text{quarantine}}$$

The IDPS communicates with the switch via:
- SNMP SET to change VLAN membership
- RADIUS CoA (Change of Authorization)
- 802.1X re-authentication

### 2.2.4 Rate Limiting / Throttling

Bandwidth of suspicious flow is throttled:

$$B_{\text{new}} = \min(B_{\text{original}},\; B_{\text{cap}})$$

A common formula for token-bucket throttling:

$$T_{\text{burst}} = C \times t_{\text{interval}}$$

where $C$ = capacity (bytes) and $t_{\text{interval}}$ = refill interval.

### 2.2.5 Honeypot / Sinkhole Redirection

Malicious traffic is **redirected** to a decoy:

$$DST_{IP} = IP_{\text{honeypot}} \quad \text{for } IP_d \in \text{blacklist}$$

The attacker wastes time on a fake system while defenders collect intelligence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Metrics Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Formula | Symbol | Unit | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate | ratio | Measures alert noise |
| $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | False Negative Rate | ratio | Measures missed attacks |
| $T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{act}}$ | Total response time | ms / s | End-to-end latency |
| $SEQ_{\text{RST}}$ window | Sequence acceptance range | integer | TCP reset validity |
| $B_{\text{cap}}$ | Throttle bandwidth cap | Mbps | Rate limit enforcement |
| $\Delta t_{\text{shun}}$ | Shun duration | s / min / h | Blocking window |
| $T_{\text{burst}} = C \times t_i$ | Token bucket burst | bytes | Throttle capacity |
| $MTTR_{\text{IDPS}}$ | Mean time to respond | s | Operational SLA |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Active response is mandatory in **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**, **5G core networks**, and **Zero-Trust architectures** where any undetected intrusion has catastrophic consequences. Modern **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)** platforms implement these formulaic rules at scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — Response Selection Logic

## 3.1 The Decision Flow (How an IDPS Chooses a Response)

Let the IDPS event tuple be $E = (T_s, IP_s, IP_d, P_s, P_d, S, C)$ where:

- $T_s$ = timestamp
- $S$ = severity score (0–10)
- $C$ = confidence score (0.0–1.0)
- Remaining fields are the standard 4-tuple.

The IDPS computes a **Response Priority Index (RPI)**:

$$RPI = w_1 \cdot S + w_2 \cdot (10 \cdot C) - w_3 \cdot FPR_{recent}$$

Typical weights: $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$.

### Step 1 — Compute RPI
$$RPI = 0.5 \cdot S + 0.3 \cdot (10 \cdot C) - 0.2 \cdot FPR_{recent}$$

### Step 2 — Threshold Classification

The IDPS then classifies using:

$$
\text{Action}(E) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Class A (Log only)} & \text{if } RPI < 3 \\
\text{Class B (Auto-alert)} & \text{if } 3 \le RPI < 6 \\
\text{Class C (Block + RST)} & \text{if } 6 \le RPI < 9 \\
\text{Class D (Quarantine)} & \text{if } RPI \ge 9
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Response

If Class C is selected, the IDPS executes **two simultaneous actions**:

1. **TCP RST injection** to both endpoints.
2. **Dynamic ACL** entry with shun duration $\Delta t$.

If Class D is selected, the IDPS also initiates a **VLAN move** via SNMP or RADIUS CoA.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An IDPS detects an event with $S = 8$, $C = 0.9$, and $FPR_{recent} = 0.05$. Determine the response class and the specific actions to be taken.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the RPI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPI &= 0.5 \cdot S + 0.3 \cdot (10 \cdot C) - 0.2 \cdot FPR_{recent} \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 8 + 0.3 \cdot (10 \cdot 0.9) - 0.2 \cdot 0.05 \\
&= 4.0 + 2.7 - 0.01 \\
&= 6.69
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Classify the response**

Since $6 \le RPI = 6.69 < 9$, the event falls into **Class C — Active + Automated**.

**Step 3: Specify the actions**

1. Inject a **TCP RST** packet to both $IP_s$ and $IP_d$ with a sequence number in the valid acceptance window.
2. Add a **shun rule** in the ACL:
   $$\text{ACL} \leftarrow \text{ACL} \cup \{(IP_s,\; \Delta t = 1\text{ hour})\}$$
3. Generate a **SIEM log entry** for the SOC dashboard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that even in a Class C response, the IDPS **always logs** — the prevention action is *additional* to logging, not a replacement. This is a frequently tested KTU point.

## 3.3 Trade-off Matrix for Response Selection

| Trade-off | Passive Response | Active Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Risk of false positives** | Safe (no traffic impact) | High (may block legit users) |
| **Latency impact on traffic** | None | Adds $1$–$10$ ms per packet |
| **Forensic value** | High (full traffic captured) | Lower (blocks further evidence) |
| **Attack containment** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Suitability for production** | Safe to enable | Requires tuning + whitelists |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IDPS Response Classification Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Malicious Event Detected] --> B[Compute RPI Score]
    B --> C{RPI Threshold Check}
    C -->|RPI less than 3| D[Class A: Log Only]
    C -->|RPI between 3 and 6| E[Class B: Auto Alert via Email or SIEM]
    C -->|RPI between 6 and 9| F[Class C: TCP Reset plus Shun Rule]
    C -->|RPI greater than 9| G[Class D: Quarantine via VLAN Move]
    D --> H[Security Analyst Review]
    E --> H
    F --> I[Block plus Log to SIEM]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Forensic Investigation]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Inline vs Passive IDPS Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph In["Internet"]
        A[Attacker]
    end
    subgraph InlinePath["Inline IDPS Architecture"]
        B[Perimeter Router] --> C[IDPS Sensor Inline]
        C --> D[Internal Firewall]
    end
    subgraph PassivePath["Passive IDPS Architecture"]
        E[Perimeter Switch] --> F[Internal Firewall]
        E --> G[SPAN Port Tap]
        G --> H[IDPS Sensor Read Only]
    end
    A --> InlinePath
    A --> PassivePath
    D --> I[Internal Network]
    F --> I
    H -.alert.-> J[SOC Console]
    C -.alert.-> J
```

## 4.3 Cooperative Response Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IDPS as IDPS Sensor
    participant SOAR as SOAR Platform
    participant FW as Firewall
    participant SOC as Security Analyst
    IDPS->>SOAR: Alert with RPI score 7.2
    SOAR->>SOC: Notify analyst for approval
    SOC->>SOAR: Approve active block
    SOAR->>FW: Push dynamic ACL rule
    FW->>FW: Block source IP for 1 hour
    FW-->>SOAR: Confirmation of rule applied
    SOAR-->>IDPS: Update event status to contained
```

## 4.4 Response Option Decision Matrix (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Core1["Response Dimensions"]
        D1[Axis 1: Automation Level]
        D2[Axis 2: Action Type]
    end
    subgraph Class1["Passive Responses"]
        CA[Class A: Manual Log]
        CB[Class B: Auto Notify]
    end
    subgraph Class2["Active Responses"]
        CC[Class C: Auto Block]
        CD[Class D: Manual Confirm]
    end
    D1 --> CA
    D1 --> CB
    D1 --> CC
    D1 --> CD
    D2 --> CA
    D2 --> CB
    D2 --> CC
    D2 --> CD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
