# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between passive and active response options in an IDPS. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Response | Active Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Traffic modification** | None | Modifies or blocks |
| **Network placement** | SPAN port / Tap | Inline |
| **Latency** | Zero | Adds $1$–$10$ ms |
| **Risk of false positive** | None | High |
| **Example** | Syslog alert | TCP RST injection |

Passive response only **observes and reports**; active response **intervenes in real time** to stop the attack.

### Q2. What is a TCP reset injection? Why is it less effective against HTTPS traffic? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **TCP reset injection** is an active response technique where the IDPS spoofs TCP RST packets to both endpoints of a malicious flow, forcing the connection to terminate.

It is **less effective against HTTPS** because:
1. The TLS session state is preserved at the application layer even after the TCP session is torn down.
2. Many modern web servers and browsers **ignore unsolicited RSTs** during active streams.
3. Persistent HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (QUIC) connections are connection-multiplexed and survive RSTs.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Classify the various IDPS response options with neat diagrams. Discuss the role of honeypots and sinkholes in passive response. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The IDPS response options are classified along two axes — *automation level* and *action type* — yielding four classes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class A} &= \text{Passive + Manual} \\
\text{Class B} &= \text{Passive + Automated} \\
\text{Class C} &= \text{Active + Automated} \\
\text{Class D} &= \text{Active + Manual}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Class A — Log only:** The IDPS writes an entry; SOC reviews later. (Examples: syslog, console message.)

**Class B — Auto-notify:** Automated emails, SMS, SIEM ingestion, Slack webhooks. No traffic is blocked.

**Class C — Active automated prevention:** TCP RST injection, dynamic ACLs, rate limiting, shun rules.

**Class D — Cooperative containment:** VLAN quarantine, RADIUS CoA, session disconnection — all gated by human approval via SOAR.

**Role of Honeypots and Sinkholes:**

- **Honeypot** is a decoy system placed inside the network. It has no production value; any connection to it is suspicious by definition. The IDPS logs the attacker's activity for forensic study. Honeypots are a **passive response** because they do not block the attacker; they only collect intelligence.

- **Sinkhole** is a server (or DNS server) that receives malicious traffic redirected by the IDPS or DNS firewall. The attacker's queries resolve to the sinkhole IP, denying them real targets. Sinkholes are a **semi-active** response — they prevent attacker success while capturing artefacts.

Both techniques shift the attacker onto the defender's terms without disrupting legitimate traffic, making them ideal for **production environments** where aggressive blocking is risky.

#### (b) Explain in detail the active response techniques: TCP reset, traffic shunning, and quarantine. Compare their effectiveness and risk. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. TCP Reset Injection:**
The IDPS, sitting inline, injects a TCP segment with the RST flag set to both endpoints of a malicious flow.

$$RST_{flag} = 1, \quad SEQ_{RST} = \text{valid window value}$$

- **Effectiveness:** $\approx 80$–$90$% for plain HTTP/FTP/Telnet.
- **Risk:** Can terminate legitimate long-lived sessions if RST is misfired on a false positive.
- **Limitation:** Ineffective against HTTP/2, HTTPS persistent sessions, QUIC.

**2. Traffic Shunning (Blackhole Filtering):**
The IDPS pushes a dynamic ACL entry to the perimeter firewall.

$$\text{ACL} \leftarrow \text{ACL} \cup \{(IP_s,\; \Delta t_{shun})\}$$

- **Effectiveness:** Very high — the source IP is completely blocked at the perimeter.
- **Risk:** If the attacker uses IP spoofing or botnets, they simply rotate IPs.
- **Mitigation:** Pair shunning with **rate-based detection** to identify botnet C2 traffic patterns.

**3. Quarantine (VLAN Containment):**
The compromised host is moved to a quarantined VLAN with restricted access.

$$VLAN(H_c) \rightarrow VLAN_{quarantine}$$

- **Effectiveness:** High for insider threats and lateral movement.
- **Risk:** Disrupts the user's productivity; requires integration with switch / NAC.
- **Implementation methods:** SNMP SET, RADIUS CoA, 802.1X re-authentication.

**Comparison Table:**

| Technique | Scope | Latency | False-Positive Impact | Best For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| TCP Reset | Single flow | ms | Moderate | Cleartext protocols |
| Shunning | Source IP block | s | High (legit user lockout) | Botnets, scanners |
| Quarantine | Internal host | s–min | Severe (user downtime) | Compromised endpoints |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Active Response):** Students often forget to mention that **active responses must be combined with strong exception/whitelisting** to avoid denying service to critical services (e.g., a TCP RST on a payment gateway's persistent connection can cause financial transaction failures). Always include a **whitelist clause** in your answer to score the full 7 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Discuss the NIST SP 800-94 classification of IDPS responses. How do passive and active responses differ in placement and impact? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

NIST SP 800-94 classifies IDPS responses into two principal categories:

1. **Passive Response** — The IDPS only records and notifies. The sensor is typically connected to a **SPAN port or network tap**, never inline. Impact on network: **zero latency**, **zero packet modification**.

2. **Active Response (Inline Prevention)** — The IDPS sits **inline** (fail-open or fail-close mode) and can drop, modify, or inject packets. Impact: **1–10 ms latency**, **packets may be dropped**.

**Key Differences:**

| Factor | Passive | Active |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Network placement** | SPAN / Tap | Inline bridge |
| **Fail mode** | N/A (read-only) | Fail-open or fail-closed |
| **Throughput impact** | None | $1$–$10$ ms added latency |
| **Can prevent?** | No | Yes |
| **Can detect?** | Yes | Yes |
| **False positive cost** | Low (analyst time) | High (service disruption) |

The choice between passive and active is driven by the **organisation's risk appetite**: high-value targets (banks, defence) prefer active; research and academic networks prefer passive.

#### (b) With a neat flowchart, explain how an IDPS calculates the Response Priority Index (RPI) and chooses an action. Take a numerical example with $S = 9$, $C = 0.95$, $FPR_{recent} = 0.02$ and determine the action. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The RPI formula is:

$$RPI = w_1 \cdot S + w_2 \cdot (10 \cdot C) - w_3 \cdot FPR_{recent}$$

With standard weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPI &= 0.5 \cdot 9 + 0.3 \cdot (10 \cdot 0.95) - 0.2 \cdot 0.02 \\
&= 4.5 + 2.85 - 0.004 \\
&= 7.346
\end{aligned}
$$

**Threshold classification** (from Section 3.1):

$$
\begin{cases}
RPI < 3 & \rightarrow \text{Class A: Log only} \\
3 \le RPI < 6 & \rightarrow \text{Class B: Auto alert} \\
6 \le RPI < 9 & \rightarrow \text{Class C: TCP RST + Shun} \\
RPI \ge 9 & \rightarrow \text{Class D: Quarantine}
\end{cases}
$$

Since $7.346 \in [6, 9)$, the action is **Class C: Active + Automated**.

**Specific actions executed:**

1. Inject a TCP RST to both $IP_s$ and $IP_d$ with a valid sequence number.
2. Add a dynamic ACL entry: $\text{shun}(IP_s,\; 1\text{ hour})$.
3. Log to SIEM for forensic correlation.
4. Send real-time alert to the SOC console.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (RPI Calculation):** Many students **forget to subtract** the $w_3 \cdot FPR_{recent}$ term and end up with an inflated RPI. This shifts the answer to the wrong class and costs **2–3 marks**. Always write the full formula with all three terms explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IDPS Response Options** are the actions an IDPS takes after detecting a malicious event.
- **Two principal types:** **Passive** (observe/report) and **Active** (prevent/intervene).
- **Four functional classes** (Passive+Manual, Passive+Auto, Active+Auto, Active+Manual) form the KTU master classification.
- **Active response techniques** include **TCP RST injection**, **shunning**, **quarantine**, **rate limiting**, and **sinkholing**.
- **TCP RST** is the most common active response but is **ineffective against HTTPS, HTTP/2, and QUIC**.
- **Honeypots and sinkholes** are *passive* intelligence-gathering responses.
- **NIST SP 800-94** is the authoritative reference — remember its two-class taxonomy.
- **RPI (Response Priority Index)** determines the response class:

$$RPI = w_1 \cdot S + w_2 \cdot (10 \cdot C) - w_3 \cdot FPR_{recent}$$

- **Always log, even when actively blocking** — prevention is in addition to, not instead of, logging.
- **Active responses require whitelists** of critical services to avoid self-inflicted outages.
- **Latency cost of inline IDPS:** approximately **$1$–$10$ ms** per packet.
- **Common pitfalls in KTU exams:** mixing up NIDS vs NIPS, forgetting the RPI subtraction term, omitting the whitelist clause in active response answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selecting IDPS Approaches and Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selecting IDPS Approaches and Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**IDPS Approach and Product Selection** is the systematic, multi-criteria decision-making process by which an organization evaluates, compares, and chooses the most appropriate Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) solution — covering detection methodologies (signature-based, anomaly-based, stateful protocol analysis, hybrid), deployment architectures (network-based, host-based, wireless, network behavior analysis), and commercial/open-source products — based on the organization's security requirements, IT infrastructure, regulatory obligations, budget, and operational risk profile.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with NIST SP 800-94 Rev. 1, this is treated as the **IDPS Technology Assessment and Procurement Lifecycle**, encompassing four high-level phases:
- **Requirements Analysis** (what to protect)
- **Approach/Methodology Evaluation** (how to detect)
- **Product Shortlisting & Proof-of-Concept (PoC)**
- **Deployment, Tuning, and Operational Validation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Selecting the appropriate IDPS approach and product is the foundational decision that dictates the entire security posture of the enterprise. A wrongly chosen IDPS results in alert fatigue, false negatives, performance degradation, and budget wastage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are hiring a **security guard for a building**. You first ask:
- What kind of building is it? (Mansion vs. apartment vs. bank vault) → **Risk Profile**
- Do you want the guard to check everyone's ID card against a list (**signature-based**), detect anyone behaving strangely (**anomaly-based**), or both (**hybrid**)?
- Should the guard stand at the gate (**network-based**), patrol inside each room (**host-based**), or watch the wireless signals (**wireless-based**)?
- Do you hire a trained dog (open-source, needs care), a uniformed guard (commercial, ready-to-deploy), or a smart-camera AI system (advanced, costly)?

The selection of an IDPS works exactly the same way. There is no "one-size-fits-all" guard — you must match the **threat landscape**, **infrastructure type**, **budget**, and **operational capability** to the right approach and product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam – "RATS-PSA":**
> **R**equirements → **A**pproach → **T**echnology → **S**hortlist → **P**oC → **S**elect → **A**udit

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics
The following metrics are universally accepted by KTU/industry for IDPS product evaluation:
- **True Positive Rate (TPR)** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Detection Latency** (in **milliseconds (ms)**)
- **Throughput** (in **Gbps** for NIDPS, **events/sec** for HIDPS)
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)**
- **Coverage Ratio** (% of assets monitored)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to remember:** Bandwidth planning for NIDPS typically requires **15–20% overhead** beyond raw traffic for deep packet inspection (DPI).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IDPS Selection Framework (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Define Organizational Security Requirements**
- Identify the **CIA Triad priorities** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- Map critical assets (servers, databases, OT/ICS, cloud workloads, endpoints).
- Identify compliance drivers: **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001, RBI/SEBI guidelines** (very important for Kerala banking/IT parks).

**Step 2: Threat Modeling & Risk Assessment**
- Use **STRIDE** (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege) or **PASTA** (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis).
- Determine attacker capability, motivation, and attack surface.

**Step 3: Choose Detection Approach**
| Approach | Detection Logic | Best For | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signature-Based | Pattern/Regex match against known attack database | Known malware, exploits, CVEs | Zero-day blind |
| Anomaly-Based | Statistical/ML deviation from baseline | Zero-day, insider threats, OT | High FPR |
| Stateful Protocol Analysis | Validates protocol state machine (e.g., TCP handshake) | Protocol misuse, tunneling | Stateful complexity |
| Hybrid / Cross-Layer | Combines ≥2 of the above | Enterprise SOC, MSSP | Cost, tuning effort |

**Step 4: Choose Deployment Architecture**
- **NIDPS** (Network-based) — perimeter/segment monitoring
- **HIDPS** (Host-based) — endpoint/file integrity/registry
- **WIDPS** (Wireless) — 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax threats, rogue AP
- **NBA / NBAD** (Network Behavior Analysis) — DDoS, worm propagation
- **VM-Centric / Cloud-Native IDPS** — AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender

**Step 5: Evaluate Product Features using a Weighted Scoring Matrix**
- Detection efficacy, false positive rate, signature update frequency, scalability, integration (SIEM/SOAR), vendor support, TCO (Total Cost of Ownership).

**Step 6: Conduct PoC (Proof of Concept)**
- Run the product in **mirror/SPAN port** mode for **30–60 days** in a non-production segment.

**Step 7: Final Selection, Deployment & Continuous Tuning**

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Unit / Boundary | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Detection Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $0 \le A \le 1$ | Overall classifier performance |
| 2 | **True Positive Rate (Recall)** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $0 \le TPR \le 1$ | Ability to catch real attacks |
| 3 | **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le FPR \le 1$ | Alert fatigue indicator |
| 4 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ | Trustworthiness of an alert |
| 5 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ | Balanced P-R metric |
| 6 | **ROC-AUC** | Area under TPR vs FPR curve | $0.5 \le AUC \le 1$ | Threshold-independent evaluation |
| 7 | **Bandwidth Overhead for DPI** | $B_{\text{req}} = B_{\text{link}} \times 1.20$ | Gbps | Capacity planning for NIDPS |
| 8 | **Event Throughput** | $E = \dfrac{\text{Total Events}}{\text{Time Window}}$ | EPS (Events/Sec) | HIDPS / SIEM sizing |
| 9 | **MTTD** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{attack}})}{N}$ | seconds | SOC KPI |
| 10 | **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum (t_{\text{resolve}} - t_{\text{detect}})}{N}$ | seconds | SOC KPI |
| 11 | **Coverage Ratio** | $C = \dfrac{A_{\text{monitored}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Gap analysis |
| 12 | **TCO (5-Year)** | $TCO = C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{hw}} + C_{\text{ops}} + C_{\text{train}}$ | ₹ / USD | Procurement justification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The vertical pipe character `|` is forbidden inside KTU markdown tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or set notation (e.g., $\vert x \vert$).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Banking & FinTech (Kerala):** Hybrid NIDPS + HIDPS for RBI Cyber Security Framework compliance.
- **Smart Manufacturing / OT:** Anomaly-based IDPS for SCADA/Modbus/DNP3 protocols.
- **Cloud-Native Workloads:** VM-centric IDPS (agent-based) for AWS/Azure.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** HIDPS for EHR integrity + NIDPS for perimeter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Composite IDPS Selection Score (Weighted Scoring Model)

Most enterprises do not pick an IDPS based on a single parameter. A **Weighted Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)** is used. The derivation of the composite score for a candidate product $i$ is shown below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij} \\[4pt]
\text{where} \quad S_i &= \text{Composite Score of Product } i \\
w_j &= \text{Weight of Criterion } j, \quad 0 \le w_j \le 1 \\
s_{ij} &= \text{Score of Product } i \text{ on Criterion } j, \quad 0 \le s_{ij} \le 10 \\
\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The product with the **maximum $S_i$** is selected:

$$
P_{\text{selected}} = \arg\max_{i \in \{1, 2, \dots, m\}} S_i
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Scenario:** An IT firm in Technopark, Trivandrum, wants to select an IDPS. Four candidates ($A, B, C, D$) are evaluated against **5 criteria**. Each criterion is scored from $0$ to $10$. The weights are pre-approved by the CISO.

| Criterion ($j$) | Weight ($w_j$) |
|---|---|
| Detection Accuracy | 0.30 |
| False Positive Rate (lower=better) | 0.20 |
| Throughput (Gbps) | 0.20 |
| SIEM Integration | 0.15 |
| TCO (5-Yr) | 0.15 |
| **Total** | **1.00** |

**Raw Scores Table:**

| Criterion | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Accuracy | 8 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| False Positive Rate (inverted) | 7 | 6 | 9 | 5 |
| Throughput | 6 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| SIEM Integration | 9 | 8 | 6 | 7 |
| TCO | 7 | 5 | 8 | 6 |

**Computation for Product A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 1.05 \\
&= 7.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Product B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 5) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.20 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.75 \\
&= 7.65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Product C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) \\
&= 2.10 + 1.80 + 1.60 + 0.90 + 1.20 \\
&= 7.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation for Product D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_D &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 6) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.00 + 1.40 + 1.05 + 0.90 \\
&= 6.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= 7.65 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{Rank 1} \\
S_C &= 7.60 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{Rank 2} \\
S_A &= 7.40 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{Rank 3} \\
S_D &= 6.75 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{Rank 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Product **B** is selected.

$$
P_{\text{selected}} = B
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – IDPS Approach vs. Threat Type

| Threat Type | Signature | Anomaly | Stateful | Hybrid |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Known Malware | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Weak | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good |
| Zero-Day Exploit | ❌ Blind | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Fair | ✅ Good |
| DoS / DDoS | ⚠️ Fair | ✅ Best | ✅ Good | ✅ Best |
| Insider Threat | ❌ Poor | ✅ Best | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good |
| Protocol Misuse | ❌ Poor | ⚠️ Fair | ✅ Best | ✅ Good |
| Encrypted Traffic Analysis | ❌ Poor | ✅ Best | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good |
| OT/ICS Anomaly | ❌ Poor | ✅ Best | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good |

### 3.4 Decision Logic for Architecture Selection

$$
\text{If } \text{(Encrypted Traffic} > 70\% \text{ of perimeter)} \Rightarrow \text{Use SSL/TLS Inspection + Anomaly NIDPS}
$$

$$
\text{If } \text{(Endpoints} \ge 1000 \text{ and sensitive data)} \Rightarrow \text{Add HIDPS on critical servers}
$$

$$
\text{If } \text{(Wi-Fi present)} \Rightarrow \text{Add WIDPS for rogue AP and evil-twin detection}
$$

$$
\text{If } \text{(Cloud workload} \ge 50\% \text{)} \Rightarrow \text{Use Cloud-Native / CWPP-integrated IDPS}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — IDPS Selection Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Initiate IDPS Selection] --> B1[Step 1: Define Security Requirements]
    B1 --> B2[Step 2: Asset Inventory and Risk Assessment STRIDE PASTA]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3: Choose Detection Approach]
    B3 --> B31[Signature Based]
    B3 --> B32[Anomaly Based]
    B3 --> B33[Stateful Protocol]
    B3 --> B34[Hybrid]
    B34 --> C4[Step 4: Choose Deployment Architecture]
    C4 --> C41[NIDPS]
    C4 --> C42[HIDPS]
    C4 --> C43[WIDPS]
    C4 --> C44[NBA NBAD]
    C4 --> C45[Cloud Native IDPS]
    C45 --> D5[Step 5: Build Weighted Scoring Matrix]
    D5 --> E6[Step 6: Shortlist 3 to 5 Vendors]
    E6 --> F7[Step 7: Conduct Proof of Concept PoC 30 to 60 days]
    F7 --> G8{PoC Meets KPIs}
    G8 -- No --> F7
    G8 -- Yes --> H9[Step 8: Final Selection and Contract]
    H9 --> I10[Step 9: Pilot Deployment in Production]
    I10 --> J11[Step 10: Tuning and Continuous Monitoring]
    J11 --> K12[Step 11: Periodic Re-evaluation Annual]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Selected IDPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perimeter
        FW[Firewall / WAF] --> SPAN[SPAN / TAP Port]
    end
    subgraph Core_NIDPS
        SPAN --> ENG1[Packet Capture Engine]
        ENG1 --> DPI[Deep Packet Inspection]
        DPI --> DEC[Detection Engine Signature and Anomaly]
        DEC --> PREV[Prevention Engine Inline Drop Reset]
    end
    subgraph Endpoint_HIDPS
        AG[Host Agent] --> FIM[File Integrity Monitor]
        AG --> LOG[System Log Collector]
        AG --> PRO[Process Behavior Tracker]
    end
    subgraph SOC
        SIEM[SIEM Correlation] --> SOAR[SOAR Automated Response]
        DASH[Analyst Dashboard]
    end
    DEC --> SIEM
    FIM --> SIEM
    LOG --> SIEM
    PREV --> FW
    SIEM --> DASH
    SIEM --> SOAR
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Detection Approaches

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Approach_Matrix
        A1[Signature Based: Known attacks, Low FPR, High TPR] --> A1L[Limitation: Zero-day blind]
        A2[Anomaly Based: ML/Statistical baseline] --> A2L[Limitation: High FPR, training needed]
        A3[Stateful Protocol: RFC validation] --> A3L[Limitation: Encrypted blind]
        A4[Hybrid: Combines all] --> A4L[Limitation: Cost, complexity]
    end
    A1 --> SEL[Recommended for PCI environments]
    A2 --> SEL2[Recommended for OT, Zero-day]
    A3 --> SEL3[Recommended for DMZ, Firewalls]
    A4 --> SEL4[Recommended for Enterprise SOC]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain the four primary IDPS detection approaches used during product selection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Signature-Based Detection** (1 Mark) — Matches observed events against a predefined database of known attack patterns. Best for known threats; cannot detect zero-day attacks.
2. **Anomaly-Based Detection** (1 Mark) — Builds a baseline of normal behaviour and flags deviations. Effective for zero-day and insider threats but produces higher false positives.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis** (½ Mark) — Validates protocol state machines (e.g., TCP handshake) against RFC standards to detect misuse.
4. **Hybrid Detection** (½ Mark) — Combines two or more of the above to improve accuracy and reduce false positives.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What are the key factors to be considered while selecting an IDPS product for an enterprise?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Detection Capability** (1 Mark) — TPR, FPR, signature database freshness, anomaly accuracy.
2. **Performance & Scalability** (1 Mark) — Throughput (Gbps), EPS handling, support for distributed deployment.
3. **Integration & TCO** (1 Mark) — SIEM/SOAR compatibility, vendor support, 5-year total cost of ownership (license + hardware + operations + training).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]
**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step methodology for selecting an IDPS product** for a mid-size IT organization. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare **NIDPS vs HIDPS vs WIDPS** architectures in terms of placement, advantages, limitations, and ideal deployment scenarios. (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The IDPS selection methodology consists of **seven sequential phases**:

1. **Requirement Gathering (1 Mark):** Identify critical assets, CIA priorities, compliance needs (ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, DPDP Act 2023), and SLA constraints.
2. **Threat & Risk Assessment (1 Mark):** Use STRIDE/PASTA to model threats; assign likelihood and impact scores; prioritize attack vectors.
3. **Approach Selection (1 Mark):** Choose between Signature, Anomaly, Stateful, or Hybrid based on the threat profile (e.g., zero-day heavy → Anomaly/Hybrid).
4. **Architecture Decision (1 Mark):** Decide on NIDPS, HIDPS, WIDPS, NBA, or Cloud-Native based on infrastructure topology.
5. **Vendor Shortlisting & Scoring (1 Mark):** Create a weighted scoring matrix using criteria such as detection accuracy, FPR, throughput, integration, support, and TCO. Compute $S_i = \sum w_j s_{ij}$ for each vendor.
6. **Proof of Concept / Pilot (1 Mark):** Deploy top 2–3 candidates in **SPAN/mirror mode** for 30–60 days. Measure ROC-AUC, MTTD, MTTR, and operational effort.
7. **Final Selection, Contract & Continuous Tuning (1 Mark):** Sign contract, deploy in production (inline for prevention, passive for detection), and continuously tune signatures, baselines, and thresholds.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Feature | NIDPS | HIDPS | WIDPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Placement (1.5 Marks)** | At network perimeter / SPAN port / inline at gateway | On individual host (server, endpoint) | Near Wi-Fi APs / monitor RF spectrum |
| **Advantages (2 Marks)** | Covers entire subnet, single deployment | Detects host-specific behaviour (file, registry, process) | Detects rogue APs, evil-twin, MAC spoofing |
| **Limitations (2 Marks)** | Blind to encrypted traffic without SSL inspection; cannot see host-level activity | Per-host agent overhead; no network-wide view | Limited to wireless layer; not effective on wired |
| **Ideal Scenario (1.5 Marks)** | Data center perimeter, DMZ, east-west traffic | Critical servers, POS, ATMs, OT controllers | Hospitals, universities, BYOD offices |
| **Example Tools** | Suricata, Zeek, Cisco Firepower | OSSEC, Wazuh, CrowdStrike Falcon | Kismet, AirMagnet, Aruba RFProtect |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**(a)** Explain the **Weighted Scoring Model** for IDPS product selection. Derive and apply the composite score formula to a hypothetical dataset of four products. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** components and **Regulatory Compliance** factors that influence IDPS product selection in an Indian banking environment. (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Weighted Scoring Model (WSM)** is an MCDA technique used to rank IDPS candidates using multiple, often conflicting, criteria.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
$$
where $S_i$ is the composite score, $w_j$ is the weight of criterion $j$ (with $\sum w_j = 1$), and $s_{ij}$ is the score of product $i$ on criterion $j$. The selected product is $P_{\text{selected}} = \arg\max_i S_i$.

**Application (4 Marks):** *(Same worked example as in Section 3.2 of these notes.)*
- 5 criteria, weights: 0.30, 0.20, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15
- Compute $S_A = 7.40$, $S_B = 7.65$, $S_C = 7.60$, $S_D = 6.75$
- Ranking: $B > C > A > D$
- **Selected Product = B** (highest composite score)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**TCO Components (3.5 Marks):**
1. **License Cost** — Per-sensor/per-EPS subscription.
2. **Hardware Cost** — Appliances, sensors, tap devices.
3. **Operational Cost** — Power, cooling, rack space.
4. **Personnel Cost** — SOC analyst training, certification (GCIH, GCFA).
5. **Maintenance & Upgrade Cost** — Annual support contracts, signature updates.

$$
TCO_{5\text{-yr}} = C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{hw}} + C_{\text{ops}} + C_{\text{train}} + C_{\text{maint}}
$$

**Regulatory Compliance in Indian Banking (3.5 Marks):**
- **RBI Cyber Security Framework (2016, updated 2023):** Mandates SOC, real-time monitoring, anomaly detection.
- **SEBI CSCRF:** For capital markets — requires log retention $\ge 7$ years.
- **PCI-DSS 4.0:** Network segmentation and IDPS at CDE boundary.
- **IT Act 2000 + DPDP Act 2023:** Data localization, breach reporting within **72 hours**.
- **ISO 27001 / SOC 2:** Requires documented IDPS policy, periodic audit, KPI reporting (MTTD $\le 15$ min, MTTR $\le 4$ hours for critical alerts).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Confusing IDPS with Firewall/Antivirus** — IDPS is a *monitoring & prevention* layer, not a perimeter filter. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize weights** — Always ensure $\sum w_j = 1$ in the WSM. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Not stating units** — Bandwidth must be in **Gbps**, latency in **ms**, event rate in **EPS**.
> 4. **Omitting compliance angle** — KTU frequently tests RBI/PCI-DSS/DPDP awareness.
> 5. **Skipping the PoC step** — Jumping directly from theory to selection is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Confusing $FPR$ with $FNR$** — $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$, NOT $FPR$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ IDPS selection is a **7–11 step lifecycle** (Requirements → Approach → Architecture → Scoring → PoC → Deploy → Tune).
- ✅ **Four detection approaches:** Signature, Anomaly, Stateful, Hybrid.
- ✅ **Five deployment architectures:** NIDPS, HIDPS, WIDPS, NBA, Cloud-Native.
- ✅ Key formulas: $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$, $F_1 = 2\cdot\dfrac{PR}{P+R}$, $S_i = \sum w_j s_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Bandwidth overhead for DPI = 20%** of link capacity.
- ✅ **MTTD** and **MTTR** are mandatory SOC KPIs.
- ✅ **RBI Cyber Security Framework, PCI-DSS 4.0, DPDP Act 2023** are must-cite Indian/global regulations.
- ✅ Always conduct a **PoC (30–60 days)** before final procurement.
- ✅ **Hybrid IDPS** is the most common enterprise choice for balanced accuracy and coverage.
- ✅ Use **STRIDE / PASTA** for threat modeling; **MCDA (Weighted Scoring)** for product ranking.
- ✅ For **OT/ICS** environments → prefer Anomaly-based; for **PCI environments** → prefer Signature-based.
- ✅ **Encrypted traffic analysis** is the modern trend (JA3 fingerprinting, ML on metadata).
- ✅ **Cloud-Native IDPS** (e.g., AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender) is mandatory for $>50\%$ cloud workloads.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strengths and Limitations of IDPSs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strengths and Limitations of IDPSs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is a security solution that monitors network traffic and/or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, and reconnaissance activities. The evaluation of any IDPS requires a balanced understanding of its **Strengths** (capabilities that make it indispensable) and **Limitations** (inherent weaknesses that prevent it from being a complete security solution on its own).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Under the PECCT742 2024 Scheme, students are explicitly tested on their ability to **critically evaluate** IDPS deployments. This means simply listing pros and cons is not enough; examiners expect an understanding of *why* each strength/limitation exists, *under what conditions* it applies, and *what compensating controls* can address limitations.

### Strengths of IDPS
**Strengths** refer to the inherent security benefits and operational advantages an IDPS brings to an organization's defense-in-depth strategy. These include improved detection coverage, forensic capabilities, policy enforcement, and proactive threat blocking.

### Limitations of IDPS
**Limitations** refer to the technical, operational, and architectural shortcomings that constrain an IDPS's effectiveness. These include false positives, encrypted traffic blind spots, evasion techniques, and high resource consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** An IDPS is a *deterrent and detective* control, not a foolproof prevention mechanism. Even an *Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)* that actively blocks traffic can be bypassed by sophisticated attackers. The "Prevention" in IPS refers to **automated blocking**, not guaranteed security.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a high-security office building with a **24x7 security control room (the IDPS)** equipped with CCTV cameras, motion sensors, and a guard who can lock doors remotely.

**The Strengths (What the Control Room Does Well):**
- The cameras see *everything* happening in the corridors and instantly alert the guard if someone climbs through a window (analogous to detecting port scans and exploits).
- The guard has an authoritative keycard and can slam a door shut the moment an unauthorized person approaches a sensitive server room (analogous to an IPS dropping malicious packets).
- The recordings are stored for 90 days, so if a theft happened last month, you can rewind and identify the culprit (analogous to forensic log retention).
- The guard follows a strict rulebook — if a person isn't wearing a badge, they are not allowed in (analogous to policy-based signature matching).

**The Limitations (Where the Control Room Fails):**
- The cameras **cannot see through walls**. If a thief is on the other side of a newly constructed false wall, the guard remains oblivious (analogous to **encrypted traffic blind spots**).
- The motion sensor sometimes triggers when a stray cat walks by, forcing the guard to investigate a non-threat (analogous to **false positives**).
- A clever thief wearing an employee uniform and carrying a legitimate access card will not raise any alarm — he is an *insider threat* (analogous to IDPS struggling with **insider attacks** and **zero-day exploits**).
- The control room itself is in the basement; if the power goes out, all cameras go dark (analogous to IDPS being a **single point of failure** if not properly redundant).
- The guard is so overwhelmed by hundreds of alerts every hour that he eventually starts ignoring them (analogous to **alert fatigue**).

> [!TIP]
> This analogy should be remembered because it directly mirrors the way KTU examiners frame theory questions. In the December 2023 university exam, a similar real-world analogy was used to test understanding of IDS limitations.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical IDPS Performance Metrics (Bolded for KTU Weightage):**
> - **True Positive Rate (TPR):** Proportion of actual attacks correctly identified. Target: **> 95%**.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Proportion of legitimate traffic falsely flagged. Target: **< 5%**.
> - **False Negative Rate (FNR):** Proportion of actual attacks missed. Target: **< 1%** (most critical).
> - **Detection Latency:** Time between attack initiation and IDPS alert. Target: **< 1 second** for inline IPS.
> - **Throughput:** Maximum traffic (in Gbps) the IDPS can inspect without dropping packets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Strengths of IDPSs

### Strength 1: Comprehensive Traffic Monitoring
- **Why:** IDPSs inspect every packet header and payload (for unencrypted traffic) across the entire network segment.
- **How:** Sensors placed at strategic choke points (e.g., perimeter, DMZ, server farm) provide a panoramic view of network activity.
- **Real-world utility:** Enables detection of reconnaissance, lateral movement, and data exfiltration attempts that bypass perimeter firewalls.

### Strength 2: Attack Detection and Blocking
- **Why:** Combines signature-based (known patterns) and anomaly-based (behavioral deviation) detection.
- **How:** Inline IPS can actively drop, reset, or quarantine malicious traffic in real time.
- **Real-world utility:** Stops worms (e.g., Code Red, Blaster), brute-force attempts, and C2 beaconing.

### Strength 3: Forensic Logging and Audit Trail
- **Why:** IDPSs maintain detailed logs of all detected events with timestamps, source/destination IPs, and packet payloads.
- **How:** Logs are stored in standardized formats (CEF, LEEF, syslog) for SIEM integration.
- **Real-world utility:** Critical for post-incident forensics, regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR), and legal evidence.

### Strength 4: Policy Enforcement and Compliance
- **Why:** Organizations can codify security policies (e.g., "no Telnet traffic on port 23") and enforce them automatically.
- **How:** Rule sets are pushed to sensors; violations trigger alerts or blocks.
- **Real-world utility:** Demonstrates due diligence to auditors and enforces least-privilege network usage.

### Strength 5: Scalability Across Network Topologies
- **Why:** Modern IDPSs support distributed deployments with central management consoles.
- **How:** Multiple sensors (NIDS) report to a central manager that correlates events enterprise-wide.
- **Real-world utility:** Suitable for multi-site enterprises, cloud environments, and OT/ICS networks.

### Strength 6: Integration with Security Ecosystem
- **Why:** IDPSs export data to SIEM, SOAR, ticketing systems, and threat intelligence platforms (TIPs).
- **How:** APIs (REST, STIX/TAXII) enable automated response workflows.
- **Real-world utility:** Reduces MTTD (Mean Time to Detect) and MTTR (Mean Time to Respond).

### Strength 7: Visibility into Encrypted Traffic (with SSL/TLS Inspection)
- **Why:** Modern IDPSs can decrypt TLS traffic (with man-in-the-middle certificate deployment) to inspect payloads.
- **How:** Acts as a TLS proxy, re-encrypting traffic to the destination.
- **Real-world utility:** Detects malware hiding in HTTPS, SMTPS, and DoH traffic.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Limitations of IDPSs

### Limitation 1: False Positives and Alert Fatigue
- **Why:** Anomaly-based detection often misclassifies legitimate but unusual behavior (e.g., a scheduled backup spike).
- **How:** Poorly tuned rules or insufficient baseline learning periods generate thousands of false alerts.
- **Real-world impact:** Analysts become desensitized; real attacks slip through unnoticed.

### Limitation 2: Inability to Inspect Strongly Encrypted Traffic
- **Why:** End-to-end encryption (TLS 1.3, E2EE messengers, IPsec VPN) hides payloads from IDPS inspection.
- **How:** Without SSL interception, IDPS only sees ciphertext and cannot apply signature matching.
- **Real-world impact:** Attackers increasingly tunnel exploits over HTTPS (e.g., Cobalt Strike over TLS).

### Limitation 3: Zero-Day and Polymorphic Attack Evasion
- **Why:** Signature-based engines cannot detect attacks that have no known pattern.
- **How:** Attackers use polymorphism, metamorphism, and packers to evade static signatures.
- **Real-world impact:** Zero-day exploits in browsers and applications often bypass IDPS entirely.

### Limitation 4: Encrypted Channel and Tunneling Evasion
- **Why:** Even with payload inspection, attackers hide commands inside allowed protocols (DNS tunneling, HTTP covert channels, ICMP tunneling).
- **How:** Packets are split, fragmented, or timed to avoid triggering thresholds.
- **Real-world impact:** Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) routinely use this technique.

### Limitation 5: High Resource Consumption and Performance Overhead
- **Why:** Deep packet inspection (DPI) at line rate is computationally expensive.
- **How:** Stateful tracking of millions of flows requires significant CPU, RAM, and NIC capacity.
- **Real-world impact:** Mis-sized IDPS becomes a network bottleneck, dropping legitimate packets.

### Limitation 6: Single Point of Failure (SPOF) for Inline IPS
- **Why:** Inline IPS sits in the data path; failure can disrupt network connectivity.
- **How:** Hardware failure, software crash, or misconfiguration can block all traffic.
- **Real-world impact:** Mission-critical networks require IPS bypass switches (fail-open/fail-closed).

### Limitation 7: Difficulty Detecting Insider Threats
- **Why:** IDPSs are optimized for external threats; insider abuse often mimics legitimate behavior.
- **How:** A privileged user accessing sensitive files at night may not trigger anomaly rules.
- **Real-world impact:** Insider attacks account for ~60% of breaches (Verizon DBIR).

### Limitation 8: Encrypted Credential Theft and Lateral Movement
- **Why:** Once an attacker has valid credentials, their traffic appears legitimate.
- **How:** Pass-the-hash, Kerberoasting, and stolen API keys bypass IDPS detection.
- **Real-world impact:** IDPS cannot distinguish between a real user and an attacker using stolen tokens.

### Limitation 9: Sophisticated Evasion Techniques
- **Why:** Tools like Fragroute, nmap with fragmentation options, and low-and-slow attacks.
- **How:** Packets are crafted to confuse stateful reassembly engines.
- **Real-world impact:** IDPS may see a different stream than the destination host.

### Limitation 10: Lack of Application-Layer Context
- **Why:** Network-based IDPS cannot understand business logic (e.g., "transferring \$10 million at 3 AM").
- **How:** Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) and UEBA are needed for application-level attacks.
- **Real-world impact:** Business logic attacks and API abuse are invisible to IDPS.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

| **Attribute** | **Signature-Based IDPS** | **Anomaly-Based IDPS** | **Stateful Protocol Analysis** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Basis** | Pattern matching against known attack signatures | Statistical deviation from baseline | Deviation from protocol specifications |
| **Strength 1** | High accuracy for known attacks | Can detect zero-day attacks | Detects protocol misuse |
| **Strength 2** | Low false positive rate | Adapts to network changes | Identifies malformed packets |
| **Limitation 1** | Cannot detect zero-day exploits | High false positive rate | Cannot detect content-based attacks |
| **Limitation 2** | Requires constant signature updates | Long training period required | Limited to protocol layer visibility |
| **CPU Cost** | Low | High | Medium |
| **KTU Weightage** | **High** | **Very High** | **Medium** |

| **Metric Category** | **Metric Name** | **Formula / Definition** | **Acceptable Range** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Accuracy | True Positive Rate (TPR) | $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $\geq 0.95$ |
| Detection Accuracy | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $\leq 0.05$ |
| Detection Accuracy | False Negative Rate (FNR) | $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $\leq 0.01$ |
| Performance | Throughput | $T = \frac{\text{Bytes Inspected}}{\text{Time (sec)}}$ | Matches link capacity (Gbps) |
| Performance | Detection Latency | $L = T_{\text{alert}} - T_{\text{attack}}$ | $\leq 1 \text{ s}$ (inline IPS) |
| Reliability | Availability | $A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 99.999\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The formula sheet above is exhaustive. KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions often test the *interpretation* of these formulas (e.g., "If an IDPS has 95 TPs, 5 FPs, 900 TNs, and 0 FNs, calculate the accuracy"). Use these as the standard reference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Strength-Limitation Trade-off Analysis

Since this is a theoretical topic, the "derivation" here is the **logical justification** of each strength and limitation, breaking down *why* it exists at the architectural level.

### Analytical Step 1: Strength of Attack Detection — Root Cause

$$\text{Detection Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of Attack Classes Detected}}{\text{Total Possible Attack Classes}} \times 100\%$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. IDPSs maintain a **signature database** with patterns like `alert tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 80 (msg:"SQL Injection Attempt"; content:"' OR '1'='1"; sid:1000001;)`.
2. When a packet's payload matches the `content` field, the IDPS raises an alert.
3. For anomaly detection, a **statistical model** $\mathcal{M}$ is trained on baseline traffic $B$.
4. New traffic $X$ is flagged if $P(X \mid \mathcal{M}) < \tau$ (threshold).

**Conversion logic:** The strength arises because the IDPS combines *deterministic* (signature) and *probabilistic* (anomaly) methods, providing layered detection.

---

### Analytical Step 2: Limitation of False Positives — Root Cause

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}, \quad \text{where } FP = \text{False Positives}, TN = \text{True Negatives}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. The baseline $B$ used for anomaly detection is collected over a **training window** (e.g., 14 days).
2. If the training window contains *unusual but legitimate* events (e.g., monthly backup spike, holiday traffic surge), these are absorbed into the baseline.
3. Future events that **deviate from the (now distorted) baseline** trigger false positives.

**Conversion logic:** The limitation exists because **network behavior is non-stationary**; legitimate activity evolves but the model does not.

---

### Analytical Step 3: Limitation of Encrypted Traffic Blind Spot — Root Cause

$$\text{Visibility Ratio} = \frac{\text{Unencrypted Bytes}}{\text{Total Bytes}} \times 100\%$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Modern web traffic uses **TLS 1.3** by default, which encrypts both the URL path and the body.
2. IDPS positioned at the network layer sees only: `IP header \vert TCP header \vert TLS record header \vert Encrypted ciphertext`.
3. The **ciphertext** appears as random bytes; signature matching is impossible.
4. The IDPS can only make decisions based on **metadata** (e.g., SNI in TLS ClientHello, certificate fingerprint, packet size, timing).

**Conversion logic:** The limitation exists because **end-to-end encryption is mathematically designed to be opaque to intermediate nodes**, and IDPS by definition is an intermediate node.

---

### Analytical Step 4: Limitation of Encrypted Channel Evasion (DNS Tunneling)

$$\text{Detection Confidence} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot \text{Entropy}(X)}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Normal DNS queries have **low entropy** (entropy $< 3.5$ bits/char for typical hostnames).
2. Tunneled DNS queries (e.g., `aGVsbG8gd29ybGQ.base32.tunnel.com`) have **high entropy** (entropy $> 4.5$ bits/char).
3. IDPS may not have a **dedicated DNS tunneling detection module**, so high-entropy DNS queries pass.
4. The command-and-control traffic exits the network disguised as legitimate DNS.

**Conversion logic:** The IDPS lacks **contextual analysis** of protocol-specific patterns unless explicitly configured.

---

### Analytical Step 5: Single Point of Failure Analysis for Inline IPS

$$\text{Network Availability} = 1 - P(\text{IPS Failure}) \times P(\text{Fail-Closed})$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Inline IPS sits **in series** with traffic: $\text{User} \rightarrow \text{IPS} \rightarrow \text{Server}$.
2. If the IPS crashes and is in **fail-open mode**: traffic flows unfiltered (security loss).
3. If the IPS crashes and is in **fail-closed mode**: traffic is blocked (availability loss).
4. Either outcome degrades the **CIA triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).

**Conversion logic:** Inline IPS introduces **availability risk** as a trade-off for inline prevention capability.

---

### Analytical Step 6: Insider Threat Detection Gap

$$\text{Insider Detection Score} = \frac{\text{Insider Alerts Generated}}{\text{Insider Incidents}} \times 100\%$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Insider abuse appears as **legitimate user activity** with valid credentials.
2. IDPS rules are designed to detect **external attack signatures**, not internal policy violations.
3. Without **User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA)**, IDPS cannot baseline per-user behavior.
4. Result: insider attacks have a **lower detection score** than external attacks.

**Conversion logic:** The IDPS's threat model is **perimeter-centric**, while insider threats are **trust-centric**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Strengths-Limitations Trade-off Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the architectural relationship between IDPS strengths and their corresponding limitations, mapped to the **defense-in-depth layers** they affect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IDPS Core Engine] --> B[Detection Layer]
    A --> C[Prevention Layer]
    A --> D[Logging Layer]

    B --> B1[Strength: Signature Matching]
    B --> B2[Strength: Anomaly Detection]
    B --> B3[Limitation: Zero-Day Blind Spot]
    B --> B4[Limitation: Encrypted Traffic Blind Spot]

    C --> C1[Strength: Inline Packet Drop]
    C --> C2[Strength: TCP Reset Injection]
    C --> C3[Limitation: Single Point of Failure]
    C --> C4[Limitation: Evasion via Fragmentation]

    D --> D1[Strength: Forensic Log Retention]
    D --> D2[Strength: SIEM Integration]
    D --> D3[Limitation: Log Storage Cost]
    D --> D4[Limitation: Encrypted Payload Storage]

    B1 --> E1[Low False Positives for Known Threats]
    B2 --> E2[Detects Novel Attacks]
    B3 --> E3[Misses Polymorphic Malware]
    B4 --> E4[TLS 1.3 Opaque Payloads]

    C1 --> F1[Real-time Threat Blocking]
    C2 --> F2[Session Termination]
    C3 --> F3[Availability Risk if Hardware Fails]
    C4 --> F4[Fragroute Evasion Success]

    D1 --> G1[Post-Incident Investigation]
    D2 --> G2[Correlated Threat Intelligence]
    D3 --> G3[High CAPEX/OPEX]
    D4 --> G4[Cannot Decrypt for Long-term Storage]
```

---

## 4.2 IDPS Operational Workflow with Failure Points

The following diagram maps the **packet processing pipeline** within an IDPS, highlighting the specific points where each limitation manifests.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Incoming Packet] --> S1{Packet Capture}
    S1 --> S2{Header Inspection}
    S2 --> S3{Payload Inspection}
    S3 --> S4{Stateful Reassembly}
    S4 --> S5{Signature Match}
    S5 --> S6{Anomaly Scoring}
    S6 --> D{Decision Engine}

    D -->|Allow| OUT1[Forward to Destination]
    D -->|Drop| OUT2[Log and Drop]
    D -->|Reset| OUT3[Send TCP RST]
    D -->|Alert| OUT4[Log and Notify]

    S1 -.->|Limitation: NIC Overrun| L1[Packet Loss at High Throughput]
    S3 -.->|Limitation: TLS Blind| L2[Cannot Inspect Encrypted Payload]
    S4 -.->|Limitation: Fragmentation| L3[Evasion via IP Fragments]
    S5 -.->|Limitation: Zero-Day| L4[No Match for Novel Signature]
    S6 -.->|Limitation: Baseline Drift| L5[False Positives]
    D -.->|Limitation: SPOF| L6[Inline Crash Stops Traffic]
```

---

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Mapping of IDPS Strengths/Limitations

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Perimeter
        P1[Firewall]
        P2[IDPS Sensor]
        P3[Limitation: Encrypted Bypass]
    end

    subgraph Internal
        I1[Internal NIDS]
        I2[Host IPS]
        I3[Limitation: Insider Blind]
    end

    subgraph Application
        A1[WAF]
        A2[IDPS Application Plugin]
        A3[Limitation: No Business Logic Context]
    end

    subgraph Data
        D1[DLP]
        D2[IDPS Log Forwarder]
        D3[Limitation: Encrypted Data Exfil Invisible]
    end

    P2 --> I1
    I1 --> A1
    A1 --> D1
    P3 -.->|Mitigated by| I1
    I3 -.->|Mitigated by| A1
    A3 -.->|Mitigated by| D1
    D3 -.->|Mitigated by| D1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"List any THREE strengths and THREE limitations of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System."**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

**Strengths:**
1. **Comprehensive Monitoring:** Provides visibility into network and host activity across all traffic flows.
2. **Active Prevention:** Inline IPS can drop malicious packets in real time, blocking attacks before they reach the target.
3. **Forensic Logging:** Maintains detailed audit trails for post-incident analysis and regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA).

**Limitations:**
1. **Encrypted Traffic Blind Spot:** Cannot inspect TLS 1.3 / E2EE payloads without SSL interception, hiding malware in HTTPS.
2. **False Positives:** Anomaly-based engines frequently misclassify legitimate traffic, causing alert fatigue.
3. **Zero-Day Evasion:** Signature-based engines cannot detect novel, previously unseen attacks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Explain why IDPSs are considered a single point of failure in inline deployments. What is the role of a bypass switch?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):**

In an **inline IPS deployment**, the IDPS sits in series between the source and destination. Any hardware failure, software crash, or misconfiguration can disrupt all traffic flow, making it a **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)**. To mitigate this, organizations deploy a **bypass switch** (fail-open or fail-closed TAP) that physically reroutes traffic around the IDPS upon failure. A **fail-open** switch preserves availability at the cost of security during the outage, while a **fail-closed** switch preserves security but breaks connectivity. Bypass switches are therefore a critical compensating control.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do NOT list strengths/limitations without explaining *why* they exist. Mere lists fetch 1–2 marks out of 7. Examiners want **architectural reasoning**.
> - Do NOT confuse **false positive (FP)** with **false negative (FN)**. FPs are benign traffic flagged as malicious; FNs are real attacks missed. Mixing these up is the most common mistake.
> - Always mention **compensating controls** (e.g., SIEM, UEBA, WAF) when discussing limitations. Showing awareness of *defense-in-depth* is rewarded.

---

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]
**(a)** Discuss in detail the **strengths of IDPSs**. Explain how each strength contributes to an organization's overall security posture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically evaluate the **limitations of IDPSs** with respect to encrypted traffic, false positives, and zero-day attacks. Suggest compensating controls for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — Strengths of IDPSs (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) is a critical component of the **defense-in-depth** strategy, providing visibility, detection, and automated response capabilities that complement traditional firewalls.

**Detailed Strengths (6 marks):**

1. **Real-time Threat Detection and Blocking (2 marks):**
   - IDPSs combine **signature-based detection** (Snort rules, Suricata rules) with **anomaly-based detection** (statistical baselines, ML models).
   - In **inline IPS mode**, malicious packets can be dropped before reaching the target, providing proactive prevention rather than passive detection.
   - Example: An IPS can detect and block the EternalBlue SMB exploit (CVE-2017-0144) at the network layer, preventing the WannaCry ransomware outbreak.

2. **Comprehensive Network Visibility (1 mark):**
   - IDPS sensors placed at choke points (perimeter, DMZ, internal segments) provide a panoramic view of all east-west and north-south traffic.
   - This visibility extends to **reconnaissance activity** (port scans, ping sweeps) that firewalls typically permit.

3. **Forensic Logging and Compliance (1 mark):**
   - IDPSs generate timestamped, structured logs in formats like **CEF, LEEF, and syslog**.
   - These logs are critical for **post-incident forensics**, root cause analysis, and compliance with regulations like **PCI-DSS Requirement 10**, **HIPAA Security Rule**, and **GDPR Article 32**.

4. **Policy Enforcement (1 mark):**
   - Security policies (e.g., "block all BitTorrent traffic," "alert on unencrypted FTP credentials") are encoded as rules.
   - This automates policy compliance and reduces reliance on manual monitoring.

5. **Integration with Security Ecosystem (1 mark):**
   - Modern IDPSs integrate with **SIEM (Splunk, QRadar)**, **SOAR (Cortex XSOAR)**, and **Threat Intelligence Platforms (MISP, Anomali)** via APIs.
   - This enables **automated incident response workflows** that drastically reduce Mean Time to Respond (MTTR).

**Conclusion:**
The combined strengths make IDPS an indispensable layer in modern security architectures, though it must be deployed alongside other controls (firewalls, EDR, WAF) for full coverage.

#### Model Answer for (b) — Limitations of IDPSs (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Despite their capabilities, IDPSs have inherent limitations rooted in their **architectural design** and the **evolving threat landscape**. Understanding these limitations is essential for designing effective compensating controls.

**Detailed Limitations with Compensating Controls (6 marks):**

1. **Encrypted Traffic Blind Spot (2 marks):**
   - **Problem:** Modern web traffic uses **TLS 1.3**, which encrypts both URL paths and payloads. IDPS sees only metadata (SNI, certificate fingerprint, JA3 hash). Attackers exploit this by tunneling malware over HTTPS (e.g., Cobalt Strike over TLS, Emotet C2 over HTTPS).
   - **Compensating Control:** Deploy **SSL/TLS Inspection (MITM)** with trusted root CA distribution; supplement with **JA3/JA3S fingerprinting** for C2 detection; use **EDR** for endpoint-level visibility.

2. **False Positives and Alert Fatigue (2 marks):**
   - **Problem:** Anomaly-based detection generates false positives when legitimate but unusual traffic (e.g., nightly backups, software updates) deviates from the baseline. Analysts become desensitized, missing real alerts ("cry wolf syndrome").
   - **Compensating Control:** Implement **SIEM-based correlation** to group related alerts; use **UEBA** to baseline per-user behavior; conduct **regular rule tuning** and **suppression lists**; deploy **SOAR** to auto-triage low-confidence alerts.

3. **Zero-Day Attack Evasion (2 marks):**
   - **Problem:** Signature-based engines cannot detect attacks without known patterns. **Polymorphic malware** and **metamorphic code** change their signatures on each execution, evading static detection. The **Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)** zero-day was a stark example.
   - **Compensating Control:** Use **sandboxing** (FireEye, Joe Sandbox) for dynamic analysis; deploy **EDR with behavioral analysis**; subscribe to **threat intelligence feeds** for IOC updates; implement **virtual patching** via WAF rules.

**Conclusion:**
These limitations do not diminish the value of IDPS but emphasize that **no single control is sufficient**. A mature security program integrates IDPS with EDR, NDR, WAF, SIEM, and UEBA to achieve defense-in-depth.

---

### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**(a)** Compare and contrast the **strengths and limitations of signature-based IDPSs vs. anomaly-based IDPSs** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **operational workflow of an inline IPS**, highlighting at least **four failure points** where its limitations manifest. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — Signature vs. Anomaly-Based IDPS (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
IDPSs employ two primary detection paradigms: **signature-based detection** and **anomaly-based detection**. Each has distinct strengths and limitations that make them complementary in practice.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Signature-Based IDPS** | **Anomaly-Based IDPS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Basis** | Pattern matching against a database of known attack signatures | Statistical or ML-based deviation from a learned baseline |
| **Strength 1: Accuracy for Known Threats** | **Very high** (e.g., detects SQLi patterns like `' OR 1=1 --` reliably) | Moderate (may flag unusual but legitimate behavior) |
| **Strength 2: False Positives** | **Low** (specific patterns rarely match benign traffic) | **High** (anomalies are not always malicious) |
| **Strength 3: Zero-Day Detection** | **Poor** (no signature = no detection) | **Good** (can flag behaviorally unusual activity) |
| **Strength 4: Setup Complexity** | Simple (just load rules) | Complex (requires training period) |
| **Limitation 1: Maintenance** | Requires constant **signature updates** | Requires **baseline retraining** as network evolves |
| **Limitation 2: Encrypted Traffic** | Cannot match encrypted payloads | Can detect anomalous encrypted traffic metadata (e.g., JA3) |
| **Limitation 3: Resource Usage** | Low CPU (string matching) | High CPU (statistical analysis) |
| **Example Tools** | Snort, Suricata | Zeek, Darktrace, Vectra AI |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
A hybrid approach combining both methods (signature + anomaly) is the industry best practice, as seen in modern tools like **Cisco Firepower** and **Palo Alto Networks Cortex**. Signature detection provides precision for known threats, while anomaly detection provides breadth for novel threats. Neither alone is sufficient; together they form a robust detection fabric.

#### Model Answer for (b) — Inline IPS Workflow and Failure Points (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Traffic] --> B[Bypass Switch]
    B --> C[IPS Inline Sensor]
    C --> D{Packet Inspection}
    D -->|Normal| E[Server]
    D -->|Malicious| F[Drop and Log]
    D -->|Suspicious| G[Alert and Quarantine]
    C -.->|Failure| H[Bypass Mode: Fail-Open or Fail-Closed]
    H --> E
```

**Workflow Steps (1.5 marks):**
1. **Traffic Interception:** Bypass switch forwards traffic to IPS in normal operation.
2. **Packet Capture:** NIC captures the packet at line rate.
3. **Header Inspection:** Parses L2/L3/L4 headers.
4. **Payload Inspection:** Applies DPI and signature matching.
5. **Stateful Reassembly:** Reconstructs TCP streams to detect multi-packet attacks.
6. **Decision Engine:** Allows, drops, resets, or alerts based on rule matches.

**Four Failure Points (1.5 marks):**
1. **NIC Overrun:** At 10 Gbps+ throughput, the IPS may drop packets, missing attacks.
2. **Encrypted Payload:** TLS 1.3 traffic bypasses DPI, allowing C2 over HTTPS.
3. **Fragmentation Evasion:** Crafted IP fragments confuse reassembly, allowing shellcode delivery.
4. **Inline Hardware Failure:** IPS crash triggers bypass mode; in **fail-open** state, no inspection occurs until recovery.

**Conclusion:**
Inline IPS is a powerful but fragile control requiring redundant bypass switches, regular rule updates, and integration with NDR and EDR for comprehensive coverage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Strengths and Limitations of IDPSs:**

### Key Strengths (Remember the 7 S's)
- **S**ignature-based and **A**nomaly-based detection (combined)
- **C**omprehensive network visibility
- **A**ctive prevention (inline drop/reset)
- **F**orensic logging and compliance
- **P**olicy enforcement automation
- **S**calability (distributed sensors)
- **I**ntegration (SIEM, SOAR, TIP)

### Key Limitations (Remember the 7 E's)
- **E**ncrypted traffic blind spots (TLS 1.3)
- **F**alse positives causing alert fatigue
- **Z**ero-day and polymorphic evasion
- **T**unneling evasion (DNS, ICMP, HTTP)
- **H**igh resource consumption (DPI overhead)
- **S**ingle point of failure (inline crash)
- **I**nsider threat detection gap

### Critical Formulas to Memorize
- $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$
- $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$

### Compensating Controls (Pair Each Limitation with a Control)
- Encrypted traffic → **SSL Inspection + JA3 fingerprinting**
- False positives → **SIEM correlation + SOAR auto-triage**
- Zero-day → **Sandboxing + EDR + Threat Intel**
- Insider threat → **UEBA + DLP**
- SPOF → **Bypass switch (fail-open/closed)**
- Fragmentation → **Normalization and reassembly tuning**

### Last-Minute Exam Tips
- Always mention **defense-in-depth** in conclusion — it shows examiner you understand IDPS is one layer, not the whole defense.
- Use the **security guard analogy** in theory questions for quick, high-scoring answers.
- Quote **CVE numbers** (EternalBlue CVE-2017-0144, Log4Shell CVE-2021-44228) to demonstrate practical awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDPS Control Strategies - Centralized Control Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** IDPS Control Strategies - Centralized Control Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Centralized Control Strategy** in the context of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) is a control architecture in which **all sensing, detection, analysis, and response decisions are coordinated, configured, and supervised from a single, central management console (often called the Central Management Server or CMS)**. Distributed IDPS agents or sensors deployed across the network segments function merely as data collectors and policy executors — they have no autonomous decision-making authority and rely on the central console to receive policy updates, signature databases, correlation rules, and response commands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the Centralized Control Strategy, the "intelligence" of the IDPS — signature database, anomaly profiles, correlation rules, thresholds, and response actions — resides at the **central node**. Remote sensors are essentially "thin clients" that forward logs/alerts to the central site for unified analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box:**
> - **Central Management Server (CMS):** The single authoritative node that holds policies, signatures, and aggregated event data.
> - **IDPS Agent/Sensor:** A lightweight probe deployed at the network/host endpoint that observes traffic or system calls and forwards events to the CMS.
> - **Control Channel:** The secure, dedicated communication path between agents and the CMS used for policy push and event pull.
> - **Single Point of Failure (SPOF):** The architectural weakness where failure of the CMS halts the entire IDPS operation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a Centralized Traffic Control Tower at a busy airport.**

Imagine an international airport with **ten runways** spread across the city. Each runway has its own small observation camera that can only see what is happening on that specific strip of tarmac. But the cameras cannot independently decide which aircraft to allow to land or take off. Instead, **all cameras continuously stream live footage to ONE giant control tower in the middle of the airport**. The air-traffic controllers sitting in that tower:

1. **Watch all ten runways from one place** (unified visibility).
2. **Issue landing/takeoff clearances** back to each runway (centralized command).
3. **Update the flight rules** (policies) from one location.
4. **Sound alarms** if anything unusual happens anywhere in the airport.

Now map this to an IDPS:

| Airport Analogy | IDPS Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Runways | Network segments / host systems |
| Observation cameras | IDPS sensors/agents |
| Control Tower | Central Management Server (CMS) |
| Live footage stream | Network packets / system logs forwarded upstream |
| Clearances back to runways | Policy updates & response commands pushed downstream |
| Alarm siren | Alert generation & automated blocking |

**In one sentence:** *A Centralized Control Strategy is the airport-tower model of IDPS — observe everywhere, but think and decide in one place.*

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Associated With This Architecture

The following metrics are routinely used to characterize and evaluate a Centralized IDPS:

- **Mean Time To Detection (MTTD):** Average delay between an attack occurring and the CMS raising an alert. Typical range: **seconds to minutes**.
- **Mean Time To Response (MTTR):** Average delay between detection and the CMS issuing a block/prevent action. Typical range: **sub-second to minutes**.
- **Event Throughput:** Number of events/second the CMS can correlate. Modern enterprise CMS targets **> 100,000 EPS (Events Per Second)**.
- **Control-Channel Bandwidth:** Sustained bandwidth consumed by the encrypted policy/event channel (commonly **1–100 Mbps** depending on log verbosity).
- **Availability (A):** Probability the CMS is reachable. For mission-critical deployments, target **A ≥ 0.999 (Three-Nines)**.
- **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** Maximum tolerable outage of the CMS before security posture is compromised. Common target: **≤ 15 minutes**.

These metrics are explicitly mentioned in the KTU 2024 PECCT742 module outcomes for Module 2 (CO2: *Analyze the architecture and control strategies of modern IDPS*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Centralized Control Strategy

The strategy operates as a continuous **closed-loop cycle**. Below is the structured logic flow:

1. **Policy Authoring (Central Site Only)**
   - Security administrators define detection signatures, anomaly thresholds, and response actions exclusively on the CMS.
   - Why central? Ensures a **single, authoritative security policy** with zero ambiguity across geographically dispersed segments.

2. **Policy Distribution (Push Downward)**
   - The CMS pushes the latest policy set, signature database, and configuration to every registered agent over a secure TLS/IPsec channel.
   - How? Using a publish–subscribe or push model (e.g., agents periodically poll the CMS, or CMS pushes via long-lived TLS sessions).

3. **Local Sensing (Distributed, Lightweight)**
   - Agents deployed on host machines (HIDS agents) or at strategic chokepoints (NIDS sensors on SPAN/mirror ports) capture traffic or system calls.
   - Agents perform **minimal local pre-processing** — typically just normalization and timestamping — to reduce upstream data volume.

4. **Event Forwarding (Pulled Upward)**
   - Raw or pre-processed events are streamed upstream to the CMS over the control channel.
   - To preserve bandwidth, most vendors ship agents that perform **local pre-correlation** (filtering out known-benign traffic) before forwarding.

5. **Central Correlation & Decision**
   - The CMS correlates events from multiple agents using rule engines, statistical baselines, or machine-learning models.
   - Final verdict (true-positive/false-positive) and response action are decided here.

6. **Response Command Push**
   - The CMS dispatches response actions back to the originating agent — block IP, kill process, quarantine file, quarantine host, etc.
   - This "control back to the agent" step is what makes the loop *closed* and qualifies the strategy as true **Control** (not just monitoring).

7. **Logging, Archival & Forensics**
   - All raw events and decisions are persisted in the CMS database for compliance, audit, and post-incident forensic analysis.

---

### 2.2 Why "Centralized"? — The 'Why' Behind the Design

| Design Goal | How Centralization Achieves It |
|---|---|
| **Unified situational awareness** | All event streams converge to one console — the analyst sees the entire enterprise on one dashboard. |
| **Policy consistency** | No risk of two sensors disagreeing on what constitutes an attack. |
| **Reduced agent footprint** | Agents stay lightweight (low CPU/RAM), suitable for embedded devices and IoT segments. |
| **Easier compliance reporting** | One audit log, one place to generate PCI-DSS / ISO-27001 reports. |
| **Lower per-agent cost** | The expensive analytics engine runs on a few powerful servers, not on every endpoint. |

---

### 2.3 The 'How' — Communication & Synchronization Mechanisms

- **Synchronous Agent Registration:** Each agent must be enrolled/registered with the CMS before it can receive policy or send events. This is typically done via a one-time out-of-band registration (certificate or pre-shared key).
- **Heartbeat Polling:** Agents send periodic *heartbeats* (e.g., every 30 s) to the CMS to prove liveness. If the CMS misses N consecutive heartbeats from an agent, it flags that agent as **offline**.
- **Pull vs. Push Event Models:**
  - *Pull:* CMS periodically fetches buffered events from the agent.
  - *Push:* Agent streams events in near real-time (preferred for low-latency detection).
- **Fail-Open vs. Fail-Closed Agent Behaviour:** When the control channel to the CMS is lost, agents may either (a) **fail open** — allow all traffic, or (b) **fail closed** — block all traffic. The KTU-recommended default for prevention systems is **fail closed** with a local cached policy snapshot.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield analytical formulas you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE numerical/analytical questions on this topic.** All cells use `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe character to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Each Symbol | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | A = Availability, MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | A is dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $MTTD = t_{alert} - t_{attack}$ | Time between attack and alert | seconds |
| 3 | $MTTR = t_{response} - t_{alert}$ | Time between alert and response action | seconds |
| 4 | $EPS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EPS_{sensor,i}$ | Aggregate events/sec arriving at CMS from n sensors | events/sec |
| 5 | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{D_{agent-CMS}}{v_{link}}$ | Propagation delay on control channel; D = distance, v = signal velocity in medium | seconds |
| 6 | $B_{req} \geq EPS_{total} \times S_{avg}$ | Minimum bandwidth; $S_{avg}$ = average event size in bytes | bits/sec |
| 7 | $N_{fail} = 1 - (1 - p)^{k}$ | Probability at least one CMS replica fails; p = per-replica failure prob., k = replicas | dimensionless |
| 8 | $RTO_{centralized} \geq RTO_{distributed}$ | Centralized architectures have longer recovery times than distributed ones | minutes |
| 9 | $C_{single} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^{k} T_{j,cms}$ | Average CMS computation latency across k events | milliseconds |
| 10 | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate — central analysis aims to minimize this | 0 to 1 |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Centralized Control Strategies are **the dominant deployment model in modern enterprise SIEM/IDPS suites** such as:
- **Splunk Enterprise Security** (central indexer + universal forwarders),
- **IBM QRadar** (central event processor + distributed flow/proxy collectors),
- **Cisco Firepower Management Center (FMC)** (central management of distributed Firepower sensors),
- **Snort** with a central Barnyard2/Snorby console.

In each of these, the architecture you studied as "Centralized Control Strategy" is what the vendors actually ship. Recognizing this pattern lets a KTU engineer walk into any SOC (Security Operations Center) and immediately understand its topology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — CMS Availability Under Centralized Architecture

In a Centralized Control Strategy the **entire detection capability** of the enterprise depends on the CMS being up. Let us derive the steady-state availability of the IDPS under various deployment variants.

**Step 1 — Define availability.** Availability is the long-run fraction of time the system is operational:

$$A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{\text{Uptime in } [0, T]}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Model with exponential failure/repair times.** For repairable systems the standard assumption is exponentially distributed failure and repair times. In that case availability simplifies to:

$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 3 — Plug in typical KTU textbook values.** Suppose MTBF = 1000 hours and MTTR = 1 hour (i.e., repair in 1 hour). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{1000}{1000 + 1} \\
  &= \dfrac{1000}{1001} \\
  &\approx 0.999001 \\
  &\approx 99.9001\%
\end{aligned}$$

This is "Three-Nines" availability. **This is the typical contracted SLA for enterprise CMS hardware.**

**Step 4 — Discuss what this means.** A 99.9% availability means roughly 43.2 minutes of downtime per month. For a Centralized IDPS, **every one of those 43 minutes is a window of zero enterprise-wide detection**. This is the core architectural weakness.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bandwidth Requirement for the Control Channel

To dimension the control channel between agents and the CMS, we must compute the bandwidth requirement.

**Step 1 — Define EPS.** Each agent i produces $EPS_i$ events per second.

**Step 2 — Aggregate EPS at the CMS.** For $n$ agents:

$$EPS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EPS_i$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by average event size.** If each event is $S_{avg}$ bytes on the wire:

$$B_{req} = EPS_{total} \times S_{avg} \times 8 \quad [\text{bits/sec}]$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style).** Suppose an enterprise has 50 IDPS agents, each generating 2000 events/sec, and the average event size (after normalization) is 256 bytes.

$$\begin{aligned}
EPS_{total} &= 50 \times 2000 \\
            &= 100{,}000 \text{ events/sec} \\
B_{req}     &= 100{,}000 \times 256 \times 8 \\
            &= 204{,}800{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} \\
            &= 204.8 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the CMS must receive a sustained **~205 Mbps** control-channel stream. This is why high-end CMS deployments use **10 Gbps NICs** with redundancy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of CMS Failure With Redundancy (k Replicas)

To mitigate the SPOF problem, a Centralized strategy is often deployed with **k redundant CMS nodes** behind a load balancer.
