**Step 1 — Define per-replica failure probability.** Let each replica fail in a given window with probability $p$.

**Step 2 — Probability that *all* replicas are up.**

$$P_{all\_up} = (1 - p)^{k}$$

**Step 3 — Probability that *at least one* replica has failed** (causing reduced performance):

$$N_{fail} = 1 - (1 - p)^{k}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** With $k = 2$ replicas and $p = 0.05$:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{fail} &= 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{2} \\
         &= 1 - (0.95)^{2} \\
         &= 1 - 0.9025 \\
         &= 0.0975
\end{aligned}$$

Compare with a single CMS (k = 1):

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{fail} &= 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{1} \\
         &= 1 - 0.95 \\
         &= 0.05
\end{aligned}$$

**Counter-intuitive result:** With 2 replicas, the probability of *at least one* failing is actually *higher* (9.75%) than with a single replica (5%). This is expected — two components fail more often than one. The relevant metric is the probability of **complete system failure**, which *does* drop. For 2 replicas:

$$P_{total\_fail} = p^{2} = 0.05^{2} = 0.0025 = 0.25\%$$

Down from 5% to 0.25% — a **20× improvement** in *total* outage probability.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Centralized vs. Other Strategies (preview for Module 2)

| Property | Centralized | Hierarchical | Fully Distributed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decision authority | Single CMS | Tiered (regional + central) | Each agent decides |
| SPOF risk | High | Medium | Low |
| Scalability | Limited by CMS | Better | Best |
| Policy consistency | Best | Good | Weakest |
| Agent complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Bandwidth to core | High | Medium | Low |
| Latency for response | Highest | Medium | Lowest |

This matrix is repeatedly tested in KTU Module-2 examinations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Centralized Control Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid block below renders the **logical data-flow topology** of a Centralized Control Strategy. It is not a physical patch-panel diagram; per the KTU-Premier-Engine rule, we substitute a functional architecture flow for complex physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EDGE["NETWORK EDGE AND HOST SEGMENTS"]
        AG1["IDPS Agent A\nHost Segment 1\nHIDS probe"]
        AG2["IDPS Agent B\nHost Segment 2\nHIDS probe"]
        AG3["Sensor C\nDMZ NIDS probe"]
        AG4["Sensor D\nServer Farm NIDS probe"]
        AG5["Sensor E\nOT and IoT probe"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CENTRAL CONTROL TIER"]
        LB["Load Balancer\nFront End"]
        CMS1["Central Management Server\nPrimary Node"]
        CMS2["Central Management Server\nHot Standby"]
        DB["Event and Policy Database"]
        CON["Security Analyst Console\nGUI and Dashboards"]
    end

    subgraph RESP["RESPONSE SUBSYSTEMS"]
        FW["Firewall API\nBlock IP or Port"]
        NAC["NAC Quarantine\nSwitch VLAN change"]
        TI["Threat Intel Push\nTo all agents"]
    end

    AG1 -->|event stream| LB
    AG2 -->|event stream| LB
    AG3 -->|event stream| LB
    AG4 -->|event stream| LB
    AG5 -->|event stream| LB

    LB --> CMS1
    LB --> CMS2
    CMS1 <--> DB
    CMS2 <--> DB
    CMS1 --> CON
    CMS2 --> CON

    AG1 -. policy push .-> CMS1
    AG2 -. policy push .-> CMS1
    AG3 -. policy push .-> CMS1
    AG4 -. policy push .-> CMS1
    AG5 -. policy push .-> CMS1

    CMS1 -->|block command| FW
    CMS1 -->|quarantine command| NAC
    CMS1 -->|signature update| TI
```

---

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Operational Cycle of Centralized Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1\nPolicy Authoring\nat CMS"] --> B["Step 2\nPolicy Push Down\nto all Agents"]
    B --> C["Step 3\nLocal Sensing\nby each Agent"]
    C --> D["Step 4\nEvent Forward Up\nto CMS"]
    D --> E["Step 5\nCentral Correlation\nand Decision"]
    E --> F["Step 6\nResponse Push Down\nfrom CMS"]
    F --> G["Step 7\nLogging and Audit\nat CMS"]
    G --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading tip for KTU exams:** The arrows in this loop are *bidirectional in time* but *unidirectional in authority* — the CMS always pushes, the agents always pull. If a student writes "agents decide responses" in the exam, marks will be deducted.

---

### 4.3 Failure-Mode Topology — Where the Single Point of Failure Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AG1["Agent A"] -->|healthy| CMS["CENTRAL MANAGEMENT SERVER\nTHE SINGLE POINT OF FAILURE"]
    AG2["Agent B"] -->|healthy| CMS
    AG3["Agent C"] -->|healthy| CMS
    AG4["Agent D"] -->|healthy| CMS

    CMS -. if CMS fails .-> X["All Agents Become Blind\nFail Open or Fail Closed\nper local policy cache"]

    style CMS fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#660000
    style X fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#664400
```

The red-highlighted node is the **architectural liability** of any Centralized Control Strategy. KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask students to identify this node and propose mitigations (clustering, geo-redundancy, hybrid hierarchical, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[Q.A1 — 3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> Define *Centralized Control Strategy* in the context of an IDPS. List any two of its advantages.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Centralized Control Strategy** is an IDPS architecture in which all sensors/agents deployed across the network are managed, configured, and controlled from a single Central Management Server (CMS). The CMS holds the policy database, signature database, and correlation engine; remote agents act only as data collectors and policy executors.

*Two advantages (1.5 marks each):*
1. **Unified visibility & policy consistency** — a single console monitors the entire enterprise, ensuring no conflicting detection rules across segments.
2. **Lightweight agents** — expensive analytics run on the CMS only, allowing agents to be deployed on low-resource hosts/IoT devices.

---

**[Q.A2 — 3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> What is meant by the term *Single Point of Failure* in a Centralized IDPS architecture? State one mitigation technique.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** in a Centralized IDPS is the Central Management Server itself — if the CMS crashes or becomes unreachable, the entire IDPS loses coordination, even if every agent is still running.

*Mitigation (1.5 marks):* Deploy the CMS as a **redundant cluster** (e.g., active-passive hot-standby or active-active with a load balancer) so that if one node fails, another seamlessly takes over.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Centralized Control" with "Centralized Logging."** Centralized logging only aggregates events for review; centralized *control* means the CMS also *issues response commands*. Many students write only about log forwarding and lose 4 marks.
> 2. **Always mention the SPOF problem and a mitigation.** Examiners specifically look for the candidate to recognize that "centralized" implies a single failure point.
> 3. **Use the word *agent* correctly.** Agents are *sensors plus local executors*, not mere log forwarders. If you say "agent just sends logs," you will lose 2 marks on the response-action sub-part.
> 4. **In numerical bandwidth questions, show the EPS summation step explicitly.** Skipping straight to "B = 205 Mbps" without showing $EPS_{total}$ loses 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Centralized Control Strategy for an IDPS with the help of a labeled block diagram. Clearly identify the role of (i) the Central Management Server, (ii) the IDPS agents, and (iii) the control channel.

**(b) [7 Marks)** An enterprise deploys 40 IDPS agents in a Centralized Control Strategy. Each agent generates, on average, 1500 events/sec, and each event averages 300 bytes after normalization. Compute (i) the aggregate events per second at the CMS, and (ii) the minimum control-channel bandwidth required, in Mbps. State one engineering consequence if this bandwidth is underestimated.

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The student must draw:

- Multiple agents feeding into a single CMS
- The CMS connected to a database and an analyst console
- A bidirectional control channel between CMS and agents

**Role description (4 marks, ~1.33 each):**

1. **Central Management Server (CMS):** Hosts the policy engine, signature database, and correlation rules. It receives events from all agents, performs centralized analysis, decides whether the event stream constitutes an attack, and issues response commands (block, quarantine, alert) back to the relevant agent(s).

2. **IDPS Agents:** Deployed at network segments (NIDS) or on hosts (HIDS). They capture traffic/logs, normalize them, optionally perform local pre-filtering, and forward events upstream. They also receive and apply policy/response commands from the CMS.

3. **Control Channel:** A secure, encrypted logical channel (typically TLS over TCP) that carries (i) policy/signature pushes from CMS → agent, and (ii) event streams from agent → CMS. Heartbeats are also exchanged on this channel to confirm liveness.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Aggregate EPS (3 marks):**

$$EPS_{total} = n \times EPS_{per\_agent}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
EPS_{total} &= 40 \times 1500 \\
            &= 60{,}000 \text{ events/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Minimum bandwidth (3 marks):**

$$B_{req} = EPS_{total} \times S_{avg} \times 8$$

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{req} &= 60{,}000 \times 300 \times 8 \\
        &= 144{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} \\
        &= 144 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering consequence (1 mark):** If the bandwidth is underestimated, the control channel will suffer congestion and packet drops. As a result, (i) events will be lost at the CMS, leading to *missed detections*, and (ii) policy updates will be delayed, leaving agents running stale signatures — a serious security risk.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the Centralized Control Strategy with the Distributed Control Strategy in terms of (i) scalability, (ii) single-point-of-failure risk, (iii) policy consistency, and (iv) agent complexity. Tabulate your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Centralized IDPS has its CMS deployed as an active-passive cluster of 2 nodes. The per-node probability of failure in a 30-day window is $p = 0.04$. Compute (i) the probability that *both* nodes fail simultaneously in that window, and (ii) the resulting availability improvement over a single-node CMS deployment. State the key limitation of the Centralized model that this clustering only partially addresses.

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (Tabulated Comparison):**

| Property | Centralized Control | Distributed Control |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Scalability | Limited by CMS throughput (memory, CPU, NIC bandwidth). Adding more agents eventually saturates the CMS. | Scales horizontally — adding more agents does not strain a single central node. |
| (ii) SPOF risk | High — CMS failure halts the entire enterprise IDPS. | Low — each agent can make local decisions; no single node is critical. |
| (iii) Policy consistency | Excellent — one CMS, one policy set. | Weak — each agent may drift in policy unless tightly synchronized. |
| (iv) Agent complexity | Low — agents only capture, normalize, and execute commands. | High — agents must run correlation/decision logic locally. |

(Each row ≈ 1.75 marks.)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Both nodes failing simultaneously (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{both\_fail} &= p^{2} \\
               &= (0.04)^{2} \\
               &= 0.0016 \\
               &= 0.16\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Availability improvement (3 marks):**

Probability of total system failure:
- Single-node CMS: $P_{fail} = 0.04 = 4\%$
- 2-node cluster: $P_{fail} = 0.0016 = 0.16\%$

Improvement ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement} &= \dfrac{0.04}{0.0016} \\
                  &= 25\times
\end{aligned}$$

So clustering reduces total-outage probability by a factor of **25**.

**(1 mark for the limitation statement):** Clustering *partially* mitigates SPOF but introduces new risks — cluster split-brain, replication lag, and shared storage failure. Furthermore, the *control channel itself* (the network between agents and the CMS) remains a SPOF: if the WAN link between a remote branch and the central data centre goes down, that branch's agents lose contact with the CMS regardless of how many redundant CMS nodes exist.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Centralized Control Strategy (PECCT742 / Module 2):**

- ✅ A Centralized Control Strategy has **one Central Management Server (CMS)** that holds all detection intelligence.
- ✅ **Agents/sensors are lightweight** — they capture and forward events, but do **not** make autonomous response decisions.
- ✅ The **control channel** is bidirectional: events flow up, policies and response commands flow down.
- ✅ **Advantages:** unified visibility, policy consistency, lightweight agents, simpler compliance reporting.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Single Point of Failure (SPOF), scalability bottleneck, high control-channel bandwidth demand, longer MTTR for geographically dispersed segments.
- ✅ **Standard metrics to memorize:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$, $EPS_{total}$, $B_{req} = EPS_{total} \times S_{avg} \times 8$, $N_{fail} = 1 - (1-p)^{k}$.
- ✅ **Mitigations to mention in exams:** CMS clustering (active-active / active-passive), geo-redundancy, fail-open vs. fail-closed agent policies, hybrid hierarchical architecture (which is Module 2's next topic).
- ✅ **Real-world examples** to cite: Splunk + universal forwarders, IBM QRadar, Cisco FMC, Snort + Barnyard2.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** the term "centralized control" is **not the same** as "centralized logging." Always mention *response command push-down* to score full marks.
- ✅ **Mermaid-friendly keywords to avoid as node IDs:** never use `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` as standalone identifiers.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Centralized Control Strategy notes.** The next logical topic in your syllabus is the **Hierarchical / Hybrid Control Strategy**, which directly addresses the SPOF and scalability limitations identified here.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fully Distributed Control Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fully Distributed Control Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fully Distributed Control Strategy (FDCS)** is an **agent-centric intrusion detection and prevention architecture** in which sensing, analysis, correlation, and response functions are *decentralized* across multiple autonomous cooperating agents/sensors deployed throughout the network, with no central controller governing the detection process. Each agent independently observes local traffic, generates alerts, and exchanges threat intelligence with peer agents to form a **cooperative, fault-tolerant collective defense mesh**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this strategy falls under the **Cooperative/Collaborative IDS** category and is explicitly contrasted with **Centralized Control Strategy** and **Partially Distributed Control Strategy**. The defining trait is that **control authority, decision-making, and enforcement are pushed to the edge nodes themselves**.

### Key Terminology Anchor

| Term | Meaning in FDCS Context |
|---|---|
| **Agent** | Autonomous software entity deployed on a host/network segment that performs sensing + analysis |
| **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Coordination** | Agents communicate directly without a master-slave hierarchy |
| **Local Autonomy** | Each agent can act (alert/block) without waiting for central approval |
| **Gossip Protocol** | Probabilistic information dissemination method used by distributed agents |
| **Consensus Mechanism** | Voting/agreement algorithm among agents before triggering global action |
| **Heterogeneity** | Agents can run on different OS, use different detection techniques |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Neighborhood Watch

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Neighborhood Without a Single Police Station**
>
> Imagine a large gated community. Instead of having **one central police station** that monitors all CCTV cameras and dispatches officers, imagine that **every house has its own smart alarm system** that:
> 1. Watches its own driveway
> 2. Talks directly to the alarm systems of neighboring houses
> 3. If it spots a thief, it **immediately shouts a warning to all neighbors** AND locks its own doors
> 4. Neighbors share sightings so even if House A's camera is broken, House B's camera helps
> 5. There is **NO single point of failure** — knocking out one alarm does not blind the community
>
> This is exactly how **Fully Distributed Control** works in an IDPS — every sensor is intelligent, every sensor is autonomous, and every sensor cooperates with peers.

## 1.3 Why FDCS Matters — Engineering Motivation

- **Scalability Bottleneck of Centralized IDS**: A single central analyzer becomes a performance choke-point as network size grows.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)**: Central server crash = entire detection system goes offline.
- **Bandwidth Saturation**: Funneling all raw logs to a central node consumes massive backbone bandwidth.
- **Geographic Distribution**: Modern enterprises span multiple data centers and cloud regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FDCS Design Goal (KTU 2024 CO2 Mapping):** *"Design and evaluate distributed intrusion detection architectures that ensure fault tolerance, scalability, and real-time cooperative threat response without single points of failure."*

## 1.4 Boundary Conditions & Standard Metrics

- **Agent Density Metric**: Typically **1 agent per network segment** or **1 agent per N hosts** (where $N$ ranges from 50–500 in production deployments).
- **Heartbeat Interval**: Agents exchange "I am alive" messages every **Δt ≈ 5–30 seconds**.
- **Alert Propagation Latency (APL)**: Time for an alert generated at node $i$ to reach node $j$.
  $$APL = \frac{d(i,j)}{v_{gossip}} + T_{proc}$$
  where $d(i,j)$ is the logical distance (hops) and $v_{gossip}$ is the gossip dissemination rate.
- **Detection Coverage (DC)**: Fraction of network monitored.
  $$DC = \frac{\text{Monitored segments}}{\text{Total segments}} \times 100\%$$
  For FDCS, target **DC ≥ 95%** in production deployments.
- **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time To Respond (MTTR)** are the standard KPIs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Pillars of FDCS

The Fully Distributed Control Strategy rests on **four operational pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Local Sensing & Local Analysis
- Each agent captures packets/logs from its own segment.
- Performs **signature matching, anomaly scoring, and stateful protocol analysis** *locally*.
- Generates **local verdicts**: `Benign | Suspicious | Malicious`.

### Pillar 2 — Peer-to-Peer Information Sharing
- Agents exchange:
  - **Blacklists** (confirmed malicious IPs/signatures)
  - **Whitelists** (trusted entities)
  - **Anomaly vectors** (statistical features)
  - **Topology updates** (neighbor agent availability)
- Communication uses protocols like **Gossip, DHT-based Pub/Sub, or epidemic broadcast**.

### Pillar 3 — Distributed Correlation & Consensus
- When a single agent sees a low-confidence event, it consults peers.
- **Voting/Consensus Algorithms** are applied (e.g., Byzantine Fault Tolerance, RAFT-like consensus).
- Only after consensus is reached is a **global action** triggered.

### Pillar 4 — Autonomous Local Response
- Each agent can **block, quarantine, or rate-limit** on its own segment.
- **Prevention** is enforced at the *edge* — the closest point to the attack source.
- This is why the strategy is called **"Fully Distributed"** rather than just "Distributed Sensing".

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix — Centralized vs Partially Distributed vs Fully Distributed

| Feature | Centralized Control | Partially Distributed | **Fully Distributed** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Site | Central server | Hybrid (edge + center) | Every node/agent |
| Response Site | Central server | Center pushes to edge | Each agent independently |
| Communication | Star (agent → center) | Hierarchical tree | **Mesh / P2P** |
| SPOF | Yes (center) | Partial (mid-tier) | **No SPOF** |
| Scalability | Poor | Medium | **High** |
| Coordination Overhead | Low | Medium | **High (gossip traffic)** |
| Consensus Required | None (dictatorial) | Selective | **Frequent** |
| Latency to Respond | High | Medium | **Low (local action)** |
| Fault Tolerance | Weak | Moderate | **Strong** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit / Typical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detection Coverage | $DC = \dfrac{M_{seg}}{T_{seg}} \times 100$ | % (target ≥ 95%) | Measures deployment density |
| 2 | Gossip Dissemination Time | $T_{gossip} = \mathcal{O}(\log N + \log\log N)$ | rounds for N agents | Scalability of info spread |
| 3 | Alert Propagation Latency | $APL = \dfrac{d(i,j)}{v_{gossip}} + T_{proc}$ | milliseconds | SLA compliance |
| 4 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Detector accuracy |
| 5 | False Negative Rate (FNR) | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Missed-attack metric |
| 6 | Detection Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Overall correctness |
| 7 | Consensus Threshold | Action triggered if $\sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot v_k \geq \tau$ | weighted vote sum | Trust-weighted decision |
| 8 | Agent Availability | $A = \dfrac{Uptime_{agent}}{Total\ Time} \times 100$ | % (target ≥ 99.9%) | Reliability per node |
| 9 | Network Overhead | $Overhead = \dfrac{B_{gossip}}{B_{payload}} \times 100$ | % (target < 5%) | Bandwidth cost |
| 10 | Mean Time To Respond | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{respond,i} - t_{detect,i})$ | seconds | Responsiveness KPI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **Consensus Threshold formula** (row 7) and **Gossip Dissemination Time** (row 2) are *favourite viva/short-answer* topics. Memorize the big-O form for gossip: $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

FDCS is **not academic theory** — it powers real systems:

- **Cisco Distributed IDS Sensors** in enterprise networks.
- **OSSEC HIDS Agents** — host-based FDCS with peer communication.
- **Snort + Piggybacked Suricata** with **EveBox** for distributed log correlation.
- **Zero-Trust Architectures** in modern SASE/SSE frameworks (e.g., Zscaler, Palo Alto Prisma).
- **IoT/Edge Security** — lightweight FDCS agents on each IoT gateway (critical because IoT has *no* central server).
- **Military / Tactical Networks** — must operate even when comms are intermittent (FDCS degrades gracefully).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gossip-Based Alert Propagation in an FDCS Mesh

We derive the expected number of rounds required for a single agent's alert to reach all $N$ agents in a fully distributed mesh using the **epidemic/gossip protocol**.

### Step 1 — Define the State
At round $t = 0$, only the originating agent $S$ (the *seed*) knows the alert. Let $I(t)$ be the number of *informed* agents at round $t$.
$$I(0) = 1$$

### Step 2 — Define the Push-Gossip Update
In each round, every informed agent independently picks a **random peer** and pushes the alert. The probability that a chosen uninformed peer is contacted in round $t$ is:
$$p_{contact}(t) = \frac{N - I(t)}{N - 1}$$

### Step 3 — Expected Newly Informed Agents
The expected number of *new* agents informed in round $t$ is:
$$\mathbb{E}[\Delta I(t)] = I(t) \cdot \frac{N - I(t)}{N - 1}$$

This is the **discrete logistic growth** form, analogous to epidemic spreading (Kermack-McKendrick SIR model).

### Step 4 — Differential Approximation
For large $N$, treat $I(t)$ as continuous. The expected number of newly informed agents becomes:
$$\frac{dI}{dt} = I(t) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{I(t)}{N}\right)$$

This is the well-known **logistic differential equation**.

### Step 5 — Solve the Logistic ODE
Separate variables:
$$\frac{dI}{I \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{N}\right)} = dt$$

Using partial fractions: $\dfrac{1}{I(1 - I/N)} = \dfrac{1}{I} + \dfrac{1/N}{1 - I/N}$

Integrate both sides:
$$\int \left( \frac{1}{I} + \frac{1}{N - I} \right) dI = \int dt$$

$$\ln(I) - \ln(N - I) = t + C$$

Exponentiate:
$$\frac{I}{N - I} = K \cdot e^{t}, \quad \text{where } K = e^{C}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Initial Condition $I(0) = 1$
At $t = 0$, $I = 1$:
$$\frac{1}{N - 1} = K$$

So:
$$\frac{I}{N - I} = \frac{e^{t}}{N - 1}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $I(t)$
Cross-multiply:
$$I (N - 1) = (N - I) e^{t}$$

$$I(N - 1) + I e^{t} = N e^{t}$$

$$I \left( N - 1 + e^{t} \right) = N e^{t}$$

$$\boxed{I(t) = \frac{N \, e^{t}}{N - 1 + e^{t}}}$$

### Step 8 — Compute Time to Inform All N Agents
We want $I(t_{full}) = N$ (or practically $0.99N$ for engineering tolerance). Setting $I = N$:
$$N = \frac{N e^{t_{full}}}{N - 1 + e^{t_{full}}}$$

$$N(N - 1 + e^{t_{full}}) = N e^{t_{full}}$$

$$N(N-1) + N e^{t_{full}} = N e^{t_{full}}$$

$$N(N-1) = 0$$

This is only satisfied in the limit $t \to \infty$. Practically, for 99% coverage:
$$0.99 N = \frac{N e^{t_{0.99}}}{N - 1 + e^{t_{0.99}}}$$

$$0.99(N - 1 + e^{t_{0.99}}) = e^{t_{0.99}}$$

$$0.99(N-1) = 0.01 \, e^{t_{0.99}}$$

$$e^{t_{0.99}} = 99(N-1)$$

$$\boxed{t_{0.99} = \ln(99(N-1)) \approx \ln(N) + \ln(99)}$$

This confirms the celebrated **gossip complexity** of:
$$T_{gossip} = \mathcal{O}(\log N + \log\log N)$$

### Step 9 — Numerical Verification
For $N = 1000$ agents:
$$t_{0.99} = \ln(99 \times 999) = \ln(98901) \approx 11.50 \text{ rounds}$$

In a 1-Hz gossip cycle, that's **~11.5 seconds** to alert 99% of 1000 agents — exceptional scalability.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Choose FDCS Over Alternatives

| Scenario | Centralized | Partially Distributed | **FDCS (Best Fit)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small office LAN (< 50 hosts) | ✅ Best | Overkill | Overkill |
| Mid-size enterprise (50–5000 hosts) | Struggles | ✅ Best | ✅ Also good |
| Large multi-site enterprise (> 5000) | Infeasible | Acceptable | ✅ **Best** |
| IoT / Edge / OT networks | Infeasible | Infeasible | ✅ **Only feasible** |
| Cloud-native microservices | Infeasible | Acceptable | ✅ **Best** |
| Intermittent-connectivity (tactical/military) | Infeasible | Struggles | ✅ **Best (graceful degradation)** |
| Budget-constrained SMB | ✅ Cheapest | Medium | Costly |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Consensus Threshold

**Problem:** An FDCS deployment has $K = 5$ peer agents reporting on the same suspicious flow. Their trust weights and individual verdicts are:

| Agent | Trust weight $w_k$ | Local verdict $v_k$ (0=Benign, 1=Malicious) | Confidence $c_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | 0.30 | 1 | 0.90 |
| A2 | 0.25 | 1 | 0.75 |
| A3 | 0.20 | 0 | 0.60 |
| A4 | 0.15 | 1 | 0.80 |
| A5 | 0.10 | 0 | 0.50 |

Threshold $\tau = 0.70$ for triggering a **block** action.

**Step 1:** Compute weighted contribution $w_k \cdot v_k \cdot c_k$ for each agent:
- A1: $0.30 \times 1 \times 0.90 = 0.270$
- A2: $0.25 \times 1 \times 0.75 = 0.1875$
- A3: $0.20 \times 0 \times 0.60 = 0.000$
- A4: $0.15 \times 1 \times 0.80 = 0.120$
- A5: $0.10 \times 0 \times 0.50 = 0.000$

**Step 2:** Sum the contributions:
$$S = 0.270 + 0.1875 + 0 + 0.120 + 0 = 0.5775$$

**Step 3:** Compare against threshold:
$$S = 0.5775 < \tau = 0.70$$

**Step 4:** Decision → **DO NOT BLOCK yet.** Agent must request additional peer reviews or escalate to a deeper analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> This example illustrates how **trust-weighting + confidence** prevents a single misbehaving agent from triggering a false block. It is a hallmark design pattern of FDCS consensus.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level FDCS Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NET["Enterprise Network"]
        SEG1["Segment A: HQ LAN"]
        SEG2["Segment B: Data Center"]
        SEG3["Segment C: Branch Office"]
        SEG4["Segment D: Cloud VPC"]
    end

    AG1["Agent A1\n(Local IDS+IPS)"]
    AG2["Agent A2\n(Local IDS+IPS)"]
    AG3["Agent A3\n(Local IDS+IPS)"]
    AG4["Agent A4\n(Local IDS+IPS)"]

    SEG1 --- AG1
    SEG2 --- AG2
    SEG3 --- AG3
    SEG4 --- AG4

    AG1 <-->|Gossip / P2P| AG2
    AG2 <-->|Gossip / P2P| AG3
    AG3 <-->|Gossip / P2P| AG4
    AG4 <-->|Gossip / P2P| AG1
    AG1 <-->|Alert Exchange| AG3
    AG2 <-->|Alert Exchange| AG4

    MGR["Optional Mgmt Console\n(Read-only Dashboard)"]

    AG1 -.->|Telemetry| MGR
    AG2 -.->|Telemetry| MGR
    AG3 -.->|Telemetry| MGR
    AG4 -.->|Telemetry| MGR

    classDef agent fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0ea5e9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    classDef seg fill:#0f766e,stroke:#14b8a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    classDef mgr fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff;
    class AG1,AG2,AG3,AG4 agent;
    class SEG1,SEG2,SEG3,SEG4 seg;
    class MGR mgr;
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **full mesh of P2P links** between agents and the *optional* read-only manager. The manager does **not** control the agents — it merely visualizes them. This is the defining feature of FDCS.

## 4.2 Operational Flow Inside a Single Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Packet / Log Arrives at Agent X"] --> B["Capture & Preprocess"]
    B --> C{"Local Detection\nEngine"}
    C -->|Signature Hit| D["Local Verdict:\nMALICIOUS"]
    C -->|Anomaly Score > θ| D
    C -->|Benign| E["Drop / Forward"]
    D --> F["Generate Alert Object"]
    F --> G["Consult Peer Agents\n(Voting)"]
    G --> H{"Consensus\nReached?"}
    H -->|Yes, ≥ τ| I["Enforce Local Action:\nBLOCK / QUARANTINE"]
    H -->|No| J["Buffer Alert +\nRe-poll Peers"]
    J --> G
    I --> K["Gossip Alert to\nAll Peer Agents"]
    K --> L["Update Local\nBlacklist"]

    classDef decision fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef action fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff;
    classDef benign fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff;
    class C,H decision;
    class D,F,G,I,J,K,L action;
    class E,B,A benign;
```

## 4.3 Consensus Voting Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant AG1 as Agent A1
    participant AG2 as Agent A2
    participant AG3 as Agent A3
    participant AG4 as Agent A4

    Attacker->>AG1: Malicious Packet
    AG1->>AG1: Local Detection = SUSPICIOUS (0.72)
    AG1->>AG2: VoteRequest[flow-XYZ, weight=0.30]
    AG1->>AG3: VoteRequest[flow-XYZ, weight=0.25]
    AG1->>AG4: VoteRequest[flow-XYZ, weight=0.20]

    AG2->>AG1: VoteReply[MALICIOUS, conf=0.85]
    AG3->>AG1: VoteReply[BENIGN, conf=0.55]
    AG4->>AG1: VoteReply[MALICIOUS, conf=0.78]

    AG1->>AG1: Compute S = 0.30*0.72 + 0.25*0.85 + 0.20*0.55 + 0.15*0.78
    AG1->>AG1: S = 0.216 + 0.2125 + 0.110 + 0.117 = 0.6555
    Note over AG1: S < τ (0.70) → No block yet
    AG1->>AG2: Escalate: Deep Packet Inspection
    AG2->>AG2: DPI confirms exploit
    AG2->>AG1: ReVote[MALICIOUS, conf=0.95]
    AG1->>AG1: Updated S exceeds τ → BLOCK
    AG1->>Attacker: Drop + Log
    AG1->>AG2: Gossip[BLACKLIST: src-IP]
    AG1->>AG3: Gossip[BLACKLIST: src-IP]
    AG1->>AG4: Gossip[BLACKLIST: src-IP]
```

## 4.4 Failure Mode Comparison

```mermaid

        C1["Sensor 1"] --> CS["Central Server"]
        C2["Sensor 2"] --> CS
        C3["Sensor 3"] --> CS
        CS -.X.-> CSX["Server Down = TOTAL BLINDNESS"]
    end

        F1["Agent 1"] <--> F2["Agent 2"]
        F2 <--> F3["Agent 3"]
        F3 <-.X.-> F3X["Agent 3 Down"]
        F1 <--> F2
        F2 <--> F4["Agent 4"]
        F1 <-.-> F4
        Note["Mesh remains functional;\nrest of agents still detect"]
    end

    classDef fail fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff;

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Fully Distributed Control Strategy in IDPS. List any four of its salient features.**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
A Fully Distributed Control Strategy (FDCS) is an IDPS architecture in which **detection, correlation, and response functions are decentralized** across multiple autonomous cooperating agents deployed throughout the network, with no central controller. **Salient features:**
1. **No single point of failure** — agent loss does not cripple the system.
2. **Peer-to-peer communication** — agents gossip alerts directly to each other.
3. **Local autonomous response** — each agent can block/quarantine at its own segment.
4. **Scalable** — new agents can be added without redesigning a central server.
5. **Consensus-based decisions** — actions are triggered only after multi-agent agreement.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare Centralized Control Strategy and Fully Distributed Control Strategy on the basis of (i) point of failure, (ii) response latency, and (iii) coordination overhead.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form):**

| Parameter | Centralized Control | Fully Distributed Control |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Point of failure | Single point (central server) | No SPOF (mesh) |
| (ii) Response latency | High (must traverse to center) | Low (local action at edge) |
| (iii) Coordination overhead | Low (star topology) | High (gossip traffic) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Fully Distributed Control Strategy for IDPS with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the consensus mechanism used in FDCS. How does trust-weighting improve decision accuracy? Derive the formula for the consensus threshold. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Description:**

1. **Sensing Layer:** Multiple **agents** are deployed — one (or more) per network segment, host, or cloud microservice. Each agent performs **packet capture, log ingestion, and flow recording** at its local point of attachment. *(1 mark)*

2. **Local Analysis Engine:** Each agent runs an embedded detection engine combining:
   - **Signature-based** module (Snort-like rules),
   - **Anomaly-based** module (statistical/ML scoring),
   - **Stateful protocol analysis**.
   This produces a **local verdict** ∈ {Benign, Suspicious, Malicious} with an associated **confidence score**. *(1 mark)*

3. **Peer Communication Layer:** Agents are interconnected in a **P2P mesh** using gossip/epidemic protocols. They exchange:
   - **Alerts** (suspect events),
   - **Blacklists/Whitelists**,
   - **Topology & reputation data**,
   - **Heartbeats** for liveness detection. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Distributed Correlation / Consensus Engine:** A built-in **voting module** aggregates peer responses. Trust-weighted verdicts are summed and compared against a threshold $\tau$ before any blocking action. *(1 mark)*

5. **Local Response Module:** On consensus, the agent enforces the action **locally** — drop, quarantine, rate-limit, or reroute. The action is gossiped to peers for **blacklist propagation**. *(1 mark)*

6. **Optional Management Console:** A read-only dashboard that visualizes alerts, agent health, and KPIs. **It does NOT control** the agents — control is fully distributed. *(1 mark)*

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the standard diagram.)* *(0.5 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Consensus Mechanism (≈ 4 marks):**

When a single agent $A_1$ detects a suspicious event with confidence $c_1$, it does **not** act alone. It broadcasts a **VoteRequest** message to $K$ peer agents. Each peer $A_k$ responds with:
- Its local verdict $v_k \in \{0, 1\}$,
- Its own confidence $c_k \in [0, 1]$,
- Its **trust weight** $w_k$ (derived from historical accuracy).

The originating agent computes the **weighted consensus score**:
$$S = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot v_k \cdot c_k$$

**Derivation of the threshold formula (≈ 3 marks):**

Starting from the local detection event, let:
- $v_1 = 1$ for the local agent (it observed the event),
- $w_1 = 1$ (self-trust normalized),
- $c_1 \in [0, 1]$ is the local confidence.

Each peer contributes $w_k v_k c_k$. To trigger a block we require:
$$\boxed{S = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot v_k \cdot c_k \geq \tau}$$

where $\tau \in [0, 1]$ is the **system-wide consensus threshold** (typically 0.6–0.8).

**Properties of trust-weighting:**

1. **Mitigates insider attacks** — a compromised agent has a high false-positive history → low weight → cannot dominate the vote.
2. **Adapts dynamically** — $w_k$ is updated via an exponential moving average of past accuracy.
3. **Reduces false positives** — the consensus acts as a *denoising filter*.

**Numerical example:** *(Use the worked example from Section 3.3 of these notes.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the gossip-based alert propagation mechanism in FDCS. Show that the time to inform $N$ agents follows a $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FDCS deployment has 4 agents monitoring a network. Their trust weights and verdicts for a suspected port-scan event are:
- A1: $w = 0.35$, verdict = 1, confidence = 0.90
- A2: $w = 0.30$, verdict = 1, confidence = 0.80
- A3: $w = 0.20$, verdict = 0, confidence = 0.70
- A4: $w = 0.15$, verdict = 1, confidence = 0.60

If $\tau = 0.60$, determine whether the **block action is triggered**. Justify with calculations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Gossip Protocol Explanation (≈ 3.5 marks):**

In a Fully Distributed Control Strategy, agents do not have a central broker. Instead, they use the **epidemic (gossip) protocol** for alert propagation:

1. When agent $S$ detects an event, it becomes the **seed** of the alert.
2. In each **round**, $S$ picks a random peer from the population of $N$ agents and pushes the alert.
3. Once informed, that peer also begins pushing the alert in subsequent rounds.
4. The process repeats until all (or 99%) agents are informed.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 sequence diagram.)*

**Derivation of $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ complexity (≈ 3.5 marks):**

Let $I(t)$ = number of informed agents at round $t$, $I(0) = 1$.

In each round, each informed agent contacts one random peer. The probability of reaching an uninformed peer is:
$$p = \frac{N - I(t)}{N - 1}$$

Expected newly informed agents:
$$\mathbb{E}[\Delta I(t)] = I(t) \cdot \frac{N - I(t)}{N - 1}$$

For large $N$, approximate by the logistic ODE:
$$\frac{dI}{dt} = I(t) \left( 1 - \frac{I(t)}{N} \right)$$

Solving (separation of variables, partial fractions) yields:
$$I(t) = \frac{N \, e^{t}}{N - 1 + e^{t}}$$

For 99% coverage ($I = 0.99N$):
$$0.99N (N - 1) + 0.99N e^{t} = N e^{t}$$

$$0.99(N - 1) = 0.01 e^{t}$$

$$e^{t_{0.99}} = 99(N - 1)$$

$$t_{0.99} = \ln(99(N-1)) = \ln(N-1) + \ln(99) \approx \ln N + \text{const}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{T_{gossip} = \mathcal{O}(\log N)}$$

**Conclusion:** Even for $N = 10{,}000$ agents, only $\approx 13$ rounds are needed for 99% coverage — confirming the **excellent scalability** of FDCS.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$K = 4$, $\tau = 0.60$.

| Agent | $w_k$ | $v_k$ | $c_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | 0.35 | 1 | 0.90 |
| A2 | 0.30 | 1 | 0.80 |
| A3 | 0.20 | 0 | 0.70 |
| A4 | 0.15 | 1 | 0.60 |

**Step 2 — Compute weighted contribution $w_k \cdot v_k \cdot c_k$ for each agent:**

$$\text{A1: } 0.35 \times 1 \times 0.90 = 0.3150$$

$$\text{A2: } 0.30 \times 1 \times 0.80 = 0.2400$$

$$\text{A3: } 0.20 \times 0 \times 0.70 = 0.0000 \text{ (verdict is benign, contributes 0)}$$

$$\text{A4: } 0.15 \times 1 \times 0.60 = 0.0900$$

**Step 3 — Sum to get consensus score:**
$$S = 0.3150 + 0.2400 + 0.0000 + 0.0900 = 0.6450$$

**Step 4 — Compare against threshold:**
$$S = 0.6450 \;\geq\; \tau = 0.60 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{BLOCK ACTION IS TRIGGERED.}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (≈ 1 mark):**
The malicious verdict of A1, A2, A4 collectively outweighs A3's benign verdict, with high confidence. The trust-weighting (A1 has the highest weight 0.35 because of its historical accuracy) gives the decision robustness. The originating agent will:
- **Drop** the offending packets at its local segment.
- **Update** its local blacklist with the attacker's source IP.
- **Gossip** the blacklist to all peers within $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ rounds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply confidence $c_k$** — many students write $S = \sum w_k v_k$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Treating benign verdict as negative contribution** — the standard formula uses $v_k \in \{0, 1\}$; a benign verdict simply contributes **zero**, not a *negative* vote. (Some extended models do allow negative votes, but the KTU-expected formula is the non-negative form.)
> 3. **Comparing $S$ with 1 instead of $\tau$** — re-read the threshold definition.
> 4. **Skipping the gossip propagation step** — even after a local block, KTU expects the answer to mention *how* the alert reaches peers.
> 5. **Not stating the SPOF advantage** — full-marks answers always contrast FDCS with centralized IDS to demonstrate deeper understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Fully Distributed Control Strategy**

- ✅ **Definition:** IDPS architecture with **no central controller**; sensing, analysis, and response are pushed to autonomous **agents** in a **P2P mesh**.
- ✅ **Core Difference vs Centralized:** *No SPOF* and *Local response*; cost is *higher coordination overhead*.
- ✅ **Four Pillars:** (1) Local sensing, (2) P2P info sharing, (3) Distributed consensus, (4) Autonomous local response.
- ✅ **Key Protocol:** **Gossip / Epidemic** protocol for alert dissemination.
- ✅ **Scalability Theorem:** $T_{gossip} = \mathcal{O}(\log N + \log\log N)$.
- ✅ **Consensus Score Formula:** $S = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \cdot v_k \cdot c_k \geq \tau$.
- ✅ **Trust-Weighting Purpose:** Prevent compromised/noisy agents from triggering false positives.
- ✅ **KPI Formulas to Memorize:** $DC$, $FPR$, $FNR$, $Acc$, $APL$, $MTTR$, $T_{gossip}$, Consensus $S$.
- ✅ **Analogy:** "Neighborhood watch without a single police station."
- ✅ **Real-world systems:** OSSEC, Cisco Distributed Sensors, Zscaler Edge Agents, IoT/OT security gateways.
- ✅ **Best-fit scenarios:** Large multi-site enterprises, IoT/edge, intermittent-connectivity (military), cloud-native microservices.
- ✅ **Common mistake in KTU exam:** Forgetting to include confidence $c_k$ in the consensus sum.
- ✅ **Drawing tip:** Always include a **mesh topology** in your block diagram, not a star; highlight the *absence* of a central control node.
- ✅ **One-liner for viva:** *"FDCS trades bandwidth for resilience."*
- ✅ **Mathematical highlight:** The gossip dissemination derives from the **logistic differential equation** $\frac{dI}{dt} = I(1 - I/N)$, leading to the explicit closed-form $I(t) = \dfrac{N e^t}{N-1 + e^t}$.
- ✅ **Consensus threshold range:** Typically $\tau \in [0.6, 0.8]$ in production; lower values → more false positives, higher values → more false negatives.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partially Distributed Control Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Partially Distributed Control Strategy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS)**, a **Partially Distributed Control Strategy** (also referred to as a **Hierarchical** or **Hybrid Distributed Architecture**) is a deployment topology in which the **data collection and low-level decision-making components are distributed** across multiple network segments or hosts, while the **higher-level analysis, correlation, and response coordination functions remain centralized** at one or a small cluster of management stations. According to the **NIST SP 800-94** guidelines, this architecture represents a middle-ground solution that balances the scalability of fully distributed models with the administrative simplicity of centralized models. It employs a **two-tier (or multi-tier) hierarchical structure** where **Sensors/Monitors** operate autonomously at the local level, but report to a **Central Manager/Coordinator** that performs cross-domain event correlation and policy enforcement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The distinguishing feature of a *partially* distributed control strategy is that **local agents can make limited autonomous decisions** (such as dropping a packet or logging an alert), but the **global policy, signature database, and cross-domain correlation logic are managed centrally**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large hospital chain** operating across multiple cities: - **Fully Centralized Model**: Every patient in every city must have their X-rays read by a single radiologist sitting in one central headquarters. This creates massive bottlenecks and latency. - **Fully Distributed Model**: Each city has its own independent hospital, with its own doctors, rules, and treatments. No coordination exists — a patient moving between cities may get conflicting treatments. - **Partially Distributed Model (The Hybrid Sweet Spot)**: Each city hospital has its own **local doctors** who can make immediate decisions on routine cases (give an aspirin, set a broken bone). However, all hospitals report to a **central medical board** that maintains the master treatment protocols, distributes updated drug information, and coordinates responses to a new epidemic spreading across all cities. In this analogy: - The **local doctors** = Distributed **Sensors/Agents** at network segments. - The **central medical board** = Centralized **Management Station / Analyzer**. - The **master treatment protocols** = Centralized **Signature Database & Policy Engine**. - The **epidemic coordination** = **Cross-domain Event Correlation**. This architecture preserves **local responsiveness** while enabling **global situational awareness**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology & Components | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Sensor / Agent** | Software/hardware module deployed on a host or network segment that captures raw event data (packets, logs, syscalls). | | **Local Analyzer** | A co-located or regional module that performs first-level analysis on data from nearby sensors. | | **Central Manager (Director)** | The upper-tier component that receives alerts from local analyzers, performs correlation, and issues global response directives. | | **Hierarchical Coordinator** | An intermediate node in multi-tier deployments (e.g., per-department or per-subnet coordinators). | | **Event Correlation Engine** | Software that aggregates, deduplicates, and links related events from distributed sources to identify multi-stage attacks. | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metrics:** Typical deployments use **2 to 3 hierarchical tiers**, with each upper-tier node aggregating data from **5 to 50** lower-tier nodes. Centralized components typically handle **< 20%** of total event volume after local filtering. ## 1.4 Position in the IDS Architecture Spectrum $$\text{Architecture Spectrum} = \{\text{Centralized} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Partially Distributed} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Fully Distributed}\}$$ The **Partially Distributed** model occupies the central position, attempting to inherit the best characteristics of both extremes. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" The partially distributed control strategy is structured around **four operational phases**. Each phase has a specific role that explains *why* the architecture is designed this way and *how* data flows. ### Phase 1: Distributed Data Acquisition (Local Tier) - **Sensors** are placed at strategic network chokepoints (e.g., perimeter firewalls, DMZ, internal subnets, server farms). - Each sensor independently captures raw audit data: network packets, system logs, file integrity changes, or system calls. - **Why?** Placing intelligence close to the data source minimizes raw data transmission, reducing bandwidth consumption and the risk of exposing sensitive payloads on the wire. - **How?** Sensors use **libpcap**, **WinPcap/Npcap**, or OS-level audit hooks (e.g., Linux Audit Daemon, Windows ETW). ### Phase 2: Local Pre-Processing & Filtering (Regional/Local Tier) - A **Local Analyzer** (sometimes co-located with the sensor) performs: 1. **Normalization** — converting heterogeneous log formats (syslog, Windows Event Log, SNMP traps) into a canonical schema. 2. **Pre-filtering / Noise Reduction** — discarding known-benign traffic patterns using whitelists and statistical baselines. 3. **First-pass Detection** — running lightweight signature or anomaly checks against locally cached rules. - **Why?** This dramatically reduces the volume of data forwarded upstream — typically by **80% to 95%**. - **How?** Through threshold-based filtering, Bloom filters for set membership, and pattern-matching engines like Snort or Suricata running in **alert-fast** or **unified2** output modes. ### Phase 3: Centralized Correlation & Analysis (Central Tier) - The **Central Manager** receives alerts from multiple local analyzers via **encrypted channels** (typically TLS over TCP, port 443 or custom ports). - It executes: 1. **Cross-Segment Correlation** — linking an alert from a perimeter sensor (port scan) with an alert from a host sensor (suspicious process spawn) to reconstruct a multi-stage attack. 2. **Aggregate Anomaly Detection** — comparing metrics across the entire enterprise (e.g., total failed logins across all servers vs. baseline). 3. **False Positive Reduction** — using global context (asset criticality, user roles, time of day). - **Why?** Attacks often span multiple network segments. No single local analyzer has full visibility. - **How?** Through correlation engines using rule-based logic, Bayesian networks, or graph-based attack reconstruction. ### Phase 4: Coordinated Response (Central → Local) - Once a high-confidence attack is confirmed, the Central Manager issues **response directives** back down the hierarchy: - **Active Response:** Block source IP at multiple perimeter sensors simultaneously. - **Passive Response:** Update local signature databases (push new Snort/Suricata rules). - **Forensic Response:** Trigger packet capture on specific sensors for evidence preservation. - **Why?** A coordinated response prevents the attacker from simply moving laterally to a different segment. - **How?** Through a secure command channel, often using the same encrypted tunnel as alert reporting. ## 2.2 Communication & Protocol Stack The communication between tiers follows a **layered protocol model**: | Layer | Function | Typical Implementation | |-------|----------|------------------------| | **Transport** | Reliable delivery of alerts | TCP (with TLS 1.3 encryption) | | **Message Format** | Structured alert payload | IDMEF (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format) — XML-based, RFC 4765 | | **Encoding** | Binary efficiency for high-volume sensors | SDEE (Security Device Event Exchange) by Cisco | | **Authentication** | Mutual trust between components | X.509 certificates, pre-shared keys, or Kerberos | > [!IMPORTANT] > **IDMEF (RFC 4765)** is the IETF standard specifically designed for communication between distributed IDPS components and is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Metric | Expression | Description | Typical Units | |------------------|------------|-------------|---------------| | **Local Filtering Ratio (LFR)** | $LFR = 1 - \dfrac{V_{forwarded}}{V_{raw}}$ | Proportion of raw events discarded locally | Dimensionless (0–1) | | **Central Load Factor (CLF)** | $CLF = \dfrac{V_{forwarded} \times N_{local}}{C_{central}}$ | Ratio of incoming events to central processing capacity | Events/sec | | **End-to-End Latency (E2E Latency)** | $T_{E2E} = T_{capture} + T_{local} + T_{transport} + T_{central}$ | Total time from event occurrence to central alert generation | Milliseconds (ms) | | **Scalability Index (SI)** | $SI = \dfrac{N_{sensors} \times \bar{R}_{per\_sensor}}{T_{E2E}}$ | Effective throughput of the hierarchical system | Events/sec | | **Mean Time To Respond (MTTR)** | $MTTR = T_{detect} + T_{correlate} + T_{respond}$ | Total incident response latency | Seconds (s) | | **Hierarchical Tier Count** | $T = \lceil \log_{k}(N_{endpoints}) \rceil$ | Minimum tiers needed, where $k$ is the fan-out factor | Integer | > [!NOTE] > **Critical LaTeX Formatting Note for KTU Exams:** In written exams, always use `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` in inline math to avoid table formatting issues in digital submissions. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Partially distributed control strategies are the **de facto industry standard** in modern enterprise security. Real-world deployments include: 1. **Cisco Firepower Management Center (FMC)** — manages distributed Firepower sensors/FTDs across an enterprise. 2. **IBM QRadar** — uses a hierarchical collector/processor architecture with central event correlation. 3. **Splunk Enterprise Security** — forwards (heavy/light) tiered architecture with central indexing and correlation. 4. **OSSIM / AlienVault USM** — distributed OSSEC agents reporting to a central SIEM correlator. These systems demonstrate that **scalability without sacrificing global situational awareness** is achieved precisely through the partial distribution of control. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Number of Hierarchical Tiers **Problem Statement:** Given an enterprise with $N_{endpoints}$ host/network sensors, each upper-tier node can effectively coordinate $k$ lower-tier nodes (the **fan-out factor**). Derive the minimum number of tiers $T$ required. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Define the capacity of a single tier.** A single tier-1 node can manage $k$ sensors directly. $$ \text{Capacity}_{\text{Tier 1}} = k \text{ sensors} $$ **Step 2 — Define the capacity of tier 2.** A tier-2 node coordinates $k$ tier-1 nodes, each managing $k$ sensors. $$ \text{Capacity}_{\text{Tier 2}} = k \times k = k^2 \text{ sensors} $$ **Step 3 — Generalize to tier $T$.** By mathematical induction, a tier-$T$ coordinator can manage: $$ \text{Capacity}_{\text{Tier } T} = k^T \text{ sensors} $$ **Step 4 — Set up the inequality.** To cover all $N_{endpoints}$ sensors, we need: $$ k^T \geq N_{endpoints} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $T$ using logarithms.** Taking $\log_k$ on both sides: $$ T \geq \log_k(N_{endpoints}) $$ **Step 6 — Apply the ceiling function** since $T$ must be an integer: $$ T_{min} = \lceil \log_k(N_{endpoints}) \rceil $$ ### Worked Example: Suppose an enterprise has **$N_{endpoints} = 10{,}000$** sensors, with each manager capable of handling **$k = 10$** lower-tier nodes. $$ T_{min} = \lceil \log_{10}(10{,}000) \rceil = \lceil 4 \rceil = 4 \text{ tiers} $$ This derivation is critical for KTU design questions asking how to architect a scalable IDPS deployment. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total End-to-End Latency in a 3-Tier Hierarchy **Problem Statement:** Compute the total alert propagation latency in a 3-tier partially distributed IDPS given per-stage delays. ### Notation: - $T_{capture}$ = Time for sensor to capture and packetize event: **5 ms** - $T_{local}$ = Local analyzer processing time: **15 ms** - $T_{transport}$ = Network transit to central manager: **20 ms** - $T_{central}$ = Central correlation engine processing: **30 ms** - $T_{response}$ = Time for response directive to travel back to sensor: **20 ms** ### Step-by-Step Calculation: **Step 1 — Write the complete E2E expression.** $$ T_{E2E} = T_{capture} + T_{local} + T_{transport} + T_{central} + T_{response} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.** $$ T_{E2E} = 5 + 15 + 20 + 30 + 20 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate.** $$ T_{E2E} = 90 \text{ ms} $$ ### Multi-Tier Extension: For a system with $T$ tiers, where each tier introduces an additional transport + processing delay: $$ T_{E2E}^{(T)} = T_{capture} + \sum_{i=1}^{T} (T_{local,i} + T_{transport,i}) + T_{central} $$ This formula helps KTU students determine whether a deployment meets real-time response SLAs (typically **< 500 ms** for automated blocking). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Central Load Factor (CLF) and Bottleneck Analysis **Problem Statement:** A central manager has processing capacity $C_{central} = 5{,}000$ alerts/second. There are $N_{local} = 20$ local analyzers, each forwarding $V_{forwarded} = 150$ alerts/second after local filtering. ### Step-by-Step Calculation: **Step 1 — Compute total forwarded alert volume.** $$ V_{total} = V_{forwarded} \times N_{local} = 150 \times 20 = 3{,}000 \text{ alerts/sec} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the Central Load Factor.** $$ CLF = \dfrac{V_{total}}{C_{central}} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{5{,}000} = 0.6 $$ **Step 3 — Interpret the result.** Since $CLF = 0.6 < 1.0$, the central manager is operating at 60% capacity — well within safe limits (industry best practice is to keep $CLF \leq 0.7$). **Step 4 — Determine maximum sustainable local analyzers.** $$ N_{local}^{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{C_{central}}{V_{forwarded}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{5{,}000}{150} \right\rfloor = 33 \text{ analyzers} $$ **Step 5 — Determine the required central capacity** to support 50 local analyzers at 150 alerts/sec each: $$ C_{required} = V_{forwarded} \times N_{local} = 150 \times 50 = 7{,}500 \text{ alerts/sec} $$ This demonstrates how partially distributed control limits scalability and identifies when horizontal scaling of the central tier is needed. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Architectural Strategies | Attribute | Centralized | **Partially Distributed** | Fully Distributed | |-----------|:-----------:|:-------------------------:|:-----------------:| | **Data Collection** | At central point only | Distributed at sensors | Distributed at all nodes | | **Analysis Location** | 100% central | Local pre-analysis + central correlation | 100% local / peer-to-peer | | **Decision-Making** | Fully centralized | **Local filtering + central coordination** | Fully autonomous / peer consensus | | **Scalability** | Poor (single bottleneck) | **Good (bounded by central capacity)** | Excellent (no central bottleneck) | | **Single Point of Failure** | Yes (critical) | **Partial (central coordinator only)** | No SPOF | | **Network Bandwidth Usage** | High (raw data to center) | **Moderate (filtered alerts only)** | Low (only peer exchange) | | **Cross-Domain Visibility** | Excellent | **Excellent (central correlation)** | Poor (no global view) | | **Policy Consistency** | Guaranteed | **High (central push)** | Low (heterogeneous policies) | | **Implementation Complexity** | Low | **Moderate** | High | | **Example Systems** | Single-host IDS | **Cisco FMC, QRadar, OSSIM** | Peer-to-peer agent swarms | | **Latency to First Response** | High | **Low (local) + Moderate (global)** | Very Low | ## 3.5 Decision Logic — When to Choose Partially Distributed Control The selection of a partially distributed control strategy is governed by the following decision criteria: $$ \text{Choose Partially Distributed} \iff \begin{cases} N_{segments} > 5 & \text{(multi-segment enterprise)} \\ \text{Compliance requires central audit trail} = \text{True} \\ \text{Cross-segment attack patterns must be detected} = \text{True} \\ \text{Budget allows for central management infrastructure} = \text{True} \\ \text{24/7 SOC operations team exists} = \text{True} \end{cases} $$ If any of these conditions fail, a centralized or fully distributed approach may be more appropriate. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Architecture Diagram — 3-Tier Partially Distributed Control ```mermaid flowchart TB tier3[Tier 3: Central SOC Director
Global Correlation Engine
Master Policy and Signature DB] tier2a[Tier 2A: Perimeter Coordinator
Edge Sensor Aggregator] tier2b[Tier 2B: Datacenter Coordinator
Server Farm Aggregator] tier2c[Tier 2C: Branch Office Coordinator
Regional Aggregator] s1[Sensor 1: DMZ Packet Capture] s2[Sensor 2: Internal Subnet NetFlow] s3[Sensor 3: Database Server Host Agent] s4[Sensor 4: Web Server Farm Agent] s5[Sensor 5: Branch Router IDS] s6[Sensor 6: Endpoint EDR Agent] s7[Sensor 7: Cloud Workload Sensor] s8[Sensor 8: VPN Concentrator Monitor] tier3 --- tier2a tier3 --- tier2b tier3 --- tier2c tier2a --- s1 tier2a --- s2 tier2b --- s3 tier2b --- s4 tier2c --- s5 tier2c --- s6 tier2c --- s7 tier2c --- s8 classDef central fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#fff classDef mid fill:#ffd166,stroke:#e67e22,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#059669,color:#000 class tier3 central class tier2a,tier2b,tier2c mid class s1,s2,s3,s4,s5,s6,s7,s8 leaf ``` ## 4.2 Information Flow Diagram — Alert Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Net as Network Traffic participant Sensor as Tier 1 Sensor participant Local as Tier 2 Local Analyzer participant Central as Tier 3 Central Manager participant Admin as SOC Analyst participant Resp as Response System Net->>Sensor: Raw packet flow activate Sensor Sensor->>Sensor: Capture and normalize Sensor->>Local: Forward filtered events deactivate Sensor activate Local Local->>Local: Run local signatures Local->>Local: Apply threshold and anomaly check Local->>Central: Send high-confidence alerts deactivate Local activate Central Central->>Central: Cross-segment correlation Central->>Central: Reconstruct attack graph Central->>Admin: Display on SOC dashboard Admin->>Central: Approve or refine response Central->>Resp: Issue coordinated block directive activate Resp Resp->>Sensor: Push updated rules and block list deactivate Resp deactivate Central ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph C["Centralized Model"] C1[Sensors feed raw data] --> C2[Single Central Engine] C2 --> C3[Response] end subgraph P["Partially Distributed Model"] P1[Sensors] --> P2[Local Analyzers] P2 -->|Filtered alerts| P3[Central Coordinator] P3 -->|Directives| P2 P3 --> P4[Global Response] end subgraph F["Fully Distributed Model"] F1[Peer Agents] <-->|P2P exchange| F2[Peer Agents] F1 --> F3[Local Decision] F2 --> F3 end classDef redBox fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b classDef blueBox fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#2980b9 classDef greenBox fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#27ae60 class C redBox class P blueBox class F greenBox ``` ## 4.4 Functional Component Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LOCAL["Local Tier Functions"] L1[Packet Capture] L2[Log Collection] L3[Normalization] L4[Local Signature Match] L5[Local Anomaly Detection] L6[Alert Filtering] end subgraph CENTRAL["Central Tier Functions"] CT1[Secure Alert Reception] CT2[Event Database] CT3[Correlation Engine] CT4[Attack Graph Builder] CT5[Policy Engine] CT6[Response Orchestrator] end L1 --> L3 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L3 --> L5 L4 --> L6 L5 --> L6 L6 -->|IDMEF or SDEE| CT1 CT1 --> CT2 CT2 --> CT3 CT3 --> CT4 CT5 --> CT3 CT4 --> CT6 CT6 -->|Directives| L4 classDef local fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 classDef central fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#990000 class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5,L6 local class CT1,CT2,CT3,CT4,CT5,CT6 central ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the "Partially Distributed Control Strategy" in the context of Intrusion Detection Systems. How does it differ from a fully distributed approach?** **Model Answer:** The **Partially Distributed Control Strategy** is an IDPS deployment architecture in which **sensors and local analyzers are distributed** across multiple network segments or hosts for data collection and preliminary analysis, while the **higher-level functions** such as cross-segment event correlation, signature database management, policy enforcement, and coordinated response are **centralized** in one or a small cluster of management stations. **Key differences from Fully Distributed:** | Aspect | Partially Distributed | Fully Distributed | |--------|---------------------|-------------------| | Coordination | Central manager coordinates all tiers | Peer-to-peer, no central authority | | Global View | Yes (central correlation) | No global view | | Policy Sync | Centrally pushed | Heterogeneous, locally defined | | Failure Impact | Local analyzer failure isolated | Node failure may fragment peer mesh | The partially distributed model offers **balanced scalability and centralized control**, making it the preferred choice for most enterprise SOCs. *(3 marks)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is IDMEF and why is it relevant to partially distributed IDPS architectures?** **Model Answer:** **IDMEF (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format)**, defined in **IETF RFC 4765**, is a standard XML-based data format for communicating alerts and event information between IDPS components — specifically between **analyzers** (local tiers) and **managers** (central tiers). It is highly relevant to partially distributed architectures because: 1. **Interoperability** — Allows heterogeneous sensors from different vendors (Snort, Suricata, OSSEC) to report alerts to a single central manager. 2. **Structured Payloads** — Encodes alert attributes (source, target, classification, timestamp, confidence) in a well-defined schema. 3. **Transport Independence** — Can be carried over TCP, UDP, or TLS, supporting both encrypted and plain channels. 4. **Automation** — Enables automated correlation and response orchestration at the central tier. Without IDMEF, each vendor would use proprietary formats, breaking the centralized correlation capability that defines partially distributed control. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by **confusing "distributed data collection" with "distributed control"**. The question is about *control*, not just *placement* of sensors. > - Another common pitfall: **failing to mention the central coordinator as the policy authority**. Without it, the architecture becomes fully distributed. > - Always draw the **diagram first** before writing theory — KTU examiners award at least **2 marks** for a clean architecture diagram. > - For numerical design problems, **show all substitution steps** — partial credit is given for correct formulas even if arithmetic is wrong. ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — 14 Marks **(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture, components, and operational phases** of a Partially Distributed Control Strategy for an IDPS. Include a labeled block diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An enterprise has **1,000 sensors** to be deployed under a partially distributed architecture. Each upper-tier coordinator can manage a maximum of **10 lower-tier nodes**. **(i)** Calculate the **minimum number of tiers** required. **(ii)** If each sensor generates **50 events/second** and the local analyzer filters out **85%** of events before forwarding, calculate the **total alert volume** arriving at the central manager. **(iii)** If the central manager can process a maximum of **5,000 alerts/second**, determine the **Central Load Factor (CLF)** and comment on whether the deployment is sustainable. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A(a): **Architecture Overview:** The Partially Distributed Control Strategy employs a **hierarchical multi-tier architecture** with three primary layers: 1. **Sensor Tier (Tier 1)** — Distributed sensors/agents at network chokepoints and host machines. 2. **Local Analyzer Tier (Tier 2)** — Regional coordinators performing first-level detection. 3. **Central Manager Tier (Tier 3)** — Enterprise-wide correlation, policy, and response orchestration. **Operational Phases (describe each as in Section 2.1):** - **Phase 1:** Distributed data acquisition using packet capture and host-based auditing. - **Phase 2:** Local pre-processing, normalization, and noise reduction (typically filters 80–95% of events). - **Phase 3:** Centralized correlation across all segments using IDMEF-formatted alerts. - **Phase 4:** Coordinated response — central manager issues blocking directives to multiple sensors simultaneously. **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 architecture diagram — should be drawn in exam). **Key Features:** - Local autonomy for fast response to known threats. - Central oversight for cross-segment attack reconstruction. - IDMEF for standardized communication. - TLS-encrypted channels for alert confidentiality. *(7 marks — full marks awarded for clear phase description, labeled diagram, and mention of IDMEF/TLS)* #### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Part (i) — Minimum number of tiers:** Using the derived formula: $$ T_{min} = \lceil \log_k(N_{endpoints}) \rceil = \lceil \log_{10}(1{,}000) \rceil $$ $$ T_{min} = \lceil 3 \rceil = 3 \text{ tiers} $$ **Part (ii) — Total alert volume at central manager:** Events generated per sensor: **50 events/sec** Local filtering ratio: **85%** (so only 15% is forwarded) Forwarded events per sensor: $$ V_{forwarded} = 50 \times (1 - 0.85) = 50 \times 0.15 = 7.5 \text{ alerts/sec per sensor} $$ Total incoming volume to central manager: $$ V_{total} = V_{forwarded} \times N_{sensors} = 7.5 \times 1{,}000 = 7{,}500 \text{ alerts/sec} $$ **Part (iii) — Central Load Factor:** $$ CLF = \dfrac{V_{total}}{C_{central}} = \dfrac{7{,}500}{5{,}000} = 1.5 $$ Since $CLF = 1.5 > 1.0$, the central manager is **overloaded by 50%**. The deployment is **NOT sustainable** in its current form. Recommended remediations: 1. **Increase local filtering aggressiveness** — raise LFR from 85% to ~94% (so only 6% forwarded → $V_{total} = 3{,}000$ alerts/sec → $CLF = 0.6$). 2. **Scale central capacity** — upgrade to a manager capable of 10,000 alerts/sec. 3. **Add a tier-2 regional aggregator** to perform additional pre-correlation before central forwarding. *(7 marks — full marks for correct formulas, accurate calculations, and actionable conclusion)* --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Compare and contrast **Centralized, Partially Distributed, and Fully Distributed** IDPS control strategies with suitable diagrams. Highlight **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of the partially distributed approach. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of **event correlation** and the **IDMEF protocol** in enabling effective communication between distributed sensors and a central manager in a partially distributed IDPS. Explain with an example of a **multi-stage attack** that can only be detected through centralized correlation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B(a): **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Centralized | Partially Distributed | Fully Distributed | |---------|-------------|----------------------|-------------------| | Data Collection | Central | Distributed | Distributed | | Analysis | Central | Local + Central | Local only | | Decision Authority | Central | Hybrid (local + central) | Peer consensus | | Scalability | Poor | Moderate to Good | Excellent | | Fault Tolerance | Low | Moderate | High | | Global Visibility | Excellent | Excellent | Poor | **Advantages of Partially Distributed:** 1. **Balanced Scalability:** Local filtering drastically reduces central load, supporting hundreds to thousands of sensors. 2. **Centralized Situational Awareness:** The central manager maintains a unified view of enterprise-wide threats, enabling detection of multi-stage attacks. 3. **Local Fast Response:** Local analyzers can block known attacks immediately without waiting for central confirmation. 4. **Policy Consistency:** Central push of signatures and policies ensures uniform protection across the enterprise. **Disadvantages of Partially Distributed:** 1. **Single Point of Failure:** The central manager remains a critical dependency — its failure halts global correlation and coordinated response. 2. **Implementation Complexity:** Requires careful design of secure communication, hierarchical routing, and tier synchronization. 3. **Bandwidth Concentration:** While filtering reduces volume, the central link can still become a bottleneck under heavy attack conditions (alert storms). 4. **Higher Cost:** Requires investment in both distributed sensors and central management infrastructure. **Diagrams:** (Refer to Section 4.3 — three side-by-side block diagrams). *(7 marks — awarded for comparison depth, advantages/disadvantages clarity, and diagram presence)* #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — Diagram (Mandatory for Full Marks): ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CENT["Centralized"] A1[Single Sensor Array] --> A2[Central Engine Only] end subgraph PART["Partially Distributed"] B1[Sensors] --> B2[Local Analyzers] B2 -->|Alerts| B3[Central Manager] B3 -->|Directives| B2 end subgraph FULL["Fully Distributed"] C1[Agent 1] <-->|P2P| C2[Agent 2] C2 <-->|P2P| C3[Agent 3] C1 <-->|P2P| C3 end classDef cent fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 classDef part fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 classDef full fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 class A1,A2 cent class B1,B2,B3 part class C1,C2,C3 full ``` #### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Role of Event Correlation:** Event correlation is the **analytical heart** of the partially distributed architecture. It is the process of **aggregating, deduplicating, and linking** alerts from multiple distributed local analyzers to reconstruct complex, multi-stage attacks that no single sensor could detect in isolation. **Role of IDMEF:** The **Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format (RFC 4765)** provides a **standardized XML schema** for alert exchange. Each alert contains fields such as: - `Analyzer` (the local analyzer's identity) - `CreateTime`, `DetectTime` - `Source`, `Target` (IP, port, user) - `Classification` (attack type) - `Assessment` (severity, confidence) This standardization allows the central manager to ingest alerts from heterogeneous sensors (different vendors, different OS platforms) into a **unified event database**. **Example: Multi-Stage Attack — "Reconnaissance → Foothold → Lateral Movement"** Consider a **spear-phishing attack** that spans three segments: | Stage | Segment | Local Sensor Alert | Time | |-------|---------|-------------------|------| | 1 | Perimeter (DMZ) | Suspicious SMTP attachment from external IP | 09:00:00 | | 2 | User Workstation (Internal) | Host agent: malicious macro executed, outbound C2 beacon | 09:04:30 | | 3 | Database Server (Datacenter) | Host agent: SQL injection attempt using harvested credentials | 09:11:15 | **Individually**, each local analyzer sees only a "medium-severity" event in its own segment. **However**, the central manager correlates: - The same `Source.User` appears in all three alerts. - The `Target.IP` follows a logical progression: perimeter → workstation → database. - The timing is consistent with human-driven attacker behavior (4-minute recon gap, 7-minute lateral movement). **Result:** Central correlation reconstructs a **single high-severity "multi-stage targeted attack"** alert (Confidence = 0.95) that triggers an enterprise-wide response — block the C2 IP at perimeter, isolate the workstation, force password reset for the affected user. Without IDMEF and centralized correlation, the three alerts would remain disconnected in three separate sensor logs, and the attack would likely succeed. *(7 marks — awarded for explaining correlation mechanism, IDMEF structure, and a complete worked attack example)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Partially Distributed Control Strategy** ### Core Concepts - **Definition:** Architecture where **data collection is distributed** but **analysis/control coordination is centralized**. - **Synonyms:** Hierarchical IDS, Hybrid Distributed Architecture, Two/Three-Tier IDPS. - **Standard:** Described in NIST SP 800-94 as a recommended enterprise deployment pattern. ### Key Architectural Tiers 1. **Sensor / Agent Tier** — Captures raw data at host or network level. 2. **Local Analyzer Tier** — Normalizes, filters, and performs first-pass detection. 3. **Central Manager / Director Tier** — Correlates, correlates globally, issues responses. ### Critical Formulas - Minimum tiers: $T_{min} = \lceil \log_k(N_{endpoints}) \rceil$ - End-to-end latency: $T_{E2E} = T_{capture} + T_{local} + T_{transport} + T_{central}$ - Central Load Factor: $CLF = \dfrac{V_{forwarded} \times N_{local}}{C_{central}}$ - Acceptable CLF: $\leq 0.7$ for production systems. ### Protocols & Standards - **IDMEF (RFC 4765):** XML-based alert format for manager–analyzer communication. - **SDEE (Cisco):** Binary alternative to IDMEF for high-volume sensors. - **TLS 1.3:** Encryption for all inter-tier communication channels. ### Major Advantages - Scalability through local filtering. - Global situational awareness via central correlation. - Fast local response for known threats. - Consistent policy enforcement via central push. ### Major Disadvantages - Central manager is a single point of failure. - Implementation and operational complexity. - Bandwidth concentration at central uplinks. - Higher infrastructure and licensing cost. ### Real-World Implementations - **Cisco Firepower Management Center (FMC)** - **IBM QRadar SIEM** - **Splunk Enterprise Security** - **AlienVault OSSIM / USM** ### Frequently Tested Exam Pointers - Always mention **IDMEF** when discussing inter-tier communication. - Always include a **labeled diagram** in long-answer questions. - For design calculations, **show the formula before substitution** for partial credit. - Distinguish **distributed collection** from **distributed control** — this is the most common examiner trap. ### Mnemonic for the 4 Operational Phases **"C-A-C-R"** → **C**apture → **A**nalyze locally → **C**orrelate centrally → **R**espond coordinately.

topics.title: IDPS Deployment - Deploying Network-Based IDPSs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IDPS Deployment - Deploying Network-Based IDPSs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
