## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Network-Based Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (NIDPS / NIPS)** is a security control that monitors network traffic across one or more network segments for malicious activity, policy violations, reconnaissance attempts, and exploit-bearing payloads. **Deployment** refers to the deliberate, architecturally-informed placement of NIDPS sensors (appliances or virtual sensors), analysis engines, and management consoles within a network topology so as to maximize **visibility**, minimize **evasion opportunities**, and balance **detection fidelity** with **performance overhead**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A NIDPS does not operate in isolation — its *value* is entirely a function of *where* it is placed. A perfectly tuned NIDPS placed at the wrong choke point yields incomplete situational awareness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "CCTV Network of a Bank Vault"

Imagine a bank vault with several rooms: the outer gate, the lobby, the safe-deposit room, and the inner vault. A security manager (the **CISO**) wants to install CCTV cameras (the **NIDPS sensors**) but faces a real-world dilemma:

| Real-World Decision | NIDPS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Do you place a camera *outside the building* or *inside the lobby*? | Do you place the sensor *outside the perimeter firewall* or *behind it*? |
| Do cameras record passively, or do they trip the lock (active prevention)? | **Passive IDS mode** (alert-only) vs. **Inline IPS mode** (block traffic) |
| A camera pointed at a wall sees nothing — placement determines coverage | A sensor attached to the wrong switch port sees no traffic |
| Cameras that record 24/7 generate huge files — some footage must be summarized | Sensors that log every packet overwhelm storage; **flow sampling** is used |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Anchor in Memory:**
> - **Sensor**: The packet-capturing probe, deployed on a network segment.
> - **SPAN Port (Switched Port Analyzer)**: A mirror port on a switch that copies traffic to the sensor.
> - **Network TAP (Test Access Point)**: A hardware device that physically splits the optical/electrical signal for inspection.
> - **Inline Sensor / IPS**: Placed directly in the data path; can drop or modify packets.
> - **Passive Sensor / IDS**: Sees a copy of traffic; cannot block.

### 1.3 The Three Core Deployment Objectives

1. **Detection Coverage** — capture traffic from all critical network segments (servers, DMZ, user VLANs, inter-VLAN trunk).
2. **Evasion Resistance** — defeat techniques such as fragmentation, encryption, timing attacks, and protocol smuggling.
3. **Operational Continuity** — the sensor must never become a single point of failure, especially in inline mode.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU viva voice: If asked *"Why is deployment planning the hardest part of NIDPS?"*, the answer is — **evasion-resistance and segment visibility are conflicting goals**. A sensor with full deep-packet visibility (e.g., doing SSL-MITM) handles evasion well but consumes resources; a lightweight flow sensor covers traffic volume but misses payload-level attacks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The NIDPS Architectural Stack

A deployed NIDPS is composed of **three logical tiers**, regardless of whether the product is hardware, software, or virtual:

**Tier 1 — Sensor Layer (Packet Acquisition)**
- Network interface cards (often with **promiscuous mode** enabled) or dedicated capture ASICs.
- Methods of obtaining traffic:
  - **SPAN/Mirror port** — software-driven, may drop packets under load.
  - **Network TAP** — hardware, lossless, full-duplex.
  - **Inline bypass** — sensor sits in the wire; uses **fail-open** or **fail-closed** relays.
- **Encapsulation stripping** (VLAN tags, MPLS labels, GRE, IPsec ESP partial parsing).

**Tier 2 — Analysis Engine (Detection Core)**
- **Signature-based detection** — pattern matching against rule sets (Snort, Suricata rule syntax).
- **Anomaly-based detection** — statistical deviation from a learned baseline.
- **Stateful protocol analysis** — validates that packets conform to the protocol's state machine (e.g., RFC-compliant TCP handshake).
- **Protocol decoders** — reassemble HTTP, DNS, SMB, TLS, etc., before applying signatures.

**Tier 3 — Management & Response Console**
- Centralized correlation, alerting (SNMP traps, syslog, email), tuning interfaces, and signature updates.
- The console itself **does not** see packets — it receives *events* from sensors.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Deployment Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** Most mark losses occur when students describe *what* a deployment does, but fail to explain *why* a particular placement is chosen. Always tie placement to a specific threat model.

- **Why place a sensor at the network perimeter?**  
  To catch inbound reconnaissance, malware delivery, and exploit attempts *before* they reach internal hosts. This is the **outermost layer of defense**.

- **Why place a sensor *behind* the perimeter firewall?**  
  A firewall filters out obvious junk; the behind-firewall sensor focuses on **tunneled threats** (e.g., allowed HTTPS carrying malware, allowed SSH with brute force). This is called **"egress filtering"** or **"internal threat detection"**.

- **Why place a sensor at the DMZ?**  
  The DMZ hosts public-facing servers. Attacks such as **SQL injection, XSS, web-shell uploads** are visible only at the *application layer*, which a sensor inside the DMZ can inspect with full HTTP context.

- **Why place a sensor on the internal backbone (core switch)?**  
  To detect **lateral movement**, **insider threats**, **east-west attacks** (e.g., ransomware pivoting between VLANs), and **compromised endpoints beaconing to C2 servers**.

### 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> While NIDPS is primarily architectural, KTU 2024 often tests **sensor performance metrics** and **Bayesian detection** fundamentals.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Packet Capture Rate (PCR)** | $PCR = \frac{P_{captured}}{P_{received}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**; an NIDPS should aim for **PCR = 100%** under load |
| 2 | **Sensor Throughput** | $T_{sensor} = \frac{B_{processed}}{t}$ (in bps or pps) | **bps** = bits per second; **pps** = packets per second |
| 3 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Probability an alert is raised for *benign* traffic |
| 4 | **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Probability a *real* attack is missed |
| 5 | **Bayes' Theorem for IDPS** | $P(Attack \mid Alert) = \frac{P(Alert \mid Attack) \cdot P(Attack)}{P(Alert)}$ | Used to compute the *post-alert* probability of a true attack |
| 6 | **Inline Latency Budget** | $L_{budget} = L_{baseline} + L_{sensor}$ | $L_{sensor} \le 50\,\mu s$ typically tolerated |
| 7 | **Link Saturation by Sensor** | $U = \frac{T_{sensor}}{L_{capacity}} \le 0.7$ | Recommended max utilization (**70% rule**) |
| 8 | **Coverage Ratio** | $C = \frac{N_{monitored\_segments}}{N_{total\_critical\_segments}}$ | Higher is better; KTU expects $\ge 0.9$ for production |
| 9 | **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum (t_{detected} - t_{attack\_start})}{N_{attacks}}$ | Reported in **seconds/minutes** |
| 10 | **Bypass Switch Activation Time** | $t_{bypass} \le 4\,ms$ (typical) | Hardware-fail-open capability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, a poorly deployed NIDPS is the single most common reason enterprises experience **breaches that "should have been detected."** Major compliance frameworks — **PCI-DSS Requirement 11.4**, **NIST SP 800-94**, and **ISO 27001 A.12.6.1** — mandate documented NIDPS placement. Engineers must produce **network diagrams showing sensor placement**, **signature update SOPs**, and **incident-response runbooks** triggered by NIDPS alerts.

> [!TIP]
> A useful interview-grade fact: **fail-open vs. fail-closed** is decided by *security policy*, not by the vendor. A financial trading floor chooses fail-open (a network outage costs \$millions/minute). A SCADA network chooses fail-closed (an unsafe actuator command could cause physical damage).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing a Sensor's Throughput Requirement

**Problem:** An organization has a 10 Gbps backbone link. The NIDPS sensor must process every packet at wire speed. Compute the *minimum sustained packet processing rate* (pps) assuming minimum-sized Ethernet frames.

**Step 1 — Recall the minimum Ethernet frame size.**  
The IEEE 802.3 minimum frame is **64 bytes** (header + payload + CRC) but the *on-wire* minimum including the **8-byte preamble + 12-byte inter-frame gap** is:

$$
L_{min} = 64 + 8 + 12 = 84 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bits.**  
$$
L_{min\_bits} = 84 \times 8 = 672 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time to transmit one minimum frame on a 10 Gbps link.**  
$$
t_{frame} = \frac{L_{min\_bits}}{B_{link}} = \frac{672}{10 \times 10^9} = 6.72 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute packets per second.**  
$$
PPS = \frac{1}{t_{frame}} = \frac{1}{6.72 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 14.88 \times 10^6 \text{ pps}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the 70% utilization rule for sustainable sensor operation.**  
$$
PPS_{sensor} = PPS \times 0.7 \approx 10.42 \text{ Mpps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A 10 Gbps sensor must sustain **~10.4 million packets per second**. This is why enterprise NIDPS appliances use dedicated **Cavium/Marvell NPU chips** or **DPDK-accelerated** x86 pipelines — general-purpose CPUs cannot reach this rate with software-only processing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bayesian Alert Confidence

**Problem:** A NIDPS raises an alert. Historical data shows the signature fires with 95% recall on actual attacks (True Positive Rate = 0.95) and a 2% false-positive rate (FPR = 0.02). Baseline traffic shows 1 in 10,000 packets is malicious. Compute the probability that this alert represents a *real attack*.

**Step 1 — Define events.**  
Let $A$ = real attack, $B$ = alert triggered.

$$
P(B \mid A) = 0.95, \quad P(B \mid \neg A) = 0.02, \quad P(A) = 0.0001
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(\neg A)$.**  
$$
P(\neg A) = 1 - P(A) = 0.9999
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the law of total probability to find $P(B)$.**  
$$
P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)
$$
$$
P(B) = (0.95 \times 0.0001) + (0.02 \times 0.9999)
$$
$$
P(B) = 0.000095 + 0.019998 \approx 0.02009
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' theorem.**  
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)} = \frac{0.000095}{0.02009} \approx 0.00473
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage.**  
$$
P(A \mid B) \approx 0.473\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-intuitive KTU Insight:** Even a 95%-accurate signature, applied to *very rare* malicious packets, gives an alert that is correct only **~0.47% of the time**. This is the **base-rate fallacy** and is the single most important reason SIEM correlation and alert-prioritization exist. Students often lose marks by *not* quoting this number when discussing NIDPS tuning.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inline IPS Latency Budget

**Problem:** An online trading application has a baseline round-trip latency of 4 ms. Adding an inline IPS must not push latency beyond 5 ms. Compute the *maximum acceptable IPS processing latency* and validate against a typical 10 Gbps sensor rated at 35 μs.

**Step 1 — Define the budget.**  
$$
L_{budget} = L_{max} - L_{baseline} = 5 \text{ ms} - 4 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with sensor's measured latency.**  
$$
L_{sensor} = 35\,\mu s = 0.035 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute headroom.**  
$$
H = L_{budget} - L_{sensor} = 1 - 0.035 = 0.965 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The 0.965 ms of headroom allows for **deep-packet-inspection** of TLS handshakes (which can briefly add 200–500 μs) without breaching the SLA. This is why low-latency NIDPS designs avoid *synchronous disk I/O* in the fast path.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Placement vs. Threat

| Placement Location | Visibility | Common Threats Detected | Drawbacks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outside Perimeter Firewall** | Inbound to public IP space | Port scans, DoS, malware delivery | Encrypted payload inspection limited |
| **Inside Perimeter Firewall (LAN edge)** | Inbound + Outbound + Lateral (limited) | C2 beacons, malware C&C, insider exfil | High volume of allowed traffic |
| **DMZ (between firewall and public servers)** | Inbound to DMZ servers | Web exploits, SQLi, brute force | Requires HTTP/FTP/SMTP decoders |
| **Internal Core / Backbone** | East-West traffic | Lateral movement, ransomware propagation | Encrypted east-west traffic is opaque |
| **Behind VPN Concentrator** | Decrypted VPN traffic | Insider abuse, malware over VPN | Privacy/regulatory concerns |
| **Cloud VPC (East-West)** | Inter-subnet traffic in AWS/Azure/GCP | Cloud-native attacks, IAM abuse | Requires cloud-tap (VPC Traffic Mirroring) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Deployed NIDPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Internet[Internet] --> PERIM[Perimeter Firewall]
    PERIM --> DMZF[DMZ Firewall]
    DMZF --> DMZSW[DMZ Switch]
    DMZSW --> WebSrv[Web Server]
    DMZSW --> MailSrv[Mail Server]
    DMZF --> CoreSW[Core Switch]
    CoreSW --> UserVLAN[User VLAN]
    CoreSW --> ServerVLAN[Server VLAN]
    CoreSW --> DBVLAN[Database VLAN]

    TAP1[Perimeter TAP] -.captures.-> S1[Sensor 1 Outside FW]
    SPAN1[DMZ SPAN Port] -.mirrors.-> S2[Sensor 2 DMZ]
    SPAN2[Core SPAN Port] -.mirrors.-> S3[Sensor 3 Core]

    S1 --> ENG1[Analysis Engine 1]
    S2 --> ENG2[Analysis Engine 2]
    S3 --> ENG3[Analysis Engine 3]
    ENG1 --> CONS[Central Management Console]
    ENG2 --> CONS
    ENG3 --> CONS
    CONS --> SIEM[Enterprise SIEM]
    CONS --> ANA[Security Analyst Workstation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show *forward traffic flow*; dotted arrows show *out-of-band monitoring* into sensors. The Central Management Console is the *only* component the analyst interacts with. SIEM ingestion is one-way (logs/alerts), not bidirectional.

### 4.2 Inline IPS Deployment with Hardware Bypass

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FW[Firewall] --> TRX1[Inline IPS Sensor]
    TRX1 --> BPC[Bypass Relay Card]
    BPC --> SW1[Aggregation Switch]
    BPC -.fail-open path.-> SW1
    TRX1 -.management only.-> MGT[NIDPS Manager]
    MGT --> SIG[Signature Update Server]
    MGT --> ALERT[Alert Channel]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A *fail-open* bypass relay mechanically short-circuits the inline sensor if it loses power or crashes. This is *always* implemented in hardware (an FPGA-controlled relay), not in software, because the CPU is the very thing that has failed.

### 4.3 Encrypted Traffic Inspection (SSL/TLS MITM) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Internal Client] --> SW1[LAN Switch]
    SW1 --> INLINE[Inline IPS with SSL MITM]
    INLINE --> FW[Perimeter Firewall]
    FW --> EXT[External HTTPS Server]
    CA[Int CA for re-signing] -.trusted root.-> INLINE
    INLINE --> EVT[Decrypted Event Stream]
    EVT --> CONS[Console]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** SSL inspection requires the **client's browser to trust the NIDPS re-issuance CA**. If the CA is not distributed via Group Policy or MDM, users see certificate warnings and bypass the inspection. The NIDPS becomes invisible.

### 4.4 Cloud Deployment (VPC Traffic Mirroring in AWS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP1[App EC2] --> ENI1[ENI Mirror Source]
    APP2[App EC2] --> ENI2[ENI Mirror Source]
    ENI1 -.mirror session.-> MIRROR[VPC Traffic Mirror Target]
    ENI2 -.mirror session.-> MIRROR
    MIRROR --> VSENS[Virtual NIDPS Sensor ENI]
    VSENS --> ENGSG[Cloud Analysis Engine]
    ENGSG --> CLOLG[CloudWatch / S3 Logs]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a Network TAP and a SPAN port. Which is preferred for high-throughput enterprise NIDPS deployment and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Network TAP** is a hardware device physically spliced into a cable that splits the optical/electrical signal, sending one copy to the sensor and passing the original traffic onward. It is **passive, full-duplex, and lossless**, even during switch failures.
A **SPAN port** is a software feature on a switch that mirrors selected VLAN/port traffic to a designated mirror port. It is **lossy under congestion**, may drop frames when the mirror port's buffer fills, and is dependent on switch CPU resources.
For high-throughput (10 Gbps+) NIDPS, **TAP is preferred** because of its guaranteed full-duplex capture and zero packet loss. (1 mark TAP, 1 mark SPAN, 1 mark justification.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "fail-open" and "fail-closed" behavior in inline IPS deployment. Give one scenario where each is appropriate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fail-open**: On sensor failure, traffic flows uninterrupted. Used where **availability outweighs confidentiality** (e.g., stock-trading networks, hospital life-support).
- **Fail-closed**: On sensor failure, traffic is blocked. Used where **integrity/safety outweighs availability** (e.g., SCADA/ICS, military command networks). (1 mark each definition, 1 mark for contrasting scenarios.)

---

### Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — NIDPS Placement Architecture**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
Explain, with the help of a network diagram, the **strategic placement of Network-Based IDPS sensors** in a typical enterprise with the following zones: Internet, perimeter firewall, DMZ, internal core, and a server farm. Justify *why* each placement is chosen with reference to the threat model.

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
Discuss the **technical challenges of deploying NIDPS in switched and encrypted environments**. Specifically address: (i) SPAN port limitations, (ii) handling of VLAN-tagged traffic, (iii) SSL/TLS inspection, and (iv) link aggregation (LACP) scenarios.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution [7 Marks]:**
1. **Outside the perimeter firewall (1 mark):** Catches reconnaissance, volumetric DDoS, and exploit attempts *before* they are filtered. Acts as the *first line of visibility* into Internet-borne threats.
2. **Inside the perimeter firewall, before the core (1 mark):** Detects threats that *passed* the firewall — e.g., allowed HTTPS traffic carrying malware, allowed ports used as covert channels, and C2 beaconing.
3. **DMZ zone (1 mark):** Required for full HTTP/SMTP/FTP context to detect **web application attacks** (SQLi, XSS, command injection) targeting public-facing servers.
4. **Internal core switch (1 mark):** Catches **lateral movement**, ransomware spread between VLANs, and insider threats — the *east-west* traffic that perimeter sensors never see.
5. **Server farm segment (1 mark):** Detects **database exploits**, credential abuse, and east-west attacks between application and database tiers.
6. **Diagram (1 mark):** A labeled block diagram showing all five sensor placements with arrows for traffic flow.
7. **Threat-model linkage (1 mark):** Each placement is explicitly tied to a specific attack class (e.g., perimeter sensor for worm propagation, core sensor for Kerberoasting).

**(b) Solution [7 Marks]:**
- **(i) SPAN port limitations (2 marks):** SPAN is a *best-effort* copy. Under congestion, the switch prioritizes *real* forwarding and may drop mirrored frames — leading to silent detection gaps. Mitigation: use dedicated monitoring VLANs and bound SPAN traffic.
- **(ii) VLAN-tagged traffic (1 mark):** A sensor must strip 802.1Q tags before signature matching; misconfigured sensors see the *outer* tag and miss inner payloads. The sensor's NIC must operate in *promiscuous mode* and include a VLAN decoder.
- **(iii) SSL/TLS inspection (2 marks):** Encrypted traffic is opaque. Two approaches: (a) **SSL-MITM** with an internal re-signing CA — defeats encryption but breaks E2E guarantees; (b) **metadata-only inspection** (JA3 fingerprinting, SNI analysis, certificate fingerprinting) — privacy-preserving but limited detection.
- **(iv) LACP / Link aggregation (2 marks):** When traffic is hashed across multiple links, a single SPAN port may see only one direction or one hash bucket, producing **asymmetric flow** reconstruction. Solution: deploy sensors on **both** member links, or use a **TAP aggregator** that combines LACP member streams.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Encrypted Traffic & High Availability**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
Describe the architecture of an **inline IPS that performs SSL/TLS inspection**. Include a labeled diagram, explain the role of the **re-signing Certificate Authority (CA)**, and discuss the **legal and privacy implications**.

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
Explain **high-availability (HA) deployment** of NIDPS in inline mode. Cover: (i) active-passive sensor pairs, (ii) state synchronization, (iii) hardware bypass behavior, and (iv) link failure recovery.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution [7 Marks]:**
- **Architecture (2 marks):** The IPS terminates the client's TLS session, opens the plaintext, applies signatures, then re-encrypts to the original destination. The client must trust the IPS's issuing CA.
- **Diagram (2 marks):** A clear block diagram showing: Client → IPS (decrypt → inspect → re-encrypt) → Server, with the re-signing CA off to the side.
- **Re-signing CA (1 mark):** A subordinate CA signed by the enterprise's internal root. Its certificate is distributed to all managed endpoints via GPO/MDM.
- **Legal/Privacy (2 marks):** Decryption of employee traffic may violate wire-tap laws (e.g., ECPA, GDPR, India's IT Act). A documented **acceptable-use policy** and **employee consent** are mandatory.

**(b) Solution [7 Marks]:**
- **Active-passive (2 marks):** Two identical sensors; the active one handles traffic, the passive one stays synchronized. On failure, the passive takes over via VRRP or vendor-proprietary heartbeats.
- **State sync (2 marks):** TCP session tables, signature state, and connection tracking must replicate from active to passive in real-time. A 50–200 ms sync delay is typical.
- **Bypass behavior (2 marks):** Both sensors have hardware bypass relays; on complete cluster failure, the *upstream* switch's relay short-circuits the traffic to maintain connectivity (fail-open) or blocks it (fail-closed).
- **Recovery (1 mark):** On a single sensor's failure, traffic is re-routed through the surviving sensor; full throughput is restored once the failed sensor reboots and re-syncs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TAP vs. SPAN functionally:** Students often describe both as "the sensor reads the traffic." *Examiner expects:* hardware vs. software, lossless vs. lossy, full-duplex vs. best-effort.
> 2. **Forgetting asymmetric routing in LACP:** A sensor sees only one half of a session and produces false alerts. This is a **favourite 7-mark question**.
> 3. **Ignoring the base-rate fallacy:** Many students describe NIDPS as "95% accurate" and stop there. *Examiner expects:* the Bayesian demonstration that 95% signature accuracy yields only ~0.5% *alert confidence* on rare attacks.
> 4. **Skipping legal/ethical context in SSL-MITM:** Marks lost when the answer is *purely* technical without addressing employee consent and regulatory compliance.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **Network-Based IDPS** is a packet-level security monitor deployed at strategic chokepoints.
- [x] **Deployment** is a function of *threat model* + *network topology* + *evasion resistance*.
- [x] **Sensor placement** is chosen at the **perimeter**, **DMZ**, **core**, and **server farm** — each for a distinct threat class.
- [x] **TAP > SPAN** for high-throughput, lossless capture.
- [x] **Inline IPS** introduces latency (typically 30–50 μs) and requires a **hardware bypass** for fault tolerance.
- [x] **Encrypted traffic** is the single biggest deployment challenge; mitigated via **SSL-MITM** or **metadata-based** detection.
- [x] **Switched environments** require **promiscuous-mode NICs**, **VLAN decoders**, and **LACP-aware aggregation**.
- [x] **Bayes' Theorem** quantifies the *base-rate fallacy*: high signature accuracy ≠ high alert confidence.
- [x] **Fail-open** is chosen for availability; **fail-closed** for safety/integrity.
- [x] **High availability** uses active-passive pairs with state synchronization and hardware bypass relays.
- [x] **Cloud NIDPS** uses VPC Traffic Mirroring (AWS) or vTAP (Azure/GCP).
- [x] **Compliance frameworks** (PCI-DSS 11.4, NIST SP 800-94) mandate documented NIDPS placement.
- [x] **KTU 14-mark questions** almost always combine a *placement* sub-question with an *encrypted/switched* sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deploying Host-Based IDPSs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deploying Host-Based IDPSs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Host-Based Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (HIDPS)** is a security mechanism that monitors and analyzes the internals of a computing system—such as system calls, file system modifications, log files, registry changes, process executions, and network traffic originating from or destined to the host—to detect and optionally prevent malicious activity. When deployed, the HIDPS operates at the granularity of a single endpoint (host), in contrast to a Network-Based IDPS (NIDPS), which monitors network segments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT742 – Module 2):**
> *"Deploying Host-Based IDPSs involves the strategic placement, configuration, and integration of HIDPS sensors (agents) on individual hosts to monitor system-level events, with centralized management consoles providing correlation, alerting, and response actions."*

### Key Components of a Deployed HIDPS

| Component | Function | Deployment Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor / Agent** | Collects host-level telemetry | Installed on each protected endpoint |
| **Management Server** | Aggregates, correlates, and analyzes events | Centralized server on a secure VLAN |
| **Database** | Stores events, signatures, baselines | Backend repository (SQL/NoSQL) |
| **Response Module** | Executes prevention actions (block, kill, quarantine) | Co-located with agent |
| **Console / Dashboard** | Analyst interface for monitoring | SOC workstation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Security Guard Inside the House"

Imagine a large office building. A **Network-Based IDPS** is like a **security guard at the main gate**—he checks everyone who comes in or goes out of the building. He is efficient, but he cannot see what happens *inside* each office.

A **Host-Based IDPS**, on the other hand, is like **placing a personal security guard inside every individual office room**. This guard:
- Knows exactly which drawer was opened (file integrity).
- Notices when an employee uses a tool they've never touched before (anomalous process).
- Detects if someone copies files at 3 AM (behavior anomaly).
- Can lock the door instantly if something is suspicious (prevention/inline response).

This is precisely what deploying a HIDPS means—putting sensors **close to the assets themselves** so that threats that bypass perimeter defenses (or originate from inside) can still be caught.

## 1.3 Why Deployment Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A HIDPS is only as effective as its deployment topology. A poorly deployed agent (e.g., wrong monitoring scope, disabled self-protection, missing log forwarding) creates a **false sense of security**—a phenomenon examiners frequently use as a question stem.

The three pillars of HIDPS deployment are:
1. **Coverage** – Every critical host must have a sensor.
2. **Configuration** – Policies must reflect the host's role and risk profile.
3. **Integration** – The HIDPS must interoperate with SIEM, firewalls, and patch management systems.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Deployment

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time between intrusion and detection.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time between detection and containment.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{False Positives}}{\text{False Positives} + \text{True Negatives}}$
- **Agent CPU Overhead:** Typically **2%–8%** of host CPU under normal load.
- **Event Throughput:** Measured in **Events Per Second (EPS)**, typically 100–10,000 EPS per agent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deployment Topologies

Host-Based IDPSs can be deployed in three primary topologies. Understanding when to use each is a frequent KTU question.

### 2.1.1 Agent-Based Deployment (Most Common)

In an **agent-based** deployment, a software sensor is installed directly on the host. The agent hooks into the OS kernel to monitor:
- System calls (via kernel modules or eBPF probes on Linux).
- File system changes (via inotify / File System Filter drivers on Windows).
- Registry modifications (Windows-specific).
- Process creations and terminations.
- Network sockets opened by user processes.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Agent is installed via MSI (Windows), RPM/DEB (Linux), or MDM push (macOS/iOS/Android).
2. Agent registers with the management server over **TCP/443** (TLS-encrypted).
3. Management server pushes a **policy bundle** to the agent.
4. Agent begins collecting events using OS-native APIs.
5. Events are batched and forwarded to the management server (often every 30–60 seconds).
6. On detection, agent executes local response (kill process, block IP, quarantine file) **before** reporting.

### 2.1.2 Agentless Deployment

Used in environments where installing agents is impractical—**virtual machines, cloud workloads, legacy systems, or OT/ICS devices**.

**Mechanisms:**
- Hypervisor-level introspection (e.g., VMware vSphere APIs).
- Log collection via WMI, SSH, or Syslog.
- API-based monitoring (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Agentless HIDPS has **lower host overhead** but **higher latency** in detection and **less granular visibility** (cannot see in-process behavior).

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Deployment

Combines agent-based monitoring on critical endpoints (servers, domain controllers) with agentless monitoring on ephemeral or un-agentable assets (cloud VMs, IoT, OT).

## 2.2 Pre-Deployment Planning Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5-Phase Deployment Model (NIST SP 800-94 aligned):**

1. **Asset Classification** – Identify crown-jewel hosts (Domain Controllers, DB servers, ERP).
2. **Risk Assessment** – Map threats to assets using STRIDE or PASTA.
3. **Policy Engineering** – Define detection rules and response actions per asset class.
4. **Pilot Deployment** – Deploy in **monitor-only mode** on a non-production segment for **2–4 weeks**.
5. **Full Rollout** – Transition to **prevent mode** in phases (10% → 50% → 100%).

## 2.3 Mode of Operation

| Mode | Action on Detection | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitor-Only (Detection)** | Logs and alerts; no blocking | Pilot phase, sensitive servers |
| **Block / Prevent (Inline)** | Actively blocks malicious activity | Production endpoints |
| **Hybrid** | Alerts for low-severity, blocks for high-severity | Recommended KTU best practice |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Lower is better; target $< 5\%$ |
| False Negative Rate | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{\text{FN}}{\text{FN} + \text{TP}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Critical metric; must be $\approx 0$ |
| Detection Latency | $L = t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{intrusion}}$ | Seconds | Inline HIDPS: $L < 1$ s |
| Agent Throughput | $T = \dfrac{E}{t}$ | EPS | Events per second |
| Storage Requirement | $S = T \times \bar{e} \times R$ | Bytes | $\bar{e}$ = avg event size, $R$ = retention |
| Coverage Ratio | $C = \dfrac{H_{\text{protected}}}{H_{\text{total}}}$ | Ratio | Target: $C \geq 0.95$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse FPR and FNR. Remember — **FPR = False Alarm Rate** (Type I error), **FNR = Miss Rate** (Type II error).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

HIDPS deployment is foundational to:
- **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** – Modern evolution of HIDPS (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint).
- **Extended Detection and Response (XDR)** – HIDPS feeds into XDR alongside NIDPS, email security, and cloud telemetry.
- **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** – Continuous host monitoring is a ZTA pillar (NIST SP 800-207).
- **Compliance** – PCI-DSS Req. 11.5, HIPAA §164.308(a)(1), ISO 27001 A.12.4 mandate host-based monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sizing the HIDPS Backend Storage

> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> *"An organization plans to deploy a HIDPS across 500 hosts. Each host generates an average of 200 events/day, with an average event size of 512 bytes. The organization wants a 90-day retention window. Calculate the total storage required for the HIDPS database."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total events per day across the fleet:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{day}} &= N_{\text{hosts}} \times E_{\text{per host}} \\
E_{\text{day}} &= 500 \times 200 \\
E_{\text{day}} &= 100{,}000 \text{ events/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total events over the retention period (90 days):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= E_{\text{day}} \times R_{\text{days}} \\
E_{\text{total}} &= 100{,}000 \times 90 \\
E_{\text{total}} &= 9{,}000{,}000 \text{ events}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Storage required before indexing overhead:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{raw}} &= E_{\text{total}} \times \bar{e} \\
S_{\text{raw}} &= 9{,}000{,}000 \times 512 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{raw}} &= 4{,}608{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{raw}} &= 4.608 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply indexing and metadata overhead (typical factor: 2.5×):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{final}} &= S_{\text{raw}} \times O_{\text{index}} \\
S_{\text{final}} &= 4.608 \times 2.5 \\
S_{\text{final}} &= 11.52 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Approximately **11.52 GB** of storage is needed for 90 days. KTU examiners often add a **RAID-1 (mirroring) factor of 2×**, yielding a final answer of **23.04 GB**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Deployment Decision

| Host Type | Recommended Mode | Agent Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Domain Controller | Prevent (Inline) | Kernel-level | High-value asset; full visibility needed |
| Public Web Server | Monitor + Block | Agent-based with WAF tie-in | DDoS mitigation at network edge |
| Legacy ERP Server | Monitor-Only | Agentless via Syslog | OS too old for modern agent |
| Cloud VM (ephemeral) | Monitor | Agentless (API) | Lifecycle too short for agent install |
| Developer Workstation | Hybrid | User-mode agent | Balance between visibility and performance |
| ICS / SCADA Controller | Monitor-Only | Agentless (network tap) | Cannot risk agent crash on controller |

## 3.3 Self-Protection Mechanism (Derivation of Trust)

> **Why this is critical:** A HIDPS agent running on a compromised host is itself a target. Attackers first attempt to **kill, disable, or tamper** with the agent. Hence, deployment must include **self-protection** features.

The trust chain is bootstrapped as follows:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Boot} &\rightarrow \text{TPM/UEFI Measured} \rightarrow \text{Kernel} \\
&\rightarrow \text{HIDPS Driver (signed)} \rightarrow \text{User-space Agent}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the agent is **kernel-level**, it is harder to kill; if **user-level**, it is easier to tamper. Therefore, **kernel-level agents are preferred for high-security deployments**.

## 3.4 Coverage Calculation Example

> **Question:** *"A company has 800 endpoints. After HIDPS deployment, 760 endpoints are reporting events. Compute the coverage ratio."*
$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{H_{\text{protected}}}{H_{\text{total}}} \\
C &= \dfrac{760}{800} \\
C &= 0.95 \quad \text{or} \quad 95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

A coverage of **95%** is the **minimum acceptable threshold** in most KTU-evaluated deployments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HIDPS Deployment Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL NETWORK / INTERNET"]
        attacker["ATTACKER NODE"]
    end

    subgraph PERIM["PERIMETER LAYER"]
        firewall["FIREWALL / UTM"]
        nidps["NETWORK BASED IDPS"]
    end

    subgraph CORP["CORPORATE NETWORK / DATA CENTER"]
        mgmt["HIDPS MANAGEMENT SERVER"]
        db["EVENT DATABASE"]

        siem["SIEM CORRELATION ENGINE"]
    end

    subgraph HOSTS["PROTECTED ENDPOINT HOSTS"]
        dc["DOMAIN CONTROLLER WITH HIDPS AGENT"]
        web["WEB SERVER WITH HIDPS AGENT"]
        erp["ERP SERVER AGENTLESS MONITORING"]
        dev["DEVELOPER WORKSTATION WITH HIDPS AGENT"]
    end

    attacker --> firewall
    firewall --> nidps
    nidps --> dc
    nidps --> web
    nidps --> erp
    nidps --> dev

    dc -- TLS 443 --> mgmt
    web -- TLS 443 --> mgmt
    dev -- TLS 443 --> mgmt
    erp -- SYSLOG --> mgmt

    mgmt --> db
    mgmt --> console
    mgmt --> siem
    siem --> console
```

## 4.2 HIDPS Deployment Workflow (Lifecycle View)

```mermaid

    A["PHASE 1 ASSET DISCOVERY"] --> B["PHASE 2 RISK CLASSIFICATION"]
    B --> C["PHASE 3 POLICY DESIGN"]

    D --> E{"TUNE FALSE POSITIVES BELOW 5 PERCENT"}

    E -- NO --> D
    F --> G["PHASE 6 EXPAND TO 50 PERCENT"]

```

## 4.3 Agent Communication Sequence Diagram

```mermaid

    participant Agent as HIDPS Agent on Host
    participant Server as Management Server

    Agent->>Server: TLS Handshake and Registration

    Agent->>Agent: Monitor OS Events
    Agent->>Server: Batch Event Upload Every 30 Seconds
    Server->>Server: Correlate and Score Events
    Server->>Console: High Severity Alert
    Console->>Server: Acknowledge and Investigate

    Agent->>Agent: Execute Local Prevention Action
```

## 4.4 Deployment Mode Decision Matrix (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    Q1{"IS HOST A CRITICAL PRODUCTION SERVER"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"DOES IT SUPPORT MODERN AGENTS"}

    Q2 -- NO --> M2["AGENTLESS MONITOR ONLY"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{"IS HOST EPHEMERAL OR CLOUD VM"}
    Q3 -- YES --> M3["AGENTLESS API BASED"]
    Q3 -- NO --> M4["USER MODE AGENT HYBRID MODE"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
*"Differentiate between agent-based and agentless deployment of Host-Based IDPSs. Give one example scenario for each."*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Agent-Based | Agentless |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Software sensor installed on host | No host installation; uses APIs or remote queries |
| Visibility | High (system calls, file integrity, registry) | Limited (logs, hypervisor introspection) |
| Latency | Low (real-time local analysis) | Higher (depends on poll interval) |
| Host Overhead | 2%–8% CPU | Negligible |
| Tamper Resistance | Medium–High (with self-protection) | Low |
| **Example** | CrowdStrike Falcon on a Domain Controller | Monitoring a legacy Windows NT 4.0 server via WMI event log forwarding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 3-mark answer expects **at least 3 distinct differences** plus **one example scenario each**. Skipping the example forfeits 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
*"List and briefly explain any three pre-deployment considerations for a Host-Based IDPS."*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Asset Classification:** Identify critical hosts (Domain Controllers, database servers) that need kernel-level protection versus low-value assets that can be agentless.
2. **Policy Tuning:** Start in monitor-only mode to baseline normal behavior, then enable prevention. Improper tuning leads to alert fatigue.
3. **Network Bandwidth:** Each agent forwards events over the network. For a 500-host deployment, ensure management VLAN has sufficient bandwidth (typically **1 Gbps minimum**).
4. **Self-Protection:** The agent itself must be hardened against termination; kernel-level agents offer stronger protection.
5. **Integration:** The HIDPS must feed into SIEM/SOAR for correlation and automated response.

*(Any three well-explained points earn full 3 marks.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Slot A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *"Explain the architecture of a Host-Based IDPS in detail. With the help of a block diagram, describe the role of the sensor, management server, database, and response module."*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"An organization has 1,200 endpoints. A HIDPS is to be deployed with each agent generating 350 events/day, average event size 1 KB, and 180-day retention. Assuming an indexing overhead of 3× and RAID-1 mirroring, calculate the total storage required. Also, if the coverage after deployment is found to be 1,140 endpoints, compute the coverage ratio and comment on its adequacy."*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Slot B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *"Discuss the agent-based, agentless, and hybrid deployment models of Host-Based IDPSs. Compare their advantages and limitations with a suitable use case for each."*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"Describe the 5-phase deployment model recommended for HIDPS rollout. Why is a pilot phase in monitor-only mode critical, and what KPIs should be measured before transitioning to prevent mode?"*

---

## Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a) — Architecture

A Host-Based IDPS consists of four logical tiers:

1. **Sensor / Agent Layer:** Sits on the protected host. Hooks into the OS kernel to capture system calls, file system changes (via filter drivers), registry mutations, and process lineage. On Windows, it uses **ETW (Event Tracing for Windows)** and **Minifilter drivers**; on Linux, it uses **eBPF** or **LSM (Linux Security Module)** hooks.

2. **Management Server:** Central brain. Receives events over TLS from agents, performs correlation, signature matching, and behavioral baselining. Pushes updated policies back to agents.

3. **Database:** Stores raw events, normalized alerts, and forensic artifacts. Usually a time-series DB (Elasticsearch, ClickHouse) for fast querying.

4. **Response Module:** Executes actions—**terminate process, block IP, quarantine file, roll back registry, isolate host from network**. In **inline** mode, the response is automatic; in **monitor-only** mode, it is manual via the console.

```
[ Host Kernel ] --> [ Agent Buffer ] --TLS 443--> [ Management Server ] --> [ DB ]
                                                    |
                                                    v
                                            [ Response Engine ]
                                                    |
                                                    v
                                          [ SOC Console + SIEM ]
```

### Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical

**Step 1: Total events per day**
$$
E_{\text{day}} = 1200 \times 350 = 420{,}000 \text{ events/day}
$$

**Step 2: Total events over 180 days**
$$
E_{\text{total}} = 420{,}000 \times 180 = 75{,}600{,}000 \text{ events}
$$

**Step 3: Raw storage**
$$
S_{\text{raw}} = 75{,}600{,}000 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 77{,}414{,}400{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 77.41 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4: Apply 3× indexing overhead**
$$
S_{\text{indexed}} = 77.41 \times 3 = 232.24 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5: Apply RAID-1 mirroring (factor 2×)**
$$
S_{\text{final}} = 232.24 \times 2 = 464.48 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 6: Coverage ratio**
$$
C = \frac{1140}{1200} = 0.95 = 95\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 95% coverage is **acceptable** as it meets the industry baseline. The 60 unprotected endpoints must be remediated via alternative methods (agentless monitoring or network-based IDPS coverage).

### Solution to Question B(a) — Deployment Models

| Model | Mechanism | Advantage | Limitation | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Agent-Based** | Software sensor on host | Deep visibility, low latency, real-time prevention | Host overhead, agent management | Critical servers, workstations |
| **Agentless** | Remote API/Log collection | No host impact, fast deployment | Limited visibility, higher latency | Cloud VMs, legacy, OT/ICS |
| **Hybrid** | Mixed approach | Balanced coverage and performance | Complex to manage | Large heterogeneous enterprises |

### Solution to Question B(b) — 5-Phase Model

The 5-phase model is:
1. **Discovery & Classification** – Inventory hosts, tag by criticality.
2. **Policy Engineering** – Build detection rules.
3. **Pilot (Monitor-Only)** – Deploy on 5–10% of non-critical hosts for 2–4 weeks.
4. **Tuning** – Adjust thresholds to bring FPR below **5%**.
5. **Phased Rollout** – Expand in waves: 10% → 50% → 100%, enabling **Prevent mode** only after stability is confirmed.

**Why pilot matters:** It establishes a **behavioral baseline** of the environment. Without this, signature-based rules generate excessive false positives.

**Key KPIs before moving to Prevent mode:**
- False Positive Rate $< 5\%$
- Agent uptime $> 99.5\%$
- Event forwarding success rate $> 98\%$
- Mean time to detect (MTTD) within SLA

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing FPR and FNR** — they are *not* complements in general; FPR + TPR = 1 only for a balanced binary classifier. Read the formula carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting indexing overhead** in storage calculations — most students stop at the raw byte count and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Conflating HIDPS with antivirus** — antivirus uses *signature-based* file scanning; HIDPS is *behavioral* and *system-call-aware*.
> 4. **Skipping the self-protection discussion** — a HIDPS agent without tamper resistance is a deployment failure; examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **Not distinguishing monitor-only vs. prevent mode** — this is a frequent 2-mark short-answer discriminator.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **HIDPS** monitors *one host*; **NIDPS** monitors *one network segment*. Do not confuse.
- Deployment topologies: **Agent-based, Agentless, Hybrid** — choose based on host criticality, OS age, and visibility needs.
- Two operating modes: **Monitor-Only (passive)** and **Prevent (inline/active)**. Always **pilot in Monitor-Only**.
- **Self-protection** (kernel-level agent, signed driver) is mandatory — otherwise attackers kill the agent first.
- Key formulas to memorize:
  - $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$
  - $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN+TP}$
  - $C = \dfrac{H_{\text{protected}}}{H_{\text{total}}}$ (target $\geq 0.95$)
  - $S = T \times \bar{e} \times R \times O_{\text{index}} \times O_{\text{RAID}}$
- **Coverage ratio of 95%** is the industry-accepted minimum threshold.
- **Indexing overhead** is typically **2.5×–3×**; **RAID-1 mirroring** adds another **2×**.
- Integration with **SIEM/SOAR** is essential for correlation and automated response.
- Compliance mandates: **PCI-DSS 11.5, HIPAA §164.308(a)(1), ISO 27001 A.12.4, NIST SP 800-94**.
- Modern HIDPS = foundation of **EDR/XDR** platforms; the same deployment principles apply.
- For **OT/ICS/legacy** environments, use **agentless** monitoring to avoid disrupting uptime-sensitive controllers.
- Always perform **kernel-level hook verification** post-deployment; an unhooked agent is invisible to itself.
- Documentation deliverables: **deployment topology diagram, policy matrix, coverage report, tuning log** — all expected in viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring the Effectiveness of IDPSs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measuring the Effectiveness of IDPSs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Measuring the Effectiveness of IDPSs** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative process of evaluating how accurately, reliably, and efficiently an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System identifies malicious activity, generates actionable alerts, and blocks attacks, while minimizing the cost of incorrect decisions (false alarms and missed attacks). It uses a defined set of **performance metrics** drawn from information theory, statistics, and signal detection theory to express the *goodness* of the detector.

The two principal viewpoints adopted in KTU/industry literature (Axelsson, McHugh, et al.) are:

1. **The Tester/Analyst's View (Intrinsic Quality):** How well does the IDPS distinguish "attack" from "normal"? Measured using a *confusion matrix* and derived ratios.
2. **The Operator/Enterprise View (Operational Utility):** How usable is the IDPS in a live environment? Measured by *alert volume, time-to-detect, analyst workload, and cost of response*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Effectiveness is *not* a single number. It is a *vector* of metrics — and the **Base Rate Fallacy** tells us that even a 99 % accurate detector can be practically useless if the underlying prevalence of attacks is extremely low (which is the case in real networks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **smoke alarm** installed in a large office complex.

- A **real fire** is an *attack*; *cooking smoke or steam* is *normal traffic*.
- A good alarm rings **only when there is fire** (True Positive) and stays **silent on toast** (True Negative).
- A bad alarm either **stays silent during a fire** (False Negative — *missed detection, possibly deadly*) or **screams every time someone boils water** (False Positive — *false alarm, causes chaos, people unplug it*).

**Measuring effectiveness** is the act of giving the alarm a "report card" that answers:
- *How often does it catch real fires?* → Detection Rate
- *How often does it cry wolf?* → False Positive Rate
- *When it rings, how likely is it to be a real fire?* → Precision
- *What is the *real-world* operational cost of running it?* → Operator Metrics

In a **10,000-room building where 1 room catches fire per year** (low base rate), an alarm that rings 100 times has only a 1 % chance of being a *real* fire per ring — even if it is 99 % accurate internally. This is the famous **Base Rate Fallacy**, the single most-examined pitfall in IDPS evaluation.

### 1.3 Key Performance Categories (Mind-Map Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **The four pillars of IDPS effectiveness measurement:**
> 1. **Detection Performance** — Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Accuracy, F-Score.
> 2. **Cost-Based Performance** — Expected cost of a misclassification (false positive vs. false negative).
> 3. **Operational Performance** — Throughput, latency, alert volume, MTTD (Mean Time To Detect).
> 4. **Usability / Operator Performance** — Analyst hours per alert, false-alarm fatigue, ROC curves.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold (Must Memorize for KTU)

- **True Positive (TP):** Attack correctly flagged as attack.
- **True Negative (TN):** Normal event correctly passed as normal.
- **False Positive (FP) / False Alarm:** Normal event wrongly flagged as attack (Type-I error).
- **False Negative (FN) / Miss:** Attack wrongly passed as normal (Type-II error).
- **Detection Rate (DR) / True Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall / Sensitivity.**
- **False Positive Rate (FPR) / False Alarm Rate.**
- **Specificity (SPC) / True Negative Rate (TNR).**
- **Precision / Positive Predictive Value (PPV).**
- **Negative Predictive Value (NPV).**
- **F-Measure (F1-Score).**
- **Accuracy (ACC).**
- **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC).**
- **Base Rate (ω) — Prevalence of attacks in the stream.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Confusion Matrix (4-Quadrant Truth Table)

Every IDPS evaluation reduces every observation to one of four cells:

| | **Predicted Attack** | **Predicted Normal** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP (Hit) | FN (Miss) |
| **Actual Normal** | FP (False Alarm) | TN (Correct Rejection) |

The cells satisfy:

$$TP + FN + FP + TN = N_{total}$$

where $N_{total}$ is the total number of events observed in the evaluation window.

### 2.2 Derived Ratios — The Core 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why Sensitivity?** An IDPS that *misses real attacks* is failing its primary job. Sensitivity measures the proportion of real attacks that *did* get caught.
- **Why Specificity?** In a high-traffic network, "not crying wolf" matters as much as "catching the wolf."
- **Why Precision?** A high-TPR detector can still flood the SOC with garbage. Precision answers: *"Of everything the IDPS flagged, what fraction was actually an attack?"*
- **Why F1?** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — penalizes the *worst* of the two.
- **Why ROC?** Because a single threshold setting produces a single (FPR, TPR) point; the **ROC curve** is the locus of all such points as the threshold varies.

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values inside markdown tables to avoid syntax breakage.**

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Plain-English Meaning** | **Range / Goal** |
|---|---|---|---|
| True Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall / Sensitivity | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Of all real attacks, how many were caught? | $0 \le TPR \le 1$; **higher is better** |
| False Negative Rate (FNR) / Miss Rate | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{TP+FN} = 1 - TPR$ | Of all real attacks, how many slipped through? | $0 \le FNR \le 1$; **lower is better** |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) / False Alarm Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Of all normal events, how many were falsely flagged? | $0 \le FPR \le 1$; **lower is better** |
| True Negative Rate (TNR) / Specificity | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{FP+TN} = 1 - FPR$ | Of all normal events, how many were correctly allowed? | $0 \le TNR \le 1$; **higher is better** |
| Precision / Positive Predictive Value (PPV) | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | When the IDPS alarms, how often is it a *real* attack? | $0 \le PPV \le 1$; **higher is better** |
| Negative Predictive Value (NPV) | $NPV = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FN}$ | When the IDPS is silent, how often is it *truly* clean? | $0 \le NPV \le 1$; **higher is better** |
| Accuracy (ACC) | $ACC = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall fraction of correct decisions | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| F1-Score (F-Measure) | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR}$ | Harmonic balance of Precision and Recall | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$; **higher is better** |
| Base Rate / Prevalence (ω) | $\omega = \dfrac{TP+FN}{N_{total}}$ | Fraction of events that are attacks | $0 < \omega \ll 1$ in real networks |
| Expected Cost (C) | $C = C_{FP} \cdot FPR \cdot (1-\omega) + C_{FN} \cdot FNR \cdot \omega$ | Weighted misclassification cost per event | **Minimize C** |
| Youden's J Statistic | $J = TPR + TNR - 1 = TPR - FPR$ | Optimal threshold discriminator | Max J → best threshold |
| Cohen's Kappa (κ) | $\kappa = \dfrac{ACC - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | Accuracy corrected for chance | $\kappa > 0.8$ is excellent |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Formulas

In **SOC Operations Centres** (e.g., managed security providers in Technopark, Trivandrum), the **PPV** is the metric analysts intuitively care about: *"If I get paged at 3 AM, is this real?"* The **F1** is the metric published in *academic* papers (e.g., on the NSL-KDD or CICIDS-2017 datasets). The **Expected Cost (C)** is the metric a *CISO* uses when negotiating the IDPS budget — they assign a dollar value to $C_{FN}$ (data breach cost) and $C_{FP}$ (analyst-hour cost) and choose the threshold that minimizes C.

### 2.5 The Base Rate Fallacy (Axelsson's Theorem)

Stephen Axelsson’s seminal 1999/2000 work showed that for an IDPS:

$$PPV \approx \dfrac{TPR \cdot \omega}{TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1 - \omega)}$$

When the **base rate $\omega$ is very small** (e.g., $10^{-5}$ in a busy enterprise), even a tiny $FPR$ **destroys** the $PPV$. This single equation is the most-frequently tested concept in KTU module-2 questions.

### 2.6 ROC and AUC — The Visual Yardstick

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots $TPR$ (y-axis) against $FPR$ (x-axis) as the detection threshold is swept from very strict to very lenient.

- The **diagonal line** is a *random* classifier.
- A curve **bending toward the top-left** is better.
- **AUC = 1.0** → perfect detector; **AUC = 0.5** → useless.
- AUC is **insensitive to base rate**, which is its chief advantage over raw accuracy.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Precision (PPV) from Bayes' Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show mathematically *why* a low base rate breaks a "99 % accurate" detector.

**Step 1 — State the prior probabilities.**
Let $\omega = P(A)$ be the probability that a randomly chosen event is an *attack*, and $1-\omega = P(N)$ be the probability it is *normal*. (Here $A$ = Attack, $N$ = Normal.)

$$P(A) = \omega, \quad P(N) = 1 - \omega$$

**Step 2 — Define the detector's conditional probabilities (likelihoods).**

$$P(+\vert A) = TPR \quad \text{(sensitivity)}$$

$$P(+\vert N) = FPR \quad \text{(false alarm rate)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem** to find the *posterior* probability that an alarm ("+") is a real attack.

$$P(A \vert +) = \dfrac{P(+\vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(+)}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the denominator using the Law of Total Probability.**

$$P(+) = P(+\vert A)\,P(A) + P(+\vert N)\,P(N) = TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1-\omega)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the Posterior / Precision.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPV \;=\; P(A \vert +) \;=\; \dfrac{TPR \cdot \omega}{TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1-\omega)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **Axelsson / Bayesian-PPV formula**, derivable directly from Bayes' rule and the two intrinsic detector properties TPR and FPR.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — The "99 % Myth"

A vendor claims: *"Our IDPS has 99 % accuracy and 99 % detection rate (TPR), with only 1 % false positive rate (FPR)."* In a network with $\omega = 0.0001$ (1 attack per 10,000 events), find the *actual* PPV.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$TPR = 0.99, \quad FPR = 0.01, \quad \omega = 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator (true attacks caught per 10,000 events).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= TPR \cdot \omega = 0.99 \times 10^{-4} = 9.9 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (total alarms per 10,000 events).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= TPR\cdot\omega + FPR\cdot(1-\omega) \\
&= 9.9 \times 10^{-5} + 0.01 \times (1 - 10^{-4}) \\
&= 9.9 \times 10^{-5} + 0.009999 \approx 0.010098
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute PPV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPV = \dfrac{9.9 \times 10^{-5}}{0.010098} \approx 0.0098 = 0.98\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Interpretation:** Of **every 10,000 alarms**, only about **98 are real attacks** — the rest are false alarms. A 99 % accurate, 99 % TPR detector is *operationally useless* on this network. **This is the Base Rate Fallacy.**

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Confusion-Matrix to Metrics

Suppose an IDPS is tested on a labelled dataset of $N = 100{,}000$ events containing $200$ attacks. The reported confusion matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= 180, \quad FN = 20, \quad FP = 1{,}500, \quad TN = 98{,}320
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute TPR, FPR, Specificity, Precision, Accuracy, and F1.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity (TPR / Recall).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} = \dfrac{180}{200} = 0.900 \;(90\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate (FPR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{99{,}820} \approx 0.0150 \;(1.50\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Specificity (TNR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TNR = \dfrac{TN}{FP+TN} = 1 - FPR \approx 0.9850 \;(98.50\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Precision (PPV).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} = \dfrac{180}{1{,}680} \approx 0.1071 \;(10.71\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Accuracy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACC = \dfrac{TP+TN}{N} = \dfrac{180 + 98{,}320}{100{,}000} = 0.9850 \;(98.50\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — F1-Score.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \dfrac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \times 0.1071 \times 0.900}{0.1071 + 0.900} \\
&= \dfrac{0.1929}{1.0071} \approx 0.1915
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away:** The Accuracy is 98.5 % — *deceptively* high — because the dataset is **99.8 % normal**. The **F1 of 0.19** honestly reflects that the detector is not useful in the real world.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Expected Cost Optimization

A CISO sets the cost of a missed attack at $C_{FN} = \$50{,}000$ and the analyst cost of investigating a false alarm at $C_{FP} = \$100$. With $\omega = 0.001$, compare two candidate thresholds:

- **Threshold X:** $TPR = 0.95$, $FPR = 0.05$.
- **Threshold Y:** $TPR = 0.80$, $FPR = 0.01$.

**Step 1 — Expected cost for Threshold X (per 1,000 events).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_X &= C_{FP}\cdot FPR\cdot(1-\omega) + C_{FN}\cdot FNR\cdot\omega \\
&= 100 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.999 + 50{,}000 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.001 \\
&= 4.995 + 2.500 = \$7.495
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected cost for Threshold Y (per 1,000 events).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_Y &= 100 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 0.999 + 50{,}000 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 0.001 \\
&= 0.999 + 10.000 = \$10.999
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the minimum-cost threshold.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_X = \$7.495 < C_Y = \$10.999 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Threshold X is preferred.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even though Y has a much lower false-alarm rate, the *dollar cost* of the missed attacks dominates because $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$. Cost-sensitive analysis — not raw FPR — drives real-world threshold selection.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Question** | **Best Metric** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| "Of all attacks, how many did we catch?" | TPR / Recall | Coverage of true positives |
| "Of all alerts, how many were real?" | PPV / Precision | Operator's trust |
| "How often does it cry wolf?" | FPR | Operator fatigue |
| "Is the dataset balanced — can I trust accuracy?" | Check $\omega$ first | Base-rate check |
| "Which threshold minimizes business loss?" | Expected Cost $C$ | Dollar-weighted |
| "What single number summarizes the detector?" | AUC or F1 | Threshold-independent |

