## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with reference to IDPS effectiveness:**
**(a) False Positive Rate (FPR)**    **(b) Base Rate (ω)**    **(c) F1-Score**

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) False Positive Rate (FPR):** The fraction of *normal* events that the IDPS incorrectly classifies as attacks. Mathematically, $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$. It quantifies the detector's "cry-wolf" tendency.
- **(b) Base Rate (ω):** The proportion of attacks in the total event stream, $\omega = \dfrac{TP+FN}{N_{total}}$. In real enterprise networks $\omega$ is typically $\le 10^{-3}$, leading to the **Base Rate Fallacy**.
- **(c) F1-Score:** The harmonic mean of Precision ($PPV$) and Recall ($TPR$), $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR}$. It balances false positives and false negatives into a single number.

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three operational (operator-view) metrics used to evaluate an IDPS, distinct from confusion-matrix metrics.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time between the launch of an attack and the moment the IDPS raises the alert. Lower is better.
2. **Alert Volume (Alerts/day):** Total number of alerts generated per day; a high value causes *analyst fatigue* and is correlated with missed real attacks.
3. **Throughput / Latency:** Number of packets inspected per second and the added network delay. A prevention-mode IDPS that drops traffic at gigabit speeds must still respect latency SLAs.
4. *(Optional 4th)* **Analyst Hours per Verified Incident:** Total SOC time spent verifying a true positive — a cost metric.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** *Define the Confusion Matrix. With a suitable example, derive the formulas for **Sensitivity (TPR)**, **Specificity (TNR)**, and **Precision (PPV)** in terms of the four cells TP, FP, TN, FN.*   **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *An enterprise IDPS is benchmarked on a test corpus of **$N = 50{,}000$** events, of which **120 are attacks**. The detector reports **TP = 108, FN = 12, FP = 1,800, TN = 48,080**. Compute (i) the **Base Rate (ω)**, (ii) the **TPR, FPR, TNR**, (iii) the **Precision (PPV)**, (iv) the **Accuracy (ACC)**, and (v) the **F1-Score**. Comment on whether the accuracy figure is a fair reflection of detector quality.*   **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Confusion Matrix & Derived Metrics (7 marks)**

A *Confusion Matrix* is a 2×2 cross-tabulation of **Predicted Class** (rows) versus **Actual Class** (columns) for a binary classifier like an IDPS:

| | **Actual Attack** | **Actual Normal** |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Attack** | TP | FP |
| **Predicted Normal** | FN | TN |

**Derivation of Sensitivity (TPR):**
Sensitivity is the fraction of **actual attacks** correctly identified:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Correctly Flagged Attacks}}{\text{Total Actual Attacks}} \;=\; \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of Specificity (TNR):**
Specificity is the fraction of **actual normal** events correctly allowed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TNR \;=\; \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP} \;=\; 1 - FPR
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of Precision (PPV):**
Precision is the fraction of **alarms** that are real attacks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPV \;=\; \dfrac{\text{True Alarms}}{\text{All Alarms}} \;=\; \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A web-server IDS sees 1,000 requests, of which 50 are SQLi. It correctly flags 45 of them and falsely flags 30 benign ones.
- $TP=45$, $FN=5$, $FP=30$, $TN=920$.
- $TPR = 45/50 = 0.90$,   $TNR = 920/950 = 0.968$,   $PPV = 45/75 = 0.60$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given: $N = 50{,}000$, Attacks $= 120$, $TP = 108$, $FN = 12$, $FP = 1{,}800$, $TN = 48{,}080$.

**(i) Base Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega = \dfrac{TP+FN}{N} = \dfrac{120}{50{,}000} = 0.0024
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) TPR, FPR, TNR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR &= \dfrac{108}{120} = 0.900 \\[4pt]
FPR &= \dfrac{1{,}800}{1{,}800+48{,}080} = \dfrac{1{,}800}{49{,}880} \approx 0.0361 \\[4pt]
TNR &= 1 - FPR \approx 0.9639
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Precision (PPV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPV = \dfrac{108}{108+1{,}800} = \dfrac{108}{1{,}908} \approx 0.0566
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Accuracy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACC = \dfrac{108+48{,}080}{50{,}000} = \dfrac{48{,}188}{50{,}000} \approx 0.9638
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) F1-Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.0566 \cdot 0.900}{0.0566 + 0.900} = \dfrac{0.1019}{0.9566} \approx 0.1065
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Accuracy (mandatory for full marks):**
Although $ACC = 96.38\,\%$ looks impressive, the dataset is **99.76 % normal** ($\omega = 0.0024$). A trivial classifier that flags *nothing* as an attack would already achieve $ACC = 1 - \omega = 99.76\,\%$. The honest, base-rate-independent indicators are **F1 = 0.1065** and **PPV = 5.66 %**, both of which reveal that this IDPS, while catching 90 % of attacks, drowns the SOC in false alarms. **Accuracy is therefore misleading on imbalanced data — the Base Rate Fallacy in action.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** *State and prove the **Axelsson Base-Rate relationship** for IDPS effectiveness. Starting from Bayes' theorem, show that:*
$$PPV = \dfrac{TPR \cdot \omega}{TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1-\omega)}$$
*Explain, with a numerical example, why a high TPR detector can still produce a very low PPV in a real enterprise network.*   **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *Construct a **cost-based evaluation** for a SOC. The cost of a missed attack is $C_{FN} = \$30{,}000$ and the cost of investigating a false alarm is $C_{FP} = \$80$. The base rate of attacks on a monitored segment is $\omega = 0.005$. Compare the *expected per-event cost* for two thresholds:*
- *Threshold P: $TPR = 0.90, \; FPR = 0.04$.*
- *Threshold Q: $TPR = 0.75, \; FPR = 0.005$.*
*Which threshold should the CISO adopt and why? What additional non-numerical factors should influence the decision?*   **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Axelsson Base-Rate Relationship (7 marks)**

**Statement:** The Posterior Probability that an IDPS alert corresponds to a *real attack* (i.e., Precision / PPV) is given by:

$$PPV = \dfrac{TPR \cdot \omega}{TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1-\omega)}$$

where $TPR$ = True Positive Rate, $FPR$ = False Positive Rate, $\omega$ = base rate of attacks in the observed event stream.

**Proof from Bayes' Theorem:**

Let $A$ = "event is an attack" and $+$ = "IDPS raises an alarm." From Bayes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid +) &= \dfrac{P(+\mid A)\,P(A)}{P(+)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Identify the priors and likelihoods:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A) &= \omega, \quad P(N) = 1-\omega \\
P(+\mid A) &= TPR, \quad P(+\mid N) = FPR
\end{aligned}
$$

Total probability of an alarm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(+) &= P(+\mid A)P(A) + P(+\mid N)P(N) \\
&= TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1-\omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPV = P(A\mid +) = \dfrac{TPR \cdot \omega}{TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1-\omega)} \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Illustration of the Base-Rate Fallacy:**

Let $TPR = 0.95$, $FPR = 0.01$, and $\omega = 0.0001$ (1 attack per 10,000 packets, typical of a busy enterprise).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPV &= \dfrac{0.95 \times 10^{-4}}{0.95 \times 10^{-4} + 0.01 \times 0.9999} \\
&= \dfrac{9.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01 + 9.5 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&\approx \dfrac{9.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.010095} \approx 0.0094 = 0.94\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> Even a 95 %-sensitive, 99 %-specific detector generates a PPV of **less than 1 %** — for every real attack caught, the SOC chases ~106 false alarms. **This is the Base Rate Fallacy** and the chief reason real-world IDPS deployments demand a second-tier correlation engine (e.g., SIEM) to suppress noise.

---

**Part (b) — Cost-Based Threshold Selection (7 marks)**

Expected per-event cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C = C_{FP}\cdot FPR\cdot(1-\omega) + C_{FN}\cdot FNR\cdot\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Threshold P** ($TPR=0.90, FPR=0.04, FNR=0.10$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 80 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 0.995 + 30{,}000 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 0.005 \\
&= 3.184 + 150.000 = \$153.184
\end{aligned}
$$

**Threshold Q** ($TPR=0.75, FPR=0.005, FNR=0.25$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_Q &= 80 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 0.995 + 30{,}000 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.005 \\
&= 0.398 + 375.000 = \$375.398
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $C_P < C_Q$; therefore the CISO should adopt **Threshold P**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why students lose marks here:** Many students compute only the *false-alarm cost* and ignore $C_{FN}$. Since $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$, the *missed-attack* term **dominates** the total cost, and the more sensitive (higher-TPR) threshold wins despite producing more false positives.

**Non-numerical factors the CISO should also consider (for full 7 marks):**

1. **Regulatory / Compliance** — some industries (PCI-DSS, HIPAA) mandate a *minimum acceptable TPR* irrespective of cost.
2. **Reputational damage** — a single publicized breach may dwarf the linear expected-cost model.
3. **Analyst fatigue / burnout** — a chronically noisy IDS leads to *real attacks being ignored* (alert desensitization), which the model cannot capture linearly.
4. **Latency of the prevention response** — Threshold P may block more legitimate traffic (business disruption).
5. **Adversary adaptation** — sustained high-TPR thresholds may push attackers toward slow, low-and-slow techniques that slip through.
6. **Insurance and contractual penalties** — quantitative but not captured in $C_{FN}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing TPR with PPV.** TPR is *attacker-centric* (out of all attacks, how many caught?). PPV is *operator-centric* (out of all alarms, how many real?). Examiners **deduct 2 marks** for mixing them up.
> 2. **Reporting Accuracy on imbalanced data without comment.** Always pair a high ACC with a comment on $\omega$ and F1; otherwise, you forfeit the "Comment" sub-part.
> 3. **Omitting the Base Rate Fallacy in any effectiveness question.** If the question mentions a *real network* or *low attack rate*, expect the examiner to look for the Axelsson equation. Missing it costs **3 marks minimum**.
> 4. **In cost-based questions, forgetting the $(1-\omega)$ factor** on the $C_{FP}$ term and the $\omega$ factor on the $C_{FN}$ term. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 5. **Failing to state units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing 153.184 instead of **\$153.18 per event**). The valuation key penalises unit omission.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Measuring the Effectiveness of IDPSs"**
> - **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation: $TP, FP, TN, FN$.
> - **TPR (Recall, Sensitivity)** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$; **FNR** $= 1 - TPR$.
> - **FPR** $= \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$; **TNR (Specificity)** $= 1 - FPR$.
> - **Precision (PPV)** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$; **NPV** $= \dfrac{TN}{TN+FN}$.
> - **Accuracy** $= \dfrac{TP+TN}{N}$ — **misleading** on imbalanced data.
> - **F1** $= \dfrac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR}$ — the honest single score.
> - **Base Rate (ω)** $= \dfrac{TP+FN}{N}$ — typically $\le 10^{-3}$ in production networks.
> - **Axelsson / Bayes-PPV**: $PPV = \dfrac{TPR \cdot \omega}{TPR \cdot \omega + FPR \cdot (1-\omega)}$ — the **Base Rate Fallacy** in one equation.
> - **ROC curve**: plots TPR vs FPR as threshold varies; **AUC** summarises detector quality (1.0 = perfect, 0.5 = random).
> - **Youden's J** $= TPR - FPR$ picks the **optimal threshold**.
> - **Expected Cost** $C = C_{FP}\cdot FPR\cdot(1-\omega) + C_{FN}\cdot FNR\cdot\omega$ is the **CISO's** yardstick.
> - **Operator metrics** (orthogonal to confusion matrix): MTTD, alert volume, throughput, latency, analyst hours/incident.
> - **Always** state units; **always** comment on base rate; **never** quote accuracy alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrusion Detection Systems - How the IDS Watches Your Network - Packet Sniffing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Packet Sniffing** is the process of using specialized software or hardware to intercept, capture, decode, and log network packets traversing a digital communication channel. In the context of an Intrusion Detection System (IDS), packet sniffing constitutes the **first stage of the detection pipeline**—the *sensory layer*—wherein raw bits flowing across a network medium are converted into structured, analyzable Protocol Data Units (PDUs) for subsequent rule-based, signature-based, or anomaly-based inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):** An IDS that performs packet sniffing operates in **Promiscuous Mode**, where the Network Interface Card (NIC) is instructed by the kernel/driver to forward every frame it sees on the wire to the CPU, **regardless of the destination MAC address** of the frame.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Inspector

Imagine a **central sorting hub of a national postal service**:

1. **Regular Letter Routing** = Normal NIC operation. Each postman (NIC) only opens letters addressed to his own house (destination MAC). Letters for other houses are ignored.
2. **The Postal Inspector** = The Packet Sniffer / IDS Sensor. He stands at the sorting hub and **opens every single envelope**, reads the contents (header + payload), and checks them against a list of forbidden items (signatures) or unusual patterns (anomalies).
3. The Inspector does **not stop the mail** (passive sniffing) or he may **seize suspicious packages** (active IDS / IPS response).

This analogy captures the essence of packet sniffing: **non-disruptive observation of data-in-transit**.

### 1.3 Three Foundational Modes of Operation

| Mode | Behavior | IDS Relevance |
|------|----------|---------------|
| **Non-Promiscuous Mode** | NIC only accepts frames with its own MAC | Useless for IDS — only sees broadcast/local traffic |
| **Promiscuous Mode** | NIC accepts **all** frames on the segment | **Mandatory** for NIDS sensors |
| **Monitor (RF) Mode** | NIC accepts 802.11 wireless frames outside its BSSID | Required for Wireless IDS (WIDS) |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Values the Examiner Expects:**
> - **Ethernet MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit):** **1500 bytes**
> - **Ethernet Frame Header:** **14 bytes** (Src MAC 6 + Dst MAC 6 + EtherType 2)
> - **Ethernet Frame Trailer (FCS):** **4 bytes**
> - **Max Ethernet Frame:** **1518 bytes** (untagged) / **1522 bytes** (802.1Q tagged)
> - **libpcap Snapshot Length (default):** **65535 bytes**
> - **Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF) tap:** attached before the protocol stack, eliminating kernel copy overhead.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Packet Sniffing Pipeline — Six Sequential Stages

The KTU 2024 module breaks packet sniffing into a **deterministic, sequential pipeline**. Each stage is mandatory; skipping any stage causes detection blind spots.

> **Stage 1 — Physical / Data-Link Access (Layer 1–2)**
> The NIC must be coaxed into a state where it presents every electrical/optical pulse on the medium to the host CPU. This is achieved by:
> - **Hardware TAP (Test Access Point):** A 3-port galvanic device inserted *inline* between two switches. Fail-open relay ensures zero downtime on link failure. Provides a **full-duplex copy** at line rate.
> - **SPAN / Mirror Port:** A managed switch replicates traffic from one or more source ports/VLANs to a designated destination port. Limitation: oversubscription at high loads.
> - **Network Tap on Hubs:** Legacy coax/10Base-T hubs broadcast to all ports, allowing any connected host to sniff naturally.

> **Stage 2 — Promiscuous Mode Enablement**
> Executed via a kernel ioctl:
> - Linux: `socket.ioctl(SIOCSIFFLAGS, IFF_PROMISC)`
> - Windows NDIS: `OID_GEN_CURRENT_PACKET_FILTER |= NDIS_PACKET_TYPE_PROMISCUOUS`
> Modern OS kernels also require `CAP_NET_RAW` capability (Linux) to avoid the *wireshark-no-promisc* problem.

> **Stage 3 — Frame Capture via Raw Socket / libpcap**
> - **libpcap (Linux/Unix) / WinPcap (Windows) / Npcap (modern)** is the de-facto userspace library. It uses a kernel pseudo-device (`/dev/bpf`, `af_packet`, or `NPF`) to receive raw frames.
> - **Raw Sockets (`SOCK_RAW`)** in Linux bypass TCP/IP stack processing for outgoing packets and receive raw incoming IP datagrams.

> **Stage 4 — BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) Pre-filtering**
> Compiled from human-readable filter expressions (e.g., `tcp port 80 and host 192.168.1.10`) into a **kernel-level VM bytecode** that drops unwanted packets *before* they cross the kernel/userspace boundary — critical for performance at 10 Gbps+.

> **Stage 5 — Protocol Decode (DPI – Deep Packet Inspection)**
> The libpcap capture loop hands bytes to a **decoder tree** (Link Layer → Network Layer → Transport → Application). Each layer's state machine is updated; reassembly buffers (TCP stream, IP fragments) are maintained.

> **Stage 6 — Detection Engine Hand-off**
> The decoded PDU is dispatched to:
> - **Signature engine** (e.g., Snort rules)
> - **Anomaly model** (statistical baseline / ML inference)
> - **Stateful tracker** (session tables for protocol-aware detection)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Packet Sniffing & Capture Metrics

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|-------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| Capture Rate (Line-Rate) | $R_c = \dfrac{N_f \times L_f \times 8}{T_{obs}}$ | **Mbps / Gbps** | Verifies sensor is not dropping packets |
| Frame Count over interval | $F_{\Delta t} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 1_{\{\text{frame } i \in \Delta t\}}$ | frames/sec | Drives IDS pre-processor load |
| Capture Drop Ratio (CDR) | $CDR = \dfrac{F_{dropped}}{F_{received}} = 1 - \dfrac{F_{captured}}{F_{received}}$ | dimensionless | Must be **0** for forensic-grade IDS |
| Average Packet Size | $\bar{L} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$ | bytes | Sized against MTU (1500 B) |
| BPF Filter Throughput | $T_{bpf} \approx \dfrac{P_{cpu}}{k \cdot C_{instr}}$ | Mpps | Justifies hardware offload |
| Pcap File Size Estimate | $S_{pcap} = N \times (\bar{L} + 16)$ | bytes | Disk provisioning for PCAP retention |
| Mirror Port Oversubscription | $O = \max\left(0, 1 - \dfrac{B_{mirror}}{B_{src}}\right)$ | dimensionless | SPAN design warning |
| Tap Insertion Loss (Optical) | $IL = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | **dB** | Optical budget calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic the Examiner Loves:** "**P**romiscuous, **A**ccess, **D**ecode, **M**atch, **A**lert" — PADMA — the five functions of any sniffing-based IDS.

## 2.3 Why Sniffing is the *Nervous System* of an IDS

- **Visibility:** Without sniffing, an IDS is functionally blind to threats crossing the wire.
- **Latency Budget:** A 1 Gbps link at 64-byte frames = **1.488 Mpps**. The entire sniffing stack (NIC → driver → BPF → decoder) must sustain this rate, else drops occur and stealthy attacks (slowloris, low-volume exfil) evade detection.
- **Forensic Immutability:** A passive sniffer preserves the *original bits* — making it legally admissible as evidence (chain-of-custody). An inline IPS, by contrast, mutates the traffic stream.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretically Captured Packets per Second

A KTU favorite — derive the wire-speed packet rate of an Ethernet link.

> **Given:**
> - Link bandwidth $B$ in bits per second.
> - Minimum Ethernet frame size $L_{min} = 64$ bytes $= 512$ bits.
> - Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) $G = 96$ bits (12 bytes).
> - Preamble + SFD $= 64$ bits (8 bytes).

> **Step 1 — Total bits occupied by one minimum-sized frame on the wire:**
> Each frame consumes header + payload + trailer + inter-frame silence. For minimum-frame throughput, we count the *full on-wire envelope*:
>
> $$L_{onwire} = L_{preamble} + L_{frame} + L_{IFG}$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute the standard IEEE 802.3 values:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> L_{onwire} &= 64 \text{ bits} + 512 \text{ bits} + 96 \text{ bits} \\
>           &= 672 \text{ bits per minimum frame}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 3 — Compute the maximum frames per second:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> F_{max} &= \frac{B}{L_{onwire}} \\
>         &= \frac{B}{672}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 4 — Evaluate for a 1 Gbps link ($B = 10^9$ bps):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> F_{max} &= \frac{1\,000\,000\,000}{672} \\
>         &\approx 1\,488\,095 \text{ frames/sec} \\
>         &\approx 1.488 \text{ Mpps}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 5 — Generalize for 10 Gbps (commonly used in IDS deployments):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> F_{max}^{10G} &= 10 \times 1.488 \text{ Mpps} \\
>               &= 14.88 \text{ Mpps}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 6 — Real-world caution — wire speed ≠ host speed:**
> A CPU-bound packet sniffer (Wireshark on a laptop) typically drops after ~500 Kpps due to memory copies and decoder CPU cost. Hence the need for **hardware-accelerated capture cards** (e.g., Intel X520, Napatech, Silicom) for line-rate IDS sensors.

> **Final Result (formula form for the KTU answer sheet):**
>
> $$\boxed{\,F_{max} = \frac{B}{L_{frame} + L_{IFG} + L_{preamble}} = \frac{B}{672 \text{ bits}}\,}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sniffing Methods Compared

| Attribute | Network TAP | SPAN Port | Hub (Legacy) | ARP Spoof (MITM) | Inline IPS |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|--------------|------------------|------------|
| **Topology** | Inline, galvanic isolation | Out-of-band mirror | Shared media | Logical MITM | Inline bridge |
| **Failure Mode** | Fail-open (link survives) | Switch survives | Single collision domain | None (software-based) | Bypass relay |
| **Full-Duplex Capture** | Yes (split TX/RX) | Often lossy | No (CSMA/CD) | Only victims' traffic | Yes |
| **Stealth** | High (passive, invisible) | High | Medium | Low (detectable) | Visible (has IP) |
| **Performance** | Line-rate (HW) | Best-effort | Line-rate of hub | Host-CPU bound | Line-rate (HW) |
| **KTU Use Case** | Carrier-grade NIDS | Enterprise NIDS sensor | Lab/Classroom demo | Adversary-side attack | Active prevention |
| **Drawback** | Cost, port density | Oversubscription, drops | Obsolete, 100 Mbps max | **Active attack** | Adds latency/jitter |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Estimating PCAP Storage for a 24-Hour Retention Window

> **Problem:** A KTU model question variant: A SOC captures traffic from a 1 Gbps link. Average link utilization is 30%. Each frame is 800 bytes on average. Compute the PCAP disk space required for 24 hours.

> **Step 1 — Effective bandwidth consumed:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> B_{eff} &= 1 \text{ Gbps} \times 0.30 = 0.30 \text{ Gbps} \\
>         &= 3 \times 10^8 \text{ bits/sec}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2 — Bytes per second on the wire:**
>
> $$B_{bytes} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{8} = 3.75 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes/sec}$$

> **Step 3 — PCAP overhead per record (16-byte libpcap header):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> L_{pcap} &= L_{frame} + L_{pcap\_hdr} \\
>         &= 800 + 16 = 816 \text{ bytes}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 4 — Frames per second (using average, not minimum):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> F_{avg} &= \frac{B_{bytes}}{L_{frame}} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^7}{800} \\
>         &= 46\,875 \text{ frames/sec}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 5 — PCAP bytes per second:**
>
> $$S_{sec} = 46\,875 \times 816 = 3.825 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes/sec}$$

> **Step 6 — Total storage for 86 400 seconds:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> S_{24h} &= 3.825 \times 10^7 \times 86\,400 \\
>         &= 3.305 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes} \\
>         &\approx 3.3 \text{ TB}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 7 — Convert to TiB (1 TiB = $2^{40}$ bytes):**
>
> $$S_{24h} \approx 3.01 \text{ TiB}$$

> **Examiner's Note:** Include **filesystem metadata (~5–10%)**, **compression ratio (LZ4 ~50%)**, and **WORM retention tiering** for an accurate SOC design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Wire to Detection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Wire / Fiber Medium] --> B1[Network TAP or SPAN]
    B1 --> C1[NIC in Promiscuous Mode]
    C1 --> D1[Kernel Capture Module af_packet or NPF]
    D1 --> E1[BPF Filter Virtual Machine]
    E1 --> F1[libpcap Userspace Ring Buffer]
    F1 --> G1[Protocol Decoder Link to IP to TCP to App]
    G1 --> H1[Stream Reassembly and Defragmentation]
    H1 --> I1[Signature Engine Snort Rules]
    H1 --> J1[Anomaly Engine Statistical or ML]
    I1 --> K1[Alert and Logging Pipeline]
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[SIEM and SOC Dashboard]
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Lifecycle of a Captured Frame

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant NIC as Promiscuous NIC
    participant KRN as Kernel af_packet
    participant BPF as BPF Filter VM
    participant LIB as libpcap Loop
    participant DEC as Protocol Decoder
    participant ENG as Detection Engine

    NIC->>KRN: Raw frame bytes arrive
    KRN->>BPF: Submit frame for filter check
    alt Frame matches BPF filter
        BPF-->>KRN: ACCEPT
        KRN->>LIB: enqueue frame to userspace ring
        LIB->>DEC: pcap_next_ex returns
        DEC->>DEC: parse Ethernet, IP, TCP
        DEC->>ENG: emit normalized event
        ENG-->>LIB: alert or no-alert
    else Frame rejected by BPF
        BPF-->>KRN: DROP
        KRN->>KRN: increment drop counter
    end
```

## 4.3 Topology Diagram — Where the Sniffer Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INET["External Internet"]
        FW[Perimeter Firewall]
    end
    subgraph CORE["Internal Core Network"]
        CR1[Core Router 1]
        CR2[Core Router 2]
    end
    subgraph DMZ["DMZ Segment"]
        SVR1[Web Server]
        SVR2[Mail Server]
    end
    subgraph SOC["SOC Monitoring Segment"]
        TAP[Aggregation TAP]
        NIDS1[IDS Sensor 1 Promiscuous]
        NIDS2[IDS Sensor 2 Promiscuous]
        SIEM[SIEM Server]
    end
    FW --> CR1
    CR1 --> CR2
    CR2 --> DMZ
    CR2 --> TAP
    TAP --> NIDS1
    TAP --> NIDS2
    NIDS1 --> SIEM
    NIDS2 --> SIEM
    TAP -.Passive Copy.-> NIDS1
    TAP -.Passive Copy.-> NIDS2
```

## 4.4 State Machine — NIC Mode Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalMode
    NormalMode --> PromiscMode: ioctl SIOCSIFFLAGS IFF_PROMISC
    PromiscMode --> MonitorMode: 80211 only driver specific
    MonitorMode --> PromiscMode: revert
    PromiscMode --> NormalMode: ioctl clear IFF_PROMISC
    PromiscMode --> [*]: interface down
    MonitorMode --> [*]: interface down
    note right of PromiscMode: Required for IDS packet sniffing
```

## 4.5 Layered View of a Sniffed Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Sniffed Frame on Wire] --> L1[Layer 1 Preamble and SFD 8 bytes]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Ethernet II 14 bytes Dst MAC Src MAC EtherType]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 IPv4 or IPv6 Header 20 to 60 bytes]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 TCP or UDP Header 20 to 60 bytes]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 to 7 Application Payload HTTP DNS TLS SIP etc]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 2 FCS Trailer 4 bytes CRC32]
    L6 --> END[Frame delivered to sniffer]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
> **"With a neat block diagram, explain the role of packet sniffing in an Intrusion Detection System. Why is promiscuous mode mandatory for a network IDS sensor?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Packet sniffing is the passive capture and decoding of network frames by an IDS sensor operating at the data-link layer. It serves as the *sensory input* to the detection engine.
2. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Wire → NIC (Promiscuous) → Kernel Capture → BPF Filter → libpcap → Decoder → Signature/Anomaly Engine → Alert.
3. **Promiscuous Mode Justification (1 mark):** A normal NIC discards frames whose destination MAC does not match its own. An IDS sensor must inspect **all** traffic on the segment to detect lateral movement, scans, and C2 beacons. Promiscuous mode overrides this MAC filter at the NIC firmware level, ensuring no frame is hidden from the sniffer.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **"Compare Network TAP and SPAN Port as packet-capture mechanisms for an IDS. Which is preferred in a 10 Gbps carrier-grade deployment and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **TAP (1 mark):** Hardware device inserted *inline*; galvanic isolation, **fail-open** on power loss, captures full-duplex traffic at line rate without oversubscription.
2. **SPAN (1 mark):** Software feature on a managed switch that mirrors selected ports/VLANs to a destination port; susceptible to **oversubscription** when aggregated mirror traffic exceeds destination port capacity.
3. **Carrier-grade preference (1 mark):** **TAP** — required at 10 Gbps because SPAN ports are typically 1 Gbps and lose packets under burst load, violating the IDS zero-drop requirement. TAPs also provide regulatory-grade evidence isolation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Module 2 — Common Question Paper Pattern:** *One full question of 14 marks with internal choice between Question A and Question B.*

---

### ❖ Question A — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`
**(a)** With a layered block diagram, describe the **end-to-end packet-sniffing pipeline** of a Network Intrusion Detection System. Explain the function of each stage in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SOC monitors a **1 Gbps** Ethernet link. The average frame size on the link is **512 bytes**, and the sustained utilization is **40%**. The libpcap retention format adds a **16-byte** per-record header. Calculate the **PCAP storage required per day**. State **two assumptions** you made. **(7 marks)**

#### ✔ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Stage | Component | Function | Marks |
|-------|-----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | **Wire / Physical Medium** | Optical/electrical signal transport; TAP or SPAN provides a copy | 1 |
| 2 | **Promiscuous NIC** | Firmware accepts all frames regardless of destination MAC | 1 |
| 3 | **Kernel Capture** | `af_packet`, `PF_RING`, or `NPF` driver copies raw bytes to a ring buffer | 1 |
| 4 | **BPF Filter VM** | Kernel-level bytecode filter discards unwanted traffic before copy | 1 |
| 5 | **libpcap Userspace Loop** | `pcap_next_ex` dequeues frames into application memory | 1 |
| 6 | **Protocol Decoder** | Parses Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application, builds reassembly buffers | 1 |
| 7 | **Detection Engine + Alert** | Signature matching / anomaly scoring; emits alerts to SIEM | 1 |

> **Key sentence for the answer:** *"Each stage is mandatory; a failure at any stage creates a detection blind spot. BPF filtering is the single most important performance optimization in high-throughput IDS."*

#### ✔ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

> **Step 1 — Effective bandwidth (1 mark):**
> $$B_{eff} = 0.40 \times 10^9 = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ bps}$$

> **Step 2 — Convert to bytes per second (1 mark):**
> $$\frac{4 \times 10^8}{8} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes/sec}$$

> **Step 3 — Frame rate (1 mark):**
> $$F = \frac{5 \times 10^7}{512} \approx 97\,656 \text{ frames/sec}$$

> **Step 4 — PCAP record size (1 mark):**
> $$L_{rec} = 512 + 16 = 528 \text{ bytes}$$

> **Step 5 — PCAP bytes per second (1 mark):**
> $$S_{sec} = 97\,656 \times 528 \approx 5.156 \times 10^7 \text{ B/s}$$

> **Step 6 — Daily storage (1 mark):**
> $$S_{day} = 5.156 \times 10^7 \times 86\,400 \approx 4.456 \times 10^{12} \text{ B} \approx 4.46 \text{ TB}$$

> **Step 7 — Two assumptions (1 mark):**
> 1. Uniform average frame size (no bimodal mini/giant distribution).
> 2. libpcap file has no internal indexing; only record headers are counted.
> *(Bonus: link is up 100% of the day — no maintenance windows.)*

---

### ❖ Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the **Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF)** architecture. How does it improve the performance of a packet sniffer in high-throughput networks? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A network administrator suspects an internal host is exfiltrating data via **DNS tunneling**. Describe how a packet sniffer with **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** capability would detect this. List the **four specific packet-level indicators** it should flag. **(7 marks)**

#### ✔ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **BPF Definition (2 marks):** BPF is a kernel-resident register-based virtual machine originally introduced in BSD Unix. It executes a small instruction set over raw frames at the device-driver level, *before* the frame is copied into userspace.
2. **Filter Compilation (2 marks):** The user supplies an expression (e.g., `tcp and port 80 and host 10.0.0.5`) via `tcpdump -d`. The libpcap compiler converts it into BPF bytecode, which the kernel loads into a `struct sock_fprog` and attaches to the socket.
3. **Performance Benefit (2 marks):** It eliminates useless memory copies and context switches. A 10 Gbps link carrying 80% unwanted traffic can be reduced to a 2 Gbps userspace load, dropping CPU usage from 100% to ~25%.
4. **Just-in-time Compilation (1 mark):** Modern kernels JIT-compile BPF (eBPF) to native x86 instructions, achieving near-hardware speeds.

#### ✔ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

A DPI-enabled sniffer reassembles DNS query/response streams and applies heuristics:

1. **Anomalous Label Length (2 marks):** DNS labels exceeding the typical 5–15-character distribution (e.g., `aGFja2VyaXNoZXJlLmNvbQ==.example.com`).
2. **High Entropy Subdomains (2 marks):** Shannon entropy of the FQDN > 4.0 bits/char, indicating encoded/encrypted payload.
3. **Unusual Query Frequency (1.5 marks):** More than N queries/sec from a single host to a single authoritative server (beaconing).
4. **TXT Record Abuse (1.5 marks):** Excessive use of NULL, TXT, or KEY record types for bulk data exfil (most legitimate traffic uses A/AAAA/MX).

> **Snort rule pattern:**
> `alert udp $HOME_NET any -> any 53 (msg:"DNS tunnel suspected"; byte_test:1,>,50,0; sid:1000001;)`

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks in Module 2 packet-sniffing questions:**
> 1. **Confusing promiscuous mode with monitor mode** — monitor is **wireless-only (802.11)**; promiscuous is **wired (Ethernet)**. The examiner will deduct 1 mark for the swap.
> 2. **Forgetting the PCAP per-record header (16 bytes)** in storage calculations. Always state it explicitly.
> 3. **Stating "TAP is software"** — TAPs are *physical hardware* devices; SPAN is the *software* alternative. This distinction is a 2-marker.
> 4. **Omitting the unit conversions** (bits ↔ bytes ↔ TB/TiB). Marks are reserved for dimensional clarity.
> 5. **Not naming libpcap/WinPcap** when describing the capture library — the syllabus lists it as a *must-know* component.
> 6. **Failing to mention fail-open behavior** of a TAP in power-loss scenarios — a classic 1-mark "twist" question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 / Packet Sniffing:**

- 🎯 **Packet Sniffing** = passive capture + decode of frames on a network medium.
- 🎯 **Promiscuous Mode** is the *sine qua non* of any NIDS sensor — without it, the IDS sees only broadcast and self-addressed traffic.
- 🎯 **Capture pipeline (PADMA mnemonic):** Promiscuous → Access → Decode → Match → Alert.
- 🎯 **TAP vs SPAN:** TAP = hardware, line-rate, fail-open, preferred at ≥1 Gbps. SPAN = software, oversubscribed, may drop.
- 🎯 **Hub-based sniffing** is a *classroom-only* concept; production deployments never use hubs.
- 🎯 **libpcap** is the canonical capture library; **WinPcap is deprecated → use Npcap**; **PF_RING** is the high-speed Linux alternative.
- 🎯 **BPF** is a kernel-resident virtual machine that filters packets *before* userspace — it is the single biggest performance lever.
- 🎯 **Wire-speed formula:** $F_{max} = \dfrac{B}{L_{frame} + L_{IFG} + L_{preamble}} = \dfrac{B}{672}$ for minimum frames.
- 🎯 **Standard Ethernet numbers to memorize:** MTU 1500 B, Min Frame 64 B, Max Frame 1518 B, IFG 96 bits, Preamble+SFD 64 bits.
- 🎯 **PCAP storage = $N \times (L_{frame} + 16)$**; always remember the 16-byte record header.
- 🎯 **DPI** (Deep Packet Inspection) decodes up to Layer 7; required for application-layer attack detection (DNS tunnel, C2, HTTP exfil).
- 🎯 **Stealth advantage of sniffing:** passive — invisible to the adversary, no latency, no jitter, no IP address.
- 🎯 **Legal note (KTU tip):** Wiretapping laws vary; sniffing traffic you do not own or administer is a criminal offence under IT Act §66 (India) and similar statutes.
- 🎯 **Tools named in syllabus:** Wireshark, tcpdump, TShark, Snort, Suricata, Zeek.
- 🎯 **Forensic admissibility:** pcap-NG with SHA-256 hashing and chain-of-custody metadata is the modern standard.

---

topics.title: Log Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Log Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Log Parsing** is the systematic process of transforming raw, unstructured or semi-structured log messages—generated by operating systems, network devices, applications, and security appliances—into a structured, machine-readable format (typically key-value pairs or event templates) by extracting constant template strings and variable dynamic parameters.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT742), Log Parsing is defined as the *first and most critical preprocessing stage* in the log analysis pipeline of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS). It converts heterogeneous log streams (e.g., **Syslog**, **Windows Event Logs**, **Apache access logs**, **firewall logs**, **IDS/IPS alerts**) into canonical event templates of the form:

$$E = \langle \text{Timestamp}, \text{EventTemplate}, \text{Parameters} \rangle$$

where the **EventTemplate** is a recurring constant skeleton and **Parameters** contain variable data such as IP addresses, user IDs, port numbers, and timestamps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Log parsing is the *foundation block* of all downstream IDS operations—including **anomaly detection**, **signature matching**, **correlation**, and **alert generation**. A poorly parsed log leads to cascading false positives and missed attacks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you receive **1 million letters** from different people around the world, all written in different handwriting styles, formats, and languages, but every letter says essentially the same thing: *"I am leaving the building at TIME on DATE."*

A **Log Parser** is like a highly efficient **postal sorting clerk** who:
1. Opens each letter (raw log line)
2. Identifies the common sentence structure (template) — *"I am leaving the building at TIME on DATE"*
3. Extracts the unique values — *5:30 PM, 12th October*

After processing, the clerk has neatly organized cards: each card has the **same template** and **different extracted values**. Now a security guard (the IDS) can quickly scan millions of cards and flag unusual departures (e.g., an unauthorized person, an odd time, a wrong door).

In cybersecurity terms: the *log message* is the letter, the *parser* is the clerk, the *event template* is the recurring structure, and the *parameters* are the extracted fields that the IDS engine inspects for indicators of compromise (IoCs).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally accepted in log parsing research (CTI, **LogPAI benchmarks**, KTU evaluation rubrics):

- **Parsing Accuracy (PA):** Ratio of correctly parsed log lines, expressed in percentage.
- **Grouping Accuracy (GA):** Ability to group log lines generated from the same template.
- **Template Accuracy (TA):** Precision of the extracted templates.
- **Parsing Speed:** Log lines processed per second (**LPS**), with state-of-the-art parsers achieving **≥ 5,000 LPS**.
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of precision and recall for token classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Log Parsing Matters in IDPS:**
> - Modern enterprise environments generate **terabytes of logs daily** (50+ GB/day is common in mid-size SOCs).
> - Raw logs are **unreadable to rule engines** (e.g., Snort, Suricata, Zeek) which expect structured fields.
> - Parsing reduces a log line from ~200 characters to ~20 tokens — a **90% dimensionality reduction**.

### 1.4 Real-World Significance in Cybersecurity

| Domain | Log Source | Parsed Field of Interest | Attack Detected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Network | Firewall (iptables) | `src_ip`, `dst_port`, `action` | Port scanning, exfiltration |
| Web | Apache/Nginx access log | `status_code`, `user_agent` | SQLi, XSS, brute force |
| Authentication | Windows Security Log (4625) | `user`, `logon_type`, `src_ip` | Brute force, lateral movement |
| Host | Linux syslog (`/var/log/auth.log`) | `user`, `command`, `sudo` | Privilege escalation |
| IDS | Snort alert log | `signature_id`, `proto` | Confirmed intrusion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Log Parsing Pipeline (Operational Stages)

A robust log parsing pipeline in an enterprise IDPS follows **five sequential stages**:

1. **Log Collection (Ingestion):** Aggregation of logs from distributed sources using protocols such as **Syslog (RFC 5424)**, **SNMP traps**, **Windows Event Forwarding (WEF)**, or agents like **Filebeat**, **Fluentd**, **Logstash**.
2. **Normalization:** Standardization of timestamps (typically to **ISO 8601 / UTC**), character encoding (UTF-8), and line delimiters.
3. **Tokenization:** Splitting each log line into a sequence of atomic tokens using delimiters (space, tab, comma, semicolon, brackets).
4. **Template Extraction:** Identification of the constant skeleton (template) that remains invariant across all instances of the same log type.
5. **Parameter Extraction:** Mapping variable tokens (IPs, timestamps, IDs) to typed fields for downstream consumption.

### 2.2 Core Parsing Techniques (Taxonomy)

Log parsing techniques are broadly classified into four families, each with a distinct algorithmic philosophy:

#### A. Heuristic / Rule-Based Methods
- **Principle:** Use handcrafted regular expressions or expert-defined patterns.
- **Examples:** **RE parser**, **Logpider**, **SLCT (Simple Logfile Clustering Tool)**.
- **Strength:** High precision on known formats.
- **Weakness:** Brittle to log format evolution; requires manual maintenance.

#### B. Grammar-Based Methods
- **Principle:** Learn probabilistic context-free grammars (PCFGs) or finite-state automata from log sequences.
- **Examples:** **LOGMINE**, **CLF (Cluster Log Format)**.
- **Strength:** Captures structural recursion.
- **Weakness:** Computationally expensive; struggles with highly noisy logs.

#### C. Clustering-Based Methods
- **Principle:** Group similar log lines via string similarity metrics and elect a representative template per cluster.
- **Examples:** **LogCluster**, **Drain (the industry gold standard)**, **Spell**, **LFA (Log Forensic Analysis)**.
- **Strength:** Unsupervised, robust to parameter variation.
- **Weakness:** Sensitive to threshold hyperparameters.

#### D. Machine Learning / Deep Learning Methods
- **Principle:** Train neural models (LSTMs, Transformers, BERT variants like **LogBERT**) to predict template positions.
- **Examples:** **LogPai**, **Neurallog**, **Uniparser**.
- **Strength:** Adaptive to new formats.
- **Weakness:** Requires labeled data, high GPU cost.

### 2.3 The Drain Algorithm (Detailed Mechanics)

**Drain** is the de-facto industry standard parser and the most-asked log parsing topic in KTU examinations. It builds a **fixed-depth prefix tree (trie)** of log lines:

1. **Preprocessing:** Replace all digits and IPs with the wildcard token `<*>`.
2. **Depth-based search:** Search the trie from the root, comparing tokens at each depth.
3. **Similarity check:** Within a leaf node, compute the similarity between candidate log line and existing log groups using the **log sequence count vector**.
4. **Update:** If similarity exceeds threshold (typically **0.7**), merge into existing group; otherwise, create a new group.
5. **Template update:** When a new token appears in a group, replace it with `<*>` to generalize the template.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Notation | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Event Tuple | $E = \langle T, M, P \rangle$ | Event = Timestamp + Message + Parameters | Universal |
| 2 | Tokenization | $L = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$ | Log line split into $n$ tokens | $n$ varies |
| 3 | Template Extraction | $T = \text{Tokens}(L) \setminus V$ | Template = tokens minus variable set | Constant |
| 4 | Word Count Vector | $\vec{v} = (c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n)$ | Count of each token at depth $i$ | $\mathbb{Z}^n$ |
| 5 | Drain Similarity Score | $S = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(t_i = g_i)}{n}$ | Fraction of identical tokens | $0 \leq S \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Edit Distance (Levenshtein) | $d_{edit}(s_1, s_2)$ | Min. insertions/deletions/substitutions | $\mathbb{N}_0$ |
| 7 | Jaccard Similarity (token sets) | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Token-set overlap coefficient | $0 \leq J \leq 1$ |
| 8 | Parsing Accuracy | $PA = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of correctly parsed lines | Target $\geq 95\%$ |
| 9 | Grouping Accuracy | $GA = \frac{N_{correct\_groups}}{N_{total\_groups}} \times 100\%$ | Correct cluster assignments | Target $\geq 90\%$ |
| 10 | Throughput | $\text{LPS} = \frac{N_{lines}}{T_{seconds}}$ | Lines parsed per second | $\geq 5{,}000$ LPS |
| 11 | F1 Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | $0 \leq F_1 \leq 1$ |
| 12 | Preprocessing Regex | `\d+` $\rightarrow$ `<NUM>` | Digit-to-wildcard substitution | Universal |
| 13 | IP Regex | `\d{1,3}(\.\d{1,3}){3}` $\rightarrow$ `<IP>` | IPv4-to-wildcard substitution | Universal |
| 14 | Drain Tree Depth | $d_{max} = 4$ (default) | Maximum prefix tree depth | Constant |
| 15 | Similarity Threshold | $\tau$ | Drain merge threshold | $0.5 \leq \tau \leq 0.9$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Always remember the difference between **PA (Parsing Accuracy)** and **GA (Grouping Accuracy)**. PA measures line-level correctness, while GA measures cluster-level correctness. Many students confuse them in Part B answers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Splunk Enterprise Security (ES):** Internally uses a custom Drain-variant parser for ingesting > **1 PB/day** of customer logs.
- **Elastic Stack (ELK):** The **Grok** processor in Logstash performs regex-based log parsing with **> 400 pre-built patterns**.
- **Microsoft Sentinel:** Uses **KQL (Kusto Query Language)** parsers that operate on already-structured event types.
- **Open-Source:** **LogPAI benchmark suite** (loghub) provides 16 real-world log datasets widely used for evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Parsing an Apache Access Log (End-to-End)

Consider the following three raw log lines from an Apache HTTP server:

```
192.168.1.10 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 +0000] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326
192.168.1.11 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:37 +0000] "POST /login.php HTTP/1.1" 401 512
10.0.0.5 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:38 +0000] "GET /admin.php HTTP/1.1" 403 289
```

**Step 1 — Tokenization (split on whitespace and special delimiters):**

For Line 1, applying whitespace tokenization:

$$L_1 = \{\text{`192.168.1.10'}, \text{`-'}, \text{`-'}, \text{`[10/Oct/2024:13:55:36'}, \text{`+0000]'}, \text{`"GET'}, \text{`/index.html'}, \text{`HTTP/1.1"'}, \text{`200'}, \text{`2326'}\}$$

This yields $n = 10$ tokens.

**Step 2 — Preprocessing (digit and IP substitution):**

Apply the substitution rules:
- `\d{1,3}(\.\d{1,3}){3}` $\rightarrow$ `<IP>`
- `\d+` $\rightarrow$ `<NUM>`

The processed line becomes:

$$\hat{L}_1 = \{\text{`<IP>'}, \text{`-'}, \text{`-'}, \text{`[10/Oct/2024:13:55:36'}, \text{`+0000]'}, \text{`"GET'}, \text{`/index.html'}, \text{`HTTP/1.1"'}, \text{`<NUM>'}, \text{`<NUM>'}\}$$

**Step 3 — Template Extraction (constant identification):**

Comparing $\hat{L}_1$, $\hat{L}_2$, $\hat{L}_3$ across all three lines, the constant skeleton is:

$$T = \text{`<IP> - - [<*>] "<*> <*> <*>" <NUM> <NUM>'}$$

The 6th, 7th, 8th tokens are HTTP method, URL, and protocol — all of which are variable. They must be replaced by `<*>` (note: Drain places the wildcard *after* observing a second occurrence with a different value).

**Step 4 — Parameter Extraction (typed field mapping):**

| Field | Value | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `src_ip` | `192.168.1.10` | IPv4Address |
| `timestamp` | `10/Oct/2024:13:55:36` | RFC3164 DateTime |
| `http_method` | `GET` | Enum |
| `url` | `/index.html` | String |
| `http_version` | `HTTP/1.1` | Enum |
| `status_code` | `200` | Integer |
| `bytes_sent` | `2326` | Integer |

**Step 5 — Event Tuple Construction:**

$$E_1 = \langle T_1, M_1, P_1 \rangle = \langle \text{`10/Oct/2024:13:55:36'}, T, P_1 \rangle$$

where $P_1 = \{\text{src\_ip}=192.168.1.10, \text{status}=200, \text{bytes}=2326, \dots\}$.

### 3.2 Drain Algorithm — Detailed Walkthrough

**Given Input:** 4 log lines from an SSH service.

```
sshd[1234]: Accepted password for admin from 10.0.0.5 port 22 ssh2
sshd[1235]: Failed password for admin from 10.0.0.6 port 22 ssh2
sshd[1236]: Accepted password for root from 10.0.0.7 port 22 ssh2
sshd[1237]: Invalid user hacker from 10.0.0.8 port 22 ssh2
```

**Step A — Preprocess** (replace digits and IPs with wildcards):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1' &= \text{`sshd[<*>]: Accepted password for admin from <IP> port <NUM> ssh2'} \\
L_2' &= \text{`sshd[<*>]: Failed password for admin from <IP> port <NUM> ssh2'} \\
L_3' &= \text{`sshd[<*>]: Accepted password for root from <IP> port <NUM> ssh2'} \\
L_4' &= \text{`sshd[<*>]: Invalid user hacker from <IP> port <NUM> ssh2'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Token Sequence (count per position):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position 0:} \quad & \{\text{`sshd'} : 4\} \\
\text{Position 1:} \quad & \{\text{`<*>'}: 4\} \\
\text{Position 2:} \quad & \{\text{`Accepted'}: 2, \text{`Failed'}: 1, \text{`Invalid'}: 1\} \\
\text{Position 3:} \quad & \{\text{`password'}: 2, \text{`user'}: 1, \text{`password'}: 1\} \\
\dots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Drain Search & Group Formation:**

Drain traverses the trie depth-by-depth. At depth 2, it encounters 3 distinct tokens; it creates **3 separate log groups** corresponding to the three distinct event types:

- **Group 1:** "Accepted password for * from * port * ssh2"
- **Group 2:** "Failed password for * from * port * ssh2"
- **Group 3:** "Invalid user * from * port * ssh2"

**Step D — Template Finalization:**

After all 4 lines are processed, the final templates are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \text{`sshd[<*>]: Accepted password for <*> from <IP> port <NUM> ssh2'} \\
T_2 &= \text{`sshd[<*>]: Failed password for <*> from <IP> port <NUM> ssh2'} \\
T_3 &= \text{`sshd[<*>]: Invalid user <*> from <IP> port <NUM> ssh2'}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Similarity Score Derivation (Token-Count Vector Method)

For two log lines $L_a$ and $L_b$ of equal length $n$, the **Drain similarity** is computed as:

$$
S(L_a, L_b) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\bigl(t_i^{(a)} = t_i^{(b)}\bigr)
$$

**Worked Calculation:** Let $L_a = \text{`Accepted password for admin from 10.0.0.5 port 22'}$ and $L_b = \text{`Accepted password for root from 10.0.0.7 port 22'}$.

Token-wise comparison (after preprocessing, both contain `<IP>` and `<NUM>`):

| Position $i$ | $t_i^{(a)}$ | $t_i^{(b)}$ | Match? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Accepted | Accepted | $\checkmark$ |
| 1 | password | password | $\checkmark$ |
| 2 | for | for | $\checkmark$ |
| 3 | admin | root | $\times$ |
| 4 | from | from | $\checkmark$ |
| 5 | `<IP>` | `<IP>` | $\checkmark$ |
| 6 | port | port | $\checkmark$ |
| 7 | `<NUM>` | `<NUM>` | $\checkmark$ |

Therefore:

$$
S(L_a, L_b) = \frac{7}{8} = 0.875
$$

Since $S = 0.875 > \tau = 0.7$ (default Drain threshold), Drain will **merge** $L_b$ into the group of $L_a$, correctly identifying them as belonging to the same template.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Parser Comparison

| Parser | Type | Year | Avg. PA | Avg. GA | Speed (LPS) | Online? | KTU-Recommended? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RE | Rule-based | — | 0.62 | 0.58 | 50,000+ | Yes | For known formats |
| SLCT | Heuristic | 2003 | 0.78 | 0.72 | 12,000 | No | No |
| LogCluster | Clustering | 2016 | 0.86 | 0.83 | 8,500 | No | Yes |
| **Drain** | **Clustering** | **2017** | **0.96** | **0.93** | **22,000** | **Yes** | **★ Highly Recommended** |
| Spell | Streaming | 2016 | 0.85 | 0.80 | 18,000 | Yes | No |
| LogPAI | Deep Learning | 2021 | 0.92 | 0.88 | 1,200 | No | Research only |
| LogBERT | Transformer | 2022 | 0.94 | 0.91 | 450 | No | Research only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Log Parsing Pipeline — End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[syslog sources] --> B[log collector]
    C[windows event log] --> B
    D[firewall logs] --> B
    E[application logs] --> B
    B --> F[normalization layer]
    F --> G[tokenization engine]
    G --> H[template extractor]
    H --> I[parameter binder]
    I --> J[structured event store]
    J --> K[IDS rule engine]
    J --> L[anomaly detection ML]
    J --> M[SIEM dashboard]
```

### 4.2 Drain Algorithm Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[raw log line] --> B[preprocess digits and IPs]
    B --> C[tokenize on whitespace]
    C --> D{prefix tree search}
    D -->|match found at depth d| E[compare with existing log groups]
    D -->|no match| F[create new log group]
    E --> G{similarity S greater than threshold tau}
    G -->|yes| H[merge into existing group]
    G -->|no| F
    H --> I[update template with new wildcards]
    F --> I
    I --> J[emit event tuple]
```
