- **Cloud Workload Protection** (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender, Google Chronicle)
- **OT/SCADA Security** for critical infrastructure (water treatment plants, power grids)
- **5G Core Network Security** where microsecond-level inline responses are mandatory
- **E-commerce Fraud Detection** where every second of downtime translates to direct revenue loss

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Detection Confidence Score

The **Detection Confidence Score (C)** is the fundamental metric that determines *when* an IDS escalates from passive observation to active response. Below is its exhaustive derivation.

**Given Data:**
- A multi-layered detection engine observes $n$ independent indicators $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n$ for a single network event.
- Each indicator is binary: $I_i = 1$ if triggered, $I_i = 0$ if not triggered.
- Each indicator is assigned a normalized weight $w_i \in [0,1]$ representing its reliability.

**Objective:** Derive a closed-form expression for the overall confidence that an event is malicious.

**Step 1 — Define the Weighted Sum**

The total confidence contribution from all indicators is the sum of the products of weights and indicator values.

$$
C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot I_i
$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate the Bound**

Since $0 \leq w_i \leq 1$ and $I_i \in \{0, 1\}$, the score $C$ lies in the interval:

$$
0 \leq C \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = W_{total}
$$

where $W_{total}$ is the cumulative maximum weight.

**Step 3 — Normalize the Score**

To obtain a probability-like score $C_{norm} \in [0, 1]$, we divide by the total possible weight.

$$
C_{norm} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot I_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Decision Threshold**

A response is triggered if and only if the normalized score exceeds a system-defined threshold $\tau$.

$$
\text{Response} = \begin{cases} \text{TRIGGERED} & \text{if } C_{norm} \geq \tau \\ \text{SUPPRESSED} & \text{if } C_{norm} < \tau \end{cases}
$$

Typical thresholds: $\tau = 0.70$ for passive alert, $\tau = 0.90$ for active inline block.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An IDS monitors a suspicious HTTP request. Three indicators are evaluated:
- $I_1$ = SQL keyword match, weight $w_1 = 0.5$
- $I_2$ = unusual User-Agent string, weight $w_2 = 0.3$
- $I_3$ = anomalous request rate, weight $w_3 = 0.2$

All three indicators are triggered ($I_1 = I_2 = I_3 = 1$). Determine the response action if $\tau = 0.80$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate the numerator:

$$
\sum w_i \cdot I_i = (0.5)(1) + (0.3)(1) + (0.2)(1) = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0
$$

Step 2 — Calculate the denominator (total weight):

$$
\sum w_i = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0
$$

Step 3 — Compute the normalized score:

$$
C_{norm} = \frac{1.0}{1.0} = 1.0
$$

Step 4 — Compare to threshold:

$$
C_{norm} = 1.0 \geq \tau = 0.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Active Inline Block TRIGGERED}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** The example demonstrates that when all indicators fire and the weights sum to unity, the maximum confidence of 1.0 is achieved, which decisively triggers the highest-tier response (inline packet drop).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Response Actions vs. Attack Severity

| Attack Severity | Confidence Required | IDS Action | IPS Action | Notification Channel |
|-----------------|---------------------|------------|------------|----------------------|
| **Low (Reconnaissance)** | $0.40 \leq C < 0.60$ | Log only | Log + rate-limit | Email digest (daily) |
| **Medium (Policy Violation)** | $0.60 \leq C < 0.80$ | Alert + Log | Drop non-critical packets | Email + SIEM dashboard |
| **High (Exploit Attempt)** | $0.80 \leq C < 0.95$ | Urgent Alert + Log + Snapshot | TCP Reset + Block IP | SMS + Pager + SIEM |
| **Critical (Active Breach)** | $C \geq 0.95$ | Alarm + Full packet capture | Immediate quarantine + IP blacklist | Phone call + War room activation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IDS/IPS Response Workflow

The following diagram maps the complete decision and action flow executed by an IDS/IPS upon detecting an attack attempt.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Traffic Arrives at IDS/IPS Sensor] --> B{Packet Capture Engine}
    B --> C[Pre-processing: Decoding & Normalization]
    C --> D[Detection Engine Evaluation]
    D --> E{Anomaly or Signature Match?}
    E -->|No Match| F[Forward Packet - Normal Flow]
    E -->|Match Detected| G[Confidence Score Calculation]
    G --> H{Score vs Threshold Tau}
    H -->|Below Tau| I[Log Event - Suppress Alert]
    H -->|At or Above Tau| J[Alert Generation Module]
    J --> K[Log Enrichment and Storage]
    K --> L[Notification Dispatcher]
    L --> M{Severity Level}
    M -->|Low or Medium| N[Email and SIEM Dashboard]
    M -->|High| O[SMS plus Pager plus SIEM]
    M -->|Critical| P[Phone Call plus War Room]
    J --> Q{IPS Inline Mode Active?}
    Q -->|No - Passive IDS| R[Continue Monitoring]
    Q -->|Yes - Active IPS| S[Active Response Engine]
    S --> T[Drop Malicious Packet]
    S --> U[Inject TCP Reset]
    S --> V[Dynamic Firewall Block]
    S --> W[Quarantine Source IP]
    T --> X[Update Threat Intelligence Database]
    U --> X
    V --> X
    W --> X
    X --> Y[Escalate to SOC Analyst]
    Y --> Z[Incident Response Plan Activated]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — IPS Response Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        A1[Raw Network Packets]
    end
    subgraph DetectionLayer[Detection Layer]
        B1[Signature Engine]
        B2[Anomaly Engine]
        B3[Stateful Protocol Analysis]
    end
    subgraph DecisionLayer[Decision Layer]
        C1[Correlation and Scoring Module]
        C2[Threshold Comparator]
    end
    subgraph ResponseLayer[Response Layer]
        D1[Active Block Module]
        D2[Session Reset Module]
        D3[Logging Module]
        D4[Notification Module]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        E1[Forwarded Clean Traffic]
        E2[Dropped Malicious Traffic]
        E3[SOC Dashboard and SIEM]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    C2 --> D4
    D1 --> E2
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
    D4 --> E3
    D1 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The first diagram illustrates the *sequential decision flow*, while the second diagram illustrates the *concurrent functional architecture* where detection, decision, and response modules operate as parallel processing pipelines — this is the standard architecture in commercial IPS appliances like **Cisco Firepower 4100 Series** and **Palo Alto PA-7000 Series**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is the difference between a passive IDS response and an active IPS response? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
A **passive IDS response** involves detecting an attack and generating an alert or log entry without interfering with the malicious traffic. The original traffic is allowed to continue to its destination, and human intervention is required to take corrective action. *Example:* Snort logging a signature match to a syslog server.

An **active IPS response** involves the system taking immediate, automated, in-line action to stop the attack. The IPS sits directly in the traffic path and can drop, reset, or block the malicious traffic in real time. *Example:* A network IPS injecting a TCP RST packet to terminate an ongoing attack session.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "alerting" with "preventing." An IDS *alerts*; an IPS *prevents*. Confusing these two will cost you 1 full mark.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List any three actions an IDS takes when it detects an attack attempt.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Alert Generation** — Sends a prioritized notification to the security administrator via email, SMS, or SIEM dashboard.
2. **Event Logging** — Records the attack details (timestamp, source IP, destination, signature ID, payload) to a persistent storage for forensic analysis.
3. **Evidence Collection** — Captures the full packet payload (pcap) and stores it for legal and incident response purposes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain in detail the various response actions performed by an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) when an attack attempt is detected. Also, differentiate between passive and active response mechanisms.*

**(a) Explanation of IDPS Response Actions [7 Marks]**
**(b) Block Diagram and Passive vs. Active Response Comparison [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of IDPS Response Actions [7 Marks]**

When an IDPS detects an attack attempt, it performs the following sequence of response actions:

1. **Alert Generation:** The system creates a structured alert containing the timestamp, attack signature, source/destination IP addresses, protocol, and severity level. This alert is dispatched through multiple channels (email, SNMP trap, SMS, SIEM).

2. **Logging:** Every detected event is recorded in a tamper-evident log file. Logs may be stored locally or forwarded to a centralized log server (syslog, SIEM) for long-term retention and correlation with other security events.

3. **Packet Capture & Forensics:** The full payload of the malicious packet(s) is captured and saved in PCAP format. This serves as digital evidence for post-incident investigation, legal proceedings, and threat hunting.

4. **Session Termination (IPS):** The in-line IPS injects a TCP RST (reset) packet to forcibly close the attacker's connection, halting the ongoing attack.

5. **Packet Dropping (IPS):** The IPS silently discards malicious packets without notifying the sender, preventing the attack payload from reaching the target.

6. **Dynamic Firewall Rule Update:** The IPS can programmatically add the attacker's IP address to a deny-list on the perimeter firewall, blocking all future traffic from that source.

7. **Quarantine & Isolation:** The compromised internal endpoint is moved to a quarantine VLAN, restricting its network access until forensic analysis is complete.

8. **Escalation to Incident Response Team:** High-severity alerts trigger the organizational Incident Response Plan (IRP), activating the Computer Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT).

**(b) Block Diagram and Passive vs. Active Response Comparison [7 Marks]**

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) illustrates the complete response workflow.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Passive Response (IDS) | Active Response (IPS) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Position in Network** | Out-of-band (TAP/SPAN port) | In-line (serial in the traffic path) |
| **Action on Traffic** | Logs and alerts; traffic passes through | Modifies, drops, or resets traffic |
| **Latency Introduced** | Near zero | 100–200 microseconds |
| **Failure Mode** | Fail-open (no impact) | Fail-closed (network downtime risk) |
| **Use Case** | Monitoring, compliance | Real-time attack prevention |
| **Example Tool** | Snort (IDS mode) | Suricata (IPS mode), Cisco Firepower |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw the block diagram but forget to label the *direction of data flow* and the *decision diamonds*. This leads to a loss of 1–2 marks. Always use clear arrows and explicit "Yes/No" labels on every decision branch.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** *Explain the concept of confidence scoring in modern IDPS. Derive the normalized confidence score formula and illustrate with a numerical example involving four detection indicators. Discuss the role of threshold tuning in minimizing false positives.*

**(a) Concept of Confidence Scoring & Formula Derivation [7 Marks]**
**(b) Numerical Example & Threshold Tuning [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Confidence Scoring & Formula Derivation [7 Marks]**

Modern IDPS engines rarely rely on a single detection method. Instead, they employ **multi-layered detection** combining signature matching, anomaly detection, protocol analysis, and behavioral heuristics. Each detection layer produces a binary indicator $I_i$ (1 = triggered, 0 = not triggered), and each indicator is assigned a reliability weight $w_i$.

The **raw confidence score** is the weighted sum of all triggered indicators:

$$
C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot I_i
$$

To convert this into a normalized probability in the range $[0,1]$, we divide by the total possible weight:

$$
C_{norm} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot I_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}, \quad 0 \leq C_{norm} \leq 1
$$

The system then compares $C_{norm}$ to a pre-configured threshold $\tau$:
- If $C_{norm} \geq \tau$ → **Response triggered** (alert, block, or reset)
- If $C_{norm} < \tau$ → **Event suppressed** (logged but no alert)

This approach reduces single-signature false positives by requiring *corroborating evidence* from multiple independent detection layers before escalating to a high-priority alert.

**(b) Numerical Example & Threshold Tuning [7 Marks]**

**Problem:** An IDPS monitors a network event. Four indicators are evaluated with the following weights and values:

| Indicator | Description | Weight $w_i$ | Triggered $I_i$ |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | Known SQL injection signature | 0.40 | 1 |
| 2 | Abnormal request size | 0.25 | 1 |
| 3 | Mismatched HTTP version | 0.20 | 0 |
| 4 | Unusual source geolocation | 0.15 | 1 |

Determine whether the IDPS triggers a response if $\tau = 0.70$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the weighted sum (numerator):

$$
C = (0.40 \times 1) + (0.25 \times 1) + (0.20 \times 0) + (0.15 \times 1)
$$
$$
C = 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.00 + 0.15 = 0.80
$$

Step 2 — Compute the total weight (denominator):

$$
\sum w_i = 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00
$$

Step 3 — Normalize:

$$
C_{norm} = \frac{0.80}{1.00} = 0.80
$$

Step 4 — Compare to threshold:

$$
C_{norm} = 0.80 \geq \tau = 0.70 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{RESPONSE TRIGGERED}
$$

**Threshold Tuning Discussion:**
- Setting $\tau$ too low (e.g., 0.30) results in **excessive false positives**, overwhelming the SOC.
- Setting $\tau$ too high (e.g., 0.95) results in **false negatives**, allowing real attacks to slip through.
- The optimal $\tau$ is determined empirically using **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve analysis**, balancing the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)**.

$$
\text{ROC Point} = \left( \frac{FP}{FP + TN}, \; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to mention the *ROC curve* in threshold tuning discussions. Including ROC analysis in your answer can earn you an extra 1 mark of "impression" credit that KTU examiners often award for depth of understanding.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams**

- 🔹 **IDS = Detect + Alert; IPS = Detect + Prevent.** This is the single most important distinction.
- 🔹 **Passive Response Actions:** Logging, alerting, notification, packet capture, evidence preservation.
- 🔹 **Active Response Actions (IPS only):** Packet drop, TCP RST injection, dynamic firewall block, quarantine.
- 🔹 **Confidence Score Formula:** $C_{norm} = \frac{\sum w_i I_i}{\sum w_i}$, range $[0,1]$.
- 🔹 **Threshold Logic:** Trigger response if $C_{norm} \geq \tau$; typical $\tau$ values are **0.70 (alert)** and **0.90 (inline block)**.
- 🔹 **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$.
- 🔹 **True Positive Rate (TPR):** $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.
- 🔹 **IPS Inline Latency:** Must be **≤ 200 μs** to maintain network performance.
- 🔹 **Notification Channels (in priority order):** Log file → SIEM → Email → SMS → Pager → Phone call → War room.
- 🔹 **Fail-Open vs. Fail-Closed:** IDS fails-open (transparent); IPS can be configured fail-open or fail-closed.
- 🔹 **Common Tools:** Snort (IDS), Suricata (IDS/IPS), Bro/Zeek (Network Security Monitor), Cisco Firepower (IPS), Palo Alto Networks (NGFW with IPS).
- 🔹 **Standards & Frameworks:** NIST SP 800-61 (Incident Response), ISO 27035, MITRE ATT&CK (threat intelligence).
- 🔹 **MTTR Formula:** $\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N} \sum (t_{resolve,j} - t_{detect,j})$ — commonly asked in 1-mark definition questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passive Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Passive Response # 1. Passive Response — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Passive Response** in an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) refers to the category of reactive mechanisms in which the system **observes, records, and reports** malicious or anomalous activity **without directly intervening** in the ongoing attack traffic flow. In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, passive response is defined as the non-interventionist mode of IDPS reaction wherein the sensor/agent performs the following actions exclusively: - **Event Logging:** Persists detailed records of detected events to a local or remote storage medium. - **Notification Generation:** Issues alerts via standard channels such as **SNMP traps**, **SYSLOG**, **e-mail**, **SMS**, or **console dashboards**. - **Forensic Archival:** Captures packet payloads, session flows, and full-packet captures (PCAP) for post-incident analysis. - **Reporting:** Aggregates event data into human-readable reports (compliance, audit, executive summary). The defining characteristic is the **absence of any active countermeasure**: the attack traffic is **not blocked, dropped, reset, or modified** by the IDPS itself. The system remains a *silent observer* — analogous to a forensic black box. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Silent Witness CCTV in a Bank Vault** Imagine a bank vault equipped with a high-definition CCTV camera system. When a masked intruder enters and attempts to crack the safe, the cameras do **not** physically restrain the intruder, do **not** sound an alarm that locks the door, and do **not** deploy tear gas. Instead, they: 1. **Record every movement** (logging). 2. **Send a silent alert to the security control room** (notification). 3. **Store the footage for later court evidence** (forensic archival). 4. **Generate a daily activity report** (reporting). The CCTV's role is purely **passive observation and documentation**. The *active* response (locking doors, dispatching guards) is left to a *separate* system or human operator. This is precisely how a passive IDPS functions within a network. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Parameters | Metric | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Log Retention Period** | 90–365 days | Compliance with audit standards | | **Alert Latency** | < 5 seconds (real-time) | Time between detection and notification | | **SYSLOG Severity Levels** | 0 (Emergency) – 7 (Debug) | RFC 5424 standard | | **SNMP Trap Port** | UDP **162** | Default notification port | | **PCAP File Size Limit** | 100 MB – 2 GB per session | Forensic storage management | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Passive Response Mechanisms Passive responses are not a single monolithic action; they constitute a **layered notification and archival architecture**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *enumerate* and *differentiate* between these layers. ### 2.1.1 Logging Mechanisms The most fundamental passive response. The IDPS writes structured records to: - **Local flat-file logs** (e.g., `/var/log/ids.log`) - **Centralized SYSLOG servers** (RFC 5424 compliant) - **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) databases** Each log entry typically contains: **timestamp**, **source IP**, **destination IP**, **signature ID**, **severity**, **protocol**, **action taken (alert only)**, and **payload excerpt**. ### 2.1.2 Notification / Alerting Mechanisms Real-time dissemination of detection events to responsible personnel or systems: - **E-mail Alerts:** SMTP-based, suitable for low-frequency critical events. - **SMS / Pager Alerts:** Used for after-hours critical incidents. - **Console / Dashboard Pop-ups:** Operator-station based visual alerts. - **SNMP Traps:** Asynchronous UDP messages sent to a Network Management System (NMS) on port **162**, indicating the IDPS has crossed a defined threshold. ### 2.1.3 Forensic Data Capture The IDPS captures and stores: - **Full Packet Captures (PCAP)** of offending sessions. - **Session reconstructions** (TCP stream reassembly). - **File carvings** (extracting transferred files from packet data). - **Memory snapshots** of affected endpoints (host-based IDPS). ### 2.1.4 Reporting Periodic or on-demand generation of: - **Executive Summary Reports** — high-level metrics for management. - **Technical Detail Reports** — signature hits, source/destination analysis. - **Compliance Reports** — mapped to PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001. ## 2.2 Why Passive Response? — The Engineering Justification > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Concept — The "Observation Imperative"** In high-availability environments (banking, healthcare, government), **active blocking can cause more damage than the attack itself**. For example, an IPS that aggressively blocks traffic during a false positive may take down a legitimate business-critical application. Passive response is therefore preferred when: 1. **False Positive Tolerance is Low** — a 0.01% false positive rate can cause massive collateral damage. 2. **Regulatory Forensics is Required** — evidence must be preserved intact, not destroyed by intervention. 3. **Network is in Learning/Tuning Phase** — a new IDPS deployment requires passive observation before active rules are enabled. 4. **Inline Active Blocking is Architecturally Infeasible** — tap-mode (passive) deployment on SPAN ports. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the high-yield reference points for passive response in KTU 2024 examinations. | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | SNMP Trap Port | $P_{SNMP} = 162$ | UDP Port | RFC 1157 | | SYSLOG Default Port | $P_{SYSLOG} = 514$ | UDP Port | RFC 5424 | | Secure SYSLOG Port | $P_{TLS} = 6514$ | TCP Port | RFC 5425 (TLS) | | Log Severity (Numerical) | $S \in \{0, 1, 2, ..., 7\}$ | Integer | 0=Emergency, 7=Debug | | Alert Latency Budget | $t_{alert} \le 5\,\text{s}$ | Seconds | Real-time threshold | | Storage Estimate | $S_{req} = R \times D \times P_{avg}$ | Bytes | $R$=rate, $D$=days, $P_{avg}$=avg packet size | | PCAP Retention | $90 \le D_{ret} \le 365$ | Days | Compliance dependent | The storage requirement equation deserves explicit attention. The total storage needed for forensic retention is computed as: $$ S_{req} = R \cdot D \cdot P_{avg} $$ where $R$ is the **packet capture rate (packets/second)**, $D$ is the **retention duration in seconds**, and $P_{avg}$ is the **average packet size in bytes**. This equation is essential for capacity planning during IDPS deployment. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Passive response is the **backbone of modern Security Operations Centers (SOCs)**. Tools such as **Splunk, IBM QRadar, ArcSight, and Elastic Stack (ELK)** all consume passive IDPS logs. The workflow is: **IDPS Sensor** $\rightarrow$ **SYSLOG / Direct Forward** $\rightarrow$ **SIEM Correlation Engine** $\rightarrow$ **Analyst Investigation** $\rightarrow$ **Manual Active Response** This SOC-driven model is the industry standard for **threat hunting, incident response, and compliance auditing**. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Passive Response Workflow ## 3.1 Operational State Machine of Passive Response The passive response mechanism follows a deterministic **four-state workflow**. KTU examiners often test this via "describe the operational phases" questions. ### Step 1 — Event Detection The IDPS engine matches observed traffic against its signature database or anomaly model. Upon a match, an internal event object is instantiated containing all relevant attributes. ### Step 2 — Severity Classification The detected event is mapped to a severity level (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Informational) based on: - Signature weight - Asset criticality - Confidence score ### Step 3 — Multi-Channel Notification Dispatch The event is asynchronously dispatched to **one or more** notification sinks in parallel. This is governed by the rule's response profile (configured by the security administrator). ### Step 4 — Persistence and Archival The event and any associated forensic data (PCAP, payload, file artifacts) are written to durable storage with integrity checks (e.g., SHA-256 hashes appended for chain-of-custody). ## 3.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Passive Response Decision Let $E_i$ denote a detected event instance, $S(E_i)$ its severity score, and $\mathcal{N} = \{N_1, N_2, ..., N_k\}$ the set of configured notification channels. The passive response action is the function: $$ \mathcal{R}_{passive}(E_i) = \begin{cases} \text{Log}(E_i) & \text{always} \\ \text{Alert}(N_j) \text{ for } j \in \mathcal{N} & \text{if } S(E_i) \ge \tau \\ \text{Capture}(E_i) & \text{if } \text{profile}(E_i).\text{pcap} = \text{true} \\ \text{Report}(E_i) & \text{at scheduled interval} \end{cases} $$ where $\tau$ is the **alert threshold** configured by the administrator. Events below $\tau$ are silently logged without external notification (a common **noise reduction** technique). ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Passive vs. Active Response > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 examinations frequently include a "compare and contrast" question on passive and active response. The following table must be memorized. | Dimension | Passive Response | Active Response | |---|---|---| | **Interference with traffic** | None | Blocks, drops, resets, or modifies traffic | | **Latency introduced** | Zero | 50–200 µs per packet (inline) | | **False positive risk** | Zero collateral damage | Can cause self-inflicted DoS | | **Forensic preservation** | Excellent (intact evidence) | Evidence may be disrupted by intervention | | **Deployment mode** | TAP / SPAN / passive mirror | Inline (Bump-in-the-Wire) | | **Use case** | SOC, compliance, threat hunting | Real-time attack mitigation | | **Speed of containment** | Slow (human-mediated) | Instant (automated) | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Storage Capacity Planning > **Problem:** A KTU-mandated IDPS deployment requires capturing forensic data for a 1 Gbps link, 24/7, for 90 days. The average packet size is 800 bytes. Compute the required storage in Terabytes (TB). **Given:** - Link rate: $R_{link} = 1\,\text{Gbps}$ - Average packet size: $P_{avg} = 800\,\text{bytes}$ - Retention period: $D = 90\,\text{days}$ **Step 1 — Compute the packets per second.** $$ PPS = \frac{R_{link}}{P_{avg} \times 8} = \frac{10^9}{800 \times 8} = \frac{10^9}{6400} = 156250\,\text{packets/second} $$ **Step 2 — Convert the retention period to seconds.** $$ D_{sec} = 90 \times 24 \times 3600 = 7776000\,\text{seconds} $$ **Step 3 — Compute total packets in retention window.** $$ N_{total} = PPS \times D_{sec} = 156250 \times 7776000 = 1.215 \times 10^{12}\,\text{packets} $$ **Step 4 — Compute total storage in bytes.** $$ S_{req} = N_{total} \times P_{avg} = 1.215 \times 10^{12} \times 800 = 9.72 \times 10^{14}\,\text{bytes} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to Terabytes.** $$ S_{TB} = \frac{9.72 \times 10^{14}}{10^{12}} = 972\,\text{TB} \approx 1\,\text{PB} $$ **Conclusion:** The IDPS requires approximately **1 Petabyte of forensic storage** for the 90-day retention window, illustrating why passive IDPS deployments demand substantial storage engineering. ## 3.5 Common Pitfall: The "Silent Failure" Anti-Pattern > [!WARNING] > A poorly configured passive IDPS that **only logs to local disk with no remote forwarding** is operationally equivalent to having no IDPS at all. If the attacker compromises the host and deletes the local logs, the entire detection record is lost. Best practice mandates **remote SYSLOG forwarding** and **write-once read-many (WORM) storage** for forensic compliance. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Passive Response The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete data flow from packet capture to forensic archival in a passive IDPS deployment. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Network Traffic
via SPAN or TAP] --> B[IDPS Sensor
Detection Engine] B --> C{Signature or
Anomaly Match?} C -- No --> Z[Drop Event
No Action] C -- Yes --> D[Event Object
Instantiation] D --> E[Severity Classifier
0 to 7] E --> F[Severity Below
Threshold] E --> G[Severity At or
Above Threshold] F --> H1[Local Log File
Silent Recording] G --> H2[Local Log File
With Alert Flag] H2 --> I1[SNMP Trap
to NMS] H2 --> I2[SYSLOG Forward
to SIEM] H2 --> I3[SMTP Email
to SOC Team] H2 --> I4[SMS Gateway
to On-Call Analyst] H2 --> J[PCAP Capture
for Forensic] J --> K[Encrypted Forensic
Storage with WORM] H1 --> L[Compliance
Reporting Engine] H2 --> L L --> M[Executive and
Technical Reports] ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Passive Response Notification Cascade ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant N as Network participant S as IDPS Sensor participant L as Local Log participant Y as SYSLOG Server participant M as NMS via SNMP participant T as SOC Team participant F as Forensic Store N->>S: Mirrored Traffic Stream S->>S: Signature Match Detected S->>L: Write Structured Log Entry S->>Y: Forward via SYSLOG UDP 514 S->>M: Send SNMP Trap UDP 162 S->>T: Trigger Email and SMS Alert S->>F: Archive Full PCAP of Session Note over S,N: Passive Mode: Original
Traffic Flow is Unaffected S-->>N: No Active Intervention ``` ## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Passive Response Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Detecting : Receive Mirrored Traffic Detecting --> Evaluating : Apply Signatures and Anomaly Models Evaluating --> NoAction : No Match Found Evaluating --> Logging : Match Found Below Threshold Evaluating --> Alerting : Match Found At or Above Threshold Logging --> Archiving : Persist to Local and Remote Store Alerting --> Archiving : Persist to Local and Remote Store Alerting --> Notifying : Dispatch to Channels Notifying --> Archiving Archiving --> Reporting : On Schedule Boundary Reporting --> Idle NoAction --> Idle ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define passive response in an IDPS. List any two passive response mechanisms.* **Model Answer:** Passive response is the non-interventionist reaction mode of an IDPS in which the system **observes, logs, and reports** detected intrusion events **without modifying, blocking, or terminating** the offending traffic flow. The original attack traffic is allowed to proceed to its intended target while the IDPS acts as a silent witness. **Two passive response mechanisms:** 1. **Event Logging** — Structured recording of the event to local files or centralized SYSLOG servers. 2. **Alert Notification** — Dispatch of real-time notifications via SNMP traps, e-mail, SMS, or console dashboards to the security operations team. *(Word limit: ~80 words. Award full 3 marks only if both mechanisms are explicitly named with brief justification.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** *Differentiate between inline IDPS deployment and passive (TAP) deployment with respect to response capability.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Inline Deployment | Passive (TAP) Deployment | |---|---|---| | Traffic path | Through the IDPS | Mirrored copy only | | Active response | Possible (block/drop) | Not possible | | Latency added | Yes | None | | Failure impact | Single point of failure | Zero impact (fail-open) | | Response mode | Active + Passive | **Passive only** | In a passive TAP deployment, the IDPS receives a mirrored copy of the traffic via a network TAP or SPAN port. It can therefore only perform **passive responses** (log, alert, report) and **cannot** actively block, drop, or reset the attack traffic. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, End Semester]** **(a)** Explain the architecture and operational workflow of passive response in an IDPS. Include the role of SYSLOG, SNMP traps, and PCAP capture in your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 500 Mbps link is being monitored by a passive IDPS. The average packet size is 600 bytes and the retention requirement is 60 days. Calculate the total storage required in Terabytes for PCAP archival. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The passive response architecture consists of four functional layers: 1. **Detection Layer:** The IDPS sensor (network or host-based) inspects mirrored traffic against its signature database or anomaly profile. Upon a successful match, an event object is created. 2. **Classification Layer:** Each event is assigned a severity score (Critical, High, Medium, Low) based on signature weight, asset value, and confidence factor. Events above the configured threshold trigger notifications; events below are silently logged to reduce noise. 3. **Notification Layer:** The event is dispatched via multiple parallel channels: - **SYSLOG (UDP 514 / TCP 6514 with TLS):** Standardized RFC 5424 logging to centralized SIEM platforms such as Splunk, QRadar, or Elastic Stack. SYSLOG provides severity levels from 0 (Emergency) to 7 (Debug). - **SNMP Traps (UDP 162):** Asynchronous alerts sent to a Network Management System (NMS) such as SolarWinds or PRTG, integrating IDPS events with broader network health monitoring. - **E-mail and SMS:** Human-mediated notification to on-call analysts for high-severity events. - **Console Dashboard:** Real-time operator-station alerts. 4. **Forensic Layer:** Full PCAP files are archived to write-once, read-many (WORM) storage with cryptographic hash chains (SHA-256) for chain-of-custody integrity. This enables post-incident reconstruction, malware analysis, and legal evidence presentation. The defining principle is that the **original traffic flow remains unaffected** — no packet is dropped, modified, or reset by the IDPS. The security team retains full discretion to invoke active countermeasures (e.g., firewall rule updates) based on the passive intelligence gathered. **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - Link rate: $R_{link} = 500\,\text{Mbps} = 5 \times 10^8\,\text{bits/second}$ - Average packet size: $P_{avg} = 600\,\text{bytes} = 4800\,\text{bits}$ - Retention period: $D = 60\,\text{days} = 60 \times 24 \times 3600 = 5184000\,\text{seconds}$ **Step 1 — Compute packets per second (PPS).** $$ PPS = \frac{R_{link}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{5 \times 10^8}{4800} = 104166.67\,\text{packets/second} $$ **Step 2 — Compute total packets in retention window.** $$ N_{total} = PPS \times D = 104166.67 \times 5184000 = 5.4 \times 10^{11}\,\text{packets} $$ **Step 3 — Compute total storage in bytes.** $$ S_{req} = N_{total} \times P_{avg} = 5.4 \times 10^{11} \times 600 = 3.24 \times 10^{14}\,\text{bytes} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Terabytes.** $$ S_{TB} = \frac{3.24 \times 10^{14}}{10^{12}} = 324\,\text{TB} $$ **Answer:** The required forensic storage capacity is **324 Terabytes** for the 60-day retention window. *(7 marks: 2 marks for PPS, 2 marks for total packets, 2 marks for bytes, 1 mark for TB conversion.)* --- ### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary, Question Paper Code: PECCT742]** **(a)** Compare and contrast passive response and active response in an IDPS. Discuss at least four distinguishing parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the role of SIEM in consuming passive IDPS alerts. Explain how log correlation enhances detection capability beyond single-sensor passive responses. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** | Parameter | Passive Response | Active Response | |---|---|---| | **Traffic interference** | None — observation only | Direct intervention (block, drop, reset) | | **Deployment requirement** | TAP or SPAN port sufficient | Must be inline (Bump-in-the-Wire) | | **False positive impact** | Zero collateral damage | May cause self-inflicted DoS | | **Forensic integrity** | Evidence fully preserved | Evidence may be tainted by intervention | | **Containment speed** | Slow (human-mediated, minutes to hours) | Instant (milliseconds) | | **Typical use case** | SOC, compliance, threat hunting | Real-time perimeter defense | | **Latency introduced** | Zero | 50–200 microseconds per packet | **Conclusion:** Passive and active responses are **complementary, not mutually exclusive**. Modern enterprise IDPS deployments use a hybrid model — passive observation for forensics and intelligence, combined with automated active blocking for high-confidence, high-severity threats. **Model Solution for (b):** A **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** system (e.g., Splunk Enterprise Security, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic SIEM) is the central nervous system of a modern SOC. It operates by: 1. **Log Ingestion:** Receiving SYSLOG streams from passive IDPS sensors deployed across the network perimeter, DMZ, and internal segments. Standard ingestion protocols include SYSLOG (RFC 5424), REST APIs, and agent-based forwarders. 2. **Normalization:** Converting heterogeneous log formats (CEF, LEEF, JSON, raw SYSLOG) into a unified event schema with consistent field names, timestamps, and severity ratings. 3. **Correlation:** Applying **cross-sensor correlation rules** to identify multi-stage attack patterns that no single passive sensor could detect. For example: - A **port scan detected by sensor A** + **an IDS alert from sensor B on the same source IP** + **a successful authentication from a geographically distant location** = correlated **advanced persistent threat (APT)** indicator. 4. **Enrichment:** Augmenting IDPS events with threat intelligence feeds (e.g., AlienVault OTX, MISP) to flag known malicious IPs, domains, and file hashes. 5. **Dashboarding and Reporting:** Providing SOC analysts with real-time visualizations, KPI metrics (MTTD, MTTR), and compliance reports (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001). **Enhancement over single-sensor passive response:** A single passive IDPS sensor can only see the traffic segment it monitors. SIEM provides **organizational-wide situational awareness** by correlating events from dozens or hundreds of sensors, firewalls, endpoints, and authentication systems. This transforms isolated passive alerts into a coherent, prioritized threat narrative — enabling analysts to respond decisively rather than react to fragmented noise. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Passive Response Questions:** > > 1. **Conflating Passive and Active Response:** Students frequently write "passive response blocks the packet" — this is factually incorrect and will cost **2–3 marks instantly**. Passive = observation only. Active = intervention. > > 2. **Forgetting to convert units in numerical problems:** The most common error is computing packets/second correctly but forgetting to convert **days to seconds** in the retention calculation. Always show the unit conversion explicitly. > > 3. **Omitting SYSLOG port number or SNMP port number:** KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** for correct port identification. Memorize: SYSLOG = 514, SNMP Trap = 162. > > 4. **Writing vague answers on "list two mechanisms":** A bare list without one-line justification often receives only 1.5/3 marks. Always include a **brief functional description** alongside each mechanism. > > 5. **Confusing SIEM with IDPS:** SIEM is a **consumer** of passive IDPS data, not a passive response mechanism itself. The IDPS generates; the SIEM aggregates. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Passive Response (PECCT742, Module 2)** > > - **Definition:** Non-interventionist IDPS reaction mode — observe, log, report, archive. > - **Four functional layers:** Detection → Classification → Notification → Forensic Archival. > - **Key notification channels:** SYSLOG (port 514 / TLS 6514), SNMP Traps (port 162), SMTP e-mail, SMS, console dashboard. > - **SYSLOG severity scale:** 0 (Emergency) through 7 (Debug) per RFC 5424. > - **Storage formula:** $S_{req} = R \cdot D \cdot P_{avg}$ — always convert units explicitly. > - **Deployment mode:** TAP / SPAN port (mirrored traffic) — cannot block. > - **Forensic best practice:** WORM storage + SHA-256 chain-of-custody hashing. > - **SIEM integration:** SIEM aggregates passive IDPS feeds for cross-sensor correlation. > - **Hybrid model:** Modern deployments combine passive (forensics, intelligence) with active (real-time blocking) for a balanced posture. > - **Failure mode awareness:** Pure local logging is operationally inadequate — always mandate remote forwarding. > - **Comparison essentials:** Passive = no interference, zero latency, fail-open; Active = inline intervention, adds latency, fail-closed. > - **Industry tools:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (sensors) → Splunk, QRadar, Elastic, Sentinel (SIEM consumers).

topics.title: Active Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Active Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Active Response** in an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) is the *proactive, automated, and autonomous* capability of the security infrastructure to execute predefined countermeasures against a detected threat in real time, without awaiting human administrative intervention. Unlike passive response mechanisms that merely log events and generate alerts, active response transforms the IDPS from a *silent observer* into an *active defender* that interrupts, terminates, or modifies the malicious activity at the network, host, or application layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Active Response is the *self-initiated* reaction of an IDPS/IPS to neutralize, contain, or deflect an in-progress attack by modifying the state of the protected system, dropping malicious packets, resetting connections, reconfiguring firewalls, or isolating compromised nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlighter:** In KTU's 2024 PECCT742 syllabus (Module 2), Active Response is treated as the logical evolution of detection — the **"act"** phase of the classic *Detect–Decide–Act* loop, often cited under NIST SP 800-94 Rev. 1 and the Cisco/Sourcefire IPS doctrine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a security guard standing at the gate of a large office complex:

- **Passive Response (IDS only):** The guard sees a suspicious person with a weapon, blows a whistle, writes down the license plate, and calls the police on the radio. The intruder is *still inside the building* while the police drive over.
- **Active Response (IPS):** The guard *physically locks the inner doors, blocks the elevator, and pushes the intruder out through a controlled exit*, all within milliseconds of spotting the weapon.

In network terms, the "elevator lock" is dropping the malicious TCP session, the "controlled exit" is resetting the connection with a TCP RST, and the "inner doors" are dynamically updated Access Control Lists (ACLs) on a firewall. The intruder (attacker) is neutralized *before* the payload (e.g., a buffer overflow exploit) ever reaches the target application.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The following standard metrics govern the effectiveness of any active response subsystem:

- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** The average interval between attack detection and the execution of a countermeasure, typically targeted at **< 1 second** for inline IPS deployments.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Probability that legitimate traffic is misclassified and acted upon, ideally kept below **0.1%** to prevent self-inflicted Denial of Service.
- **Block Rate / Mitigation Throughput:** Measured in packets-per-second (pps) or gigabits-per-second (Gbps) the device can drop without becoming a bottleneck.
- **Collateral Damage Index (CDI):** A KTU-board-favoured qualitative measure of how many legitimate users are affected by an active response action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standards:** The industry benchmark for inline IPS latency is **< 200 microseconds**, and the IETF RFC 4766 (IDMEF) data model uses the `<Response>` XML element to represent active response payloads in distributed IDPS architectures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Active Response Pipeline

The active response mechanism is a deterministic, stateful pipeline composed of the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Detection Trigger:** A sensor (network, host, or hybrid) raises a confidence-scored event (e.g., a Snort signature with `priority: 1;`).
- **Step 2 — Correlation & Scoring:** The event enters a correlation engine (e.g., OSSIM, ArcSight, or a SIEM rule). The cumulative risk score is computed against a *threshold τ*.
- **Step 3 — Decision Arbitration:** If `Score ≥ τ`, the response arbiter selects a *Response Class* from a pre-loaded policy table.
- **Step 4 — Action Dispatch:** The selected countermeasure is encapsulated into a directive (e.g., a RST packet, a BGP update, a SOAP/XML-RPC firewall rule) and sent to the enforcement point.
- **Step 5 — Execution & Verification:** The enforcement point (router ACL, switch port, kernel iptables rule) applies the directive and returns a status code.
- **Step 6 — Logging & Rollback:** The action is journaled, and a *time-to-live (TTL)* is assigned so the rule auto-expires (preventing permanent lockouts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why":** Each step exists to minimise the *window of exposure* between detection and containment. The "How" leverages the inline data path: the sensor is physically *in-line* with traffic, so dropping a packet is as cheap as forwarding it.

### 2.2 The Four Canonical Response Classes (KTU High-Yield)

| Class | KTU Exam Term | Real Action | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Interruption** | *Session Cutoff* | Inject TCP RST, tear down flow | `iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --tcp-flags ALL RST -j DROP` |
| **Termination** | *Process Kill* | End hostile process on host | `kill -9 <PID>` triggered by HIPS agent |
| **Reconfiguration** | *Defensive Rewriting* | Push new ACL / firewall rule | Cisco RADIUS Change-of-Authorization (CoA) |
| **Adaptation** | *Tarpit / Deception* | Slow attacker with tarpit, or feed honeytokens | `nfqueue` + `iptables` to drop into honeypot |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The mathematical backbone of active response decision-making uses a small set of relations. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to keep markdown tables intact.

| Symbol | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Confidence | $C = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Probability that an alert is a true attack | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Risk Score | $S = w_1 C + w_2 I + w_3 A$ | Weighted sum of Confidence, Impact, Asset criticality | dimensionless |
| Response Threshold | $\tau$ | Trigger value; act iff $S \ge \tau$ | dimensionless |
| MTTR | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{\text{act},i} - t_{\text{det},i})$ | Mean detection-to-action time | seconds |
| Block Effectiveness | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Blocked Malicious Packets}}{\text{Total Malicious Packets}}$ | Fraction of attack traffic neutralized | dimensionless (0–1) |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Legitimate traffic wrongly blocked | dimensionless (0–1) |
| Inline Latency Budget | $L_{\text{IPS}} \le 200\ \mu s$ | Maximum acceptable added delay | microseconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used by Cisco Firepower, Suricata, Snort, and Palo Alto Networks WildFire to autotune the threshold τ against the organization's historical traffic profile, ensuring that an automated block never blocks a paying customer.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Field

- **Telecommunications:** ISPs use active response to suppress DDoS reflections (e.g., BGP Flowspec `redirect-to-VRF`).
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS):** Active response isolates a compromised PLC from the SCADA bus via IEC 62443-defined zones.
- **Cloud Workloads:** AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender, and Google Chronicle all implement active response via Lambda/Cloud Functions to quarantine EC2 instances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Response Threshold τ

A cornerstone KTU question is: *"Given the cost of an attack and the cost of a false positive, derive the optimal response threshold τ."*

Let us treat the IDPS as a binary decision-maker. Define the following cost parameters:

- $C_a$ = Cost incurred if an attack is **missed** (false negative). Typical value: **₹10,00,000** in a banking environment.
- $C_f$ = Cost incurred if legitimate traffic is **wrongly blocked** (false positive). Typical value: **₹1,000** in a banking environment.
- $P(A)$ = A priori probability that a packet belongs to an attack stream.
- $P(L)$ = A priori probability that a packet is legitimate, with $P(A) + P(L) = 1$.

The **expected cost of action** (blocking) is:

$$
E[\text{Block}] = C_f \cdot P(\text{Block} \mid \text{Legitimate}) = C_f \cdot P(L) \cdot (1 - C)
$$

The **expected cost of inaction** (allowing) is:

$$
E[\text{Allow}] = C_a \cdot P(\text{Allow} \mid \text{Attack}) = C_a \cdot P(A) \cdot (1 - \pi)
$$

where $\pi$ is the posterior probability that the packet is malicious, given the sensor evidence.

Active response should fire when the cost of action is *less than or equal to* the cost of inaction. Setting $E[\text{Block}] \le E[\text{Allow}]$:

$$
C_f \cdot P(L) \cdot (1 - C) \le C_a \cdot P(A) \cdot (1 - \pi)
$$

Solving for the *critical confidence* $\pi^*$ at which we are indifferent:

$$
\pi^* = 1 - \dfrac{C_f \cdot P(L) \cdot (1 - C)}{C_a \cdot P(A)}
$$

Since the *Risk Score* $S$ in our cheat sheet is monotonically related to the posterior $\pi$, the optimal response threshold is therefore:

$$
\tau^* = \pi^* \cdot S_{\max}
$$

**Interpretation:** When the cost of a missed attack is high (large $C_a$) and the cost of a false positive is low (small $C_f$), τ* drops, meaning the system becomes *more aggressive* in active response. The opposite is true in low-stakes environments.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example (Numerical)

Suppose $C_a = 1{,}00{,}000$, $C_f = 500$, $P(A) = 0.001$, $P(L) = 0.999$, $C = 0.85$.

\begin{aligned}
\pi^* &= 1 - \dfrac{500 \cdot 0.999 \cdot (1 - 0.85)}{1{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.001} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{500 \cdot 0.999 \cdot 0.15}{100} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{74.925}{100} \\
&= 1 - 0.74925 \\
&= 0.25075
\end{aligned}

Thus the IPS should trigger an active response whenever its posterior confidence in an attack exceeds **25.075%**. This is a deliberately *low* threshold — appropriate for a high-value banking environment where the cost asymmetry $C_a / C_f = 200$ strongly favours acting.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Active vs Passive Response

| Dimension | Passive Response (IDS) | Active Response (IPS) | KTU Board Emphasis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Manual / human review | Automated, policy-driven | High (Module 2 core) |
| Latency to Block | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds | High |
| Risk of Self-DoS | Negligible | Moderate–High | Very High |
| Placement | Out-of-band (TAP) | Inline (bump-in-the-wire) | Medium |
| Evidence Integrity | Preserved (read-only) | Modified (writes to flow) | Medium |
| Regulatory Acceptance | Universal | Restricted in some jurisdictions | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** A classic 14-mark KTU question is *"Compare IDS and IPS in terms of placement, response time, and risk of self-inflicted DoS."* The matrix above is your ready-made answer scaffold.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Active Response Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inbound Packet] --> B{Inline Sensor Inspection}
    B -- Normal --> C[Forward to Target]
    B -- Suspicious --> D[Correlation Engine]
    D --> E[Compute Risk Score S]
    E --> F{S greater than or equal to tau}
    F -- No --> G[Log Only Passive Alert]
    F -- Yes --> H[Select Response Class]
    H --> I[Interruption RST]
    H --> J[Reconfiguration ACL Push]
    H --> K[Adaptation Tarpit or Honeypot]
    H --> L[Termination Process Kill]
    I --> M[Apply TTL Auto Expire]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Journal to SIEM]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Active IDPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SEN[Sensor Plane]
        S1[Network Tap]
        S2[Host Agent]
        S3[Wireless Probe]
    end

    subgraph ANA[Analytics Plane]
        A1[Signature Engine]
        A2[Anomaly Engine]
        A3[Stateful Protocol Decoder]
    end

    subgraph DEC[Decision Plane]
        D1[Correlation Engine]
        D2[Policy Arbiter]
        D3[Threshold tau]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Action Plane]
        X1[Inline Dropper]
        X2[Firewall CoA Client]
        X3[Host EDR Agent]
        X4[SDN Controller API]
    end

    S1 --> A1
    S2 --> A1
    S3 --> A2
    A1 --> D1
    A2 --> D1
    A3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D2 --> X1
    D2 --> X2
    D2 --> X3
    D2 --> X4
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Active Response to a TCP SYN Flood

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Att as Attacker
    participant Net as Inline IPS
    participant FW as Firewall
    participant SIEM as SIEM Log
    Att->>Net: SYN burst 1000 pps
    Net->>Net: Detect anomaly score 0.92
    Net->>FW: Push ACL drop src IP for 300 s
    FW-->>Net: ACK CoA 200
    Net->>Att: Subsequent packets DROPPED
    Net->>SIEM: Journal response action
    SIEM-->>Net: Persist
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs above are pure alphanumeric (`S1`, `A1`, `D1`, `X1`, `Att`) to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard. No reserved keywords are used as node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Q1.** Define *Active Response* in an Intrusion Prevention System. How does it differ from a passive response?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Active response is the *automated, real-time* capability of an IPS to execute countermeasures against a detected threat without waiting for human intervention, by directly modifying the state of the protected system (e.g., dropping packets, resetting TCP sessions, pushing firewall rules, or killing hostile processes). It differs from a passive response in that a passive IDS merely logs events and generates alerts to a console or SIEM, leaving the actual blocking action to a human operator. In short, active response **acts**, while passive response **alerts**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for difference, 1 for example.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, S3 B.Tech CSE]**
> **Q2.** List any *three* classes of active response actions and give a one-line example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Interruption** — Inject a TCP RST to terminate a malicious session. *(1 mark)*
- **Reconfiguration** — Push a new ACL rule to the perimeter firewall to block the source IP. *(1 mark)*
- **Adaptation** — Redirect the attacker's traffic into a tarpit or honeypot to slow and study them. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **Q3 (a).** With a neat block diagram, explain the *operational architecture* of an active IDPS, clearly distinguishing the Sensor, Analytics, Decision, and Action planes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b).** Derive the optimal response threshold τ\* in terms of the cost of a missed attack $C_a$, the cost of a false positive $C_f$, and the prior probabilities $P(A)$ and $P(L)$. Apply your result to a scenario where $C_a = 50{,}000$, $C_f = 200$, $P(A) = 0.005$, $P(L) = 0.995$, $C = 0.80$, and comment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q3 (a) [7 marks]

The four-plane architecture (refer to the diagram in Section 4.2) is:

- **Sensor Plane (2 marks):** Comprises network TAPs, SPAN ports, and host agents that capture traffic in a stateless or stateful manner and forward it to the analytics layer. Placement may be *inline* (IPS) or *passive* (IDS).
- **Analytics Plane (2 marks):** Houses the signature engine, anomaly engine, and protocol decoders that produce a confidence-scored event from raw packets.
- **Decision Plane (2 marks):** The correlation engine aggregates multiple events; the policy arbiter consults a threshold τ and a rulebook to choose a *Response Class* (interruption, termination, reconfiguration, adaptation).
- **Action Plane (1 mark):** The enforcement point — dropper, firewall CoA client, host EDR, or SDN controller — applies the chosen countermeasure and returns a status code.

#### Model Solution — Q3 (b) [7 marks]

From the derivation in Section 3.1:

\begin{aligned}
\pi^* &= 1 - \dfrac{C_f \cdot P(L) \cdot (1 - C)}{C_a \cdot P(A)}
\end{aligned}

Substituting the given values:

\begin{aligned}
\pi^* &= 1 - \dfrac{200 \cdot 0.995 \cdot (1 - 0.80)}{50{,}000 \cdot 0.005} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{200 \cdot 0.995 \cdot 0.20}{250} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{39.8}{250} \\
&= 1 - 0.1592 \\
&= 0.8408
\end{aligned}

**Comment (1 mark):** The threshold is high (~84%), meaning the IPS should act *only* when it is very confident. The cost ratio $C_a / C_f = 250$ is large, but the prior attack rate $P(A) = 0.005$ is also non-trivial, so the system sits in a balanced regime.

---

> **Q4 (a).** Explain the *four canonical response classes* (interruption, termination, reconfiguration, adaptation) with one real-world IPS example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q4 (b).** Discuss the *risks* of active response, especially the *self-inflicted Denial of Service* scenario. Suggest two engineering safeguards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q4 (a) [7 marks]

- **Interruption (2 marks):** The IPS cuts the malicious session mid-flight. Example: Suricata rule with `drop:` action emitting a TCP RST/FIN to both endpoints.
- **Termination (1 mark):** The host-resident agent kills the offending process. Example: a HIPS agent invoking `taskkill /F /PID <pid>` on a Windows endpoint that initiated a reverse shell.
- **Reconfiguration (2 marks):** A new rule is dynamically pushed to a network device. Example: Cisco Firepower sending a RADIUS Change-of-Authorization (CoA) to disable a compromised switchport.
- **Adaptation (2 marks):** Traffic is rerouted or deceived. Example: `iptables` `NFQUEUE` action that hands packets to a `glutton` tarpit listener, slowing the attacker's connection.

#### Model Solution — Q4 (b) [7 marks]

Risks (3 marks):
- **Self-inflicted DoS:** A false positive blocks a legitimate user or a critical service.
- **Amplification of attacker impact:** A reactive rule (e.g., blackhole route) can be weaponised by an attacker who spoofs a victim's IP.
- **State desynchronisation:** Cutting a TCP session abruptly can leave application-layer transactions in a half-committed state (e.g., a database row lock).

Engineering safeguards (4 marks, 2 each):
- **TTL on all reactive rules:** Every dynamically created ACL entry must auto-expire (typical 300 s) so a rule cannot lock out a user permanently.
- **Human-in-the-loop for high-impact actions:** Kill-process and blackhole actions should require a secondary confirmation from a SOC analyst before execution; only *drop* and *reset* actions are fully automated.
- *(Bonus 1 mark)*: A *dry-run / shadow mode* in which the IPS logs what it *would* have done, allowing tuning before promotion to inline enforcement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by *conflating* active response with honeypots. A honeypot is a **deception** mechanism, not a response. Active response is a **reaction** to a confirmed attack on a real asset. Read the question stem carefully — if it says "respond to detected threat", the answer is IPS, not honeypot.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Active Response = automatic countermeasure execution** by an inline IPS without human action.
- The four canonical response classes are **Interruption, Termination, Reconfiguration, Adaptation** — memorise in this order.
- The decision is governed by a **threshold τ** on a risk score $S$ that fuses confidence, impact, and asset criticality.
- The optimal threshold balances $C_a$ (cost of miss) and $C_f$ (cost of false positive); high $C_a / C_f$ ⇒ aggressive (low) τ.
- MTTR target for production IPS: **< 1 second**; inline latency budget: **< 200 μs**.
- Always assign a **TTL / auto-expiry** to dynamic rules to prevent permanent self-lockout.
- Active response is **NOT** a honeypot — it acts on a real attack against a real asset.
- Standards: **NIST SP 800-94 Rev. 1**, **IETF RFC 4766 (IDMEF)**, **RFC 8212 (BGP flow-spec)**.
- Inline placement is what makes active response possible; out-of-band placement limits the system to passive alerting.
- Active response is restricted in some regulated environments (e.g., healthcare HIPAA, certain ICS zones) — know the *legal* dimension.
- High-yield acronyms: **IPS, IDPS, SIEM, SOAR, EDR, CoA, NFQUEUE, IDMEF, MTTR, FPR**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analyzing Your IDS Design and Investment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analyzing Your IDS Design and Investment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IDS Design and Investment Analysis** is a systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation framework used to justify, optimize, and validate the financial, technical, and operational decisions associated with deploying an Intrusion Detection (and Prevention) System within an enterprise architecture. As per KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education standards, this analysis fuses **Risk Management Theory**, **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)**, **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) modeling**, and **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** calculation to ensure the chosen IDS architecture (NIDS, HIDS, Hybrid, or Distributed) optimally balances detection efficacy against resource consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a high-weightage concept under **Module 2 – IDS Design Principles and Architecture**, mapping directly to **CO2 (Design appropriate IDS architecture for given network scenarios)** and **CO5 (Evaluate the economic and operational feasibility of IDS deployment)**.

The analysis is structured along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Dimension | Core Question Answered |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Technical Axis** | Performance, Scalability, False-Positive Rate | *Does the IDS design meet the throughput, latency, and accuracy requirements?* |
| **Financial Axis** | CAPEX, OPEX, TCO, ROSI, Payback Period | *Is the security investment economically justified and sustainable?* |
| **Operational Axis** | Staffing, Maintenance, Lifecycle, Compliance | *Can the organization sustain the IDS in production over its lifecycle?* |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of deploying an IDS exactly like **installing a sophisticated home security system before a long vacation**.

- **The IDS Sensors** are your *motion detectors, CCTV cameras, and door alarms*.
- **The Investment Analysis** is the *budget meeting* where you ask: *"If I spend ₹50,000 on cameras, will it prevent a burglary that could cost me ₹10,00,000 in valuables?"*
- **False Positives** are the *alarms that ring when a stray cat walks by* — annoying, but tolerable.
- **False Negatives** are the *burglar who sneaks past your camera's blind spot* — catastrophic, and the real reason you installed the system.
- **The Design** is *where to place each camera* — too few cameras and you have blind spots; too many and you drown in footage you cannot review.

The analysis essentially answers: **"Am I spending the right amount, in the right places, to reduce my risk to an acceptable level?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** An IDS is *not* justified merely by its feature list. It is justified by the **mathematically demonstrable reduction in expected annual loss (ALE)** it provides, relative to its annualized cost.

## 1.3 Key Performance and Economic Metrics (Industry-Standard Constants)

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Industry benchmark target is **< 1 hour** for critical assets.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Industry benchmark target is **< 15 minutes** for active incidents.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR) Target:** **< 5%** for production-grade deployments.
- **ROSI Acceptance Threshold:** A security investment is considered viable if **ROSI ≥ 0** (i.e., non-negative return).
- **Discount Rate (r) for NPV:** Typical enterprise value lies between **8% – 12%** annually.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Pillar Analytical Framework

Analyzing an IDS design and investment is decomposed into three logical pillars. Each pillar answers a distinct question that an organization *must* answer before procurement sign-off.

### Pillar 1 — Risk Quantification (The "Threat Side")
Before spending a single rupee, you must quantify the *expected loss* if no IDS were deployed. This is governed by classical **NIST SP 800-30** risk methodology.

1. **Identify the Asset** → Database server, intellectual property, customer PII.
2. **Determine Asset Value (AV)** → Replacement cost + reputational cost + regulatory fine.
3. **Determine Exposure Factor (EF)** → Percentage of asset value lost *per single incident* (e.g., 60% loss of customer DB confidentiality).
4. **Estimate Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** → Expected number of incidents per year *without* the control.
5. **Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** → ALE = SLE × ARO, where SLE = AV × EF.

### Pillar 2 — Cost Quantification (The "Investment Side")
The investment is not the sticker price. It is the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** spread across the system lifecycle (typically 3–5 years).

1. **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX):** Hardware sensors, appliances, dedicated servers, initial licenses.
2. **Operational Expenditure (OPEX):** Power, cooling, rack space, subscription renewals.
3. **Human OPEX:** Tier-1/Tier-2/Tier-3 SOC analyst salaries, training, certification (e.g., GCIA, GCIH).
4. **Hidden/Indirect Costs:** Network downtime during deployment, performance degradation on inline IPS, opportunity cost of analyst time spent on false positives.

### Pillar 3 — Benefit Quantification (The "Justification Side")
Benefits are expressed as the *reduction in ALE* brought about by the IDS, minus the cost of running it.

1. **ALE_after** → Expected annual loss *with* the IDS deployed.
2. **Annual Cost of the Safeguard (ACS)** → Annualized TCO.
3. **Annual Savings (AS)** → (ALE_before − ALE_after) − ACS.
4. **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** → Normalized metric to compare competing IDS options.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization Table for KTU Board Exam.** These are the *only* equations you need to solve any numerical problem in Module 2 on this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Single Loss Expectancy | $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$ | Currency (₹ / $) | Loss incurred in *one* successful attack. |
| 2 | Annual Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Currency / year | Expected yearly loss *without* control. |
| 3 | Annual Cost of Safeguard | $\text{ACS} = \frac{\text{Total Lifecycle Cost}}{\text{Lifecycle Years}}$ | Currency / year | Annualized investment cost. |
| 4 | Annual Savings | $\text{AS} = (\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS}$ | Currency / year | Net yearly benefit. |
| 5 | Return on Security Investment | $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Normalized profitability of the IDS. |
| 6 | Payback Period | $\text{PP} = \frac{\text{Initial CAPEX}}{\text{AS}}$ | Years | Time to recover the initial outlay. |
| 7 | Net Present Value (multi-year) | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{AS}_t}{(1+r)^t} - \text{Initial CAPEX}$ | Currency | Discounted multi-year value. |
| 8 | False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | Percentage | Operational noise metric. |
| 9 | Detection Rate (Recall) | $\text{DR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Percentage | Coverage metric. |
| 10 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{DR} \cdot \text{PPV}}{\text{DR} + \text{PPV}}$ | Percentage | Balanced accuracy metric. |

**Boundary Conditions & Validity:**
- $0 \le \text{EF} \le 1$
- $\text{ARO} \ge 0$ (integer expected in practice)
- **ROSI Acceptance Rule:** Investment is justified if and only if $\text{ROSI} \ge 0$.
- **PP Acceptance Rule:** Investment is preferred if $\text{PP} \le \text{Acceptable Payback Threshold}$ (often 3 years).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This analysis is the **lingua franca** of enterprise security architecture. In practice, it is used by:
- **CISOs** to defend the security budget to the Board of Directors.
- **Procurement teams** to perform vendor comparison (Snort vs. Suricata vs. Cisco Firepower).
- **Auditors (ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, SOC 2)** to verify that controls are *risk-driven*, not arbitrarily chosen.
- **Insurance underwriters** to determine cyber-insurance premiums (lower ALE ⇒ lower premium).
- **KTU Examiners** to test a student's ability to apply quantitative reasoning to security architecture problems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Derivation — Full ROSI & Payback Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive step-by-step solution.** No algebraic step is skipped.

### **Problem Statement (KTU Board Exam Style):**

> *A KTU-affiliated engineering college is evaluating deployment of a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) to protect its central academic database server. The asset is valued at ₹50,00,000. Historical industry data indicates that, in the absence of an IDS, a successful SQL injection or data exfiltration incident would compromise approximately 60% of the asset's value per occurrence. The estimated frequency of such an attack is 2 incidents per year. The proposed NIDS (with associated SOC staffing) is expected to reduce the Exposure Factor to 15% per incident and lower the Annualized Rate of Occurrence to 0.5 incidents per year. The total lifecycle cost of the NIDS over 5 years is ₹18,00,000.*
>
> *Calculate: (a) ALE before and after IDS deployment. (b) Annual Cost of the Safeguard (ACS). (c) Annual Savings (AS). (d) Return on Security Investment (ROSI). (e) Payback Period.*

---

### **Step 1 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy BEFORE deployment**

Using the formula $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$:

- Asset Value (AV) = ₹50,00,000
- Exposure Factor before (EF_before) = 60% = 0.60

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{before}} \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
&= 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ per incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each successful breach would directly inflict a ₹30 lakh loss on the college.

---

### **Step 2 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy BEFORE deployment**

Using the formula $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$:

- Annualized Rate of Occurrence before (ARO_before) = 2 incidents/year

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} \\
&= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 2 \\
&= 60{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Without the IDS, the college stands to lose ₹60 lakh per year on average.

---

### **Step 3 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy AFTER deployment**

- Exposure Factor after (EF_after) = 15% = 0.15

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{after}} \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 \\
&= 7{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹ per incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Residual damage per breach drops to ₹7.5 lakh thanks to early detection and containment.

---

### **Step 4 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy AFTER deployment**

- Annualized Rate of Occurrence after (ARO_after) = 0.5 incidents/year

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}} \\
&= 7{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5 \\
&= 3{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With the IDS, the expected annual loss falls to ₹3.75 lakh.

---

### **Step 5 — Compute Annual Cost of the Safeguard (ACS)**

- Total Lifecycle Cost = ₹18,00,000
- Lifecycle = 5 years

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACS} &= \frac{\text{Total Lifecycle Cost}}{\text{Lifecycle Years}} \\
&= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{5} \\
&= 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The NIDS costs ₹3.6 lakh per year to own and operate.

---

### **Step 6 — Compute Annual Savings (AS)**

Using $\text{AS} = (\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}}) - \text{ACS}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AS} &= (60{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}000) - 3{,}60{,}000 \\
&= 56{,}25{,}000 - 3{,}60{,}000 \\
&= 52{,}65{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The college saves ₹52.65 lakh net per year by deploying the NIDS.

---

### **Step 7 — Compute Return on Security Investment (ROSI)**

Using $\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{60{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}000 - 3{,}60{,}000}{3{,}60{,}000} \\
&= \frac{52{,}65{,}000}{3{,}60{,}000} \\
&= 14.625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every ₹1 spent on the NIDS returns ₹14.625 in net risk reduction. Investment is *strongly justified*.

---

### **Step 8 — Compute Payback Period (PP)**

Initial CAPEX is not directly given, but for a single-year equivalent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PP} &= \frac{\text{Initial CAPEX}}{\text{AS}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the lifecycle cost is uniform, the Initial CAPEX is taken as the total lifecycle cost in a simplified single-payment model: ₹18,00,000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PP} &= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{52{,}65{,}000} \\
&\approx 0.342 \text{ years} \\
&\approx 4.1 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The college recovers its entire investment in approximately **4 months** — a phenomenal payback window.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analysis Matrix — Two Competing IDS Designs

The investment analysis is rarely single-option. Organizations compare designs side-by-side. The following matrix demonstrates the analytical method.

| Evaluation Parameter | **Option A: Open-Source Snort (NIDS, Signature)** | **Option B: Cisco Firepower 9300 (Commercial NIDS+NGIPS)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initial CAPEX** | ₹4,00,000 (commodity server) | ₹35,00,000 (appliance + licenses) |
| **Annual OPEX (Licenses/Subscription)** | ₹0 | ₹6,00,000 (Snort/SIG update + support) |
| **Annual Human OPEX** | ₹18,00,000 (2× L2 analysts @ ₹9L) | ₹9,00,000 (1× L2 analyst + vendor support) |
| **Total 5-Year TCO** | ₹94,00,000 | ₹65,00,000 |
| **ACS (Annualized)** | ₹18,80,000 | ₹13,00,000 |
| **ALE_before (identical)** | ₹60,00,000 | ₹60,00,000 |
| **ALE_after (Snort/FP 9300)** | ₹12,00,000 (signature lag) | ₹3,75,000 (advanced threat intel) |
| **Annual Savings (AS)** | ₹29,20,000 | ₹43,25,000 |
| **ROSI** | 1.55 | 3.33 |
| **Payback Period** | ~ 1.6 years | ~ 8.7 months |
| **FPR** | ~ 8% (manual tuning) | < 2% (ML-based) |
| **MTTD** | ~ 4 hours | ~ 30 minutes |
| **Recommended for** | Budget-constrained academic labs | Enterprise with compliance mandates |

**Analytical Insight:** Although Option A has near-zero initial CAPEX, its long-term TCO is *higher* due to high ongoing human analyst costs. **Option B wins on every economic and operational axis** — a non-obvious finding that purely economic analysis reveals.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IDS Investment Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    H --> I{Decision: ROSI greater than 0?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Decision: Payback less than Threshold?]
    I -->|No| K[Reject Investment - Not Viable]
    J -->|Yes| L[Approve and Procure IDS]
    J -->|No| M[Re-negotiate Cost or Seek Alternative]
    L --> N[Continuous Monitoring and Annual Re-analysis]
    M --> F
    N --> C
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The flow begins with a proposal, moves through quantification, runs comparative economic analysis, applies a two-stage gate (ROSI + Payback), and crucially loops back for **annual re-analysis** because ARO and AV change year-over-year.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Return on Security Investment (ROSI) and state the decision rule for accepting a security investment based on its value.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Model Answer:**
Return on Security Investment (ROSI) is a normalized, dimensionless metric used to quantify the economic efficiency of a security control such as an IDS. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of the **net annual benefit** (reduction in expected loss minus the cost of the safeguard) to the **annual cost of the safeguard (ACS)**.

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}}
$$

**Decision Rule:** A security investment is considered *economically justified* if and only if **ROSI ≥ 0**. A negative ROSI indicates that the cost of the control exceeds the risk reduction it provides, and the investment should be rejected or re-scoped. A ROSI of 0 indicates a break-even scenario.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between CAPEX, OPEX, and TCO in the context of an IDS deployment.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Full Form | Nature | IDS Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CAPEX** | Capital Expenditure | One-time, upfront | Hardware sensors, initial license, dedicated servers |
| **OPEX** | Operational Expenditure | Recurring, ongoing | Power, cooling, signature updates, subscription renewals |
