| **TCO** | Total Cost of Ownership | Sum across lifecycle | CAPEX + Σ(OPEX over n years) + Human OPEX + Indirect costs |

**Key Distinction:** TCO is the *holistic* metric used for long-term decision-making, whereas CAPEX alone often misleads procurement teams into selecting the cheapest sticker-price option, which may carry hidden long-term OPEX burdens (as demonstrated in the comparative matrix in Section 3.2).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 2, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain the **three-pillar framework** for analyzing an IDS design and investment. For each pillar, list at least three specific metrics or data inputs that must be collected. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A regional bank is evaluating two NIDS options:
> - **Option X:** Open-source Zeek with custom signatures, total 5-year cost ₹22,00,000, expected to reduce ALE from ₹80,00,000/year to ₹15,00,000/year.
> - **Option Y:** Commercial Darktrace with 5-year cost ₹40,00,000, expected to reduce ALE from ₹80,00,000/year to ₹6,00,000/year.
>
> Calculate the **Annual Cost of Safeguard (ACS)**, **Annual Savings (AS)**, **ROSI**, and **Payback Period** for each option. Recommend the better option with justification. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The three-pillar framework consists of:

**Pillar 1 — Risk Quantification:** Identifies *what* we are protecting and *how much* it could cost us.
- *Inputs:* Asset Value (AV), Exposure Factor (EF), Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO), threat intelligence feeds, historical incident logs.

**Pillar 2 — Cost Quantification:** Identifies *what it costs* to deploy and operate the IDS over its lifecycle.
- *Inputs:* CAPEX (hardware, software), OPEX (subscription, power, cooling), Human OPEX (SOC salaries, training), Indirect costs (downtime, performance hit).

**Pillar 3 — Benefit Quantification:** Translates technical risk reduction into *monetary terms* to justify the spend.
- *Inputs:* ALE_before, ALE_after, Annual Savings (AS), ROSI, Payback Period (PP), NPV over lifecycle.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**For Option X (Zeek):**

- ACS_X = 22,00,000 / 5 = ₹4,40,000 per year
- AS_X = (80,00,000 − 15,00,000) − 4,40,000 = 65,00,000 − 4,40,000 = ₹60,60,000 per year
- ROSI_X = 60,60,000 / 4,40,000 = 13.77
- PP_X = 22,00,000 / 60,60,000 ≈ 0.363 years ≈ 4.36 months

**For Option Y (Darktrace):**

- ACS_Y = 40,00,000 / 5 = ₹8,00,000 per year
- AS_Y = (80,00,000 − 6,00,000) − 8,00,000 = 74,00,000 − 8,00,000 = ₹66,00,000 per year
- ROSI_Y = 66,00,000 / 8,00,000 = 8.25
- PP_Y = 40,00,000 / 66,00,000 ≈ 0.606 years ≈ 7.27 months

**Recommendation:** **Option Y (Darktrace) is the better choice.** Although Option X yields a higher *normalized* ROSI (13.77 vs 8.25), Option Y provides **higher absolute Annual Savings (₹66L vs ₹60.6L)** and a **lower residual risk** (ALE_after of ₹6L vs ₹15L). For a bank operating under strict regulatory mandates, the lower residual ALE and advanced ML-based detection outweigh the marginal ROSI difference.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Discuss the **role of False Positive Rate (FPR)** and **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** as non-monetary metrics in IDS investment analysis. Why must these be considered alongside ROSI? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A healthcare startup holds Protected Health Information (PHI) of 1,00,000 patients. The asset value is ₹2,00,00,000. Historical data shows 1 breach per year, with an exposure factor of 80%. A proposed HIDS has a 5-year TCO of ₹50,00,000, and is expected to reduce the ARO to 0.2 per year and the EF to 10%. Compute: **(i)** SLE_before, **(ii)** ALE_before, **(iii)** ALE_after, **(iv)** ACS, **(v)** ROSI. State with reason whether the investment should be approved. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

FPR and MTTD are **operational and technical metrics** that ROSI alone cannot capture. They are critical because:

- **FPR** measures *analyst fatigue and alert trust*. A high FPR (>10%) leads to alert desensitization, causing real attacks to be missed — a catastrophic false-negative consequence. FPR is computed as FP / (FP + TN) and should ideally remain below 5%.
- **MTTD** measures *detection latency*. A ROSI-positive IDS with a 24-hour MTTD is operationally inferior to a ROSI-neutral IDS with a 15-minute MTTD, because faster detection enables containment before exfiltration completes.
- These metrics also feed into **ALE_after** estimation, so they indirectly affect ROSI. An IDS with poor FPR and MTTD *over-estimates* its ALE_after and yields an artificially inflated ROSI — a common pitfall in vendor pitches.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) SLE_before:**
$$
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**(ii) ALE_before:**
$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \times 1 = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**(iii) ALE_after:**
- SLE_after = 2,00,00,000 × 0.10 = ₹20,00,000
- ALE_after = 20,00,000 × 0.2 = ₹4,00,000 per year

**(iv) ACS:**
$$
\text{ACS} = 50{,}00{,}000 / 5 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**(v) ROSI:**
$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{(1{,}60{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000) - 10{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = \frac{1{,}46{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 14.6
$$

**Decision:** **Approve the investment.** ROSI = 14.6 (≫ 0) and the absolute ALE reduction is ₹1.56 crore/year, far exceeding the ₹10 lakh annual cost. For PHI under HIPAA-equivalent regulations, the residual ALE of ₹4L is also operationally acceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide TCO by lifecycle years** when computing ACS — a single error that invalidates the entire 14-mark answer.
> 2. **Confusing ARO_before and ARO_after** — many students reuse the same ARO on both sides, which inflates the apparent benefit.
> 3. **Reporting ROSI as a percentage** — ROSI is a *ratio* (e.g., 14.6), not 1460%.
> 4. **Ignoring the Payback Period** — the question often *implicitly* tests whether you know that a higher ROSI with longer PP may be less attractive than a lower ROSI with shorter PP.
> 5. **Not stating the decision rule** — always end with a clear *"Investment is approved/rejected because ROSI ≥/≤ 0"*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **IDS Investment Analysis** is the convergence of *risk quantification*, *cost modelling*, and *benefit justification* — never one alone.
- [x] The **ALE = SLE × ARO** equation is the *single most important formula* in this module; commit it to memory verbatim.
- [x] **ROSI is a ratio, not a percentage** — it tells you how many rupees of net benefit you receive per rupee of security spend.
- [x] **TCO is the only honest cost metric** — never justify a decision on CAPEX alone.
- [x] **Decision Rule for ROSI:** Invest if ROSI ≥ 0; reject if ROSI < 0.
- [x] **Decision Rule for Payback Period:** Accept if PP ≤ organizational threshold (typically 3 years).
- [x] **FPR and MTTD** are non-monetary metrics that *must* be reported alongside ROSI for a complete analysis.
- [x] An IDS is *not* justified by its feature count; it is justified by the **mathematically demonstrable reduction in ALE**.
- [x] For KTU board exams, always **show units** (₹/year, years) and **state the decision rule explicitly** in your conclusion.
- [x] A **higher ROSI does not always mean a better choice** — consider absolute Annual Savings, Payback Period, residual ALE, and operational metrics (FPR, MTTD) in the final recommendation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: False Positives versus False Negatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** False Positives versus False Negatives ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of an **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** or **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)**, every observed network event, packet, or behavioural action must be classified into one of two categories: **malicious (intrusion)** or **benign (legitimate)**. Because no detector is perfect, the classification produces four mutually exclusive outcomes. Two of these are errors that govern the practical utility of any IDS: - **False Positive (FP) — Type I Error:** A *legitimate* activity is incorrectly classified as an intrusion (or attack) by the IDS. The system raises an alarm when no actual attack is occurring. - **False Negative (FN) — Type II Error:** A *genuine* intrusion or attack is incorrectly classified as legitimate activity. The system fails to raise an alarm when a real attack is in progress. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A **False Positive** is a "false alarm" — the IDS signals an attack that did not occur. A **False Negative** is a "missed detection" — a real attack slips past the IDS undetected. False negatives are universally considered more dangerous than false positives because they imply a breach that the security infrastructure is blind to. The complete **confusion matrix** that frames these concepts is: | Predicted → / Actual ↓ | Predicted: Attack | Predicted: Benign | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Actual: Attack** | True Positive (TP) | **False Negative (FN)** | | **Actual: Benign** | **False Positive (FP)** | True Negative (TN) | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Airport Security Metal Detector:** > Imagine a metal detector at an airport security checkpoint. Every passenger walks through. > - **True Positive:** A passenger carrying a weapon is detected. The alarm rings. ✔ > - **True Negative:** A passenger with no weapon walks through silently. ✔ > - **False Positive:** A passenger with a belt buckle or watch triggers the alarm. The guard does a manual pat-down and clears them. ✘ *(Type I Error — false alarm)* > - **False Negative:** A clever smuggler hides a ceramic knife in a shoe and walks through. No alarm. The knife reaches the aircraft. ✘ *(Type II Error — missed detection)* > > The airport can either **lower the detector's sensitivity** (fewer false alarms, but more knives get through) or **raise the sensitivity** (catch every knife, but pat down every grandmother with a hip implant). This is precisely the **trade-off** an IDS engineer faces daily. ### 1.3 Core Operational Metrics The two error types are operationalised through the following standard metrics, all of which are examinable: - **Detection Rate (DR) / True Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall / Sensitivity:** $$\text{DR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ - **False Positive Rate (FPR) / False Alarm Rate:** $$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ - **Precision / Positive Predictive Value (PPV):** $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ - **Accuracy:** $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$ - **F1-Score (harmonic mean of Precision and Recall):** $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Base-Rate Fallacy:** In a typical enterprise network carrying millions of packets per second, **>99.9% of traffic is benign**. Even an IDS with a tiny 0.1% false positive rate will generate *thousands* of false alarms per hour — overwhelming the Security Operations Center (SOC). Conversely, missing even 0.01% of real attacks can be catastrophic. This asymmetry drives the entire FP-vs-FN discussion in IDS design. ### 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters in KTU 2024 In the 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, Module 2 of PECCT742 evaluates Course Outcomes related to the *evaluation, tuning, and limitation analysis* of IDSs. The FP/FN dichotomy is the foundational concept upon which all advanced evaluation topics (ROC curves, threshold tuning, cost-sensitive detection) are built. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the FP–FN Trade-off The operational reality of an IDS can be broken down into the following analytical steps: - **Step 1 — Define the Detection Threshold:** Every classifier (signature-based, anomaly-based, or hybrid) operates on an internal *threshold τ*. If the computed *maliciousness score* of an event exceeds τ, an alarm is raised. - **Step 2 — Apply the Threshold to Live Traffic:** Each event is scored and compared against τ, producing a binary verdict (alarm / no alarm). - **Step 3 — Compare Verdict to Ground Truth:** Each verdict is mapped to one of the four cells of the confusion matrix. - **Step 4 — Aggregate Counts:** The number of TP, FP, TN, and FN events are accumulated over the evaluation window. - **Step 5 — Compute Performance Metrics:** The aggregated counts feed into detection rate, false positive rate, precision, F1-score, etc. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind the Trade-off:** Lowering τ makes the IDS *more sensitive* → catches more real attacks (↑TP) but also flags more benign events (↑FP). Raising τ makes the IDS *more conservative* → fewer false alarms (↓FP) but more attacks slip through (↑FN). **No single threshold simultaneously minimises both errors.** This is the **FP–FN trade-off curve**, visualised as the ROC curve. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE — memorise the metric definitions and the relationships between them.** | # | Metric Name | Formula | Unit / Range | KTU Significance | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | | 1 | Detection Rate (TPR) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better; measures attack coverage. | | 2 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Lower is better; measures false alarm burden. | | 3 | False Negative Rate (FNR) | $\dfrac{FN}{TP+FN} = 1 - TPR$ | $[0,1]$ | Lower is better; measures missed attacks. | | 4 | Specificity (TNR) | $\dfrac{TN}{FP+TN} = 1 - FPR$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better; correctly cleared benign traffic. | | 5 | Precision (PPV) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Higher is better; trustworthiness of an alarm. | | 6 | Accuracy | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Misleading on imbalanced data (base-rate fallacy). | | 7 | F1-Score | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Best single metric for imbalanced IDS data. | | 8 | Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) | $\dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $[-1,1]$ | Robust metric for imbalanced IDS evaluation. | | 9 | ROC–AUC | Area Under ROC Curve | $[0,1]$ | Threshold-independent overall classifier quality. | ### 2.3 Relationship Between the Metrics (The "How") The metrics are not independent. The most important relationships are: $$FNR = 1 - TPR$$ $$Specificity = 1 - FPR$$ $$Precision = \frac{TPR \cdot P}{TPR \cdot P + FPR \cdot (1-P)}$$ where $P$ is the **prior probability** (base rate) of attack traffic. This last equation reveals the **Base-Rate Fallacy**: even with a fixed TPR and FPR, precision collapses as the base rate of attacks falls, because the absolute number of false positives dwarfs the number of true positives. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In a deployed SOC, the FP/FN balance translates directly into: - **Operational cost:** High FP → analyst fatigue, alert desensitisation, wasted man-hours. - **Security risk:** High FN → successful breaches, data exfiltration, regulatory penalties. - **Tuning cycle:** SOC teams continuously retune thresholds, whitelist known-benign patterns, and refine signatures to push the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** toward the top-left corner (high TPR, low FPR). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: FNR from Confusion Matrix Primitives **Problem Statement:** Starting from the four-cell confusion matrix, derive the **False Negative Rate** and show its algebraic relationship to the **True Positive Rate**. **Step 1 — Write the row marginal for the "Actual Attack" class.** The total number of actual attack events in the evaluation set is the sum of the two cells in the *Actual = Attack* row: $$N_{\text{attacks}} = TP + FN$$ This represents every event in the dataset that truly *is* an attack, regardless of what the IDS decided. **Step 2 — Identify the count of missed attacks (false negatives).** Among these $N_{\text{attacks}}$ events, the ones the IDS labelled as "benign" are the false negatives: $$N_{\text{missed}} = FN$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio.** By definition, the False Negative Rate is the proportion of actual attacks that the IDS failed to detect: $$\text{FNR} = \frac{N_{\text{missed}}}{N_{\text{attacks}}} = \frac{FN}{TP + FN}$$ **Step 4 — Relate FNR to TPR.** Since the True Positive Rate is defined as the proportion of actual attacks that the IDS *did* detect: $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ Adding FNR and TPR: $$FNR + TPR = \frac{FN}{TP+FN} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{FN+TP}{TP+FN} = 1$$ Therefore: $$FNR = 1 - TPR$$ This is the **complementarity identity** and is highly examinable. ### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Numerical Type Question (model):** > An IDS evaluated over a 24-hour window on an enterprise backbone processes $1{,}000{,}000$ events. Ground-truth analysis reveals $1{,}000$ real attacks occurred. The IDS raises $1{,}500$ alarms, of which $900$ correspond to real attacks. Compute: (i) TPR, (ii) FPR, (iii) Precision, (iv) F1-Score, (v) Accuracy. **Step 1 — Populate the confusion matrix from the given data.** - $TP = 900$ (alarms that were real attacks) - Total alarms $= 1{,}500$, so $FP = 1{,}500 - 900 = 600$ (alarms that were not real attacks) - Real attacks $= 1{,}000$, so $FN = 1{,}000 - 900 = 100$ (real attacks the IDS missed) - Benign events $= 1{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}000 = 999{,}000$; of these, $TN = 999{,}000 - 600 = 998{,}400$ **Step 2 — Compute the True Positive Rate (Detection Rate).** $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{900}{900+100} = \frac{900}{1000} = 0.90 \; (90\%)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the False Positive Rate.** $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{600}{600+998{,}400} = \frac{600}{999{,}000} \approx 6.006 \times 10^{-4} \; (0.0606\%)$$ **Step 4 — Compute Precision.** $$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{900}{900+600} = \frac{900}{1500} = 0.60 \; (60\%)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation — The Base-Rate Fallacy in Action:** Even though TPR is an excellent 90% and FPR is a tiny 0.06%, the **Precision is only 60%**. Of the 1,500 alarms that wake up the SOC analyst at 3 AM, *600 are false alarms*. This is why F1-Score is preferred over Accuracy for IDS evaluation. **Step 5 — Compute F1-Score.** $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.60 \cdot 0.90}{0.60 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.54}{1.50} = 2 \cdot 0.36 = 0.72$$ **Step 6 — Compute Accuracy.** $$Accuracy = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{900+998{,}400}{1{,}000{,}000} = \frac{999{,}300}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.9993 \; (99.93\%)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Accuracy is misleading here:** A "dumb" IDS that *never fires an alarm* would achieve $TN = 999{,}000$ and $FN = 1{,}000$, yielding Accuracy $= 99.90\%$ — comparable to our 99.93% — yet it would have caught *zero* real attacks. Accuracy on highly imbalanced IDS data is virtually useless; F1, MCC, or ROC-AUC must be reported instead. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Behaviour of FP vs FN | Dimension | False Positive (Type I) | False Negative (Type II) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Benign traffic flagged as attack. | Real attack labelled as benign. | | **Analogy** | Smoke alarm triggered by burnt toast. | Fire alarm that fails to ring during a real fire. | | **SOC Impact** | Alert fatigue, wasted analyst time, possible blocking of legitimate users. | Silent breach, data exfiltration, regulatory non-compliance. | | **Tuning Response** | *Raise* the detection threshold τ. | *Lower* the detection threshold τ. | | **Severity in Security** | Operational nuisance, but reversible. | Catastrophic — often undetected for months. | | **Statistical Name** | Type I Error ($\alpha$). | Type II Error ($\beta$). | | **Mitigation** | Whitelisting, baselining, multi-stage correlation, ML precision tuning. | Threat intelligence feeds, anomaly ensembles, behaviour-based detection. | | **Most Common Cause** | Anomaly-based IDS on novel but legitimate behaviour. | Zero-day exploits, evasion techniques, encrypted payloads. | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: How a Detection Decision Becomes a Confusion-Matrix Entry The following functional block diagram maps the journey of a single network event from the moment it enters the IDS sensor until it is counted in one of the four cells of the confusion matrix. This is the architecture examiners expect you to sketch in a 7-mark descriptive sub-question. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Network Event Arrives at IDS Sensor] --> B[Feature Extractor
Packet Headers Payload Behaviour] B --> C[Detection Engine
Signature OR Anomaly OR Hybrid] C --> D{Maliciousness Score
Compared with Threshold tau} D -- Score greater than tau --> E[IDS Raises ALARM
Predicted Attack] D -- Score less than or equal to tau --> F[IDS Silent
Predicted Benign] E --> G{Ground Truth
Is it truly an attack?} F --> H{Ground Truth
Is it truly an attack?} G -- Yes --> I1[Counted as TRUE POSITIVE TP] G -- No --> I2[Counted as FALSE POSITIVE FP] H -- Yes --> I3[Counted as FALSE NEGATIVE FN] H -- No --> I4[Counted as TRUE NEGATIVE TN] I1 --> J[Aggregate into Confusion Matrix] I2 --> J I3 --> J I4 --> J J --> K[Compute TPR FPR Precision F1] ``` ### 4.2 The ROC Curve: The Trade-off Visualised The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is the canonical visualisation of the FP-vs-FN trade-off. It plots **TPR (y-axis)** against **FPR (x-axis)** as the threshold $\tau$ is swept from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Threshold tau very low
IDS extremely sensitive] --> B[High TPR
High FPR
Many false alarms] A1[Threshold tau very high
IDS extremely conservative] --> A2[Low TPR
Low FPR
Many missed attacks] B --> C[ROC Curve
Plots TPR vs FPR] A2 --> C C --> D[Ideal Point
Top Left Corner
TPR=1, FPR=0] C --> E[Random Guess
Diagonal Line y=x
AUC = 0.5] C --> F[Area Under Curve AUC
Closer to 1 is better] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpreting the ROC curve for KTU exams:** > - A curve hugging the **top-left corner** = an excellent IDS. > - The **diagonal line** $y = x$ represents a random classifier (no discriminative power). > - The **AUC (Area Under Curve)** is a single scalar summarising IDS quality across all thresholds. An AUC of 0.99 indicates a near-perfect detector. ### 4.3 Threshold-Adjustment Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[SOC Analyst Reviews IDS Metrics] --> Q1{Which error dominates?} Q1 -- Too many false alarms --> R1[RAISE threshold tau] R1 --> R1a[FPR decreases, TPR may also decrease] R1a --> R1b[Monitor for rise in missed attacks] Q1 -- Too many missed attacks --> R2[LOWER threshold tau] R2 --> R2a[TPR increases, FPR may also increase] R2a --> R2b[Monitor for alert-fatigue in SOC] R1b --> End[Re-evaluate ROC and F1 after tuning cycle] R2b --> End ``` ### 4.4 Cost-Asymmetric Error Framework In real security operations, FPs and FNs are not equally costly. A **cost-sensitive** IDS assigns a higher penalty $C_{FN}$ to a missed attack than to a false alarm $C_{FP}$. The total expected cost is: $$E[\text{Cost}] = C_{FP} \cdot FP + C_{FN} \cdot FN$$ ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Each FP incurs cost C_FP] --> C[Total Operational Cost] B[Each FN incurs cost C_FN] --> C C --> D{Tune tau to MINIMISE E Cost} D -- FP dominates --> E[Raise tau] D -- FN dominates --> F[Lower tau] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Most KTU textbook examples treat FP and FN as equal-cost, but in production deployments, $C_{FN}$ is typically 100× to 10,000× larger than $C_{FP}$. This is why modern SIEM platforms (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic SIEM) use **risk-scoring** rather than raw counts. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]** **Q1. Differentiate between False Positive and False Negative in the context of an Intrusion Detection System. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - A **False Positive (Type I Error)** occurs when the IDS classifies a *legitimate* network activity as an intrusion, generating a false alarm. For example, an IDS flagging a routine software update as a malware download. **(1.5 marks)** - A **False Negative (Type II Error)** occurs when the IDS fails to detect an *actual* intrusion, allowing it to pass through silently. For example, a zero-day exploit that bypasses signature matching. **(1.5 marks)** - Key contrast: False positives degrade operational efficiency (alert fatigue), whereas false negatives represent a direct security breach. > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]** **Q2. Why is accuracy considered a misleading metric for evaluating an IDS? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - Network traffic is extremely **imbalanced** — typically <0.1% of events are real attacks (base-rate fallacy). **(1 mark)** - An IDS that *never* raises an alarm (i.e., classifies everything as benign) would achieve >99.9% accuracy, yet it would have **missed every single attack**. **(1 mark)** - Hence, accuracy fails to penalise the model for the very errors that matter (false negatives). **F1-Score, MCC, or ROC-AUC** are preferred. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Module 2, 2024 Scheme]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Define the four outcomes of an IDS classification — True Positive, True Negative, False Positive, and False Negative — and construct the **confusion matrix**. Explain why a False Negative is generally considered more dangerous than a False Positive in a production network. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** *Definition of the four outcomes:* - **True Positive (TP):** The IDS correctly identifies a real attack. E.g., a SYN flood is detected and an alert is raised. **(1 mark)** - **True Negative (TN):** The IDS correctly identifies legitimate traffic as benign. E.g., a routine HTTPS request to Google passes through without alarm. **(1 mark)** - **False Positive (FP):** The IDS incorrectly flags benign traffic as an attack (Type I error). E.g., a VoIP packet is misinterpreted as a covert channel. **(1 mark)** - **False Negative (FN):** The IDS fails to detect a real attack (Type II error). E.g., a polymorphic virus evades the signature database. **(1 mark)** *Confusion matrix:* **(2 marks)** | Predicted → / Actual ↓ | Predicted: Attack | Predicted: Benign | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Actual: Attack** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) | | **Actual: Benign** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) | *Why FN > FP in severity:* - A False Negative means a successful, undetected breach — data exfiltration, lateral movement, or ransomware deployment may be ongoing for weeks or months before discovery. - A False Positive, while disruptive, is reversible: the SOC can re-tune rules, whitelist the legitimate activity, and refine baselines. **(1 mark)** --- **(b)** An IDS is deployed on a campus network. Over a one-week evaluation period, the system raises **800 alarms**. Ground-truth analysis reveals that **1,000 real attacks** occurred during the same period, of which the IDS correctly detected **750**. The total volume of benign events observed was **499,000**. Compute the following: **(i)** True Positive Rate, **(ii)** False Positive Rate, **(iii)** Precision, **(iv)** F1-Score, **(v)** Accuracy, and **(vi)** Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Comment on the practical implications of your results. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Populate the confusion matrix:* **(1 mark)** - $TP = 750$ - $FN = 1{,}000 - 750 = 250$ - $FP = 800 - 750 = 50$ - $TN = 499{,}000 - 50 = 498{,}950$ *Step 2 — Compute TPR (Detection Rate):* **(1 mark)** $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{750}{750+250} = \frac{750}{1000} = 0.75$$ *Step 3 — Compute FPR:* **(1 mark)** $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{50}{50+498{,}950} = \frac{50}{499{,}000} \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-4}$$ *Step 4 — Compute Precision:* **(1 mark)** $$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{750}{750+50} = \frac{750}{800} = 0.9375$$ *Step 5 — Compute F1-Score:* **(1 mark)** $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9375 \times 0.75}{0.9375 + 0.75} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.703125}{1.6875} = 0.8333$$ *Step 6 — Compute Accuracy:* **(0.5 marks)** $$Accuracy = \frac{750+498{,}950}{500{,}000} = 0.9994 \; (99.94\%)$$ *Step 7 — Compute MCC:* **(0.5 marks)** $$MCC = \frac{(750)(498{,}950) - (50)(250)}{\sqrt{(800)(1000)(499{,}000)(498{,}950)}}$$ $$= \frac{374{,}212{,}500 - 12{,}500}{\sqrt{3.992 \times 10^{14}}} = \frac{374{,}200{,}000}{1{,}997{,}999} \approx 0.836$$ *Step 8 — Practical implications:* **(1 mark)** - **TPR = 75%:** The IDS misses 1 in 4 real attacks — unacceptable for high-security environments; threshold should be lowered. - **FPR ≈ 0.01%:** Excellent — minimal alert fatigue. - **Precision = 93.75%:** When the IDS *does* alert, it is very likely correct. - **Accuracy = 99.94%** is misleadingly high due to the imbalanced dataset. - **F1 = 0.83** and **MCC = 0.84** are robust indicators that the IDS is good but not excellent — recommend an ensemble or anomaly-based supplement. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **Base-Rate Fallacy** in IDS evaluation. Using the precision formula, show mathematically why even a small false positive rate becomes operationally intolerable in high-volume networks. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** *Definition of Base-Rate Fallacy:* **(2 marks)** The base-rate fallacy refers to the phenomenon in which the *prior probability* (base rate) of attack traffic in real networks is so low (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$) that even a low false positive rate produces an overwhelming absolute number of false alarms, drowning out the rare true positives and crippling the SOC's ability to triage. *Mathematical demonstration:* **(4 marks)** Recall the precision identity: $$Precision = \frac{TPR \cdot P}{TPR \cdot P + FPR \cdot (1-P)}$$ Suppose an IDS achieves $TPR = 0.95$ and $FPR = 0.001$ (0.1% — seemingly excellent). Evaluate precision at two base rates: **Case 1 — Moderate base rate $P = 0.01$ (1% of events are attacks):** $$Precision = \frac{0.95 \times 0.01}{0.95 \times 0.01 + 0.001 \times 0.99} = \frac{0.0095}{0.0095 + 0.00099} = \frac{0.0095}{0.01049} \approx 0.906$$ **Case 2 — Realistic base rate $P = 0.0001$ (0.01% of events are attacks):** $$Precision = \frac{0.95 \times 0.0001}{0.95 \times 0.0001 + 0.001 \times 0.9999} = \frac{0.000095}{0.000095 + 0.0009999} = \frac{0.000095}{0.0010949} \approx 0.0868$$ *Interpretation:* **(1 mark)** At a realistic base rate, only ~8.7% of alarms are real attacks. For every true alarm, there are ~10.5 false alarms. In a 10 Gbps link carrying ~10 million packets/second, this means the SOC would face **hundreds of thousands of false alarms per hour**, making manual triage impossible. The FPR *must* be in the $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ range to be operationally viable. --- **(b)** Draw and explain the **ROC curve** for IDS evaluation. What is **AUC**, and why is it considered threshold-independent? How does the **cost-sensitive detection** framework modify the FP–FN trade-off in practice? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** *ROC curve — definition and axes:* **(2 marks)** The ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve plots the **True Positive Rate** on the y-axis against the **False Positive Rate** on the x-axis as the detection threshold $\tau$ is swept from its minimum to maximum value. Each point on the curve represents a (FPR, TPR) pair at one specific threshold. *ROC curve — interpretation:* **(2 marks)** - A perfect IDS curve passes through the point $(0, 1)$ — top-left corner: 100% detection with zero false alarms. - A random classifier follows the diagonal $y = x$ line. - Curves bowing toward the top-left indicate better IDS performance. - The closer the curve is to the top-left corner across the entire range, the better the detector. *AUC — Area Under Curve:* **(1 mark)** **AUC** is the scalar area under the ROC curve, ranging from 0 to 1. It represents the probability that the IDS ranks a randomly chosen attack event higher than a randomly chosen benign event. An AUC of 0.99 indicates an almost-perfect detector. AUC is **threshold-independent** because it integrates performance over *all* possible threshold values, providing a single metric summarising the entire trade-off curve rather than a snapshot at one $\tau$. *Cost-sensitive framework:* **(2 marks)** In real deployments, FPs and FNs are not equally costly. The cost-sensitive framework assigns asymmetric penalties: $$E[\text{Cost}] = C_{FP} \cdot FP + C_{FN} \cdot FN$$ where $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$ (a missed attack may cost millions in breach remediation, whereas a false alarm costs analyst-minutes). The optimal threshold $\tau^*$ is then chosen to **minimise $E[\text{Cost}]$** rather than to maximise TPR or minimise FPR in isolation. This typically biases the operating point toward lower thresholds (more sensitive) than a pure accuracy-based optimum would suggest, accepting more false positives to avoid the catastrophic cost of false negatives. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in FP/FN questions:** > 1. **Confusing the denominators.** A frequent error is computing TPR as $TP / (TP+FP+FN+TN)$ (i.e., dividing by the entire dataset). The correct denominator for TPR is *only* the actual-attack row: $TP+FN$. > 2. **Reporting accuracy on imbalanced data.** Examiners will explicitly deduct marks if a student quotes "99.9% accuracy" without acknowledging the base-rate fallacy. Always pair accuracy with F1 or MCC. > 3. **Forgetting units and ranges.** All rate metrics are unitless and lie in $[0, 1]$. Expressing them as percentages must be unambiguous. > 4. **Ignoring the asymmetry of severity.** If a 14-mark question asks "which is more dangerous, FP or FN?", a balanced "both are bad" answer scores zero. The expected answer is **FN, with justification (silent breach, regulatory impact, dwell time)**. > 5. **Skipping the confusion matrix in the answer.** Even if not explicitly asked, sketching the 2×2 matrix is worth 2 free marks and signals structured thinking to the examiner. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — False Positives vs False Negatives:** - ☐ **FP = false alarm** (Type I); **FN = missed attack** (Type II). Memorise the mapping. - ☐ The four-cell **confusion matrix** is the universal framework — sketch it first in every numerical. - ☐ $TPR = TP / (TP+FN)$; $FPR = FP / (FP+TN)$; $FNR = 1 - TPR$; $Specificity = 1 - FPR$. - ☐ **Precision** answers: *"Of the alarms raised, how many were real?"* (trustworthiness of alerts). - ☐ **Recall / Detection Rate** answers: *"Of the real attacks, how many were caught?"* (coverage). - ☐ **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — best for imbalanced data. - ☐ **Accuracy is misleading** on imbalanced data; always pair with F1, MCC, or ROC-AUC. - ☐ The **Base-Rate Fallacy**: low base rate of attacks collapses precision even at low FPR. - ☐ **ROC curve** plots TPR vs FPR; **AUC** is its threshold-independent scalar summary. - ☐ **FN is operationally catastrophic**; FP is a nuisance. Cost-sensitive tuning reflects this asymmetry. - ☐ An IDS threshold $\tau$ controls the trade-off — lower for security-critical systems, higher for usability-sensitive systems. - ☐ Practical FP targets in production: $FPR < 10^{-5}$ to keep the SOC viable. - ☐ MCC and F1 are the **preferred** evaluation metrics for KTU numerical questions on IDS performance.

topics.title: IDPS Technologies - Components and Architecture
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔐 IDPS Technologies — Components and Architecture

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is a security solution whose architecture is fundamentally a modular assembly of specialized components that collaborate to **monitor, analyze, detect, log, and actively respond** to malicious activities targeting an information system. According to the **NIST SP 800-94 (Rev. 1)** reference framework adopted by KTU's PECCT742 curriculum, an IDPS is engineered as a **distributed sensor-analyzer-decision fabric**, where every component is purpose-built to fulfill one of the four canonical functional roles: *sensing, analyzing, managing, and responding*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Exam Standard)**  
> *"An IDPS architecture consists of four principal components — **Sensors / Agents, Analyzers (Detection Engines), Management Consoles, and Database Servers** — interconnected through a secure control channel to provide real-time identification and mitigation of cyber-intrusions across hosts, networks, and application layers."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Bank Vault" Model

Imagine a high-security bank vault as a real-world metaphor for an IDPS:

| Bank Vault Element | IDPS Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| CCTV Cameras & Door Sensors | **Sensors / Agents** | Capture raw data (network packets, log entries, syscalls) |
| Central Control Room (with AI Analyst) | **Analyzer / Detection Engine** | Interprets data, correlates events, identifies threats |
| Security Manager's Dashboard | **Management Console** | Operator interface, policy configuration, alerting |
| Forensic Evidence Locker | **Database Server** | Long-term storage of logs, alerts, and audit trails |

Just as a bank cannot rely on *only* cameras — it needs analysts, dashboards, and record-keeping — an IDPS cannot function without **all four components working in tandem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Architecture Matters in KTU Examinations**  
> Examiners at KTU frequently test whether a student understands the *separation of concerns* principle. Knowing *which component does what* is worth 3–7 marks per question. The architecture diagram is the single most-asked visual in the **Module 3** portion of PECCT742.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Used in IDPS Architecture

When discussing IDPS components, the following engineering metrics are universally applied (and frequently appear in numerical problems on the KTU exam):

- **Throughput (Gbps / Mbps):** Maximum traffic a sensor can analyze per second. Enterprise sensors range from **1 Gbps to 100 Gbps**.
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average delay between attack onset and IDPS detection. Industry target: **< 5 seconds**.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **False Negative Rate (FNR):** $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$
- **Detection Latency:** Time from packet capture to alert generation, typically **< 1 ms** for inline prevention.
- **Session Capacity:** Maximum concurrent TCP sessions a sensor can track (ranges from **100,000 to 10,000,000+**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Memorize **FPR** and **FNR** formulas — they are guaranteed **2-mark short-answer** questions in KTU Module 3 examinations.

---

## 1.4 Why the Four-Component Architecture is Universally Adopted

Modern IDPS vendors (Cisco, Palo Alto, Snort, Suricata, OSSEC, Wazuh) all converge on the same architectural pattern because of three engineering necessities:

1. **Scalability:** Sensors can be replicated across the network perimeter without duplicating analyzers.
2. **Centralized Policy Enforcement:** A single management console can govern thousands of sensors.
3. **Forensic Integrity:** A dedicated database server ensures **tamper-evident storage** required for compliance (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — Components and Architecture

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of IDPS Architecture

### Pillar 1 — Sensors / Agents (The "Eyes and Ears")

**Definition:** Sensors are passive or inline capture points deployed at strategic locations to collect raw event data.

**Two Physical Deployment Modes:**
- **Passive Mode (TAP / SPAN port):** Sensors *observe* a copy of traffic — they cannot block anything.
- **Inline Mode (Bump-in-the-Wire):** Sensors sit *directly in the traffic path* — they can drop, reset, or quarantine packets.

**Sensor Placement Zones in KTU Curriculum:**

1. **Perimeter / DMZ Sensor** — detects external attacks
2. **Internal Backbone Sensor** — detects lateral movement
3. **Host-Based Agent** — monitors system calls, file integrity, registry
4. **Application-Layer Sensor** — embedded within web/app servers

**Data Types Captured by Sensors:**
- Network packets (headers + payloads)
- OS audit logs (Windows Event Logs, Linux syslog)
- Application logs (Apache, IIS, DBMS)
- File system changes (checksums, MAC timestamps)
- System call traces (strace, auditd)

---

### Pillar 2 — Analyzer / Detection Engine (The "Brain")

**Definition:** The analyzer is the computational core that ingests sensor data and applies detection logic to classify events as benign or malicious.

**Three Detection Methodologies (mapped to KTU Module 2):**

| Methodology | How it Works | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature-Based** | Matches traffic against a database of known attack patterns (e.g., Snort rules) | Very low FPR, fast | Cannot detect zero-day attacks |
| **Anomaly-Based** | Builds statistical baseline; flags deviations | Detects unknown threats | High FPR |
| **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Tracks protocol state machines (TCP, HTTP) | Detects protocol misuse | Resource-intensive |

**Analytical Steps Inside the Engine:**

1. **Preprocessing:** Packet defragmentation, TCP reassembly, normalization
2. **Pattern Matching:** Rule evaluation against signatures
3. **Statistical Comparison:** Distance from trained baseline
4. **Correlation:** Cross-event analysis (e.g., 5 failed logins + 1 success = brute force)
5. **Scoring:** Risk score assignment (e.g., CVSS-based)
6. **Decision:** Alert / Log / Drop / Reset

---

### Pillar 3 — Management Console (The "Command Center")

**Definition:** The management console is the operator-facing interface that allows security administrators to configure sensors, tune detection rules, view alerts, and generate reports.

**Functional Capabilities (KTU Board-Exam Favorite):**

- Real-time dashboard with **alert severity color coding** (Critical, High, Medium, Low)
- **Rule editor** for signature customization
- **Policy distribution** to remote sensors
- **Incident response workflow** with ticketing integration
- **Report generation** (compliance, executive summary)
- **Sensor health monitoring** (uptime, CPU, disk)

> [!NOTE]
> **Advanced Concept (Beyond KTU Basics):** Modern consoles use **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)** integrations to auto-trigger playbooks via APIs.

---

### Pillar 4 — Database Server (The "Memory")

**Definition:** The database server provides persistent, indexed, and queryable storage for all IDPS events, alerts, and configuration data.

**Storage Categories:**

- **Hot Storage (SSD, last 7–30 days):** Fast-query, real-time analytics
- **Warm Storage (HDD, last 90 days):** Investigative queries
- **Cold Storage (Tape/Cloud Archive, 1+ year):** Compliance retention

**Schema Highlights:**
- `event_id`, `timestamp`, `sensor_id`, `src_ip`, `dst_ip`, `signature_id`, `severity`, `action_taken`

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse the *Database Server* with the *Analyzer*. Remember — the **Analyzer does the thinking**, the **Database does the remembering**.

---

## 2.2 Architectural Topologies (KTU High-Yield)

### Topology 1 — Centralized Architecture
All sensors forward data to a **single analyzer** and a **single database**.

**Advantages:** Simplified management, low cost.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure; bandwidth bottleneck.

### Topology 2 — Distributed Architecture
Each sensor has its **own local analyzer**; only summaries are forwarded to a central console.

**Advantages:** High availability, scalable, resilient.
**Disadvantages:** Complex management, higher cost.

### Topology 3 — Hierarchical (Hybrid) Architecture
A **multi-tier model**: Edge sensors → Regional analyzers → Central command.

**Advantages:** Balance of performance and central control.
**Disadvantages:** Middle-tier failure cascades.

---

## 2.3 IDPS Technology Categories (Syllus Anchor Point)

| Technology | Sensor Location | What it Monitors | Prevention Capability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NIDS** (Network-based IDS) | Network TAP / SPAN | Network packets | None (passive) |
| **NIPS** (Network-based IPS) | Inline appliance | Network packets | Yes (drop/reset) |
| **HIDS** (Host-based IDS) | On the host | OS logs, syscalls, files | None (passive) |
| **HIPS** (Host-based IPS) | On the host | OS + application behavior | Yes (block syscalls) |
| **Hybrid IDPS** | Network + Host | Combined telemetry | Optional |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Equation | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **False Positive Rate** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Ratio (0–1) | < 0.05 (good) |
| **False Negative Rate** | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Ratio (0–1) | < 0.01 (good) |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Ratio (0–1) | > 0.95 |
| **Precision** | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Ratio (0–1) | > 0.90 |
| **Recall (Detection Rate)** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Ratio (0–1) | > 0.99 (critical) |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Recall}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | > 0.90 |
| **Detection Latency** | $t_d = t_{\text{alert}} - t_{\text{event}}$ | ms / s | < 1000 ms |
| **Throughput Bound** | $\text{Thr} = \dfrac{\text{Packets Processed}}{\Delta t}$ | pps / Gbps | 1–100 Gbps |
| **Alert Volume Rate** | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{\text{alerts}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$ | alerts/min | < 10/min (manageable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not just academic — they form the **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** vendors sign with enterprises. A vendor quoting "99% detection rate" is providing the **Recall** metric.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production environments, the four-component architecture directly enables:

- **SOC (Security Operations Center)** workflows
- **PCI-DSS 11.4** compliance (continuous monitoring requirement)
- **NIST CSF** "Detect" function mapping
- **Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207)** sensor deployment
- **Incident Response** evidence preservation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — IDPS Detection Performance Metrics

To derive the foundational performance equations from first principles, let us define the **confusion matrix** variables precisely:

- **TP** (True Positive) — Malicious event correctly classified as malicious
- **TN** (True Negative) — Benign event correctly classified as benign
- **FP** (False Positive) — Benign event incorrectly classified as malicious
- **FN** (False Negative) — Malicious event incorrectly classified as benign

---

### Derivation 1 — False Positive Rate (FPR)

**Starting Principle:** FPR is the conditional probability that the IDPS raises an alert *given* the traffic is actually benign.

$$
\text{P(Alert} \mid \text{Benign)} = \dfrac{\text{Number of benign events flagged}}{\text{Total number of benign events}}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify Numerator:**  
The number of benign events flagged is, by definition, **FP** (False Positives).

**Step 2 — Identify Denominator:**  
The total number of benign events in the universe is the sum of all benign events *correctly classified (TN)* plus those *incorrectly flagged (FP)*.

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A high FPR means the IDPS "cries wolf" — operators tune down sensitivity, increasing FNR (a classic anti-correlation trade-off).

---

### Derivation 2 — Detection Rate (Recall / True Positive Rate)

**Starting Principle:** Detection rate is the conditional probability that the IDPS raises an alert *given* the traffic is actually malicious.

$$
\text{P(Alert} \mid \text{Malicious)} = \dfrac{\text{Malicious events detected}}{\text{Total malicious events}}
$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:** Successfully detected malicious events = **TP**.
**Step 2 — Denominator:** Total malicious events = TP + FN.

$$
\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Security teams prioritize **maximizing Recall** because missing a real attack (FN) is far costlier than investigating a false alarm (FP).

---

### Derivation 3 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)

**Starting Principle:** F1 balances precision and recall into a single metric.

$$
F_1 = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{1}{\text{Prec}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{Recall}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute definitions:**

$$
F_1 = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{TP + FP}{TP} + \dfrac{TP + FN}{TP}}
$$

**Step 2 — Common denominator:**

$$
F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent balanced form:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Recall}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harmonic Mean?** The harmonic mean **penalizes extreme imbalance**. If either precision or recall is near zero, F1 collapses — exactly the desired behavior for security metrics.

---

### Derivation 4 — Detection Latency

$$
t_d = t_{\text{alert}} - t_{\text{event}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{event}}$ = timestamp recorded by the **sensor** at packet capture
- $t_{\text{alert}}$ = timestamp recorded by the **management console** when an alert is raised

**Engineering Constraint:** For inline prevention (NIPS), $t_d$ must be less than the attacker's **dwell time** (time between initial compromise and action). Industry rule: $t_d < 100 \text{ ms}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Pattern)

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Style):**
> *"An IDPS analyzed 10,000 network events in a one-hour window. It produced the following confusion matrix: TP = 480, FP = 30, TN = 9,400, FN = 90. Calculate the FPR, FNR, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on the system's effectiveness."*

**Step 1 — Compute FPR:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{30}{30 + 9400} = \dfrac{30}{9430} \approx 0.00318
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FNR:**

$$
\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 480} = \dfrac{90}{570} \approx 0.1579
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \dfrac{480 + 9400}{10000} = \dfrac{9880}{10000} = 0.988
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Precision:**

$$
\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{480}{480 + 30} = \dfrac{480}{510} \approx 0.9412
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Recall:**

$$
\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{480}{480 + 90} = \dfrac{480}{570} \approx 0.8421
$$

**Step 6 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.9412 \cdot 0.8421}{0.9412 + 0.8421}
$$

**Intermediate Calculation:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 2 \cdot 0.7927 = 1.5854 \qquad \text{Denominator} = 1.7833
$$

$$
F_1 = \dfrac{1.5854}{1.7833} \approx 0.8891
$$

**Step 7 — Examiner's Commentary:**

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 98.8% | ✅ Excellent (skewed by class imbalance) |
| FPR | 0.32% | ✅ Excellent — minimal alert noise |
| FNR | 15.8% | ⚠️ Concerning — 1 in 6 attacks missed |
| Precision | 94.1% | ✅ High alert trustworthiness |
| Recall | 84.2% | ⚠️ Below enterprise SLA (99% target) |
| F1-Score | 0.889 | ⚠️ Acceptable but improvable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Although overall accuracy is high, the **FNR of 15.8%** indicates the IDPS is *missing real attacks* — a critical security gap. Recommendation: retrain the anomaly detection module or add supplementary signatures.

---

## 3.3 Component Capability Comparative Matrix

| Capability | Sensor | Analyzer | Mgmt Console | Database |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Packet Capture | ✅ Primary | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Signature Matching | ⚠️ Optional | ✅ Primary | ⚠️ Edit only | ❌ |
| Real-Time Alerting | ❌ | ✅ Generates | ✅ Displays | ❌ |
| Long-Term Retention | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ Short-term | ✅ Primary |
| Policy Distribution | ❌ | ⚠️ Receives | ✅ Primary | ❌ |
| Forensic Query | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ Frontend | ✅ Primary |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IDPS Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    netA[External Network Traffic]:::src
    netB[Internal Network Segment]:::src
    hostA[Critical Server Host 1]:::src
    hostB[Critical Server Host 2]:::src

    sensorN1[Network Sensor 1 NIDS NIPS]:::sensor
    sensorN2[Network Sensor 2 NIDS NIPS]:::sensor
    sensorH1[Host Agent 1 HIDS HIPS]:::sensor
    sensorH2[Host Agent 2 HIDS HIPS]:::sensor

    analyzer1[Analyzer Detection Engine]:::analyzer
    analyzer2[Secondary Analyzer Correlation]:::analyzer

    console[Management Console SOC Dashboard]:::console

    db1[Hot Database Last 30 Days SSD]:::db
    db2[Cold Database Compliance Archive]:::db

    firewall[Inline Prevention Action Drop Reset Quarantine]:::action
    siem[External SIEM Feed Forwarder]:::ext

    netA --> sensorN1
    netB --> sensorN2
    hostA --> sensorH1
    hostB --> sensorH2

    sensorN1 --> analyzer1
    sensorN2 --> analyzer1
    sensorH1 --> analyzer1
    sensorH2 --> analyzer2

    analyzer1 --> console
    analyzer2 --> console
    analyzer1 --> firewall

    console --> db1
    db1 --> db2
    analyzer1 --> siem

    classDef src fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    classDef sensor fill:#9333ea,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff
    classDef analyzer fill:#dc2626,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef console fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef db fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    classDef action fill:#ea580c,stroke:#c2410c,color:#ffffff
    classDef ext fill:#475569,stroke:#334155,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Centralized vs Distributed Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CENT[Centralized Topology]
        direction TB
        s1c[Edge Sensor A] --> ac[Central Analyzer]
        s2c[Edge Sensor B] --> ac
        s3c[Edge Sensor C] --> ac
        ac --> dc[Central Database]
        ac --> mc1[Management Console]
    end

    subgraph DIST[Distributed Topology]
        direction TB
        s1d[Sensor A] --> a1[Local Analyzer 1]
        s2d[Sensor B] --> a2[Local Analyzer 2]
        s3d[Sensor C] --> a3[Local Analyzer 3]
        a1 --> mc2[Central Management Console]
        a2 --> mc2
        a3 --> mc2
        a1 --> dd[Distributed DB Sync]
        a2 --> dd
        a3 --> dd
    end

    classDef cent fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef dist fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff

    class CENT cent
    class DIST dist
```

---

## 4.3 Inline (Prevention) vs Passive (Detection) Data Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Source Host
    participant Tap as Sensor Inline or TAP
    participant Eng as Detection Engine
    participant Cons as Management Console
    participant Dst as Destination Host
    participant Db as Event Database

    Src->>Tap: Send packet
    Tap->>Eng: Forward packet metadata
    Eng->>Eng: Evaluate against rules
    alt Threat Detected Inline IPS
        Eng-->>Tap: DROP and RESET
        Tap-->>Src: TCP RST sent
        Eng->>Cons: Critical alert raised
    else Benign Traffic
        Tap->>Dst: Forward packet normally
    end
    Eng->>Db: Log event tuple
    Cons->>Db: Query historical alerts
```

---

## 4.4 Hybrid IDPS Layered Topology (Enterprise Reference Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    internet[Internet]:::src
    perimeter[Perimeter NIPS Inline]:::sensor
    coreSwitch[Core Backbone NIDS Passive]:::sensor
    subnetA[Subnet A HIDS Agents]:::sensor
    subnetB[Subnet B HIDS Agents]:::sensor
    appServer[Application Server App Layer Sensor]:::sensor
    soc[Central SOC Management Console]:::console
    siemCore[SIEM Big Data Correlation]:::ext
    evidence[Tamper Evident Evidence Store]:::db

    internet --> perimeter
    perimeter --> coreSwitch
    coreSwitch --> subnetA
    coreSwitch --> subnetB
    subnetA --> appServer

    perimeter --> soc
    coreSwitch --> soc
    subnetA --> soc
    subnetB --> soc
    appServer --> soc

    soc --> siemCore
    soc --> evidence

    classDef src fill:#0f172a,stroke:#020617,color:#ffffff
    classDef sensor fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef console fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef ext fill:#52525b,stroke:#3f3f46,color:#ffffff
    classDef db fill:#075985,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the four major components of an IDPS architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four major components of an IDPS architecture are:

1. **Sensors / Agents** — Deployed at strategic points (network TAPs, host kernels, application servers) to capture raw event data such as packets, system calls, and logs.
2. **Analyzer / Detection Engine** — Processes sensor data using signature matching, anomaly detection, and stateful protocol analysis to classify events as benign or malicious.
3. **Management Console** — Provides the operator interface for configuring sensors, viewing real-time alerts, managing policies, and generating compliance reports.
4. **Database Server** — Stores all events, alerts, and configuration data in structured form for forensic analysis, audit, and historical reporting.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a centralized and distributed IDPS architecture. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Centralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | All sensors forward to one central analyzer | Each sensor has local analyzer |
| **Advantage** | Simplified management and lower cost | High availability and scalability |
| **Disadvantage** | Single point of failure | Higher complexity and cost |

A distributed architecture is preferred in **enterprise-grade deployments** where network scale exceeds the processing capacity of a single analyzer.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from the choice pair.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a Network-based Intrusion Prevention System (NIPS). Explain how the four components interact during inline packet processing.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Architecture Overview:**  
A NIPS operates inline in the data path. Every packet traversing the network segment *must* pass through the NIPS sensor before reaching its destination.

**Component Interaction (Inline Flow):**

1. **Sensor (Inline Bump-in-the-Wire):**  
   Receives the packet, performs **Layer 2–7 normalization**, captures the packet header + payload, and timestamps it.

2. **Analyzer (Detection Engine):**  
   Applies the following pipeline:
   - **TCP Reassembly** → Defragmentation → **Protocol Decoding**  
   - **Signature Matching** against rule database  
   - **Anomaly Score Computation** against baseline  
   - **Decision Output** → `ALLOW` / `DROP` / `RESET` / `ALERT`

3. **Prevention Action (Inline):**  
   If `DROP`, the sensor discards the packet and may emit a TCP RST to terminate the session.

4. **Management Console Notification:**  
   The console receives a **syslog/SNMP alert** with severity, signature ID, source IP, destination IP, and payload snapshot.

5. **Database Server Logging:**  
   The full event tuple is written to the persistent event database for forensic retention.

**Block Diagram Reference:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes — the inline path runs from the analyzer back to the firewall/prevention action node.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A host-based IDPS recorded the following confusion matrix on a production server over 24 hours:**  

**TP = 195**, **FP = 25**, **TN = 9,720**, **FN = 60**.

**Calculate the False Positive Rate, False Negative Rate, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on the suitability of this HIDS for PCI-DSS compliance.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — FPR Calculation:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{25}{25 + 9720} = \dfrac{25}{9745} \approx 0.00257
$$

**Step 2 — FNR Calculation:**

$$
\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP} = \dfrac{60}{60 + 195} = \dfrac{60}{255} \approx 0.2353
$$

**Step 3 — Accuracy Calculation:**

$$
\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{10000} = \dfrac{195 + 9720}{10000} = \dfrac{9915}{10000} = 0.9915
$$

**Step 4 — Precision Calculation:**

$$
\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{195}{195 + 25} = \dfrac{195}{220} \approx 0.8864
$$

**Step 5 — Recall Calculation:**

$$
\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{195}{195 + 60} = \dfrac{195}{255} \approx 0.7647
$$

**Step 6 — F1-Score Calculation:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.8864 \cdot 0.7647}{0.8864 + 0.7647}
$$

**Intermediate:**

$$
\text{Num} = 2 \cdot 0.6778 = 1.3556 \qquad \text{Den} = 1.6511
$$

$$
F_1 = \dfrac{1.3556}{1.6511} \approx 0.821
$$

**Step 7 — Examiner's Commentary:**

| Metric | Value | PCI-DSS Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 99.15% | ✅ Excellent |
| FPR | 0.26% | ✅ Acceptable |
| FNR | **23.53%** | ❌ **Fails compliance** |
| Precision | 88.64% | ⚠️ Moderate |
| Recall | **76.47%** | ❌ **Fails compliance** |
| F1-Score | 0.821 | ⚠️ Below SLA |

**Conclusion:** The HIDS **fails PCI-DSS 11.4** which mandates effective detection of all unauthorized access. An FNR of 23.53% means nearly 1 in 4 attacks goes undetected — a **critical compliance violation**. Recommended remediation: tune anomaly thresholds, add behavioral rules, and integrate supplementary NIDS logs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the centralized, distributed, and hierarchical IDPS architectures with their advantages, disadvantages, and suitable deployment scenarios.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| Architecture | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage | Deployment Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralized** | Single analyzer + DB serves all sensors | Simple management, low cost | SPOF, bandwidth bottleneck | Small offices, single-site SMB |
| **Distributed** | Each sensor has local analyzer; only summaries forwarded | High resilience, scales well | Higher cost, complex sync | Multi-site enterprises |
| **Hierarchical** | Multi-tier: edge → regional → central | Balances central control & local speed | Middle-tier failure cascade | Large enterprises, MSSPs, telcos |

**Detailed Explanation:**

- **Centralized:** All sensors forward raw or preprocessed data to one central analyzer. Best when sensor count < 10 and total throughput < 1 Gbps.
- **Distributed:** Each sensor acts as an autonomous IDPS, performing local detection and forwarding only metadata. Used in **SPAN-based enterprise cores**.
- **Hierarchical:** Edge sensors perform first-stage filtering; regional analyzers correlate; the central SOC aggregates. Optimal for **MSSPs managing thousands of tenants**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast the roles of the *Analyzer* and the *Database Server* in an IDPS. Why is the separation of these two components critical for enterprise-grade deployments?**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Analyzer (Detection Engine) | Database Server |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Role** | Real-time threat detection & decision-making | Persistent storage & forensic retrieval |
| **Data Lifecycle** | Transient — processes events in milliseconds | Long-term — retains events for years |
| **Compute Profile** | CPU-intensive (pattern matching, statistics) | I/O-intensive (indexed queries, writes) |
| **Failure Impact** | Detection stops; alerts missed | Alerts lost; compliance failure |
| **Backup Strategy** | Stateless replicas | Frequent snapshots, off-site replication |

**Why Separation is Critical:**

1. **Independent Scaling:** Analyzers can be scaled horizontally (more CPU); databases vertically (more RAM, faster SSDs) without mutual interference.
2. **Performance Isolation:** A heavy forensic query on the DB does not stall real-time detection.
3. **Forensic Integrity (Chain of Custody):** Storing alerts in a separate, write-once or append-only database ensures **legal admissibility**.
4. **Disaster Recovery:** A database server can be replicated to a secondary site while analyzers continue to operate locally.

**Conclusion:** Separation of compute (analyzer) and storage (database) is a foundational design principle borrowed from **data engineering** — applied to IDPS to achieve **performance, resilience, and compliance** simultaneously.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 3 (IDPS Components & Architecture):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "NIDS" with "NIPS"** — NIDS is *passive* (detection only); NIPS is *inline* (prevention). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Mixing up FPR and Recall** — Students frequently invert these. Remember: FPR is about *benign* events; Recall is about *malicious* events.
> 3. **Forgetting units in latency calculations** — Always write **ms** or **s** explicitly.
> 4. **Not labeling the management console vs database server** — Many students draw only the sensor-analyzer pair and miss 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" behind architectural choices** — When asked "Why hierarchical?", merely naming it scores 2/7. Explaining *load balancing + regional correlation* scores full marks.
> 6. **Computing Accuracy on imbalanced data without comment** — 99% accuracy sounds great but is misleading on imbalanced datasets. Always compute F1 alongside.
> 7. **Omitting block diagrams** — In 14-mark questions, a missing diagram costs **2–3 marks minimum**.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ An IDPS has **four components**: Sensors, Analyzer, Management Console, Database Server.
- ✅ **Sensors** capture; **Analyzers** decide; **Consoles** display; **Databases** remember.
- ✅ **NIDS is passive** (TAP/SPAN); **NIPS is inline** (bump-in-the-wire).
- ✅ **HIDS monitors hosts** (logs, syscalls, file integrity); **HIPS adds blocking**.
- ✅ Three detection methods: **Signature, Anomaly, Stateful Protocol Analysis**.
- ✅ Three topologies: **Centralized, Distributed, Hierarchical**.
- ✅ Key metrics: **FPR, FNR, Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1-Score, Latency**.
- ✅ $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$, $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$.
- ✅ Hierarchical = edge → regional → central; used by MSSPs.
- ✅ PCI-DSS 11.4 mandates **effective detection** — high FNR fails compliance.
- ✅ Always annotate **units** (ms, Gbps, alerts/min) in numerical answers.
- ✅ Block diagrams are **mandatory** for 14-mark architecture questions.
- ✅ Separation of **compute (analyzer)** and **storage (database)** enables scale and forensic integrity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Architecture** in the context of Intrusion Detection & Prevention Systems (IDPS) refers to the **structured design and topological arrangement** of network components — routers, switches, firewalls, sensors, monitoring appliances, and traffic redirection mechanisms — that collectively enable the **systematic capture, inspection, correlation, and analysis of network traffic** for the purpose of identifying and mitigating malicious activity.

In the KTU 2024 PECCT742 syllabus, *Network Architectures* covers the engineering blueprints that determine **where**, **how**, and **at what layer** an IDPS sensor is physically or logically placed inside an organization's network topology to maximize visibility, minimize blind spots, and ensure both detection fidelity and operational continuity.

### Formal Components of an IDPS Network Architecture

A KTU-compliant IDPS network architecture consists of the following mandatory conceptual building blocks:

1. **Sensing Layer** — Network TAPs, Switched Port Analyzers (SPAN), inline bypass switches, and host-based collectors that tap into the wire or read packet copies.
2. **Transport Layer** — Dedicated VLANs (often VLAN 1, VLAN 666, or a dedicated *monitoring VLAN*), GRE tunnels, or encrypted IPsec tunnels used to carry mirrored traffic back to a central analysis engine.
3. **Analysis Layer** — The correlation engine (Snort, Suricata, Zeek, proprietary SIEM-integrated engines) that consumes the mirrored stream and applies signature, anomaly, or stateful protocol analysis.
4. **Response Layer** — Inline blocking appliances, RST-injection endpoints, blackhole-routing scripts (BGP / RTBH), or northbound API triggers to firewalls and SDN controllers.
5. **Management Plane** — Out-of-band (OoB) management network (often **10.0.0.0/24** or a dedicated management VRF) used for configuring sensors, pulling logs, and pushing firmware.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a hospital building as the **network**, and an **IDPS as the security camera + emergency response system**.

- The **cameras** (Network TAPs / SPAN ports) are placed at *strategic choke points* — the main entrance, the ICU corridor, and outside the pharmacy — exactly where an IDPS sensor is placed at perimeter, DMZ, and internal segment boundaries.
- The **CCTV recording room** is the **central analysis engine** — all camera feeds (mirrored traffic) converge here for review.
- The **intercom from the security desk to lock the doors** is the **inline prevention action** (TCP RST injection, ACL push, BGP blackhole).
- A hospital with **only one camera at the front door** has terrible coverage (single-point architecture) — just as a single IDPS sensor at the perimeter is vulnerable to insider threats, lateral movement, and encrypted blind spots.
- A hospital with **cameras everywhere, networked to a regional control room** is a *hierarchical distributed architecture* — exactly the gold-standard modern SOC design.

### Why Network Architecture Matters in IDPS

| Threat Vector | Architectural Failure Mode |
|---|---|
| **External reconnaissance / scanning** | No sensor on the Internet edge |
| **Lateral movement / east-west traffic** | Sensors only at perimeter (north-south only) |
| **Encrypted C2 channels (TLS 1.3, DoH)** | No SSL/TLS inspection (MITM proxy) on sensor path |
| **Insider exfiltration** | No sensor on internal server-segment VLAN |
| **Single point of failure** | All sensors in one rack; no HA / clustering |
| **Inline latency sensitive apps** | IPS placed inline on VoIP / trading floor links |
| **Cloud workloads** | Legacy physical appliance cannot inspect east-west VPC traffic |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standardized metrics** are explicitly referenced in the KTU 2024 PECCT742 syllabus and are essential for exam answers:

- **IEEE 802.1Q** — VLAN tagging standard used to segment monitoring traffic.
- **RFC 5101** — Specification of the IP Flow Information Export (IPFIX) protocol for traffic flow records.
- **IEEE 802.1ag / ITU-T Y.1731** — Connectivity Fault Management (CFM) used in carrier-grade IDPS architectures.
- **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** — Standard Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**; Jumbo frames = **9000 bytes** (used in TAP aggregation links).
- **Network TAP optical split ratio** — Typically **50:50** for single-mode fiber, but **70:30 / 80:20 / 90:10** splits are common for long-haul links to minimize insertion loss.
- **Port mirroring (SPAN) bandwidth** — Hard-coded per-platform; Cisco default SPAN ratio is **1:1** (1 Gbps monitored link can fully saturate a 1 Gbps SPAN destination port, risking **packet drops** above ~80% utilization).
- **IDS vs IPS failure mode** — An **IDS fails OPEN** (traffic continues); an **IPS fails CLOSED** (traffic blocked) — this is a **design-level architectural decision**, not a configuration.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** for enterprise-class TAPs is typically **> 500,000 hours**.
- **Three-way handshake completion rate** of a healthy network ≈ **99.5%**; below this, an IDPS sensor must be investigated for invisible drops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Network Architectures

### 2.1.1 The OSI Layer as an Architectural Compass

Every IDPS architecture decision is rooted in **at which OSI layer** the sensor operates. The deeper the layer, the more semantic context — but the higher the processing cost.

| OSI Layer | Protocol Examples | What IDPS Can See | Architectural Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Physical** | 10BASE-T, 100BASE-FX | Raw bits on copper/fiber | Must use **TAP**; cannot rely on switch logic |
| **L2 — Data Link** | Ethernet, ARP, STP, VLAN tags | MAC addresses, frame errors | **Inline bypass switches** operate here |
| **L3 — Network** | IP, ICMP, IPsec | Source/dest IPs, TTL, fragmentation | **Most IDPS sensors are L3-aware** |
| **L4 — Transport** | TCP, UDP, SCTP | Ports, flags, sequence numbers | **Stateful inspection** lives here |
| **L7 — Application** | HTTP, DNS, SMB, TLS SNI | Full URI, payload, certificates | Requires **deep packet inspection (DPI)** + SSL MITM |

### 2.1.2 Architectural Classification (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes IDPS network architectures into **three meta-categories**:

#### A. Passive Monitoring Architecture
- Sensor receives a **copy** of traffic via SPAN port or Network TAP.
- **Cannot block** traffic; can only alert, log, or trigger out-of-band responses (e.g., firewall API call).
- *Failure mode:* **Fail-open** — sensor crash has zero impact on production traffic.

#### B. Inline / In-Path Architecture (IPS Mode)
- Sensor sits **physically in the wire** between two network segments (e.g., between switch and firewall).
- Traffic must pass through the sensor; it can **drop, modify, or rate-limit** packets in real time.
- **Latency-sensitive**; requires **bypass switch** for hardware-level failover.
- *Failure mode:* **Fail-closed** (drop) or **fail-open** (bypass) — set architecturally.

#### C. Tap Aggregation / Sensor Cluster Architecture
- Multiple SPAN sources (often 8–48 ports) are aggregated via a **TAP aggregation switch** (e.g., Cisco Nexus 3550, Gigamon GigaVUE, Ixia Vision ONE) to feed one or more sensors.
- **Load balancing** across multiple sensors using **packet-based**, **flow-based**, or **application-based** distribution.
- The **de-facto architecture** in modern enterprise SOCs.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Sub-Modules

### 2.2.1 Placement Strategies (Sensor Position in the Topology)

The KTU syllabus emphasizes **five canonical sensor placements**:

1. **Outside the perimeter firewall** — Catches inbound attacks *before* firewall filtering; high false-positive volume; useful for threat-intel correlation.
2. **Inside the perimeter firewall (DMZ edge)** — Most common; sees attacks that bypassed the firewall; sees outbound C2 callbacks.
3. **At the internal server farm / data center core** — Detects **lateral movement** and **insider threats**.
4. **At the WAN edge / VPN concentrator** — Inspects encrypted VPN tunnels after decryption; critical for remote-workforce IDPS.
5. **On the cloud transit gateway / vTAP** — For AWS Transit Gateway, Azure VWAN, GCP VPC; uses **Virtual TAP** (vTAP) or **ERSPAN** (Encapsulated Remote SPAN, RFC 7348) for east-west visibility.

### 2.2.2 Network TAP vs. SPAN Port (Architectural Trade-off)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the **highest-frequency 3-mark questions** in KTU 2024 ESE for PECCT742.

| Feature | Network TAP | SPAN Port |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer of operation** | L1 (Physical) | L2 (Switch ASIC) |
| **Packet fidelity** | 100% — sees CRC errors, malformed frames, late collisions | Filters out errors; misses FCS errors, runts |
| **Failure mode** | Fail-open (passive optical) — total transparency on power loss | Switch-dependent; SPAN port stops mirroring on switch CPU overload |
| **Performance impact** | Zero on production traffic | Can drop packets at > line rate; competes for switch ASIC buffers |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high (per link) | Free (software-configured) |
| **Encrypted visibility** | Yes — full copy, decrypt separately | Yes — but switch can't decrypt |
| **Encapsulation** | None — raw frames | Often encapsulated in **ERSPAN** (GRE over IP) for remote SPAN |

### 2.2.3 Encrypted Traffic Inspection Architecture

With **> 85%** of enterprise web traffic now HTTPS (Google Transparency Report metric), the modern IDPS architecture **must** include:

- **SSL/TLS Inspection Appliance** (a MITM proxy) placed inline *before* the IDPS sensor.
- Architecture: `Client → Firewall → SSL Decryptor → IDPS → Server`.
- Decryptor re-signs traffic with an **internal CA** (pushed via Group Policy / MDM to all endpoints).
- **Privacy-sensitive traffic** (banking, healthcare portals) is added to a **do-not-decrypt list**.

### 2.2.4 Cloud & Virtualization Architecture

- **East-West traffic** inside a single VPC is invisible to perimeter sensors.
- Solutions: **AWS VPC Traffic Mirroring** (via ENI target), **Azure vTAP**, **GCP packet mirroring**, **Cisco Tetration**, **Guardicore / Akamai Segmentation**, or **VMware Distributed Switch + port mirroring**.
- Virtual sensors (e.g., **Cisco Secure Network Analytics Virtual Edition**) are deployed as VMs inside the hypervisor.

### 2.2.5 High-Availability Architecture

- **Active-Active** sensor cluster — all sensors process traffic simultaneously; state synchronized via cluster protocol (e.g., Snort's `daq` engine with HA mode).
- **Active-Passive** — primary processes traffic; standby hot-spares.
- **Inline Bypass Switch** — physical L1 device that **electrically shorts** the traffic path when the IPS fails (microsecond failover).
- **Geographic redundancy** — sensors at multiple data centers; logs forwarded to a central SIEM.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for solving numerical / analytical questions in KTU 2024 ESE for this module. **Memorize each with its boundary condition.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Description / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Effective bandwidth utilization** | $U = \dfrac{B_{used}}{B_{capacity}} \times 100\%$ | If $U > 80\%$, packet drops are statistically likely on SPAN ports — use TAP instead. |
