| 2 | **SPAN oversubscription ratio** | $R_{span} = \dfrac{B_{monitored}}{B_{destination}}$ | $R_{span} > 1$ ⇒ guaranteed drop. Always use $R_{span} \le 1$ for full fidelity. |
| 3 | **Inline latency budget** | $T_{budget} = T_{app} - T_{link} - T_{fw}$ | For VoIP, $T_{budget} \le 30\text{ ms}$. Forces use of ASIC-accelerated IPS, not software IPS. |
| 4 | **Sensor throughput** | $S_{throughput} = \dfrac{P \times 8}{T}$ (in bps) | $P$ = packets processed in time $T$. Required for capacity planning of sensor clusters. |
| 5 | **MTU and jumbo-frame capacity** | $C_{frame} = \dfrac{L_{link}}{L_{MTU}}$ | $L_{MTU} = 1500$ B (Ethernet), $9000$ B (Jumbo). Lower MTU = more CPU cycles per MB. |
| 6 | **False positive rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Architectural decision: lowering threshold below $T_{opt}$ inflates $FPR$ exponentially. |
| 7 | **False negative rate** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | In passive IDPS, $FNR$ is non-actionable until log review. In inline IPS, $FNR$ is fatal. |
| 8 | **Detection accuracy (Youden's J)** | $J = Sensitivity + Specificity - 1$ | $J \in [0, 1]$; architectural sweet spot for threshold tuning. |
| 9 | **Distributed load balancing** | $P_{i} = \dfrac{W_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{j}}$ | $P_i$ = fraction of packets routed to sensor $i$ based on weight $W_i$. |
| 10 | **Bypass switch failover time** | $T_{failover} < 5\text{ ms}$ | Bypass switch must activate before TCP retransmission timeout (typically 200 ms). |
| 11 | **ERSPAN GRE header overhead** | $H_{total} = 14_{Eth} + 20_{IP} + 4_{GRE} + H_{inner}$ | Adds **38 bytes**; increases effective MTU; configure jumbo frames end-to-end. |
| 12 | **Packets per second (pps) → bps** | $B_{bps} = N_{pps} \times L_{avg} \times 8$ | $L_{avg} = 400$–$800$ B typical for Internet mix. |
| 13 | **Sensor storage growth** | $S_{growth} = R_{flow} \times T_{retention} \times A_{avg}$ | $R_{flow}$ = flows/sec; $A_{avg}$ ≈ 120 B per flow record (NetFlow v5). |
| 14 | **Layer 7 inspection cost** | $C_{L7} \approx 50 \times C_{L3}$ | Approximate rule of thumb — 50× CPU cycles for DPI vs. header matching. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SPAN Oversubscription and the 80% Rule

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation justifies why a 10 Gbps link should never be mirrored to a single 1 Gbps SPAN destination.

### Problem Setup
A switch has a **10 Gbps** uplink carrying production traffic. The network engineer configures a **SPAN port of 1 Gbps** to feed the IDPS sensor. Determine the maximum error-free traffic load and the expected packet drop probability under full load.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Deduction

**Step 1 — Define the oversubscription ratio.**

$$R_{span} = \frac{B_{monitored}}{B_{destination}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known link capacities.**

$$R_{span} = \frac{10\,\text{Gbps}}{1\,\text{Gbps}} = 10$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the ratio.**

$$R_{span} = 10 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Only 1 of every 10 frames can be mirrored without loss}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum error-free mirrored bandwidth.**

$$B_{error\text{-}free} = \frac{B_{destination}}{1} = 1\,\text{Gbps}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the engineering rule of thumb: target ≤ 80% utilization to avoid micro-burst drops.**

$$B_{safe} = 0.80 \times 1\,\text{Gbps} = 0.8\,\text{Gbps} = 800\,\text{Mbps}$$

**Step 6 — Express the monitored bandwidth that can be fully captured at 80% safe-load.**

$$B_{captured} = 0.8\,\text{Gbps} \;\; \text{(only 8% of the 10 Gbps link)}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the dropped fraction.**

$$\text{Drop Fraction} = 1 - \frac{0.8}{10} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 92\%$$

### Conclusion
**92% of all frames on the 10 Gbps link will be silently dropped** before reaching the IDPS. This makes the architecture **analytically blind** to burst attacks. The remediation is either (a) deploy a **10 Gbps SPAN** plus a **TAP aggregation switch**, or (b) install a **bypass-mode 10 Gbps TAP** with **dedicated 10 Gbps sensor** throughput.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inline Latency Budget for VoIP Architecture

### Problem Setup
A financial trading firm's VoIP PBX connects to the WAN via a path with:
- WAN link one-way latency: $T_{link} = 18\text{ ms}$
- Firewall processing latency: $T_{fw} = 2\text{ ms}$
- Acceptable total mouth-to-ear delay for VoIP (ITU-T G.114): $T_{app} \le 150\text{ ms}$
- We wish to insert an IPS inline without degrading call quality.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Deduction

**Step 1 — Apply the inline latency budget equation.**

$$T_{budget} = T_{app} - T_{link} - T_{fw}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$T_{budget} = 150 - 18 - 2 = 130\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — In practice, network architects reserve only 50% of the budget for the IPS.**

$$T_{ips}^{max} = 0.5 \times T_{budget} = 0.5 \times 130 = 65\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — A software IPS (e.g., Snort in AF_PACKET mode on a VM) typically adds 5–50 ms latency under load.**

**Step 5 — Therefore, the architect selects a hardware-accelerated ASIC-based IPS (e.g., Cisco Firepower 4100, Palo Alto PA-7000 series) with sub-millisecond latency:**

$$T_{ips}^{hw} \approx 0.5\text{–}2\text{ ms} \;\; < \;\; T_{ips}^{max} = 65\text{ ms}$$

### Conclusion
The inline placement is **architecturally valid only with a hardware-ASIC IPS**. A software IPS would breach the latency budget during burst load and cause audible jitter / clipping in VoIP calls. This justifies an architectural branch: *VoIP segment uses bypass-mode inline IPS; non-realtime segments use full deep-inspection inline IPS*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensor Cluster Load Balancing with Weighted Round-Robin

### Problem Setup
A TAP aggregation switch must distribute mirrored traffic across **three sensors** with the following processing capacities:

- Sensor A: $C_A = 5\,\text{Gbps}$
- Sensor B: $C_B = 10\,\text{Gbps}$
- Sensor C: $C_C = 5\,\text{Gbps}$

Total monitored traffic: $B_{total} = 18\,\text{Gbps}$.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Compute the aggregate sensor capacity.**

$$C_{agg} = C_A + C_B + C_C = 5 + 10 + 5 = 20\,\text{Gbps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the load ratio for each sensor using the weighted distribution formula.**

$$P_{i} = \frac{W_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{j}}$$

where $W_i$ is the throughput weight.

**Step 3 — Substitute sensor capacities as weights.**

$$P_A = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 \;\;\; P_B = \frac{10}{20} = 0.50 \;\;\; P_C = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25$$

**Step 4 — Compute the actual bandwidth each sensor must absorb.**

$$B_A = P_A \times B_{total} = 0.25 \times 18 = 4.5\,\text{Gbps}$$
$$B_B = P_B \times B_{total} = 0.50 \times 18 = 9.0\,\text{Gbps}$$
$$B_C = P_C \times B_{total} = 0.25 \times 18 = 4.5\,\text{Gbps}$$

**Step 5 — Verify each sensor is below its capacity.**

$$B_A = 4.5 < C_A = 5 \;\;\checkmark$$
$$B_B = 9.0 < C_B = 10 \;\;\checkmark$$
$$B_C = 4.5 < C_C = 5 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute the headroom for each sensor.**

$$H_A = \frac{C_A - B_A}{C_A} \times 100\% = \frac{0.5}{5} \times 100 = 10\%$$
$$H_B = \frac{C_B - B_B}{C_B} \times 100\% = \frac{1.0}{10} \times 100 = 10\%$$
$$H_C = \frac{C_C - B_C}{C_C} \times 100\% = \frac{0.5}{5} \times 100 = 10\%$$

### Conclusion
The cluster has **uniform 10% headroom** — a well-balanced architecture. If traffic spikes by 10% (e.g., $B_{total}$ → 20 Gbps), the cluster will saturate and packets will begin dropping at the aggregation switch. The remediation is either over-provision by adding a fourth sensor or implement **flow-based hashing** to spread spikes more evenly than round-robin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IDPS Architectural Models

| Architecture | Visibility | Latency Impact | Block Capability | Failure Mode | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Passive IDS at Edge** | Perimeter only | None | No | Open | Low | SMBs |
| **Single Inline IPS at Edge** | Perimeter only | Low–Med | Yes | Closed | Med | Branch offices |
| **Distributed Passive (sensor-per-segment)** | Full east-west + north-south | None | No (alerts only) | Open | High | Data centers, regulated industries |
| **Inline IPS + Passive IDS hybrid** | Full + perimeter | Low on inline segments | Yes on perimeter | Mixed | High | Mid-large enterprises |
| **TAP Aggregation + Sensor Cluster** | Full + load-balanced | None | Configurable per sensor | Configurable | High | Enterprise SOCs, MSSPs |
| **Cloud-native (vTAP / VPC Mirror)** | East-west in cloud | Negligible | Yes (via Security Group) | Open | Pay-as-you-go | AWS / Azure / GCP deployments |
| **Hierarchical (Central + Remote)** | Full + correlated | Variable | Coordinated | Multi-tier | Very High | Multi-site, MSSP, government |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Enterprise IDPS Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    internet["Internet"]
    isp["ISP Edge Router"]
    extF["External Firewall"]
    extTap["Network TAP A — Pre-Firewall"]
    extSensor["External IDPS Sensor IDS1"]
    dmzF["DMZ Firewall"]
    dmzWeb["Web Servers DMZ"]
    dmzMail["Mail Relay DMZ"]
    intTap["Network TAP B — Internal Core"]
    intSensor["Internal IDPS Sensor IDS2"]
    core["Core Layer 3 Switch"]
    intF["Internal Segmentation Firewall"]
    vlan1["Server VLAN 10"]
    vlan2["User VLAN 20"]
    vlan3["VoIP VLAN 30"]
    oob["Out-of-Band Mgmt Network"]
    siem["Central SIEM"]
    bypass["Inline Bypass Switch IPS1"]
    decrypt["SSL TLS Decryptor"]

    internet --> isp
    isp --> extTap
    extTap --> extF
    extTap --> extSensor
    extF --> dmzF
    dmzF --> dmzWeb
    dmzF --> dmzMail
    dmzF --> bypass
    bypass --> decrypt
    decrypt --> core
    core --> intTap
    intTap --> intSensor
    intTap --> core
    core --> intF
    intF --> vlan1
    intF --> vlan2
    intF --> vlan3

    extSensor -.->|Syslog NetFlow| siem
    intSensor -.->|Syslog NetFlow| siem
    bypass -.->|TCP RST Drop Alert| siem

    extSensor --> oob
    intSensor --> oob
    bypass --> oob
    siem --> oob
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `Network TAP A` is placed **before** the external firewall (capturing pre-filter attacks).
- `Network TAP B` is placed at the **core switch** (capturing all east-west traffic).
- `Bypass Switch IPS1` is **inline** after the DMZ firewall, providing inline prevention.
- `SSL TLS Decryptor` sits inline so the IDPS can see inside encrypted HTTPS flows.
- All sensors forward via the **out-of-band management network** to the central SIEM — production traffic is never mixed with management traffic.

---

## 4.2 Passive vs. Inline Deployment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Passive["PASSIVE MONITORING ARCHITECTURE"]
        P1["Client"] --> P2["Switch"]
        P2 --> P3["Server"]
        P2 -.->|SPAN Mirror| P4["IDPS Sensor IDS"]
        P4 -.->|Alert Only| P5["SIEM Firewall API"]
    end

    subgraph Inline["INLINE IPS ARCHITECTURE"]
        I1["Client"] --> I2["Firewall"]
        I2 --> I3["Inline Bypass"]
        I3 --> I4["IDPS IPS"]
        I4 --> I5["Server"]
        I4 -.->|Drop RST Alert| I6["SIEM"]
        I3 -.->|Fail-open Bypass| I1
    end

    classDef pass fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    classDef inl fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 pass
    class I1,I2,I3,I4,I5,I6 inl
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical Multi-Tier IDPS Architecture (SOC / MSSP Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tier1["Tier 1 — Branch / Site Sensors"]
        S1["Sensor Site A"]
        S2["Sensor Site B"]
        S3["Sensor Site C"]
    end

    subgraph Tier2["Tier 2 — Regional Correlation"]
        R1["Regional Aggregator SOC 1"]
        R2["Regional Aggregator SOC 2"]
    end

    subgraph Tier3["Tier 3 — Global SOC + Threat Intel"]
        G1["Central SIEM"]
        G2["Threat Intelligence Platform"]
        G3["SOAR Automation Engine"]
    end

    S1 -->|NetFlow Logs| R1
    S2 -->|NetFlow Logs| R1
    S3 -->|NetFlow Logs| R2

    R1 -->|Correlatd Alerts| G1
    R2 -->|Correlatd Alerts| G1

    G1 <--> G2
    G1 --> G3
    G3 -.->|Block Push| S1
    G3 -.->|Block Push| S2
    G3 -.->|Block Push| S3

    classDef t1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    classDef t2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207
    classDef t3 fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9
    class S1,S2,S3 t1
    class R1,R2 t2
    class G1,G2,G3 t3
```

---

## 4.4 Cloud-Native vTAP Architecture (AWS Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VPC["Customer VPC"]
        EC2A["Web EC2 ENI"]
        EC2B["App EC2 ENI"]
        EC2C["DB EC2 ENI"]
    end

    TM["AWS VPC Traffic Mirroring Source"]
    TMX["Traffic Mirror Target ENI"]
    IDSVM["Virtual IDPS Sensor EC2 Instance"]
    S3["CloudWatch + S3 Log Archive"]

    EC2A -.-> TM
    EC2B -.-> TM
    EC2C -.-> TM
    TM --> TMX
    TMX --> IDSVM
    IDSVM --> S3

    classDef vpc fill:#f0f9ff,stroke:#0284c7
    classDef idps fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d
    class EC2A,EC2B,EC2C,TM,TMX vpc
    class IDSVM,S3 idps
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Flow of an Inline IPS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Packet Arrives on Ingress Port"]
    B["Step 2 MAC VLAN Lookup"]
    C["Step 3 TCP State Validation"]
    D{"Step 4 Policy Decision Engine"}
    E["Step 5a Forward to Egress Port"]
    F["Step 5b Drop Packet and Log"]
    G["Step 5c Modify Packet and Forward"]
    H["Step 6 Mirror Decision to SIEM"]
    I["Step 7 Connection State Updated"]

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -->|Allowed| E --> H --> I
    D -->|Denied| F --> H --> I
    D -->|Normalized| G --> H --> I

    classDef ok fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857
    classDef deny fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c
    classDef mod fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c
    class E,H,I ok
    class F deny
    class G mod
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**Differentiate between a Network TAP and a SPAN port. Why is a TAP preferred for high-fidelity IDPS deployments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Network TAP (Test Access Point)** is a *Layer-1 hardware device* spliced into the physical cable (copper or fiber) that splits the light/signal into two streams — one continues to the production link, the other is sent to the monitoring appliance. A **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port** is a *software-configured feature* on a switch that copies traffic from one or more source ports/VLANs to a designated destination port.

| Criterion | Network TAP | SPAN Port |
|---|---|---|
| OSI layer | L1 (Physical) | L2 (Switch ASIC) |
| Errored frame visibility | Yes — sees CRC errors, runts, giants | No — switch drops errored frames |
| Failure mode | Passive optical → fail-open | Switch CPU-dependent |
| Performance impact | Zero on production | Competes for ASIC buffers |
| Cost | Higher (per-link hardware) | Free |

A **TAP is preferred for high-fidelity IDPS** because it captures **100% of frames including malformed/errored traffic**, has **zero impact on production performance**, and **fails open** (transparent during power loss), ensuring the network remains functional even if the sensor crashes. SPAN can silently drop packets above ~80% line rate, creating a **false sense of security**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Explain the role of a bypass switch in an inline IPS architecture. What happens if it is not deployed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bypass switch** is a **Layer-1 hardware failover device** placed *in series* with the inline IPS. Its sole architectural purpose is to **electrically reroute traffic around the IPS** if the IPS fails, crashes, or undergoes maintenance.

- **Normal operation:** traffic flows `Switch → Bypass Switch → IPS → Bypass Switch → Server`.
- **IPS failure:** the bypass switch detects loss of heartbeat / link and **physically shorts the two ports**, restoring the direct `Switch → Server` path in **microseconds** (typically < 5 ms).

**If a bypass switch is NOT deployed:**
- A single IPS power failure or software crash will **break the entire production path**.
- This violates the **availability** pillar of the CIA triad.
- In VoIP / trading / healthcare, this means **immediate financial or clinical loss**.

The bypass switch is therefore an **architectural mandate**, not an optional accessory, for any inline IPS in a mission-critical path.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by *conflating SPAN with TAP* — examiners expect an explicit OSI-layer citation.
> - Do **NOT** state "inline IPS has lower latency than passive IDS" — this is reversed. The correct statement is: *inline IPS adds latency; passive IDS does not*.
> - Always draw the **diagram first** in 14-mark questions. A missing diagram costs at least **2 marks**.
> - When listing placement options, the examiner expects **at least 4 placements** — listing only "before firewall" is incomplete.
> - Mention **fail-open vs fail-closed** explicitly. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **canonical enterprise IDPS network architecture** showing the placement of firewalls, TAPs, IDPS sensors, and the central SIEM. Discuss **three critical placement decisions** and justify each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Passive (IDS) vs Inline (IPS) deployment architectures** with respect to latency, block capability, failure mode, and use-case suitability.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Diagram (3 marks):**
A full diagram identical to **Section 4.1** above — Internet → ISP → TAP → Firewall → Inline Bypass → SSL Decryptor → Core Switch → TAP → Internal Sensors → OOB Network → SIEM.

**Critical Placement Decisions (4 marks):**

1. **TAP at perimeter (pre-firewall)** — captures reconnaissance, volumetric DDoS, and pre-filter attacks. Essential for **threat intelligence enrichment** and **post-incident forensics**. Without this placement, the IDPS never sees the attack that the firewall blocked, leading to **incomplete situational awareness**.

2. **TAP at the core / data center** — captures **east-west lateral movement** (e.g., Pass-the-Hash, SMB exploitation, ransomware spread). Modern breaches (e.g., SolarWinds, Colonial Pipeline) involved lateral movement that **perimeter-only sensors completely missed**. This placement is non-negotiable for mature SOCs.

3. **Inline IPS with bypass switch at the DMZ egress** — provides **active blocking** of outbound C2 callbacks, exfiltration attempts, and known-malware payloads. The bypass switch guarantees **zero single-point-of-failure**. Placed here (post-firewall) so that the IPS is not overwhelmed by the full Internet attack volume.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Aspect | Passive IDS | Inline IPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Position in topology** | Off-path (mirror copy) | In-path (serial) |
| **Latency** | **Zero** — production traffic is unaffected | Adds **0.5 ms – 50 ms** depending on hardware/software |
| **Block capability** | Cannot block; can only alert, log, or trigger **out-of-band** API responses (firewall rule push, BGP RTBH) | Can **drop, modify, rate-limit, reset** in real time |
| **Failure mode** | **Fail-open** — sensor crash = no impact on traffic | **Fail-open (with bypass)** or **fail-closed (no bypass)** — architectural decision |
| **Coverage fidelity** | Sees **all** mirrored frames (subject to SPAN limits) | Sees only **what passes through** it |
| **Best for** | Internal segments, east-west traffic, full-payload forensics | Perimeter, DMZ egress, VoIP/trading (only with hardware bypass) |
| **Cost** | Lower — no HA hardware needed | Higher — bypass switch + HA cluster |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A **hybrid architecture** combining both is the KTU-recommended enterprise design — passive IDS at the core for visibility, inline IPS at the perimeter for active blocking.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Network TAP and SPAN port** as traffic-capture mechanisms. Derive the **mathematical relationship for SPAN oversubscription** and show how it leads to silent packet drops under high load.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture of a hierarchical IDPS** for a multi-site enterprise. Include in your answer the roles of **Tier-1 sensors**, **Tier-2 regional aggregators**, and **Tier-3 global SIEM/SOAR**, with a labeled diagram.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Network TAP (3 marks):**
A Network TAP is an L1 hardware splitter that creates a **deterministic, full-fidelity copy** of the wire signal. Optical TAPs use a **beam splitter** (typical ratios 50:50, 70:30, 90:10) — the live port continues to production, the monitor port feeds the sensor. Copper TAPs use inductive coupling. **Failure mode is fail-open** — no power = transparent.

**SPAN Port (2 marks):**
A SPAN port is a software feature on managed switches (Cisco, Juniper, Arista) that copies frames from source ports/VLANs to a destination port. The destination port runs at the **same speed** as the source — oversubscription causes drops. **ERSPAN (Encapsulated Remote SPAN, RFC 7348)** wraps mirrored frames in **GRE + IP** for transport over L3, adding **38 bytes of header overhead** per frame.

**Mathematical Derivation of SPAN Oversubscription (2 marks):**

$$R_{span} = \frac{B_{monitored}}{B_{destination}}$$

If $R_{span} > 1$, the destination port **cannot keep up** with the source. For example, a 10 Gbps uplink mirrored to a 1 Gbps SPAN gives $R_{span} = 10$. Applying the **80% utilization safety threshold**:

$$B_{safe} = 0.8 \times B_{destination} = 0.8 \times 1\,\text{Gbps} = 800\,\text{Mbps}$$

Drop fraction at full 10 Gbps load:

$$\text{Drop} = 1 - \frac{0.8}{10} = 0.92 \;\;(92\%)$$

Hence **92% of frames are silently dropped**, producing catastrophic IDPS blind spots.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Hierarchical Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**
Reference **Section 4.3** above (Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3).

**Tier-1 Sensors — Local Collection (2 marks):**
Deployed at each site (branch office, data center, cloud VPC). Each performs **local detection, local buffering, and local first-line alerting**. Forwarded artifacts: NetFlow v5/v9/IPFIX, syslog CEF, PCAP samples. Sensors typically use **Snort, Suricata, or Zeek** with signature feeds from a central source.

**Tier-2 Regional Aggregators — Correlation (1 mark):**
Consolidate multiple Tier-1 feeds, perform **cross-site correlation** (e.g., failed logins from site A + lateral scan from site B = coordinated attack), apply **STIX/TAXII threat intel** enrichment, and reduce alert volume by **deduplication** before escalation.

**Tier-3 Global SOC + SOAR — Response (1 mark):**
Houses the **central SIEM** (Splunk, Elastic, QRadar), the **Threat Intelligence Platform** (MISP, Anomali), and the **SOAR engine** (Cortex XSOAR, Splunk SOAR). SOAR receives high-confidence alerts and **automates response playbooks** — pushing ACL changes back to Tier-1 firewalls via API, or executing BGP RTBH to null-route C2 infrastructure. This closes the loop in **seconds rather than hours**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ An **IDPS architecture** = sensor placement + capture mechanism + transport + analysis + response layer.
- ⭐ **Network TAP** = L1, full-fidelity, fail-open, **passive optical** preferred for mission-critical links.
- ⭐ **SPAN port** = L2, errored-frame-blind, ASIC-buffer-limited, **free but lossy** above ~80% utilization.
- ⭐ **Inline IPS** = in-path, can block, requires **bypass switch** for HA; adds latency.
- ⭐ **Passive IDS** = out-of-path, cannot block, **zero latency impact**, fail-open by definition.
- ⭐ **Five canonical placements**: pre-firewall, DMZ, internal core, WAN edge, cloud vTAP.
- ⭐ **SPAN oversubscription formula**: $R_{span} = B_{monitored} / B_{destination}$; **safe only if ≤ 1**.
- ⭐ **ERSPAN overhead**: **38 bytes** per mirrored frame → require jumbo frames end-to-end.
- ⭐ **Encrypted traffic (TLS 1.3, QUIC)** requires an **SSL/TLS decryptor** placed inline *before* the IDPS.
- ⭐ **Cluster load balancing** uses **weighted round-robin or 5-tuple hashing** to spread packets across sensors.
- ⭐ **Hierarchical architectures** scale to MSSPs and multi-site enterprises via **3-tier SOC model**.
- ⭐ **Cloud-native vTAP** (AWS VPC Traffic Mirroring, Azure vTAP) is mandatory for **east-west visibility** in cloud workloads.
- ⭐ **Bypass switch failover time** must be **< 5 ms** (faster than TCP RTO).
- ⭐ **Inline latency budget** for VoIP = $T_{budget} \le 65$ ms after link + firewall overhead.
- ⭐ **MTU** = **1500 B** standard, **9000 B** jumbo (recommended for SPAN aggregation).
- ⭐ **Symmetric vs asymmetric routing** must be considered — asymmetric traffic breaks stateful IDPS.
- ⭐ **Out-of-band management network** is mandatory — never carry sensor logs on the production plane.
- ⭐ **NetFlow v5/v9/IPFIX (RFC 5101)** is the canonical flow-export protocol for IDPS-tier telemetry.
- ⭐ **Hierarchical correlation** reduces SOC alert fatigue by **2–3 orders of magnitude** via dedup.
- ⭐ **Virtual sensors** (Cisco Virtual Defense, Snort VM) require **hypervisor-level port mirroring**.
- ⭐ Always **draw the diagram first** in 14-mark answers — a clean, labeled diagram is worth **2–3 marks** by itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS)**, the term **Security Capabilities** refers to the comprehensive set of functional attributes, operational features, and analytical competencies that an IDPS solution must possess to effectively **monitor**, **detect**, **analyze**, **log**, and **respond** to malicious activities targeting an organization's information assets. These capabilities define the *what*, *how*, and *why* of an IDPS's defensive envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT742 Module 3):**
> *Security Capabilities encompass the spectrum of functions an IDPS performs — from raw data acquisition (packet capture, log harvesting, host telemetry) to refined threat identification (signature matching, anomaly scoring, stateful inspection) and culminating in protective actions (session termination, packet dropping, quarantine, and forensic preservation).*

The five principal pillars of IDPS Security Capabilities are:

1. **Information Collection Capabilities** — Acquiring data from network, host, and application layers.
2. **Detection Capabilities** — Identifying malicious patterns, behaviors, or policy violations.
3. **Logging Capabilities** — Persistent recording of events for forensic and compliance use.
4. **Prevention Capabilities** — Active, real-time mitigation of detected threats.
5. **Performance / Analytical Capabilities** — Measurement of detection accuracy, throughput, and operational efficacy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Security System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Hospital"**
>
> Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. The hospital's overall security system is exactly an IDPS:
>
> - **CCTV cameras at every entrance, hallway, and pharmacy** = *Information Collection* (raw data gathering from network sensors / host agents).
> - **AI software that recognizes a known thief's face from a criminal database** = *Signature-Based Detection*.
> - **Motion sensors that flag unusual activity at 3 AM when no patients are around** = *Anomaly-Based Detection*.
> - **The security logbook where every visitor's entry is noted with timestamp** = *Logging Capability*.
> - **Automatic door-locking that traps a suspected intruder instantly** = *Prevention Capability* (active response).
> - **A dashboard showing "false alarm rate: 0.5%"** = *Performance & Analytical Capability*.
>
> Just as a hospital cannot function with only cameras but no response mechanism, an IDPS without prevention or logging capabilities is fundamentally incomplete.

## 1.3 Core Capability Metrics (Standard KTU Terminology)

The following standardized metrics are central to evaluating IDPS security capabilities and appear frequently in KTU examinations:

- **True Positive (TP)** — A real attack correctly identified as an attack.
- **False Positive (FP)** — A legitimate activity wrongly flagged as an attack (false alarm).
- **True Negative (TN)** — A legitimate activity correctly passed as benign.
- **False Negative (FN)** — A real attack missed by the system.
- **Detection Rate (DR)** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Accuracy (ACC)** = $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlight:** A high-quality IDPS is judged not merely by catching attacks (high DR) but also by minimizing false alarms (low FPR). The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is the standard graphical tool used to evaluate this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of the Five Security Capabilities

### 2.1.1 Information Collection Capabilities

This is the **first and foundational capability** — without accurate data ingestion, no detection or prevention is possible.

- **Network-Based Collection:** Captures raw packets using techniques like **port mirroring (SPAN)**, **network TAPs**, or **inline (in-band) sensors**. Protocols inspected include TCP, UDP, ICMP, HTTP/S, DNS, SMB, FTP, SMTP.
- **Host-Based Collection:** OS-level agents collect **system calls**, **audit logs**, **file integrity hashes (e.g., MD5, SHA-256)**, **registry changes**, and **process execution traces**.
- **Application-Based Collection:** Application-layer logs from web servers (Apache/Nginx), databases (MySQL/PostgreSQL), and middleware.
- **Wireless Collection:** 802.11 frame capture (monitor mode) for rogue AP and evil-twin detection.
- **Cloud / Virtual Collection:** Hypervisor-level introspection, vSwitch traffic mirroring, and cloud-native log streams (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** The placement of the sensor (inline vs. passive tap) fundamentally determines whether the IDPS can perform **prevention** or only **detection**.

### 2.1.2 Detection Capabilities

Detection is the analytical heart of an IDPS. The three principal detection methodologies are:

1. **Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)**
   - Uses pre-defined patterns (signatures) of known attacks.
   - Example: Snort rule `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"/etc/passwd"; msg:"Password file access attempt";)`.
   - **Strength:** Very low false positive rate, fast.
   - **Weakness:** Zero-day blind.

2. **Anomaly-Based Detection**
   - Builds a statistical or ML model of "normal" behavior and flags deviations.
   - Techniques: statistical profiling, clustering (k-means), neural networks, autoencoders.
   - **Strength:** Can detect zero-day attacks.
   - **Weakness:** Higher false positive rate.

3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis**
   - Understands the expected state machine of protocols (e.g., RFC-compliant HTTP, DNS, FTP).
   - Flags deviations such as out-of-order TCP flags, malformed DNS queries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Modern enterprise IDPS (e.g., Suricata, Snort 3.x, Zeek) are **hybrid systems** — they combine all three detection methodologies simultaneously.

### 2.1.3 Logging Capabilities

- **Alert Logs:** Concise notifications of detected events (severity, timestamp, signature ID, source/destination).
- **Session/Packet Logs:** Full payload capture for forensic replay.
- **Operational Logs:** System health, sensor uptime, configuration changes.
- **Compliance Logs:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR-aligned audit trails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Logs must be **tamper-resistant** (write-once, hashed, or sent to a SIEM in real time) to preserve forensic integrity — a core KTU Module 3 takeaway.

### 2.1.4 Prevention Capabilities

Only possible when the IDPS is deployed **inline (in-band)**:

- **Session Termination:** TCP RST injection to kill attacker connection.
- **Packet Dropping:** Drop malicious packets at the firewall/IPS engine.
- **IP Blacklisting / Blocking:** Dynamic ACL updates.
- **Quarantine:** Isolate compromised host via 802.1X or NAC integration.
- **Rate Limiting / Throttling:** Slow down brute-force attempts.

### 2.1.5 Performance & Analytical Capabilities

Quantified by:
- **Throughput** (Gbps of traffic inspected).
- **Latency** introduced (microseconds to milliseconds).
- **Detection Latency** (time from attack onset to alert).
- **Scalability** (number of sensors, rules, concurrent flows).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Metric / Formula** | **LaTeX Expression** | **Units / Range** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall) | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | 0 to 1 (ideal = 1) | Measures how many real attacks are caught |
| False Positive Rate (Fall-out) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | 0 to 1 (ideal = 0) | Measures false alarm burden |
| Precision | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | 0 to 1 | Quality of positive alerts |
| Accuracy | $ACC = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | 0 to 1 | Overall correctness |
| F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR}$ | 0 to 1 | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| Matthew's Correlation Coefficient | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced datasets |
| Throughput Requirement | $T_{req} = N \cdot \overline{P} \cdot 8$ | bits per second (bps) | Sizing IDPS hardware |
| Detection Latency | $t_{det} = t_{capture} + t_{analysis} + t_{alert}$ | seconds | Speed of response |
| Cost-Sensitive Detection | $Cost = C_{FP} \cdot FP + C_{FN} \cdot FN$ | currency units | Optimize threshold |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Notice:** All vertical bars (absolute value or conditional probability) inside the table above are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table parser corruption. Always follow this convention in KTU exam answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These capabilities are deployed across:
- **Enterprise perimeters** (Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto Networks IPS).
- **Data centers** (Suricata in NFV/SDN environments).
- **Cloud workloads** (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender, GCP Chronicle).
- **OT/SCADA networks** (Dragos, Nozomi Networks).
- **Endpoint domain** (EDR solutions like CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** The global IDPS market is shifting from pure NIDS/HIDS toward **Extended Detection and Response (XDR)**, which integrates network, endpoint, cloud, and identity capabilities under a unified analytical fabric — directly extending the traditional "Security Capabilities" framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deriving the Optimal Detection Threshold Using the Youden Index

The **Youden Index (J-statistic)** is the standard KTU-recommended method to find the optimal threshold for an anomaly-based IDPS detector.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Confusion Matrix

For an IDPS over a fixed evaluation window, the following counts are observed:

$$
TP = 47, \quad FP = 8, \quad FN = 3, \quad TN = 192
$$

We are asked to compute the **Detection Rate**, **False Positive Rate**, **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **F1-Score**, and **Youden Index**.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall)

The detection rate is the proportion of actual attacks correctly identified.

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
DR = \frac{47}{47 + 3} = \frac{47}{50} = 0.94
$$

**Interpretation:** The IDPS catches 94% of all real attacks. This is a strong detection rate by industry standards.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute False Positive Rate

The FPR is the proportion of legitimate activities wrongly flagged.

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
FPR = \frac{8}{8 + 192} = \frac{8}{200} = 0.04
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 4% of legitimate traffic is misclassified — well within the <5% benchmark for production-grade IDPS.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Accuracy

$$
ACC = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

$$
ACC = \frac{47 + 192}{47 + 192 + 8 + 3} = \frac{239}{250} = 0.956
$$

**Interpretation:** The IDPS is correct 95.6% of the time overall.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Precision

$$
Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
Precision = \frac{47}{47 + 8} = \frac{47}{55} \approx 0.8545
$$

**Interpretation:** When the IDPS raises an alert, it is correct ~85.45% of the time.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute F1-Score

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8545 \cdot 0.94}{0.8545 + 0.94}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8032}{1.7945} = 2 \cdot 0.4476 = 0.8952
$$

**Interpretation:** F1 of 0.8952 indicates excellent balance between precision and recall.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Compute the Youden Index (Optimal Threshold Metric)

The Youden Index is defined as:

$$
J = Sensitivity - (1 - Specificity) = DR - FPR
$$

Substituting:

$$
J = 0.94 - 0.04 = 0.90
$$

**Interpretation:** A Youden Index of 0.90 is considered excellent (theoretical maximum is 1.0). The current detection threshold is near-optimal.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Specificity (True Negative Rate)

$$
Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{192}{192 + 8} = \frac{192}{200} = 0.96
$$

Verify Youden:

$$
J = 0.94 - (1 - 0.96) = 0.94 - 0.04 = 0.90 \;\;\; \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 KTU Analytical Matrix — Mapping Capabilities to Attack Types

The following matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** because examiners frequently ask: *"Which IDPS capability counters which attack class?"*

| **Attack Class** | **Primary Capability Required** | **Secondary Capability** | **Detection Method** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Port Scan / Reconnaissance | Information Collection | Logging | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
| DoS / DDoS (SYN flood) | Prevention (inline) | Performance (throughput) | Anomaly-Based |
| Buffer Overflow | Detection (signature) | Logging | Signature-Based |
| SQL Injection | Detection (signature) | Logging | Signature / Heuristic |
| Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) | Application-level detection | Logging | Signature / Behavioral |
| Insider Threat (data exfil) | Host-based collection | Anomaly detection | Anomaly / Behavioral |
| Rootkit Installation | Host-based collection (file integrity) | Logging | Signature + Anomaly |
| Zero-Day Exploit | Anomaly / Heuristic detection | Logging | Anomaly / ML |
| Brute Force Login | Detection (rate-based) | Prevention (block) | Stateful + Threshold |
| Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) | Detection (cert / ARP anomalies) | Prevention (inline) | Stateful + Anomaly |
| Wireless Rogue AP | Wireless collection | Logging | Anomaly (signal/BSSID) |
| Cloud Misconfiguration | Cloud-native log collection | Anomaly | Policy + Anomaly |
| Lateral Movement | Host + Network correlation | Logging (SIEM) | Behavioral Analytics |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Notice that **Logging** appears as a *secondary capability* for nearly every attack class. This is by design — logging is the universal "fallback" that enables post-incident forensics, regulatory compliance, and SIEM correlation. Examiners often test this principle.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost-Optimal Threshold via Expected Loss Minimization

For an organization that values false negatives (missed attacks) at $C_{FN}$ = \$10,000 and false positives at $C_{FP}$ = \$50, the expected loss per event at threshold $\tau$ is:

$$
E[Loss \mid \tau] = C_{FP} \cdot P(FP \mid \tau) + C_{FN} \cdot P(FN \mid \tau)
$$

For our confusion matrix counts (treating counts as probabilities over the dataset):

$$
E[Loss] = 50 \cdot 8 + 10000 \cdot 3 = 400 + 30000 = 30{,}400
$$

If we could raise the threshold to eliminate 2 false positives but accept 1 additional false negative:

$$
E[Loss]_{new} = 50 \cdot 6 + 10000 \cdot 4 = 300 + 40000 = 40{,}300
$$

$$
\Delta E[Loss] = 40{,}300 - 30{,}400 = +9{,}900
$$

**Conclusion:** Lowering the threshold (the current setting) is the cost-optimal decision. This is the **expected-loss framework** for IDPS threshold tuning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Pillar IDPS Security Capabilities Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **canonical KTU-aligned block architecture** of an IDPS, mapping the five security capabilities as functional modules with their data flow interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Network Traffic Incoming] --> B1[Inline Sensor / TAP]
    A2[Host Endpoint Agent] --> C1[Host Telemetry Collector]
    A3[Application Server Logs] --> D1[App Log Aggregator]
    A4[Cloud API Stream] --> E1[Cloud Log Ingestor]

    B1 --> F1[Information Collection Module]
    C1 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1

    F1 --> G1[Signature Engine]
    F1 --> G2[Anomaly Engine]
    F1 --> G3[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]
    F1 --> G4[Heuristic and ML Engine]

    G1 --> H1[Detection Capability Layer]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1

    H1 --> I1[Logging and Alerting Module]
    I1 --> J1[SIEM and Forensics Storage]

    H1 --> K1[Prevention Decision Engine]
    K1 --> K2[Session Reset RST]
    K1 --> K3[Packet Drop]
    K1 --> K4[Dynamic ACL Block]
    K1 --> K5[Quarantine Host]

    I1 --> L1[Performance and Analytics Dashboard]
    K1 --> L1
    F1 --> L1

    L1 --> M1{Tuning Loop}
    M1 -->|Update Rules| G1
    M1 -->|Retrain Models| G2
    M1 -->|Update Thresholds| G3
```

**Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU 14-mark diagram questions):**

- **Top Tier (Sources):** Data ingestion from network, host, application, and cloud.
- **Information Collection Module:** The central data-normalization layer.
- **Detection Layer:** Four parallel engines run simultaneously — this is the **hybrid detection paradigm** emphasized in KTU Module 3.
- **Logging Module:** Diverts to SIEM and forensic archive.
- **Prevention Engine:** Only reachable if the sensor is **inline** (block diagram branch).
- **Performance Dashboard:** Feeds a **closed-loop tuning cycle** back to the detection engines — a feature of mature, self-improving IDPS.

## 4.2 Capability-to-Attack Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAP1[Information Collection] --> ATK1[Reconnaissance]
    CAP1 --> ATK2[Wireless Attacks]
    CAP1 --> ATK3[Cloud Misconfig]

    CAP2[Detection Capability] --> ATK4[Zero Day]
    CAP2 --> ATK5[Buffer Overflow]
    CAP2 --> ATK6[SQLi and XSS]
    CAP2 --> ATK7[Insider Threat]

    CAP3[Logging Capability] --> ATK8[Compliance Audit]
    CAP3 --> ATK9[Forensic Investigation]
    CAP3 --> ATK10[Incident Reconstruction]

    CAP4[Prevention Capability] --> ATK11[DDoS]
    CAP4 --> ATK12[Brute Force]
    CAP4 --> ATK13[Lateral Movement]

    CAP5[Performance Capability] --> ATK14[High Throughput DoS]
    CAP5 --> ATK15[Encrypted Traffic Filter]
```

## 4.3 Inline vs. Passive Sensor Capability Diagram

This diagram is **critical for KTU 14-mark questions** that contrast NIDS (Network Intrusion Detection System) vs. NIPS (Network Intrusion Prevention System).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inline_NIPS
        I1[Inbound Packet] --> I2{IDPS Engine}
        I2 -->|Benign| I3[Forward to Internal Network]
        I2 -->|Malicious| I4[Drop and Log]
        I4 --> I5[Active Response RST Block Drop]
    end

    subgraph Passive_NIDS
        P1[SPAN Port Mirror] --> P2{IDPS Engine}
        P2 -->|Benign| P3[Discard]
        P2 -->|Malicious| P4[Generate Alert Only]
        P4 --> P5[Passive Response Alert Log]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Diagram:** Only the **inline (NIPS)** configuration enables the **Prevention Capability**. A passive (NIDS) configuration can *only* **Detect and Log**. This single distinction is worth 3-4 marks in KTU 14-mark essay questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**List and briefly explain the five major security capabilities of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five major security capabilities of an IDPS are:

1. **Information Collection Capability** — Acquiring raw data from network packets, host audit logs, application logs, and cloud telemetry for analysis. (0.6 marks)
2. **Detection Capability** — Analyzing collected data using signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis techniques to identify malicious activity. (0.6 marks)
3. **Logging Capability** — Persistently recording detected events, alert details, and forensic data in tamper-resistant storage for investigation and compliance. (0.6 marks)
4. **Prevention Capability** — Taking real-time active countermeasures such as dropping malicious packets, terminating sessions via TCP RST, or quarantining compromised hosts. (0.6 marks)
5. **Performance / Analytical Capability** — Quantifying the IDPS's detection accuracy, throughput, latency, false positive/negative rates, and tuning thresholds for optimal operation. (0.6 marks)

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Differentiate between inline and passive sensor deployment in IDPS. Which one enables prevention capability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Inline (In-Band) Sensor** | **Passive (Out-of-Band) Sensor** |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic Path | Traffic physically flows through the IDPS | Traffic mirrored via SPAN/TAP, not through IDPS |
| Latency | Adds small forwarding latency | Zero forwarding latency |
| Failure Impact | Failure can break connectivity (fail-open/closed) | Failure has no impact on traffic flow |
| Capabilities | **Detection + Prevention** | **Detection + Logging only** |
| Use Case | NIPS, Web Application Firewall | NIDS, forensic sensor |

The **inline sensor** is the one that enables **Prevention Capability** because the IDPS sits in the data path and can drop or modify malicious packets before they reach the destination. (1 mark for naming inline)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 3 Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two alternatives. We provide two fully-modeled 14-mark questions below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Explain in detail the three primary detection methodologies used in IDPS. Compare their strengths and weaknesses with respect to zero-day attack detection.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The three primary detection methodologies are:

**(1) Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection) — 2.5 marks**

- Operates by comparing observed events against a database of pre-defined attack patterns (signatures).
- Example: Snort rule: `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"cmd.exe"; msg:"Possible command execution";)`.
- **Strengths:** Very low false positive rate; fast matching; easy to interpret alerts.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot detect zero-day attacks (no signature exists yet); requires constant rule updates; signature explosion.

**(2) Anomaly-Based Detection — 2.5 marks**

- Builds a baseline model of "normal" behavior (statistical, ML, or rule-based) and flags statistically significant deviations.
- Techniques: statistical profiling, k-means clustering, neural networks, autoencoders, isolation forests.
- **Strengths:** Can detect previously unseen (zero-day) attacks; adapts to network evolution.
- **Weaknesses:** Higher false positive rate; requires a "training period"; computationally expensive.

**(3) Stateful Protocol Analysis — 2 marks**

- Tracks the protocol state machine (e.g., expected TCP handshake sequence, DNS query/response pairing) and flags deviations.
- **Strengths:** Detects protocol abuse and malformed traffic; effective against reconnaissance.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot detect attacks that conform to RFC behavior (e.g., application-layer exploits within valid HTTP); requires deep protocol decoders.

**Comparison for Zero-Day Detection:**

| **Methodology** | **Zero-Day Detection Capability** |
|---|---|
| Signature-Based | **Poor** — no signature exists |
| Anomaly-Based | **Excellent** — learns deviation, not signature |
| Stateful Protocol | **Moderate** — only if zero-day violates protocol semantics |

**Conclusion:** For zero-day defense, IDPS must be **hybrid**, layering anomaly detection on top of signature-based engines (2 marks for synthesis).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**For an IDPS evaluated over a 30-day window, the following confusion matrix was obtained: TP = 320, FP = 25, FN = 40, TN = 1615. Calculate the Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Accuracy, Precision, F1-Score, and Youden Index. Comment on the IDPS's operational quality.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Detection Rate (1 mark)**

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{320}{320+40} = \frac{320}{360} \approx 0.8889
$$

**Step 2: False Positive Rate (1 mark)**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{25}{25+1615} = \frac{25}{1640} \approx 0.0152
$$

**Step 3: Accuracy (1 mark)**

$$
ACC = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{320+1615}{320+1615+25+40} = \frac{1935}{2000} = 0.9675
$$

**Step 4: Precision (1 mark)**

$$
Precision = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{320}{320+25} = \frac{320}{345} \approx 0.9275
$$

**Step 5: F1-Score (1 mark)**

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9275 \cdot 0.8889}{0.9275 + 0.8889}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8244}{1.8164} = 2 \cdot 0.4539 = 0.9078
$$

**Step 6: Youden Index (1 mark)**

$$
J = DR - FPR = 0.8889 - 0.0152 = 0.8737
$$

**Step 7: Operational Quality Comment (1 mark)**

The IDPS demonstrates **excellent operational quality**:
- Detection Rate of 88.89% is high.
- False Positive Rate of 1.52% is well below the 5% industry benchmark.
- F1-Score of 0.9078 indicates strong balance.
- Youden Index of 0.8737 is near-optimal.
- The only weakness is the **FN count of 40**, suggesting the threshold could be marginally lowered to catch more attacks — at the cost of slightly higher FPR.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Discuss the Logging Capability of IDPS in detail. What are the different types of logs maintained, and what are the key design considerations for log integrity and storage?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Logging Capability of an IDPS refers to the systematic, persistent recording of all detected events, alerts, and operational metadata to support forensic investigation, regulatory compliance, and post-incident analysis.

**Types of Logs Maintained (4 marks):**

1. **Alert Logs** — Concise records of detected threats, including timestamp, severity, signature ID, source/destination IP, and action taken.
2. **Session/Packet Logs** — Full or partial payload capture of suspicious sessions for forensic replay (e.g., pcap format).
3. **Operational Logs** — Sensor health, restart events, configuration changes, rule updates, and system errors.
4. **Audit/Compliance Logs** — Tamper-resistant records aligned with regulatory frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001).
5. **Statistical Logs** — Aggregated traffic baselines and anomaly scores for trend analysis.

**Key Design Considerations (2 marks):**

- **Integrity:** Use cryptographic hashing (SHA-256), write-once storage, or real-time forwarding to a SIEM to prevent log tampering by attackers.
- **Storage:** Tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) with retention aligned to compliance (typically 90 days hot, 1-7 years cold).
- **Time Synchronization:** NTP-based uniform timestamps across all sensors.
- **Standardization:** Use common formats (CEF, LEEF, Syslog, JSON) for SIEM ingestion.
- **Scalability:** Distributed logging with log shippers (e.g., Filebeat, Fluentd).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the Prevention Capability of an IDPS. List and describe at least five active prevention techniques. Under what deployment configuration is prevention possible?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Prevention Capability of an IDPS refers to its ability to take **real-time, active countermeasures** to block, terminate, or contain detected threats before they can cause damage.

**Deployment Requirement (1 mark):** Prevention is possible **only in inline (in-band) deployment** where the IDPS sensor sits physically in the traffic path (i.e., configured as a NIPS, not a NIDS).

**Five Active Prevention Techniques (5 marks):**

1. **Session Termination (TCP RST Injection):** The IDPS injects a TCP RESET packet to both ends of an attack session, abruptly tearing it down. (1 mark)
2. **Packet Dropping:** Malicious packets are silently dropped at the IPS engine, preventing them from reaching the destination host. (1 mark)
3. **IP Blacklisting / Dynamic ACL Updates:** The IDPS pushes real-time ACL changes to the perimeter firewall or router to block attacker IP addresses. (1 mark)
4. **Quarantine of Compromised Hosts:** Integration with NAC (Network Access Control) like 802.1X allows the IDPS to isolate an infected endpoint to a remediation VLAN. (1 mark)
5. **Rate Limiting / Throttling:** The IDPS enforces bandwidth or connection-rate caps to mitigate brute-force or low-and-slow DoS attacks. (1 mark)

**Additional Note:** Modern IDPS can also integrate with **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)** platforms to trigger automated playbooks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Detection with Prevention:** A passive NIDS **cannot prevent** — students often mistakenly credit prevention capability to a SPAN-mirrored sensor. Always state the deployment mode explicitly. (-2 marks risk)
> 2. **Skipping Mathematical Units:** In the confusion-matrix questions, always show the **denominator's sum** before computing the ratio. Examiners deduct marks for incomplete fractions. (-1 mark risk)
> 3. **Omitting the F1 or Youden Index:** When a question asks for "operational quality", F1 and Youden are the **two most valued metrics**. Skipping them is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 4. **One-line answers in 14-mark questions:** Each sub-part requires **structured headings**, **examples**, and a **concluding remark**. A 3-line answer for a 7-mark question will be capped at 3-4 marks maximum.
> 5. **Ignoring Log Integrity:** When discussing logging, students often list log types but forget to mention **tamper resistance** — a 1-mark item that is frequently tested.
> 6. **Forgetting Hybrid Detection:** In any "compare detection methods" question, the **concluding synthesis line** ("modern IDPS use hybrid approaches") is worth 1-2 marks and is often omitted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Security Capabilities (Module 3):**

- **Five Pillars:** Information Collection → Detection → Logging → Prevention → Performance/Analytics.
- **Sensor Placement Matters:** Inline = Detection + Prevention; Passive (SPAN/TAP) = Detection + Logging only.
- **Three Detection Methods:** Signature (low FP, zero-day blind), Anomaly (catches zero-day, higher FP), Stateful Protocol (protocol abuse).
- **Hybrid IDPS** is the industry norm — combine all three detection methods in a single engine.
- **Key Metrics (must memorize formulas):** DR, FPR, Accuracy, Precision, F1, Youden Index.
- **Youden Index Formula:** $J = DR - FPR$; range 0 to 1; >0.8 considered excellent.
- **Logging is Forensic Backbone:** Always tamper-resistant, NTP-synchronized, SIEM-fed.
- **Prevention Techniques (memorize 5):** TCP RST, Packet Drop, Dynamic ACL, Quarantine, Rate Limiting.
- **Information Collection Sources:** Network TAP/SPAN, Host agents, Application logs, Cloud streams, Wireless monitor mode.
- **Modern Evolution:** Traditional IDPS is converging into **XDR (Extended Detection and Response)** — a KTU-favored "Industry 4.0" angle.
- **Cloud-Native IDPS:** AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender, GCP Chronicle — frequently cited in KTU viva questions.
- **Common Pitfall:** NIDS ≠ NIPS. The letter "P" in NIPS = Prevention. Always check sensor mode in exam answers.
- **Industry Benchmark:** FPR < 5% and DR > 90% are the standard production targets for a well-tuned IDPS.
- **Examiner's Favorite:** Confusion-matrix calculations (always appear in at least one KTU ESE paper per academic year).
- **Closing Line to Use in Essays:** *"An effective IDPS is not defined by the number of alerts it generates, but by its ability to balance high detection accuracy with minimal false positives while enabling real-time prevention."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prevention Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prevention Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prevention Capabilities** in the context of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) refer to the **active, automated response mechanisms** that an in-line security device can execute to **intercept, block, contain, or terminate** a malicious network flow or host process *before* it completes its attack objective. Unlike passive detection (which only logs and alerts), prevention capabilities convert the IDPS from a *witness* into a *first responder*.

In the KTU 2024 scheme terminology, an **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is the operational realization of these prevention capabilities, distinguished from a pure **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** by its ability to sit directly in the data path and enforce countermeasures in real time.

The formal taxonomy recognized by KTU PECCT742 Module 3 categorizes prevention capabilities along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Options | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Mode** | Inline vs Passive (Tap/SPAN) | Inline = in the traffic path; Passive = copy only |
| **Response Timing** | Proactive vs Reactive | Proactive = anticipate threat; Reactive = respond to observed event |
| **Response Action** | Soft vs Hard | Soft = log/alert/reset; Hard = drop/shut/isolate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Prevention capabilities are *only* available when the IDPS sensor is deployed **inline** (in-line / fail-open or fail-closed mode). A tap-mode sensor physically cannot block traffic — it can only see a copy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bouncer at the Club Door

Imagine a busy nightclub entrance:

- An **IDS** is a CCTV camera mounted above the door. It records every face, every incident, and forwards footage to a security office. The bouncers only find out about trouble *after* the cameras do — by which time the fight is already happening inside.
- An **IPS (Prevention Capabilities enabled)** is the bouncer standing *at* the door itself. The bouncer has a guest list (signature database), a metal detector (anomaly engine), and a direct physical connection to the door. When a troublemaker arrives:
  - The bouncer can **refuse entry** (drop packet).
  - The bouncer can **cut the music and shine a spotlight** (TCP reset / ICMP unreachable).
  - The bouncer can **put the offender in a side room** (quarantine / sandbox).
  - The bouncer can **call the police and lock the door** (block source IP / isolate host).

The crucial difference is **physical placement**: the bouncer must be *in the doorway*, not just watching from across the street. This is why the **inline deployment requirement** is the single most defining characteristic of prevention capability in IDPS.

### 1.3 Why Prevention Capabilities Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Speed Mismatch Problem:** Modern network attacks (ransomware propagation, credential stuffing, worm exploits) complete in **milliseconds to seconds**. Human incident response teams need **minutes to hours**. Without automated prevention, the *mean time to contain* (MTTC) of an attack is bounded below by human reaction time, which is unacceptable for high-velocity threats. Prevention capabilities collapse MTTC to **sub-second** values.

The standard KTU-recognized performance benchmark is that an inline IPS must make its block/allow decision within **< 1 millisecond** per packet to avoid degrading line-rate throughput on a **1 Gbps** link, and within **< 100 microseconds** on **10 Gbps** links — these are the engineering constraints that drive the entire prevention subsystem design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Prevention Capability

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus structure for Module 3, prevention capabilities decompose into five primary operational pillars. Each pillar is an *engineered response* the IPS can invoke when its detection subsystem fires a high-confidence alert.

#### Pillar 1 — Packet Drop (Hard Block)

- The IPS silently **discards** offending packets and forwards no response to either endpoint.
- The attacker never knows why their payload never reached the target; the legitimate recipient never processes it.
- This is the **most common and safest** prevention action because it causes no protocol-level side effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Note:** Packet drop is also called *blackhole filtering* when applied to entire IP prefixes. It is the same primitive used by BGP Flowspec and RTBH (Remotely Triggered Black Hole) routing.

#### Pillar 2 — Connection Termination (TCP Reset / RST Injection)

- For TCP flows, the IPS injects a **TCP RST** packet to *both* the client and the server, using the correct sequence/acknowledgment numbers spoofed from the observed flow.
- Both endpoints immediately tear down the half-open and full-open sockets, freeing resources.
- This is essential for stopping **session-layer attacks** (e.g., HTTP session hijacking, FTP bounce, SSH brute force mid-session).

The TCP RST injection requires the IPS to maintain per-flow state. Formally, for a TCP flow with state $S = (src\_ip, dst\_ip, src\_port, dst\_port, seq\_num, ack\_num)$, the RST packet is constructed as:

$$
\text{RST}_{payload} = \{(FIN=0, RST=1, ACK=1) \mid seq = S.ack\_num, ack = S.seq\_num + 1\}
$$

This makes the reset **un-forgeable** by the attacker in real time, because the sequence number window is held inside the IPS state table.

#### Pillar 3 — Rate Limiting & Traffic Shaping (Soft Throttle)

- The IPS does *not* block the offending flow outright; instead it **caps the bandwidth** or **packet rate** the source can send toward the target.
- Useful for **DoS / DDoS mitigation** where outright blocking is risky (a real customer might share a NATed IP with an attacker).
- The rate is enforced using a **token bucket** algorithm with parameters $(r, b)$ where $r$ is the refill rate (tokens/sec) and $b$ is the burst capacity (tokens).

The token-bucket occupancy at time $t$ after a packet of size $p$ bytes arrives is governed by:

$$
T(t) = \min\left(b, \, T(t-1) + r \cdot \Delta t - p\right)
$$

- If $T(t) \geq 0$, the packet is forwarded; if $T(t) < 0$, the packet is queued or dropped (configurable).

#### Pillar 4 — Quarantine & Host Isolation

- The IPS uses a coordination channel (e.g., **RADIUS CoA**, **802.1X**, or a **northbound API** to a switch/SDN controller) to push the offending host into a **remediation VLAN**.
- The host retains IP connectivity only to a captive portal / patch server; all other destinations become unreachable.
- This pillar is critical for **post-compromise containment** of malware-infected endpoints (lateral movement prevention).

#### Pillar 5 — Application-Layer Enforcement

- The IPS terminates the application protocol itself (acts as a **reverse proxy / transparent bridge**) and can issue protocol-specific responses:
  - HTTP: respond with **HTTP 403 Forbidden** or inject a JavaScript CAPTCHA.
  - SMTP: reject the message with **SMTP 5xx** codes at the **MAIL FROM** or **RCPT TO** stage.
  - DNS: respond with a **NXDOMAIN** or a sinkhole IP for malicious domains.
- This is the deepest prevention capability and requires full protocol reassembly and L7 intelligence.

### 2.2 The Decision Lattice: When Does Each Pillar Fire?

The IPS does not invoke prevention capabilities blindly. The decision follows a **confidence ladder** mapped to the alert's severity and false-positive cost:

| Detection Confidence | False-Positive Cost | Recommended Pillar | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High ($\geq$ 0.95)** | Low (e.g., known malware hash) | Hard Block (Pillar 1) or RST (Pillar 2) | Direct, immediate, no user impact |
| **High ($\geq$ 0.95)** | High (e.g., production IP) | Quarantine (Pillar 4) | Isolate for forensics, do not drop |
| **Medium (0.70 – 0.95)** | Low | Rate Limit (Pillar 3) | Throttle, do not block |
| **Medium (0.70 – 0.95)** | High | App-Layer Challenge (Pillar 5) | Force re-authentication, then re-judge |
| **Low ($<$ 0.70)** | Any | Log only (no prevention) | Detection mode — prevent is unsafe |

### 2.3 The Inline Imperative: Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed

Because an inline IPS is a *physical* part of the wire, its failure mode is a safety-critical design choice.

- **Fail-Open (Bypass Mode):** If the IPS crashes, traffic flows *around* it through a hardware relay. The network stays up; the organization is unprotected for the duration of the failure.
- **Fail-Closed (Block-All Mode):** If the IPS crashes, all traffic is stopped. The network goes down; the organization is provably secure.

The selection is governed by the **availability vs integrity trade-off**, quantified by the formula:

$$
\text{Choose Fail-Open if } \quad \text{MTBF}_{IPS}^{-1} \cdot C_{downtime} \;>\; \text{MTBF}_{IPS}^{-1} \cdot C_{breach}
$$

where $C_{downtime}$ is the cost of network outage and $C_{breach}$ is the cost of an unfiltered breach. For most enterprise deployments $C_{breach} \gg C_{downtime}$, so **fail-closed** is preferred; for medical/aviation/life-safety networks the inequality reverses, and **fail-open** is mandatory.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize the entries below; they appear frequently in 14-mark derivation questions.**

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Rule | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{max}$ | Max throughput sustained by inline IPS | $L_{max} = \dfrac{N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate}}{H_{overhead}}$ | Gbps | $P_{rate}$ is per-core packet rate |
| $T_{decision}$ | Per-packet decision budget | $T_{decision} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot L_{max}}$ | seconds | 2x for symmetric full-duplex |
| $MTTC$ | Mean Time to Contain | $MTTC = T_{detect} + T_{respond}$ | seconds | IPS aims for $MTTC < 1$ s |
| $T(t)$ | Token bucket occupancy | $T(t) = \min(b, \, T(t-1) + r\Delta t - p)$ | bytes | Negative = drop/queue |
| $FPR$ | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | dimensionless | Trade-off vs $TPR$ |
| $TPR$ | True Positive Rate | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless | Also called *Recall* |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair (sensor) | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum t_{repair}}{N_{incidents}}$ | hours | Drives fail-open/closed choice |
| $R_{score}$ | Composite risk score | $R_{score} = w_1 C + w_2 V + w_3 T$ | 0–100 | Confidence, Vulnerability, Threat |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Prevention capabilities are not academic — they are the **economic justification** for purchasing an IPS instead of a cheaper IDS. The ROI calculation most enterprises use is:

$$
ROI_{\%} = 100 \times \dfrac{(C_{breach} \cdot P_{breach}^{noIPS}) - (C_{breach} \cdot P_{breach}^{IPS}) - C_{IPS}}{C_{IPS}}
$$

A typical mid-size enterprise sees a **3-year ROI in the 200% – 600%** range when prevention capabilities are enabled, primarily because $P_{breach}^{IPS}$ drops by an order of magnitude versus detection-only mode.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following section provides **exhaustive derivations** of the key quantitative relationships in IPS prevention capability. Every algebraic step is written out in full; no shortcuts or implicit jumps are taken.

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Sustainable Line-Rate Through an Inline IPS

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark setup):** An inline IPS has $N_{cores} = 8$ processing cores. Each core can inspect $P_{rate} = 1.2$ million packets per second (Mpps) at full signature-database load. The IPS must perform a bidirectional inspection, so it counts both ingress and egress packets against the budget. What is the maximum throughput $L_{max}$ in Gbps, assuming an average packet size of $S_{avg} = 800$ bytes? Take the hardware-internal inspection overhead factor as $H_{overhead} = 1.15$ (i.e., 15% of throughput is consumed by memory bus, DMA, and state-table access, leaving 85% for the actual decision).

**Step 1 — Define the raw aggregate packet rate.** The $N_{cores}$ cores operate in parallel, so the total raw packet-processing rate is:

$$
P_{total} = N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
P_{total} = 8 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{6} = 9.6 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert packets to bytes using the average packet size.** The line-rate in bits per second is:

$$
L_{raw} = P_{total} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8
$$

Substituting:

$$
L_{raw} = 9.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot 800 \cdot 8 = 61.44 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/second}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the overhead factor.** A fraction of the raw throughput is consumed by the bus and memory access overhead; only the remainder is available for the actual decision pipeline:

$$
L_{max} = \frac{L_{raw}}{H_{overhead}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
L_{max} = \frac{61.44 \times 10^{9}}{1.15} = 53.426 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/second}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Gbps.**

$$
L_{max} = 53.426 \; \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The IPS can sustain approximately **53.4 Gbps** of bidirectional inspected throughput under full signature load. Beyond this rate, packets will be dropped *by the IPS itself*, not by the network — a self-inflicted outage that must be planned for in capacity engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The $H_{overhead}$ factor is *not* optional. Forgetting to divide by it is the most common mark-losing error in this derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Per-Packet Decision Budget for Latency-Sensitive Links

**Problem:** Given the throughput $L_{max}$ derived above, what is the maximum allowable per-packet decision time $T_{decision}$ if the link is full-duplex (i.e., packets are arriving on both ingress and egress simultaneously)?

**Step 1 — Total packets per second at line rate.** The aggregate number of packets per second that must be processed is:

$$
P_{line} = \frac{L_{max}}{S_{avg} \cdot 8}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{line} = \frac{53.426 \times 10^{9}}{800 \cdot 8} = 8.347 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to seconds per packet.** The reciprocal gives the per-packet time budget:

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{1}{P_{line}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{1}{8.347 \times 10^{6}} = 1.198 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the full-duplex factor of 2** (the IPS must service ingress and egress independently; each direction gets half the time budget):

$$
T_{decision} = \frac{T_{packet}}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{decision} = \frac{1.198 \times 10^{-7}}{2} = 5.99 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to microseconds.**

$$
T_{decision} \approx 60 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** Each packet must be decided upon within **approximately 60 microseconds** to keep up with line rate. This is the engineering reason modern IPS appliances use **ASIC/FPGA acceleration** rather than general-purpose CPUs beyond 10 Gbps.

### 3.3 Derivation: Token-Bucket Rate-Limit Verification

**Problem:** A rate-limit rule is configured with refill rate $r = 10 \; \text{MB/s}$ and burst capacity $b = 2 \; \text{MB}$. A flow attempts to send a burst of 10 packets, each of size 200 KB, back-to-back ($\Delta t \to 0$ between them). Verify whether each packet is admitted or rejected.

**Step 1 — Initialize the bucket.** Assume the bucket starts full:

$$
T(0) = b = 2 \times 10^{6} \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Process Packet 1 ($p_1 = 200 \; \text{KB} = 200{,}000$ bytes, $\Delta t \approx 0$).**

$$
T(1) = \min(b, \, T(0) + r \cdot 0 - p_1) = \min(2{,}000{,}000, \, 2{,}000{,}000 - 200{,}000) = 1{,}800{,}000 \; \text{bytes}
$$

Since $T(1) \geq 0$: **Admit**.

**Step 3 — Process Packet 2 ($p_2 = 200{,}000$ bytes, $\Delta t \approx 0$).**

$$
T(2) = \min(2{,}000{,}000, \, 1{,}800{,}000 - 200{,}000) = 1{,}600{,}000 \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Admit**.

**Step 4 — Process Packet 3 to Packet 10 (similar arithmetic).** Each packet consumes 200 KB, so by Packet 10:

$$
T(10) = 2{,}000{,}000 - 10 \cdot 200{,}000 = 0 \; \text{bytes}
$$

The 10th packet brings the bucket to exactly zero. If an 11th packet arrives, $T(11) = -200{,}000 < 0$ and it would be **rejected / queued**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The bucket admits the first 10 packets instantly (since $\Delta t \approx 0$ means no refill happens during the burst), then requires a refill of:

$$
t_{refill} = \frac{200{,}000}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \; \text{ms}
$$

before the next 200 KB packet can be admitted. This is the *enforcement* behavior of the rate-limit pillar.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: IDS vs IPS Capability

| Capability Attribute | IDS (Detection Only) | IPS (Prevention Enabled) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inline placement required** | No (tap/SPAN OK) | Yes (must be in wire path) | IPS is a *chokepoint* by design |
| **Latency contribution** | 0 (passive copy) | 1 – 100 $\mu s$ per packet | Budget must be allocated in SLA |
| **Single point of failure** | No | Yes (unless bypass) | Fail-open / fail-closed required |
| **False positive blast radius** | Operator inbox | Production outage | Confidence threshold must be higher for IPS |
| **Detection latency** | Seconds to minutes | Sub-millisecond | IPS collapses MTTC by 3 orders of magnitude |
| **Cost (CAPEX)** | Low (sensor only) | High (appliance + bypass) | Justified by breach-cost ROI |
| **Throughput loss** | 0 | 5% – 25% | Trade-off with security depth |
| **State-table size** | Optional | Mandatory | Deep inspection needs $\geq 1$ M concurrent flows |

### 3.5 The Prevention Action Selection Matrix (Decision Tree)

The IPS chooses among the five prevention pillars via a deterministic decision tree driven by the detection subsystem's output and contextual policy. The matrix below is the canonical reference for KTU 14-mark questions on the topic.

| Step | Decision Variable | Possible Values | Branch Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the sensor inline? | No | Downgrade to IDS mode (no prevention) |
| 2 | Confidence score $C$ of alert | $C \geq 0.95$ | Eligible for hard action |
|  |  | $0.70 \leq C < 0.95$ | Soft action only |
|  |  | $C < 0.70$ | Log only |
| 3 | Asset value $V$ of target | High | Prefer Quarantine, not Drop |
|  |  | Low | Prefer Drop or RST |
| 4 | Is the source IP a NAT'd shared address? | Yes | Avoid hard block; use Rate Limit |
|  |  | No | Hard block permissible |
| 5 | Is the protocol L7 reassembled? | Yes | App-layer challenge available |
|  |  | No | L3/L4 action only |
| 6 | Is the action reversible? | No | Require operator approval (soft block) |
|  |  | Yes | Auto-apply |
