## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Reminder

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid blocks below use alphanumeric node identifiers prefixed with letters (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `d1`), and labels contain only plain uppercase text — no bold, italics, or HTML markup inside the quoted labels. This guarantees first-attempt rendering in Mermaid 10+.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Inline IPS in a Network Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1[EXTERNAL NETWORK] --> n2[EDGE ROUTER]
    n2 --> n3[BYPASS SWITCH FAIL OPEN RELAY]
    n3 --> n4[IPS APPLIANCE INLINE ENGINE]
    n4 --> n5[INTERNAL CORE SWITCH]
    n5 --> n6[PROTECTED SERVERS AND HOSTS]
    n4 --> n7[MANAGEMENT CONSOLE SIGNATURE DB]
    n4 --> n8[SIEM AND LOG AGGREGATOR]
    n9[OPERATOR] --> n7
    n8 --> n10[INCIDENT RESPONSE PLATFORM]
    n3 -. FAULT STATE BYPASS .- n5
```

**Interpretation:**
- Packets traverse the **bypass switch** which is a hardware relay sitting in series with the IPS. Under normal conditions, the relay is "engaged" — all traffic passes *through* the IPS. If the IPS fails (heartbeat loss), the relay physically shorts around it, restoring connectivity.
- The IPS streams its prevention-action decisions and alerts to the **SIEM** and the **Management Console**, closing the loop with the operator.

### 4.3 Prevention Capability Workflow — Packet Arrival to Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[PACKET ARRIVES AT INLINE IPS] --> p2{PROTOCOL REASSEMBLY BUFFER}
    p2 -->|TCP UDP RAW| p3[STATE TABLE LOOKUP]
    p2 -->|L7 PAYLOAD READY| p4[DEEP INSPECTION ENGINE]
    p3 --> p5[SIGNATURE MATCH MODULE]
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6{CONFIDENCE SCORE C}
    p6 -->|C GREATER 0.95| p7[HARD ACTION SELECTOR]
    p6 -->|0.70 TO 0.95| p8[SOFT ACTION SELECTOR]
    p6 -->|C LESS 0.70| p9[LOG ONLY AND FORWARD]
    p7 --> p10[DROP PACKET]
    p7 --> p11[INJECT TCP RST]
    p7 --> p12[QUARANTINE HOST]
    p8 --> p13[RATE LIMIT WITH TOKEN BUCKET]
    p8 --> p14[APP LAYER CHALLENGE]
    p9 --> p15[ALERT TO SIEM]
    p10 --> p16[ACTION LOGGED]
    p11 --> p16
    p12 --> p16
    p13 --> p16
    p14 --> p16
    p15 --> p16
```

**Interpretation:**
- The flow makes the **confidence-thresholded branching** explicit: high-confidence alerts trigger hard prevention; medium-confidence alerts trigger soft prevention; low-confidence alerts only log.
- Every action — whether prevent or log-only — is recorded, providing an audit trail for compliance (PCI-DSS 10.x, ISO 27001 A.12.4).

### 4.4 Layered Defense Model: Where Prevention Capabilities Live

```mermaid
graph TB
    l7[LAYER 7 APPLICATION] --> l6[LAYER 6 PRESENTATION]
    l6 --> l5[LAYER 5 SESSION]
    l5 --> l4[LAYER 4 TRANSPORT]
    l4 --> l3[LAYER 3 NETWORK]
    l3 --> l2[LAYER 2 DATA LINK]
    l2 --> l1[LAYER 1 PHYSICAL]
    a1[APP LAYER PREVENTION HTTP 403 SMTP 5XX DNS NXDOMAIN] -.-> l7
    a2[SESSION PREVENTION TCP RST] -.-> l5
    a3[RATE LIMIT AND SHAPING] -.-> l4
    a4[PACKET DROP AND IP BLOCK] -.-> l3
    a5[PORT SHUTDOWN AND MAC FILTER] -.-> l2
    a6[PHYSICAL DISCONNECT RELAY] -.-> l1
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each OSI layer has a corresponding prevention primitive. A well-engineered IPS will invoke the primitive at the *highest* layer that can definitively stop the attack — because higher-layer enforcement causes less collateral damage than lower-layer enforcement.

### 4.5 State Diagram — IPS Sensor Operational Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> initState
    initState --> detectState : BOOT OK AND SIGNATURES LOADED
    detectState --> preventState : INLINE ENABLED AND POLICY ACTIVE
    preventState --> detectState : OPERATOR SWITCHES TO MONITOR MODE
    preventState --> bypassState : HEARTBEAT FAILURE OR WATCHDOG TIMEOUT
    bypassState --> preventState : IPS RESTORED AND HEALTHY
    bypassState --> failClosedState : POLICY SAYS FAIL CLOSED
    detectState --> failClosedState : POLICY SAYS FAIL CLOSED
    failClosedState --> preventState : IPS RESTORED
    bypassState --> [*] : POWER CYCLE
```

**Interpretation:**
- The state machine encodes the **fail-open vs fail-closed** policy choice and the **operator override** path (e.g., taking the IPS into "detect-only" mode for forensic analysis of a new attack without risking prevention false positives).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) with respect to deployment and response capability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **IDS** is deployed *passively* — it receives a copy of network traffic via a SPAN port or network tap, and it analyzes the copy without sitting in the data path. Its response is limited to **alerting and logging**; it cannot stop the attack because the malicious traffic has already been forwarded by the switch. In contrast, an **IPS** is deployed *inline* — it sits physically in the wire path between two network segments, and every packet must pass through it. This inline placement grants the IPS the ability to take **active prevention actions** such as dropping packets, injecting TCP RSTs, rate-limiting, or quarantining hosts. The trade-off is that an IPS introduces latency and a single point of failure, which is mitigated by a hardware bypass relay. In summary: IDS = witness; IPS = gatekeeper. *(3 marks — 1 for deployment, 1 for response, 1 for trade-off.)*

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by "fail-open" mode in an IPS, and in which deployment scenario is it preferred over fail-closed mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fail-open mode** means that if the IPS appliance crashes, loses power, or otherwise becomes unresponsive, the bypass relay automatically closes a parallel circuit that allows traffic to flow *around* the IPS without inspection. The network remains available, but is unprotected for the duration of the failure. **Fail-closed mode** does the opposite — all traffic is blocked if the IPS fails. Fail-open is preferred in **life-safety and availability-critical networks** such as hospital medical-device networks, aviation control systems, and some industrial SCADA segments, where a network outage could directly threaten human safety or regulatory compliance. The decision is governed by the inequality $C_{breach} \cdot t_{outage} \ll C_{downtime} \cdot t_{outage}$, i.e., the cost of a brief unprotected window is less than the cost of a brief total outage. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for mechanism, 1 for scenario + justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **five major prevention capabilities** of an IPS, with one example scenario for each, and clearly state at which OSI layer each capability operates.

**(b) [7 marks]** An inline IPS has 16 processing cores, each capable of inspecting 1.5 million packets per second. The average packet size on the monitored link is 600 bytes. The hardware inspection overhead consumes 20% of the raw throughput. **(i)** Compute the maximum sustained line-rate throughput of the IPS in Gbps. **(ii)** Compute the per-packet decision budget in microseconds, assuming full-duplex operation. **(iii)** If the link is upgraded from 10 Gbps to 40 Gbps, will this IPS be sufficient? Justify with calculation.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Five Major Prevention Capabilities [7 marks — 1.4 marks each]:**

1. **Packet Drop (Layer 3, Network Layer):** The IPS silently discards offending packets. The attacker's payload never reaches the victim. *Example:* Blocking an ICMP echo flood from a botnet — the IPS drops each ICMP packet matching the flood signature.

2. **TCP Reset Injection (Layer 4, Transport Layer):** For TCP sessions, the IPS injects a spoofed TCP RST to both endpoints using the correct sequence numbers, forcing immediate teardown. *Example:* Killing an ongoing SSH brute-force session mid-login — the RST arrives before the attacker can guess the password.

3. **Rate Limiting (Layer 3/4, Network/Transport):** The IPS applies a token-bucket throttle to cap the bandwidth or packet rate from a source. *Example:* Slowing a single user who is downloading exfiltrated data at 1 GB/s down to 10 MB/s.

4. **Host Quarantine (Layer 2/3, Data Link/Network):** The IPS uses RADIUS CoA or 802.1X to push the infected host into a remediation VLAN. *Example:* Moving an endpoint that triggered a "Conficker worm" signature into a patch-and-reboot VLAN.

5. **Application-Layer Enforcement (Layer 7, Application):** The IPS responds with protocol-specific blocks. *Example:* Returning HTTP 403 to a request carrying a SQL-injection payload in the URI.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often list *detection* methods (signature, anomaly, stateful) under the question "prevention capabilities" and lose 4–5 marks. Read the question carefully — it asks for *prevention* (response actions), not *detection* (analysis methods). Detection and prevention are different subsystems; the question explicitly asks about prevention.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks — split as 2 + 2 + 3]:**

**(b-i) Maximum sustained throughput [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{total} = N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate} = 16 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{6} = 24 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

$$
L_{raw} = P_{total} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8 = 24 \times 10^{6} \cdot 600 \cdot 8 = 115.2 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/second}
$$

$$
H_{overhead} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.20} = 1.25
$$

$$
L_{max} = \frac{L_{raw}}{H_{overhead}} = \frac{115.2 \times 10^{9}}{1.25} = 92.16 \; \text{Gbps}
$$

**Answer: 92.16 Gbps.** *(2 marks — 1 for setup, 1 for final value with units.)*

**(b-ii) Per-packet decision budget [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{line} = \frac{L_{max}}{S_{avg} \cdot 8} = \frac{92.16 \times 10^{9}}{600 \cdot 8} = 19.2 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{1}{P_{line}} = 5.208 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{s} = 52.08 \; \mu s
$$

$$
T_{decision} = \frac{T_{packet}}{2} = 26.04 \; \mu s
$$

**Answer: ≈ 26 μs per packet (full-duplex).** *(2 marks — 1 for full-duplex factor, 1 for final value.)*

**(b-iii) Sufficiency for 40 Gbps upgrade [3 marks]:**

The calculated $L_{max} = 92.16$ Gbps **exceeds** the 40 Gbps target. Therefore, in principle, the IPS is sufficient.

However, the KTU-recommended engineering practice is to operate an IPS at **no more than 70%** of its theoretical maximum to leave headroom for traffic bursts, signature database growth, and TLS decryption overhead. The safe sustained capacity is:

$$
L_{safe} = 0.70 \cdot L_{max} = 0.70 \cdot 92.16 = 64.5 \; \text{Gbps}
$$

Since $L_{safe} = 64.5 \; \text{Gbps} > 40 \; \text{Gbps}$, the IPS is sufficient even under the 70% rule. **Conclusion: Yes, the IPS is sufficient for the 40 Gbps upgrade**, with a comfortable margin of 24.5 Gbps. *(3 marks — 1 for comparison, 1 for the 70% headroom principle, 1 for the final conclusion.)*

---

#### Question B-B. (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a decision-flow diagram, describe how an IPS selects between **hard prevention** (drop, RST, quarantine) and **soft prevention** (rate limit, app-layer challenge). Explain the role of **confidence score**, **asset value**, and **false-positive cost** in this selection.

**(b) [7 marks]** A company is evaluating whether to deploy prevention capabilities (IPS) or detection-only (IDS). The estimated cost of a single breach is $C_{breach} = \$2{,}000{,}000$. Without IPS, the annual probability of a breach is $P_{noIPS} = 0.30$. With IPS, it drops to $P_{IPS} = 0.04$. The IPS appliance plus 3-year maintenance costs $C_{IPS} = \$450{,}000$. **(i)** Compute the expected annual loss without IPS. **(ii)** Compute the expected annual loss with IPS. **(iii)** Compute the 3-year ROI in percent and state whether the deployment is justified.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IPS Action Selection Flow [7 marks]:**

The IPS follows a layered decision process:

1. **Confidence Score Check (C):** The detection subsystem emits a confidence $C \in [0, 1]$.
   - If $C \geq 0.95$: the alert is *high-confidence*; hard prevention is eligible.
   - If $0.70 \leq C < 0.95$: *medium-confidence*; only soft prevention is permitted.
   - If $C < 0.70$: *low-confidence*; log only, no prevention.

2. **Asset Value (V) of the destination:** If the target is a high-value asset (e.g., domain controller, payment server), the IPS prefers *quarantine* over *drop* because dropping may destroy forensic evidence. Drop is reserved for low-value targets.

3. **False-Positive Cost (FPC):** A metric representing the operational impact of an erroneous block. If the FPC is high (e.g., blocking a paying customer's IP), the IPS downgrades from hard to soft action — e.g., from drop to rate-limit — even if $C$ is high.

The composite decision rule is:

$$
\text{Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Hard Block (Drop / RST)} & \text{if } C \geq 0.95 \text{ and } FPC = \text{Low} \\
\text{Quarantine} & \text{if } C \geq 0.95 \text{ and } V = \text{High} \\
\text{Rate Limit} & \text{if } 0.70 \leq C < 0.95 \text{ or } FPC = \text{High} \\
\text{App-Layer Challenge} & \text{if } 0.70 \leq C < 0.95 \text{ and } L7 \text{ available} \\
\text{Log Only} & \text{if } C < 0.70
\end{cases}
$$

A properly drawn flowchart (mermaid or hand-drawn in the exam) showing the three decision boxes — *Confidence*, *Asset Value*, *FPC* — feeding into the five terminal action nodes earns full marks. *(7 marks — 2 for the rules, 2 for the decision matrix, 2 for the diagram, 1 for the composite rule.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students treat *confidence score* as the *only* input to the action selector and ignore asset value and FPC. This loses 3 marks. The examiner is specifically testing whether you understand that **prevention is risk-managed, not just confidence-managed**.

---

**(b) ROI Calculation [7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3]:**

**(b-i) Expected Annual Loss (EAL) without IPS [2 marks]:**

$$
EAL_{noIPS} = P_{noIPS} \cdot C_{breach} = 0.30 \cdot \$2{,}000{,}000 = \$600{,}000
$$

**Answer: \$600,000 per year.** *(2 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for value.)*

**(b-ii) Expected Annual Loss (EAL) with IPS [2 marks]:**

$$
EAL_{IPS} = P_{IPS} \cdot C_{breach} = 0.04 \cdot \$2{,}000{,}000 = \$80{,}000
$$

**Answer: \$80,000 per year.** *(2 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for value.)*

**(b-iii) 3-Year ROI [3 marks]:**

Annual avoided loss:

$$
\Delta EAL = EAL_{noIPS} - EAL_{IPS} = \$600{,}000 - \$80{,}000 = \$520{,}000
$$

3-year avoided loss:

$$
B_{3yr} = 3 \cdot \Delta EAL = 3 \cdot \$520{,}000 = \$1{,}560{,}000
$$

Net benefit:

$$
N = B_{3yr} - C_{IPS} = \$1{,}560{,}000 - \$450{,}000 = \$1{,}110{,}000
$$

3-year ROI:

$$
ROI_{\%} = 100 \times \frac{N}{C_{IPS}} = 100 \times \frac{1{,}110{,}000}{450{,}000} \approx 246.7\%
$$

**Answer: ROI ≈ 246.7%.** The deployment is **strongly justified** — the company recovers its IPS cost 2.47 times over 3 years, purely from breach-loss avoidance. *(3 marks — 1 for each of: avoided loss, net benefit, final ROI% + justification.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam. It is the highest-density summary of the topic.**

- **Prevention ≠ Detection.** Detection = recognize; Prevention = act. Both subsystems coexist inside an IPS.
- **Inline placement is mandatory** for prevention. Tap/SPAN-mode sensors can detect but never prevent.
- **Five Prevention Pillars:** (1) Packet Drop, (2) TCP RST Injection, (3) Rate Limit (Token Bucket), (4) Host Quarantine, (5) App-Layer Enforcement.
- **Confidence-Ladder Rule:** $C \geq 0.95$ → hard action; $0.70 \leq C < 0.95$ → soft action; $C < 0.70$ → log only.
- **Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed** decision is governed by $C_{breach}$ vs $C_{downtime}$. Fail-open for life-safety; fail-closed for high-value enterprise.
- **TCP RST sequence number rule:** $seq = S.ack\_num$, $ack = S.seq\_num + 1$ — this is what makes the reset unspoofable.
- **Token Bucket formula:** $T(t) = \min(b, \, T(t-1) + r\Delta t - p)$. Negative = drop.
- **Per-Packet Decision Budget:** $T_{decision} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot P_{line}}$ for full-duplex. 60 μs is the typical target for 10 Gbps links.
- **Throughput formula:** $L_{max} = \dfrac{N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8}{H_{overhead}}$.
- **70% Headroom Rule:** Never operate an IPS above 70% of theoretical $L_{max}$ — reserve the remainder for bursts and signature growth.
- **ROI formula:** $ROI_{\%} = 100 \times \dfrac{3 \cdot (P_{noIPS} - P_{IPS}) \cdot C_{breach} - C_{IPS}}{C_{IPS}}$.
- **OSI Layer → Prevention Mapping:** L7 = App enforcement; L5 = TCP RST; L4 = Rate limit; L3 = Drop / IP block; L2 = Port shutdown; L1 = Physical disconnect.
- **Common Exam Traps:**
  - Forgetting the full-duplex factor of 2 in $T_{decision}$ (–1 mark).
  - Forgetting to divide by $H_{overhead}$ in $L_{max}$ (–1 mark).
  - Confusing prevention with detection mechanisms (–4 to –5 marks on 14-mark Q).
  - Not justifying the fail-open/closed choice with a cost argument (–2 marks).
  - Using $|x|$ in a markdown table — use $\vert x \vert$ instead to avoid breaking the table parser (–1 mark for an unreadable table).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network-Based IDPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network-Based IDPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Network-Based Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (NIDPS / NIPS)** is a security control mechanism that monitors, analyses, and responds to traffic traversing a specific protected network segment in real-time. It operates by capturing packets directly from the network wire (via passive tap, span/mirror port, or in-line bump-in-the-wire deployment) and applying pattern matching, protocol decoding, stateful inspection, statistical baselining, or behavioural heuristics to identify malicious activity.

Per the KTU 2024 PECCT742 syllabus framing, a NIDPS is positioned as a **distributed, in-promiscuous-mode packet processing engine** that distinguishes itself from Host-Based IDPS (HIDPS) by the source of telemetry — *network packets* rather than *host artefacts* (logs, syscalls, file integrity).

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition Anchor
> A **NIDPS** is a security appliance or software agent that examines packets in real time as they traverse a network, comparing them against signatures, protocol-state violations, and statistical baselines, and either raises an alert (Detection) or actively blocks / drops / resets the offending flow (Prevention).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Customs Checkpost at a Highway"

Imagine a busy multi-lane highway connecting two cities. The highway is your corporate LAN/WAN, and the vehicles are the packets. A NIDPS is like a **sophisticated customs and traffic checkpost stationed at the toll booth**:

- **Inspection Lane (Sensors)**: Just as customs officers peer into every vehicle, NIDPS sensors inspect every packet header and payload.
- **Manifest Verification (Signature Matching)**: If the cargo manifest (packet signature) matches a list of known smuggled items (attack signatures), the vehicle is flagged.
- **Behavioural Profiling (Anomaly Detection)**: If a vehicle moves at 250 km/h on a road where the average is 80 km/h, officers suspect foul play — this is anomaly detection.
- **Driver Behaviour (Stateful Inspection)**: If a driver claims to be a diplomat but is dressed as a farmer, the state context is violated.
- **Two Modes of Action**:
   * **Detection Mode (IDS only)** → Officer radios HQ: "Suspicious vehicle heading north."
   * **Prevention Mode (IPS)** → Officer physically pulls the vehicle over and confiscates the cargo.

### 1.3 Classification of NIDPS by Deployment Posture

> [!NOTE] KTU High-Yield Distinction
> KTU examiners frequently test the difference between *passive* and *in-line* NIDPS deployment.

| Deployment Mode | Tap Mechanism | Effect on Traffic Flow | Latency Introduced |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive (IDS)** | SPAN port / Network TAP | Read-only — does not modify or block traffic | **~0 ms** (observation only) |
| **In-line (IPS)** | Bump-in-the-wire (B/W) | Active — can drop, reject, modify, or rate-limit | **<1 ms** typical, failure mode = fail-open or fail-closed |
| **Hybrid** | TAP + inline engine | Combination of both | Variable |

### 1.4 Key Operational Metrics (Bold-Form Constants)

- **Throughput**: Measured in **Gbps** (Gigabits per second) — modern sensors handle **1–100 Gbps**.
- **Packet Processing Rate**: **MPPS** (Million Packets Per Second) — 64-byte packets at 1 Gbps ≈ **1.488 Mpps**.
- **Session Capacity**: Concurrent tracked flows — typically **1M – 10M** sessions.
- **Detection Latency**: Time from packet arrival to alert — typical **<10 μs** for stateless, **<1 ms** for stateful.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of a NIDPS

A canonical NIDPS is decomposed into **six functional subsystems**, each performing a dedicated role. Understanding these layers is critical for KTU long-answer questions.

1. **Packet Capture Subsystem (Sensor / TAP Interface)**
   - Places the NIC in **promiscuous mode** (or **monitor mode** for wireless).
   - Uses libpcap / PF_RING / DPDK for high-speed ring-buffer ingestion.
   - In wireless: 802.11 frame capture with radiotap headers.

2. **Protocol Decoder / Normalizer**
   - Re-assembles Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application-layer PDUs.
   - Defeats **evasion techniques** (e.g., IP fragmentation, TCP overlapping segments, URL encoding).
   - Performs **TCP reassembly** with configurable overlap policy (Favor Newer / Older / OS).

3. **Detection Engine**
   - Three primary paradigms: **Signature-based**, **Anomaly-based**, **Stateful Protocol Analysis**.
   - May use pattern matchers (Aho-Corasick automaton, Hyperscan regex engine).

4. **Knowledge / Rule Base**
   - Signature database (e.g., Snort/Suricata rule format, Emerging Threats ET-SID).
   - Anomaly baselines (mean, variance, EWMA distributions per flow tuple).

5. **Response / Action Subsystem**
   - Passive: log, SNMP trap, syslog, email, dashboard.
   - Active: TCP RST injection, ICMP unreachable, session table purge, rate-limit, quarantine via 802.1X / RADIUS CoA.

6. **Management & Correlation Console**
   - Centralized SIEM ingestion, event de-duplication, cross-sensor correlation.

### 2.2 Detection Methodologies — KTU High-Yield Comparison

> [!NOTE] KTU Examiner Focus
> Examiner expects you to enumerate the three detection paradigms AND state the mathematical/statistical principle behind each.

| Method | Underlying Principle | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature / Misuse** | String/regex match against known attack patterns | Very low FP, deterministic | Zero-day blind |
| **Anomaly (Statistical)** | Deviation from learned baseline (μ ± kσ) | Detects novel attacks | High FP, requires training |
| **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Verifies protocol FSM conformance | Detects logical exploits | Slow, complex to maintain |
| **Hybrid / Reputation** | Combines signature + threat-intel IP/URL feeds | Real-time IOC matching | Depends on feed freshness |

### 2.3 Sensor Placement Topologies

Where you place the sensor dictates *what* you can see. KTU expects familiarity with the four canonical positions:

1. **Outside the firewall (Pre-firewall / DMZ-edge)** — Sees all internet-borne attacks including those the firewall will block. High volume of noise.
2. **Inside the firewall (Internal segment)** — Sees attacks that *bypassed* the firewall and insider threats.
3. **Behind the IDS itself (TAP at the choke point)** — Used for traffic mirroring without SPAN encapsulation overhead.
4. **In-line at the gateway (IPS mode)** — Single chokepoint providing prevention.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Critical equations table for KTU numerical/derivation problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Line-rate packets at wire speed | $P = \dfrac{B}{8 \cdot S} \times 10^{6}$ | Mpps | $B$ = bandwidth (Gbps), $S$ = packet size (bytes) |
| 2 | Throughput ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{proc}}{P_{wire}}$ | dimensionless | $\eta \ge 1$ required to avoid packet loss |
| 3 | Detection Rate (Recall) | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | True positive rate |
| 4 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Specificity = 1 − FPR |
| 5 | Precision | $Prec = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Confidence of alert |
| 6 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Prec \cdot DR}{Prec + DR}$ | $[0,1]$ | Harmonic mean |
| 7 | Anomaly score (z-statistic) | $z = \dfrac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Threshold $z > k$ (commonly $k=3$) |
| 8 | Shannon entropy of payload | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/byte | Used to detect encrypted/encoded attacks |
| 9 | CUSUM cumulative sum | $S_t = \max(0, S_{t-1} + z_t - \mu_0 - k)$ | dimensionless | Sequential change detection |
| 10 | Shannon capacity (wire) | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | Nyquist-limited throughput |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

NIDPS technology is foundational in:
- **Enterprise SOCs** (Security Operations Centers) for perimeter defence.
- **ISP backbone traffic scrubbing** (e.g., BGP FlowSpec-based mitigation of DDoS).
- **OT/ICS networks** for SCADA protocol anomaly detection (Modbus, DNP3, IEC-60870-5-104).
- **Cloud VPC traffic mirroring** (AWS VPC Traffic Mirroring, Azure vTAP) feeding virtual NIDS.
- **Encrypted Traffic Analysis (ETA)** — modern NIDPS extract *meta-features* (TLS handshake fingerprint, JA3/JA3S hashes, certificate anomalies) without decrypting payload.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Sensor Throughput to Avoid Packet Loss

Given a link of bandwidth $B$ Gbps carrying uniformly-sized packets of size $S$ bytes, derive the maximum packet arrival rate at the sensor.

**Step 1 — Convert bandwidth to bits per second.**
$$B_{bps} = B \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/second}$$

**Step 2 — Convert packet size to bits per packet.**
$$S_{bits} = S \times 8 \text{ bits/packet}$$

**Step 3 — Compute packet arrival rate (packets per second).**
$$P_{pps} = \dfrac{B_{bps}}{S_{bits}} = \dfrac{B \times 10^{9}}{8 \cdot S}$$

**Step 4 — Express in Millions of Packets Per Second (Mpps) for industry convention.**
$$P_{Mpps} = \dfrac{B}{8 \cdot S} \quad \text{(since } \frac{10^{9}}{10^{6}} = 10^{3} \text{ cancels one factor of } B \text{ in Gbps)}$$

Wait — correction. The Gbps unit already contains $10^9$. To express in millions ($10^6$), we must scale by $10^3$. So:

$$P_{Mpps} = \dfrac{B \times 10^{9}}{8 \cdot S \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{B \times 10^{3}}{8 \cdot S}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for $B = 1$ Gbps, $S = 64$ bytes (smallest Ethernet frame).**
$$P_{Mpps} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{3}}{8 \times 64} = \dfrac{1000}{512} = 1.953125 \text{ Mpps}$$

The widely-quoted industry rule of thumb is therefore:
$$P_{wire} \approx \dfrac{1.488 \text{ Mpps}}{1 \text{ Gbps at 64-byte frames}}$$

The 1.488 figure corresponds to the *standard* Ethernet minimum of 84 bytes (64 payload + 8 preamble + 12 IFG), not 64. Refining:

$$P_{Mpps} = \dfrac{1000}{8 \times (64+8+12)} = \dfrac{1000}{672} = 1.488 \text{ Mpps}$$

**Step 6 — Sensor throughput ratio.**
A sensor must satisfy:
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{proc}}{P_{wire}} \geq 1$$

If $\eta < 1$, the kernel ring buffer overruns and packets are dropped at the NIC — defeating forensic capture.

### 3.2 Derivation: Anomaly Score Using Gaussian Baseline

**Premise:** A NIDPS maintains per-destination flow records. For destination $D$, the inter-arrival time of packets follows $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ under benign traffic.

**Step 1 — Z-score of an observed sample $x$.**
$$z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Squared Mahalanobis-style distance for multivariate flow feature vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.**
$$D^{2} = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$$

**Step 3 — Decision rule.**
$$\text{Anomaly flag} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } D^{2} > \chi^{2}_{n, \alpha} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\chi^{2}_{n, \alpha}$ is the chi-squared critical value at significance level $\alpha$ with $n$ degrees of freedom.

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
Suppose $n = 2$ flow features (packet rate, byte rate) and the engine computes $D^{2} = 7.5$. For $\alpha = 0.05$, $\chi^{2}_{2, 0.05} = 5.991$. Since $7.5 > 5.991$, the flow is flagged as anomalous.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detection Method Selection Criteria

> [!NOTE] Use this matrix to select the right detection paradigm for a KTU design question.

| Attack Class | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Known worm signature | Signature (Aho-Corasick) | Deterministic, <μs match |
| Zero-day exploit | Anomaly + Sandbox | No prior signature exists |
| Protocol violation (HTTP smuggling) | Stateful Protocol Analysis | FSM deviation detected |
| Slow-rate DDoS | Stateful + EWMA CUSUM | Long-window statistical drift |
| Insider data exfiltration | Anomaly + DLP correlation | Behavioural deviation from user profile |
| Encrypted C2 channel | ETA / JA3 fingerprint | Cannot decrypt, but TLS metadata reveals |
| Encrypted payload / C2 beaconing | Inter-arrival entropy + certificate anomaly | Statistical + signature hybrid |

### 3.4 Derivation: ROC Trade-off Between False Positives and Detection Rate

A NIDPS tuning parameter (e.g., anomaly threshold $k$) trades off $FPR$ and $DR$. Under a Gaussian model, the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** is analytical:

**Step 1 — Probability of detection.**
$$P_D(k) = P(Z > k) = 1 - \Phi(k - \mu_1/\sigma)$$

**Step 2 — Probability of false alarm.**
$$P_F(k) = P(Z > k \mid H_0) = 1 - \Phi(k)$$

**Step 3 — Parametric ROC curve.** Vary $k \in [0, \infty)$ and plot $P_F$ vs. $P_D$.

**Step 4 — Area Under the Curve (AUC).**
$$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} P_D(P_F) \, dP_F$$

A perfect NIDPS has $AUC = 1.0$; a random classifier has $AUC = 0.5$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NIDPS Functional Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *internal data flow* of a Network-Based IDPS — from packet ingestion to response action. Use this as the canonical answer for "Explain the architecture of a NIDPS" (14-mark KTU questions).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Wire / TAP / SPAN] --> B[Packet Capture Engine]
    B --> C[Protocol Decoder and Normalizer]
    C --> D[TCP Reassembly Buffer]
    D --> E[Detection Engine]
    F[Signature Database] --> E
    G[Anomaly Baseline Engine] --> E
    H[Stateful Protocol FSM Table] --> E
    E --> I{Threat Detected?}
    I -- No --> J[Drop / Forward Packet]
    I -- Yes --> K[Response Engine]
    K --> L[Log and Alert to SIEM]
    K --> M[Active Block - TCP RST / Drop]
    K --> N[Quarantine via RADIUS CoA]
    E --> O[Management Console Dashboard]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Sensor Placement Topology (Enterprise Perimeter)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Internet[Internet] --> FW1[Edge Firewall]
    FW1 --> SPAN1[SPAN Port A]
    FW1 --> DMZ[DMZ Zone]
    DMZ --> IDS1[NIDPS Sensor 1 - Pre-Firewall]
    SPAN1 --> IDS1
    DMZ --> InternalCore[Internal Core LAN]
    InternalCore --> TAP1[Network TAP B]
    TAP1 --> IDS2[NIDPS Sensor 2 - Internal]
    InternalCore --> ServerFarm[Server Farm]
    ServerFarm --> IPS1[In-line IPS Sensor 3]
    IPS1 --> DBCluster[Database Cluster]
    IDS1 --> SOC[SOC SIEM Console]
    IDS2 --> SOC
    IPS1 --> SOC
```

### 4.3 Detection Engine — Three-Stage Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Packet In] --> P2{Stage 1: Signature Match?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Alert - Known Attack]
    P2 -- No --> P4{Stage 2: Protocol FSM Violation?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Alert - Protocol Anomaly]
    P4 -- No --> P6{Stage 3: Statistical Anomaly Score above threshold?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Alert - Behavioural Anomaly]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Mark Flow as Benign]
    P3 --> R[Response Engine]
    P5 --> R
    P7 --> R
    P8 --> Q[Continue Monitoring]
```

### 4.4 NIDPS Deployment Modes (Passive vs. In-line)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Passive Mode
        A1[Client] --> A2[Switch]
        A2 -- SPAN Mirror --> A3[NIDPS Sensor]
        A2 --> A4[Server]
    end
    subgraph Inline Mode
        B1[Client] --> B2[NIDPS IPS Engine]
        B2 --> B3[Server]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between a Network-Based IDPS and a Host-Based IDPS. List any three distinguishing factors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | NIDPS | HIDPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Telemetry Source** | Network packets (wire-level) | Host artefacts (logs, syscalls, registry, file integrity) |
| **Scope** | Monitors entire subnet segment | Monitors a single host |
| **Deployment** | SPAN port, TAP, or in-line gateway | Installed as agent on each endpoint |
| **Encrypted Traffic Visibility** | Blind to host-level encrypted content (uses ETA) | Can inspect pre-encryption host data |
| **Stealth** | Difficult to detect by attacker | Easier to detect and disable by attacker |
| **Performance Impact** | On network throughput | On host CPU/memory |

*Any three correct distinctions with justification → 3 marks.*

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "False Positive" and "False Negative" in the context of an IDPS. Why is minimizing false negatives more critical than minimizing false positives in a NIDPS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **False Positive (FP)**: A legitimate packet/flow is incorrectly flagged as malicious. (1 mark)
- **False Negative (FN)**: A malicious packet/flow passes undetected. (1 mark)
- **Criticality of FN**: A missed attack means compromise has occurred; the NIDPS has failed its primary security objective. A false positive only triggers wasted analyst time. The asymmetry of cost — *breach vs. inconvenience* — is why **recall (1 − FNR)** is weighted higher than precision in security engineering. (1 mark)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]
**Q: (a) Explain the architecture of a Network-Based Intrusion Detection and Prevention System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each component. (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe the three detection methodologies used in a NIDPS. Compare their strengths and weaknesses with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of NIDPS (7 marks):**

A NIDPS consists of six cooperating subsystems, as shown in the diagram in SECTION 4.1.

1. **Packet Capture Subsystem (1 mark):** The sensor's NIC operates in **promiscuous mode**, accepting all frames regardless of destination MAC. A **SPAN port** or **passive optical TAP** delivers mirrored traffic. For in-line IPS, the sensor sits as a bump-in-the-wire (B/W). High-speed capture uses **DPDK / PF_RING / AF_XDP** to bypass kernel overhead.

2. **Protocol Decoder and Normalizer (1 mark):** Decodes layer 2–7 PDUs, performs **IP defragmentation** and **TCP reassembly**, and applies **normalization** (e.g., RFC-compliant TTL handling, HTTP URI canonicalization) to defeat evasion.

3. **TCP Reassembly and Flow Tracker (1 mark):** Maintains per-flow state tables (5-tuple hash), tracks session state, buffers out-of-order segments, and enforces timeout-based eviction.

4. **Detection Engine (2 marks):** The core processor applying signature matching, protocol FSM validation, and statistical anomaly scoring in parallel. May use **Aho-Corasick** for multi-pattern matching, **Hyperscan** for regex, and **EWMA / CUSUM** for sequential change detection.

5. **Response / Action Subsystem (1 mark):** On detection: passive actions (log, SNMP trap, syslog) or active actions (TCP RST injection, session purge, quarantine via 802.1X / RADIUS CoA, drop packet in inline mode).

6. **Management & Correlation Console (1 mark):** Centralized event aggregation, dashboard, tuning interface, rule update, and SIEM forwarding.

---

**(b) Three Detection Methodologies (7 marks):**

1. **Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection (2.5 marks):**
   - **Principle:** Pattern match packet headers/payloads against a database of known attack signatures.
   - **Example:** Snort rule `alert tcp any any → $HOME_NET 80 (content: "/cgi-bin/phf"; msg:"PHF exploit";)`.
   - **Strengths:** Very low false positive rate, deterministic, real-time.
   - **Weaknesses:** Ineffective against zero-day attacks; requires continuous signature updates.

2. **Anomaly-Based Detection (2.5 marks):**
   - **Principle:** Establish a statistical baseline of "normal" behaviour (e.g., packet rate, byte rate, connection frequency per host). Flag deviations exceeding threshold $k\sigma$.
   - **Example:** A web server normally receives 100 req/sec; sudden spike to 10,000 req/sec triggers volumetric anomaly alert.
   - **Strengths:** Capable of detecting novel / zero-day attacks.
   - **Weaknesses:** High false positive rate; requires training period; susceptible to baseline poisoning.

3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis (2 marks):**
   - **Principle:** Models each protocol as a finite state machine (FSM) and validates that observed transitions conform to the RFC-defined state graph.
   - **Example:** HTTP request smuggling where `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers disagree — FSM detects illegal transition.
   - **Strengths:** Detects logic-level exploits invisible to pattern matching.
   - **Weaknesses:** Slow (state lookup overhead); difficult to maintain across protocol versions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: (a) Discuss the different sensor placement strategies for a NIDPS in an enterprise network. What are the advantages and disadvantages of each? (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain how the performance of a NIDPS is evaluated. Derive the relationship between detection rate and false positive rate using the ROC framework. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sensor Placement Strategies (7 marks):**

1. **Outside the Firewall (Pre-Firewall / Internet Edge) (2 marks):**
   - **Advantage:** Sees *all* incoming attacks including those the firewall will block. Provides threat intelligence on attacker reconnaissance.
   - **Disadvantage:** High false positives due to massive noise (port scans, botnet probes, harmless pings). Wastes sensor CPU.

2. **Inside the Firewall (Internal Choke Point) (2 marks):**
   - **Advantage:** Detects attacks that *bypassed* the firewall and insider threats. Lower false positive rate due to cleaned traffic.
   - **Disadvantage:** Misses attacks stopped at the firewall; cannot identify firewall-bypass efficacy.

3. **Behind Critical Servers (DMZ) (1.5 marks):**
   - **Advantage:** Protects high-value assets; tightest scrutiny on application-layer attacks.
   - **Disadvantage:** Limited to north-south traffic only; misses lateral movement.

4. **In-line at the Gateway (IPS Mode) (1.5 marks):**
   - **Advantage:** Active prevention — can drop malicious traffic in real time.
   - **Disadvantage:** Single point of failure; introduces latency; misconfiguration can cause outage.

---

**(b) Performance Evaluation & ROC Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Confusion Matrix Foundation (2 marks):**
   A NIDPS output is binarized — for each observed flow: TP (correctly flagged attack), TN (correctly passed benign), FP (benign flagged as attack), FN (attack passed as benign).

2. **Detection Rate and False Positive Rate (2 marks):**
   $$DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} \quad \text{and} \quad FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$$

3. **ROC Curve Derivation (3 marks):**
   Under a Gaussian anomaly model with threshold $k$:

   $$P_D(k) = 1 - \Phi\left(\frac{k - \mu_1}{\sigma}\right)$$

   $$P_F(k) = 1 - \Phi\left(\frac{k - \mu_0}{\sigma}\right)$$

   Eliminate $k$:
   $$P_D = \Phi\left(\frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma} + \Phi^{-1}(1 - P_F)\right)$$

   The **AUC** (area under ROC) summarizes the sensor's inherent discriminative power:
   $$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} P_D(P_F) \, dP_F$$

   Higher AUC → better sensor. An ideal NIDPS achieves AUC = 1.0.

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in NIDPS questions:**
> 1. **Conflating IDS and IPS** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you say "NIDPS" when answering an IDS-only question or vice versa. Always state the deployment mode explicitly.
> 2. **Skipping the formula derivation** — for ROC/throughput questions, a final numerical answer without the algebraic steps loses at least 2 marks. KTU expects *every* intermediate step.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — "1.488 Mpps" with no unit annotation = 0 for that line. Always annotate with **Mbps, Gbps, Mpps, μs, ms**.
> 4. **Omitting evasion resistance** — for normalizer/decoding questions, you MUST mention fragmentation, overlapping TCP segments, and HTTP encoding evasion. Missing these costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing signature with anomaly** — signature detection = *known patterns*; anomaly = *statistical deviation*. KTU examiners flag this confusion repeatedly.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Network-Based IDPS

- **NIDPS** = packet-level monitoring of network traffic using promiscuous-mode sensors and either signature, anomaly, or stateful analysis.
- **Two deployment postures**: Passive (SPAN/TAP, IDS only) and In-line (IPS, with active block).
- **Three detection paradigms**: Signature (low FP, zero-day blind), Anomaly (high FP, zero-day capable), Stateful Protocol (logic-level exploits, slow).
- **Four canonical sensor placements**: Pre-firewall, Post-firewall, DMZ, and In-line gateway — each with distinct threat visibility.
- **Wire-speed throughput formula**: $P_{Mpps} = \dfrac{B \times 10^{3}}{8 \times S_{total}}$ where $S_{total} = 84$ bytes for 64-byte payload frames.
- **Anomaly score** uses $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ or Mahalanobis $D^2$ for multivariate; threshold via $\chi^2_{n,\alpha}$.
- **ROC analysis**: $DR$ vs. $FPR$ trade-off; AUC quantifies sensor quality.
- **Evasion resistance** requires normalization: IP defrag, TCP reassembly, HTTP canonicalization, IPv6 extension header handling.
- **Modern extensions**: Encrypted Traffic Analysis (ETA) using JA3/JA3S fingerprinting, certificate metadata analysis, inter-arrival timing entropy.
- **Failure mode of in-line IPS**: Always specify **fail-open** (traffic passes on sensor crash) vs. **fail-closed** (traffic blocked) — KTU expects you to choose based on the deployment context.
- **Key vendors/examples**: Snort, Suricata, Zeek (formerly Bro), Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto Networks, Check Point. Mentioning these strengthens long answers.
- **Remember the 5-tuple**: (src IP, dst IP, src port, dst port, protocol) — the basis of every flow record.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networking Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Networking Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Networking** is the disciplined practice of interconnecting geographically distributed computing devices (nodes) through a unified suite of physical media, logical addressing schemes, and standardized communication protocols, enabling the controlled exchange of data packets and the shared consumption of distributed resources. Within the domain of **Intrusion Detection & Prevention Systems (IDPS)**, a networking overview is not a peripheral topic — it is the foundational substrate upon which every detection rule, signature, and anomaly threshold is constructed.

From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, the networking overview for IDPS contextualizes the *battlefield* where intrusions occur. An intrusion detection sensor must parse frames, segments, packets, and application-layer flows in real time; therefore, the analyst/engineer must master the layered reference models, addressing hierarchies, and protocol semantics that govern how data traverses a network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECCT742 – Module 3):**
> The networking overview bridges *raw packet inspection* (technical depth) with *threat surface mapping* (defensive strategy). Every IDPS rule, whether signature-based (Snort-style) or anomaly-based (statistical), is ultimately a *boolean predicate* evaluated against fields inside a protocol data unit (PDU).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal System

Imagine the **global postal service** as a network:

| Postal Concept | Network Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Letter (content) | **Payload / Data** |
| Envelope with full address | **Packet Header (IP + TCP/UDP)** |
| Postal code (PIN) | **IP Address (Network + Host portion)** |
| House number + street | **Port Number + MAC Address** |
| Sorting office (city level) | **Router (Layer 3 device)** |
| Local post office (street level) | **Switch (Layer 2 device)** |
| Universal postal rules | **OSI / TCP-IP Reference Model** |
| Registered post acknowledgment | **TCP ACK (handshake)** |
| Ordinary post (no ack) | **UDP (connectionless)** |

Just as a postal inspector can intercept, inspect, and either *forward* (pass) or *seize* (drop) suspicious mail, an **IDPS** sits inline (IPS) or on a span port (IDS), parses each "envelope" (packet), and applies detection logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Networks are *layered abstractions*. A packet is not just bytes — it is a nested structure of headers, each added (encapsulation) or removed (decapsulation) as it traverses the seven (or four) layers. An IDPS engineer who does not understand this nesting cannot write effective detection rules.

## 1.3 Why Networking is the *First Pillar* of IDPS

1. **Attack Surface Mapping** — You cannot detect an attack on a protocol you do not understand.
2. **Traffic Baselining** — Anomaly detection requires knowing *normal* protocol behavior.
3. **Protocol Decoding** — Signature rules depend on accurate field-level parsing.
4. **Evasion Awareness** — Attackers use protocol-level tricks (fragmentation, encoding) to bypass sensors.
5. **Forensic Reconstruction** — Post-incident analysis is impossible without a clean networking mental model.

The KTU 2024 outcome-based education framework tags this knowledge under **CO (Course Outcome) alignment with Bloom's L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze)** cognitive levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI Seven-Layer Reference Model (ISO/IEC 7498-1)

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model is the canonical, vendor-neutral abstraction of network communication. It decomposes the complex task of data exchange into **seven hierarchical layers**, each providing services to the layer above and consuming services from the layer below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (Top-Down):** *"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"*
> **Mnemonic (Bottom-Up):** *"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"*

| Layer # | Layer Name | PDU Name | Key Protocols/Standards | IDPS Relevance |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| **7** | **Application** | Data / Message | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, SSH, TLS | Payload inspection, DLP, application-layer attacks (SQLi, XSS) |
| **6** | **Presentation** | Data | SSL/TLS, MIME, ASCII, JPEG | Encryption/decryption boundaries, certificate inspection |
| **5** | **Session** | Data | NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP | Session hijacking detection, RPC enumeration |
| **4** | **Transport** | **Segment** (TCP) / **Datagram** (UDP) | TCP, UDP | Port scanning, SYN floods, connection tracking |
| **3** | **Network** | **Packet** | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP | IP spoofing, fragmentation attacks, traceroute analysis |
| **2** | **Data Link** | **Frame** | Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11), ARP, PPP | MAC flooding, ARP spoofing, VLAN hopping |
| **1** | **Physical** | **Bits** | Copper, Fiber, RF, IEEE 802.3 physical | Wire tapping, physical layer anomalies |

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Layering

Each layer solves a *specific class of problem*:

- **Layer 1 (Physical):** Solves the *transmission of raw bits* over a medium (voltage, light pulse, RF).
- **Layer 2 (Data Link):** Solves *reliable delivery across a single hop* (MAC addressing, framing, error detection via CRC).
- **Layer 3 (Network):** Solves *end-to-end delivery across multiple hops* (logical addressing via IP, routing).
- **Layer 4 (Transport):** Solves *process-to-process delivery* (port multiplexing, reliability, flow control).
- **Layer 5 (Session):** Solves *dialog management* (establish, maintain, terminate sessions).
- **Layer 6 (Presentation):** Solves *syntax and semantics* (encoding, compression, encryption).
- **Layer 7 (Application):** Solves *user-facing services* (file transfer, email, web).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encapsulation Rule:** As data moves **down** the stack on the sender side, each layer *appends* its own header (and sometimes trailer). As data moves **up** the stack on the receiver side, each layer *strips* its corresponding header. This nested structure is exactly what a Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) engine exploits.

## 2.2 The TCP/IP Four-Layer (DoD) Model

The practical model used by the modern Internet is the **TCP/IP model** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite** or **DoD model**), defined in IETF RFC 1122 and successor documents.

| TCP/IP Layer | Maps to OSI Layers | Core Protocols | Encapsulation Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Application** | 5, 6, 7 | HTTP, DNS, FTP, SSH, SMTP | **Data / Message** |
| **Transport** | 4 | TCP, UDP, DCCP, SCTP | **Segment / Datagram** |
| **Internet** | 3 | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP | **Packet (Datagram)** |
| **Link (Network Access)** | 1, 2 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ARP, PPP | **Frame** |

> [!NOTE]
> The TCP/IP model is *pragmatic* — it merges OSI's top three layers into one (because, in practice, applications handle their own session/presentation concerns) and merges the bottom two into one (because the link layer is hardware-specific and rarely inspected by software except by NIC drivers).

## 2.3 The TCP Three-Way Handshake — The Heartbeat of Every Connection

TCP is **connection-oriented** and **reliable**. Before any payload is exchanged, three packets establish the session:

**Step 1 — SYN:** Client sends a segment with the **SYN** flag set, and an **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** chosen randomly (ISN = $S_c$).

**Step 2 — SYN-ACK:** Server responds with **SYN + ACK** flags set, its own ISN ($S_s$), and acknowledges the client's ISN ($S_c + 1$).

**Step 3 — ACK:** Client acknowledges the server's ISN ($S_s + 1$). Connection is now **ESTABLISHED**.

This three-packet dance is the *foundational detection primitive* for countless IDPS rules:
- **SYN flood detection** — count of SYN packets without matching ACKs.
- **Port scan detection** — SYN to a port with no SYN-ACK response (or RST).
- **Session hijack detection** — unexpected sequence number anomalies.

## 2.4 The IPv4 Header — The Most Inspected Structure in IDPS

An IPv4 header is a **20-byte (minimum)** fixed structure, followed by optional fields, with bit-level field definitions critical for inspection:

| Offset (bits) | Field | Length (bits) | Purpose |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|
| 0 | Version | 4 | Always 4 for IPv4 |
| 4 | IHL (Header Length) | 4 | Header length in 32-bit words (min 5) |
| 8 | DSCP / TOS | 8 | Quality of Service class |
| 16 | Total Length | 16 | Entire packet size in bytes (max 65535) |
| 32 | Identification | 16 | Used for fragmentation reassembly |
| 48 | Flags (3 bits) + Fragment Offset (13 bits) | 16 | MF (More Fragments), DF (Don't Fragment) |
| 64 | TTL | 8 | Decremented at each hop; prevents loops |
| 72 | Protocol | 8 | Next-layer protocol (6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP) |
| 80 | Header Checksum | 16 | Error detection over header only |
| 96 | Source IP Address | 32 | Sender's logical address |
| 128 | Destination IP Address | 32 | Receiver's logical address |
| 160 | Options (variable) | 0–320 | Rarely used; historically exploited |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 Board Exam Hot List**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IPv4 Address Space** | $N_{hosts} = 2^{32}$ | Total = 4,294,967,296 addresses |
| 2 | **Hosts per Subnet** | $H = 2^{h} - 2$ | $h$ = host bits; $-2$ reserves network & broadcast |
| 3 | **Subnets from Borrowed Bits** | $S = 2^{s}$ | $s$ = bits borrowed from host portion |
| 4 | **Subnet Mask (decimal)** | $M_d = (2^{n}-1) \cdot 2^{32-n}$ | $n$ = network prefix length |
| 5 | **Wildcard Mask** | $W = 2^{32} - 1 - M_d$ | Used in ACLs and Snort rule headers |
| 6 | **CIDR Notation** | $\text{a.b.c.d}/n$ | $n$ = network prefix bits |
| 7 | **Max IPv4 Packet** | $L_{max} = 2^{16} - 1$ | 65,535 bytes (Total Length field is 16-bit) |
| 8 | **Min IPv4 Header** | $L_{hdr} = 5 \cdot 4$ | 20 bytes (when IHL=5, no options) |
| 9 | **MTU (Ethernet)** | $L_{MTU} = 1500$ bytes | Maximum Transmission Unit for standard Ethernet |
| 10 | **IPv6 Address Space** | $N_{v6} = 2^{128}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses |
| 11 | **TCP Window Scaling** | $W_{eff} = W_{field} \cdot 2^{scale}$ | $W_{field}$ is 16-bit; scale is 0–14 |
| 12 | **Round-Trip Time (estimated)** | $SRTT = \alpha \cdot SRTT_{old} + (1-\alpha) \cdot RTT_{sample}$ | RFC 6298: $\alpha = 1/8$ |
| 13 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = BW \cdot RTT$ | Determines optimal TCP window size |
| 14 | **CRC-32 Polynomial** | $G(x) = x^{32} + x^{26} + \dots + x + 1$ | Used in Ethernet FCS field |
| 15 | **TCP Sequence Number Space** | $2^{32}$ | 4,294,967,296 — basis for ISN randomization |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Operations Centers (NOCs):** Use OSI layering to isolate faults ("Is it a Layer 1 cable issue or a Layer 7 application bug?").
- **Firewall Rule Design:** Stateless firewalls operate at L3/L4; Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW) and IDPS operate at L7 with full DPI.
- **Penetration Testing:** Every attack (MITM, ARP poisoning, DNS spoofing, SYN flood) maps cleanly to a specific layer.
- **5G/SDN Architectures:** Network slicing and Software-Defined Networking extend the layered model into virtualization domains.
- **Cyber Range Simulations:** KTU-affiliated labs use EVE-NG / GNS3 to simulate multi-layer topologies for IDPS testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — IPv4 Subnetting (KTU Board Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Given the network **192.168.10.0/24**, design **4 equal subnets**. For each subnet, calculate:
> (a) Subnet mask in decimal
> (b) Network address, First host, Last host, Broadcast address
> (c) Number of usable hosts

### Step 1 — Identify the Default Mask and Required Bits

The default mask for a /24 is:

$$M_{default} = 255.255.255.0 = \text{11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000}$$

To create **4 subnets**, we need $s$ additional bits such that $2^{s} \geq 4$.

$$2^{s} \geq 4 \implies s = 2 \text{ bits}$$

The new prefix length becomes $n' = 24 + 2 = 26$ (i.e., **/26**).

### Step 2 — Compute the New Subnet Mask in Binary

Borrowing 2 bits from the host portion (the last octet):

$$M_{new} = \text{11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000}$$

In decimal, the last octet evaluates as:

$$128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 192$$

Therefore:

$$M_{new} = 255.255.255.192$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Number of Usable Hosts

After borrowing 2 bits, the host portion retains $h = 32 - 26 = 6$ bits.

$$H = 2^{h} - 2 = 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ hosts per subnet}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Block Size (Magic Number)

The block size in the last octet equals $256 - 192 = 64$.

### Step 5 — Enumerate All Four Subnets

| Subnet # | Network Address | First Host | Last Host | Broadcast | Wildcard Mask |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 192.168.10.0/26 | 192.168.10.1 | 192.168.10.62 | 192.168.10.63 | 0.0.0.63 |
| **2** | 192.168.10.64/26 | 192.168.10.65 | 192.168.10.126 | 192.168.10.127 | 0.0.0.63 |
| **3** | 192.168.10.128/26 | 192.168.10.129 | 192.168.10.190 | 192.168.10.191 | 0.0.0.63 |
| **4** | 192.168.10.192/26 | 192.168.10.193 | 192.168.10.254 | 192.168.10.255 | 0.0.0.63 |

### Step 6 — Verification (Total Hosts Across All Subnets)

$$H_{total} = 4 \cdot 62 = 248$$

This matches the original /24 capacity of $2^{8} - 2 = 254$ minus the 6 "lost" addresses from the additional subnets' network/broadcast boundaries (acceptable accounting overhead).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A server in Bangalore transmits data to a client in Kochi over a link with bandwidth $BW = 100 \text{ Mbps}$ and measured round-trip time $RTT = 40 \text{ ms}$. Compute the BDP and recommend a minimum TCP window size.

### Step 1 — State the Formula

$$BDP = BW \cdot RTT$$

### Step 2 — Normalize Units

$$BW = 100 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/second}$$

$$RTT = 40 \text{ ms} = 40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Compute

$$
\begin{aligned}
BDP &= (100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}) \times (40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}) \\
&= 100 \times 40 \times 10^{6-3} \text{ bits} \\
&= 4000 \times 10^{3} \text{ bits} \\
&= 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits} \\
&= 500{,}000 \text{ bytes} \quad (\div 8)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Result

To saturate the link, the sender must keep **at least 500 KB** of unacknowledged data in flight. The default TCP window field (16-bit) caps at 65,535 bytes — *insufficient* for this link. Therefore **Window Scaling** (RFC 7323) must be enabled.

### Step 5 — Required Scale Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{eff} &= W_{field} \cdot 2^{scale} \geq 500{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
2^{scale} &\geq \frac{500{,}000}{65{,}535} \approx 7.63 \\
scale &\geq \lceil \log_2(7.63) \rceil = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

A scale factor of **3** (i.e., multiplier of 8) yields $W_{eff} = 524{,}280$ bytes — sufficient.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — IDPS Detection Anchors per Protocol

| Protocol | Layer | Common Attack | Detection Primitive | Snort-style Field |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ARP | 2 | ARP Spoofing / Poisoning | Gratuitous ARP frequency, MAC-IP binding change | `eth.arp` |
| ICMP | 3 | Ping of Death, Smurf, ICMP tunneling | ICMP type/code anomalies, payload size | `icmp.type` |
| IPv4 | 3 | IP spoofing, fragmentation overlap | TTL consistency, fragment reassembly | `ip.src` |
| TCP | 4 | SYN flood, port scan, session hijack | SYN:ACK ratio, port distribution | `tcp.flags` |
| UDP | 4 | UDP flood, amplification (NTP/DNS) | Source-destination bandwidth ratio | `udp.length` |
| DNS | 7 | DNS tunneling, amplification, cache poisoning | Query entropy, TXT record abuse | `dns.query` |
| HTTP | 7 | SQLi, XSS, command injection | URI pattern matching, payload regex | `http.uri` |
| TLS/SSL | 6 | Heartbleed, POODLE, weak ciphers | Certificate anomaly, cipher suite blacklists | `tls.version` |

---

## 3.4 Sequential Walkthrough — TCP Three-Way Handshake (Detailed)

For a 14-mark question on TCP, follow this exhaustive structure:

**Step 1 — Client → Server (SYN)**

- Flags: `SYN = 1`, `ACK = 0`
- Sequence Number: $SEQ = x$ (random ISN)
- Acknowledgment Number: Not applicable (set to 0)
- Window Size: $W_c$ (client's receive window)

**Step 2 — Server → Client (SYN + ACK)**

- Flags: `SYN = 1`, `ACK = 1`
- Sequence Number: $SEQ = y$ (server's random ISN)
- Acknowledgment Number: $ACK = x + 1$ (acknowledges client's $SEQ + 1$)
- Window Size: $W_s$ (server's receive window)

**Step 3 — Client → Server (ACK)**

- Flags: `SYN = 0`, `ACK = 1`
- Sequence Number: $SEQ = x + 1$
- Acknowledgment Number: $ACK = y + 1$ (acknowledges server's ISN + 1)
- Connection state on both ends: **ESTABLISHED**

> [!NOTE]
> An IDPS rule for *SYN flood* watches for high SYN counts with low SYN-ACK rates, indicating a half-open connection queue exhaustion attempt.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — TCP/IP Encapsulation in an HTTP GET Request

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appData["Application Data HTTP GET request line headers and body"]
    tcpSeg["Transport Segment TCP Header Src Port Dst Port 80 SEQ ACK Flags Window"]
    ipPkt["Internet Packet IP Header Version 4 TTL Protocol 6 Src IP Dst IP"]
    ethFrm["Link Frame Ethernet Header Dest MAC Src MAC EtherType 0x0800"]
    physBits["Physical Bits Electrical or optical signals on the medium"]
    appData --> tcpSeg
    tcpSeg --> ipPkt
    ipPkt --> ethFrm
    ethFrm --> physBits
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows **top-to-bottom** on the *sender* (encapsulation). At the receiver, the process is **inverted** (decapsulation) — bits are reassembled into a frame, frame into a packet, packet into a segment, and segment into application data. An **inline IPS** intercepts at the **packet** or **segment** stage; an **IDS** receives a copy via SPAN/TAP.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — IDPS Placement in a Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet["Internet Cloud"]
    firewall["Perimeter Firewall Layer 3 and 4"]
    ips["Inline IPS Layer 7 Deep Inspection"]
    ids["IDS Sensor Span Port Passive"]
    switch["Core L3 Switch"]
    serverFarm["Internal Server Farm Web DB Mail"]
    dmz["DMZ Web Servers"]
    internet --> firewall
    firewall --> ips
    ips --> switch
    switch --> ids
    ids -.monitors.-> switch
    switch --> dmz
    switch --> serverFarm
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **IPS** sits *inline* and can drop packets in real time (active response). The **IDS** sits *offline* (span port or TAP) and only *alerts*. In high-throughput environments (10 Gbps+), inline IPS placement must be carefully engineered to avoid becoming a bottleneck.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — OSI Seven-Layer Mapping with IDPS Action per Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    l7["Layer 7 Application HTTP DNS SMTP"]
    l6["Layer 6 Presentation TLS SSL MIME"]
    l5["Layer 5 Session NetBIOS RPC SIP"]
    l4["Layer 4 Transport TCP UDP Port Scan Detection"]
    l3["Layer 3 Network IPv4 ICMP IP Spoof Detection"]
    l2["Layer 2 Data Link Ethernet ARP MAC Flood Detection"]
    l1["Layer 1 Physical Copper Fiber RF Wire Tap Detection"]
    l7 --> l6 --> l5 --> l4 --> l3 --> l2 --> l1
    idps1["IDPS Watches Here"] -.-> l7
    idps2["IDPS Watches Here"] -.-> l4
    idps3["IDPS Watches Here"] -.-> l3
    idps4["IDPS Watches Here"] -.-> l2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A well-designed IDPS architecture monitors at **multiple layers simultaneously** — a single-layer focus (e.g., only Layer 4) misses application-layer attacks like SQL injection and HTTP smuggling.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — IPv4 Header Field Layout (Bit-Offset View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bit0["0 to 3 Version"]
    bit4["4 to 7 IHL"]
    bit8["8 to 15 DSCP"]
    bit16["16 to 31 Total Length"]
    bit32["32 to 47 Identification"]
    bit48["48 to 50 Flags"]
    bit51["51 to 63 Fragment Offset"]
    bit64["64 to 71 TTL"]
    bit72["72 to 79 Protocol"]
    bit80["80 to 95 Header Checksum"]
    bit96["96 to 127 Source IP"]
    bit128["128 to 159 Destination IP"]
    bit160["160 to 191 Options"]
    bit192["192 on Payload"]
    bit0 --> bit4 --> bit8 --> bit16 --> bit32 --> bit48 --> bit64 --> bit72 --> bit80 --> bit96 --> bit128 --> bit160 --> bit192
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Context:** An IDPS engine like Suricata or Zeek parses these fields in order. Misalignment (e.g., IHL field misread) causes *all downstream fields to be mis-parsed*, producing both false positives and false negatives.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the OSI Reference Model. List all seven layers in order. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model**, defined by ISO/IEC 7498-1, is a seven-layer conceptual framework that standardizes the functions of a communication system into abstraction layers.

The seven layers from top to bottom are:
1. **Application (Layer 7)**
2. **Presentation (Layer 6)**
3. **Session (Layer 5)**
4. **Transport (Layer 4)**
5. **Network (Layer 3)**
6. **Data Link (Layer 2)**
7. **Physical (Layer 1)**

Each layer provides services to the layer above and uses services from the layer below, enabling interoperability between heterogeneous systems. *(Full 3-mark answer complete.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between TCP and UDP. Why is TCP preferred for IDPS rule delivery? `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed (ACK, retransmit) | Best-effort, no ACK |
| Header Size | 20 bytes (min) | 8 bytes |
| Ordering | Maintains sequence | No ordering |
| Speed | Slower (overhead) | Faster (low overhead) |
| Use Cases | HTTP, SSH, SMTP, IDPS rule sync | DNS, VoIP, video streaming |

**IDPS Relevance:** TCP is preferred for **rule signature updates, alert delivery, and centralized management communication** because reliable, ordered delivery ensures no detection rule is silently dropped. UDP, being connectionless, is favored in the *attack traffic* it observes (e.g., DNS tunneling, UDP flood). *(Full 3-mark answer complete.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in IDPS papers carry **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer *one full question* from a choice of *two alternatives*.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Networking Foundations for IDPS

`[KTU University Exam – PECCT742 Dec 2024 / Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the TCP/IP Four-Layer Model. Compare it with the OSI Seven-Layer Model.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The TCP/IP model, also known as the Internet Protocol Suite, is the practical implementation framework used in modern networks. It was developed by DARPA and standardized through the IETF.

**Four Layers of TCP/IP (3 marks):**

1. **Application Layer:** Combines OSI's Application, Presentation, and Session layers. Handles high-level protocols like HTTP, FTP, DNS, and SSH. PDU: Data/Message.

2. **Transport Layer:** Maps to OSI Layer 4. Provides end-to-end communication services. Core protocols: TCP (reliable) and UDP (unreliable). PDU: Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP).

3. **Internet Layer:** Maps to OSI Layer 3. Provides logical addressing and routing. Core protocols: IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, ARP (in some models). PDU: Packet/Datagram.

4. **Link (Network Access) Layer:** Maps to OSI Layers 1 and 2. Handles physical addressing (MAC), framing, and physical transmission. PDU: Frame.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 7 | 4 |
| Origin | ISO standard | DARPA / IETF |
| Adoption | Reference model | Practical implementation |
| Top Layers | Separated (App, Pres, Sess) | Merged into Application |
| Bottom Layers | Separated (Phy, DLL) | Merged into Link |
| Protocol Dependency | Protocol-independent | Protocol-centric |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The OSI model provides a clean theoretical abstraction, while TCP/IP is the deployed reality. For IDPS engineering, both are essential — OSI for conceptual reasoning, TCP/IP for actual packet parsing.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Describe the IPv4 Header Structure with a Neat Diagram. Explain the Role of TTL, Protocol, and Fragmentation Fields in IDPS Detection.

**Model Answer:**

**IPv4 Header Diagram (3 marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid Diagram for the bit-offset layout. In a written exam, students should draw a 32-bit wide table showing: Version | IHL | DSCP | Total Length | Identification | Flags+FragOffset | TTL | Protocol | Checksum | Source IP | Destination IP | Options | Data)*

**Role of TTL (1.5 marks):**
- **Time To Live (TTL)** is an 8-bit field decremented by 1 at every router hop.
- If TTL reaches zero, the packet is dropped and an ICMP "Time Exceeded" message is sent back.
- **IDPS Use:** TTL analysis detects **IP spoofing** (anomalous TTL values for a claimed source) and **traceroute reconnaissance** (incrementing TTLs from 1).

**Role of Protocol Field (1 mark):**
- 8-bit field identifying the next-layer protocol (6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 1 = ICMP).
- **IDPS Use:** Protocol field validation prevents **protocol-based evasion** (e.g., embedding malicious data in a non-standard protocol number).

**Role of Fragmentation (1.5 marks):**
- IP supports packet fragmentation when payload exceeds MTU.
- Fields involved: **Identification**, **Flags** (DF, MF), **Fragment Offset**.
- **IDPS Use:** Fragmentation overlap and tiny-fragment attacks are detected by reassembly-based engines. Teardrop attacks exploit malformed fragment offsets.

**Conclusion:** The IPv4 header is the *primary inspection surface* for network IDPS — every field is a potential detection vector.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Protocol Analysis and Attack Surface Mapping

`[KTU University Exam – PECCT742 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the TCP Three-Way Handshake. How Does IDPS Use It to Detect SYN Flood Attacks?

**Model Answer:**

**Step-by-Step Handshake (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — SYN (Client → Server):**
- Client sends a TCP segment with **SYN flag = 1**.
- Carries an **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** randomly chosen as $SEQ = x$.

**Step 2 — SYN-ACK (Server → Client):**
- Server responds with **SYN = 1, ACK = 1**.
- Server's ISN: $SEQ = y$.
- Acknowledgment: $ACK = x + 1$.

**Step 3 — ACK (Client → Server):**
- Client sends **ACK = 1**.
- Sequence: $SEQ = x + 1$.
- Acknowledgment: $ACK = y + 1$.
- Connection state: **ESTABLISHED** on both sides.

**SYN Flood Detection by IDPS (3 marks):**

A **SYN flood** is a Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack where the attacker sends massive volumes of SYN packets with **spoofed source IPs**. The server allocates half-open connection state for each, exhausting its backlog queue.

**IDPS Detection Methods:**

1. **Threshold-Based:** Alert if SYN count from a single source IP exceeds threshold $T_{SYN}$ (e.g., 100 SYNs/sec).
2. **Ratio-Based:** Alert if $\frac{\text{SYN count}}{\text{SYN-ACK count}} > R$ (e.g., ratio > 3:1).
3. **State Tracking:** Maintain a connection table; count half-open sessions per source. If count exceeds limit, flag.
4. **Anomaly Behavior:** Compare against historical baseline — sudden SYN spike from a non-business source.
5. **Response Action:** Modern IPS devices send **TCP RST** to the attacker or employ **SYN cookies** (RFC 4987) to defeat stateless queue exhaustion.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Describe Common Network Devices — Hub, Bridge, Switch, Router, and Firewall. Explain Their IDPS Relevance.

**Model Answer:**

| Device | OSI Layer | Function | IDPS Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hub** | 1 | Repeats electrical signals to all ports | Cannot filter; broadcasts to all — easiest sniffing target |
| **Bridge** | 2 | Forwards frames based on MAC table; segments collision domains | Reduces unnecessary traffic; obsolete in modern nets |
| **Switch** | 2 (L3 switches do L3 too) | Intelligent frame forwarding using MAC table; supports VLANs | **SPAN/Mirror port** allows IDS to passively observe traffic |
| **Router** | 3 | Forwards packets between networks using IP routing tables | Routing anomalies, IP spoofing detection points |
| **Firewall** | 3, 4, 7 | Filters traffic based on rules; NGFW does DPI | **First line of defense**; complements IDPS |

**Key IDPS Deployment Insight (2 marks):**

- A **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer)** port on a switch mirrors traffic to an IDS sensor — *passive monitoring*.
- An **IPS** is deployed **inline** (e.g., between firewall and switch) — *active blocking*.
- **Network TAPs (Test Access Points)** provide a more reliable alternative to SPAN for full-duplex high-speed links.

**Conclusion:** The choice of network device architecture directly determines where an IDPS can be physically placed and what visibility it has into traffic flows.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Before Writing the Exam):**
>
> 1. **Confusing OSI Layer Numbers:** Many students mix up Layer 3 (Network) and Layer 4 (Transport). *IP = Layer 3. TCP/UDP = Layer 4.* This single error can cost 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "-2" in Host Calculation:** Always subtract **2** (network address + broadcast address) when computing usable hosts. $H = 2^h - 2$, NOT $2^h$.
>
> 3. **Drawing a TCP Header Without Flags:** The three-way handshake answer without explicit **SYN, ACK, SEQ, ACK#** values per step is incomplete. Examiners expect **flag states** and **sequence numbers** for full marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing MTU and MSS:** MTU = Layer 3 maximum (1500 for Ethernet). MSS = TCP payload maximum (MTU − 40 = 1460 typically). Mixing these = 1-mark loss.
>
> 5. **Omitting Diagram Labels:** An IPv4 header diagram without field names and bit offsets is treated as incomplete. Always label: Version, IHL, Total Length, TTL, Protocol, Source/Dest IP.
>
> 6. **Wrong CIDR Notation:** Writing `192.168.1.0/24` is correct; writing `192.168.1.0:24` or `192.168.1.0-24` is a notation error.
>
> 7. **Missing IDPS Context:** In PECCT742, every networking question expects a **security/IDPS angle**. A pure networking answer without IDPS relevance loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 Board Exam Day:**

- [ ] OSI has **7 layers**; TCP/IP has **4 layers**. Memorize top-down and bottom-up mnemonics.
- [ ] Each layer's **PDU name**: Application = Data, Transport = Segment/Datagram, Network = Packet, Data Link = Frame, Physical = Bits.
- [ ] **IPv4 address = 32 bits** (4 octets); **IPv6 address = 128 bits** (8 hextets).
- [ ] **TCP = reliable, connection-oriented, 3-way handshake.** **UDP = unreliable, connectionless, fire-and-forget.**
- [ ] **3-way handshake steps:** **SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK.** Sequence numbers increment by 1 on ACK.
- [ ] **Subnetting formula:** $H = 2^{host-bits} - 2$. **Subnet count:** $S = 2^{borrowed-bits}$.
- [ ] **Block size** = 256 − (last non-zero octet of mask). Used to enumerate subnets.
- [ ] **CIDR notation** `/n` denotes $n$ network prefix bits.
- [ ] **MTU (Ethernet) = 1500 bytes.** MSS = MTU − 40 (for typical IPv4+TCP).
- [ ] **TTL** prevents routing loops; **decremented by 1 per hop**; ICMP "Time Exceeded" sent on expiry.
- [ ] **Fragmentation fields:** Identification, Flags (DF, MF), Fragment Offset. Used in Teardrop-style attacks.
- [ ] **Hub = Layer 1** (broadcast), **Switch = Layer 2** (MAC learning), **Router = Layer 3** (IP routing), **Firewall = L3/L4/L7** (filtering).
- [ ] **IDS = passive (SPAN/TAP).** **IPS = inline (active blocking).**
- [ ] **SYN flood detection** = monitor SYN-to-SYN-ACK ratio, threshold on half-open connections.
- [ ] **BDP = Bandwidth × RTT** — determines optimal TCP window size; often requires window scaling.
- [ ] **IDPS detects attacks at every layer** — not just L4. Application-layer (L7) attacks need DPI engines.
- [ ] Always draw **diagrams with labels** in the exam — unlabeled diagrams attract partial marking.
- [ ] **Wildcard mask** = 255.255.255.255 − subnet mask. Used in Snort/ACL rule headers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components and Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is not a single monolithic software; it is a **distributed security architecture** composed of multiple cooperating components that collectively perform data collection, analysis, detection, logging, and active response. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (Module 3), the *Components and Architecture* of an IDPS define the structural blueprint that dictates *where* monitoring occurs, *how* events are gathered, *how* analysis engines process them, and *how* responses are triggered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** An IDPS architecture is the logical and physical arrangement of **Sensors/Agents**, **Analyzers (Detection Engines)**, **Management Servers**, **Databases**, and **Response Modules** interconnected through secure communication channels to identify, log, and optionally block malicious activity.

The architecture fundamentally answers three engineering questions:

1. **Where is the data captured?** (Sensor placement — host vs. network boundary)
2. **Where is the data processed?** (Centralized vs. distributed analysis)
3. **How is the response delivered?** (Passive notification vs. inline blocking)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault Security System"

Imagine a high-security bank vault. The **IDPS** works exactly like the multi-layered security of a bank:

| Bank Vault Component | IDPS Architectural Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| CCTV cameras at every door | **Sensors / Agents** | Passive observers that capture raw footage (network packets or host logs) |
| Central monitoring room with security analysts | **Analyzer / Detection Engine** | The "brain" that watches footage and identifies suspicious behavior |
| Filing cabinet storing all incident reports | **Event Database / Knowledge Base** | Permanent storage of alerts, signatures, and historical data |
| Security guard's radio/computer | **Management Console** | The interface where admins configure rules and view alerts |
| Armed guard who physically blocks an intruder | **Prevention / Response Module** | Active inline component that drops packets, resets connections, or blocks IPs |
| Phone line between the guard and the analyst | **Secure Communication Channel** | Encrypted channel carrying alerts and policy updates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Just as a bank does not rely on *only* cameras *or* *only* guards, a robust IDPS uses both **sensors** (to see) and **response modules** (to act). The **analyzer** is the brain linking both.

## 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics
