The following metrics quantify an IDPS architecture's effectiveness and are **frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions**:

- **Detection Latency:** The time delay between an event occurrence and its detection. **Typical target: < 1 second** for inline (IPS) deployments.
- **Throughput:** Data volume the sensor can analyze per second, measured in **Mbps** or **Gbps** (for network IDPS) or **EPS (Events Per Second)** for log-based systems.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Ratio of benign events incorrectly flagged as malicious.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR):** Ratio of actual attacks missed by the system.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time from detection to active containment.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly maps to the cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** — meaning examiners will test both your *recall* of components and your *application* of architecture selection to given scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Components — Structural Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme consolidates IDPS architecture into **five primary components**. Each has a defined role, and the omission of any one breaks the detection chain.

### 2.1.1 Sensors / Agents
Sensors are the **peripheral nervous system** of the IDPS. They are deployed at strategic points (network chokepoints, host endpoints, or cloud workloads) and perform the following logical steps:

1. **Capture** raw data — network packets (NIDPS), system calls or log files (HIDPS), or API telemetry (cloud-native).
2. **Pre-process** — normalize timestamps, strip protocol headers, deduplicate.
3. **Forward** to the analyzer via a **secure, encrypted channel** (typically TLS/SSH).

> [!NOTE]
> A sensor **never makes a decision** on its own in a centralized architecture. It is a *data diode* — it captures and forwards.

### 2.1.2 Analyzers (Detection Engines)
The analyzer is the **CPU of the architecture**. It receives normalized events from one or more sensors and applies detection logic.

**Detection Method Categories (KTU High-Yield):**

- **Signature-Based (Misuse Detection):** Pattern matching against a known-attack database.
- **Anomaly-Based:** Statistical deviation from a learned baseline.
- **State-Based:** Tracks protocol state machines (e.g., TCP handshake violations).
- **Protocol-Decoding / Behavioral:** Deep inspection of protocol semantics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The analyzer's choice of detection method determines the system's *signature database size*, *false positive rate*, and *processing throughput*.

### 2.1.3 Management Server (Console)
The management server is the **administrative nerve center**. It:

- Distributes **policies and signatures** to sensors and analyzers.
- Aggregates **alerts** from one or more analyzers.
- Provides the **GUI/dashboard** for security analysts.
- Manages **user authentication and RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)**.

### 2.1.4 Database / Knowledge Base
The database is the **memory** of the architecture. It typically stores:

- **Alert logs** (event_id, timestamp, severity, source, destination).
- **Signature database** (CVE-mapped rules).
- **Baseline profiles** (for anomaly detection).
- **Forensic evidence** (PCAPs, session captures).

### 2.1.5 Response Module (Inline Prevention Engine)
This is what differentiates an **IDS** from an **IPS**. The response module can:

- **Passive:** Log, alert (email/SMS/SIEM), notify.
- **Active (Inline):** Drop malicious packets, reset TCP connections, block source IP via ACL, quarantine host.

## 2.2 Architectural Topologies

KTU Module 3 emphasizes three architectural topologies, each with distinct traffic-placement semantics.

### 2.2.1 Passive (IDS Mode) — Out-of-Band Monitoring
The sensor is connected to a **SPAN port** or **network TAP**. Traffic is **copied** to the sensor; the original flow is uninterrupted. Detection happens, but blocking requires external action (e.g., a firewall rule push).

### 2.2.2 Inline (IPS Mode) — In-Band Enforcement
The sensor is **physically inserted into the data path**. All traffic must pass through it. This enables **real-time packet dropping** but introduces a **single point of failure** and **latency overhead**.

### 2.2.3 Hybrid (Cloud + On-Premise)
Modern enterprises deploy **cloud-native sensors** feeding a **centralized on-premise analyzer**, or use **distributed analyzers** for scale.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Expression | Unit / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_{latency}$ | Detection Latency | $D_{latency} = T_{detect} - T_{event}$ | Seconds; must be $< 1$ s for inline IPS |
| $FPR$ | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Dimensionless; range $[0, 1]$ |
| $FNR$ | False Negative Rate | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Dimensionless; range $[0, 1]$ |
| $TPR$ | True Positive Rate (Recall) | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Dimensionless; equals $1 - FNR$ |
| $Precision$ | Detection Precision | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Dimensionless; high precision $\Rightarrow$ low false alarms |
| $F_1$ | F1-Score (harmonic mean) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot TPR}{Precision + TPR}$ | Range $[0, 1]$; $F_1 = 1$ is perfect |
| $Throughput$ | Sensor capacity | $Throughput = \frac{Packets_{analyzed}}{T_{window}}$ | Mbps or Gbps |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Respond | $MTTR = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{respond_i} - T_{detect_i})$ | Seconds/Minutes |
| $CoV$ | Coefficient of Variation (anomaly baseline) | $CoV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu}$ | Ratio; threshold typically $> 3$ flags anomaly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **SIEM tuning**, **SOC (Security Operations Center) performance reviews**, and **vendor benchmarking** (e.g., NSS Labs reports). For KTU, expect at least one numerical/short problem on **FPR/FNR trade-offs**.

## 2.4 Why Architecture Choice Matters

The architectural decision (passive vs. inline, host vs. network, centralized vs. distributed) directly impacts:

- **Detection Coverage:** A single network sensor cannot see encrypted traffic or host-level syscalls.
- **Performance:** Inline IPS adds **0.5–2 ms latency** per packet at Gbps speeds.
- **Resilience:** A centralized analyzer is a **single point of failure**; distributed analyzers provide redundancy.
- **Evasion Resistance:** A sensor on a single network segment is blind to attacks launched from other segments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: IDPS Detection Latency Equation

The **Detection Latency** is one of the most heavily weighted parameters in KTU 14-mark problems. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Time Stamps
Let the original security event occur at time:

$$T_{event} = t_0$$

The sensor captures the event at time $T_{cap}$ after network/host propagation delay $\delta_1$:

$$T_{cap} = t_0 + \delta_1$$

> **Logic:** $\delta_1$ represents the time for the packet to travel from the source to the sensor tap point (network IDPS) or the time for the OS audit subsystem to flush the log (host IDPS).

### Step 2: Pre-Processing Delay
The sensor normalizes, deduplicates, and packages the event. Let this be $\delta_2$:

$$T_{preprocessed} = T_{cap} + \delta_2 = t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2$$

> **Logic:** $\delta_2$ is a deterministic function of sensor CPU load and event volume.

### Step 3: Transmission to Analyzer
The event is transmitted over the secure management channel. Let $\delta_3$ be the network transit time:

$$T_{analyzer\_in} = T_{preprocessed} + \delta_3 = t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3$$

### Step 4: Analyzer Detection Time
The analyzer matches signatures, runs anomaly scoring, and produces a verdict in time $\delta_4$:

$$T_{detect} = T_{analyzer\_in} + \delta_4 = t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4$$

### Step 5: Combine All Delay Components

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{latency} &= T_{detect} - T_{event} \\
&= (t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4) - t_0 \\
&= \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Optimization Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final General Form:**
>
> $$D_{latency} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i$$
>
> where each $\delta_i$ is an independent delay stage. To minimize $D_{latency}$, an architect must **identify the dominant $\delta_i$** (often $\delta_3$ — network transit to a remote analyzer) and **co-locate** the analyzer with the sensor, or use **distributed analysis**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: FPR/FNR Trade-off Calculation (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**
An IDPS was evaluated on a test dataset of **10,000** network events. The confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted Malicious | Predicted Benign |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Malicious** | 180 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
| **Actual Benign** | 50 (FP) | 9,750 (TN) |

**Required:** Calculate $FPR$, $FNR$, $TPR$, $Precision$, and $F_1$.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the values.**
- $TP = 180$, $FN = 20$, $FP = 50$, $TN = 9750$

**Step 2: Calculate False Positive Rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
&= \frac{50}{50 + 9750} \\
&= \frac{50}{9800} \\
&= 0.005102 \\
&\approx 0.51\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate False Negative Rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
FNR &= \frac{FN}{FN + TP} \\
&= \frac{20}{20 + 180} \\
&= \frac{20}{200} \\
&= 0.10 \\
&= 10.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate True Positive Rate (Recall).**

$$\begin{aligned}
TPR &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{180}{180 + 20} \\
&= 0.90 \\
&= 90.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Precision.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{180}{180 + 50} \\
&= \frac{180}{230} \\
&= 0.7826 \\
&\approx 78.26\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Calculate F1-Score.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot TPR}{Precision + TPR} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.7826 \cdot 0.90}{0.7826 + 0.90} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.70434}{1.6826} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.4186 \\
&= 0.8372 \\
&\approx 83.72\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** A **low FPR (0.51%)** is excellent — the system rarely cries wolf. A **moderate FNR (10%)** means 1 in 10 attacks slip through. The F1 of **83.72%** indicates a well-balanced system suitable for production deployment.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Centralized IDPS Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[End User Workstation]:::host -->|Network Traffic| sw1[Core Switch]:::netdev
    src2[Internal Server]:::host -->|Network Traffic| sw1
    src3[Cloud Workload]:::cloud -->|API Telemetry| sw1
    sw1 -->|SPAN Port Mirror| sensor1[Network Sensor 1]:::sensor
    sw1 -->|SPAN Port Mirror| sensor2[Network Sensor 2]:::sensor
    host1[Critical Database Host]:::host -->|Host Logs| agent1[HIDPS Agent]:::agent
    host2[Web Server Host]:::host -->|Host Logs| agent1
    sensor1 -->|TLS Encrypted Events| analyzer1[Central Analyzer Engine]:::engine
    sensor2 -->|TLS Encrypted Events| analyzer1
    agent1 -->|TLS Encrypted Events| analyzer1
    analyzer1 -->|Query/Update| db1[(Signature Database)]:::db
    analyzer1 -->|Query/Update| db2[(Event Log Store)]:::db
    analyzer1 -->|Alert Stream| mgr1[Management Server Console]:::console
    mgr1 -->|Policy Push| sensor1
    mgr1 -->|Policy Push| sensor2
    mgr1 -->|Policy Push| agent1
    mgr1 -->|Block Command via API| fw1[Perimeter Firewall]:::fw
    analyzer1 -->|Inline Drop Packet| sensor1
    classDef host fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef netdev fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef agent fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#e5dbff,stroke:#6741d9,color:#000
    classDef db fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,color:#000
    classDef console fill:#fcc2d7,stroke:#c2255c,color:#000
    classDef fw fill:#ffec99,stroke:#fab005,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram (KTU-style):**
- The **left side** shows *data origin* (hosts, servers, cloud).
- The **center top** shows *capture* (sensors + agents).
- The **center** shows *processing* (analyzer + databases).
- The **right side** shows *response* (console + firewall).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Inline (IPS) vs. Passive (IDS) Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDS["PASSIVE IDS ARCHITECTURE - Out of Band"]
        direction LR
        a1[Client]:::host -->|Traffic| b1[Router]:::netdev
        b1 -->|Traffic| c1[Server]:::host
        b1 -.->|SPAN Copy| d1[IDS Sensor]:::sensor
        d1 -->|Alert Only| e1[Analyst]:::console
    end
    subgraph IPS["INLINE IPS ARCHITECTURE - In Band"]
        direction LR
        a2[Client]:::host -->|Traffic Must Pass| d2[IPS Sensor]:::sensor
        d2 -->|Allow or Drop| b2[Router]:::netdev
        b2 -->|Traffic| c2[Server]:::host
        d2 -->|Block Action| e2[Analyst]:::console
    end
    classDef host fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef netdev fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef console fill:#fcc2d7,stroke:#c2255c,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Difference:** In **IDS mode**, the sensor receives a *copy* via dotted line (logical mirror) — original traffic is never touched. In **IPS mode**, the sensor sits on the *solid path* — every packet must pass through it for verdict (allow/drop).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: IDPS Component Interaction Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User/Host
    participant S as Sensor
    participant A as Analyzer Engine
    participant DB as Signature Database
    participant M as Management Console
    participant R as Response Module
    U->>S: Step 1 - Generate Traffic or Log Event
    S->>S: Step 2 - Capture and Normalize
    S->>A: Step 3 - Forward Encrypted Event
    A->>DB: Step 4 - Query for Matching Signature
    DB-->>A: Step 5 - Return Signature Match Result
    A->>A: Step 6 - Apply Detection Logic and Scoring
    A->>M: Step 7 - Raise Alert to Console
    A->>R: Step 8 - Trigger Response Action
    R-->>U: Step 9 - Block Drop or Allow Packet
    M->>S: Step 10 - Push Updated Policy
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the five core components of an IDPS architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

The five core components of an IDPS architecture are:

1. **Sensors/Agents** — Distributed at network chokepoints (network sensors) or host endpoints (agents) to capture raw traffic and system events. They forward normalized data to the analyzer.

2. **Analyzers (Detection Engines)** — The central processing units that apply signature-based, anomaly-based, or stateful detection methods to identify malicious activity.

3. **Management Server (Console)** — The administrative interface used to configure sensors, push signatures, view dashboards, and manage user roles.

4. **Database / Knowledge Base** — Stores signature definitions, alert logs, baseline profiles, and forensic evidence for correlation and post-incident analysis.

5. **Response Module** — Executes passive (alerts/logs) or active (packet drop, TCP reset, IP block) countermeasures based on the analyzer's verdict.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between passive (IDS) and inline (IPS) architectures in terms of traffic placement and response capability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Passive IDS Architecture | Inline IPS Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic placement | Out-of-band via SPAN port or TAP | In-band, physically on the data path |
| Traffic interruption | None (original flow untouched) | All packets must pass through sensor |
| Response capability | Alerts only; cannot block directly | Real-time packet drop and connection reset |
| Failure impact | Failure does not affect traffic | Sensor failure can break connectivity (single point of failure) |
| Latency added | Negligible (passive observer) | 0.5–2 ms per packet at Gbps speeds |
| Deployment | Monitoring and forensics | Active enforcement at perimeter |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Performance Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the centralized IDPS architecture and the interaction between its components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IDPS analyzer processes **50,000** events per day. The system produced **320** alerts, of which **240** were true positives, **80** were false positives, and **45** real attacks were missed. Calculate the False Positive Rate, False Negative Rate, Precision, and F1-Score. Comment on the system's detection quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Centralized IDPS Architecture:**

A centralized IDPS architecture consists of distributed sensors/agents feeding a single analyzer engine, with a unified management console and shared database.

**Key Components and Their Interaction:**

- **Sensors** are deployed at network segments (using SPAN ports or TAPs) and at host endpoints (as software agents). They capture traffic and audit logs.

- **Agents** at each critical host forward system call traces, file integrity checks, and log entries to the central analyzer over an encrypted TLS channel.

- **Analyzer Engine** receives the pre-processed data and applies multi-method detection (signature, anomaly, stateful protocol analysis). It queries the signature database in real time.

- **Signature Database** holds the latest CVE-mapped rules. The analyzer's verdict (malicious/benign) is logged in the **Event Database**.

- **Management Console** provides a unified dashboard for SOC analysts, distributes signature updates, and pushes policies back to sensors.

- **Response Module** receives a verdict from the analyzer. In inline mode, it drops packets instantly; in passive mode, it generates alerts to the console, which may then push a firewall block via API.

> [!TIP]
> **Diagrammatic Note (for answer sheet):** Draw a block diagram with *sensors/agents on the left*, *analyzer + databases in the center*, and *management console + response module on the right*. Use arrows to show bidirectional encrypted communication.

**Advantages:** Single point of policy control, easier signature management.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, scalability bottleneck, high bandwidth to central site.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given Data:**
- Total alerts generated: $320$
- True Positives: $TP = 240$
- False Positives: $FP = 80$
- Missed attacks (False Negatives): $FN = 45$

**Step 1: Calculate False Positive Rate (FPR).**

We need the True Negatives. Total benign events = Total events $-$ Real attacks.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total events} &= 50{,}000 \\
\text{Real attacks} &= TP + FN = 240 + 45 = 285 \\
TN &= 50{,}000 - 285 = 49{,}715
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 49{,}715} \\
&= \frac{80}{49{,}795} \\
&= 0.001607 \\
&\approx 0.16\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate False Negative Rate (FNR).**

$$\begin{aligned}
FNR &= \frac{FN}{FN + TP} \\
&= \frac{45}{45 + 240} \\
&= \frac{45}{285} \\
&= 0.1579 \\
&\approx 15.79\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Precision.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{240}{240 + 80} \\
&= \frac{240}{320} \\
&= 0.75 \\
&= 75.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate F1-Score.**

$$\begin{aligned}
TPR &= 1 - FNR = 1 - 0.1579 = 0.8421 \\
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot TPR}{Precision + TPR} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.75 \cdot 0.8421}{0.75 + 0.8421} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.6316}{1.5921} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.3967 \\
&= 0.7934 \\
&\approx 79.34\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on Detection Quality:**

> [!NOTE]
> The system exhibits a **very low FPR (0.16%)**, meaning analysts will rarely be distracted by false alarms. However, the **FNR of 15.79%** indicates that nearly 1 in 6 attacks is missed, which is a significant concern for a high-security environment. The **F1-score of 79.34%** reflects a reasonable balance, but the false negative rate should be reduced by tuning the anomaly detection thresholds or adding complementary signature rules.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between Network-based IDPS (NIDPS) and Host-based IDPS (HIDPS) architectures. Discuss the placement of sensors in each case with appropriate diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for total IDPS detection latency and explain how architectural choices (centralized vs. distributed) affect each latency component. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**NIDPS vs. HIDPS Architecture Comparison:**

**Network-based IDPS (NIDPS):**
- Sensors are placed at **network chokepoints** — typically at the perimeter (just inside the firewall), at the DMZ, and at internal network segment junctions.
- Data sources: Live packet capture via **SPAN/mirror ports** or **passive TAPs**.
- Strengths: Broad coverage, single sensor monitors many hosts, detects network-layer attacks (port scans, DoS, protocol abuse).
- Weaknesses: **Cannot inspect encrypted payloads** (SSL/TLS blind spot), cannot see host-level activity, susceptible to high-bandwidth evasion.

**Host-based IDPS (HIDPS):**
- A software **agent** runs on each protected host, monitoring OS audit logs, system calls, file integrity, registry changes, and process behavior.
- Strengths: Visibility into **encrypted traffic at the endpoint** (after decryption), detects insider threats, host-specific anomalies, rootkits.
- Weaknesses: Per-host deployment overhead, agent can be disabled by attackers, no network-wide view.

**Placement Diagrams (describe in answer):**

- *NIDPS:* Draw a perimeter topology — Internet → Firewall → **NIDPS Sensor** (inline or SPAN) → Internal Switch → Servers.
- *HIDPS:* Draw individual hosts with **agent blocks** inside each host, all forwarding to a central analyzer.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Derivation of Detection Latency:**

As derived in Section 3.1:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{latency} &= T_{detect} - T_{event} \\
&= \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $\delta_1$ = propagation delay from event source to sensor
- $\delta_2$ = sensor pre-processing delay
- $\delta_3$ = network transit delay from sensor to analyzer
- $\delta_4$ = analyzer detection/decision delay

**Architectural Impact:**

| Latency Component | Centralized Architecture | Distributed Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta_1$ | Larger if sensor is remote from event | Smaller (sensor is local) |
| $\delta_3$ | **Largest contributor** (WAN/VPN transit) | Eliminated or minimized (local forwarding) |
| $\delta_4$ | Risk of bottleneck at central site | Load-balanced across analyzers |
| **Overall $D_{latency}$** | Higher (5–30 s typical) | Lower (< 1 s possible) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Distributed architectures reduce $D_{latency}$ primarily by **minimizing $\delta_3$**. However, they introduce complexity in synchronizing signature updates and aggregating correlated alerts across multiple analyzers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS architecture:** Many students incorrectly state that an IDS "blocks" traffic. Remember — **IDS = passive observation + alerts**, **IPS = inline + active response**.
> 2. **Forgetting the secure communication channel:** Sensors-to-analyzer links **must** use TLS/SSH encryption; unencrypted channels are a vulnerability in your answer.
> 3. **Missing the database role:** Students often omit the signature/event database when listing components. Always include it as a **separate architectural element**.
> 4. **Confusing FPR and FNR:** The denominator is different — FPR uses $(FP + TN)$, FNR uses $(FN + TP)$. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Not commenting on architecture trade-offs:** A 14-mark answer that only lists components without discussing **centralized vs. distributed** or **inline vs. passive** trade-offs will lose up to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Components and Architecture**

- **Five Core Components (Memorize Order):** Sensor/Agent → Analyzer → Database → Management Console → Response Module.
- **IDS = Out-of-Band (Passive), IPS = In-Band (Inline).**
- **Sensor placement:** NIDPS at chokepoints (perimeter, DMZ, segment junctions); HIDPS agent on each protected host.
- **Detection methods:** Signature-based (low FPR, high FNR for novel attacks), Anomaly-based (high TPR, higher FPR).
- **Detection Latency formula:** $D_{latency} = \sum \delta_i$ — minimize $\delta_3$ (sensor-to-analyzer transit) by co-location or distribution.
- **FPR formula:** $FPR = FP / (FP + TN)$.
- **FNR formula:** $FNR = FN / (FN + TP)$.
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — best single metric for IDPS accuracy.
- **Single point of failure:** Centralized analyzer architecture has this risk — mitigate with HA clustering or distributed analyzers.
- **Encrypted channel:** All sensor-to-analyzer and analyzer-to-console communication must use **TLS 1.3 or SSH**.
- **Inline IPS failure mode:** If the IPS fails closed, traffic stops; if it fails open, traffic flows uninspected — this is a **critical architectural decision**.
- **Throughput units:** NIDPS uses **Mbps/Gbps**, HIDPS uses **EPS (Events Per Second)**.
- **Cloud-native sensors:** Modern architectures use agent-based or eBPF-based sensors inside cloud workloads (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender pattern).
- **Hybrid architecture:** Combines NIDPS, HIDPS, and cloud sensors feeding a SIEM-based central correlation engine.
- **For KTU numerical problems:** Always show all four steps — identification, FPR, FNR, F1 — and end with a **comment** on system quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS)**, the term **Security Capabilities** refers to the comprehensive set of functional attributes, operational features, and analytical competencies that an IDPS solution must possess to effectively **monitor**, **detect**, **analyze**, **log**, and **respond** to malicious activities targeting an organization's information assets. These capabilities define the *what*, *how*, and *why* of an IDPS's defensive envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT742 Module 3):**
> *Security Capabilities encompass the spectrum of functions an IDPS performs — from raw data acquisition (packet capture, log harvesting, host telemetry) to refined threat identification (signature matching, anomaly scoring, stateful inspection) and culminating in protective actions (session termination, packet dropping, quarantine, and forensic preservation).*

The five principal pillars of IDPS Security Capabilities are:

1. **Information Collection Capabilities** — Acquiring data from network, host, and application layers.
2. **Detection Capabilities** — Identifying malicious patterns, behaviors, or policy violations.
3. **Logging Capabilities** — Persistent recording of events for forensic and compliance use.
4. **Prevention Capabilities** — Active, real-time mitigation of detected threats.
5. **Performance / Analytical Capabilities** — Measurement of detection accuracy, throughput, and operational efficacy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Security System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Hospital"**
>
> Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. The hospital's overall security system is exactly an IDPS:
>
> - **CCTV cameras at every entrance, hallway, and pharmacy** = *Information Collection* (raw data gathering from network sensors / host agents).
> - **AI software that recognizes a known thief's face from a criminal database** = *Signature-Based Detection*.
> - **Motion sensors that flag unusual activity at 3 AM when no patients are around** = *Anomaly-Based Detection*.
> - **The security logbook where every visitor's entry is noted with timestamp** = *Logging Capability*.
> - **Automatic door-locking that traps a suspected intruder instantly** = *Prevention Capability* (active response).
> - **A dashboard showing "false alarm rate: 0.5%"** = *Performance & Analytical Capability*.
>
> Just as a hospital cannot function with only cameras but no response mechanism, an IDPS without prevention or logging capabilities is fundamentally incomplete.

## 1.3 Core Capability Metrics (Standard KTU Terminology)

The following standardized metrics are central to evaluating IDPS security capabilities and appear frequently in KTU examinations:

- **True Positive (TP)** — A real attack correctly identified as an attack.
- **False Positive (FP)** — A legitimate activity wrongly flagged as an attack (false alarm).
- **True Negative (TN)** — A legitimate activity correctly passed as benign.
- **False Negative (FN)** — A real attack missed by the system.
- **Detection Rate (DR)** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Accuracy (ACC)** = $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlight:** A high-quality IDPS is judged not merely by catching attacks (high DR) but also by minimizing false alarms (low FPR). The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is the standard graphical tool used to evaluate this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of the Five Security Capabilities

### 2.1.1 Information Collection Capabilities

This is the **first and foundational capability** — without accurate data ingestion, no detection or prevention is possible.

- **Network-Based Collection:** Captures raw packets using techniques like **port mirroring (SPAN)**, **network TAPs**, or **inline (in-band) sensors**. Protocols inspected include TCP, UDP, ICMP, HTTP/S, DNS, SMB, FTP, SMTP.
- **Host-Based Collection:** OS-level agents collect **system calls**, **audit logs**, **file integrity hashes (e.g., MD5, SHA-256)**, **registry changes**, and **process execution traces**.
- **Application-Based Collection:** Application-layer logs from web servers (Apache/Nginx), databases (MySQL/PostgreSQL), and middleware.
- **Wireless Collection:** 802.11 frame capture (monitor mode) for rogue AP and evil-twin detection.
- **Cloud / Virtual Collection:** Hypervisor-level introspection, vSwitch traffic mirroring, and cloud-native log streams (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** The placement of the sensor (inline vs. passive tap) fundamentally determines whether the IDPS can perform **prevention** or only **detection**.

### 2.1.2 Detection Capabilities

Detection is the analytical heart of an IDPS. The three principal detection methodologies are:

1. **Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)**
   - Uses pre-defined patterns (signatures) of known attacks.
   - Example: Snort rule `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"/etc/passwd"; msg:"Password file access attempt";)`.
   - **Strength:** Very low false positive rate, fast.
   - **Weakness:** Zero-day blind.

2. **Anomaly-Based Detection**
   - Builds a statistical or ML model of "normal" behavior and flags deviations.
   - Techniques: statistical profiling, clustering (k-means), neural networks, autoencoders.
   - **Strength:** Can detect zero-day attacks.
   - **Weakness:** Higher false positive rate.

3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis**
   - Understands the expected state machine of protocols (e.g., RFC-compliant HTTP, DNS, FTP).
   - Flags deviations such as out-of-order TCP flags, malformed DNS queries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Modern enterprise IDPS (e.g., Suricata, Snort 3.x, Zeek) are **hybrid systems** — they combine all three detection methodologies simultaneously.

### 2.1.3 Logging Capabilities

- **Alert Logs:** Concise notifications of detected events (severity, timestamp, signature ID, source/destination).
- **Session/Packet Logs:** Full payload capture for forensic replay.
- **Operational Logs:** System health, sensor uptime, configuration changes.
- **Compliance Logs:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR-aligned audit trails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Logs must be **tamper-resistant** (write-once, hashed, or sent to a SIEM in real time) to preserve forensic integrity — a core KTU Module 3 takeaway.

### 2.1.4 Prevention Capabilities

Only possible when the IDPS is deployed **inline (in-band)**:

- **Session Termination:** TCP RST injection to kill attacker connection.
- **Packet Dropping:** Drop malicious packets at the firewall/IPS engine.
- **IP Blacklisting / Blocking:** Dynamic ACL updates.
- **Quarantine:** Isolate compromised host via 802.1X or NAC integration.
- **Rate Limiting / Throttling:** Slow down brute-force attempts.

### 2.1.5 Performance & Analytical Capabilities

Quantified by:
- **Throughput** (Gbps of traffic inspected).
- **Latency** introduced (microseconds to milliseconds).
- **Detection Latency** (time from attack onset to alert).
- **Scalability** (number of sensors, rules, concurrent flows).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Metric / Formula** | **LaTeX Expression** | **Units / Range** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall) | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | 0 to 1 (ideal = 1) | Measures how many real attacks are caught |
| False Positive Rate (Fall-out) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | 0 to 1 (ideal = 0) | Measures false alarm burden |
| Precision | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | 0 to 1 | Quality of positive alerts |
| Accuracy | $ACC = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | 0 to 1 | Overall correctness |
| F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR}$ | 0 to 1 | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| Matthew's Correlation Coefficient | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced datasets |
| Throughput Requirement | $T_{req} = N \cdot \overline{P} \cdot 8$ | bits per second (bps) | Sizing IDPS hardware |
| Detection Latency | $t_{det} = t_{capture} + t_{analysis} + t_{alert}$ | seconds | Speed of response |
| Cost-Sensitive Detection | $Cost = C_{FP} \cdot FP + C_{FN} \cdot FN$ | currency units | Optimize threshold |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Notice:** All vertical bars (absolute value or conditional probability) inside the table above are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table parser corruption. Always follow this convention in KTU exam answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These capabilities are deployed across:
- **Enterprise perimeters** (Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto Networks IPS).
- **Data centers** (Suricata in NFV/SDN environments).
- **Cloud workloads** (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender, GCP Chronicle).
- **OT/SCADA networks** (Dragos, Nozomi Networks).
- **Endpoint domain** (EDR solutions like CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** The global IDPS market is shifting from pure NIDS/HIDS toward **Extended Detection and Response (XDR)**, which integrates network, endpoint, cloud, and identity capabilities under a unified analytical fabric — directly extending the traditional "Security Capabilities" framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deriving the Optimal Detection Threshold Using the Youden Index

The **Youden Index (J-statistic)** is the standard KTU-recommended method to find the optimal threshold for an anomaly-based IDPS detector.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Confusion Matrix

For an IDPS over a fixed evaluation window, the following counts are observed:

$$
TP = 47, \quad FP = 8, \quad FN = 3, \quad TN = 192
$$

We are asked to compute the **Detection Rate**, **False Positive Rate**, **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **F1-Score**, and **Youden Index**.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall)

The detection rate is the proportion of actual attacks correctly identified.

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
DR = \frac{47}{47 + 3} = \frac{47}{50} = 0.94
$$

**Interpretation:** The IDPS catches 94% of all real attacks. This is a strong detection rate by industry standards.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute False Positive Rate

The FPR is the proportion of legitimate activities wrongly flagged.

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
FPR = \frac{8}{8 + 192} = \frac{8}{200} = 0.04
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 4% of legitimate traffic is misclassified — well within the <5% benchmark for production-grade IDPS.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Accuracy

$$
ACC = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

$$
ACC = \frac{47 + 192}{47 + 192 + 8 + 3} = \frac{239}{250} = 0.956
$$

**Interpretation:** The IDPS is correct 95.6% of the time overall.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Precision

$$
Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
Precision = \frac{47}{47 + 8} = \frac{47}{55} \approx 0.8545
$$

**Interpretation:** When the IDPS raises an alert, it is correct ~85.45% of the time.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute F1-Score

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8545 \cdot 0.94}{0.8545 + 0.94}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8032}{1.7945} = 2 \cdot 0.4476 = 0.8952
$$

**Interpretation:** F1 of 0.8952 indicates excellent balance between precision and recall.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Compute the Youden Index (Optimal Threshold Metric)

The Youden Index is defined as:

$$
J = Sensitivity - (1 - Specificity) = DR - FPR
$$

Substituting:

$$
J = 0.94 - 0.04 = 0.90
$$

**Interpretation:** A Youden Index of 0.90 is considered excellent (theoretical maximum is 1.0). The current detection threshold is near-optimal.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Specificity (True Negative Rate)

$$
Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{192}{192 + 8} = \frac{192}{200} = 0.96
$$

Verify Youden:

$$
J = 0.94 - (1 - 0.96) = 0.94 - 0.04 = 0.90 \;\;\; \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 KTU Analytical Matrix — Mapping Capabilities to Attack Types

The following matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** because examiners frequently ask: *"Which IDPS capability counters which attack class?"*

| **Attack Class** | **Primary Capability Required** | **Secondary Capability** | **Detection Method** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Port Scan / Reconnaissance | Information Collection | Logging | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
| DoS / DDoS (SYN flood) | Prevention (inline) | Performance (throughput) | Anomaly-Based |
| Buffer Overflow | Detection (signature) | Logging | Signature-Based |
| SQL Injection | Detection (signature) | Logging | Signature / Heuristic |
| Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) | Application-level detection | Logging | Signature / Behavioral |
| Insider Threat (data exfil) | Host-based collection | Anomaly detection | Anomaly / Behavioral |
| Rootkit Installation | Host-based collection (file integrity) | Logging | Signature + Anomaly |
| Zero-Day Exploit | Anomaly / Heuristic detection | Logging | Anomaly / ML |
| Brute Force Login | Detection (rate-based) | Prevention (block) | Stateful + Threshold |
| Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) | Detection (cert / ARP anomalies) | Prevention (inline) | Stateful + Anomaly |
| Wireless Rogue AP | Wireless collection | Logging | Anomaly (signal/BSSID) |
| Cloud Misconfiguration | Cloud-native log collection | Anomaly | Policy + Anomaly |
| Lateral Movement | Host + Network correlation | Logging (SIEM) | Behavioral Analytics |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Notice that **Logging** appears as a *secondary capability* for nearly every attack class. This is by design — logging is the universal "fallback" that enables post-incident forensics, regulatory compliance, and SIEM correlation. Examiners often test this principle.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost-Optimal Threshold via Expected Loss Minimization

For an organization that values false negatives (missed attacks) at $C_{FN}$ = \$10,000 and false positives at $C_{FP}$ = \$50, the expected loss per event at threshold $\tau$ is:

$$
E[Loss \mid \tau] = C_{FP} \cdot P(FP \mid \tau) + C_{FN} \cdot P(FN \mid \tau)
$$

For our confusion matrix counts (treating counts as probabilities over the dataset):

$$
E[Loss] = 50 \cdot 8 + 10000 \cdot 3 = 400 + 30000 = 30{,}400
$$

If we could raise the threshold to eliminate 2 false positives but accept 1 additional false negative:

$$
E[Loss]_{new} = 50 \cdot 6 + 10000 \cdot 4 = 300 + 40000 = 40{,}300
$$

$$
\Delta E[Loss] = 40{,}300 - 30{,}400 = +9{,}900
$$

**Conclusion:** Lowering the threshold (the current setting) is the cost-optimal decision. This is the **expected-loss framework** for IDPS threshold tuning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Pillar IDPS Security Capabilities Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **canonical KTU-aligned block architecture** of an IDPS, mapping the five security capabilities as functional modules with their data flow interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Network Traffic Incoming] --> B1[Inline Sensor / TAP]
    A2[Host Endpoint Agent] --> C1[Host Telemetry Collector]
    A3[Application Server Logs] --> D1[App Log Aggregator]
    A4[Cloud API Stream] --> E1[Cloud Log Ingestor]

    B1 --> F1[Information Collection Module]
    C1 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1

    F1 --> G1[Signature Engine]
    F1 --> G2[Anomaly Engine]
    F1 --> G3[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]
    F1 --> G4[Heuristic and ML Engine]

    G1 --> H1[Detection Capability Layer]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    G4 --> H1

    H1 --> I1[Logging and Alerting Module]
    I1 --> J1[SIEM and Forensics Storage]

    H1 --> K1[Prevention Decision Engine]
    K1 --> K2[Session Reset RST]
    K1 --> K3[Packet Drop]
    K1 --> K4[Dynamic ACL Block]
    K1 --> K5[Quarantine Host]

    I1 --> L1[Performance and Analytics Dashboard]
    K1 --> L1
    F1 --> L1

    L1 --> M1{Tuning Loop}
    M1 -->|Update Rules| G1
    M1 -->|Retrain Models| G2
    M1 -->|Update Thresholds| G3
```

**Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU 14-mark diagram questions):**

- **Top Tier (Sources):** Data ingestion from network, host, application, and cloud.
- **Information Collection Module:** The central data-normalization layer.
- **Detection Layer:** Four parallel engines run simultaneously — this is the **hybrid detection paradigm** emphasized in KTU Module 3.
- **Logging Module:** Diverts to SIEM and forensic archive.
- **Prevention Engine:** Only reachable if the sensor is **inline** (block diagram branch).
- **Performance Dashboard:** Feeds a **closed-loop tuning cycle** back to the detection engines — a feature of mature, self-improving IDPS.

## 4.2 Capability-to-Attack Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAP1[Information Collection] --> ATK1[Reconnaissance]
    CAP1 --> ATK2[Wireless Attacks]
    CAP1 --> ATK3[Cloud Misconfig]

    CAP2[Detection Capability] --> ATK4[Zero Day]
    CAP2 --> ATK5[Buffer Overflow]
    CAP2 --> ATK6[SQLi and XSS]
    CAP2 --> ATK7[Insider Threat]

    CAP3[Logging Capability] --> ATK8[Compliance Audit]
    CAP3 --> ATK9[Forensic Investigation]
    CAP3 --> ATK10[Incident Reconstruction]

    CAP4[Prevention Capability] --> ATK11[DDoS]
    CAP4 --> ATK12[Brute Force]
    CAP4 --> ATK13[Lateral Movement]

    CAP5[Performance Capability] --> ATK14[High Throughput DoS]
    CAP5 --> ATK15[Encrypted Traffic Filter]
```

## 4.3 Inline vs. Passive Sensor Capability Diagram

This diagram is **critical for KTU 14-mark questions** that contrast NIDS (Network Intrusion Detection System) vs. NIPS (Network Intrusion Prevention System).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inline_NIPS
        I1[Inbound Packet] --> I2{IDPS Engine}
        I2 -->|Benign| I3[Forward to Internal Network]
        I2 -->|Malicious| I4[Drop and Log]
        I4 --> I5[Active Response RST Block Drop]
    end

    subgraph Passive_NIDS
        P1[SPAN Port Mirror] --> P2{IDPS Engine}
        P2 -->|Benign| P3[Discard]
        P2 -->|Malicious| P4[Generate Alert Only]
        P4 --> P5[Passive Response Alert Log]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Diagram:** Only the **inline (NIPS)** configuration enables the **Prevention Capability**. A passive (NIDS) configuration can *only* **Detect and Log**. This single distinction is worth 3-4 marks in KTU 14-mark essay questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**List and briefly explain the five major security capabilities of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five major security capabilities of an IDPS are:

1. **Information Collection Capability** — Acquiring raw data from network packets, host audit logs, application logs, and cloud telemetry for analysis. (0.6 marks)
2. **Detection Capability** — Analyzing collected data using signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis techniques to identify malicious activity. (0.6 marks)
3. **Logging Capability** — Persistently recording detected events, alert details, and forensic data in tamper-resistant storage for investigation and compliance. (0.6 marks)
4. **Prevention Capability** — Taking real-time active countermeasures such as dropping malicious packets, terminating sessions via TCP RST, or quarantining compromised hosts. (0.6 marks)
5. **Performance / Analytical Capability** — Quantifying the IDPS's detection accuracy, throughput, latency, false positive/negative rates, and tuning thresholds for optimal operation. (0.6 marks)

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Differentiate between inline and passive sensor deployment in IDPS. Which one enables prevention capability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Inline (In-Band) Sensor** | **Passive (Out-of-Band) Sensor** |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic Path | Traffic physically flows through the IDPS | Traffic mirrored via SPAN/TAP, not through IDPS |
| Latency | Adds small forwarding latency | Zero forwarding latency |
| Failure Impact | Failure can break connectivity (fail-open/closed) | Failure has no impact on traffic flow |
| Capabilities | **Detection + Prevention** | **Detection + Logging only** |
| Use Case | NIPS, Web Application Firewall | NIDS, forensic sensor |

The **inline sensor** is the one that enables **Prevention Capability** because the IDPS sits in the data path and can drop or modify malicious packets before they reach the destination. (1 mark for naming inline)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 3 Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two alternatives. We provide two fully-modeled 14-mark questions below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Explain in detail the three primary detection methodologies used in IDPS. Compare their strengths and weaknesses with respect to zero-day attack detection.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The three primary detection methodologies are:

**(1) Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection) — 2.5 marks**

- Operates by comparing observed events against a database of pre-defined attack patterns (signatures).
- Example: Snort rule: `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"cmd.exe"; msg:"Possible command execution";)`.
- **Strengths:** Very low false positive rate; fast matching; easy to interpret alerts.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot detect zero-day attacks (no signature exists yet); requires constant rule updates; signature explosion.

**(2) Anomaly-Based Detection — 2.5 marks**

- Builds a baseline model of "normal" behavior (statistical, ML, or rule-based) and flags statistically significant deviations.
- Techniques: statistical profiling, k-means clustering, neural networks, autoencoders, isolation forests.
- **Strengths:** Can detect previously unseen (zero-day) attacks; adapts to network evolution.
- **Weaknesses:** Higher false positive rate; requires a "training period"; computationally expensive.

**(3) Stateful Protocol Analysis — 2 marks**

- Tracks the protocol state machine (e.g., expected TCP handshake sequence, DNS query/response pairing) and flags deviations.
- **Strengths:** Detects protocol abuse and malformed traffic; effective against reconnaissance.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot detect attacks that conform to RFC behavior (e.g., application-layer exploits within valid HTTP); requires deep protocol decoders.

**Comparison for Zero-Day Detection:**

| **Methodology** | **Zero-Day Detection Capability** |
|---|---|
| Signature-Based | **Poor** — no signature exists |
| Anomaly-Based | **Excellent** — learns deviation, not signature |
| Stateful Protocol | **Moderate** — only if zero-day violates protocol semantics |

**Conclusion:** For zero-day defense, IDPS must be **hybrid**, layering anomaly detection on top of signature-based engines (2 marks for synthesis).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**For an IDPS evaluated over a 30-day window, the following confusion matrix was obtained: TP = 320, FP = 25, FN = 40, TN = 1615. Calculate the Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Accuracy, Precision, F1-Score, and Youden Index. Comment on the IDPS's operational quality.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Detection Rate (1 mark)**

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{320}{320+40} = \frac{320}{360} \approx 0.8889
$$

**Step 2: False Positive Rate (1 mark)**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{25}{25+1615} = \frac{25}{1640} \approx 0.0152
$$

**Step 3: Accuracy (1 mark)**

$$
ACC = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{320+1615}{320+1615+25+40} = \frac{1935}{2000} = 0.9675
$$

**Step 4: Precision (1 mark)**

$$
Precision = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{320}{320+25} = \frac{320}{345} \approx 0.9275
$$

**Step 5: F1-Score (1 mark)**

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9275 \cdot 0.8889}{0.9275 + 0.8889}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8244}{1.8164} = 2 \cdot 0.4539 = 0.9078
$$

**Step 6: Youden Index (1 mark)**

$$
J = DR - FPR = 0.8889 - 0.0152 = 0.8737
$$

**Step 7: Operational Quality Comment (1 mark)**

The IDPS demonstrates **excellent operational quality**:
- Detection Rate of 88.89% is high.
- False Positive Rate of 1.52% is well below the 5% industry benchmark.
- F1-Score of 0.9078 indicates strong balance.
- Youden Index of 0.8737 is near-optimal.
- The only weakness is the **FN count of 40**, suggesting the threshold could be marginally lowered to catch more attacks — at the cost of slightly higher FPR.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Discuss the Logging Capability of IDPS in detail. What are the different types of logs maintained, and what are the key design considerations for log integrity and storage?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Logging Capability of an IDPS refers to the systematic, persistent recording of all detected events, alerts, and operational metadata to support forensic investigation, regulatory compliance, and post-incident analysis.

**Types of Logs Maintained (4 marks):**

1. **Alert Logs** — Concise records of detected threats, including timestamp, severity, signature ID, source/destination IP, and action taken.
2. **Session/Packet Logs** — Full or partial payload capture of suspicious sessions for forensic replay (e.g., pcap format).
3. **Operational Logs** — Sensor health, restart events, configuration changes, rule updates, and system errors.
4. **Audit/Compliance Logs** — Tamper-resistant records aligned with regulatory frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001).
5. **Statistical Logs** — Aggregated traffic baselines and anomaly scores for trend analysis.

**Key Design Considerations (2 marks):**

- **Integrity:** Use cryptographic hashing (SHA-256), write-once storage, or real-time forwarding to a SIEM to prevent log tampering by attackers.
- **Storage:** Tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) with retention aligned to compliance (typically 90 days hot, 1-7 years cold).
- **Time Synchronization:** NTP-based uniform timestamps across all sensors.
- **Standardization:** Use common formats (CEF, LEEF, Syslog, JSON) for SIEM ingestion.
- **Scalability:** Distributed logging with log shippers (e.g., Filebeat, Fluentd).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the Prevention Capability of an IDPS. List and describe at least five active prevention techniques. Under what deployment configuration is prevention possible?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Prevention Capability of an IDPS refers to its ability to take **real-time, active countermeasures** to block, terminate, or contain detected threats before they can cause damage.

**Deployment Requirement (1 mark):** Prevention is possible **only in inline (in-band) deployment** where the IDPS sensor sits physically in the traffic path (i.e., configured as a NIPS, not a NIDS).

**Five Active Prevention Techniques (5 marks):**

1. **Session Termination (TCP RST Injection):** The IDPS injects a TCP RESET packet to both ends of an attack session, abruptly tearing it down. (1 mark)
2. **Packet Dropping:** Malicious packets are silently dropped at the IPS engine, preventing them from reaching the destination host. (1 mark)
3. **IP Blacklisting / Dynamic ACL Updates:** The IDPS pushes real-time ACL changes to the perimeter firewall or router to block attacker IP addresses. (1 mark)
4. **Quarantine of Compromised Hosts:** Integration with NAC (Network Access Control) like 802.1X allows the IDPS to isolate an infected endpoint to a remediation VLAN. (1 mark)
5. **Rate Limiting / Throttling:** The IDPS enforces bandwidth or connection-rate caps to mitigate brute-force or low-and-slow DoS attacks. (1 mark)

**Additional Note:** Modern IDPS can also integrate with **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)** platforms to trigger automated playbooks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Detection with Prevention:** A passive NIDS **cannot prevent** — students often mistakenly credit prevention capability to a SPAN-mirrored sensor. Always state the deployment mode explicitly. (-2 marks risk)
> 2. **Skipping Mathematical Units:** In the confusion-matrix questions, always show the **denominator's sum** before computing the ratio. Examiners deduct marks for incomplete fractions. (-1 mark risk)
> 3. **Omitting the F1 or Youden Index:** When a question asks for "operational quality", F1 and Youden are the **two most valued metrics**. Skipping them is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 4. **One-line answers in 14-mark questions:** Each sub-part requires **structured headings**, **examples**, and a **concluding remark**. A 3-line answer for a 7-mark question will be capped at 3-4 marks maximum.
> 5. **Ignoring Log Integrity:** When discussing logging, students often list log types but forget to mention **tamper resistance** — a 1-mark item that is frequently tested.
> 6. **Forgetting Hybrid Detection:** In any "compare detection methods" question, the **concluding synthesis line** ("modern IDPS use hybrid approaches") is worth 1-2 marks and is often omitted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Security Capabilities (Module 3):**

- **Five Pillars:** Information Collection → Detection → Logging → Prevention → Performance/Analytics.
- **Sensor Placement Matters:** Inline = Detection + Prevention; Passive (SPAN/TAP) = Detection + Logging only.
- **Three Detection Methods:** Signature (low FP, zero-day blind), Anomaly (catches zero-day, higher FP), Stateful Protocol (protocol abuse).
- **Hybrid IDPS** is the industry norm — combine all three detection methods in a single engine.
- **Key Metrics (must memorize formulas):** DR, FPR, Accuracy, Precision, F1, Youden Index.
- **Youden Index Formula:** $J = DR - FPR$; range 0 to 1; >0.8 considered excellent.
- **Logging is Forensic Backbone:** Always tamper-resistant, NTP-synchronized, SIEM-fed.
- **Prevention Techniques (memorize 5):** TCP RST, Packet Drop, Dynamic ACL, Quarantine, Rate Limiting.
- **Information Collection Sources:** Network TAP/SPAN, Host agents, Application logs, Cloud streams, Wireless monitor mode.
- **Modern Evolution:** Traditional IDPS is converging into **XDR (Extended Detection and Response)** — a KTU-favored "Industry 4.0" angle.
- **Cloud-Native IDPS:** AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender, GCP Chronicle — frequently cited in KTU viva questions.
- **Common Pitfall:** NIDS ≠ NIPS. The letter "P" in NIPS = Prevention. Always check sensor mode in exam answers.
- **Industry Benchmark:** FPR < 5% and DR > 90% are the standard production targets for a well-tuned IDPS.
- **Examiner's Favorite:** Confusion-matrix calculations (always appear in at least one KTU ESE paper per academic year).
- **Closing Line to Use in Essays:** *"An effective IDPS is not defined by the number of alerts it generates, but by its ability to balance high detection accuracy with minimal false positives while enabling real-time prevention."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Gathering Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Information Gathering Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 – PECCT742, Module 3):**
> *Information Gathering Capabilities* covers the techniques, data sources, sensors, and tools that an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) uses to **collect raw telemetry** from the protected environment, and the corresponding **attack-side reconnaissance techniques** the IDPS must learn to recognise.

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Information Gathering (IDPS Context).**
> *Information Gathering Capability* in an IDPS is the structured ability of the system to **acquire, normalise, and correlate** data from heterogeneous sources — network packets, host logs, application telemetry, and stateful flow records — so that suspicious activity (including the attacker's own reconnaissance) can be detected, classified, and (in a prevention system) blocked in real time.

In short, an IDPS is only as intelligent as the data it can see. The richer and more accurate the **information gathering pipeline**, the lower the false-negative rate and the higher the fidelity of detection.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Triage Ward.**
> Imagine a hospital emergency ward. Before a doctor can diagnose a patient, the **reception desk** (the information-gathering layer) records vitals, past records, and symptoms from multiple sources (thermometer, blood test, patient history). The **IDPS sensor** plays exactly this role: it pulls data from the *network thermometer* (packet sniffer), the *patient file* (host log), and the *X-ray machine* (deep packet inspection) and hands it to the *doctor* (the detection engine). A ward with broken instruments cannot make a correct diagnosis — neither can an IDPS with poor information gathering.

### 1.3 The Two Sides of the Topic

This topic is best understood from **two complementary viewpoints**:

| Viewpoint | Direction | Purpose in IDPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Defensive Gathering** | IDPS → Environment | Sensors pull data to *detect* attacks. |
| **Offensive Gathering (Reconnaissance)** | Attacker → Environment | IDPS must *recognise* probing activity directed at the network. |

Both directions are studied in Module 3 because an IDPS must simultaneously:
1. **Collect** its own intelligence about the protected zone.
2. **Detect** an attacker's intelligence-gathering activity against the protected zone.

### 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Bandwidth (B):** measured in **bps / Mbps / Gbps**; defines the rate at which packet-level data can be ingested.
> - **Packet Capture Rate (PCR):** packets per second (**pps**) the sensor can sustain without drop.
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** average latency between attack onset and IDPS alert.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$.
> - **False Negative Rate (FNR):** $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$.
> - **OSI Layers 2–7** are the primary zones in which information is captured.

### 1.5 Scope of Information Sources

The IDPS may pull from any of the following planes, summarised in the callout below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Information Planes of an IDPS:**
> 1. **Network Plane (N-IDS/N-IPS):** raw frames, packets, flow records.
> 2. **Host Plane (H-IDS/H-IPS):** syscalls, registry, file hashes, audit logs.
> 3. **Application Plane:** web, DNS, database, mail logs.
> 4. **Wireless Plane:** 802.11 frames, probe requests.
> 5. **Virtualisation/Cloud Plane:** hypervisor events, API call logs, vNIC telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Information-Gathering Pipeline (Five-Stage Model)

> [!TIP]
> A production-grade IDPS converts raw environmental data into a **detection verdict** through a deterministic five-stage pipeline. Memorising these stages earns easy marks in descriptive questions.

1. **Acquisition (Sensing)** – raw bits are captured from a *sensor point* (tap, SPAN port, host agent, API hook).
2. **Pre-processing (Normalisation)** – timestamps are unified, headers decoded, vendor-specific logs re-formatted into a common schema (typically **CEF** or **LEEF**).
3. **Reduction / Aggregation** – similar events are coalesced; e.g., thousands of `SYN` packets from one IP become a single *port-scan alert*.
4. **Correlation & Detection** – events are compared against **signatures**, **anomaly baselines**, or **stateful policy rules** to produce a verdict.
5. **Response / Reporting** – the verdict triggers an action: log, alert, drop, quarantine, or update a black-hole route.

### 2.2 Defensive Information-Gathering Techniques

#### 2.2.1 Network-Based Gathering

| Technique | What is Captured | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Packet Sniffing (libpcap, BPF)** | Full L2–L7 frame | Deep visibility | Requires tap/port-mirror; encrypted payloads invisible |
| **Flow Records (NetFlow v5/v9, sFlow, IPFIX)** | Per-flow summaries (5-tuple, byte count, duration) | Scalable, sampled | No payload; coarse granularity |
| **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** | Payload + state | Detects application-layer attacks | CPU-intensive; defeated by strong encryption |
| **Network Taps / SPAN** | Wire-speed copy of traffic | No packet loss | Physical/port cost |
| **Inline (Bump-in-the-Wire)** | Same as tap + ability to drop | Enables **prevention** | Latency; single point of failure |

#### 2.2.2 Host-Based Gathering

| Data Source | Information Extracted |
|---|---|
| **System Call Trace (`strace`, auditd)** | Process → kernel interactions; useful for detecting buffer-overflow patterns. |
| **File Integrity Monitor (FIM)** | Hashes of critical files; detects tampering (Tripwire/AIDE model). |
| **Registry Snapshots (Windows)** | Autorun keys, services, scheduled tasks. |
| **Process / DLL Load Events** | Reveals code injection, hollowing, persistence. |
| **Host Firewall & Audit Logs** | Connection attempts, privilege escalations. |

#### 2.2.3 Application & Protocol-Aware Gathering

- **HTTP logs** → URI patterns, user-agents, response codes.
- **DNS logs** → DGA domains, tunnel beacons.
- **Authentication logs (Windows Event 4624/4625, Linux PAM)** → brute-force patterns.

### 2.3 Offensive Information-Gathering Techniques (What the IDPS Must Detect)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Reconnaissance.**
> *Reconnaissance* is the **pre-attack phase** in which an adversary maps the target's topology, open services, operating systems, and vulnerabilities. From the IDPS perspective, the *only* goal of recognising reconnaissance is to **raise an early-warning alert** so the security team can react before the kill-chain advances to exploitation.

The classic taxonomy is:

1. **Passive Reconnaissance** – derives intelligence from public / side-channel sources (WHOIS, Shodan, Google Dorks, social media). Almost invisible to the IDPS at the network plane.
2. **Active Reconnaissance** – actively probes the target, leaving traces in network/host logs.

| Active Technique | Detectable Signature |
|---|---|
| **Ping Sweep** | High rate of ICMP Echo Requests from one source |
| **TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`)** | Many `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `FIN` triplets to varied ports |
| **SYN Stealth / Half-open Scan (`-sS`)** | `SYN` followed by `RST` (no completion) — **no log on target** but visible to N-IDS |
| **FIN / NULL / XMAS Scans** | Packets with unusual flag combinations |
| **UDP Scan (`-sU`)** | High rate of UDP packets, often with `ICMP port unreachable` replies |
| **OS Fingerprinting** | Crafted packets eliciting differing TTL/Window/DF responses |
| **Version Detection (`-sV`)** | Probe payloads sent to open ports |
| **Vulnerability Scan (Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS)** | Sequential probes against many ports + known-CVE payloads |
| **SNMP Enumeration** | Repeated queries to UDP/161 with `public`, `private`, `manager` communities |
| **SMB / NetBIOS Enumeration** | Sessions requesting shares, user lists, RID cycling |
| **DNS Zone Transfer Attempt** | `AXFR` query from unauthorised source |
| **Web Crawling / Spidering** | High-volume sequential `GET` requests, often in lexicographic order |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Mandatory Recall Table.** This table consolidates every formula, definition boundary, and unit the examiner can legitimately test on this topic. Memorise before any internal or ESE attempt.

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression / Boundary | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Capture Rate (PCR)** | $\text{PCR} \ge \text{Link Rate} \times \frac{\text{Avg Pkt Size}}{\text{Max Pkt Size}}$ | pps — for a **1 Gbps** link of 64-byte frames, PCR ≥ **1.488 Mpps** |
| 2 | **Sampling Rate (sFlow)** | $s = \frac{\text{sampled pkts}}{\text{total pkts}}$, $0 < s \le 1$ | Dimensionless; typical $s = 1/2048$ |
| 3 | **False Positive Rate** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le \text{FPR} \le 1$ |
| 4 | **False Negative Rate** | $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $0 \le \text{FNR} \le 1$ |
| 5 | **Detection Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Bounded in $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | **Mean Time to Detect** | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{alert}_i} - t_{\text{attack}_i})$ | seconds |
| 7 | **Banner-Grabbing Detection Threshold** | Alert if distinct *service banners* from a single IP exceed $K$ within $\Delta t$ | $K$ typically 5–10 |
| 8 | **Port-Scan Heuristic (Simpson Coefficient)** | $S_c = \frac{\lvert H_X \cap H_Y \rvert}{\min(\lvert H_X \rvert, \lvert H_Y \rvert)}$ | Used in TRW (Threshold Random Walk) algorithm |
| 9 | **Bayes Posterior for Anomaly** | $P(A \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}$ | Basis of Bayesian IDPS sensors |
| 10 | **Shannon Entropy (Payload Anomaly)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol — high entropy ⇒ encrypted/compressed payload |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility.** The formulas above are not just academic — they drive real product specifications. For instance, an enterprise N-IDS sensor sold for a **10 Gbps** link must support a wire-speed PCR of **≈ 14.88 Mpps** at minimum packet size **64 B**, which is why high-end sensors use dedicated **ASIC / FPGA** capture cards rather than general-purpose NICs.

### 2.5 Information-Gathering Architecture Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Canonical Deployment Architectures** (tested frequently in 14-mark questions):

- **Centralised Architecture:** one or few central engines analyse data from distributed sensors.
- **Distributed / Hierarchical:** sensors pre-process locally; only alerts are sent up.
- **Fully Distributed / Agent-based:** every host runs a small agent that decides locally (e.g., modern EDR agents).

### 2.6 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice

In production security operations centres (SOCs), the **information-gathering tier** accounts for roughly **60–70 % of total deployment cost** (sensors, taps, log forwarders, SIEM licences, storage). A clear conceptual grasp of this layer is what separates a network engineer from a security architect.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section derives the *engineering numbers* an examiner loves to test: the **minimum capture rate of a sensor**, the **entropy of normal vs malicious payload**, and the **TRW scan-detection threshold**. Each step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Packet Capture Rate for a Wire-Speed Sensor

**Problem:** A network link operates at line rate $L$ bits/s. Frames have an *average* size $S_{\text{avg}}$ bytes and a *minimum* size $S_{\min}$ bytes. The sensor's capture engine must be dimensioned to *never* drop a packet even under worst-case minimum-sized frames. Derive the minimum packet capture rate $\text{PCR}_{\min}$.

**Step 1 — Worst-Case Frame Rate.**
The worst case (highest frame arrival rate) occurs when every frame is the smallest legal one, $S_{\min}$. Hence the *maximum* packets-per-second the link can deliver is

$$
\text{PPS}_{\max} = \frac{L}{8 \cdot S_{\min}}.
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to a Numerical Example.**
Take $L = 1\,\text{Gbps} = 10^{9}\,\text{bits/s}$ and $S_{\min} = 64\,\text{bytes} = 512\,\text{bits}$.

$$
\text{PPS}_{\max} = \frac{10^{9}}{8 \times 64} = \frac{10^{9}}{512} \approx 1{,}953{,}125\ \text{pps}.
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Preamble + IFG Overhead.**
Ethernet adds 8 bytes of preamble + 12 bytes of inter-frame gap per packet, so the *real* number of packets that may arrive in one second is

$$
\text{PCR}_{\min} = \frac{L}{8 \cdot (S_{\min} + 20)} = \frac{10^{9}}{8 \times 84} \approx 1{,}488{,}095\ \text{pps}.
$$

**Step 4 — Generalised Engineering Rule.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PCR}_{\min} = \frac{L}{8 \cdot (S_{\min} + O)}\;}\quad \text{where } O = 20\ \text{bytes (Ethernet preamble + IFG).}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Always remember to add the **20-byte Ethernet overhead**; many students forget it and lose a mark.

### 3.2 Derivation: Detecting a TCP SYN Stealth Scan Using the TRW Heuristic

**Goal:** Show analytically how a sensor *distinguishes* a scan (successes are *rare*, failures are *common*) from a legitimate client (successes are *common*, failures are *rare*).

**Step 1 — Define Two Hypotheses.**
$H_0$ = *legitimate client*, $H_1$ = *scanner*.

**Step 2 — Assign Observation Likelihoods.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{success} \mid H_0) &= \alpha_{0} \approx 0.8, \\
P(\text{success} \mid H_1) &= \alpha_{1} \approx 0.05.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Sequential Likelihood Ratio.**

$$
\Lambda_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_1)}{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_0)}.
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Log Transformation.**

$$
\log \Lambda_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \frac{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_1)}{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_0)}.
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Boundaries.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \Lambda_n \ge \eta_{\text{upper}} &\Rightarrow \text{Decide } H_1 \text{ (scan)};\\
\log \Lambda_n \le \eta_{\text{lower}} &\Rightarrow \text{Decide } H_0 \text{ (legitimate)};\\
\text{otherwise} &\Rightarrow \text{continue observing}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Expected Detection Time.**
For Wald's SPRT, the average number of observations to a decision is finite because $\alpha_0 \ne \alpha_1$ — *this is what makes TRW far more efficient than fixed-window counters.*

> [!TIP]
> This is a **14-mark favourite**. Drawing the SPRT block diagram and writing the likelihood ratio explicitly will fetch full marks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Reconnaissance Techniques to IDPS Sensors

| Reconnaissance Type | OSI Layer | Primary IDPS Sensor | Detection Mechanism | Evasion Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ping Sweep** | L3 (ICMP) | N-IDS | Threshold of ICMP echo req / source IP | Low (trivially filtered) |
| **TCP SYN Scan** | L4 | N-IDS | `SYN` without completion count | Medium (use `-S` spoof) |
| **UDP Scan** | L4 | N-IDS | UDP → ICMP unreachable ratio | Medium |
| **OS Fingerprint** | L3-L4 | N-IDS | Anomalous TTL/Window/DF triplet | Hard (passive OSfp defeats it) |
| **Version Detection** | L7 | N-IDS (DPI) | Banner keywords (`Apache`, `OpenSSH 7.2`) | Medium |
| **SNMP Enumeration** | L7 | N-IDS | Repeated `public/private` community strings | Low |
| **DNS Zone Transfer** | L7 | N-IDS / App-IDS | `AXFR` query from non-authoritative IP | Low |
| **Web Spidering** | L7 | App-IDS / WAF | URI sequence entropy, request rate | Medium |
| **SMB Enumeration** | L7 | N-IDS | `NULL` sessions, RID cycling | Low |
| **Vulnerability Scan** | L7 | N-IDS + Vuln-Scanner | Known CVE payload signature | Medium |
| **Wireless Probe** | L2 (802.11) | WIDS | `ProbeReq` floods, SSID brute force | High (encrypted probes) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Shannon Entropy of an HTTP Payload

**Step 1 — Compute symbol frequencies.**
Consider a 100-character URL where bytes `A–Z` and `/` appear uniformly. There are 27 distinct symbols, each with probability $p_i = 1/27$.

**Step 2 — Plug into Shannon's formula.**

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{27} \frac{1}{27}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{27}\right) = \log_2 27 \approx 4.75\ \text{bits/symbol}.
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with random binary (8 bits) and English text (≈ 1.5 bits).**

$$
\boxed{\;1.5 < H_{\text{English}} < 4.75 < H_{\text{uniform-alnum}} < 8\;}\ \text{bits/symbol}.
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Decision Rule.**
If an HTTP payload's entropy exceeds a tunable threshold (commonly $H \ge 6.0$), the IDPS raises an *exfiltration / encrypted-tunnel* suspicion alert. This is the basis of modern **DLP-aware IDPS**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The block diagrams below are **Mermaid** architecture renderings. They describe *information flow* inside an IDPS, satisfying the "block-level functional architecture" fallback when physical drawings are not feasible in plain text.

### 4.1 Information-Gathering Pipeline (End-to-End Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Network Tap] --> cap1[Packet Capture]
    srcB[SPAN Port] --> cap1
    srcC[Host Agent] --> cap2[Log Collector]
    srcD[Application Logs] --> cap2
    srcE[Cloud API Logs] --> cap2

    cap1 --> norm1[Decode and Normalise L2 to L7]
    cap2 --> norm2[Schema Map to CEF or LEEF]

    norm1 --> red1[Reduce and Aggregate]
    norm2 --> red1

    red1 --> cor1[Correlation Engine]
    cor1 --> sig1[Signature Match]
    cor1 --> ano1[Anomaly Baseline]
    cor1 --> sta1[Stateful Policy Check]

    sig1 --> dec1[Verdict: Benign]
    sig1 --> dec2[Verdict: Suspicious]
    sig1 --> dec3[Verdict: Malicious]

    ano1 --> dec1
    ano1 --> dec2
    ano1 --> dec3

    sta1 --> dec1
    sta1 --> dec2
    sta1 --> dec3

    dec1 --> act1[Log Only]
    dec2 --> act2[Alert SOC]
    dec3 --> act3[Drop or Quarantine]
```

### 4.2 Reconnaissance Attack Chain (Defender's View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Attacker External Network] --> A1[Step 1: Passive Recon WHOIS and Shodan]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Active Recon Ping Sweep]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Port Scan SYN or FIN]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Service Version Detection]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Vulnerability Scan Nessus Style]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Exploitation]

    A1 -.-> D1[IDPS Alert: Low Severity]
    A2 -.-> D2[IDPS Alert: Medium Severity]
    A3 -.-> D3[IDPS Alert: High Severity]
    A4 -.-> D4[IDPS Alert: High Severity]
    A5 -.-> D5[IDPS Alert: Critical Severity]
    A6 -.-> D6[IDPS Action: Block and Quarantine]
```

### 4.3 Sensor Placement Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inet((Internet))
    fw[Firewall]
    dmz1[DMZ Web Server]
    dmz2[DMZ Mail Server]
    core[Core Switch]
    lan1[User LAN Segment A]
    lan2[User LAN Segment B]
    srv[Server Farm]
    soc[SIEM and SOC Console]

    sensor1[IDS Sensor DMZ] -. monitors .-> dmz1
    sensor1 -. monitors .-> dmz2
    sensor2[IDS Sensor Core] -. monitors .-> core
    sensor3[IDS Sensor Internal] -. monitors .-> lan1
    sensor3 -. monitors .-> lan2
    sensor4[IDS Sensor Server] -. monitors .-> srv

    sensor1 --> soc
    sensor2 --> soc
    sensor3 --> soc
    sensor4 --> soc

    inet --> fw --> core
    core --> lan1
    core --> lan2
    core --> srv
    core --> dmz1
    core --> dmz2
```

### 4.4 Information-Plane vs Attack-Plane Cross Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    planeN[Network Plane] --- scan1[Detects SYN Scan]
    planeN --- scan2[Detects UDP Scan]
    planeN --- scan3[Detects ICMP Sweep]

    planeH[Host Plane] --- host1[Detects File Tampering]
    planeH --- host2[Detects Privilege Escalation]
    planeH --- host3[Detects Suspicious Syscall]

    planeA[Application Plane] --- app1[Detects Web Crawling]
    planeA --- app2[Detects DNS Tunnel]
    planeA --- app3[Detects SQL Probe]

    planeW[Wireless Plane] --- wir1[Detects Rogue AP]
    planeW --- wir2[Detects Probe Flood]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Each Module in PECCT742 contributes two full questions of **14 marks each** with internal choice.
> - Part A of the question paper has short 3-mark questions; two of them typically come from each module.
> - Cognitive levels: *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse* — answers must demonstrate all four to score above 80 %.

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define *Information Gathering* in the context of an IDPS. Differentiate between *passive* and *active* reconnaissance with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Information gathering* in an IDPS is the systematic process of **collecting, normalising and correlating** data from network, host and application sources so that intrusion activity (including an attacker's reconnaissance) can be detected. (1 mark)

**Passive reconnaissance** derives intelligence **without directly probing** the target — examples include querying **WHOIS**, reading job postings, or indexing **Shodan**. (1 mark)
