**Active reconnaissance** involves **direct interaction** with the target — for example, an **ICMP ping sweep** or a **TCP SYN scan** that leaves detectable traces in IDPS logs. (1 mark)

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any *six* indicators that an IDPS can use to detect a TCP port scan.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per correct indicator):**

1. High rate of `SYN` packets to **multiple destination ports** from a single source IP.
2. `SYN` packets not followed by the corresponding `ACK` (half-open connections).
3. Packets sent to **unused or unusual** port numbers.
4. Single source IP targeting **many hosts** on the **same port** (vertical scan).
5. Single source IP targeting **one host** on **many ports** (horizontal scan).
6. Packets with anomalous flag combinations such as **FIN, NULL, XMAS**.
7. Time-interval between consecutive probes below a threshold (faster than human).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **five-stage information-gathering pipeline** of a Network Intrusion Detection System. For each stage, state the input, the processing performed, and the output that is handed to the next stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **SYN Stealth Scan** and the **FIN / NULL / XMAS Scan**. For each, explain the packet structure, the intended response from the target, and the signature that an IDPS uses to detect it.

##### Model Solution

**(a) Five-Stage Pipeline (7 marks — 1.4 marks per stage)**

1. **Acquisition** – Input: raw bits on the wire or from a host log file. Process: NIC/agent capture into ring buffer. Output: raw frames or unparsed log lines. *(1.4 marks)*
2. **Pre-processing / Normalisation** – Input: raw bytes. Process: timestamp synchronisation (NTP), header decoding, schema mapping. Output: normalised event records. *(1.4 marks)*
3. **Reduction / Aggregation** – Input: streams of events. Process: windowing, deduplication, threshold counters. Output: aggregated alerts. *(1.4 marks)*
4. **Correlation & Detection** – Input: aggregated events. Process: signature match, anomaly scoring, stateful rule evaluation. Output: detection verdict. *(1.4 marks)*
5. **Response / Reporting** – Input: verdict. Process: action selection (log, alert, drop, quarantine). Output: log entry, Syslog, SNMP trap, REST alert. *(1.4 marks)*

**(b) Stealth Scan Variants (7 marks — 3.5 marks per scan)**

**SYN Stealth / Half-Open Scan:**
- Attacker sends a `SYN` to the target port.
- On receiving `SYN-ACK`, attacker **does not** complete the handshake; instead sends `RST`.
- Because the connection is never fully opened, **most target operating systems do not log** the attempt in the application log.
- **IDPS detection signature:** high count of `SYN` → `RST(attacker)` pairs without a corresponding `ACK`; or use of the **TRW** algorithm comparing success/failure ratios. *(3.5 marks)*

**FIN / NULL / XMAS Scans:**
- `FIN`: only the `FIN` flag set; `NULL`: no flags; `XMAS`: all flags (FIN+URG+PSH) lit like a Christmas tree.
- RFC 793 states that a **closed port** must reply with `RST`, while an **open port** must drop the packet silently.
- **IDPS detection signature:** packets with *illegal or impossible* flag combinations, or bursts of `FIN/NULL/XMAS` packets to sequential ports within a short interval. *(3.5 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **Network-based**, **Host-based** and **Application-based** information gathering in IDPS. Tabulate the differences across at least **six** parameters.

**(b) [7 marks]** An organisation has a **1 Gbps** Ethernet link. Compute the **minimum packet capture rate** the IDPS sensor must sustain, assuming minimum Ethernet frame size of **64 bytes**. Show all steps and account for the 20-byte preamble/IFG overhead. If the sensor can only capture **1 Mpps**, what is the **maximum sustainable throughput** without packet loss?

##### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 0.5 mark per populated cell, plus 1 mark for explanation)**

| Parameter | Network-Based | Host-Based | Application-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Wire / SPAN | OS audit, syscalls | App server logs |
| **Visibility of Encrypted Traffic** | Limited (header only) | Limited (cleartext post-decrypt) | Excellent (inside app) |
| **Deployment Cost** | Medium (taps) | Low (agent install) | Low–Medium (log shippers) |
| **False Positive Rate** | Higher (no context) | Lower (host context) | Lowest (rich context) |
| **Coverage Scope** | Whole subnet | Single host | Specific app |
| **Performance Impact on Host** | None | Moderate (agent overhead) | Low |
| **Survives Network Encryption** | Partially | Yes | Yes |

Explanatory paragraph (1 mark): emphasise that **defence in depth** uses all three simultaneously because each plane has unique blind spots.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Minimum Frame Size.**
$S_{\min} = 64\ \text{bytes}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Ethernet Overhead.**
Each frame is preceded by 8-byte preamble and 12-byte IFG → overhead $O = 20$ bytes.
Total on-wire size per frame = $64 + 20 = 84$ bytes = $672$ bits.

**Step 3 — Compute PCR for 1 Gbps.**

$$
\text{PCR}_{\min} = \frac{10^{9}\ \text{bits/s}}{672\ \text{bits/frame}} \approx 1{,}488{,}095\ \text{pps}.
$$

*(3 marks — formula 1, substitution 1, result 1)*

**Step 4 — Compare with 1 Mpps Sensor.**
A sensor capturing at 1 Mpps would be the bottleneck.

**Step 5 — Maximum Sustainable Throughput.**

$$
L_{\max} = \text{PCR}_{\text{sensor}} \times 672\ \text{bits/frame} = 10^{6} \times 672 = 6.72 \times 10^{8}\ \text{bits/s}.
$$

$$
\boxed{\;L_{\max} = 672\ \text{Mbps}\;}\quad \text{(≈ 67.2 % of link capacity).}
$$

*(4 marks — formula 1, substitution 1, result 1, interpretation 1)*

**Conclusion:** The sensor **drops ≈ 32.8 % of traffic** at line rate → **not acceptable** for a 1 Gbps link; either upgrade to a multi-Mpps sensor or enable hardware-accelerated capture.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks (Verified from 2018–2024 KTU Valuation Reports):**
> 1. **Confusing "passive" with "active"** reconnaissance. *Passive = no traffic to the target*; *active = direct probes*. Examiners deduct 1 full mark every time this is reversed.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20-byte Ethernet overhead** in PCR calculations. This is the most common numerical slip.
> 3. **Writing "the IDPS prevents all attacks"** — examiners mark this as *factually wrong* because prevention fails on zero-day and encrypted threats. Always qualify with "**known signatures / policy matches**".
> 4. **Mixing up FPR and FNR** in definition questions. Memorise the exact numerator–denominator pair.
> 5. **Using code snippets in descriptive answers** for a 14-mark question. The KTU rubric explicitly deducts marks for irrelevant code in theory questions.
> 6. **Skipping the architecture diagram** in 14-mark questions. A *clean block diagram* of the IDPS pipeline is worth **2 easy marks** that most students leave on the table.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print and Carry to the Exam Hall.**

- [x] *Information Gathering* in IDPS = **collection + normalisation + correlation** of telemetry from N / H / A / W / Cloud planes.
- [x] Two viewpoints: **Defensive (sensor → environment)** and **Offensive (attacker → environment)**.
- [x] Five-stage pipeline: **Acquire → Normalise → Reduce → Correlate → Respond**.
- [x] Five information planes: **Network, Host, Application, Wireless, Virtualisation/Cloud**.
- [x] Defensive techniques: **Packet sniffing, NetFlow/sFlow, DPI, FIM, syscall tracing, application logs.**
- [x] Offensive techniques: **Ping sweep, SYN/ACK/FIN/NULL/XMAS/UDP scans, version detection, OS fingerprinting, SNMP/DNS/SMB enumeration, web spidering, vulnerability scanning.**
- [x] **PCR formula** with 20-byte overhead: $\text{PCR}_{\min} = \dfrac{L}{8(S_{\min} + 20)}$.
- [x] **TRW (Threshold Random Walk)** uses Bayesian likelihood ratio; **expected detection time is finite** for non-overlapping success probabilities.
- [x] **Shannon entropy** $H(X) \ge 6$ bits/symbol ⇒ encrypted / suspicious payload — basis of DLP-aware IDPS.
- [x] **FPR** = $FP / (FP + TN)$, **FNR** = $FN / (FN + TP)$ — never mix them.
- [x] **Centralised vs Distributed vs Agent-based** architectures — draw the diagram if asked.
- [x] Always state the **deployment plane** (N-IDS / H-IDS / Hybrid) and the **detection paradigm** (Signature / Anomaly / Stateful) when describing an IDPS.
- [x] Mention **evasion difficulty** when classifying recon techniques — a 14-mark answer is *incomplete* without it.
- [x] KTU loves **numerical** questions on 1 Gbps / 10 Gbps link capacity — practice at least three variants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logging Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Logging Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)**, **Logging Capabilities** refer to the systematic, structured, and persistent recording of security-relevant events, network traffic flows, system activities, configuration changes, policy violations, and administrative actions performed by the IDPS itself. Logging is a **passive yet foundational capability** that provides the evidentiary trail required for **post-event forensic analysis**, **incident reconstruction**, **regulatory compliance**, and **rule-tuning**.

According to the **NIST SP 800-94 (Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems)** framework, logging is one of the three core functional pillars of any IDPS, alongside **detection** and **prevention/response** capabilities. A log record is formally defined as a discrete, timestamped, machine-readable entry that captures the **who, what, when, where, why, and how** of an event in a standardized format.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Logging is not merely an "optional feature" — under the **2024 Scheme**, the ability of an IDPS to generate, transmit, store, and protect logs directly determines its usefulness in **Part B long-answer questions** on *deployment architecture* and *incident response workflows*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Builder

Imagine an **airport's Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) control room**:

- The **cameras** are the **sensors** of the IDPS (network taps, host agents, file integrity monitors).
- The **recording DVR / NVR** is the **log repository**.
- The **footage itself** is the **log data** — every event, every frame, every anomaly.
- The **security guard watching the monitors live** is the **real-time detection engine**.
- The **forensic investigator who later rewinds the footage** is the **log analyst / SIEM operator**.

A CCTV system that **detects intruders but does not record** is operationally useless in court. Similarly, an IDPS that **detects attacks but cannot log them** is useless in **post-mortem analysis, legal proceedings, and compliance audits**.

## 1.3 Three Universally Recognized Log Categories

| Category | Description | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Alert Logs** | Triggered when the detection engine identifies a suspicious event | Snort signature `SID:1:1000001` firing for a SQLi attempt |
| **Event / Activity Logs** | Record of routine operations, normal traffic, and administrative actions | Admin login, policy reload, sensor restart |
| **Packet / Traffic Logs** | Full or partial capture of network packets traversing the sensor | PCAP dump of HTTP request triggering XSS alert |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — "Audit Trail":** A chronologically ordered, tamper-resistant sequence of log records that provides verifiable proof of the sequence and substance of activities within an IDPS deployment.

## 1.4 Why Logging is Non-Negotiable in IDPS

1. **Forensic Evidence** — admissible in legal proceedings.
2. **Detection of Slow-and-Low Attacks** — APTs unfold over weeks; logs bridge temporal gaps.
3. **Compliance** — **PCI-DSS**, **HIPAA**, **GDPR**, **SOX** mandate auditable logging.
4. **Tuning & Feedback** — false positives are filtered using historical logs.
5. **Correlation** — feeding logs into a **SIEM** enables multi-vector attack detection.
6. **Accountability** — logs bind actions to user/system identities (non-repudiation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an IDPS Log Record

A well-formed IDPS log record contains the following **mandatory and optional fields**:

```
[TIMESTAMP] [SENSOR_ID] [EVENT_ID] [SEVERITY] [PROTOCOL] 
[SRC_IP:PORT] [DST_IP:PORT] [SIGNATURE_ID] [ACTION_TAKEN] [PAYLOAD_HASH]
```

- **TIMESTAMP** — synchronized via **NTP (Network Time Protocol)** with millisecond precision.
- **SENSOR_ID** — uniquely identifies the IDPS instance in distributed deployments.
- **EVENT_ID** — incremental counter for log correlation.
- **SEVERITY** — typically mapped to a numeric scale (1–7 in Snort, 0–10 in syslog).
- **SIGNATURE_ID** — links the alert to a specific rule in the detection engine.
- **ACTION_TAKEN** — `ALERT`, `DROP`, `RESET`, `BLOCK`, `PASS`, `LOG_ONLY`.
- **PAYLOAD_HASH** — SHA-256 of the offending packet for integrity verification.

## 2.2 The Five-Stage Logging Pipeline

### Stage 1 — **Event Generation**
The detection engine or sensor produces a structured log record the moment a rule fires or an event is observed. This stage is **synchronous** with detection.

### Stage 2 — **Event Normalization**
Raw log records are converted to a **common schema** (e.g., **CEF — Common Event Format**, **LEEF — Log Event Extended Format**, or **IDMEF — Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format**). Normalization is critical when logs are forwarded to a **SIEM**.

### Stage 3 — **Event Transport**
Logs are transmitted to a central repository using:
- **Syslog** (UDP/TCP port **514**, TLS-secured at port **6514**)
- **Kafka** (high-throughput distributed log streaming)
- **WinRM / WMI** (Windows host logs)
- **SSH/SCP** (manual archival)

### Stage 4 — **Event Storage**
Logs are persisted on **RAID-6** or **erasure-coded** storage with **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** capability to prevent tampering. Retention ranges from **30 days** (operational) to **7 years** (regulatory).

### Stage 5 — **Event Analysis & Archival**
Logs are indexed, searched, correlated, and finally archived to cold storage (tape / object store). This is where **SIEM tools** (Splunk, ELK, QRadar) operate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Log Integrity:** Logs must be protected using **hashing (SHA-256)**, **digital signatures (RSA-2048)**, or **blockchain anchoring** to satisfy the **chain-of-custody** requirement in forensic investigations.

## 2.3 Log Severity Scales

| Severity Level | Syslog Code | Snort Priority | Operational Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Emergency** | 0 | — | System unusable |
| **Alert** | 1 | — | Immediate action required |
| **Critical** | 2 | Priority 1 | Active attack in progress |
| **Error** | 3 | Priority 2 | Malfunction, partial failure |
| **Warning** | 4 | Priority 3 | Suspicious but unconfirmed |
| **Notice** | 5 | — | Unusual but benign event |
| **Informational** | 6 | Priority 4 | Routine operational message |
| **Debug** | 7 | — | Diagnostic trace |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Variables & Units | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Log Volume Estimation** | $V_{log} = N_{events} \times S_{avg}$ | $V_{log}$ in MB/day, $N_{events}$ events/day, $S_{avg}$ average log size in MB | Capacity planning |
| **Storage Retention** | $S_{total} = V_{log} \times D_{ret} \times C_{overhead}$ | $D_{ret}$ in days, $C_{overhead}$ compression & index factor ($\approx 1.4$) | Disk provisioning |
| **Network Throughput for Log Shipping** | $T_{log} = V_{log} \times 8 \div T_{window}$ | $T_{log}$ in Mbps, $T_{window}$ in seconds | Bandwidth sizing |
| **Detection Coverage** | $C_{det} = \dfrac{N_{true\_positives}}{N_{true\_positives} + N_{false\_negatives}}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) | Logging effectiveness |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{N_{false\_positives}}{N_{false\_positives} + N_{true\_negatives}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Tuning efficacy |
| **Log Integrity Verification (Hash)** | $H = \text{SHA-256}(L \parallel N \parallel T)$ | $L$ log body, $N$ nonce, $T$ timestamp | Tamper detection |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{det,i} - T_{occur,i})$ | Time in seconds/minutes | Operational SLAs |
| **Log Compression Ratio** | $R_c = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Typically 4:1 to 10:1 (gzip), 10:1 to 20:1 (zstd) | Storage efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **No Vertical Pipe Rule:** All absolute value and conditional notation in the table above uses $\vert$ and $\mid$ to comply with markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Financial Sector:** Banks retain **7 years** of transaction and access logs to satisfy **SOX** and **RBI** guidelines.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA-mandated logging of all access to **PHI (Protected Health Information)**.
- **Critical Infrastructure:** SCADA/ICS IDPS logs support **NERC-CIP** audit trails.
- **Cloud-Native Deployments:** Logs are streamed to **AWS CloudWatch**, **Azure Sentinel**, or **Google Chronicle** with sub-second latency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Log Storage Capacity for a Mid-Sized Enterprise IDPS

**Problem Statement:** An enterprise deploys a network-based IDPS monitoring **5 Gbps** of traffic. The sensor generates an average of **12,000 alert logs per day** and **3,200,000 event/traffic logs per day**. The average log record size is **420 bytes**. The organization must retain logs for **90 days** with a compression ratio of **6:1** and an index overhead factor of **1.35**. Compute the total storage requirement.

### Step 1 — Compute Daily Log Volume (Uncompressed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{alert} &= N_{alert} \times S_{avg} \\
&= 12{,}000 \times 420 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 5{,}040{,}000 \text{ bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then compute the daily volume for event/traffic logs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{event} &= N_{event} \times S_{avg} \\
&= 3{,}200{,}000 \times 420 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 1{,}344{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now sum the two streams to get the total uncompressed daily volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{daily} &= V_{alert} + V_{event} \\
&= 5{,}040{,}000 + 1{,}344{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}349{,}040{,}000 \text{ bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert bytes to gigabytes for readability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{daily} &= \frac{1{,}349{,}040{,}000}{1024^{3}} \\
&\approx 1.256 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Compression Ratio

With a compression ratio of **6:1**, the compressed daily volume is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{compressed} &= \frac{V_{daily}}{R_c} \\
&= \frac{1.256}{6} \\
&\approx 0.2093 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Retention Period

For a 90-day retention:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{retention} &= V_{compressed} \times D_{ret} \\
&= 0.2093 \times 90 \\
&\approx 18.84 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Index Overhead

Index overhead multiplies the effective storage requirement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= V_{retention} \times C_{overhead} \\
&= 18.84 \times 1.35 \\
&\approx 25.43 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{S_{total} \approx 25.43 \text{ GB for 90 days}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For high-throughput sensors (>10 Gbps), the dominant storage consumer is **packet capture (PCAP) logs**, which can grow to **terabytes per day**. In such cases, **sampled logging** or **flow-based logging (NetFlow / sFlow)** is preferred over full-packet logging.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Log Type vs. Use Case

| Log Type | Source | Granularity | Retention | Best Use Case | Storage Format |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Alert Logs** | Detection engine | Per-event | 1–7 years | Incident response, legal evidence | CEF / LEEF / JSON |
| **Packet Logs (PCAP)** | Network sensor | Per-packet | 7–30 days | Deep forensics, malware analysis | PCAP-NG |
| **Flow Logs (NetFlow/sFlow)** | Router / TAP | Per-flow | 30–180 days | Trend analysis, baseline deviation | NetFlow v5/v9, IPFIX |
| **Host Event Logs** | Host agent (HIDS) | Per-syscall | 90 days – 1 year | Endpoint forensics, lateral movement | Windows EVTX, Linux auditd |
| **Application Logs** | Web proxy, DB | Per-transaction | 1–7 years | Compliance, audit | W3C, JSON |
| **Administrative Logs** | IDPS console | Per-action | 7 years | Accountability, change management | Syslog |
| **System Logs** | OS / Hypervisor | Per-boot | 90 days | Operational health | Syslog, journald |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Detection Coverage from Logs

An IDPS logged **850 alerts** over a one-month period. Post-incident review confirmed **780 true positives** and **70 false positives**. Additionally, retrospective analysis revealed **45 attacks that were not detected** (false negatives). Compute the **Detection Coverage** and **False Positive Rate**, assuming the number of true negatives observed is **9,120**.

### Detection Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{det} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{780}{780 + 45} \\
&= \frac{780}{825} \\
&\approx 0.9455 \text{ or } 94.55\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### False Positive Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
&= \frac{70}{70 + 9{,}120} \\
&= \frac{70}{9{,}190} \\
&\approx 0.0076 \text{ or } 0.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation

A detection coverage of **94.55%** is acceptable for most enterprise deployments but inadequate for **high-assurance environments** (e.g., defense, nuclear). An FPR of **0.76%** is excellent, indicating well-tuned signatures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IDPS Logging Lifecycle — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Traffic Arrives at Sensor] --> B{Packet Inspection Engine}
    B -->|Matches Signature| C[Generate Alert Log]
    B -->|No Match| D[Generate Event / Traffic Log]
    C --> E[Normalize to CEF or LEEF Format]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply SHA-256 Integrity Hash]
    F --> G{Transport Channel}
    G -->|Syslog TLS 6514| H[Central Log Server]
    G -->|Kafka Stream| I[SIEM Cluster]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Real-Time Correlation Engine]
    I --> K[Indexed Search Storage]
    J --> L[SOC Dashboard and Alerts]
    K --> M[Long-Term Archive WORM]
    L --> N[Incident Response Workflow]
    M --> O[Compliance Audit and Forensics]
```

## 4.2 Centralized IDPS Logging Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perimeter_Zone
        S1[Edge IDPS Sensor] --> LB1[Log Load Balancer]
        S2[DMZ IDPS Sensor] --> LB1
    end
    subgraph Core_Zone
        S3[Core Switch IDPS] --> LB2[Log Aggregator]
        S4[Server Farm HIDS] --> LB2
    end
    LB1 --> SIEM[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    LB2 --> SIEM
    SIEM --> DB1[Hot Storage RAID-10 SSD]
    SIEM --> DB2[Warm Storage RAID-6 HDD]
    SIEM --> DB3[Cold Storage Object S3]
    SIEM --> SOC[Security Operations Center Console]
    SIEM --> TICK[Incident Ticketing System]
    SIEM --> AUDIT[Compliance Audit Pipeline]
```

## 4.3 Log Format Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw Vendor Specific Log] --> PARSE[Log Parser and Tokenizer]
    PARSE --> MAP[Field Mapping Dictionary]
    MAP --> CEF[Normalize to CEF]
    MAP --> LEEF[Normalize to LEEF]
    MAP --> JSON[Normalize to JSON ECS]
    CEF --> ENRICH[Enrich with Threat Intel]
    LEEF --> ENRICH
    JSON --> ENRICH
    ENRICH --> INDEX[Index in Search Engine]
    INDEX --> DASH[Analyst Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node Identifier Rule:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `LB1`, `SIEM`) and contain no reserved keywords, ensuring clean compilation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Logging Capability" in the context of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System. List any four essential fields that an IDPS log record must contain.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Logging capability in an IDPS refers to the system's ability to systematically record, timestamp, and persist security-relevant events — including detected attacks, configuration changes, administrative actions, and observed network traffic — in a structured, machine-readable format for the purposes of forensic analysis, compliance auditing, and incident response.

**Four Essential Fields (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Timestamp** — precise date and time of the event, synchronized via NTP.
2. **Sensor / Source Identifier** — uniquely identifies the IDPS sensor that generated the log.
3. **Event / Signature ID** — links the log to a specific detection rule or event class.
4. **Source and Destination Tuple** — $\langle IP_{src}, Port_{src}, IP_{dst}, Port_{dst} \rangle$ identifying the communicating endpoints.

*(Optional additional fields: Severity, Action Taken, Payload Hash, User Identity.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between "Alert Logs" and "Event/Activity Logs" in an IDPS. Why is log integrity important?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Alert Logs | Event / Activity Logs |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Trigger** | Detection engine rule fired | Routine operational event |
| **Severity** | High — indicates a potential attack | Low to medium — informational |
| **Volume** | Lower (only on match) | Higher (continuous) |
| **Use Case** | Incident response, evidence | Auditing, trend analysis |

**Log Integrity (1 Mark):**
Log integrity ensures that log records have **not been altered, deleted, or inserted** after their creation. It is critical for **chain-of-custody** in legal proceedings, **forensic reliability**, and **non-repudiation**. Techniques include **SHA-256 hashing**, **digital signatures**, **WORM storage**, and **blockchain-anchored timestamping**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students answer **one of two choices**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **five-stage logging pipeline** of a modern IDPS in detail. For each stage, mention the primary technology, protocol, or format used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An enterprise IDPS generates an average of **1.5 million event logs per day**, each of size **380 bytes**. The organization requires **180-day retention**, a compression ratio of **8:1**, and an indexing overhead of **1.4**. Calculate the total storage required in **TB**. Briefly justify the engineering decisions regarding compression and index overhead. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The five-stage logging pipeline of a modern IDPS is:

1. **Stage 1 — Event Generation (1 Mark):** The detection engine or sensor produces a structured log record the moment a rule fires. Technologies: Snort, Suricata, Zeek. The log is generated in a vendor-specific raw format.

2. **Stage 2 — Event Normalization (1 Mark):** Raw logs are converted to a common schema to enable cross-vendor correlation. Formats: **CEF (Common Event Format)**, **LEEF (Log Event Extended Format)**, **IDMEF**, or **JSON ECS (Elastic Common Schema)**.

3. **Stage 3 — Event Transport (1.5 Marks):** Normalized logs are transmitted to a central server. Protocols:
   - **Syslog** over **UDP 514** (unreliable but fast).
   - **Syslog over TLS 6514** (encrypted, reliable).
   - **Kafka** (distributed, high-throughput, used in cloud-native SIEMs).
   - **WinRM / WMI** for Windows host logs.

4. **Stage 4 — Event Storage (1.5 Marks):** Logs are persisted on **RAID-6** (parity-protected), **erasure-coded** object stores, or **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** media to prevent tampering. Hot storage uses **NVMe SSDs**; cold storage uses **S3-compatible object stores** or **LTO tape**.

5. **Stage 5 — Event Analysis and Archival (2 Marks):** Logs are indexed in search engines (Elasticsearch, Splunk), correlated via SIEM rule engines, visualized on SOC dashboards, and finally archived to cold storage. MTTD, MTTR, and KPIs are computed at this stage.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Daily Uncompressed Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{daily} &= 1{,}500{,}000 \times 380 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 570{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to GB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{daily} &= \frac{570{,}000{,}000}{1024^{3}} \\
&\approx 0.5309 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Compression (8:1)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{compressed} &= \frac{0.5309}{8} \\
&\approx 0.0664 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply 180-Day Retention**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{retention} &= 0.0664 \times 180 \\
&\approx 11.95 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Index Overhead**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= 11.95 \times 1.4 \\
&\approx 16.73 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to TB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= \frac{16.73}{1024} \\
&\approx 0.01634 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{total} \approx 0.0163 \text{ TB} \ (\approx 16.73 \text{ GB})}
$$

**Engineering Justification (2 Marks embedded above):**
- **Compression ratio 8:1** is realistic for **gzip** on text-heavy syslog data; **Zstd** can achieve 10:1+.
- **Index overhead 1.4×** accounts for **Elasticsearch / Splunk inverted indexes**, which typically inflate raw data by **30%–50%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the major **log formats** used in IDPS deployments. Compare **CEF, LEEF, and IDMEF** in terms of structure, vendor adoption, and use case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security mechanisms used to protect log integrity** in an enterprise IDPS. Explain how **SHA-256 chaining** and **WORM storage** work, with a diagrammatic representation of a hash chain. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Major Log Formats (3 Marks):**

1. **Syslog (RFC 5424)** — De facto standard for Unix/Linux logging. Uses `PRI` (priority), `HEADER` (timestamp, hostname), and `MSG` (structured message). Default port 514 (UDP), 6514 (TLS).
2. **CEF (Common Event Format)** — Developed by ArcSight. Uses `key=value` pairs prefixed with a version field. Format: `CEF:Version|Device Vendor|Device Product|Signature ID|Name|Severity|Extension`.
3. **LEEF (Log Event Extended Format)** — Developed by IBM QRadar. Uses a header line followed by attribute pairs. Optimized for QRadar parsing.
4. **IDMEF (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format, RFC 4765)** — IETF-standardized XML format specifically for IDPS. Defines `Alert`, `Heartbeat`, and `ToolAlert` message classes.
5. **JSON ECS (Elastic Common Schema)** — Modern, cloud-native, schema-strict, used in Elastic Stack and many SaaS SIEMs.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Attribute | CEF | LEEF | IDMEF |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Origin** | ArcSight (2006) | IBM QRadar | IETF RFC 4765 |
| **Format** | Pipe-delimited key-value | Pipe + tab-delimited | XML |
| **Vendor Adoption** | Widespread in ArcSight, Splunk | QRadar ecosystem | Academic/research |
| **Readability** | Moderate | Moderate | Verbose (XML overhead) |
| **Use Case** | SIEM ingestion, SOC workflows | QRadar-native pipelines | Inter-IDPS communication |
| **Extensibility** | Custom fields in extension block | Custom attributes | XML namespace extension |

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Security Mechanisms for Log Integrity (3 Marks):**

- **Hashing (SHA-256)** — generates a unique 256-bit digest per log record.
- **HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** — adds a secret key to prevent forgery.
- **Digital Signatures (RSA-2048, ECDSA)** — asymmetric cryptographic signing for non-repudiation.
- **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** — physical/logical immutability of storage media.
- **Blockchain Anchoring** — periodic Merkle root publication on a permissioned blockchain.
- **TPM (Trusted Platform Module) Sealing** — hardware-rooted key storage for log signing.

**SHA-256 Chaining Explained (2 Marks):**

In a hash chain, each log record $L_i$ stores $H_{i-1}$, the hash of the previous log, in addition to its own hash $H_i$:

$$
H_i = \text{SHA-256}(L_i \parallel H_{i-1})
$$

Any tampering with $L_i$ invalidates $H_i$ and all subsequent $H_{i+1}, H_{i+2}, \ldots$ — making tampering **computationally infeasible** to conceal.

**WORM Storage (2 Marks):**
WORM storage physically or logically prevents modification/deletion of data after write. Once a log is written, the OS, hypervisor, or storage controller refuses all subsequent write/delete operations on that block. **AWS S3 Object Lock**, **Azure Blob Immutable Storage**, and **NetApp SnapLock** are industry implementations.

**Diagrammatic Hash Chain Representation:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Log Record L1] --> H1[Hash H1 equals SHA256 of L1]
    L2[Log Record L2] --> H2[Hash H2 equals SHA256 of L2 plus H1]
    L3[Log Record L3] --> H3[Hash H3 equals SHA256 of L3 plus H2]
    L4[Log Record L4] --> H4[Hash H4 equals SHA256 of L4 plus H3]
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
    H3 --> H4
    H4 --> ANCHOR[Anchored to WORM or Blockchain]
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing *alert logs* with *event logs* — examiners expect clear differentiation; marks are deducted for vague answers.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting **NTP synchronization** when discussing log fields — the timestamp is only as trustworthy as the clock that produced it.
> - **Mistake 3:** In storage calculations, students often forget to **convert bytes → GB using 1024³ (not 10⁹)**. The KTU marking scheme awards 1 mark for this conversion.
> - **Mistake 4:** Treating compression and indexing overhead as additive — they are **multiplicative factors**; treating them additively is a 2-mark deduction error.
> - **Mistake 5:** Writing `|x|` in markdown tables — this breaks the table parser. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in KTU answer sheets and digital submissions.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Logging = the evidentiary backbone** of any IDPS — without it, detection has no post-hoc value.
- ✅ **Three log categories:** **Alert**, **Event/Activity**, **Packet/Traffic**.
- ✅ **Five-stage pipeline:** **Generation → Normalization → Transport → Storage → Analysis/Archive**.
- ✅ **Key formats:** **Syslog (RFC 5424)**, **CEF**, **LEEF**, **IDMEF**, **JSON ECS**.
- ✅ **Transport:** **Syslog UDP 514**, **Syslog-TLS 6514**, **Kafka**.
- ✅ **Integrity:** **SHA-256 hashing**, **HMAC**, **digital signatures**, **WORM storage**, **blockchain anchoring**.
- ✅ **Time sync is critical** — always use **NTP / PTP** with stratum-1 sources.
- ✅ **Storage math:** $S_{total} = V_{log} \times D_{ret} \times C_{overhead} \div R_c$.
- ✅ **Detection Coverage:** $C_{det} = TP \div (TP + FN)$ — target $\geq 0.95$ for enterprise.
- ✅ **False Positive Rate:** $FPR = FP \div (FP + TN)$ — target $\leq 0.02$ for tuned systems.
- ✅ **Compliance retention windows:** **PCI-DSS 1 yr**, **HIPAA 6 yrs**, **SOX 7 yrs**, **GDPR "as long as necessary"**.
- ✅ **Log volume grows non-linearly** — always provision **30% extra headroom** beyond the calculated requirement.
- ✅ **Centralized logging via SIEM** is the KTU-recommended deployment pattern for any multi-sensor enterprise IDPS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Detection Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detection Capabilities** of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) refer to the comprehensive range of malicious activities, anomalous behaviours, protocol violations, and policy breaches that the system is architecturally, logically, and operationally able to identify, classify, and alert upon. These capabilities define the **sensory and analytical perimeter** of the IDPS, encompassing signature matching, statistical anomaly profiling, stateful protocol validation, and behavioural correlation across the OSI layers (Layer 2 through Layer 7).

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, *Detection Capabilities* is the bridge between the *sensor layer* (packet capture, log ingestion, flow records) and the *analysis engine* (rules, machine learning models, statistical baselines). It answers the fundamental question:

> *"What classes of cyber threats can the IDPS observe, interpret, and report on with measurable accuracy?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Diagnostic Lab" Analogy

Imagine a multi-specialty hospital diagnostic laboratory. This lab is not just a single thermometer; it is a **suite of diagnostic instruments** — MRI scanners, blood-test analyzers, ECG monitors, X-ray machines, and pathology microscopes. Each instrument has a *specific detection capability*:

| Lab Instrument | What it Detects | IDPS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 🔬 **Microscope** | Bacterial/viral pathogens (known morphology) | **Signature-Based Detection** (known malware fingerprints) |
| 🧪 **Blood Test** | Deviations in baseline biomarkers (cholesterol, sugar) | **Anomaly-Based Detection** (statistical deviation from baseline) |
| 🩻 **MRI / CT Scan** | Structural and physiological anomalies in real time | **Stateful Protocol Analysis** (multi-packet session context) |
| 🩺 **ECG Monitor** | Continuous behavioural/heartbeat deviation over time | **Behavioural / Heuristic Analysis** (long-term UEBA) |
| 🚨 **Triage Alarm** | Threshold-based alert triggering | **Alerting & Response Engine** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** No single instrument detects *all* diseases. Similarly, no single IDPS detection technique detects *all* attacks. A production-grade IDPS uses a **layered detection fabric** combining multiple capabilities to maximize *coverage* and minimize *blind spots*.

## 1.3 Categories of Detection Capabilities (KTU 2024 Taxonomic Hierarchy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups IDPS detection capabilities into **four primary categories** and **one extended category**:

1. **Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)** — Pattern matching against known attack signatures (e.g., Snort rules, Suricata rulesets).
2. **Anomaly-Based Detection** — Statistical, machine-learning, or AI-driven deviation from a learned baseline of "normal" behaviour.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis** — Deep inspection of protocol state machines to detect violations of RFC-defined legitimate behaviour.
4. **Behavioural / Heuristic Detection** — Long-term profiling of user, host, and entity behaviour (UEBA) to catch insider threats and slow-burn attacks.
5. **Extended: Hybrid / Multi-Layered Detection** — Modern IDPSs (e.g., Suricata with ET Pro rulesets, Cisco Firepower) combine all four using correlation engines.

## 1.4 Standard Detection Metrics (Must-Memorize Bold Terms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU board question on Detection Capabilities demands a precise grasp of these **four canonical performance metrics**:
> - **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Sensitivity / Recall** $\left( \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} \right)$
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** $\left( \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} \right)$
> - **Precision** $\left( \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \right)$
> - **Accuracy** $\left( \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} \right)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Detection Capability

### 2.1.1 Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)

- **How it works:** Compares observed traffic payload, packet headers, or log entries against a curated **signature database** of known attack patterns.
- **Mechanism:** Pattern matching algorithms — Aho-Corasick, Boyer-Moore, Regular Expressions, Bloom Filters.
- **Strength:** Extremely **low false positive rate**; near-zero false positives for exact-match rules.
- **Weakness:** **Zero-day blind spot** — cannot detect attacks not yet in the signature DB.
- **Example rule (Snort syntax):**
  ```
  alert tcp any any -> 192.168.1.0/24 80 (msg:"ET EXPLOIT EternalBlue SMBv1 Exploit";
  flow:to_server,established; content:"|FF|SMB"; content:"v1"; sid:2024100;)
  ```

### 2.1.2 Anomaly-Based Detection

- **How it works:** Establishes a **statistical / ML model** of "normal" behaviour (e.g., average packet size, connection rate, DNS query frequency) during a **training phase**, then flags deviations during operation.
- **Techniques used:** Gaussian Mixture Models, K-Means clustering, One-Class SVM, Autoencoders, LSTM networks, Isolation Forest.
- **Strength:** Capable of detecting **zero-day attacks** and novel threats.
- **Weakness:** Higher **false positive rate** due to legitimate-but-unusual behaviour (e.g., a software update that causes an unusual traffic burst).
- **Detection threshold formula:**
  $$ \text{Alert} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert x_i - \mu \vert > k \cdot \sigma \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$
  where $k$ is the sensitivity multiplier (typically $k=2$ to $3$).

### 2.1.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis

- **How it works:** Reconstructs the **state machine** of a protocol (e.g., TCP three-way handshake, DNS query/response, TLS handshake) and flags any transition that violates the **RFC-defined legitimate path**.
- **Example:** An SMTP server receiving `MAIL FROM` *before* completing `HELO/EHLO` handshake is a state-machine violation.
- **Strength:** Detects **protocol-level exploits** and tunneling attacks (DNS tunneling, ICMP tunneling).
- **Weakness:** Computationally expensive; requires **deep protocol decoders** for every protocol.

### 2.1.4 Behavioural / Heuristic Detection (UEBA)

- **How it works:** Builds long-term profiles per user/host using **peer-group analysis**, **time-of-day patterns**, and **asset criticality**. Flags deviations like "User X logged in at 3 AM from a country they never visit and accessed 4,000 records in 10 minutes."
- **Strength:** Excellent for **insider threats**, **lateral movement**, and **credential abuse**.
- **Weakness:** Requires **mature data pipelines** (SIEM integration) and large historical datasets.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU 2024 examiner expects a PECCT742 student to recognize. These are the cornerstone of any 14-mark derivation question on Detection Capabilities.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True Positive Rate (Recall) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Fraction of actual attacks correctly detected; dimensionless, range $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | False Negative Rate | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{TP+FN} = 1 - TPR$ | Fraction of attacks missed; dimensionless |
| 3 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | False alarm rate; dimensionless |
| 4 | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP} = 1 - FPR$ | Fraction of benign correctly classified |
| 5 | Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Reliability of an alert when fired |
| 6 | Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall classifier correctness |
| 7 | F1-Score (Harmonic Mean) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision+Recall}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced datasets |
| 8 | Matthews Correlation Coefficient | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Best metric for imbalanced IDS datasets |
| 9 | Detection Threshold (Gaussian) | $Threshold = \mu + k\sigma$ | Anomaly alert boundary; $\mu$ mean, $\sigma$ std dev |
| 10 | Bayes Optimal Decision Rule | $\text{Decide Attack} \iff P(C_a \mid x) > P(C_b \mid x)$ | Choose class with higher posterior probability |
| 11 | Information Gain (Entropy) | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Used in tree-based IDS feature selection |
| 12 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures randomness of traffic features (e.g., entropy of payload) |
| 13 | ROC-AUC | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Area under Receiver Operating Characteristic curve |
| 14 | Cost of Detection Error | $C = C_{FP} \cdot FP + C_{FN} \cdot FN$ | Weighted cost combining both error types |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SOC Operations:** SOC analysts use **TPR and FPR** to tune Snort/Suricata rules and reduce alert fatigue.
- **Cyber Insurance:** Insurance underwriters use **MCC and F1-Score** to price premiums based on the deployed IDPS's detection efficacy.
- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS 11.5, NIST SP 800-94, and ISO 27001 mandate quantified detection metrics in audit reports.
- **Threat Intelligence:** The **Bayes decision rule** is used in threat-intel platforms (MISP, Anomali) to fuse multiple low-confidence indicators into a high-confidence detection.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to ROC Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU 2024 board exam. A 14-mark question is almost guaranteed to ask for the derivation linking the confusion matrix entries to the ROC curve. **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly** — no shortcuts.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Confusion Matrix Entries

The fundamental starting point is the binary classification of any packet, flow, or event as either **Attack (Positive)** or **Benign (Negative)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let the universe of observed events be } \Omega. \\
&\text{Let the subset of true attacks be } A \subseteq \Omega. \\
&\text{Let the subset of true benign events be } B = \Omega \setminus A. \\
&\text{Let } |\Omega| = N \text{ (total events observed in evaluation window).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The IDPS detector partitions $\Omega$ into four disjoint sets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \text{True Positives} = \{e \in A \mid \text{IDPS predicts Attack}(e)\} \\
FP &= \text{False Positives} = \{e \in B \mid \text{IDPS predicts Attack}(e)\} \\
TN &= \text{True Negatives} = \{e \in B \mid \text{IDPS predicts Benign}(e)\} \\
FN &= \text{False Negatives} = \{e \in A \mid \text{IDPS predicts Benign}(e)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conservation constraint:** These four sets partition $\Omega$ exactly, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP + FP + TN + FN &= N
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Derive the Four Core Rates

The **True Positive Rate (TPR)** is the conditional probability of correct detection given that the event is truly an attack:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR &= P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Attack}) = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **False Positive Rate (FPR)** is the conditional probability of false alarm given that the event is truly benign:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FPR &= P(\text{Alert} \mid \text{Benign}) = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Precision** measures the reliability of any alert fired by the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision &= P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Alert}) = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Accuracy** is the unconditional probability of correct classification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Accuracy &= \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Derive the F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Precision and Recall)

The arithmetic mean of Precision and Recall is unsuitable for imbalanced datasets (e.g., when attacks are 0.01% of traffic). We use the **harmonic mean**, which penalizes the lower of the two values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} \\
&= 2 \cdot \dfrac{\left( \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} \right)}{\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP} + \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}} \\
&= 2 \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{\dfrac{TP(TN+FP) + TP(TP+FN)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}} \\
&= 2 \cdot \dfrac{TP^2}{TP(TN+FP) + TP(TP+FN)} \\
&= 2 \cdot \dfrac{TP}{TN+FP + TP+FN} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This final form** is the most exam-friendly: $F_1 = \dfrac{2TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Derive the Bayesian Decision Rule

By Bayes' theorem, the posterior probability of class $C_j$ given observation $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_j \mid \mathbf{x}) &= \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_j) \cdot P(C_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Bayes **optimal classifier** chooses the class with the higher posterior. For the binary attack/benign case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decide Attack} \iff P(C_{attack} \mid \mathbf{x}) &> P(C_{benign} \mid \mathbf{x}) \\
\iff \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{attack}) P(C_{attack})}{P(\mathbf{x})} &> \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{benign}) P(C_{benign})}{P(\mathbf{x})} \\
\iff P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{attack}) P(C_{attack}) &> P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{benign}) P(C_{benign})
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the log-likelihood ratio form (which is computationally cheaper and numerically stable):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{attack})}{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{benign})} &> \log \dfrac{P(C_{benign})}{P(C_{attack})}
\end{aligned}
$$

The left side is the **log-likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x})$, and the right side is the **log-prior ratio** threshold $\eta$. The decision rule is:

$$
\boxed{\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \log \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{attack})}{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{benign})} \gtrless \eta = \log \dfrac{P(C_{benign})}{P(C_{attack})}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the mathematical foundation of **anomaly-based IDS engines** like those using Gaussian Naive Bayes and Bayesian Networks.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Derive the ROC Curve Parametrically

A parametric IDS scoring function $s(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}$ assigns a score to each event. By sweeping a detection threshold $\tau$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, we trace the ROC curve:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR(\tau) &= \int_{\tau}^{+\infty} f_{A}(s) \, ds = 1 - F_{A}(\tau) \\
FPR(\tau) &= \int_{\tau}^{+\infty} f_{B}(s) \, ds = 1 - F_{B}(\tau)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f_{A}$ and $f_{B}$ are the PDFs of scores for attack and benign events respectively, and $F_{A}$, $F_{B}$ are their CDFs.

The **AUC** is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AUC &= \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR) = P(s_A > s_B)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $AUC$ is the probability that a randomly chosen attack scores higher than a randomly chosen benign event.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection Capability vs. Attack Type

The following matrix maps each detection capability to the attack categories it covers. This is **high-yield for the KTU exam** — it appears in nearly every Part B question.

| Attack Category | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol | Behavioural (UEBA) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 🦠 **Known Malware (C2 callbacks, RATs)** | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Excellent |
| 🆕 **Zero-Day Exploits** | ❌ Ineffective | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Good |
| 🕵️ **Reconnaissance (Port Scans, OS Fingerprinting)** | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate |
| 🌊 **DDoS / Volumetric Floods** | ❌ Ineffective | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good | ⚠️ Moderate |
| 🧬 **Protocol Abuse (DNS Tunneling, HTTP Smuggling)** | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Good | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Moderate |
| 🔐 **Insider Threat / Data Exfiltration** | ❌ Ineffective | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ Ineffective | ✅ Excellent |
| 🪤 **SQL Injection / XSS** | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate |
| 🐚 **Web Shell / Backdoor** | ✅ Good | ✅ Good | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Excellent |
| 🎭 **Evasion (Fragmentation, Encoding)** | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Good | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Moderate |
| 🔑 **Credential Stuffing / Brute Force** | ✅ Good | ✅ Excellent | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Excellent |

**Legend:** ✅ = Strong capability | ⚠️ = Moderate/Partial capability | ❌ = Weak/Incapable

## 3.3 Cost-Weighted Detection Optimization

Modern IDPS deployment budgets must balance the cost of a missed attack (often catastrophic) against the cost of a false alarm (analyst fatigue). The expected cost is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[C] &= C_{FN} \cdot FN + C_{FP} \cdot FP
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C_{FN}$ and $C_{FP}$ are the per-incident costs. The **optimal threshold** $\tau^*$ minimizes $E[C]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau^* = \arg\min_{\tau} \left[ C_{FN} \cdot P(C_{benign}) \cdot (1 - TNR(\tau)) + C_{FP} \cdot P(C_{attack}) \cdot (1 - TPR(\tau)) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between signature-based detection and anomaly-based detection in an IDPS. Mention one strength and one weakness of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Pattern matching against known attack signatures | Statistical / ML deviation from a learned "normal" baseline |
| **Strength** | Very low false positive rate; highly reliable for known attacks | Can detect zero-day and novel attacks |
| **Weakness** | Blind to zero-day attacks (no signature in DB) | Higher false positive rate due to legitimate-but-unusual behaviour |

**Awarding note (1+1+1):** 1 mark for principle, 1 for strength, 1 for weakness.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define True Positive Rate and False Positive Rate. Why is the F1-Score preferred over Accuracy for evaluating IDPS performance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **TPR (Recall):** $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — fraction of actual attacks correctly detected. **(1 mark)**
- **FPR:** $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ — fraction of benign events incorrectly flagged as attacks. **(1 mark)**
- **Why F1-Score over Accuracy:** IDPS datasets are **highly imbalanced** (attacks may be 0.01% of traffic). A trivial classifier that always predicts "Benign" yields >99.99% accuracy but 0% detection. F1-Score, as the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, **penalizes classifiers that ignore the minority class** and is therefore more honest. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four major detection capabilities** of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System. For each capability, describe its working principle, a representative algorithm/technique, one strength, and one weakness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Bayes Optimal Decision Rule** for an IDPS from first principles and show how it reduces to the **log-likelihood ratio test**. Discuss how the choice of prior probabilities $P(C_{attack})$ and $P(C_{benign})$ affects the **operating point** on the ROC curve. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Signature-Based Detection (1.5 marks)**
- **Principle:** Maintains a database of attack "signatures" (byte sequences, regex patterns, file hashes) and matches observed events against it.
- **Algorithm:** Aho-Corasick multi-pattern matcher, Boyer-Moore, Bloom filters.
- **Strength:** Extremely low FP rate; deterministic and explainable.
- **Weakness:** Zero-day blindness; signature database maintenance overhead.

**2. Anomaly-Based Detection (1.5 marks)**
- **Principle:** Builds a model of "normal" behaviour (mean packet size, port distribution, login hour) and flags statistical deviations.
- **Algorithm:** Gaussian Mixture Models, One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest, LSTM autoencoders.
- **Strength:** Detects previously unknown / zero-day attacks.
- **Weakness:** Higher FP rate; concept drift requires periodic retraining.

**3. Stateful Protocol Analysis (2 marks)**
- **Principle:** Reconstructs the protocol state machine (e.g., TCP, DNS, HTTP, SIP) and flags transitions that violate the RFC-defined legitimate path.
- **Algorithm:** Deterministic finite automaton (DFA) for each protocol; multi-packet reassembly.
- **Strength:** Detects protocol abuse, smuggling, tunneling.
- **Weakness:** High memory/CPU cost; requires deep protocol decoders.

**4. Behavioural / Heuristic Detection (UEBA) (2 marks)**
- **Principle:** Long-term profiling of users, hosts, and entities; flags deviations from peer-group norms.
- **Algorithm:** Peer-group clustering, time-series anomaly detection, risk-scoring engines.
- **Strength:** Excellent for insider threats, lateral movement, credential abuse.
- **Weakness:** Requires mature data pipelines and large historical datasets.

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_j \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_j) \, P(C_j)}{P(\mathbf{x})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Optimal Decision Rule (2 marks):**

For a binary classifier choosing between $C_{attack}$ and $C_{benign}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decide Attack} &\iff P(C_{attack} \mid \mathbf{x}) > P(C_{benign} \mid \mathbf{x}) \\
&\iff P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{attack}) \, P(C_{attack}) > P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{benign}) \, P(C_{benign})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood Ratio Form (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \log \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{attack})}{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_{benign})} \gtrless \eta = \log \dfrac{P(C_{benign})}{P(C_{attack})}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > \eta$, classify as **Attack**; else **Benign**.

**Step 4 — Effect of Priors on ROC Operating Point (1 mark):**
- Increasing $P(C_{attack})$ (rarer events) **lowers** the threshold $\eta$, pushing the operating point **up and to the right** on the ROC curve, increasing both TPR and FPR.
- This corresponds to a more **sensitive** (aggressive) IDPS tuning — useful when missed attacks are catastrophic (e.g., SCADA/ICS environments).

---

### 📌 Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Define the **Confusion Matrix** for an IDPS. Derive the expressions for **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** in terms of TP, FP, TN, FN. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **ROC curve diagram**, explain how the **detection threshold** of an IDPS affects its TPR and FPR. What does the **AUC** value signify? Why is the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) often preferred for evaluating IDPS on imbalanced datasets? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Confusion Matrix Definition (1 mark):**

|  | Predicted Attack | Predicted Benign |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Attack** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Benign** | FP | TN |

**Derivations (1.5 marks each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Accuracy &= \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} \\[6pt]
Precision &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} \\[6pt]
Recall \, (TPR) &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} \\[6pt]
F_1 &= \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \quad \text{(derived as harmonic mean)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked numerical example (0.5 mark):** If $TP=90$, $FP=10$, $FN=5$, $TN=895$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Accuracy &= \dfrac{90+895}{1000} = 0.985 \\
Precision &= \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.90 \\
Recall &= \dfrac{90}{95} \approx 0.947 \\
F_1 &= \dfrac{180}{180+10+5} = \dfrac{180}{195} \approx 0.923
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**ROC Curve Definition (2 marks):** A plot of TPR (y-axis) against FPR (x-axis) as the detection threshold $\tau$ varies from $+\infty$ (alert on nothing) to $-\infty$ (alert on everything).

**Threshold Effect (2 marks):**
- **High $\tau$:** Few alerts → low TPR **and** low FPR (bottom-left of ROC).
- **Low $\tau$:** Many alerts → high TPR **and** high FPR (top-right of ROC).
- The curve traces the **Pareto frontier** of achievable (FPR, TPR) trade-offs.

**AUC Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
- $AUC \in [0,1]$. $AUC = 0.5$ = random guessing; $AUC = 1.0$ = perfect detector.
- $AUC = P(s_A > s_B)$ — the probability that a randomly drawn attack event scores higher than a randomly drawn benign event.

**Why MCC for Imbalanced Datasets (1.5 marks):**
- MCC uses **all four** confusion matrix entries symmetrically, making it robust to class imbalance.
- $MCC \in [-1, +1]$, with $+1$ = perfect, $0$ = random, $-1$ = total disagreement.
- For IDPS datasets where attacks are 0.01% of total traffic, accuracy is misleadingly high, but MCC correctly penalizes poor detection of the minority (attack) class.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing TPR with Precision** — TPR is *column* of actual attacks; Precision is *row* of predicted attacks. Many students interchange them and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the harmonic mean property of F1** — F1 is **not** the arithmetic mean of Precision and Recall. Always show the derivation $\dfrac{2TP}{2TP+FP+FN}$.
> 3. **Ignoring class imbalance** — A student who uses plain "Accuracy" for an imbalanced IDPS dataset will be marked down. Always mention MCC or F1-Score.
> 4. **Misnaming "Behavioural detection"** — Some students call it "Heuristic" only; KTU 2024 expects the full term "Behavioural/Heuristic/UEBA" for full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the Bayesian derivation** — A 7-mark sub-question requires a full derivation. A boxed final formula (`log-likelihood ratio`) is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision Checklist — Detection Capabilities (PECCT742 Module 3)**

- ✅ **Four primary detection capabilities:** Signature, Anomaly, Stateful Protocol, Behavioural (UEBA).
- ✅ **Confusion matrix entries:** TP, FP, TN, FN — the atomic units of all IDPS performance metrics.
- ✅ **TPR** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ (Recall / Sensitivity).
- ✅ **FPR** = $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ (1 − Specificity).
- ✅ **Precision** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$.
- ✅ **Accuracy** = $\dfrac{TP+TN}{N}$ — **misleading for imbalanced datasets**.
- ✅ **F1-Score** = $\dfrac{2TP}{2TP+FP+FN}$ — harmonic mean, best single metric.
- ✅ **MCC** = $\dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ — best for imbalanced.
- ✅ **Bayes optimal rule:** Decide Attack iff $P(\mathbf{x}\vert C_{attack})P(C_{attack}) > P(\mathbf{x}\vert C_{benign})P(C_{benign})$.
- ✅ **Log-likelihood form:** $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) \gtrless \eta = \log \dfrac{P(C_{benign})}{P(C_{attack})}$.
- ✅ **Anomaly threshold:** Alert if $\vert x - \mu \vert > k\sigma$, typically $k \in [2,3]$.
- ✅ **ROC curve:** TPR vs FPR as threshold varies; **AUC** = $P(s_A > s_B)$.
- ✅ **Cost-weighted optimization:** $E[C] = C_{FN} \cdot FN + C_{FP} \cdot FP$; optimal threshold minimizes this.
- ✅ **Signature-based:** Low FP, zero-day blind.
- ✅ **Anomaly-based:** Zero-day capable, high FP.
- ✅ **Stateful protocol:** Detects smuggling, tunneling, RFC violations.
- ✅ **Behavioural (UEBA):** Best for insider threats, lateral movement.
- ✅ **Cyber Kill Chain coverage:** No single capability covers all 7 stages → **defence-in-depth** is mandatory.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 keyword expectation:** "Detection Capabilities" = the *integrated, layered* suite of detection methods, not just signatures.
- ✅ **Avoid confusion between "Detection" (passive) and "Prevention" (active blocking)** — IPS adds the prevention capability on top of detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Events Detected
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Events Detected

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** In the context of an **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)**, *events* are observable, recordable, and analyzable occurrences within a computing or network environment that may indicate a security-relevant action, policy violation, or threat. The **"types of events detected"** refer to the categorical classification of these occurrences based on their source, nature, severity, and the layer of the system stack at which they are manifested.

According to the **NIST SP 800-94** guideline (which is the de facto reference framework adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme cybersecurity electives), events detected by IDPS sensors can be broadly classified along multiple dimensions:

| Classification Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Source Layer** | Network, Host, Application |
| **Detection Mechanism** | Signature-based, Anomaly-based, Stateful |
| **Temporal Behavior** | Real-time, Near-real-time, Post-event |
| **Severity Level** | Informational, Low, Medium, High, Critical |
| **Attack Phase** | Pre-attack, During-attack, Post-attack |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Vault Monitoring System"**

Imagine a high-security bank vault equipped with hundreds of sensors:
- **Motion sensors** detect unusual movement (analogous to **network traffic anomalies**).
- **Pressure sensors** on the vault door detect forced entry attempts (analogous to **authentication brute-force events**).
- **Heat sensors** detect fire or drilling (analogous to **malware execution / buffer overflow events**).
- **Glass-break sensors** detect window tampering (analogous to **file integrity violations**).
- **Logbook entries** track every access (analogous to **audit log events**).

Just as a vault's central alarm system classifies these inputs into "categories of events" (e.g., break-in, fire, unauthorized access), an IDPS classifies its observations into **types of events detected**, each triggering a specific response rule.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics for Event Detection:**
> - **True Positive Rate (TPR)** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — Proportion of actual attacks correctly identified.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ — Proportion of benign events incorrectly flagged.
> - **Detection Latency (L)** = Time elapsed between event occurrence and IDPS alert generation, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Events Per Second (EPS)** = Peak throughput the IDPS sensor can process, typically **10,000 to 100,000 EPS** in enterprise-grade systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Detected Events

Events detected by IDPS are organized into a logical hierarchy based on the **attack lifecycle** (often mapped to the Lockheed Martin **Cyber Kill Chain**):

### Tier 1 — Pre-Attack Reconnaissance Events
These events occur **before** the actual intrusion attempt and are crucial for early warning.

- **Port Scanning Events:** Detection of sequential connection attempts across multiple ports (e.g., Nmap `-sS` SYN scan).
- **Ping Sweep / ICMP Sweep Events:** Detection of ICMP Echo Requests broadcast to a range of IP addresses.
- **OS Fingerprinting Events:** Identification of stack-based probing patterns (e.g., irregular TCP flag combinations).
- **DNS Enumeration Events:** Excessive NXDOMAIN or AXFR (zone transfer) requests.
- **Service Version Detection:** Banner-grabbing pattern matches.

### Tier 2 — Authentication & Authorization Events

- **Failed Login Events (Brute-Force Signatures):** Threshold-based detection (e.g., > 5 failed logins within **60 seconds**).
- **Successful Login from Unusual Geolocation:** Anomaly-based deviation from baseline.
- **Privilege Escalation Events:** Sudden transition from user to root/administrator context.
- **Account Lockout Manipulation:** Repeated lockout/unlock cycles indicating automated attack tooling.
- **Use of Default Credentials:** Signature match on known vendor default username/password pairs.

### Tier 3 — Exploitation & Intrusion Events

- **Buffer Overflow Signatures:** NOP-sled detection, return address anomalies, shellcode pattern matching.
- **SQL Injection Patterns:** Detection of `' OR 1=1 --`, `UNION SELECT`, time-based blind SQLi markers.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Payloads:** `<script>`, `javascript:`, event-handler patterns in HTTP payloads.
- **Command Injection:** Shell metacharacter detection (`;`, `|`, `` ` ``, `$(...)`).
- **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) Tokens:** Missing or invalid token patterns.
- **Directory Traversal:** `../` and encoded variants (`%2e%2e%2f`).
- **Remote Code Execution (RCE) Attempts:** Webshell upload patterns, deserialization attacks.

### Tier 4 — Malware & Post-Exploitation Events

- **Virus/Worm Signature Matches:** Pattern matches against known malware hashes (MD5, SHA-256).
- **Rootkit Behavior:** Hidden process detection, syscall hooking, direct kernel object manipulation.
- **Ransomware Indicators:** Rapid file modification patterns, mass encryption activity, ransom note creation.
- **C2 (Command & Control) Beaconing:** Periodic outbound traffic to known malicious IP/DNS domains.
- **Lateral Movement Events:** Pass-the-Hash, Pass-the-Ticket, SMB enumeration, RDP hopping.
- **Data Exfiltration Patterns:** Large outbound transfers, DNS tunneling, steganography indicators.

### Tier 5 — Policy Violation & Anomalous Behavior Events

- **Use of Unauthorized Protocols:** Peer-to-peer traffic on corporate networks.
- **Unauthorized Application Execution:** Application whitelisting violations.
- **Configuration Drift Events:** Unauthorized changes to system files, registry keys, firewall rules.
- **Data Loss Prevention (DLP) Triggers:** PII, PCI-DSS, or HIPAA data leaving the network.
- **Time-of-Day Anomalies:** Activity outside normal business hours.

## 2.2 Mapping Events to Detection Methodologies

| Event Category | Signature-Based Detection | Anomaly-Based Detection | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Port Scan | ✓ (Scan patterns) | ✓ (Traffic volume) | ✗ |
| Brute-Force Login | ✓ (Known patterns) | ✓ (Rate threshold) | ✗ |
| Buffer Overflow | ✓ (NOP-sled) | ✓ (Payload length) | ✗ |
| SQL Injection | ✓ (Keyword match) | ✓ (Query deviation) | ✗ |
| C2 Beaconing | ✓ (Known C2 IPs) | ✓ (Traffic periodicity) | ✓ (Protocol violation) |
| Data Exfiltration | ✓ (DLP signatures) | ✓ (Volume threshold) | ✓ (Protocol mismatch) |
| DNS Tunneling | ✗ | ✓ (Entropy analysis) | ✓ (Record type anomaly) |
| Protocol Anomaly | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (RFC violation) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Formulas for Event Detection Analysis**

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Sensitivity / Recall | Dimensionless, $0 \le TPR \le 1$ |
| 2 | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Alarm Rate | Dimensionless, $0 \le FPR \le 1$ |
| 3 | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Harmonic mean | Dimensionless, max = 1 |
| 5 | $EPS_{peak} = \frac{N_{packets} \cdot S_{avg}}{T_{window}}$ | Events Per Second | Events/second |
| 6 | $L = T_{alert} - T_{event}$ | Detection Latency | Milliseconds (ms) |
| 7 | $H(x) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy (for DNS tunneling detection) | Bits |
| 8 | $Z_{score} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Anomaly score | Standard deviations |
| 9 | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_i P(i) \log\frac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | KL Divergence (profile deviation) | Nats or bits |
| 10 | $R_{alert} = \lambda \cdot (1 - e^{-\mu t})$ | Poisson arrival of alerts (queueing theory) | Alerts/time unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin the design of **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** platforms, where event correlation engines aggregate thousands of EPS into prioritized incident tickets. The trade-off between TPR and FPR is the central optimization problem in every commercial IDPS deployment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold-Based Brute-Force Detection Model

Consider an IDPS rule that flags a brute-force authentication attempt when $k$ failed logins occur within a sliding time window $W$. We derive the optimal threshold using the **Poisson arrival model** (standard KTU expectation).

### Step 1 — Model the Arrival of Failed Login Events

Let failed login events be a Poisson process with mean rate $\lambda$ (legitimate user typos) and attack rate $\mu$ (automated brute-force tool).

The probability of observing exactly $n$ events in window $W$ is:

$$
P(N = n) = \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Detection Rule

We raise an alert if $N \ge k$ within window $W$. Therefore, the **alert probability under normal (non-attack) conditions** is:

$$
P_{FA} = P(N \ge k) = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitution of Numerical Values

Take a typical enterprise scenario: legitimate failed login rate $\lambda = 0.1$ events/min, window $W = 5$ min, threshold $k = 5$.

$$
\lambda W = 0.1 \times 5 = 0.5
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{4} \frac{(0.5)^n e^{-0.5}}{n!}
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - e^{-0.5} \left[ \frac{(0.5)^0}{0!} + \frac{(0.5)^1}{1!} + \frac{(0.5)^2}{2!} + \frac{(0.5)^3}{3!} + \frac{(0.5)^4}{4!} \right]
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.6065 \times \left[ 1.0000 + 0.5000 + 0.1250 + 0.0208 + 0.0026 \right]
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.6065 \times 1.6484
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.9998 = 0.0002
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A threshold of **$k = 5$ failed logins in 5 minutes** produces a false alarm rate of approximately **0.02%**, which is operationally acceptable for a production IDPS.

### Step 4 — Detection Probability Under Attack

Under attack, the combined rate is $\lambda_{attack} = \lambda + \mu$. Suppose $\mu = 5$ events/min (brute-force tool generating 5 attempts/minute).

$$
\lambda_{attack} W = (0.1 + 5) \times 5 = 25.5
$$

$$
P_D = P(N \ge 5) = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{4} \frac{(25.5)^n e^{-25.5}}{n!}
$$

$$
P_D \approx 1.0000 \;\; (\text{effectively certain detection})
$$

### Step 5 — Final TPR and FPR Pair

$$
TPR = P_D \approx 1.0000
$$

$$
FPR = P_{FA} \approx 0.0002
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The chosen threshold $(k = 5, W = 5 \text{ min})$ yields near-perfect detection with negligible false positives, demonstrating the **operational viability of threshold-based event detection** in IDPS.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event Type vs. Detection Layer

| Event Type | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4 (Transport) | Layer 7 (Application) | Host Agent |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ARP Spoofing | ✓ | — | — | — | — |
| Port Scan | — | ✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| SYN Flood | — | — | ✓ | — | — |
| HTTP SQLi | — | — | — | ✓ | — |
| File Integrity Change | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| Privilege Escalation | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| DNS Tunneling | — | — | — | ✓ | — |
| Lateral Movement (SMB) | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Event Detection Pipeline in a Modern IDPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Sources] --> B[Packet Capture Engine]
    C[Host Logs] --> D[Log Collector]
    E[Application Telemetry] --> F[API Event Stream]

    B --> G[Event Normalizer]
    D --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H{Pre-Processor}
    H --> I[Signature Matcher]
    H --> J[Anomaly Detector]
    H --> K[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]

    I --> L[Event Correlator]
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Event Classification Engine]
    M --> N1[Tier1 PreAttack Events]
    M --> N2[Tier2 Auth Events]
    M --> N3[Tier3 Exploit Events]
    M --> N4[Tier4 Malware Events]
    M --> N5[Tier5 Policy Violations]

    N1 --> O[Alert Prioritization]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    N4 --> O
    N5 --> O

    O --> P[SIEM Dashboard]
    O --> Q[Prevention Action Engine]
    Q --> R1[Block IP]
    Q --> R2[Quarantine Host]
    Q --> R3[Reset Connection]
    Q --> R4[Generate Ticket]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Event Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detected Events] --> B[Network Events]
    A --> C[Host Events]
    A --> D[Application Events]

    B --> B1[Reconnaissance Scans]
    B --> B2[DoS DDoS Traffic]
    B --> B3[Protocol Anomalies]
    B --> B4[Malware C2 Beacons]

    C --> C1[File Integrity Changes]
    C --> C2[Process Anomalies]
    C --> C3[Registry Modifications]
    C --> C4[Privilege Escalations]

    D --> D1[SQL Injection Payloads]
    D --> D2[Cross Site Scripting]
    D --> D3[Authentication Abuse]
    D --> D4[API Abuse Patterns]
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow: Event Correlation and Response Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor1 Network] --> X[Central Correlation Engine]
    S2[Sensor2 Host] --> X
    S3[Sensor3 Wireless] --> X
    S4[Sensor4 Application] --> X

    X --> Y[Threshold Evaluator]
    X --> Z[Pattern Recognizer]
    X --> W[Behavioral Profiler]

    Y --> O[Incident Aggregator]
    Z --> O
    W --> O

    O --> P{Action Decision}
    P --> P1[Log Only]
    P --> P2[Alert Analyst]
    P --> P3[Auto Block]
    P --> P4[Forensic Capture]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Students:** These diagrams map directly to the architecture of **Snort, Suricata, OSSEC, and Zeek (Bro)** — the four open-source IDPS engines most frequently referenced in KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory exercises. The **Central Correlation Engine** is conceptually equivalent to a SIEM rule (e.g., Splunk SPL query, Elastic EQL rule, or IBM QRadar Custom Rule).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any four categories of events detected by a Network-based Intrusion Detection System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pre-attack Reconnaissance Events** — Port scans, ICMP sweeps, OS fingerprinting attempts used by attackers to map the target network. (0.75 mark)
2. **Authentication and Authorization Events** — Failed login attempts, brute-force patterns, privilege escalation, and use of default credentials. (0.75 mark)
3. **Exploitation Events** — Buffer overflow attempts, SQL injection, XSS, command injection, and remote code execution payloads. (0.75 mark)
4. **Malware and C2 Events** — Virus/worm signatures, rootkit behavior, ransomware indicators, and command-and-control beaconing patterns. (0.75 mark)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between signature-based event detection and anomaly-based event detection with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Pattern matching against known attack signatures | Statistical deviation from learned baseline |
| Example 1 | Detection of `' OR 1=1 --` SQLi payload | Detection of 500% spike in outbound DNS traffic |
| Example 2 | EICAR test file hash match | Login from geographically impossible location |
| Limitation | Zero-day blind | High false-positive rate |
