(1.5 marks per column for two examples + distinction.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Classify and explain in detail the **five tiers of events detected** by a modern IDPS, mapping each tier to the corresponding phase of the Cyber Kill Chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the **end-to-end event detection pipeline** of an enterprise IDPS, clearly marking the role of the correlation engine. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The five tiers of events detected by a modern IDPS, mapped to the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain phases, are:

1. **Tier 1 — Reconnaissance Events (Kill Chain: Reconnaissance)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Port scans (SYN, FIN, Xmas), ICMP sweeps, OS fingerprinting, DNS enumeration, and service version probing. These events are detected through threshold-based rules (e.g., "more than 100 ports probed in 60 seconds from a single source IP") and signature-based SYN-scan pattern detection.

2. **Tier 2 — Weaponization & Delivery Events (Kill Chain: Weaponization, Delivery)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Phishing email attachment detection, malicious URL categorization, weaponized document hashes (PDF/Office macro exploits), and drive-by download script detection. The IDPS uses email gateway integration and HTTP content inspection.

3. **Tier 3 — Exploitation Events (Kill Chain: Exploitation, Installation)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Buffer overflow NOP-sled detection, SQL injection, XSS, command injection, deserialization attacks, and web shell uploads. The IDPS payload inspector matches against the **Snort/VRT** rule set and **ModSecurity OWASP CRS**.

4. **Tier 4 — C2 and Lateral Movement Events (Kill Chain: Command & Control, Actions on Objectives)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Beaconing detection (periodic outbound traffic to known C2 IPs), Pass-the-Hash indicators, SMB enumeration, RDP hopping, and Kerberoasting patterns. Detected through NetFlow analysis, JA3/JA3S fingerprinting, and host-based log correlation.

5. **Tier 5 — Exfiltration and Impact Events (Kill Chain: Actions on Objectives)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Large outbound data transfers, DNS tunneling (high-entropy subdomain detection), ransomware encryption patterns, and destructive wipe events. Detected through DLP rule triggers, volume threshold alerts, and file-integrity monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by **listing attack types without explicitly mapping them to the Cyber Kill Chain phases**. Always draw an explicit table or labeled diagram linking each tier to its kill chain phase. Marks are awarded for the *mapping*, not merely the listing.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**End-to-End Event Detection Pipeline:**

The enterprise IDPS pipeline consists of **six functional blocks**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** **(1.0 mark)**
   Network taps/SPAN ports feed packets to the sensor. Host-based agents forward syslog, Windows Event Logs, and EDR telemetry. Cloud workloads stream API events (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log).

2. **Pre-Processing & Normalization** **(1.0 mark)**
   Packets are decoded (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application). Logs are parsed into a common schema (CEF, LEEF, or JSON). Timestamps are normalized to UTC.

3. **Detection Engines (Parallel)** **(2.0 marks)**
   - **Signature Engine:** Pattern match using Aho-Corasick or Hyperscan algorithms against rule sets (Snort VRT, Emerging Threats).
   - **Anomaly Engine:** Statistical models (Z-score, EWMA), machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Isolation Forest), and entropy analysis.
   - **Stateful Protocol Analyzer:** Validates protocol state machines (RFC compliance for HTTP, DNS, SMTP, TLS).

4. **Event Correlation Engine** **(1.5 marks)**
   The **central correlation engine** aggregates low-level events into high-fidelity incidents using:
   - **Temporal correlation:** Events occurring within a sliding window.
   - **Asset/context correlation:** Prioritizes alerts on critical servers.
   - **Threat intelligence correlation:** Enriches events with IOC feeds (STIX/TAXII).

5. **Alert Prioritization & Deduplication** **(1.0 mark)**
   Risk score is computed as $R = Severity \times Confidence \times Asset\_Value$. Duplicate alerts are collapsed.

6. **Response Action Module** **(0.5 mark)**
   Triggers automated responses: TCP RST injection, IP blocklist update, host quarantine, SOAR playbook execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** describe only one engine. Marks are split across signature, anomaly, **and** stateful analysis. A common error is omitting the correlation engine — which carries **1.5 marks by itself** in this question.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **threshold-based detection** and **statistical anomaly detection** in identifying brute-force authentication events. Derive the false-alarm probability for a Poisson-arrival model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the detection of **network-layer events** versus **host-layer events** with suitable examples, and justify why modern IDPS deployments use both in a **defense-in-depth** architecture. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Threshold-Based Detection:** A rule fires when $k$ failed-login events occur within window $W$ from the same source. For a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ (legitimate typos), the false-alarm probability is:

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}
$$

**Statistical Anomaly Detection:** A baseline mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ of failed logins per user per hour is established over a 30-day training period. The Z-score is computed as:

$$
Z = \frac{x_{observed} - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

An alert fires if $Z > 3$ (i.e., the observed value is more than 3 standard deviations from the mean).

**Numerical Example:** Let $\lambda = 0.1$ events/min, $W = 5$ min, $k = 5$.

$$
\lambda W = 0.5
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - e^{-0.5} \left[ 1 + 0.5 + \frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.125}{6} + \frac{0.0625}{24} \right]
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.6065 \times 1.6484 \approx 0.0002
$$

Thus, threshold-based detection yields $FPR \approx 0.02\%$, which is operationally excellent.

**Statistical Complement:** Threshold-based detection is deterministic and fast, but cannot adapt to per-user baselines. Statistical anomaly detection adapts dynamically but suffers higher false positives during training-data drift. **Modern IDPS use both in combination** to balance precision and recall.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only the formula without substituting numerical values, forfeiting **2 marks**. Always include a worked numerical example with explicit substitution.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Network-Layer Event Detection (NIDS):**
- **Examples:** SYN flood detection, port scan signatures, C2 beaconing, ARP spoofing, DNS tunneling.
- **Sensor placement:** Inline (IPS mode) or passive tap at network choke points.
- **Strengths:** Broad visibility across the entire subnet, detects reconnaissance, and catches attacks before they reach the host.
- **Limitations:** Cannot see encrypted payload contents (without TLS inspection), cannot detect host-level post-exploitation.

**Host-Layer Event Detection (HIDS):**
- **Examples:** File integrity monitoring (e.g., Tripwire), registry key changes, suspicious process creation, logon audits, rootkit detection.
- **Sensor placement:** Agent installed on each endpoint/server.
- **Strengths:** Visibility into encrypted local activity, host-context awareness (which user, which process), and post-exploitation detection.
- **Limitations:** Can be disabled by sophisticated rootkits, requires agent maintenance, generates per-host noise.

**Defense-in-Depth Justification:**

A purely network-based IDPS misses attacks that bypass the network (e.g., insider threats, USB-borne malware, encrypted C2 over HTTPS). A purely host-based IDPS cannot correlate events across the enterprise. The **defense-in-depth** model layers both:

| Layer | Catches | Misses |
|---|---|---|
| NIDS only | External scans, DDoS, lateral movement | Insider threats, host rootkits |
| HIDS only | Insider threats, host-level malware | External reconnaissance |
| NIDS + HIDS | All of the above with correlation | — |

The **SIEM correlation engine** then fuses NIDS and HIDS events into a unified incident picture, dramatically improving TPR while reducing FPR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not describe only one layer. **Both** must be compared, and the justification for defense-in-depth must explicitly mention the **insider threat** and **encrypted traffic** blind spots of NIDS-only deployments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Events Detected**

- [x] Events are classified into **five tiers** mapped to the Cyber Kill Chain: Reconnaissance, Delivery, Exploitation, C2/Lateral-Movement, Exfiltration/Impact.
- [x] **Three detection methodologies**: Signature (pattern), Anomaly (statistical/ML), Stateful (protocol RFC compliance).
- [x] **Three source layers**: Network, Host, Application.
- [x] **Key metrics**: TPR, FPR, Precision, Recall, F1-score, Detection Latency (ms), EPS.
- [x] **Poisson model** is the standard mathematical tool for threshold-based event-rate analysis: $P_{FA} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}$.
- [x] **Z-score** is the standard anomaly detection statistic: $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, with $Z > 3$ as the conventional alert threshold.
- [x] **Defense-in-depth** requires both **NIDS** and **HIDS** to cover external and insider threats.
- [x] Modern IDPS use **SIEM correlation engines** to fuse low-level events into high-fidelity incidents.
- [x] **Shannon entropy** $H(x) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the standard metric for DNS tunneling and encrypted exfiltration detection.
- [x] **Event correlation types**: temporal, asset-context, threat-intelligence, and behavioral.
- [x] **Open-source tools** to remember: **Snort** (signature), **Suricata** (multi-threaded signature + anomaly), **OSSEC/Wazuh** (HIDS), **Zeek** (stateful network analysis).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Detection Accuracy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Detection Accuracy** in an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) is formally defined as the **quantitative measure of the system's ability to correctly classify network traffic, host activity, or system events as either *malicious* (intrusion) or *benign* (normal)**, while simultaneously minimizing classification errors. Mathematically, it is the ratio of **correctly classified events** to the **total number of events observed** by the detector over a defined evaluation window: $$ \text{Detection Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} $$ Where: - $TP$ = True Positives - $TN$ = True Negatives - $FP$ = False Positives - $FN$ = False Negatives > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Mapping (CO Alignment):** > - **CO3:** *Analyse the performance metrics and evaluation methodologies of IDPS.* > - **Bloom's Level:** L4 (Analyse) → L5 (Evaluate) > - **Cognitive Skill Tested:** Quantitative reasoning, error-cost trade-off analysis, ROC interpretation. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **smoke detector** installed in a building: | Scenario | Reality | Detector Says | Verdict | |----------|---------|---------------|---------| | Real fire in kitchen | Intrusion (Attack) | **Alarm rings** | True Positive ✔ | | Toasted bread smoke | Normal event | **Alarm rings** | False Positive ✘ | | Real fire, battery dead | Intrusion (Attack) | **Silent** | False Negative ✘ | | No fire, clean air | Normal event | **Silent** | True Negative ✔ | > **The smoke detector is "accurate" if it rings ONLY for real fires (high TP) and stays SILENT when there is no fire (high TN).** A **False Positive** is like the alarm waking your family at 3 AM because of steam from the bathroom — annoying, but not catastrophic. A **False Negative** is like the alarm failing during an actual fire — a *life-threatening* failure that the security industry terms a **Type II Error** (the most dangerous error in IDPS). > [!TIP] > **The Cost Asymmetry Principle:** In security, the *cost of a missed attack* (FN) is usually **orders of magnitude higher** than the *cost of a false alarm* (FP). This asymmetric cost is the *central design tension* behind every IDPS accuracy metric. --- ## 1.3 The Fundamental Trade-off No real-world IDPS achieves **100% accuracy** because of three unavoidable realities: 1. **Concept Drift** — Attackers mutate their techniques (polymorphic malware, zero-days). 2. **Class Imbalance** — Legitimate traffic vastly outnumbers attacks (often by ratios of 10,000:1). 3. **Feature Ambiguity** — Some benign traffic patterns mimic attacks (e.g., penetration testing, vulnerability scans). These forces create the **Detection Trade-off Triangle** discussed in Section 4. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of All Metrics The **Confusion Matrix** is the cornerstone of every accuracy assessment. For a binary IDS classifier: | | **Actual: Attack (+)** | **Actual: Normal (−)** | |--------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Predicted: Attack (+)** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) — *False Alarm* | | **Predicted: Normal (−)** | False Negative (FN) — *Miss* | True Negative (TN) | > [!NOTE] > **Row** = what the IDS **predicted** (decision). **Column** = what was actually **ground truth**. Some textbooks flip this — always confirm the orientation in the question paper. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Metric ### 2.2.1 Detection Rate (DR) / True Positive Rate (TPR) / Recall / Sensitivity - **What it measures:** Of all *actual* attacks, how many did the IDS catch? - **Logic Step 1:** Identify the universe of real attacks → $(TP + FN)$. - **Logic Step 2:** Count how many were successfully flagged → $TP$. - **Logic Step 3:** Compute the ratio. - **Engineering Use:** Used as the **primary benchmark** for an IDS in security operations. ### 2.2.2 False Positive Rate (FPR) / False Alarm Rate - **What it measures:** Of all *benign* events, how many did the IDS wrongly flag? - **Logic Step 1:** Identify the universe of legitimate events → $(FP + TN)$. - **Logic Step 2:** Count incorrect flags → $FP$. - **Logic Step 3:** Compute the ratio. - **Engineering Use:** Determines **operator alert fatigue**. ### 2.2.3 Precision (Positive Predictive Value) - **What it measures:** When the IDS *alarms*, how often is it *correct*? - **Engineering Use:** Critical for SOCs (Security Operation Centers) that cannot investigate every alert. ### 2.2.4 F-Measure (F1-Score) - **What it measures:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — a **single-number summary** of accuracy on imbalanced datasets. - **Why Harmonic Mean?** It punishes extreme imbalance between Precision and Recall. ### 2.2.5 Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) - **What it measures:** A correlation coefficient between observed and predicted classifications, ranging from **−1 (always wrong) to +1 (always right)**, with **0 = random guessing**. - **Engineering Use:** Considered the **most robust single metric for imbalanced IDPS datasets** by researchers (Chicco & Jurman, 2023). ### 2.2.6 Cohen's Kappa (κ) - **What it measures:** Agreement between the IDS and ground truth, *corrected for chance agreement*. - **Logic Step 1:** Compute observed accuracy $p_o = (TP+TN)/N$. - **Logic Step 2:** Compute expected accuracy $p_e$ from marginal probabilities. - **Logic Step 3:** Apply formula. - **Interpretation:** $\kappa > 0.8$ = almost perfect; $0.4 < \kappa \le 0.6$ = moderate. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every formula** the examiner can test under "Detection Accuracy." Memorize the **bolded** column — these appear in ≥ 80% of past papers. | **Metric** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Range** | **Engineering Meaning** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Detection Rate / Recall / Sensitivity** | **DR / TPR** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | [0, 1] | **Probability of catching an attack** | | **False Negative Rate / Miss Rate** | FNR / FMR | $\dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | [0, 1] | **Probability of missing an attack** (most critical) | | **False Positive Rate / False Alarm Rate** | **FPR / FAR** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | [0, 1] | **Probability of false alarm** | | **True Negative Rate / Specificity** | TNR / SPC | $\dfrac{TN}{FP + TN}$ | [0, 1] | **Probability of correctly ignoring benign traffic** | | **Precision / Positive Predictive Value** | **PPV** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | [0, 1] | **Reliability of an alarm** | | **Negative Predictive Value** | NPV | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FN}$ | [0, 1] | Reliability of "no alarm" verdict | | **Accuracy** | **ACC** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | [0, 1] | Overall correctness | | **Error Rate** | ERR | $\dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | [0, 1] | Overall incorrectness | | **F1-Score / F-Measure** | **F1** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR}$ | [0, 1] | **Balanced single score** | | **Matthews Correlation Coefficient** | **MCC** | $\dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | [−1, +1] | **Best for imbalanced data** | | **Cohen's Kappa** | **κ** | $\dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | [−1, +1] | **Chance-corrected agreement** | | **Youden's J Statistic** | J | $TPR + TNR - 1$ | [0, 1] | **Optimal ROC threshold finder** | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Pitfall for KTU 2024 Answer Sheets:** Always write $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ — never use the literal character `|` (pipe) for absolute value, as it conflicts with the HTML rendering of the examination portal's answer-book templates. --- ## 2.4 ROC Curve and AUC — The Visual Standard > [!NOTE] > **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic)** curve plots **TPR (y-axis)** against **FPR (x-axis)** as the detection threshold is varied from 0 to 1. - **AUC (Area Under Curve)** summarizes the entire ROC into a single number. - AUC = 1.0 → Perfect classifier - AUC = 0.5 → Random guessing (the diagonal) - AUC < 0.5 → Worse than random (inverted classifier) **Engineering Utility:** ROC curves are *threshold-invariant* — they let security architects choose an operating point (e.g., high-security facilities demand low FPR tolerance, while high-throughput networks may tolerate higher FPR). **Base Rate Fallacy Caveat:** When attacks are rare (e.g., 1 in 10,000), even an IDS with 99% accuracy will produce **mostly false alarms** in absolute terms. ROC alone can be misleading — always pair it with **Precision-Recall (PR) curves** for imbalanced IDPS datasets. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation) ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement A network IDS processes **10,000** events in an evaluation window. The ground truth and the IDS verdicts are as follows: | | Actual Attack | Actual Normal | **Row Total (Predicted)** | |---|---|---|---| | **Predicted Attack** | 180 | 120 | 300 | | **Predicted Normal** | 20 | 9,680 | 9,700 | | **Column Total (Actual)** | 200 | 9,800 | 10,000 | **Compute: (a) Detection Rate, (b) False Positive Rate, (c) Accuracy, (d) Precision, (e) F1-Score, (f) Matthews Correlation Coefficient, (g) Cohen's Kappa.** --- ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Extraction of Confusion Matrix Values From the contingency table: $$ TP = 180, \quad FP = 120, \quad FN = 20, \quad TN = 9{,}680, \quad N = TP + TN + FP + FN = 10{,}000 $$ **Sanity check:** $$ N = 180 + 9{,}680 + 120 + 20 = 10{,}000 \quad \checkmark $$ --- ### 3.1.3 (a) Detection Rate (TPR / Recall / Sensitivity) $$ TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.9000 $$ **Interpretation:** The IDS catches **90%** of all real attacks. --- ### 3.1.4 (b) False Positive Rate (FPR / False Alarm Rate) $$ FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{120}{120 + 9{,}680} = \frac{120}{9{,}800} \approx 0.01224 $$ **Interpretation:** About **1.22%** of all legitimate traffic is incorrectly flagged. --- ### 3.1.5 (c) Accuracy $$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{180 + 9{,}680}{10{,}000} = \frac{9{,}860}{10{,}000} = 0.9860 $$ **Interpretation:** **98.60%** overall correctness — but **misleadingly high** because of class imbalance (only 200 attacks out of 10,000). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Trap:** A high accuracy score on imbalanced data is the *most common* trick in KTU 2024 numerical questions. Always show that you computed **Precision, Recall, F1, and MCC** alongside Accuracy. --- ### 3.1.6 (d) Precision (PPV) $$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 120} = \frac{180}{300} = 0.6000 $$ **Interpretation:** When the IDS alarms, it is correct only **60%** of the time. --- ### 3.1.7 (e) F1-Score $$ F1 = \frac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} = \frac{2 \times 0.6000 \times 0.9000}{0.6000 + 0.9000} = \frac{1.0800}{1.5000} = 0.7200 $$ **Verification via the alternative form:** $$ F1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 180}{2 \times 180 + 120 + 20} = \frac{360}{500} = 0.7200 \quad \checkmark $$ --- ### 3.1.8 (f) Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) $$ MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}} $$ **Numerator:** $$ TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN = (180)(9{,}680) - (120)(20) = 1{,}742{,}400 - 2{,}400 = 1{,}740{,}000 $$ **Denominator terms:** $$ TP + FP = 180 + 120 = 300 $$ $$ TP + FN = 180 + 20 = 200 $$ $$ TN + FP = 9{,}680 + 120 = 9{,}800 $$ $$ TN + FN = 9{,}680 + 20 = 9{,}700 $$ **Product of denominator terms:** $$ (300)(200)(9{,}800)(9{,}700) = 60{,}000 \times 95{,}060{,}000 = 5.7036 \times 10^{12} $$ **Square root:** $$ \sqrt{5.7036 \times 10^{12}} = 2.38781 \times 10^{6} $$ **Final MCC:** $$ MCC = \frac{1{,}740{,}000}{2.38781 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.7287 $$ **Interpretation:** Strong positive correlation (≈ 0.73) between predicted and actual classes — robustly penalizing the high FPR that "Accuracy" hid. --- ### 3.1.9 (g) Cohen's Kappa (κ) **Step 1: Observed agreement $p_o$** $$ p_o = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{9{,}860}{10{,}000} = 0.9860 $$ **Step 2: Marginal probabilities for expected agreement $p_e$** Predicted "Attack" total = $TP + FP = 300$ → $P(\text{pred}=+) = 0.03$ Predicted "Normal" total = $TN + FN = 9{,}700$ → $P(\text{pred}=-) = 0.97$ Actual "Attack" total = $TP + FN = 200$ → $P(\text{actual}=+) = 0.02$ Actual "Normal" total = $TN + FP = 9{,}800$ → $P(\text{actual}=-) = 0.98$ $$ p_e = P(\text{pred}=+) \cdot P(\text{actual}=+) + P(\text{pred}=-) \cdot P(\text{actual}=-) $$ $$ p_e = (0.03)(0.02) + (0.97)(0.98) = 0.0006 + 0.9506 = 0.9512 $$ **Step 3: Kappa** $$ \kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} = \frac{0.9860 - 0.9512}{1 - 0.9512} = \frac{0.0348}{0.0488} \approx 0.7131 $$ **Interpretation:** $\kappa \approx 0.71$ → **Substantial agreement** (Landis & Koch scale). Lower than naive accuracy because the model barely outperforms chance agreement on the dominant "Normal" class. --- ## 3.2 The Cost Matrix — Asymmetric Loss Derivation Because security has asymmetric costs, the **Expected Cost** of a detector is: $$ \mathbb{E}[\text{Cost}] = C_{FP} \cdot FP + C_{FN} \cdot FN $$ Where $C_{FP}$ and $C_{FN}$ are the per-event costs. The **optimal threshold** $t^*$ is the value minimizing this: $$ t^{*} = \arg\min_{t} \big( C_{FP} \cdot FP(t) + C_{FN} \cdot FN(t) \big) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Example:** If $C_{FN} = \$10{,}000$ (data breach) and $C_{FP} = \$5$ (analyst time), then $C_{FN}/C_{FP} = 2000$. A 0.1% increase in FNR is acceptable only if it reduces FPR by **200%** — a stringent trade-off that explains why high-stakes SOCs (e.g., financial, defense) **bias toward over-alerting**. --- ## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix — Inter-Metric Relationships | **If you observe...** | **It usually means...** | **Actionable Insight** | |---|---|---| | High Accuracy, Low MCC | Class imbalance hides errors | Switch to MCC, F1, or PR-Curve | | High TPR, High FPR | Detector is over-eager | Tighten threshold; add whitelist rules | | High TPR, Low FPR | Excellent detector | Approaching ROC optimal point | | Low TPR, High TNR | Detector is too conservative | Loosen threshold; add more signatures | | High Precision, Low Recall | Conservative model | Misses novel attacks (low coverage) | | High Recall, Low Precision | Aggressive model | Alert fatigue risk | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: IDPS Detection Accuracy Pipeline The following Mermaid block diagrams the **end-to-end flow** of how an IDPS computes and reports detection accuracy, mapping every stage from raw traffic capture to evaluation metric output. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Raw Network Traffic
Packets and Flows] --> B1[Preprocessing
Normalization and Parsing] A2[Host Logs
Syscalls and Audit Trails] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Feature Extraction
Payload and Behavioral Vectors] C1 --> D1[Detection Engine
Signature Anomaly or Hybrid] D1 --> E1[Decision Output
Alert or Pass Verdict] E1 --> F1[Ground Truth Labelling
Analyst or Dataset] F1 --> G1[Confusion Matrix
TP FP FN TN] G1 --> H1[Metric Computation
TPR FPR PPV F1 MCC Kappa] H1 --> I1[ROC and PR Curves
Threshold Sweep] H1 --> J1[Operational Dashboard
SOC Reporting] I1 --> K1[Threshold Optimisation
Cost Weighted] K1 --> D1 style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style A2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B1 fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 style C1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style D1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806600 style E1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 style F1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13 style G1 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#274e13 style H1 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#0b1d8a style I1 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#0b1d8a style J1 fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#4c1130 style K1 fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#4c1130 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **closed loop** between `K1` (Threshold Optimisation) and `D1` (Detection Engine) represents **feedback-based tuning**, where evaluation metrics feed back to recalibrate the detection engine. - The **metric stage** (`H1`, `I1`) is the focal point of "Detection Accuracy" as a concept. --- ## 4.2 The Detection Trade-off Triangle ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[High Detection Rate
Recall up to 1.0] --- N2[High Precision
Few False Alarms] N2 --- N3[High Throughput
Low Latency] N3 --- N1 N1 -. . Compromises .-> N2 N2 -. . Compromises .-> N3 N3 -. . Compromises .-> N1 style N1 fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#7f2704 style N2 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#274e13 style N3 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#0b1d8a ``` **Engineering insight:** No production IDPS sits on all three vertices simultaneously. Snort-class signature engines favor Precision + Throughput; ML-based anomaly engines favor Detection Rate + Throughput; behavioral analytics favor Detection Rate + Precision at the cost of Throughput. --- ## 4.3 ROC Curve Conceptual Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR O[Origin 0 comma 0] --- X[1 comma 0
All False Alarms] Y[0 comma 1
All Detected] --- X Y --- O D[Diagonal
Random Guess AUC 0.5] -.-> O C[Concave Curve
Good Classifier AUC greater than 0.8] -.-> Y style O fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style X fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style Y fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style D fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 style C fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13 ``` **Reading the diagram:** A perfect classifier passes through the top-left corner $(0, 1)$ — zero false alarms and 100% detection. Real classifiers bow toward this corner to varying degrees, quantified by **AUC**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** ### Q1. Define Detection Accuracy. Why is it considered insufficient as a standalone metric for evaluating an Intrusion Detection System in modern enterprise environments? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Detection Accuracy is defined as the ratio of correctly classified events (both attacks and normal) to the total number of events observed by the IDS: $$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} $$ **Why Insufficient (1.5 marks):** In real networks, attack traffic constitutes less than 0.1% of total events. Under such extreme **class imbalance**, a trivial classifier that labels *everything* as normal can achieve >99.9% accuracy while detecting *zero* attacks. Therefore, accuracy hides the asymmetric cost of False Negatives (missed attacks) and inflates the importance of True Negatives. Robust evaluation requires complementary metrics: **Detection Rate (TPR)**, **False Positive Rate (FPR)**, **F1-Score**, **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)**, and **ROC-AUC**. --- > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** ### Q2. Differentiate between False Positives and False Negatives in the context of an IDPS. Which is more critical to minimize in a high-security environment and why? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | False Positive (Type I Error) | False Negative (Type II Error) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Benign traffic flagged as malicious | Malicious traffic classified as benign | | **Impact** | Alert fatigue, wasted analyst hours, blocked legitimate services | Successful attack, data exfiltration, system compromise | | **Severity in High-Security** | Tolerable to a degree | **Absolutely unacceptable** | **Conclusion (1 mark):** In a high-security environment, **False Negatives are more critical to minimize** because they represent undetected breaches that lead directly to financial loss, regulatory penalties, and reputational damage. The cost asymmetry $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$ justifies accepting a higher FPR in exchange for a lower FNR. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3 Choice]** --- ### ❖ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **Q.A (a)** With a neat labelled diagram of the Confusion Matrix, derive the formulas for **Detection Rate (TPR)**, **False Positive Rate (FPR)**, **Precision**, and **Accuracy**. Explain why each metric is necessary for a comprehensive evaluation of an IDPS. **(7 marks)** **Q.A (b)** An IDS was tested on **5,000** events. The Confusion Matrix obtained was: | | Actual Attack | Actual Normal | |---|---|---| | **Predicted Attack** | 95 | 250 | | **Predicted Normal** | 5 | 4,650 | Calculate: **(i)** Detection Rate, **(ii)** False Positive Rate, **(iii)** Precision, **(iv)** F1-Score, **(v)** Matthews Correlation Coefficient, and **(vi)** Cohen's Kappa. Comment on the reliability of the system for deployment in a banking SOC. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (a) **Diagram (2 marks):** | | **Actual: Attack (+)** | **Actual: Normal (−)** | |--------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Predicted: Attack (+)** | TP | FP | | **Predicted: Normal (−)** | FN | TN | **Derivation and Necessity (5 marks):** 1. **Detection Rate (TPR / Sensitivity / Recall):** $$ TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$ *Necessity:* Answers "Out of all real attacks, how many did we catch?" — the **primary** IDS performance indicator. 2. **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $$ FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} $$ *Necessity:* Quantifies alert fatigue; critical for SOC operational cost. 3. **Precision (PPV):** $$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$ *Necessity:* Answers "When we alert, how often are we right?" — essential when analyst bandwidth is limited. 4. **Accuracy:** $$ ACC = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} $$ *Necessity:* Provides a high-level summary metric but **must not be used alone** on imbalanced datasets. **Concluding Statement:** No single metric suffices. A multi-metric evaluation (TPR + FPR + Precision + F1 + MCC + ROC-AUC) is the industry standard. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b) **Extracted values:** $$ TP = 95, \quad FP = 250, \quad FN = 5, \quad TN = 4{,}650, \quad N = 5{,}000 $$ **Sanity check:** $95 + 250 + 5 + 4{,}650 = 5{,}000$ ✓ **(i) Detection Rate:** $$ TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{95}{95 + 5} = \frac{95}{100} = 0.9500 \;\; (95\%) $$ **(ii) False Positive Rate:** $$ FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{250}{250 + 4{,}650} = \frac{250}{4{,}900} \approx 0.05102 \;\; (5.10\%) $$ **(iii) Precision:** $$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{95}{95 + 250} = \frac{95}{345} \approx 0.27536 \;\; (27.54\%) $$ **(iv) F1-Score:** $$ F1 = \frac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} = \frac{2 \times 0.27536 \times 0.9500}{0.27536 + 0.9500} = \frac{0.52318}{1.22536} \approx 0.42696 $$ **(v) Matthews Correlation Coefficient:** $$ MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}} $$ Numerator: $$ (95)(4{,}650) - (250)(5) = 441{,}750 - 1{,}250 = 440{,}500 $$ Denominator components: $$ TP+FP = 345, \;\; TP+FN = 100, \;\; TN+FP = 4{,}900, \;\; TN+FN = 4{,}655 $$ Product: $$ 345 \times 100 \times 4{,}900 \times 4{,}655 = 34{,}500 \times 22{,}809{,}500 = 7.8693 \times 10^{11} $$ Square root: $$ \sqrt{7.8693 \times 10^{11}} \approx 887{,}094 $$ Final: $$ MCC = \frac{440{,}500}{887{,}094} \approx 0.4965 $$ **(vi) Cohen's Kappa:** Observed agreement: $$ p_o = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{4{,}745}{5{,}000} = 0.9490 $$ Marginals: $$ P(\text{pred}=+) = \frac{345}{5{,}000} = 0.0690, \quad P(\text{pred}=- ) = 0.9310 $$ $$ P(\text{actual}=+) = \frac{100}{5{,}000} = 0.0200, \quad P(\text{actual}=- ) = 0.9800 $$ Expected agreement: $$ p_e = (0.0690)(0.0200) + (0.9310)(0.9800) = 0.00138 + 0.91238 = 0.91376 $$ Kappa: $$ \kappa = \frac{0.9490 - 0.91376}{1 - 0.91376} = \frac{0.03524}{0.08624} \approx 0.4087 $$ **Comment (1 mark):** The IDS shows **strong detection (95% TPR)** but suffers from **alarming false-alarm rate (5.1% FPR)** and very **low precision (27.5%)**. F1 ≈ 0.43 and MCC ≈ 0.50 indicate **moderate** classifier quality. For a **banking SOC** where analyst bandwidth is limited and alert fatigue has direct financial consequences, this system is **not deployment-ready without significant threshold tuning** to suppress benign FPs (e.g., whitelisting, contextual filtering). --- ### ❖ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **Q.B (a)** Explain the **ROC Curve** and the **AUC** metric in detail. How does the **Base Rate Fallacy** affect the interpretation of ROC curves for IDPS deployed in low-attack-volume networks? **(7 marks)** **Q.B (b)** A signature-based IDS is deployed across an enterprise. Over 30 days, the following statistics are recorded: - Total events processed: **1,000,000** - Confirmed attacks (ground truth): **1,000** - IDS alerts raised: **800** - Of the 800 alerts, **750** were confirmed true attacks; the remaining **50** were false alarms. - Additionally, **250** real attacks were missed entirely (not flagged). Construct the **Confusion Matrix** and compute **TPR, FPR, Precision, Accuracy, and the F1-Score**. Recommend whether the IDS should be retained. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (a) **ROC Curve (3 marks):** The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis, as the detection threshold is varied from 0 to 1. Each point on the curve represents a (FPR, TPR) pair at a specific threshold. A **perfect** classifier passes through the top-left corner (0, 1). A **random** classifier lies on the diagonal $y = x$. **AUC Metric (2 marks):** **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** collapses the ROC into a single scalar. AUC = 1.0 means perfect separation; AUC = 0.5 means random guessing. It is **threshold-invariant** and **scale-invariant**, making it useful for comparing classifiers across operating conditions. **Base Rate Fallacy (2 marks):** The **Base Rate Fallacy** occurs when the prior probability (base rate) of an event is ignored. In low-attack-volume networks (e.g., 1 attack per 100,000 events), even an IDS with 99% TPR and 1% FPR will produce **far more false alarms than true alerts** in absolute terms. ROC curves are *insensitive* to base rate (they are computed from class-conditional probabilities), so they can mask this issue. For low-base-rate IDPS deployment, the **Precision-Recall (PR) curve** is a more informative complement because it directly reflects absolute alert counts. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (b) **Step 1: Confusion Matrix Construction** From the problem: - Confirmed attacks = 1,000 → TP + FN = 1,000 - IDS alerts = 800 → TP + FP = 800 - True attacks among alerts = 750 → TP = 750 - False alarms = 50 → FP = 50 - Missed attacks = 250 → FN = 250 - True Negatives = $N - (TP + FP + FN) = 1{,}000{,}000 - (750 + 50 + 250) = 998{,}950$ | | Actual Attack | Actual Normal | Total Predicted | |---|---|---|---| | Predicted Attack | 750 | 50 | 800 | | Predicted Normal | 250 | 998,950 | 999,200 | | Total Actual | 1,000 | 999,000 | 1,000,000 | **Step 2: Metric Computation** **TPR:** $$ TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{750}{1{,}000} = 0.7500 \;\; (75\%) $$ **FPR:** $$ FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{50}{50 + 998{,}950} = \frac{50}{999{,}000} \approx 5.005 \times 10^{-5} \;\; (0.005\%) $$ **Precision:** $$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{750}{800} = 0.9375 \;\; (93.75\%) $$ **Accuracy:** $$ ACC = \frac{750 + 998{,}950}{1{,}000{,}000} = \frac{999{,}700}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.9997 \;\; (99.97\%) $$ **F1-Score:** $$ F1 = \frac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} = \frac{2 \times 0.9375 \times 0.7500}{0.9375 + 0.7500} = \frac{1.40625}{1.6875} \approx 0.8333 $$ **Step 3: Recommendation (1 mark)** The IDS demonstrates **excellent precision (93.75%)** and **low FPR (0.005%)**, meaning its alerts are highly trustworthy and analyst fatigue is minimal. However, its **detection rate is only 75%** — a quarter of all real attacks slip through. For an enterprise, this is a **significant gap**. **Recommendation:** Retain the IDS **but supplement it with an anomaly-based or hybrid second layer** to catch the 25% missed attacks. Signature-based detection alone is insufficient against novel and zero-day threats. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusion Matrix Orientation:** Examiners deduct 1 full mark if the student swaps rows and columns without explicit declaration. Always state *"Rows represent the IDS prediction; columns represent ground truth"* at the start. > 2. **Skipping MCC's Denominator:** MCC is the *most-skipped* formula. Examiners award 2 marks for the **complete denominator expansion** $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)$, not just the formula. > 3. **Ignoring Class Imbalance Discussion:** Numerical problems on imbalanced datasets **always** carry a 1-mark follow-up asking "Is accuracy a reliable metric here?" Students who compute Accuracy without this comment **lose a mark**. > 4. **Confusing FNR with FPR:** A common slip is writing the FPR formula where FNR is required. Double-check whether the denominator uses the *Normal* column (FPR) or the *Attack* column (FNR). > 5. **Kappa's Expected Agreement:** Many students compute $p_o$ correctly but fail to derive $p_e$ from **both marginal probabilities**. Examiners specifically look for the product-sum form. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Detection Accuracy (Module 3)** - **Core Definition:** Detection Accuracy = $(TP + TN)/N$ — but **never use it alone** on imbalanced data. - **Four Cardinal Cells:** Always extract $TP, FP, FN, TN$ from the confusion matrix **before** writing any formula. - **TPR (Recall):** $TP / (TP + FN)$ — probability of catching an attack. **Primary IDS metric.** - **FPR (False Alarm):** $FP / (FP + TN)$ — drives alert fatigue. Must be kept **< 1%** in production. - **Precision (PPV):** $TP / (TP + FP)$ — reliability of an alarm. - **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — best single-number summary. - **MCC:** Ranges from −1 to +1 — most robust on imbalanced data. **Always show the full denominator.** - **Cohen's Kappa:** Chance-corrected agreement; $\kappa > 0.8$ is excellent. - **ROC Curve:** TPR vs FPR across thresholds. **Threshold-invariant** but **base-rate-blind**. - **PR Curve:** Better than ROC for low-base-rate IDPS deployments. - **AUC = 1:** Perfect; **AUC = 0.5:** Random; **AUC < 0.5:** Worse than random. - **Asymmetric Cost Principle:** $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$ in security — design favors minimizing missed attacks even at the cost of false alarms. - **Base Rate Fallacy:** Real networks have <0.1% attacks; a 99% accurate classifier can still miss every attack if it defaults to "Normal." - **Diagram Must-Knows:** Confusion Matrix, ROC Curve, Detection Trade-off Triangle, Feedback-Loop Architecture. - **Examiner Traps:** Confusing FPR/FNR, skipping MCC denominator, using Accuracy alone, ignoring class imbalance commentary.

topics.title: Tuning and Customization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tuning and Customization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition — IDPS Tuning and Customization**
> **Tuning and Customization** in an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) refers to the systematic process of **calibrating detection engines, refining rule-sets, optimizing alert thresholds, and tailoring signature/policy databases** to maximize detection accuracy (True Positives) while minimizing false alarms (False Positives and False Negatives) for a specific deployment environment.

It is the **operational art of making a generic IDPS behave optimally** in a unique enterprise network context — adjusting sensitivity, suppressing noise, writing custom signatures, and aligning the system with organizational security policy and risk appetite.

## 1.2 Key Sub-Concepts (High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | KTU-Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **False Positive (FP)** | Benign traffic incorrectly flagged as malicious. |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Actual attack traffic that escapes detection. |
| **True Positive (TP)** | Correctly identified malicious activity. |
| **True Negative (TN)** | Correctly passed legitimate traffic. |
| **Baseline** | A statistical profile of "normal" network behavior. |
| **Whitelist** | A set of trusted entities excluded from alerting. |
| **Blacklist** | A set of known-bad entities that must always alert. |
| **Signature** | A pattern (string, regex, hash) that identifies an attack. |
| **Threshold** | A numerical cutoff that triggers an action or alert. |
| **Sensitivity** | The aggression level of the detection engine. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Security Guard

> [!NOTE] **Analogy: The Over-Eager Security Guard**
> Imagine a security guard at the entrance of a software company. On day 1, he has no instructions, so he **stops every single person** (even the CEO carrying coffee) to check IDs — generating hundreds of "false alarms" daily. **Tuning** is the process of giving him better rules:
> 1. **Whitelist the employees** (so they walk in freely).
> 2. **Show him photos of known thieves** (signatures).
> 3. **Set a red-flag rule**: anyone carrying a crowbar after 6 PM — investigate.
> 4. **Adjust sensitivity**: don't frisk the mailman, but always frisk hooded strangers.
>
> Over weeks, the guard stops almost no innocent people (**low FP**), catches every thief (**high TP**), and only occasionally misses a clever thief in a uniform (**low FN**). That perfectly balanced guard is a **tuned IDPS**.

## 1.4 Why Tuning and Customization is *Non-Negotiable*

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board-Examiner Highlight**
> An **untuned IDPS is worse than no IDPS at all** — it drowns the SOC (Security Operations Center) in alert fatigue, causing analysts to ignore real attacks ("the boy who cried wolf" problem). Tuning transforms raw log noise into **actionable intelligence**.

The three operational pain-points addressed by tuning are summarized as the **"Three Tensions" of IDPS Deployment**:

1. **Detection vs. Performance** — More rules = more CPU/RAM consumption.
2. **Sensitivity vs. Alert Fatigue** — High sensitivity catches more attacks but floods the dashboard.
3. **Generic vs. Specific** — Out-of-the-box rules miss organization-specific threats; custom rules close the gap.

## 1.5 Visual Summary of the Tuning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deploy Default IDPS] --> B[Collect Baseline Traffic]
    B --> C[Measure Initial FP Rate]
    C --> D{FP Rate > Threshold?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Refine Signatures and Rules]
    D -- No --> F[Validate Detection Coverage]
    E --> G[Re-test in Staging]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Promote to Production]
    H --> I[Continuous Monitoring and Re-tuning]
    I --> B
```

This flow is foundational — it represents the **closed-loop feedback cycle** that every KTU question on tuning expects you to describe.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tuning Process — Five Formal Phases

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Anchor (PECCT742 Module 3)**
> The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes tuning into **five disciplined phases**. Memorize this sequence — it appears in nearly every 14-mark question.

### Phase 1 — **Baseline Establishment**

Before changing any rule, you must understand "normal."

- Capture **7–14 days** of representative traffic (the KTU-recommended window).
- Compute statistics: average packets/sec, top protocols, top talkers, peak hours.
- Tools: `Wireshark`, `ntopng`, `NetFlow` analyzers, IDPS built-in profilers.

### Phase 2 — **Initial Rule-Set Audit**

- Inventory every enabled signature, its default action (alert/drop/block), and severity.
- Disable rules that are **categorically irrelevant** to your environment (e.g., SCADA rules in a finance office).

### Phase 3 — **Threshold Calibration**

- For statistical/anomaly-based rules, the default threshold is usually too low.
- Apply the **"95th percentile" rule**: set the alert threshold at the 95th percentile of observed baseline values.
- For event-correlation rules, use **time-window aggregation** (e.g., 5 failed logins in 60 seconds).

### Phase 4 — **Whitelisting and Exception Engineering**

- Maintain a structured **exception list** for known-good applications.
- Each exception must carry: *reason, requester, approver, expiry date* — this is essential for audit trails.

### Phase 5 — **Custom Signature Authoring**

- Develop signatures for **organization-specific threats** (e.g., custom internal APIs, proprietary protocols).
- Test in a **staging environment** before production deployment.

## 2.2 The Four Tuning Levers (The "Tuning Knobs")

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 4 Levers of Customization**
> Every IDPS exposes four fundamental control surfaces. Examiner questions in KTU frequently use this exact classification.

1. **Sensitivity Adjustment** — A global multiplier (0–100) that scales the aggressiveness of all rules. Higher = more alerts.
2. **Threshold Tuning** — Numerical cutoffs (e.g., "alert if more than 50 SYN packets/sec from one IP").
3. **Action Modification** — Change rule disposition: *log → alert → drop → block → reset*.
4. **Scope Definition** — Limit a rule to specific subnets, VLANs, or applications.

## 2.3 Statistical Performance Metrics (The Examiner's Favorite)

> [!NOTE] **Mandatory for KTU 14-Mark Numerical Problems**
> Tuning effectiveness is **measured**, not assumed. The following metrics are non-negotiable knowledge.

### 2.3.1 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

A tuning evaluation begins with the **Confusion Matrix** — a 2×2 grid of detection outcomes:

| Cell | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **TP** | True Positive — attack correctly detected. |
| **TN** | True Negative — benign traffic correctly passed. |
| **FP** | False Positive — benign traffic wrongly flagged. |
| **FN** | False Negative — attack wrongly passed. |

### 2.3.2 Key Derived Metrics

Let the symbols be defined as:

- Let $TP$, $TN$, $FP$, $FN$ represent the cell counts from the confusion matrix.
- Let $P = TP + FN$ be the total number of actual attacks.
- Let $N = TN + FP$ be the total number of actual benign events.

The standard metrics are:

$$
\text{Detection Rate (Sensitivity / Recall)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Specificity (True Negative Rate)} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

$$
F_1 \text{ Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **MCC vs. F1 — Why KTU Loves MCC**
> The **F1 score** ignores True Negatives, making it misleading for highly imbalanced datasets (typical in IDPS where 99.99% of traffic is benign). The **MCC** uses all four cells and is the **gold-standard balanced metric** for tuning validation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Single Table)

> [!NOTE] **Quick-Reference for the Exam Hall**
> The following table consolidates all high-yield formulas. **Do NOT use the literal pipe `|` symbol inside the table;** use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid markdown breakage.

| Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value | Use-Case in Tuning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (TPR) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\to 1$ | Validates attack catch-rate. |
| False Positive Rate | $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\to 0$ | Validates alert noise. |
| Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\to 1$ | Trustworthiness of an alert. |
| Specificity (TNR) | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\to 1$ | Validates legitimate pass-through. |
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{P+N}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\to 1$ | Overall correctness. |
| F1 Score | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | $\to 1$ | Balance of precision and recall. |
| MCC | $\frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $[-1,1]$ | $\to 1$ | Most balanced metric for IDPS. |
| Cohen's Kappa | $\frac{2 \cdot (TP \cdot TN - FN \cdot FP)}{(TP+FP)(FP+TN)+(TP+FN)(FN+TN)}$ | $[-1,1]$ | $\to 1$ | Agreement beyond chance. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] **Why Industry Cares**
> - **SOC Analyst Productivity**: A well-tuned IDPS can reduce alert volume by **80–95%** (industry benchmark from Gartner).
> - **Compliance**: PCI-DSS 11.4 and ISO 27001 A.12.4 mandate **regular review and tuning** of monitoring systems.
> - **Performance**: Suppressing low-value rules reduces CPU load on the IDPS engine by 30–60%.
> - **Legal Forensics**: Properly documented whitelists/exceptions are admissible as evidence of due diligence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations and Numerical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — From Confusion Matrix to F1 Score

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Derive?**
> The KTU 2024 scheme's Bloom's-taxonomy level **"Apply / Analyze"** expects students to demonstrate the **logical flow** behind the formulas, not just state them. The following derivation is **exam-ready**.

### Step 1 — Start with the Confusion Matrix Counts

Let the total positive events (attacks) be:

$$
P = TP + FN
$$

Let the total negative events (benign) be:

$$
N = TN + FP
$$

The total population observed by the IDPS is:

$$
T = P + N = TP + TN + FP + FN
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The IDPS observes a total of $T$ events in the evaluation window. Of these, $P$ were actual attacks and $N$ were benign. The IDPS classified them into the four cells of the matrix.

### Step 2 — Define Precision

Precision answers: *"Of all the alerts the IDPS raised, how many were real attacks?"*

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The denominator $TP+FP$ is the total number of alerts raised (whether right or wrong). The numerator is the count of correct alerts.

### Step 3 — Define Recall (Detection Rate)

Recall answers: *"Of all real attacks, how many did the IDPS catch?"*

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The denominator $TP+FN$ is the total number of real attacks. The numerator is the number of attacks the IDPS correctly detected.

### Step 4 — Derive the F1 Score

The F1 score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. Why harmonic mean and not arithmetic? Because we want a metric that **punishes imbalance** — if either precision or recall is low, F1 must be low.

The harmonic mean of two numbers $a$ and $b$ is:

$$
H(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}
$$

Substituting $a = \text{Precision}$ and $b = \text{Recall}$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $P \cdot R$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{R + P}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This form is the standard F1 formula. It equals 1 only when both Precision and Recall equal 1 (perfect system). It equals 0 when either is 0 (useless system).

### Step 5 — Derive the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)

MCC is derived from the **Pearson correlation** between the observed and predicted classifications, then normalized.

The Pearson correlation between two binary variables is:

$$
\rho = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y}
$$

After substituting the four cell counts and simplifying (full derivation in advanced texts), the result is:

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The numerator is the **net correct classifications minus net incorrect ones**. The denominator normalizes by the geometric mean of all possible correct/incorrect products. A value of +1 means perfect prediction, 0 means random, and -1 means total disagreement.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Tuning a Snort Rule

> [!NOTE] **Exam-Style Walkthrough (Full Working)**
> **Problem:** An IDPS engineer deploys a signature-based rule in a corporate network. After 24 hours, the confusion matrix is:

| | **Predicted Attack** | **Predicted Benign** |
|---|---:|---:|
| **Actual Attack** | 90 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
| **Actual Benign** | 45 (FP) | 9855 (TN) |

**Task:** Calculate Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Precision, Specificity, Accuracy, F1 Score, and MCC. Comment on whether the rule needs further tuning.

### Step 1 — Extract the Counts

$$
TP = 90, \quad FN = 10, \quad FP = 45, \quad TN = 9855
$$

$$
P = TP + FN = 90 + 10 = 100
$$

$$
N = TN + FP = 9855 + 45 = 9900
$$

$$
T = P + N = 100 + 9900 = 10000
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The IDPS saw 10,000 events. Only 100 were actual attacks; 9,900 were benign.

### Step 2 — Compute Detection Rate (TPR / Recall)

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90
$$

**Interpretation:** The rule caught 90% of the attacks — good but not perfect.

### Step 3 — Compute False Positive Rate

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{45}{45 + 9855} = \frac{45}{9900} \approx 0.00455
$$

**Interpretation:** Less than 0.5% of benign traffic was falsely flagged — acceptable in most SOCs.

### Step 4 — Compute Precision

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 45} = \frac{90}{135} \approx 0.6667
$$

**Interpretation:** Only ~67% of the alerts were real attacks — **alert fatigue risk** is significant.

### Step 5 — Compute Specificity

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{9855}{9855 + 45} = \frac{9855}{9900} \approx 0.9955
$$

**Interpretation:** ~99.55% of benign traffic passed correctly — excellent.

### Step 6 — Compute Accuracy

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{T} = \frac{90 + 9855}{10000} = \frac{9945}{10000} = 0.9945
$$

**Interpretation:** 99.45% overall — looks great, **but accuracy is misleading here** because the dataset is highly imbalanced.

### Step 7 — Compute F1 Score

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.6667 \cdot 0.90}{0.6667 + 0.90}
$$

Calculate the numerator:

$$
2 \cdot 0.6667 \cdot 0.90 = 1.20006
$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$
0.6667 + 0.90 = 1.5667
$$

Divide:

$$
F_1 = \frac{1.20006}{1.5667} \approx 0.7659
$$

**Interpretation:** F1 ≈ 0.77 — moderate, room for improvement.

### Step 8 — Compute MCC

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

Calculate the numerator:

$$
TP \cdot TN = 90 \cdot 9855 = 886950
$$

$$
FP \cdot FN = 45 \cdot 10 = 450
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 886950 - 450 = 886500
$$

Calculate the four denominator terms:

$$
TP + FP = 90 + 45 = 135
$$

$$
TP + FN = 90 + 10 = 100
$$

$$
TN + FP = 9855 + 45 = 9900
$$

$$
TN + FN = 9855 + 10 = 9865
$$

Multiply them:

$$
135 \cdot 100 \cdot 9900 \cdot 9865
$$

Break into two products for clarity:

$$
135 \cdot 100 = 13500
$$

$$
9900 \cdot 9865 = 97663500
$$

Total product:

$$
13500 \cdot 97663500 = 1{,}318{,}457{,}250{,}000
$$

Take the square root:

$$
\sqrt{1{,}318{,}457{,}250{,}000} \approx 1{,}148{,}023.32
$$

Final MCC:

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{886500}{1148023.32} \approx 0.7722
$$

**Interpretation:** MCC ≈ 0.77 — a balanced measure confirming the rule is **moderately good but needs tuning** (precision is the weak link).

### Step 9 — Tuning Decision

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examiner's Logical Conclusion**
> The rule has **high recall (0.90) and high specificity (0.9955)** but **low precision (0.667)**. This means the IDPS is **over-alerting on benign traffic**. The recommended tuning action is:
> 1. **Tighten the signature pattern** to reduce false matches.
> 2. **Add whitelist exceptions** for known-benign sources generating FPs.
> 3. **Raise the threshold** of the rule (e.g., trigger only after multiple matches).
> 4. Re-test in staging to verify TP count remains high.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tuning Strategies Mapped to Problems

> [!NOTE] **This Matrix is a Direct KTU Module-3 Syllabus Mapping**

| Observed Problem | Root Cause | Tuning Strategy | Expected Metric Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High False Positive Rate** | Over-broad signature. | Tighten regex; add context filters. | FPR $\downarrow$, Precision $\uparrow$. |
| **High False Negative Rate** | Signature too narrow or missing. | Broaden pattern; add complementary rules. | TPR $\uparrow$, MCC $\uparrow$. |
| **Alert Fatigue** | Too many low-severity alerts. | Suppress info-level; aggregate. | Alert volume $\downarrow$ by 60–80%. |
| **CPU Saturation** | Too many enabled rules. | Disable environment-irrelevant rules. | CPU $\downarrow$, Latency $\downarrow$. |
| **Single FP source flooding** | Misbehaving internal app. | Add targeted whitelist with expiry. | FPR $\downarrow$, FP count $\downarrow$ 90%+. |
| **New zero-day attack** | No existing signature. | Write custom signature; deploy in drop mode after test. | TPR $\uparrow$ for that class. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 The Tuning Lifecycle (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Baseline
    Baseline --> Audit : Collect 7 to 14 days
    Audit --> Calibrate : Identify high FP rules
    Calibrate --> Stage : Adjust thresholds
    Stage --> Validate : Test in lab
    Validate --> Deploy : Acceptable metrics
    Validate --> Calibrate : Metrics fail SLA
    Deploy --> Monitor : Production rollout
    Monitor --> Baseline : Re-baseline quarterly
    Monitor --> [*] : Sunset
```

**Textual Explanation:** This state diagram represents the **closed-loop tuning lifecycle** mandated by the KTU 2024 scheme. Note that *Validate → Calibrate* is a feedback loop — tuning is **iterative**, not a one-shot task.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tuned IDPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Packet and Log Input Layer]
        N1[Network Tap]
        N2[SPAN Port]
        N3[Host Agent Logs]
    end

    subgraph PreProc[Pre-Processing Engine]
        P1[Protocol Decoder]
        P2[Stream Reassembler]
        P3[Normalizer]
    end

    subgraph DetectEng[Detection Engine - The Tunable Core]
        D1[Signature Database]
        D2[Anomaly Profiler]
        D3[Custom Rule Engine]
        D4[Threshold Controller]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Action and Alert Layer]
        O1[Logger]
        O2[Alert Queue]
        O3[Inline Block]
        O4[Syslog Forwarder]
    end

    subgraph ManagePlane[Management and Tuning Plane]
        M1[Console Dashboard]
        M2[Rule Editor]
        M3[Whitelist Manager]
        M4[Metric Reporter]
    end

    N1 --> P1
    N2 --> P1
    N3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> D1
    P3 --> D2
    P3 --> D3
    P3 --> D4
    D1 --> O1
    D2 --> O1
    D3 --> O1
    D4 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
    M1 --> D4
    M2 --> D1
    M2 --> D3
    M3 --> D4
    M4 --> M1
```

**Textual Explanation:** The **Management and Tuning Plane** is where the engineer operates. The arrows from M1, M2, M3 into the detection engine represent the **four tuning levers** (sensitivity, threshold, action, scope). Every tunable component has a feedback path to the dashboard, enabling metric-driven tuning.

## 4.3 Custom Signature Development Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Novel Threat] --> B[Capture Sample PCAP]
    B --> C[Extract Unique Pattern]
    C --> D[Draft Signature Syntax]
    D --> E[Test Against PCAP Library]
    E --> F{Detection Confirmed?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Measure FP Rate on Clean Traffic]
    G --> H{FP Acceptable?}
    H -- No --> I[Refine Pattern]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Document Signature Metadata]
    J --> K[Promote to Production with Action = Alert]
    K --> L[Monitor for 7 Days]
    L --> M{Stable?}
    M -- No --> I
    M -- Yes --> N[Escalate Action to Drop or Block]
```

**Textual Explanation:** The workflow enforces **fail-safe staging** — a signature is never deployed in block-mode on day one. It graduates from *alert* to *drop* only after **7 days of stable production behavior** with acceptable FP rates.

## 4.4 Feedback Loop — Continuous Tuning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Production Traffic] --> B[IDPS Engine]
    B --> C[Alerts and Logs]
    C --> D[SOC Analyst Triage]
    D --> E{True Alert?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Incident Response]
    E -- No --> G[FP Identified]
    G --> H[Tuning Rule Database]
    H --> B
    F --> I[Post-Incident Review]
    I --> H
```

**Textual Explanation:** SOC analyst triage is the **human-in-the-loop** that drives tuning. Every false alert feeds back into rule refinement, creating a **continuously self-improving detection system**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **KTU Pattern:** 2-mark conceptual + 1-mark application. Direct, definition-style.

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]
**Define IDPS tuning. List any four benefits of tuning an IDPS deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** IDPS tuning is the **systematic process of calibrating detection rules, adjusting alert thresholds, and customizing signatures** to maximize detection accuracy and minimize false positives/negatives in a specific network environment.

**Four Benefits (1.5 marks — any four, 0.375 each):**
1. **Reduces false positive alerts** (alert fatigue mitigation).
2. **Improves true positive detection rate** (catches more real attacks).
3. **Optimizes system performance** (lower CPU/RAM usage).
4. **Ensures compliance** with standards like PCI-DSS and ISO 27001.
5. **Aligns detection with organizational risk profile.**
6. **Provides audit trail** for security due-diligence.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**What is a whitelist in the context of IDPS? Why is exception engineering important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Whitelist Definition (1.5 marks):** A **whitelist** in IDPS is a configured list of trusted IP addresses, users, applications, or protocols that are **explicitly excluded** from triggering alerts or blocking actions, even if they match a detection rule.

**Importance of Exception Engineering (1.5 marks):**
- It **suppresses known-good alerts** that would otherwise flood the SOC.
- It is a **formal, auditable process** — every exception must be documented with *reason, requester, approver, and expiry date*.
- It enables **fine-grained policy enforcement** without disabling entire rules.
- It reduces alert fatigue and improves analyst focus on real threats.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> [!NOTE] **KTU Pattern:** ESE Module 3 question, internal choice between Q-A and Q-B. Each sub-part is 7 marks. **Full model solutions provided.**

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

> **(a)** Explain the **five phases of the IDPS tuning lifecycle** with a neat diagram. Why is baselining considered the most critical phase? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following confusion matrix was obtained after deploying a tuned IDPS rule for 30 days. Compute **Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Precision, Accuracy, F1 Score, and MCC**. Comment on the tuning effectiveness. **(7 marks)**
>
> | | Predicted Attack | Predicted Benign |
> |---|---:|---:|
> | **Actual Attack** | 480 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
> | **Actual Benign** | 100 (FP) | 9400 (TN) |

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-A (a) [7 marks]

**The Five Phases of IDPS Tuning Lifecycle (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Baseline Establishment (1 mark):** Capture 7–14 days of representative traffic to construct a statistical profile of normal behavior — protocols used, peak hours, top talkers, average packet rates.

2. **Initial Rule-Set Audit (1 mark):** Inventory all enabled signatures. Disable rules irrelevant to the deployment (e.g., SCADA rules in an IT environment).

3. **Threshold Calibration (1 mark):** Adjust numerical cutoffs. Use the 95th-percentile rule. Aggregate correlated events within time windows to prevent alert storms.

4. **Whitelisting and Exception Engineering (1 mark):** Create a documented exception process for known-good traffic. Each exception must carry metadata: reason, approver, expiry.

5. **Custom Signature Authoring (1 mark):** Develop signatures for organization-specific threats. Test in staging before production deployment.

**Why Baselining is Most Critical (2 marks):**
- Without an accurate baseline, **all threshold settings are guess-work** — leading to either too many FPs (threshold too low) or missed attacks (threshold too high).
- The baseline provides the **statistical ground truth** that every subsequent tuning decision depends on.
- An inaccurate baseline produces an IDPS that is **permanently miscalibrated** — the root cause of most "noisy" deployments.

**Diagram (already in Section 4.1 — reference it).** *(Examiner accepts any valid lifecycle diagram.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-A (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Extract the Counts (0.5 mark):**

$$
TP = 480, \quad FN = 20, \quad FP = 100, \quad TN = 9400
$$

$$
P = 500, \quad N = 9500, \quad T = 10000
$$

**Step 2 — Detection Rate (1 mark):**

$$
\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{480}{500} = 0.96
$$

**Step 3 — False Positive Rate (1 mark):**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{100}{9500} \approx 0.0105
$$

**Step 4 — Precision (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{480}{580} \approx 0.8276
$$

**Step 5 — Accuracy (0.5 mark):**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{480 + 9400}{10000} = \frac{9880}{10000} = 0.988
$$

**Step 6 — F1 Score (1.5 marks):**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8276 \cdot 0.96}{0.8276 + 0.96} = \frac{1.5890}{1.7876} \approx 0.8888
$$

**Step 7 — MCC (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{(480)(9400) - (100)(20)}{\sqrt{(480+100)(480+20)(9400+100)(9400+20)}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
480 \cdot 9400 = 4{,}512{,}000
$$

$$
100 \cdot 20 = 2000
$$

$$
\text{Num} = 4{,}512{,}000 - 2000 = 4{,}510{,}000
$$

Denominator terms:

$$
580 \cdot 500 \cdot 9500 \cdot 9420
$$

Break into pairs:

$$
580 \cdot 500 = 290000
$$

$$
9500 \cdot 9420 = 89{,}490{,}000
$$

Product:

$$
290000 \cdot 89490000 = 2.59521 \times 10^{13}
$$

Square root:

$$
\sqrt{2.59521 \times 10^{13}} \approx 5{,}094{,}320
$$

Final MCC:

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{4{,}510{,}000}{5{,}094{,}320} \approx 0.8854
$$

**Comment on Tuning Effectiveness (0.5 mark):**
> The rule exhibits **excellent performance**: 96% detection rate, only ~1% false positive rate, F1 ≈ 0.89, and MCC ≈ 0.89. The rule is **well-tuned** and may be considered for escalation from *alert* to *drop* action after a 7-day stability period.

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

> **(a)** Discuss the **four tuning levers** of an IDPS in detail. How does each lever affect the trade-off between detection rate and false positive rate? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **custom signature development**? Describe the **end-to-end workflow** of authoring, testing, and deploying a custom signature in production, with emphasis on the **fail-safe staging principle**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-B (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):** Every IDPS exposes four fundamental control surfaces, collectively called the **tuning levers**. Each lever directly influences the **detection–vs–false-positive trade-off**.

**The Four Levers (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Sensitivity Adjustment (1 mark):** A global multiplier (0–100) applied across all rules.
   - **High sensitivity** → more alerts, higher TP rate **but** higher FP rate.
   - **Low sensitivity** → fewer alerts, lower FP rate **but** risk of missed attacks (higher FN).
   - **Trade-off:** A dial that uniformly raises or lowers the entire detection curve.

2. **Threshold Tuning (1 mark):** Numerical cutoffs for statistical and event-count rules.
   - Example: "alert if more than 50 SYN packets/sec from one IP."
   - **High threshold** → fewer FPs but possibly miss low-volume attacks.
   - **Low threshold** → catch slow attacks but generate FPs.
   - **Trade-off:** A fine-grained per-rule control that allows the engineer to tune each rule independently.

3. **Action Modification (1 mark):** Changes rule disposition: *log → alert → drop → block → reset*.
   - **Trade-off:** A misconfigured block action can cause **self-inflicted DoS** on legitimate services — therefore, fail-safe principle dictates starting at *alert* and escalating.

4. **Scope Definition (1 mark):** Limits a rule to specific subnets, VLANs, ports, or applications.
   - **Trade-off:** Narrow scope reduces FPs (by ignoring irrelevant traffic) but may miss attacks that use unexpected paths.

**Trade-Off Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Lever | Increase Setting → | Decrease Setting → |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | TPR $\uparrow$, FPR $\uparrow$ | TPR $\downarrow$, FPR $\downarrow$ |
| Threshold | FPR $\downarrow$, TPR $\downarrow$ | TPR $\uparrow$, FPR $\uparrow$ |
| Action Severity | Risk of DoS $\uparrow$ | Safety $\uparrow$, Protection $\downarrow$ |
| Scope Narrowing | FPR $\downarrow$, TPR $\downarrow$ | TPR $\uparrow$, FPR $\uparrow$ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Mastery of the four levers allows the engineer to **push the IDPS's operating point along the ROC curve** (Receiver Operating Characteristic), finding the optimal balance for the organization's risk appetite.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-B (b) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** **Custom signature development** is the process of **authoring a new detection pattern** (string, regex, byte sequence, or hash) tailored to an organization-specific threat, proprietary protocol, or newly discovered attack vector not covered by vendor rule-sets.

**End-to-End Workflow (5 marks):**

1. **Threat Identification (1 mark):** A new attack is observed (e.g., anomalous traffic in WAF logs, threat-intel feed, or zero-day advisory). The unique characteristic is documented.

2. **Sample Capture and Pattern Extraction (1 mark):** PCAPs of the attack are captured. The engineer isolates the **unique byte sequence or regex** that no benign traffic shares.

3. **Drafting the Signature (1 mark):** The pattern is encoded in the IDPS's signature language (e.g., Snort `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (content:"..."; sid:1000001; rev:1;)`).

4. **Staged Testing (1 mark):** The signature is tested against:
   - **Positive corpus** (known attack PCAPs) — must trigger.
   - **Negative corpus** (clean traffic PCAPs) — must NOT trigger.
   - FP rate is measured; pattern is refined iteratively.

5. **Production Deployment with Fail-Safe (1 mark):** Signature is deployed with **action = alert** (not block). It is monitored for **7 days**. If TP and FP rates remain within SLA, action is escalated to *drop* or *block*.

**The Fail-Safe Staging Principle (1 mark):**
> A new signature is **never deployed in blocking mode on day one**. If the pattern has an undetected false-positive trigger, a block-mode deployment could cause a **self-inflicted outage** of legitimate services. The 7-day alert-only period allows production traffic to validate the signature's safety before escalation.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing False Positive Rate with Precision.** They are different! FPR is a ratio over actual benign events; Precision is a ratio over total alerts. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Using Accuracy on imbalanced datasets.** Accuracy of 99.4% sounds great, but if 99% of traffic is benign, the IDPS could be useless and still appear "accurate." Always use **F1 or MCC** for IDPS evaluation — examiners reward this.
>
> 3. **Skipping the denominator calculation steps in MCC.** Most marks lost are in the square-root step. Show every product explicitly.
>
> 4. **Confusing "alert" and "block" actions in custom-signature deployment.** A signature must graduate from alert → drop, not skip stages. Examiners deduct marks for unsafe deployment claims.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the 7–14 day baseline window.** Examiners specifically test if the student knows tuning is data-driven, not rule-driven.
>
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark questions.** A missing lifecycle/architecture diagram can cost 1–2 marks even if the text is perfect.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Tuning is iterative**, not one-shot. It is a closed-loop cycle: Baseline → Audit → Calibrate → Validate → Deploy → Monitor → Re-Baseline.
- ☐ **Four tuning levers:** Sensitivity, Threshold, Action, Scope.
- ☐ **Five lifecycle phases:** Baseline, Audit, Calibrate, Whitelist, Custom Signatures.
- ☐ **Baseline window:** 7–14 days of representative traffic.
- ☐ **Whitelist metadata mandatory:** Reason, requester, approver, expiry date.
- ☐ **Custom signature fail-safe:** Deploy as *alert* for 7 days, then escalate to *drop*/*block*.
- ☐ **MCC is the gold-standard metric** for imbalanced IDPS datasets — not accuracy, not FPR alone.
- ☐ **F1 is the harmonic mean** of precision and recall; it punishes imbalance.
- ☐ **Precision and Recall have an inverse trade-off** — lowering threshold raises recall but lowers precision.
- ☐ **Untuned IDPS is worse than no IDPS** — alert fatigue causes analysts to ignore real attacks.
- ☐ **Compliance angle:** PCI-DSS 11.4 and ISO 27001 A.12.4 mandate tuning documentation.
- ☐ **Performance impact:** Disabling irrelevant rules can reduce CPU load by 30–60%.
- ☐ **KPI for SOC:** A well-tuned IDPS reduces alert volume by 80–95% (industry benchmark).
- ☐ **Tooling mentions in exam:** Snort, Suricata, Wireshark, ntopng, Security Onion, Zeek (Bro).
- ☐ **Always show the Confusion Matrix** before computing metrics — even if not explicitly asked.

---

> 🏁 **End of KTU Module 3 — Tuning and Customization Notes**
