### 2.4.6 Throughput & Performance Formulas

$$
\text{Packet Inspection Rate (Mpps)} = \frac{\text{Link Bandwidth (Mbps)}}{8 \times \text{Avg. Packet Size (bytes)}}
$$

$$
\text{Effective Throughput} = \text{Line Rate} \times (1 - \text{Drop Rate})
$$

$$
\text{Mean Time to Detect} \quad MTTD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{attack start},i})
$$

$$
\text{Mean Time to Respond} \quad MTTR = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{contain},i} - T_{\text{detect},i})
$$

### 2.4.7 Engine Constraint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All performance formulas use **\vert** (or absolute-value bars via `\lvert \rvert`) — never the unescaped pipe `|` character, since it would break the surrounding markdown table.

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Detection Rate | $DR = TP / (TP + FN)$ | How many real attacks caught |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = FP / (FP + TN)$ | Noise level of alerts |
| Precision | $PPV = TP / (TP + FP)$ | Trustworthiness of an alert |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot PPV \cdot DR / (PPV + DR)$ | Balanced single-number score |
| AUC | Integral of ROC curve | Overall classifier quality |
| MTTD | Average detection latency | SOC SLA compliance |
| MTTR | Average containment latency | IR efficiency |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

IDPS implementation underpins:
- **National Critical Infrastructure Protection** (power grids, water treatment — **NERC CIP** compliance).
- **Banking & Finance** (PCI-DSS 11.4 mandates IDS at perimeter and critical segments).
- **Cloud & SaaS** (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender, Google Chronicle).
- **OT/ICS Security** (Dragos, Nozomi Networks for SCADA/Modbus/DNP3).
- **5G & Telecom** (signaling intrusion in SS7/Diameter networks).
- **DevSecOps** (runtime threat detection in containers via Falco, Cilium Tetragon).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Base Rate Fallacy in IDPS (Bayesian Derivation)

This is a **favorite KTU 14-mark derivation**. We derive how a highly accurate detector becomes practically useless in low-base-rate environments.

**Given:**
- $P(A) = $ base rate of attacks in the traffic.
- $P(\neg A) = 1 - P(A) = $ base rate of benign traffic.
- $DR = P(\text{Alert} \mid A) = $ True Positive Rate (Sensitivity).
- $FPR = P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg A) = $ False Positive Rate.
- We need: $P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = $ Posterior probability that an alert is a real attack.

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(\text{Alert})}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the denominator using the Law of Total Probability.**

The probability of seeing an alert comes from two mutually exclusive sources: the alert is due to a *real attack* OR the alert is a *false positive* on benign traffic.

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = P(\text{Alert} \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the metric definitions.**

Recognize that $P(\text{Alert} \mid A) = DR$ and $P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg A) = FPR$. Therefore:

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = DR \cdot P(A) + FPR \cdot (1 - P(A))
$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form.**

$$
P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{DR \cdot P(A)}{DR \cdot P(A) + FPR \cdot (1 - P(A))}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration (a classic KTU sub-question).**

Suppose the base rate of attacks is $P(A) = 0.0001$ (1 in 10,000 packets), the detector has $DR = 0.99$ and $FPR = 0.001$.

Compute the numerator:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.99 \times 0.0001 = 0.000099
$$

Compute the benign false-positive term:

$$
\text{Denominator}_1 = 0.001 \times (1 - 0.0001) = 0.001 \times 0.9999 = 0.0009999
$$

Total denominator:

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = 0.000099 + 0.0009999 = 0.0010989
$$

Posterior probability:

$$
P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{0.000099}{0.0010989} \approx 0.0901 = 9.01\%
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even with **99% sensitivity** and **0.1% FPR**, only **9% of alerts are real attacks**. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy** — high accuracy does NOT translate to high alert trustworthiness in low-base-rate environments. The SOC receives ~10 false alerts for every 1 true attack, causing **alert fatigue**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ROC Curve Construction (Threshold Sweep)

The **ROC curve** is generated by sweeping the detection threshold and plotting $(FPR(\tau), DR(\tau))$ for each threshold $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Define the score function.**

Let the anomaly detector output a real-valued **anomaly score** $S(x) \in [0, 1]$ for each input $x$. Traffic is flagged as an attack if $S(x) \geq \tau$.

**Step 2 — For a given threshold $\tau$, compute the rates.**

$$
DR(\tau) = P(S(x) \geq \tau \mid A) = \frac{\lvert \{x_i \in A : S(x_i) \geq \tau\}\rvert}{\lvert A \rvert}
$$

$$
FPR(\tau) = P(S(x) \geq \tau \mid \neg A) = \frac{\lvert \{x_i \in \neg A : S(x_i) \geq \tau\}\rvert}{\lvert \neg A \rvert}
$$

(Here $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ denotes set cardinality, *not* absolute value, written with `\lvert \rvert` to avoid markdown conflicts.)

**Step 3 — Trace the curve.**

- At $\tau = 0$: $DR = 1$, $FPR = 1$ (everything flagged → right-top corner).
- At $\tau = 1$: $DR = 0$, $FPR = 0$ (nothing flagged → left-bottom corner).
- As $\tau$ increases monotonically, both rates decrease.

**Step 4 — Compute AUC via trapezoidal rule.**

$$
AUC = \int_{0}^{1} DR(\tau) \, d(FPR(\tau)) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(DR_{i+1} + DR_i)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of AUC values.**

| AUC | Classifier Quality |
| :--- | :--- |
| $1.0$ | Perfect — separable distributions |
| $0.9 \leq AUC < 1.0$ | Excellent |
| $0.8 \leq AUC < 0.9$ | Good |
| $0.7 \leq AUC < 0.8$ | Fair |
| $0.5 \leq AUC < 0.7$ | Poor |
| $AUC = 0.5$ | Random |
| $AUC < 0.5$ | Worse than random (label-flipped) |

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput and Packet Processing Capacity

**Given:**
- Link bandwidth: $B = 10$ Gbps.
- Average packet size: $L = 800$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Convert bandwidth to bytes per second.**

$$
B_{\text{bytes}} = 10 \times 10^9 \, \text{bits/s} \div 8 = 1.25 \times 10^9 \, \text{bytes/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute packets per second (pps).**

$$
\text{pps} = \frac{B_{\text{bytes}}}{L} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^9}{800} = 1.5625 \times 10^6 \, \text{packets/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Throughput} = 1.5625 \, \text{Mpps}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective throughput with a 0.5% drop rate.**

$$
\text{Effective Throughput} = 1.5625 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.005) = 1.5547 \, \text{Mpps}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** A software IDPS running on a 4-core x86 server can typically sustain **1–3 Mpps** with DPI; beyond that, **DPDK, FPGA, or kernel-bypass (e.g., netmap, AF_XDP)** is required.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cost-Benefit of IDPS Implementation

**Given:**
- Number of attacks per year prevented: $N = 50$.
- Average loss per successful attack: $L = \$200{,}000$.
- Detection Rate: $DR = 0.95$.
- Implementation cost (hardware + 3-year license + SOC analyst time): $C = \$500{,}000$.

**Step 1 — Expected loss prevented.**

$$
\text{Savings} = N \times DR \times L = 50 \times 0.95 \times 200{,}000 = \$9{,}500{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Net benefit.**

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = \text{Savings} - C = 9{,}500{,}000 - 500{,}000 = \$9{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — ROI.**

$$
ROI = \frac{9{,}000{,}000}{500{,}000} \times 100\% = 1800\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** A high DR makes the IDPS economically justifiable even at high implementation cost. This is the standard **management-pitch metric** for IDPS deployment.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — F1-Score from Confusion Matrix

**Given TP=950, FP=50, FN=50, TN=98950:**

**Step 1 — Precision.**

$$
PPV = \frac{950}{950 + 50} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95
$$

**Step 2 — Recall (Detection Rate).**

$$
DR = \frac{950}{950 + 50} = 0.95
$$

**Step 3 — F1-Score.**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.95 \times 0.95}{0.95 + 0.95} = \frac{2 \times 0.9025}{1.9} = 0.95
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy.**

$$
Acc = \frac{950 + 98950}{950 + 50 + 50 + 98950} = \frac{99900}{100000} = 0.999 = 99.9\%
$$

**Step 5 — Critical observation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy = 99.9% is misleading** because the base rate is so low (0.1%). A trivial classifier that flags *nothing* as an attack would also achieve 99.9% accuracy. This reinforces why **F1-score, DR, and FPR are more meaningful** than accuracy in IDPS evaluation.

## 3.6 Summary Matrix of Derivable Results

| Derivation | Key Equation | KTU Typical Mark Weight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Base Rate Fallacy (Bayes) | $P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{DR \cdot P(A)}{DR \cdot P(A) + FPR \cdot (1 - P(A))}$ | 7 marks |
| ROC & AUC | $AUC = \int DR \, d(FPR)$ | 7 marks |
| Throughput (Mpps) | $\text{pps} = B_{\text{bytes}} / L$ | 3–5 marks |
| ROI | $ROI = (N \cdot DR \cdot L - C) / C$ | 5–7 marks |
| F1 / Accuracy Paradox | $F_1 = 2 \cdot PPV \cdot DR / (PPV + DR)$ | 3–5 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IDPS Implementation Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["Phase 1: Requirements and Policy Definition"] --> phase2["Phase 2: Architectural Design and Sensor Placement"]
    phase2 --> phase3["Phase 3: Sensor and Agent Deployment"]
    phase3 --> phase4["Phase 4: Rule and Baseline Configuration"]
    phase4 --> phase5["Phase 5: Testing Validation and Tuning"]
    phase5 --> phase6["Phase 6: Operations and Continuous Improvement"]
    phase6 -.feedback loop.-> phase4
    phase5 -.regression on failure.-> phase3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The forward path represents the linear deployment sequence.
- The **dashed feedback loop** from Phase 6 → Phase 4 signifies **continuous rule retraining and tuning** based on operational telemetry.
- The **dashed regression** from Phase 5 → Phase 3 indicates that if a validation test fails, the team must reconfigure the sensors (re-deploy) before re-testing.

## 4.2 Inline vs. Passive Deployment Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internet["Internet"] --> firewall["Perimeter Firewall"]
    firewall --> tapA["Network TAP SPAN Mirror"]
    tapA --> nids["Passive NIDS Sensor"]
    firewall --> ips["Inline NIPS Appliance"]
    ips --> coreSwitch["Core L3 Switch"]
    coreSwitch --> servers["Server Farm and Endpoints"]
    nids --> siem["Central SIEM Correlation Engine"]
    ips -.alert and block.-> siem
    siem --> soc["SOC Analyst Console"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **NIDS** sits on a TAP/SPAN port — it sees a copy of the traffic but **cannot block**.
- The **NIPS** sits **inline** between the firewall and the core switch — it can drop packets in real time but introduces a single point of failure.
- Both feed the **SIEM** for centralized correlation and SOC visualization.

## 4.3 Hybrid (NIDS + HIDS) Implementation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    perimeter["Internet and DMZ"] --> nipsA["Perimeter Inline NIPS"]
    nipsA --> coreNet["Internal Core Network"]
    coreNet --> nidsB["Internal Passive NIDS Sensor"]
    coreNet --> vlanSeg["VLAN Segmentation"]
    vlanSeg --> serverA["Application Server 1 with HIDS Agent"]
    vlanSeg --> serverB["Database Server with HIPS Agent"]
    vlanSeg --> serverC["Cloud VM with CWPP Agent"]
    nipsA --> correlation["Correlation Engine and SIEM"]
    nidsB --> correlation
    serverA --> correlation
    serverB --> correlation
    serverC --> correlation
    correlation --> socDash["SOC Dashboard and IR Playbooks"]
    correlation --> response["Automated Response Orchestrator"]
    response -.SOAR API.-> nipsA
    response -.quarantine VLAN.-> vlanSeg
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Defense in depth**: NIPS at perimeter, NIDS internally for east-west traffic, HIDS/HIPS on critical servers, and CWPP (Cloud Workload Protection Platform) agents on cloud VMs.
- **Centralized correlation** in SIEM reduces alert noise.
- **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response)** automates response — pushing firewall blocks, quarantining VLANs, and isolating endpoints.

## 4.4 IDPS Implementation Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Decide IDPS Type"] --> q1{"Traffic volume greater than 1 Gbps?"}
    q1 -- "Yes" --> nids["Deploy NIDS with TAP and DPI Offload"]
    q1 -- "No" --> q2{"Need active blocking of attacks?"}
    q2 -- "Yes" --> nips["Deploy Inline NIPS in HA Pair"]
    q2 -- "No" --> hids["Deploy HIDS for Endpoint Visibility"]
    nids --> tune["Phase 4 to 6: Rule Tuning Testing and Operations"]
    nips --> tune
    hids --> tune
    tune --> monitor["Continuous KPI Monitoring and Improvement"]
```

## 4.5 Implementation Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    test1["1. Unit Test Individual Rules"] --> test2["2. Replay Labeled PCAPs e.g. CICIDS"]
    test2 --> test3["3. Red Team Penetration Test"]
    test3 --> test4["4. False Positive Audit on Production Mirror"]
    test4 --> test5["5. Stress Test Peak Load"]
    test5 --> test6["6. Sign Off and Go Live"]
    test6 -.ongoing.-> test4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **labeled PCAP replay** (e.g., from **CICIDS-2017**, **NSL-KDD**, **UNSW-NB15**) provides ground-truth evaluation of DR and FPR.
- The **false-positive audit** uses a one-week production mirror traffic to quantify noise.
- The **stress test** validates the throughput claims of the vendor under peak load.
- After **go-live**, periodic **regression testing** ensures new rules don't break the baseline.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define IDPS implementation. List the six phases of the IDPS implementation lifecycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** IDPS implementation is the end-to-end process of deploying, configuring, tuning, and maintaining an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System within a production network, encompassing sensor placement, rule engineering, baseline establishment, alert correlation, and response automation.

**Six Phases (2 marks):**
1. Requirements and Policy Definition.
2. Architectural Design and Sensor Placement.
3. Sensor / Agent Deployment.
4. Rule and Baseline Configuration.
5. Testing, Validation, and Tuning.
6. Operations, Monitoring, and Continuous Improvement.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Inline (NIPS) and Passive (NIDS) deployment modes in IDPS implementation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inline (NIPS) | Passive (NIDS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position | **In-line** with traffic flow | Off a **TAP / SPAN port** |
| Can block packets? | ✅ Yes (TCP reset, drop) | ❌ No (detection only) |
| Latency | **< 100 µs** (introduces delay) | **0 µs** (no delay) |
| Failure impact | **Single point of failure** (unless HA) | Zero impact on traffic |
| Use case | Perimeter / DMZ enforcement | High-throughput backbone monitoring |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 3 typically offers an internal choice between an *architectural* question and an *evaluation-metrics* question. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a)** Describe the **six phases of IDPS implementation** in detail with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Inline NIPS, Passive NIDS, and Hybrid HIDS+NIDS deployment topologies** with diagrams. Discuss the placement considerations for each. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Six Phases of IDPS Implementation (7 marks):**

1. **Requirements & Policy Definition (1 mark):** Identify assets, define threat model, establish CIA priorities, comply with regulatory mandates (PCI-DSS 11.4, NIST SP 800-94, ISO 27001). Document incident response policy.

2. **Architectural Design & Sensor Placement (1 mark):** Decide NIDS vs. NIPS vs. HIDS, choose inline vs. passive mode, design network choke points (perimeter, data center, east-west traffic).

3. **Sensor / Agent Deployment (1 mark):** Hardware appliances on 1U/2U rack, software agents on hosts, promiscuous-mode NICs, management out-of-band, NTP/SNMP/DNS integration.

4. **Rule & Baseline Configuration (1 mark):** Import community rule sets (Snort VRT, Emerging Threats), tune thresholds, run a **2–4 week learning period** for anomaly engines, whitelist business-critical false positives.

5. **Testing, Validation & Tuning (1.5 marks):** Replay labeled PCAPs (CICIDS-2017, NSL-KDD), conduct red-team simulations, measure DR, FPR, MTTD, MTTR. Iterate on rules.

6. **Operations & Continuous Improvement (1.5 marks):** 24/7 SOC monitoring, quarterly rule audit, signature updates, ML model retraining, periodic regression testing.

**Part (b) — Topologies (7 marks):**

**Inline NIPS (3 marks):** Placed **in series** between the firewall and the internal switch. Operates in **Layer-2 transparent bridge mode**. Can drop, reset, or rate-limit malicious traffic. Requires **HA (Active-Active or Active-Passive)** to avoid single-point-of-failure. Latency typically **< 100 µs**. Suited for perimeter/DMZ.

**Passive NIDS (2 marks):** Connected to a **network TAP (Test Access Point)** or **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer)** mirror port. Operates **out-of-band** — sees a copy of traffic but **cannot block**. Used on 10/40/100 Gbps backbone links where inline insertion would be risky. Latency: **zero** on production traffic.

**Hybrid HIDS + NIDS (2 marks):** NIDS at perimeter + NIDS on internal core + HIDS agents on critical servers. Provides **defense in depth** — NIDS catches network-borne attacks; HIDS detects host-level anomalies (file integrity, registry changes, syscall anomalies). Cloud workloads get **CWPP** agents (e.g., Falco for Kubernetes). All telemetry is centrally correlated in **SIEM + SOAR**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Confusion Matrix** for an IDPS. Derive the **Detection Rate (DR)**, **False Positive Rate (FPR)**, **Precision (PPV)**, and **F1-Score** formulas. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a base rate of attacks $P(A) = 0.0001$, detection rate $DR = 0.99$, and false positive rate $FPR = 0.001$, apply **Bayes' Theorem** to compute the posterior probability $P(A \mid \text{Alert})$. Explain the **Base Rate Fallacy** and its operational impact on SOC analysts. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Confusion Matrix (7 marks):**

A confusion matrix tabulates the IDPS's predictions against ground truth (1 mark):

| | Actual Attack (A) | Actual Benign (¬A) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Flagged as Attack** | TP | FP |
| **Flagged as Benign** | FN | TN |

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity):** Fraction of actual attacks correctly identified.

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**False Positive Rate:** Fraction of benign traffic wrongly flagged.

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

**Precision (Positive Predictive Value):** Of all alerts raised, the fraction that are real attacks.

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of precision and recall (balances both).

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot DR}{PPV + DR}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Bayes' Theorem and Base Rate Fallacy (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — State Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{P(\text{Alert} \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(\text{Alert})}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Expand denominator using the Law of Total Probability.**

$$
P(\text{Alert}) = P(\text{Alert} \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Substitute metric definitions** $P(\text{Alert} \mid A) = DR$ and $P(\text{Alert} \mid \neg A) = FPR$:

$$
P(A \mid \text{Alert}) = \frac{DR \cdot P(A)}{DR \cdot P(A) + FPR \cdot (1 - P(A))}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Numerical substitution.**

Numerator: $0.99 \times 0.0001 = 0.000099$.
Denominator: $0.000099 + 0.001 \times 0.9999 = 0.000099 + 0.0009999 = 0.0010989$.
Posterior: $0.000099 / 0.0010989 \approx 0.0901 = 9.01\%$.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Base Rate Fallacy interpretation.**

Despite the IDPS having **99% sensitivity** and **0.1% FPR**, only **~9% of alerts correspond to real attacks**. For every 1 true positive, the SOC receives ~10 false positives. This causes **alert fatigue**, missed detections, and analyst burnout. Operational mitigations include **alert tuning, whitelisting, tiered alert prioritization, and SIEM correlation**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing NIDS with NIPS in deployment questions.** NIDS = passive / detection only; NIPS = inline / can block. KTU deducts 1–2 marks for swapping these.
> 2. **Forgetting the Base Rate Fallacy.** A 99% accurate detector is not 99% useful in low-base-rate traffic. KTU examiners explicitly test this in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Reporting "Accuracy" as the sole metric.** Accuracy is misleading in IDPS due to class imbalance. Always report **DR, FPR, Precision, and F1-Score** together.
> 4. **Skipping the unit or the % sign** when writing $DR = 0.99$ — write $DR = 99\%$.
> 5. **Drawing inline placement BEFORE the firewall.** NIPS must be placed **after the firewall and before the internal network**, not at the WAN edge.
> 6. **Confusing SIEM with IDPS.** SIEM is the **correlator/log aggregator**, not the detector. IDPS is the **producer** of events.
> 7. **Skipping the management plane vs. data plane distinction** in deployment diagrams.
> 8. **Forgetting to mention HA (High Availability)** for inline NIPS — examiners specifically look for the single-point-of-failure discussion.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Implementation: High-Density Revision Points**

- **Implementation is a 6-phase lifecycle:** Requirements → Architecture → Deployment → Configuration → Testing → Operations.
- **NIDS is passive (TAP/SPAN), NIPS is inline (bridge mode), HIDS is host-resident.** Memorize the table comparing latency, blocking ability, and failure impact.
- **Inline NIPS requires HA** (active-active or active-passive) to avoid single-point-of-failure.
- **Hybrid = NIDS + HIDS + SIEM + SOAR** — defense in depth.
- **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation of all evaluation metrics. $DR$, $FPR$, $PPV$, $F_1$ all derive from it.
- **Accuracy is misleading** in IDPS due to class imbalance. Use **F1, DR, FPR** as primary metrics.
- **Base Rate Fallacy:** even a 99%-accurate detector gives only ~9% trustworthy alerts at base rate $10^{-4}$. Apply **Bayes' Theorem** rigorously.
- **ROC curve** = plot of DR vs. FPR as threshold varies. **AUC** summarizes detector quality. AUC = 1 is perfect; AUC = 0.5 is random.
- **Throughput** = bandwidth / (8 × avg. packet size) in packets per second (Mpps).
- **ROI of IDPS** = (attacks prevented × avg. loss × DR − cost) / cost. Always positive when $DR > 5\%$.
- **Implementation challenges** to enumerate: encrypted traffic, high bandwidth, evasion, false-positive fatigue, rule conflicts.
- **TLS/QUIC encrypted traffic** requires **SSL inspection / MITM re-encryption** for DPI to work.
- **HIDS agents** can conflict with antivirus on endpoints — note resource overhead.
- **Testing datasets** in KTU answers: DARPA 1998/1999, KDD Cup 1999, NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15.
- **Compliance standards** to cite: PCI-DSS 11.4, NIST SP 800-94, ISO 27001 A.12.4, NERC CIP for OT.
- **MTTD + MTTR** are the SOC's two key SLAs. Reducing MTTD requires better detection; reducing MTTR requires better orchestration (SOAR).
- **Inline placement is a "bump-in-the-wire"** — appears as a transparent Layer-2 bridge to the network.
- **Tuning is iterative and never-ending** — implementation is a process, not a project.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless IDPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wireless IDPS # Wireless Intrusion Detection & Prevention System (WIDPS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wireless Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (WIDPS):** A specialized category of Network Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (NIDPS) that monitors the Radio Frequency (RF) spectrum and 802.11 (Wi-Fi) frame traffic to identify, log, alert, and optionally block unauthorized wireless activity, rogue devices, and protocol-level attacks against a Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN). WIDPS extends classical IDPS principles into the **wireless domain**, addressing threats that traditional wired IDPS systems cannot detect because the attack vector is the **air interface (Layer 1 & 2 of the OSI model)** rather than a physical Ethernet cable. ### Key Operational Differences from Wired IDPS | Parameter | Wired IDPS | Wireless IDPS | |-----------|-----------|---------------| | Medium | Copper / Fiber cables | Radio Frequency (RF) airwaves | | Primary Layer | Layer 2–7 | Layer 1 & Layer 2 (802.11 frames) | | Attack Surface | LAN segment | 360° RF propagation radius | | Sensor Placement | Tap on switch SPAN port | Distributed across physical coverage area | | Threat Visibility | Packet headers, payloads | Signal strength, SSID, BSSID, channel, frame types | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Airport Security Analogy:** Imagine a large international airport (your WLAN). A Wired IDPS is like checking passports and luggage **after** travelers enter the secure corridor. A Wireless IDPS, however, behaves like the **perimeter radar and ground-motion sensors** that detect suspicious activity in the airfield *before* intruders reach the terminal — sniffing the airwaves just like radar sniffs the sky. Even a hacker **parked outside your building** in a van can be spotted because their rogue transmitter leaks RF energy. ### Core Functional Goals 1. **Detection** — Spot rogue Access Points (APs), evil twins, MAC spoofing, DoS, and unauthorized associations. 2. **Classification** — Distinguish a legitimate client from a malicious one based on frame behaviour. 3. **Prevention** — Terminate offending sessions via deauthentication / disassociation frames or block MAC addresses. 4. **Forensics & Compliance** — Maintain logs of wireless events for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 audits. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Wireless IDPS is an explicit module sub-topic and questions frequently appear in ESE asking students to differentiate WIDPS from wired IDPS and list attacks detected by it. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Wireless IDPS A WIDPS deployment consists of **three logical components** mapped onto physical hardware: ### Component 1 — Wireless Sensors (Collectors) - Dedicated **hardware sensors** placed at strategic physical locations. - Operate in **RF monitor mode** — they do not associate with any AP; they passively sniff all 802.11 frames on every channel. - Continuously perform **channel hopping** (e.g., dwell time ≈ **250 ms** per channel across 2.4 GHz band of 11–13 channels and 5 GHz UNII bands). ### Component 2 — Wireless Analyzer / Server - Centralized server aggregating data from multiple sensors. - Performs **signature matching**, **anomaly scoring**, and **stateful protocol analysis**. - Maintains a database of authorised devices (whitelist of SSIDs, BSSIDs, MAC OUIs). ### Component 3 — Management Console - Web-based or GUI dashboard for the security administrator. - Visualizes RF heatmaps, attack timelines, sensor health, and policy violations. ## 2.2 Taxonomy of Wireless Threats Detected by WIDPS > [!NOTE] > The following threat taxonomy aligns directly with KTU Module-3 expected outcomes. 1. **Rogue Access Point** — An unauthorized AP connected to the wired backbone, creating a backdoor. 2. **Evil Twin** — A rogue AP mimicking the legitimate SSID to lure clients. 3. **Ad-hoc / IBSS Network** — Clients forming peer-to-peer Wi-Fi direct connections bypassing infrastructure. 4. **MAC Address Spoofing** — Attacker clones an authorised client's MAC to bypass ACLs. 5. **War Driving / Wardriving** — Passive scanning of Wi-Fi networks from a moving vehicle. 6. **Wireless Denial of Service (WDoS)** - RF Jamming (Layer 1 noise flooding) - Deauthentication / Disassociation Flood (802.11w mitigation) - CTS/RTS Flood 7. **Mis-association / Honeypot AP** — Client unknowingly connects to attacker-controlled open SSID. 8. **Frame Injection / Replay** — Crafted 802.11 management/control frames. 9. **Krack Attack (Key Reinstallation Attack)** — Exploits 4-way handshake in WPA2. 10. **Unauthorized Client Association** — MAC not on corporate whitelist. ## 2.3 Wireless IDPS Detection Methodologies ### A. Signature-Based Detection Maintains a database of known attack signatures: $$\text{Alert} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \text{Match}(\text{Frame}_{802.11}, \text{Signature}_i) = \text{TRUE} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ Examples: deauth flood signatures, known WEP cracking tool frames. ### B. Anomaly-Based Detection Builds a baseline of "normal" RF behaviour, then flags deviations: $$\text{Anomaly Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$$ where $x_i$ = observed feature, $\mu_i$ = baseline mean, $w_i$ = feature weight. Examples: sudden spike in probe requests, unusual SSID broadcast power, anomalous association rate. ### C. State-Based / Protocol-Aware Detection Tracks the **state machine** of 802.11 (e.g., the 4-way EAPOL handshake). Any violation of expected transitions triggers an alert. ### D. RF-Level / Physical Layer Analysis Uses **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** patterns: $$\text{Link Quality} = \text{RSSI}_{\text{dBm}} - \text{Noise Floor}_{\text{dBm}}$$ A sudden negative shift may indicate jamming. ## 2.4 Prevention Mechanisms | Mechanism | Action | |-----------|--------| | **Active Containment** | Wireless sensor sends spoofed deauthentication frames to disconnect rogue clients | | **Wired-side Block** | Sensor instructs switch to shut the rogue AP's switchport | | **MAC ACL Push** | Pushes offending MAC to AP controller blacklist | | **Channel Blacklisting** | Disables the rogue AP's channel via RRM | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are examinable under KTU PECCT742 Module 3. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | $d$: metres, $f$: Hz, $c$: **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | 2 | RSSI Threshold Detection | $\text{RSSI}_{\text{alert}} = \mu_{\text{RSSI}} - k \cdot \sigma_{\text{RSSI}}$ | $k$ = sensitivity factor (commonly 2–3) | | 3 | Detection Probability | $P_d = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$: arrival rate, $t$: dwell time | | 4 | Frame Flood Detection | $\text{Alert if } F > \text{Threshold}_{fpm}$ | $F$: frames per minute per channel | | 5 | Sensor Coverage Area | $A_c = \pi r^2$ | $r$: effective radius from FSPL | | 6 | Number of Sensors Required | $N_s = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{\text{floor}}}{A_c \cdot \eta} \right\rceil$ | $\eta$: overlap factor (0.6 – 0.8) | | 7 | SNR for Decoding | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - N_0$ | $N_0$: noise floor ≈ **−100 dBm** (2.4 GHz) | | 8 | Deauth Flood Threshold | $\text{Alert if } N_{\text{deauth}} > 30 / \text{sec}$ | Empirical constant | | 9 | Anomaly Score (Mahalanobis) | $D^2 = (x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)$ | $\Sigma$: covariance matrix | | 10 | False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Ratio, dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas enable RF coverage planning during WIDPS deployment. A network engineer must guarantee that every square metre of the office floor is within at least two sensor ranges (overlap factor η) to prevent blind spots where attackers could operate undetected. ## 2.6 WIDPS vs Classical NIDPS — Key Differences > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** | Aspect | Classical NIDPS | Wireless IDPS | |--------|-----------------|---------------| | Visibility Layer | Encrypted payload after decryption | Pre-association 802.11 frames | | Encryption Handling | Inspects decrypted content | Cannot decrypt WPA2/WPA3 traffic, must rely on metadata | | Sensor Physical Placement | Network choke points | RF coverage zones | | Response Action | TCP RST, drop packet | Deauthentication frame, switchport disable | | Primary Threat | Malware, exploits, recon | Rogue AP, spoofing, RF jamming | | Dependency | Needs switch SPAN port | Needs RF monitor-mode hardware | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Path Loss for Sensor Coverage Planning The WIDPS deployment engineer must compute the maximum distance $d$ at which a sensor can still reliably decode an 802.11 frame. ### Step 1 — Start with the Friis Transmission Equation The received power $P_r$ in free space is given by: $$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$$ where: - $P_t$ = transmit power (Watts) - $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains (linear, dimensionless) - $\lambda$ = wavelength = $\dfrac{c}{f}$ - $d$ = distance between transmitter and receiver (metres) ### Step 2 — Convert to Decibel Form Taking $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides: $$P_{r,\text{dBm}} = P_{t,\text{dBm}} + G_{t,\text{dBi}} + G_{r,\text{dBi}} - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4\pi}{c} \right)$$ ### Step 3 — Recognise the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) Term The last three terms combined give the FSPL: $$\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4\pi}{c} \right)$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute Speed of Light Constant $$20\log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8} \right) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) - 20\log_{10}(3 \times 10^8)$$ $$= 20(1.0969) - 20(9.4771) = 21.938 - 189.542 = -167.604 \text{ dB}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Working FSPL Formula $$\boxed{\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.45}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** At **2.4 GHz**, a sensor receiving a **−80 dBm** frame from an AP transmitting at **20 dBm** over **30 m** has a link margin suitable for decoding 802.11g/n OFDM symbols. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Number of Wireless Sensors Given the floor area $A_{\text{floor}}$, compute $N_s$ sensors needed for full RF coverage. ### Step 1 — Determine Sensor Coverage Radius Using FSPL from Section 3.1, the maximum distance $r$ is when received power equals sensor sensitivity $S$: $$S_{\text{dBm}} = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}(r)$$ Solving for $r$: $$r = 10^{\dfrac{P_t + G_t + G_r - S - 32.45 - 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})}{20}} \text{ (km)}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Coverage Area per Sensor Each sensor covers a circular footprint: $$A_c = \pi r^2$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Overlap Factor To eliminate blind spots and ensure redundancy, an overlap factor $\eta$ (typically **0.6 – 0.8**) is applied: $$\text{Effective Coverage per Sensor} = A_c \cdot \eta$$ ### Step 4 — Total Sensors Required $$\boxed{N_s = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{\text{floor}}}{A_c \cdot \eta} \right\rceil}$$ ### Numerical Example Given: - Floor area $A_{\text{floor}} = 1200 \text{ m}^2$ - Sensor radius $r = 15 \text{ m}$ - Overlap $\eta = 0.7$ Compute $A_c$: $$A_c = \pi \times (15)^2 = \pi \times 225 \approx 706.86 \text{ m}^2$$ Effective coverage per sensor: $$A_c \cdot \eta = 706.86 \times 0.7 = 494.80 \text{ m}^2$$ Required sensors: $$N_s = \left\lceil \dfrac{1200}{494.80} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.425 \rceil = 3 \text{ sensors}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The engineer must install **at least 3 sensors** to cover 1200 m² under the given RF conditions. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deauthentication Flood Detection Threshold Attackers send bursts of deauth frames to disconnect legitimate clients. WIDPS uses a **Poisson arrival model**. ### Step 1 — Poisson Probability For arrival rate $\lambda$ over interval $t$: $$P(N = k) = \dfrac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$ ### Step 2 — Baseline Normal Behaviour Under normal operations, deauths occur at rate $\lambda_{\text{normal}} = 0.1 / \text{sec}$. ### Step 3 — Attack Threshold Set alert threshold at 3× normal: $$\lambda_{\text{alert}} = 3 \cdot \lambda_{\text{normal}} = 0.3 / \text{sec}$$ ### Step 4 — Detection Probability Over Dwell Time $t = 5$ sec $$P_d = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{\text{alert}} t} = 1 - e^{-0.3 \times 5} = 1 - e^{-1.5}$$ $$P_d = 1 - 0.2231 = 0.7769 \approx 77.7\%$$ ### Step 5 — Interpretation > [!NOTE] > There is a **77.7% probability** the sensor detects the deauth flood within a 5-second window. To reach **>99%**, dwell time must increase or threshold must lower. $$t_{99\%} = \dfrac{-\ln(0.01)}{\lambda_{\text{alert}}} = \dfrac{4.605}{0.3} \approx 15.35 \text{ sec}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Detection Coverage Comparison | Attack Type | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | State-Based | RF Layer | |-------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------|----------| | Rogue AP | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | Evil Twin | ⚠️ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ | | Deauth Flood | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | MAC Spoofing | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | | RF Jamming | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | Krack Attack | ✅ | ⚠️ | ✅ | ❌ | | War Driving | ⚠️ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | Legend: ✅ Strong | ⚠️ Partial | ❌ Ineffective # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WIDPS Deployment Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RF["RF Airspace (Layer 1 & 2)"] CL1[Legitimate Client Laptop] CL2[Smartphone Client] ROGUE1[Rogue AP - Attacker Van] ROGUE2[Evil Twin AP] ATTACK[Deauth Flood Tool] end subgraph SENSORS["Wireless Sensor Layer"] S1[Sensor 1 - Monitor Mode] S2[Sensor 2 - Monitor Mode] S3[Sensor 3 - Monitor Mode] end subgraph BACKBONE["Wired Backbone"] SW[Core L3 Switch] AP1[Authorized AP 1] AP2[Authorized AP 2] WLC[Wireless LAN Controller] end subgraph ANALYSIS["Central Analysis Engine"] SIG[Signature Database] ANOM[Anomaly Engine] STATE[Protocol State Machine] DB[(Authorized Device Whitelist)] end subgraph MGMT["Management Plane"] CON[Admin GUI Console] ALERT[Alert / Syslog / SIEM] RESP[Response Engine - Deauth / Port Shutdown] end CL1 -. 802.11 frames .-> S1 CL2 -. 802.11 frames .-> S2 ROGUE1 -. rogue frames .-> S1 ROGUE2 -. evil twin .-> S3 ATTACK -. flood .-> S2 S1 -->|encapsulated| SW S2 -->|encapsulated| SW S3 -->|encapsulated| SW SW --> WLC SW --> ANALYSIS SIG --> RESP ANOM --> RESP STATE --> RESP DB --> SIG RESP --> CON RESP --> ALERT RESP -->|deauth frame| ROGUE1 RESP -->|deauth frame| ROGUE2 RESP -->|switchport disable| SW classDef attack fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef sensor fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2c3e50,color:#000 classDef core fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000 classDef mgmt fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#e67e22,color:#000 class ROGUE1,ROGUE2,ATTACK attack class S1,S2,S3 sensor class WLC,AP1,AP2,SW,SIG,ANOM,STATE,DB core class CON,ALERT,RESP mgmt ``` ## 4.2 Wireless IDPS Detection Workflow (Sequence Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RF Frame Captured] --> B{Is frame destined to
known SSID?} B -- Yes --> C{Check Source MAC
vs Whitelist} B -- No --> D[Rogue SSID Alert] C -- Not Found --> E[MAC Spoof / Unauthorized Alert] C -- Found --> F{Frame Count vs
Threshold?} F -- Exceeded --> G[Deauth Flood Alert] F -- Normal --> H{State Machine
Violation?} H -- Yes --> I[Protocol Anomaly Alert] H -- No --> J[Pass - Log Frame] D --> K[Response Engine] E --> K G --> K I --> K K --> L{Active Prevention
Enabled?} L -- Yes --> M[Send Deauth / Shut Port] L -- No --> N[Log + Notify Admin] ``` ## 4.3 Sensor Placement Strategy (Coverage Zones) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ZONE_A["Zone A - Lobby"] SA[Sensor A] R1[Coverage Radius r1] end subgraph ZONE_B["Zone B - Workstation Bay"] SB[Sensor B] R2[Coverage Radius r2] end subgraph ZONE_C["Zone C - Server Room"] SC[Sensor C] R3[Coverage Radius r3] end SA --- SB SB --- SC R1 -. overlaps .-> R2 R2 -. overlaps .-> R3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q:** Define Wireless Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (WIDPS). List any four wireless threats it detects. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **WIDPS Definition (1.5 marks):** A Wireless Intrusion Detection and Prevention System is a specialized network security system that monitors the Radio Frequency (RF) spectrum and 802.11 wireless frames to detect, alert, and optionally prevent unauthorized activities, rogue devices, and protocol-level attacks against a WLAN. **Four Wireless Threats (0.5 mark each = 2 marks):** 1. **Rogue Access Point** — unauthorized AP connected to the wired LAN. 2. **Evil Twin** — rogue AP cloning a legitimate SSID. 3. **Deauthentication Flood** — DoS via mass 802.11 deauth frames. 4. **MAC Address Spoofing** — cloning an authorized client MAC. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q:** Differentiate between Wired IDPS and Wireless IDPS on the basis of (i) Layer of operation, (ii) Sensor placement, and (iii) Response mechanism. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Wired IDPS | Wireless IDPS | |-----------|-----------|---------------| | (i) Layer | Operates at Layer 2–7 (post-decryption) | Operates at Layer 1 & 2 (RF + 802.11 frames) | | (ii) Sensor Placement | SPAN port on switches at choke points | Distributed across physical RF coverage zones | | (iii) Response | TCP RST, packet drop, IP block | Deauthentication frames, switchport disable, MAC ACL | *(1 mark per correct row)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]** **Q (a)** Explain the architecture of a Wireless IDPS with a neat diagram. List the major components and their functions. **(7 marks)** **Q (b)** Discuss the various wireless attacks detected by WIDPS. Explain in detail the Deauthentication Flood attack and how WIDPS detects it. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer to Q (a) — 7 Marks **1. Introduction (1 mark):** A WIDPS architecture consists of three logical layers — sensors, analyzer, and management console — connected over the wired backbone. **2. Components and Functions (5 marks):** - **Wireless Sensors:** Hardware devices operating in **RF monitor mode**. They passively scan all Wi-Fi channels by hopping, capture every 802.11 frame, and forward them to the analyzer. They do not associate with any AP. - **Central Analyzer:** Performs signature matching against a database of known attack patterns, runs anomaly detection using statistical baselines, and executes protocol state machines for 802.11 handshakes. - **Management Console:** Web GUI for the security administrator showing real-time RF heatmaps, alerts, sensor health, and historical reports. - **Database / Whitelist:** Stores authorized SSIDs, BSSIDs, MAC OUI prefixes, and policy rules. - **Response Engine:** Initiates active prevention — spoofing deauth frames to rogue clients or instructing the wired switch to shut the rogue AP's port. **3. Diagram Reference (1 mark):** Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1 above. --- #### Model Answer to Q (b) — 7 Marks **1. Wireless Attacks List (2 marks):** - Rogue AP, Evil Twin, Ad-hoc Network, MAC Spoofing, War Driving, RF Jamming, Deauthentication Flood, Krack Attack, Frame Injection. **2. Deauthentication Flood Attack (3 marks):** - Attacker spoofs the **AP's MAC address** in the Source Address field and broadcasts **802.11 management frames of subtype 0x0C (Deauthentication)** to all clients. - Each legitimate client receives the deauth, drops its association, and is disconnected from the Wi-Fi. - A flood of these frames causes a **complete Wireless DoS** because clients cannot reconnect faster than they are kicked off. - Tools used: `aireplay-ng -0`, MDK3, Scapy scripts. **3. WIDPS Detection Method (2 marks):** - The sensor's analyzer maintains a **threshold counter** for deauth frames per second per BSSID. - Using the Poisson arrival model from Section 3.3, when observed count exceeds $\lambda_{\text{alert}}$ within a sliding window, an alert is raised. - Response engine sends **counter-deauthentication frames** to the attacker and disables the rogue's switchport via SNMP/CoA. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]** **Q (a)** Explain the detection methodologies used in Wireless IDPS with examples. Compare signature-based and anomaly-based detection in the wireless context. **(7 marks)** **Q (b)** A floor of dimensions 40 m × 30 m requires WIDPS coverage. Each sensor has an effective radius of 12 m. Using an overlap factor η = 0.75, calculate the number of sensors required. Show all steps. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer to Q (a) — 7 Marks **1. Signature-Based Detection (2 marks):** Matches 802.11 frames against a database of known attack byte patterns. Example: detecting `aireplay-ng` deauth frames by recognizing the vendor-specific Information Element pattern. Limitation: cannot detect zero-day attacks. **2. Anomaly-Based Detection (2 marks):** Builds a statistical baseline of normal RF behaviour (probe requests per second, association rate, RSSI distribution). Any significant deviation triggers an alert. Example: sudden spike in probe requests from a single MAC indicates **wardriving**. **3. State-Based Detection (1 mark):** Tracks the 802.11 state machine. Example: detection of Krack attack where the same EAPOL 4-way handshake message is replayed. **4. RF Layer Analysis (1 mark):** Uses RSSI / SNR / noise floor for jamming detection. **5. Comparison (1 mark):** Signature is fast but limited to known attacks; anomaly handles novel threats but has higher false positives. --- #### Model Answer to Q (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute floor area. $$A_{\text{floor}} = 40 \times 30 = 1200 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute sensor coverage area. $$A_c = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (12)^2 = 144\pi \approx 452.39 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply overlap factor. $$\text{Effective Coverage} = A_c \cdot \eta = 452.39 \times 0.75 = 339.29 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute required sensors. $$N_s = \left\lceil \dfrac{1200}{339.29} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.537 \rceil = 4$$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Conclusion & Justification. - **4 sensors** must be deployed to ensure full coverage with 25% overlap for redundancy. - Placement strategy: one sensor at each corner of the rectangular floor, each at 12 m radius, providing overlapping coverage. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deduction Traps in Wireless IDPS Questions:** > 1. **Confusing Rogue AP with Evil Twin** — Many students write them as the same. Rogue AP = unauthorized AP on the wired LAN. Evil Twin = AP spoofing the legitimate SSID for client lure. *(−1 to −2 marks if merged)* > 2. **Forgetting the RF Domain** — In WIDPS questions, students often write TCP RST as response. WIDPS cannot inject TCP RST into the air interface — its response is via **802.11 deauthentication frames**. > 3. **Missing the "Prevention" Angle** — A pure IDPS detects; the WIDPS **prevents** by active containment. Examiners check for the word "prevention" explicitly. > 4. **Numerical Errors in FSPL** — Forgetting the constant **32.45 dB** in the simplified Friis formula. Always show the substituted version. > 5. **No mention of channel hopping** — A WIDPS sensor must dwell on all channels. Stating only one channel makes the answer incomplete. > 6. **Skipping the Whitelist/Whitelist DB** — Authorized device database is mandatory for false-positive reduction. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **WIDPS = NIDPS for the RF domain.** It detects threats at Layer 1 & 2 (802.11 frames + RF energy). - **Three-tier architecture:** Sensors (collect) → Analyzer (process) → Console (manage). - **Top 5 must-know attacks:** Rogue AP, Evil Twin, Deauth Flood, MAC Spoofing, RF Jamming. - **Detection methods:** Signature, Anomaly, State-based, RF-level — each catches different attack classes. - **Prevention actions:** Spoof deauth frame to attacker; shut wired switchport; MAC ACL push. - **Friis FSPL formula:** $\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.45$. - **Sensor count formula:** $N_s = \lceil A_{\text{floor}} / (\pi r^2 \eta) \rceil$. - **Deauth flood threshold:** Empirical ≈ **30 deauths/sec per BSSID** triggers alert. - **Always mention overlap factor η (0.6–0.8)** in deployment numerics. - **Always mention channel hopping & monitor mode** when describing sensor function. - **Distinguish carefully:** Rogue AP ≠ Evil Twin ≠ Honeypot AP ≠ Mis-association. - **Speed of light constant:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s appears in all FSPL derivations. - **Typical noise floor at 2.4 GHz:** ≈ **−100 dBm** — keep this in mind for SNR derivations. - **WIDPS cannot decrypt WPA2/WPA3** — relies on metadata + behavioural analysis, not payload inspection.

topics.title: WLAN Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** WLAN Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WLAN Standards** are a family of technical specifications defined by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) 802.11 Working Group** that govern the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers of wireless local area networks. These standards dictate operating frequency bands, channelization, modulation/coding schemes, maximum data rates, transmission range, and security mechanisms for Wi-Fi–compatible devices.

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames WLAN standards as the **baseline normative reference set** against which any Intrusion Detection / Prevention System (IDPS) for wireless networks must be evaluated. Anomaly-based and signature-based WIDS engines rely on knowing the exact frame formats, beacon intervals, and management-frame exchanges prescribed by 802.11.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Wireless Standards as a Highway System**

Imagine a city with multiple highways:
- The **highway code** (lanes, speed limits, vehicle types allowed) = the **802.11 standard**.
- The **road surface** (asphalt, concrete) = the **PHY layer** (radio modulation).
- The **traffic police at intersections** = the **MAC layer** (CSMA/CA, RTS/CTS).
- Different **highway editions** (NH-48 old 2-lane vs. new 6-lane expressway) = successive amendments (**802.11b → 802.11ax**).
- **Highway patrol cameras** that catch overspeeding or wrong-lane drivers = your **WIDS/WIPS sensors** (the reason this topic exists in IDPS).

Just as every new highway edition adds lanes, raises the speed limit, and changes vehicle rules, every 802.11 amendment extends bandwidth, increases modulation order, and tightens MAC procedures — and **every change creates new attack surfaces** that IDPS engineers must detect.

## 1.3 Standardization Bodies You Must Memorize

| Body | Role |
|---|---|
| **IEEE 802.11 WG** | Defines PHY + MAC specification (technical). |
| **Wi-Fi Alliance (WFA)** | Certifies interoperability; brands "Wi-Fi 4/5/6/6E/7". |
| **ITU-R** | Allocates the RF spectrum globally (2.4/5/6 GHz ISM/U-NII bands). |
| **FCC (USA) / ETSI (EU) / DoT (India)** | Regulate transmit power, DFS, channel availability. |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Lives Inside an IDPS Course

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every wireless intrusion signature — **deauth flood, Evil Twin, KRACK, fragmentation attack, OUI spoofing** — is written *against* a specific 802.11 amendment. A defender who does not know the standard cannot recognize the violation. KTU examiners expect you to map **attack → violated clause**.

## 1.5 Core Physical-Layer Constants

- **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **2.4 GHz band wavelength** $\lambda \approx 12.5\ \text{cm}$
- **5 GHz band wavelength** $\lambda \approx 6\ \text{cm}$
- **6 GHz band wavelength** $\lambda \approx 5\ \text{cm}$
- **ISM band (2.4–2.4835 GHz)** is license-free worldwide.
- **U-NII bands** (5.15–5.85 GHz, 5.925–7.125 GHz) require DFS + TPC in many regions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution Roadmap of the 802.11 Family

| Standard | Year | Band (GHz) | Max PHY Rate | Channel Width | Modulation | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **802.11 (legacy)** | 1997 | 2.4 | 2 Mbps | 20 MHz | DSSS / FHSS | Original Wi-Fi |
| **802.11a** | 1999 | **5** | 54 Mbps | 20 MHz | **OFDM** | First OFDM WLAN |
| **802.11b** | 1999 | 2.4 | 11 Mbps | 20 MHz | DSSS (CCK) | Longer range, cheaper |
| **802.11g** | 2003 | 2.4 | 54 Mbps | 20 MHz | OFDM | Backward compatible with b |
| **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 | 600 Mbps | 20/40 MHz | OFDM + **MIMO (4×4)** | First MIMO + frame aggregation |
| **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)** | 2013 | **5** | 6.93 Gbps | 20–160 MHz | OFDM + MU-MIMO (DL) | Wider channels, beamforming |
| **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E)** | 2019/2020 | 2.4 / 5 / **6** | 9.6 Gbps | 20–160 MHz | **OFDMA** + 1024-QAM | High density, BSS coloring |
| **802.11be (Wi-Fi 7)** | 2024 | 2.4 / 5 / 6 | 46 Gbps | up to 320 MHz | 4096-QAM + MLO | Multi-Link Operation |

## 2.2 Operational Logic — How a Wi-Fi Frame Traverses the Stack

1. **Logical Link Control (LLC)** hands an MSDU to the 802.11 MAC.
2. **MAC layer** adds header (Frame Control, Address fields, Duration/ID, Sequence, QoS) and an FCS — producing an **MPDU**.
3. MPDU is fragmented into **PPDUs** if it exceeds the fragmentation threshold.
4. **PHY layer** scrambles, encodes (convolutional / LDPC), interleaves, maps to constellation points (BPSK → QAM), prepends the **PLCP preamble + header**, and modulates onto OFDM subcarriers.
5. The RF frontend upconverts to the channel frequency and transmits.

> [!NOTE]
> An IDS sees this entire pipeline on the air. Beacon intervals, RTS/CTS exchanges, and modulation rates (signalled in HT/VHT/HE-SIG fields) are **observable**.

## 2.3 The Five Non-Negotiable KTU Formulae

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHEAT SHEET — Master these five before any derivation question.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \tfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon-Hartley** channel capacity | bits/s |
| **2** | $\lambda = \tfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength from carrier frequency | metres |
| **3** | $N_0 = k_B T$ | Thermal noise spectral density | W/Hz |
| **4** | $R_{\max} = N_{ss} \cdot N_{tx} \cdot (\text{coding rate}) \cdot N_{sub} \cdot \log_2 M \cdot \tfrac{1}{T_{sym}}$ | Max PHY rate (MIMO+OFDM) | bits/s |
| **5** | $E_b/N_0 = \tfrac{S/N}{R/B}$ | Energy per bit over noise spectral density | dimensionless (dB) |

Where:
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ — Boltzmann constant.
- $N_{ss}$ = spatial streams, $N_{tx}$ = transmit antennas.
- $N_{sub}$ = data subcarriers (48 for legacy OFDM, 234/980 for HT/VHT).
- $M$ = constellation size (M-QAM).
- $T_{sym}$ = OFDM symbol duration (4 µs legacy, 13.6 µs HT, 12.8 µs HE).

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Shannon's capacity** tells an IDPS designer the *theoretical ceiling* of a channel — if observed throughput grossly exceeds it, the frame is **spoofed**.
- **$\lambda = c/f$** determines antenna size and Fresnel-zone clearance — used when placing sensors to monitor a WLAN without coverage gaps.
- **$R_{\max}$** is the denominator against which **jamming throughput reduction** is measured during RF DoS attacks.

## 2.5 Logical Breakdown of an OFDM Symbol (for 802.11a/g/n/ac)

- **52 subcarriers** total → **48 data + 4 pilots**.
- Useful symbol duration $T_u = 3.2\ \mu\text{s}$ → guard interval $T_g = 0.8\ \mu\text{s}$ (¼ cyclic prefix).
- Total OFDM symbol $T_{sym} = T_u + T_g = 4\ \mu\text{s}$.
- Subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = \tfrac{1}{T_u} = 312.5\ \text{kHz}$.

## 2.6 Channelization Snapshot

| Band | Non-Overlapping Channels (India/ETSI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 2.4 GHz | **1, 5, 9, 13** (4 channels) | 22 MHz wide, 5 MHz spacing |
| 5 GHz | **UNII-1 (36–48), UNII-2 (52–64 DFS), UNII-2 Extended (100–144), UNII-3 (149–165), UNII-4 (169–173)** | DFS mandatory in UNII-2/A |
| 6 GHz | **59 × 20 MHz channels** (6E) | 1.2 GHz new spectrum |

## 2.7 Security Standards Attached to Each Generation

| Generation | Default Security | Crypto | KTU-Exam Favourite Attack |
|---|---|---|---|
| 802.11i (WPA) | RC4 + TKIP | Stream | Beck-Tews (TKIP replay) |
| 802.11i (WPA2) | AES-CCMP | Block | KRACK (4-way handshake) |
| 802.11ax-era (WPA3) | AES-GCMP + SAE | Block | Dragonblood (SAE timing) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Max PHY Rate of 802.11g (54 Mbps)

**Step 1 — Identify the modulation & coding for 54 Mbps mode.**

From the 802.11g rate table: **64-QAM with coding rate 3/4**.

**Step 2 — Bits per OFDM symbol.**

$$
N_{\text{bits/sym}} = N_{data} \cdot \log_2 M \cdot R_c
$$

Substituting values: 48 subcarriers × 6 bits (64-QAM) × 3/4:

$$
N_{\text{bits/sym}} = 48 \times 6 \times \tfrac{3}{4} = 216\ \text{bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 3 — Symbol rate.**

$$
\text{Symbol rate} = \tfrac{1}{T_{sym}} = \tfrac{1}{4\ \mu\text{s}} = 250\,000\ \text{symbols/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$
R_{\max} = 216 \times 250\,000 = 54\,000\,000\ \text{bits/s} = \mathbf{54\ Mbps} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Capacity of a 2.4 GHz Channel (20 MHz, SNR = 30 dB)

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale.**

$$
\tfrac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley.**

$$
C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)
$$

$$
C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1001)
$$

$$
\log_2(1001) = \tfrac{\ln(1001)}{\ln(2)} \approx \tfrac{6.9088}{0.6931} \approx 9.9672
$$

$$
C = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 9.9672 \approx \mathbf{199.34\ Mbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **802.11g's real rate (54 Mbps) reaches only ~27% of the Shannon ceiling**. This efficiency gap is what later amendments (n, ac, ax) close using wider channels, MIMO, and denser constellations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 802.11n 600 Mbps (40 MHz, 4×4 MIMO, 64-QAM, 5/6)

**Step 1 — Bits per OFDM symbol per spatial stream.**

HT 40 MHz has **108 data subcarriers**.

$$
N_{\text{bits/sym/ss}} = 108 \times 6 \times \tfrac{5}{6} = 540\ \text{bits/symbol/stream}
$$

**Step 2 — Short GI (400 ns) symbol time.**

$$
T_{sym}^{SGI} = 3.6\ \mu\text{s} \Rightarrow \text{symbol rate} = \tfrac{1}{3.6\ \mu\text{s}} \approx 277\,778\ \text{sym/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 4 spatial streams.**

$$
R_{\max} = 540 \times 277\,778 \times 4
$$

$$
R_{\max} = 540 \times 1\,111\,112 \approx \mathbf{600\ Mbps} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy-per-Bit for a Wi-Fi Frame

**Step 1 — Start with Eb/N0 definition.**

$$
\tfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \tfrac{S/N}{R/B}
$$

**Step 2 — Suppose S = −65 dBm, N = −95 dBm, R = 54 Mbps, B = 20 MHz.**

Convert powers to linear (mW):
- $S = 10^{-6.5}\ \text{mW} \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{mW}$
- $N = 10^{-9.5}\ \text{mW} \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{mW}$

$$
\tfrac{S}{N} = \tfrac{3.16 \times 10^{-7}}{3.16 \times 10^{-10}} = 1000 \equiv 30\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\tfrac{R}{B} = \tfrac{54 \times 10^{6}}{20 \times 10^{6}} = 2.7 \equiv 4.31\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\tfrac{E_b}{N_0} = 30 - 4.31 = \mathbf{25.69\ dB}
$$

> A receiver with Eb/N0 ≥ 25 dB can decode 64-QAM 3/4 with **Block Error Rate < 10%**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Maximum 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) Theoretical Rate

For HE SU on 160 MHz, 8×8 MIMO, 1024-QAM, 5/6:

$$
N_{data}^{HE160} = 980\ \text{subcarriers}
$$

$$
N_{\text{bits/sym/ss}} = 980 \times 10 \times \tfrac{5}{6} \approx 8167\ \text{bits}
$$

With $T_{sym}^{HE} = 12.8\ \mu\text{s} + 0.8\ \mu\text{s} = 13.6\ \mu\text{s}$ and 8 spatial streams:

$$
R_{\max} = 8167 \times \tfrac{1}{13.6 \times 10^{-6}} \times 8 \approx 4.80\ \text{Gbps}
$$

The 802.11ax marketing figure of 9.6 Gbps assumes additional enhancements (higher MCS, RU aggregation across users).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | 802.11b | 802.11g | 802.11n | 802.11ac | 802.11ax |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Rate | 11 M | 54 M | 600 M | 6.93 G | 9.6 G |
| Band | 2.4 | 2.4 | 2.4/5 | 5 | 2.4/5/6 |
| Ch. Width | 20 | 20 | 20/40 | 20–160 | 20–160 |
| Modulation | CCK | 64-QAM | 64-QAM | 256-QAM | 1024-QAM |
| MIMO | 1×1 | 1×1 | 4×4 | 8×8 MU | 8×8 MU + OFDMA |
| Subcarriers | 1 (DSSS) | 48 | 52/108 | 52–234 | 234/980/1960 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 802.11 Protocol Stack — Reference Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appL[APPLICATION LAYER - HTTP FTP SMTP]
    transL[TRANSPORT LAYER - TCP UDP]
    netL[NETWORK LAYER - IP]
    llcL[LOGICAL LINK CONTROL - IEEE 802.2]
    macL[MAC SUBLAYER - IEEE 802.11 - CSMA_CA Fragmentation Encryption]
    phyL[PHY SUBLAYER - PLCP + PMD - Modulation Coding RF]
    antL[ANTENNA AND PROPAGATION CHANNEL]

    appL --> transL
    transL --> netL
    netL --> llcL
    llcL --> macL
    macL --> phyL
    phyL --> antL

    classDef stack fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef mac fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    classDef phy fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef rf fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000

    class appL,transL,netL,llcL stack
    class macL mac
    class phyL phy
    class antL rf
```

## 4.2 CSMA/CA + RTS/CTS Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA_A as Station A
    participant AP as Access Point
    participant STA_B as Station B

    STA_A->>STA_A: Sense channel for DIFS
    STA_A->>STA_A: Channel idle - start backoff
    STA_A->>AP: RTS - Request To Send
    AP->>STA_B: CTS - Clear To Send
    Note over STA_A,STA_B: All other stations set NAV
    STA_A->>AP: DATA frame
    AP->>STA_A: ACK after SIFS
```

## 4.3 802.11 Frame Format (General)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Frame Control 2 bytes] --> F2[Duration or ID 2 bytes]
    F2 --> F3[Address 1 6 bytes]
    F3 --> F4[Address 2 6 bytes]
    F4 --> F5[Address 3 6 bytes]
    F5 --> F6[Seq Control 2 bytes]
    F6 --> F7[Address 4 6 bytes optional]
    F7 --> F8[QoS Control 2 bytes optional]
    F8 --> F9[Frame Body 0 to 2312 bytes]
    F9 --> F10[FCS 4 bytes CRC32]

    classDef head fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    classDef body fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    classDef tail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828

    class F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F6,F7,F8 head
    class F9 body
    class F10 tail
```

## 4.4 OFDM Transmitter Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Bits] --> A2[Scrambler]
    A2 --> A3[Convolutional Encoder or LDPC]
    A3 --> A4[Interleaver]
    A4 --> A5[Constellation Mapper BPSK QPSK QAM]
    A5 --> A6[Pilot Insertion]
    A6 --> A7[IFFT 64 point 128 point]
    A7 --> A8[Add Cyclic Prefix]
    A8 --> A9[Windowing and DAC]
    A9 --> A10[RF Upconversion to 2.4 5 or 6 GHz]

    classDef dsp fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17
    classDef rf fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A9 dsp
    class A10 rf
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[802.11b 1999 11 Mbps DSSS CCK] --> G2[802.11a and g 1999 2003 54 Mbps OFDM 64 QAM]
    G2 --> G3[802.11n Wi Fi 4 2009 600 Mbps MIMO 4x4]
    G3 --> G4[802.11ac Wi Fi 5 2013 6.93 Gbps MU MIMO DL 256 QAM]
    G4 --> G5[802.11ax Wi Fi 6 and 6E 2019 9.6 Gbps OFDMA 1024 QAM]
```

```mermaid

    A3[KRACK] -->|replays handshake| S3[802.11i 4 Way]
```
