# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**List any three differences between IEEE 802.11b and IEEE 802.11a standards.**

**Model Answer (3 key points):**

1. **Frequency band:** 802.11b operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**, whereas 802.11a operates in the **5 GHz U-NII band**.
2. **Modulation:** 802.11b uses **DSSS with CCK**, while 802.11a uses **OFDM with up to 64-QAM**.
3. **Maximum data rate:** 802.11b supports **11 Mbps**, while 802.11a supports **54 Mbps**. (Additional valid point: 802.11a has shorter range and stricter DFS requirements.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the purpose of the cyclic prefix in OFDM-based WLAN standards such as 802.11a/g/n?**

**Model Answer (3 sentences):**

The cyclic prefix (CP) is a copy of the tail of each OFDM symbol prepended to its beginning. Its purpose is to **absorb multipath delay spread** so that inter-symbol interference (ISI) is eliminated when the CP duration exceeds the channel's maximum excess delay. In 802.11a/g, the CP is **0.8 µs** long (¼ of the 3.2 µs useful symbol), making the system robust to delay spreads up to ~250 ns in indoor environments.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the OFDM transmitter functional chain for an IEEE 802.11a/g compliant WLAN. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the maximum achievable PHY data rate of IEEE 802.11g (54 Mbps mode). State clearly all subcarrier counts, modulation order, coding rate, and OFDM symbol duration used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

The OFDM transmitter chain for 802.11a/g consists of the following functional blocks (refer to **Section 4.4 diagram**):

1. **Scrambler** — XORs input bits with a 127-bit pseudo-random sequence to prevent long runs of 0s or 1s that would break timing recovery.
2. **Forward Error Correction (FEC) encoder** — convolutional encoder with rate **½**, **2/3**, or **¾** depending on the data rate. Puncturing achieves the higher rates.
3. **Interleaver** — spreads burst errors across the coded bit stream so the convolutional Viterbi decoder can correct them.
4. **Constellation mapper** — maps groups of bits onto BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, or 64-QAM symbols.
5. **Pilot insertion** — adds 4 known pilot subcarriers (subcarrier indices −21, −7, +7, +21) for channel estimation and tracking.
6. **IFFT (64-point)** — maps 52 subcarriers (48 data + 4 pilots) onto the time domain; remaining 12 are null (DC + guard bands).
7. **Cyclic prefix insertion** — copies the last 16 samples (0.8 µs) of each 64-sample IFFT output and prepends them.
8. **Windowing + DAC** — smooths symbol transitions to reduce spectral sidelobes.
9. **RF upconverter** — translates the baseband signal to 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz carrier.

**Mapping to marks (typical KTU split):**
- Block identification — 2 marks
- Function of each block — 3 marks
- Neat diagram — 2 marks

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

We apply the generic PHY rate formula:

$$
R_{\max} = N_{data} \cdot \log_2 M \cdot R_c \cdot \tfrac{1}{T_{sym}}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the rate-54 Mbps parameters from the 802.11g specification:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Modulation ($M$) | 64-QAM → $\log_2 M = 6$ bits/symbol |
| Coding rate ($R_c$) | 3/4 |
| Data subcarriers ($N_{data}$) | 48 |
| Useful OFDM time ($T_u$) | 3.2 µs |
| Cyclic prefix ($T_g$) | 0.8 µs |
| Total symbol time ($T_{sym}$) | $T_u + T_g = 4.0\ \mu\text{s}$ |

**Step 2 — Bits per OFDM symbol:**

$$
N_{\text{bits/sym}} = 48 \times 6 \times \tfrac{3}{4} = 216\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Symbol rate:**

$$
\text{Symbol rate} = \tfrac{1}{4.0\ \mu\text{s}} = 250\,000\ \text{symbols/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
R_{\max} = 216 \times 250\,000 = 54\,000\,000\ \text{bits/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\max} = 54\ \text{Mbps}} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Do **not** use 52 subcarriers for data — 4 are pilots, leaving **48**.
> - Do **not** forget to include the cyclic prefix in $T_{sym}$ (use **4 µs**, not 3.2 µs).
> - Do **not** confuse **PHY rate** (54 Mbps) with **MAC throughput** (~22–25 Mbps after CSMA/CA back-off and ACK overhead).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare IEEE 802.11n and IEEE 802.11ac with respect to (i) maximum channel width, (ii) MIMO configuration, (iii) highest modulation order, (iv) supported frequency band, and (v) maximum achievable PHY rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 20 MHz Wi-Fi channel has a measured signal power of **−65 dBm** and a noise floor of **−95 dBm**. Compute (i) the SNR in dB, (ii) the Shannon-Hartley channel capacity, and (iii) comment on why the achievable throughput is well below this capacity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

| # | Parameter | 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Max channel width | **40 MHz** | **160 MHz** (80+80 contiguous) |
| (ii) | MIMO | Up to **4×4** SU-MIMO | Up to **8×8** MU-MIMO (DL only in Wave 1; UL added in Wave 2) |
| (iii) | Modulation | Up to **64-QAM** | Up to **256-QAM** |
| (iv) | Frequency band | 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz | **5 GHz only** |
| (v) | Max PHY rate | **600 Mbps** | **6.93 Gbps** |

**Why 802.11ac is faster — one-line insight:** wider channels × more spatial streams × denser constellation = $\sim 11.5\times$ throughput over 802.11n.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — SNR in dB.**

$$
\text{SNR (dB)} = P_{signal} - P_{noise} = -65 - (-95) = \mathbf{30\ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert SNR to linear scale.**

$$
\tfrac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 1000
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \tfrac{S}{N}\right) = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1001)
$$

$$
\log_2(1001) \approx 9.967
$$

$$
C \approx 20 \times 10^{6} \times 9.967 \approx \mathbf{199.34\ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on achievable throughput.**

The actual MAC throughput is only about **27% of Shannon's limit** (~54 Mbps for 802.11g, ~75 Mbps for 802.11n SISO). The gap exists because:

1. Practical modulation (up to 64-QAM / 256-QAM) does not reach the Gaussian-input optimum that Shannon assumed.
2. **CSMA/CA contention** introduces back-off idle time and collision-induced retransmissions.
3. **MAC/PHY overheads** — preamble, PLCP header, ACK, inter-frame spaces (DIFS/SIFS), block ACKs, RTS/CTS — consume airtime.
4. **Imperfect channel coding** — convolutional/LDPC codes have non-zero gap to capacity.
5. **Half-duplex radios** cannot simultaneously transmit and receive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **add dB values directly** to convert SNR to linear. Use $10^{\text{dB}/10}$ for power ratios.
> - Do **not** confuse **bandwidth $B$** (20 MHz) with **data rate $R$** (Mbps).
> - Forgetting to **mention CSMA/CA** when asked "why throughput < capacity" costs 1–2 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before Every KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Two foundational bodies:** **IEEE 802.11** (technical spec) and **Wi-Fi Alliance** (certification/branding).
- ✅ **Three operational bands:** **2.4 GHz** (range + congestion), **5 GHz** (capacity), **6 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E — clean spectrum).
- ✅ **Four modulation families:** DSSS (b) → OFDM (a/g/n/ac/ax) → OFDMA (ax) → MLO (be).
- ✅ **Five equations to master:** Shannon, $\lambda=c/f$, $R_{\max}$ formula, $E_b/N_0$, $N_0 = k_B T$.
- ✅ **48 data subcarriers** in legacy OFDM, **108** for HT40, **234** for VHT40, **980** for HE160.
- ✅ **802.11g = 54 Mbps** = $48 \times 6 \times \tfrac{3}{4} \times 250\text{k}$.
- ✅ **802.11n max = 600 Mbps** requires **4×4 MIMO + 40 MHz + 64-QAM 5/6 + SGI**.
- ✅ **802.11ac max = 6.93 Gbps** with **8×8 MU-MIMO + 160 MHz + 256-QAM**.
- ✅ **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)** adds **OFDMA** for multi-user scheduling and **BSS coloring** for spatial reuse.
- ✅ **WPA3 uses SAE (Dragonfly)** instead of PSK 4-way handshake — defends against offline dictionary attacks.
- ✅ **Cyclic prefix length = 0.8 µs** (¼ of useful 3.2 µs in 802.11a/g) — guards against ISI from multipath.
- ✅ **Channelization memory tip:** Only **1, 6, 11** were popular in 2.4 GHz (US); ETSI adds **13**; many modern routers auto-select.
- ✅ **Always remember:** PHY rate ≠ MAC throughput — PHY is the marketing number; MAC throughput is ~40–60% of PHY.
- ✅ **WIDS link:** Every wireless attack targets a specific clause — know the clause, know the attack, write the signature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WLAN Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** WLAN Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a WLAN? (Formal Definition)

A **Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)** is a flexible data communications system that uses **Radio Frequency (RF)** technology, specifically spread-spectrum modulation in the **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz** Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) bands, to transmit and receive data over the air. The most widely adopted family of standards governing WLANs is the **IEEE 802.11** suite (commonly branded as **Wi-Fi®** by the Wi-Fi Alliance).

From an **Intrusion Detection & Prevention System (IDPS)** perspective, a WLAN is uniquely vulnerable because the medium (the open RF spectrum) is inherently **broadcast in nature** — anyone with a compatible wireless adapter within range can potentially sniff, inject, or disrupt traffic, which makes understanding its components critical for designing detection sensors and prevention mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT742 – Module 3):**
> WLAN components form the **physical and link-layer building blocks** that an IDPS must monitor. The placement of IDS/IPS sensors, rogue AP detection engines, and wireless packet analyzers directly depends on understanding the role of each component listed below.

## 1.2 The Core WLAN Component Set

A functional WLAN is composed of **four essential element categories** plus several **supporting/security elements**. The following is the canonical decomposition used in KTU reference material:

| # | Component Category | Primary Function | IDPS Relevance |
|---|--------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Stations (STAs)** / Wireless NICs | End-user devices that participate in the WLAN | Source of legitimate/attack traffic |
| 2 | **Access Points (APs)** | RF-to-Ethernet bridge (Layer 2) | Trust anchor; rogue AP target |
| 3 | **Antennas** | Radiate / capture electromagnetic energy | Define the wireless attack boundary |
| 4 | **Wireless LAN Controllers (WLCs)** | Centralized AP management & policy | Enforce IDPS policy globally |
| 5 | **Authentication Server (RADIUS/AAA)** | Identity verification (802.1X/EAP) | First line of NAC defense |
| 6 | **Distribution System (DS)** | Wired backbone connecting APs | Backhaul for sensor data |
| 7 | **Security Sensors (WIPS/WIDS)** | Detect/prevent wireless attacks | The IDPS layer itself |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building Telephone" Model

Imagine an old-style **office switchboard telephone system**:
- The **wireless devices** (laptops, phones) are like **employees** wanting to make calls.
- The **Access Point (AP)** is the **switchboard operator** sitting in the middle of the room, listening for someone who "picks up a phone" and then connecting them to the right line.
- The **antenna** is the **operator's ears and voice** — a bigger/quieter room (high-gain antenna) means the operator can hear further and be heard further.
- The **WLC** is the **head supervisor** who tells each operator which extensions to use and checks whether an operator is behaving suspiciously.
- The **AAA Server (RADIUS)** is the **security desk at the entrance** that verifies the ID badge of every "employee" before they are allowed to use the phone system.
- The **WIPS sensor** is the **plain-clothes security guard** walking around listening to conversations to spot imposters, eavesdroppers, or anyone talking nonsense (DoS).

This analogy keeps the abstract networking components intuitive and memorable for KTU viva-voce and short-answer questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the KTU exam:** **"SAAWA"** → **S**tations, **A**Ps, **A**ntennas, **W**LC, **A**AA — the five essential WLAN components. The rest (DS, sensors) are *supporting* elements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Component Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Wireless Stations (STAs) — The Clients

A **station (STA)** is any device containing a **wireless network interface controller (WNIC)** that conforms to the IEEE 802.11 standard. STAs can be of three sub-types:

- **Laptops / Desktops** with PCIe/M.2 Wi-Fi cards.
- **Mobile devices** (smartphones, tablets) using embedded SoC radios.
- **IoT/Embedded devices** (printers, sensors, badge readers, IP cameras).

Each STA has a globally unique **48-bit MAC address** burned into the radio, which serves as its Layer-2 identity. STAs operate in two logical modes:

1. **Infrastructure mode** — STA communicates only via an AP (the 99% real-world case).
2. **Ad-hoc (IBSS) mode** — STAs form a peer-to-peer mesh without an AP. IDPS-wise, IBSS networks are **high-risk** because most enterprise WIPS sensors cannot see into them.

**Why this matters for IDPS:** The STA's **driver/firmware** is the first place where **deauthentication, disassociation, or evil-twin** attacks are launched. A WIDS sensor fingerprints STA behavior (probe request patterns, roaming signatures) to detect anomalies.

---

### 2.1.2 Access Points (APs) — The WLAN Gateway

An **Access Point** is a Layer-2 bridging device that:
1. Translates between the **802.11 (wireless)** and **802.3 (Ethernet)** MAC/PHY layers.
2. Performs **beaconing** (broadcasts an SSID every ~100 ms by default, called the **Beacon Interval**).
3. Manages **association** and **re-association** state machines.
4. Optionally performs **encryption** (WPA2/WPA3) and **frame translation**.

**AP Classification:**

| Type | Description | Typical Use | IDPS Note |
|------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| **FAT (Autonomous) AP** | Self-contained; config stored locally | Small offices, home | Hard to monitor centrally |
| **FIT (Lightweight) AP** | Config pushed by a WLC; tunneled via CAPWAP | Enterprise campus | Centralized WIPS possible |
| **Cloud-Managed AP** | Managed via a public cloud controller | Distributed branches | Logs forwarded to cloud SIEM |
| **Rogue AP** | Unauthorized AP (intentional or accidental) | N/A | **Primary WIPS detection target** |

---

### 2.1.3 Antennas — The RF Boundary

The **antenna** converts electrical signals (on a copper trace) into electromagnetic waves (in free space) and vice versa. Two key parameters govern its behavior:

- **Gain (dBi)** — Ability to focus RF energy in a particular direction. *Higher = more directional and longer reach*.
- **Radiation Pattern** — A 3D plot of radiated power vs. angle.

| Antenna Type | Pattern | Typical Gain | IDPS Implication |
|--------------|---------|--------------|------------------|
| **Omnidirectional (Dipole)** | Donut-shaped, 360° | 2–5 dBi | Standard ceiling AP; defines symmetric cell |
| **Yagi / Patch (Directional)** | Narrow beam | 8–18 dBi | Point-to-point backhaul; creates elongated cell |
| **Sector** | 60°–120° fan | 10–14 dBi | Outdoor campus coverage |
| **Parabolic Dish** | Pencil beam | 20–30 dBi | Long-range PtP links |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IDPS Insight:** A **rogue AP** can be detected only if its RF signal falls within the WIPS sensor's antenna coverage. Antenna selection directly determines the **attack surface visibility** of your WIPS.

---

### 2.1.4 Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) — The Central Brain

The **WLC** is the centralized management plane in an enterprise WLAN. It performs:
- **CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points)** tunneling — RFC 5415/5416.
- **Radio Resource Management (RRM)** — dynamic channel/power assignment.
- **Client load balancing** across APs.
- **Policy enforcement** (ACLs, QoS, firewall rules pushed to APs).
- **Aggregation of WIDS events** from multiple APs/sensors.

For IDPS, the WLC is the **single pane of glass** — all anomaly events, rogue detections, and containment decisions (deauthenticate the rogue, block the STA) are orchestrated here.

---

### 2.1.5 AAA / Authentication Server (RADIUS)

In **802.1X port-based access control**, three logical roles exist:

- **Supplicant** — the STA requesting access.
- **Authenticator** — the AP (or WLC) acting as the gatekeeper.
- **Authentication Server** — the **RADIUS** (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service) or **TACACS+** server that holds the credential database.

Protocols used: **EAP-TLS, EAP-TTLS, PEAP, EAP-FAST**. The choice of EAP method directly affects IDPS visibility — **EAP-TLS** with certificates is the most attack-resistant.

---

### 2.1.6 Distribution System (DS)

The DS is the **wired Ethernet backbone** (along with Layer-2/3 switches and routers) that interconnects all APs and the WLC. In CAPWAP-based architectures, all client traffic may be **tunneled** through the WLC over the DS using CAPWAP data channels.

---

### 2.1.7 WIPS / WIDS Sensors (Security-Specific Components)

These are the **dedicated IDPS sensors** for the wireless layer:
- **WIDS** (Wireless Intrusion Detection System) — passive monitoring, alerting only.
- **WIPS** (Wireless Intrusion Prevention System) — adds active countermeasures (rogue containment via deauth frames).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although WLAN engineering is conceptually dominant, the following **RF and capacity formulas** are routinely asked in KTU Part-A 3-mark and Part-B 14-mark questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Friis Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $\text{FSPL}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ = distance, $f$ = frequency | d in km, f in MHz, result in dB |
| 2 | **Received Signal Power** | $P_{r} = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - \text{FSPL} - L_{c}$ | $P_t$ = TX power, $G_t$/$G_r$ = gains, $L_c$ = cable loss | All in dB/dBm |
| 3 | **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP)** | $\text{EIRP}_{dBm} = P_{t} + G_{t} - L_{c}$ | Total radiated power from antenna | dBm |
| 4 | **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = SNR | C in bps, B in Hz |
| 5 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = P_{r} - N_{floor}$ | $N_{floor} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$ | dB |
| 6 | **Link Budget** | $\text{Link Margin} = P_{r} - \text{Receiver Sensitivity}$ | Receiver sensitivity in dBm | dB |
| 7 | **Receiver Sensitivity (thermal noise)** | $S_{dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + N_{F} + \text{SNR}_{req}$ | $N_F$ = noise figure, $\text{SNR}_{req}$ = required SNR | dBm |
| 8 | **Cell Radius (simplified, free-space)** | $R \approx 10^{\frac{P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - S_{min} - 32.44 - 20\log_{10}(f)}{20}}$ | Plug-in form of FSPL | km |
| 9 | **Frame Transmission Time** | $T_{tx} = \frac{\text{Payload} + \text{Headers}}{\text{Data Rate}}$ | 802.11n: add **A-MPDU** aggregation | μs / ms |
| 10 | **802.11 Throughput (effective)** | $\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Payload}}{\text{Frame Air-Time}}$ | Includes DIFS, SIFS, ACK, contention | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Warning:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ in a markdown table cell, **never** use the `|` pipe character. Use the LaTeX form `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid corrupting the table grid.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Formulas in IDPS Engineering

- **EIRP** and **FSPL** together predict **how far an attacker's signal can reach**, letting a WIPS engineer place sensors at the **cell boundary** so they hear the attacker *before* the legitimate AP does.
- **Shannon capacity** sets the **upper bound on detection data rate** a sensor can backhaul over the air.
- **Link margin** defines the **minimum SNR** at which the WIPS can still reliably decode (and therefore classify) an 802.11 management frame such as a Deauth flood.
- The **frame air-time** calculation is essential for sizing **sensor backhaul capacity** when WIDS logs are tunneled over CAPWAP to the WLC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

The Friis transmission equation describes how received power falls off with distance in free space.

**Step 1 — Start with the isotropic radiator power density.**
For an ideal isotropic antenna transmitting power $P_t$, the power density at distance $d$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

*Logic:* Power is spread uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$, whose area is $4\pi d^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Account for transmit antenna gain.**
A real antenna focuses energy. With transmit gain $G_t$ (linear, not dB):

$$S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the effective aperture of the receive antenna.**
The effective aperture $A_{e}$ of a receive antenna is related to its gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$ by:

$$A_{e} = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Received power is the product of density and aperture.**

$$P_{r} = S \cdot A_{e} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB form.**

$$P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Isolate the path-loss term.**
Define FSPL as the loss when $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic):

$$\text{FSPL}_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

*Logic:* Substituting $\lambda = c/f$ and the constants $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, the numeric term collapses to 32.44 when $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Receiver Sensitivity

A wireless receiver needs a minimum SNR to decode a given modulation and coding scheme (MCS).

**Step 1 — Thermal noise floor.**

$$N_{thermal} = kTB$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant), $T$ = temperature in K.

**Step 2 — Convert to dBm at 290 K (standard noise temperature).**

$$N_{dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$$

*Logic:* $10\log_{10}(1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290) = -204$ dBW = **-174 dBm/Hz**; adding the bandwidth in dB-Hz yields the total noise.

**Step 3 — Add the receiver's own noise figure $N_F$ (in dB).**
This accounts for amplifier/filter thermal noise inside the radio.

**Step 4 — Add the required SNR for the chosen MCS.**

$$S_{min,dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + N_{F} + \text{SNR}_{req}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
For an AP with $B = 20$ MHz channel, $N_F = 6$ dB, and $\text{SNR}_{req} = 25$ dB (needed for 64-QAM 3/4 in 802.11n):

$$S_{min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(20 \times 10^{6}) + 6 + 25$$
$$S_{min} = -174 + 73 + 6 + 25 = -70 \text{ dBm}$$

**Interpretation:** The receiver needs at least **-70 dBm** to decode 64-QAM. A WIPS sensor at the cell edge must operate at *this* sensitivity to maintain equivalent detection coverage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component-to-Attack Mapping (For KTU 14-Mark Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** where students are asked *"Map WLAN components to the attacks they enable or detect."*

| WLAN Component | Attack Enabled (if compromised) | Attack Detected (IDPS role) | IDPS Mechanism |
|----------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| **STA / WNIC** | Deauth flood, MAC spoofing, packet injection | Malicious STA behavior, evil twin client | WIDS signature & behavioral analytics |
| **Access Point** | Rogue AP, evil twin, AP impersonation | Rogue AP, channel abuse | WIPS — RF fingerprinting, RRM anomalies |
| **Antenna (Yagi/High-gain)** | Long-range war-driving, directional DoS | None directly | Defines sensor visibility footprint |
| **WLC** | Compromise ⇒ all APs compromised | Centralized policy violation logging | SIEM integration, CAPWAP log analysis |
| **AAA / RADIUS** | Credential brute-force, EAP downgrade | Auth-failure storms, EAP downgrade attempts | 802.1X-aware WIDS, AAA server log SIEM rules |
| **Distribution System (DS)** | VLAN hopping, CAPWAP MITM, sniffing on backhaul | Encrypted traffic anomaly, lateral movement | Wired-side NDR/IPS at the DS switches |
| **WIDS/WIPS Sensor** | (Sensor-blind) jamming — bypasses all | Evil twin, deauth, rogue, jamming | RF spectrum analysis + signature DB |

---

## 3.4 Derivative Worked Example — Link Budget for a WIPS Sensor

**Problem:** An enterprise AP transmits at $P_t = 20$ dBm on channel 36 (5.18 GHz). The WIPS sensor uses an omnidirectional antenna with $G_t = G_r = 4$ dBi. Cable loss $L_c = 2$ dB. The WIPS receiver noise figure is $N_F = 7$ dB, bandwidth $B = 20$ MHz, required $\text{SNR} = 20$ dB. Find:
1. The maximum sensor range for reliable detection.
2. The minimum detectable signal of the WIPS sensor.

**Step 1 — Compute sensor minimum detectable signal (sensitivity).**

$$S_{min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(20 \times 10^{6}) + 7 + 20$$
$$S_{min} = -174 + 73 + 7 + 20 = -74 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute EIRP.**

$$\text{EIRP} = 20 + 4 - 2 = 22 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 3 — Invert FSPL to find range.**
At max range, $P_r = S_{min} = -74$ dBm, and:

$$P_r = \text{EIRP} + G_r - \text{FSPL}$$
$$\text{FSPL} = 22 + 4 - (-74) = 100 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Solve FSPL for $d$.**

$$100 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(5180) + 32.44$$
$$20\log_{10}(d) = 100 - 74.29 - 32.44 = -6.73$$
$$\log_{10}(d) = -0.3365 \Rightarrow d = 10^{-0.3365} \approx 0.461 \text{ km} = 461 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation for IDPS.**
The WIPS sensor can detect the AP at ranges up to **~461 m** in free space. In a real indoor office (with walls, multipath), this drops to roughly **30–60 m** — which is why multiple sensors are required per floor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End WLAN Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sta1["STA Laptop"]
    sta2["STA Smartphone"]
    sta3["STA IoT Camera"]
    ap1["AP Lightweight Ceiling Mount"]
    ap2["AP Outdoor Sector Antenna"]
    ant1["Omnidirectional 2dBi Antenna"]
    ant2["Yagi 14dBi Directional Antenna"]
    wlc["Wireless LAN Controller WLC"]
    aaa["RADIUS AAA Server"]
    sw["Distribution System L3 Switch"]
    wips["WIPS Sensor RF Tap"]
    cloud["Cloud SIEM and SOC"]
    nms["Network Management System NMS"]

    sta1 -- 802.11 association and auth --> ap1
    sta2 -- 802.11 association and auth --> ap1
    sta3 -- 802.11 association and auth --> ap2
    ap1 --- ant1
    ap2 --- ant2
    ap1 -- CAPWAP tunnel control and data --> wlc
    ap2 -- CAPWAP tunnel control and data --> wlc
    wlc -- RADIUS packets UDP 1812 and 1813 --> aaa
    wlc -- Ethernet backhaul --> sw
    aaa -- LDAP or AD sync --> sw
    wips -- RF sniffing and spectrum analysis --> cloud
    wlc -- Syslog and SNMP traps --> nms
    nms -- Forward alerts --> cloud
    sw -- NetFlow and SPAN --> cloud
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU viva tip):**
- Solid arrows = **data plane** traffic (user frames, CAPWAP data, NetFlow).
- Dashed-equivalent edges (the WIPS sensor link) = **out-of-band monitoring** that does not sit in the user data path.
- The **WLC is the central policy point**; the **RADIUS is the central identity point**; the **SIEM is the central analytics point**.

---

## 4.2 IDPS Sensor Placement Around WLAN Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph enterpriseCell["Enterprise WLAN Cell"]
        direction TB
        ap["Access Point"]
        c1["Client 1"]
        c2["Client 2"]
        rogue["Rogue AP Attacker"]
        ap --- c1
        ap --- c2
    end

    subgraph idpsLayer["IDPS Sensor Layer"]
        direction TB
        sensor1["WIPS Sensor 1 Ceiling"]
        sensor2["WIPS Sensor 2 Wall"]
    end

    subgraph decisionPlane["Decision Plane"]
        direction TB
        correlator["SIEM Correlation Engine"]
        console["SOC Console"]
    end

    sensor1 -- passive 802.11 frames --> correlator
    sensor2 -- passive 802.11 frames --> correlator
    rogue -- RF energy within range --> sensor1
    rogue -- RF energy within range --> sensor2
    correlator --> console
```

**Engineering note:** Sensors are placed so that their **omnidirectional coverage overlaps** with the AP cell. The dashed-style edges denote that the WIPS sensor is **listening only** — it is not in the user traffic path, which makes it resilient to direct DoS.

---

## 4.3 Logical Layering of WLAN + IDPS Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer5["Layer 5 SOC and SIEM Cross Correlation"]
    layer4["Layer 4 WIPS Central Policy Engine on WLC"]
    layer3["Layer 3 RF Air Interface 802.11 Frame Analysis"]
    layer2["Layer 2 Sensor Mesh Wired Backhaul"]
    layer1["Layer 1 Physical Antennas and Spectrum Sensors"]

    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1
```

This bottom-up stack matches the **OSI-aligned sensor hierarchy** commonly tested in KTU 14-mark questions on "Discuss the layered architecture of a WIDS/WIPS deployment."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: List and briefly explain any three essential components of a WLAN.**

**Model Answer (3 key points, 1 mark each):**

1. **Stations (STAs):** End-user devices equipped with an 802.11-compliant wireless network interface card. They generate and consume the user data traffic and are identified by their unique 48-bit MAC address.
2. **Access Points (APs):** Layer-2 bridge devices that connect the wireless 802.11 medium to the wired 802.3 Ethernet distribution system. They perform beaconing, association, and frame translation.
3. **Wireless LAN Controller (WLC):** The central management device that controls lightweight APs via the CAPWAP protocol, enforces security/QoS policies, and aggregates WIDS events for the enterprise.

*(Any other valid component such as AAA server, antennas, DS, or WIPS sensor with a 1-sentence explanation is also acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
**Q: Differentiate between an Autonomous (FAT) AP and a Lightweight (FIT) AP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form recommended):**

| Parameter | FAT (Autonomous) AP | FIT (Lightweight) AP |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Configuration** | Standalone; config stored locally | Config pushed by a central WLC |
| **Management** | Managed individually | Managed centrally via CAPWAP |
| **Scalability** | Low (≤ a few APs) | High (hundreds to thousands) |
| **Roaming** | Slow Layer-2 roaming | Fast, controller-assisted roaming |
| **IDPS Suitability** | Poor — no central event correlation | Excellent — WLC aggregates WIDS events |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Drawing a WLAN diagram without labeling all components** — up to **2 marks lost** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing 802.11 (WLAN) with 802.15 (Bluetooth) or 802.16 (WiMAX)** — **1–2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Omitting the role of the WLC/AAA** when asked about *"components"* — students often list only AP and antenna, **losing 3–4 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting units (dBi vs dBd vs dBm)** in any formula derivation — **1 mark** is docked per formula.
> 5. **Writing paragraphs instead of tables** in 7-mark comparison sub-parts — examiners expect tabular form for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Wireless LAN with a neat block diagram, identifying **all seven** major components and stating the function of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the **Free-Space Path Loss** formula, derive the maximum range of a 5 GHz AP with the following data:
- $P_t = 18$ dBm, $G_t = 6$ dBi, $G_r = 2$ dBi, $L_c = 1$ dB, Receiver sensitivity $S_{min} = -78$ dBm. Take the operating frequency $f = 5.2$ GHz.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

1. **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a block diagram showing **STA, AP, Antenna, WLC, AAA, DS, and WIPS sensor** (use the Mermaid architecture from SECTION 4.1 as a reference for a clean sketch).
2. **Component Functions (4 marks — ½ mark each, 8 points but best 4):**
   - **STA:** End device with 802.11 radio.
   - **AP:** Bridges wireless 802.11 ↔ wired 802.3.
   - **Antenna:** Converts electrical signals to RF waves and back; defines cell shape.
   - **WLC:** Centralized AP management, CAPWAP control, RRM.
   - **AAA Server:** Verifies identity using 802.1X/EAP/RADIUS.
   - **Distribution System:** Wired Ethernet backbone interconnecting APs and WLC.
   - **WIPS/WIDS Sensor:** Dedicated RF monitors that detect/prevent wireless attacks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute EIRP.**

$$\text{EIRP} = P_t + G_t - L_c = 18 + 6 - 1 = 23 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total path loss allowed.**

$$\text{FSPL}_{max} = \text{EIRP} + G_r - S_{min} = 23 + 2 - (-78) = 103 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Invert FSPL formula.**

$$103 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(5200) + 32.44$$
$$20\log_{10}(d) = 103 - 74.32 - 32.44 = -3.76$$
$$\log_{10}(d) = -0.188 \Rightarrow d = 10^{-0.188} \approx 0.648 \text{ km} \approx 648 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — State the result clearly.**
The AP can be detected by a sensor up to **≈ 648 m** in free space. In an indoor environment, this shrinks to a few tens of meters due to walls, multipath, and human-body attenuation. **[Final 1 mark for engineering interpretation.]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Repeated Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the **WLC, AAA server, and WIPS sensor** in an enterprise WLAN security architecture. How do they collectively enforce IDPS policies?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wireless sensor has a noise figure of 5 dB, a 20 MHz channel bandwidth, and requires a 22 dB SNR to decode 64-QAM ¾. Compute the **receiver sensitivity** and explain its IDPS significance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

1. **WLC (2 marks):** The Wireless LAN Controller is the **centralized brain** of an enterprise WLAN. It pushes configurations to FIT APs via CAPWAP, manages Radio Resource Management, and acts as the **policy enforcement point** for IDPS rules (ACLs, rogue containment, quarantine VLANs).
2. **AAA Server (2 marks):** The RADIUS/TACACS+ server stores user credentials and enforces **802.1X authentication**. From an IDPS perspective, it provides the **authoritative identity log** — every authentication success/failure is fed to the SIEM, enabling detection of credential-stuffing or EAP-downgrade attacks.
3. **WIPS Sensor (2 marks):** A dedicated **out-of-band RF listener** that decodes 802.11 frames (including management frames like Deauth, Disassoc, Probe, Beacon) and runs signature + behavioral analytics. On detecting a rogue AP or DoS, it sends a **containment order** to the WLC.
4. **Collective Enforcement (1 mark):** The flow is: **WIPS detects** → **WLC receives alert** → **RADIUS correlates with auth logs** → **SIEM applies threat intel** → **SOC analyst confirms** → **WLC triggers containment** (e.g., deauthenticate rogue). This closed-loop is the heart of a wireless IDPS.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Recall the sensitivity formula.**

$$S_{min,dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + N_{F} + \text{SNR}_{req}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values.**
- $B = 20$ MHz → $10\log_{10}(20 \times 10^{6}) = 73$ dB-Hz
- $N_F = 5$ dB
- $\text{SNR}_{req} = 22$ dB

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$S_{min} = -174 + 73 + 5 + 22 = -74 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 4 — IDPS Interpretation (2 marks).**
- A sensor with **-74 dBm** sensitivity can hear an AP transmitting at +20 dBm EIRP up to **~580 m** in free space (using FSPL).
- **Lower (more negative) sensitivity = better detection range** for the WIPS.
- In practice, sensitivity is **degraded by 15–25 dB** indoors (walls, multipath, noise floor), reducing the practical WIPS coverage radius to **~30–50 m**, which is why **multiple overlapping sensors** are mandatory on each floor.
- This sensitivity number is also the **minimum RSSI threshold** used to fingerprint rogue APs — anything weaker than this is treated as noise, not a real frame.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Mnemonic "SAAWA"** = **S**tations, **A**Ps, **A**ntennas, **W**LC, **A**AA — five essential WLAN components.
- ✅ The **AP** is a **Layer-2 bridge** between 802.11 (air) and 802.3 (Ethernet).
- ✅ The **WLC** manages **FIT APs** via **CAPWAP** (RFC 5415/5416).
- ✅ The **AAA server** uses **RADIUS** (UDP 1812/1813) and **802.1X/EAP** for authentication.
- ✅ **FAT AP** = standalone; **FIT AP** = controller-managed. **Rogue AP** = unauthorized — top WIPS detection target.
- ✅ **EIRP** = $P_t + G_t - L_c$ (in dB). Governs how far an attack signal can reach — and how far your WIPS can hear.
- ✅ **FSPL** = $20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz).
- ✅ **Receiver sensitivity** = $-174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + N_F + \text{SNR}_{req}$ in dBm.
- ✅ **Shannon capacity** = $B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the upper bound on detection backhaul data rate.
- ✅ **WIDS** = passive detection only. **WIPS** = adds **active containment** (e.g., rogue deauth).
- ✅ Always use **tabular format** in 7-mark comparison sub-parts to earn full marks.
- ✅ Always include **units (dBm / dBi / MHz / km)** in every FSPL/sensitivity derivation.
- ✅ Memorize the **Component-to-Attack Matrix** (Section 3.3) — it is a 14-mark favorite.
- ✅ For IDPS context, the **WIPS sensor is the wireless-layer analog of a NIDS tap on a wired switch** — it sits *out of band* and never carries user traffic.
- ✅ **Indoor cell radius** ≈ 30–60 m; **outdoor free-space** range can be hundreds of meters — drive your sensor density accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threats Against WLANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threats Against WLANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**WLAN (Wireless Local Area Network)** is a flexible data communication system that uses radio frequency (RF) technology to transmit and receive data over the air, minimizing the need for wired connections. It typically conforms to the **IEEE 802.11 family** of standards (a/b/g/n/ac/ax/be) and operates primarily in the **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and the newer **6 GHz** unlicensed ISM (Industrial, Scientific, and Medical) radio bands.

A **Threat Against a WLAN** refers to any malicious activity, vulnerability exploitation, or attack vector specifically targeting the wireless medium, the access points (APs), the wireless clients (STAs), or the protocols governing the 802.11 MAC layer, with the intent of unauthorized data access, service disruption, credential theft, or unauthorized network entry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 2024 Scheme explicitly requires understanding the *threat surface* unique to the **shared, unbounded RF medium** — distinguishing WLAN attacks from traditional wired network attacks. Key threats enumerated include **Eavesdropping, Rogue/Unauthorized APs, Evil Twin, Jamming, MAC Spoofing, WEP/WPA Cracking, and Denial of Service (DoS)** attacks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **wireless LAN as a conversation happening in the middle of a crowded public park** (the RF spectrum):

- **Eavesdropping** is like a stranger standing nearby with a hidden tape recorder, listening to every word you say.
- **Rogue AP / Evil Twin** is like an imposter setting up a fake information kiosk with your friend's name, so people walk up and whisper their secrets to the imposter thinking it's your friend.
- **Jamming** is someone playing loud music to drown out your conversation so no one can hear you.
- **MAC Spoofing** is like someone wearing a fake name badge to enter a private meeting room reserved only for members.
- **WEP/WPA Cracking** is like someone figuring out the secret code phrase that opens the door to your private meeting.

The fundamental issue is that the wireless signal is **broadcast in all directions** and is accessible to **anyone within range** with a compatible receiver. There are no physical walls to keep intruders out — only cryptographic locks.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **ISM Bands:** 2.412 – 2.484 GHz, 5.150 – 5.825 GHz, 5.925 – 7.125 GHz
- **Channel Bandwidth:** 20 MHz, 40 MHz, 80 MHz, 160 MHz, 320 MHz (802.11be)
- **Typical Indoor Range:** ~35 m (for 2.4 GHz), ~15 m (for 5 GHz)
- **Maximum Open Air Range:** Up to ~250 m with high-gain antennas (802.11n/ac)
- **Maximum Raw Data Rate (802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6E):** **9.6 Gbps**
- **Default Authentication/Encryption:** **WPA3-SAE (Mandatory for Wi-Fi 6E certification)**
- **WLAN Threat Detection Latency Target (Enterprise WIDS):** < **500 ms**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Shared Secret Key:** A cryptographic key known to both the client and the Access Point, used for encrypting and decrypting frames. Its compromise leads to full breach of confidentiality.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Wireless Threat Surface — A Structured Breakdown

The wireless attack surface can be divided into **three concentric layers**:

### Layer A — The Physical RF Layer
- **RF Jamming (DoS):** Transmission of high-power noise on the same frequency band, raising the SNR below the receiver's sensitivity threshold.
- **Spectrum Saturation:** Continuous transmission of valid or invalid frames to overwhelm the channel.
- **Signal Interference & Disassociation Floods:** Forcing clients to disconnect from legitimate APs.

### Layer B — The MAC / Link Layer (IEEE 802.11)
- **MAC Address Spoofing:** Changing a client's MAC address to bypass MAC-based Access Control Lists.
- **Frame Injection / Forgery:** Crafting rogue 802.11 management/control/data frames.
- **Authentication & Association Flooding:** Exhausting the AP's CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table.
- **Deauthentication / Disassociation Attack:** Sending spoofed deauth frames to forcibly disconnect clients (a primitive for further attacks).

### Layer C — The Upper / Network Layer (Logical)
- **Eavesdropping (Sniffing):** Capturing unencrypted 802.11 frames in monitor mode.
- **Rogue AP / Evil Twin:** Deploying an unauthorized AP to lure clients.
- **WEP Cracking:** Exploiting the weak IV (Initialization Vector) of RC4 stream cipher in WEP.
- **WPA/WPA2-PSK Cracking:** Offline dictionary attack against the 4-way handshake.
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** Positioned between client and AP to intercept/relay traffic.
- **KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack):** Exploiting the 802.11i 4-way handshake via nonce reuse.

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of Major Threats

### 2.2.1 Eavesdropping
- **How:** Attacker places the wireless NIC into **Monitor (RFMON) mode** to capture all 802.11 frames in the air, including management, control, and data frames.
- **Why it works:** The RF medium is a broadcast domain — every frame is transmitted to all stations within range.
- **Defense:** Use strong link-layer encryption (WPA3), network-level encryption (TLS/IPsec), and a strong pre-shared key.

### 2.2.2 Rogue Access Point & Evil Twin
- **Rogue AP:** Any unauthorized AP connected to the wired enterprise network, bypassing security policy.
- **Evil Twin:** An AP with the same SSID (and possibly BSSID spoof) as a legitimate AP, luring clients via stronger signal.
- **How:** Attacker sets up a soft AP (e.g., hostapd on Linux) broadcasting the same SSID, often using a higher-power antenna.
- **Defense:** WIDS (Wireless IDS) with rogue AP detection, 802.1X port-based authentication, certificate-based authentication, PMF (Protected Management Frames).

### 2.2.3 MAC Address Spoofing
- **How:** Attacker changes the MAC address of their wireless adapter (via `ifconfig` or driver tools) to one that is whitelisted in the AP's MAC filter.
- **Defense:** MAC filtering (weak), 802.1X (strong), WIDS anomaly detection (detects impossible roaming patterns).

### 2.2.4 WEP Cracking
- **Vulnerability:** WEP uses a **24-bit IV** with the **RC4 stream cipher**; due to the **IV collision birthday paradox**, an attacker can recover the key after collecting approximately **40,000 – 85,000** frames.
- **Tools:** Aircrack-ng, Cain & Abel.
- **Defense:** Migrate to WPA2 or WPA3; WEP is fundamentally broken.

### 2.2.5 WPA2-PSK Offline Dictionary Attack
- **Vulnerability:** The 4-way handshake (EAPOL) can be captured; an attacker runs an offline brute-force/dictionary attack against the captured ANonce/SNonce/MIC.
- **Defense:** Long, high-entropy passphrase (≥ 15 characters, random); migrate to WPA3-SAE which resists offline dictionary attacks.

### 2.2.6 KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack — 2017)
- **Vulnerability:** Forces nonce reuse by replaying message 3 of the 4-way handshake, causing reinstallation of an already-used key.
- **Impact:** Decryption, packet replay, TCP connection hijacking, HTTP content injection.
- **Defense:** Patch AP and client firmware; enable PMF (802.11w).

### 2.2.7 Deauthentication Attack
- **How:** Attacker sends spoofed **Deauthentication (Type 0, Subtype 12)** or **Disassociation (Type 0, Subtype 10)** management frames using the victim's MAC as the source, causing the AP or client to terminate the association.
- **Defense:** Enable **802.11w Protected Management Frames (PMF)**, which require MIC and management frame protection.

### 2.2.8 Jamming (Physical Layer DoS)
- **Constant Jamming:** Continuous transmission of energy on the channel.
- **Random Jamming:** Random bursts to collide with legitimate frames.
- **Reactive Jamming:** Transmits only when sensing valid traffic.
- **Defense:** Frequency hopping, spread spectrum (already inherent in 802.11 DSSS/OFDM), directional antennas, RF jamming detection sensors, policy/legal recourse.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula or Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WEP IV Size** | **24** | bits | Too small → IV reuse in hours |
| **WEP Effective Key Length** | 40 (WEP-40) / 104 (WEP-104) | bits | Combined with IV = 64/128 bits |
| **Frames needed to crack WEP** | $\approx 40{,}000$ to $85{,}000$ | frames | Via Aircrack-ng statistical attack |
| **Birthday Paradox Threshold** | $k \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{N}$ | frames | For $N = 2^{24}$, $\approx 4700$ collisions |
| **WPA2-PSK Key Derivation** | $PMK = PBKDF2(passphrase, SSID, 4096, 256)$ | bits | PBKDF2-SHA1 iterations |
| **WPA3 SAE Authentication** | Dragonfly Key Exchange (Hunting-and-Pecking) | — | Resists offline dictionary attack |
| **802.11 Frame Max Body** | 2312 | bytes | MSDU limit before fragmentation |
| **802.11w (PMF) Protection** | BIP (Broadcast Integrity Protocol) | — | Uses AES-128-CMAC for mgmt frames |
| **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| **Snell's / Fresnel Zone Radius** | $r_n \approx 17.3 \sqrt{\frac{n \cdot d_1 \cdot d_2}{f \cdot D}}$ | m | Critical for jamming/signal reach |
| **Receiver Sensitivity (Wi-Fi 6)** | $-82$ (for 20 MHz, MCS 0) | dBm | Lower = more sensitive |
| **CSMA/CA Backoff Slot** | $0$ to $CW$ slots | slots | $CW$ doubles up to $CW_{max}$ |
| **Channel Bandwidths** | $20, 40, 80, 160, 320$ | MHz | 802.11n/ac/ax/be respectively |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** WEP is **broken by design**; WPA2-PSK is vulnerable to **offline dictionary attacks**; only **WPA3-SAE** (or **802.1X EAP-TLS** with WPA2-Enterprise) provides **strong resistance** to credential recovery. Always state this hierarchy in exam answers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Security Audits:** Companies hire ethical hackers to perform WLAN penetration tests using tools like Aircrack-ng, Kismet, and Wireshark.
- **Hospital / Healthcare Networks:** Protect patient PHI (HIPAA / DPDP Act 2023 India) on wireless medical devices.
- **Industrial IoT:** Wireless sensor networks in SCADA environments must defend against jamming to prevent catastrophic downtime.
- **Educational Institutions:** Open campuses with thousands of BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) clients are prime targets for rogue APs.
- **Defense & Military:** Secure tactical radio networks demand anti-jamming, anti-eavesdropping — WLAN threats overlap with broader electronic warfare.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: WEP IV Collision Probability (Birthday Paradox)

The WEP IV is **24 bits** long. Each packet selects a new IV from this space. The probability of **at least one IV collision** after $k$ packets, when $N = 2^{24}$, is given by the **Birthday paradox approximation**.

### Step 1 — Define the Probability Space
The number of possible IVs is:

$$N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$$

### Step 2 — Probability of NO Collision on the $k$-th Packet
The probability that the $k$-th packet has an IV different from all previous $k-1$ packets:

$$P(\text{no collision on } k\text{-th}) = 1 - \frac{k - 1}{N}$$

### Step 3 — Cumulative Probability of NO Collision After $k$ Packets
Using the product rule (independence assumption):

$$P(\text{no collision after } k) = \prod_{i=1}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Exponential Approximation
For small $i/N$, use $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$:

$$P(\text{no collision after } k) \approx \exp\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{i}{N}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{k(k-1)}{2N}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Probability of AT LEAST ONE Collision

$$P(\text{collision after } k) = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{k(k-1)}{2N}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Compute the 50% Threshold
Set $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$:

$$0.5 = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2N}\right) \implies \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2N}\right) = 0.5 \implies \frac{k^2}{2N} = \ln 2$$

$$k = \sqrt{2 N \ln 2} = 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{N}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation for WEP
Substituting $N = 2^{24}$:

$$k = 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{16{,}777{,}216} \approx 1.177 \cdot 4096 \approx 4822 \text{ packets}$$

### Step 8 — Interpretation
After only **~4822 packets**, there is a **50% chance** that two packets share the same IV. Once two ciphertexts encrypted with the same keystream are identified, XORing them cancels the keystream and reveals plaintext via statistical crib-dragging. This is why Aircrack-ng can break WEP in minutes on a busy network.

## 3.2 Derivation: WPA2-PSK Passphrase Strength (Search-Space)

The WPA2-PSK key is derived from the passphrase and the SSID via PBKDF2. The effective keyspace is bounded by the passphrase entropy.

### Step 1 — Assume Passphrase of Length $L$ from Alphabet Size $A$
A typical mixed-case alphanumeric + symbol alphabet:

$$A = 26 + 26 + 10 + 33 = 95 \text{ characters}$$

### Step 2 — Total Search Space

$$S = A^{L} = 95^{L}$$

### Step 3 — Bits of Entropy

$$H = L \cdot \log_2(95) \approx L \cdot 6.57 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Example Values

| Length $L$ | Search Space $95^L$ | Entropy (bits) | Time @ $10^{10}$ keys/s |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | $7.4 \times 10^{11}$ | 39.4 | ~74 s |
| 8 | $6.6 \times 10^{15}$ | 52.5 | ~7.4 days |
| 12 | $5.4 \times 10^{23}$ | 78.8 | ~1.7 million years |
| 16 | $4.4 \times 10^{31}$ | 105.1 | Effectively infinite |

### Step 5 — Conclusion
A **12-character truly random passphrase** provides **~78.8 bits of entropy** — generally sufficient against offline dictionary attacks given current GPU speeds of $\approx 10^{10}$ keys/s for PBKDF2-SHA1 (4096 iterations).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — WLAN Threats vs. Defense

| Threat | Protocol / Mechanism Exploited | Attacker's Tool | Primary Defense |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | Unencrypted 802.11 | Wireshark, Kismet | WPA2/WPA3, TLS |
| WEP Cracking | RC4 weak IV | Aircrack-ng | Migrate to WPA2/WPA3 |
| WPA2-PSK Crack | 4-way handshake | hashcat, aircrack-ng | Strong passphrase, WPA3-SAE |
| Evil Twin / Rogue AP | Open / shared SSID | hostapd, KARMA | PMF, 802.1X, WIDS |
| MAC Spoofing | Weak ACL | macchanger | 802.1X, WIDS anomaly detection |
| Deauth Attack | Mgmt frames unprotected | aireplay-ng, mdk4 | 802.11w PMF |
| KRACK | 4-way handshake nonce reuse | krackattack script | Firmware patch, PMF |
| Jamming | RF energy | SDR (HackRF, USRP) | Directional antennas, spread spectrum |
| Association Flood | AP CAM table exhaustion | mdk3 | Rate limiting, WIDS |
| Replay Attack | Lack of freshness | tcpreplay | Sequence counters, PMF |
| Fragmentation Attack | 802.11 fragmentation | file2air | Disable fragmentation, PMF |
| Hole 196 — Client Isolation Bypass | Group key misuse | custom scripts | 802.11w, proper key mgmt |

## 3.4 KTU High-Yield Numerical: Frame Capture Time for WEP Crack

If an AP transmits **600 packets/second** (typical busy network), how long to capture 85,000 frames?

$$t = \frac{N}{R} = \frac{85{,}000}{600} \approx 141.7 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.4 \text{ minutes}$$

This explains why **WEP is dead** — full key recovery in under 3 minutes of passive capture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level WLAN Threat Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    attacker[Malicious Node]
    sniffer[Eavesdropper Sniffer]
    evilap[Evil Twin AP]
    jammer[RF Jammer]
    spoof[MAC Spoofer]
    deauth[Deauth Attacker]
    crack[WEP WPA2 Cracker]
    legitimate[Legitimate AP]
    client1[Wireless Client STA1]
    client2[Wireless Client STA2]
    wids[WIDS Sensor]
    controller[Central WIDS Controller]
    sniffer -- Captures frames --> legitimate
    evilap -- Lures clients --> client1
    jammer -- Floods RF --> legitimate
    spoof -- Bypasses MAC filter --> legitimate
    deauth -- Forged deauth frames --> client1
    crack -- Offline dictionary --> legitimate
    legitimate -- Normal traffic --> client1
    legitimate -- Normal traffic --> client2
    wids -- Monitors air --> legitimate
    wids -- Detects anomalies --> controller
    controller -- Correlates events --> wids
```

## 4.2 Layered WLAN Attack Flow

```mermaid

    phase1[Phase 1 Recon]
    phase2[Phase 2 Eavesdrop]

```


## 4.4 Security Mechanism Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    wep[WEP 1999 RC4 24 bit IV]
    wpa[WPA 2003 TKIP RC4]
    wpa2[WPA2 2004 AES CCMP]
    wpa3[WPA3 2018 SAE GCMP 256]
    ow[Open No Auth]
    wep --> wpa
    wpa --> wpa2
    wpa2 --> wpa3
    ow -. Weakest .-> wep
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The arrows in the security timeline show increasing cryptographic strength. **WEP → WPA → WPA2 → WPA3** is the standard KTU answer for "evolution of WLAN security." The dashed line from *Open* to *WEP* emphasizes that WEP is the *first* standardized security mechanism, but is now considered **broken by design**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Rogue Access Point and an Evil Twin. List two detection mechanisms for each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Rogue AP** is an unauthorized access point connected to the wired enterprise network without explicit authorization from the network administrator. It often results from an employee plugging in a personal wireless router. It may broadcast any SSID.

An **Evil Twin** is a malicious access point specifically configured to mimic a legitimate AP — same SSID, often same BSSID spoofed, sometimes higher transmit power — to lure wireless clients into connecting.

**Detection of Rogue AP:**
1. Wired-side scanning: WIDS polls all switch ports (using SNMP/CDP) to identify AP-class devices unauthorized on the LAN.
2. RF-side signature: A rogue AP is detected when an AP's MAC is seen transmitting and its traffic also appears on the wired segment.

**Detection of Evil Twin:**
1. WIDS compares BSSID, channel, encryption type, and signal strength to known-good AP database; mismatches flag an evil twin.
2. Anomaly detection: Clients connecting to an AP outside their normal mobility pattern.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a deauthentication attack? How does 802.11w (Protected Management Frames) mitigate it?**

**Model Answer:**

A **deauthentication attack** is a Layer-2 Denial of Service in which the attacker injects **spoofed Deauthentication (Type=0, Subtype=12) or Disassociation (Type=0, Subtype=10) management frames**, using the victim's MAC as the source address. The AP or client receiving such a frame immediately terminates the association, disconnecting the client.

**Mitigation by 802.11w (PMF):**
802.11w introduces the **Broadcast Integrity Protocol (BIP)** using **AES-128-CMAC** to compute a Management MIC (MMIE) appended to protected management frames. The receiver validates the MIC using a shared integrity group key (IGTK). Spoofed deauth frames fail the integrity check and are dropped, neutralizing the attack.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the WEP encryption mechanism. Show mathematically why WEP is considered cryptographically broken, using the **Birthday Paradox** to compute the number of packets required for a 50% IV collision probability. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Describe the **KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack)** with a neat diagram of the 4-way handshake and explain how it violates confidentiality. (7 marks)

---

### Model Answer for Q.A(a)

**WEP Encryption Mechanism:**

WEP uses the **RC4 stream cipher** with a 24-bit IV concatenated to a 40-bit (WEP-40) or 104-bit (WEP-104) shared secret key, producing a 64-bit or 128-bit effective key. The plaintext frame M is XORed with the keystream K = RC4(IV || Key) to produce ciphertext C = M ⊕ K. The IV is transmitted in clear in the frame header.

**Birthday Paradox Derivation:**

Let $N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ be the IV space.

$$P(\text{collision after } k) = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2N}\right)$$

Setting $P = 0.5$:

$$k = \sqrt{2N \ln 2} = 1.177\sqrt{N} \approx 4822 \text{ packets}$$

With a busy network transmitting ~600 packets/sec, this corresponds to **~8 seconds** for a 50% collision probability. Once two ciphertexts $C_1, C_2$ share the same keystream, $C_1 \oplus C_2 = M_1 \oplus M_2$ reveals the XOR of plaintexts, exploitable via statistical attacks (e.g., FMS). Thus WEP is broken by design.

---

### Model Answer for Q.A(b)

**KRACK — Key Reinstallation Attack:**

KRACK (disclosed in 2017 by Vanhoef) exploits the **802.11i 4-way handshake**. Normally:

```
AP        Client
 |  Msg 1: ANonce   |
 | ----------------> |
 |                   |
 |  Msg 2: SNonce    |
 | <---------------- |
 |                   |
 |  Msg 3: GTK, MIC  |
 | ----------------> |
 |                   |
 |  Msg 4: Ack       |
 | <---------------- |
```

**The Attack:**

1. The attacker captures Msg 3 of the handshake.
2. The attacker **replays Msg 3** to the client (without forwarding Msg 4).
3. The client, on receiving Msg 3, **reinstalls the same PTK** and resets its transmit nonce counter and replay counter to zero.
4. This causes the client to reuse nonces (PTK + nonce) that have already been used, allowing the attacker to decrypt frames via XOR keystream recovery.

**Why Confidentiality is Breached:**
The keystream derived from a reused nonce is identical. An attacker can XOR captured frames across multiple retransmissions to recover plaintext, enabling **decryption, replay, and TCP injection** of unencrypted HTTP traffic.

**Defense:** Patch all clients and APs to enforce strict 4-way handshake state-machine rules; install only-once PTK semantics; enable 802.11w PMF.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** List and explain **any five** major threats against WLANs with the protocol layer they target. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Compare **WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3** across the following parameters: cipher, key management, IV size, vulnerabilities, and recommended use case. (7 marks)

---

### Model Answer for Q.B(a)

| # | Threat | Layer Targeted | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Eavesdropping** | Data link (L2) | Monitor-mode capture of unencrypted 802.11 frames |
| 2 | **Rogue/Evil Twin AP** | Data link + Network | Fake AP with same SSID lures clients into associating |
| 3 | **MAC Spoofing** | Data link (L2) | Attacker clones whitelisted MAC to bypass MAC filter |
| 4 | **Deauthentication Attack** | Data link (L2 management) | Forged deauth/disassoc frames force client disconnection |
| 5 | **WEP IV Reuse Attack** | Data link (L2) | Birthday paradox on 24-bit IV recovers RC4 keystream |
| 6 | **KRACK** | Data link (L2) | 4-way handshake nonce reuse via Msg-3 replay |

---

### Model Answer for Q.B(b)

| Parameter | WEP | WPA | WPA2 | WPA3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cipher** | RC4 | RC4 + TKIP | AES-128 CCMP | AES-256 GCMP |
| **Key Management** | Static shared key | Static PSK + 4-way handshake | 4-way handshake (EAP or PSK) | SAE (Dragonfly) + forward secrecy |
| **IV / Nonce Size** | 24-bit IV | 48-bit IV | 48-bit PN | 48-bit PN + fresh nonces per SAE |
| **Major Vulnerability** | IV collision → keystream recovery | TKIP MIC weakness | KRACK (if unpatched), offline PSK crack | Dragonfly side-channel (research) |
| **Offline Dictionary Attack** | N/A (key static) | Yes | Yes | **No** (SAE resists) |
| **Recommended Use** | **Never use** (deprecated) | Legacy fallback | Mainstream for years | **Current best practice** |
| **Year Introduced** | 1999 | 2003 | 2004 | 2018 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make on WLAN Threat questions (lose up to 4–5 marks per answer):**
> 1. **Conflating Rogue AP and Evil Twin** — these are *not* synonyms. A rogue AP can be benign (employee's home router); an evil twin is always a malicious impersonation.
> 2. **Forgetting 802.11w PMF** when discussing deauth attacks — you will lose 2 marks for omitting the defense mechanism.
> 3. **Stating "WPA2 is uncrackable"** — wrong. WPA2-PSK is vulnerable to offline dictionary attack against the 4-way handshake. Only WPA3-SAE resists this.
> 4. **Missing the Birthday Paradox math** in WEP questions — always derive $k = 1.177\sqrt{N}$ explicitly for full 7 marks.
> 5. **Drawing a 4-way handshake diagram without arrow labels (ANonce, SNonce, MIC, GTK)** — labeled diagrams carry full marks; unlabeled ones carry partial credit.
> 6. **Confusing WPA and WPA2 cipher** — WPA uses TKIP/RC4; WPA2 uses CCMP/AES. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 7. **Omitting the dBm/IV size/units** in numeric answers — always include units: 24 bits, -82 dBm, 20 MHz, etc.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **WLAN = shared RF medium → inherently insecure without cryptographic protection.**
- **Three attack layers:** Physical RF, MAC (802.11), and Upper/Logical.
- **WEP is broken** (24-bit IV → ~4800 packets for 50% IV collision via Birthday Paradox).
- **WPA2-PSK is crackable offline**; always use ≥ 12-character random passphrases.
- **WPA3-SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)** resists offline dictionary attacks via the Dragonfly key exchange.
- **KRACK exploits 4-way handshake nonce reuse**; mitigated by firmware patches + 802.11w PMF.
- **Deauthentication attacks** are mitigated by **802.11w Protected Management Frames (BIP + AES-128-CMIC).**
- **Rogue AP detection** is best performed by WIDS using both wired-side (port scanning) and RF-side (anomaly) sensors.
- **Evil Twin** is detected by comparing BSSID, channel, encryption, and signal strength to a known-good AP database.
- **MAC filtering is NOT a security control** — easily spoofed; use 802.1X instead.
- **Jamming** is a physical-layer DoS; defenses include spread spectrum, directional antennas, RF jamming sensors, and policy/legal recourse.
- **802.11w PMF** is **mandatory for Wi-Fi 6E (802.11ax @ 6 GHz) certification** — a KTU favorite for "recent standards" questions.
- **Always state units** (dBm, bits, MHz) and **always derive formulas** (e.g., $k = 1.177\sqrt{N}$ for WEP) in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components and Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is not a single monolithic software; it is a **distributed security architecture** composed of multiple cooperating components that collectively perform data collection, analysis, detection, logging, and active response. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (Module 3), the *Components and Architecture* of an IDPS define the structural blueprint that dictates *where* monitoring occurs, *how* events are gathered, *how* analysis engines process them, and *how* responses are triggered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** An IDPS architecture is the logical and physical arrangement of **Sensors/Agents**, **Analyzers (Detection Engines)**, **Management Servers**, **Databases**, and **Response Modules** interconnected through secure communication channels to identify, log, and optionally block malicious activity.

The architecture fundamentally answers three engineering questions:

1. **Where is the data captured?** (Sensor placement — host vs. network boundary)
2. **Where is the data processed?** (Centralized vs. distributed analysis)
3. **How is the response delivered?** (Passive notification vs. inline blocking)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault Security System"

Imagine a high-security bank vault. The **IDPS** works exactly like the multi-layered security of a bank:

| Bank Vault Component | IDPS Architectural Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| CCTV cameras at every door | **Sensors / Agents** | Passive observers that capture raw footage (network packets or host logs) |
| Central monitoring room with security analysts | **Analyzer / Detection Engine** | The "brain" that watches footage and identifies suspicious behavior |
| Filing cabinet storing all incident reports | **Event Database / Knowledge Base** | Permanent storage of alerts, signatures, and historical data |
| Security guard's radio/computer | **Management Console** | The interface where admins configure rules and view alerts |
| Armed guard who physically blocks an intruder | **Prevention / Response Module** | Active inline component that drops packets, resets connections, or blocks IPs |
| Phone line between the guard and the analyst | **Secure Communication Channel** | Encrypted channel carrying alerts and policy updates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Just as a bank does not rely on *only* cameras *or* *only* guards, a robust IDPS uses both **sensors** (to see) and **response modules** (to act). The **analyzer** is the brain linking both.

## 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics

The following metrics quantify an IDPS architecture's effectiveness and are **frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions**:

- **Detection Latency:** The time delay between an event occurrence and its detection. **Typical target: < 1 second** for inline (IPS) deployments.
- **Throughput:** Data volume the sensor can analyze per second, measured in **Mbps** or **Gbps** (for network IDPS) or **EPS (Events Per Second)** for log-based systems.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Ratio of benign events incorrectly flagged as malicious.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR):** Ratio of actual attacks missed by the system.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time from detection to active containment.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly maps to the cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** — meaning examiners will test both your *recall* of components and your *application* of architecture selection to given scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Components — Structural Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme consolidates IDPS architecture into **five primary components**. Each has a defined role, and the omission of any one breaks the detection chain.

### 2.1.1 Sensors / Agents
Sensors are the **peripheral nervous system** of the IDPS. They are deployed at strategic points (network chokepoints, host endpoints, or cloud workloads) and perform the following logical steps:

1. **Capture** raw data — network packets (NIDPS), system calls or log files (HIDPS), or API telemetry (cloud-native).
2. **Pre-process** — normalize timestamps, strip protocol headers, deduplicate.
3. **Forward** to the analyzer via a **secure, encrypted channel** (typically TLS/SSH).

> [!NOTE]
> A sensor **never makes a decision** on its own in a centralized architecture. It is a *data diode* — it captures and forwards.

### 2.1.2 Analyzers (Detection Engines)
The analyzer is the **CPU of the architecture**. It receives normalized events from one or more sensors and applies detection logic.

**Detection Method Categories (KTU High-Yield):**

- **Signature-Based (Misuse Detection):** Pattern matching against a known-attack database.
- **Anomaly-Based:** Statistical deviation from a learned baseline.
- **State-Based:** Tracks protocol state machines (e.g., TCP handshake violations).
- **Protocol-Decoding / Behavioral:** Deep inspection of protocol semantics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The analyzer's choice of detection method determines the system's *signature database size*, *false positive rate*, and *processing throughput*.

### 2.1.3 Management Server (Console)
The management server is the **administrative nerve center**. It:

- Distributes **policies and signatures** to sensors and analyzers.
- Aggregates **alerts** from one or more analyzers.
- Provides the **GUI/dashboard** for security analysts.
- Manages **user authentication and RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)**.

### 2.1.4 Database / Knowledge Base
The database is the **memory** of the architecture. It typically stores:

- **Alert logs** (event_id, timestamp, severity, source, destination).
- **Signature database** (CVE-mapped rules).
- **Baseline profiles** (for anomaly detection).
- **Forensic evidence** (PCAPs, session captures).

### 2.1.5 Response Module (Inline Prevention Engine)
This is what differentiates an **IDS** from an **IPS**. The response module can:

- **Passive:** Log, alert (email/SMS/SIEM), notify.
- **Active (Inline):** Drop malicious packets, reset TCP connections, block source IP via ACL, quarantine host.

## 2.2 Architectural Topologies

KTU Module 3 emphasizes three architectural topologies, each with distinct traffic-placement semantics.

### 2.2.1 Passive (IDS Mode) — Out-of-Band Monitoring
The sensor is connected to a **SPAN port** or **network TAP**. Traffic is **copied** to the sensor; the original flow is uninterrupted. Detection happens, but blocking requires external action (e.g., a firewall rule push).

### 2.2.2 Inline (IPS Mode) — In-Band Enforcement
The sensor is **physically inserted into the data path**. All traffic must pass through it. This enables **real-time packet dropping** but introduces a **single point of failure** and **latency overhead**.

### 2.2.3 Hybrid (Cloud + On-Premise)
Modern enterprises deploy **cloud-native sensors** feeding a **centralized on-premise analyzer**, or use **distributed analyzers** for scale.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Expression | Unit / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_{latency}$ | Detection Latency | $D_{latency} = T_{detect} - T_{event}$ | Seconds; must be $< 1$ s for inline IPS |
| $FPR$ | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Dimensionless; range $[0, 1]$ |
| $FNR$ | False Negative Rate | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Dimensionless; range $[0, 1]$ |
| $TPR$ | True Positive Rate (Recall) | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Dimensionless; equals $1 - FNR$ |
| $Precision$ | Detection Precision | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Dimensionless; high precision $\Rightarrow$ low false alarms |
| $F_1$ | F1-Score (harmonic mean) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot TPR}{Precision + TPR}$ | Range $[0, 1]$; $F_1 = 1$ is perfect |
| $Throughput$ | Sensor capacity | $Throughput = \frac{Packets_{analyzed}}{T_{window}}$ | Mbps or Gbps |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Respond | $MTTR = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{respond_i} - T_{detect_i})$ | Seconds/Minutes |
| $CoV$ | Coefficient of Variation (anomaly baseline) | $CoV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu}$ | Ratio; threshold typically $> 3$ flags anomaly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **SIEM tuning**, **SOC (Security Operations Center) performance reviews**, and **vendor benchmarking** (e.g., NSS Labs reports). For KTU, expect at least one numerical/short problem on **FPR/FNR trade-offs**.

## 2.4 Why Architecture Choice Matters

The architectural decision (passive vs. inline, host vs. network, centralized vs. distributed) directly impacts:

- **Detection Coverage:** A single network sensor cannot see encrypted traffic or host-level syscalls.
- **Performance:** Inline IPS adds **0.5–2 ms latency** per packet at Gbps speeds.
- **Resilience:** A centralized analyzer is a **single point of failure**; distributed analyzers provide redundancy.
- **Evasion Resistance:** A sensor on a single network segment is blind to attacks launched from other segments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: IDPS Detection Latency Equation

The **Detection Latency** is one of the most heavily weighted parameters in KTU 14-mark problems. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Time Stamps
Let the original security event occur at time:

$$T_{event} = t_0$$

The sensor captures the event at time $T_{cap}$ after network/host propagation delay $\delta_1$:

$$T_{cap} = t_0 + \delta_1$$

> **Logic:** $\delta_1$ represents the time for the packet to travel from the source to the sensor tap point (network IDPS) or the time for the OS audit subsystem to flush the log (host IDPS).

### Step 2: Pre-Processing Delay
The sensor normalizes, deduplicates, and packages the event. Let this be $\delta_2$:

$$T_{preprocessed} = T_{cap} + \delta_2 = t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2$$

> **Logic:** $\delta_2$ is a deterministic function of sensor CPU load and event volume.

### Step 3: Transmission to Analyzer
The event is transmitted over the secure management channel. Let $\delta_3$ be the network transit time:

$$T_{analyzer\_in} = T_{preprocessed} + \delta_3 = t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3$$

### Step 4: Analyzer Detection Time
The analyzer matches signatures, runs anomaly scoring, and produces a verdict in time $\delta_4$:

$$T_{detect} = T_{analyzer\_in} + \delta_4 = t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4$$

### Step 5: Combine All Delay Components

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{latency} &= T_{detect} - T_{event} \\
&= (t_0 + \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4) - t_0 \\
&= \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Optimization Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final General Form:**
>
> $$D_{latency} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i$$
>
> where each $\delta_i$ is an independent delay stage. To minimize $D_{latency}$, an architect must **identify the dominant $\delta_i$** (often $\delta_3$ — network transit to a remote analyzer) and **co-locate** the analyzer with the sensor, or use **distributed analysis**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: FPR/FNR Trade-off Calculation (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**
An IDPS was evaluated on a test dataset of **10,000** network events. The confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted Malicious | Predicted Benign |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Malicious** | 180 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
| **Actual Benign** | 50 (FP) | 9,750 (TN) |

**Required:** Calculate $FPR$, $FNR$, $TPR$, $Precision$, and $F_1$.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the values.**
- $TP = 180$, $FN = 20$, $FP = 50$, $TN = 9750$

**Step 2: Calculate False Positive Rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
&= \frac{50}{50 + 9750} \\
&= \frac{50}{9800} \\
&= 0.005102 \\
&\approx 0.51\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate False Negative Rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
FNR &= \frac{FN}{FN + TP} \\
&= \frac{20}{20 + 180} \\
&= \frac{20}{200} \\
&= 0.10 \\
&= 10.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate True Positive Rate (Recall).**

$$\begin{aligned}
TPR &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{180}{180 + 20} \\
&= 0.90 \\
&= 90.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Precision.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{180}{180 + 50} \\
&= \frac{180}{230} \\
&= 0.7826 \\
&\approx 78.26\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Calculate F1-Score.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot TPR}{Precision + TPR} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.7826 \cdot 0.90}{0.7826 + 0.90} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.70434}{1.6826} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.4186 \\
&= 0.8372 \\
&\approx 83.72\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** A **low FPR (0.51%)** is excellent — the system rarely cries wolf. A **moderate FNR (10%)** means 1 in 10 attacks slip through. The F1 of **83.72%** indicates a well-balanced system suitable for production deployment.

---
