# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Centralized IDPS Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[End User Workstation]:::host -->|Network Traffic| sw1[Core Switch]:::netdev
    src2[Internal Server]:::host -->|Network Traffic| sw1
    src3[Cloud Workload]:::cloud -->|API Telemetry| sw1
    sw1 -->|SPAN Port Mirror| sensor1[Network Sensor 1]:::sensor
    sw1 -->|SPAN Port Mirror| sensor2[Network Sensor 2]:::sensor
    host1[Critical Database Host]:::host -->|Host Logs| agent1[HIDPS Agent]:::agent
    host2[Web Server Host]:::host -->|Host Logs| agent1
    sensor1 -->|TLS Encrypted Events| analyzer1[Central Analyzer Engine]:::engine
    sensor2 -->|TLS Encrypted Events| analyzer1
    agent1 -->|TLS Encrypted Events| analyzer1
    analyzer1 -->|Query/Update| db1[(Signature Database)]:::db
    analyzer1 -->|Query/Update| db2[(Event Log Store)]:::db
    analyzer1 -->|Alert Stream| mgr1[Management Server Console]:::console
    mgr1 -->|Policy Push| sensor1
    mgr1 -->|Policy Push| sensor2
    mgr1 -->|Policy Push| agent1
    mgr1 -->|Block Command via API| fw1[Perimeter Firewall]:::fw
    analyzer1 -->|Inline Drop Packet| sensor1
    classDef host fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef netdev fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef agent fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#e5dbff,stroke:#6741d9,color:#000
    classDef db fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,color:#000
    classDef console fill:#fcc2d7,stroke:#c2255c,color:#000
    classDef fw fill:#ffec99,stroke:#fab005,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram (KTU-style):**
- The **left side** shows *data origin* (hosts, servers, cloud).
- The **center top** shows *capture* (sensors + agents).
- The **center** shows *processing* (analyzer + databases).
- The **right side** shows *response* (console + firewall).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Inline (IPS) vs. Passive (IDS) Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDS["PASSIVE IDS ARCHITECTURE - Out of Band"]
        direction LR
        a1[Client]:::host -->|Traffic| b1[Router]:::netdev
        b1 -->|Traffic| c1[Server]:::host
        b1 -.->|SPAN Copy| d1[IDS Sensor]:::sensor
        d1 -->|Alert Only| e1[Analyst]:::console
    end
    subgraph IPS["INLINE IPS ARCHITECTURE - In Band"]
        direction LR
        a2[Client]:::host -->|Traffic Must Pass| d2[IPS Sensor]:::sensor
        d2 -->|Allow or Drop| b2[Router]:::netdev
        b2 -->|Traffic| c2[Server]:::host
        d2 -->|Block Action| e2[Analyst]:::console
    end
    classDef host fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef netdev fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef console fill:#fcc2d7,stroke:#c2255c,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Difference:** In **IDS mode**, the sensor receives a *copy* via dotted line (logical mirror) — original traffic is never touched. In **IPS mode**, the sensor sits on the *solid path* — every packet must pass through it for verdict (allow/drop).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: IDPS Component Interaction Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User/Host
    participant S as Sensor
    participant A as Analyzer Engine
    participant DB as Signature Database
    participant M as Management Console
    participant R as Response Module
    U->>S: Step 1 - Generate Traffic or Log Event
    S->>S: Step 2 - Capture and Normalize
    S->>A: Step 3 - Forward Encrypted Event
    A->>DB: Step 4 - Query for Matching Signature
    DB-->>A: Step 5 - Return Signature Match Result
    A->>A: Step 6 - Apply Detection Logic and Scoring
    A->>M: Step 7 - Raise Alert to Console
    A->>R: Step 8 - Trigger Response Action
    R-->>U: Step 9 - Block Drop or Allow Packet
    M->>S: Step 10 - Push Updated Policy
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the five core components of an IDPS architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

The five core components of an IDPS architecture are:

1. **Sensors/Agents** — Distributed at network chokepoints (network sensors) or host endpoints (agents) to capture raw traffic and system events. They forward normalized data to the analyzer.

2. **Analyzers (Detection Engines)** — The central processing units that apply signature-based, anomaly-based, or stateful detection methods to identify malicious activity.

3. **Management Server (Console)** — The administrative interface used to configure sensors, push signatures, view dashboards, and manage user roles.

4. **Database / Knowledge Base** — Stores signature definitions, alert logs, baseline profiles, and forensic evidence for correlation and post-incident analysis.

5. **Response Module** — Executes passive (alerts/logs) or active (packet drop, TCP reset, IP block) countermeasures based on the analyzer's verdict.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between passive (IDS) and inline (IPS) architectures in terms of traffic placement and response capability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Passive IDS Architecture | Inline IPS Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic placement | Out-of-band via SPAN port or TAP | In-band, physically on the data path |
| Traffic interruption | None (original flow untouched) | All packets must pass through sensor |
| Response capability | Alerts only; cannot block directly | Real-time packet drop and connection reset |
| Failure impact | Failure does not affect traffic | Sensor failure can break connectivity (single point of failure) |
| Latency added | Negligible (passive observer) | 0.5–2 ms per packet at Gbps speeds |
| Deployment | Monitoring and forensics | Active enforcement at perimeter |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Performance Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the centralized IDPS architecture and the interaction between its components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IDPS analyzer processes **50,000** events per day. The system produced **320** alerts, of which **240** were true positives, **80** were false positives, and **45** real attacks were missed. Calculate the False Positive Rate, False Negative Rate, Precision, and F1-Score. Comment on the system's detection quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Centralized IDPS Architecture:**

A centralized IDPS architecture consists of distributed sensors/agents feeding a single analyzer engine, with a unified management console and shared database.

**Key Components and Their Interaction:**

- **Sensors** are deployed at network segments (using SPAN ports or TAPs) and at host endpoints (as software agents). They capture traffic and audit logs.

- **Agents** at each critical host forward system call traces, file integrity checks, and log entries to the central analyzer over an encrypted TLS channel.

- **Analyzer Engine** receives the pre-processed data and applies multi-method detection (signature, anomaly, stateful protocol analysis). It queries the signature database in real time.

- **Signature Database** holds the latest CVE-mapped rules. The analyzer's verdict (malicious/benign) is logged in the **Event Database**.

- **Management Console** provides a unified dashboard for SOC analysts, distributes signature updates, and pushes policies back to sensors.

- **Response Module** receives a verdict from the analyzer. In inline mode, it drops packets instantly; in passive mode, it generates alerts to the console, which may then push a firewall block via API.

> [!TIP]
> **Diagrammatic Note (for answer sheet):** Draw a block diagram with *sensors/agents on the left*, *analyzer + databases in the center*, and *management console + response module on the right*. Use arrows to show bidirectional encrypted communication.

**Advantages:** Single point of policy control, easier signature management.
**Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, scalability bottleneck, high bandwidth to central site.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given Data:**
- Total alerts generated: $320$
- True Positives: $TP = 240$
- False Positives: $FP = 80$
- Missed attacks (False Negatives): $FN = 45$

**Step 1: Calculate False Positive Rate (FPR).**

We need the True Negatives. Total benign events = Total events $-$ Real attacks.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total events} &= 50{,}000 \\
\text{Real attacks} &= TP + FN = 240 + 45 = 285 \\
TN &= 50{,}000 - 285 = 49{,}715
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
FPR &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 49{,}715} \\
&= \frac{80}{49{,}795} \\
&= 0.001607 \\
&\approx 0.16\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate False Negative Rate (FNR).**

$$\begin{aligned}
FNR &= \frac{FN}{FN + TP} \\
&= \frac{45}{45 + 240} \\
&= \frac{45}{285} \\
&= 0.1579 \\
&\approx 15.79\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Precision.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{240}{240 + 80} \\
&= \frac{240}{320} \\
&= 0.75 \\
&= 75.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate F1-Score.**

$$\begin{aligned}
TPR &= 1 - FNR = 1 - 0.1579 = 0.8421 \\
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot TPR}{Precision + TPR} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.75 \cdot 0.8421}{0.75 + 0.8421} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.6316}{1.5921} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.3967 \\
&= 0.7934 \\
&\approx 79.34\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on Detection Quality:**

> [!NOTE]
> The system exhibits a **very low FPR (0.16%)**, meaning analysts will rarely be distracted by false alarms. However, the **FNR of 15.79%** indicates that nearly 1 in 6 attacks is missed, which is a significant concern for a high-security environment. The **F1-score of 79.34%** reflects a reasonable balance, but the false negative rate should be reduced by tuning the anomaly detection thresholds or adding complementary signature rules.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between Network-based IDPS (NIDPS) and Host-based IDPS (HIDPS) architectures. Discuss the placement of sensors in each case with appropriate diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for total IDPS detection latency and explain how architectural choices (centralized vs. distributed) affect each latency component. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**NIDPS vs. HIDPS Architecture Comparison:**

**Network-based IDPS (NIDPS):**
- Sensors are placed at **network chokepoints** — typically at the perimeter (just inside the firewall), at the DMZ, and at internal network segment junctions.
- Data sources: Live packet capture via **SPAN/mirror ports** or **passive TAPs**.
- Strengths: Broad coverage, single sensor monitors many hosts, detects network-layer attacks (port scans, DoS, protocol abuse).
- Weaknesses: **Cannot inspect encrypted payloads** (SSL/TLS blind spot), cannot see host-level activity, susceptible to high-bandwidth evasion.

**Host-based IDPS (HIDPS):**
- A software **agent** runs on each protected host, monitoring OS audit logs, system calls, file integrity, registry changes, and process behavior.
- Strengths: Visibility into **encrypted traffic at the endpoint** (after decryption), detects insider threats, host-specific anomalies, rootkits.
- Weaknesses: Per-host deployment overhead, agent can be disabled by attackers, no network-wide view.

**Placement Diagrams (describe in answer):**

- *NIDPS:* Draw a perimeter topology — Internet → Firewall → **NIDPS Sensor** (inline or SPAN) → Internal Switch → Servers.
- *HIDPS:* Draw individual hosts with **agent blocks** inside each host, all forwarding to a central analyzer.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Derivation of Detection Latency:**

As derived in Section 3.1:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{latency} &= T_{detect} - T_{event} \\
&= \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \delta_4
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $\delta_1$ = propagation delay from event source to sensor
- $\delta_2$ = sensor pre-processing delay
- $\delta_3$ = network transit delay from sensor to analyzer
- $\delta_4$ = analyzer detection/decision delay

**Architectural Impact:**

| Latency Component | Centralized Architecture | Distributed Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta_1$ | Larger if sensor is remote from event | Smaller (sensor is local) |
| $\delta_3$ | **Largest contributor** (WAN/VPN transit) | Eliminated or minimized (local forwarding) |
| $\delta_4$ | Risk of bottleneck at central site | Load-balanced across analyzers |
| **Overall $D_{latency}$** | Higher (5–30 s typical) | Lower (< 1 s possible) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Distributed architectures reduce $D_{latency}$ primarily by **minimizing $\delta_3$**. However, they introduce complexity in synchronizing signature updates and aggregating correlated alerts across multiple analyzers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS architecture:** Many students incorrectly state that an IDS "blocks" traffic. Remember — **IDS = passive observation + alerts**, **IPS = inline + active response**.
> 2. **Forgetting the secure communication channel:** Sensors-to-analyzer links **must** use TLS/SSH encryption; unencrypted channels are a vulnerability in your answer.
> 3. **Missing the database role:** Students often omit the signature/event database when listing components. Always include it as a **separate architectural element**.
> 4. **Confusing FPR and FNR:** The denominator is different — FPR uses $(FP + TN)$, FNR uses $(FN + TP)$. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Not commenting on architecture trade-offs:** A 14-mark answer that only lists components without discussing **centralized vs. distributed** or **inline vs. passive** trade-offs will lose up to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Components and Architecture**

- **Five Core Components (Memorize Order):** Sensor/Agent → Analyzer → Database → Management Console → Response Module.
- **IDS = Out-of-Band (Passive), IPS = In-Band (Inline).**
- **Sensor placement:** NIDPS at chokepoints (perimeter, DMZ, segment junctions); HIDPS agent on each protected host.
- **Detection methods:** Signature-based (low FPR, high FNR for novel attacks), Anomaly-based (high TPR, higher FPR).
- **Detection Latency formula:** $D_{latency} = \sum \delta_i$ — minimize $\delta_3$ (sensor-to-analyzer transit) by co-location or distribution.
- **FPR formula:** $FPR = FP / (FP + TN)$.
- **FNR formula:** $FNR = FN / (FN + TP)$.
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — best single metric for IDPS accuracy.
- **Single point of failure:** Centralized analyzer architecture has this risk — mitigate with HA clustering or distributed analyzers.
- **Encrypted channel:** All sensor-to-analyzer and analyzer-to-console communication must use **TLS 1.3 or SSH**.
- **Inline IPS failure mode:** If the IPS fails closed, traffic stops; if it fails open, traffic flows uninspected — this is a **critical architectural decision**.
- **Throughput units:** NIDPS uses **Mbps/Gbps**, HIDPS uses **EPS (Events Per Second)**.
- **Cloud-native sensors:** Modern architectures use agent-based or eBPF-based sensors inside cloud workloads (AWS GuardDuty, Azure Defender pattern).
- **Hybrid architecture:** Combines NIDPS, HIDPS, and cloud sensors feeding a SIEM-based central correlation engine.
- **For KTU numerical problems:** Always show all four steps — identification, FPR, FNR, F1 — and end with a **comment** on system quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS), a **Sensor Location** refers to the deliberate strategic placement of network or host-based monitoring probes (sensors) at specific topological points within an IT infrastructure so that they can observe, capture, and analyze the maximum amount of security-relevant traffic while maintaining operational efficiency. The selection of a sensor location is governed by the principles of **network visibility**, **attack surface reduction**, **choke-point identification**, and **evasion resistance**.

According to the NIST SP 800-94 (Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems) framework, sensor placement directly determines the *coverage* and *evasion resistance* of an IDPS deployment. A sensor is the data-collection component of the IDPS, and its placement determines what traffic it can "see."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sensor placement is a critical architectural decision in IDPS engineering. Incorrect placement results in **blind spots**, **asymmetric routing issues**, and **evasion vulnerabilities**, which are frequently tested in KTU ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home security system. You wouldn't put CCTV cameras only in your bedroom while leaving the front door, windows, and garage completely unwatched — that would be useless, even if you have the most expensive cameras in the world.

Similarly, an IDPS sensor is like a CCTV camera for your network. The **location** of the camera matters more than the brand. A **Network-Based IDPS (NIDPS) sensor** should be placed where all critical traffic flows — much like placing a camera at the **main gate** of a residential colony. A **Host-Based IDPS (HIDPS) sensor** sits *inside* a critical server (like a security guard posted inside a bank vault).

### 1.2.1 Simple Real-World Mapping

| Real-World Element | Network Equivalent | IDPS Sensor Analogy |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Main Gate of Building | Network Perimeter / Edge Router | NIDPS Sensor |
| Building Lobby | DMZ Network | NIDPS Sensor |
| Inside Bank Vault | Critical Server | HIDPS Agent |
| Hallway Intersections | Internal Choke Points | NIDPS Sensor |
| Wi-Fi Access Points | Wireless Network | Wireless IDPS Sensor |

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard placement metrics used in IDPS engineering:

- **Sensor Coverage Ratio (SCR):** The fraction of total network traffic observable by the sensor.
- **Latency Threshold:** Maximum tolerable delay introduced by the sensor, typically **< 1 ms** for inline IPS.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF) Tolerance:** A metric evaluating redundancy.
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Influenced by sensor location.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Often location-dependent due to traffic asymmetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition for Exam:** *A sensor location is the topological coordinate in a network architecture where an IDPS probe is installed to maximize visibility of malicious traffic while minimizing blind spots and evasion opportunities.*

---

## 1.4 Categories of Sensor Locations

Sensor placements in modern enterprise networks are broadly classified into the following zones:

1. **Network-Based Sensor Locations**
   - Perimeter (before the firewall)
   - DMZ (between firewall layers)
   - Internal Network Choke Points
   - Server Farm / Data Center Core
   - Branch Office Edge

2. **Host-Based Sensor Locations**
   - Critical Servers (DB, Application, Authentication)
   - End-user Workstations (selectively)
   - Virtual Hosts / Hypervisors
   - Cloud Workloads

3. **Hybrid and Specialized Locations**
   - Wireless Sensor Placement (near WLCs / APs)
   - Cloud-Native Sensor Placement (VPC traffic mirroring)
   - Operational Technology (OT) / ICS Network Segments

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Governing Sensor Placement

The placement of IDPS sensors follows five foundational engineering principles:

### Principle 1: Choke-Point Identification
A **choke point** is a topological location through which all (or nearly all) traffic between two network segments must pass. Examples include routers, firewalls, layer-3 switches, and core switches.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever asked about optimal sensor placement, the first answer should *always* be — *"At the network choke point."*

### Principle 2: Defense-in-Depth (Layered Placement)
Multiple sensors are deployed at distinct layers so that if one sensor fails or is evaded, others provide detection. This is called **Defense-in-Depth**.

### Principle 3: Traffic Asymmetry Avoidance
On asymmetric routed networks (common in load-balanced environments), a sensor placed at the **egress path** may see only outbound packets and miss the inbound responses, leading to incomplete state tracking. Placement must account for routing paths.

### Principle 4: Evasion Resistance
An attacker may use **fragmentation**, **encoding**, or **encrypted tunnels** to evade sensors placed deep inside the network. Sensors must be placed *before* encryption points if encrypted payload inspection is required.

### Principle 5: Performance Budget (Inline vs Passive)
- **Passive (NIDS mode):** Sensor sees a copy of traffic via **SPAN port** or **network TAP**. **Zero latency impact.**
- **Inline (NIPS mode):** Sensor sits physically in the path. Adds latency and can become a **SPOF** if not redundant.

## 2.2 Standard Network Sensor Placement Points

| Placement Zone | Traffic Observed | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outside the Firewall (Internet Edge)** | All inbound + outbound traffic | Detects attacks *before* firewall filtering | High noise, encrypted traffic blind |
| **DMZ (between firewalls)** | Traffic to/from public-facing servers | Detects attacks targeting web/email servers | Limited internal visibility |
| **Inside the Internal Firewall** | East-West + North-South traffic | Detects internal reconnaissance, lateral movement | High volume, performance load |
| **Server Farm / Data Center Core** | Server-to-server traffic | Detects DB attacks, privilege escalation | Requires high-speed taps |
| **Branch / Remote Site Edge** | Site-specific traffic | Localized detection | Limited context |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Sensor Location Engineering**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor Coverage Ratio | $SCR = \frac{T_{observed}}{T_{total}}$ | Fraction of traffic seen by sensor |
| Network Visibility | $V = \frac{N_{choke\ points\ monitored}}{N_{total\ choke\ points}}$ | Decimal or percentage form |
| Inline Latency Bound | $L_{sensor} < L_{threshold}$ where $L_{threshold} = 1\ ms$ | For inline IPS deployment |
| Redundancy Factor | $R = N_{sensors\ parallel}$ | For SPOF elimination |
| Evasion Probability | $P_{evade} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - e_i)$ | Where $e_i$ is evasion probability of $i$-th sensor |
| Detection Confidence (Bayesian) | $P(D \mid S) = \frac{P(S \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(S)}$ | Confidence of detection given sensor signal |

**Units & Notes:**
- $T_{observed}$ and $T_{total}$ are measured in **packets/sec** or **bytes/sec**.
- $L_{sensor}$ is measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- $V$ is dimensionless (range $\vert 0,1 \mid$).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World IDPS Deployments

- **Enterprise Networks:** Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto, and Suricata sensors are deployed at perimeter, DMZ, and core switches.
- **Cloud (AWS):** VPC Traffic Mirroring acts as a "virtual TAP" enabling cloud-native sensor placement.
- **ICS/SCADA Networks:** Sensors are placed at the boundary between IT and OT networks (Purdue Model Levels 3–4 boundary).
- **Zero Trust Architectures:** Sensors are placed at every microsegment boundary.

---

## 2.5 Architectural Considerations

### 2.5.1 Switched vs. Shared Media
On **legacy shared-media Ethernet (hubs)**, a sensor naturally sees all traffic — no configuration needed. On modern **switched networks**, a sensor requires either:
- A **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port** mirror, OR
- A dedicated **Network TAP (Test Access Point)**.

### 2.5.2 Encrypted Traffic Handling
If traffic is encrypted (TLS/SSH/IPsec), sensors must be placed:
- **Before encryption** (on the originating host — requires endpoint agent), OR
- **After decryption** (on a TLS-terminating proxy / load balancer).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Assuming sensors can decrypt all traffic by default. *They cannot.* Placement must coincide with the decryption boundary.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Analytical Problem: Optimal Sensor Placement for Maximum Coverage

### Problem Setup
A corporate network has $N$ critical choke points. We want to place the minimum number of sensors such that every choke point is monitored. This is equivalent to the **Minimum Dominating Set** problem in graph theory.

### Derivation

Let the network graph be $G = (V, E)$, where:
- $V$ = set of choke points (nodes)
- $E$ = set of communication links (edges)

A **sensor placement** is a subset $S \subseteq V$ such that every vertex in $V$ is either:
1. In $S$ (has its own sensor), OR
2. Adjacent to a vertex in $S$ (visible via a neighboring sensor).

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$
\forall v \in V : \quad v \in S \quad \lor \quad \exists u \in S : (u, v) \in E
$$

**Step-by-Step Algorithm (Greedy Minimum Dominating Set):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Initialize } S = \emptyset, \quad \text{Covered} = \emptyset \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{While Covered} \neq V: \\
                    & \quad \text{Select } v^* = \arg\max_{v \in V} \vert N(v) \setminus \text{Covered} \vert \\
                    & \quad S \leftarrow S \cup \{v^*\} \\
                    & \quad \text{Covered} \leftarrow \text{Covered} \cup N(v^*) \cup \{v^*\} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Return } S
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $N(v)$ denotes the set of neighbors of vertex $v$ in the graph $G$.

> **Logic Explanation:** This algorithm greedily places a sensor at the choke point that covers the maximum number of *uncovered* neighbors, ensuring optimal sensor utilization per placement.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem: Calculating Detection Confidence for Layered Sensors

### Problem Setup
Three sensors (S1, S2, S3) are placed in layers. Each has an independent evasion probability $e_1 = 0.05$, $e_2 = 0.10$, $e_3 = 0.08$.

### Derivation

The probability that an attack evades **all** sensors is:

$$
P_{evade\ all} = \prod_{i=1}^{3} e_i = e_1 \times e_2 \times e_3
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{evade\ all} &= 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.08 \\
                &= 0.0004 \\
                &= 0.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **overall detection probability** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{detect} &= 1 - P_{evade\ all} \\
           &= 1 - 0.0004 \\
           &= 0.9996 \\
           &= 99.96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Step 1: Each sensor independently fails to detect with probability $e_i$.
- Step 2: For the attack to evade ALL sensors, all independent failures must occur → use product rule.
- Step 3: Complement gives the detection probability.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem: Sensor Coverage Ratio in a Branch Office

### Problem Setup
A branch office routes 60% of its traffic through the central HQ (asymmetric routing). A NIDPS sensor is placed only at HQ. What is the effective $SCR$ for branch-local attacks?

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
SCR &= \frac{T_{observed}}{T_{total}} \\
    &= \frac{T_{centralized}}{T_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Given:
- $T_{centralized} = 0.60 \times T_{total}$ (60% observed at HQ sensor)
- $T_{local} = 0.40 \times T_{total}$ (40% never reaches HQ sensor)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SCR &= \frac{0.60 \cdot T_{total}}{T_{total}} \\
    &= 0.60 \\
    &= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** 40% of traffic is *branch-local* (e.g., between local clients and a local printer/file server) and never traverses the HQ sensor. Hence 40% is a **blind spot**.

---

## 3.4 Decision Matrix: Where to Place a Sensor — KTU Style

Use this decision matrix during KTU exam answers:

| Scenario | Recommended Sensor Location | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Detect inbound internet attacks | **Outside Firewall (Internet Edge)** | Sees attacks before filtering |
| Detect attacks on Web Servers | **DMZ** | Web traffic choke point |
| Detect lateral movement / insider threats | **Internal Core Switch** | Sees East-West traffic |
| Detect database exfiltration | **In front of DB Server** | Last-mile visibility |
| Detect Wi-Fi attacks | **Adjacent to WLC / APs** | Wireless segment visibility |
| Detect VM-to-VM attacks | **Hypervisor / vSwitch TAP** | Virtual environment visibility |
| Detect cloud workload attacks | **VPC Traffic Mirror / Sidecar** | Cloud-native equivalent of TAP |
| Detect OT/ICS attacks | **Purdue Level 3.5 Boundary** | IT/OT demarcation point |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Enterprise Network Sensor Placement Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    internetNode[Internet] --> edgeRouter[Edge Router]
    edgeRouter --> outerFirewall[Outer Firewall]
    outerFirewall --> sensorS1[Sensor S1 - Perimeter NIDPS]
    sensorS1 --> innerFirewall[Inner Firewall DMZ]
    innerFirewall --> dmzSensors[DMZ Sensors NIDPS]
    dmzSensors --> webServer[Web Server]
    dmzSensors --> mailServer[Mail Server]
    innerFirewall --> coreSwitch[Core Layer 3 Switch]
    coreSwitch --> sensorS2[Sensor S2 - Internal Choke Point]
    sensorS2 --> userVLAN[User VLAN]
    sensorS2 --> serverFarm[Server Farm Switch]
    serverFarm --> dbServer[Database Server]
    serverFarm --> appServer[Application Server]
    dbServer --> hidpsAgent1[Host Sensor HIDPS Agent]
    appServer --> hidpsAgent2[Host Sensor HIDPS Agent]
    serverFarm --> wirelessCtrl[Wireless Controller]
    wirelessCtrl --> apSensor[Wireless Sensor AP]
    coreSwitch --> cloudGateway[Cloud Gateway]
    cloudGateway --> vpcMirror[VPC Traffic Mirror]
    vpcMirror --> cloudSensor[Cloud Native Sensor]

    style internetNode fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style sensorS1 fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff
    style dmzSensors fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff
    style sensorS2 fill:#2ecc71,color:#ffffff
    style hidpsAgent1 fill:#f39c12,color:#ffffff
    style hidpsAgent2 fill:#f39c12,color:#ffffff
    style apSensor fill:#9b59b6,color:#ffffff
    style vpcMirror fill:#16a085,color:#ffffff
    style cloudSensor fill:#16a085,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Blue boxes (S1, DMZ):** Perimeter / NIDPS — placed at network edges.
- **Green box (S2):** Internal choke point NIDPS — for East-West traffic.
- **Orange boxes (HIDPS agents):** Host-based sensors inside critical servers.
- **Purple box:** Wireless-specific sensor.
- **Teal boxes:** Cloud-native sensor placement via VPC Traffic Mirroring.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Choke Point Identification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Network Topology] --> B[Map All Traffic Flows]
    B --> C[Find Convergence Points]
    C --> D{Is Point Mandatory for Traffic?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mark as Choke Point]
    D -- No --> F[Optional Observation Point]
    E --> G[Place Primary NIDPS Sensor]
    F --> H[Place Secondary HIDPS Agent]
    G --> I[Validate with SCR Formula]
    H --> I
    I --> J{SCR Greater Than Threshold}
    J -- Yes --> K[Deployment Approved]
    J -- No --> L[Add Redundant Sensor Layer]

    style A fill:#2c3e50,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#2980b9,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#e67e22,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#16a085,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
```

**Logic Flow Explanation:**
1. Begin by mapping the topology.
2. Identify all convergence points (routers, firewalls, core switches).
3. Distinguish mandatory vs. optional choke points.
4. Place NIDPS at mandatory choke points; HIDPS as secondary.
5. Validate using the $SCR$ formula.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Inline vs Passive Sensor Placement

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PassiveMode["PASSIVE SENSOR PLACEMENT (NIDS Mode)"]
        src1[Traffic Source] --> sw1[Switch]
        sw1 --> spanPort[SPAN Port Mirror]
        spanPort --> passiveSensor[Passive NIDS Sensor]
        sw1 --> dest1[Destination Host]
    end

    subgraph InlineMode["INLINE SENSOR PLACEMENT (NIPS Mode)"]
        src2[Traffic Source] --> inlineSensor[Inline NIPS Sensor]
        inlineSensor --> dest2[Destination Host]
        inlineSensor -.Blocks.-> blockAction[Drops Malicious Packets]
    end

    style passiveSensor fill:#3498db,color:#ffffff
    style inlineSensor fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style blockAction fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
```

**Key Insight:** The **passive sensor** observes a mirrored copy and never blocks traffic. The **inline sensor** physically interrupts the traffic flow and can drop packets in real-time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Choke Point" in the context of IDPS sensor placement. Why is it important to place network-based sensors at choke points?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A choke point is a topological location in the network through which all (or nearly all) traffic between two segments must necessarily pass (e.g., a router, firewall, or core switch).
- **Importance (1 Mark):** Placing a sensor at a choke point ensures maximum visibility with minimum sensor count, yielding a high Sensor Coverage Ratio (SCR).
- **Strategic Benefit (1 Mark):** It enables cost-efficient monitoring of large traffic volumes and reduces blind spots caused by alternate routing paths.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between passive and inline sensor placements in a Network-Based IDPS. Mention one deployment scenario for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Passive (1 Mark):** The sensor receives a copy of traffic via a SPAN port or network TAP. It does not interrupt the traffic flow and adds zero latency. *Example:* Monitoring DMZ web traffic without affecting throughput.
- **Inline (1 Mark):** The sensor is placed physically in the data path and can drop malicious packets in real-time. Adds latency; may become a SPOF. *Example:* IPS deployment between edge router and firewall to block inbound malware.
- **Trade-off (1 Mark):** Inline offers prevention capability, whereas passive only offers detection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram, the **different network-based sensor locations** in a typical enterprise architecture. Discuss the role of choke points in maximizing visibility. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An enterprise network has three sensors placed in series: at the perimeter, in the DMZ, and at the internal core. If each sensor independently evades an attack with probability 0.04, 0.06, and 0.05 respectively, calculate the **overall probability that an attack is detected**. Justify your answer mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Introduce the layered enterprise topology — Internet → Edge Router → Firewall → DMZ → Core → Server Farm.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Enumerate sensor locations:
- **Outside Firewall:** Detects pre-filtered attacks.
- **DMZ:** Detects attacks on web/mail servers.
- **Internal Core (Choke Point):** Detects lateral movement.
- **Server Farm:** Detects DB/App-layer attacks.
- **Host Layer:** HIDPS agents on critical servers.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Explain choke point concept — topological convergence of traffic. Cite router, firewall, and L3 switch as classical choke points. Justify with the $SCR$ formula.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Present the layered diagram (refer Section 4.1) showing all sensor placements and color-code by type.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the evasion probabilities:
$$
e_1 = 0.04, \quad e_2 = 0.06, \quad e_3 = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Apply the independent evasion product rule:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{evade\ all} &= e_1 \times e_2 \times e_3 \\
               &= 0.04 \times 0.06 \times 0.05 \\
               &= 0.00012
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute the overall detection probability:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{detect} &= 1 - P_{evade\ all} \\
           &= 1 - 0.00012 \\
           &= 0.99988 \\
           &\approx 99.99\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Conclude that defense-in-depth with multiple sensors drastically reduces evasion probability. Justify mathematically by comparing single-sensor detection (96%) vs layered detection (99.99%).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** What are the **architectural considerations** for placing IDPS sensors in **switched networks**? Explain the role of **SPAN ports** and **Network TAPs**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a branch office, only **60% of traffic** flows through the central HQ. A NIDPS sensor is deployed at the HQ. Compute the **Sensor Coverage Ratio (SCR)** and identify the **blind spot percentage**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Switched networks isolate traffic by VLAN/port, so a sensor connected to a normal switch port sees *only its own traffic*. This necessitates mirror mechanisms.

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Explain **SPAN Port:**
- A Switched Port Analyzer mirrors traffic from one or more source ports to a destination port where the sensor is connected.
- **Limitations:** SPAN ports drop packets under congestion; configuration errors are common; many switches limit SPAN source count.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Explain **Network TAP:**
- A hardware device that physically splits the optical/electrical signal.
- Provides a **full-duplex copy** with **zero packet loss**.
- Preferred for high-speed (10G/40G/100G) production environments.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** State the given data:
$$
T_{centralized} = 0.60 \cdot T_{total}
$$

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Apply the SCR formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
SCR &= \frac{T_{observed}}{T_{total}} \\
    &= \frac{0.60 \cdot T_{total}}{T_{total}} \\
    &= 0.60 = 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute the blind spot:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Blind Spot} &= 1 - SCR \\
                  &= 1 - 0.60 \\
                  &= 0.40 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** 40% of branch-local traffic is invisible to the HQ sensor. **Recommendation:** Place a secondary sensor at the branch office to cover the local East-West traffic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Confusing *passive* with *inline* placement. *(Loss: 2 marks)* — Always state the mode explicitly.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to compute the **complement** $1 - P_{evade\ all}$ in detection probability problems. *(Loss: 3 marks)*
> - **Pitfall 3:** Writing $SCR$ as a percentage without showing the **unit-less ratio derivation**. *(Loss: 1 mark)*
> - **Pitfall 4:** Omitting the **diagram** in 7-mark questions — diagrams are *mandatory* and carry 2 marks.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Ignoring **encrypted traffic** implications when describing placement at choke points. *(Loss: 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sensor Locations**

- ✅ A **choke point** is the **single best location** for a network-based sensor.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** = multiple sensors at multiple layers (perimeter, DMZ, internal core, host).
- ✅ **Passive (NIDS)** = mirror traffic via SPAN/TAP, no blocking, no latency.
- ✅ **Inline (NIPS)** = physically in path, can block, introduces latency, possible SPOF.
- ✅ **Switched networks** require **SPAN ports** or **Network TAPs** for sensor visibility.
- ✅ **Encrypted traffic** must be inspected *before encryption* or *after decryption*.
- ✅ **Sensor Coverage Ratio (SCR)** = $\frac{T_{observed}}{T_{total}}$ — measures placement effectiveness.
- ✅ **Detection probability** for layered sensors: $P_{detect} = 1 - \prod e_i$.
- ✅ **Asymmetric routing** causes blind spots — place sensors on **both directions** of traffic.
- ✅ **Cloud-native placement** uses VPC Traffic Mirroring (AWS), vTAP (Azure), or sidecar pods (Kubernetes).
- ✅ **OT/ICS networks** require sensors at the **Purdue Level 3.5 IT/OT boundary**.
- ✅ **Always** draw a diagram in 7-mark and 14-mark answers to earn full marks.
- ✅ **HIDPS** agents are deployed on **critical servers** (DB, AD, Auth) — not on every endpoint.
- ✅ **Wireless sensors** must be placed at the WLC or via AP-embedded IDS modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Gathering Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Information Gathering Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 – PECCT742, Module 3):**
> *Information Gathering Capabilities* covers the techniques, data sources, sensors, and tools that an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) uses to **collect raw telemetry** from the protected environment, and the corresponding **attack-side reconnaissance techniques** the IDPS must learn to recognise.

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Information Gathering (IDPS Context).**
> *Information Gathering Capability* in an IDPS is the structured ability of the system to **acquire, normalise, and correlate** data from heterogeneous sources — network packets, host logs, application telemetry, and stateful flow records — so that suspicious activity (including the attacker's own reconnaissance) can be detected, classified, and (in a prevention system) blocked in real time.

In short, an IDPS is only as intelligent as the data it can see. The richer and more accurate the **information gathering pipeline**, the lower the false-negative rate and the higher the fidelity of detection.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Triage Ward.**
> Imagine a hospital emergency ward. Before a doctor can diagnose a patient, the **reception desk** (the information-gathering layer) records vitals, past records, and symptoms from multiple sources (thermometer, blood test, patient history). The **IDPS sensor** plays exactly this role: it pulls data from the *network thermometer* (packet sniffer), the *patient file* (host log), and the *X-ray machine* (deep packet inspection) and hands it to the *doctor* (the detection engine). A ward with broken instruments cannot make a correct diagnosis — neither can an IDPS with poor information gathering.

### 1.3 The Two Sides of the Topic

This topic is best understood from **two complementary viewpoints**:

| Viewpoint | Direction | Purpose in IDPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Defensive Gathering** | IDPS → Environment | Sensors pull data to *detect* attacks. |
| **Offensive Gathering (Reconnaissance)** | Attacker → Environment | IDPS must *recognise* probing activity directed at the network. |

Both directions are studied in Module 3 because an IDPS must simultaneously:
1. **Collect** its own intelligence about the protected zone.
2. **Detect** an attacker's intelligence-gathering activity against the protected zone.

### 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Bandwidth (B):** measured in **bps / Mbps / Gbps**; defines the rate at which packet-level data can be ingested.
> - **Packet Capture Rate (PCR):** packets per second (**pps**) the sensor can sustain without drop.
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** average latency between attack onset and IDPS alert.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR):** $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$.
> - **False Negative Rate (FNR):** $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$.
> - **OSI Layers 2–7** are the primary zones in which information is captured.

### 1.5 Scope of Information Sources

The IDPS may pull from any of the following planes, summarised in the callout below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Information Planes of an IDPS:**
> 1. **Network Plane (N-IDS/N-IPS):** raw frames, packets, flow records.
> 2. **Host Plane (H-IDS/H-IPS):** syscalls, registry, file hashes, audit logs.
> 3. **Application Plane:** web, DNS, database, mail logs.
> 4. **Wireless Plane:** 802.11 frames, probe requests.
> 5. **Virtualisation/Cloud Plane:** hypervisor events, API call logs, vNIC telemetry.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Information-Gathering Pipeline (Five-Stage Model)

> [!TIP]
> A production-grade IDPS converts raw environmental data into a **detection verdict** through a deterministic five-stage pipeline. Memorising these stages earns easy marks in descriptive questions.

1. **Acquisition (Sensing)** – raw bits are captured from a *sensor point* (tap, SPAN port, host agent, API hook).
2. **Pre-processing (Normalisation)** – timestamps are unified, headers decoded, vendor-specific logs re-formatted into a common schema (typically **CEF** or **LEEF**).
3. **Reduction / Aggregation** – similar events are coalesced; e.g., thousands of `SYN` packets from one IP become a single *port-scan alert*.
4. **Correlation & Detection** – events are compared against **signatures**, **anomaly baselines**, or **stateful policy rules** to produce a verdict.
5. **Response / Reporting** – the verdict triggers an action: log, alert, drop, quarantine, or update a black-hole route.

### 2.2 Defensive Information-Gathering Techniques

#### 2.2.1 Network-Based Gathering

| Technique | What is Captured | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Packet Sniffing (libpcap, BPF)** | Full L2–L7 frame | Deep visibility | Requires tap/port-mirror; encrypted payloads invisible |
| **Flow Records (NetFlow v5/v9, sFlow, IPFIX)** | Per-flow summaries (5-tuple, byte count, duration) | Scalable, sampled | No payload; coarse granularity |
| **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** | Payload + state | Detects application-layer attacks | CPU-intensive; defeated by strong encryption |
| **Network Taps / SPAN** | Wire-speed copy of traffic | No packet loss | Physical/port cost |
| **Inline (Bump-in-the-Wire)** | Same as tap + ability to drop | Enables **prevention** | Latency; single point of failure |

#### 2.2.2 Host-Based Gathering

| Data Source | Information Extracted |
|---|---|
| **System Call Trace (`strace`, auditd)** | Process → kernel interactions; useful for detecting buffer-overflow patterns. |
| **File Integrity Monitor (FIM)** | Hashes of critical files; detects tampering (Tripwire/AIDE model). |
| **Registry Snapshots (Windows)** | Autorun keys, services, scheduled tasks. |
| **Process / DLL Load Events** | Reveals code injection, hollowing, persistence. |
| **Host Firewall & Audit Logs** | Connection attempts, privilege escalations. |

#### 2.2.3 Application & Protocol-Aware Gathering

- **HTTP logs** → URI patterns, user-agents, response codes.
- **DNS logs** → DGA domains, tunnel beacons.
- **Authentication logs (Windows Event 4624/4625, Linux PAM)** → brute-force patterns.

### 2.3 Offensive Information-Gathering Techniques (What the IDPS Must Detect)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Reconnaissance.**
> *Reconnaissance* is the **pre-attack phase** in which an adversary maps the target's topology, open services, operating systems, and vulnerabilities. From the IDPS perspective, the *only* goal of recognising reconnaissance is to **raise an early-warning alert** so the security team can react before the kill-chain advances to exploitation.

The classic taxonomy is:

1. **Passive Reconnaissance** – derives intelligence from public / side-channel sources (WHOIS, Shodan, Google Dorks, social media). Almost invisible to the IDPS at the network plane.
2. **Active Reconnaissance** – actively probes the target, leaving traces in network/host logs.

| Active Technique | Detectable Signature |
|---|---|
| **Ping Sweep** | High rate of ICMP Echo Requests from one source |
| **TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`)** | Many `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `FIN` triplets to varied ports |
| **SYN Stealth / Half-open Scan (`-sS`)** | `SYN` followed by `RST` (no completion) — **no log on target** but visible to N-IDS |
| **FIN / NULL / XMAS Scans** | Packets with unusual flag combinations |
| **UDP Scan (`-sU`)** | High rate of UDP packets, often with `ICMP port unreachable` replies |
| **OS Fingerprinting** | Crafted packets eliciting differing TTL/Window/DF responses |
| **Version Detection (`-sV`)** | Probe payloads sent to open ports |
| **Vulnerability Scan (Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS)** | Sequential probes against many ports + known-CVE payloads |
| **SNMP Enumeration** | Repeated queries to UDP/161 with `public`, `private`, `manager` communities |
| **SMB / NetBIOS Enumeration** | Sessions requesting shares, user lists, RID cycling |
| **DNS Zone Transfer Attempt** | `AXFR` query from unauthorised source |
| **Web Crawling / Spidering** | High-volume sequential `GET` requests, often in lexicographic order |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Mandatory Recall Table.** This table consolidates every formula, definition boundary, and unit the examiner can legitimately test on this topic. Memorise before any internal or ESE attempt.

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression / Boundary | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Capture Rate (PCR)** | $\text{PCR} \ge \text{Link Rate} \times \frac{\text{Avg Pkt Size}}{\text{Max Pkt Size}}$ | pps — for a **1 Gbps** link of 64-byte frames, PCR ≥ **1.488 Mpps** |
| 2 | **Sampling Rate (sFlow)** | $s = \frac{\text{sampled pkts}}{\text{total pkts}}$, $0 < s \le 1$ | Dimensionless; typical $s = 1/2048$ |
| 3 | **False Positive Rate** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le \text{FPR} \le 1$ |
| 4 | **False Negative Rate** | $\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $0 \le \text{FNR} \le 1$ |
| 5 | **Detection Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Bounded in $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | **Mean Time to Detect** | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{\text{alert}_i} - t_{\text{attack}_i})$ | seconds |
| 7 | **Banner-Grabbing Detection Threshold** | Alert if distinct *service banners* from a single IP exceed $K$ within $\Delta t$ | $K$ typically 5–10 |
| 8 | **Port-Scan Heuristic (Simpson Coefficient)** | $S_c = \frac{\lvert H_X \cap H_Y \rvert}{\min(\lvert H_X \rvert, \lvert H_Y \rvert)}$ | Used in TRW (Threshold Random Walk) algorithm |
| 9 | **Bayes Posterior for Anomaly** | $P(A \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}$ | Basis of Bayesian IDPS sensors |
| 10 | **Shannon Entropy (Payload Anomaly)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol — high entropy ⇒ encrypted/compressed payload |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility.** The formulas above are not just academic — they drive real product specifications. For instance, an enterprise N-IDS sensor sold for a **10 Gbps** link must support a wire-speed PCR of **≈ 14.88 Mpps** at minimum packet size **64 B**, which is why high-end sensors use dedicated **ASIC / FPGA** capture cards rather than general-purpose NICs.

### 2.5 Information-Gathering Architecture Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Canonical Deployment Architectures** (tested frequently in 14-mark questions):

- **Centralised Architecture:** one or few central engines analyse data from distributed sensors.
- **Distributed / Hierarchical:** sensors pre-process locally; only alerts are sent up.
- **Fully Distributed / Agent-based:** every host runs a small agent that decides locally (e.g., modern EDR agents).

### 2.6 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice

In production security operations centres (SOCs), the **information-gathering tier** accounts for roughly **60–70 % of total deployment cost** (sensors, taps, log forwarders, SIEM licences, storage). A clear conceptual grasp of this layer is what separates a network engineer from a security architect.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section derives the *engineering numbers* an examiner loves to test: the **minimum capture rate of a sensor**, the **entropy of normal vs malicious payload**, and the **TRW scan-detection threshold**. Each step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Packet Capture Rate for a Wire-Speed Sensor

**Problem:** A network link operates at line rate $L$ bits/s. Frames have an *average* size $S_{\text{avg}}$ bytes and a *minimum* size $S_{\min}$ bytes. The sensor's capture engine must be dimensioned to *never* drop a packet even under worst-case minimum-sized frames. Derive the minimum packet capture rate $\text{PCR}_{\min}$.

**Step 1 — Worst-Case Frame Rate.**
The worst case (highest frame arrival rate) occurs when every frame is the smallest legal one, $S_{\min}$. Hence the *maximum* packets-per-second the link can deliver is

$$
\text{PPS}_{\max} = \frac{L}{8 \cdot S_{\min}}.
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to a Numerical Example.**
Take $L = 1\,\text{Gbps} = 10^{9}\,\text{bits/s}$ and $S_{\min} = 64\,\text{bytes} = 512\,\text{bits}$.

$$
\text{PPS}_{\max} = \frac{10^{9}}{8 \times 64} = \frac{10^{9}}{512} \approx 1{,}953{,}125\ \text{pps}.
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Preamble + IFG Overhead.**
Ethernet adds 8 bytes of preamble + 12 bytes of inter-frame gap per packet, so the *real* number of packets that may arrive in one second is

$$
\text{PCR}_{\min} = \frac{L}{8 \cdot (S_{\min} + 20)} = \frac{10^{9}}{8 \times 84} \approx 1{,}488{,}095\ \text{pps}.
$$

**Step 4 — Generalised Engineering Rule.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PCR}_{\min} = \frac{L}{8 \cdot (S_{\min} + O)}\;}\quad \text{where } O = 20\ \text{bytes (Ethernet preamble + IFG).}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Always remember to add the **20-byte Ethernet overhead**; many students forget it and lose a mark.

### 3.2 Derivation: Detecting a TCP SYN Stealth Scan Using the TRW Heuristic

**Goal:** Show analytically how a sensor *distinguishes* a scan (successes are *rare*, failures are *common*) from a legitimate client (successes are *common*, failures are *rare*).

**Step 1 — Define Two Hypotheses.**
$H_0$ = *legitimate client*, $H_1$ = *scanner*.

**Step 2 — Assign Observation Likelihoods.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{success} \mid H_0) &= \alpha_{0} \approx 0.8, \\
P(\text{success} \mid H_1) &= \alpha_{1} \approx 0.05.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Sequential Likelihood Ratio.**

$$
\Lambda_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_1)}{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_0)}.
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Log Transformation.**

$$
\log \Lambda_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \frac{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_1)}{P(\text{obs}_i \mid H_0)}.
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Boundaries.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \Lambda_n \ge \eta_{\text{upper}} &\Rightarrow \text{Decide } H_1 \text{ (scan)};\\
\log \Lambda_n \le \eta_{\text{lower}} &\Rightarrow \text{Decide } H_0 \text{ (legitimate)};\\
\text{otherwise} &\Rightarrow \text{continue observing}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Expected Detection Time.**
For Wald's SPRT, the average number of observations to a decision is finite because $\alpha_0 \ne \alpha_1$ — *this is what makes TRW far more efficient than fixed-window counters.*

> [!TIP]
> This is a **14-mark favourite**. Drawing the SPRT block diagram and writing the likelihood ratio explicitly will fetch full marks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Reconnaissance Techniques to IDPS Sensors

| Reconnaissance Type | OSI Layer | Primary IDPS Sensor | Detection Mechanism | Evasion Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ping Sweep** | L3 (ICMP) | N-IDS | Threshold of ICMP echo req / source IP | Low (trivially filtered) |
| **TCP SYN Scan** | L4 | N-IDS | `SYN` without completion count | Medium (use `-S` spoof) |
| **UDP Scan** | L4 | N-IDS | UDP → ICMP unreachable ratio | Medium |
| **OS Fingerprint** | L3-L4 | N-IDS | Anomalous TTL/Window/DF triplet | Hard (passive OSfp defeats it) |
| **Version Detection** | L7 | N-IDS (DPI) | Banner keywords (`Apache`, `OpenSSH 7.2`) | Medium |
| **SNMP Enumeration** | L7 | N-IDS | Repeated `public/private` community strings | Low |
| **DNS Zone Transfer** | L7 | N-IDS / App-IDS | `AXFR` query from non-authoritative IP | Low |
| **Web Spidering** | L7 | App-IDS / WAF | URI sequence entropy, request rate | Medium |
| **SMB Enumeration** | L7 | N-IDS | `NULL` sessions, RID cycling | Low |
| **Vulnerability Scan** | L7 | N-IDS + Vuln-Scanner | Known CVE payload signature | Medium |
| **Wireless Probe** | L2 (802.11) | WIDS | `ProbeReq` floods, SSID brute force | High (encrypted probes) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Shannon Entropy of an HTTP Payload

**Step 1 — Compute symbol frequencies.**
Consider a 100-character URL where bytes `A–Z` and `/` appear uniformly. There are 27 distinct symbols, each with probability $p_i = 1/27$.

**Step 2 — Plug into Shannon's formula.**

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{27} \frac{1}{27}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{27}\right) = \log_2 27 \approx 4.75\ \text{bits/symbol}.
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with random binary (8 bits) and English text (≈ 1.5 bits).**

$$
\boxed{\;1.5 < H_{\text{English}} < 4.75 < H_{\text{uniform-alnum}} < 8\;}\ \text{bits/symbol}.
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Decision Rule.**
If an HTTP payload's entropy exceeds a tunable threshold (commonly $H \ge 6.0$), the IDPS raises an *exfiltration / encrypted-tunnel* suspicion alert. This is the basis of modern **DLP-aware IDPS**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Each Module in PECCT742 contributes two full questions of **14 marks each** with internal choice.
> - Part A of the question paper has short 3-mark questions; two of them typically come from each module.
> - Cognitive levels: *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse* — answers must demonstrate all four to score above 80 %.

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define *Information Gathering* in the context of an IDPS. Differentiate between *passive* and *active* reconnaissance with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Information gathering* in an IDPS is the systematic process of **collecting, normalising and correlating** data from network, host and application sources so that intrusion activity (including an attacker's reconnaissance) can be detected. (1 mark)

**Passive reconnaissance** derives intelligence **without directly probing** the target — examples include querying **WHOIS**, reading job postings, or indexing **Shodan**. (1 mark)

**Active reconnaissance** involves **direct interaction** with the target — for example, an **ICMP ping sweep** or a **TCP SYN scan** that leaves detectable traces in IDPS logs. (1 mark)

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any *six* indicators that an IDPS can use to detect a TCP port scan.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per correct indicator):**

1. High rate of `SYN` packets to **multiple destination ports** from a single source IP.
2. `SYN` packets not followed by the corresponding `ACK` (half-open connections).
3. Packets sent to **unused or unusual** port numbers.
4. Single source IP targeting **many hosts** on the **same port** (vertical scan).
5. Single source IP targeting **one host** on **many ports** (horizontal scan).
6. Packets with anomalous flag combinations such as **FIN, NULL, XMAS**.
7. Time-interval between consecutive probes below a threshold (faster than human).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **five-stage information-gathering pipeline** of a Network Intrusion Detection System. For each stage, state the input, the processing performed, and the output that is handed to the next stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **SYN Stealth Scan** and the **FIN / NULL / XMAS Scan**. For each, explain the packet structure, the intended response from the target, and the signature that an IDPS uses to detect it.

##### Model Solution

**(a) Five-Stage Pipeline (7 marks — 1.4 marks per stage)**

1. **Acquisition** – Input: raw bits on the wire or from a host log file. Process: NIC/agent capture into ring buffer. Output: raw frames or unparsed log lines. *(1.4 marks)*
2. **Pre-processing / Normalisation** – Input: raw bytes. Process: timestamp synchronisation (NTP), header decoding, schema mapping. Output: normalised event records. *(1.4 marks)*
3. **Reduction / Aggregation** – Input: streams of events. Process: windowing, deduplication, threshold counters. Output: aggregated alerts. *(1.4 marks)*
4. **Correlation & Detection** – Input: aggregated events. Process: signature match, anomaly scoring, stateful rule evaluation. Output: detection verdict. *(1.4 marks)*
5. **Response / Reporting** – Input: verdict. Process: action selection (log, alert, drop, quarantine). Output: log entry, Syslog, SNMP trap, REST alert. *(1.4 marks)*

**(b) Stealth Scan Variants (7 marks — 3.5 marks per scan)**

**SYN Stealth / Half-Open Scan:**
- Attacker sends a `SYN` to the target port.
- On receiving `SYN-ACK`, attacker **does not** complete the handshake; instead sends `RST`.
- Because the connection is never fully opened, **most target operating systems do not log** the attempt in the application log.
- **IDPS detection signature:** high count of `SYN` → `RST(attacker)` pairs without a corresponding `ACK`; or use of the **TRW** algorithm comparing success/failure ratios. *(3.5 marks)*

**FIN / NULL / XMAS Scans:**
- `FIN`: only the `FIN` flag set; `NULL`: no flags; `XMAS`: all flags (FIN+URG+PSH) lit like a Christmas tree.
- RFC 793 states that a **closed port** must reply with `RST`, while an **open port** must drop the packet silently.
- **IDPS detection signature:** packets with *illegal or impossible* flag combinations, or bursts of `FIN/NULL/XMAS` packets to sequential ports within a short interval. *(3.5 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **Network-based**, **Host-based** and **Application-based** information gathering in IDPS. Tabulate the differences across at least **six** parameters.

**(b) [7 marks]** An organisation has a **1 Gbps** Ethernet link. Compute the **minimum packet capture rate** the IDPS sensor must sustain, assuming minimum Ethernet frame size of **64 bytes**. Show all steps and account for the 20-byte preamble/IFG overhead. If the sensor can only capture **1 Mpps**, what is the **maximum sustainable throughput** without packet loss?

##### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 0.5 mark per populated cell, plus 1 mark for explanation)**

| Parameter | Network-Based | Host-Based | Application-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Wire / SPAN | OS audit, syscalls | App server logs |
| **Visibility of Encrypted Traffic** | Limited (header only) | Limited (cleartext post-decrypt) | Excellent (inside app) |
| **Deployment Cost** | Medium (taps) | Low (agent install) | Low–Medium (log shippers) |
| **False Positive Rate** | Higher (no context) | Lower (host context) | Lowest (rich context) |
| **Coverage Scope** | Whole subnet | Single host | Specific app |
| **Performance Impact on Host** | None | Moderate (agent overhead) | Low |
| **Survives Network Encryption** | Partially | Yes | Yes |

Explanatory paragraph (1 mark): emphasise that **defence in depth** uses all three simultaneously because each plane has unique blind spots.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Minimum Frame Size.**
$S_{\min} = 64\ \text{bytes}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Ethernet Overhead.**
Each frame is preceded by 8-byte preamble and 12-byte IFG → overhead $O = 20$ bytes.
Total on-wire size per frame = $64 + 20 = 84$ bytes = $672$ bits.

**Step 3 — Compute PCR for 1 Gbps.**

$$
\text{PCR}_{\min} = \frac{10^{9}\ \text{bits/s}}{672\ \text{bits/frame}} \approx 1{,}488{,}095\ \text{pps}.
$$

*(3 marks — formula 1, substitution 1, result 1)*

**Step 4 — Compare with 1 Mpps Sensor.**
A sensor capturing at 1 Mpps would be the bottleneck.

**Step 5 — Maximum Sustainable Throughput.**

$$
L_{\max} = \text{PCR}_{\text{sensor}} \times 672\ \text{bits/frame} = 10^{6} \times 672 = 6.72 \times 10^{8}\ \text{bits/s}.
$$

$$
\boxed{\;L_{\max} = 672\ \text{Mbps}\;}\quad \text{(≈ 67.2 % of link capacity).}
$$

*(4 marks — formula 1, substitution 1, result 1, interpretation 1)*

**Conclusion:** The sensor **drops ≈ 32.8 % of traffic** at line rate → **not acceptable** for a 1 Gbps link; either upgrade to a multi-Mpps sensor or enable hardware-accelerated capture.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks (Verified from 2018–2024 KTU Valuation Reports):**
> 1. **Confusing "passive" with "active"** reconnaissance. *Passive = no traffic to the target*; *active = direct probes*. Examiners deduct 1 full mark every time this is reversed.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20-byte Ethernet overhead** in PCR calculations. This is the most common numerical slip.
> 3. **Writing "the IDPS prevents all attacks"** — examiners mark this as *factually wrong* because prevention fails on zero-day and encrypted threats. Always qualify with "**known signatures / policy matches**".
> 4. **Mixing up FPR and FNR** in definition questions. Memorise the exact numerator–denominator pair.
> 5. **Using code snippets in descriptive answers** for a 14-mark question. The KTU rubric explicitly deducts marks for irrelevant code in theory questions.
> 6. **Skipping the architecture diagram** in 14-mark questions. A *clean block diagram* of the IDPS pipeline is worth **2 easy marks** that most students leave on the table.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print and Carry to the Exam Hall.**

- [x] *Information Gathering* in IDPS = **collection + normalisation + correlation** of telemetry from N / H / A / W / Cloud planes.
- [x] Two viewpoints: **Defensive (sensor → environment)** and **Offensive (attacker → environment)**.
- [x] Five-stage pipeline: **Acquire → Normalise → Reduce → Correlate → Respond**.
- [x] Five information planes: **Network, Host, Application, Wireless, Virtualisation/Cloud**.
- [x] Defensive techniques: **Packet sniffing, NetFlow/sFlow, DPI, FIM, syscall tracing, application logs.**
- [x] Offensive techniques: **Ping sweep, SYN/ACK/FIN/NULL/XMAS/UDP scans, version detection, OS fingerprinting, SNMP/DNS/SMB enumeration, web spidering, vulnerability scanning.**
- [x] **PCR formula** with 20-byte overhead: $\text{PCR}_{\min} = \dfrac{L}{8(S_{\min} + 20)}$.
- [x] **TRW (Threshold Random Walk)** uses Bayesian likelihood ratio; **expected detection time is finite** for non-overlapping success probabilities.
- [x] **Shannon entropy** $H(X) \ge 6$ bits/symbol ⇒ encrypted / suspicious payload — basis of DLP-aware IDPS.
- [x] **FPR** = $FP / (FP + TN)$, **FNR** = $FN / (FN + TP)$ — never mix them.
- [x] **Centralised vs Distributed vs Agent-based** architectures — draw the diagram if asked.
- [x] Always state the **deployment plane** (N-IDS / H-IDS / Hybrid) and the **detection paradigm** (Signature / Anomaly / Stateful) when describing an IDPS.
- [x] Mention **evasion difficulty** when classifying recon techniques — a 14-mark answer is *incomplete* without it.
- [x] KTU loves **numerical** questions on 1 Gbps / 10 Gbps link capacity — practice at least three variants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Events Detected
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Events Detected

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** In the context of an **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)**, *events* are observable, recordable, and analyzable occurrences within a computing or network environment that may indicate a security-relevant action, policy violation, or threat. The **"types of events detected"** refer to the categorical classification of these occurrences based on their source, nature, severity, and the layer of the system stack at which they are manifested.

According to the **NIST SP 800-94** guideline (which is the de facto reference framework adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme cybersecurity electives), events detected by IDPS sensors can be broadly classified along multiple dimensions:

| Classification Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Source Layer** | Network, Host, Application |
| **Detection Mechanism** | Signature-based, Anomaly-based, Stateful |
| **Temporal Behavior** | Real-time, Near-real-time, Post-event |
| **Severity Level** | Informational, Low, Medium, High, Critical |
| **Attack Phase** | Pre-attack, During-attack, Post-attack |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Vault Monitoring System"**

Imagine a high-security bank vault equipped with hundreds of sensors:
- **Motion sensors** detect unusual movement (analogous to **network traffic anomalies**).
- **Pressure sensors** on the vault door detect forced entry attempts (analogous to **authentication brute-force events**).
- **Heat sensors** detect fire or drilling (analogous to **malware execution / buffer overflow events**).
- **Glass-break sensors** detect window tampering (analogous to **file integrity violations**).
- **Logbook entries** track every access (analogous to **audit log events**).

Just as a vault's central alarm system classifies these inputs into "categories of events" (e.g., break-in, fire, unauthorized access), an IDPS classifies its observations into **types of events detected**, each triggering a specific response rule.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics for Event Detection:**
> - **True Positive Rate (TPR)** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — Proportion of actual attacks correctly identified.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ — Proportion of benign events incorrectly flagged.
> - **Detection Latency (L)** = Time elapsed between event occurrence and IDPS alert generation, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Events Per Second (EPS)** = Peak throughput the IDPS sensor can process, typically **10,000 to 100,000 EPS** in enterprise-grade systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Detected Events

Events detected by IDPS are organized into a logical hierarchy based on the **attack lifecycle** (often mapped to the Lockheed Martin **Cyber Kill Chain**):

### Tier 1 — Pre-Attack Reconnaissance Events
These events occur **before** the actual intrusion attempt and are crucial for early warning.

- **Port Scanning Events:** Detection of sequential connection attempts across multiple ports (e.g., Nmap `-sS` SYN scan).
- **Ping Sweep / ICMP Sweep Events:** Detection of ICMP Echo Requests broadcast to a range of IP addresses.
- **OS Fingerprinting Events:** Identification of stack-based probing patterns (e.g., irregular TCP flag combinations).
- **DNS Enumeration Events:** Excessive NXDOMAIN or AXFR (zone transfer) requests.
- **Service Version Detection:** Banner-grabbing pattern matches.

### Tier 2 — Authentication & Authorization Events

- **Failed Login Events (Brute-Force Signatures):** Threshold-based detection (e.g., > 5 failed logins within **60 seconds**).
- **Successful Login from Unusual Geolocation:** Anomaly-based deviation from baseline.
- **Privilege Escalation Events:** Sudden transition from user to root/administrator context.
- **Account Lockout Manipulation:** Repeated lockout/unlock cycles indicating automated attack tooling.
- **Use of Default Credentials:** Signature match on known vendor default username/password pairs.

### Tier 3 — Exploitation & Intrusion Events

- **Buffer Overflow Signatures:** NOP-sled detection, return address anomalies, shellcode pattern matching.
- **SQL Injection Patterns:** Detection of `' OR 1=1 --`, `UNION SELECT`, time-based blind SQLi markers.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Payloads:** `<script>`, `javascript:`, event-handler patterns in HTTP payloads.
- **Command Injection:** Shell metacharacter detection (`;`, `|`, `` ` ``, `$(...)`).
- **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) Tokens:** Missing or invalid token patterns.
- **Directory Traversal:** `../` and encoded variants (`%2e%2e%2f`).
- **Remote Code Execution (RCE) Attempts:** Webshell upload patterns, deserialization attacks.

### Tier 4 — Malware & Post-Exploitation Events

- **Virus/Worm Signature Matches:** Pattern matches against known malware hashes (MD5, SHA-256).
- **Rootkit Behavior:** Hidden process detection, syscall hooking, direct kernel object manipulation.
- **Ransomware Indicators:** Rapid file modification patterns, mass encryption activity, ransom note creation.
- **C2 (Command & Control) Beaconing:** Periodic outbound traffic to known malicious IP/DNS domains.
- **Lateral Movement Events:** Pass-the-Hash, Pass-the-Ticket, SMB enumeration, RDP hopping.
- **Data Exfiltration Patterns:** Large outbound transfers, DNS tunneling, steganography indicators.

### Tier 5 — Policy Violation & Anomalous Behavior Events

- **Use of Unauthorized Protocols:** Peer-to-peer traffic on corporate networks.
- **Unauthorized Application Execution:** Application whitelisting violations.
- **Configuration Drift Events:** Unauthorized changes to system files, registry keys, firewall rules.
- **Data Loss Prevention (DLP) Triggers:** PII, PCI-DSS, or HIPAA data leaving the network.
- **Time-of-Day Anomalies:** Activity outside normal business hours.

## 2.2 Mapping Events to Detection Methodologies

| Event Category | Signature-Based Detection | Anomaly-Based Detection | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Port Scan | ✓ (Scan patterns) | ✓ (Traffic volume) | ✗ |
| Brute-Force Login | ✓ (Known patterns) | ✓ (Rate threshold) | ✗ |
| Buffer Overflow | ✓ (NOP-sled) | ✓ (Payload length) | ✗ |
| SQL Injection | ✓ (Keyword match) | ✓ (Query deviation) | ✗ |
| C2 Beaconing | ✓ (Known C2 IPs) | ✓ (Traffic periodicity) | ✓ (Protocol violation) |
| Data Exfiltration | ✓ (DLP signatures) | ✓ (Volume threshold) | ✓ (Protocol mismatch) |
| DNS Tunneling | ✗ | ✓ (Entropy analysis) | ✓ (Record type anomaly) |
| Protocol Anomaly | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (RFC violation) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Formulas for Event Detection Analysis**

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Sensitivity / Recall | Dimensionless, $0 \le TPR \le 1$ |
| 2 | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Alarm Rate | Dimensionless, $0 \le FPR \le 1$ |
| 3 | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Harmonic mean | Dimensionless, max = 1 |
| 5 | $EPS_{peak} = \frac{N_{packets} \cdot S_{avg}}{T_{window}}$ | Events Per Second | Events/second |
| 6 | $L = T_{alert} - T_{event}$ | Detection Latency | Milliseconds (ms) |
| 7 | $H(x) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy (for DNS tunneling detection) | Bits |
| 8 | $Z_{score} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Anomaly score | Standard deviations |
| 9 | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_i P(i) \log\frac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | KL Divergence (profile deviation) | Nats or bits |
| 10 | $R_{alert} = \lambda \cdot (1 - e^{-\mu t})$ | Poisson arrival of alerts (queueing theory) | Alerts/time unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin the design of **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** platforms, where event correlation engines aggregate thousands of EPS into prioritized incident tickets. The trade-off between TPR and FPR is the central optimization problem in every commercial IDPS deployment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold-Based Brute-Force Detection Model

Consider an IDPS rule that flags a brute-force authentication attempt when $k$ failed logins occur within a sliding time window $W$. We derive the optimal threshold using the **Poisson arrival model** (standard KTU expectation).

### Step 1 — Model the Arrival of Failed Login Events

Let failed login events be a Poisson process with mean rate $\lambda$ (legitimate user typos) and attack rate $\mu$ (automated brute-force tool).

The probability of observing exactly $n$ events in window $W$ is:

$$
P(N = n) = \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Detection Rule

We raise an alert if $N \ge k$ within window $W$. Therefore, the **alert probability under normal (non-attack) conditions** is:

$$
P_{FA} = P(N \ge k) = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitution of Numerical Values

Take a typical enterprise scenario: legitimate failed login rate $\lambda = 0.1$ events/min, window $W = 5$ min, threshold $k = 5$.

$$
\lambda W = 0.1 \times 5 = 0.5
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{4} \frac{(0.5)^n e^{-0.5}}{n!}
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - e^{-0.5} \left[ \frac{(0.5)^0}{0!} + \frac{(0.5)^1}{1!} + \frac{(0.5)^2}{2!} + \frac{(0.5)^3}{3!} + \frac{(0.5)^4}{4!} \right]
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.6065 \times \left[ 1.0000 + 0.5000 + 0.1250 + 0.0208 + 0.0026 \right]
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.6065 \times 1.6484
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.9998 = 0.0002
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A threshold of **$k = 5$ failed logins in 5 minutes** produces a false alarm rate of approximately **0.02%**, which is operationally acceptable for a production IDPS.

### Step 4 — Detection Probability Under Attack

Under attack, the combined rate is $\lambda_{attack} = \lambda + \mu$. Suppose $\mu = 5$ events/min (brute-force tool generating 5 attempts/minute).

$$
\lambda_{attack} W = (0.1 + 5) \times 5 = 25.5
$$

$$
P_D = P(N \ge 5) = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{4} \frac{(25.5)^n e^{-25.5}}{n!}
$$

$$
P_D \approx 1.0000 \;\; (\text{effectively certain detection})
$$

### Step 5 — Final TPR and FPR Pair

$$
TPR = P_D \approx 1.0000
$$

$$
FPR = P_{FA} \approx 0.0002
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
