> **Conclusion:** The chosen threshold $(k = 5, W = 5 \text{ min})$ yields near-perfect detection with negligible false positives, demonstrating the **operational viability of threshold-based event detection** in IDPS.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event Type vs. Detection Layer

| Event Type | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) | Layer 4 (Transport) | Layer 7 (Application) | Host Agent |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ARP Spoofing | ✓ | — | — | — | — |
| Port Scan | — | ✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| SYN Flood | — | — | ✓ | — | — |
| HTTP SQLi | — | — | — | ✓ | — |
| File Integrity Change | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| Privilege Escalation | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| DNS Tunneling | — | — | — | ✓ | — |
| Lateral Movement (SMB) | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Event Detection Pipeline in a Modern IDPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Sources] --> B[Packet Capture Engine]
    C[Host Logs] --> D[Log Collector]
    E[Application Telemetry] --> F[API Event Stream]

    B --> G[Event Normalizer]
    D --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H{Pre-Processor}
    H --> I[Signature Matcher]
    H --> J[Anomaly Detector]
    H --> K[Stateful Protocol Analyzer]

    I --> L[Event Correlator]
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Event Classification Engine]
    M --> N1[Tier1 PreAttack Events]
    M --> N2[Tier2 Auth Events]
    M --> N3[Tier3 Exploit Events]
    M --> N4[Tier4 Malware Events]
    M --> N5[Tier5 Policy Violations]

    N1 --> O[Alert Prioritization]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    N4 --> O
    N5 --> O

    O --> P[SIEM Dashboard]
    O --> Q[Prevention Action Engine]
    Q --> R1[Block IP]
    Q --> R2[Quarantine Host]
    Q --> R3[Reset Connection]
    Q --> R4[Generate Ticket]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Event Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detected Events] --> B[Network Events]
    A --> C[Host Events]
    A --> D[Application Events]

    B --> B1[Reconnaissance Scans]
    B --> B2[DoS DDoS Traffic]
    B --> B3[Protocol Anomalies]
    B --> B4[Malware C2 Beacons]

    C --> C1[File Integrity Changes]
    C --> C2[Process Anomalies]
    C --> C3[Registry Modifications]
    C --> C4[Privilege Escalations]

    D --> D1[SQL Injection Payloads]
    D --> D2[Cross Site Scripting]
    D --> D3[Authentication Abuse]
    D --> D4[API Abuse Patterns]
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow: Event Correlation and Response Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor1 Network] --> X[Central Correlation Engine]
    S2[Sensor2 Host] --> X
    S3[Sensor3 Wireless] --> X
    S4[Sensor4 Application] --> X

    X --> Y[Threshold Evaluator]
    X --> Z[Pattern Recognizer]
    X --> W[Behavioral Profiler]

    Y --> O[Incident Aggregator]
    Z --> O
    W --> O

    O --> P{Action Decision}
    P --> P1[Log Only]
    P --> P2[Alert Analyst]
    P --> P3[Auto Block]
    P --> P4[Forensic Capture]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Students:** These diagrams map directly to the architecture of **Snort, Suricata, OSSEC, and Zeek (Bro)** — the four open-source IDPS engines most frequently referenced in KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory exercises. The **Central Correlation Engine** is conceptually equivalent to a SIEM rule (e.g., Splunk SPL query, Elastic EQL rule, or IBM QRadar Custom Rule).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any four categories of events detected by a Network-based Intrusion Detection System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pre-attack Reconnaissance Events** — Port scans, ICMP sweeps, OS fingerprinting attempts used by attackers to map the target network. (0.75 mark)
2. **Authentication and Authorization Events** — Failed login attempts, brute-force patterns, privilege escalation, and use of default credentials. (0.75 mark)
3. **Exploitation Events** — Buffer overflow attempts, SQL injection, XSS, command injection, and remote code execution payloads. (0.75 mark)
4. **Malware and C2 Events** — Virus/worm signatures, rootkit behavior, ransomware indicators, and command-and-control beaconing patterns. (0.75 mark)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between signature-based event detection and anomaly-based event detection with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Pattern matching against known attack signatures | Statistical deviation from learned baseline |
| Example 1 | Detection of `' OR 1=1 --` SQLi payload | Detection of 500% spike in outbound DNS traffic |
| Example 2 | EICAR test file hash match | Login from geographically impossible location |
| Limitation | Zero-day blind | High false-positive rate |

(1.5 marks per column for two examples + distinction.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Classify and explain in detail the **five tiers of events detected** by a modern IDPS, mapping each tier to the corresponding phase of the Cyber Kill Chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the **end-to-end event detection pipeline** of an enterprise IDPS, clearly marking the role of the correlation engine. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The five tiers of events detected by a modern IDPS, mapped to the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain phases, are:

1. **Tier 1 — Reconnaissance Events (Kill Chain: Reconnaissance)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Port scans (SYN, FIN, Xmas), ICMP sweeps, OS fingerprinting, DNS enumeration, and service version probing. These events are detected through threshold-based rules (e.g., "more than 100 ports probed in 60 seconds from a single source IP") and signature-based SYN-scan pattern detection.

2. **Tier 2 — Weaponization & Delivery Events (Kill Chain: Weaponization, Delivery)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Phishing email attachment detection, malicious URL categorization, weaponized document hashes (PDF/Office macro exploits), and drive-by download script detection. The IDPS uses email gateway integration and HTTP content inspection.

3. **Tier 3 — Exploitation Events (Kill Chain: Exploitation, Installation)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Buffer overflow NOP-sled detection, SQL injection, XSS, command injection, deserialization attacks, and web shell uploads. The IDPS payload inspector matches against the **Snort/VRT** rule set and **ModSecurity OWASP CRS**.

4. **Tier 4 — C2 and Lateral Movement Events (Kill Chain: Command & Control, Actions on Objectives)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Beaconing detection (periodic outbound traffic to known C2 IPs), Pass-the-Hash indicators, SMB enumeration, RDP hopping, and Kerberoasting patterns. Detected through NetFlow analysis, JA3/JA3S fingerprinting, and host-based log correlation.

5. **Tier 5 — Exfiltration and Impact Events (Kill Chain: Actions on Objectives)** **(1.4 marks)**
   Large outbound data transfers, DNS tunneling (high-entropy subdomain detection), ransomware encryption patterns, and destructive wipe events. Detected through DLP rule triggers, volume threshold alerts, and file-integrity monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by **listing attack types without explicitly mapping them to the Cyber Kill Chain phases**. Always draw an explicit table or labeled diagram linking each tier to its kill chain phase. Marks are awarded for the *mapping*, not merely the listing.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**End-to-End Event Detection Pipeline:**

The enterprise IDPS pipeline consists of **six functional blocks**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** **(1.0 mark)**
   Network taps/SPAN ports feed packets to the sensor. Host-based agents forward syslog, Windows Event Logs, and EDR telemetry. Cloud workloads stream API events (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log).

2. **Pre-Processing & Normalization** **(1.0 mark)**
   Packets are decoded (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application). Logs are parsed into a common schema (CEF, LEEF, or JSON). Timestamps are normalized to UTC.

3. **Detection Engines (Parallel)** **(2.0 marks)**
   - **Signature Engine:** Pattern match using Aho-Corasick or Hyperscan algorithms against rule sets (Snort VRT, Emerging Threats).
   - **Anomaly Engine:** Statistical models (Z-score, EWMA), machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Isolation Forest), and entropy analysis.
   - **Stateful Protocol Analyzer:** Validates protocol state machines (RFC compliance for HTTP, DNS, SMTP, TLS).

4. **Event Correlation Engine** **(1.5 marks)**
   The **central correlation engine** aggregates low-level events into high-fidelity incidents using:
   - **Temporal correlation:** Events occurring within a sliding window.
   - **Asset/context correlation:** Prioritizes alerts on critical servers.
   - **Threat intelligence correlation:** Enriches events with IOC feeds (STIX/TAXII).

5. **Alert Prioritization & Deduplication** **(1.0 mark)**
   Risk score is computed as $R = Severity \times Confidence \times Asset\_Value$. Duplicate alerts are collapsed.

6. **Response Action Module** **(0.5 mark)**
   Triggers automated responses: TCP RST injection, IP blocklist update, host quarantine, SOAR playbook execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** describe only one engine. Marks are split across signature, anomaly, **and** stateful analysis. A common error is omitting the correlation engine — which carries **1.5 marks by itself** in this question.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **threshold-based detection** and **statistical anomaly detection** in identifying brute-force authentication events. Derive the false-alarm probability for a Poisson-arrival model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the detection of **network-layer events** versus **host-layer events** with suitable examples, and justify why modern IDPS deployments use both in a **defense-in-depth** architecture. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Threshold-Based Detection:** A rule fires when $k$ failed-login events occur within window $W$ from the same source. For a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ (legitimate typos), the false-alarm probability is:

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}
$$

**Statistical Anomaly Detection:** A baseline mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ of failed logins per user per hour is established over a 30-day training period. The Z-score is computed as:

$$
Z = \frac{x_{observed} - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

An alert fires if $Z > 3$ (i.e., the observed value is more than 3 standard deviations from the mean).

**Numerical Example:** Let $\lambda = 0.1$ events/min, $W = 5$ min, $k = 5$.

$$
\lambda W = 0.5
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - e^{-0.5} \left[ 1 + 0.5 + \frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.125}{6} + \frac{0.0625}{24} \right]
$$

$$
P_{FA} = 1 - 0.6065 \times 1.6484 \approx 0.0002
$$

Thus, threshold-based detection yields $FPR \approx 0.02\%$, which is operationally excellent.

**Statistical Complement:** Threshold-based detection is deterministic and fast, but cannot adapt to per-user baselines. Statistical anomaly detection adapts dynamically but suffers higher false positives during training-data drift. **Modern IDPS use both in combination** to balance precision and recall.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only the formula without substituting numerical values, forfeiting **2 marks**. Always include a worked numerical example with explicit substitution.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Network-Layer Event Detection (NIDS):**
- **Examples:** SYN flood detection, port scan signatures, C2 beaconing, ARP spoofing, DNS tunneling.
- **Sensor placement:** Inline (IPS mode) or passive tap at network choke points.
- **Strengths:** Broad visibility across the entire subnet, detects reconnaissance, and catches attacks before they reach the host.
- **Limitations:** Cannot see encrypted payload contents (without TLS inspection), cannot detect host-level post-exploitation.

**Host-Layer Event Detection (HIDS):**
- **Examples:** File integrity monitoring (e.g., Tripwire), registry key changes, suspicious process creation, logon audits, rootkit detection.
- **Sensor placement:** Agent installed on each endpoint/server.
- **Strengths:** Visibility into encrypted local activity, host-context awareness (which user, which process), and post-exploitation detection.
- **Limitations:** Can be disabled by sophisticated rootkits, requires agent maintenance, generates per-host noise.

**Defense-in-Depth Justification:**

A purely network-based IDPS misses attacks that bypass the network (e.g., insider threats, USB-borne malware, encrypted C2 over HTTPS). A purely host-based IDPS cannot correlate events across the enterprise. The **defense-in-depth** model layers both:

| Layer | Catches | Misses |
|---|---|---|
| NIDS only | External scans, DDoS, lateral movement | Insider threats, host rootkits |
| HIDS only | Insider threats, host-level malware | External reconnaissance |
| NIDS + HIDS | All of the above with correlation | — |

The **SIEM correlation engine** then fuses NIDS and HIDS events into a unified incident picture, dramatically improving TPR while reducing FPR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not describe only one layer. **Both** must be compared, and the justification for defense-in-depth must explicitly mention the **insider threat** and **encrypted traffic** blind spots of NIDS-only deployments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Events Detected**

- [x] Events are classified into **five tiers** mapped to the Cyber Kill Chain: Reconnaissance, Delivery, Exploitation, C2/Lateral-Movement, Exfiltration/Impact.
- [x] **Three detection methodologies**: Signature (pattern), Anomaly (statistical/ML), Stateful (protocol RFC compliance).
- [x] **Three source layers**: Network, Host, Application.
- [x] **Key metrics**: TPR, FPR, Precision, Recall, F1-score, Detection Latency (ms), EPS.
- [x] **Poisson model** is the standard mathematical tool for threshold-based event-rate analysis: $P_{FA} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda W)^n e^{-\lambda W}}{n!}$.
- [x] **Z-score** is the standard anomaly detection statistic: $Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, with $Z > 3$ as the conventional alert threshold.
- [x] **Defense-in-depth** requires both **NIDS** and **HIDS** to cover external and insider threats.
- [x] Modern IDPS use **SIEM correlation engines** to fuse low-level events into high-fidelity incidents.
- [x] **Shannon entropy** $H(x) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the standard metric for DNS tunneling and encrypted exfiltration detection.
- [x] **Event correlation types**: temporal, asset-context, threat-intelligence, and behavioral.
- [x] **Open-source tools** to remember: **Snort** (signature), **Suricata** (multi-threaded signature + anomaly), **OSSEC/Wazuh** (HIDS), **Zeek** (stateful network analysis).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prevention Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prevention Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prevention Capabilities** in the context of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) refer to the **active, automated response mechanisms** that an in-line security device can execute to **intercept, block, contain, or terminate** a malicious network flow or host process *before* it completes its attack objective. Unlike passive detection (which only logs and alerts), prevention capabilities convert the IDPS from a *witness* into a *first responder*.

In the KTU 2024 scheme terminology, an **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** is the operational realization of these prevention capabilities, distinguished from a pure **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** by its ability to sit directly in the data path and enforce countermeasures in real time.

The formal taxonomy recognized by KTU PECCT742 Module 3 categorizes prevention capabilities along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Options | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Mode** | Inline vs Passive (Tap/SPAN) | Inline = in the traffic path; Passive = copy only |
| **Response Timing** | Proactive vs Reactive | Proactive = anticipate threat; Reactive = respond to observed event |
| **Response Action** | Soft vs Hard | Soft = log/alert/reset; Hard = drop/shut/isolate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Prevention capabilities are *only* available when the IDPS sensor is deployed **inline** (in-line / fail-open or fail-closed mode). A tap-mode sensor physically cannot block traffic — it can only see a copy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bouncer at the Club Door

Imagine a busy nightclub entrance:

- An **IDS** is a CCTV camera mounted above the door. It records every face, every incident, and forwards footage to a security office. The bouncers only find out about trouble *after* the cameras do — by which time the fight is already happening inside.
- An **IPS (Prevention Capabilities enabled)** is the bouncer standing *at* the door itself. The bouncer has a guest list (signature database), a metal detector (anomaly engine), and a direct physical connection to the door. When a troublemaker arrives:
  - The bouncer can **refuse entry** (drop packet).
  - The bouncer can **cut the music and shine a spotlight** (TCP reset / ICMP unreachable).
  - The bouncer can **put the offender in a side room** (quarantine / sandbox).
  - The bouncer can **call the police and lock the door** (block source IP / isolate host).

The crucial difference is **physical placement**: the bouncer must be *in the doorway*, not just watching from across the street. This is why the **inline deployment requirement** is the single most defining characteristic of prevention capability in IDPS.

### 1.3 Why Prevention Capabilities Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Speed Mismatch Problem:** Modern network attacks (ransomware propagation, credential stuffing, worm exploits) complete in **milliseconds to seconds**. Human incident response teams need **minutes to hours**. Without automated prevention, the *mean time to contain* (MTTC) of an attack is bounded below by human reaction time, which is unacceptable for high-velocity threats. Prevention capabilities collapse MTTC to **sub-second** values.

The standard KTU-recognized performance benchmark is that an inline IPS must make its block/allow decision within **< 1 millisecond** per packet to avoid degrading line-rate throughput on a **1 Gbps** link, and within **< 100 microseconds** on **10 Gbps** links — these are the engineering constraints that drive the entire prevention subsystem design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Prevention Capability

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus structure for Module 3, prevention capabilities decompose into five primary operational pillars. Each pillar is an *engineered response* the IPS can invoke when its detection subsystem fires a high-confidence alert.

#### Pillar 1 — Packet Drop (Hard Block)

- The IPS silently **discards** offending packets and forwards no response to either endpoint.
- The attacker never knows why their payload never reached the target; the legitimate recipient never processes it.
- This is the **most common and safest** prevention action because it causes no protocol-level side effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Note:** Packet drop is also called *blackhole filtering* when applied to entire IP prefixes. It is the same primitive used by BGP Flowspec and RTBH (Remotely Triggered Black Hole) routing.

#### Pillar 2 — Connection Termination (TCP Reset / RST Injection)

- For TCP flows, the IPS injects a **TCP RST** packet to *both* the client and the server, using the correct sequence/acknowledgment numbers spoofed from the observed flow.
- Both endpoints immediately tear down the half-open and full-open sockets, freeing resources.
- This is essential for stopping **session-layer attacks** (e.g., HTTP session hijacking, FTP bounce, SSH brute force mid-session).

The TCP RST injection requires the IPS to maintain per-flow state. Formally, for a TCP flow with state $S = (src\_ip, dst\_ip, src\_port, dst\_port, seq\_num, ack\_num)$, the RST packet is constructed as:

$$
\text{RST}_{payload} = \{(FIN=0, RST=1, ACK=1) \mid seq = S.ack\_num, ack = S.seq\_num + 1\}
$$

This makes the reset **un-forgeable** by the attacker in real time, because the sequence number window is held inside the IPS state table.

#### Pillar 3 — Rate Limiting & Traffic Shaping (Soft Throttle)

- The IPS does *not* block the offending flow outright; instead it **caps the bandwidth** or **packet rate** the source can send toward the target.
- Useful for **DoS / DDoS mitigation** where outright blocking is risky (a real customer might share a NATed IP with an attacker).
- The rate is enforced using a **token bucket** algorithm with parameters $(r, b)$ where $r$ is the refill rate (tokens/sec) and $b$ is the burst capacity (tokens).

The token-bucket occupancy at time $t$ after a packet of size $p$ bytes arrives is governed by:

$$
T(t) = \min\left(b, \, T(t-1) + r \cdot \Delta t - p\right)
$$

- If $T(t) \geq 0$, the packet is forwarded; if $T(t) < 0$, the packet is queued or dropped (configurable).

#### Pillar 4 — Quarantine & Host Isolation

- The IPS uses a coordination channel (e.g., **RADIUS CoA**, **802.1X**, or a **northbound API** to a switch/SDN controller) to push the offending host into a **remediation VLAN**.
- The host retains IP connectivity only to a captive portal / patch server; all other destinations become unreachable.
- This pillar is critical for **post-compromise containment** of malware-infected endpoints (lateral movement prevention).

#### Pillar 5 — Application-Layer Enforcement

- The IPS terminates the application protocol itself (acts as a **reverse proxy / transparent bridge**) and can issue protocol-specific responses:
  - HTTP: respond with **HTTP 403 Forbidden** or inject a JavaScript CAPTCHA.
  - SMTP: reject the message with **SMTP 5xx** codes at the **MAIL FROM** or **RCPT TO** stage.
  - DNS: respond with a **NXDOMAIN** or a sinkhole IP for malicious domains.
- This is the deepest prevention capability and requires full protocol reassembly and L7 intelligence.

### 2.2 The Decision Lattice: When Does Each Pillar Fire?

The IPS does not invoke prevention capabilities blindly. The decision follows a **confidence ladder** mapped to the alert's severity and false-positive cost:

| Detection Confidence | False-Positive Cost | Recommended Pillar | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High ($\geq$ 0.95)** | Low (e.g., known malware hash) | Hard Block (Pillar 1) or RST (Pillar 2) | Direct, immediate, no user impact |
| **High ($\geq$ 0.95)** | High (e.g., production IP) | Quarantine (Pillar 4) | Isolate for forensics, do not drop |
| **Medium (0.70 – 0.95)** | Low | Rate Limit (Pillar 3) | Throttle, do not block |
| **Medium (0.70 – 0.95)** | High | App-Layer Challenge (Pillar 5) | Force re-authentication, then re-judge |
| **Low ($<$ 0.70)** | Any | Log only (no prevention) | Detection mode — prevent is unsafe |

### 2.3 The Inline Imperative: Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed

Because an inline IPS is a *physical* part of the wire, its failure mode is a safety-critical design choice.

- **Fail-Open (Bypass Mode):** If the IPS crashes, traffic flows *around* it through a hardware relay. The network stays up; the organization is unprotected for the duration of the failure.
- **Fail-Closed (Block-All Mode):** If the IPS crashes, all traffic is stopped. The network goes down; the organization is provably secure.

The selection is governed by the **availability vs integrity trade-off**, quantified by the formula:

$$
\text{Choose Fail-Open if } \quad \text{MTBF}_{IPS}^{-1} \cdot C_{downtime} \;>\; \text{MTBF}_{IPS}^{-1} \cdot C_{breach}
$$

where $C_{downtime}$ is the cost of network outage and $C_{breach}$ is the cost of an unfiltered breach. For most enterprise deployments $C_{breach} \gg C_{downtime}$, so **fail-closed** is preferred; for medical/aviation/life-safety networks the inequality reverses, and **fail-open** is mandatory.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize the entries below; they appear frequently in 14-mark derivation questions.**

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Rule | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{max}$ | Max throughput sustained by inline IPS | $L_{max} = \dfrac{N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate}}{H_{overhead}}$ | Gbps | $P_{rate}$ is per-core packet rate |
| $T_{decision}$ | Per-packet decision budget | $T_{decision} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot L_{max}}$ | seconds | 2x for symmetric full-duplex |
| $MTTC$ | Mean Time to Contain | $MTTC = T_{detect} + T_{respond}$ | seconds | IPS aims for $MTTC < 1$ s |
| $T(t)$ | Token bucket occupancy | $T(t) = \min(b, \, T(t-1) + r\Delta t - p)$ | bytes | Negative = drop/queue |
| $FPR$ | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | dimensionless | Trade-off vs $TPR$ |
| $TPR$ | True Positive Rate | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless | Also called *Recall* |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair (sensor) | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum t_{repair}}{N_{incidents}}$ | hours | Drives fail-open/closed choice |
| $R_{score}$ | Composite risk score | $R_{score} = w_1 C + w_2 V + w_3 T$ | 0–100 | Confidence, Vulnerability, Threat |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Prevention capabilities are not academic — they are the **economic justification** for purchasing an IPS instead of a cheaper IDS. The ROI calculation most enterprises use is:

$$
ROI_{\%} = 100 \times \dfrac{(C_{breach} \cdot P_{breach}^{noIPS}) - (C_{breach} \cdot P_{breach}^{IPS}) - C_{IPS}}{C_{IPS}}
$$

A typical mid-size enterprise sees a **3-year ROI in the 200% – 600%** range when prevention capabilities are enabled, primarily because $P_{breach}^{IPS}$ drops by an order of magnitude versus detection-only mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following section provides **exhaustive derivations** of the key quantitative relationships in IPS prevention capability. Every algebraic step is written out in full; no shortcuts or implicit jumps are taken.

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Sustainable Line-Rate Through an Inline IPS

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark setup):** An inline IPS has $N_{cores} = 8$ processing cores. Each core can inspect $P_{rate} = 1.2$ million packets per second (Mpps) at full signature-database load. The IPS must perform a bidirectional inspection, so it counts both ingress and egress packets against the budget. What is the maximum throughput $L_{max}$ in Gbps, assuming an average packet size of $S_{avg} = 800$ bytes? Take the hardware-internal inspection overhead factor as $H_{overhead} = 1.15$ (i.e., 15% of throughput is consumed by memory bus, DMA, and state-table access, leaving 85% for the actual decision).

**Step 1 — Define the raw aggregate packet rate.** The $N_{cores}$ cores operate in parallel, so the total raw packet-processing rate is:

$$
P_{total} = N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
P_{total} = 8 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{6} = 9.6 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert packets to bytes using the average packet size.** The line-rate in bits per second is:

$$
L_{raw} = P_{total} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8
$$

Substituting:

$$
L_{raw} = 9.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot 800 \cdot 8 = 61.44 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/second}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the overhead factor.** A fraction of the raw throughput is consumed by the bus and memory access overhead; only the remainder is available for the actual decision pipeline:

$$
L_{max} = \frac{L_{raw}}{H_{overhead}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
L_{max} = \frac{61.44 \times 10^{9}}{1.15} = 53.426 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/second}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Gbps.**

$$
L_{max} = 53.426 \; \text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The IPS can sustain approximately **53.4 Gbps** of bidirectional inspected throughput under full signature load. Beyond this rate, packets will be dropped *by the IPS itself*, not by the network — a self-inflicted outage that must be planned for in capacity engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The $H_{overhead}$ factor is *not* optional. Forgetting to divide by it is the most common mark-losing error in this derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Per-Packet Decision Budget for Latency-Sensitive Links

**Problem:** Given the throughput $L_{max}$ derived above, what is the maximum allowable per-packet decision time $T_{decision}$ if the link is full-duplex (i.e., packets are arriving on both ingress and egress simultaneously)?

**Step 1 — Total packets per second at line rate.** The aggregate number of packets per second that must be processed is:

$$
P_{line} = \frac{L_{max}}{S_{avg} \cdot 8}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{line} = \frac{53.426 \times 10^{9}}{800 \cdot 8} = 8.347 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to seconds per packet.** The reciprocal gives the per-packet time budget:

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{1}{P_{line}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{1}{8.347 \times 10^{6}} = 1.198 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the full-duplex factor of 2** (the IPS must service ingress and egress independently; each direction gets half the time budget):

$$
T_{decision} = \frac{T_{packet}}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{decision} = \frac{1.198 \times 10^{-7}}{2} = 5.99 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to microseconds.**

$$
T_{decision} \approx 60 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** Each packet must be decided upon within **approximately 60 microseconds** to keep up with line rate. This is the engineering reason modern IPS appliances use **ASIC/FPGA acceleration** rather than general-purpose CPUs beyond 10 Gbps.

### 3.3 Derivation: Token-Bucket Rate-Limit Verification

**Problem:** A rate-limit rule is configured with refill rate $r = 10 \; \text{MB/s}$ and burst capacity $b = 2 \; \text{MB}$. A flow attempts to send a burst of 10 packets, each of size 200 KB, back-to-back ($\Delta t \to 0$ between them). Verify whether each packet is admitted or rejected.

**Step 1 — Initialize the bucket.** Assume the bucket starts full:

$$
T(0) = b = 2 \times 10^{6} \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Process Packet 1 ($p_1 = 200 \; \text{KB} = 200{,}000$ bytes, $\Delta t \approx 0$).**

$$
T(1) = \min(b, \, T(0) + r \cdot 0 - p_1) = \min(2{,}000{,}000, \, 2{,}000{,}000 - 200{,}000) = 1{,}800{,}000 \; \text{bytes}
$$

Since $T(1) \geq 0$: **Admit**.

**Step 3 — Process Packet 2 ($p_2 = 200{,}000$ bytes, $\Delta t \approx 0$).**

$$
T(2) = \min(2{,}000{,}000, \, 1{,}800{,}000 - 200{,}000) = 1{,}600{,}000 \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Admit**.

**Step 4 — Process Packet 3 to Packet 10 (similar arithmetic).** Each packet consumes 200 KB, so by Packet 10:

$$
T(10) = 2{,}000{,}000 - 10 \cdot 200{,}000 = 0 \; \text{bytes}
$$

The 10th packet brings the bucket to exactly zero. If an 11th packet arrives, $T(11) = -200{,}000 < 0$ and it would be **rejected / queued**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** The bucket admits the first 10 packets instantly (since $\Delta t \approx 0$ means no refill happens during the burst), then requires a refill of:

$$
t_{refill} = \frac{200{,}000}{10 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \; \text{ms}
$$

before the next 200 KB packet can be admitted. This is the *enforcement* behavior of the rate-limit pillar.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: IDS vs IPS Capability

| Capability Attribute | IDS (Detection Only) | IPS (Prevention Enabled) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inline placement required** | No (tap/SPAN OK) | Yes (must be in wire path) | IPS is a *chokepoint* by design |
| **Latency contribution** | 0 (passive copy) | 1 – 100 $\mu s$ per packet | Budget must be allocated in SLA |
| **Single point of failure** | No | Yes (unless bypass) | Fail-open / fail-closed required |
| **False positive blast radius** | Operator inbox | Production outage | Confidence threshold must be higher for IPS |
| **Detection latency** | Seconds to minutes | Sub-millisecond | IPS collapses MTTC by 3 orders of magnitude |
| **Cost (CAPEX)** | Low (sensor only) | High (appliance + bypass) | Justified by breach-cost ROI |
| **Throughput loss** | 0 | 5% – 25% | Trade-off with security depth |
| **State-table size** | Optional | Mandatory | Deep inspection needs $\geq 1$ M concurrent flows |

### 3.5 The Prevention Action Selection Matrix (Decision Tree)

The IPS chooses among the five prevention pillars via a deterministic decision tree driven by the detection subsystem's output and contextual policy. The matrix below is the canonical reference for KTU 14-mark questions on the topic.

| Step | Decision Variable | Possible Values | Branch Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the sensor inline? | No | Downgrade to IDS mode (no prevention) |
| 2 | Confidence score $C$ of alert | $C \geq 0.95$ | Eligible for hard action |
|  |  | $0.70 \leq C < 0.95$ | Soft action only |
|  |  | $C < 0.70$ | Log only |
| 3 | Asset value $V$ of target | High | Prefer Quarantine, not Drop |
|  |  | Low | Prefer Drop or RST |
| 4 | Is the source IP a NAT'd shared address? | Yes | Avoid hard block; use Rate Limit |
|  |  | No | Hard block permissible |
| 5 | Is the protocol L7 reassembled? | Yes | App-layer challenge available |
|  |  | No | L3/L4 action only |
| 6 | Is the action reversible? | No | Require operator approval (soft block) |
|  |  | Yes | Auto-apply |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Reminder

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid blocks below use alphanumeric node identifiers prefixed with letters (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `d1`), and labels contain only plain uppercase text — no bold, italics, or HTML markup inside the quoted labels. This guarantees first-attempt rendering in Mermaid 10+.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Inline IPS in a Network Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1[EXTERNAL NETWORK] --> n2[EDGE ROUTER]
    n2 --> n3[BYPASS SWITCH FAIL OPEN RELAY]
    n3 --> n4[IPS APPLIANCE INLINE ENGINE]
    n4 --> n5[INTERNAL CORE SWITCH]
    n5 --> n6[PROTECTED SERVERS AND HOSTS]
    n4 --> n7[MANAGEMENT CONSOLE SIGNATURE DB]
    n4 --> n8[SIEM AND LOG AGGREGATOR]
    n9[OPERATOR] --> n7
    n8 --> n10[INCIDENT RESPONSE PLATFORM]
    n3 -. FAULT STATE BYPASS .- n5
```

**Interpretation:**
- Packets traverse the **bypass switch** which is a hardware relay sitting in series with the IPS. Under normal conditions, the relay is "engaged" — all traffic passes *through* the IPS. If the IPS fails (heartbeat loss), the relay physically shorts around it, restoring connectivity.
- The IPS streams its prevention-action decisions and alerts to the **SIEM** and the **Management Console**, closing the loop with the operator.

### 4.3 Prevention Capability Workflow — Packet Arrival to Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[PACKET ARRIVES AT INLINE IPS] --> p2{PROTOCOL REASSEMBLY BUFFER}
    p2 -->|TCP UDP RAW| p3[STATE TABLE LOOKUP]
    p2 -->|L7 PAYLOAD READY| p4[DEEP INSPECTION ENGINE]
    p3 --> p5[SIGNATURE MATCH MODULE]
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6{CONFIDENCE SCORE C}
    p6 -->|C GREATER 0.95| p7[HARD ACTION SELECTOR]
    p6 -->|0.70 TO 0.95| p8[SOFT ACTION SELECTOR]
    p6 -->|C LESS 0.70| p9[LOG ONLY AND FORWARD]
    p7 --> p10[DROP PACKET]
    p7 --> p11[INJECT TCP RST]
    p7 --> p12[QUARANTINE HOST]
    p8 --> p13[RATE LIMIT WITH TOKEN BUCKET]
    p8 --> p14[APP LAYER CHALLENGE]
    p9 --> p15[ALERT TO SIEM]
    p10 --> p16[ACTION LOGGED]
    p11 --> p16
    p12 --> p16
    p13 --> p16
    p14 --> p16
    p15 --> p16
```

**Interpretation:**
- The flow makes the **confidence-thresholded branching** explicit: high-confidence alerts trigger hard prevention; medium-confidence alerts trigger soft prevention; low-confidence alerts only log.
- Every action — whether prevent or log-only — is recorded, providing an audit trail for compliance (PCI-DSS 10.x, ISO 27001 A.12.4).

### 4.4 Layered Defense Model: Where Prevention Capabilities Live

```mermaid
graph TB
    l7[LAYER 7 APPLICATION] --> l6[LAYER 6 PRESENTATION]
    l6 --> l5[LAYER 5 SESSION]
    l5 --> l4[LAYER 4 TRANSPORT]
    l4 --> l3[LAYER 3 NETWORK]
    l3 --> l2[LAYER 2 DATA LINK]
    l2 --> l1[LAYER 1 PHYSICAL]
    a1[APP LAYER PREVENTION HTTP 403 SMTP 5XX DNS NXDOMAIN] -.-> l7
    a2[SESSION PREVENTION TCP RST] -.-> l5
    a3[RATE LIMIT AND SHAPING] -.-> l4
    a4[PACKET DROP AND IP BLOCK] -.-> l3
    a5[PORT SHUTDOWN AND MAC FILTER] -.-> l2
    a6[PHYSICAL DISCONNECT RELAY] -.-> l1
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each OSI layer has a corresponding prevention primitive. A well-engineered IPS will invoke the primitive at the *highest* layer that can definitively stop the attack — because higher-layer enforcement causes less collateral damage than lower-layer enforcement.

### 4.5 State Diagram — IPS Sensor Operational Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> initState
    initState --> detectState : BOOT OK AND SIGNATURES LOADED
    detectState --> preventState : INLINE ENABLED AND POLICY ACTIVE
    preventState --> detectState : OPERATOR SWITCHES TO MONITOR MODE
    preventState --> bypassState : HEARTBEAT FAILURE OR WATCHDOG TIMEOUT
    bypassState --> preventState : IPS RESTORED AND HEALTHY
    bypassState --> failClosedState : POLICY SAYS FAIL CLOSED
    detectState --> failClosedState : POLICY SAYS FAIL CLOSED
    failClosedState --> preventState : IPS RESTORED
    bypassState --> [*] : POWER CYCLE
```

**Interpretation:**
- The state machine encodes the **fail-open vs fail-closed** policy choice and the **operator override** path (e.g., taking the IPS into "detect-only" mode for forensic analysis of a new attack without risking prevention false positives).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention System (IPS) with respect to deployment and response capability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **IDS** is deployed *passively* — it receives a copy of network traffic via a SPAN port or network tap, and it analyzes the copy without sitting in the data path. Its response is limited to **alerting and logging**; it cannot stop the attack because the malicious traffic has already been forwarded by the switch. In contrast, an **IPS** is deployed *inline* — it sits physically in the wire path between two network segments, and every packet must pass through it. This inline placement grants the IPS the ability to take **active prevention actions** such as dropping packets, injecting TCP RSTs, rate-limiting, or quarantining hosts. The trade-off is that an IPS introduces latency and a single point of failure, which is mitigated by a hardware bypass relay. In summary: IDS = witness; IPS = gatekeeper. *(3 marks — 1 for deployment, 1 for response, 1 for trade-off.)*

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by "fail-open" mode in an IPS, and in which deployment scenario is it preferred over fail-closed mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fail-open mode** means that if the IPS appliance crashes, loses power, or otherwise becomes unresponsive, the bypass relay automatically closes a parallel circuit that allows traffic to flow *around* the IPS without inspection. The network remains available, but is unprotected for the duration of the failure. **Fail-closed mode** does the opposite — all traffic is blocked if the IPS fails. Fail-open is preferred in **life-safety and availability-critical networks** such as hospital medical-device networks, aviation control systems, and some industrial SCADA segments, where a network outage could directly threaten human safety or regulatory compliance. The decision is governed by the inequality $C_{breach} \cdot t_{outage} \ll C_{downtime} \cdot t_{outage}$, i.e., the cost of a brief unprotected window is less than the cost of a brief total outage. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for mechanism, 1 for scenario + justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **five major prevention capabilities** of an IPS, with one example scenario for each, and clearly state at which OSI layer each capability operates.

**(b) [7 marks]** An inline IPS has 16 processing cores, each capable of inspecting 1.5 million packets per second. The average packet size on the monitored link is 600 bytes. The hardware inspection overhead consumes 20% of the raw throughput. **(i)** Compute the maximum sustained line-rate throughput of the IPS in Gbps. **(ii)** Compute the per-packet decision budget in microseconds, assuming full-duplex operation. **(iii)** If the link is upgraded from 10 Gbps to 40 Gbps, will this IPS be sufficient? Justify with calculation.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Five Major Prevention Capabilities [7 marks — 1.4 marks each]:**

1. **Packet Drop (Layer 3, Network Layer):** The IPS silently discards offending packets. The attacker's payload never reaches the victim. *Example:* Blocking an ICMP echo flood from a botnet — the IPS drops each ICMP packet matching the flood signature.

2. **TCP Reset Injection (Layer 4, Transport Layer):** For TCP sessions, the IPS injects a spoofed TCP RST to both endpoints using the correct sequence numbers, forcing immediate teardown. *Example:* Killing an ongoing SSH brute-force session mid-login — the RST arrives before the attacker can guess the password.

3. **Rate Limiting (Layer 3/4, Network/Transport):** The IPS applies a token-bucket throttle to cap the bandwidth or packet rate from a source. *Example:* Slowing a single user who is downloading exfiltrated data at 1 GB/s down to 10 MB/s.

4. **Host Quarantine (Layer 2/3, Data Link/Network):** The IPS uses RADIUS CoA or 802.1X to push the infected host into a remediation VLAN. *Example:* Moving an endpoint that triggered a "Conficker worm" signature into a patch-and-reboot VLAN.

5. **Application-Layer Enforcement (Layer 7, Application):** The IPS responds with protocol-specific blocks. *Example:* Returning HTTP 403 to a request carrying a SQL-injection payload in the URI.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often list *detection* methods (signature, anomaly, stateful) under the question "prevention capabilities" and lose 4–5 marks. Read the question carefully — it asks for *prevention* (response actions), not *detection* (analysis methods). Detection and prevention are different subsystems; the question explicitly asks about prevention.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks — split as 2 + 2 + 3]:**

**(b-i) Maximum sustained throughput [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{total} = N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate} = 16 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{6} = 24 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

$$
L_{raw} = P_{total} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8 = 24 \times 10^{6} \cdot 600 \cdot 8 = 115.2 \times 10^{9} \; \text{bits/second}
$$

$$
H_{overhead} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.20} = 1.25
$$

$$
L_{max} = \frac{L_{raw}}{H_{overhead}} = \frac{115.2 \times 10^{9}}{1.25} = 92.16 \; \text{Gbps}
$$

**Answer: 92.16 Gbps.** *(2 marks — 1 for setup, 1 for final value with units.)*

**(b-ii) Per-packet decision budget [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{line} = \frac{L_{max}}{S_{avg} \cdot 8} = \frac{92.16 \times 10^{9}}{600 \cdot 8} = 19.2 \times 10^{6} \; \text{packets/second}
$$

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{1}{P_{line}} = 5.208 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{s} = 52.08 \; \mu s
$$

$$
T_{decision} = \frac{T_{packet}}{2} = 26.04 \; \mu s
$$

**Answer: ≈ 26 μs per packet (full-duplex).** *(2 marks — 1 for full-duplex factor, 1 for final value.)*

**(b-iii) Sufficiency for 40 Gbps upgrade [3 marks]:**

The calculated $L_{max} = 92.16$ Gbps **exceeds** the 40 Gbps target. Therefore, in principle, the IPS is sufficient.

However, the KTU-recommended engineering practice is to operate an IPS at **no more than 70%** of its theoretical maximum to leave headroom for traffic bursts, signature database growth, and TLS decryption overhead. The safe sustained capacity is:

$$
L_{safe} = 0.70 \cdot L_{max} = 0.70 \cdot 92.16 = 64.5 \; \text{Gbps}
$$

Since $L_{safe} = 64.5 \; \text{Gbps} > 40 \; \text{Gbps}$, the IPS is sufficient even under the 70% rule. **Conclusion: Yes, the IPS is sufficient for the 40 Gbps upgrade**, with a comfortable margin of 24.5 Gbps. *(3 marks — 1 for comparison, 1 for the 70% headroom principle, 1 for the final conclusion.)*

---

#### Question B-B. (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a decision-flow diagram, describe how an IPS selects between **hard prevention** (drop, RST, quarantine) and **soft prevention** (rate limit, app-layer challenge). Explain the role of **confidence score**, **asset value**, and **false-positive cost** in this selection.

**(b) [7 marks]** A company is evaluating whether to deploy prevention capabilities (IPS) or detection-only (IDS). The estimated cost of a single breach is $C_{breach} = \$2{,}000{,}000$. Without IPS, the annual probability of a breach is $P_{noIPS} = 0.30$. With IPS, it drops to $P_{IPS} = 0.04$. The IPS appliance plus 3-year maintenance costs $C_{IPS} = \$450{,}000$. **(i)** Compute the expected annual loss without IPS. **(ii)** Compute the expected annual loss with IPS. **(iii)** Compute the 3-year ROI in percent and state whether the deployment is justified.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IPS Action Selection Flow [7 marks]:**

The IPS follows a layered decision process:

1. **Confidence Score Check (C):** The detection subsystem emits a confidence $C \in [0, 1]$.
   - If $C \geq 0.95$: the alert is *high-confidence*; hard prevention is eligible.
   - If $0.70 \leq C < 0.95$: *medium-confidence*; only soft prevention is permitted.
   - If $C < 0.70$: *low-confidence*; log only, no prevention.

2. **Asset Value (V) of the destination:** If the target is a high-value asset (e.g., domain controller, payment server), the IPS prefers *quarantine* over *drop* because dropping may destroy forensic evidence. Drop is reserved for low-value targets.

3. **False-Positive Cost (FPC):** A metric representing the operational impact of an erroneous block. If the FPC is high (e.g., blocking a paying customer's IP), the IPS downgrades from hard to soft action — e.g., from drop to rate-limit — even if $C$ is high.

The composite decision rule is:

$$
\text{Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Hard Block (Drop / RST)} & \text{if } C \geq 0.95 \text{ and } FPC = \text{Low} \\
\text{Quarantine} & \text{if } C \geq 0.95 \text{ and } V = \text{High} \\
\text{Rate Limit} & \text{if } 0.70 \leq C < 0.95 \text{ or } FPC = \text{High} \\
\text{App-Layer Challenge} & \text{if } 0.70 \leq C < 0.95 \text{ and } L7 \text{ available} \\
\text{Log Only} & \text{if } C < 0.70
\end{cases}
$$

A properly drawn flowchart (mermaid or hand-drawn in the exam) showing the three decision boxes — *Confidence*, *Asset Value*, *FPC* — feeding into the five terminal action nodes earns full marks. *(7 marks — 2 for the rules, 2 for the decision matrix, 2 for the diagram, 1 for the composite rule.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students treat *confidence score* as the *only* input to the action selector and ignore asset value and FPC. This loses 3 marks. The examiner is specifically testing whether you understand that **prevention is risk-managed, not just confidence-managed**.

---

**(b) ROI Calculation [7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3]:**

**(b-i) Expected Annual Loss (EAL) without IPS [2 marks]:**

$$
EAL_{noIPS} = P_{noIPS} \cdot C_{breach} = 0.30 \cdot \$2{,}000{,}000 = \$600{,}000
$$

**Answer: \$600,000 per year.** *(2 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for value.)*

**(b-ii) Expected Annual Loss (EAL) with IPS [2 marks]:**

$$
EAL_{IPS} = P_{IPS} \cdot C_{breach} = 0.04 \cdot \$2{,}000{,}000 = \$80{,}000
$$

**Answer: \$80,000 per year.** *(2 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for value.)*

**(b-iii) 3-Year ROI [3 marks]:**

Annual avoided loss:

$$
\Delta EAL = EAL_{noIPS} - EAL_{IPS} = \$600{,}000 - \$80{,}000 = \$520{,}000
$$

3-year avoided loss:

$$
B_{3yr} = 3 \cdot \Delta EAL = 3 \cdot \$520{,}000 = \$1{,}560{,}000
$$

Net benefit:

$$
N = B_{3yr} - C_{IPS} = \$1{,}560{,}000 - \$450{,}000 = \$1{,}110{,}000
$$

3-year ROI:

$$
ROI_{\%} = 100 \times \frac{N}{C_{IPS}} = 100 \times \frac{1{,}110{,}000}{450{,}000} \approx 246.7\%
$$

**Answer: ROI ≈ 246.7%.** The deployment is **strongly justified** — the company recovers its IPS cost 2.47 times over 3 years, purely from breach-loss avoidance. *(3 marks — 1 for each of: avoided loss, net benefit, final ROI% + justification.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam. It is the highest-density summary of the topic.**

- **Prevention ≠ Detection.** Detection = recognize; Prevention = act. Both subsystems coexist inside an IPS.
- **Inline placement is mandatory** for prevention. Tap/SPAN-mode sensors can detect but never prevent.
- **Five Prevention Pillars:** (1) Packet Drop, (2) TCP RST Injection, (3) Rate Limit (Token Bucket), (4) Host Quarantine, (5) App-Layer Enforcement.
- **Confidence-Ladder Rule:** $C \geq 0.95$ → hard action; $0.70 \leq C < 0.95$ → soft action; $C < 0.70$ → log only.
- **Fail-Open vs Fail-Closed** decision is governed by $C_{breach}$ vs $C_{downtime}$. Fail-open for life-safety; fail-closed for high-value enterprise.
- **TCP RST sequence number rule:** $seq = S.ack\_num$, $ack = S.seq\_num + 1$ — this is what makes the reset unspoofable.
- **Token Bucket formula:** $T(t) = \min(b, \, T(t-1) + r\Delta t - p)$. Negative = drop.
- **Per-Packet Decision Budget:** $T_{decision} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot P_{line}}$ for full-duplex. 60 μs is the typical target for 10 Gbps links.
- **Throughput formula:** $L_{max} = \dfrac{N_{cores} \cdot P_{rate} \cdot S_{avg} \cdot 8}{H_{overhead}}$.
- **70% Headroom Rule:** Never operate an IPS above 70% of theoretical $L_{max}$ — reserve the remainder for bursts and signature growth.
- **ROI formula:** $ROI_{\%} = 100 \times \dfrac{3 \cdot (P_{noIPS} - P_{IPS}) \cdot C_{breach} - C_{IPS}}{C_{IPS}}$.
- **OSI Layer → Prevention Mapping:** L7 = App enforcement; L5 = TCP RST; L4 = Rate limit; L3 = Drop / IP block; L2 = Port shutdown; L1 = Physical disconnect.
- **Common Exam Traps:**
  - Forgetting the full-duplex factor of 2 in $T_{decision}$ (–1 mark).
  - Forgetting to divide by $H_{overhead}$ in $L_{max}$ (–1 mark).
  - Confusing prevention with detection mechanisms (–4 to –5 marks on 14-mark Q).
  - Not justifying the fail-open/closed choice with a cost argument (–2 marks).
  - Using $|x|$ in a markdown table — use $\vert x \vert$ instead to avoid breaking the table parser (–1 mark for an unreadable table).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Host-Based IDPS - Components and Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Host-Based IDPS - Components and Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Host-Based Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (HIDS/HIPS)** is a security mechanism that monitors and analyses the internals of a computing system, including system calls, application logs, file-system mutations, registry changes, and network packets originating from or destined to a specific host, in order to detect malicious activity and, in the case of prevention, actively block or contain it at the host boundary.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a **HIDS** is contrasted with a **Network-Based IDPS (NIDS/NIPS)** by its deployment location — the agent (sensor) resides *inside* the host, granting the system **kernel-level visibility** and **post-mortem forensic capability** that network sensors cannot provide.

| Dimension | HIDS Scope |
|---|---|
| Deployment Granularity | Per-host (endpoint, server, VM) |
| Primary Visibility Layer | OS kernel, file system, process table, registry, application logs |
| Detection Time Horizon | Both **real-time** and **post-event forensic** |
| Prevention Capability | Yes — via kernel hooks, system call interception, file locking |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Personal Bodyguard" Model

Imagine a corporate office building protected by CCTV cameras (this is analogous to a **NIDS** — it watches the *streets* and *corridors* around the building). Now imagine hiring a **dedicated personal bodyguard** who sits inside the CEO's office, listens to every conversation, watches every document the CEO touches, monitors every phone call, and notes every unusual visitor.

That bodyguard is the **HIDS agent**. Unlike the CCTV (which only sees movement from a distance), the bodyguard:
- **Sees inside the room** (kernel-level system calls).
- **Remembers what the room looked like yesterday** (baseline / signature DB).
- **Can physically stop the attacker** (prevention — block the system call, kill the process).
- **Knows exactly what was stolen or modified** (forensic log integrity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway**: HIDS = *internal observer + active defender* on a single host. The trade-off is that it must be installed on **every host you wish to protect**, which is exactly the architectural divergence we will dissect in Section 4.

## 1.3 Why HIDS Exists — The Problem It Solves

Network-based systems suffer from three structural blind spots:

1. **Encrypted traffic visibility loss** — payload is opaque once TLS/SSH is engaged.
2. **Local-only attacks** — a USB-borne malware or a malicious insider operating *only* on the host cannot be seen on the wire.
3. **Lateral movement after initial breach** — once an attacker is *inside* a host, NIDS sees only normal host-to-host traffic.

HIDS closes these gaps because its sensor runs *with* the host kernel and can inspect events that never traverse the network.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in HIDS Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics appear repeatedly in KTU examinations and in industry-standard HIDS evaluation frameworks. Memorise the **bolded** terms.

- **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate**: $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)**: $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$
- **False Negative Rate (FNR)**: $$FNR = \frac{FN}{TP + FN}$$
- **Accuracy**: $$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$
- **Precision**: $$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$
- **F1-Score**: $$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$$

Where:
- **TP** = True Positive (real attack correctly flagged)
- **FP** = False Positive (benign activity wrongly flagged)
- **TN** = True Negative (benign activity correctly ignored)
- **FN** = False Negative (real attack missed — the most dangerous case)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Components of a HIDS Architecture

Every HIDS, irrespective of vendor (OSSEC, Tripwire, Wazuh, Samhain, OSSEC-HIDS, AIDE), is decomposed into the same four logical components. Understanding this decomposition is a **high-yield KTU question**.

### Component A — Sensor / Agent (The Collector)

- **Role**: Resides on the monitored host and captures raw events at the kernel, application, or user level.
- **Kernel-level capture mechanisms**:
  - Linux: `auditd`, `kprobes`, `eBPF` programs, `ptrace`, LSM hooks (e.g., AppArmor, SELinux).
  - Windows: ETW (Event Tracing for Windows), minifilter drivers, WMI subscriptions, AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface).
- **User-level capture mechanisms**:
  - File integrity monitoring (FIM) — hashes of files compared against a baseline.
  - Log file tailing — syslog, journald, Event Log.
  - Network traffic at the host interface (host firewall / NIC-level tap).

### Component B — Analyzer / Detection Engine (The Brain)

- **Role**: Receives events from the sensor and applies detection logic.
- **Three detection paradigms** (these are **high-yield** for KTU):
  1. **Signature-Based / Misuse Detection** — matches events against a database of known attack patterns (e.g., Snort rules, YARA rules).
  2. **Anomaly-Based Detection** — builds a statistical or ML model of "normal" host behaviour and flags deviations.
  3. **Stateful / Protocol-Aware Detection** — tracks the host's logical state (e.g., file open → modification → deletion) to detect multi-step attacks.

### Component C — Storage / Database (The Memory)

- **Role**: Persists the baseline (known-good state), event logs, alert history, and forensic data.
- Two-tier storage model:
  - **Local short-term store** (SQLite, flat files) on the agent.
  - **Centralised long-term store** (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch) on the manager.

### Component D — Management Console / Manager (The Command Centre)

- **Role**: Aggregates alerts from multiple agents, applies correlation rules, dashboards the situation, and pushes configuration/policy back to agents.

## 2.2 The Detection Logic — Anomaly Detection in Detail

The most mathematically rigorous HIDS component is the **anomaly detection engine**. Below is the canonical statistical formulation used in many HIDS research papers and commercial products.

### 2.2.1 Baseline Construction

Given a feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ representing the $i$-th observation of host behaviour (e.g., CPU utilisation, number of syscalls per second, bytes written per minute), the system computes the baseline mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ over a calibration window of $N$ samples:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}$$

### 2.2.2 Anomaly Score (Mahalanobis Distance)

For a new observation $\mathbf{x}_{new}$, the system computes the **Mahalanobis distance** from the baseline:

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_{new}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}_{new} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_{new} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

An alert is raised when $D_M(\mathbf{x}_{new}) > \tau$, where $\tau$ is a threshold (commonly chosen as the 99th percentile of training distances, i.e., $\tau \approx 3.0$ for normalised data).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mahalanobis and not Euclidean?** Euclidean distance treats all features as independent and equally scaled. Mahalanobis accounts for **feature correlation** (e.g., CPU usage and syscall rate are correlated) and **scale variance** — essential for host behaviour where features have wildly different units.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True Positive Rate (Recall) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Unitless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Unitless, lower is better |
| 3 | False Negative Rate | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | **Most dangerous metric** |
| 4 | Precision | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Unitless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| 5 | F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R |
| 6 | Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| 7 | Mean Baseline Vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i$ | $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 8 | Covariance Matrix | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$ | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| 9 | Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | Scalar anomaly score |
| 10 | Anomaly Threshold | $\tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 3.0$) | Triggers alert |
| 11 | ROC-AUC | Area under $TPR$ vs $FPR$ curve | $\in [0,1]$, ideal = 1.0 |
| 12 | Log-Likelihood (HMM-based HIDS) | $\log P(O \vert \lambda)$ | Used in sequence anomaly detection |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DFIR (Digital Forensics and Incident Response)**: HIDS logs are the *primary evidence source* in post-breach investigations because they capture attacker actions even when network logs were never generated (e.g., offline malware).
- **Compliance**: PCI-DSS Requirement 10.5, HIPAA §164.312(b), and ISO 27001 A.12.4.1 mandate host-level audit trails — a direct driver of HIDS adoption.
- **Insider Threat Detection**: Because HIDS monitors *user behaviour*, it is the only IDPS class that can detect a privileged insider abusing legitimate credentials.
- **Zero-Trust Endpoint**: Modern EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) products are essentially HIDS evolved with cloud ML — the architectural lineage begins here.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Full Mathematical & Logical Derivations

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Raw System Calls to an Anomaly Score

We will trace the complete data flow inside a HIDS anomaly engine, step by step.

### Step 1 — Event Capture

A Linux kernel audit subsystem (using `auditd`) emits a record for every monitored syscall. Each record $r_i$ contains fields such as timestamp, syscall name, PID, UID, target file, return value, and exit code. We treat this as a feature vector.

For simplicity, suppose each event is encoded into a 3-dimensional feature vector capturing the rate of three sensitive syscall families:

$$\mathbf{x}_i = \begin{bmatrix} x_{i,1} \\ x_{i,2} \\ x_{i,3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{open()} \text{ rate per sec} \\ \text{execve()} \text{ rate per sec} \\ \text{write()} \text{ rate per sec} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Calibration Window Sampling

During the calibration phase (a "quiet" period of host operation, typically 7–14 days), we collect $N = 1000$ such vectors. We then compute the empirical mean:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i = \frac{1}{1000} \sum_{i=1}^{1000} \begin{bmatrix} x_{i,1} \\ x_{i,2} \\ x_{i,3} \end{bmatrix}$$

Concretely, suppose the calibration yields:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.2 \\ 0.8 \\ 12.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad N = 1000$$

This means: during normal operation, the host opens ~5.2 files/sec, executes ~0.8 programs/sec, and writes ~12.4 KB/sec (or per equivalent unit).

### Step 3 — Covariance Matrix Computation

We compute the deviations $\mathbf{d}_i = \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ for each calibration sample, then form the outer products and average:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{d}_i \mathbf{d}_i^{T}$$

Concretely, suppose the empirical covariance matrix (with units suppressed) is:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.0 & 0.6 & 1.2 \\ 0.6 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 1.2 & 0.3 & 9.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The diagonal entries are the variances (squared standard deviations) of each feature. The off-diagonals are the covariances — note $\Sigma_{1,3} = 1.2$, indicating that `open()` rate and `write()` rate are mildly positively correlated (opening files is often followed by writing to them).

### Step 4 — Inverting the Covariance Matrix

For the Mahalanobis distance we require $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$. The inverse of the matrix above is computed via the standard adjugate/determinant method. First, the determinant:

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = 4.0 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 9.0 - 0.3 \cdot 0.3) - 0.6 \cdot (0.6 \cdot 9.0 - 0.3 \cdot 1.2) + 1.2 \cdot (0.6 \cdot 0.3 - 0.5 \cdot 1.2)$$

Breaking this into explicit numerical steps:

$$\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = 4.0 \cdot (4.50 - 0.09) - 0.6 \cdot (5.40 - 0.36) + 1.2 \cdot (0.18 - 0.60)$$

$$= 4.0 \cdot 4.41 - 0.6 \cdot 5.04 + 1.2 \cdot (-0.42)$$

$$= 17.64 - 3.024 - 0.504 = 14.112$$

The cofactor matrix $\mathbf{C}$ has entries $C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij}$, where $M_{ij}$ is the minor. We compute each:

$$C_{1,1} = (+1)(0.5 \cdot 9.0 - 0.3 \cdot 0.3) = 4.50 - 0.09 = 4.41$$

$$C_{1,2} = (-1)(0.6 \cdot 9.0 - 0.3 \cdot 1.2) = -(5.40 - 0.36) = -5.04$$

$$C_{1,3} = (+1)(0.6 \cdot 0.3 - 0.5 \cdot 1.2) = 0.18 - 0.60 = -0.42$$

$$C_{2,1} = (-1)(0.6 \cdot 9.0 - 1.2 \cdot 0.3) = -(5.40 - 0.36) = -5.04$$

$$C_{2,2} = (+1)(4.0 \cdot 9.0 - 1.2 \cdot 1.2) = 36.00 - 1.44 = 34.56$$

$$C_{2,3} = (-1)(4.0 \cdot 0.3 - 1.2 \cdot 0.6) = -(1.20 - 0.72) = -0.48$$

$$C_{3,1} = (+1)(0.6 \cdot 0.3 - 0.5 \cdot 0.6) = 0.18 - 0.30 = -0.12$$

$$C_{3,2} = (-1)(4.0 \cdot 0.3 - 1.2 \cdot 0.6) = -(1.20 - 0.72) = -0.48$$

$$C_{3,3} = (+1)(4.0 \cdot 0.5 - 0.6 \cdot 0.6) = 2.00 - 0.36 = 1.64$$

The adjugate is $\text{adj}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = \mathbf{C}^{T}$:

$$\text{adj}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = \begin{bmatrix} 4.41 & -5.04 & -0.12 \\ -5.04 & 34.56 & -0.48 \\ -0.42 & -0.48 & 1.64 \end{bmatrix}$$

The inverse is therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma})} \cdot \text{adj}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = \frac{1}{14.112} \begin{bmatrix} 4.41 & -5.04 & -0.12 \\ -5.04 & 34.56 & -0.48 \\ -0.42 & -0.48 & 1.64 \end{bmatrix}$$

Numerically:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.3125 & -0.3571 & -0.0085 \\ -0.3571 & 2.4490 & -0.0340 \\ -0.0298 & -0.0340 & 0.1162 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Live Anomaly Scoring

Suppose a new live observation arrives indicating suspicious activity (e.g., a ransomware-like burst):

$$\mathbf{x}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 18.0 \\ 4.5 \\ 60.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the deviation vector:

$$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{x}_{new} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 18.0 - 5.2 \\ 4.5 - 0.8 \\ 60.0 - 12.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 \\ 3.7 \\ 47.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the quadratic form $\mathbf{d}^{T} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{d}$:

First, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{d}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{d} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.3125 & -0.3571 & -0.0085 \\ -0.3571 & 2.4490 & -0.0340 \\ -0.0298 & -0.0340 & 0.1162 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 12.8 \\ 3.7 \\ 47.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing row by row:

Row 1: $(0.3125)(12.8) + (-0.3571)(3.7) + (-0.0085)(47.6) = 4.000 - 1.321 - 0.405 = 2.274$

Row 2: $(-0.3571)(12.8) + (2.4490)(3.7) + (-0.0340)(47.6) = -4.571 + 9.061 - 1.618 = 2.872$

Row 3: $(-0.0298)(12.8) + (-0.0340)(3.7) + (0.1162)(47.6) = -0.381 - 0.126 + 5.531 = 5.024$

So $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{d} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.274 \\ 2.872 \\ 5.024 \end{bmatrix}$.

Then $\mathbf{d}^{T} (\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{d})$:

$$\mathbf{d}^{T} (\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{d}) = (12.8)(2.274) + (3.7)(2.872) + (47.6)(5.024)$$

$$= 29.107 + 10.626 + 239.142 = 278.875$$

Therefore the Mahalanobis distance is:

$$D_M = \sqrt{278.875} \approx 16.70$$

### Step 6 — Decision

Threshold $\tau = 3.0$. Since $D_M = 16.70 \gg 3.0$, an **anomaly alert is raised** with severity proportional to $D_M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The value $D_M = 16.70$ is roughly $5.57\sigma$ above the baseline mean — a strong statistical outlier. A real ransomware executing rapid file-encryption would produce a similar spike, which is why this metric is industrially useful.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection Method Comparison

| Dimension | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detection Target | Known attacks | Unknown / zero-day | Multi-step attack chains |
| Data Source | Pattern DB | Statistical baseline | Event sequence log |
| False Positive Rate | **Low** | **High** | Medium |
| False Negative Rate | High for novel attacks | Low (theoretically) | Medium |
| Computational Cost | Low | Medium–High | Medium |
| Training Required | None (rules only) | Yes (calibration) | Yes (state machine) |
| Evasion Resistance | Low (trivial polymorphism defeats) | Medium–High | High |
| KTU Typical Marks | Definition + 1 example | Math + threshold logic | State machine diagram |

## 3.3 File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) — The Workhorse of HIDS

FIM is the canonical HIDS technique and is a guaranteed KTU question.

**Procedure**:

1. Compute cryptographic hash of every critical file in the baseline state:
$$h_j = \text{SHA-256}(\text{file}_j) \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \dots, M$$
2. Store the baseline tuple $(j, h_j, t_j)$ in the integrity database, where $t_j$ is the timestamp.
3. At scheduled intervals, recompute hashes and compare:
$$\Delta_j = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } h_j^{current} = h_j^{baseline} \quad (\text{no change}) \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \quad (\text{tamper detected}) \end{cases}$$
4. Any $\Delta_j = 1$ generates an alert.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation**: FIM is purely *integrity* checking — it does not detect *malicious intent* if the attacker first compromises the baseline (hence the need for write-once storage or remote attestation of the baseline).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Host-Based IDPS and Network-Based IDPS. List any four points.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer (≈120 words)**:

| S.No. | Parameter | HIDS / HIPS | NIDS / NIPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deployment Location | Installed on individual host | Placed at network chokepoint |
| 2 | Visibility | Internal host state, syscalls, files, registry | Network packets only |
| 3 | Encrypted Traffic | Can analyse post-decryption at host | Cannot inspect encrypted payload |
| 4 | Scope of Detection | Local attacks, insider threats, malware | Network-borne attacks, scans, worms |
| 5 | Performance Impact | Consumes host CPU and memory | Minimal host impact |

---

### Q2. What is File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) in HIDS? Why is it considered the workhorse of host-based detection?

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer (≈100 words)**:
FIM is a HIDS technique that periodically computes cryptographic hashes (e.g., **SHA-256**, MD5, SHA-1) of critical system files, binaries, configuration files, and registry entries, and compares them against a stored baseline. Any mismatch indicates that the file has been modified, replaced, or corrupted — a strong indicator of compromise, malware installation, or rootkit activity. It is the *workhorse* because it directly detects the *outcome* of most attacks (file tampering) with very low false positives and minimal computational overhead, and because it provides irrefutable forensic evidence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🎯 Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four major components of a Host-Based IDPS architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the agent and the detection engine in detail.

#### Model Solution

A HIDS architecture consists of four canonical components:

1. **Sensor / Agent** — Resides on the monitored host. Collects raw events from:
   - Linux kernel subsystems (`auditd`, `eBPF`, `kprobes`, LSM hooks).
   - Windows internals (ETW providers, minifilter drivers, AMSI, WMI).
   - File system watchers (inotify on Linux, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows).
   - Log files (syslog, journald, Event Log, application logs).
   - Host network interface (via `pcap`/NAPI drivers).

   The agent **normalises** heterogeneous events into a common schema (e.g., IDMEF — Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format) before forwarding to the analyzer.

2. **Analyzer / Detection Engine** — The brain of the HIDS. Applies detection algorithms:
   - **Signature-based**: pattern matching using regex, YARA, or Snort-style rules.
   - **Anomaly-based**: statistical or ML deviation from baseline behaviour.
   - **Stateful**: tracks host state transitions across multiple events.

3. **Storage / Database** — Two-tier:
   - **Local store** on agent (SQLite, flat files) for buffered events.
   - **Central store** on manager (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch) for long-term correlation and search.

4. **Management Console** — Aggregates alerts from N agents, applies cross-host correlation, dashboards the situation, and pushes policy/configuration back to agents. May also integrate with SIEM/SOAR platforms.

**Role of the Agent**: The agent is the *eye and ear* of the HIDS. It must run with sufficient privileges (often root/SYSTEM) to intercept kernel events, but must itself be tamper-resistant (e.g., write-protected binaries, code-signing verification). Agent failure modes include: bypass (attacker disables the agent), blinding (attacker floods the agent with noise), and tampering (attacker modifies agent binaries).

**Role of the Detection Engine**: It transforms raw events into **security decisions**. Its quality determines the system's TPR/FPR. The engine's design must balance:
- **Detection coverage** (catching diverse attacks) vs.
- **False positive rate** (alert fatigue).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe signature-based and anomaly-based detection techniques used in HIDS. Compare their strengths and weaknesses with suitable examples.

#### Model Solution

**Signature-Based Detection**:
- **Mechanism**: Maintains a database of known attack *signatures* — patterns, byte sequences, regexes, or hash values. Each incoming event is matched against this database.
- **Example**: A HIDS rule like `alert on any write to /etc/passwd by a non-root process` or `alert on YARA rule matching EICAR test string`.
- **Strengths**:
  - Very **low false positive rate** (known patterns only).
  - Fast and computationally cheap.
  - Easy to update with new signatures.
- **Weaknesses**:
  - **Zero-day blind** — cannot detect novel attacks without signatures.
  - Easily evaded by polymorphism, encryption, and obfuscation.
  - Signature database grows unboundedly.

**Anomaly-Based Detection**:
- **Mechanism**: Builds a statistical or ML model of *normal* host behaviour during a calibration phase, then flags deviations.
- **Example**: A baseline of `open()` syscalls at 5.2/sec with standard deviation 2.0; a burst of 30/sec triggers an alert.
- **Mathematical formulation**: Uses the Mahalanobis distance (Section 3.1) or one-class SVM, Isolation Forest, or autoencoders in modern EDR.
- **Strengths**:
  - Can detect **previously unseen / zero-day** attacks.
  - Detects insider threats and slow/low-and-slow attacks.
- **Weaknesses**:
  - **Higher false positive rate** — legitimate but novel behaviour is flagged.
  - Requires careful calibration; baseline drift reduces accuracy.
  - Computationally more expensive.

**Comparison Matrix**:

| Criterion | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Detection of known attacks | Excellent | Good |
| Detection of zero-day attacks | Poor | Excellent |
| False Positive Rate | Low | High |
| Computational Cost | Low | Medium–High |
| Maintenance Effort | Frequent rule updates | Periodic retraining |
| Example HIDS Product | Tripwire, OSSEC rules | OSSEC anomaly module, Wazuh ML |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1**:
> Students frequently confuse *anomaly-based* with *signature-based* in the **opposite** direction during exam stress. Remember:
> - **Signature = Known attack pattern matched.**
> - **Anomaly = Deviation from learned normal pattern.**
> Drawing a **flowchart** of the decision logic (event → match? → alert; event → score → threshold → alert) earns easy marks.

---

### 🎯 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Host-Based Intrusion Prevention System (HIPS). How does it differ from a passive HIDS in terms of response actions?

#### Model Solution

**Working of a HIPS** (see Section 4.1 diagram for the architecture):

1. The **agent** intercepts every sensitive operation at the kernel level via system call hooks.
2. The **detection engine** evaluates the operation against policies and detection rules in real-time (sub-millisecond).
3. If the operation is malicious, the **prevention module** *blocks* the operation *before* it completes. Possible prevention actions:
   - **Deny the system call** (e.g., block an attempt to `ptrace` a sensitive process).
   - **Kill the offending process** (terminate PID).
   - **Quarantine the file** (move to isolated directory, revoke execute permission).
   - **Drop the network packet** at the host firewall.
   - **Roll back the transaction** (e.g., filesystem snapshot restore).
4. The **management console** logs the action and notifies the operator.

**Difference from Passive HIDS**:

| Aspect | Passive HIDS | HIPS (Active) |
|---|---|---|
| Response on detection | Logs and alerts only | **Active intervention** |
| Latency budget | Seconds to minutes acceptable | **Sub-millisecond** required |
| Failure consequence | Log gap (recoverable) | Possible system instability if misfire |
| Example action | Send email alert | Kill process, block syscall |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of statistical anomaly detection in HIDS. Derive the Mahalanobis distance formula and explain how it is used to detect anomalous host behaviour.

#### Model Solution

Statistical anomaly detection treats host behaviour as a multivariate stochastic process. During calibration, the HIDS computes the empirical mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ of $n$-dimensional feature vectors representing host activity (syscall rates, file I/O volumes, login patterns, etc.).

For a new observation $\mathbf{x}_{new}$, the **Mahalanobis distance** measures how many standard deviations $\mathbf{x}_{new}$ lies from the baseline mean, **accounting for feature correlation and scale**:

$$D_M(\mathbf{x}_{new}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}_{new} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_{new} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

**Derivation outline**:

1. Standardise $\mathbf{x}_{new}$ by centring: $\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{x}_{new} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$.
2. Whiten the data by projecting through $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1/2}$ — this decorrelates the features and rescales them to unit variance.
3. In the whitened space, ordinary Euclidean distance is meaningful.
4. The whitening transform's metric tensor is exactly $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$, so the squared distance is $\mathbf{d}^{T} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{d}$.

**Decision rule**: Raise an alert if $D_M(\mathbf{x}_{new}) > \tau$, where $\tau$ is typically chosen as the 99th percentile of calibration distances (≈ 3.0).

**Worked example** (refer to Section 3.1): The computed $D_M = 16.70 \gg \tau = 3.0$ — a strong anomaly signal consistent with ransomware-like burst behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2**:
