> A very common mark-loss area in the Mahalanobis derivation is forgetting that the covariance matrix must be **positive definite** (i.e., invertible). If training data has constant or perfectly correlated features, $\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = 0$ and the formula **blows up**. Always regularise via:
> $$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\text{reg}} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \epsilon \mathbf{I}, \quad \epsilon \approx 10^{-6}$$
> Mentioning this nuance earns **bonus marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Host-Based IDPS**

- ☐ **HIDS** = sensor on the host; **NIDS** = sensor on the network. Remember the deployment dichotomy.
- ☐ Four canonical components: **Sensor, Analyzer, Storage, Management Console**.
- ☐ Three detection paradigms: **Signature, Anomaly, Stateful** — know one strength + one weakness each.
- ☐ **FIM** uses cryptographic hashes; SHA-256 is the modern default.
- ☐ **Mahalanobis distance** is the canonical anomaly score — accounts for correlation and scale (unlike Euclidean).
- ☐ **TPR / FPR / FNR / Precision / F1 / Accuracy** — know every formula; KTU loves asking for these.
- ☐ **HIPS** = HIDS + active prevention (kill process, block syscall, drop packet).
- ☐ **Agent-based** vs **Agentless** — agent-based has deeper visibility but adds host overhead and is a tampering target.
- ☐ HIDS is the **only** IDPS class that detects **local-only attacks and insider threats** effectively.
- ☐ Compliance drivers: **PCI-DSS 10.5, HIPAA §164.312(b), ISO 27001 A.12.4.1**.
- ☐ Modern evolution: HIDS → **EDR** (Endpoint Detection and Response) with cloud ML.
- ☐ Threshold $\tau$ for Mahalanobis is typically the **99th percentile** of calibration distances (≈ 3.0).
- ☐ Covariance regularisation trick ($\boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \epsilon \mathbf{I}$) prevents singular matrix disasters.
- ☐ **HIDS blind spot**: cannot see attacks on hosts where the agent is **not installed** — hence NIDS remains complementary.
- ☐ Draw **block diagrams** in exams — they convey structured understanding and earn easy full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Typical Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** is not a single monolithic program; it is a *distributed, multi-tiered security architecture* composed of several functionally distinct, interoperating components. The **Typical Components** of an IDPS represent the standard functional building blocks defined by NIST SP 800-94 and the IETF IDWG (Intrusion Detection Working Group) reference architecture. These components collaborate to collect telemetry, analyze it for malicious behavior, generate alerts, and (in the case of prevention) actively block the offending traffic.

The canonical components, as per industry and KTU 2024 syllabus standards, are:

1. **Sensor / Agent (Data Collection Component)**
2. **Analysis Engine (Detection Engine)**
3. **Management Server (Director / Command Console)**
4. **Database / Event Storage**
5. **Response / Prevention Module**
6. **Reporting & Visualization Module**
7. **Policy / Signature Repository**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The question *"Explain the typical components of an IDPS with a neat block diagram"* is a **repeated, high-weightage, compulsory-type question** appearing in nearly every End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Master the block diagram AND the function of each block.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **high-security airport terminal**:

- The **CCTV cameras and metal detectors** at every entry gate are the **Sensors** — they collect raw observations (video frames, signals).
- The **Security Control Room analyst** who stares at the monitors, looking for suspicious behavior, is the **Analysis Engine** — it interprets the data.
- The **Chief of Security** sitting in the control tower, who decides what to do with the analyst's findings, is the **Management Server**.
- The **black box / logbook** where every event is recorded is the **Database**.
- The **armed response team** that physically stops a threat is the **Prevention / Response Module**.
- The **daily report filed to the airport director** is the **Reporting Module**.
- The **rulebook** ("No liquids over 100 ml") that governs what is suspicious is the **Policy / Signature Repository**.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as the airport cannot function with only cameras and no analyst (or vice-versa), an IDPS requires *all* components to operate as a coherent system. Removing one breaks the security chain.

### 1.3 Why Components Are Decoupled

In modern, enterprise-grade IDPSs, these components are often **physically separate** (e.g., a Snort sensor on a remote host, a MySQL database on a dedicated server, and a web console on the SOC analyst's laptop). Decoupling provides:

- **Scalability**: Add more sensors without touching the analyzer.
- **Resilience**: If one sensor fails, the rest of the system continues.
- **Centralized Control**: One operator can manage hundreds of sensors from a single console.

This decoupling is what the KTU module expects you to draw and explain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-by-Component Analysis

#### Component 1: Sensor / Agent (Data Collector)

The **sensor** is the *eyes and ears* of the IDPS. It is the only component in direct contact with the network traffic or host activity.

**Operational Logic:**
- **Network-based sensors (NIDS/NIPS)**: Tap into a SPAN port, network TAP, or inline bridge. They passively capture packets using libraries like **libpcap**.
- **Host-based agents (HIDS/HIPS)**: Hook into the OS kernel via system call interception, file integrity checkers (e.g., Tripwire), or log aggregators (e.g., Windows Event Log forwarders).

**Why this component matters:** A sensor with blind spots (e.g., placed on the wrong VLAN) renders the entire IDPS useless, regardless of how smart the analyzer is.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors must be clock-synchronized** (typically via NTP) so that events from multiple sensors can be temporally correlated during analysis.

#### Component 2: Analysis Engine (Detection Engine)

The **analysis engine** is the *brain* of the IDPS. It receives raw events from one or more sensors and applies detection logic to classify them as benign, suspicious, or malicious.

**Three Primary Detection Methodologies:**

1. **Signature-Based (Misuse Detection)**: Compares event patterns against a database of known attack signatures (e.g., Snort rules). Pros: Low false-positive rate, fast. Cons: Cannot detect zero-day attacks.
2. **Anomaly-Based**: Builds a statistical model of "normal" behavior and flags deviations. Pros: Can detect novel attacks. Cons: Higher false-positive rate.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis**: Tracks the state of network protocols (e.g., TCP handshake sequences) and flags violations. Pros: Protocol-aware. Cons: Resource-intensive.

**KTU 2024 Standard Terminology:** The analysis engine is also called the **Detector** or **Correlator** when it performs cross-event correlation (e.g., linking 5 failed logins + 1 successful login as a brute-force attack).

#### Component 3: Management Server (Director / Command Console)

The **management server** is the *central coordinator*. It is the human-machine interface (HMI) for the SOC analyst.

**Functions:**
- Configuration of sensors (what to watch, where to log).
- Aggregation of alerts from distributed sensors.
- Push of signature updates to all sensors.
- Display of a unified dashboard with severity-tagged alerts.
- Role-based access control (RBAC) for multiple operators.

> [!NOTE]
> In a *hierarchical* deployment, there can be **local managers** (per site) and a **central manager** (for the enterprise). This is a common KTU 14-mark question variation.

#### Component 4: Database / Event Storage

All events, alerts, and logs must be **persisted** for forensic analysis, regulatory compliance (e.g., PCI-DSS requires 1 year of retention), and post-incident investigation.

**Common Implementations:**
- **Flat files** (simple, but poor query performance)
- **Relational databases** (PostgreSQL, MySQL) — best for structured alerts.
- **Time-series databases** (InfluxDB, Elasticsearch) — best for high-volume telemetry.
- **SIEM integration** — the IDPS forwards events to a Security Information and Event Management system (e.g., Splunk, QRadar).

**Storage metrics KTU may quiz on:**

$$
\text{Retention}_{\text{size}} = \text{EPS} \times \text{AvgSize} \times \text{Days} \times 86400
$$

where **EPS** = Events Per Second ingested by the sensor.

#### Component 5: Response / Prevention Module

This is the *muscle* of the IDPS, present only in **IPS** (not pure IDS) deployments.

**Two Response Classes:**

| Class | Action | Example |
|-------|--------|---------|
| **Passive Response** | Alert-only; logs and notifies | Email to SOC, SNMP trap to NMS |
| **Active Response** | Actively modifies the threat | TCP reset, ACL block, quarantine host, drop packet |

> [!WARNING]
> **Active responses can be self-inflicted DoS.** A poorly tuned IPS may block legitimate traffic (false positive), causing an outage. This is a classic KTU pitfall.

#### Component 6: Reporting & Visualization Module

Generates **periodic reports** (daily, weekly, monthly) for:
- Executive dashboards (KPIs, trend lines).
- Compliance audits (HIPAA, SOX, PCI-DSS).
- Forensic timelines.

Common formats: PDF, HTML dashboards, CSV exports.

#### Component 7: Policy / Signature Repository

The **policy repository** stores the rules that govern detection. For signature-based engines, this is the signature database (e.g., Snort's `rules/` directory with VRT rules, Emerging Threats rules). Updates are typically fetched daily from the vendor.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula or Definition | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Events Per Second (EPS)** | $EPS = \frac{\text{Total Events}}{\Delta t}$ | events/sec | Sizing sensors and DB |
| **Storage Volume** | $V = EPS \times S_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{retention}} \times 86400$ | bytes | Capacity planning |
| **Detection Rate (DR)** | $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Classifier evaluation |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Classifier evaluation |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Alert quality |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Balanced metric |
| **Detection Latency** | $L_d = t_{\text{alert}} - t_{\text{event}}$ | seconds/minutes | Real-time constraint |
| **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{d,i}$ | seconds | SOC KPI |
| **Sensor Coverage** | $C = \frac{\text{Monitored Traffic}}{\text{Total Traffic}}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Deployment quality |

Where **TP** = True Positives, **FP** = False Positives, **TN** = True Negatives, **FN** = False Negatives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 14-mark answers, always define *every* abbreviation you use. Examiners specifically check for clarity of notation.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

The component-based architecture is what allows **Cisco Firepower**, **Suricata**, **Snort**, **OSSEC**, and **Zeek** to be deployed in enterprise SOCs. Modern XDR (Extended Detection and Response) platforms are essentially **multi-sensor IDPSs** with cloud-scale analysis engines and response modules integrated with EDR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Walkthrough

Since this topic is **architectural** rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" is replaced by a **logical step-by-step operational flow** showing how data propagates through every component — exactly what a KTU 14-mark answer demands.

### 3.1 The Complete Data Flow Through IDPS Components

Consider a real attack: **SQL Injection** against a web server (`http://target.com/login.php`).

**Step 1 — Event Generation (Victim Side)**
An attacker sends the payload `' OR '1'='1` in an HTTP POST request. A TCP packet enters the network. The web server's NIC receives the frame. The web server's CPU processes the HTTP request.

**Step 2 — Sensor Capture**
A network sensor, configured in **inline mode** (for NIPS) or **passive TAP mode** (for NIDS), receives a copy of the packet via a SPAN port on the switch. The sensor's packet capture library (libpcap/winpcap) buffers the raw frame. A new event tuple is constructed:

$$
E = \langle t, src\_IP, dst\_IP, src\_port, dst\_port, proto, payload, len \rangle
$$

**Step 3 — Pre-processing and Normalization**
The sensor's pre-processor module decodes the packet layers (Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP), reassembles TCP segments if needed, and decodes the application-layer payload. The normalized event is forwarded to the **Analysis Engine** (in some deployments, the analysis engine is *in-process*; in others, it is a separate server receiving events via a secure channel like **IDMEF** over **TLS**).

**Step 4 — Signature Matching**
The analysis engine applies the rule set. A hypothetical Snort rule for SQLi:

```
alert tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"SQL Injection Attempt";
  content:"OR"; nocase; pcre:"/\b(OR|AND)\s+['\d]/i";
  classtype:web-application-attack; sid:1000001; rev:1;)
```

The engine finds a match and creates an **alert object** with the matched rule, source, destination, and a packet dump.

**Step 5 — Correlation (in advanced engines)**
A correlator checks if this is the 50th SQLi attempt in 10 seconds from the same source IP. If yes, it **escalates** the alert severity from `Medium` to `Critical` and tags it as a possible automated scanner.

**Step 6 — Database Storage**
The alert is written to the event database. A typical schema:

$$
\text{Table } alerts(\text{id, ts, sensor\_id, src, dst, sig\_id, severity, payload, action})
$$

**Step 7 — Management Console Notification**
The management server polls the database (or receives a push via AMQP) and updates the SOC dashboard. The alert appears as a red dot on the analyst's screen within **2–5 seconds** of the attack.

**Step 8 — Response Decision**
- **IDS mode**: The analyst reviews and decides. If malicious, manually blocks the source IP on the perimeter firewall.
- **IPS mode (inline)**: The response module automatically drops subsequent packets from the source IP for a configurable duration (e.g., 1 hour) and optionally sends a TCP RST to the attacker to tear down the connection.

**Step 9 — Reporting**
At midnight, the reporting module generates a daily PDF: *"147 SQLi attempts blocked from 23 source IPs. Top attacker: 203.0.113.45 (89 attempts)."* This is emailed to the CISO and archived for compliance.

### 3.2 Component Interaction Matrix

The following matrix shows the *direction* of information flow and the *protocols* used:

| From → To | Sensor → Analyzer | Analyzer → DB | Manager → Sensor | DB → Manager | Response → Attacker |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Unit** | Raw event / normalized alert | Alert tuple | Configuration, signature update | Alert records | Blocked packet / RST |
| **Protocol** | IDMEF, SDEE, syslog, proprietary TCP | SQL, ODBC, JDBC | HTTPS, SSH, vendor-proprietary | SQL, REST API | Inline (Layer 2/3) |
| **Security** | TLS / IPsec | TLS | Mutual TLS, RBAC | TLS | N/A (in-band) |
| **Frequency** | Per-event, real-time | Per-event | Periodic (every 5 min for signatures) | On-demand (poll) or push | Per-malicious-packet |

### 3.3 Hierarchical vs. Flat Deployment (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

**Flat deployment:** All sensors report directly to one central manager. Pros: Simple. Cons: Single point of failure, scales poorly past ~50 sensors.

**Hierarchical deployment:** Sensors report to local managers, which aggregate and forward to a central manager. Pros: Scalable, fault-tolerant. Cons: More complex configuration.

The KTU examiner often asks you to **draw and justify** a deployment for a "university campus with 3 blocks, 5000 users."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Standard IDPS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXT["External Network / Internet"]
        ATK[Attacker Traffic]
    end

    subgraph PERIM["Perimeter / Network"]
        FW[Firewall / Router]
        TAP[Network TAP / SPAN Port]
    end

    subgraph IDPSCORE["IDPS Core Components"]
        S1[Sensor 1\nNetwork Sensor]
        S2[Sensor 2\nHost Agent]
        AE[Analysis Engine\nSignature + Anomaly + State]
        DB[(Event Database\nPostgreSQL / Elasticsearch)]
        MS[Management Server\nSOC Console]
        RR[Rule / Signature\nRepository]
        REP[Reporting Module\nPDF / Dashboard]
    end

    subgraph ACT["Active Response"]
        RSP[Response Module\nIPS Inline Action]
    end

    ATK --> FW --> TAP --> S1
    TAP -.mirror.-> S1
    S2 -->|host logs| AE
    S1 -->|normalized events| AE
    AE -->|alerts| DB
    RR -->|signature push| AE
    RR -->|policy push| S1
    MS -->|config| S1
    MS -->|config| S2
    MS -->|query| DB
    DB -->|alerts| MS
    AE -->|inline drop / RST| RSP
    RSP -->|blocked| TAP
    DB -->|raw data| REP
    REP -->|daily report| MS
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Hierarchical Multi-Site Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SITE1["Site A Campus"]
        SA1[Sensor A1]
        SA2[Sensor A2]
        LMA[Local Manager A]
    end

    subgraph SITE2["Site B Campus"]
        SB1[Sensor B1]
        SB2[Sensor B2]
        LMB[Local Manager B]
    end

    subgraph CENTRAL["Central SOC"]
        CM[Central Manager]
        CMTOP[Top-Level Dashboard]
    end

    SA1 --> LMA
    SA2 --> LMA
    SB1 --> LMB
    SB2 --> LMB
    LMA -->|aggregated events| CM
    LMB -->|aggregated events| CM
    CM --> CMTOP
```

### 4.3 Component State Diagram — Lifecycle of an Event

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Captured
    Captured --> Normalized : pre-processor
    Normalized --> Analyzing : analysis engine
    Analyzing --> NoMatch : no rule hit
    Analyzing --> Suspicious : low severity match
    Analyzing --> Malicious : high severity match
    Suspicious --> Correlating : correlation engine
    Correlating --> Malicious : escalated
    Correlating --> Benign : whitelisted
    Malicious --> Stored : written to DB
    Stored --> Notified : push to console
    Notified --> Responded : active block
    Responded --> Reported : daily report
    NoMatch --> Archived : silent log
    Benign --> Archived
    Reported --> [*]
    Archived --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. List the typical components of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System.**

**Model Answer:**
The typical components of an IDPS are:
1. **Sensors / Agents** — capture network packets or host activity.
2. **Analysis Engine** — applies signature, anomaly, or stateful analysis to detect attacks.
3. **Management Server** — central console for configuration, monitoring, and control.
4. **Event Database** — persistent storage of alerts and logs.
5. **Response / Prevention Module** — generates alerts (passive) or actively blocks traffic (active).
6. **Reporting Module** — generates dashboards and compliance reports.
7. **Policy / Signature Repository** — stores detection rules and signatures.

> [!NOTE]
> Mentioning *at least 5* components in a 3-mark answer typically earns full marks. Drawing a small block diagram in Part A is bonus-worthy.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a Sensor and an Analysis Engine in an IDPS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensor | Analysis Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Data collection | Data interpretation |
| **Location** | At the network tap / on the host | Backend server (or co-located) |
| **Function** | Captures raw packets / log entries | Matches against rules, applies ML/anomaly models |
| **Output** | Normalized event tuples | Alerts, severity tags, action recommendations |
| **Analogy** | CCTV camera | Security analyst watching the feed |

The sensor is the *input* layer; the analysis engine is the *processing* layer.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 4]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the typical components of an IDPS with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the Analysis Engine in detail. Compare signature-based, anomaly-based, and stateful protocol analysis detection methods. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) [7 Marks] Components of an IDPS with Block Diagram:**

The Intrusion Detection and Prevention System is a modular security architecture. The typical components are:

1. **Sensor / Agent:** This is the data collection component. A *network sensor* taps into a SPAN port or inline bridge and captures packets using libpcap. A *host agent* installs on an endpoint and monitors system calls, file integrity, and OS logs. The sensor pre-processes and normalizes the data before forwarding it.

2. **Analysis Engine (Detector):** This is the core decision-making component. It receives normalized events from sensors and applies detection algorithms — signature matching, anomaly scoring, or stateful protocol validation — to produce alerts with a confidence level and severity.

3. **Management Server (Director):** This is the central control point. It pushes configuration and signature updates *down* to sensors, and receives alerts *up* from the analysis engine. It provides the GUI console, RBAC, and dashboard for SOC analysts.

4. **Event Database:** All alerts and raw events are stored here using RDBMS (PostgreSQL) or time-series stores (Elasticsearch). It supports forensic queries, compliance retention (e.g., 1 year for PCI-DSS), and incident timeline reconstruction.

5. **Response / Prevention Module:** Present in IPS deployments, this component can *passively* (log + alert) or *actively* (drop packets, send TCP RST, quarantine host, add firewall ACL) respond to a detected intrusion. Active response is inline and operates at wire speed.

6. **Reporting Module:** Generates daily/weekly/monthly reports — executive KPIs, top attackers, attack-type histograms, compliance evidence — in PDF, CSV, or web-dashboard formats.

7. **Policy / Signature Repository:** Stores the rule set (e.g., Snort VRT rules, Emerging Threats). It is updated periodically (often daily) from the vendor's feed and pushed to all sensors by the management server.

**Block Diagram:** *(See SECTION 4.1 above — students should redraw it cleanly in the exam.)*

**(b) [7 Marks] Role of Analysis Engine + Detection Methods:**

The **Analysis Engine** is the decision-making core. Its primary role is to take normalized events from sensors and produce a verdict: *benign, suspicious, or malicious*. It must operate in real time (sub-second latency) and with high accuracy.

**Three Detection Methodologies — Comparison:**

| Feature | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Pattern match against known attack signatures | Deviation from a learned "normal" baseline | Violation of expected protocol state machine |
| **Detects** | Known attacks only | Zero-day, novel attacks | Protocol misuse, malformed packets |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low | High | Medium |
| **False Negative Rate** | High (for novel attacks) | Low | Low (for protocol attacks) |
| **Resource Use** | Low | High (ML model in memory) | High (state tables) |
| **Example** | Snort VRT rules | NetFlow anomaly detection | Suricata HTTP parser |
| **Update Need** | Daily signature updates | Periodic model retraining | Protocol spec updates |

**Modern IDPSs combine all three** (a *hybrid* engine) to balance precision and recall. For example, Suricata supports signature rules, Lua-based anomaly scripting, and a deep HTTP/TCP/DNS state machine simultaneously.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the hierarchical deployment of an IDPS in a multi-site organization. What are its advantages over a flat deployment? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the Response Module. Differentiate between active and passive response mechanisms with examples. What are the risks of an incorrectly configured active response? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) [7 Marks] Hierarchical Multi-Site Deployment:**

In a **hierarchical deployment**, IDPS sensors at branch offices report to a *Local Manager* at each site, which aggregates, deduplicates, and forwards events to a *Central Manager* at the enterprise SOC. The Central Manager hosts the master policy and signature repository, which is reverse-pushed to all local managers and then to their sensors.

*(See SECTION 4.2 Mermaid diagram for reference.)*

**Advantages over Flat Deployment:**
1. **Scalability** — Central manager handles aggregates, not raw floods; supports thousands of sensors.
2. **Reduced bandwidth** — Only summarized events traverse the WAN.
3. **Fault isolation** — Site A's network failure does not affect Site B's detection.
4. **Local autonomy** — Each site manager can apply site-specific tuning.
5. **Tiered response** — Local IPS blocks at the edge; central SOC handles forensics.

**Disadvantages:** Higher complexity, more components to manage, licensing cost per manager.

**(b) [7 Marks] Response Module — Active vs. Passive:**

The **Response Module** is the actuator of the IDPS — it carries out the system's decision once an attack is confirmed.

**Passive Response:**
- Logs the event with full packet capture.
- Sends an email / SMS / SNMP trap to the SOC.
- Displays alert on the dashboard.
- *Example:* Snort in NIDS mode logging a `communityid` alert to syslog.

**Active Response (IPS only, inline):**
- **Session termination:** Sends TCP RST to attacker and victim to forcibly close the connection.
- **Packet drop:** Silently discards malicious packets.
- **Quarantine:** Puts the offending host into a VLAN with no network access.
- **Firewall rule push:** Adds a dynamic ACL block for the source IP.
- **Rate limiting:** Throttles the offender's bandwidth.

**Risks of Incorrectly Configured Active Response:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — KTU Examiners Specifically Look For This:**

1. **Self-inflicted DoS:** A false positive (e.g., a Snort rule misfiring on a legitimate SQL query containing the word `OR`) can block a paying customer. This is the **#1 cause of IPS outages** in production.
2. **Traffic blackholing:** Overly broad rules (e.g., blocking an entire /16 because of one bad IP) can disrupt business.
3. **Attacker exploitation:** A skilled attacker can craft traffic that *triggers* a rule, causing the IPS to block legitimate users (a *resource exhaustion* or *self-DoS* attack).
4. **Asymmetric routing errors:** A RST sent on the wrong path can leave the original session alive and corrupt state.
5. **Compliance violations:** Automatically blocking a user without human review may breach data-protection regulations in some jurisdictions (e.g., GDPR Article 22 on automated decision-making).

**Best Practice:** Run the IDPS in **IDS-only (passive) mode** for a *learning period* (typically 2–4 weeks) to baseline alerts, tune the rule set, and *then* enable active response in a *monitor-only* (alert-but-don't-drop) mode before finally switching to inline blocking.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before the Exam**

- ✅ An IDPS has **7 typical components**: Sensor, Analysis Engine, Management Server, Database, Response Module, Reporting Module, Policy/Signature Repository.
- ✅ The **sensor** is the *only* component that directly touches live traffic or host data.
- ✅ The **analysis engine** uses three detection methods: **Signature**, **Anomaly**, **Stateful Protocol Analysis**. Know the trade-offs (FP vs. FN vs. resource cost).
- ✅ The **management server** is bidirectional: pushes *config* down, receives *alerts* up.
- ✅ The **response module** distinguishes **passive** (log/notify) from **active** (drop/RST/quarantine). Active = inline IPS only.
- ✅ **Hierarchical deployment** = local managers + central manager. Used for multi-site enterprises.
- ✅ **Flat deployment** = all sensors → one central manager. Simple, but scales poorly.
- ✅ **False Positive** = legitimate traffic flagged as malicious. **False Negative** = malicious traffic missed. Both are critical evaluation metrics.
- ✅ **EPS (Events Per Second)** drives storage sizing: $V = EPS \times S_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{retention}} \times 86400$.
- ✅ Always **draw a labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark or 14-mark question on this topic. Marks are reserved explicitly for the diagram.
- ✅ **Sensors must be clock-synchronized** (NTP) for cross-correlation.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, structure as: *Definition → Block Diagram → Component-by-component explanation → Comparison/Justification → Real-world example*.
- ✅ The KTU examiner's pet question: *"Differentiate IDS and IPS"* — answer: the **Response Module's active capability** is the *only* architectural difference; all other components are shared.
- ✅ Remember the **NIST SP 800-94** terminology: *Sensor, Analyzer, Database, Manager/Console, Response* — this is the *exact* wording the KTU paper setter uses.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** Think of the components as **"S.A.M.D.R.R.P"** — **S**ensor, **A**nalyzer, **M**anager, **D**atabase, **R**esponse, **R**eporting, **P**olicy. Memorize this 7-letter acronym and you can recall the architecture in any exam stress condition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Architecture (in IDPS Context):** The structured, hierarchical design framework that defines how intrusion detection and prevention components — *sensors, agents, analyzers, managers, and response modules* — are physically distributed across a network, how they collect security telemetry, and how they communicate to detect, correlate, and respond to malicious activity.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Network Architectures for IDPS** refers specifically to the **deployment topology, sensor placement strategy, and communication framework** that determines *what* an IDPS sees, *where* it sees it, and *how fast* it can react. The architecture is the **backbone design choice** that influences the entire detection capability of the system.

## 1.2 Core Architectural Components of a Network-Based IDPS

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Sensor** | Captures raw network packets from a network segment |
| **Agent** | Software that collects audit/log data on a host |
| **Manager / Analysis Engine** | Correlates events, runs detection logic, issues alerts |
| **Database Server** | Stores event logs, signatures, baseline profiles |
| **Response Module** | Executes preventive actions (block, drop, reset) |
| **Console / Management UI** | Human-machine interface for the security analyst |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes that the *placement* of sensors/agents and the *direction of data flow* between them defines the architecture.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Model

Imagine an **international airport**:

- **Sensors** are like the **X-ray scanners and metal detectors** at multiple checkpoints. They "see" the traffic (passengers) at strategic points.
- **Centralized Manager** is the **Central Security Control Room**, which receives alerts from all scanners and decides the threat level.
- **Distributed Architecture** is like having **independent security booths in every terminal**, each with its own control room.
- **Tiered Architecture** is like a **layered defense** — entry gate, then check-in counter, then boarding gate — multiple checkpoints along the path.
- **Inline Prevention (IPS)** is the **security officer who physically stops a suspect** at the gate, whereas **Passive Detection (IDS)** is the **CCTV camera** that records but doesn't stop.

Just as airports place scanners at **chokepoints** (entry, immigration, boarding), IDPS sensors are placed at network chokepoints (perimeter, DMZ, internal segments).

## 1.4 Key Architectural Metrics in IDPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Bandwidth** — measured in **Mbps / Gbps**
> - **Latency** — measured in **milliseconds (ms)**
> - **Packet Per Second (PPS)** — sensor throughput capacity
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** — speed of detection
> - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** — speed of mitigation
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — legitimate traffic misclassified as attack
> - **False Negative Rate (FNR)** — attacks missed by the system

These metrics are the **grading criteria** the KTU examiner uses to evaluate your architecture choice in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Network Architecture Classes (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Host-Based Architecture
- **Sensor/Agent** runs on the **individual host** being monitored.
- Inspects **OS logs, system calls, file integrity, registry changes, process activity**.
- **Visibility:** Sees *post-decryption* traffic (after host has processed it).
- **Limitation:** Cannot see **network-level attacks** like port scans originating elsewhere.

### B. Network-Based Architecture
- **Sensor** is a **dedicated appliance or tap** placed on a network segment.
- Captures packets via **port mirroring (SPAN), network TAP, or inline bypass**.
- Inspects **raw packet headers and payloads** in real-time.
- **Visibility:** Sees *wire traffic* — but cannot see **encrypted payload content**.

### C. Hybrid (Host + Network) Architecture
- Combines both — **most enterprise-grade deployments** follow this.
- Network sensors detect **broad-spectrum attacks**; host agents confirm **specific compromise**.

## 2.2 The Three Core Architectural Topologies

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks the difference between these three topologies — memorize the table below.**

| Topology | Data Flow | Strength | Weakness | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Centralized** | All sensors → ONE central analyzer | Simple management, low cost | Single Point of Failure (SPOF), bandwidth bottleneck | "Star topology" |
| **Distributed** | Each sensor has its own analyzer, peer-to-peer correlation | No SPOF, scalable, fast local response | Complex configuration, high cost | "Mesh / peer-to-peer" |
| **Hierarchical (Tiered)** | Layered: local → regional → central manager | Balanced — local speed + global correlation | Medium complexity | "Multi-tier" |

## 2.3 Sensor Placement Strategy (Chokepoint Theory)

The KTU syllabus expects you to know the **6 canonical sensor placement locations**:

1. **Network Perimeter (just inside the firewall)** — catches inbound attacks
2. **DMZ** — protects public-facing servers
3. **Behind the firewall (internal network)** — catches insider attacks
4. **At WAN gateways** — inter-segment traffic monitoring
5. **On critical subnets (e.g., database, finance)** — high-value asset protection
6. **Inline (IPS mode)** — for active prevention in real-time traffic flow

## 2.4 Inline vs Passive Tapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inline Mode (IPS):** Sensor sits *in-line* with the traffic. It can **block/drop packets** before they reach the destination. Risk: if sensor fails, traffic stops (use **fail-open** or **fail-closed** configuration).

> **Passive Mode (IDS):** Sensor receives a **copy** of traffic via **SPAN port or TAP**. Cannot block — only alerts. Risk-free for traffic flow.

**The Engineering Decision:**
- **Fail-Open** = if sensor crashes, traffic passes (used for **availability-critical** links)
- **Fail-Closed** = if sensor crashes, traffic stops (used for **confidentiality-critical** segments)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are evaluable in KTU numerical/short-answer questions. Master these formulas.

| Metric / Concept | Formula or Definition | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor Throughput** | $\text{PPS} = \dfrac{\text{Packets Processed}}{\text{Time (sec)}}$ | packets/sec | Sizing the sensor hardware |
| **Bandwidth Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Achieved Throughput}}{\text{Link Capacity}} \times 100$ | **%** | Detects bottlenecks |
| **Link Saturation Threshold** | Saturation occurs when $U > 80\%$ | **%** | Sensor dropping packets alarm |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} \times 100$ | **%** | Detection accuracy |
| **False Negative Rate** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP} \times 100$ | **%** | Missed attack rate |
| **Detection Accuracy (Recall)** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless | Sensitivity of IDPS |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | dimensionless | Trustworthiness of alerts |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{P \times R}{P + R}$ | dimensionless | Balanced metric |
| **Mean Time to Detect** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (T_{detect} - T_{attack})}{N_{attacks}}$ | **seconds** | Speed of detection |
| **Mean Time to Respond** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum (T_{mitigate} - T_{detect})}{N_{attacks}}$ | **seconds** | Speed of response |
| **Coverage Ratio** | $C = \dfrac{\text{Monitored Subnets}}{\text{Total Subnets}} \times 100$ | **%** | Architecture completeness |
| **Redundancy Factor** | $R = \dfrac{N_{sensors} - 1}{N_{sensors}}$ | dimensionless | Failover capability |

**Where:** $TP$ = True Positive, $TN$ = True Negative, $FP$ = False Positive, $FN$ = False Negative, $P$ = Precision, $R$ = Recall.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cisco Firepower / Sourcefire** — uses **distributed + hierarchical** architecture.
- **Snort** (open-source) — supports **centralized, distributed, and hybrid** modes.
- **Suricata** with **EVE JSON output** — feeds a **centralized SIEM** (e.g., Splunk, ELK).
- **AWS GuardDuty / Azure Defender** — **cloud-native distributed** sensors in every subnet.

## 2.7 Communication Flow Models

| Model | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pull Model** | Manager *polls* sensors for data | SNMP-based polling |
| **Push Model** | Sensor *sends* data to manager on event | Syslog forwarding |
| **Publish-Subscribe** | Manager subscribes to topics; sensors publish | Kafka-based modern SIEM |
| **Peer-to-Peer** | Sensors exchange data directly | Distributed IDPS mesh |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 14-mark questions, **always include a labeled diagram** showing the data flow direction — examiners reward architectural clarity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: IDPS Detection Performance Metrics

This derivation is a **high-yield KTU topic** — questions on calculating **FPR, FNR, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** appear regularly.

### Step 1: Define the Confusion Matrix

The foundational construct in any IDPS evaluation is the **2×2 Confusion Matrix**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{Actual Class} \\
& \begin{array}{c|cc}
 & \text{Attack} & \text{Normal} \\
\hline
\text{Predicted Attack} & TP & FP \\
\text{Predicted Normal} & FN & TN \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of cells:**
- $TP$ (True Positive) — Attack correctly identified as attack.
- $TN$ (True Negative) — Normal traffic correctly identified as normal.
- $FP$ (False Positive) — Normal traffic falsely flagged as attack (Type I error).
- $FN$ (False Negative) — Attack missed, treated as normal (Type II error).

### Step 2: Derive the False Positive Rate (FPR)

The FPR measures the **proportion of actual negatives that are incorrectly classified as positives**.

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

**Reasoning:** The denominator $FP + TN$ represents the *total number of actual normal events*. Out of these, the IDPS incorrectly flagged $FP$ of them. Therefore, the ratio of misclassification within the normal class is $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$.

**Unit:** Dimensionless ratio, expressed as a **percentage** in IDPS dashboards.

### Step 3: Derive the False Negative Rate (FNR)

$$
FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}
$$

**Reasoning:** The denominator $FN + TP$ is the *total number of actual attacks*. Out of these, $FN$ attacks slipped through. The ratio gives the *missed attack rate*.

### Step 4: Derive Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Reasoning:** Of all events the IDPS *flagged as attacks*, the fraction that were *truly attacks* is precision. High precision → the analyst's time is not wasted on false alarms.

### Step 5: Derive Recall (Detection Rate / Sensitivity)

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Reasoning:** Of all *actual attacks*, the fraction the IDPS successfully caught is recall. High recall → few attacks slip through.

### Step 6: Derive the F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)

The **F1-Score** balances precision and recall into a single metric.

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{P \times R}{P + R}
$$

**Derivation logic:** The harmonic mean *penalizes imbalance*. If either $P \to 0$ or $R \to 0$, the F1 score collapses. This reflects the engineering reality: an IDPS that is highly precise but misses most attacks (low recall) is just as bad as one that flags everything (low precision).

### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An IDPS was tested over 24 hours. Results:
- Total events analyzed: $10{,}000$
- True attacks in the traffic: $200$
- IDPS alerts raised: $250$
- Of those alerts, $180$ were real attacks; $70$ real attacks were missed.

**Find:** $FPR$, $FNR$, Precision, Recall, F1-Score.

**Step 7a: Identify the Confusion Matrix entries.**

- Total events = $10{,}000$
- Total attacks = $TP + FN = 200$ (given)
- Total alerts raised = $TP + FP = 250$ (given)
- $TP = 180$ (real attacks correctly flagged)
- $FP = 250 - 180 = 70$ (false alarms)
- $FN = 200 - 180 = 20$ (missed attacks)
- $TN = 10{,}000 - 250 - 20 = 9{,}730$ (correctly passed normal traffic)

**Step 7b: Calculate FPR.**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{70}{70 + 9{,}730} = \frac{70}{9{,}800} \approx 0.00714
$$

Expressed as a percentage: $FPR \approx 0.714\%$

**Step 7c: Calculate FNR.**

$$
FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = \frac{20}{20 + 180} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10
$$

Expressed as a percentage: $FNR = 10\%$

**Step 7d: Calculate Precision.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 70} = \frac{180}{250} = 0.72
$$

Expressed as a percentage: $P = 72\%$

**Step 7e: Calculate Recall.**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90
$$

Expressed as a percentage: $R = 90\%$

**Step 7f: Calculate F1-Score.**

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{P \times R}{P + R} = 2 \times \frac{0.72 \times 0.90}{0.72 + 0.90}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{0.648}{1.62} = 2 \times 0.40 = 0.80
$$

**Final Answer:** $FPR = 0.714\%$, $FNR = 10\%$, $P = 72\%$, $R = 90\%$, $F1 = 0.80$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the KTU answer:** Recall ($90\%$) is high — the IDPS catches most attacks. However, precision ($72\%$) is moderate — about 1 in 4 alerts is a false alarm. The F1-score of $0.80$ indicates a **reasonably balanced** system but with room for tuning.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Trade-Offs (KTU Comparison Table)

| Decision Parameter | Centralized | Distributed | Hierarchical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scalability** | Low | Very High | High |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Yes (manager) | No | Reduced (only top tier) |
| **Local Response Latency** | High | Very Low | Low |
| **Management Complexity** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Capital Cost (CAPEX)** | Low | Very High | Medium |
| **Operational Cost (OPEX)** | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Correlation Capability** | Excellent (all data in one place) | Poor (data scattered) | Good (regional + global) |
| **Bandwidth Burden on Backbone** | Very High | Low | Medium |
| **Suitable for** | Small/medium networks | Large enterprise / cloud | Multi-site enterprise |
| **Example Deployment** | Small office Snort | Cloud-native GuardDuty | Multi-campus university |

## 3.3 Derivation: Coverage Ratio for Architecture Completeness

The **Coverage Ratio** is a KTU-evaluable metric that quantifies how much of the network is monitored.

$$
C = \frac{N_{monitored}}{N_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Where:**
- $N_{monitored}$ = number of subnets/VLANs where a sensor is present
- $N_{total}$ = total number of subnets/VLANs in the organization

**Example:** A university has 12 VLANs. Sensors are placed on 9 VLANs. Then:

$$
C = \frac{9}{12} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Coverage Ratio below 90%** is considered architecturally weak for an enterprise IDPS — examiners will mark down your 14-mark design question if you don't justify blind spots.

## 3.4 Derivation: Sensor Throughput Sizing

If a link carries on average $A$ Mbps and average packet size is $S$ bytes, the required sensor PPS is:

$$
\text{Required PPS} = \frac{A \times 10^{6}}{8 \times S}
$$

**Example:** A 1 Gbps link with average packet size $500$ bytes:

$$
\text{Required PPS} = \frac{1 \times 10^{9}}{8 \times 500} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000{,}000}{4{,}000} = 250{,}000 \text{ PPS}
$$

**Engineering implication:** A sensor rated below $250{,}000$ PPS will drop packets on a 1 Gbps link, leading to **false negatives**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Centralized IDPS Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INET["INTERNET PERIMETER"]
        FW1["EDGE FIREWALL"]
    end
    subgraph DMZ_AREA["DMZ ZONE"]
        WEB1["WEB SERVER"]
        MAIL1["MAIL SERVER"]
    end
    subgraph CORE_NET["INTERNAL CORE NETWORK"]
        USR1["USER WORKSTATIONS"]
        DB1["DATABASE SERVER"]
        APP1["APP SERVER"]
    end
    subgraph CTRL_CENTER["CENTRAL MANAGEMENT CLUSTER"]
        SENSOR1["SENSOR NODE A"]
        SENSOR2["SENSOR NODE B"]
        SENSOR3["SENSOR NODE C"]
        MANAGER1["CENTRAL ANALYSIS MANAGER"]
        DBSTORE["EVENT DATABASE"]
        CONSOLE1["OPERATOR CONSOLE"]
    end
    FW1 -->|SPAN TAP| SENSOR1
    WEB1 -->|SPAN TAP| SENSOR2
    DB1 -->|SPAN TAP| SENSOR3
    SENSOR1 -->|RAW EVENTS| MANAGER1
    SENSOR2 -->|RAW EVENTS| MANAGER1
    SENSOR3 -->|RAW EVENTS| MANAGER1
    MANAGER1 -->|STORE| DBSTORE
    MANAGER1 -->|ALERT| CONSOLE1
    MANAGER1 -.->|BLOCK COMMAND| FW1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three sensors placed at three chokepoints (perimeter, DMZ, internal) all forward events to a single central manager. The manager correlates, stores, and alerts. The dashed line shows the *response* path.

## 4.2 Distributed IDPS Architecture (Mesh Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGION_A["REGION A"]
        SA["SENSOR A"]
        AA["ANALYZER A"]
    end
    subgraph REGION_B["REGION B"]
        SB["SENSOR B"]
        AB["ANALYZER B"]
    end
    subgraph REGION_C["REGION C"]
        SC["SENSOR C"]
        AC["ANALYZER C"]
    end
    SA --> AA
    SB --> AB
    SC --> AC
    AA <-->|PEER EXCHANGE| AB
    AB <-->|PEER EXCHANGE| AC
    AC <-->|PEER EXCHANGE| AA
    AA -->|LOCAL ALERT| OPA["OPERATOR A"]
    AB -->|LOCAL ALERT| OPB["OPERATOR B"]
    AC -->|LOCAL ALERT| OPC["OPERATOR C"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each region has its own sensor-analyzer pair that can issue *local* responses immediately. The analyzers also peer-exchange data for global correlation — **no single point of failure**.

## 4.3 Hierarchical (Tiered) IDPS Architecture — KTU Most-Frequent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TIER1["TIER 1 EDGE SENSORS"]
        T1A["SENSOR PERIMETER"]
        T1B["SENSOR DMZ"]
        T1C["SENSOR GUEST WIFI"]
    end
    subgraph TIER2["TIER 2 REGIONAL ANALYZERS"]
        T2A["REGIONAL ANALYZER NORTH"]
        T2B["REGIONAL ANALYZER SOUTH"]
    end
    subgraph TIER3["TIER 3 CENTRAL CORRELATION CENTER"]
        T3A["CENTRAL CORRELATION ENGINE"]
        T3B["GLOBAL EVENT WAREHOUSE"]
        T3C["SOC ANALYST CONSOLE"]
    end
    T1A --> T2A
    T1B --> T2A
    T1C --> T2B
    T2A -->|CORRELATED EVENTS| T3A
    T2B -->|CORRELATED EVENTS| T3A
    T3A --> T3B
    T3A --> T3C
    T3A -.->|POLICY UPDATE| T2A
    T3A -.->|POLICY UPDATE| T2B
```

**Reading the diagram:** Edge sensors (Tier 1) do fast local detection → regional analyzers (Tier 2) do correlation within a region → central engine (Tier 3) does global correlation. Policy updates flow *downward* (dashed lines).

## 4.4 Inline vs Passive Tapping — Architecture Choice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PASSIVE["PASSIVE IDS MODE"]
        P1["SWITCH"] -->|TRAFFIC| P2["DESTINATION"]
        P1 -.->|SPAN COPY| P3["SENSOR IDS"]
    end
    subgraph INLINE["INLINE IPS MODE"]
        I1["SOURCE"] -->|TRAFFIC| I2["SENSOR IPS"]
        I2 -->|IF SAFE| I3["DESTINATION"]
        I2 -.->|IF MALICIOUS| I4["DROP / BLOCK"]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** The IDS receives a *copy* — cannot block. The IPS sits *in-line* — can drop malicious packets before they reach the destination.

## 4.5 Hybrid Host + Network Architecture (Enterprise Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERIM["PERIMETER"]
        EDGEFW["EDGE FIREWALL"]
        NETSENS["NETWORK SENSOR"]
    end
    subgraph INTERNAL["INTERNAL SUBNET"]
        HOSTA["HOST A"]
        HA["HOST AGENT A"]
        HOSTB["HOST B"]
        HB["HOST AGENT B"]
        HOSTC["HOST C"]
        HC["HOST AGENT C"]
    end
    subgraph MGMT["MANAGEMENT TIER"]
        MGR["CENTRAL MANAGER + SIEM"]
        DB["EVENT CORRELATION DB"]
        UI["SOC DASHBOARD"]
    end
    EDGEFW --> NETSENS
    HOSTA --- HA
    HOSTB --- HB
    HOSTC --- HC
    NETSENS -->|NETWORK ALERTS| MGR
    HA -->|HOST EVENTS| MGR
    HB -->|HOST EVENTS| MGR
    HC -->|HOST EVENTS| MGR
    MGR --> DB
    MGR --> UI
```

**Reading the diagram:** Network sensor sees *wire-level* attacks (scans, DDoS). Host agents see *post-decryption* host activity (malware, file changes). The central manager correlates both — this is the **gold standard** KTU expects you to justify in 14-mark answers.

## 4.6 Data Flow Communication Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PULL["PULL MODEL"]
        PM1["MANAGER"] -->|POLL REQUEST| PS1["SENSOR"]
        PS1 -->|RESPONSE| PM1
    end
    subgraph PUSH["PUSH MODEL"]
        PU1["SENSOR"] -->|EVENT DRIVEN DATA| PU2["MANAGER"]
    end
    subgraph PUBSUB["PUBLISH SUBSCRIBE MODEL"]
        PB1["SENSOR"] -->|PUBLISH TOPIC| PB2["MESSAGE BUS KAFKA"]
        PB2 -->|SUBSCRIBE| PB3["MANAGER"]
        PB2 -->|SUBSCRIBE| PB4["SIEM"]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three communication paradigms — Pull (manager-initiated), Push (sensor-initiated), Pub/Sub (event bus, used in modern cloud IDPS).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**Q: Differentiate between host-based and network-based IDPS architectures. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Host-Based IDPS | Network-Based IDPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor location** | Installed on individual host | Placed on network segment (SPAN/TAP) |
| **Data source** | OS logs, system calls, file integrity, registry | Raw packet headers and payloads |
| **Visibility** | Sees *post-decryption* traffic | Sees *wire-level* traffic (even encrypted headers) |
| **Encrypted traffic** | Can inspect *decrypted* content | Cannot inspect encrypted payload |
| **Resource usage** | Consumes host CPU/RAM | Dedicated appliance, no host impact |
| **Deployment scale** | One per host (high count) | One per segment (lower count) |

**Advantage — Host-Based:** Can detect attacks *after* encryption is terminated, e.g., a host agent can detect a process behaving maliciously *inside* an SSL tunnel — a network sensor cannot.

**Advantage — Network-Based:** A single sensor can monitor traffic for **thousands of hosts** on a segment, making it economical and broad-reach.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**Q: What is a chokepoint in IDPS sensor placement? List any four canonical chokepoints.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **chokepoint** is a *network location through which all (or most) traffic of interest must pass*, making it the optimal place to install a sensor for maximum visibility with minimum sensors.

**Four canonical chokepoints:**

1. **Network Perimeter (just inside the firewall)** — captures all inbound traffic from the internet, including attacks.
2. **DMZ** — monitors traffic to and from public-facing servers (web, mail, DNS).
3. **Critical subnet gateway** (e.g., the switch port leading to the database VLAN) — catches lateral movement.
4. **WAN gateway / Inter-VLAN routing point** — captures east-west traffic between segments.

**Why it matters:** Placing a sensor *off* a chokepoint is the most common architectural mistake — the sensor sees nothing and the enterprise has a false sense of security.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 14-mark questions have **internal choice** — two alternative questions per slot. Solve **both styles** below for exam readiness.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**Q: Design a hierarchical (tiered) network IDPS architecture for a multi-campus university with three campuses. Justify sensor placement, describe the data flow between tiers, and compute the coverage ratio given the following data.**

- **Campus A:** 8 VLANs, sensors placed on 7
- **Campus B:** 6 VLANs, sensors placed on 6
- **Campus C:** 4 VLANs, sensors placed on 3

**(a) [7 Marks] — Architecture Design and Justification**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Choose architecture — Hierarchical (Tiered).**
Justification: A multi-campus university has *geographically distributed* networks that need *both* local fast response (e.g., a DDoS on Campus A) *and* global correlation (e.g., a coordinated attack across all three campuses). A purely centralized model would create a backbone bottleneck and a single point of failure; a purely distributed model would miss cross-campus correlation. A tiered model balances both.

**Step 2: Tier 1 — Edge sensors on each campus.**
- Campus A: 7 sensors (one per VLAN except blind spot)
- Campus B: 6 sensors (full coverage)
- Campus C: 3 sensors (one blind spot)

Each edge sensor uses **passive SPAN tapping** on the core switch of its VLAN, monitoring both ingress and egress.

**Step 3: Tier 2 — Regional (per-campus) analyzer.**
- One analyzer per campus aggregates events from its edge sensors.
- Performs *local correlation* and *fast local response* (e.g., block internal port scan).
- Reduces data sent to Tier 3 by filtering false positives locally.

**Step 4: Tier 3 — Central correlation engine.**
- Located at the main campus data center.
- Receives correlated events from all three regional analyzers.
- Performs *global correlation*, runs long-term analytics, manages signatures.
- Pushes policy updates *downward* to Tier 2 (dashed line in architecture diagram).

**Step 5: Communication model — Push + Pub/Sub hybrid.**
- Edge sensors *push* events to regional analyzer (event-driven).
- Regional analyzer *publishes* correlated events to a central message bus (e.g., Kafka); the central engine *subscribes*.

**Step 6: Failure mode — Fail-open at edge, fail-closed at Tier 3 critical links.**
- Edge sensors are fail-open (don't block legitimate traffic if sensor crashes).
- The central engine issues blocking commands via firewall API — but the firewall itself never fails closed because the central engine is only an *advisory* layer.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Coverage Ratio Calculation**

**Given data:**
- Campus A: $N_A = 8$ VLANs, monitored $M_A = 7$
- Campus B: $N_B = 6$ VLANs, monitored $M_B = 6$
- Campus C: $N_C = 4$ VLANs, monitored $M_C = 3$

**Total VLANs:**

$$
N_{total} = N_A + N_B + N_C = 8 + 6 + 4 = 18
$$

**Total monitored VLANs:**

$$
M_{total} = M_A + M_B + M_C = 7 + 6 + 3 = 16
$$

**Coverage Ratio:**

$$
C = \frac{M_{total}}{N_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{16}{18} \times 100 = 88.89\%
$$

**Per-campus coverage:**

- Campus A: $C_A = \frac{7}{8} \times 100 = 87.5\%$
- Campus B: $C_B = \frac{6}{6} \times 100 = 100\%$ (fully covered — strongest)
- Campus C: $C_C = \frac{3}{4} \times 100 = 75\%$ (weakest — needs additional sensor)

**Engineering recommendation:** Overall coverage $88.89\%$ is below the **$90\%$ enterprise benchmark**. Specifically, **Campus C's blind spot** (1 uncovered VLAN) should be addressed by adding a sensor on that VLAN, raising $M_C$ to 4 and overall coverage to $\frac{17}{18} \times 100 = 94.44\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2025 Expected Pattern]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] Compare centralized, distributed, and hierarchical IDPS architectures across scalability, fault tolerance, and correlation capability. State one real-world product for each.**

**(b) [7 Marks] An IDPS recorded the following 24-hour statistics: total events = 50,000; true attacks = 500; alerts raised = 600; of those alerts, 480 were real attacks. Compute FPR, FNR, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Comment on the system's reliability.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Comparison Table**

| Criterion | Centralized | Distributed | Hierarchical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scalability** | Limited — central manager becomes bottleneck | Excellent — no central bottleneck | Good — regional tier absorbs load |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Poor — manager is SPOF | Excellent — no SPOF | Moderate — only top tier is risk |
| **Correlation Capability** | Excellent (all data in one place) | Weak (data fragmented, peer-only) | Strong (regional + global) |
| **Management Complexity** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Best Fit** | Small/medium network | Large cloud / IoT / multi-tenant | Multi-campus / multi-tenant enterprise |

**Real-world products:**
- **Centralized** → *Sourcefire (now Cisco Firepower)* in single-manager mode.
- **Distributed** → *AWS GuardDuty* (cloud-native, region-scoped analyzers).
- **Hierarchical** → *Splunk Enterprise Security* feeding regional + global correlation.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Metric Computation**

**Step 1: Identify confusion matrix entries.**

- Total events = $50{,}000$
- Actual attacks: $TP + FN = 500$
- Alerts raised: $TP + FP = 600$
- $TP = 480$
- $FP = 600 - 480 = 120$
- $FN = 500 - 480 = 20$
- $TN = 50{,}000 - 600 - 20 = 49{,}380$

**Step 2: Compute FPR.**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{120}{120 + 49{,}380} = \frac{120}{49{,}500} \approx 0.00242
$$

$FPR \approx 0.242\%$

**Step 3: Compute FNR.**

$$
FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = \frac{20}{20 + 480} = \frac{20}{500} = 0.04
$$

$FNR = 4\%$

**Step 4: Compute Precision.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{480}{480 + 120} = \frac{480}{600} = 0.80
$$

$P = 80\%$

**Step 5: Compute Recall.**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{480}{480 + 20} = \frac{480}{500} = 0.96
$$

$R = 96\%$

**Step 6: Compute F1-Score.**

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{P \times R}{P + R} = 2 \times \frac{0.80 \times 0.96}{0.80 + 0.96}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{0.768}{1.76} = 2 \times 0.4364 \approx 0.873
$$

**Step 7: Reliability Commentary.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment for KTU answer:** The system has a **high recall of 96%** — it catches almost all attacks — and a **moderate precision of 80%** — about 1 in 5 alerts is a false alarm. The **F1-Score of 0.873** indicates a **reliable, well-balanced** IDPS. However, the FNR of 4% (20 missed attacks) is still concerning for a high-security environment. To improve, the threshold for raising alerts should be **tightened** (raising precision at the cost of slightly lowering recall), or a **second-stage analyzer** (e.g., behavioral ML) should be added to catch the $FN$ cases.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS** — examiners deduct **2 marks** if you mix up passive vs inline modes. *IDS = alerts only. IPS = can block.*
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — $FPR$ must be shown as a **percentage** or **ratio** explicitly. Leaving a bare decimal will cost **1 mark**.
> 3. **Drawing a diagram without labels** — unlabeled diagrams in 14-mark answers fetch **partial credit only**. Always label *sensors, managers, data flow direction, and chokepoints*.
> 4. **Using "centralized" and "distributed" interchangeably** — they are **opposite** extremes on the architectural spectrum. Hierarchical is the *middle ground*. Examiners flag this misuse instantly.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Justify" verb in design questions** — a design without justification is *incomplete*. Always tie every architectural choice to a **metric** (F1, MTTD, coverage, fault tolerance).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Network Architecture in IDPS** = the design framework of sensor placement + communication flow + analysis tier.
- **Three topologies:** Centralized (SPOF risk), Distributed (no SPOF, complex), Hierarchical (balanced — most common in enterprises).
- **Three sensor types:** Host-based (post-decryption visibility), Network-based (wire-level visibility), Hybrid (both — enterprise gold standard).
- **Chokepoint principle:** Place sensors where traffic *must* pass (perimeter, DMZ, inter-VLAN, WAN gateway, critical subnets).
- **IDS vs IPS:** IDS = passive (SPAN/TAP), IPS = inline (can block). Inline sensors need **fail-open** or **fail-closed** configuration.
- **Key metrics to memorize:** $FPR$, $FNR$, Precision, Recall, $F1\text{-Score}$, Coverage Ratio, MTTD, MTTR, Required PPS.
- **Communication models:** Pull (manager polls), Push (sensor sends), Pub/Sub (modern Kafka-based).
- **$F1 \to 1$** = excellent system; **$F1 \to 0$** = poor system. KTU benchmarks: **$F1 \geq 0.85$** is "production-grade".
- **Coverage benchmark:** Aim for $\geq 90\%$ subnets covered. Below that = architecturally weak.
- **Real-world mapping:** Snort (open-source), Suricata (high-speed), Cisco Firepower (enterprise), AWS GuardDuty (cloud).
- **Exam mantra for design questions:** *Topology → Justification → Sensor placement → Data flow → Failure mode → Metric evaluation.*
- **Common KTU buzzwords to use:** *chokepoint, fail-open, fail-closed, sensor, agent, analyzer, manager, correlation, single point of failure, defense in depth, layered defense.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agent Locations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Agent Locations

# 📍 AGENT LOCATIONS IN IDPS — DEPLOYMENT STRATEGY

---

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *In an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS), the **agent** (also called a **sensor** or **monitoring engine**) is the software/hardware component that collects data, analyses it for signs of intrusions, and (in prevention systems) takes action. The **Agent Location** refers to the **strategic logical and physical position** in a network architecture where the IDPS agent is placed so that it can monitor the right traffic, see the right data, and protect the right assets.*

In other words, *where* you place the agent is just as critical as *what* the agent does. An IDS/IPS is only as effective as the visibility of traffic at its deployment point.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🏢

> **Intuition (Plain English):**
> Think of your network as a **large office building** with many entry/exit points. A security guard (the **agent**) can stand:
> - At the **main gate** (perimeter) — sees everyone entering, but misses inside theft.
> - At every **floor corridor** (internal) — sees internal movement, but misses outside threats.
> - At the **vault door** (host-based) — only sees who tries to open the vault, but is highly specific.
>
> Just like in physical security, the **location** of the guard determines **what they can see, what they can stop, and what they miss**. The same logic applies to IDPS agents in a digital network.

### 1.3 Why Agent Location Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlights (KTU 2024 – CO4 / CO5 Mapping):**
> - Determines **visibility** of attack traffic.
> - Affects **false positive** and **false negative** rates.
> - Influences **performance overhead** on the monitored segment.
> - Impacts **scalability, latency, and response time**.
> - Decides whether the agent can act in **inline-blocking** (IPS) mode or only **passive-monitoring** (IDS) mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis of Agent Locations

## 2.1 Classification of IDPS Agent Locations

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4) categorises agent deployment into **four primary locations**, which can also be **combined into hybrid architectures**.

### 📌 2.1.1 Location Type 1 — Network-Based (NIDS / NIPS Agent)

The agent is **attached to a network segment** (via a tap, span port, or inline bridge). It analyses **packets flowing on the wire**.

| Sub-Location | Position in Architecture | Visibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Outside the firewall** | Internet ↔ Firewall | Sees all *inbound* attacks, scans, worms. |
| **In the DMZ** | Between Internet-facing firewall and internal firewall | Sees traffic to public servers (Web, Mail, DNS). |
| **Inside the firewall (Internal network)** | LAN/core switches | Sees **east-west** traffic, lateral movement, insider threats. |
| **Between firewall and IDS** | Inline with firewall | Full content inspection, can perform inline blocking. |

> **Use case:** Perimeter defence, server farm protection, segmentation enforcement.

### 📌 2.1.2 Location Type 2 — Host-Based (HIDS / HIPS Agent)

The agent is **installed on the individual host/end-point** (server, workstation, laptop). It analyses:
- System calls
- File-system changes
- Registry modifications
- Running processes
- Log files
- Network sockets opened by the host

> **Use case:** Detecting host-specific attacks (rootkits, keyloggers, file integrity violations, insider misuse).

### 📌 2.1.3 Location Type 3 — Wireless Agent Location

The agent is placed on **wireless access points (APs)** or **wireless controllers**. It analyses **802.11 frames** for:
- Rogue APs
- MAC spoofing
- Evil twin attacks
- De-authentication floods
- Wireless encryption downgrades

### 📌 2.1.4 Location Type 4 — Application-Layer / Hybrid Agent

The agent is **embedded inside the application or placed on the application server**. It understands **application protocol semantics** (HTTP, SQL, LDAP).
- Example: Web Application Firewall (WAF) inside an Apache/Nginx server.

---

## 2.2 Hybrid / Distributed Agent Architecture

Modern enterprises rarely rely on a single location. They deploy a **distributed multi-agent architecture** combining:

$$\text{Hybrid IDPS} = \text{Network Agent (Perimeter)} + \text{Host Agent (Servers)} + \text{Wireless Agent (APs)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 4 specifically stresses that *no single agent location can detect all types of intrusions*. A **defence-in-depth** strategy requires **multiple agents at multiple locations**, coordinated by a **Central Management Server (CMS)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Parameter | Network-Based (NIDS/NIPS) | Host-Based (HIDS/HIPS) | Wireless Agent | Application Agent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Network packets on the wire | System calls, logs, files | 802.11 frames | Application transactions |
| **Deployment Point** | Span port, tap, inline bridge | On the host OS | On AP / controller | Inside app or app server |
| **Best Detects** | Scans, worms, DoS, protocol abuse | Rootkits, file tampering, insider misuse | Rogue AP, MAC spoofing | SQLi, XSS, business-logic attacks |
| **Cannot Easily Detect** | Host-internal activity, encrypted payloads | Network-wide scans | Wired attacks | Network-layer attacks |
| **Performance Impact** | Low on host, high on sensor | Moderate on host | Low | High on app server |
| **Encrypted Traffic Visibility** | Limited (sees ciphertext only) | Good (sees post-decryption) | Limited | Good (post-TLS) |
| **Response Action** | Packet drop, TCP reset (IPS mode) | Kill process, quarantine file | De-auth client, block MAC | Block request, drop session |
| **Coverage Scope** | Broad (whole segment) | Narrow (one host) | Local (one SSID/BSS) | Very narrow (one app) |
| **Failure Impact** | Whole segment at risk | Only that host | Only that AP | Only that application |

---

## 2.4 The Four-Plane Agent Placement Model (KTU Preferred)

A clean way to remember agent locations is the **Four-Plane Model**:

| Plane | Network Location | Agent Role |
|---|---|---|
| **1. External / Perimeter Plane** | Outside the firewall, at ISP edge | First line of defence — detects mass scans, worms |
| **2. DMZ Plane** | Between outer & inner firewall (public servers) | Protects public-facing services |
| **3. Internal / Core Plane** | Inside the LAN, at core/distribution switches | Detects lateral movement, insider threats |
| **4. Host / Endpoint Plane** | On the end device itself | Last line — detects host-specific compromise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Examiners love this):** *"Defence in depth demands that each plane has at least one agent."*

---

## 2.5 Operational Considerations When Choosing a Location

When deciding *where* to place an agent, a KTU examiner expects these factors:

1. **Visibility of traffic** — Can the agent see the *interesting* packets?
2. **Stealth** — Should the agent be **stealthy** (invisible IP, no response to ping) to evade detection by attackers? Common for NIDS in perimeter plane.
3. **Bypass resistance** — Can an attacker route around the agent? (Inline IPS = no bypass; passive IDS = easily bypassed).
4. **Performance** — Inline agents add latency (typically 50–500 μs); agents on busy 10 Gbps links need hardware acceleration.
5. **Single point of failure** — Inline agents can become a bottleneck/failure point; passive agents do not.
6. **Encrypted traffic analysis** — Agents placed *after* TLS termination can see plaintext; agents before only see ciphertext.
7. **Cost of sensors** — A 10 Gbps tap is more expensive than a 1 Gbps tap.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — How to Choose the Right Agent Location

This section follows the **exam-style analytical reasoning** that KTU valuators look for in 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Decision Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the assets to protect.**
List servers, subnets, and the *sensitivity* of the data they hold.

**Step 2 — Map the traffic flow.**
Where does ingress traffic come from? Where does east-west traffic go? Where is the encrypted TLS terminated?

**Step 3 — Select candidate agent locations from the four planes.**
For each plane, decide whether you need a **Network-based** or **Host-based** agent.

**Step 4 — Decide mode: Passive (IDS) vs Inline (IPS).**
- Passive → place via SPAN/TAP; cannot block.
- Inline → place as a bridge/bump-in-the-wire; can drop packets.

**Step 5 — Evaluate failure-tolerance and latency budget.**

**Step 6 — Integrate with the Central Management Server (CMS).**
All agents report to a unified console for correlation.

## 3.2 Detailed Deployment Scenarios (Worked Examples)

### 📘 Scenario A — Web Server in a DMZ

**Setup:** Public Web server behind a firewall, in a DMZ.

- **Agent 1 (Network, in-line IPS, perimeter-plane):** Sits *between* the outer firewall and the Web server. Inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic, blocks SQLi/XSS, terminates inline.
- **Agent 2 (Host-based HIPS on the Web server):** Monitors the Web server process, file system, and logs. Detects rootkits, file tampering, privilege escalation.
- **Agent 3 (Application-layer WAF inside the Web app):** Inspects Layer-7 semantics, blocks business-logic attacks.

> **Total:** 3 agents, 3 planes, defence-in-depth.

### 📘 Scenario B — Enterprise Campus Network

- **Agent 1 (Network NIDS at perimeter):** Catches inbound scans and worms.
- **Agent 2 (Network NIDS at core switches, inside firewall):** Catches east-west lateral movement.
- **Agent 3 (Wireless sensor on each AP):** Catches rogue APs and evil-twin.
- **Agent 4 (HIDS on all servers):** Catches host-level compromise.
- **Central Management Server (CMS):** Correlates all four.

### 📘 Scenario C — Encrypted Traffic Environment

TLS 1.3 encrypts most traffic. Placement must account for this:

- **Option 1:** Place a **TLS-terminating proxy** at the network edge; place the network IPS **after** the proxy. Agent can inspect plaintext.
- **Option 2:** Use a **host-based agent** on the end-point — it sees plaintext after the OS has decrypted it.
- **Option 3:** Use **SSL/TLS fingerprinting** at the network agent — sees metadata (SNI, certificate, JA3 hash) only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Examination Trap:** Students often place a network IDS *before* TLS termination and claim it can inspect payloads. KTU valuation key deducts marks because the agent only sees ciphertext.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical / Analytical Reasoning (Coverage Formula)

A useful analytical tool for KTU 14-mark answers is the **Coverage Equation**:

$$C = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot A_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} A_j}$$

Where:
- $C$ = *Coverage Ratio* (between 0 and 1)
- $n$ = number of agent locations deployed
- $V_i$ = visibility factor of agent $i$ (0 = cannot see, 1 = fully sees)
- $A_i$ = traffic volume visible to agent $i$
- $m$ = total number of traffic segments
- $A_j$ = traffic volume of segment $j$

### Worked Numerical Example

> Suppose a network has **3 segments** with traffic volumes $A_1 = 40$ GB/day, $A_2 = 30$ GB/day, $A_3 = 30$ GB/day (total = 100 GB/day).
>
> The admin deploys 2 agents:
> - Agent-1 covers segment 1 fully: $V_1 = 1$, $A_1 = 40$
> - Agent-2 covers segment 3 fully: $V_2 = 1$, $A_3 = 30$
>
> Segment 2 is **uncovered**.

**Step 1 — Compute numerator (covered traffic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Covered} &= (V_1 \cdot A_1) + (V_2 \cdot A_3) \\
&= (1 \cdot 40) + (1 \cdot 30) \\
&= 40 + 30 \\
&= 70 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator (total traffic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= A_1 + A_2 + A_3 \\
&= 40 + 30 + 30 \\
&= 100 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Coverage Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{70}{100} \\
&= 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** $C = 0.70$ means **70% of network traffic** is monitored, leaving a **30% blind spot** in segment 2.

**Step 5 — Remediation:** Add a third agent in segment 2 to raise $C$ to 1.00.

> [!NOTE]
> This formula frequently appears as a sub-question in KTU 14-mark problems on agent placement strategy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four-Plane Agent Placement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Internet[Internet] -->|Inbound Traffic| FW1[Outer Firewall]
    FW1 -->|Filtered Traffic| DMZplane[DMZ Plane Agent NIPS]
    DMZplane --> DMZ[DMZ Public Servers]
    DMZ --> FW2[Inner Firewall]
    FW2 -->|Internal Traffic| Coreplane[Core Plane Agent NIDS]
    Coreplane --> LAN[Internal LAN]
    LAN --> HostAgent1[Host Agent HIDS on Server A]
    LAN --> HostAgent2[Host Agent HIDS on Server B]
    LAN --> AP[Wireless Access Point]
    AP --> WifiAgent[Wireless Agent on AP]
    AP --> Client[Wireless Client]
    HostAgent1 --> AppAgent[Application Layer Agent]
    DMZplane -.Reports to.-> CMS[Central Management Server]
    Coreplane -.Reports to.-> CMS
    HostAgent1 -.Reports to.-> CMS
    HostAgent2 -.Reports to.-> CMS
    WifiAgent -.Reports to.-> CMS
    AppAgent -.Reports to.-> CMS
    CMS --> Admin[Security Administrator Console]
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - Four planes (DMZ, Core, Host, Wireless) all have agents.
> - Each agent reports to a **Central Management Server (CMS)**.
> - The CMS correlates events and presents them to the security admin.

---

## 4.2 Inline vs Passive Agent Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Passive_Mode[Passive IDS Mode]
        direction LR
        P_Traffic[Network Traffic] --> P_Switch[Switch]
        P_Switch -->|SPAN/TAP copy| P_Agent[IDS Agent]
        P_Agent --> P_Alerts[Alerts to CMS]
        P_Switch --> P_Continue[Traffic continues to host]
    end

    subgraph Inline_Mode[Inline IPS Mode]
        direction LR
        I_Traffic[Network Traffic] --> I_Agent[IPS Agent]
        I_Agent -->|Inspect| I_Allow[Allow / Forward]
        I_Agent -->|Block| I_Drop[Drop / Reset]
        I_Allow --> I_Continue[Traffic continues]
    end
```

> **Key insight:**
> - **Passive (IDS):** Traffic is *copied* to the agent. The agent cannot block, only alert. Failure of the agent has no network impact.
> - **Inline (IPS):** Traffic physically *passes through* the agent. The agent can drop packets in real time. Failure can disrupt traffic unless the IPS has a fail-open or fail-closed mode.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing an Agent Location

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: What to protect?]) --> Q1{Asset is a network segment?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need to block traffic?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Deploy Network IPS - Inline]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4[Deploy Network IDS - Passive TAP]
    Q1 -->|No| Q5{Asset is a single host?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Deploy Host based IPS on the host]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7{Asset is wireless network?}
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q8[Deploy Wireless Agent on AP Controller]
    Q7 -->|No| Q9{Asset is a web application?}
    Q9 -->|Yes| Q10[Deploy Application Layer Agent WAF]
    Q9 -->|No| End([Re-evaluate asset classification])
    Q3 --> Central[Report to Central Management Server]
    Q4 --> Central
    Q6 --> Central
    Q8 --> Central
    Q10 --> Central
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List and briefly explain any three common agent locations in an IDPS deployment.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Network-Based Agent (NIDS/NIPS):** Deployed at strategic network points (perimeter, DMZ, core). Monitors packets flowing on the wire using SPAN ports, network TAPs, or inline bridges. Best for detecting scans, worms, and protocol-based attacks. *(1 mark)*

2. **Host-Based Agent (HIDS/HIPS):** Installed on individual hosts/servers. Monitors system calls, log files, file integrity, and process activity. Best for detecting rootkits, file tampering, and insider misuse. *(1 mark)*
