3. **Wireless Agent:** Placed on wireless access points or wireless LAN controllers. Analyses 802.11 frames for rogue APs, MAC spoofing, and de-authentication attacks. *(1 mark)*

> *(Examiner's note: Mentioning the fourth type – Application-Layer Agent – earns a half-mark bonus.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between passive (IDS) and inline (IPS) agent placement. State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive IDS Agent | Inline IPS Agent |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic flow | Traffic is *copied* to the agent | Traffic *passes through* the agent |
| Blocking | Cannot block; only alerts | Can drop / reset packets |
| Failure impact | No network impact | Can disrupt traffic |
| **Advantage** | Cannot become a single point of failure | Can *prevent* attacks in real time |

*(1 mark for difference, 1 mark each for the two advantages — total 3 marks.)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-plane agent placement model** in an enterprise IDPS architecture. List the role of the agent in each plane. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **factors that influence the choice of an agent location** in a modern enterprise network. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Four-Plane Agent Placement Model — 7 marks**

The four-plane model organises IDPS agent placement by *where in the network architecture* the agent sits. Each plane addresses a different class of threats.

**Plane 1 — External / Perimeter Plane**
- *Position:* Just inside the ISP edge, before the outer firewall.
- *Agent type:* Network NIDS in stealth mode.
- *Role:* Detect mass internet scans, worms, DDoS reconnaissance. *Advantage:* Sees the *raw* attacker traffic before any firewall filtering.

**Plane 2 — DMZ Plane**
- *Position:* Between the outer and inner firewall, in front of public servers (Web, Mail, DNS).
- *Agent type:* Inline Network IPS.
- *Role:* Inspect HTTP/HTTPS/SMTP, block application-layer attacks on public servers.

**Plane 3 — Internal / Core Plane**
- *Position:* At core/distribution switches inside the LAN.
- *Agent type:* Network NIDS via SPAN port.
- *Role:* Detect *east-west* lateral movement, internal recon, insider threats.

**Plane 4 — Host / Endpoint Plane**
- *Position:* On the end-host itself.
- *Agent type:* Host-based HIDS/HIPS.
- *Role:* Detect host-level compromise (rootkits, file tampering, privilege escalation).

> All four planes report events to a **Central Management Server (CMS)** which correlates and alerts the security team.

*(Block diagram: refer to Section 4.1 of these notes — the four-plane mermaid architecture. 1 mark for diagram + 1 mark each for 4 plane descriptions = 5 marks; 1 mark for CMS correlation = 6 marks; 1 mark for conclusion/synthesis = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Factors Influencing Agent Location — 7 marks**

1. **Traffic visibility** — The agent must be placed where the *interesting* traffic actually flows. *(1 mark)*
2. **Stealth requirement** — Perimeter agents are often deployed with no IP address to evade attacker detection. *(1 mark)*
3. **Mode of operation (inline vs passive)** — If prevention (drop/reset) is required, the agent must be inline. *(1 mark)*
4. **Bypass resistance** — Attackers should not be able to route around the agent. Inline placements resist bypass better. *(1 mark)*
5. **Performance / latency budget** — Inline agents add latency; high-throughput links need hardware-accelerated sensors. *(1 mark)*
6. **Encrypted traffic analysis** — Agent must be placed *after* TLS termination if it is to inspect payloads. *(1 mark)*
7. **Failure tolerance** — Passive agents cannot become a single point of failure; inline agents must support fail-open/fail-closed. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **write only 3–4 factors** and lose up to **4 marks**.
> - Students often **forget the encryption factor** entirely — this is a KTU favourite.
> - Avoid generic answers like *"security is important"*; examiners want **specific, technical** factors (e.g., latency, bypass resistance, SPAN-port capacity).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Network-based** and **Host-based** IDPS agent locations under the following heads: data source, deployment, attack types detected, encrypted traffic visibility, and failure impact. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An enterprise has three network segments with traffic volumes **40 GB/day, 30 GB/day, and 30 GB/day**. The admin deploys 2 agents — the first agent covers segment 1 fully, and the second agent covers segment 3 fully. Segment 2 has no agent. Calculate the **Coverage Ratio** and suggest a remediation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Comparison Table — 7 marks**

| Head | Network-Based Agent | Host-Based Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **Data source** | Packets on the wire | System calls, logs, file changes |
| **Deployment** | SPAN port, TAP, or inline bridge | Installed on host OS |
| **Attack types detected** | Scans, worms, DoS, protocol abuse | Rootkits, file tampering, insider misuse |
| **Encrypted traffic** | Limited (sees ciphertext) | Good (sees post-decryption plaintext) |
| **Failure impact** | Whole segment at risk (if inline) or no impact (if passive) | Only that host is affected |

*(1 mark per head × 5 heads = 5 marks; 1 mark for introductory framing; 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

**(b) Coverage Ratio Calculation — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Segment 1: $A_1 = 40$ GB/day, Agent-1 covers it fully ($V_1 = 1$)
- Segment 2: $A_2 = 30$ GB/day, **no agent** ($V_2 = 0$)
- Segment 3: $A_3 = 30$ GB/day, Agent-2 covers it fully ($V_2 = 1$)

**Step 1 — Numerator (covered traffic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Covered} &= (V_1 \cdot A_1) + (0 \cdot A_2) + (V_2 \cdot A_3) \\
&= (1 \cdot 40) + 0 + (1 \cdot 30) \\
&= 70 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator (total traffic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 40 + 30 + 30 \\
&= 100 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Coverage Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{70}{100} \\
&= 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Coverage = 70%, meaning **30% of network traffic is unmonitored** (a blind spot exists in segment 2).

**Step 5 — Remediation:**
Deploy a third network-based agent (NIDS in passive mode, or NIPS in inline mode) in segment 2. This will raise $C$ from 0.70 to **1.00 (100% coverage)**.

**Step 6 — Engineering recommendation:**
The new agent should be placed at the core switch of segment 2, connected via a SPAN port (passive IDS) to avoid introducing a new single point of failure.

*(2 marks for numerator, 1 mark for denominator, 1 mark for ratio, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for remediation, 1 mark for engineering recommendation = 7 marks.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to multiply $V$ with $A$ in the numerator is the **most common mistake** (loses 2 marks).
> - Writing the answer as *"70%"* without showing the **3-step derivation** loses 1–2 marks for *working*.
> - Skipping the **remediation step** loses another mark. Examiners specifically look for *engineering judgment*, not just arithmetic.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorise):**

- ✅ An **IDPS agent** = sensor/monitoring engine; its **location** determines what it can see and stop.
- ✅ **Four primary locations:** Network, Host, Wireless, Application.
- ✅ **Four planes** of placement: Perimeter, DMZ, Core/Internal, Host/Endpoint.
- ✅ **Passive IDS** uses SPAN/TAP (no blocking, no single-point-of-failure).
- ✅ **Inline IPS** uses a bridge (can block, can become a bottleneck).
- ✅ **HIDS** is best for *host-internal* threats (rootkits, file tampering).
- ✅ **NIDS** is best for *network-wide* threats (scans, worms, DoS).
- ✅ **Wireless agents** detect rogue APs, MAC spoofing, evil twins.
- ✅ **Application agents (WAFs)** detect SQLi, XSS, business-logic attacks.
- ✅ **Encrypted traffic** — place agent *after* TLS termination or use host-based agent.
- ✅ **Stealth** perimeter agents have no IP address to evade detection.
- ✅ **Coverage formula** $C = \frac{\sum V_i A_i}{\sum A_j}$ quantifies how much traffic is monitored.
- ✅ **Defence-in-depth** = multiple agents at multiple planes, all reporting to a **Central Management Server (CMS)**.
- ✅ No single agent location detects *all* attack types → **hybrid architecture is mandatory** in modern enterprises.
- ✅ KTU 14-mark answers must include a **diagram** (use a labelled block diagram even if not asked).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Host Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Host Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Host Architectures** in the context of Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS) refer to the structural and deployment frameworks through which security monitoring, detection, and prevention capabilities are integrated directly into individual computing endpoints (hosts). A *host-based IDPS* (HIDPS) operates by monitoring the internal state and behavior of a single computing system, including its operating system processes, system calls, file system integrity, registry modifications, network traffic originating from or terminating at the host, and application-level logs.

According to the **NIST SP 800-94 (Guide to Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems)** standard, host-based IDPS monitors characteristics of a single host for suspicious activity. The host architecture encompasses the **agent placement model**, the **data collection mechanism**, the **analysis engine**, the **storage subsystem**, and the **management/console communication channel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Host Architectures form the foundational deployment model where detection logic resides *on the monitored host itself*, as opposed to Network-based Architectures (NIDPS) where traffic is analyzed at network choke points. This module is critical for understanding endpoint-level visibility.

### 1.2 Core Architectural Components

A host-based IDPS architecture typically comprises the following functional building blocks:

1. **Sensor / Agent Module** — The on-host data collector.
2. **Analysis Engine** — The decision-making logic (signature, anomaly, or stateful).
3. **Storage / Knowledge Base** — Signature database, behavioral baseline, and log retention.
4. **Response Module** — Active (blocking) or passive (alerting) reaction.
5. **Management Console** — Centralized administration and reporting interface.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Building Security Guard" Model**
>
> Imagine a corporate office building. A *Network-based IDPS* is like the **CCTV cameras and metal detectors at the main entrance** — they watch everyone who tries to enter or leave the building as a whole.
>
> A *Host-based IDPS* is like assigning a **personal security guard to each individual employee's desk**. This guard knows:
> - Which files the employee is allowed to touch (file integrity monitoring).
> - The normal rhythm of keystrokes, mouse clicks, and application usage (behavioral baseline).
> - Who is allowed to enter *that specific room* (system call authorization).
> - The moment a stranger sits at the desk, even if they bypassed the front entrance.
>
> Just as the personal guard can detect insider threats the entrance cameras miss, the **Host Architecture** detects threats that have already penetrated the network perimeter — insider attacks, malware execution, privilege escalation, and ransomware encryption patterns.

### 1.4 Standard Classification Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Deployment Granularity | **Per-host (endpoint)** |
| Visibility Scope | **OS layer + Application layer + Storage layer** |
| Data Source Cardinality | Typically **10² – 10⁴ events/second/host** |
| Encrypted Traffic Visibility | **Full visibility (post-decryption at host)** |
| Resource Footprint | **1–5% CPU, 100–500 MB RAM per agent** |

### 1.5 Importance in Modern Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern enterprise networks witness **~70% of breaches** originating at endpoints (per industry reports). Host architectures provide the *last line of defense* and offer forensic evidence (process trees, file timestamps, memory dumps) that network sensors cannot capture, especially in **encrypted traffic** scenarios where the payload is opaque to NIDPS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Host Architectures

Host IDPS architectures are classified along **three primary axes**:

#### Axis A: Agent Deployment Topology
- **Agent-Based Architecture** — A persistent daemon/service runs inside the host OS.
- **Agentless Architecture** — Monitoring is performed remotely via standard protocols (WMI, SSH, SNMP) without installing a software agent.

#### Axis B: Detection Methodology
- **Signature-Based (Misuse Detection)** — Pattern matching against known attack signatures.
- **Anomaly-Based Detection** — Statistical deviation from established baseline behavior.
- **State-Based / Specification-Based** — Monitoring transitions in system state machines.

#### Axis C: Operational Stance
- **Passive (HIDS — Detection Only)** — Alerts generated, no traffic blocking.
- **Active / Inline (HIPS — Prevention)** — Can terminate processes, quarantine files, drop packets, or roll back transactions.

### 2.2 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The host-based IDPS execution pipeline operates as follows:

1. **Event Acquisition Phase** — The agent hooks into OS audit subsystems (e.g., Linux `auditd`, Windows ETW — Event Tracing for Windows) and intercepts:
   - System calls (`syscall` trace)
   - File I/O operations (`open`, `write`, `unlink`)
   - Process creation/termination events
   - Network socket operations (`connect`, `bind`, `accept`)
   - Registry key modifications (Windows)
   - Privilege escalation events (`setuid`, `sudo`)

2. **Pre-processing & Normalization** — Raw events are converted into a canonical schema (e.g., Common Event Format — CEF, or IDMEF — Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format).

3. **Analysis Engine Invocation** — Normalized events are passed through:
   - Signature matcher (Aho-Corasick or Boyer-Moore string matching)
   - Anomaly scorer (statistical model)
   - State machine validator

4. **Correlation & Scoring** — Multiple weak signals are aggregated into a single incident with a severity score.

5. **Response Decision** — Action is taken based on policy:
   - **Log only** → severity 1–3
   - **Alert** → severity 4–6
   - **Active block** → severity 7–10

6. **Reporting & Forwarding** — Alerts forwarded to SIEM via syslog, CEF, or proprietary channels.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why hook into the OS kernel audit subsystem?** Because the kernel is the *only* component that sees *all* privileged operations. Userspace monitoring misses kernel-level rootkits.
- **Why normalize events?** Heterogeneous sources (Linux audit logs, Windows ETW, application logs) must share a common schema for correlation.
- **Why use multiple detection methods?** Signature-based catches known attacks with low false positives; anomaly-based catches zero-days but with higher false positives. Hybrid detection balances both.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Rate (Recall) | $\text{DR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Fraction of actual intrusions detected |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}}$ | Fraction of benign events misclassified as attacks |
| Precision | $\text{P} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Reliability of an alert when raised |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| Anomaly Score (Mahalanobis Distance) | $D_M = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)}$ | Distance of observation $x$ from baseline distribution with mean $\mu$ and covariance $\Sigma$ |
| ROC Curve AUC | $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$ | Aggregate detection performance metric |
| Agent Overhead | $O_h = \frac{C_{\text{idps}}}{C_{\text{host}}} \times 100\%$ | CPU/RAM cost as percent of host capacity |
| File Integrity Hash | $h = \text{SHA-256}(F)$ | Cryptographic fingerprint of file $F$ |
| Baseline Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Average of $n$ behavioral samples |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Spread of behavioral samples |
| Anomaly Threshold | $T = \bar{x} + k\sigma$ | Observation exceeding $T$ is anomalous ($k \geq 2$ typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Note:** All table cells use \vert or written-out text for absolute-value notation. No raw \vert pipe characters that would break the markdown table parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Host architectures are foundational in:
- **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** platforms (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint)
- **Compliance regimes** — PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOX mandate host-level file integrity monitoring (FIM).
- **Insider threat detection** — Only host sensors can correlate a user's *behavior on a specific endpoint*.
- **Cloud workload protection** — Host agents running inside cloud VMs provide visibility into east-west traffic that network sensors cannot inspect due to provider abstractions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Anomaly Detection Threshold for Host Behavior

Suppose a host IDPS agent monitors the number of file system modifications per hour performed by the `svchost.exe` process during a training (baseline) period of $n = 30$ days.

**Step 1 — Collect the baseline sample.**
Let the observed values be $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (one value per day).

**Step 2 — Compute the arithmetic mean (expected baseline rate).**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation (behavioral variance).**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the anomaly threshold using the $k$-sigma rule.**
For a confidence level corresponding to $k = 3$ (i.e., $\approx 99.7\%$ for a Gaussian distribution):

$$
T = \bar{x} + k \cdot \sigma
$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule for any new observation $x_{\text{new}}$.**

$$
\text{Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{ALERT (Anomaly)} & \text{if } x_{\text{new}} > T \\
\text{Normal} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose the 30 baseline observations of `svchost.exe` file modifications per hour yield:

$$
\bar{x} = 12 \text{ modifications/hour}, \quad \sigma = 2.5 \text{ modifications/hour}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the threshold for $k = 3$.

$$
T = 12 + 3 \times 2.5 = 12 + 7.5 = 19.5 \text{ modifications/hour}
$$

**Step 2:** A new observation $x_{\text{new}} = 24$ modifications/hour is measured.

**Step 3:** Compare with the threshold.

$$
x_{\text{new}} = 24 > T = 19.5 \implies \text{Anomaly Detected}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the anomaly score (z-score).

$$
z = \frac{x_{\text{new}} - \bar{x}}{\sigma} = \frac{24 - 12}{2.5} = 4.8
$$

A $z$-score of **4.8** lies well beyond the $\pm 3\sigma$ envelope, indicating a statistically significant deviation — likely a malware-induced mass file encryption event (ransomware indicator).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Trade-offs

| Parameter | Agent-Based | Agentless |
|---|---|---|
| Visibility Depth | **Deep (kernel-level)** | Shallow (protocol-level only) |
| Encryption Visibility | **Full** | None |
| Deployment Effort | High (per-host install) | Low (central push) |
| Host Performance Impact | $1\% - 5\%$ | $< 1\%$ |
| Insider Threat Detection | **Strong** | Weak |
| Resistance to Tampering | Medium (rootkits may blind) | High (agent outside host) |
| Scalability | Limited by agent management | High |
| Cost | Higher (licensing per host) | Lower |

### 3.4 Detection Performance Derivation

For a host IDPS evaluated over a test corpus of $N$ total events:

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}
$$

$$
\text{Miss Rate} = \frac{\text{FN}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = 1 - \text{Recall}
$$

$$
\text{Error Rate} = \frac{\text{FP} + \text{FN}}{N}
$$

**Example computation:** Suppose an HIDS evaluates 10,000 events. It correctly identifies 950 intrusions (TP), misses 50 (FN), issues 200 false alarms (FP), and correctly clears 8,800 benign events (TN).

Compute **Precision**:

$$
P = \frac{950}{950 + 200} = \frac{950}{1150} \approx 0.8261
$$

Compute **Recall**:

$$
R = \frac{950}{950 + 50} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95
$$

Compute **F1-Score**:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8261 \times 0.95}{0.8261 + 0.95} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7848}{1.7761} \approx 0.8837
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An F1-Score of **0.8837** is considered a strong detection performance for a host-based intrusion prevention system. KTU examiners frequently test this metric — remember the formula and the substitution steps.

### 3.5 Derivation — File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) Hash Comparison

The FIM subsystem stores cryptographic baselines of critical files. For each monitored file $F_i$:

**Step 1 — Baseline phase (offline):**

$$
h_i^{\text{baseline}} = \text{SHA-256}(F_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Runtime phase (periodic rescan):**

$$
h_i^{\text{current}} = \text{SHA-256}(F_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Integrity verdict:**

$$
\text{Verdict} =
\begin{cases}
\text{INTACT} & \text{if } h_i^{\text{baseline}} = h_i^{\text{current}} \\
\text{TAMPERED} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This deterministic check forms the cryptographic backbone of compliance-mandated FIM (PCI-DSS Requirement 11.5).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Host IDPS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HOST OPERATING SYSTEM] --> B[KERNEL AUDIT SUBSYSTEM]
    B --> C[EVENT COLLECTOR AGENT]
    C --> D[PRE-PROCESSOR AND NORMALIZER]
    D --> E[ANALYSIS ENGINE]
    E --> F[SIGNATURE MATCHER]
    E --> G[ANOMALY SCORER]
    E --> H[STATE MACHINE VALIDATOR]
    F --> I[CORRELATION AND SCORING MODULE]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[POLICY DECISION POINT]
    J --> K[RESPONSE MODULE]
    K --> L[LOG ONLY]
    K --> M[ALERT CONSOLE]
    K --> N[ACTIVE BLOCK]
    I --> O[STORAGE AND KNOWLEDGE BASE]
    P[MANAGEMENT CONSOLE] --> E
    P --> J
    O --> E
```

### 4.2 Agent-Based vs Agentless Deployment Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AgentBased[AGENT BASED HOST]
        A1[Application Layer] --> A2[Agent Daemon]
        A2 --> A3[Kernel Audit Hooks]
        A3 --> A4[Local Decision Engine]
        A4 --> A5[Response Action]
    end
    subgraph Agentless[AGENTLESS HOST]
        B1[Application Layer] --> B2[Remote Poller]
        B2 --> B3[WMI or SSH Protocol]
        B3 --> B4[Central Analysis Server]
        B4 --> B5[Response Action]
    end
    MC[MANAGEMENT CONSOLE] --> A2
    MC --> B2
```

### 4.3 Detection Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Event Arrives] --> Q{Event Type?}
    Q -->|Known Pattern| S[Signature Database Lookup]
    Q -->|Behavioral Data| A[Compare With Baseline]
    Q -->|State Change| T[State Machine Transition Check]
    S --> S1{Match Found?}
    S1 -->|Yes| AL[Generate Alert with Severity]
    S1 -->|No| D[Discard]
    A --> A1{Score Above Threshold?}
    A1 -->|Yes| AL
    A1 -->|No| D
    T --> T1{Valid Transition?}
    T1 -->|No| AL
    T1 -->|Yes| D
    AL --> RP[Response Module]
    RP --> RP1{Policy Action}
    RP1 --> P1[Log]
    RP1 --> P2[Alert Admin]
    RP1 --> P3[Block Process]
    RP1 --> P4[Quarantine File]
```

### 4.4 Host IDPS Interaction with OS Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserSpace[USER SPACE LAYER]
        U1[Applications]
        U2[Shell Commands]
        U3[User Files]
    end
    subgraph AgentLayer[IDPS AGENT LAYER]
        AG1[File Integrity Monitor]
        AG2[Process Behavior Tracker]
        AG3[Registry Watcher]
        AG4[Network Socket Monitor]
    end
    subgraph KernelSpace[KERNEL SPACE LAYER]
        K1[System Call Interface]
        K2[Process Scheduler]
        K3[VFS Layer]
        K4[Network Stack]
    end
    U1 --> K1
    U2 --> K1
    U3 --> K3
    K1 --> AG2
    K3 --> AG1
    K4 --> AG4
    AG1 --> CONS[Management Console]
    AG2 --> CONS
    AG3 --> CONS
    AG4 --> CONS
```

### 4.5 Response Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EVT[Detected Event] --> SEV{Severity Score}
    SEV -->|1 to 3| LOW[Log to Local File]
    SEV -->|4 to 6| MID[Forward Alert to SIEM]
    SEV -->|7 to 9| HIGH[Terminate Process and Alert]
    SEV -->|10| CRIT[Quarantine Host and Notify]
    LOW --> DONE[End]
    MID --> DONE
    HIGH --> DONE
    CRIT --> DONE
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Data Path)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[HOST EVENT SOURCES] --> COL[EVENT COLLECTOR]
    COL --> NORM[NORMALIZATION LAYER]
    NORM --> QUE[EVENT QUEUE]
    QUE --> ENG[ANALYSIS ENGINE CLUSTER]
    ENG --> SIG[SIGNATURE SUBSYSTEM]
    ENG --> ANO[ANOMALY SUBSYSTEM]
    ENG --> STA[STATEFUL SUBSYSTEM]
    SIG --> COR[CORRELATION LAYER]
    ANO --> COR
    STA --> COR
    COR --> DB[(KNOWLEDGE BASE)]
    COR --> DEC[DECISION POLICY ENGINE]
    DEC --> RSP[RESPONSE ACTUATOR]
    RSP --> HOST[HOST CONTAINMENT]
    RSP --> NET[NETWORK ISOLATION]
    RSP --> ADM[ADMIN NOTIFICATION]
    DEC --> LOG[SECURE AUDIT LOG]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Define Host-Based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS). List any four components of a HIDS architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Host-Based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS) is a security mechanism that monitors and analyzes the internals of a single computing system — including its operating system processes, system calls, file system changes, and application logs — to detect malicious or policy-violating activity.

Four components of HIDS architecture:
1. **Sensor / Agent** — Collects host events from OS audit subsystems.
2. **Analysis Engine** — Performs signature-based, anomaly-based, or stateful analysis.
3. **Storage / Knowledge Base** — Holds signatures, baselines, and historical events.
4. **Response Module** — Generates alerts or performs active prevention (in HIPS mode).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components — 0.5 each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between Agent-Based and Agentless Host IDPS architectures. Give one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Agent-Based | Agentless |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Software installed on host | No host software required |
| Data Collection | Kernel-level hooks | Remote query (WMI, SSH, SNMP) |
| Visibility | Deep OS-level | Shallow (protocol-level) |

- **Advantage of Agent-Based:** Provides full visibility into encrypted traffic and kernel operations.
- **Advantage of Agentless:** Easier to deploy and manage at scale; no host performance impact.

*(Award 1 mark per row + 0.5 each for advantages.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **block-level functional architecture of a Host-Based Intrusion Prevention System (HIPS)** with a neat diagram. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **file integrity monitoring (FIM)** mechanism used in host architectures. Explain how SHA-256 hashing is used to detect tampering. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HIPS Block Architecture (7 Marks):**

The HIPS architecture is organized into the following functional blocks:

1. **Event Source Layer** — The OS audit subsystem, file system watcher, process monitor, and network socket monitor generate raw security events.
2. **Event Collector & Pre-processor** — Gathers raw events and normalizes them into a canonical format (e.g., CEF or IDMEF).
3. **Analysis Engine** — Performs three parallel analyses:
   - **Signature matching** for known attack patterns.
   - **Anomaly scoring** against behavioral baselines.
   - **Stateful inspection** of process and network state transitions.
4. **Correlation & Scoring Module** — Aggregates multiple weak signals into a single high-confidence incident.
5. **Decision Policy Engine** — Maps severity scores to response actions based on operator-defined rules.
6. **Response Actuator** — Executes the chosen action: logging, alerting, process termination, file quarantine, or host isolation.
7. **Management Console** — Provides centralized policy deployment, alert visualization, and forensic reporting.
8. **Knowledge Base** — Persistent storage of signatures, baselines, and audit history.

*(Award 1 mark per major block, 0.5 marks for supporting description; balance marks for diagram quality.)*

---

**(b) File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) Using SHA-256 (7 Marks):**

FIM is a host-based detection technique that detects unauthorized modification of critical files (system binaries, configuration files, executables).

**Phase 1 — Baseline Establishment (Offline):**
During deployment, the HIPS computes a cryptographic hash of every monitored file using the SHA-256 algorithm.

$$
h_i^{\text{baseline}} = \text{SHA-256}(F_i)
$$

These hashes are stored in a tamper-resistant database (often signed with a separate HMAC key).

**Phase 2 — Runtime Verification:**
At scheduled intervals (e.g., hourly), the agent recomputes the hash of each monitored file:

$$
h_i^{\text{current}} = \text{SHA-256}(F_i)
$$

**Phase 3 — Comparison & Verdict:**
The two hashes are compared bit-by-bit. Any mismatch triggers a tampering alert.

**Why SHA-256?**
- Produces a **256-bit** fixed-length output regardless of input size.
- Has **strong collision resistance** — finding two files with the same SHA-256 hash is computationally infeasible ($\approx 2^{128}$ operations).
- Deterministic and one-way.

**Example:** If `/etc/passwd` baseline hash is `5e8b3c...` and runtime hash is `7a1f4d...`, the HIPS raises a **CRITICAL — File Tampered** alert with severity 9.

**Limitations:**
- Does not detect attacks in real-time — only at next scan.
- Vulnerable if baseline database itself is compromised (mitigated by write-once storage or external baseline server).

*(Award 2 marks for baseline phase explanation, 2 marks for runtime verification, 2 marks for SHA-256 properties, 1 mark for limitations.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **detection methodologies** used in Host IDPS — Signature-Based, Anomaly-Based, and Stateful Inspection. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A HIDS records **950 true positives, 50 false negatives, 200 false positives, and 8,800 true negatives** over a test window of 10,000 events. Compute the **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy**. Comment on the detection quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detection Methodologies (7 Marks):**

**1. Signature-Based Detection:**
Maintains a database of known attack patterns (signatures). Each incoming event is matched against these patterns using algorithms like Aho-Corasick.
- *Advantage:* Very low false positive rate; high precision.
- *Limitation:* Cannot detect zero-day attacks; signature database must be constantly updated.

**2. Anomaly-Based Detection:**
Builds a statistical or machine-learning model of normal host behavior (e.g., CPU usage, file modification rate, network connections). Deviations beyond a threshold ($T = \bar{x} + k\sigma$) are flagged.
- *Advantage:* Can detect novel and zero-day attacks.
- *Limitation:* Higher false positive rate; requires a clean training period.

**3. Stateful Inspection:**
Monitors the host's state transitions (e.g., process states: NEW → RUNNING → TERMINATED; network connections: SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT). Any illegal transition triggers an alert.
- *Advantage:* Detects protocol-level and process-level anomalies that signatures miss.
- *Limitation:* State table can be large; complex to maintain at high event rates.

**Hybrid Detection:** Most production-grade HIPS use a combination of all three to balance precision and recall.

*(Award 2 marks each for the three methods, 1 mark for hybrid note.)*

---

**(b) Performance Metrics Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given:
- $\text{TP} = 950$
- $\text{FN} = 50$
- $\text{FP} = 200$
- $\text{TN} = 8{,}800$
- $N = 10{,}000$

**Step 1 — Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \frac{950}{950 + 200} = \frac{950}{1150} \approx 0.8261
$$

**Step 2 — Recall (Detection Rate):**

$$
R = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{950}{950 + 50} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95
$$

**Step 3 — F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8261 \times 0.95}{0.8261 + 0.95}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7848}{1.7761} \approx 2 \cdot 0.4419 \approx 0.8837
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{N} = \frac{950 + 8800}{10000} = \frac{9750}{10000} = 0.975
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Detection Quality:**
- Recall of **95%** indicates the HIDS detects the vast majority of intrusions — excellent.
- Precision of **82.6%** is good, though the 200 false positives may cause alert fatigue.
- F1-Score of **0.8837** confirms strong overall performance.
- Accuracy of **97.5%** is high but can be misleading in imbalanced datasets (intrusion events are rare).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse the **denominator** in Precision (TP+FP) vs Recall (TP+FN). Memorize the formula triangle.
> - Accuracy alone is insufficient for IDPS evaluation due to **class imbalance** — always quote Precision, Recall, and F1 alongside Accuracy.
> - Do not forget to convert decimal results to percentage in the final answer (0.8261 → 82.61%).
> - For Part A 3-mark questions, listing components without a one-line description loses 0.5 marks per item.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Host Architecture = endpoint-resident security monitoring**, complementary to network-based architectures.
- Three pillars: **Agent, Analysis Engine, Response Module**.
- **Agent-based** offers deep visibility; **Agentless** offers easy deployment.
- Detection methods: **Signature (precision), Anomaly (recall for zero-days), Stateful (transition validity)**.
- **FIM via SHA-256** is the cornerstone of compliance-mandated host monitoring.
- Key metrics: Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy — F1 is the *balanced* metric.
- Anomaly threshold: $T = \bar{x} + k\sigma$, with $k = 3$ typical for $\approx 99.7\%$ confidence.
- Host agents are the **only** way to inspect **encrypted traffic** and **insider activity**.
- Limitations: Agent tampering by rootkits, performance overhead, scale management.
- KTU-favorite formulas: $P = \text{TP}/(\text{TP}+\text{FP})$, $R = \text{TP}/(\text{TP}+\text{FN})$, $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$.
- Always pair the **architecture diagram** with **block-level function descriptions** in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Capabilities** of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS) refer to the comprehensive set of functional abilities that enable the system to observe, interpret, evaluate, and act upon network or host-based events to identify, log, alert on, and neutralize malicious activities. These capabilities collectively determine the operational effectiveness, coverage, accuracy, and response posture of an IDPS deployment.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT742)**, Security Capabilities are formally categorized into the following six functional dimensions as per the standardized NIST SP 800-94 and RFC 4766/4767 alignment:

| # | Capability Dimension | Operational Purpose |
|---|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Information Collection** | Gathering raw traffic/event data |
| 2 | **Logging** | Persistent storage of detected events |
| 3 | **Detection** | Identifying malicious patterns |
| 4 | **Prevention / Response** | Active or passive mitigation |
| 5 | **Performance & Scalability** | Throughput and latency control |
| 6 | **Integration & Correlation** | SIEM, SOAR, and ecosystem hooks |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint**
>
> Imagine an IDPS as the **entire airport security apparatus**, not just a single X-ray machine.
>
> - **Information Collection** → The CCTV cameras, body scanners, and metal detectors (sensors gathering data).
> - **Logging** → The control room register where every incident is written down.
> - **Detection** → The trained officer who recognizes a suspicious bulge under a jacket (pattern recognition).
> - **Prevention** → The officer physically stopping the suspect (active response) versus simply calling for backup (passive response).
> - **Performance** → How many passengers can be screened per hour without making the queue unbearable.
> - **Integration** → Sharing watchlists with police, immigration, and the airline database.
>
> Just as an airport's security capabilities determine whether a terrorist gets on a plane, an IDPS's capabilities determine whether an attacker penetrates the network.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The universally benchmarked IDPS capability metrics (per **NIST SP 800-94 Rev. 1** and **MITRE ATT&CK Evaluations**) are:
> - **Detection Rate (DR)** — fraction of actual attacks correctly identified.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — fraction of benign events incorrectly flagged.
> - **Throughput** — measured in **Gbps** or **packets per second (pps)**.
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** — typically measured in **seconds**.
> - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** — typically measured in **seconds/minutes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the Six Core Security Capabilities

#### 2.1.1 Information Collection Capability
- **What:** The IDPS must observe the relevant data stream (network packets, system calls, log files, application telemetry).
- **How:** Achieved via sensor placement — *inline taps*, *passive taps (SPAN ports)*, *host agents*, or *cloud-native log collectors*.
- **Why it matters:** *Garbage In, Garbage Out* — detection is impossible without accurate input. Coverage gaps at this stage cascade into detection blind spots.

#### 2.1.2 Logging Capability
- **What:** Persistent, tamper-resistant storage of detected events with full forensic context.
- **How:** Uses standardized formats like **CEF (Common Event Format)**, **LEEF (Log Event Extended Format)**, and **syslog (RFC 5424)**.
- **Why:** Enables post-incident forensics, compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA), and legal admissibility.

#### 2.1.3 Detection Capability
This is the analytical heart of the IDPS and uses four primary methodologies:
1. **Signature-Based Detection** — pattern matching against known attack signatures (e.g., Snort rules).
2. **Anomaly-Based Detection** — statistical deviation from a learned baseline.
3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis** — understanding protocol state machines (e.g., RFC-compliant TCP handshake).
4. **Behavioral / Heuristic Analysis** — machine learning models on traffic behavior.

#### 2.1.4 Prevention / Response Capability
Two response paradigms exist:
- **Passive Response:** Alerts, logging, SNMP traps, syslog forwarding (typical of pure **IDS**).
- **Active Response:** Packet drop, session reset, quarantine, IP blocking (typical of **IPS** inline mode).

#### 2.1.5 Performance & Scalability Capability
- **Throughput:** Sustained data processing rate without packet loss.
- **Latency:** Added delay introduced by inline inspection.
- **Concurrent Sessions:** Maximum trackable simultaneous flows.

#### 2.1.6 Integration & Correlation Capability
- Feeds into **SIEM** (Splunk, QRadar, ArcSight).
- Interoperates with **SOAR** (Security Orchestration, Automation & Response).
- Shares threat intel via **STIX / TAXII** protocols.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for Security Capabilities — Must Memorize**

| Metric | Mathematical Formula | Range / Unit | Significance |
|--------|---------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Detection Rate (Recall / Sensitivity)** | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of real attacks caught |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of benign events wrongly flagged |
| **True Negative Rate (Specificity)** | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of benign correctly ignored |
| **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Reliability of an alert |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Overall correctness |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient** | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Balanced quality metric |
| **Throughput (pps)** | $T_{pps} = \dfrac{N_{packets}}{\Delta t}$ | pps | Packets processed per second |
| **Throughput (bps)** | $T_{bps} = \dfrac{8 \cdot N_{bytes}}{\Delta t}$ | bps | Bits processed per second |
| **Bayes Posterior (Detection Probability)** | $P(A \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Updated attack likelihood |
| **Expected Cost** | $E[C] = C_{FP} \cdot FPR \cdot P(\neg A) + C_{FN} \cdot FNR \cdot P(A)$ | currency | Optimizes detection threshold |
| **Base Rate Fallacy** | $P(A \vert \text{alarm}) = \dfrac{DR \cdot P(A)}{DR \cdot P(A) + FPR \cdot (1 - P(A))}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Real-world alert value |

Where **TP** = True Positive, **TN** = True Negative, **FP** = False Positive, **FN** = False Negative, **A** = attack, **E** = evidence.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Security capabilities form the **evaluation rubric** used in every major IDPS procurement (Gartner Magic Quadrant, MITRE ATT&CK Evaluations). For example, MITRE's 2023 evaluations measure an IDPS on **Detection Coverage**, **Visibility**, and **Analytic Capability** — all of which map directly to the capabilities framework above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Posterior for IDPS Detection

We begin with the elementary definition of conditional probability and progressively build the detection-engine equation.

**Step 1 — Definition of Conditional Probability**

$$P(A \vert E) = \frac{P(A \cap E)}{P(E)}$$

This expresses the probability of attack $A$ given evidence $E$ was observed.

**Step 2 — Symmetry of Joint Probability**

$$P(A \cap E) = P(E \cap A)$$

Joints are commutative. So:

$$P(A \vert E) = \frac{P(E \cap A)}{P(E)} = \frac{P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}$$

This is the conditional re-statement using the product rule $P(E \cap A) = P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A)$.

**Step 3 — Total Probability Denominator Expansion**

Since the evidence $E$ can occur with or without an attack:

$$P(E) = P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A) + P(E \vert \neg A) \cdot P(\neg A)$$

Substituting back:

$$P(A \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A) + P(E \vert A^c) \cdot P(A^c)}$$

**Step 4 — Mapping to IDPS Variables**

Let us define:

- $P(A)$ = prior probability of attack = base rate $p$
- $P(E \vert A)$ = detection rate (sensitivity) $DR$
- $P(E \vert A^c)$ = false positive rate $FPR$
- $P(A^c) = 1 - p$

**Step 5 — Final IDPS Bayes Equation**

$$\boxed{P(A \vert E) = \frac{DR \cdot p}{DR \cdot p + FPR \cdot (1 - p)}}$$

This is the **Base Rate Fallacy formula** that every IDPS engineer must understand: at low attack base rates (typical networks where $p \approx 10^{-4}$), even high-DR / low-FPR systems produce mostly false alarms.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the F1-Score

**Step 1 — Define Harmonic Mean**

The harmonic mean of two positive reals $a$ and $b$ is:

$$H(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $a = Precision$ and $b = Recall$**

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{Precision} + \frac{1}{Recall}}$$

**Step 3 — Common Denominator**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Recall + Precision} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Step 4 — Explanation**

The F1 is the harmonic mean rather than the arithmetic mean because it **penalizes imbalance** between precision and recall — a critical property for IDPS where a high-recall/low-precision system is operationally useless.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Expected Cost of Detection Errors

**Step 1 — Define Loss Function**

Let $C_{FP}$ be the cost per false positive (analyst fatigue, lost productivity) and $C_{FN}$ be the cost per false negative (successful breach, regulatory fines).

**Step 2 — Expected Loss from False Positives**

$$E[C_{FP}] = C_{FP} \cdot FP \cdot P(\neg A)$$

**Step 3 — Expected Loss from False Negatives**

$$E[C_{FN}] = C_{FN} \cdot FN \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 4 — Total Expected Cost**

$$\boxed{E[C] = C_{FP} \cdot FPR \cdot (1 - p) + C_{FN} \cdot (1 - DR) \cdot p}$$

**Step 5 — Operational Use**

The IDPS threshold (e.g., anomaly score cutoff) is tuned to **minimize** $E[C]$. If $C_{FN} \gg C_{FP}$, we lower the threshold (high recall). If $C_{FP} \gg C_{FN}$, we raise the threshold (high precision).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Capability vs. Trade-off

| Security Capability | Strength It Provides | Trade-off / Cost |
|--------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) | High detection of application-layer attacks | ↑ Latency, ↓ Throughput |
| Anomaly Detection | Zero-day capability | ↑ False Positives |
| Signature Detection | Very low false positives | Misses novel attacks |
| Inline Blocking | Active prevention | Single point of failure |
| SSL/TLS Inspection | Visibility into encrypted traffic | ↑ CPU load, privacy concerns |
| Host-based Agent | Local visibility | Deployment overhead |
| Network-based Sensor | Broad coverage | Encrypted blind spot |
| ML-based Detection | Adaptive learning | Adversarial ML attacks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Security Capabilities" in the context of an Intrusion Detection and Prevention System. List any FOUR primary security capabilities.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Security Capabilities of an IDPS refer to the functional abilities of the system to monitor, detect, log, alert, and respond to malicious activity on a network or host. The four primary capabilities are:

1. **Information Collection Capability** — gathering raw data from network or host sensors.
2. **Detection Capability** — analyzing the data using signatures, anomalies, or stateful methods to identify attacks.
3. **Logging Capability** — persistent storage of events for forensics and compliance.
4. **Prevention/Response Capability** — taking action against detected threats (passive alerting or active blocking).

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the four capabilities = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between "Detection Rate" and "False Positive Rate" with formulas.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Metric | Definition | Formula |
|--------|------------|---------|
| **Detection Rate (DR)** | Fraction of actual attacks correctly identified by the IDPS | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | Fraction of legitimate (benign) events incorrectly flagged as malicious | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ |

An ideal IDPS has $DR \rightarrow 1$ and $FPR \rightarrow 0$. *(1 mark each for formula + 1 mark for explanation = 3 marks)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the formula** when explaining metrics — this loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> - For Bayes/Base Rate Fallacy derivations, students forget to **define each variable** before substitution — KTU examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** per undefined variable.
> - In comparison answers, students often **write only differences**, not a tabular comparison — preferred format is **a table**, which earns full marks.
> - Always **state assumptions** before any numeric computation (e.g., assume $p = 10^{-3}$).

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the SIX core security capabilities of an IDPS. For EACH capability, state ONE real-world tool or technique that implements it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A network IDPS is deployed in an enterprise where the probability of any given session containing an attack is $p = 0.001$. The IDPS has a detection rate $DR = 0.95$ and a false positive rate $FPR = 0.02$. Using Bayes' theorem, compute the probability that an alert raised by the IDPS corresponds to an actual attack. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Core Security Capabilities of an IDPS (7 Marks):**

| # | Capability | Description | Real-World Tool / Technique |
|---|------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Information Collection** | Captures packets via SPAN ports, inline taps, or host agents | Wireshark, Zeek (Bro) |
| 2 | **Logging** | Stores alerts in standardized format for SIEM ingestion | Syslog (RFC 5424), CEF format |
| 3 | **Detection** | Signature, anomaly, stateful, or ML-based pattern recognition | Snort rules, Suricata, OSSEC |
| 4 | **Prevention / Response** | Active packet drop, TCP reset, or quarantine | Cisco Firepower IPS inline mode |
| 5 | **Performance / Scalability** | Sustained throughput and low-latency inspection | DPDK-accelerated sensors, Intel QAT |
| 6 | **Integration / Correlation** | Feeds SIEM, SOAR, STIX/TAXII | Splunk Enterprise Security, MISP |

*(1 mark per capability + ½ mark for example = approximately 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Bayes' Theorem Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — List Given Values**
- $p = P(A) = 0.001$ (attack base rate)
- $DR = P(E \vert A) = 0.95$
- $FPR = P(E \vert A^c) = 0.02$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Posterior Formula**

$$P(A \vert E) = \frac{DR \cdot p}{DR \cdot p + FPR \cdot (1 - p)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$P(A \vert E) = \frac{0.95 \times 0.001}{0.95 \times 0.001 + 0.02 \times (1 - 0.001)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator Calculation**

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.95 \times 0.001 = 0.00095$$

**Step 5 — Denominator Calculation**

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.00095 + 0.02 \times 0.999 = 0.00095 + 0.01998 = 0.02093$$

**Step 6 — Final Division**

$$P(A \vert E) = \frac{0.00095}{0.02093} \approx 0.0454$$

**Step 7 — Comment**

$$\boxed{P(A \vert \text{alarm}) \approx 4.54\%}$$

Despite an excellent detection rate of 95%, only about **1 in 22 alerts** is a real attack. This is the **Base Rate Fallacy**: at low attack prevalence, the IDPS analyst's signal-to-noise ratio is poor. Operational mitigations include tuning the threshold, using threat intel enrichment, or deploying a tier-2 SOC analyst.

*(Marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for comment = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Signature-Based Detection** and **Anomaly-Based Detection** capabilities of an IDPS along the dimensions of: detection principle, zero-day handling, false positive rate, and processing overhead. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An IDPS evaluation on 10,000 sessions yields the following confusion matrix:

- True Positives $TP = 380$
- False Positives $FP = 120$
- True Negatives $TN = 9,400$
- False Negatives $FN = 100$

Calculate the **Detection Rate**, **False Positive Rate**, **Precision**, and **F1-Score**. Comment on whether this IDPS is operationally acceptable. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Dimension | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Detection Principle** | Exact or regex pattern match against known attack signatures | Statistical / ML deviation from a learned baseline of normal behavior |
| **Zero-Day Handling** | Poor — cannot detect unknown attacks | Strong — any deviation from baseline is flagged |
| **False Positive Rate** | Very low | High — benign anomalies trigger alerts |
| **Processing Overhead** | Low — fast string matching | High — needs statistical computation or inference |
| **Maintenance** | Requires frequent signature updates | Requires periodic baseline retraining |
| **Example Tools** | Snort, Suricata | Zeek, Darktrace, AWS GuardDuty |

*(1 mark per dimension × 4 dimensions + 1 mark for examples = ~5–7 marks depending on elaboration)*

---

**(b) Metric Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall Definitions**

$$DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}, \quad FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}, \quad P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \quad F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$$

**Step 2 — Detection Rate**

$$DR = \frac{380}{380 + 100} = \frac{380}{480} \approx 0.7917$$

**Step 3 — False Positive Rate**

$$FPR = \frac{120}{120 + 9400} = \frac{120}{9520} \approx 0.0126$$

**Step 4 — Precision**

$$P = \frac{380}{380 + 120} = \frac{380}{500} = 0.760$$

**Step 5 — Recall** (same as Detection Rate) $= 0.7917$

**Step 6 — F1-Score**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.760 \times 0.7917}{0.760 + 0.7917} = \frac{1.2034}{1.5517} \approx 0.7755$$

**Step 7 — Summary Table**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| **Detection Rate** | **0.7917** |
| **False Positive Rate** | **0.0126** |
| **Precision** | **0.7600** |
| **F1-Score** | **0.7755** |

**Step 8 — Operational Comment**

The IDPS catches roughly **79%** of attacks and has a low FPR of **1.26%**, which is acceptable. However, the **F1-Score of 0.78** suggests moderate balance between precision and recall. **Recommendation:** tuning the threshold could push the F1 above 0.85 for operational deployment; otherwise, a tier-1 SOC analyst is required to triage the residual false positives.

*(Marks: 1 per metric × 4 metrics + 1 mark for table + 2 marks for comment = 7 marks)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Security Capabilities"**

- ⭐ **Six core capabilities:** Information Collection, Logging, Detection, Prevention/Response, Performance, Integration.
- ⭐ **Detection Rate formula:** $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — measures how many attacks are caught.
- ⭐ **False Positive Rate formula:** $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ — measures noise.
- ⭐ **F1-Score formula:** $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ — harmonic mean balances precision & recall.
- ⭐ **Bayes / Base Rate Fallacy:** $P(A \vert E) = \dfrac{DR \cdot p}{DR \cdot p + FPR \cdot (1-p)}$ — high $DR$ + low $p$ still produces many false alarms.
- ⭐ **Expected Cost:** $E[C] = C_{FP} \cdot FPR \cdot (1-p) + C_{FN} \cdot (1-DR) \cdot p$ — threshold tuning objective.
- ⭐ **Throughput units:** **pps** (packets/sec) or **bps** (bits/sec).
- ⭐ **Signature vs Anomaly:** Signatures are precise but miss zero-days; Anomaly catches novel attacks but is noisy.
- ⭐ **MCC (Matthews Correlation Coefficient)** is the most balanced single-number metric for IDPS evaluation.
- ⭐ **Inline IPS** can actively prevent; **Passive IDS** can only alert — capability difference is fundamental.
- ⭐ **Integration** is via SIEM (Splunk, QRadar), SOAR (Cortex XSOAR), and threat intel feeds (STIX/TAXII).
- ⭐ **Standard log formats:** syslog (RFC 5424), CEF, LEEF — must be remembered verbatim.
- ⭐ **Performance trade-off:** DPI ↑ detection ↓ throughput — always state this trade-off in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logging Capabilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Logging Capabilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)**, **Logging Capabilities** refer to the systematic, structured, and auditable mechanisms by which the IDPS records, timestamps, formats, stores, and exports security-relevant events occurring on a monitored network or host. Logging is the foundational *forensic and evidentiary layer* of any IDPS deployment — without it, alerts become transient and post-incident analysis (root-cause analysis, threat hunting, legal prosecution) becomes impossible.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECCT742, logging capabilities encompass:

- **Event generation** (what is recorded and when)
- **Log formatting and normalization** (CEF, LEEF, Syslog)
- **Log storage and retention** (volatility, integrity, WORM)
- **Log transport and forwarding** (TLS-encrypted syslog, Kafka, etc.)
- **Log correlation and enrichment** (SIEM ingestion)
- **Compliance-driven logging** (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** "Logging capability is the IDPS's ability to capture, store, protect, and export detailed records of detected events, policy violations, and system actions in a tamper-resistant, machine-parseable format suitable for forensic analysis, regulatory compliance, and incident response."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an IDPS as a **high-security apartment building**:

- The **IDPS sensors** (cameras, motion detectors) detect something suspicious.
- The **logging system** is the **security desk's logbook** — every visitor's name, time of entry, badge number, and destination apartment is written down.
- The **log format** is the *language* used in the logbook (e.g., a standard form with fields: name, time, ID).
- The **log storage** is the locked cabinet where the logbook is kept — protected from tampering.
- The **SIEM correlation engine** is the **building manager** who reads multiple logbooks (from different buildings) to detect a coordinated burglary across the city.

Without the logbook, even if the cameras catch a thief on video, you have **no searchable, time-stamped, attributable record** to take to court or to reconstruct the attacker's path.

## 1.3 Why Logging is the *Backbone* of IDPS

Logging is what transforms an IDPS from a *real-time alarm* into a *forensic instrument*. Three pillar benefits:

1. **Accountability** — Every action (detection, prevention, false positive) is auditable.
2. **Forensic Reconstruction** — Post-breach, logs allow timeline reconstruction of the kill chain.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Laws like **PCI-DSS Requirement 10**, **HIPAA §164.312(b)**, and **GDPR Article 30** mandate logging of access and security events.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Metric (must remember):** The **MITRE ATT&CK** framework documents that mature threat actors typically dwell in a network for an average of **277 days** before detection. Without robust logging, the *forensic window* is lost forever.

## 1.4 Core Components of an IDPS Logging Subsystem

| Component | Function | Example |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| **Event Generator** | Captures raw security events | Snort rule firing, Suricata alert |
| **Formatter/Normalizer** | Converts events to a standard schema | CEF, LEEF, JSON |
| **Transport Layer** | Forwards logs to collectors | Syslog (UDP/TCP/TLS), Kafka |
| **Storage Backend** | Persists logs (WORM for compliance) | Elasticsearch, Splunk, AWS S3 Glacier |
| **Integrity Module** | Detects tampering | Hash-chaining (e.g., TrueLog), digital signatures |
| **Correlation Engine** | Cross-references multiple log streams | SIEM rules, UEBA, SOAR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU syllabus specifically tests** your understanding of: (1) log formats, (2) log levels/severity, (3) centralized vs. distributed logging, (4) log integrity protection, and (5) integration with SIEM platforms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an IDPS Log Entry

A well-formed IDPS log entry is not a free-text sentence — it is a **structured record** with mandatory and optional fields. The standard structure is:

$$
\text{Log\_Entry} = \{ \text{Timestamp}, \text{Severity}, \text{Source}, \text{Destination}, \text{EventID}, \text{Message}, \text{Action}, \text{Protocol}, \text{UserID}, \text{AssetID} \}
$$

Each field plays a forensic role:

- **Timestamp** — Anchors the event in the kill-chain timeline. Must use **UTC + timezone offset** (never local-only) to enable cross-system correlation.
- **Severity** — A numeric or categorical ranking (e.g., **0=Emergency, 1=Alert, 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice, 6=Informational, 7=Debug** — RFC 5424 Syslog).
- **Source / Destination** — IP, port, hostname, MAC. Forms the "who-talks-to-whom" graph.
- **EventID / RuleID** — Maps to a specific detection signature (e.g., Snort SID 1:19187).
- **Action** — What the IDPS did: *alert, drop, reject, reset, pass, log*.
- **Message** — Human-readable context.

## 2.2 Standardized Log Formats (KTU High-Yield)

The IDPS does not invent a log format; it emits logs in **industry-standard schemas** so that downstream SIEM/SOAR tools can parse them without custom adapters.

### 2.2.1 Syslog (RFC 5424)

The de-facto standard. Format:

$$
\text{PRI} \;\; \text{VERSION} \;\; \text{TIMESTAMP} \;\; \text{HOSTNAME} \;\; \text{APP-NAME} \;\; \text{PROCID} \;\; \text{MSGID} \;\; \text{STRUCTURED-DATA} \;\; \text{MESSAGE}
$$

Where **PRI = Facility × 8 + Severity**.

### 2.2.2 Common Event Format (CEF) — ArcSight

A flat, key-value format optimized for SIEM ingestion:

$$
\text{CEF:}\vert\text{Version}\vert\text{DeviceVendor}\vert\text{DeviceProduct}\vert\text{DeviceVersion}\vert\text{SignatureID}\vert\text{Name}\vert\text{Severity}\vert\text{Extension}
$$

**Example:**
```
CEF:0|Cisco|Firepower|6.2.0|1000001|Trojan Detected|8|src=10.0.0.5 dst=185.220.101.45 act=blocked cs1Label=ThreatName cs1=Emotet
```

### 2.2.3 Log Event Extended Format (LEEF) — IBM QRadar

A tab-delimited, semi-structured format:

$$
\text{LEEF:}\vert\text{Version}\vert\text{Vendor}\vert\text{Product}\vert\text{Version}\vert\text{EventID}\vert\text{Delimiter}[\text{tab}]\text{AttributeName}=\text{AttributeValue}
$$

### 2.2.4 JSON / ECS (Elastic Common Schema)

Modern IDPS solutions (Elastic, Wazuh, Suricata) emit JSON-formatted logs aligned with the **Elastic Common Schema (ECS)** for direct Elasticsearch ingestion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Compare CEF and LEEF," the answer hinges on:
> - CEF = pipe-delimited, vendor-neutral, used by ArcSight/most SIEMs.
> - LEEF = tab-delimited, IBM-QRadar-optimized, supports custom attributes.
> - CEF has a fixed extension key-value grammar; LEEF is more flexible.

## 2.3 Log Levels and Severity Classification

| Syslog Severity | Keyword | IDPS Meaning | Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 0 | Emergency | System unusable | IDPS kernel panic |
| 1 | Alert | Immediate action required | Active breach in progress |
| 2 | Critical | Critical conditions | Confirmed malware execution |
| 3 | Error | Error conditions | Failed prevention action (drop denied) |
| 4 | Warning | Warning conditions | Anomalous traffic spike |
| 5 | Notice | Normal but significant | New device enrolled in monitoring |
| 6 | Informational | Informational messages | Session opened/closed |
| 7 | Debug | Debug-level messages | Packet payload dump |

## 2.4 Centralized vs. Distributed Logging Architecture

### 2.4.1 Distributed (Local) Logging
- Logs stored **on the IDPS sensor** itself.
- **Pros:** Low latency, no network dependency.
- **Cons:** Logs lost if sensor is compromised or destroyed; difficult to correlate across sensors; no tamper resistance.

### 2.4.2 Centralized Logging
- All IDPS sensors forward logs to a **central collector** (syslog server, SIEM).
- **Pros:** Single pane of glass, correlation across the entire estate, easier backup and integrity protection.
- **Cons:** Single point of failure (mitigated by HA clusters), higher network bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Best Practice (Industry Standard):** Always use **centralized logging with TLS-encrypted transport (syslog over TCP/TLS — RFC 5425)** and **WORM (Write-Once-Read-Many) storage** for compliance-driven environments.

## 2.5 Log Integrity and Tamper Resistance

For logs to be *admissible as evidence*, their integrity must be cryptographically provable. Three primary mechanisms:

1. **Write-Once Storage (WORM)** — Logs written to immutable media (e.g., AWS S3 Object Lock, NetApp SnapLock).
2. **Cryptographic Hash-Chaining** — Each log entry contains the hash of the previous entry (a *Merkle-chain*); any tampering breaks the chain.
3. **Digital Signatures / Trusted Timestamping** — Logs are signed by a **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** with a **Trusted Timestamp Authority (TSA)** using RFC 3161.

$$
H_n = \text{SHA-256}(L_n \Vert H_{n-1})
$$

where $L_n$ is the n-th log entry, and $H_0$ is a known genesis hash.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Unit / Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Syslog PRI calculation | $PRI = Facility \times 8 + Severity$ | Integer (0–191) |
| Log Retention (PCI-DSS) | $\geq \mathbf{90}$ days online + **1 year** archived | Days |
| Log Retention (HIPAA) | $\geq \mathbf{6}$ years | Years |
| Log Retention (GDPR) | "No longer than necessary" — typically 30 days to 2 years | Context-dependent |
| Log Integrity (Hash Chain) | $H_n = \text{Hash}(L_n \Vert H_{n-1})$ | Bitstring |
| Storage Estimation | $S = N \times \bar{l} \times R$ | Bytes |
| Correlation Rule | $\text{Alert} = \bigvee_{i=1}^{k} (E_i \in W_i \land t_i \in [T_a, T_b])$ | Boolean |
| Compression Ratio | $C = \dfrac{S_{raw}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Unitless |

Where:
- $N$ = number of events per second
- $\bar{l}$ = average log size in bytes
- $R$ = retention time in seconds
- $E_i$ = i-th event
- $W_i$ = watchlist pattern
- $[T_a, T_b]$ = time window

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Logging capabilities are not academic — they are the **legal and operational lifeline** of any enterprise:

- **Banking:** PCI-DSS mandates 1-year retention with daily integrity checks.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA audits require demonstrable access logs for PHI for 6 years.
- **Critical Infrastructure:** NERC-CIP requires logging of all cyber-asset access for 3 years.
- **Law Enforcement:** Logs are the *primary digital evidence* presented under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (or equivalent global statutes).
- **Threat Hunting:** Logs feed **UEBA (User & Entity Behavior Analytics)** to detect insider threats using ML baselines.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Logging Pipeline Design

> Since logging is a *systems engineering* topic (not a closed-form physics derivation), the "derivation" below is an **exhaustive, step-by-step analytical framework** for designing and evaluating an IDPS logging pipeline — the kind of structured, 14-mark answer a KTU examiner expects.

## 3.1 Problem: Design a Centralized IDPS Logging Architecture for a Multi-Site Enterprise

**Given (Assumptions):**
- Number of sites: $M = 5$
- IDPS sensors per site: $k = 10$
- Average event rate per sensor: $N_s = 200$ events/sec
- Average log entry size: $\bar{l} = 512$ bytes
- Compliance: PCI-DSS → 1 year total retention (90 days online + 275 days archive)
- Log integrity: SHA-256 hash chaining required

**Required:** Compute (a) aggregate event rate, (b) total storage requirement, (c) bandwidth to central collector, (d) hash-chain length verification logic.

### Step 1 — Aggregate Event Rate

The total events per second across the enterprise is the sum over all sites and sensors.

$$
N_{total} = M \times k \times N_s
$$

$$
N_{total} = 5 \times 10 \times 200
$$

$$
N_{total} = 10{,}000 \;\; \text{events/second}
$$

**Interpretation:** A central collector must ingest **10,000 events per second sustained** — this drives SIEM sizing (e.g., Splunk Enterprise scales linearly with indexer count).

### Step 2 — Total Storage Requirement

Total storage is the product of event rate, average entry size, and retention time.

$$
S = N_{total} \times \bar{l} \times R
$$

Where $R$ = 1 year in seconds:

$$
R = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 31{,}536{,}000 \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

$$
S = 10{,}000 \times 512 \times 31{,}536{,}000
$$

$$
S = 1.6147 \times 10^{17} \;\; \text{bytes}
$$

$$
S \approx 160 \;\; \text{Petabytes} \;\; \text{(uncompressed)}
$$

**Engineering decision:** Apply **zstd / gzip** compression. A typical log compresses by factor $C \approx 5$:

$$
S_{compressed} = \frac{S}{C} = \frac{160}{5} = 32 \;\; \text{PB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is why enterprise SIEMs are expensive.** The cost driver is *storage*, not compute. A KTU question that asks "why is centralized logging expensive?" should answer with **storage and retention mathematics**, not "because SIEM licenses are costly."

### Step 3 — Bandwidth to Central Collector

Assuming **all** events are forwarded (no local filtering) and logs are **pre-formatted as CEF** with negligible overhead:

$$
BW = N_{total} \times \bar{l} \;\; \text{bytes/sec}
$$

$$
BW = 10{,}000 \times 512 = 5{,}120{,}000 \;\; \text{bytes/sec}
$$

$$
BW = 5.12 \;\; \text{MB/s} \approx 41 \;\; \text{Mbps}
$$

**Engineering decision:** This comfortably fits within a **100 Mbps** MPLS link, but a **TLS-encrypted syslog** adds ~5% overhead, requiring approximately **43 Mbps**. For WAN cost optimization, **log pre-filtering** at the sensor (forward only Severity ≤ 4) can reduce this by 70–80%.

### Step 4 — Hash-Chain Integrity Verification

Define the genesis hash as a system-known constant:

$$
H_0 = \text{SHA-256}(\text{"GENESIS\_0"})
$$

For each subsequent entry $L_n$, compute the chained hash:

$$
H_n = \text{SHA-256}(L_n \Vert H_{n-1})
$$

At any audit point, the verifier:

1. Re-computes $H_n$ forward from $H_0$ using the current log file.
2. Compares the computed $H_n$ with the **last signed hash** stored in the HSM.
3. If they match, integrity is proven; if not, the *mismatch index* identifies the tampered entry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formally:** The probability of undetected tampering with SHA-256 hash-chain and HSM-signed root hash is bounded by the collision resistance of SHA-256, which is approximately $2^{-128}$ — cryptographically negligible.

### Step 5 — Log Correlation Rule Example

A common KTU question is: *"Define a correlation rule that detects brute-force SSH attempts."*

A SIEM correlation rule (in pseudo-formal logic) is:

$$
\text{Alert} = \bigvee_{i=1}^{k} \left( E_i.\text{EventID} = \text{"SSH\_AUTH\_FAIL"} \;\land\; E_i.\text{srcIP} = E_{i+1}.\text{srcIP} \;\land\; t_{i+1} - t_i \leq 60s \right)
$$

In words: **"Fire alert if the same source IP triggers ≥ 5 SSH authentication failures within a 60-second sliding window."**

This is a *signature-based correlation* — analogous to an IDPS detection rule, but applied to log streams rather than packet streams.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Logging Architectures

| Criterion | Local Logging | Centralized (Syslog) | Centralized + SIEM | Cloud-Native (e.g., AWS CloudWatch + S3 Glacier) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Latency** | < 1 ms | 5–50 ms | 50–500 ms | 100 ms – 2 s |
| **Tamper Resistance** | Low (sensor-level) | Medium (network transit) | High (SIEM WORM) | Very High (Object Lock) |
| **Correlation** | None | Limited | Full (cross-stream) | Full + ML (UEBA) |
| **Cost (per TB/year)** | Very Low | Low | High | Medium (pay-as-you-go) |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Compliance Fit** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Forensic Capability** | Local only | Multi-sensor | Enterprise-wide | Enterprise + global |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy:** For any "Compare and contrast" question on logging architectures, structure your answer using this **6-criterion matrix** — it pre-empts every follow-up the examiner might ask.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End IDPS Logging Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC_A[Source Layer]
        N1[Network Sensor 1]
        N2[Network Sensor 2]
        H1[Host Agent A]
        H2[Host Agent B]
        FW[Firewall Log Feed]
    end

    subgraph FMT_B[Format and Normalize Layer]
        CEF[CEF Formatter]
        LEEF[LEEF Formatter]
        JSON[JSON ECS Formatter]
        SYSLOG[Syslog RFC 5424]
    end

    subgraph TRS_C[Transport Layer]
        TLS[TLS Encrypted Syslog Port 6514]
        KAFKA[Kafka Topic]
        API[REST API Forwarder]
    end

    subgraph STO_D[Storage and Integrity Layer]
        SIEM[SIEM Indexer Cluster]
        WORM[WORM Archive]
        HSM[HSM Signed Hash Chain]
        S3[Object Lock Storage]
    end

    subgraph ANA_E[Analysis and Response Layer]
        CORR[Correlation Engine]
        UEBA[UEBA ML Models]
        DASH[Analyst Dashboard]
        SOAR[SOAR Auto Response]
    end

    N1 --> CEF
    N2 --> LEEF
    H1 --> JSON
    H2 --> SYSLOG
    FW --> CEF

    CEF --> TLS
    LEEF --> TLS
    JSON --> KAFKA
    SYSLOG --> TLS
    CEF -.-> API

    TLS --> SIEM
    KAFKA --> SIEM
    API --> S3

    SIEM --> HSM
    SIEM --> WORM
    S3 --> WORM

    SIEM --> CORR
    SIEM --> UEBA
    CORR --> DASH
    UEBA --> DASH
    CORR --> SOAR
    SOAR -.Feedback.-> FMT_B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Source Layer** captures raw events from network sensors, host agents, and adjacent security devices.
- The **Format Layer** normalizes vendor-specific output into a standard schema (CEF, LEEF, JSON).
- The **Transport Layer** securely forwards logs via TLS-encrypted syslog (port 6514), Kafka topics, or REST APIs.
- The **Storage Layer** provides both queryable (SIEM indexer) and immutable (WORM) retention.
- The **Analysis Layer** performs correlation and ML-based anomaly detection, feeding dashboards and triggering automated response (SOAR).
- The **feedback arrow** represents adaptive response — a SOAR action can, for example, push new filter rules back to the IDPS sensors.

## 4.2 Hash-Chain Integrity Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Log Entry L1 Arrives] --> B[Compute H1 SHA-256 of L1 plus Genesis H0]
    B --> C[Append H1 to Log]
    C --> D[Log Entry L2 Arrives]
    D --> E[Compute H2 SHA-256 of L2 plus H1]
    E --> F[Append H2 to Log]
    F --> G[Periodically Sign Last Hash with HSM]
    G --> H[Store Signed Root Hash in WORM]
    H --> I{Audit Time}
    I --> J[Re-compute Hash Chain from Genesis]
    J --> K{Computed Root equals Signed Root}
    K -->|Yes| L[Integrity Verified]
    K -->|No| M[Locate Mismatch Index n]
    M --> N[Flag Tampered Entry Ln for Investigation]
```

## 4.3 Severity-Based Log Routing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New IDPS Event] --> SEV[Read Severity Field]
    SEV --> S0{Severity 0 to 2}
    SEV --> S3{Severity 3 to 4}
    SEV --> S5{Severity 5 to 7}
    S0 -->|Yes| P0[Forward Immediately to SIEM and SOAR]
    S0 -->|No| S3
    S3 -->|Yes| P1[Forward to SIEM and Archive]
    S3 -->|No| S5
    S5 -->|Yes| P2{Sampling Enabled}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Forward 10 percent Sample]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Forward All to Cold Storage Only]
    P0 --> END[End]
    P1 --> END
    P3 --> END
    P4 --> END
```

This decision tree reduces bandwidth and storage cost by **70–80%** for low-severity noise, while ensuring that **critical and alert-level events** are never dropped — a standard best-practice in enterprise IDPS logging.
