| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| Total Inline Inspection Time | $T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | seconds |
| Single-packet processing time | $T_{proc} = \dfrac{C_{pkt}}{R_{sensor}}$ | seconds, $C_{pkt}$ = packet complexity score |
| Required sensor throughput | $R_{req} \geq \alpha \cdot B_{link}$ | bps, $\alpha$ = safety factor (1.2–1.5) |
| Bypass switch failover time | $T_{fail} \leq 5\,\text{s}$ (industry standard) | seconds |
| Passive blocking effective rate | $R_{block} \leq \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot RTT}$ | blocks/sec (TCP RST race) |
| Mirrored traffic ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{B_{mirror}}{B_{link}}$ | dimensionless, $\rho \le 1$ |
| IDPS-to-Firewall latency tolerance | $L_{tol} = 0.05 \cdot RTT$ | typically 5% of RTT |
| False-positive cost function | $C_{FP} = N_{alerts} \cdot T_{analyst} \cdot C_{analyst/hr}$ | currency units |
| Detection confidence threshold | $P_{trigger} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| Inline fail-open probability | $P_{failopen} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | $n$ = independent components |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute value and set notation in the formula table uses `\vert` and `\mid` rather than the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Direct IDPS integration is the operational backbone of:
- **Enterprise NIPS deployments** (Cisco Firepower, Palo Alto Networks, Check Point).
- **Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA) gateways** where every flow is inspected inline.
- **Industrial Control System (ICS) / SCADA networks** where fail-closed behavior protects physical infrastructure.
- **Cloud-native IDPS** (AWS Network Firewall, Azure Firewall Premium) integrated via cloud-vendor APIs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing an Inline IDPS Sensor

A KTU-favourite problem: *"A 10 Gbps link carries traffic with an average packet size of 800 bytes. If the IDPS introduces 30 microseconds of processing latency per packet and the maximum tolerable end-to-end latency for VoIP is 150 ms, what is the maximum packet rate the sensor can sustain?"*

**Step 1 — Convert link bandwidth to packet rate.**

The link is $B = 10\,\text{Gbps}$. With average packet size $L = 800\,\text{bytes} = 6400\,\text{bits}$, the wire-rate packet rate is:

$$
P_{wire} = \frac{B}{L} = \frac{10 \times 10^{9}}{6400}
$$

$$
P_{wire} = 1{,}562{,}500\ \text{packets/sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sensor's processing-limited throughput.**

The sensor adds $T_{proc} = 30\,\mu\text{s}$ per packet, so the maximum rate it can process is:

$$
P_{sensor} = \frac{1}{T_{proc}} = \frac{1}{30 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_{sensor} \approx 33{,}333\ \text{packets/sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to wire rate.**

$$
P_{sensor} \ll P_{wire}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single 30 µs-per-packet engine **cannot keep up** with 10 Gbps wire rate. KTU answer: you would need either a faster ASIC-based engine, multiple sensors in parallel (load-sharing), or a bypass switch that drops the IDPS out of path during peak.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fail-Open Probability in a Redundant Pair

**Problem:** Two IDPS sensors in active-passive HA, each with an independent failure probability of $p = 0.02$ per year. Find the probability the system fails *closed* (i.e., drops all traffic) over a year.

**Step 1 — Probability that *both* sensors fail in the same year.**

Assuming independent failures:

$$
P_{both} = p \times p = p^{2} = (0.02)^{2} = 0.0004
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that the *system* survives the year.**

$$
P_{survive} = 1 - P_{both} = 1 - 0.0004 = 0.9996
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to availability percentage.**

$$
A = P_{survive} \times 100\% = 99.96\%
$$

This corresponds to roughly **"two nines"** plus — well within the **"five nines" (99.999%)** SLA target, so the design is acceptable for fail-open deployments.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TCP RST Blocking Latency for Passive IDPS

A passive IDPS detects an attack in $T_{detect} = 10\,\text{ms}$ and sends a TCP RST to the source. The RTT is $80\,\text{ms}$.

**Step 1 — Time for the RST to reach the source.**

The RST propagates at line speed; effectively the source receives it in roughly $\dfrac{RTT}{2}$:

$$
T_{RST\,arrival} = \frac{RTT}{2} = \frac{80}{2} = 40\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Time the attacker session was active before tear-down.**

$$
T_{active} = T_{detect} + T_{RST\,arrival} = 10 + 40 = 50\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the attack window.**

If the attack is a single-packet exploit (e.g., Heartbleed), the damage is done in microseconds — much less than the 50 ms needed for RST-based blocking. Hence **TCP RST is useless for single-packet attacks**; only inline IDPS can prevent them. This is a classic KTU conclusion.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Integration Decision Table

| Question to Ask | If YES → Choose | If NO → Choose |
|----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Must we block single-packet exploits? | **Inline IDPS** | Passive OK |
| Is link latency-critical (VoIP, HFT)? | **Passive / Bypass-inline** | Inline OK |
| Is sensor reliability < 99.9%? | **Passive + fail-open** | Inline OK |
| Is the link > 10 Gbps? | **Passive + dedicated TAP** | Inline with NP |
| Do we need forensic packet capture? | **TAP-based passive** | Inline OK |
| Are we inside a SCADA/ICS zone? | **Inline fail-CLOSED** | Passive |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Inline IDPS Packet Processing (KTU Walk-through)

1. **Packet enters ingress port** → MAC/CRC stripped.
2. **Decapsulation** → L2/L3/L4 headers parsed.
3. **Normalization** → Fragment reassembly, TCP stream reassembly.
4. **Signature matching** → Compare against Snort/Suricata ruleset.
5. **Protocol anomaly check** → Verify HTTP, DNS, SMB, etc. conform to RFCs.
6. **Behavioral check** → Compare against learned baseline.
7. **Decision:** Allow / Drop / Reject / Reset.
8. **Logging:** Emit CEF/LEEF event to SIEM.
9. **Forwarding:** Egress port transmits the (possibly modified) packet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** A modern inline sensor can run **all nine steps** in under 100 µs per packet at 1 Gbps using NP/ASIC acceleration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Inline IDPS Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    internetNode[INTERNET] -->|ingress traffic| routerNode[EDGE ROUTER]
    routerNode -->|raw flow| firewallNode[STATEFUL FIREWALL]
    firewallNode -->|filtered flow| idpsNode[INLINE IDPS SENSOR]
    idpsNode -->|clean flow| coreSwitchNode[CORE SWITCH]
    coreSwitchNode --> dmzNode[DMZ SERVERS]
    coreSwitchNode --> lanNode[INTERNAL LAN]
    idpsNode -. alert .-> siemNode[SIEM CORRELATOR]
    siemNode --> socNode[SOC ANALYST CONSOLE]
    socNode -. mitigation action .-> routerNode
    socNode -. mitigation action .-> firewallNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows = data-plane traffic.
- Dotted arrows = management-plane signalling and alerts.
- The **INLINE IDPS SENSOR** is positioned *between* the firewall and the core switch, allowing it to see only filtered traffic and to drop malicious packets before they reach the LAN.

## 4.2 Inline vs Passive Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inlineBlock[INLINE DEPLOYMENT]
        i1[CLIENT] --> i2[IDPS INLINE]
        i2 --> i3[SERVER]
        i2 -. alert .-> i4[SIEM]
    end
    subgraph passiveBlock[PASSIVE DEPLOYMENT]
        p1[CLIENT] --> p2[SWITCH SPAN or TAP]
        p2 --> p3[SERVER]
        p2 --> p4[IDPS PASSIVE]
        p4 -. TCP RST .-> p1
        p4 -. alert .-> p5[SIEM]
    end
```

## 4.3 IDPS-to-Firewall Sandwich Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    netExt[EXTERNAL NET] --> fw[FIREWALL]
    fw --> idps1[IDPS MODE 1: DETECT + DROP]
    idps1 --> internalNet[INTERNAL LAN]
    idps1 -. CEF .-> siem1[SIEM]
    siem1 -. dynamic ACL .-> fw
```

## 4.4 SIEM-Centric Detection Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[IDPS SENSOR A] --> siemNode2[SIEM]
    sensorB[FIREWALL LOG] --> siemNode2
    sensorC[EDR AGENT] --> siemNode2
    siemNode2 --> analyst[SOC ANALYST]
    analyst -->|playbook| soar[SOAR PLATFORM]
    soar -->|containment| routerCtl[ROUTER]
    soar -->|host quarantine| edrCtl[EDR PLATFORM]
    soar -->|block IP| fwCtl[FIREWALL]
```

## 4.5 Direct Integration Coupling Layers (Stack View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer5[LAYER 5: RESPONSE COUPLING - TCP RESET, ACL PUSH, BGP WITHDRAW]
    layer4[LAYER 4: POLICY COUPLING - UNIFIED RULESET, CONFLICT RESOLUTION]
    layer3[LAYER 3: DATA COUPLING - STIX TAXII, IOC SHARING]
    layer2[LAYER 2: LOGICAL COUPLING - SYSLOG, SNMP, API]
    layer1[LAYER 1: PHYSICAL COUPLING - TAP, SPAN, INLINE PORT, BYPASS]
    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compliance Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`), and labels are kept as plain uppercase text without bold/italic markdown that would break the renderer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between inline IDPS and passive (monitor-only) IDPS. Mention the role of a bypass switch in inline deployments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inline IDPS | Passive / Monitor IDPS |
|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Traffic Path** | Sits in series (in-band) | Receives mirrored copy (out-of-band) |
| **Prevention Capability** | Can drop, reject, or modify packets | Cannot directly block; relies on TCP RST or external ACL push |
| **Latency** | Adds processing latency | Negligible |
| **Failure Impact** | Can fail-open or fail-closed via bypass switch | Always fail-open |

A **bypass switch** is a hardware device (relay or optical) that automatically **shunts traffic around the IDPS** within 5 seconds if the sensor crashes, power-cycles, or hangs, thus preserving network availability.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]
**Explain the difference between a SPAN port and a Network TAP. Why is a TAP preferred for IDPS deployments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port:** A *software-configured* mirror on a managed switch that copies frames from one or more source ports/VLANs to a designated monitoring port. It is **best-effort** — under congestion it can drop mirrored frames silently.
- **Network TAP (Test Access Point):** A *hardware* splitter that physically copies the optical or electrical signal passing through a link. It provides a **lossless, full-duplex** copy.
- **Why TAP is preferred for IDPS:** Because a TAP never drops frames (even at line rate), captures both sides of a full-duplex conversation cleanly, and remains operational even if the switch crashes. SPAN consumes switch ASIC resources and may lose packets exactly when attacks happen — precisely the moment the IDPS most needs them.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In 14-mark questions, students **lose 2–3 marks** for confusing **inline vs passive** even after writing the correct diagram.
> - Always draw the **topology first** before writing the explanation; the diagram alone fetches **3 marks** in KTU 2024.
> - Mentioning **fail-open vs fail-closed** is mandatory; the examiner specifically scans for these terms.
> - Avoid using vague phrases like *"the IDPS handles traffic."* Use precise verbs: **mirrors, drops, resets, pushes ACL, signals via CoA.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[a] With neat diagrams, explain the architecture of an inline IDPS integrated with a stateful firewall. Discuss three advantages and two limitations of this deployment. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture:** The classic *Firewall → Inline IDPS → Core Switch* arrangement.

- The firewall performs fast L3/L4 filtering, blocking obvious IP/port-based attacks.
- The inline IDPS then performs deep packet inspection (DPI) on the traffic that passed the firewall — focusing on application-layer attacks (SQLi, XSS, C2 beaconing) and protocol anomalies.
- The core switch distributes clean traffic to the DMZ and LAN segments.

**Three advantages:**
1. **Real-time prevention** — single-packet exploits are blocked before reaching the host.
2. **Defense in depth** — firewall handles volume, IDPS handles sophistication.
3. **Centralized visibility** — every east-west and north-south flow is visible at one chokepoint.

**Two limitations:**
1. **Performance bottleneck** — the IDPS is a single point of failure; mis-tuning causes dropped legitimate traffic.
2. **Encrypted traffic blind spot** — IDPS cannot inspect TLS-encrypted payload without SSL/TLS interception (MITM proxy).

**[b] Compare SPAN port mirroring and Network TAP for feeding traffic to a passive IDPS. Under what circumstances would you choose a TAP? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | SPAN Port | Network TAP |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Implementation** | Software mirror on a switch | Hardware splitter on the wire |
| **Frame fidelity** | Best-effort, may drop on congestion | 100% lossless, line-rate |
| **Failure mode** | Stops mirroring if switch crashes | Passive — keeps working without power |
| **Full-duplex visibility** | Single mirror stream (may interleave) | Two separate TX/RX streams |
| **Cost** | Free (switch feature) | ~$200–$2000 per link |
| **Impact on source** | Consumes switch ASIC bandwidth | Zero impact |

**Choose a TAP when:**
1. You need **guaranteed full-fidelity capture** for compliance/IDS.
2. You must monitor **full-duplex links** (10G/40G/100G).
3. You need the capture to **survive a switch failure**.
4. You cannot afford **SPAN-induced congestion** on the production switch.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[a] Explain the concept of direct IDPS integration with SIEM. Describe how STIX/TAXII is used for threat-intel sharing in such an architecture. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SIEM Integration:**
The IDPS forwards alerts via **syslog (CEF/LEEF)** to a central SIEM. The SIEM correlates IDPS events with firewall logs, EDR telemetry, and authentication records to build a high-confidence incident picture. A SOC analyst uses the SIEM console to triage, and a **SOAR** (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) platform executes playbooks — for example, *"if IDPS detects lateral SMB scan from host X, quarantine X via EDR and block its IP at the firewall."*

**STIX/TAXII:**
- **STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression)** is a **JSON/XML language** for describing threat intel: indicators, TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures), threat actors, campaigns.
- **TAXII (Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information)** is the **HTTPS-based transport** for sharing STIX documents between organizations.
- **Workflow:** External feed (e.g., CERT-In) → TAXII server → STIX parser in IDPS → auto-generated detection rules → inline blocking.

**Benefit:** Within minutes of a global threat being announced, the IDPS is updated and blocking the new IoC — *zero human delay.*

**[b] Discuss the role of Change of Authorization (CoA) in coupling a passive IDPS with a Network Access Control (NAC) / RADIUS infrastructure. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**CoA — The Dynamic Control Channel:**
- A passive IDPS detects that a host on the LAN is exhibiting malware behaviour (e.g., C2 traffic).
- The IDPS sends a **RADIUS CoA-Request** to the NAC / RADIUS server identifying the endpoint by MAC or 802.1X session ID.
- The NAC pushes a new authorization — for example, **move the host's port into a quarantine VLAN**, or apply a **role-based ACL** that blocks its internet egress.
- The switch port is dynamically reconfigured within seconds; the infected host is isolated *without any human intervention*.

**Three advantages:**
1. **Sub-minute containment** of compromised endpoints.
2. **Scales** to thousands of ports without manual ACL edits.
3. **Closes the loop** between detection (IDPS) and enforcement (NAC).

**Two challenges:**
1. CoA requires the IDPS, NAC, and switch to be **RADIUS-compatible and vendor-interoperable**.
2. A **false positive** in the IDPS can wrongly quarantine a critical production host — so the confidence threshold for auto-CoA must be carefully tuned.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Direct IDPS Integration**

- **Inline IDPS = in-band = in the traffic path = can drop packets.** Passive = out-of-band = mirror only = cannot directly drop.
- **Bypass switch** is mandatory in any inline deployment; it preserves availability on sensor failure.
- **Fail-Open** → availability priority (typical enterprise). **Fail-Closed** → security priority (SCADA/ICS).
- **TAP > SPAN** for guaranteed lossless capture. SPAN is "free" but best-effort.
- **Firewall → IDPS → LAN** is the canonical order. Reverse order used when IDPS must see outer headers.
- **SIEM** is the *correlation brain*; **SOAR** is the *automation brain*; IDPS is the *sensor feeder*.
- **STIX (data format) + TAXII (transport)** = modern threat-intel sharing — replace legacy CSV/IP-list feeds.
- **CoA (RADIUS)** enables dynamic quarantine from a passive IDPS via NAC.
- **Key formula:** Sensor throughput $\geq \alpha \cdot B_{link}$ with safety factor $\alpha = 1.2$–$1.5$.
- **Key fact:** TCP RST cannot stop single-packet attacks; only inline IDPS can.
- **Key metric:** Modern inline sensors add **< 500 µs** latency; bypass failover **< 5 s**.
- **Acronyms to memorize:** NIPS, NIDS, DPI, TAP, SPAN, CEF, LEEF, STIX, TAXII, CoA, NAC, SOAR, ICS/SCADA, ZTNA.
- **Exam mantra:** Always draw the **diagram first** (3 marks secured) before writing prose.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Do not claim that "passive IDPS prevents attacks" — it *detects and triggers response*, but does not *prevent* in the strict sense.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indirect IDPS Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Indirect IDPS Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indirect IDPS Integration** is a deployment architecture in which the Intrusion Detection and Prevention System does **not** sit directly in the live data path (inline). Instead, the IDPS receives **replicas of network traffic, log records, or alert notifications** from intermediary network devices such as switches, routers, firewalls, or Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platforms, and performs detection/prevention logic on the mirrored data.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, indirect integration is also referred to as **"Passive / Out-of-Band IDPS Integration"** because the sensor operates *passively* on duplicated data streams while the actual production traffic continues to flow uninterrupted through the original network devices.

The three principal categories of indirect integration recognized in the syllabus are:

1. **Switch-based integration** (SPAN ports, mirror ports, network TAPs)
2. **Device-log-based integration** (Firewall, Router, Server log forwarding to IDPS/SIEM)
3. **SIEM / Centralized Management Platform integration** (correlation engines, SOAR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Direct Integration:** In *direct* integration, the IDPS is placed serially (inline) so that every packet physically traverses it. In *indirect* integration, the IDPS is *out-of-band* — it is a passive listener or a log consumer, never the active forwarding engine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Central CCTV Control Room":**
> Imagine a large university campus with 20 buildings. Instead of placing a security guard with a metal detector at the entrance of *every* building (direct/inline integration), the university installs **cameras** in each building that **mirror** their footage to a **single central monitoring room**.
>
> - The students still walk freely through the buildings — their path is **not blocked** by the cameras.
> - The security officer in the control room watches **copies** of the feeds and raises an alarm if he spots suspicious activity.
> - The cameras are the **network switches/TAPs**, the control room is the **IDPS sensor**, and the footage is the **mirrored traffic/logs**.
>
> This is precisely how indirect IDPS integration works: observe without interrupting.

## 1.3 Core Metrics in Indirect Integration

| Metric | Typical Value / Standard | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **SPAN Port Bandwidth** | 1 Gbps / 10 Gbps | Capacity of mirrored traffic replication |
| **TAP Insertion Loss** | < 0.3 dB | Signal degradation introduced by passive TAPs |
| **Log Forwarding Protocol** | **Syslog (UDP/TCP 514)**, SNMPv3 (UDP 161/162) | Industry-standard log shipping protocols |
| **SIEM Event Rate** | **EPS — Events Per Second** (typically 5,000–100,000 EPS) | Volume of log events the SIEM can ingest |
| **Detection Latency (Indirect)** | 200 ms – 5 s | Delay from event occurrence to IDPS alert |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 expects students to clearly differentiate between *direct* and *indirect* integration mechanisms and explain **why** indirect integration is preferred in high-throughput enterprise backbones (where inline placement would become a single point of failure).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of Indirect IDPS Integration

The indirect integration model consists of **four primary logical components**, each playing a specific role in the detection chain:

### 2.1.1 Traffic/Event Source
- The origin of the data under observation.
- Examples: a server, a router, a firewall, a database, an application.
- **Why it matters:** Indirect integration starts with a *source* that can be observed.

### 2.1.2 Replication/Mirroring Mechanism
- The device that copies live traffic or log events to a side channel.
- Three sub-types:
  - **SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) Port** — a software-configured mirror on a managed switch.
  - **Network TAP (Test Access Point)** — a hardware splitter that physically copies optical/electrical signals.
  - **Log Forwarder** — an agent (e.g., `rsyslog`, `syslog-ng`, NXLog) that ships logs over the network.

### 2.1.3 IDPS Sensor / Collector
- Receives the mirrored traffic or log stream.
- Performs signature matching, anomaly detection, or stateful protocol analysis.
- **Crucial point:** The sensor does NOT forward production traffic — it only *analyzes*.

### 2.1.4 Response Orchestrator (Optional)
- A SIEM or SOAR platform that correlates IDPS alerts with other security telemetry and triggers automated responses.
- Examples: Splunk, IBM QRadar, Elastic Security, Microsoft Sentinel.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — The "How"

The indirect integration process follows this structured sequence:

1. **Production traffic** flows normally between the user and the server.
2. The **mirror/TAP device** creates a duplicate copy of the traffic (or the source device generates a log entry for the event).
3. The duplicate data is transported over a **dedicated monitoring VLAN** or **physical monitoring port** to the IDPS sensor.
4. The IDPS sensor **parses**, **normalizes**, and **inspects** the duplicate stream using its detection engine.
5. On a positive detection, the sensor emits an **alert** (to syslog, SNMP trap, or to a SIEM).
6. The SIEM/analyst **correlates** the alert and decides whether to act.
7. The action — when required — is **executed indirectly**, typically by instructing an inline device (e.g., a firewall) to block the malicious source, since the IDPS itself is out-of-band.

## 2.3 Three Major Indirect Integration Topologies

### Topology A — Switch SPAN / TAP Integration
- **Mechanism:** Switch copies frames to a SPAN port; the IDPS sensor is connected to that port.
- **Use Case:** Traffic analysis, full-packet capture, NIDS mode.
- **Limitation:** SPAN ports can drop packets under congestion (oversubscription).

### Topology B — Firewall/UTM Log Integration
- **Mechanism:** Firewall sends connection logs (allowed/denied flows) to the IDPS or SIEM via **syslog**.
- **Use Case:** Detecting reconnaissance, policy violations, volumetric attacks at perimeter.
- **Limitation:** Visibility limited to *what the firewall sees*, not internal lateral traffic.

### Topology C — SIEM-Centric Integration
- **Mechanism:** Multiple devices (firewalls, servers, endpoints, IDPS) forward logs to a SIEM; the SIEM correlates events and may invoke the IDPS for deeper packet-level analysis.
- **Use Case:** Enterprise SOC (Security Operations Center) environments.

## 2.4 The "Why" — Advantages of Indirect Integration

- **No inline latency penalty** — production traffic is never delayed by the IDPS.
- **No single point of failure** — if the IDPS crashes, traffic continues to flow.
- **Simplified scaling** — additional sensors can be added without touching the production path.
- **Multi-source correlation** — log-based integration enables cross-device analytics.
- **Forensic preservation** — the original traffic is untouched, so post-incident analysis is reliable.

## 2.5 The "Trade-off" — Disadvantages of Indirect Integration

- **Cannot block inline in real time** — the IDPS cannot drop a malicious packet *in transit*; it must instruct another device to do so.
- **SPAN port oversubscription** — high-volume traffic may exceed the mirror port's capacity, causing silent packet loss in the IDPS.
- **Detection lag** — log-based integration introduces a delay (typically 1–10 seconds) between the event and the alert.
- **Encrypted traffic blindness** — the IDPS sees only the encrypted stream unless decryption is configured at the source device.
- **Coordinated response complexity** — requires pre-defined playbooks and API integrations.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SPAN Mirror Bandwidth Required** | $B_{mirror} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_{port_i}$ | $B_{mirror}$ = mirror port capacity, $B_{port_i}$ = mirrored source port bandwidth | Sizing the IDPS-connected switch port |
| **TAP Insertion Loss** | $L_{TAP} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | $P_{in}$ = input optical power, $P_{out}$ = output optical power | Optical link budget calculation |
| **SIEM Event Rate (EPS)** | $EPS = \dfrac{E_{total}}{T_{window}}$ | $E_{total}$ = events in window, $T_{window}$ = time in seconds | Capacity planning for SIEM ingestion |
| **Detection Latency (Indirect)** | $T_{detect} = T_{mirror} + T_{transport} + T_{analysis}$ | $T_{mirror}$ = mirroring delay, $T_{transport}$ = network delay, $T_{analysis}$ = sensor processing time | SLA design for SOC alerts |
| **Log Volume per Day** | $V_{log} = EPS \times 86400 \times S_{avg}$ | $S_{avg}$ = average log size in bytes | Storage sizing for IDPS/SIEM |
| **Effective Detection Coverage** | $C_{eff} = \dfrac{N_{monitored}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{monitored}$ = monitored sources, $N_{total}$ = total sources | Audit metric for SOC coverage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When answering theory questions, students are expected to **name** the protocol (e.g., *Syslog over UDP 514*) rather than just say "logs are sent." Always mention the protocol *and* the port number for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing a SPAN Mirror for an IDPS Sensor

**Problem Statement:**
A managed switch has **4 source ports**, each carrying traffic at the following rates:
- Port 1 (server): **600 Mbps**
- Port 2 (server): **400 Mbps**
- Port 3 (user VLAN trunk): **900 Mbps**
- Port 4 (guest Wi-Fi gateway): **200 Mbps**

The IDPS sensor is connected to a dedicated SPAN port. **What is the minimum SPAN port bandwidth required so that no mirrored frame is lost?**

### Step 1 — Identify the total traffic to be mirrored
The IDPS must receive a copy of *every* frame from all four sources. Hence the required mirror bandwidth equals the **arithmetic sum** of all source bandwidths.

### Step 2 — Apply the SPAN bandwidth formula
Using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
B_{mirror} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_{port_i}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the values
$$
B_{mirror} \geq B_{port_1} + B_{port_2} + B_{port_3} + B_{port_4}
$$

$$
B_{mirror} \geq 600 \text{ Mbps} + 400 \text{ Mbps} + 900 \text{ Mbps} + 200 \text{ Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{mirror} \geq 2100 \text{ Mbps} = 2.1 \text{ Gbps}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical engineering interpretation
Since standard switch ports come in fixed capacities (**1 Gbps** or **10 Gbps**), the next-valid configuration is a **10 Gbps SFP+ SPAN port**. This gives a **headroom factor** of:

$$
H = \frac{B_{available}}{B_{required}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ Mbps}}{2100 \text{ Mbps}} \approx 4.76\times
$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion
**The minimum SPAN port capacity required is 2.1 Gbps; in practice, a 10 Gbps SFP+ port must be provisioned to avoid packet loss during traffic bursts.** The KTU-typical full-sentence answer should explicitly state this conclusion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Computing SIEM Event Rate for a Firewall

**Problem Statement:**
A perimeter firewall generates an average of **3 log entries per connection** (start, end, bytes-transferred). During a one-hour monitoring window, the firewall records **1,80,000 connections**. **Calculate the EPS (Events Per Second) and the daily log volume, assuming each log entry averages 220 bytes.**

### Step 1 — Determine the total number of events in the window
$$
E_{total} = N_{connections} \times \text{logs per connection}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 180{,}000 \times 3 = 540{,}000 \text{ events}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the EPS formula
$$
EPS = \frac{E_{total}}{T_{window}}
$$

$$
EPS = \frac{540{,}000 \text{ events}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 150 \text{ events/second}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the daily log volume
$$
V_{log} = EPS \times 86400 \times S_{avg}
$$

$$
V_{log} = 150 \times 86{,}400 \times 220 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
V_{log} = 2{,}851{,}200{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to a more readable unit

$$
V_{log} = \frac{2{,}851{,}200{,}000}{1024^3} \approx 2.66 \text{ GiB/day}
$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion
The firewall produces **150 EPS** and approximately **2.66 GiB of logs per day**. A SIEM ingesting this stream must support at least this EPS load with a sustained headroom of ~30% to absorb spikes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how an apparently "quiet" firewall still generates a substantial daily log volume. This is why *indirect* IDPS integration through a SIEM is preferred over direct database logging — storage, indexing, and retention are centralized.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Detection Latency Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A log-based indirect IDPS integration has the following delay components:
- $T_{mirror}$ = 50 ms (log generation + kernel buffering)
- $T_{transport}$ = 30 ms (network transit from server to IDPS)
- $T_{analysis}$ = 120 ms (signature matching and correlation)

**Compute the total detection latency and classify it against typical SLA tiers.**

### Step 1 — Apply the latency formula

$$
T_{detect} = T_{mirror} + T_{transport} + T_{analysis}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$
T_{detect} = 50 \text{ ms} + 30 \text{ ms} + 120 \text{ ms} = 200 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Classification against SLA tiers
| SLA Tier | Typical Threshold | Our Value | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real-time inline blocking | < 50 ms | 200 ms | Not met (as expected for indirect) |
| Near-real-time alerting | 100 ms – 1 s | 200 ms | **Met** |
| Forensic / batch analysis | > 1 s | 200 ms | Exceeded |

### Step 4 — Conclusion
**The total detection latency is 200 ms, which comfortably meets the "near-real-time alerting" SLA tier typical of indirect IDPS deployments.** This justifies the use of indirect integration for SOC monitoring use cases.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Direct vs Indirect Integration

| Evaluation Parameter | Direct (Inline) IDPS | Indirect IDPS |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic visibility | Sees every packet live | Sees mirrored / logged copy |
| Inline blocking capability | **Yes** (drop, reset, quarantine) | **No** (instructs another device) |
| Single point of failure | **Yes** | **No** |
| Latency introduced | 100 µs – 5 ms | ~0 ms to production traffic |
| Deployment complexity | Moderate (requires network re-routing) | Low (passive tap or log shipper) |
| Forensic reliability | Original traffic modified | **Original untouched** |
| Use case fit | Perimeter IPS, critical segments | SOC, SIEM, post-event analysis |
| KTU 2024 expectation | 2-mark conceptual question | 7–14 mark design/analytical question |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Indirect IDPS Integration

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the logical flow of data in a typical *indirect* IDPS deployment integrating a switch SPAN, a firewall log forwarder, and a SIEM correlation engine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Workstations] -->|Legitimate Traffic| B[Core L3 Switch]
    C[Internet] -->|Ingress / Egress| B
    B -->|Production Forwarding| D[Internal Server Farm]
    B -.->|SPAN Mirror Copy| E[IDPS Sensor NIDS]
    F[Perimeter Firewall] -.->|Syslog UDP 514| G[SIEM Platform]
    E -.->|Alerts via API| G
    H[Endpoint Agents] -.->|Logs via Syslog| G
    G -->|Correlated Alerts| I[SOC Analyst Dashboard]
    I -->|Manual or Automated Action| F
    F -->|Block / Quarantine Rule| A
    I -->|Forensic Review| J[Log Archive]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = production traffic flow (untouched by the IDPS).
- **Dotted arrows** = telemetry, mirrored traffic, and alert propagation — the *indirect* integration paths.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Detection-to-Response Chain in Indirect Integration

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant Switch
    participant IDPS
    participant SIEM
    participant Firewall
    participant SOC

    Attacker->>Switch: Malicious Packet (Recon Scan)
    Switch-->>IDPS: Mirrored Copy via SPAN
    IDPS->>IDPS: Signature Match — "Port Scan Detected"
    IDPS->>SIEM: Alert Forwarded (CEF / Syslog)
    SIEM->>SIEM: Correlate with Firewall Logs
    SIEM->>SOC: High-Severity Alert
    SOC->>Firewall: Push Block Rule (API)
    Firewall-->>Attacker: Subsequent Packets Denied
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sources["Event / Traffic Sources"]
        S1[Servers]
        S2[Routers]
        S3[Firewalls]
        S4[Applications]
    end

    subgraph Collectors["Replication and Forwarding Layer"]
        R1[SPAN Port]
        R2[Network TAP]
        R3[Syslog Agent]
    end

    subgraph IDPS["IDPS Sensor Array"]
        I1[Packet Decoder]
        I2[Signature Engine]
        I3[Anomaly Engine]
    end

    subgraph Response["Response and Orchestration"]
        M1[SIEM]
        M2[SOAR Playbook]
        M3[Inline Firewall Instruction]
    end

    S1 --> R1
    S2 --> R1
    S2 --> R2
    S3 --> R3
    S4 --> R3
    R1 --> I1
    R2 --> I1
    R3 --> I1
    I1 --> I2
    I1 --> I3
    I2 --> M1
    I3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECCT742, Module 4]**
Define **Indirect IDPS Integration**. List any **two advantages** it offers over direct (inline) integration.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (2 marks):** Indirect IDPS Integration is a deployment model in which the IDPS does not process live traffic inline. Instead, it receives *copies* of network traffic via SPAN ports or network TAPs, or ingests *log records* from security devices via Syslog/SNMP, and performs detection analysis on the replicated data.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark, any two):**
> 1. The IDPS does **not introduce latency** into the production traffic path.
> 2. The IDPS is **not a single point of failure**; if it crashes, traffic continues to flow normally.
> 3. The **original traffic remains unmodified**, preserving forensic integrity.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECCT742, Module 4]**
Name the **two standard hardware/software mechanisms** used to mirror traffic to an IDPS sensor in a switch-based indirect integration. State **one limitation** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Mechanism 1 — SPAN Port (1 mark):** A software-configured port on a managed switch that mirrors frames from one or more source ports.
> *Limitation:* SPAN ports can suffer from **oversubscription**, silently dropping mirrored frames when the source traffic exceeds the SPAN port's capacity (typically 1 Gbps or 10 Gbps).
>
> **Mechanism 2 — Network TAP (1 mark):** A passive hardware device inserted inline on a physical link that splits the optical/electrical signal.
> *Limitation:* TAPs introduce a small **insertion loss** (< 0.3 dB) and require **physical interruption** of the link during installation, and they cannot easily filter traffic.
>
> *(1 mark reserved for coherence and accurate use of terminology.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** between two sub-parts. The student is given an internal choice and must answer **one** of the two alternatives.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, PECCT742, Module 4]**
**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three topologies** of indirect IDPS integration. Compare them on the basis of *data source*, *typical deployment*, and *limitation*.

**(b) [7 marks]** A managed switch has **six 1 Gbps source ports** that are to be mirrored to a single IDPS-connected SPAN port. Calculate the **minimum SPAN port bandwidth required**. If only 1 Gbps SPAN ports are available, what **engineering recommendation** would you provide?

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**1. Topology 1 — Switch SPAN / Mirror Port Integration (2.5 marks)**
- **Data Source:** Live network frames traversing the switch.
- **Typical Deployment:** Enterprise campus, data center server farm, where the IDPS performs Network-based Intrusion Detection (NIDS).
- **Working:** The switch is configured to copy ingress/egress frames of selected source ports to a designated SPAN (mirror) port, to which the IDPS sensor is connected.
- **Limitation:** Mirror ports are subject to oversubscription — under traffic bursts, mirrored frames are silently dropped, leading to incomplete detection.

**2. Topology 2 — Firewall/UTM Log Integration (2 marks)**
- **Data Source:** Syslog records (allowed connections, denied connections, threat events) emitted by the firewall.
- **Typical Deployment:** Perimeter, DMZ, and cloud-edge deployments.
- **Working:** The firewall forwards structured log entries to the IDPS or directly to a SIEM over UDP/TCP port **514** using the Syslog protocol (RFC 5424).
- **Limitation:** Visibility is restricted to *firewall-perceived* events — internal east-west traffic and post-decryption flows are not visible to the IDPS.

**3. Topology 3 — SIEM-Centric Multi-Source Integration (2.5 marks)**
- **Data Source:** Logs from heterogeneous devices (firewalls, servers, endpoints, applications, IDPS itself).
- **Typical Deployment:** Enterprise SOC, MSSP environments.
- **Working:** A SIEM (e.g., Splunk, QRadar, Elastic) acts as the central correlation engine. The IDPS may push alerts to the SIEM, and the SIEM may query the IDPS via API for deeper context.
- **Limitation:** Complex to configure; correlation rules must be carefully tuned to avoid alert fatigue; depends on time synchronization (NTP) across all sources.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.3 should be redrawn neatly for full marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Apply the SPAN bandwidth formula**

$$
B_{mirror} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_{port_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$
B_{mirror} \geq 6 \times 1 \text{ Gbps} = 6 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result against available port speeds**
Standard switch SPAN ports come in two common capacities: **1 Gbps (RJ-45/SFP)** and **10 Gbps (SFP+)**. The 6 Gbps requirement **exceeds** a 1 Gbps SPAN port's capacity and **fits comfortably** within a 10 Gbps SFP+ SPAN port.

**Step 4 — Engineering recommendation (3 marks)**
- **Recommendation:** Provision a **10 Gbps SFP+ SPAN port** for the IDPS sensor connection.
- **Justification:**
  - A 1 Gbps SPAN port would suffer oversubscription and silently drop mirrored frames during peak loads, leading to **missed detections**.
  - A 10 Gbps SPAN port provides a **headroom factor** of:
  $$
  H = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ Mbps}}{6000 \text{ Mbps}} \approx 1.67\times
  $$
  - This 67% headroom absorbs typical traffic bursts (e.g., backup windows, broadcast storms).
- **Alternative approach:** If 10 Gbps SPAN is unavailable, **distribute the load** across two IDPS sensors, each receiving a SPAN of 3 source ports, effectively halving the per-sensor requirement to 3 Gbps.

**Step 5 — Conclusion**
The minimum SPAN port capacity is **6 Gbps**, and a **10 Gbps SFP+ SPAN port is the recommended engineering choice** to ensure lossless mirroring under all load conditions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, PECCT742, Module 4]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **direct (inline)** and **indirect (out-of-band)** IDPS integration. Compare them on **at least six parameters**, and identify two scenarios where indirect integration is **clearly preferred**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A SOC deploys an indirect IDPS integrated with a SIEM. The SIEM ingests logs at an average rate of **8,50,000 events per hour**. Compute the **EPS (Events Per Second)** and the **daily log volume** if each event averages **180 bytes**. Comment on the **storage planning** implications.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Direct (Inline) IDPS | Indirect IDPS |
|---|---|---|
| Placement | In the live data path (serial) | Out-of-band, on mirrored/log data |
| Blocking capability | **Yes** — can drop, reset, or rate-limit packets | **No** — must instruct another inline device |
| Latency introduced | Yes (typically 100 µs – 5 ms) | None to production traffic |
| Single point of failure | **Yes** — failure breaks connectivity | **No** — passive observation |
| Forensic integrity | Traffic may be modified/dropped | Original traffic is preserved |
| Deployment complexity | Higher (network re-routing required) | Lower (TAP or log agent) |
| Detection latency | Real-time | Near-real-time (200 ms – 10 s) |
| Best-suited environment | Critical perimeters, IPS mode | SOC, SIEM, NIDS mode |

**Two scenarios where indirect integration is preferred (2 marks):**
1. **High-throughput enterprise backbones (e.g., 40 Gbps / 100 Gbps data center cores):** An inline IDPS would become a bottleneck and a single point of failure; indirect integration preserves full bandwidth.
2. **SOC/SIEM environments requiring multi-source correlation:** Log-based indirect integration allows the IDPS alerts to be correlated with firewall, EDR, and identity logs in a central platform.
3. *(Optional valid third scenario — 0.5 bonus credit may be awarded for a third valid point.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Calculate EPS**

$$
EPS = \frac{E_{total}}{T_{window}} = \frac{850{,}000 \text{ events}}{3600 \text{ s}} \approx 236.11 \text{ EPS}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate daily event count**

$$
E_{daily} = EPS \times 86{,}400 = 236.11 \times 86{,}400 \approx 2{,}04{,}00{,}000 \text{ events/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate daily log volume**

$$
V_{log} = E_{daily} \times S_{avg} = 20{,}40{,}00{,}000 \times 180 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
V_{log} = 3{,}67{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 34.2 \text{ GiB/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Storage planning implications (3 marks)**
- A **30-day retention** policy would require:
  $$
  S_{30d} = 34.2 \text{ GiB} \times 30 \approx 1026 \text{ GiB} \approx 1 \text{ TiB}
  $$
- A **365-day retention** (typical compliance) would require:
  $$
  S_{365d} \approx 12.4 \text{ TiB}
  $$
- **Engineering recommendations:**
  - Allocate **at least 30% headroom** above the calculated figure to absorb **EPS spikes** (e.g., during attack windows or scheduled scans).
  - Implement **log compression** (typically 5:1 ratio for syslog text) to reduce on-disk storage to ~**2.5 TiB** for 365-day retention.
  - Use **tiered storage** — hot (SSD) for last 7 days, warm (HDD) for 8–90 days, cold (object storage / tape) for 90+ days.

**Step 5 — Conclusion**
The SIEM ingests **~236 EPS**, producing **~34.2 GiB/day**, totalling **~12.4 TiB/year** uncompressed. Tiered, compressed storage is essential for cost-effective long-term retention.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SPAN and TAP:** Many students use the two terms interchangeably. *SPAN* is a **software-based** switch mirror; *TAP* is a **hardware-based** physical splitter. Marking impact: up to **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in EPS calculation:** Writing "EPS = 850000" without dividing by 3600 is a frequent error. Always show the substitution step.
> 3. **Stating "logs are sent" without naming the protocol:** Always specify **Syslog over UDP 514** (or TCP 601 for reliable transport) for full credit.
> 4. **Claiming indirect IDPS can "block" packets:** It **cannot** — the IDPS must instruct an inline device. This is a classic conceptual trap. Marking impact: **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 5. **Omitting the block diagram in design questions:** A 7-mark design question without a diagram typically attracts a **−2 mark** penalty. Always redraw a neat, labeled block diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Indirect IDPS Integration**

- **Definition (1 line):** IDPS receives **mirrored traffic** or **device logs** for analysis; it is **not** in the live forwarding path.
- **Three topologies:** (i) Switch SPAN / TAP, (ii) Firewall/UTM log integration, (iii) SIEM-centric multi-source correlation.
- **Key mirroring mechanisms:** **SPAN port** (software, switch-based) and **Network TAP** (hardware, physical).
- **Log-forwarding protocols to remember:** **Syslog (UDP 514 / TCP 601)** and **SNMPv3 traps (UDP 162)**.
- **Cannot block inline** — must instruct an inline device (typically a firewall) via API or ACL push.
- **Headroom rule:** Always size mirror bandwidth at **≥ sum of source port bandwidths**, and provision the next-valid standard capacity (e.g., 10 Gbps SFP+).
- **SIEM sizing rule:** EPS = Total Events / Time Window in seconds. Add **30% headroom** for traffic spikes.
- **Latency tier:** Indirect IDPS typically delivers **near-real-time alerting (200 ms – 5 s)**, not true real-time blocking.
- **Forensic advantage:** Original traffic is unmodified → easier post-incident analysis and legal admissibility.
- **Encryption caveat:** Indirect IDPS **cannot inspect encrypted traffic** unless the source device performs TLS decryption before mirroring.
- **KTU 2024 weightage expectation:** Module 4 typically contributes **~15–20%** of the end-semester paper, with at least one **7-mark analytical/numerical question** on integration design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT) Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT) Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT) Software

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT)** is a specialized class of software/hardware appliance designed to **capture, store, replay, and analytically inspect** network traffic at the packet, session, and application layers in order to reconstruct events, extract evidentiary artefacts, and support post-incident investigation, legal prosecution, and root-cause analysis of cyber incidents.

NFATs occupy a distinctive position in the security operations stack — they are **not primarily detection engines** (like an IDS) but **investigative reconstruction platforms** that perform *time-correlated, deep-packet retrospective analysis* over captured traffic.

### Key Distinction from IDPS

| Tool Class | Primary Function | Timing |
|------------|------------------|--------|
| NIDS/NIPS | Real-time anomaly/misuse detection | **Live/Inline** |
| **NFAT** | Full-content reconstruction & forensic analysis | **Retrospective/Offline** |
| SIEM | Log correlation across heterogeneous sources | Hybrid |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The CCTV Forensics Lab of the Network**
> Imagine a city's **CCTV control room**. Live monitoring (NIDS) is like a security guard watching screens in real-time. But an NFAT is the **forensic investigator's replay room** — every byte of every packet is recorded on a massive black-box recorder, days or weeks in advance. When something suspicious happens, the investigator doesn't just "see" it; they can **rewind, zoom into any frame, reconstruct a suspect's entire movement, and pull out clear photographic evidence**. An NFAT is the **black-box flight recorder + forensic analyst** combined for a computer network.

### Defining Properties of an NFAT (Mandatory Standards)

- **Full Packet Capture (FPC)** — captures entire L2–L7 payload, not just headers.
- **Long-term indexed storage** — typically **30–90 days** of online retention, with archival tier beyond.
- **Session/flow reconstruction** — reassembles TCP streams, VoIP calls, file transfers.
- **Content carving & extraction** — pulls out transferred files, images, credentials, emails.
- **Geo-IP mapping & visualization** — spatial representation of flows.
- **Search & query engine** — Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF), regex, signature-based drill-down.
- **Tamper-evident storage** — cryptographic hashing (e.g., **SHA-256**) of evidence blobs.
- **Forensically sound** — chain-of-custody, write-blocked capture, time-synchronized (NTP, **± 100 ms drift**).

### Standard Performance Metrics (Industry Norms)

- **Capture rate:** 1 Gbps – 100 Gbps line-rate
- **Storage:** petabyte-class, rotating, deduplicated
- **Time-to-first-byte (TTFB) for replay:** < 5 s
- **Indexing speed:** ≥ 10 Gbps sustained
- **Mean search latency over 1 PB:** 30 – 120 s

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an NFAT

An NFAT, irrespective of vendor, follows a **five-stage functional pipeline**:

1. **Tap / Span Port Ingestion** — passive optical/electrical tap or switch-mirrored SPAN port feeds traffic to a sensor.
2. **Capture & Pre-processing** — libpcap/PF_RING/DPDK ring-buffers drop frames, normalize timestamps.
3. **Indexing & Storage** — flow records (NetFlow v5/v9, IPFIX), DPI metadata, and full packets are indexed (typically via **Elasticsearch / Lucene** or proprietary indices).
4. **Analysis & Reconstruction** — TCP stream reassembly (per RFC 793), VoIP (SIP/RTP) call reconstruction, HTTP transaction carving.
5. **Presentation & Export** — GUI dashboards, PCAP slicing, evidence packaging (e.g., chain-of-custody XML, **EWF/E01** disk images).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Exam questions frequently test the *difference between an NFAT and a Network IDS*. The single most important answer is: **NFATs are passive, retroactive, full-payload tools used AFTER the fact for investigation and evidence**, while NIDS are real-time, header-and-light-DPI tools used DURING the event for detection and response.

## 2.2 Core Analytical Operations Performed by NFATs

### A. Packet-Level Operations
- Raw hex dump and dissection (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application).
- BPF filtering (e.g., `tcp port 443 and host 10.0.0.5`).
- TCP flag / option analysis (SYN, SYN-ACK, RST, FIN, ECE, CWR).
- TTL, DF-bit, IP-ID, and TOS field forensics.

### B. Flow-Level Operations
- Bidirectional flow aggregation (5-tuple key).
- Volume, duration, packet-count statistics.
- Anomalous flow detection (e.g., beaconing, slow exfil).
- VoIP/SIP call ladder diagrams.

### C. Content/Carving Operations
- File extraction (HTTP, SMTP, FTP, SMB, TFTP).
- Image extraction and EXIF stripping.
- Credential / cookie recovery.
- Malware artefact extraction (PE headers, shellcode, dropped binaries).

### D. Application-Layer Reconstruction
- HTTP transaction tree (Request → Response → Cookies → Redirects).
- Email thread reconstruction (MIME multipart).
- DNS query/response timeline.
- TLS session metadata (SNI, SAN, JA3/JA3S fingerprints).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Engineering Utility |
|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Packet Loss Probability (Capture) | $P_{loss} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot t_{drop}}$ | Poisson-arrival model for ring-buffer drop probability; critical to size capture memory. |
| Mean Inter-Arrival Time (Flow) | $\bar{\Delta t} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}(t_{i+1} - t_i)$ | Used in beaconing detection. |
| Throughput (bytes/sec) | $T = \dfrac{B}{T_{dur}}$ | $B$ = total bytes; $T_{dur}$ = flow duration. |
| Storage Requirement | $S = R \times D \times 8$ bits | $R$ = link rate (bps), $D$ = retention in seconds. **At 10 Gbps × 30 days = 3.24 PB raw**. |
| Hash Integrity (Evidence) | $H = \mathrm{SHA\text{-}256}(M)$ | Tamper detection; chain-of-custody. |
| Shannon Entropy (Payload) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Encrypted/compressed exfil detection. |
| JA3 Hash | $J = \mathrm{MD5}(\mathrm{dec}(C_{ext}) \mid \mathrm{dec}(C_{cipher}))$ | TLS client fingerprinting for malware C2 identification. |
| Packet Loss from Buffer | $L_b = \max\!\left(0,\; \lambda - \mu\right) \cdot t$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate. |
| Hash Chain Block (Forensic) | $H_{n} = \mathrm{SHA}(H_{n-1} \mid D_{n} \mid t_{n})$ | Append-only evidence ledger. |
| Reconstruction Index | $I_{rec} = \dfrac{\text{assembled segments}}{\text{expected segments}}$ | Stream integrity metric, 0 ≤ I ≤ 1. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **Storage Requirement equation** $S = R \times D \times 8$ is the single most-asked NFAT formula. Be ready to compute retention requirements for a given line-rate — for instance, "A 1 Gbps link retained for 7 days requires $\approx$ **756 GB** of storage, and at 10 Gbps for 90 days this balloons to $\approx$ **9.7 TB compressed** (≈ 97 TB raw)."

## 2.4 Why and How — The Engineering Logic

> **WHY is full-packet capture so expensive?**
> Because storing L2–L7 payloads at line-rate for 30+ days is fundamentally a **storage-bandwidth-cost tradeoff**. A single 10 Gbps link, captured 24×7, produces ≈ **108 TB/day** of raw data. Hence, modern NFATs use **indexed selective retention** — keep metadata and session metadata indefinitely, but rotate full payloads through compression (typically **zstd / LZ4**, ratios 2:1 to 3:1).

> **HOW does session reconstruction tolerate packet loss?**
> TCP reassembly buffers out-of-order segments up to a **reassembly window** (default 64 KB per RFC 791 / 793). For forensic completeness, NFATs use **per-flow sliding window tracking** and report incomplete reconstructions as `partial-confidence` artefacts.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Incident Response (IR):** Determine lateral movement, dwell-time, exfil volume.
- **Legal / e-Discovery:** Court-admissible evidence under **Federal Rules of Evidence (Rule 803(6))** — Records of a Regularly Conducted Activity.
- **Threat Hunting:** Hypothesis-driven retrospective search ("Did host X contact any IOC Y in the last 90 days?").
- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX, GDPR data-flow auditability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Requirement for Full-Packet Capture

**Problem (KTU-style):** A network operations team deploys an NFAT to monitor a **10 Gbps** backbone link. The team requires **30 days** of full-packet retention with a **2.5:1** compression ratio. Calculate (a) raw storage, (b) effective storage, and (c) the number of **8 TB** HDDs required in a RAID-6 array with 25% parity overhead.

### Step 1 — Convert rate to bytes per second

$$
R_{bytes} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bits/sec}}{8} = 1.25 \times 10^{9}\ \text{B/s}
$$

**Logic:** Divide the bit-rate (10 Gbps) by 8 to get bytes; this is the **raw** traffic volume that arrives at the NFAT sensor per second.

### Step 2 — Convert retention period to seconds

$$
D_{sec} = 30\ \text{days} \times 24\ \text{h/day} \times 3600\ \text{s/h} = 2{,}592{,}000\ \text{s}
$$

**Logic:** Standard time-unit conversion; 1 day = 86,400 s, hence 30 days = 2.592 × 10⁶ s.

### Step 3 — Compute raw (uncompressed) storage

$$
S_{raw} = R_{bytes} \times D_{sec} = 1.25 \times 10^{9} \times 2.592 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
S_{raw} = 3.24 \times 10^{15}\ \text{bytes} = 3.24\ \text{PB}
$$

**Logic:** Multiply sustained throughput by total duration — gives the cumulative volume of payload that must be captured.

### Step 4 — Apply compression ratio (2.5:1)

$$
S_{eff} = \dfrac{S_{raw}}{2.5} = \dfrac{3.24\ \text{PB}}{2.5} = 1.296\ \text{PB}
$$

**Logic:** zstd / LZ4 typical on PCAP; encryption/compressed traffic is incompressible and will degrade this ratio.

### Step 5 — Add RAID-6 parity overhead (25%)

$$
S_{RAID6} = S_{eff} \times 1.25 = 1.296 \times 1.25 = 1.62\ \text{PB}
$$

**Logic:** RAID-6 reserves 2 disks' worth of parity per array; for an 8-disk array that's 2/8 = 25% overhead.

### Step 6 — Compute the number of 8 TB drives

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{1.62 \times 10^{15}}{8 \times 10^{12}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 202.5 \right\rceil = 203\ \text{drives}
$$

**Result (final):** The team requires **203 × 8 TB drives** ≈ **203 drives of 8 TB** in RAID-6, excluding hot-spares (typically add 2 spares → **205 drives**).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: NFAT Tool Comparison

| Tool | Type | Capture | Protocol Depth | Open Source | Typical Use Case |
|------|------|---------|----------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Wireshark** | Packet analyzer | Live + offline | 2000+ dissectors | Yes (GPL) | Deep packet dissection, education |
| **tcpdump / TShark** | CLI capture | Live + offline | L3–L4 (light L7) | Yes (BSD) | Headless capture, scripting |
| **NetworkMiner** | NFAT (GUI) | Live + offline | L7 file carving | Free/Commercial | Forensics on PCAPs, credential recovery |
| **Xplico** | NFAT (web-based) | Live + offline | SIP/HTTP/IMAP reconstruction | Yes (GPL) | VoIP & web mail forensics |
| **NetWitness Investigator** | Enterprise NFAT | Live (appliance) | Full L7 + metadata | Commercial | Enterprise IR, e-discovery |
| **Solera DeepSee** | Enterprise NFAT | 10/40/100 Gbps | Full + DPI | Commercial | ISP-scale forensics |
| **Niksun NetDetector** | Enterprise NFAT appliance | Multi-Gbps | Full + DB | Commercial | Compliance, government |
| **Moloch** | Open-source NFAT | 1–10 Gbps | Full + ElasticSearch | Yes (Apache) | Large-scale capture with search |
| **Arkime (Moloch v3)** | Open-source NFAT | 1–10 Gbps | Full + WISE | Yes (Apache) | Modern alternative to Moloch |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Beaconing Detection Using Inter-Arrival Time

A compromised host exhibits 50 outbound HTTPS connections to a single C2 IP over 24 hours. The inter-arrival deltas in seconds are:

$$
\Delta t = \{1200, 1198, 1202, 1199, 1201, 1200, 1203, 1197, 1201, 1200, \ldots\}
$$

### Step 1 — Mean inter-arrival time

$$
\bar{\Delta t} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (t_{i+1} - t_i)
$$

For $n = 50$ samples, summing all deltas yields $\Sigma \Delta t_i = 59{,}900$ s, hence:

$$
\bar{\Delta t} = \dfrac{59{,}900}{49} \approx 1222.4\ \text{s} \approx 20.36\ \text{minutes}
$$

### Step 2 — Standard deviation (jitter)

$$
\sigma_{\Delta t} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}(\Delta t_i - \bar{\Delta t})^2} \approx 1.87\ \text{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Coefficient of Variation (CV) — beaconing indicator

$$
\mathrm{CV} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\Delta t}}{\bar{\Delta t}} = \dfrac{1.87}{1222.4} \approx 0.0015
$$

**Interpretation:** A CV < 0.05 indicates **highly periodic beaconing** — classic C2 channel behaviour. The NFAT surfaces this as a **visualization spike** in the flow-timeline view.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NFAT High-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NETWORK TAP or SPAN PORT] --> B[PACKET CAPTURE ENGINE]
    B --> C[DEEP PACKET INSPECTION MODULE]
    B --> D[FLOW RECORDER NetFlow IPFIX]
    C --> E[SESSION RECONSTRUCTOR]
    C --> F[FILE CARVING ENGINE]
    D --> G[METADATA INDEX Elasticsearch]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[SEARCH AND QUERY API]
    H --> I[ANALYST GUI DASHBOARD]
    H --> J[EVIDENCE EXPORT E01 PCAP]
    G --> K[(LONG-TERM COLD STORAGE)]
    L[NTP TIME SYNC] --> B
    M[CRYPTO HASH CHAIN] --> K
```

## 4.2 NFAT Processing Pipeline (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[INGRESS L2 FRAME] --> S2[BPF FILTER APPLY]
    S2 --> S3[TIMESTAMP NORMALIZATION]
    S3 --> S4[PROTOCOL DISSECTION L3 L4]
    S4 --> S5[STREAM REASSEMBLY TCP UDP]
    S4 --> S6[APPLICATION DECODE TLS HTTP SIP]
    S5 --> S7[CONTENT EXTRACTION]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[INDEXING LUCENE INDEX]
    S8 --> S9[STORAGE TIER HOT WARM COLD]
    S8 --> S10[ALERT CORRELATION WITH IDS]
    S9 --> S11[ANALYST QUERY INTERFACE]
    S10 --> S11
    S11 --> S12[FORENSIC REPORT EXPORT]
```

## 4.3 NFAT vs NIDS Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Incident Already Occurred?} -->|YES| NFAT[USE NFAT RETROSPECTIVE]
    Q1 -->|NO - LIVE| Q2{Need to Block Traffic Inline?}
    Q2 -->|YES| NIPS[USE NIPS]
    Q2 -->|NO| NIDS[USE NIDS]
    NFAT --> A1[Full payload replay]
    NFAT --> A2[Session reconstruction]
    NFAT --> A3[Legal evidence chain]
    NIDS --> A4[Real-time alerting]
    NIDS --> A5[Header-based detection]
    NIPS --> A6[Inline blocking]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**"Define Network Forensic Analysis Tool (NFAT). List any four of its distinguishing features."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **NFAT** is a software/hardware system that captures, stores, and analytically inspects network traffic for **post-incident forensic reconstruction and evidence extraction**.

Four distinguishing features:
1. **Full Packet Capture (FPC)** at L2–L7.
2. **Long-term indexed storage** (30–90 days online, archival beyond).
3. **Session & application-layer reconstruction** (TCP streams, VoIP calls, HTTP transactions).
4. **Tamper-evident storage** with cryptographic hash chains for chain-of-custody.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for features — keep crisp)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
**"Differentiate between an NIDS and an NFAT in terms of timing, payload depth, and primary use."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | NIDS | NFAT |
|-----------|------|------|
| Timing | Real-time, live | Retrospective, post-hoc |
| Payload depth | Header + light DPI | Full L2–L7 payload |
| Primary use | Detection & alerting | Investigation & evidence |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the **functional architecture** of a Network Forensic Analysis Tool with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each component in detail. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss **Wireshark, NetworkMiner, and Moloch** as representative NFATs. Compare them on capture speed, reconstruction capability, and typical deployment scenario. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The functional architecture of an NFAT consists of **five stages**:

1. **Ingestion Stage (1 mark):** Traffic is mirrored from a switch's **SPAN port** or fed via a passive **network tap**. The tap ensures no inline latency and is forensically transparent.

2. **Capture & Pre-processing Stage (1 mark):** A high-performance capture engine (e.g., **PF_RING, DPDK**) places frames into a **ring buffer**. Packets are time-stamped using **NTP-synchronized clocks** (drift < **±100 ms**). BPF filters may be applied pre-capture.

3. **Indexing & Storage Stage (2 marks):** Three parallel data streams are stored:
   - **Full raw packets** (PCAP-NG) on rotating storage.
   - **Flow records** (NetFlow v9/IPFIX) for volumetric analytics.
   - **Indexed metadata** (Lucene/Elasticsearch) for fast queries.
   Storage uses a **hot–warm–cold tier** — recent captures on SSD, older on HDD, archival on object storage.

4. **Analysis & Reconstruction Stage (2 marks):**
   - **TCP stream reassembly** (per RFC 793) — segments are ordered by sequence number.
   - **Application decoders** — TLS (with JA3 fingerprints), HTTP, SIP/RTP, SMTP, DNS.
   - **File carving** — recovers transferred files, images, credentials.
   - **Session correlation** — maps user → IP → flow → file events.

5. **Presentation & Export Stage (1 mark):** Web-based GUI with timeline, geo-map, and pivot-search. Evidence export produces **PCAP-NG, E01 forensic images, or chain-of-custody XML** for court admission.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | Wireshark | NetworkMiner | Moloch / Arkime |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Capture speed** | < 1 Gbps (userspace) | < 1 Gbps | 1–10 Gbps |
| **Reconstruction** | Manual stream-follow | Automatic file carving | Session metadata + ES search |
| **Protocols** | 2000+ dissectors | 100+ (L7 focus) | 100+ (scalable) |
| **Storage** | Local file only | Local file | Cluster (Elasticsearch) |
| **GUI** | Desktop GUI | Desktop GUI | Web GUI |
| **Use case** | Education, deep-dive | Single-host forensics | Enterprise-scale, multi-petabyte |
| **License** | Open (GPL) | Free + Commercial | Open (Apache) |

Wireshark excels at **single-trace deep-dive analysis**; NetworkMiner specializes in **automatic file/credential carving**; Moloch/Arkime is engineered for **cluster-scale, search-driven forensic triage** at ISP/carrier scale.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model, Module 4]**
**(a)** Define NFAT. With a neat diagram, describe the **end-to-end workflow** of an NFAT from packet capture to evidence export. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** An organization wants to retain **full-packet capture** on a **5 Gbps** link for **45 days**. Calculate:
- (i) Raw uncompressed storage in TB.
- (ii) Compressed storage at **2:1** ratio.
- (iii) Number of **4 TB** drives required in a **RAID-5** array (single-parity, ~25% overhead). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** NFAT = Network Forensic Analysis Tool; a system for retrospective capture, storage, and analysis of network traffic for incident investigation and legal evidence.

**Workflow (6 marks, 1 each step):**

1. **Tap / SPAN** — Passive capture from a network tap or mirrored port.
2. **Capture** — Packets captured into ring-buffer (e.g., libpcap, PF_RING).
3. **Time-stamping** — NTP/PTP synchronized to ±100 ms.
4. **Indexing** — Flow records + metadata indexed in Elasticsearch/Lucene.
5. **Storage** — Hot (SSD) + Warm (HDD) + Cold (tape/object) tiers.
6. **Reconstruction & Analysis** — TCP/HTTP/VoIP reassembly, file carving.
7. **Search & Pivot** — Analyst queries metadata for IOCs.
8. **Export** — PCAP-NG, E01, court-admissible evidence package.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Raw storage:**

$$
S_{raw} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^9}{8} \times (45 \times 86400) = 6.25 \times 10^8 \times 3.888 \times 10^6
$$

$$
S_{raw} = 2.43 \times 10^{15}\ \text{bytes} = 2.43\ \text{PB} = 2430\ \text{TB}
$$

**(ii) Compressed at 2:1:**

$$
S_{comp} = \dfrac{2430}{2} = 1215\ \text{TB}
$$

**(iii) Number of 4 TB drives in RAID-5 (25% overhead):**

$$
S_{RAID5} = 1215 \times 1.25 = 1518.75\ \text{TB}
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{1518.75}{4} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 379.69 \right\rceil = 380\ \text{drives}
$$

**Final Answer (3 marks full):** **380 × 4 TB drives** (plus typically 1–2 hot-spares → 382 drives).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Confusing NFAT with NIDS** (loses 2–3 marks instantly). Remember: NFAT = **retrospective + full-payload + evidence**, NIDS = **real-time + header + detection**.
> - **Storage calculation mistakes:** Forgetting to convert **Gbps → GB/s** (divide by 8) and **days → seconds** (multiply by 86,400). Always show unit conversion steps.
> - **Skipping the compression step:** Most students omit the compression ratio — examiners specifically test this.
> - **RAID overhead confusion:** RAID-5 = single-parity ≈ 25% (for 4-disk array, it's actually 1/N where N is disk count). State the assumption explicitly.
> - **No diagram:** KTU ESE part (a) questions without a block diagram lose 1–2 marks. Always include a labelled Mermaid or sketch.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **NFAT** = **N**etwork **F**orensic **A**nalysis **T**ool — **retrospective**, full-payload, evidence-oriented.
- ✅ **Primary purpose:** post-incident investigation, reconstruction, legal evidence — **NOT real-time detection**.
- ✅ **Five-stage pipeline:** Tap → Capture → Index/Store → Reconstruct → Export.
- ✅ **Storage equation:** $S = R_{Gbps} \times D_{days} \times 86400 \times \dfrac{1}{8}$ bytes.
- ✅ **Full-packet capture** at 10 Gbps ≈ **108 TB/day** raw → drives petabyte-scale infrastructure.
- ✅ **Forensic integrity:** SHA-256 hash chains, NTP time-sync (drift ±100 ms), write-blocked storage.
- ✅ **Key tools to know:** **Wireshark, tcpdump, NetworkMiner, Xplico, Moloch/Arkime, NetWitness, Solera, Niksun**.
- ✅ **Wireshark** = deep-dive single trace; **NetworkMiner** = automatic file carving; **Moloch** = cluster-scale search.
- ✅ **Beaconing detection:** low Coefficient of Variation (CV < 0.05) over inter-arrival deltas = periodic C2.
- ✅ **JA3 / JA3S** = TLS client/server fingerprint hashes for malware identification.
- ✅ **NFAT outputs are court-admissible** under FRE Rule 803(6) when chain-of-custody is maintained.
- ✅ **Distinguish from SIEM** — SIEM correlates logs; NFAT reconstructs traffic payloads.
- ✅ **Three storage tiers:** Hot (SSD) → Warm (HDD) → Cold (tape/object), typical 30/60/365+ days.
- ✅ **Reconstruction index** $I_{rec} = \dfrac{\text{assembled}}{\text{expected}}$ — quantifies forensic completeness.
- ✅ **KTU mantra:** *"NFATs are the black-box flight recorders of networks — they don't prevent the crash, but they tell you exactly why it happened."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anti-Malware Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Anti-Malware Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anti-Malware Technologies** constitute a comprehensive suite of software, hardware, and procedural defense mechanisms engineered to **detect, prevent, quarantine, and remediate** malicious software (malware) from compromising computing systems, networks, and data assets. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum for *PECCT742 – Intrusion Detection & Prevention System*, anti-malware is positioned as a **complementary layer to IDPS (Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems)**, focusing specifically on file-level and binary-level threats rather than network traffic anomalies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** Anti-malware technologies are studied under the broader umbrella of *host-based and hybrid defense systems*, emphasizing the transition from **reactive signature matching** to **proactive behavioral and AI-driven detection**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a highly trained **security guard at the entrance of a gated community**:

| Real-World Analogy | Anti-Malware Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Resident ID cards | **Digital Signatures** (known-good file hashes) |
| Visitor list / photo match | **Signature Database** (known malware patterns) |
| Watching a stranger loiter suspiciously | **Heuristic / Behavior Monitoring** |
| Quarantining an unknown person in a holding room | **Sandboxing** |
| Banning known criminals from entry | **Blacklisting** |
| Only allowing pre-approved guests | **Application Whitelisting** |

The guard does not just *check IDs* (signature scanning) — they also *observe behavior* (heuristics), *isolate suspects* (sandboxing), and *maintain watchlists* (blacklists). Modern anti-malware operates on exactly the same **multi-layered philosophy**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **True Positive Rate (TPR):** Probability that real malware is correctly flagged.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Probability that benign software is incorrectly flagged.
- **Detection Latency:** Time between malware execution and detection (measured in **milliseconds**).
- **Signature Database Size:** Typically ranges from **10 million to 100 million entries** in enterprise-grade products.
- **Zero-Day Vulnerability Window:** The time gap between malware release and signature availability, historically **0 to 72 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Malware:** Any software intentionally designed to cause damage to, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to a computer system. The KTU 2024 module classifies malware into **8 canonical categories** (see Section 2).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Malware (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

Anti-malware technologies are designed to counter the following malware classes, each requiring distinct detection strategies:

1. **Virus** — Attaches itself to a legitimate program; spreads via user action.
2. **Worm** — Self-replicating; propagates autonomously over networks.
3. **Trojan Horse** — Disguises as legitimate software; carries hidden payload.
4. **Ransomware** — Encrypts user data and demands payment (e.g., *WannaCry*, *LockBit*).
5. **Spyware** — Covertly monitors user activity and exfiltrates data.
6. **Adware** — Displays unsolicited advertisements; often a delivery vector.
7. **Rootkit** — Gains privileged (root/admin) access and hides its presence.
8. **Botnet Agent** — Compromised host controlled remotely via C2 (Command & Control) server.

### 2.2 Layered Anti-Malware Architecture (Defense-in-Depth)

Modern anti-malware solutions implement a **layered defense model**:

- **Layer 1 — Prevention (Pre-execution):** Application whitelisting, email filtering, web filtering, exploit mitigation (DEP, ASLR).
- **Layer 2 — Detection (At/Post-execution):** Signature scanning, heuristic analysis, behavior monitoring.
- **Layer 3 — Containment (During attack):** Sandboxing, network isolation, process termination.
- **Layer 4 — Recovery (Post-incident):** Quarantine, file restoration, system rollback, forensic logging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus stresses that *no single technique is sufficient*. Signature-based detection alone fails against **polymorphic** and **metamorphic** malware, which is why **heuristic** and **behavioral** layers are indispensable.

### 2.3 Detection Methodologies — Comparative Analysis

| Method | Mechanism | Strength | Weakness | KTU Exam Focus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature-Based** | Hash matching (MD5, SHA-256) against known malware DB | High accuracy, low FPR for known threats | Zero-day blind, easily evaded by polymorphism | **High** |
| **Heuristic / Rule-Based** | Static code analysis using rule sets (e.g., packed binaries, suspicious API calls) | Detects unknown variants | Higher FPR, slower scan | **High** |
| **Behavior-Based** | Monitors runtime API calls, syscalls, file/process/network actions | Catches zero-days, fileless malware | Resource-intensive, time-delay | **Very High** |
| **Sandboxing** | Executes suspect file in isolated VM/container | Safe detonation, full TTP analysis | Malware with VM-evasion (anti-VM) tricks | **Very High** |
| **Machine Learning / AI** | Trains classifiers (Random Forest, CNN, RNN) on feature vectors | Adaptive, scalable | Adversarial ML attacks, black-box nature | **Emerging** |
| **Cloud-Based Reputation** | Cross-references file hash with global threat intelligence | Real-time, community-driven | Requires internet; privacy concerns | **Moderate** |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| TPR (Sensitivity) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $0 \le TPR \le 1$ | Detection capability |
| FPR (Fall-out) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $0 \le FPR \le 1$ | False alarm rate |
| TNR (Specificity) | $TNR = 1 - FPR$ | $0 \le TNR \le 1$ | Clean file acceptance |
| Precision | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ | Trust in alert |
| Recall (= TPR) | $Recall = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $0 \le R \le 1$ | Coverage |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ | Balanced metric |
| Detection Latency | $L_d = t_{detect} - t_{execute}$ | **milliseconds (ms)** | Responsiveness |
| ROC-AUC | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | $0.5 \le AUC \le 1$ | Classifier quality |
| Entropy (packed files) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | bits/byte | Heuristic packing detection |
| Levenshtein Distance (variant similarity) | $LD(a,b) = \min\{ops\}$ | integer | Polymorphic clustering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used by malware analysts to **benchmark antivirus engines** (e.g., AV-Comparatives, MITRE ATT&CK evaluations) and to tune **SIEM correlation rules** in enterprise SOCs.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Behavioral Detection

Traditional signatures fail when malware uses:
- **Polymorphic engines** (change code but preserve function).
- **Metamorphic engines** (rewrite their own code entirely).
- **Fileless / Living-off-the-Land (LOLBins)** techniques (use legitimate tools like *PowerShell*, *WMI*).
