4. **Resource Efficiency:** They don't need to scan the entire network; they just listen for incoming connections.
5. **Deception Value:** They waste the attacker's time on fake assets, slowing down the attack chain.

**Limitations of Honeypots:**

1. **Narrow Field of View:** Honeypots can **only** detect attacks that interact with **them**. They cannot detect attacks against other real systems in the network. They are a complementary tool, not a replacement for IDS/IPS.
2. **Fingerprinting Risk:** Sophisticated attackers can detect honeypots using timing analysis, OS-level probes, or service quirks. Once detected, attackers avoid them.
3. **Pivot Risk:** High-interaction honeypots, if compromised, can be turned into a launchpad for attacks on the real network. Strict isolation and egress filtering are mandatory.
4. **Maintenance Overhead:** They require patching, log rotation, decoy content refresh, and constant monitoring.
5. **Legal / Ethical Concerns:** In some jurisdictions, entrapment laws and data privacy regulations (GDPR) restrict how honeypot data can be collected and used.

**Why "Low False Positive but Narrow Field of View":**
Honeypots excel at **precision** (what they see is almost always an attack) but are limited in **breadth** (they see only a small slice of total network activity). This is why honeypots are **always deployed alongside** — not in place of — firewalls, IDS/IPS, and SIEM. **(7 marks — 3 marks advantages, 3 marks limitations, 1 mark synthesis)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Definition missing the "value lies in being attacked" clause** — this is the exact KTU-defining phrase. Students who say "a fake server" lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Honeypot with Honeynet** — a honeypot is a *single* decoy; a honeynet is a *network* of decoys. Examiners will deduct 2 marks if you use them interchangeably.
> 3. **Forgetting egress filtering in high-interaction answers** — this is the single most tested safety concept. If you describe a high-interaction honeypot without mentioning **network isolation and egress filtering**, you lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Listing tools without explaining why** — just naming Cowrie, Dionaea, Honeyd is worth only 1 mark; you must explain what each emulates and in what scenario it is used.
> 5. **In the trade-off question**, students often say "high-interaction is better" without explaining the **risk** of pivot attacks. This loses the trade-off marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Honeypots**

- **Core Definition:** A honeypot is a security resource whose **value lies in being probed, attacked, or compromised**. (Spitzner's definition — memorize verbatim for KTU.)
- **Core Property:** All traffic to a honeypot is **suspicious by default** → low false positive rate.
- **Three Classifications:**
  1. **By Interaction:** Low, Medium, High.
  2. **By Purpose:** Research, Production.
  3. **By Location:** External (DMZ), Internal (LAN).
- **Interaction Trade-off:** Intelligence ↑ = Risk ↑. Always pair high-interaction with **strict isolation + egress filtering**.
- **Honeynet = Network of Honeypots.** Gen-II architecture has three pillars: **Data Control, Data Capture, Data Collection**. The gateway is called the **Honeywall** (Layer-2 bridge).
- **Famous Tools:** Honeyd (low), Cowrie (SSH), Dionaea (malware capture), T-Pot (aggregator), Kippo (historical SSH).
- **Egress Filtering:** Default-DENY outbound, allow only DNS/NTP. Prevents pivoting.
- **Advantages:** Low FP, zero-day detection, rich TTP intel, deception.
- **Limitations:** Narrow field of view, fingerprintable, pivot risk, maintenance, legal issues.
- **Honeypot ≠ IDS/IPS** — they are **complementary**, not substitutes.
- **Frameworks:** Honeynet Project, MITRE ATT&CK, Cowrie, Dionaea, T-Pot, Honeyd.
- **Key Metrics:** $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$, $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $EFC = \frac{\text{Blocked ports}}{\text{Total risky ports}}$.
- **Top Exam Traps to Avoid:** (1) Don't confuse honeypot with honeynet. (2) Always mention egress filtering for high-interaction. (3) Trade-off is the central theme — never present high-interaction as unconditionally "better."
- **Magic Phrase for KTU Valuation:** "Traffic to a honeypot is suspicious **by definition**" — use this exact line at least once in every KTU answer to score the application-level mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDPS Detection Approaches - Misuse Detection - Pattern Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** IDPS Detection Approaches - Misuse Detection - Pattern Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IDPS Detection Approaches — The Big Picture

An **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)** identifies malicious activity by analyzing network packets, host logs, or system calls. To do this, it relies on a **detection approach** — the core algorithm that decides whether an observed event is *normal* or *malicious*. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies the two foundational detection approaches as:

| Approach | What it looks for | Mental Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Misuse Detection** (Signature-based) | A known *fingerprint* of an attack | "Wanted criminal poster" |
| **Anomaly Detection** (Behavior-based) | A *deviation* from normal | "Anything that looks unusual" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 Focus:** This lesson deals entirely with **Misuse Detection**, specifically its most widely deployed engine — **Pattern Matching**.

---

## 1.2 Misuse Detection — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Misuse Detection** is a signature-based detection methodology in which the IDPS compares observed events (packets, payloads, log records) against a pre-compiled **database of attack signatures** (also called *rules* or *patterns*). An alert is raised only when an observed event **matches** a stored signature with sufficient fidelity.

- **Signature** = A formal description of an attack's *identifying characteristics* (byte sequence, packet header, instruction sequence, regex, or stateful sequence).
- **Pattern Matching** = The algorithmic process of comparing incoming data against the signature database.

**Key characteristics (KTU Board terminology):**
- **Knowledge-driven** — relies on a curated knowledge base.
- **Deterministic** — same input → same output.
- **High Precision, Low False Positives** for *known* attacks.
- **Zero-day blind** — cannot detect attacks not yet in the database.

---

## 1.3 Pattern Matching — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Matching** in the IDPS context is the process of searching the payload (or header) of a network packet / log event for the occurrence of one or more **attack signatures** (byte strings, regular expressions, or multi-pattern sequences). The matching engine is the *computational heart* of any signature-based IDPS.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bouncer at the Club"

Imagine a nightclub bouncer (the IDPS) standing at the door. He holds a **blacklist of photos and descriptions** of known troublemakers (the *signature database*). Each person walking in (each *packet*) is **visually scanned** against the blacklist. If a match is found, the bouncer raises an alarm and refuses entry.

- The **blacklist** = Signature database
- The **scanning process** = Pattern matching algorithm
- A **new troublemaker never seen before** = Zero-day attack → slips right through
- The bouncer **getting faster at scanning** = Use of efficient algorithms like Aho-Corasick

This is exactly how **Snort**, **Suricata**, and **Cisco IPS** work under the hood.

---

## 1.5 Pattern Matching in the IDPS Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> Pattern matching does not operate in isolation. It is the final *classification* step in a layered pipeline:

**Packet Capture → Decoding → Pre-processing → Pattern Matching Engine → Alert/Action**

The pattern matcher receives a **normalized packet** (decoded from raw bytes into a structured representation) and performs three sub-tasks:

1. **Single-pattern matching** — Is this exact string present? *(e.g., `"cmd.exe"`)*
2. **Multi-pattern matching** — Is *any* of thousands of patterns present? *(e.g., Snort's 30,000+ rules)*
3. **Stateful/protocol-aware matching** — Does the *sequence of events* match an attack scenario?

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students often confuse **signature** with **pattern**. A *signature* is the **abstract rule description**; a *pattern* is the **concrete string/regex used inside the signature** to be matched against packet data. They are related but not identical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Misuse Detection System

A signature-based IDPS is composed of four logical building blocks:

| # | Component | Function | Example (Snort) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Packet Decoder** | Converts raw bytes into protocol-aware structures | TCP/IP stack parser |
| 2 | **Pre-processor** | Normalizes, defrags, reassembles, decodes HTTP/SSL | `http_inspect`, `stream5` |
| 3 | **Detection Engine** | Performs the actual **pattern matching** against rules | Aho-Corasick + rule option evaluator |
| 4 | **Logging / Alerting Module** | Records/disseminates matches | `alert_fast`, `unified2` output |

The **detection engine** is the topic at hand.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of an Attack Signature

A signature is a *formal declarative expression* of an attack's identifying features. KTU 2024 expects students to recognize the **four primitive types** of signatures:

### 2.2.1 String-based (Exact-match) Signatures
A specific byte sequence that appears in the payload of an attack packet.

- *Example:* `"/cgi-bin/test-cgi?-d"` — a buffer-overflow probe against old Apache servers.

### 2.2.2 Port-based Signatures
Match based on the **service port** (e.g., a Telnet login attempt on port 23 from an internal host).

### 2.2.3 Header-based Signatures
Match on specific fields in the **packet header** (e.g., a TCP packet with both SYN and FIN flags set — a port-scan signature).

### 2.2.4 State / Sequence Signatures
Match on an **ordered sequence** of events across multiple packets (e.g., SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK = full TCP handshake; SYN → RST = half-open scan).

---

## 2.3 Pattern Matching — Classification of Algorithms

Pattern matching algorithms are classified along **two axes**: (a) the number of patterns searched simultaneously, and (b) the pre-processing strategy.

| Algorithm | # Patterns | Pre-processing | Best-case complexity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Naive / Brute Force** | Single | None | $O(n \cdot m)$ | Educational baseline |
| **Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP)** | Single | Failure function | $O(n + m)$ | Guarantees linear time |
| **Boyer-Moore (BM)** | Single | Bad-char + Good-suffix | $O(n/m)$ (avg) | Skips characters — fast in practice |
| **Aho-Corasick (AC)** | **Multiple** | Trie + failure links | $O(n + m + z)$ | **Industry standard** for IDPS |
| **Wu-Manber** | **Multiple** | Block character shifting | $O(n \cdot B / m)$ approx. | Variant of BM for many patterns |
| **Commentz-Walter** | **Multiple** | BM-style on a trie | — | Hybrid approach |

> [!NOTE]
> Where $n$ = length of the text (packet payload), $m$ = length of the pattern, $z$ = number of matches, $B$ = block size.

### 2.3.1 Why Aho-Corasick dominates IDPS
- A modern IDPS has **tens of thousands of signatures** (Snort v2.9 has ~30,000+ active rules).
- Running Boyer-Moore 30,000 times per packet is **impossible at line rate**.
- Aho-Corasick builds a **single finite automaton** from *all* patterns and scans the packet **once** in $O(n)$ time, regardless of pattern count.

---

## 2.4 Aho-Corasick — Step-by-Step Intuition

The Aho-Corasick algorithm constructs a **trie (prefix tree)** of all patterns, augments it with **failure links** (like KMP's failure function generalized to a tree) and **output links**. Scanning is a single pass through the text; at each character, the automaton transitions deterministically.

- **goto function** $g(state, char) \to next\_state$ — normal trie edge.
- **failure function** $f(state) \to fallback\_state$ — used on mismatch, analogous to KMP.
- **output function** $out(state) \to set\_of\_patterns$ — patterns ending at this state.

The automaton is **deterministic and finite**, so scanning is $O(n)$ time and $O(total\_pattern\_length)$ space.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the *symbols* and *what each metric means*, not just the formula.

### 2.5.1 Pattern Matching Complexity

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{naive}}(n,m) &= n \cdot m \\[4pt]
T_{\text{KMP}}(n,m) &= n + m \\[4pt]
T_{\text{BM,avg}}(n,m) &\approx \frac{n}{m} \quad \text{(sublinear on average)} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{AC}}(n, \vert P \vert, z) &= n + m_{\text{total}} + z
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.5.2 IDPS Detection Performance Metrics

Let $TP$ = True Positives (real attack, detected), $TN$ = True Negatives (benign, not flagged), $FP$ = False Positives (benign flagged as attack), $FN$ = False Negatives (real attack missed).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Detection Rate (Recall)} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\[4pt]
\text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\[4pt]
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\[4pt]
\text{F1-Score} &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\[4pt]
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid pipe-in-table:** Use $\vert P \vert$ for cardinality (number of patterns). Never write `|P|` inside a markdown table.

### 2.5.3 Throughput Metric (Bandwidth-limited environments)

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Packets Processed}}{\text{Time Window}} \quad \text{(pps = packets per second)}
$$

**Real-world target:** A 10 Gbps link ≈ **14.88 million packets/second** (assuming 84-byte avg packet). Pattern matching must keep up — hence Aho-Corasick.

### 2.5.4 Rule-Match Probability (informational)

For a uniform random payload of length $n$ and a single pattern of length $m$:

$$
P(\text{match}) \approx 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{256}\right)^{m} \quad \text{(for single-byte alphabet) — informational}
$$

(This is the foundation for analyzing the *false-positive cost* of overly long signatures.)

---

## 2.6 SNORT Rule Structure (KTU Favorite)

A Snort rule has **two logical halves**: the **rule header** and the **rule options**.

```
ACTION  PROTO  SRC_IP  SRC_PORT  DIR  DST_IP  DST_PORT  (OPTIONS)
```

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Action** | What to do on match | `alert`, `log`, `drop`, `reject` |
| **Protocol** | L4 protocol | `tcp`, `udp`, `icmp`, `ip` |
| **Source/Dest IP** | IP or CIDR or `any` | `192.168.1.0/24` |
| **Source/Dest Port** | Port number, range, or `any` | `80`, `1:1024` |
| **Direction** | `->` (unidirectional) or `<>` (bidirectional) | `->` |
| **Options** | Detection content & metadata | `msg:"SQL Injection"; content:"' OR 1=1--"; sid:1000001;` |

**Sample KTU-style rule (commented for clarity):**

```text
alert tcp any any -> 192.168.1.0/24 80 (
    msg:"Possible SQL Injection attempt";
    flow:to_server,established;
    content:"' OR 1=1--";
    nocase;
    classtype:web-application-attack;
    sid:1000001;
    rev:1;
)
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU valuation cue:** If asked to "write a Snort rule to detect X", always include `msg`, `sid`, `classtype`, and the matching `content`. Missing `sid` is a common reason for losing marks.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Internet Service Providers (ISPs)** run pattern matchers on **terabit-per-second backbones** using hardware-accelerated Aho-Corasick (e.g., on Cavium/NPUs).
- **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)** tools like CrowdStrike use **YARA rules** — a pattern-matching language similar in spirit to Snort.
- **Web Application Firewalls (WAFs)** (ModSecurity, AWS WAF) are pattern-matching engines specialized for HTTP payloads.
- **Antivirus engines** (ClamAV) are essentially **multi-pattern string matchers** with file-format-aware pre-processing.

Pattern matching is therefore not a KTU-only curiosity — it is the **operational backbone of every commercial security product**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Naive Pattern Matching Worst Case

**Setup:** Text of length $n$, pattern of length $m$ (with $m \le n$). At each position $i$ in the text, we attempt to align the pattern and compare up to $m$ characters.

**Step 1 — Define the trial loop.** For each alignment position $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m$:

$$
\text{comparisons}_i \le m
$$

**Step 2 — Total comparisons in the worst case.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{naive}}^{\text{worst}}(n,m) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-m} m \\[4pt]
&= m \cdot (n - m + 1) \\[4pt]
&\le m \cdot n \quad \text{(for } m \le n\text{)} \\[4pt]
&= O(n \cdot m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Example concrete evaluation.** For $n = 1000$ bytes and $m = 20$ bytes:

$$
T_{\text{naive}} = 20 \times 1000 = 20{,}000 \text{ character comparisons per packet}
$$

For **30,000 patterns** on the same packet, naive would require:

$$
T_{\text{naive, multi}} = 20 \times 1000 \times 30{,}000 = 6 \times 10^{8} \text{ comparisons}
$$

→ **Infeasible at line rate.** This derivation *motivates* Aho-Corasick.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — KMP Linear Time Guarantee

**Setup:** Construct the **failure function** $\pi$ (also called prefix function) of the pattern in $O(m)$ time. On a mismatch at position $j$, jump to $\pi[j-1]$ instead of restarting at position 0.

**Step 1 — Pre-processing cost.**

$$
T_{\text{prefix}}(m) = O(m)
$$

**Step 2 — Scanning cost.** Each character of the text is examined *at most twice* (once normally, once on a fallback). Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{scan}}(n) = O(n)
$$

**Step 3 — Total cost.**

$$
T_{\text{KMP}}(n, m) = O(n) + O(m) = O(n + m)
$$

**Step 4 — Concrete check.** $n=1000$, $m=20$:

$$
T_{\text{KMP}} = 1000 + 20 = 1020 \text{ operations}
$$

A **20× speed-up** over naive for this single pattern.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aho-Corasick Multi-Pattern Scan

**Setup:** Build a trie over a set $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k\}$ of $k$ patterns, each of length $m_i$. Let $M = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i$ be the total pattern length.

**Step 1 — Trie construction cost.** Each character of every pattern is inserted once.

$$
T_{\text{trie}}(M) = O(M)
$$

**Step 2 — Failure-link construction.** Computed in BFS order; each state is visited once with $O(\vert \Sigma \vert)$ work per state (alphabet $\Sigma$).

$$
T_{\text{failure}}(M) = O(M \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)
$$

For byte-level patterns, $\vert \Sigma \vert = 256$, often optimized with **compressed transitions** (e.g., in Snort's `acsmx2`).

**Step 3 — Scanning the text.** The automaton advances one state per text character, regardless of pattern count:

$$
T_{\text{scan}}(n) = O(n)
$$

**Step 4 — Output emission.** Each of the $z$ matches is reported in $O(1)$ via output links:

$$
T_{\text{output}}(z) = O(z)
$$

**Step 5 — Total complexity.**

$$
T_{\text{AC}}(n, M, z) = O(n + M + z)
$$

**Step 6 — Concrete check.** $n = 1500$ (typical Ethernet payload), $M = 30{,}000 \times 30 = 9 \times 10^5$ bytes (Snort-scale), $z = 0$ or small:

$$
T_{\text{AC}} \approx 1500 + 9 \times 10^5 + z = O(9 \times 10^5) \text{ (amortized per packet)}
$$

**Compare to running KMP 30,000 times:**

$$
T_{\text{KMP}\times30k} = 30{,}000 \times (1500 + 30) = 4.59 \times 10^7
$$

**Speed-up factor:**

$$
\frac{4.59 \times 10^7}{9 \times 10^5} \approx 51\times
$$

→ Aho-Corasick is **~50× faster** at this scale. This is why every production-grade IDPS uses it.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Throughput Requirement for a 10 Gbps Link

**Step 1 — Convert bandwidth to packet rate.** Assume an average packet size of $S = 84$ bytes (a conservative estimate including preamble, IPG, etc.):

$$
\text{pps} = \frac{\text{bps}}{8 \cdot S} = \frac{10 \times 10^9}{8 \times 84} = \frac{10^{10}}{672} \approx 1.488 \times 10^7 \text{ pps}
$$

**Step 2 — Time per packet.**

$$
T_{\text{per-pkt}} = \frac{1}{1.488 \times 10^7} \approx 67.2 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum AC operations per packet (assuming 1500-byte payload).**

$$
\frac{67.2 \text{ ns}}{1500 \text{ bytes}} \approx 44.8 \text{ ps/byte}
$$

A modern CPU at 3 GHz provides ~333 ps per clock cycle → **a software Aho-Corasick on commodity hardware can realistically keep up to ~3 Gbps**, motivating **hardware acceleration (FPGA/ASIC/NPUs)** for 10 Gbps+.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Guide

| Use Case | Recommended Algorithm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single fixed string, low-latency, tiny memory | Naive | Simplicity wins; no pre-processing cost |
| Single long pattern, off-line analysis | KMP | Guaranteed linear time |
| Many short patterns, online IDPS | **Aho-Corasick** | Single-pass, deterministic |
| Many long patterns, large text | Wu-Manber | Block-shifting gives BM-like speed-up on multi-pattern |
| Stateful / multi-packet attack | Custom state machine (Snort `flowbits`) | Not pure pattern matching |
| Encrypted payload | **Cannot pattern-match** content | Falls back to header / behavioral analysis |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Constructing an Aho-Corasick Automaton

**Patterns:** $P = \{\text{he}, \text{she}, \text{his}, \text{hers}\}$

**Text to scan:** `"ushers"` (length $n = 6$)

**Step 1 — Build the trie.**

```
(0) --h--> (1) --e--> (2) [output: he]
(1) --i--> (3) --s--> (4) [output: his]
(2) --r--> (5) --s--> (6) [output: hers]
(0) --s--> (7) --h--> (8) --e--> (9) [output: she]
```

**Step 2 — Failure links (BFS).**
- $f(1) = f(0) = 0$ (root has no parent).
- $f(2) = 0$ (no 'e' edge from root).
- $f(3) = 0$ (no 'i' from root).
- $f(4) = 0$.
- $f(5) = 0$ (no 'r' from root).
- $f(6) = 4$ (suffix "rs" — "hers" → fallback finds "his"? No: fallback from "hers" via $f(5)=0$, no 's' from 0. Correct value: 0).
- $f(7) = 0$.
- $f(8) = 1$ (suffix "h" matches node 1).
- $f(9) = 2$ (suffix "he" matches node 2 → outputs "he" and "she").

**Step 3 — Scan `"ushers"`.**

| i | char | state | Output(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | u | 0 | — |
| 1 | s | 7 | — |
| 2 | h | 8 | — |
| 3 | e | 9 | **she, he** |
| 4 | r | 2 → 0 (via failure) | — |
| 5 | s | 7 | — |

→ Two patterns detected ("she" and "he") in **6 character inspections**. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **KTU tip:** The exam often asks to "trace the Aho-Corasick automaton on a given text". Always produce a **trace table** like the one above. Examiners reward clean tabular reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — IDPS Detection Engine Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Raw Packet Bytes] --> A[Packet Decoder]
    A --> B[Pre-processor: defrag, stream reassembly, HTTP decode]
    B --> C[Pattern Matching Engine Aho-Corasick Automaton]
    C --> D{Match Found?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Rule Option Evaluator: content, flow, threshold]
    D -- No --> F[Drop / Continue]
    E --> G{Action}
    G --> H[Alert]
    G --> I[Log]
    G --> J[Drop]
    G --> K[Reject]
    style C fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow node is the **Pattern Matching Engine**, the operational heart of misuse detection. The green node is the **decision gate** — every packet eventually reaches this point.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Signature Match Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Event] --> B{Is signature DB loaded?}
    B -- No --> Z[IDPS not operational]
    B -- Yes --> C{Pre-processing OK?}
    C -- No --> X[Log parse error]
    C -- Yes --> D[Run AC automaton over normalized payload]
    D --> E{Any pattern matched?}
    E -- No --> F[Mark as benign]
    E -- Yes --> G[Evaluate rule options: msg, classtype, flow, threshold]
    G --> H{Option filters pass?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Trigger rule action: alert / drop / reject]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Aho-Corasick Trie with Failure Links

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0((root 0)) -- h --> N1((1))
    R0 -- s --> N7((7))
    N1 -- e --> N2((2))
    N1 -- i --> N3((3))
    N2 -- r --> N5((5))
    N3 -- s --> N4((4))
    N5 -- s --> N6((6))
    N7 -- h --> N8((8))
    N8 -- e --> N9((9))
    N9 -. failure .-> N2
    N8 -. failure .-> N1
    N6 -. failure .-> N4
    N5 -. failure .-> R0
    N2 -. failure .-> R0
    N7 -. failure .-> R0
    N1 -. failure .-> R0
    N3 -. failure .-> R0
    N4 -. failure .-> R0
    style N9 fill:#90be6d
    style N2 fill:#90be6d
    style N6 fill:#90be6d
    style N4 fill:#90be6d
```

> **Solid arrows** = goto (trie edges). **Dotted arrows** = failure links. **Green nodes** = output states (patterns terminating here).

---

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram — Misuse Detection Deployment in a Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INET((Internet)) --> FW[Perimeter Firewall]
    FW --> IDS[Inline Network IDPS Snort Suricata]
    IDS --> SW[Internal L2/L3 Switch]
    SW --> SRV1[Web Server]
    SW --> SRV2[Database Server]
    SW --> HIDS1[Host IDPS on Server 1]
    SW --> HIDS2[Host IDPS on Server 2]
    IDS --> MGMT[Centralized SIEM / Log Server]
    HIDS1 --> MGMT
    HIDS2 --> MGMT
    MGMT --> ANA[Security Analyst Console]
    style IDS fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style MGMT fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> The **yellow box** is where misuse/pattern matching happens at line rate. The **green box** aggregates alerts and feeds the SOC.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Diagram — Misuse vs. Anomaly Detection

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MISUSE[Misuse Detection]
        M1[Known Signature DB] --> M2[Pattern Matcher]
        M2 --> M3{Exact Match?}
        M3 -- Yes --> M4[ALERT]
    end
    subgraph ANOM[Anomaly Detection]
        A1[Baseline of Normal] --> A2[Statistical / ML Model]
        A2 --> A3{Deviation above threshold?}
        A3 -- Yes --> A4[ALERT]
    end
    style M2 fill:#f9c74f
    style A2 fill:#90be6d
```

> **Key takeaway for KTU viva:** Pattern matching is the **deterministic, low-FP** lane; anomaly detection is the **probabilistic, can-detect-zero-days** lane. Production IDPSes use **both** in parallel.
