<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Misuse Detection and Anomaly Detection. Give one merit and one demerit of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Misuse Detection | Anomaly Detection |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Compares events against stored attack signatures | Compares events against a model of "normal" |
| **Technique** | Pattern matching, rule evaluation | Statistical, ML, clustering |
| **Merit** | Very **low false-positive rate**; high precision for known attacks | Can detect **previously unknown (zero-day)** attacks |
| **Demerit** | **Cannot detect zero-day** attacks; needs constant signature updates | Higher **false-positive rate**; expensive training phase |

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for each correct difference, ½ mark each for merit/demerit (total 3).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**List any THREE types of attack signatures used in Misuse Detection with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **String-based signature** — e.g., payload `"\\x90\\x90\\x90\\x90"` (NOP sled) indicates a buffer-overflow exploit.
2. **Port-based signature** — e.g., any traffic on TCP port **4444** (Metasploit default handler).
3. **Header-based signature** — e.g., a TCP packet with **SYN and FIN flags both set** → characteristic of a stealth port scan (NULL/SYN-FIN scan).
4. *(Bonus — State/sequence signature)* — e.g., five consecutive SYN packets from one host to five different ports within 1 s → port sweep.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (Module 4 — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Aho-Corasick pattern matching algorithm with a suitable example. Show how it is used in modern IDPS.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark).**
Single-pattern algorithms (KMP, Boyer-Moore) scale poorly to the tens of thousands of signatures a modern IDPS maintains. Aho-Corasick solves this by building **one automaton for all patterns** and scanning the text in a single pass.

**Step 2 — Three Data Structures (2 marks).**
- **goto function $g$** — trie transitions.
- **failure function $f$** — fallback state on mismatch (generalized KMP).
- **output function $out$** — list of patterns recognized at a state.

**Step 3 — Construction (2 marks).**
1. Build trie by inserting each pattern character-by-character.
2. Compute failure links by BFS: for each node, set $f(node)$ to the longest proper suffix of the current string that is also a trie node.
3. Augment output: any pattern that ends at a failure-link target is added to the current node's output list.

**Step 4 — Scanning Algorithm (1 mark).**
For each text character $c$, follow $g(state, c)$; if undefined, follow $f$ chain until defined or root is reached. Report any pattern in $out(state)$.

**Step 5 — Worked Example (1 mark).**
Refer to the worked example in Section 3.6 of these notes (patterns: `{he, she, his, hers}`, text: `"ushers"`).

**Step 6 — Application in IDPS (1 mark).**
Snort, Suricata, and Bro/Zeek implement AC for multi-pattern payload inspection. The signature set (≈30,000 rules) is compiled into one AC automaton at startup; the engine then performs per-packet matching in linear time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Students often **omit the failure-link step** and present Aho-Corasick as a plain trie (giving only $O(n \cdot M)$ worst case). The failure function is what gives AC its **linear** guarantee. Skipping it will cost at least 2 marks.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Write a Snort rule to detect a buffer-overflow attempt against an internal web server at `10.0.0.5` on port 80. The signature should look for the `"%n%n%n%n"` printf format-string sequence in the HTTP URI. Explain each field of your rule.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rule (3 marks).**

```text
alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> 10.0.0.5 80 (
    msg:"Possible printf format string attack";
    flow:to_server,established;
    http_uri;
    content:"%n%n%n%n";
    nocase;
    classtype:web-application-attack;
    reference:cve,2000-0884;
    sid:1000002;
    rev:1;
)
```

**Step 2 — Field-by-field explanation (4 marks — ½ mark each for 8 fields).**

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `alert` | Action — generate an alert (and log) on match |
| `tcp` | L4 protocol of the offending traffic |
| `$EXTERNAL_NET any` | Any source IP, any source port (Snort variable for non-trusted networks) |
| `->` | Direction — unidirectional |
| `10.0.0.5 80` | Destination = the internal web server on port 80 |
| `flow:to_server,established` | Only inspect packets going TO the server on an established connection |
| `http_uri` | Normalize and inspect the **HTTP URI** portion (decodes %-encoding, defeats simple evasion) |
| `content:"%n%n%n%n"` | The attack signature — the printf `%n` write specifier, repeated four times |
| `nocase` | Case-insensitive match |
| `classtype:web-application-attack` | Tag for severity classification |
| `sid:1000002` | Unique Snort rule ID |
| `rev:1` | Rule revision number |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Three common mistakes: (1) **forgetting `http_uri`** — then `content` matches the raw packet, not the decoded URI, allowing trivial evasion; (2) **forgetting `sid`** — Snort rejects the rule; (3) **wrong direction** (`<>` instead of `->`) — the rule then fires on server replies, not client attacks. Each costs marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2023]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare Pattern Matching, Stateful Inspection, and Protocol Decoding as misuse-detection techniques. Which is most widely deployed and why?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions (1.5 marks).**
- **Pattern Matching:** Looks for known byte strings in the payload.
- **Stateful Inspection:** Tracks connection state (e.g., TCP handshake completion) and matches only within legitimate context.
- **Protocol Decoding:** Reassembles and parses the application protocol (HTTP, DNS, FTP) and validates semantic correctness.

**Step 2 — Comparative Table (3.5 marks).**

| Criterion | Pattern Matching | Stateful Inspection | Protocol Decoding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection basis** | Byte signature | Connection state + signature | Protocol structure + semantics |
| **Evasion resistance** | Low (encodes, splits, polymorphism defeat it) | Medium | **High** (works on decoded fields) |
| **Performance cost** | **Lowest** | Medium | Highest |
| **False-positive rate** | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **False-negative rate** | High vs. novel attacks | Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Implementation in Snort** | `content`, `pcre` | `flow`, `flowbits`, `stream5` | `http_inspect`, `dns` pre-processors |

**Step 3 — Most widely deployed (2 marks).**
**Pattern matching** is the *baseline* and is most widely deployed because:
1. It is **computationally cheapest** — runs at multi-gigabit line rates in software.
2. It has **zero false positives on known attacks** when the signature is well-written.
3. It is **simple to author and update** — community signature feeds (Emerging Threats, Talos VRT) are pattern-based.
4. Stateful inspection and protocol decoding are typically **layered on top** of pattern matching in commercial IDPSes (Snort does exactly this — it is a pattern matcher enhanced with state and protocol decoders).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Derive the worst-case time complexity of the Naive pattern matching algorithm and show that Aho-Corasick achieves $O(n + M + z)$ where $n$ = text length, $M$ = total pattern length, $z$ = number of matches. Why is this important for an IDPS?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Naive complexity derivation (2 marks).**

$$
T_{\text{naive}}(n, m) = m \cdot (n - m + 1) = O(n \cdot m)
$$

*At each of the $(n-m+1)$ alignment positions, up to $m$ character comparisons are made.*

**Step 2 — Aho-Corasick construction cost (1.5 marks).**

Building the trie takes $O(M)$ where $M = \sum \vert p_i \vert$. Failure links are computed by BFS in $O(M \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$ amortized — with compressed transitions, $O(M)$ in practice.

**Step 3 — Aho-Corasick scanning cost (1.5 marks).**
Each text character triggers exactly **one state transition** (with at most a finite chain of failure-link fallbacks, all of which amortize to a constant per character). Therefore scanning is $O(n)$. Each of the $z$ matches is emitted in $O(1)$ via the output function → $O(z)$ total.

**Step 4 — Total complexity (1 mark).**

$$
T_{\text{AC}} = T_{\text{build}} + T_{\text{scan}} + T_{\text{output}} = O(M) + O(n) + O(z) = O(n + M + z)
$$

**Step 5 — Why this matters for IDPS (1 mark).**
A modern IDPS must inspect each packet at **wire speed** (e.g., 1 Gbps ≈ 1.5 M pps; 10 Gbps ≈ 15 M pps). The linear-in-$n$, pattern-count-independent complexity of Aho-Corasick is what makes signature-based inspection feasible at these rates. A naive approach (or even KMP per pattern) would collapse under load.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Many students forget the **$z$** term in the AC complexity. While $z$ is usually small, the formal bound *includes* match reporting time — a careful derivation must show the $O(z)$ term. Omitting it costs one mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **The five most common reasons KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "signature" with "pattern"** — a signature is the full rule; a pattern is the byte string inside it.
> 2. **Forgetting the failure function** when describing Aho-Corasick (treats it as a plain trie).
> 3. **Snort rules missing `sid`**, `msg`, or `http_uri` — a fully written rule must be syntactically valid.
> 4. **Stating the wrong complexity** — saying Aho-Corasick is $O(n \cdot M)$ (that's the naive bound) loses easy marks.
> 5. **Claiming misuse detection detects zero-days** — it does not. This is a factual error and costs disproportionately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and review 24 hours before the exam.**

- [x] **Misuse detection** is *signature-based*; **anomaly detection** is *behavior-based*. They are complementary, not competing.
- [x] A **signature** = full rule; a **pattern** = the byte string/regex inside the rule. Do not interchange.
- [x] Four signature types: **string, port, header, state/sequence**.
- [x] **Naive** = $O(nm)$; **KMP** = $O(n+m)$; **Boyer-Moore** = $O(n/m)$ average; **Aho-Corasick** = $O(n + M + z)$ for multiple patterns.
- [x] Aho-Corasick uses **goto (trie)**, **failure links (KMP-style)**, and **output links**.
- [x] Aho-Corasick is the **industry standard** for IDPS payload inspection (Snort, Suricata).
- [x] **Snort rule format:** `action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port (options)`.
- [x] Mandatory Snort fields to include in exam answers: `msg`, `content`, `sid`, `classtype`, and (for HTTP) `http_uri`.
- [x] A 10 Gbps link ≈ **14.88 million pps** → ~67 ns per packet. Software AC keeps up to ~3 Gbps; higher rates need **hardware acceleration (FPGA/ASIC/NPU)**.
- [x] **Merits of misuse detection:** low FP, high precision, easy to update. **Demerit:** zero-day blind; signature maintenance overhead.
- [x] Key metrics to memorize: **Detection Rate (Recall)**, **False Positive Rate**, **Precision**, **F1-Score** — and their equations.
- [x] Pattern matching **cannot inspect encrypted payloads** — for TLS/SSL traffic, only header/behavioral analysis applies.
- [x] Modern IDPSes use a **layered approach**: Protocol Decoding → Stateful Inspection → Pattern Matching (each adds resilience against evasion).
- [x] For KTU derivations, always show the **state trace table** for Aho-Corasick and the **complexity derivation** step by step — these are heavily rewarded.

> **Final note:** Pattern matching is the *most testable* sub-topic in Module 4 of PECCT742. Mastering Aho-Corasick (intuition + trace + complexity) and being able to **author a syntactically correct Snort rule** will fetch you the majority of marks in both Part A and Part B of the KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rule-Based Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rule-Based Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rule-Based Techniques** in the context of Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS) refer to a class of detection methodologies that rely on a pre-established, formally structured set of conditional expressions (rules) to identify malicious, anomalous, or policy-violating activity in network traffic or host-level events. Each rule encapsulates a specific attack signature, behavioral pattern, or policy condition, and the IDPS engine evaluates incoming events against these rules to produce a verdict (alert, drop, log, pass, or reject).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, Rule-Based Techniques are positioned as a foundational **knowledge-driven detection paradigm** under the broader umbrella of **Misuse Detection / Signature-Based Detection**, forming the backbone of classical Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) such as **Snort**, **Suricata**, and **Bro/Zeek**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Rule-Based Techniques constitute the *classical* but *still widely deployed* core of commercial and open-source IDPS engines. Mastery of Snort-style rule syntax, rule action semantics, and detection metric calculations is **mandatory** for ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highly experienced bank security guard** posted at the entrance with a printed checklist (the *rule-set*). For every customer who walks in, the guard mentally runs through the checklist:

1. Is the person wearing a helmet and holding a bag? → **Suspicious (Rule 7 fires).**
2. Is the person trying to enter after banking hours? → **Suspicious (Rule 12 fires).**
3. Is the person just walking in normally? → **Permit entry (Default rule fires).**

The guard does **not learn** new suspicious patterns on the spot. He only acts on what is **already printed on his checklist**. The moment a new type of robbery technique appears (e.g., drone-based theft), the guard will not catch it until management prints a *new rule* on his sheet.

This is exactly how a **Rule-Based IDPS** operates:
- The **checklist** = the *rule database*.
- The **customer** = the *network packet / host event*.
- The **guard's mental evaluation** = the *detection engine*.
- The **verdict** = *alert / drop / log / pass*.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate (DR)** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$
- **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score** = $2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$
- **Rule Processing Throughput** measured in **packets per second (pps)**
- **Snort Rule Action Keywords:** `alert`, `log`, `pass`, `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop`

> [!NOTE]
> A single rule firing is **not** confirmation of a true attack. The metrics above quantify the **holistic quality** of the entire rule-set, not any individual rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Rule-Based IDPS Rule (Snort-Style Syntax)

A classical Snort rule is divided into two logical halves:

| Logical Half | Section | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule Header** | Action + Protocol + Src/Dst IP + Src/Dst Port + Direction | Defines *WHO* and *WHERE* the rule applies | `alert tcp 192.168.1.0/24 any -> any 80` |
| **Rule Body / Options** | Detection payload keywords, flow, metadata | Defines *WHAT* malicious content to look for | `(msg:"Web Attack"; content:"/etc/passwd"; nocase; sid:1000001;)` |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How a Rule-Based IDPS Works

A rule-based engine executes the following structured pipeline for every incoming unit (packet or session):

1. **Packet Capture (PCAP) / Event Acquisition:** A tap, span port, or host agent feeds raw data to the engine.
2. **Pre-processing / Normalization:** Defragmentation, TCP stream reassembly, HTTP/URI decoding, deflate/gzip decompression, TLS decryption (if applicable).
3. **Rule Set Compilation:** Rules are parsed and compiled into efficient matching structures (typically Aho-Corasick automata for `content` patterns, port-based hash tables, and decision trees).
4. **Pattern Matching Phase:** Each packet/flow is scanned against the active rule-set. The engine walks the Aho-Corasick trie for `content:` matches, evaluates `byte_test` arithmetic, and checks `flow` state.
5. **Action Execution:** When a rule's full body is satisfied (logical AND across all options), the engine triggers the rule's action — `alert`, `drop`, `reject`, `log`, `pass`, or `sdrop`.
6. **Logging & Alert Generation:** Verdicts are forwarded to the logging subsystem, SIEM, or console.

### 2.3 Core 'Why' and 'How' of Each Step

- **Why pre-process?** Attackers obfuscate payloads (`%2F` for `/`, mixed case, fragmentation). Without normalization, trivial rules like `content:"/etc/passwd"` would miss obfuscated variants.
- **Why Aho-Corasick?** Multi-pattern matching in **O(n + m)** time (n = packet size, m = total rule pattern length) — essential for line-rate detection.
- **Why AND across rule options?** Reduces false positives. A rule firing must satisfy *every* condition in its body, not just one.

### 2.4 Types of Rule-Based Techniques (KTU Taxonomy)

| Type | Description | Example Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Signature-Based / Pattern Matching** | Matches fixed byte sequences in payloads | Snort `content` keyword |
| **Stateful Pattern Matching** | Matches patterns across reassembled streams, not single packets | Snort `flow:established` |
| **Protocol-Decoding Based** | Validates protocol field semantics (e.g., HTTP header anomalies) | Snort `http_uri`, `dns_query` |
| **Anomaly-Based (with Rule Thresholds)** | Uses statistical baselines and triggers when rule-defined threshold is breached | Snort `threshold` / `detection_filter` |
| **Policy / Expert-System Rules** | "If-Then" production rules encoding security policy | Bro/Zeek policy scripts |
| **Behavioral / Heuristic Rules** | Rules triggered on sequences of low-level events (correlated) | OSSEC correlation rules |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following metric and rule-priority formulas. They are recurring favourites in KTU ESE valuation.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True Positive Rate (TPR) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Also called *Sensitivity* or *Recall* |
| 2 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False alarm rate |
| 3 | False Negative Rate (FNR) | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP} = 1 - TPR$ | Miss rate |
| 4 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Trustworthiness of an alert |
| 5 | Specificity (TNR) | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Correct rejection rate |
| 6 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 7 | Detection Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| 8 | Snort Rule Action Priority (conceptual) | $\text{reject} > \text{drop} > \text{sdrop} > \text{alert} > \text{log} > \text{pass}$ | Action severity ranking |
| 9 | Rule Match Latency (per packet) | $L_p = T_{\text{pre}} + T_{\text{match}} + T_{\text{action}}$ | Total processing time per packet |
| 10 | Cohen's Kappa (rule-set agreement) | $\kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | Inter-rater reliability metric |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In any inline math, absolute value bars and `such that` symbols use `\vert` and `\mid` — never the pipe character `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Rule-Set Performance Metrics

Suppose a deployed rule-based IDPS observes the following outcomes over a 24-hour window on a monitored subnet. We will derive the *Detection Quality Metrics* of the rule-set.

**Given Counts (from logs and ground-truth audit):**

| Outcome | Count |
|---|---|
| True Positives (TP) | 820 |
| False Positives (FP) | 130 |
| True Negatives (TN) | 18,950 |
| False Negatives (FN) | 100 |

**Total Events Observed:**

$$
N = TP + FP + TN + FN
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
N = 820 + 130 + 18950 + 100 = 20000
$$

---

**Step 1 — Compute the True Positive Rate (Detection Rate):**

$$
TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
TPR = \frac{820}{820 + 100} = \frac{820}{920}
$$

$$
TPR \approx 0.8913
$$

**Interpretation:** The rule-set correctly detects **≈ 89.13 %** of actual intrusions. This is the *sensitivity* of the detection engine.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the False Positive Rate:**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
FPR = \frac{130}{130 + 18950} = \frac{130}{19080}
$$

$$
FPR \approx 0.006812
$$

**Interpretation:** About **0.68 %** of legitimate traffic is wrongly flagged. This is the *false alarm rate*.

---

**Step 3 — Compute Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
P = \frac{820}{820 + 130} = \frac{820}{950}
$$

$$
P \approx 0.8632
$$

**Interpretation:** When the rule-set fires an alert, there is an **≈ 86.32 %** chance it is a *real* intrusion.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the F1-Score:**

Recall $R = TPR \approx 0.8913$ and $P \approx 0.8632$.

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8632 \times 0.8913}{0.8632 + 0.8913}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.76948}{1.7545}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot 0.43859
$$

$$
F_1 \approx 0.8772
$$

**Interpretation:** An F1 of **0.877** indicates a *well-balanced* rule-set with both high precision and high recall.

---

**Step 5 — Compute Overall Detection Accuracy:**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}
$$

$$
Acc = \frac{820 + 18950}{20000} = \frac{19770}{20000}
$$

$$
Acc = 0.9885
$$

**Interpretation:** The rule-based engine makes the *correct decision* in **98.85 %** of all observed events.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Rule Priority and Conflict Resolution

Consider three Snort rules for inbound HTTP traffic on port 80:

- **Rule R1:** `pass tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"Allow Googlebot"; content:"Googlebot";)`
- **Rule R2:** `alert tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"Possible SQLi"; content:"union select"; nocase; sid:1001;)`
- **Rule R3:** `drop tcp any any -> any 80 (msg:"Block SQLi attempt"; content:"union select"; nocase; sid:1002; rev:2;)`

**Step 1 — Rule headers are identical (same action targets)**, so the engine proceeds to rule body evaluation.

**Step 2 — Rule body evaluation** for a packet containing the string `UNION SELECT` from a user-agent claiming to be `Googlebot`:

- R1's body (content Googlebot) → **matches**.
- R2's body (content union select, nocase) → **matches**.
- R3's body (content union select, nocase) → **matches**.

**Step 3 — Conflict resolution by action priority:**

According to the action priority hierarchy

$$
\text{reject} > \text{drop} > \text{sdrop} > \text{alert} > \text{log} > \text{pass}
$$

the highest-severity matching action is `drop` (R3). However, **Snort processes rules in textual order with a `pass` rule short-circuiting further evaluation**. Therefore, R1's `pass` action short-circuits R2 and R3, and the packet is **allowed**.

> [!NOTE]
> This example illustrates a critical KTU-board-tested concept: **rule order and `pass` semantics** can override otherwise high-severity rules — a frequent source of security misconfigurations.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Rule-Based vs. Other Techniques

| Dimension | Rule-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Detection Basis** | Predefined signatures / patterns | Statistical deviation from baseline | Protocol state machine violations |
| **Zero-Day Detection** | **Poor** (no rule = no detection) | Good | Moderate |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low (if rules are tuned) | High | Low to Moderate |
| **Performance Overhead** | Low to Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent rule updates required | Periodic baseline retraining | Protocol specification updates |
| **Transparency of Detection** | High (rule is human-readable) | Low (black-box model) | High (state machine is auditable) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rule-Based IDPS

The following Mermaid diagram maps the internal functional architecture of a typical rule-based NIDS (Snort/Suricata-style):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Network Tap / Span Port] --> A1[Packet Capture Engine]
    A1 --> A2[Preprocessor: Defragmentation]
    A2 --> A3[Preprocessor: Stream Reassembly]
    A3 --> A4[Preprocessor: Protocol Decoders HTTP DNS SMTP]
    A4 --> A5{Rule Engine: Header Match}
    A5 -- No Match --> A6[Pass / Forward Packet]
    A5 -- Header Match --> A7[Pattern Matcher: Aho Corasick Trie]
    A7 -- Body Options Match --> A8[Action Selector: Alert Drop Reject Log Pass]
    A7 -- No Body Match --> A6
    A8 --> A9[Logging Subsystem Unified2 Format]
    A8 --> A10[Alert Console / SIEM Forwarder]
    A9 --> A11[Rule Database flat text or pulled by oinkmaster]
    A11 --> A5
```

### 4.2 Snort Rule Structural Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Snort Rule Line] --> H[Rule Header]
    R --> B[Rule Body Options in parentheses]
    H --> H1[Action: alert log pass drop reject sdrop]
    H --> H2[Protocol: tcp udp icmp ip http]
    H --> H3[Source IP / Port]
    H --> H4[Direction Operator: → or ↔]
    H --> H5[Destination IP / Port]
    B --> B1[msg: Human readable alert]
    B --> B2[content: Byte pattern]
    B --> B3[flow: TCP state flag]
    B --> B4[threshold: Rate limit]
    B --> B5[sid: Unique rule ID]
    B --> B6[classtype: Attack category]
    B --> B7[metadata: CVE author references]
```

### 4.3 Rule Evaluation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Incoming Packet] --> S1[Compile Time: Load Rule Set into Trie]
    S1 --> S2{Packet Header Matches Rule Header?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Drop Rule Candidate]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4{Evaluate Rule Body Options}
    S4 -- All AND Conditions True --> S5[Rule Fires]
    S4 -- Any AND Condition False --> S3
    S5 --> S6[Resolve Action Conflicts by Priority]
    S6 --> S7[Execute Action: Alert Log Drop Reject]
    S7 --> S8[Log to Unified2 File]
    S7 --> S9[Update Rule Hit Counters]
```

### 4.4 Conflict Resolution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Multiple Rules Matched] --> C2{Is there a pass rule in the set?}
    C2 -- Yes --> C3[Execute pass and short circuit higher severity rules]
    C2 -- No --> C4[Select the highest priority action]
    C4 --> C5[reject > drop > sdrop > alert > log]
    C5 --> C6[Execute the chosen action on the packet]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024]**

**Q1. Define Rule-Based Intrusion Detection. List any four Snort rule actions with their semantics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Rule-Based Intrusion Detection is a *misuse-detection* technique in which the IDPS compares observed network packets or host events against a pre-compiled database of formally structured conditional expressions (rules). Each rule encodes the signature, context, and policy response for a specific known attack or suspicious behaviour. If a packet or event satisfies the full body of a rule, the corresponding action is triggered.

**Four Snort Rule Actions (2 Marks):**

1. **`alert`** — Generates an alert and logs the packet, but allows it to pass through the network.
2. **`log`** — Logs the packet to disk without generating a console alert.
3. **`pass`** — Explicitly ignores the packet and short-circuits evaluation of subsequent rules; used for whitelisting.
4. **`drop`** — Blocks the packet and logs the event (requires inline mode, e.g., IPS deployment).

*Additional valid actions:* **`reject`** (blocks + sends TCP RST / ICMP unreachable), **`sdrop`** (silent drop, no log).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Rule-Based Detection and Anomaly-Based Detection on the basis of (i) detection principle, (ii) zero-day attack handling, and (iii) false positive rate.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Dimension | Rule-Based Detection | Anomaly-Based Detection |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Detection Principle | Matches events against a predefined set of signatures / patterns | Detects statistical deviation from a learned baseline of normal behaviour |
| (ii) Zero-Day Attack Handling | **Poor** — cannot detect attacks without a corresponding rule | **Strong** — any deviation from baseline is flagged |
| (iii) False Positive Rate | **Low** when rules are well-tuned | **High** because legitimate but novel behaviour may deviate from baseline |

*(1 mark per correct contrasting point.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **architecture of a Rule-Based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)**. Explain each functional block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a rule-based IDPS evaluated on 10,000 events with the following confusion-matrix counts: TP = 600, FP = 200, TN = 8,800, FN = 400. Compute the **Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Precision, and F1-Score**, and comment on the overall quality of the rule-set. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### (a) Architecture of a Rule-Based NIDS (7 Marks)

A rule-based NIDS follows a **layered pipeline architecture** consisting of the following functional blocks:

1. **Packet Capture Subsystem (1 Mark):** Uses libpcap / WinPcap or a kernel-bypass framework (DPDK, AF_PACKET) to acquire raw packets from a SPAN port or network TAP. Operates in promiscuous mode.

2. **Pre-processor / Normalizer (1 Mark):** Performs IP defragmentation, TCP stream reassembly, and protocol-specific decoding (HTTP URI normalization, DNS query parsing, SMTP header extraction). Required to defeat evasion techniques like fragmentation and encoding.

3. **Rule Database (1 Mark):** Stores attack signatures in flat-text or compiled format. Updated periodically via services like *Emerging Threats*, *Snort VRT*, or *oinkmaster*. Each rule is uniquely identified by its `sid` (Snort ID).

4. **Detection Engine (2 Marks):** The core matching engine. Compiles `content:` patterns into an **Aho-Corasick automaton** for O(n) multi-pattern matching. For each packet, it:
    - Filters by rule-header (5-tuple: protocol, src/dst IP, src/dst port, direction).
    - For matching headers, runs the body options through AND-logic.
    - Uses a separate decision tree for non-content options (e.g., `byte_test`, `flow`).

5. **Action / Response Subsystem (1 Mark):** On a rule hit, executes the rule action — alert, log, pass, drop, reject, sdrop. In inline/IPS mode, blocking actions terminate the connection.

6. **Logging and Output Subsystem (1 Mark):** Alerts are written in **Unified2 binary format**, which is then consumed by tools like *Barnyard2*, *Snorby*, or forwarded to a SIEM. The architecture also maintains hit counters for tuning.

*A labelled block diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1) must accompany this answer for full marks.*

---

#### (b) Computation of Detection Quality Metrics (7 Marks)

**Given:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| TP | 600 |
| FP | 200 |
| TN | 8,800 |
| FN | 400 |
| **Total N** | $600 + 200 + 8800 + 400 = 10000$ |

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (TPR):** *(1.5 Marks)*

$$
TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{600}{600 + 400} = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60
$$

**Detection Rate = 60 %** — The rule-set catches 60 % of true intrusions.

---

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate:** *(1.5 Marks)*

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{200}{200 + 8800} = \frac{200}{9000} \approx 0.0222
$$

**False Positive Rate ≈ 2.22 %** — Roughly 1 in 45 legitimate events is wrongly alerted.

---

**Step 3 — Precision:** *(1.5 Marks)*

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{600}{600 + 200} = \frac{600}{800} = 0.75
$$

**Precision = 75 %** — Three out of four alerts are genuine.

---

**Step 4 — F1-Score:** *(1.5 Marks)*

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.75 \times 0.60}{0.75 + 0.60} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.45}{1.35}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot 0.3333 = 0.6667
$$

**F1-Score ≈ 0.667** — A *moderate* balance between precision and recall.

---

**Step 5 — Qualitative Comment (1 Mark):**

The rule-set is **moderately effective** at detecting intrusions (60 % detection) but generates a **non-negligible false alarm rate (2.22 %)** and a sub-optimal F1-score of 0.667. This indicates that the rules need *tuning*, additional rules for the missed 40 % of attacks (false negatives), and thresholding / suppression to reduce the 200 false positives. A deployed production system should target F1 ≥ 0.85 and FPR < 1 %.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the **structure of a Snort rule** in detail. Describe the function of each component of the rule header and rule body, and illustrate with one example rule for detecting an HTTP-based SQL injection attempt. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and major challenges** of Rule-Based IDPS. Compare its effectiveness against anomaly-based and stateful protocol analysis techniques. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### (a) Structure of a Snort Rule (7 Marks)

A Snort rule consists of two logical halves — the **Rule Header** and the **Rule Body (Options)** — terminated by a newline. *(1 Mark for stating the two halves.)*

**1. Rule Header (3 Marks):** The header has the form

```
<Action> <Protocol> <Src_IP> <Src_Port> <Direction> <Dst_IP> <Dst_Port>
```

- **Action** — What the engine does on a match (`alert`, `log`, `pass`, `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop`).
- **Protocol** — Layer-4 protocol (`tcp`, `udp`, `icmp`, `ip`, `http`, `dns`, etc.).
- **Source IP / Port** — Originating endpoint (e.g., `any`, `192.168.1.0/24`, `$HOME_NET`).
- **Direction Operator** — `->` (unidirectional) or `<>` (bidirectional).
- **Destination IP / Port** — Target endpoint (e.g., `any 80`, `$EXTERNAL_NET any`).

**2. Rule Body — Options in parentheses (3 Marks):** Each option is a key:value pair separated by `;`. Common options include:

| Option | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `msg` | Human-readable alert message |
| `content` | Byte pattern to match in payload |
| `nocase` | Case-insensitive matching |
| `flow` | TCP state requirement (e.g., `established,to_server`) |
| `classtype` | Category of attack (e.g., `web-application-attack`) |
| `sid` | Unique Snort rule identifier |
| `rev` | Rule revision number |
| `reference` | External references (e.g., CVE, URL) |
| `metadata` | Author, CVE tags |

**3. Example Rule — Detecting SQL Injection via HTTP URI (Bonus, contributes to clarity):**

```
alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET 80 (msg:"SQL Injection Attempt - UNION SELECT"; flow:established,to_server; http_uri; content:"union"; nocase; content:"select"; nocase; distance:0; classtype:web-application-attack; sid:1000001; rev:1;)
```

This rule fires an alert when an established TCP session to port 80 contains the keywords `union` followed by `select` (case-insensitive) in the HTTP URI, a classic SQL injection pattern.

---

#### (b) Advantages, Limitations, and Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Advantages of Rule-Based IDPS (3 Marks):**

1. **High Precision & Low FPR (when tuned):** Rules encode specific attack patterns, producing highly accurate alerts.
2. **Human-Readable & Auditable:** Every detection is traceable to a specific rule — essential for forensic accountability.
3. **Mature & Well-Supported Tooling:** Vast open-source rule repositories (Snort VRT, Emerging Threats).
4. **Deterministic & Real-Time:** No statistical training phase; rules can be deployed and enforced immediately at line rate.

**Limitations and Challenges (2 Marks):**

1. **Inability to Detect Zero-Day Attacks:** Any unknown attack lacking a corresponding rule will pass undetected.
2. **High Maintenance Burden:** Rule databases require continuous updates to remain effective.
3. **Evasion Susceptibility:** Obfuscation, fragmentation, and protocol-aware tunneling can bypass simple rules.
4. **Rule Conflicts and Ordering Bugs:** Improperly ordered `pass` rules can suppress critical alerts.
5. **Scalability Bottleneck:** Tens of thousands of rules can degrade throughput on commodity hardware.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Dimension | Rule-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zero-Day Detection | Weak | Strong | Moderate |
| False Positive Rate | Low | High | Low–Moderate |
| Interpretability | High (rules are human-readable) | Low (black-box) | High (state machines auditable) |
| Computational Cost | Low–Moderate | High | Moderate |
| Maintenance | Continuous rule updates | Periodic baseline retraining | Protocol spec updates |

**Conclusion:** Rule-based techniques are the *workhorse* of modern IDPS, but a **defence-in-depth** strategy combining them with anomaly-based and stateful methods yields the best detection coverage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Rule-Based Techniques Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing rule header with rule body** — students often mix up where the `sid` and `content` options go. *The `sid` is in the body (options), not the header.*
> 2. **Forgetting to specify `flow:established` in TCP payload rules** — this is the single most common reason a Snort rule fails to fire in practice, and examiners explicitly look for this in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Mixing up FPR and FNR** — a frequent arithmetic trap. Always check: *False Positive Rate uses the TN+FP denominator, not TP+FN.*
> 4. **Not commenting on rule-set quality** — in computational questions, the final qualitative *interpretation* of the metric values carries **1 mark** that is often lost.
> 5. **Omitting the architecture diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions — drawing a labelled block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself.
> 6. **Not mentioning rule update mechanisms** (oinkmaster, pulledpork) in maintenance-related questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Rule-Based Techniques"**

- **Definition:** Knowledge-driven misuse detection that matches events against a pre-built rule database.
- **Core Tools:** Snort, Suricata, Bro/Zeek.
- **Rule Structure:** **Header** (action + protocol + 5-tuple + direction) + **Body** (options in parentheses: `content`, `flow`, `msg`, `sid`, `classtype`, `metadata`).
- **Snort Action Hierarchy:** `reject` > `drop` > `sdrop` > `alert` > `log` > `pass`.
- **Engine Internals:** Aho-Corasick automaton for `content` matching, decision tree for non-content options, AND-logic across body options.
- **Pre-processing is Mandatory:** Defragmentation + TCP stream reassembly + protocol decoding are prerequisites for accurate detection.
- **Key Metrics — Memorize These:**
   - $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ (Detection Rate)
   - $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$
   - $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ (Precision)
   - $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$
- **Advantages:** High precision, interpretable, real-time, mature tooling.
- **Limitations:** Zero-day blindness, high maintenance, evasion-vulnerable, rule-ordering bugs.
- **Best Practice:** Use in *defence-in-depth* with anomaly and stateful techniques.
- **Exam Mantra:** Always draw the architecture diagram, always include `flow:established` for TCP payload rules, always interpret the metric values in your conclusion.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State-Based Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State-Based Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**State-Based Techniques** in Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS) are a class of signature-based and anomaly-based detection methodologies that capture and analyze the **sequential behavioral patterns** of network protocols, applications, or system processes by tracking their transitions across well-defined operational states. Unlike stateless packet inspection (which examines each packet in isolation), state-based techniques maintain a **stateful memory** of the connection or process lifecycle, allowing the IDPS to recognize deviations from the legitimate expected state evolution.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus nomenclature, state-based techniques encompass three principal sub-paradigms:

1. **Stateful Protocol Analysis (Deep Packet Inspection with State Tracking)** — Monitoring protocol state machines (e.g., TCP, HTTP, FTP) to detect deviations.
2. **State Transition Analysis** — Modeling attacks as transitions from a *secure initial state* to a *compromised final state* through intermediate vulnerable states.
3. **Finite State Machine (FSM) Based Detection** — Encoding the legitimate behavior of a system as a deterministic or non-deterministic automaton and flagging transitions that fall outside the accepted grammar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** State-based techniques are fundamental to **Network-Based IDPS (NIDPS)** and **Hybrid IDPS** architectures. They form the bridge between pure signature matching and advanced behavioral analytics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **bank security officer** stationed at the entrance of a vault. The officer is not just checking IDs in isolation (stateless) — he is maintaining a mental **ledger** of the vault's status:

- *State 1:* Vault Closed → Customer Enters
- *State 2:* One Person Inside → No One Else Enters
- *State 3:* One Person Inside → Doors Lock
- *State 4:* Transaction Authorized → Vault Opens
- *State 5:* Transaction Complete → Customer Exits → State 1 Restored

If, suddenly, the officer sees a customer **jump directly from State 1 to State 4 without passing through States 2 and 3** (i.e., bypassing the door lock and the authorization handshake), he raises an alarm. This is precisely how state-based intrusion detection works — it watches the *order* and *context* of events, not just the events themselves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** A stateless IDS might allow a packet that says "FIN, ACK" alone. A stateful IDS, however, will **only accept that packet** if it knows the connection is in the `ESTABLISHED` state — a much stronger guarantee.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **State** | A snapshot of all relevant variables that define the current condition of a system, connection, or process. |
| **State Transition** | The movement of a system from one state to another triggered by an event or input. |
| **State Transition Diagram (STD)** | A directed graph in which nodes represent states and edges represent events triggering transitions. |
| **State Table** | A matrix representation $\bigl(S \times E \rightarrow S\bigr)$ mapping (current state, event) pairs to next states. |
| **Acceptance Set** | A designated subset of states considered safe or expected. |
| **Penetration Signature** | A sequence of state transitions that characterizes a known attack pattern. |
| **Protocol State Machine** | The formal specification of how a protocol (e.g., TCP) is allowed to progress. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of State-Based IDPS

A state-based IDPS operates through a **five-stage pipeline**, each stage essential for accurate detection:

### Stage 1: State Capture (Observation)
The sensor intercepts packets, syscalls, or API calls and extracts the **event vector** $e_i$ at time $t_i$. For a TCP connection, this would include flags (SYN, ACK, FIN, RST), sequence numbers, and window sizes.

### Stage 2: State Lookup (Memory Access)
The system consults a **State Table** indexed by the 5-tuple $\langle \text{Source IP}, \text{Source Port}, \text{Dest IP}, \text{Dest Port}, \text{Protocol} \rangle$ to retrieve the current state $S_i$.

### Stage 3: Transition Evaluation
Using the protocol's **finite state machine** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$, the engine evaluates whether the observed event $e_i$ is a valid transition from $S_i$ to some $S_{i+1}$:

$$
S_{i+1} = \delta(S_i, e_i)
$$

If $\delta(S_i, e_i)$ is **undefined**, the event is anomalous — this is a state violation.

### Stage 4: Anomaly Scoring
For non-deterministic or probabilistic models, the system computes a **state likelihood**:

$$
P(S_{i+1} \mid S_i, e_i) = \frac{P(S_i, e_i \mid S_{i+1}) \cdot P(S_{i+1})}{P(S_i, e_i)}
$$

### Stage 5: Response Triggering
If the score falls below a configurable **threshold** $\theta$ (where typically $0.0 \leq \theta \leq 1.0$), the system raises an alarm, logs the event, or actively drops the packet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Legitimate network traffic follows well-defined RFC specifications. Attacks, by their very nature, must either *fabricate* packets out of order or *manipulate* state values — both of which are detectable by a properly instrumented state tracker.

## 2.2 The Two Principal Modeling Frameworks

### Framework A: Stateful Protocol Analysis
The IDS embeds the **complete RFC state machine** of each monitored protocol. Any deviation from the spec = attack.

**Example:** RFC 793 TCP has 11 canonical states (LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, LAST-ACK, TIME-WAIT, CLOSING, CLOSED). A "Christmas Tree" scan sends packets with all flags set, which **cannot legally arise** from any RFC-defined state transition.

### Framework B: State Transition Analysis
Proposed by *Porras and Kemmerer (1992, foundational concept)*, this framework models each attack as a **chain of state changes** on a target system. The system is defined by a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{S} = (S, A, T, s_0, s_f, E)
$$

where:
- $S$ = finite set of system states
- $A$ = finite set of atomic actions
- $T \subseteq S \times A \rightarrow S$ = transition function
- $s_0 \in S$ = initial (secure) state
- $s_f \in S$ = final (compromised) state
- $E \subseteq S \times S$ = edges representing privilege escalations

An attack is then a **path** in the state-space graph from $s_0$ to $s_f$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Application |
|------------------|---------|-------------|
| $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | **5-tuple of a finite automaton** | Foundation of all protocol state analysis |
| $\delta(q, a) = q'$ | **Transition function** | Determines next state $q'$ from current state $q$ on input $a$ |
| $P(S_{t+1} \mid S_t, e_t)$ | **State transition probability** | Used in probabilistic state machines (e.g., HMM-based IDPS) |
| $\theta$ (threshold) | **Detection threshold** | $\theta \in [0,1]$; lower = stricter detection, higher false positives |
| $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | **Detection rate** | Fraction of attacks correctly flagged |
| $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | **Detection precision** | Fraction of alarms that are real attacks |
| $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | **False Positive Rate** | Must be minimized for operational viability |
| $H = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | **State entropy** (bits) | Higher entropy = more disordered state; useful in anomaly scoring |
| $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{attack}})$ | **Mean Time To Detect** | Average detection latency over $N$ incidents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** State-based detection is the *backbone* of every commercial NIDS (Snort, Suricata, Zeek). It enables the IDS to defeat evasion techniques such as **session splicing, fragmentation overlap, and TCP state manipulation** — all of which are trivial against stateless systems.

## 2.4 Comparison: State-Based vs. Stateless Approaches

| Property | Stateless Inspection | State-Based Inspection |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Memory Usage | **O(1)** per packet | **O(C)** where $C$ = active connections |
| Processing Speed | Very Fast (line-rate) | Moderate (requires state lookup) |
| Evasion Resistance | Low | **High** |
| Protocol Awareness | Shallow (L3/L4) | **Deep (L3–L7)** |
| False Negative Rate | High against crafted attacks | **Low** for protocol-based attacks |
| Scalability | Excellent | Limited by connection table size |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mathematical Model of a State Transition Detector

We now derive the formal decision rule for a probabilistic state-based intrusion detector.

### Step 1: Define the State Space
Let the system state at time $t$ be denoted $S_t$, taking values from a discrete set $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ where $n = \vert S \vert$.

### Step 2: Define the Observation Sequence
Let the observed event stream be:

$$
E = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_T\}
$$

Each $e_t \in \Sigma$ where $\Sigma$ is the alphabet of possible events (e.g., $\{SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, URG\}$ for TCP).

### Step 3: Build the State Transition Probability Matrix
Define the **transition matrix** $\mathbf{A}$ of size $n \times n$:

$$
\mathbf{A}_{ij} = P(S_{t+1} = s_j \mid S_t = s_i)
$$

By the axioms of probability, each row must satisfy:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbf{A}_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

### Step 4: Emission Probability Matrix
Define the **emission matrix** $\mathbf{B}$ of size $n \times \vert \Sigma \vert$:

$$
\mathbf{B}_{ik} = P(e_t = \Sigma_k \mid S_t = s_i)
$$

with the constraint:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{\vert \Sigma \vert} \mathbf{B}_{ik} = 1 \quad \forall i
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Path Likelihood
For a candidate state sequence $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T\}$, the joint probability of observing $E$ given $Q$ is:

$$
P(E \mid Q) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(e_t \mid q_t) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} \mathbf{B}_{q_t, e_t}
$$

### Step 6: Combine with Transition Probabilities
The total probability of the state path $Q$ producing the observations $E$ is:

$$
P(E, Q) = P(q_1) \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} \mathbf{A}_{q_t, q_{t+1}} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} \mathbf{B}_{q_t, e_t}
$$

### Step 7: Anomaly Score (Negative Log Likelihood)
Take the negative log to obtain a tractable additive score:

$$
\mathcal{L}(E) = -\log P(E, \hat{Q}) = -\log P(q_1) - \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \log \mathbf{A}_{q_t, q_{t+1}} - \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \mathbf{B}_{q_t, e_t}
$$

### Step 8: Final Decision Rule
The IDPS raises an alert when:

$$
\mathcal{L}(E) > \mathcal{L}_{\text{threshold}} = -\log \theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the configured anomaly threshold. Rearranging:

$$
P(E, \hat{Q}) < \theta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{ALERT}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (TCP SYN Flood Detection)

Consider a simplified 3-state model for a server: $S = \{\text{Idle}, \text{Listen}, \text{Established}\}$. The transition matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix}
0.90 & 0.10 & 0.00 \\
0.05 & 0.80 & 0.15 \\
0.00 & 0.10 & 0.90
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Suppose we observe 50 SYN packets in 5 seconds, all of which transition the server `Idle` $\to$ `Listen` $\to$ `Listen` (because no ACK completes the handshake). The likelihood of this sequence is:

$$
P(E \mid \hat{Q}) = (0.10)^{50} \cdot (0.80)^{49} \approx 10^{-55}
$$

This is astronomically low. The negative log-likelihood is $\approx 127$ bits, vastly exceeding the threshold (typically $\mathcal{L}_{\text{threshold}} \approx 20$ bits). The detector **triggers an alert** — correctly identifying a SYN flood.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack Types vs. State-Based Detection

| Attack Class | Specific Attack | State Violation | Detection Method |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Port Scanning | SYN Scan, FIN Scan | Connection never reaches `ESTABLISHED` | Count half-open connections |
| Denial of Service | SYN Flood | Massive `SYN-RECEIVED` accumulation | Resource exhaustion threshold |
| Spoofing | IP Spoofing in TCP | Sequence number prediction impossible | Stateful sequence number tracking |
| Session Hijacking | TCP Session Stealing | Inconsistent sequence number jumps | $\Delta$SeqNumber anomaly |
| Web Attacks | HTTP Request Smuggling | Duplicate `Content-Length` or malformed chunked encoding | Application-layer FSM |
| FTP Bounce | PORT command abuse | FTP data channel state corruption | FTP command state machine |
| DNS Cache Poisoning | Birthday attack on TXID | Unauthorized TXID updates | DNS transaction FSM |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level State-Based IDPS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Traffic Input] --> B[Packet Capture Engine]
    B --> C[State Table Lookup]
    C --> D{State Exists?}
    D -- No --> E[Create New Connection Record]
    D -- Yes --> F[Retrieve Current State]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply FSM Transition Function]
    G --> H{Valid Transition?}
    H -- No --> I[Anomaly Detector: Flag Violation]
    H -- Yes --> J[Update State Table]
    I --> K[Alert Generation Module]
    J --> L[Continue Monitoring]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Response Engine: Log/Block/Reset]
```

## 4.2 Simplified TCP State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LISTEN
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD : SYN Received
    LISTEN --> SYN_SENT : Active Open SYN Sent
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RCVD : SYN+ACK Received
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : SYN+ACK Sent
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : ACK Received
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT1 : FIN Sent
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : FIN Received
    FIN_WAIT1 --> FIN_WAIT2 : ACK Received
    FIN_WAIT1 --> CLOSING : FIN Received
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : FIN Sent
    FIN_WAIT2 --> TIME_WAIT : FIN Received
    CLOSING --> TIME_WAIT : ACK Received
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : ACK Received
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : 2MSL Timeout
    CLOSED --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each box is a legal TCP state. Each arrow is a legal transition triggered by a specific flag combination. Any state-change not represented by an arrow is an **attack indicator**.

## 4.3 State Transition Analysis for a Sample Attack (Buffer Overflow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 0: Normal Execution] -->|Input length > buffer| B[State 1: Buffer Filled]
    B -->|Return Address Overwritten| C[State 2: Stack Smashed]
    C -->|Attacker Payload Executed| D[State 3: Shell Spawned]
    D --> E[State 4: System Compromised]
    A -.->|Legitimate Input| F[State 0: Continued Normal Execution]
```

**Explanation:** The solid arrows represent the **attack path** (the penetration signature). The dotted arrow represents the legitimate transition. An IDPS using state transition analysis recognizes that reaching `State 3` requires passing through `State 1 → State 2`, and can therefore block the attack at any intermediate stage.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Layer
        A1[Raw Packets In]
    end
    subgraph Capture_Layer
        B1[Packet Decoder]
        B2[Protocol Demultiplexer]
    end
    subgraph State_Engine
        C1[State Table DB]
        C2[FSM Evaluator]
        C3[Anomaly Scorer]
    end
    subgraph Decision_Layer
        D1[Threshold Comparator]
        D2[Alert Correlator]
    end
    subgraph Response_Layer
        E1[Logging Subsystem]
        E2[Active Response: Drop/Reset]
        E3[Forensic Recorder]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3 --> D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D2 --> E3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define "State Transition Analysis" in the context of Intrusion Detection Systems. How does it differ from a simple pattern-matching signature approach?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
State Transition Analysis is a state-based intrusion detection technique that models the behavior of a system as a sequence of states and the actions of an attacker as transitions between those states. The system is represented by a finite state machine $\mathcal{S} = (S, A, T, s_0, s_f, E)$ where $s_0$ is the secure initial state and $s_f$ is the compromised final state. **Difference from pattern matching:** While simple signature matching looks for a *static byte pattern* in individual packets, state transition analysis examines the *ordered sequence* of state changes across multiple events, allowing it to detect attacks that span many packets or that produce no fixed signature. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for FSM components, 1 mark for distinction.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*List any THREE advantages of stateful protocol analysis over stateless packet inspection in an IDPS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Evasion Resistance:** Stateful analysis prevents attacks based on fragmentation, session splicing, and out-of-order packet delivery (1 mark).
2. **Protocol Compliance Verification:** It can enforce RFC-defined state machines, immediately flagging illegal flag combinations (e.g., all flags set) (1 mark).
3. **Reduced False Negatives for Multi-Packet Attacks:** It detects attacks distributed across many packets, such as slowloris or TCP session hijacking, which stateless systems miss (1 mark).

---

## 📗 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a **Stateful Protocol Analyzer** for the TCP protocol. Describe how it would detect a SYN flood attack.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the anomaly score formula $\mathcal{L}(E) = -\log P(E, \hat{Q})$ for a probabilistic state-based detector and explain how the threshold $\theta$ is selected in practice.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Stateful Protocol Analysis for TCP (7 marks)**

A Stateful Protocol Analyzer maintains a **connection state table** indexed by the 5-tuple $\langle \text{SrcIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstIP}, \text{DstPort}, \text{Protocol} \rangle$. For each connection, it stores the current state from the TCP state machine (LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RCVD, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, CLOSING, LAST-ACK, TIME-WAIT, CLOSED).

**Operation Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Packet Capture:** Incoming packet is decoded into a 5-tuple.
2. **State Lookup:** The state table is queried for an existing entry.
3. **Transition Evaluation:** The TCP FSM is consulted to check if the new packet (e.g., with flags SYN, ACK, FIN) is a legal transition from the stored state.
4. **Update or Alert:** If legal, the new state is written back. If illegal, an alert is raised.

**SYN Flood Detection (3 marks):**
During a SYN flood, the attacker sends massive numbers of SYN packets from spoofed source addresses. The analyzer sees:
- Numerous new connections entering `SYN-RCVD` state
- **No corresponding ACK packets** to complete the handshake
- Accumulation of half-open connections in `SYN-RCVD`

The analyzer counts the number of half-open connections per source/destination pair. When this count exceeds a threshold (typically $> 100$ for a single source), the system raises a SYN flood alert and can activate mitigations such as SYN cookies or rate-limiting.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of the Anomaly Score (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the state path $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_T\}$ and observation sequence $E = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_T\}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Using the Markov assumption, the joint probability factors as:

$$
P(E, Q) = P(q_1) \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t) \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(e_t \mid q_t)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute matrix notation $\mathbf{A}$ (transitions) and $\mathbf{B}$ (emissions):

$$
P(E, Q) = P(q_1) \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T-1} \mathbf{A}_{q_t, q_{t+1}} \cdot \prod_{t=1}^{T} \mathbf{B}_{q_t, e_t}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Take the negative log to obtain an additive score:

$$
\mathcal{L}(E) = -\log P(q_1) - \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \log \mathbf{A}_{q_t, q_{t+1}} - \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \mathbf{B}_{q_t, e_t}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks): Threshold Selection.** The threshold $\theta$ is selected empirically using a **ROC curve** approach:
- Train on clean traffic to establish baseline likelihood.
- Vary $\theta$ from $0$ to $1$ and plot Detection Rate vs. False Positive Rate.
- Choose the operating point where **Youden's J statistic** $J = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$ is maximized.
- In practice, $\theta$ is adjusted dynamically using **adaptive thresholding** based on traffic volume.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **State Transition Analysis** technique with reference to a real-world attack (e.g., password guessing or buffer overflow). Provide a labeled state transition diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the three state-based techniques (Stateful Protocol Analysis, State Transition Analysis, and FSM-Based Detection) in a comparative table covering: detection principle, granularity, computational cost, attack coverage, and example tools.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — State Transition Analysis with a Real-World Example (7 marks)**

State Transition Analysis models a system and an attack as a state graph. **For a password guessing attack (4 marks):**

- **State 0:** User Logged Out (secure)
- **State 1:** Login Attempt with Wrong Password
- **State 2:** Multiple Failed Login Attempts Detected
- **State 3:** Account Locked (final secure state)

The **legitimate transitions** are: $S_0 \to S_1$ (wrong password) and $S_0 \to$ *Authenticated* (correct password).

The **attack path** is: $S_0 \to S_1 \to S_1 \to S_1 \to \ldots \to S_2 \to S_3$.

An IDPS recognizes the rapid repetition of $S_0 \to S_1$ transitions as a password guessing signature. The penetration signature here is the *high frequency of state 0 → state 1* transitions within a short time window.

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 0: Logged Out] -->|Wrong Password| B[State 1: Failed Attempt]
    A -->|Correct Password| C[State 4: Authenticated]
    B -->|Wrong Password| B
    B -->|3rd Failure| D[State 2: Threshold Exceeded]
    D -->|Lock Activated| E[State 3: Account Locked]
```

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Table (7 marks — 1 mark per correctly filled row plus 2 marks for example tools)**

| Criterion | Stateful Protocol Analysis | State Transition Analysis | FSM-Based Detection |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Detection Principle** | Validates packets against RFC protocol state machine | Models attacks as state sequences from secure to compromised state | Encodes system behavior as a deterministic/non-deterministic automaton |
| **Granularity** | Protocol-level (L4–L7) | System/call-level (L7/host) | Both protocol and application level |
| **Computational Cost** | Moderate (state table lookup) | High (path matching) | High (full automaton simulation) |
| **Attack Coverage** | Reconnaissance, DoS, protocol exploits | Multi-step privilege escalation, insider attacks | Comprehensive — combines both above |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **Example Tools** | Snort (stream4 preprocessor), Suricata, Zeek | USTAT, NetSTAT, IDIOT | Bro, custom IDS frameworks |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "Stateful Inspection" (a generic NIDS term) with "State Transition Analysis" (a specific attack-modeling technique). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> - **Always draw** the state transition diagram when asked about State Transition Analysis — answers without diagrams lose **at least 2 marks** in Part B.
> - When writing the FSM tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$, **do not omit** any element. A common mistake is forgetting $F$ (the final/accepting states).
> - For threshold selection, simply stating "$\theta$ is set" is insufficient — you **must justify** the choice (e.g., via ROC, Youden's index, or empirical tuning).

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **State-Based Techniques** track the *evolution* of a system/connection through predefined states rather than inspecting events in isolation.
- ✅ Three core variants: **Stateful Protocol Analysis**, **State Transition Analysis**, and **FSM-Based Detection**.
- ✅ The foundational mathematical model is the **Finite State Machine** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$.
- ✅ The **anomaly score** is computed as $-\log P(E, \hat{Q})$ and compared against a **threshold** $\theta$.
- ✅ **TCP has 11 canonical states** in RFC 793; any transition not in the spec is suspicious.
- ✅ **SYN Flood** is detected by counting half-open connections in `SYN-RCVD` state.
- ✅ **State Transition Analysis** models attacks as paths from $s_0$ (secure) to $s_f$ (compromised).
- ✅ **Advantages:** High evasion resistance, deep protocol awareness, low false negatives.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Higher memory footprint ($O(C)$ for $C$ active connections), moderate processing overhead.
- ✅ **Tools to remember:** Snort, Suricata, Zeek (Bro), USTAT, NetSTAT, IDIOT.
- ✅ **Threshold selection** is done via ROC analysis or Youden's J statistic.
- ✅ **Difference from pattern matching:** Pattern matching is *stateless* and per-packet; state-based is *sequential* and multi-event.
- ✅ **Mermaid diagram convention** in KTU answers: Use boxes (rectangles) for states and arrows (labeled with events) for transitions.
- ✅ **Always include both the formula AND its interpretation** when answering derivation questions in Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anomaly Detection - Advanced Statistical Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Anomaly Detection - Advanced Statistical Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an **Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDPS)**, *Advanced Statistical Models for Anomaly Detection* refer to a class of computational techniques that build a rigorous mathematical profile of legitimate system, network, or user behavior using probability theory, stochastic processes, and multivariate statistics. Any observed event whose statistical signature deviates *significantly* from this learned profile is flagged as a potential intrusion.

According to the **Denning model (1987)** and its modern statistical extensions, an anomaly is declared when the test statistic $T(\mathbf{x})$ computed on an observation vector $\mathbf{x}$ crosses a decision threshold $\tau$ determined by a chosen significance level (typically **$\alpha = 0.01$** or **$\alpha = 0.05$**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT742 – Module 4):**
> "Advanced statistical anomaly detection models use parametric distributions, multivariate analysis, Markov processes, Bayesian inference, and time-series techniques to profile normal behavior and statistically infer deviations indicative of attacks such as DoS, probing, R2L, and U2R."

The two principal categories are:

- **Parametric Models** – assume a known probability distribution (e.g., Gaussian, Poisson) and estimate its parameters (mean, variance, transition matrix).
- **Non-Parametric Models** – make no distributional assumption and rely on kernel density estimation, histograms, or rank statistics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy – "The Credit-Card Spending Fingerprint"**
> Imagine your bank's fraud engine. It does not know every possible fraudulent pattern. Instead, it remembers the *statistical fingerprint* of *your* normal spending: average transaction ₹2,500, 90% of transactions happen between 8 AM and 11 PM, you rarely shop abroad, and consecutive transactions are usually separated by hours, not seconds. Suddenly, it sees three transactions of ₹80,000 from a foreign country at 3 AM within 60 seconds. The probability of seeing such a sequence under "your normal model" is astronomically low — so it raises a red flag. That is exactly how statistical anomaly detection works in an IDPS, except the "transactions" are network packets, system calls, or login events, and the "fingerprint" is a probability distribution or a stochastic process.

## 1.3 Why Advanced Models are Needed

Simple threshold-based or rule-based detection fails against:
- **Zero-day attacks** (no known signature)
- **Polymorphic malware**
- **Low-and-slow attacks** (e.g., APTs)
- **Concept drift** (legitimate behavior evolves)

Statistical models address these by learning *behavioral baselines* and quantifying *deviation probabilities*.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** Two error rates dominate statistical IDS design — **False Positive Rate (FPR)** = $\alpha$ and **False Negative Rate (FNR)** = $\beta$. KTU questions frequently test the trade-off between these using the **ROC curve** and the concept of **detection power** $(1-\beta)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Statistical Anomaly Detection

A statistical IDPS works in two operational phases:

**Phase 1 — Training (Baseline Establishment):**
1. Collect observation vectors $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.
2. Choose a statistical model family (Gaussian, Markov, Bayesian, etc.).
3. Estimate model parameters $\hat{\theta}$ using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** or **Bayesian Estimation**.
4. Compute a decision threshold $\tau$ corresponding to significance level $\alpha$.

**Phase 2 — Detection (Online Inference):**
1. For each new observation $\mathbf{x}_{new}$, compute a *test statistic* or *likelihood*.
2. Compute $P(\mathbf{x}_{new} \mid \hat{\theta})$ — the probability of observing $\mathbf{x}_{new}$ under the normal model.
3. If this probability is below $\tau$, or equivalently, if the test statistic exceeds the critical value, raise an **alert**.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- *Why estimate parameters?* Because we do not know the true distribution of legitimate traffic.
- *Why use MLE?* It is asymptotically unbiased and efficient.
- *Why a threshold?* To convert a continuous probability into a binary decision (normal vs. attack).

## 2.2 Family of Advanced Statistical Models

### 2.2.1 Univariate Parametric Models
- Assume each feature $x_i$ is i.i.d. and follows a known distribution.
- **Gaussian (Normal):** $x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.
- **Poisson:** For event counts per unit time, $P(X=k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$.

### 2.2.2 Multivariate Gaussian Model
- Joint distribution of feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- Captures **correlations** between features (e.g., packet size and inter-arrival time).

### 2.2.3 Markov Chain Models
- Model **temporal dependencies** between system states, commands, or system calls.
- Used by **NIDES (Next-generation Intrusion Detection Expert System)**.

### 2.2.4 Hidden Markov Models (HMM)
- Model sequences of *observable* events generated by *hidden* states.
- Applied in **HMM-based IDS for system-call sequences** (Warrender et al., 1999).

### 2.2.5 Bayesian Networks
- Encode **causal dependencies** between variables via Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs).
- Used in **eBayes / Adaptive Bayesian IDS** for alert correlation.

### 2.2.6 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
- Projects data onto a low-dimensional subspace.
- Anomalies have **high reconstruction error** in the residual subspace.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists the **exam-critical formulas** for Module 4. Memorize the variables, assumptions, and the threshold/decision rule for each.

| # | Model / Concept | Core Formula | Decision Rule / Threshold | Variables & Units |
|---|----------------|--------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Gaussian Likelihood (univariate) | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | If $f(x) < \tau$, flag anomaly | $\mu$ in feature units, $\sigma$ in same units |
| 2 | Mahalanobis Distance² (multivariate) | $D_M^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\mathsf{T}} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ | If $D_M^2 > \chi^2_{n,\alpha}$, flag anomaly | $\boldsymbol{\mu}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ |
| 3 | Hotelling's $T^2$ Statistic | $T^2 = N(\bar{\mathbf{x}}-\boldsymbol{\mu_0})^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{S}^{-1} (\bar{\mathbf{x}}-\boldsymbol{\mu_0})$ | Compare with $F$-distribution | $N$ = sample size, $\mathbf{S}$ = sample covariance |
| 4 | Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit | $\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | If $\chi^2 > \chi^2_{k-1,\alpha}$, reject normality | $O_i$ observed, $E_i$ expected counts |
| 5 | Markov Transition Probability | $P(X_{t+1} \mid X_t, \ldots, X_1) = P(X_{t+1} \mid X_t)$ | Low transition prob. = anomaly | Transition matrix $\mathbf{A}$ with $\sum_j A_{ij}=1$ |
| 6 | HMM Forward Variable | $\alpha_t(i) = \left[\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(j) a_{ji}\right] b_i(O_t)$ | Sequence anomaly if $P(\mathbf{O}\mid\lambda) < \tau$ | $a_{ji}$ transition, $b_i(O_t)$ emission |
| 7 | Bayes Posterior | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Argmax over hypotheses | $H$ hypothesis, $E$ evidence |
| 8 | Naive Bayes Classification | $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Choose $\arg\max_k$ | $C_k$ class, $x_i$ features |
| 9 | PCA Reconstruction Error (SPE) | $\mathrm{SPE} = \lVert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \rVert^2_2 = \mathbf{x}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}^{\mathsf{T}})\mathbf{x}$ | If $\mathrm{SPE} > \delta^2_\alpha$, anomaly | $\mathbf{P}$ = loading matrix, $\delta^2_\alpha$ = SPE limit |
| 10 | Type I / II Error | $\alpha = P(\text{Reject }H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ true})$; $\beta = P(\text{Fail to reject }H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})$ | Minimize $\alpha + \beta$ subject to constraints | Dimensionless probabilities in $[0,1]$ |
| 11 | Entropy (Information Content) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | High $H(X)$ = high disorder (potential anomaly) | Measured in **bits** |
| 12 | KL Divergence (relative entropy) | $D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \sum_i P(i) \log\!\dfrac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | If $D_{KL} > \tau$, distribution shifted | Dimensionless, non-negative |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on Notation:** In all formulas above, $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ denotes the Euclidean norm. Use `\lvert` / `\rvert` in LaTeX for absolute-value bars to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Statistical Model Used | Purpose |
|--------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Network IDS (NIDS) | Multivariate Gaussian, PCA | Detect DDoS, port scans |
| Host IDS (HIDS) | HMM, Markov chain | Detect malware via system-call sequences |
| Web Application Firewall | Bayesian, Chi-square | Detect SQLi, XSS |
| SCADA / ICS Security | Time-series ARIMA | Detect slow attacks on industrial processes |
| Cloud Workload Protection | Bayesian networks | Detect anomalous API call patterns |
| Financial Fraud IDS | Hidden Markov Models | Detect card-skimming sequences |
