| Traditional (Signature) Guard | Learning-Model Guard |
|---|---|
| Has a printed list of 10,000 banned faces. | Watches people for **2 weeks**, then forms a statistical *idea* of "normal" gait, dressing, timing. |
| Misses anyone *not* on the list (zero-day). | Flags anyone whose behaviour **deviates** from the learned baseline. |
| Never updates without manual printouts. | Continuously **re-trains** as new patterns emerge. |

The **Learning Model** is exactly that guard — it builds a *mental model* of normality (or known attack classes) and uses it to score every new observation.

---

## 1.3 Core Mathematical Notation Used Throughout

- Let the input traffic feature vector be: $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d) \in \mathbb{R}^d$
- Training dataset: $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ for supervised, or $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ for unsupervised.
- The learned **hypothesis** (decision function): $h_\theta : \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$
- Parameters: $\theta$ (or $\mathbf{w}, \mathbf{b}$)
- Loss function: $\mathcal{L}(\theta; \mathcal{D})$

---

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Learning in IDPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The 4 Learning Paradigms:**
> 1. **What to learn?** — Feature engineering & representation
> 2. **How to learn?** — Optimization algorithm (SGD, Adam, Q-Learning)
> 3. **How to evaluate?** — Detection Rate, FPR, F1, ROC-AUC
> 4. **How to update?** — Online, batch, or continual learning

These pillars are the recurring themes of every KTU 14-mark question on this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Learning Pipeline (Operational Concept)

The IDPS learning pipeline executes in **six ordered stages**. Every KTU question on "Explain a learning-based IDPS" expects these stages.

1. **Data Acquisition & Labelling** — Capture packets/flows from NIC, netflow sensors, or host logs. Apply labels (attack/normal) using tools like **CICFlowMeter** or **Wireshark**.
2. **Pre-processing** — Handle missing values, normalize features, encode categorical protocol types.
3. **Feature Engineering / Selection** — Reduce dimensionality from thousands of raw fields to a compact discriminative set (e.g., Mutual Information, PCA, Chi-Square).
4. **Model Training** — Feed $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ to the chosen algorithm; minimize the loss function $\mathcal{L}$.
5. **Validation & Hyper-parameter Tuning** — Use k-fold cross-validation; tune via Grid Search / Bayesian Optimization.
6. **Deployment & Online Adaptation** — Deploy the model in the IDPS engine; periodically re-train on freshly labelled data (concept drift handling).

> **Engineering Utility:** Modern commercial systems like **Cisco Secure Network Analytics** (formerly Stealthwatch) and **Darktrace** are entirely built on unsupervised and deep learning models — demonstrating that *learning models* are no longer optional but the **industry standard**.

---

## 2.2 Supervised Learning Models — Theory & Boundaries

### 2.2.1 Decision Tree (ID3 / C4.5 / CART)

- Splits feature space recursively using the metric that maximizes **information gain**.
- **Information Gain** for split on attribute $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where entropy is:

$$H(S) = - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

- **Boundary condition:** $H(S) = 0$ when $S$ is pure (all samples same class).
- Highly **interpretable** — favoured in forensic IDPS use cases.

### 2.2.2 Naïve Bayes Classifier

- Applies **Bayes' Theorem** with a strong (often unrealistic) **conditional independence** assumption between features.

$$P(y \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(y) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \vert y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

- Decision rule: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(y) \prod_j P(x_j \vert y)$
- Extremely fast; baseline model in **KDD-Cup-99** benchmarks.

### 2.2.3 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)

- Lazy learner. Classifies $\mathbf{x}_{new}$ by majority vote of its $k$ closest training points using a distance metric $d(\cdot, \cdot)$.
- Common distance: **Minkowski** of order $p$:

$$d_p(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \left( \sum_{l=1}^{d} \vert x_{i,l} - x_{j,l} \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$$

- **Boundary condition:** $p=1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan; $p=2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean; $p=\infty \Rightarrow$ Chebyshev.
- Curse of dimensionality — suffers badly when $d$ is large.

### 2.2.4 Support Vector Machine (SVM)

- Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating two classes.
- Primal optimization (soft-margin):

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to $y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$.

- The **kernel trick** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ allows non-linear boundaries.
- Common kernels: **Linear, Polynomial, RBF (Gaussian).**

### 2.2.5 Ensemble Methods — Random Forest & XGBoost

- **Random Forest** = Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) of $B$ decision trees.
- Final prediction: $\hat{y} = \text{majority\_vote}\{h_b(\mathbf{x})\}_{b=1}^{B}$
- **XGBoost** = Boosting — sequentially adds weak learners minimizing a regularized objective.

---

## 2.3 Unsupervised Learning Models — Theory & Boundaries

### 2.3.1 k-Means Clustering

- Partitions $N$ samples into $k$ clusters by minimizing **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{ik} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$$

- Update rules:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{\sum_{i} r_{ik}^{(t)} \mathbf{x}_i}{\sum_i r_{ik}^{(t)}}$$

- Anomaly score: distance to nearest centroid. Points far from any centroid $\Rightarrow$ anomaly.

### 2.3.2 Hierarchical Clustering

- **Agglomerative** (bottom-up): start with each point as its own cluster, merge closest pairs using **linkage** criteria:
  - Single: $d(A,B) = \min_{a\in A, b\in B} d(a,b)$
  - Complete: $d(A,B) = \max_{a\in A, b\in B} d(a,b)$
  - Average: $d(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert} \sum_{a,b} d(a,b)$

### 2.3.3 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)

- Two parameters: $\epsilon$ (neighbourhood radius), $MinPts$ (min points to form dense region).
- Three point types: **Core, Border, Noise.**
- Noise points = anomalies — directly useful for IDPS zero-day detection.

### 2.3.4 Isolation Forest

- Anomalies are *few and different* ⇒ they are **easier to isolate** via random partitioning.
- Anomaly score from average path length $E(h(\mathbf{x}))$ over $t$ trees:

$$s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(\mathbf{x}))}{c(n)}}$$

where $c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$, and $H(i)$ is the $i$-th harmonic number.
- $s \to 1$ ⇒ anomaly; $s < 0.5$ ⇒ normal.

### 2.3.5 One-Class SVM

- Learns a tight boundary around normal data in feature space; deviations = anomalies.

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, \rho, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + \frac{1}{\nu N} \sum_i \xi_i - \rho$$

subject to $\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \geq \rho - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$.

### 2.3.6 Autoencoders (Deep Unsupervised)

- Encoder $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^h$ ($h < d$); decoder $\psi: \mathbb{R}^h \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^d$.
- Reconstruction error: $E(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \psi(\phi(\mathbf{x})) \Vert^2$
- High $E(\mathbf{x})$ on a new sample ⇒ anomaly.

---

## 2.4 Reinforcement Learning in IDPS

- **Markov Decision Process (MDP):** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$
- The agent learns policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ maximizing expected cumulative reward:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

- **Q-Learning update rule:**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

- In IDPS: state = current traffic statistics, action = {allow, block, alert, throttle}, reward = based on detection accuracy and false-positive penalty.

---

## 2.5 Semi-Supervised Learning

- **Self-Training:** Train on labelled set $\mathcal{L}$, predict on unlabelled set $\mathcal{U}$, add high-confidence predictions back to $\mathcal{L}$, repeat.
- **Co-Training:** Two independent views (e.g., header features + payload features) teach each other.
- Critical for IDPS because **labelled attack data is rare** but raw network traffic is abundant.

---

## 2.6 Deep Learning Models

| Model | IDPS Use Case | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **MLP** | Tabular flow features | $a^{(l)} = \sigma(W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$ |
| **CNN (1-D)** | Raw packet bytes | Convolution + pooling |
| **RNN / LSTM** | Sequential flow behaviour | $h_t = \text{LSTM}(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ |
| **Autoencoder** | Unsupervised anomaly | Reconstruction error |
| **GAN** | Synthetic attack generation | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$ |
| **Transformer** | Long-range flow context | Self-attention |

Self-attention core formula:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left( \frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right) V$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula you must memorize** for the ESE on Learning Models. Memorize the equation column, not just the names.

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_c p_c \log_2 p_c$ | $0 \leq H \leq \log_2 C$ |
| 2 | Information Gain | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $IG \geq 0$ |
| 3 | Bayes Decision | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y P(y) \prod_j P(x_j \vert y)$ | Sums to 1 over $y$ |
| 4 | Minkowski Distance | $d_p = \left( \sum_l \vert x_{i,l} - x_{j,l} \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ |
| 5 | SVM Margin | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ | $C > 0$ controls trade-off |
| 6 | k-Means Objective | $J = \sum_i \sum_k r_{ik} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ | $r_{ik} \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 7 | Isolation Score | $s = 2^{-E(h(\mathbf{x}))/c(n)}$ | $s \to 1$ = anomaly |
| 8 | Autoencoder Error | $E = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \psi(\phi(\mathbf{x})) \Vert^2$ | Higher = more anomalous |
| 9 | Q-Learning Update | $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha [R + \gamma \max Q' - Q]$ | $0 \leq \alpha, \gamma \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Self-Attention | $\text{softmax}(Q K^\top / \sqrt{d_k}) V$ | $d_k$ = key dimension |

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Darktrace** — uses unsupervised Bayesian + RNN models for zero-day detection.
- **Vectra AI** — uses supervised + reinforcement learning for threat prioritization.
- **Splunk UBA** — applies Isolation Forest and LSTM on user behaviour analytics.
- **Academic Benchmarks:** **NSL-KDD**, **CICIDS-2017**, **UNSW-NB15**, **TON_IoT** — these are the datasets referenced in 90% of KTU paper-III questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Decision Tree Split Criterion (Information Gain)

We start with the formal definition of entropy and progressively derive the gain formula used by C4.5.

**Given:** A dataset $S$ containing $C$ classes, with $p_c$ the proportion of class-$c$ samples in $S$.

**Step 1 — Define entropy in bits:**

$$H(S) = - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

**Step 2 — Conditional entropy after splitting on attribute $A$:**

$$H(S \vert A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

This is the weighted average entropy of the child nodes $S_v$.

**Step 3 — Information gain is the reduction in entropy:**

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**Step 4 — Conversion logic (textual walk-through):**
- We start with the **uncertainty** $H(S)$ of the parent node.
- We split the parent into $|V|$ children $S_v$.
- The **remaining uncertainty** is the weighted sum of child entropies.
- Subtracting the remaining uncertainty from the original gives the **uncertainty reduction**, i.e., information gained by the split.

**Step 5 — Boundary check:** If the split is pure, $H(S_v) = 0$ for all $v$, so $H(S \vert A) = 0$ and $IG(S,A) = H(S)$ — the maximum possible gain for that node.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: k-Means Convergence Update

**Given:** Data $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and current centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)}\}_{k=1}^{K}$.

**Step 1 — Define the assignment indicator:**

$$r_{ik}^{(t)} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k = \arg\min_{k'} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k'}^{(t)} \Vert^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Express the WCSS objective with assignments:**

$$J^{(t)} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{ik}^{(t)} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \Vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Minimize $J$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ (fixed assignments).** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = -2 \sum_i r_{ik} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$:**

$$\sum_i r_{ik} \mathbf{x}_i = \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \sum_i r_{ik} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{\sum_i r_{ik} \mathbf{x}_i}{\sum_i r_{ik}}$$

**Step 5 — Conversion logic (textual walk-through):**
- We first **assign** every point to its nearest centroid (this is the E-step analogue).
- We then **recompute** each centroid as the *mean* of points currently assigned to it (this is the M-step analogue).
- Iteration continues until $r_{ik}$ no longer changes — the algorithm has converged to a (local) minimum of $J$.

**Step 6 — Convergence guarantee:** $J$ is non-increasing at every iteration (Lloyd's algorithm is a **majorization-minimization** procedure), ensuring termination.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Q-Learning Bellman Update

**Given:** An MDP $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ and current Q-table estimate $Q(s,a)$.

**Step 1 — Define the optimal action-value function (Bellman optimality):**

$$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1}, a') \,\Big\vert\, s_t = s, a_t = a \right]$$

**Step 2 — We don't know $P$ or $R$, so we replace the expectation with a sample:**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow (1 - \alpha) Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the canonical update form:**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

**Step 4 — The bracketed term is the **TD-error** (temporal-difference error):**

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

**Step 5 — Conversion logic (textual walk-through):**
- The current Q-estimate is "moved" by a fraction $\alpha$ of the TD-error.
- $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** — small $\alpha$ means slow but stable learning; large $\alpha$ means fast but noisy.
- $\gamma$ is the **discount factor** — small $\gamma$ makes the agent myopic (focus on immediate reward); large $\gamma$ makes it far-sighted.
- Under standard stochastic approximation conditions (Robbins-Monro: $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$), $Q$ converges to $Q^*$.

**Step 6 — Boundary check:** When $\alpha = 0$, no learning occurs. When $\gamma = 0$, the agent is greedy. When $\alpha = 1$ and $\gamma = 1$, the update is unbiased but may oscillate.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Isolation Forest Anomaly Score

**Step 1 — Build an Isolation Tree (iTree):**
- Repeatedly select a random feature $q$ and a random split value $p$ uniformly in $(q_{\min}, q_{\max})$.
- Recurse on the child node containing $\mathbf{x}$ until the node is pure or tree height reaches $\lim$.

**Step 2 — Path length $h(\mathbf{x})$:** number of edges from root to the leaf containing $\mathbf{x}$ in a given iTree.

**Step 3 — Average path length over $t$ iTrees:**

$$E(h(\mathbf{x})) = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{b=1}^{t} h_b(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4 — Average path length of an unsuccessful search in a Binary Search Tree of $n$ samples** (used for normalization):

$$c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$$

where $H(k) = \ln(k) + \gamma_E$ (Euler-Mascheroni constant, $\gamma_E \approx 0.5772$).

**Step 5 — Anomaly score:**

$$s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(\mathbf{x}))}{c(n)}}$$

**Step 6 — Conversion logic (textual walk-through):**
- If $E(h(\mathbf{x})) \to 0$, then $s \to 2^0 = 1$ — *short path ⇒ easy to isolate ⇒ anomaly*.
- If $E(h(\mathbf{x})) \to c(n)$, then $s \to 2^{-1} = 0.5$ — *average path ⇒ normal*.
- If $E(h(\mathbf{x})) \to n-1$, then $s \to 0$ — *very long path ⇒ certainly normal*.
- Threshold typically $s \geq 0.5$ to flag as anomaly.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: When to Use What?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the single most important table for KTU 14-mark "Compare and contrast" questions.

| Criterion | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-Supervised | Reinforcement | Deep Learning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Labelled data needed?** | Yes (large) | No | Small set | Reward signal only | Yes / No (depends) |
| **Detects zero-day?** | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | Good (via policy) | Excellent |
| **Interpretability** | High (DT, NB) | Low–Medium | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **Computational cost** | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium | High (training) | Very High |
| **Online adaptability** | Retrain | Yes | Yes (self-train) | Yes (continuous) | Yes (fine-tuning) |
| **Best IDPS use** | Known attack classification | Anomaly detection | Hybrid IDPS | Adaptive response | Raw byte/payload analysis |
| **Sample algorithm** | Random Forest, SVM | k-Means, Isolation Forest | Self-Training | Q-Learning, DQN | LSTM, Autoencoder, CNN |
| **Failure mode** | Class imbalance | High FPR | Confirmation bias | Slow convergence | Data hunger, overfitting |
| **Dataset example** | NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017 | UNSW-NB15 (unlabelled) | Mix of both | Custom env | CICIDS-2017 raw |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Naïve Bayes on a Toy IDPS Log

**Given the following training data** (3 features → 2 classes):

| Flow | src_bytes | dst_bytes | protocol | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 200 | TCP | Normal |
| 2 | 120 | 220 | TCP | Normal |
| 3 | 80 | 180 | TCP | Normal |
| 4 | 5000 | 100 | UDP | Attack |
| 5 | 7000 | 80 | UDP | Attack |
| 6 | 6000 | 120 | UDP | Attack |

**Test sample:** $\mathbf{x}_{new} = (\text{src\_bytes}=4500, \text{dst\_bytes}=110, \text{protocol}=UDP)$

**Step 1 — Class priors:**
$P(\text{Normal}) = 3/6 = 0.5$, $P(\text{Attack}) = 3/6 = 0.5$.

**Step 2 — Class-conditional means and variances for continuous features** (assuming Gaussian likelihood):

For Normal class:
- $\mu_{\text{src,N}} = (100+120+80)/3 = 100$
- $\mu_{\text{dst,N}} = (200+220+180)/3 = 200$
- $\sigma^2_{\text{src,N}} = ((100-100)^2+(120-100)^2+(80-100)^2)/3 = 400/3 \approx 133.33$
- $\sigma^2_{\text{dst,N}} = ((200-200)^2+(220-200)^2+(180-200)^2)/3 = 400/3 \approx 133.33$

For Attack class:
- $\mu_{\text{src,A}} = (5000+7000+6000)/3 = 6000$
- $\mu_{\text{dst,A}} = (100+80+120)/3 = 100$
- $\sigma^2_{\text{src,A}} = ((5000-6000)^2+(7000-6000)^2+(6000-6000)^2)/3 = 2000000/3 \approx 666666.67$
- $\sigma^2_{\text{dst,A}} = ((100-100)^2+(80-100)^2+(120-100)^2)/3 = 400/3 \approx 133.33$

**Step 3 — Categorical likelihood for protocol=UDP:**
- $P(UDP \vert \text{Normal}) = 0/3 = 0$  ← **Laplace smoothing** is mandatory here, or we get zero probability.

Apply **Laplace smoothing** with $k=1$ and $V=2$ (TCP, UDP):
- $P(UDP \vert \text{Normal}) = (0+1)/(3+2) = 0.2$
- $P(UDP \vert \text{Attack}) = (3+1)/(3+2) = 0.8$

**Step 4 — Gaussian likelihood for continuous features** (using PDF):

$$P(x \vert \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

For Normal class:
- $P(4500 \vert 100, 133.33) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 133.33}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(4500-100)^2}{2 \cdot 133.33} \right) \approx 0$ (essentially zero)
- $P(110 \vert 200, 133.33) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 133.33}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(110-200)^2}{2 \cdot 133.33} \right) \approx 0.0030$

For Attack class:
- $P(4500 \vert 6000, 666666.67) \approx 0.000595$
- $P(110 \vert 100, 133.33) \approx 0.0326$

**Step 5 — Compute unnormalized posterior for each class:**

For Normal:
$$P(\text{Normal}) \cdot P(UDP \vert \text{Normal}) \cdot P(4500 \vert \text{N}) \cdot P(110 \vert \text{N}) = 0.5 \times 0.2 \times \sim 0 \times 0.0030 \approx 0$$

For Attack:
$$P(\text{Attack}) \cdot P(UDP \vert \text{Attack}) \cdot P(4500 \vert \text{A}) \cdot P(110 \vert \text{A}) = 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 0.000595 \times 0.0326 \approx 7.76 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_y P(y \vert \mathbf{x}) = \text{Attack}$$

**Conclusion:** The test sample is correctly classified as an **Attack** — the high `src_bytes` and UDP protocol gave a decisive vote to the Attack class.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IDPS Learning Pipeline — End-to-End Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Traffic Capture<br>NIC / NetFlow / Syslog] --> B[Data Pre-processing<br>Cleaning, Normalization, Encoding]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering<br>Selection and Extraction]
    C --> D[Model Training<br>Supervised / Unsupervised / RL / DL]
    D --> E[Validation and Tuning<br>k-fold CV, ROC-AUC]
    E --> F[Deployment as IDPS Engine<br>Real-time Scoring]
    F --> G[Decision Output<br>Allow / Alert / Block]
    G --> H[Feedback Loop<br>New Labelled Samples]
    H --> D
```

**Diagram Notes:**
- The feedback arc from G → D represents **online/continual learning** — the hallmark of modern adaptive IDPS.
- Each block is independently hot-swappable (e.g., swap the model block for an LSTM without redesigning the pipeline).

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Learning Models in IDPS

```mermaid
graph TD
    LM[Learning Models for IDPS]
    LM --> SUP[Supervised Learning]
    LM --> UNS[Unsupervised Learning]
    LM --> SEM[Semi-Supervised Learning]
    LM --> RL[Reinforcement Learning]
    LM --> DL[Deep Learning]

    SUP --> SUP1[Decision Tree]
    SUP --> SUP2[Random Forest]
    SUP --> SUP3[SVM]
    SUP --> SUP4[k-NN]
    SUP --> SUP5[Naive Bayes]

    UNS --> UNS1[k-Means]
    UNS --> UNS2[DBSCAN]
    UNS --> UNS3[Isolation Forest]
    UNS --> UNS4[One-Class SVM]
    UNS --> UNS5[Autoencoder]

    SEM --> SEM1[Self-Training]
    SEM --> SEM2[Co-Training]
    SEM --> SEM3[Label Propagation]

    RL --> RL1[Q-Learning]
    RL --> RL2[Deep Q-Network DQN]
    RL --> RL3[Policy Gradient]

    DL --> DL1[MLP]
    DL --> DL2[CNN]
    DL --> DL3[RNN and LSTM]
    DL --> DL4[GAN]
    DL --> DL5[Transformer]
```

---

## 4.3 Supervised vs Unsupervised — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is labelled<br>attack data available<br>in large quantity?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the attack landscape<br>static and known?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P1[Use Supervised Classifier<br>Random Forest or SVM]
    Q2 -- No --> P2[Use Semi-Supervised<br>Self-Training]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Need to detect<br>zero-day attacks?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Use Unsupervised<br>Isolation Forest or Autoencoder]
    Q3 -- No --> P4[Use Rule-Based<br>Signature IDPS]
```

---

## 4.4 Reinforcement Learning Agent — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Network Environment<br>Live Traffic] -->|state s_t| AG[IDPS Agent]
    AG -->|action a_t| ACT[Action:<br>Allow / Block / Alert / Throttle]
    ACT --> ENV
    ENV -->|reward r_t| AG
    AG -->|learns| Q[Q-Table or DQN]
```

**Conversion Logic (for the diagram):**
- The **state** $s_t$ = a feature vector summarizing the last $T$ seconds of traffic (e.g., packet rate, byte rate, unique ports).
- The **action** $a_t \in \mathcal{A} = \{\text{allow, alert, block, throttle}\}$.
- The **reward** $r_t = +10$ for true-positive block, $-5$ for false positive, $-20$ for missed attack, $+1$ for true negative.
- The agent's goal: maximize $\mathbb{E}[G_t]$ where $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$.

---

## 4.5 Anomaly Score Generation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Flow Features x] --> M[Trained Model]
    M --> S1[Raw Score s]
    S1 --> T[Threshold tau]
    T -->|s > tau| AN[Flag as Anomaly]
    T -->|s <= tau| NR[Mark as Normal]
    AN --> LOG[Alert Log and Block]
    NR --> PASS[Allow Traffic]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 4, KTU Pattern)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*List and briefly explain any three unsupervised learning models used in Intrusion Detection Systems.*

**Model Answer (3 points total — ~1 mark per model):**

1. **k-Means Clustering** — Partitions traffic into $k$ clusters by minimizing WCSS. Points far from all centroids are flagged as anomalies. *(1 mark)*

2. **Isolation Forest** — Builds an ensemble of random isolation trees. Anomalies are isolated with shorter average path lengths, yielding a score $s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-E(h(\mathbf{x}))/c(n)}$. *(1 mark)*

3. **Autoencoder** — A neural network trained to reconstruct normal traffic. The reconstruction error $E = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \psi(\phi(\mathbf{x})) \Vert^2$ is the anomaly score; high error ⇒ attack. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (Module 4, KTU Pattern)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*What is Q-learning? Write the Q-learning update equation.*

**Model Answer:**

Q-learning is a **model-free reinforcement learning algorithm** that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ by interacting with the environment. The agent maintains a Q-table and updates it after each transition $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ using the rule:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

where $\alpha$ is the learning rate, $\gamma$ is the discount factor, and the bracketed term is the **temporal-difference (TD) error**. *(3 marks: definition 1 + equation 1 + parameter explanation 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Information Gain** criterion used in the ID3 Decision Tree algorithm. Using a suitable IDPS dataset fragment, demonstrate a single split and compute the gain. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **supervised** and **unsupervised** learning models in IDPS, highlighting their suitability for detecting known vs zero-day attacks. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define Entropy (2 marks):**
Entropy of a dataset $S$ with class proportions $p_c$ is:
$$H(S) = - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$$
- $H=0$: pure node
- $H=\log_2 C$: maximum impurity

**Step 2 — Define Information Gain (2 marks):**
For an attribute $A$ partitioning $S$ into subsets $S_v$:
$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example on IDPS data (3 marks):**
Suppose we have 10 flows: 6 Normal, 4 Attack. Splitting on `protocol=TCP` gives:
- $S_{TCP}$: 6 Normal, 0 Attack (pure)
- $S_{UDP}$: 0 Normal, 4 Attack (pure)

$$H(S) = -0.6 \log_2 0.6 - 0.4 \log_2 0.4 \approx 0.971 \text{ bits}$$

Since both children are pure, $H(S_{TCP}) = H(S_{UDP}) = 0$, so:
$$IG(S, \text{protocol}) = 0.971 - 0 = 0.971 \text{ bits — perfect split.}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Supervised Learning (3 marks):**
- Requires labelled data (Normal vs Attack classes).
- Examples: Random Forest, SVM, k-NN, Naïve Bayes.
- Strength: high accuracy on **known attacks**; produces low false-positive rates.
- Weakness: cannot detect **zero-day** attacks; needs periodic retraining.

**Step 2 — Unsupervised Learning (3 marks):**
- Operates on unlabelled data; builds a model of "normality" or clusters.
- Examples: k-Means, DBSCAN, Isolation Forest, Autoencoder.
- Strength: detects **zero-day** and novel attacks.
- Weakness: higher false-positive rate; requires careful threshold tuning.

**Step 1 — Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Data | Labelled | Unlabelled |
| Zero-day detection | Poor | Strong |
| Accuracy (known attacks) | High | Moderate |
| Engineering complexity | Medium | High |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based IDPS**. Define state, action, reward and write the Q-learning update rule. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Isolation Forest** algorithm for anomaly-based intrusion detection. Derive its anomaly score equation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Architecture Diagram (2 marks):** (See Section 4.4 of these notes for the Mermaid diagram — replicate as a labelled block diagram with: Environment → Agent → Action → Reward loop.)

**Step 2 — MDP Definitions (2 marks):**
- **State $s_t$:** a feature vector summarizing the last $T$ seconds of traffic (e.g., mean packet size, packet rate, unique destination ports).
- **Action $a_t \in \mathcal{A} = \{\text{allow, alert, block, throttle}\}$:** the response the IDPS takes.
- **Reward $r_t$:** a scalar feedback signal, e.g., $+10$ for correct block, $-5$ for false positive, $-20$ for missed attack, $+1$ for true negative.
- **Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$:** the agent's strategy mapping states to actions.

**Step 3 — Q-Learning Update (2 marks):**
$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$
- $\alpha$ = learning rate, $\gamma$ = discount factor, bracketed term = TD-error.
- Under Robbins-Monro conditions, $Q$ converges to $Q^*$.

**Step 4 — One real-world example (1 mark):** RL-based IDPS in **Software-Defined Networks (SDN)** where the controller learns optimal flow-blocking rules.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Algorithm Intuition (2 marks):**
Anomalies are *few and different*; they are isolated by random partitions using fewer splits than normal points. Build an ensemble of $t$ random isolation trees (iTrees); average the path length.

**Step 2 — iTree Construction (2 marks):**
- Randomly select a feature $q$.
- Randomly select a split value $p \in [q_{\min}, q_{\max}]$.
- Recurse on the child containing the sample until leaf is reached or height limit hit.

**Step 3 — Anomaly Score Derivation (3 marks):**
- Average path length: $E(h(\mathbf{x})) = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{b=1}^{t} h_b(\mathbf{x})$
- Normalization constant (BST): $c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$
- Final score: $s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-E(h(\mathbf{x}))/c(n)}$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:** $s \to 1$ ⇒ anomaly; $s < 0.5$ ⇒ normal. Threshold typically $0.5$–$0.7$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 4):**
> 1. **Conflating "anomaly" with "attack"** — Not every anomaly is malicious (e.g., a legitimate software update may look anomalous). Examiners expect you to mention this caveat for full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Laplace smoothing in Naïve Bayes** — When a feature value never appears with a class in training, $P = 0$ kills the entire posterior. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if smoothing is not mentioned.
> 3. **Mis-stating the Q-learning equation** — The update uses $\max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a)$ (off-policy), not the action actually taken. Many students write $Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})$ — that's SARSA, not Q-learning. Examiner will deduct 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the MDP tuple** — When explaining RL in IDPS, you must define all of $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$. Vague descriptions lose up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Missing the boundary conditions** — When writing formulas like entropy or anomaly score, state the range (e.g., $0 \leq H \leq \log_2 C$). This is worth at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting the role of labelled data scarcity** — IDPS data is mostly unlabelled. Mentioning this justifies semi-supervised approaches and earns extra credit.
> 7. **No mention of concept drift** — Modern attacks evolve. Examiners expect a mention of online/continual learning to handle drift.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and keep):**

- **Five Learning Paradigms:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-Supervised, Reinforcement, Deep Learning. **Know one algorithm per paradigm by heart.**
- **Information Gain Equation:** $IG = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$. Boundary: $IG \geq 0$.
- **Naïve Bayes Decision Rule:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y P(y) \prod_j P(x_j \vert y)$. Always use **Laplace smoothing**.
- **Minkowski Distance:** $d_p = \left( \sum_l \vert x_{i,l} - x_{j,l} \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$; $p=1,2,\infty$ for Manhattan, Euclidean, Chebyshev.
- **SVM Objective:** $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ with $C > 0$ as the trade-off parameter.
- **k-Means WCSS:** $J = \sum_i \sum_k r_{ik} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ with $r_{ik} \in \{0,1\}$.
- **Isolation Forest Score:** $s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-E(h(\mathbf{x}))/c(n)}$; $s \to 1$ = anomaly.
- **Q-Learning Update:** $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]$ — **off-policy** with TD-error.
- **MDP Tuple:** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ — must define all five.
- **Autoencoder Anomaly Score:** $E = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \psi(\phi(\mathbf{x})) \Vert^2$.
- **Self-Attention:** $\text{softmax}(Q K^\top / \sqrt{d_k}) V$ — used in Transformer-based IDPS.
- **Datasets to mention:** NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15, TON_IoT.
- **Real-world systems:** Darktrace, Vectra AI, Splunk UBA — all use learning models.
- **Concept drift** is a critical challenge — mention online/continual learning.
- **Boundary conditions** to always state: $\alpha, \gamma \in [0,1]$; entropy range $0 \leq H \leq \log_2 C$; anomaly score $s \in [0, 1]$.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Anomaly ≠ Attack. Always qualify your statements.

---

*End of Module 4 — Learning Models Notes. Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification-Based Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Specification-Based Detection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Specification-Based Detection (SBD)** is a hybrid intrusion detection paradigm that monitors program or protocol execution against a formally defined set of *legitimate behavioral specifications* (rules, constraints, and state-transition models) and flags any deviation as a potential intrusion. Unlike signature-based detection (which matches known attack patterns) or statistical anomaly detection (which builds probabilistic profiles of "normal"), SBD anchors its decisions in a **manually engineered, deterministic specification of correct behavior**, thereby combining the **low false-positive rate of misuse detection** with the **ability of anomaly detection to catch novel (zero-day) attacks**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** > "Specification-based detection refers to a technique where intrusion detection is performed by monitoring the system against a set of defined specifications (rules) describing the legitimate behavior of the system entities (programs, protocols, etc.). Any violation of these specifications is reported as a possible intrusion." > [!NOTE] > **Where SBD Fits in the IDS Taxonomy:** > - **Misuse Detection (Signature-based):** Looks for *known bad*. > - **Anomaly Detection (Statistical/ML-based):** Looks for *statistical deviation from learned normal*. > - **Specification-Based Detection:** Looks for *deviation from formally specified correct behavior* — the middle ground. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Strict Airport Security Checklist" Imagine an international airport security checkpoint. The authorities do **not** wait for a known terrorist to appear (that is signature-based) nor do they build a vague statistical profile of a "normal" passenger (that is anomaly-based, prone to false positives). Instead, they publish an **explicit rulebook of legitimate conduct**: - A passenger may carry only **one** cabin bag. - Liquids must be in containers of **≤ 100 ml**. - The boarding pass name must **exactly match** the passport name. - One passenger may not occupy two seats. If anyone violates *any* of these explicitly written rules, the alarm is raised — even if the violator is someone the system has never encountered before, and even if the violator's behavior does not look "statistically unusual." The rulebook is the **specification**, and the act of matching every passenger against the rulebook is **specification-based detection**. In the same way, in a network: - The *specification* could be: "An HTTP request from a client must be preceded by a successful TCP three-way handshake." - If a packet shows an HTTP GET without the prior SYN-SYN/ACK-ACK, the IDS flags it — even if the attacker is brand new. ## 1.3 Key Metrics in SBD (Bold-Defined Standards) - **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Proportion of legitimate activities incorrectly flagged. SBD typically achieves **FPR ≈ 0.1% – 2%** in well-engineered deployments. - **True Positive Rate (TPR) / Detection Rate:** Proportion of actual intrusions correctly detected. Well-designed SBD achieves **TPR ≈ 85% – 98%**. - **Specification Coverage (C):** Fraction of program/protocol states covered by specifications, ideally **C → 1.0 (100%)**. - **Mean Time to Detection (MTTD):** Time between violation and alert; for SBD this is typically **near real-time (milliseconds to seconds)** because the check is deterministic. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of SBD (Step-by-Step Bullets) SBD operates through a **four-stage pipeline**. Understanding each stage is essential for both exam questions and lab work. 1. **Specification Authoring (Manual Engineering Stage)** - A security engineer (or formal methods specialist) writes a *formal specification* describing all *legitimate* behaviors of the monitored entity. - The specification language is typically **declarative and rule-based**, e.g., propositional logic, finite-state machines (FSM), temporal logic (LTL/CTL), or domain-specific languages (DSLs) like **Ragel, Murphi, or custom XML schemas**. - The output is a *specification document* that must be *complete* and *consistent*. 2. **Trace / Event Capture (Observation Stage)** - The monitored entity's runtime behavior is captured as a stream of *events* or *system calls* — for example: `open(file)`, `read(fd, buf, n)`, `write(fd, buf, n)`, `execve(path, argv)`, `connect(sock, addr, port)`. - On the network side, packet headers, TCP flags, and protocol fields are captured. 3. **Conformance Checking / Matching Engine (Comparison Stage)** - Each observed event (or sequence of events) is checked against the specification using a *conformance checker* or *reference monitor*. - Formally: a behavior trace $\tau = e_1, e_2, e_3, \dots, e_n$ is accepted iff $\tau \models \Phi$, where $\Phi$ is the specification formula. 4. **Violation Reporting (Alerting Stage)** - If $\tau \not\models \Phi$ (the trace violates the specification), an alert is generated with metadata: timestamp, violated rule ID, evidence, severity, and recommended response. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why manual specification?** Because it is impossible to enumerate every possible attack, but it *is* feasible to describe what is *allowed*. The philosophy is: *"describe the safe set, not the attack set."* - **Why FSMs / temporal logic?** Programs and protocols have inherent *state* (e.g., TCP has CLOSED, LISTEN, SYN-SENT, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, etc.). A finite-state representation naturally captures this. - **Why deterministic matching?** It eliminates the probabilistic noise that causes high false-positive rates in statistical anomaly detection. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Metrics Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $FPR$ | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | **0.1% – 2%** for SBD | | $TPR$ | True Positive Rate | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | **85% – 98%** for SBD | | $FNR$ | False Negative Rate | $FNR = 1 - TPR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | Should be minimized | | $Accuracy$ | Overall Correctness | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Used for overall eval | | $Precision$ | Positive Predictive Value | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | High in SBD | | $C$ | Specification Coverage | $C = \dfrac{\text{Specified States}}{\text{Total Program States}}$ | Should approach **1.0** | | $\Phi$ | Specification Formula | Boolean / temporal-logic predicate | Output ∈ {Accept, Reject} | | $\tau$ | Observed Trace | Sequence $\tau = e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n$ | $n$ = number of events | | $MTTD$ | Mean Time to Detection | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{alert,i} - t_{violation,i})$ | **< 1 s** typical | | $S_{spec}$ | Specification Set Size | Number of rules in $\Phi$ | Dozens to thousands | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > SBD is heavily used in *SCADA/ICS security* (modbus, DNP3 protocol monitoring), *VoIP security* (SIP state machine validation), *web application firewalls* (RFC compliance checks), and *database activity monitoring* (legitimate SQL pattern enforcement). Industries with strict compliance regimes (power grids, avionics, healthcare) prefer SBD because the detection logic is **auditable and explainable**, unlike black-box ML models. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Specification-Conformance Predicate The fundamental decision rule of SBD can be expressed as a Boolean predicate over an observed event trace. Let us derive it rigorously. ### 3.1.1 Notation Setup Let: - $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_m\}$ be the set of all legitimate **states** of the monitored entity (e.g., TCP connection states). - $\Sigma = \{\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \dots, \sigma_k\}$ be the set of all possible **events / actions** (e.g., `SYN`, `ACK`, `FIN`, `RST`, `DATA`). - $\Phi : \Sigma^* \times S \to \{\text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE}\}$ be the **conformance predicate** that takes a trace and a current state, and returns whether the trace conforms to the specification. - $\tau = e_1 \, \bigcirc \, e_2 \, \bigcirc \, \dots \, \bigcirc \, e_n$ be the observed trace, where $\bigcirc$ denotes the sequence operator and $e_i \in \Sigma$. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation We define the conformance of a trace inductively on its length $n$. **Base Case ($n = 0$, the empty trace $\epsilon$):** $$ \Phi(\epsilon, s_0) = \text{TRUE} \quad \forall s_0 \in S_{\text{initial}} $$ *Logic:* The empty trace trivially conforms to any specification because no violation has occurred yet. The system starts in one of the allowed initial states $s_0 \in S_{\text{initial}}$. **Inductive Step (assume $\Phi(\tau_{1..i}, s_i) = \text{TRUE}$, evaluate $\Phi(\tau_{1..(i+1)}, s_{i+1})$):** $$ \Phi(\tau_{1..(i+1)}, s_{i+1}) \;=\; \Phi(\tau_{1..i}, s_i) \;\land\; \text{Allowed}(e_{i+1}, s_i) $$ Where: $$ \text{Allowed}(e_{i+1}, s_i) \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{TRUE}, & \text{if } \delta(s_i, e_{i+1}) \in S \\[4pt] \text{FALSE}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ *Logic:* A trace of length $i+1$ is conformant **iff** (1) its prefix of length $i$ was conformant **AND** (2) the $(i+1)^{\text{th}}$ event is allowed by the specification's transition function $\delta : S \times \Sigma \to S$ in the current state $s_i$. **Final Conformance Condition (after observing the full trace $\tau$):** $$ \text{ALERT} \;=\; \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \exists \, i \in [1, n] \;\text{s.t.}\; \text{Allowed}(e_i, s_{i-1}) = \text{FALSE} \\[4pt] \text{FALSE} & \text{if } \forall \, i \in [1, n], \; \text{Allowed}(e_i, s_{i-1}) = \text{TRUE} \end{cases} $$ *Logic:* An alert is raised if and only if there exists at least one event in the trace that is not allowed by the specification in the system's current state. Otherwise, the trace is fully compliant. ### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example — TCP Three-Way Handshake **Specification (simplified):** A connection in state $s = \text{CLOSED}$ may only transition to $\text{SYN-SENT}$ upon receiving a `SYN` event from the client. Let the allowed transitions be encoded as the set: $$ T = \{\,(\text{CLOSED}, \text{SYN}) \to \text{SYN-SENT},\; (\text{SYN-SENT}, \text{SYN-ACK}) \to \text{ESTABLISHED},\; \dots \,\} $$ **Observed Trace (Normal):** $$ \tau_1 = \text{SYN} \;\to\; \text{SYN-ACK} \;\to\; \text{ACK} $$ **Evaluation:** $$ \Phi(\tau_1, \text{CLOSED}) = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} = \text{TRUE} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{No Alert} $$ **Observed Trace (Attack — Out-of-Order SYN-ACK):** $$ \tau_2 = \text{SYN-ACK} \;\to\; \text{SYN} \;\to\; \text{ACK} $$ **Evaluation:** $$ \Phi(\tau_2, \text{CLOSED}) = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{Allowed}(\text{SYN-ACK}, \text{CLOSED}) = \text{FALSE} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{ALERT} $$ *Conclusion:* The SYN-ACK packet arriving in the CLOSED state is impossible per the TCP state machine, so SBD raises an alert — this is a classic **SYN-ACK scan** (a reconnaissance attack). SBD catches it deterministically without any signature database lookup. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SBD vs. Other Paradigms | Property | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based (Statistical) | **Specification-Based** | |---|---|---|---| | Knowledge base | Known attack patterns | Learned "normal" profile | **Manually defined correct behavior** | | Detection of zero-day attacks | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good (but noisy) | ✅ **Good (if it violates a spec)** | | False Positive Rate | **Very Low** | **High** (10–30%) | **Low** (0.1–2%) | | False Negative Rate | High (misses novel) | Moderate | **Low–Moderate** | | Maintenance overhead | Update signature DB | Retrain models | **Update specifications** | | Human expertise required | Medium | High (ML) | **Very High (formal methods)** | | Auditability / Explainability | **High** | Low (black box) | **Very High (logic-based)** | | Suitability for protocol/ICS | Limited | Limited | **Excellent** | | Computational cost | Low | Medium–High | **Low–Medium** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of a Specification-Based IDS (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a Specification-Based Intrusion Detection System, mapping the data flow from the monitored entity to the alert sink. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Monitored Entity
Program or Protocol] -->|Emits Events| B[Event Capture Layer
System Calls / Packets] B -->|Normalized Event Stream| C[Preprocessor
Format and Timestamping] C -->|Event e sub i| D{Conformance Checker
Reference Monitor} E[(Specification Repository
FSM and Rules Phi)] -->|Loaded Rules| D D -->|Trace Conforms| F[State Update
Next State s sub i plus 1] D -->|Trace Violates| G[Alert Generator] F -->|Continues Monitoring| C G -->|Alert with Metadata| H[(Alert Sink
SIEM or Console)] I[Security Engineer] -->|Authors and Updates| E H -->|Feedback| I ``` ### 4.2 Worked Schematic — TCP State Machine for SBD The following Mermaid state diagram models the *legitimate* TCP connection lifecycle. Any packet whose observed state-transition is not represented here triggers a violation alert. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> CLOSED CLOSED --> LISTEN : Passive Open CLOSED --> SYNSENT : Active Open and SYN LISTEN --> SYNRCVD : Receive SYN and Send SYN ACK SYNSENT --> SYNRCVD : Receive SYN and Send SYN ACK SYNSENT --> ESTABLISHED : Receive SYN ACK and Send ACK SYNRCVD --> ESTABLISHED : Receive ACK ESTABLISHED --> FINWAIT1 : Close and Send FIN ESTABLISHED --> CLOSEWAIT : Receive FIN and Send ACK FINWAIT1 --> FINWAIT2 : Receive ACK FINWAIT2 --> TIMEWAIT : Receive FIN and Send ACK CLOSEWAIT --> LASTACK : Close and Send FIN LASTACK --> CLOSED : Receive ACK TIMEWAIT --> CLOSED : Timeout 2 MSL ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram for KTU Exam Purposes:** > - Every arrow represents a **legitimate transition** that the SBD specification allows. > - Any observed packet that demands a transition *not* present in this diagram (e.g., `SYN-ACK` from `CLOSED`, or `DATA` from `LISTEN`) triggers a **violation alert**. > - Note that `SYNRCVD → CLOSED` is *not* a direct legal transition; this catches **SYN flood / half-open attack patterns** where the handshake is abandoned. ## 4.3 Process Flow of Specification-Based Detection ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1 Author Specifications] --> P2[Step 2 Deploy Reference Monitor] P2 --> P3[Step 3 Capture Runtime Events] P3 --> P4[Step 4 Check Conformance against Phi] P4 --> P5{Conforms} P5 -->|Yes| P6[Update State and Continue] P6 --> P3 P5 -->|No| P7[Raise Alert with Evidence] P7 --> P8[Log to SIEM] P8 --> P9[Operator Review and Triage] P9 --> P1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Specification-Based Detection. How does it differ from signature-based and anomaly-based detection?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Specification-Based Detection (SBD) is an intrusion detection technique in which a set of *formally defined specifications* (rules, finite-state machines, or logical constraints) describing the **legitimate behavior** of a system, program, or protocol is maintained. The IDS monitors runtime behavior and raises an alert whenever the observed behavior **violates** any specification. **Differentiation (1 mark):** - **Signature-based:** Matches observed behavior against a database of *known attack patterns*. Cannot detect zero-day attacks. - **Anomaly-based:** Builds a statistical/ML model of *normal* behavior; flags statistical deviations. Prone to high false positives. - **Specification-based:** Encodes the *correct* behavior; flags any deviation. Combines low false-positive rate of misuse detection with the zero-day detection capability of anomaly detection. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *List any three advantages and two limitations of Specification-Based Detection.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages (2 marks — any three):** 1. **Low false positive rate** because specifications describe legitimate behavior precisely. 2. **Detects novel (zero-day) attacks** as long as they violate a defined specification. 3. **Highly auditable and explainable** — alerts can be traced back to a specific violated rule. 4. Independent of attack knowledge base; no signature database needed. 5. Well-suited for **protocols and ICS/SCADA** with well-defined state machines. **Limitations (1 mark — any two):** 1. **Labor-intensive and expert-driven** — specifications must be manually authored by domain experts. 2. **Specification coverage problem** — incomplete specifications lead to false negatives. 3. Difficult to apply to systems with highly dynamic or unstructured behavior (e.g., general-purpose user activity). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: each Module carries 14-mark questions with internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two independent alternatives of equal standard. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture and working of a Specification-Based Intrusion Detection System**. Use the TCP protocol as an example to illustrate how state-machine specifications can detect protocol-based attacks. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Compare Specification-Based Detection with Signature-Based and Anomaly-Based Detection. Discuss in which real-world scenarios SBD is the most suitable choice. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Solution to (a) — 7 marks:** The Specification-Based IDS consists of **four functional components** working in a pipeline: **1. Specification Repository (1 mark):** A formally encoded set of rules describing the *legitimate* behavior of the monitored entity. For TCP, the specification is the **TCP state machine** containing 11 states (LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, CLOSING, LAST-ACK, TIME-WAIT, CLOSED) and the legal transitions between them, as defined in **RFC 793**. **2. Reference Monitor / Conformance Checker (2 marks):** This component intercepts every TCP packet, determines the *current state* of the connection, and checks whether the incoming packet's event (e.g., `SYN`, `ACK`, `FIN`, `RST`, `DATA`) is a legal transition from that state. Formally, given the current state $s_i$ and the observed event $e_{i+1}$, it evaluates: $$ \text{Violation} \;=\; \neg \text{Allowed}(e_{i+1}, s_i) $$ If the result is TRUE, an alert is generated. **3. State Updater (1 mark):** On a legal event, the reference monitor transitions the connection to the new state $s_{i+1} = \delta(s_i, e_{i+1})$ and continues monitoring. **4. Alert Subsystem (1 mark):** Generates a structured alert with: timestamp, source/destination IP, violated rule ID, evidence (the offending packet), and severity. **TCP Example (2 marks):** - **Normal scenario:** `CLOSED → LISTEN` (server passive open), then `LISTEN → SYN-RCVD` (receipt of client SYN), then `SYN-RCVD → ESTABLISHED` (receipt of client ACK). All transitions are in the specification; no alert. - **Attack scenario:** A packet with the `RST` flag arrives in state `ESTABLISHED` from an untrusted source. The SBD, having whitelisted only certain transition sources, flags it. Another example: a **TCP NULL scan** (all flags set to 0) demands a transition that does not exist in the RFC 793 state machine — SBD raises an alert immediately, with no signature lookup required. --- **Solution to (b) — 7 marks:** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Criterion | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | **Specification-Based** | |---|---|---|---| | Detection principle | Match against known attack patterns | Detect statistical deviation from normal | Detect violation of correct-behavior specs | | Zero-day detection | No | Yes (with noise) | **Yes (deterministic)** | | False positive rate | Very low | High | **Low** | | Knowledge required | Attack signatures | Training data of normal behavior | **Formal specs of correct behavior** | | Maintenance | Frequent signature updates | Periodic model retraining | Spec updates on protocol/program change | | Auditability | High | Low (black-box) | **Very high** | | Domain suitability | General IT | Variable | **Protocols, ICS, SCADA, web** | **Real-World Scenarios (3 marks):** SBD is the **most suitable choice** in: 1. **Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA):** Protocols like Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104 have well-defined state machines. Attacks like unauthorized commands can be detected via specification violations. *Examples: Ko et al.'s specification-based IDS for SCADA.* 2. **VoIP / SIP Security:** SIP has a deterministic request-response state machine; SBD can detect SIP-based attacks like BYE-tearing, INVITE flooding, and registration hijacking. 3. **Web Application Firewalls (WAF):** Specifying RFC-compliant HTTP request formats catches protocol-level attacks (HTTP request smuggling, header injection) with high precision. 4. **Database Activity Monitoring:** Legitimate SQL patterns are specified; any deviation (e.g., a non-application user executing `DROP TABLE`) is flagged. 5. **Regulated industries** (power, healthcare, finance) where decisions must be **explainable and auditable** for compliance. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam 2023, Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **operational pipeline of Specification-Based Detection**. Explain the role of the *reference monitor* and the *conformance checker* in detail. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the **limitations and challenges** of Specification-Based Detection. How does the *specification coverage* problem affect detection accuracy? Propose two strategies to mitigate this problem. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Solution to (a) — 7 marks:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The pipeline consists of: (1) **Specification Repository** (manually authored rules/FSM), (2) **Event Capture Layer** (system call hook / packet sniffer), (3) **Preprocessor** (event normalization), (4) **Conformance Checker / Reference Monitor** (core decision engine), (5) **State Updater** (transition state on legal events), (6) **Alert Generator** (raises alerts on violations), and (7) **Alert Sink** (SIEM/console for operator review). **Reference Monitor (2 marks):** A *reference monitor* is a tamper-proof, always-invoked security module that mediates every event between the monitored entity and the system. It must satisfy three classical properties: *complete mediation* (every event is checked), *tamper-proofness* (cannot be modified by attackers), and *verifiability* (its correctness can be formally proven). In SBD, the reference monitor hosts the **conformance-checking logic** and the **state machine**. **Conformance Checker (2 marks):** The conformance checker is the algorithmic heart of the reference monitor. It receives each normalized event $e_i$, looks up the current state $s_{i-1}$ in the state table, queries the specification $\Phi$ to determine whether the transition $\delta(s_{i-1}, e_i)$ is allowed, and outputs a Boolean decision. On `TRUE`, the state is updated to $s_i = \delta(s_{i-1}, e_i)$ and monitoring continues. On `FALSE`, an alert is dispatched to the alert generator with full evidentiary context (the offending event, the violated rule ID, and the current state). --- **Solution to (b) — 7 marks:** **Limitations and Challenges (4 marks):** 1. **Specification Coverage Problem (C < 1.0):** Real systems have vast state spaces; it is practically impossible to enumerate every legal behavior. Unspecified states or transitions become blind spots, leading to false negatives. 2. **Manual Effort and Expertise:** Authoring accurate specifications requires deep domain knowledge (e.g., RFC documents, protocol internals, program semantics) and formal methods training. This makes SBD *expensive* to deploy. 3. **Specification Drift:** As software/protocols evolve (new versions, patches), specifications must be kept in sync. Outdated specifications produce both false positives (overly restrictive rules) and false negatives (missed new behaviors). 4. **Difficulty with Unstructured Behavior:** SBD works best when the monitored behavior is *stateful and structured* (protocols, system calls). It performs poorly on human-driven, ad-hoc, or content-based behaviors (e.g., free-form email content). 5. **Specification Ambiguity / Conflict:** Multiple rules may overlap or contradict, leading to inconsistent decisions. Formal verification is needed to guarantee *consistency* and *completeness*. **Effect of Coverage on Detection Accuracy (1.5 marks):** If a specification covers a fraction $C$ of the true program state space, then an attacker can exploit the *uncovered* fraction $(1 - C)$ without triggering any alert. The detection rate is upper-bounded as: $$ TPR \;\leq\; C \cdot P(\text{attack violates a specified state}) + (1 - C) \cdot 0 $$ Thus, even if every specified state is correctly enforced, $TPR$ degrades linearly with uncovered states. For example, $C = 0.8$ caps $TPR$ at most around 80% for attacks touching uncovered states. **Mitigation Strategies (1.5 marks):** 1. **Hybrid SBD + Anomaly Detection:** Combine the deterministic SBD checks with a lightweight anomaly detector over the *uncovered* state subspace. SBD provides precision on the covered part; anomaly detection provides recall on the uncovered part. 2. **Automated Specification Mining:** Use *specification inference* or *protocol reverse-engineering* tools (e.g., Angluin's $L^*$ algorithm for FSM learning, or tools like `Prospex`, `Infer`, `LearnLib`) to *automatically* derive candidate specifications from traces of legitimate behavior, reducing manual effort and improving coverage. 3. **Iterative Specification Refinement with Red-Teaming:** Continuously run penetration tests; each missed attack reveals a specification gap. The spec is iteratively updated, increasing $C$ over time. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do not** describe SBD as a "hybrid of signature and anomaly" using loose language. KTU expects the precise phrasing: *"SBD detects deviation from formally specified correct behavior."* Marks are deducted for vague answers. > 2. **Do not** confuse the *reference monitor* with the *conformance checker*. The reference monitor is the architectural component (a security kernel), and the conformance checker is the algorithmic function executed *inside* the reference monitor. > 3. **In comparison questions**, you must cover at least three dimensions (zero-day detection, FPR, auditability). Writing only "SBD is better" without a metric is treated as incomplete and attracts partial credit. > 4. **In TCP examples**, do not just say "SBD detects SYN flood." You must show the *state transition* that is violated (e.g., "SYN received in `ESTABLISHED` state is illegal per RFC 793"). The KTU rubric awards marks for the *state transition argument*, not just the attack name. > 5. **Always quantify** your claims: state FPR as 0.1–2%, TPR as 85–98%, etc. Vague qualitative answers lose 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition to memorize verbatim:** *SBD monitors system behavior against formally defined specifications of correct behavior and flags any violation.* - **Three core properties of a reference monitor:** *Complete mediation, tamper-proofness, verifiability.* - **SBD sits between misuse and anomaly detection** — it inherits low FPR from misuse detection and zero-day capability from anomaly detection. - **Key formula:** Violation = $\neg \text{Allowed}(e_{i+1}, s_i)$; Alert = $\exists i$ such that Violation is TRUE. - **TCP state machine has 11 states** per RFC 793; any out-of-spec transition = alert. - **Best-suited domains:** ICS/SCADA, VoIP/SIP, WAF, database activity monitoring, regulated industries. - **Biggest limitation:** *Specification coverage problem* (C < 1.0 leads to false negatives). - **Mitigation strategies:** Hybrid SBD + anomaly detection; automated spec mining using FSM-learning algorithms (e.g., $L^*$); iterative red-team refinement. - **Authors / canonical works to cite in answers:** *Ko, Ruschitzka, Levitt (1997)* — "Execution Monitoring of Security-Critical Programs in Distributed Systems: A Specification-Based Approach"; *Sekar et al.* — specification-based program monitoring. - **Distinguish clearly:** Specification ≠ Signature. A *specification* describes the legal behavior set; a *signature* describes an illegal pattern. - **Computational cost:** SBD is lightweight at runtime (state-table lookups), but expensive at design time (manual spec authoring). - **Auditability advantage:** SBD alerts are explainable by referencing a specific violated rule, making them regulator-friendly. - **Exam mantra:** Always end SBD answers with a *concrete protocol example* (TCP, SIP, HTTP) to score full marks.

topics.title: Hybrid Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hybrid Detection # 📘 Module 4 — Hybrid Detection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Hybrid Detection** is an advanced Intrusion Detection and Prevention (IDP) methodology that **integrates two or more distinct detection techniques — typically *Signature-Based Detection (SBD)*, *Anomaly-Based Detection (ABD)*, and *Stateful Protocol Analysis (SPA)* — within a unified architectural framework** to identify malicious activity with higher accuracy, lower false-alarm rates, and broader threat coverage than any single technique operating in isolation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT742 – Module 4):** > *Hybrid detection refers to the strategic fusion of misuse (signature) detection, anomaly detection, and stateful protocol inspection. The objective is to overcome the inherent limitations of stand-alone detectors by exploiting the complementary strengths of multiple analytical paradigms.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport Security Analogy" Imagine a modern international airport. Security is **not** enforced by a single mechanism: - **X-Ray Scanners (Signature-Based)** — Every bag is matched against a database of known dangerous item signatures (guns, explosives, sharp objects). Fast, but blind to *new* threats. - **Behavioural Profiling Officers (Anomaly-Based)** — Officers observe passenger behaviour. Someone sweating excessively, avoiding eye contact, or looping back to a checkpoint is flagged. Slow, but catches *unknown* threats. - **Boarding Pass & Passport Verifiers (Stateful Protocol Analysis)** — They check whether the *sequence* of interactions (check-in → security → gate → boarding) is logically valid. A passenger skipping check-in is blocked. Individually, each layer has gaps. **Combined**, they form a hybrid defence-in-depth system. This is exactly how **Hybrid Intrusion Detection** works in a network or host environment. ### 1.3 Why Hybrid? — The "Detection Trilemma" A single detection paradigm can typically optimise **at most two** of the following three properties: | Property | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based | Stateful Protocol | |----------|----------------|----------------|-------------------| | **Known-Attack Detection Rate** | ✅ Very High | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | | **Zero-Day Detection Capability** | ❌ Negligible | ✅ High | ⚠️ Limited | | **Low False Positive Rate** | ✅ Very Low | ❌ High | ✅ Low | > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** A hybrid system trades computational complexity for *balanced* performance across all three axes. The trade-off is explicitly managed by the **fusion logic** (rule-based, voting, Bayesian, or learning-based). ### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Must Memorise) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following four metrics appear in nearly every KTU question on detection systems. Memorise their formulas and KTU examiner expectations: > > - **True Positive (TP):** Malicious activity correctly flagged as malicious. > - **True Negative (TN):** Benign activity correctly passed as benign. > - **False Positive (FP):** Benign activity incorrectly flagged as malicious (Type-I Error). > - **False Negative (FN):** Malicious activity incorrectly passed as benign (Type-II Error). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Hybrid Detection System A typical hybrid IDPS follows a **three-tier logical pipeline**: ### Tier 1 — Data Acquisition & Pre-processing - Captures packets, system calls, log entries, or flow records. - Normalises heterogeneous data into a common feature vector $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$. - Performs protocol decoding, payload reassembly, and timestamp alignment. ### Tier 2 — Parallel Detection Engines Multiple, **independent** detection modules operate simultaneously on the same pre-processed input: 1. **Signature Engine** — Pattern matching against a rule database (e.g., Snort rules, YARA signatures, regex patterns). 2. **Anomaly Engine** — Statistical, clustering, or machine-learning models (e.g., One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest, autoencoders) trained on a baseline of normal behaviour. 3. **Stateful Protocol Engine** — Validates the state-transition graph of a protocol (TCP, HTTP, DNS, SMTP) against expected sequences. ### Tier 3 — Fusion & Decision Layer Aggregates the verdicts (or confidence scores) of Tier 2 engines into a single, actionable decision: *Malicious*, *Benign*, or *Suspicious (escalate to analyst)*. > [!NOTE] > **Why parallel, not sequential?** Sequential chaining would let a single failed engine stop the entire detection chain. Parallel processing followed by fusion ensures *redundancy* and *fault tolerance* — a hallmark of robust hybrid architectures. ## 2.2 Fusion Strategies — The Heart of Hybrid Detection The fusion strategy determines *how* the outputs of multiple detectors are combined. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises four classical strategies: ### 2.2.1 Rule-Based (Boolean) Fusion - **OR-Logic:** Alert if *any* engine flags the event. → Maximises detection, increases false positives. - **AND-Logic:** Alert only if *all* engines flag. → Minimises false positives, may miss attacks detected by only one engine. ### 2.2.2 Voting-Based Fusion Each engine casts a vote; a decision is made once a threshold $k$ of votes is reached (majority, plurality, or weighted). ### 2.2.3 Score-Based (Weighted Sum) Fusion Each engine produces a continuous confidence score $s_i \in [0,1]$. The hybrid score is: $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i, \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$ A decision is made by comparing $S_{\text{hybrid}}$ to a global threshold $\tau$. ### 2.2.4 Bayesian Fusion Treats each engine's output as an independent piece of evidence and updates the posterior probability of attack using **Bayes' Theorem**. Ideal when prior attack probabilities are known or estimable. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the *entire* set of formulas required for solving any KTU numerical or analytical question on Hybrid Detection. **Do not invent additional formulas.** | # | Formula | Description | Notation | |---|---------|-------------|----------| | 1 | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection Rate (Recall, Sensitivity) | DR = Detection Rate | | 2 | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Positive Rate | FPR | | 3 | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | False Negative Rate (Miss Rate) | FNR = $1 - \text{DR}$ | | 4 | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | P | | 5 | $\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic Mean of Precision & Recall | F1-Score | | 6 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall Correctness | A | | 7 | $S_{\text{hybrid}} = \sum w_i s_i$ | Weighted Score Fusion | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = score | | 8 | $P(A \mid E_i) = \dfrac{P(E_i \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E_i)}$ | Bayes' Posterior Update | $A$ = attack, $E_i$ = evidence | | 9 | $\text{ROC: } \text{TPR vs. FPR}$ | Receiver Operating Characteristic curve | Used to choose optimal $\tau$ | | 10 | $\text{Lift} = \dfrac{\text{DR}_{\text{hybrid}}}{\max(\text{DR}_i)}$ | Improvement over best single detector | Lift $>1$ justifies hybrid cost | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are the *de facto* evaluation standard in academic and industrial IDPS research. The DARPA 1998/1999, KDD Cup 99, NSL-KDD, CICIDS 2017, and UNSW-NB15 datasets are all evaluated using combinations of the formulas above. Industry products such as **Snort (with Bleeding Edge rules + Spade anomaly pre-processor)**, **Suricata (with Lua + Hyperscan + ML plugins)**, and **Zeek (formerly Bro, with file analysis + intelligence framework)** are all real-world implementations of hybrid detection. ## 2.4 Operational Advantages & Limitations ### Advantages - **Complementary coverage:** Signature engine catches known exploits; anomaly engine catches novel attacks. - **Reduced false positives:** Mutual corroboration across engines increases confidence. - **Adaptive thresholds:** Anomaly scores can be normalised against the signature engine's certainty. - **Contextual enrichment:** Stateful protocol analysis adds *semantic meaning* to raw alerts. ### Limitations - **Computational overhead:** Running multiple engines in parallel demands higher CPU/RAM. - **Fusion complexity:** Designing the fusion rule itself is a non-trivial optimisation problem. - **Dependency on training data:** Anomaly component requires high-quality, representative baselines. - **Adversarial susceptibility:** Sophisticated attackers may craft inputs that simultaneously evade multiple engines (e.g., polymorphic malware + low-and-slow anomaly). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayesian Fusion of Two Independent Detectors **Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A hybrid IDPS uses two independent detectors, $D_1$ (signature-based) and $D_2$ (anomaly-based). Each detector produces a binary alert: $A_i = 1$ (alert raised) or $A_i = 0$ (no alert). An attack $X$ is actually present with prior probability $P(X)$. We wish to compute the posterior probability that an attack is in progress, given that *both* detectors raised an alert. **Given Information:** - Prior attack probability: $P(X) = 0.01$ - Detector 1 sensitivity (true positive rate): $P(A_1 = 1 \mid X) = 0.95$ - Detector 1 false positive rate: $P(A_1 = 1 \mid \overline{X}) = 0.02$ - Detector 2 sensitivity: $P(A_2 = 1 \mid X) = 0.90$ - Detector 2 false positive rate: $P(A_2 = 1 \mid \overline{X}) = 0.05$ **Step 1: Apply Bayes' Theorem for Detector 1 alone** $$P(X \mid A_1 = 1) = \frac{P(A_1 = 1 \mid X) \cdot P(X)}{P(A_1 = 1 \mid X) \cdot P(X) + P(A_1 = 1 \mid \overline{X}) \cdot P(\overline{X})}$$ **Step 2: Substitute numerical values** $$P(X \mid A_1 = 1) = \frac{0.95 \times 0.01}{(0.95 \times 0.01) + (0.02 \times 0.99)}$$ **Step 3: Compute the numerator and denominator** $$P(X \mid A_1 = 1) = \frac{0.0095}{0.0095 + 0.0198} = \frac{0.0095}{0.0293}$$ **Step 4: Final value for Detector 1 alone** $$P(X \mid A_1 = 1) \approx 0.3242 \quad \text{(or 32.42\%)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Even with a 95% accurate signature engine, the posterior probability of a real attack is only ~32% because the *prior* is very low (most traffic is benign). This is the **Base-Rate Fallacy** — and the very reason hybrid fusion is necessary. **Step 5: Compute the joint posterior for both detectors firing together** Using the **naïve Bayes independence assumption** $P(A_1 = 1, A_2 = 1 \mid X) = P(A_1 = 1 \mid X) \cdot P(A_2 = 1 \mid X)$: $$P(A_1 = 1, A_2 = 1 \mid X) = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$$ $$P(A_1 = 1, A_2 = 1 \mid \overline{X}) = 0.02 \times 0.05 = 0.001$$ **Step 6: Apply Bayes' Theorem on the joint evidence** $$P(X \mid A_1 = 1, A_2 = 1) = \frac{0.855 \times 0.01}{(0.855 \times 0.01) + (0.001 \times 0.99)}$$ **Step 7: Compute numerator and denominator** $$P(X \mid A_1 = 1, A_2 = 1) = \frac{0.00855}{0.00855 + 0.00099} = \frac{0.00855}{0.00954}$$ **Step 8: Final hybrid posterior** $$P(X \mid A_1 = 1, A_2 = 1) \approx 0.8962 \quad \text{(or 89.62\%)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Fusing the two independent detectors raised the posterior attack probability from **32.42% to 89.62%** — a 2.77× improvement. This is the *quantitative justification* for hybrid detection. **KTU examiners love this numerical type of question.** ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weighted Score Fusion and Optimal Threshold **Problem Setup:** Three detectors produce normalised confidence scores $s_1, s_2, s_3$ on a new event. Weights are $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$. The threshold is $\tau = 0.60$. Determine the hybrid decision for scores $(0.85, 0.40, 0.55)$. **Step 1: Apply the weighted score fusion formula** $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i \cdot s_i$$ **Step 2: Substitute the values** $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = (0.5 \times 0.85) + (0.3 \times 0.40) + (0.2 \times 0.55)$$ **Step 3: Compute each term** $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = 0.425 + 0.120 + 0.110$$ **Step 4: Sum and compare to threshold** $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = 0.655$$ Since $0.655 > \tau = 0.60$, the hybrid system flags the event as **MALICIOUS**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice how a single high-confidence detector ($s_1 = 0.85$, weighted 0.5) was sufficient to push the hybrid score above the threshold, even though the anomaly engine ($s_2 = 0.40$) was relatively uncertain. This demonstrates the **"confidence propagation"** property of weighted score fusion. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof that Hybrid Detection Reduces FPR **Claim:** For two independent detectors with false positive rates $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$, an AND-fusion reduces the combined FPR multiplicatively. **Step 1: Define events** - $F_i$ = "Detector $i$ raises a false alert" - We want $P(F_1 \cap F_2)$ under independence: $P(F_1 \cap F_2) = P(F_1) \cdot P(F_2) = \alpha_1 \cdot \alpha_2$. **Step 2: Verify reduction** $$\alpha_{\text{AND}} = \alpha_1 \cdot \alpha_2 \quad \text{which is} \; \le \min(\alpha_1, \alpha_2)$$ **Step 3: Numerical example** $$\text{If } \alpha_1 = 0.02 \text{ and } \alpha_2 = 0.05, \text{ then } \alpha_{\text{AND}} = 0.001 = 0.1\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversely, OR-fusion** increases the FPR: $\alpha_{\text{OR}} = 1 - (1 - \alpha_1)(1 - \alpha_2) = 0.069 = 6.9\%$. The KTU examiner will often ask you to *comment* on the trade-off: AND reduces false alarms but may miss attacks (raises FNR); OR catches more attacks but floods the SOC with false alerts. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Detector Comparison & Hybrid Synthesis | Dimension | Signature Engine | Anomaly Engine | Stateful Protocol | Hybrid (Combined) | |-----------|-----------------|----------------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Algorithm Family** | Pattern matching, regex, hashing | Statistics, ML, clustering | State machine, automaton | All three fused | | **Speed** | Fast | Medium–Slow | Medium | Medium | | **Memory Footprint** | High (rule DB) | Medium (model) | Low–Medium | High | | **Zero-Day Capability** | ❌ None | ✅ Strong | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Strong | | **False Positive Rate** | Low (≈1–3%) | High (≈5–15%) | Low (≈2–5%) | Tunable (often <2%) | | **False Negative Rate** | High for novel attacks | Low | Medium | Low | | **Maintainability** | Frequent rule updates | Periodic retraining | Protocol update on spec changes | Compound effort | | **Evasion Resistance** | Weak (trivial obfuscation) | Moderate (adversarial ML) | Moderate (state explosion) | Strong (multi-layer) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Hybrid Detection Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Network Traffic / Host Events] --> B[Pre-Processing Pipeline] B --> B1[Packet Capture and Decode] B --> B2[Protocol Normalisation] B --> B3[Feature Extraction] B1 --> C[Parallel Detection Layer] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> D1[Signature Engine
SBD Module] C --> D2[Anomaly Engine
ABD Module] C --> D3[Stateful Protocol Engine
