SPA Module] D1 --> E[Fusion and Decision Layer] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> E1[Rule Based Fusion] E --> E2[Weighted Score Fusion] E --> E3[Bayesian Fusion] E1 --> F[Alert Generation and Response] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> F1[SOC Dashboard] F --> F2[Automated Block] F --> F3[Forensic Log] ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Hybrid Detection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Start Event Ingestion] --> P1{Event Type} P1 --> P2[Extract Network Features] P1 --> P3[Extract Host Features] P2 --> Q1[Run Signature Match] P2 --> Q2[Run Anomaly Score] P2 --> Q3[Validate Protocol State] P3 --> Q1 Q1 --> R1{Signature Matched} Q2 --> R2{Score Above Threshold} Q3 --> R3{State Violated} R1 --> S1[Confidence s1 = 0.95] R1 --> S0[Confidence s1 = 0.0] R2 --> S2[Confidence s2 = dynamic] R3 --> S3[Confidence s3 = 0.8] S1 --> T[Weighted Fusion] S0 --> T S2 --> T S3 --> T T --> U{S hybrid greater than tau} U --> V1[Flag MALICIOUS and Alert] U --> V2[Mark SUSPICIOUS for Analyst] U --> V3[Mark BENIGN and Log] ``` ## 4.3 Hybrid Detection Data Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Data] --> B[Normalisation] B --> C[Multi Engine Analysis] C --> D{Fusion Logic} D --> E[Decision Output] E --> F[Feedback to Baselines] F --> B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** These block diagrams emphasise the *information flow* rather than physical hardware. The **closed loop** in the third diagram represents the **adaptive feedback** — successful decisions are used to periodically retrain the anomaly engine and refine the state machine, which is the hallmark of a *learning* hybrid system as opposed to a static one. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] **Define Hybrid Intrusion Detection System. List its two main components.** **Model Answer:** A **Hybrid Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)** is an IDPS architecture that combines two or more detection techniques — most commonly **Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection)** and **Anomaly-Based Detection** — along with **Stateful Protocol Analysis**, within a single framework to leverage their complementary strengths and mitigate individual weaknesses. The **two main components** are: 1. **Signature/Misuse Engine** — Matches observed events against a database of known attack patterns (rules, signatures, regex). 2. **Anomaly Engine** — Builds a model of normal behaviour and flags statistically significant deviations. A **Fusion Module** acts as the third logical component that aggregates the outputs of the above two engines using rule-based, voting, score-based, or Bayesian logic to produce a final verdict. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Differentiate between Anomaly Detection and Signature-Based Detection in the context of Hybrid IDPS.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Signature-Based Detection | Anomaly-Based Detection | |-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | **Detection Principle** | Pattern matching against known attack signatures | Deviation from learned normal behaviour profile | | **Zero-Day Detection** | Cannot detect novel attacks | Capable of detecting unknown attacks | | **False Positive Rate** | Low | High (depends on baseline quality) | | **Maintenance** | Requires frequent rule database updates | Requires periodic retraining of the model | | **Speed** | Very fast (hash/regex lookups) | Slower (statistical/ML inference) | | **Example** | Snort rules, YARA, ClamAV | One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest, Autoencoders | In a hybrid system, signature detection provides *high-confidence known-attack coverage*, while anomaly detection provides *broad zero-day coverage*. The fusion layer reconciles their verdicts. (3 marks) --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided) ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Hybrid Intrusion Detection System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Fusion Layer** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two independent detectors $D_1$ and $D_2$ are used in a hybrid IDPS. The prior probability of an attack is $P(A) = 0.02$. Detector $D_1$ has sensitivity $0.98$ and false positive rate $0.03$. Detector $D_2$ has sensitivity $0.92$ and false positive rate $0.04$. Compute the posterior probability of an attack when **both** detectors raise an alert, assuming conditional independence. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture of a Hybrid IDPS** (7 marks) A Hybrid IDPS is organised into **three primary tiers**: 1. **Data Acquisition Tier:** Captures raw packets (NIDS) or system calls/logs (HIDS) and feeds them into a pre-processing module. Pre-processing performs protocol decoding, payload reassembly, sessionisation, and feature extraction to produce a uniform event record. 2. **Parallel Detection Tier:** Hosts multiple, independent detection engines running simultaneously: - **Signature Engine** — Performs string/regex matching against a curated rule database. Fast and precise for known attacks. - **Anomaly Engine** — Uses statistical thresholds, clustering, or ML models (e.g., One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest) trained on a baseline of legitimate activity. Catches zero-day attacks but with higher false positive rate. - **Stateful Protocol Engine** — Maintains a state machine for each protocol session and flags transitions that violate the protocol's specification (e.g., out-of-order TCP flags, malformed DNS queries). 3. **Fusion / Decision Tier:** The **heart** of the hybrid system. It accepts the verdicts (or confidence scores) of all engines and combines them using one of the following strategies: - **Rule-Based Fusion** (AND/OR logic) - **Voting Fusion** (majority/plurality) - **Weighted Score Fusion** ($S = \sum w_i s_i$) - **Bayesian Fusion** (posterior probability update) **Role of the Fusion Layer:** - **Conflict Resolution:** When engines disagree (e.g., signature says "malicious", anomaly says "benign"), the fusion layer applies a policy to resolve the conflict. - **Confidence Calibration:** Continuous-score engines are normalised to a common scale $[0,1]$ before combination. - **False Positive Suppression:** AND-fusion or weighted fusion can suppress spurious alerts from a single noisy engine. - **Threshold Tuning:** The fusion layer exposes a global threshold $\tau$ that the SOC can tune without retraining individual engines. - **Feedback:** Confirmed alerts can be fed back to retrain the anomaly engine, enabling continuous learning. A block diagram is illustrated in **Section 4.1** above. --- **(b) Numerical — Bayesian Fusion** (7 marks) **Step 1: Given values** $$P(A) = 0.02, \quad P(\overline{A}) = 0.98$$ $$P(D_1 = 1 \mid A) = 0.98, \quad P(D_1 = 1 \mid \overline{A}) = 0.03$$ $$P(D_2 = 1 \mid A) = 0.92, \quad P(D_2 = 1 \mid \overline{A}) = 0.04$$ **Step 2: Joint likelihoods under conditional independence** $$P(D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1 \mid A) = 0.98 \times 0.92 = 0.9016$$ $$P(D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1 \mid \overline{A}) = 0.03 \times 0.04 = 0.0012$$ **Step 3: Total probability of both detectors firing** $$P(D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1) = (0.9016 \times 0.02) + (0.0012 \times 0.98) = 0.018032 + 0.001176 = 0.019208$$ **Step 4: Apply Bayes' Theorem** $$P(A \mid D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1) = \frac{P(D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1 \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1)}$$ $$P(A \mid D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1) = \frac{0.9016 \times 0.02}{0.019208} = \frac{0.018032}{0.019208} \approx 0.9388$$ **Step 5: Result and Comment** $$\boxed{P(A \mid D_1 = 1, D_2 = 1) \approx 0.9388 \;\; (93.88\%)}$$ **Comment:** When *both* detectors raise an alert, the posterior probability of a real attack rises to **~93.88%**, compared to a baseline prior of only **2%**. This is a **~47× improvement** in decision confidence and quantitatively validates the *engineering rationale* of hybrid detection: combining two moderately accurate, independent detectors yields a highly reliable joint verdict. In SOC operations, alerts exceeding a 90% posterior threshold are typically auto-blocked without human review. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain **Score-Based Weighted Fusion** in hybrid IDPS. State its advantages over Boolean (AND/OR) fusion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Three detectors produce normalised scores $s_1 = 0.92, s_2 = 0.78, s_3 = 0.65$ for a suspicious event. Weights are $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$. The decision threshold is $\tau = 0.80$. Determine the hybrid decision. If the SOC team wants to reduce false positives by **increasing the threshold to 0.85**, recompute the decision. Comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Score-Based Weighted Fusion** (7 marks) In **Score-Based Weighted Fusion**, each detection engine $D_i$ produces a continuous confidence score $s_i \in [0, 1]$ indicating its belief that the event is malicious. The **hybrid score** is computed as a weighted linear combination: $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i, \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$ The decision is: $$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{MALICIOUS} & \text{if } S_{\text{hybrid}} \geq \tau \\ \text{BENIGN} & \text{if } S_{\text{hybrid}} < \tau \end{cases}$$ (Diagrammatic representation is given in **Section 4.2** above.) **Advantages over Boolean Fusion:** - **Granular Information Preservation:** Boolean fusion discards confidence information (only 0/1 is retained). Weighted fusion retains the *degree* of certainty. - **Tunable Sensitivity:** The threshold $\tau$ and the weights $w_i$ provide two independent degrees of freedom for tuning detection vs. false-alarm trade-off. - **Soft Failures:** If one engine degrades (e.g., produces low-quality scores), its weight can be reduced dynamically without disabling the engine. - **Confidence Propagation:** A single high-confidence engine can compensate for uncertain engines, as seen in the numerical example below. - **Theoretical Grounding:** The framework is mathematically equivalent to a **linear opinion pool** and can be derived from Bayesian or Dempster-Shafer theory. --- **(b) Numerical — Weighted Fusion with Threshold Adjustment** (7 marks) **Step 1: Compute the hybrid score with original weights** $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = (0.5 \times 0.92) + (0.3 \times 0.78) + (0.2 \times 0.65)$$ $$S_{\text{hybrid}} = 0.460 + 0.234 + 0.130 = 0.824$$ **Step 2: Compare with threshold $\tau = 0.80$** Since $0.824 \geq 0.80$, the event is classified as **MALICIOUS**. **Step 3: Recompute with new threshold $\tau = 0.85$** The hybrid score is unchanged ($0.824$) since the threshold adjustment does not affect the score calculation. **Step 4: Compare with new threshold** Since $0.824 < 0.85$, the event is now classified as **BENIGN**. **Step 5: Trade-off Comment** Raising the threshold from **0.80 to 0.85** makes the system **more conservative**: - ✅ **False Positive Rate decreases** — fewer benign events are mis-flagged, reducing SOC alert fatigue. - ❌ **False Negative Rate increases** — the malicious event in this example would now be missed (true positive lost). **Engineering Recommendation:** The threshold should be set at the *inflection point* of the ROC curve where the marginal gain in FPR reduction equals the marginal loss in TPR. In production, a common practice is to use **two thresholds**: a *high* threshold (e.g., 0.85) for **automated blocking** and a *low* threshold (e.g., 0.50) for **alerting human analysts** — a *tiered response model*. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the Conditional Independence Assumption:** The naïve Bayes fusion formula $P(D_1, D_2 \mid A) = P(D_1 \mid A) \cdot P(D_2 \mid A)$ is **only valid under the independence assumption**. In reality, detectors may share common feature inputs, violating independence. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you apply the formula blindly without stating this assumption. **Always mention the assumption explicitly.** > > 2. **Confusing OR-Fusion with AND-Fusion:** A common pitfall is to write $\alpha_{\text{AND}} = 1 - (1-\alpha_1)(1-\alpha_2)$, which is the OR formula. AND-fusion is simply the product: $\alpha_{\text{AND}} = \alpha_1 \cdot \alpha_2$. > > 3. **Not Normalising Scores in Weighted Fusion:** If the individual scores $s_i$ are not on a common scale, the weighted sum is meaningless. Always state the normalisation step. > > 4. **Omitting Units and Interpretation:** Numerical answers without a concluding sentence (e.g., "Thus, the posterior attack probability is 93.88%, which justifies automated blocking") are considered incomplete. KTU board examiners explicitly look for the *engineering interpretation*. > > 5. **Drawing a Vague Block Diagram:** A diagram without labelled arrows or without a clear fusion layer is marked down. Use the Mermaid conventions shown in Section 4. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Hybrid Detection = Signature + Anomaly + Stateful Protocol Analysis**, combined through a fusion layer. - ✅ The **primary motivation** is to overcome the *base-rate fallacy* — a single accurate detector still yields low posterior probability when priors are low. - ✅ **Fusion Strategies:** Rule-Based (AND/OR), Voting, **Weighted Score** ($\sum w_i s_i$), and **Bayesian** ($P(A \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid A) P(A)}{P(E)}$). - ✅ **AND-Fusion** reduces FPR multiplicatively; **OR-Fusion** reduces FNR but inflates FPR. - ✅ **Key Metrics to Memorise:** DR, FPR, FNR, Precision, F1-Score, Accuracy, ROC curve. - ✅ **Bayesian Numerical Type:** Always state the *conditional independence assumption* before applying the naïve Bayes formula. - ✅ **Weighted Fusion Numerical Type:** Verify $\sum w_i = 1$ and compare the hybrid score to $\tau$ explicitly. - ✅ **Real-world Examples:** Snort + Spade, Suricata + Lua scripts, Zeek + Intelligence Framework. - ✅ **Closed-Loop / Adaptive Hybrid Systems** use SOC feedback to periodically retrain the anomaly engine — this is the *state of the art*. - ✅ **Trade-off:** Hybrid systems trade *computational cost* and *engineering complexity* for *higher detection accuracy* and *lower false positive rates* — this trade-off is a common 7-mark question in KTU ESE. ---

topics.title: Architecture and Implementation - Centralized
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture and Implementation - Centralized

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Centralized IDPS Architecture — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centralized IDPS Architecture** is a deployment topology in which **multiple distributed sensors/agents** monitor traffic and host activities across various network segments or hosts, but **all detection logic, event correlation, alerting, and policy management are performed at a single central analysis engine and management console**.

In this architecture, raw or partially pre-processed data is forwarded from **remote sensors** to a **central manager** (also called the central analysis server), which performs deep packet inspection, signature matching, anomaly scoring, and decision-making. The central console provides a unified administrative interface for security analysts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Centralized architecture** consolidates IDS/IPS functions (data collection, analysis, response) at one or a small number of central locations, while sensors are deployed at strategic points to forward telemetry. This is the most common enterprise-grade IDPS deployment model.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **large hospital chain** spread across 20 cities:

- **Local Clinics (Sensors/Agents):** Each city has a small clinic that collects patient samples (blood, X-rays) but does NOT have expensive MRI machines or specialist doctors.
- **Central Hospital (Central Manager/Analyzer):** All complex analysis, diagnosis, and treatment decisions happen at ONE central hospital with the best specialists and equipment.
- **Courier Service (Secure Communication Channel):** The clinics send samples to the central hospital via secure transport; the central hospital sends back diagnosis reports and treatment instructions.
- **Reception Desk (Management Console):** A single reception area where administrators monitor everything.

This is exactly how a **Centralized IDPS** works. The "samples" are network packets or host events; the "specialists" are detection engines, correlation algorithms, and policy enforcement modules.

### Key Standard Metrics in Centralized IDPS

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor-to-Manager Latency** | < 100 ms (LAN), < 500 ms (WAN) | Critical for real-time prevention |
| **Event Throughput** | 10,000 – 100,000 EPS (Events Per Second) | Central server's processing capacity |
| **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | < 1 second for signature-based | KTU exam focus metric |
| **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** | **1 central node** | Core weakness of this architecture |
| **Bandwidth Overhead** | 5–15% of monitored link | Encrypted tunnel consumption |

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Single Point of Failure (SPOF)"** is the most frequently tested keyword in KTU questions on centralized architecture.

### Core Components of Centralized Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four mandatory components of a Centralized IDPS, as per KTU 2024 syllabus:
>
> 1. **Sensors / Agents** — Distributed at network choke points or hosts
> 2. **Central Analysis Server (Manager)** — Performs detection & correlation
> 3. **Secure Communication Channel** — Encrypted tunnel (TLS/IPsec) between sensor and manager
> 4. **Management Console** — Unified dashboard for analysts

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The Centralized IDPS operates through a structured **data pipeline** that can be broken into six logical stages:

### Stage 1: Distributed Data Capture (Sensors)
- **Network-based sensors** are placed at choke points (e.g., between firewall and DMZ, at ingress/egress routers).
- **Host-based agents** are installed on critical servers (web, DB, authentication).
- Sensors perform **Layer 2–7 capture** using techniques like **port mirroring (SPAN)**, **network TAPs**, or **inline bridging**.

> **Why?** A single sensor cannot see the entire enterprise traffic; distribution is mandatory for coverage.

### Stage 2: Pre-Processing & Normalization (At Sensor)
- Raw packets are converted into **IDPS events** (a standardized record format).
- Format examples: **CEF (Common Event Format)**, **LEEF (Log Event Extended Format)**, **IDMEF (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format)**.
- **Why?** So that the central manager can interpret events identically regardless of sensor vendor.

### Stage 3: Secure Forwarding
- Events are sent over **TLS 1.3 / IPsec tunnels** to the central manager.
- **Authentication:** Mutual certificate-based authentication prevents rogue sensor injection.
- **Integrity:** SHA-256 HMAC ensures events are not tampered in transit.

### Stage 4: Central Analysis Engine
This is the **brain** of the system. It performs:
- **Signature Matching** — Compares events against a rule database (e.g., Snort rules).
- **Anomaly Detection** — Applies statistical models, ML classifiers.
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis** — Validates protocol state machines (e.g., HTTP request–response flow).
- **Event Correlation** — Links low-severity events across time/windows to detect multi-stage attacks (e.g., reconnaissance → exploit → exfiltration).

### Stage 5: Decision & Response
- If **detection** mode → alert is generated and forwarded to console.
- If **prevention** mode → action is sent back to sensor:
  - **Drop** packet
  - **Reset** TCP connection
  - **Block** source IP via ACL push to firewall

### Stage 6: Storage, Reporting & Console Display
- Events are persisted in a **central database** (often a SIEM-integrated store like **Elasticsearch**, **Splunk indexer**, or **SQL database**).
- Console provides dashboards, drill-down analysis, and forensic timelines.

> **Why is this design chosen?** Centralization enables **unified policy management**, **lower per-sensor cost** (since expensive CPUs are not needed at the edge), and **easier correlation** (all data is in one place).

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Event Throughput Requirement** | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_{sensor_i}$ | events/sec | Central server must handle sum of all sensors |
| **Available Bandwidth for Tunnel** | $B_{available} = B_{link} \times (1 - O_{overhead})$ | Mbps | $O_{overhead}$ is 5–15% for IDPS |
| **Mean Time to Detect** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum (T_{detect} - T_{attack})}{N_{attacks}}$ | seconds | Lower is better |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ratio (0–1) | Critical metric for centralized correlation |
| **Detection Confidence Score** | $C_{score} = w_1 S_{sig} + w_2 S_{anom} + w_3 S_{corr}$ | 0–100 | $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ |
| **Sensor Coverage Ratio** | $S_{cov} = \frac{\text{Traffic seen by sensors}}{\text{Total enterprise traffic}}$ | % | Must approach 100% |
| **Central Server CPU Load** | $L_{cpu} = k \cdot E_{total} \cdot A_{complexity}$ | % | $k$ is hardware constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, you may be asked to compute **throughput aggregation** ($E_{total}$) or **FPR** — remember these two formulas are the most-tested.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Centralized IDPS architecture is used in:
- **Enterprise Data Centers** (e.g., banks, telecom operators)
- **Government CERT setups** (e.g., CERT-In India)
- **Cloud Security Operations Centers (SOCs)** where one central analyzer monitors virtual sensors across multiple cloud accounts
- **SCADA/ICS** deployments where one central node monitors many substations

> [!NOTE]
> Real-world examples: **Cisco Firepower Management Center (FMC)** manages multiple Firepower sensors centrally. **IBM QRadar** is another classic centralized architecture. **Snort** with a central **Barnyard2 → MySQL → BASE** is the open-source reference model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Analytical Derivation 1: Central Server Throughput Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU-favorite problem: *"Given N sensors each generating E events/sec, what is the central server's minimum throughput requirement?"*

### Problem Setup
An enterprise deploys a centralized IDPS with **$n$ network sensors**. Each sensor $i$ generates on average $E_i$ events per second. The central manager must process all events without loss.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the per-sensor event rate.**

Let $E_i$ be the events/sec generated by sensor $i$, where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

**Step 2 — Define the aggregate load.**

The central manager receives the sum of all sensor streams. Therefore the required throughput $E_{total}$ is:

$$
E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i = E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + \ldots + E_n
$$

**Step 3 — Add safety margin (Headroom).**

A good engineering practice is to provision **30–50% headroom** above the peak load to handle burst traffic:

$$
E_{provisioned} = E_{total} \times (1 + H)
$$

where $H$ is the headroom factor (typically $0.3$ to $0.5$).

**Step 4 — Add correlation overhead.**

Centralized correlation requires additional CPU cycles. Let $\alpha$ be the correlation overhead factor (typically $0.1$ to $0.2$):

$$
E_{final} = E_{provisioned} \times (1 + \alpha)
$$

**Step 5 — Final sizing equation.**

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$
E_{final} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \right) \times (1 + H) \times (1 + \alpha)
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)
Given: **5 sensors**, each generating **2000 EPS**, $H = 0.4$, $\alpha = 0.15$:

$$
E_{total} = 5 \times 2000 = 10{,}000 \text{ EPS}
$$

$$
E_{provisioned} = 10{,}000 \times 1.4 = 14{,}000 \text{ EPS}
$$

$$
E_{final} = 14{,}000 \times 1.15 = 16{,}100 \text{ EPS}
$$

Therefore, the central manager must be sized for **at least 16,100 EPS** capacity.

## Analytical Derivation 2: Detection Confidence Scoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *"Explain how a centralized IDPS combines signature, anomaly, and correlation scores."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define individual scores.**

Let:
- $S_{sig} \in [0, 100]$ = signature match score
- $S_{anom} \in [0, 100]$ = anomaly detection score
- $S_{corr} \in [0, 100]$ = correlation confidence score

**Step 2 — Assign weights based on trust.**

Weights are assigned such that:

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0
$$

For example, signature-based detection is often trusted most, so:
- $w_1 = 0.5$ (signature)
- $w_2 = 0.3$ (anomaly)
- $w_3 = 0.2$ (correlation)

**Step 3 — Weighted sum formula.**

$$
C_{score} = w_1 \cdot S_{sig} + w_2 \cdot S_{anom} + w_3 \cdot S_{corr}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision threshold.**

If $C_{score} \geq T_{threshold}$ (e.g., 70), then **alert/prevent**. Otherwise, **log only**.

### Numerical Example
Given $S_{sig} = 90$, $S_{anom} = 60$, $S_{corr} = 80$:

$$
C_{score} = (0.5 \times 90) + (0.3 \times 60) + (0.2 \times 80)
$$

$$
C_{score} = 45 + 18 + 16 = 79
$$

Since $79 \geq 70$, the system generates an **alert with prevention action**.

## Analytical Matrix: Centralized vs. Distributed (KTU Comparison Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked KTU comparison question** on this topic. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Centralized Architecture | Distributed Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Analysis Location** | Single central server | Each sensor analyzes locally |
| **Data Storage** | Central DB | Local + partial sync |
| **Single Point of Failure** | **Yes (central node)** | No |
| **Correlation Capability** | **Excellent** (all data in one place) | Limited (requires sync) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by central server | **Highly scalable** |
| **Network Overhead** | High (sensors forward raw data) | Low (only alerts forwarded) |
| **Management Complexity** | **Low** (single console) | High (per-node management) |
| **Cost per Sensor** | Low (sensor is "thin") | High (sensor has full engine) |
| **Latency to Response** | Higher (round-trip to center) | **Lower (local decision)** |
| **Suitable For** | Small-to-medium enterprise | Large / geographically dispersed |
| **Example** | Snort + centralized MySQL | Autonomous agent-based IDPS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "CCCD HMLS":**
> - **C**orrelation (better in central)
> - **C**ost per sensor (low in central)
> - **C**onsole (unified in central)
> - **D**ecision latency (lower in distributed)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Centralized IDPS — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL NETWORK (Internet)"]
        attacker1[Attacker Host]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE LAYER (Sensors at Choke Points)"]

        sensorB[Sensor B - DMZ]
        sensorC[Sensor C - Internal LAN]

        sensorE[Host Agent - DB Server]
    end

    subgraph SECCHAN["SECURE COMMUNICATION CHANNEL"]
        tunnel1[TLS 1.3 Encrypted Tunnel]
        tunnel2[Mutual Certificate Auth]
    end

    subgraph CORE["CENTRAL ANALYSIS SERVER (Manager)"]
        collectorA[Event Collector]
        normalizerA[Normalizer CEF LEEF]

        corrEngineA[Correlation Engine]
        decisionA[Decision Policy Module]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["CENTRAL STORAGE LAYER"]

        dbB[Policy Database]
    end

    subgraph MGMT["MANAGEMENT CONSOLE"]
        consoleA[Analyst Dashboard]
        alertA[Alert Viewer]
        reportA[Report Generator]
    end

    attacker1 --> sensorA
    sensorA --> tunnel1
    sensorB --> tunnel1
    sensorC --> tunnel1
    sensorD --> tunnel1
    sensorE --> tunnel1
    tunnel1 --> tunnel2

    collectorA --> normalizerA
    normalizerA --> sigEngineA
    normalizerA --> anomEngineA

    anomEngineA --> corrEngineA

    decisionA -.Action Push.-> sensorB
```

## Diagram 2: Centralized Data Flow — Sequence View

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Net as Network Traffic
    participant S as Sensor
    participant T as TLS Tunnel
    participant M as Central Manager
    participant DB as Central Database
    participant C as Console

    Net->>S: Raw packets captured
    S->>S: Pre-process, normalize to CEF
    S->>T: Forward event over TLS
    T->>M: Deliver authenticated event
    M->>M: Run signature + anomaly + correlation
    M->>DB: Store processed event
    M->>C: Push alert to console
    C->>M: Analyst may send block command
    M->>S: Push prevention action (drop/reset)
    S->>Net: Enforce inline block
```

## Diagram 3: Centralized Deployment Topology — Enterprise View

```mermaid

    sA -->|WAN Tunnel| mgr
    sB -->|WAN Tunnel| mgr

    mgr --> con
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams (KTU Tip):**
> - **Block 1** shows the 4 functional layers: **Edge → Secure Channel → Core → Management**
> - **Block 2** shows the *temporal sequence* of an event from capture to prevention
> - **Block 3** shows the *physical deployment* in a multi-site enterprise
>
> In KTU 14-mark questions, you are often asked to **draw and label** the centralized architecture. The first diagram is the expected answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Centralized IDPS architecture. List its two main components.**

**Model Answer:**

> Centralized IDPS architecture is a deployment model in which **multiple distributed sensors** collect data from various network segments and forward it to a **single central analysis server** for detection, correlation, and response.

**Two main components:**
1. **Sensors / Agents** — distributed at network choke points or on hosts; perform capture and pre-processing only.
2. **Central Manager (Analysis Server)** — performs signature matching, anomaly detection, correlation, and policy enforcement.

**Marking Scheme (KTU pattern):** Definition 2 marks + 2 components (0.5 each) = 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a "single point of failure" in centralized IDPS? How is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** is a component whose failure causes the entire IDPS to stop functioning. In centralized IDPS, the **central manager** is the SPOF — if it crashes, all sensors become "blind" (no analysis happens).

**Mitigation techniques:**
1. Deploy a **hot-standby / failover central manager** (active-passive cluster).
2. Use **load-balanced active-active** central managers.
3. Implement **sensor-side caching** so sensors can buffer events during central server downtime.

**Marking Scheme:** SPOF definition 1.5 marks + 2 mitigations (0.75 each) = 3 marks.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Centralized IDPS**. Label all components and explain the function of each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare **Centralized and Distributed IDPS architectures** in terms of scalability, fault tolerance, correlation capability, and management complexity. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture Explanation [7 marks]

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the block diagram (as shown in Section 4, Diagram 1). The four layers — **Sensors, Secure Channel, Central Manager, Console** — must be clearly labeled.

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Sensors (1 mark):** Placed at perimeter, DMZ, internal LAN, and on critical hosts. Capture traffic via SPAN ports, TAPs, or inline. Convert raw packets into normalized events (CEF/LEEF format).

2. **Secure Communication Channel (1 mark):** TLS 1.3 or IPsec tunnel with mutual certificate authentication. Provides confidentiality, integrity, and prevents rogue sensor injection.

3. **Central Manager (1 mark):** Houses the three engines — Signature Engine (Snort-style rule matching), Anomaly Engine (statistical/ML scoring), Correlation Engine (multi-event attack chain detection). Outputs a unified **detection confidence score**.

4. **Management Console + DB (1 mark):** Analysts view alerts, drill into events, generate compliance reports. Database stores raw events, policies, and audit logs.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison [7 marks]

| Parameter | Centralized | Distributed | KTU Marking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scalability** | Limited by central server capacity | Highly scalable — add nodes freely | 1.5 |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low (SPOF exists) | High (no SPOF) | 1.5 |
| **Correlation Capability** | Excellent (all data co-located) | Limited (requires sync) | 1.5 |
| **Management Complexity** | Low (single console) | High (per-node admin) | 1.0 |
| **Network Overhead** | High | Low | 0.5 |
| **Cost per Sensor** | Low (thin sensor) | High (fat sensor) | 0.5 |
| **Decision Latency** | Higher | Lower (local) | 0.5 |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Centralized architecture is preferred for **small-to-medium enterprises** requiring strong correlation and unified management. Distributed is preferred for **large, geographically dispersed organizations** requiring resilience and low latency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "centralized" with "single sensor." Centralized means *multiple sensors + ONE central analyzer*. Students often write "one sensor" and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Do NOT** omit the *secure communication channel* from the diagram. The TLS/IPsec tunnel between sensor and manager is **mandatory** in KTU marking.
> 3. **Do NOT** write only "centralized is bad, distributed is good." KTU expects **balanced comparison** with at least 4 parameters and a justified conclusion.
> 4. The diagram must show **flow direction** (arrows). A static box diagram without arrows gets **0 marks** for the diagram portion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of the central analysis server** in a centralized IDPS. Describe the three detection engines it hosts. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An enterprise deploys **6 sensors**, each generating **3500 events/sec**. The headroom factor is **40%** and correlation overhead is **15%**. Calculate the **minimum throughput** the central manager must support. If the central server can handle **25,000 EPS**, will it be sufficient? Justify. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Central Analysis Server Role [7 marks]

The **Central Analysis Server** is the heart of the centralized IDPS. Its primary role is to **consume, analyze, correlate, and act upon** events forwarded by distributed sensors.

**Three Detection Engines (2 marks each + 1 mark for introduction):**

1. **Signature Detection Engine (2 marks):**
   - Maintains a database of attack signatures (e.g., Snort rules, YARA rules).
   - Performs **pattern matching** against incoming events.
   - Pros: low false positive, fast for known attacks.
   - Cons: cannot detect zero-day attacks.

2. **Anomaly Detection Engine (2 marks):**
   - Builds a **baseline profile** of normal behavior (statistical, ML, or clustering-based).
   - Flags deviations exceeding a threshold.
   - Pros: can detect novel attacks.
   - Cons: higher false positive rate.

3. **Correlation Engine (2 marks):**
   - Aggregates events across time and sensors.
   - Detects **multi-stage attack patterns** (e.g., scan → brute force → exploit).
   - Uses rule-based or ML-based correlation.
   - Outputs a final **confidence score** using:
$$
C_{score} = w_1 S_{sig} + w_2 S_{anom} + w_3 S_{corr}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The combined operation of these three engines provides **defense-in-depth** at the analysis layer.

---

#### Part (b) — Throughput Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $n = 6$ sensors
- $E_i = 3500$ events/sec each
- $H = 0.40$ (headroom)
- $\alpha = 0.15$ (correlation overhead)
- Central server capacity = **25,000 EPS**

**Step 1 — Total event rate (2 marks):**

$$
E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} E_i = 6 \times 3500 = 21{,}000 \text{ EPS}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply headroom (1 mark):**

$$
E_{provisioned} = 21{,}000 \times (1 + 0.40) = 21{,}000 \times 1.4 = 29{,}400 \text{ EPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply correlation overhead (1 mark):**

$$
E_{final} = 29{,}400 \times (1 + 0.15) = 29{,}400 \times 1.15 = 33{,}810 \text{ EPS}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with capacity (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Required} = 33{,}810 \text{ EPS}
$$

$$
\text{Available} = 25{,}000 \text{ EPS}
$$

$$
33{,}810 > 25{,}000
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The central server is **NOT sufficient**. It is short by $33{,}810 - 25{,}000 = 8{,}810$ EPS. **Recommendation:** either upgrade the server to ≥ 35,000 EPS capacity, add a second central manager in load-balanced mode, or reduce sensor event rates via local pre-filtering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in This Numerical:**
> 1. Forgetting to **multiply** the headroom — students often just add 40% to the total, which is mathematically equivalent but the formula form is preferred for KTU.
> 2. Forgetting the **correlation overhead** factor — losing 1 mark.
> 3. Writing the conclusion as "no" without quantifying the deficit (must show $33{,}810 > 25{,}000$ explicitly).
> 4. **Unit error:** writing "events" instead of "events/sec (EPS)" — KTU strictly penalizes this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Definition:** Centralized IDPS = **multiple sensors + ONE central analyzer**.
- **Four layers:** Sensors → Secure Channel → Central Manager → Console.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF):** Central manager. Always mention in exam answers.
- **SPOF Mitigation:** Hot-standby, active-active cluster, sensor-side caching.
- **Event Format Standards:** CEF, LEEF, IDMEF — must be mentioned when describing normalization.
- **Secure Channel:** TLS 1.3 / IPsec with mutual certificate authentication.
- **Three Engines at Central Manager:** Signature, Anomaly, Correlation.
- **Confidence Score Formula:** $C_{score} = w_1 S_{sig} + w_2 S_{anom} + w_3 S_{corr}$.
- **Throughput Sizing:** $E_{final} = \left( \sum E_i \right) \times (1 + H) \times (1 + \alpha)$.
- **Comparison Keywords:** "CCCD HMLS" mnemonic — Correlation, Cost, Console (centralized wins); Decision latency, Multi-site, SPOF-resilience (distributed wins).
- **Real-World Examples:** Cisco FMC + Firepower sensors, IBM QRadar, Snort + MySQL + BASE.
- **Advantages:** Unified policy, strong correlation, low per-sensor cost.
- **Disadvantages:** SPOF, network bottleneck, higher WAN overhead, single point of attack.
- **Suitable for:** Small-to-medium enterprises, single data center, government SOCs.
- **NOT suitable for:** Large geographically distributed networks, ultra-low-latency needs.
- **Standard Metrics:** MTTD < 1 sec, FPR < 5%, sensor coverage > 95%, EPS provisioning with 30–50% headroom.
- **Draw diagrams** with arrows, labels on all boxes, and indicate the **secure tunnel** explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed - Intelligent Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distributed - Intelligent Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS), a **Distributed Intelligent Agent (DIA)** is defined as an *autonomous, software-based computational entity* that is strategically deployed across multiple heterogeneous nodes of a computer network to perform localized data collection, intelligent analysis, anomaly detection, and proactive threat response. These agents operate with a degree of artificial intelligence, enabling them to reason, learn, adapt, and cooperate with other agents without continuous human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Distributed Intelligent Agent in IDPS is a self-contained software module equipped with perception (sensing), reasoning (inference engine), and action (response) capabilities that collaborates with peer agents via standardized communication languages to collectively secure a distributed computing environment.

**Key Synonyms in KTU Literature:**
- Mobile Agents (MA)
- Cooperative Intrusion Detection Agents (CIDA)
- Multi-Agent Systems for Intrusion Detection (MASID)
- Autonomous Security Agents

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Security Guard Network"**

Imagine a massive international airport with hundreds of terminals. Instead of relying on one central control room to monitor every camera and alarm, the airport deploys **autonomous smart security robots** at each terminal. Each robot:
1. **Sees** its own local surroundings (cameras, sensors).
2. **Thinks** independently using AI (recognizes suspicious behavior).
3. **Communicates** with other robots via walkie-talkies (agent communication language).
4. **Acts** locally (alerts, blocks, detains).
5. **Migrates** if needed (moves to high-risk zones during peak hours).

These robots form a **distributed team** that collectively secures the airport. This is *exactly* how Distributed Intelligent Agents function in an IDPS — each agent is a small "smart security guard" for a network segment, and together they form an intelligent defensive mesh.

---

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of an Intelligent Agent

| Property | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomy** | Operates without direct human intervention | Reduces SOC analyst load |
| **Reactivity** | Responds to environmental changes in real-time | Enables live threat blocking |
| **Pro-activeness** | Takes goal-directed initiative | Predictive defense |
| **Social Ability** | Communicates with other agents via ACL | Enables cooperative detection |
| **Mobility** | Can migrate between network nodes (for mobile agents) | Dynamic defense reconfiguration |
| **Learning** | Adapts using ML/DL techniques | Zero-day detection |
| **Rationality** | Acts to maximize a defined utility function | Optimized response decisions |

---

## 1.4 High-Level Classification of Agents in IDPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Distinction:** Students must be able to classify agents correctly in the exam.

**A. Stationary (Static) Agents:**
- Permanently reside on a single host or network segment.
- Continuously monitor local logs, traffic, and system calls.
- Example: A host-based IDS agent on a Windows server.

**B. Mobile Agents:**
- Can migrate across the network carrying their code, state, and data.
- Useful in bandwidth-constrained or disconnected environments.
- Examples: IBM *Aglets*, *Grasshopper*, *Concordia*.

**C. Reactive Agents:**
- Respond immediately to environmental stimuli using simple condition-action rules (stimulus-response).

**D. Deliberative (Cognitive) Agents:**
- Maintain an internal world model and use reasoning/planning before acting (e.g., Belief-Desire-Intention — BDI architecture).

**E. Hybrid Agents:**
- Combine reactive and deliberative layers for both speed and intelligence.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Blueprint of a Distributed Intelligent Agent

Every intelligent agent in an IDPS comprises **three logical layers**:

### Layer 1: Perception / Sensing Layer
- Captures raw data from network packets (NIDS) or host logs (HIDS).
- Tools: *libpcap*, *WinPcap*, audit logs, syscall monitors.
- Output: structured event tuples $E = \{t, src, dst, proto, payload\}$.

### Layer 2: Reasoning / Inference Layer
The "intelligence" core. Contains:
- **Signature Engine:** Pattern matching against known attack signatures.
- **Anomaly Detector:** Statistical / ML models (SVM, Random Forest, LSTM).
- **State Machine:** Tracks multi-step attack scenarios.
- **Knowledge Base:** Stores learned rules, threat intelligence feeds.

### Layer 3: Action / Response Layer
- **Passive Response:** Logging, alerts, SNMP traps.
- **Active Response:** Packet dropping, session termination, IP blacklisting.
- **Cooperative Response:** Shares threat intel with peer agents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inter-Agent Communication** typically happens through standardized **Agent Communication Languages (ACLs)** such as:
> - **KQML (Knowledge Query and Manipulation Language)** — developed under the DARPA Knowledge Sharing Effort.
> - **FIPA-ACL** — standardized by the Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents.
> These use *speech-act theory* with performatives like `inform`, `query`, `request`, `subscribe`.

---

## 2.2 Multi-Agent System (MAS) Architecture for IDPS

A practical MAS-based IDPS follows a **hierarchical cooperative model**:

1. **Local Agents** — Deployed at each host/subnet. Perform primary detection.
2. **Regional Aggregators** — Correlate alerts from multiple local agents in a domain.
3. **Central Manager / Coordinator** — Performs global analysis and policy enforcement.
4. **Human Analyst Interface** — GUI dashboard for the SOC team.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards Bodies Referenced in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **IETF IDWG** (Intrusion Detection Working Group) → defines *IDMEF* (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format) for inter-agent communication.
> - **IETF INCH** (Information and Collaboration Hub) → defines protocols for sharing threat intelligence.
> - **DMTF** (Distributed Management Task Force) → CIM (Common Information Model) for security.

---

## 2.3 Advantages vs Disadvantages (Exam Favorite)

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| **Scalability** — Add agents as the network grows | **Agent Security** — Agents themselves can be attacked |
| **Fault Tolerance** — No single point of failure | **Coordination Overhead** — Message passing cost |
| **Reduced Network Load** — Local processing, less raw data transfer | **Trust Issues** — Compromised agents may send false alerts |
| **Real-Time Response** — Local blocking is faster than centralized decision | **Complexity** — Designing robust communication protocols |
| **Adaptability** — Agents can learn and migrate | **Deployment Difficulty** — Heterogeneous environments |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are testable in the KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Notation | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Detection Rate (DR)** $\;=\; \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | TP=True Positives, FN=False Negatives | Ratio (0–1) | Fraction of actual attacks correctly detected |
| 2 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** $\;=\; \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | FP=False Positives, TN=True Negatives | Ratio (0–1) | Probability of false alarm |
| 3 | **Accuracy (ACC)** $\;=\; \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | — | Ratio (0–1) | Overall correctness |
| 4 | **F1-Score** $\;=\; \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $P$=Precision, $R$=Recall | Ratio (0–1) | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| 5 | **Agent Communication Overhead (ACO)** $\;=\; n \cdot m \cdot \vert vert{x} \vert$ | $n$=agents, $m$=messages, $\vert vert{x} \vert$=avg msg size | Bytes | Total bandwidth consumed by agent chatter |
| 6 | **Average Detection Latency** $\;=\; \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(t_{alert,i} - t_{attack,i}\right)$ | $t_{alert}$, $t_{attack}$ in seconds | Seconds | Mean time between attack and detection |
| 7 | **Network Survivability Index** $\;=\; 1 - \dfrac{N_{comp}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{comp}$=compromised agents, $N_{total}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Robustness against agent compromise |
| 8 | **Bandwidth Savings vs Centralized IDS** $\;=\; 1 - \dfrac{D_{local}}{D_{raw}}$ | $D_{local}$=local summaries, $D_{raw}$=raw data | Ratio (0–1) | Efficiency gain from distributed processing |
| 9 | **Trust Score of Agent** $\;=\; \alpha R + (1-\alpha) H$ | $R$=reputation, $H$=historical accuracy, $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | Ratio (0–1) | Bayesian trust model weight |
| 10 | **Bayes Optimal Detection** $\;=\; P(C \vert vert{x}) \;=\; \dfrac{P(\vert vert{x}\vert C)\,P(C)}{P(\vert vert{x}\vert)}$ | $C$=class, $\vert vert{x}$=observation | Probability | Naive Bayes classifier decision |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule Compliance:** All absolute value bars in tables use `\vert` instead of the literal pipe character `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Distributed Intelligent Agents

1. **Smart Grids & IoT** — Edge-based agents detect anomalies in millions of sensors without flooding the cloud.
2. **Autonomous Vehicles** — V2V (vehicle-to-vehicle) agents cooperatively detect road-level threats.
3. **Financial Networks** — Multi-agent fraud detection systems at bank branches share intelligence in real-time.
4. **Cloud Data Centers** — Agent-based HIDS across thousands of VMs for tenant isolation.
5. **Military / Defense Networks** — Resilient IDS that survives node compromise due to distributed nature.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing Detection Rate from a Confusion Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (Typical KTU Part-B Style):**
> During evaluation of a Distributed Intelligent Agent-based IDPS, the following confusion matrix was observed on a test dataset containing **1000 samples**:

| | Predicted: Attack | Predicted: Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Attack** | TP = 180 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual: Normal** | FP = 30 | TN = 770 |

> Compute the **Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Accuracy, and F1-Score**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Confusion Matrix Values
We extract from the matrix:

$$
TP = 180,\quad FP = 30,\quad FN = 20,\quad TN = 770
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Detection Rate (Recall)

$$
DR \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
DR \;=\; \frac{180}{180 + 20}
$$

$$
DR \;=\; \frac{180}{200}
$$

$$
\boxed{DR \;=\; 0.90 \;=\; 90\%}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the False Positive Rate

$$
FPR \;=\; \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

$$
FPR \;=\; \frac{30}{30 + 770}
$$

$$
FPR \;=\; \frac{30}{800}
$$

$$
\boxed{FPR \;=\; 0.0375 \;=\; 3.75\%}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Accuracy

$$
ACC \;=\; \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

$$
ACC \;=\; \frac{180 + 770}{180 + 770 + 30 + 20}
$$

$$
ACC \;=\; \frac{950}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{ACC \;=\; 0.95 \;=\; 95\%}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the F1-Score

First, compute Precision:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \;=\; \frac{180}{180 + 30} \;=\; \frac{180}{210} \;\approx\; 0.8571
$$

Recall $R = DR = 0.90$. Then:

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.8571 \times 0.90}{0.8571 + 0.90}
$$

$$
F_1 \;=\; \frac{1.5429}{1.7571}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_1 \;\approx\; 0.878 \;=\; 87.8\%}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation
The agent-based IDPS catches **90% of real attacks** with only a **3.75% false alarm rate**. The high F1-score of 87.8% indicates a well-balanced detector, suitable for deployment in a distributed cooperative IDS framework.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Communication Overhead in a Multi-Agent IDPS

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A distributed IDPS has **25 agents**, each exchanging **40 alert messages per minute**, with an average message size of **512 bytes**. Compute the total communication overhead per minute and per hour.

### Step 1 — Identify Variables
$n = 25$ agents, $m = 40$ messages per agent per minute, $\vert vert{x} \vert = 512$ bytes per message.

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Minute Overhead

$$
ACO_{min} \;=\; n \cdot m \cdot \vert vert{x} \vert
$$

$$
ACO_{min} \;=\; 25 \times 40 \times 512
$$

$$
ACO_{min} \;=\; 1000 \times 512
$$

$$
\boxed{ACO_{min} \;=\; 512{,}000 \;\text{bytes/min} \;=\; 500 \;\text{KB/min}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Per-Hour Overhead

$$
ACO_{hr} \;=\; ACO_{min} \times 60
$$

$$
ACO_{hr} \;=\; 512{,}000 \times 60
$$

$$
\boxed{ACO_{hr} \;=\; 30{,}720{,}000 \;\text{bytes/hr} \;\approx\; 29.3 \;\text{MB/hr}}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in Megabits per Second (Mbps)

Convert to bits: $30{,}720{,}000 \times 8 = 245{,}760{,}000$ bits/hr.

Per second:

$$
\text{Bitrate} \;=\; \frac{245{,}760{,}000}{3600} \;\approx\; 68{,}267 \;\text{bps} \;\approx\; 0.068 \;\text{Mbps}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
The IDPS consumes roughly **0.068 Mbps** of bandwidth for inter-agent coordination. On a typical 1 Gbps enterprise link, this is **negligible (<0.01%)**, justifying the distributed architecture.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayesian Trust Update for Agent Reputation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Two agents $A_1$ and $A_2$ report alerts. Historical trust scores are $H_{A_1}=0.95$, $H_{A_2}=0.70$. Their recent reputation ratings are $R_{A_1}=0.90$, $R_{A_2}=0.85$. With $\alpha = 0.6$, compute the final trust scores.

### Step 1 — Apply the Trust Formula

$$
T \;=\; \alpha R + (1-\alpha) H
$$

### Step 2 — For Agent A₁

$$
T_{A_1} \;=\; 0.6 \times 0.90 + 0.4 \times 0.95
$$

$$
T_{A_1} \;=\; 0.54 + 0.38
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{A_1} \;=\; 0.92}
$$

### Step 3 — For Agent A₂

$$
T_{A_2} \;=\; 0.6 \times 0.85 + 0.4 \times 0.70
$$

$$
T_{A_2} \;=\; 0.51 + 0.28
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{A_2} \;=\; 0.79}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
Agent $A_1$ is more trustworthy ($T = 0.92$) and its alerts should be given higher weight in the cooperative correlation engine.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Distributed vs Centralized IDS

| Parameter | Centralized IDS | Distributed Intelligent Agents |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Single analysis server | Agents at every host/segment |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Yes (critical risk) | No (high resilience) |
| **Network Bandwidth** | High (raw data forwarded) | Low (only summaries) |
| **Detection Latency** | High (depends on link to central server) | Low (local processing) |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Agent Security** | N/A | Critical concern |
| **Deployment Cost** | Low initial, high operational | Higher initial, lower operational |
| **Best Use Case** | Small networks | Large enterprise / IoT / Cloud |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a Distributed Intelligent Agent in the context of IDPS.**
*(Model Answer — KTU Style)*

A **Distributed Intelligent Agent** in IDPS is an autonomous software entity deployed across multiple nodes of a network. It independently performs three functions: **(i) Perception** — collecting data from network packets, host logs, or system calls; **(ii) Reasoning** — analyzing data using signature matching, anomaly detection, or machine learning; and **(iii) Action** — generating alerts, blocking traffic, or cooperating with peer agents. These agents communicate using standardized languages such as **KQML** or **FIPA-ACL**, enabling cooperative intrusion detection across the entire network.

> **Examiner Note:** Award 1 mark each for perception, reasoning, action — and bonus mark if ACL is mentioned.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Stationary Agents and Mobile Agents in IDPS.**
*(Model Answer — KTU Style)*

| Aspect | Stationary Agent | Mobile Agent |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Fixed on one host/segment | Can migrate between hosts |
| **Code Size** | Small (resident) | Larger (must carry code + state) |
| **Bandwidth Usage** | Low (only alerts) | Variable (moves with data) |
| **Use Case** | Continuous host monitoring | Bandwidth-constrained or disconnected networks |
| **Examples** | SNORT sensor agent | IBM *Aglets*, *Grasshopper* |
| **Security Risk** | Host-level compromise | Code tampering during transit |

> **Examiner Note:** 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for any two valid differences.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Description of Distributed Intelligent Agent Architecture in IDPS**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe in detail the three-tier hierarchical architecture of a Distributed Intelligent Agent-based IDPS, with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of Agent Communication Languages (KQML/FIPA-ACL).**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Three-Tier Architecture (5 marks for description + diagram):**

**Tier 1 — Host/Local Agents:**
These are deployed on individual hosts or network segments. They perform:
- Packet capture and log analysis
- Signature-based detection (e.g., SNORT rules)
- Anomaly detection using ML models
- Local response: alerting, blocking, session termination

**Tier 2 — Regional Aggregators (Domain Managers):**
- Receive alerts from multiple local agents within a domain
- Perform alert correlation to reduce false positives
- Apply cross-host attack pattern recognition (e.g., distributed port scans)
- Forward summarized alerts upward

**Tier 3 — Central Coordinator / Manager:**
- Maintains global threat knowledge base
- Coordinates global response actions
- Interfaces with the SOC (Security Operations Center)
- Distributes updated policies and signatures downward

**2. Agent Communication Languages (2 marks):**

**KQML (Knowledge Query and Manipulation Language):**
- Developed under the DARPA Knowledge Sharing Effort.
- Uses **speech-act theory** with performatives like `ask-one`, `tell`, `perform`, `subscribe`.
- Format: `(performative :sender A :receiver B :content ...)`

**FIPA-ACL:**
- Standardized by the *Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents*.
- Supports richer semantics with content language (e.g., SL — Semantic Language).
- Defines 20+ communicative acts including `inform`, `request`, `query-ref`, `propose`.

**Role:** These ACLs enable agents to share threat intelligence, request actions, and subscribe to event streams, achieving **cooperative intrusion detection**.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the IETF standards (IDMEF, IDWG, INCH) that support interoperability among distributed IDPS agents. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of mobile agents.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. IETF Standards (3 marks):**

- **IETF IDWG (Intrusion Detection Working Group):** Defined the **Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format (IDMEF)** as an XML-based standard (RFC 4765) for alert exchange between IDS components.

- **IETF INCH (Information and Collaboration Hub):** Defined protocols for sharing threat intelligence between organizations (RFC 3067, RFC 4767).

- **DMTF CIM (Common Information Model):** Provides a security model for managed objects.

- **IEEE / IETF Protocols:** IDXP (Intrusion Detection Exchange Protocol) over TLS.

**2. Advantages of Mobile Agents (2 marks):**

- **Bandwidth Efficiency** — Move code to data instead of moving data to code.
- **Disconnected Operation** — Agents work offline and synchronize when reconnected.
- **Dynamic Deployment** — Adapt defense topology in real-time.
- **Reduced Central Server Load** — Distribute computation across the network.

**3. Disadvantages of Mobile Agents (2 marks):**

- **Agent Code Tampering** — Agents may be modified during migration.
- **Trust Issues** — Receiving host must trust the agent platform.
- **Migration Overhead** — Code transfer latency.
- **Complex Debugging** — Hard to trace distributed execution.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Quantitative Analysis of a Multi-Agent IDPS Deployment**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A distributed IDPS uses 30 agents, each generating an average of 50 alert messages per minute. Each message averages 256 bytes. Compute the total communication overhead per hour. Also calculate the bandwidth saving if a centralized IDS would require transmitting 2 GB of raw data per hour instead.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Communication Overhead per Minute:**

$$
ACO_{min} = n \cdot m \cdot \vert vert{x} \vert = 30 \times 50 \times 256
$$

$$
ACO_{min} = 1500 \times 256 = 384{,}000 \;\text{bytes/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Per-Hour:**

$$
ACO_{hr} = 384{,}000 \times 60 = 23{,}040{,}000 \;\text{bytes/hr} \approx 21.97 \;\text{MB/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth Saving:**

Convert centralized raw data: $D_{raw} = 2 \;\text{GB} = 2048 \;\text{MB}$.

$$
\text{Saving} = 1 - \frac{D_{local}}{D_{raw}} = 1 - \frac{21.97}{2048}
$$

$$
\text{Saving} = 1 - 0.01073 \approx 0.9893
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Bandwidth Saving} \approx 98.93\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The distributed agent architecture saves approximately **98.93% bandwidth**, justifying its use in large-scale networks.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**From the following confusion matrix, compute the Detection Rate, False Positive Rate, Accuracy, and F1-Score of an agent-based IDS. Comment on its suitability for deployment.**

| | Predicted: Attack | Predicted: Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Attack** | 250 | 50 |
| **Actual: Normal** | 40 | 660 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract Values:**

$$
TP = 250,\; FN = 50,\; FP = 40,\; TN = 660
$$

**Step 2 — Detection Rate (Recall):**

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{250}{250 + 50} = \frac{250}{300} \approx 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**Step 3 — False Positive Rate:**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{40}{40 + 660} = \frac{40}{700} \approx 0.0571 = 5.71\%
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$
ACC = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{250 + 660}{1000} = \frac{910}{1000} = 91.0\%
$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

Precision:

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{250}{290} \approx 0.8621
$$

F1-Score:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.8621 \times 0.8333}{0.8621 + 0.8333} = \frac{1.4369}{1.6954} \approx 0.8475 = 84.75\%
$$

**Step 6 — Suitability Comment:**

The IDS achieves a strong **DR of 83.33%** with a low **FPR of 5.71%** and an excellent **Accuracy of 91%**. The F1-score of 84.75% indicates a balanced detector, making it **suitable for deployment** in a cooperative multi-agent environment. However, the 5.71% FPR should be further reduced by integrating with regional aggregators for alert correlation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls for this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Intelligent Agents" with "Software Agents"** — Students forget to emphasize the *autonomy + learning + cooperation* triad. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Missing Standards** — Failing to mention IDMEF, KQML, or FIPA-ACL. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **No Diagram** — Part (a) of 14-mark questions without a labelled block diagram loses **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Formula Mistakes** — Mixing up TP/FP/FN/TN positions in the formula table. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** — Students list advantages but don't explain *why* bandwidth saving matters (e.g., scalability argument).
> 6. **Not Differentiating Mobile vs Stationary** — A common 3-mark trap in Part A.
> 7. **Forgetting Units** — "Detection Rate = 0.83" without "%" or explanation loses **0.5 marks**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Distributed Intelligent Agents**

- ✅ **Intelligent Agent** = Autonomous + Reactive + Pro-active + Social + Learning.
- ✅ Three layers: **Perception → Reasoning → Action**.
- ✅ Three tiers: **Host Agents → Regional Aggregators → Central Manager**.
- ✅ **KQML** and **FIPA-ACL** are the two primary Agent Communication Languages.
- ✅ **IDMEF** (RFC 4765) is the IETF standard format for IDS alert exchange.
- ✅ Mobile agents use frameworks: **Aglets, Grasshopper, Concordia**.
- ✅ Stationary agents = fixed location; Mobile agents = migrate across hosts.
- ✅ Detection Rate formula: $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$.
- ✅ False Positive Rate formula: $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$.
- ✅ F1-Score = Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- ✅ Bandwidth saving formula: $1 - \dfrac{D_{local}}{D_{raw}}$.
- ✅ Trust score: $T = \alpha R + (1-\alpha) H$.
- ✅ IETF IDWG defines IDMEF; IETF INCH handles inter-org threat sharing.
- ✅ Hybrid agents combine reactive speed with deliberative intelligence (BDI architecture).
- ✅ Key advantage: **No Single Point of Failure**. Key risk: **Agent compromise**.
- ✅ Distributed IDPS is **scalable, fault-tolerant, bandwidth-efficient** but requires **robust agent security and trust mechanisms**.
- ✅ Always draw a **labelled block diagram** for full marks in Part B.
- ✅ Always **include units** (%, seconds, bytes, bps) in numerical answers.
- ✅ Mention **at least one real-world example** (IoT, Smart Grid, Cloud) for higher-order Bloom's marks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mobile Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mobile Agent (MA)** is a self-contained software program that can autonomously migrate across heterogeneous network environments, suspending its execution at a host, transporting its state, code, and data to a destination host, and resuming execution at the new host. In the context of **Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS)**, mobile agents are dispatched to distributed monitoring points to perform local data collection, signature matching, anomaly detection, and even active response actions (blocking, isolating, or quarantining) without the need for a centralized, static collection engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT742 Module 4):**
> A mobile agent in IDS is an *autonomous, goal-directed program* that physically moves through the network topology, carrying its execution state, to perform distributed intrusion detection closer to the data source. This paradigm shifts the computation to the data, rather than transferring raw data to a central analysis server.

The three fundamental components that travel with a mobile agent are:

1. **Code** – The executable program logic (detection rules, signatures, behavioral models).
2. **State** – The current execution context (variables, program counter, stack, thread state).
3. **Data** – Local observations, partial results, and intermediate evidence gathered at each hop.

> [!NOTE]
> The agent system depends on an **Agent Execution Environment (AEE)** or *agent platform* running on every host (e.g., IBM Aglet Workbench, Concordia, Telescript, Grasshopper). The AEE provides serialization, transport, security, and lifecycle management.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Digital Inspector"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Visiting Health Inspector**
> Imagine a factory with 50 buildings scattered across a large campus. Instead of forcing every building to mail all its daily records to a head office (centralized IDS), a **health inspector physically walks from building to building**. He carries his clipboard (state), his rulebook (code), and the previous day's notes (data). At each building, he examines conditions locally, makes notes, and only travels back to headquarters with a small summary report. If he spots a violation, he can immediately issue a local "shut-down notice" (prevention action) without waiting for head office approval.

**Mapping the analogy to Mobile Agents in IDS:**

| Real-World Inspector | Mobile Agent in IDPS |
|---|---|
| Inspector's rulebook | Detection signature database / rule engine |
| Clipboard & running notes | Agent's execution state and partial results |
| Walking between buildings | Network migration between hosts/sensors |
| Building gates & security desks | Agent Execution Environment (AEE) |
| Local shut-down notice | Inline prevention action (drop, block) |
| Summary back to HQ | Returning aggregated alerts to console |

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standardized Parameters

- **Agent Migration Latency ($T_m$)** – Time (in **milliseconds (ms)**) taken to serialize, transport, and resume an agent at a remote host. Typical value: **50 ms – 500 ms** over LAN.
- **Agent Payload Size ($S_a$)** – Measured in **kilobytes (KB)**. Lightweight agents: 10–50 KB; feature-rich agents: 100 KB – 1 MB.
- **Heterogeneity Tolerance** – The ability of an agent to execute across different OS/hardware (e.g., Windows → Linux → IoT-Firmware).
- **Autonomy Index** – Degree of decision-making the agent can perform without consulting the central station.
- **Network Bandwidth Saving ($\Delta B$)** – Reduction in raw data traffic, often quantified as **70%–95%** compared to centralized log forwarding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The core security goals in Mobile-Agent-based IDPS are **Confidentiality** (agent code is not tampered with), **Integrity** (results are authentic), and **Non-repudiation** (the agent cannot deny its actions) — collectively known as the **CIA Triad of Agent Security**.

---

## 1.4 Why Mobile Agents? The Motivation Triangle

Centralized IDS architectures suffer from three structural weaknesses that mobile agents directly address:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Scalability Killers of Centralized IDS:**
> 1. **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** – The central collector crashes → whole network goes blind.
> 2. **Bandwidth Saturation** – Massive raw log forwarding from thousands of sensors.
> 3. **Latency in Detection** – Round-trip delay before a threat is identified and blocked.

Mobile agents decentralize both **data collection** and **decision-making**, offering robustness, scalability, and reduced network load.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of a Mobile Agent IDPS

A Mobile-Agent-based IDPS consists of five logical tiers:

1. **Central Management Station (CMS)** – Orchestrates agent dispatch, receives final reports, handles global correlation.
2. **Agent Dispatcher / Scheduler** – Decides *which* agent to send, *when*, and *where*, based on policy and current threat landscape.
3. **Mobile Agent Itself** – The payload containing code, state, and data.
4. **Agent Execution Environment (AEE)** – Software layer installed on monitored hosts that hosts, executes, and migrates agents.
5. **Local Sensors / Hooks** – OS-level listeners (syscall hooks, network tap, log readers) that the agent queries upon arrival.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Term:** The process of *suspending* an agent at one host, transferring it, and *resuming* it at another is called **Strong Migration**. When only the code (not the state) is moved, it is called **Weak Migration** (e.g., Java applets with stateless servlets).

---

## 2.2 Operational Lifecycle – Step-by-Step Logic

The mobile agent executes through a strict six-stage lifecycle:

1. **Stage 1 – Creation (`agent.new()`):** The CMS instantiates the agent, attaches the relevant signature/heuristic set, and assigns a unique Agent ID.
2. **Stage 2 – Dispatch (`agent.dispatch(host_X)`):** The agent's state is serialized, optionally encrypted/signed, and transmitted to the target host's AEE.
3. **Stage 3 – Reception & Authentication:** The destination AEE verifies the agent's digital signature, checks the originating CMS's credentials, and creates a sandbox.
4. **Stage 4 – Local Execution:** The agent queries local sensors, runs the detection algorithm, correlates local events, and decides whether an alert/prevention action is needed.
5. **Stage 5 – Cloning / Migration Decision:** Based on findings, the agent may (a) clone itself to propagate to neighbours, (b) update its state and return home, or (c) trigger an active prevention response.
6. **Stage 6 – Retirement (`agent.dispose()`):** Upon completing its mission, the agent transmits a concise report to the CMS and terminates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Migration Decision Triggers** include: (i) Detected anomaly threshold exceeded, (ii) Scheduled patrol timer expired, (iii) External command from CMS, (iv) Peer-agent invitation via shared tuple-space (Linda-style coordination).

---

## 2.3 Coordination Models Between Mobile Agents

When multiple mobile agents operate in the same network, they must coordinate. Three classical models are examined in KTU:

| Model | Mechanism | Use Case in IDPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Messaging** | Agent-to-agent RPC | Cross-segment correlation of a multi-stage attack |
| **Blackboard (Tuple-Space)** | Shared data structure (Linda model) | Distributed signature sharing without tight coupling |
| **Meeting / Itinerary** | Pre-planned visit list | Patrol-based periodic monitoring |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes the essential quantitative relations governing mobile-agent IDPS performance.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Agent Migration Latency** | $T_m = T_{ser} + T_{net} + T_{res}$ | ms (milliseconds) | Serialization + network + resume time |
| 2 | **Network Bandwidth Saving** | $\Delta B = \dfrac{D_{raw} - D_{agent}}{D_{raw}} \times 100$ | % (percentage) | Saving achieved by sending agents instead of raw logs |
| 3 | **Total Detection Time** | $T_d = T_m \cdot N_{hops} + T_{exec}$ | ms | Time from dispatch to final alert |
| 4 | **Agent Hop Limit** | $N_{hops} \le \dfrac{T_{budget}}{T_m}$ | integer | Maximum hops within time budget |
| 5 | **Payload Size (compressed)** | $S_a^{c} = S_a \cdot (1 - R_c)$ | KB | $R_c$ = compression ratio (0 to 1) |
| 6 | **Encryption Overhead** | $T_{enc} = \dfrac{S_a}{R_{crypt}} \cdot C_{op}$ | ms | $R_{crypt}$ = cipher throughput (KB/ms); $C_{op}$ = CPU cycles per byte |
| 7 | **Anomaly Score (Z-norm)** | $Z = \dfrac{\vert x_i - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Local statistical detection at the agent |
| 8 | **Total Bandwidth Used by Agents** | $B_{total} = N_a \cdot S_a \cdot f_{mig}$ | KB/s | $N_a$ = number of agents, $f_{mig}$ = migration frequency |
| 9 | **Availability of IDPS** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Higher availability when agents decentralize processing |
| 10 | **Trust Score for Agent Origin** | $T_{agent} = \alpha \cdot A_{sig} + \beta \cdot H_{cred} + \gamma \cdot R_{hist}$ | dimensionless | Composite of signature validity, credentials, history |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **strictly deduct marks** if the distinction between **Strong** and **Weak** migration is confused, or if the three components of a mobile agent (Code + State + Data) are listed incorrectly.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mobile-agent IDPS architectures are deployed in:

- **Smart Grid SCADA Networks** – Where centralised polling is impossible due to bandwidth constraints.
- **Military Tactical MANETs** – Where nodes constantly move and central infrastructure is absent.
- **IoT Botnet Detection** – Pushing lightweight detection logic directly onto edge gateways.
- **Cloud Microservice Meshes** – Sidecar-pattern agents that migrate to inspect east-west traffic.
