# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Bandwidth Saving ($\Delta B$) for Mobile Agent vs. Centralized Log Forwarding

**Problem Setup:**
A network has $N$ monitored hosts. Each host generates $L$ log lines per second, with an average size of $b$ bytes per line. In a centralized IDS, all logs are forwarded to a central server. In a mobile-agent architecture, a single agent of size $S_a$ KB migrates periodically to collect and compress data, returning only a summary of size $D_{agent}$.

**Step 1: Raw Data Generated Per Second (Centralized Model)**

$$
D_{raw} = N \cdot L \cdot b \quad \text{[bytes/second]}
$$

The total data forwarded to the central collector per second is the product of the number of hosts, the log rate per host, and the average log line size.

**Step 2: Data Transmitted Per Second (Mobile Agent Model)**

$$
D_{agent} = \dfrac{N \cdot S_a \cdot f_{mig}}{1}
$$

Here, $S_a$ is the agent payload in bytes, and $f_{mig}$ is the migration frequency in migrations/second. Note that aggregated summaries are usually 90% smaller than raw logs.

**Step 3: Substitute into Bandwidth Saving Formula**

$$
\Delta B = \dfrac{D_{raw} - D_{agent}}{D_{raw}}
$$

**Step 4: Express as a Percentage**

$$
\Delta B \,\% = \left( 1 - \dfrac{N \cdot S_a \cdot f_{mig}}{N \cdot L \cdot b} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 5: Cancel the Common Factor $N$**

$$
\Delta B \,\% = \left( 1 - \dfrac{S_a \cdot f_{mig}}{L \cdot b} \right) \times 100
$$

This simplified form allows the network engineer to directly compute the saving by knowing the agent size, migration frequency, and host log rate.

**Step 6: Numerical Example**

Let $N = 500$ hosts, $L = 100$ logs/sec/host, $b = 200$ bytes, $S_a = 50$ KB = 50{,}000 bytes, $f_{mig} = 0.1$ migrations/sec.

$$
D_{raw} = 500 \times 100 \times 200 = 10{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} = 10 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
D_{agent} = 500 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.1 = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} = 2.5 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
\Delta B = \dfrac{10 - 2.5}{10} = 0.75 = 75\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A **75% bandwidth saving** is achieved by using mobile agents instead of raw log forwarding.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Maximum Number of Hops Within a Detection Budget

**Problem Setup:**
A mobile agent must traverse $N_{hops}$ hosts within a maximum allowable detection time budget of $T_{budget}$ (often dictated by an SLA, e.g., 5 seconds). The migration latency per hop is $T_m$, and the local execution time per host is $T_{exec}$.

**Step 1: Total Time Equation**

$$
T_{total} = N_{hops} \cdot T_m + N_{hops} \cdot T_{exec}
$$

**Step 2: Factor Out the Common Term**

$$
T_{total} = N_{hops} \cdot (T_m + T_{exec})
$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to Solve for $N_{hops}$**

$$
N_{hops} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{T_m + T_{exec}}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Time Budget Constraint**

$$
N_{hops} \le \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_{budget}}{T_m + T_{exec}} \right\rfloor
$$

The floor function ensures we do not exceed the time budget.

**Step 5: Numerical Example**

Let $T_{budget} = 5000$ ms, $T_m = 200$ ms, $T_{exec} = 50$ ms.

$$
N_{hops} \le \left\lfloor \dfrac{5000}{200 + 50} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{5000}{250} \right\rfloor = 20 \text{ hops}
$$

**Conclusion:** The agent can visit at most **20 hosts** within the 5-second SLA.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Z-Score Based Local Anomaly Score

**Problem Setup:**
A mobile agent observes $n$ packets of certain sizes at a host. The mean packet size is $\mu$, and the standard deviation is $\sigma$. The agent computes a Z-score for each observed packet size $x_i$.

**Step 1: Z-Score Definition**

$$
Z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 2: Absolute Z-Score (Magnitude of Deviation)**

$$
\vert Z_i \vert = \dfrac{\vert x_i - \mu \vert}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 3: Thresholding**

If $\vert Z_i \vert > Z_{thr}$ (typically $Z_{thr} = 3$ for a 99.7% confidence interval in a normal distribution), the packet is flagged as anomalous.

**Step 4: Aggregate Anomaly Score (Mean of Absolute Z-Scores)**

$$
Z_{agg} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\vert x_i - \mu \vert}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 5: Decision Rule**

$$
\text{Alert} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Yes}, & \text{if } Z_{agg} > Z_{thr} \\
\text{No}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 6: Numerical Example**

Suppose $\mu = 500$ bytes, $\sigma = 50$ bytes, observations = $\{480, 510, 495, 700, 490, 505, 800\}$.

Compute individual deviations:

- $x_1 = 480 \Rightarrow \vert Z_1 \vert = \dfrac{\vert 480 - 500 \vert}{50} = 0.4$
- $x_2 = 510 \Rightarrow \vert Z_2 \vert = \dfrac{10}{50} = 0.2$
- $x_3 = 495 \Rightarrow \vert Z_3 \vert = 0.1$
- $x_4 = 700 \Rightarrow \vert Z_4 \vert = \dfrac{200}{50} = 4.0$
- $x_5 = 490 \Rightarrow \vert Z_5 \vert = 0.2$
- $x_6 = 505 \Rightarrow \vert Z_6 \vert = 0.1$
- $x_7 = 800 \Rightarrow \vert Z_7 \vert = \dfrac{300}{50} = 6.0$

Now compute the aggregate:

$$
Z_{agg} = \dfrac{0.4 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 4.0 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 6.0}{7} = \dfrac{11.0}{7} \approx 1.57
$$

Since $Z_{agg} = 1.57 < Z_{thr} = 3$, the *aggregate* does not trigger. However, **two individual packets ($x_4, x_7$) exceed the threshold**, indicating bursty anomalies that the agent should escalate to the CMS.

**Conclusion:** Mobile agents must check **both** per-event and aggregate Z-scores to detect bursty attacks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – When to Use Mobile Agents

| Scenario | Centralized IDS | Mobile-Agent IDPS | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homogeneous LAN, low log volume | Efficient, easy to manage | Overkill | **Centralized** |
| Heterogeneous WAN with bandwidth limits | Saturates links | Saves 70%+ bandwidth | **Mobile Agent** |
| Highly mobile / ad-hoc networks | Connection drops | Agents ride on the network | **Mobile Agent** |
| Real-time blocking requirements | Round-trip delay | Local prevention possible | **Mobile Agent** |
| Strict regulatory logging & archival | Easier compliance | Harder to audit | **Centralized** |
| Edge / IoT constrained devices | Heavy forwarding | Tiny agent migrates | **Mobile Agent** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CMS[Central Management Station]
    AD[Agent Dispatcher Scheduler]
    AA1[Mobile Agent Instance 1]
    AA2[Mobile Agent Instance 2]
    AEE1[Agent Execution Environment Host A]
    AEE2[Agent Execution Environment Host B]
    AEE3[Agent Execution Environment Host C]
    S1[Local Sensors Host A]
    S2[Local Sensors Host B]
    S3[Local Sensors Host C]
    DB[(Signature Rule Database)]
    LOG[(Central Alert Log)]

    CMS --> AD
    AD --> DB
    AD --> AA1
    AD --> AA2
    AA1 --> AEE1
    AA1 --> AEE2
    AA2 --> AEE2
    AA2 --> AEE3
    AEE1 --> S1
    AEE2 --> S2
    AEE3 --> S3
    S1 --> AEE1
    S2 --> AEE2
    S3 --> AEE3
    AA1 -.report.-> CMS
    AA2 -.report.-> CMS
    CMS --> LOG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows show **migration paths** (solid lines) of mobile agents from the dispatcher to hosts. The dotted lines represent **report-back channels** to the CMS. Each host runs an **AEE** that hosts the agent and exposes local sensor data.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Mobile Agent Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Dispatched: dispatch host
    Dispatched --> Authenticating: arrival at AEE
    Authenticating --> Rejected: invalid signature
    Rejected --> [*]
    Authenticating --> Executing: verified
    Executing --> Migrating: itinerary next host
    Executing --> Cloning: anomaly detected
    Executing --> Preventing: threshold crossed
    Migrating --> Authenticating
    Cloning --> Authenticating
    Preventing --> Reporting: action logged
    Executing --> Reporting: task complete
    Reporting --> Retired: report sent
    Retired --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The state machine captures the **six legal lifecycle states** of a mobile agent. Note the **forking paths** when the agent detects a threat (Cloning or Preventing) versus the normal patrol path (Migrating). Rejection (security failure) leads to immediate termination.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Mobile Agent Coordination via Blackboard Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SharedSpace[Blackboard Tuple-Space]
        BB[(Shared Blackboard)]
    end
    MA1[Mobile Agent on Host A]
    MA2[Mobile Agent on Host B]
    MA3[Mobile Agent on Host C]
    CMS[Central Management Station]

    MA1 -- read --> BB
    MA2 -- write signature --> BB
    MA3 -- read --> BB
    MA1 -- write alert --> BB
    MA2 -- read alert --> BB
    BB -. sync .-> CMS
    CMS -. global correlation .-> BB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each agent asynchronously **reads** and **writes** to a shared *blackboard* (a tuple-space). The CMS performs global correlation by inspecting the blackboard. This loose-coupling design is more robust than direct agent-to-agent RPC.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram – Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Mobile Agent. List the three components that travel with it during migration.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Mobile Agent** is an autonomous software program that can migrate across different hosts in a network, carrying its execution context, to perform tasks locally at each host without continuous central supervision.
> The three components that travel with the agent are:
> 1. **Code** – the executable program logic (detection rules/signature engine)
> 2. **State** – current execution context (variables, program counter)
> 3. **Data** – partial results and locally gathered observations
>
> *(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for the three components + 0.5 mark for example)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Strong Migration and Weak Migration in mobile agent systems.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Strong Migration | Weak Migration |
> |---|---|---|
> | **State Carried** | Full execution state (program counter, stack, heap) | Only code and data; execution must restart |
> | **Resume Point** | Resumes at the exact instruction where suspended | Restarts from a defined entry point |
> | **Language Support** | Requires language support (e.g., Telescript, Agent Tcl) | Easy in Java-like environments (applets) |
> | **Complexity** | High | Low |
> | **Use in IDS** | Long-running correlation tasks | Stateless, one-shot sensor tasks |
>
> *(1.5 marks for differentiation + 1 mark for examples + 0.5 mark for IDS context)*

## 5.2 Part B – Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a Mobile-Agent-based IDPS** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Agent Execution Environment (AEE)** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **bandwidth saving formula** $\Delta B$ for a mobile-agent IDPS compared to a centralized log-forwarding system. A network has **300 hosts**, each generating **150 log lines/sec** of average size **180 bytes**. The mobile agent has a payload of **40 KB** and migrates at a frequency of **0.05 migrations/sec**. Calculate the percentage bandwidth saved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) – Architecture Explanation (7 marks)

A Mobile-Agent-based IDPS consists of **five logical tiers**:

1. **Central Management Station (CMS):** Orchestrates agent creation, dispatch, and receives final reports. Performs global correlation. *(1 mark)*

2. **Agent Dispatcher/Scheduler:** Decides which agent to send, when, and to which host, based on policy. Maintains the signature database. *(1 mark)*

3. **Mobile Agent:** The payload itself, carrying code, state, and data. Performs local detection at each visited host. *(1 mark)*

4. **Agent Execution Environment (AEE):** A software layer on each monitored host that:
   - Authenticates incoming agents via digital signatures.
   - Provides a **sandbox** for safe execution.
   - Manages the **lifecycle** (suspend, serialize, transport, resume).
   - Exposes local sensor APIs (syscall hooks, network tap, log files).
   - *(1.5 marks for AEE detailed role)*

5. **Local Sensors/Hooks:** OS-level listeners that the agent queries once it resumes. *(0.5 mark)*

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**
*(1 mark)* — Show CMS at top, dispatcher below, AEE on each host, agents migrating along arrows, sensors at the bottom of each host.

**Advantages of this architecture:**
- Eliminates single point of failure.
- Reduces bandwidth consumption.
- Enables local prevention actions.
- *(1 mark)*

### Part (b) – Bandwidth Derivation & Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Raw data generated per second by $N$ hosts:

$$
D_{raw} = N \cdot L \cdot b
$$

Step 2: Data transmitted per second by mobile agents:

$$
D_{agent} = N \cdot S_a \cdot f_{mig}
$$

Step 3: Bandwidth saving:

$$
\Delta B = \dfrac{D_{raw} - D_{agent}}{D_{raw}}
$$

Step 4: Substitute and simplify:

$$
\Delta B = 1 - \dfrac{S_a \cdot f_{mig}}{L \cdot b}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $N = 300$, $L = 150$ logs/sec, $b = 180$ bytes, $S_a = 40$ KB = 40{,}000 bytes, $f_{mig} = 0.05$ migrations/sec.

$$
D_{raw} = 300 \times 150 \times 180 = 8{,}100{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} \approx 8.1 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
D_{agent} = 300 \times 40{,}000 \times 0.05 = 600{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} \approx 0.6 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
\Delta B = \dfrac{8{,}100{,}000 - 600{,}000}{8{,}100{,}000} = \dfrac{7{,}500{,}000}{8{,}100{,}000} \approx 0.9259
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta B \approx 92.59\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** The mobile-agent IDPS saves approximately **92.59% bandwidth** compared to centralized log forwarding. *(Verification: 1 mark)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **lifecycle of a Mobile Agent** in an IDPS context. Mention all six stages clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **three coordination models** used between multiple mobile agents in a distributed IDPS. Compare them on the basis of coupling, scalability, and suitability for IDPS. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) – Mobile Agent Lifecycle (7 marks)

The mobile agent passes through **six lifecycle stages**:

1. **Creation** – CMS instantiates the agent, attaches the signature set, and assigns a unique Agent ID. *(1 mark)*

2. **Dispatch** – State is serialized, optionally encrypted, and transmitted to the target AEE. *(1 mark)*

3. **Reception & Authentication** – Destination AEE verifies the agent's digital signature, checks CMS credentials, and creates a sandbox. *(1 mark)*

4. **Local Execution** – The agent queries local sensors, runs detection algorithms, and correlates local events. *(1 mark)*

5. **Migration/Cloning Decision** – Based on findings, the agent may clone itself, return home, or trigger prevention. *(1 mark)*

6. **Retirement** – The agent transmits a concise report to the CMS and terminates. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** Show the six stages as a cyclic flowchart with arrows indicating transitions, including failure paths (e.g., rejected agent). *(1 mark)*

### Part (b) – Coordination Models (7 marks)

**Three coordination models:**

1. **Direct Messaging (Agent-to-Agent RPC):**
   - Agents communicate by sending explicit messages to each other.
   - **Coupling:** Tight (sender must know receiver's address).
   - **Scalability:** Low (O(N²) connections).
   - **Suitability:** Cross-segment correlation of a single multi-stage attack. *(2 marks)*

2. **Blackboard (Tuple-Space) Model:**
   - Agents asynchronously read/write to a shared data structure.
   - **Coupling:** Loose (agents need not know each other).
   - **Scalability:** High (publish-subscribe pattern).
   - **Suitability:** Distributed signature sharing; ideal for collaborative detection. *(2.5 marks)*

3. **Meeting / Itinerary Model:**
   - Agents follow a pre-planned visit list of hosts.
   - **Coupling:** Medium (agent knows the itinerary but not the other agents).
   - **Scalability:** Medium.
   - **Suitability:** Patrol-based periodic monitoring. *(2.5 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the three components of a mobile agent** – Many students write "code and data" only, forgetting the **state**. This costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Mixing Strong vs. Weak migration** – Strong migration carries the program counter; weak migration does not. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Forgetting the AEE** – The Agent Execution Environment is a mandatory component; treating the agent as a "free-floating" program is incorrect.
> 4. **Using raw log forwarding numbers instead of agent numbers** in $\Delta B$ calculations. Always substitute the correct variables.
> 5. **Skipping units** in the final answer (always state **ms**, **KB**, or **%** explicitly).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – Mobile Agents in IDPS**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** A mobile agent is an *autonomous, migrating program* carrying **Code + State + Data**.
>
> ✅ **Three Pillars of Mobility:** Serialization, Transport, Resumption (lifecycle of migration).
>
> ✅ **Strong Migration** = carries full state (PC, stack); **Weak Migration** = carries only code/data.
>
> ✅ **AEE (Agent Execution Environment)** is mandatory on every host; it provides sandboxing, authentication, and lifecycle services.
>
> ✅ **Bandwidth Saving** $\Delta B = 1 - \dfrac{S_a \cdot f_{mig}}{L \cdot b}$; typical savings **70%–95%**.
>
> ✅ **Maximum Hops** $N_{hops} \le \lfloor T_{budget} / (T_m + T_{exec}) \rfloor$.
>
> ✅ **Three Coordination Models:** Direct Messaging (tight coupling), Blackboard (loose coupling), Itinerary (medium).
>
> ✅ **Z-Score Detection** $Z = \vert x - \mu \vert / \sigma$; threshold $Z_{thr} = 3$ for 99.7% confidence.
>
> ✅ **Six Lifecycle Stages:** Creation → Dispatch → Authentication → Local Execution → Migration/Cloning → Retirement.
>
> ✅ **CIA Triad** for mobile-agent security: **Confidentiality** (encryption), **Integrity** (digital signature), **Non-repudiation** (audit logs).
>
> ✅ **Advantages:** No SPOF, bandwidth efficiency, local prevention, scalability, heterogeneity tolerance.
>
> ✅ **Disadvantages:** Higher code complexity, harder to audit, security risks from malicious agents, AEE compatibility issues.
>
> ✅ **Real-world platforms:** IBM Aglets, Concordia, Telescript, Grasshopper, JADE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooperative Intrusion Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT742 - INTRUSION DETECTION & PREVENTION SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cooperative Intrusion Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Cooperative Intrusion Detection (CID)**, also termed **Collaborative Intrusion Detection (CID)**, is a distributed security paradigm in which multiple, geographically dispersed Intrusion Detection System (IDS) components — referred to as **cooperative nodes, sensors, or agents** — exchange security-relevant telemetry (alerts, logs, audit records, statistical profiles, and contextual intelligence) over a secure communication channel in order to collectively detect attack patterns that no individual sensor could reliably identify in isolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Cooperative IDS is an extension of Distributed IDS (DIDS) in which heterogeneous sensors across administrative domains share alert data, fuse evidence, and coordinate response actions to produce a **higher-confidence, attack-wide situational picture** than a standalone IDS can achieve. It is a *system-of-systems* approach to cyber defence.

The architectural distinction is critical for KTU examinations:

| Mode | Data Scope | Coordination |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standalone IDS** | Local host or single subnet | None |
| **Distributed IDS (DIDS)** | Multiple sensors, single admin domain | Centralized/hierarchical |
| **Cooperative IDS (CIDS)** | Multiple sensors, **multiple admin domains** | Peer-to-peer / federated |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Neighbourhood Watch" Model

Imagine a single house with a burglar alarm. It detects a thief only **at the moment of forced entry** — reactive and narrow. Now imagine **twenty houses on a street, each fitted with a camera, motion sensor, and a two-way radio** that automatically calls neighbouring homes when it spots a suspicious vehicle. The houses:

1. **Observe locally** (each sensor monitors its own perimeter).
2. **Communicate** (radios share sightings — "white van circling Block 5").
3. **Correlate** (three homes independently reporting the same van = high confidence attack).
4. **Respond cooperatively** (all alarms trigger, police are called collectively, neighbours lock doors in unison).

This is precisely the **philosophy of Cooperative IDS** — local observation + global correlation + coordinated response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The defining feature of cooperation is **inter-domain trust and information exchange**, not merely the presence of multiple sensors. A single company's DIDS is *distributed*; a multi-organisation cyber threat intelligence (CTI) sharing network is *cooperative*.

### 1.3 Why Cooperative IDS is Mandatory in Modern Networks

- **Modern attacks are distributed, multi-stage, and multi-vector** (e.g., APTs, botnets, DDoS from botnets spanning continents).
- A single sensor sees only a **slice** of the attack — e.g., a reconnaissance probe on Sensor A, an exploit on Sensor B, exfiltration on Sensor C.
- **Single points of failure** in centralized architectures are exploited by attackers.
- **Defence-in-Depth** demands horizontal information sharing across the security stack.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Expected Answer Hook:** When asked "Why Cooperative IDS?", always cite three benefits: (1) **broader attack coverage**, (2) **reduced false positives via evidence fusion**, (3) **coordinated response at scale**. Mention APTs as the canonical motivating threat.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Models of Cooperative IDS

The cooperative IDS literature, as reflected in the KTU 2024 syllabus, classifies cooperative architectures into **three canonical topologies**:

#### A. Centralized Cooperative Architecture
- A single **central coordination server (fusion center)** collects alerts from all member sensors.
- Performs global correlation, aggregation, and decision-making.
- **Pros:** Simple, globally consistent view, easy to audit.
- **Cons:** Single point of failure, scalability bottleneck, privacy concerns (sensors must reveal all data to one party).

#### B. Hierarchical Cooperative Architecture
- Sensors are grouped into **clusters** with **local fusion centers**, which in turn report to higher-level regional/global centers.
- Resembles a **military command structure** (platoon → battalion → division).
- **Pros:** Scalable, fault-tolerant within a tier, reduces raw data flow upward via local pre-filtering.
- **Cons:** Intermediate nodes become targets; inter-tier trust is required.

#### C. Decentralized / Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Cooperative Architecture
- No central authority. Each sensor is both a data source and a correlator.
- Communication via **gossip protocols, DHT-based overlays (e.g., Chord, Pastry), or publish-subscribe brokers**.
- **Pros:** Highly resilient, no single point of failure, naturally scales to Internet-scale deployments.
- **Cons:** Eventual consistency, harder to enforce global policies, complex trust establishment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** The transition from *Centralized → Hierarchical → P2P* is fundamentally a trade-off between **control/coherence** and **resilience/scalability**. Examiners frequently test this trade-off using the phrase *"trade-off between availability and consistency"*.

### 2.2 Functional Components of a Cooperative IDS Node

Each cooperative node typically exposes **five functional blocks**:

1. **Local Detection Engine** — produces raw alerts (signature-based, anomaly-based, or hybrid).
2. **Local Correlation / Pre-processor** — deduplicates, normalises, and clusters alerts *before* sharing.
3. **Trust & Reputation Manager** — evaluates the reliability of neighbouring nodes (Section 2.4).
4. **Communication Module** — handles secure message exchange (TLS, signed alerts via IETF IDMEF / IDWG standards).
5. **Global Fusion / Decision Engine** — combines local evidence with peer-shared evidence to refine the final verdict.

### 2.3 Information Sharing Levels (Granularity Spectrum)

Cooperation can occur at differing levels of disclosure. The KTU syllabus emphasises this spectrum:

| Level | What is Shared | Privacy Impact | Detection Power |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L0 – None** | Only local alerts, no sharing | **None** | Lowest |
| **L1 – Alert IDs** | Alert type/ID only | Minimal | Low |
| **L2 – Alert Attributes** | Source IP, port, timestamp, signature | Moderate | Medium |
| **L3 – Raw Events** | Full packet headers / log entries | High | High |
| **L4 – Full Audit Data** | Complete session captures | **Maximum** | Highest |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Sharing raw packet data (L3/L4) yields the best detection accuracy but breaks **data minimisation** principles and may violate privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR). The cooperative IDS design must balance *detection fidelity* against *information leakage*.

### 2.4 Trust & Reputation in Cooperative IDS

Cooperation is impossible without **trust**. If any node can inject alerts, the system becomes useless (or weaponised for false-flag attacks). Two dominant models:

**(a) Static Trust Model**
- Each node has a pre-configured trust value assigned by an administrator.
- Simple but **inflexible** — cannot adapt to compromised or newly joined nodes.

**(b) Dynamic / Reputation-Based Trust Model**
- Trust is computed as a function of past behaviour. A common formulation is the **Beta Reputation System** (used in collaborative IDS literature):

$$
T_i(t+1) = \alpha \cdot S_i(t) + (1-\alpha) \cdot R_i(t)
$$

where:
- $T_i(t+1)$ = updated trust score of node $i$ at time $t+1$
- $S_i(t)$ = **subjective trust** (this node's direct experience with $i$)
- $R_i(t)$ = **reputation** (recommendations from other nodes about $i$)
- $\alpha \in [0,1]$ = weight balancing direct experience vs. peer feedback

### 2.5 Evidence Fusion — Combining Cooperative Verdicts

When multiple nodes report on the same suspected event, the cooperative engine must **fuse** their opinions. Three mathematically grounded approaches appear in the KTU syllabus:

#### (i) Weighted Majority Voting
Each sensor's vote is weighted by its current trust score $T_i$:

$$
V_{\text{final}} = \begin{cases} \text{ATTACK} & \text{if } \sum_{i \in \text{attack}} T_i > \sum_{j \in \text{benign}} T_j \\ \text{BENIGN} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

#### (ii) Bayesian Fusion
If $P(A \mid E_i)$ is node $i$'s belief that attack $A$ is occurring given evidence $E_i$, then under **conditional independence**:

$$
P(A \mid E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n) = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n)}
$$

#### (iii) Dempster–Shafer Theory (DST)
Handles **uncertainty and ignorance** explicitly using belief (Bel) and plausibility (Pl) functions, combined via Dempster's rule of combination:

$$
m_{1 \oplus 2}(C) = \frac{1}{1 - K} \sum_{B \cap D = C} m_1(B) \cdot m_2(D), \quad K = \sum_{B \cap D = \emptyset} m_1(B) \cdot m_2(D)
$$

where $K$ is the **conflict mass** between the two evidence sources.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Concept | Key Term | Critical Point |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cooperative IDS Goal | **Multi-domain alert fusion** | Beyond single-admin DIDS |
| Centralized CIDS | **Fusion center** | Bottleneck, single point of failure |
| Hierarchical CIDS | **Tiered local + global correlators** | Scalable, intermediate nodes targeted |
| P2P / Decentralized CIDS | **Gossip / DHT overlay** | Resilient, eventual consistency |
| Alert Format Standard | **IETF IDMEF (RFC 4765)** | XML-based, transport via IDWG |
| Trust Models | Static vs. Beta Reputation | Dynamic models resist insider attacks |
| Evidence Fusion | Voting / Bayesian / DST | DST handles uncertainty best |
| Sharing Granularity | L0 → L4 | Trade-off with privacy |
| Key Risk | **False alert injection / collusion** | Mitigated by trust + signed messages |
| Real-World Examples | **DShield, Shadowserver, Collective Intelligence Framework** | Internet-scale cooperative IDS |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Modern **SIEM platforms (Splunk, QRadar, Elastic Security)** implement *hierarchical cooperative* architectures. **ISAC (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers)** in finance, healthcare, and energy sectors operate *cross-organisational* cooperative IDS using STIX/TAXII standards. Understanding this architecture is foundational for any SOC analyst or security engineer role.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Cooperative Alert Correlation

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to *derive* the cooperative detection procedure, not merely list components. The following is the **canonical stepwise analytical framework** examiners reward.

### 3.1 Problem Setup

Consider a cooperative IDS with $N$ sensor nodes $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N\}$ monitoring a common enterprise network. Each sensor $s_i$ observes a stream of events $E_i$ and produces a *local alert set* $A_i = \{a_{i,1}, a_{i,2}, \ldots\}$ upon detecting a suspected intrusion. The cooperative engine must produce a *global verdict* $V_{\text{global}} \in \{\text{BENIGN, ATTACK, UNCERTAIN}\}$.

**Given:**
- Local alert sets $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$.
- Trust scores $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N$ (initialised to a baseline $T_0$, then updated dynamically).
- Correlation time window $\Delta t$ within which alerts are considered related.
- Alert similarity threshold $\theta_{\text{sim}}$.

**To Find:** $V_{\text{global}}$ and the **confidence score** $C_{\text{global}}$ associated with that verdict.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Cooperative Detection Algorithm

**Step 1 — Local Detection (parallel at every node):**
Each $s_i$ runs its local IDS (signature / anomaly / hybrid) and emits alerts $A_i(t)$ within a sliding window $W = [t - \Delta t, t]$.

**Step 2 — Alert Normalisation:**
Map each alert $a_{i,j}$ into a common schema (typically **IETF IDMEF**):

$$
a_{i,j} = \{ \text{alert\_id}, \text{class}, \text{timestamp}, \text{src}, \text{dst}, \text{proto}, \text{severity} \}
$$

This eliminates heterogeneity across vendors (Snort vs Suricata vs Zeek).

**Step 3 — Secure Broadcast of Normalised Alerts:**
Node $s_i$ signs the alert bundle $A_i(t)$ using its private key and broadcasts it over the cooperative overlay:

$$
M_i = \text{Sign}_{\text{sk}_i}\big( A_i(t) \big), \quad \text{peers } s_k \text{ verify } \text{Verify}_{\text{pk}_i}(M_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Alert Similarity & Clustering:**
For every received pair $(a_{i,j}, a_{k,l})$ from nodes $i \neq k$, compute a similarity score. A common definition uses **attribute-weighted Jaccard similarity**:

$$
\text{sim}(a_{i,j}, a_{k,l}) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_m \cdot \mathbb{1}\{a_{i,j}^{(m)} = a_{k,l}^{(m)}\}
$$

where $w_m$ is the weight of attribute $m$ (e.g., $w_{\text{src}} = 0.4$, $w_{\text{dst}} = 0.4$, $w_{\text{proto}} = 0.2$, with $\sum w_m = 1$). If $\text{sim} \geq \theta_{\text{sim}}$, the two alerts are **clustered** into the same candidate incident $C_p$.

**Step 5 — Trust-Weighted Evidence Fusion:**
For each candidate cluster $C_p$ containing alerts from $K$ distinct nodes, compute the **cooperative confidence score**:

$$
C_{\text{global}}(C_p) = \frac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}(C_p)} T_i \cdot c_i}{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}(C_p)} T_i + \epsilon}
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{N}(C_p)$ = set of nodes whose alerts are in cluster $C_p$
- $T_i$ = current trust score of node $i$
- $c_i$ = local confidence of node $i$'s alert (severity-normalised, $\in [0,1]$)
- $\epsilon$ = small constant (e.g., $10^{-9}$) to prevent division by zero

**Step 6 — Decision Rule:**

$$
V_{\text{global}}(C_p) = \begin{cases}
\text{ATTACK}    & \text{if } C_{\text{global}}(C_p) \geq \tau_{\text{high}} \\
\text{BENIGN}    & \text{if } C_{\text{global}}(C_p) \leq \tau_{\text{low}} \\
\text{UNCERTAIN} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Typical thresholds: $\tau_{\text{high}} = 0.75$, $\tau_{\text{low}} = 0.25$.

**Step 7 — Trust Update (Feedback Loop):**
After the global decision is reached, nodes whose alerts **agreed** with the global verdict receive a positive trust increment, and those that **disagreed** receive a penalty. Using the Beta Reputation update:

$$
T_i(t+1) = T_i(t) + \eta \cdot \big( V_{\text{agree}} \cdot (+1) + V_{\text{disagree}} \cdot (-1) \big)
$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (e.g., $0.05$), and $T_i$ is bounded to $[0,1]$.

**Step 8 — Cooperative Response (if ATTACK):**
If $V_{\text{global}} = \text{ATTACK}$, the cooperative engine issues a coordinated response — e.g., simultaneous firewall rule updates at all affected nodes, isolation of compromised hosts, or notification to upstream peers.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Bayesian Fusion)

**Problem:** Three cooperative sensors produce the following posterior beliefs that an SSH brute-force attack is underway, given their local evidence:
- $P(A \mid E_1) = 0.80$
- $P(A \mid E_2) = 0.70$
- $P(A \mid E_3) = 0.60$

Assume the prior $P(A) = 0.10$ and conditional independence.

**Step 1:** Compute likelihoods for each node. From Bayes' rule:

$$
P(E_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid E_i) \cdot P(E_i)}{P(A)}
$$

Assuming equal evidence priors $P(E_i) = 0.5$ for simplicity, compute:

$$
P(E_1 \mid A) = \frac{0.80 \times 0.5}{0.10} = 4.0, \quad
P(E_2 \mid A) = \frac{0.70 \times 0.5}{0.10} = 3.5, \quad
P(E_3 \mid A) = \frac{0.60 \times 0.5}{0.10} = 3.0
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bayesian fusion. Under conditional independence, the joint posterior is proportional to the product of likelihoods times the prior:

$$
P(A \mid E_1, E_2, E_3) \propto P(A) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{3} P(E_i \mid A)
$$

$$
\propto 0.10 \times (4.0 \times 3.5 \times 3.0) = 0.10 \times 42.0 = 4.20
$$

**Step 3:** Normalise. The complement posterior is:

$$
P(\bar{A} \mid E_1, E_2, E_3) \propto 0.90 \times \prod_{i=1}^{3} P(E_i \mid \bar{A})
$$

Assuming $P(E_i \mid \bar{A}) = 0.5$ (evidence equally likely under benign hypothesis) and $P(\bar{A}) = 0.90$:

$$
\propto 0.90 \times (0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5) = 0.90 \times 0.125 = 0.1125
$$

**Step 4:** Final fused belief:

$$
P(A \mid E_1, E_2, E_3) = \frac{4.20}{4.20 + 0.1125} \approx \frac{4.20}{4.3125} \approx 0.9739
$$

**Interpretation:** Individually, the sensors were uncertain (one was only 60% confident). Cooperatively fused, the global confidence rises to **97.4%** — well above the $\tau_{\text{high}} = 0.75$ attack threshold. This demonstrates the **core value of cooperation**: distributed evidence dramatically suppresses uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The numerical result above is the **mathematical justification** for cooperative IDS in the exam. Memorise the form: *product of likelihoods × prior, normalised by the sum of attack and benign hypotheses*. Examiners love to give you two or three sensor confidences and ask for the fused posterior.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Cooperative IDS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NetA[Network Domain A]
        SA1[Sensor A1 - Snort]
        SA2[Sensor A2 - Suricata]
        SA3[Sensor A3 - Zeek]
    end

    subgraph NetB[Network Domain B]
        SB1[Sensor B1 - Host IDS]
        SB2[Sensor B2 - NetFlow Probe]
    end

    subgraph NetC[Network Domain C]
        SC1[Sensor C1 - Cloud IDS]
        SC2[Sensor C2 - EDR Agent]
    end

    LFC1[Local Fusion Cluster A]
    LFC2[Local Fusion Cluster B]
    LFC3[Local Fusion Cluster C]

    GFC[Global Fusion Center]

    TM[Trust and Reputation Manager]
    CR[Cooperative Response Engine]
    DB[(Shared Alert Knowledge Base)]

    SA1 --> LFC1
    SA2 --> LFC1
    SA3 --> LFC1

    SB1 --> LFC2
    SB2 --> LFC2

    SC1 --> LFC3
    SC2 --> LFC3

    LFC1 --> GFC
    LFC2 --> GFC
    LFC3 --> GFC

    GFC <--> TM
    GFC <--> DB
    GFC --> CR

    CR -.coordinated action.-> SA1
    CR -.coordinated action.-> SB1
    CR -.coordinated action.-> SC1
```

### 4.2 Cooperative Alert Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Local Detection] --> N1[Alert Normalisation IDMEF]
    N1 --> S1[Sign and Encrypt]
    S1 --> T1{Trust Check}
    T1 -- Trusted --> B1[Broadcast on Overlay]
    T1 -- Untrusted --> D1[Discard or Quarantine]
    B1 --> C1[Similarity Clustering]
    C1 --> F1[Trust Weighted Fusion]
    F1 --> D2{Decision}
    D2 -- Attack --> R1[Cooperative Response]
    D2 -- Benign --> U1[Update Trust Scores]
    D2 -- Uncertain --> H1[Escalate to Analyst]
    R1 --> U1
    H1 --> U1
    U1 -.feedback.-> T1
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Trust-Weighted Fusion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Receive alerts from N nodes] --> B[Cluster alerts by similarity]
    B --> C[For each cluster compute weighted sum]
    C --> D{Sum exceeds tau high}
    D -- Yes --> E[Verdict ATTACK]
    D -- No --> F{Sum below tau low}
    F -- Yes --> G[Verdict BENIGN]
    F -- No --> H[Verdict UNCERTAIN]
    E --> I[Update trust plus eta]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Trigger higher tier analysis]
    I --> K[Loop to next window]
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Cooperative Response Coordination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Global Verdict ATTACK] --> P1{Attack Type}
    P1 -- DDoS --> R1[Rate limit at all edge nodes]
    P1 -- Lateral Movement --> R2[Microsegment affected VLANs]
    P1 -- Exfiltration --> R3[Block egress at all gateways]
    P1 -- Malware --> R4[Quarantine endpoints via EDR]
    R1 --> LOG[Update shared threat intel]
    R2 --> LOG
    R3 --> LOG
    R4 --> LOG
    LOG --> NOTIFY[Notify SOC and upstream ISAC]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip for Diagrams:** When asked "Draw the architecture of Cooperative IDS" in the KTU 14-mark question, always include: (1) *multiple domains*, (2) *local fusion points*, (3) *global fusion center*, (4) *trust manager*, (5) *response engine*, and (6) a *feedback arrow* showing trust update. This six-element diagram is the **full-marks answer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
*Define Cooperative Intrusion Detection System. How does it differ from a Distributed IDS?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Cooperative Intrusion Detection System (CIDS)** is a distributed security architecture in which multiple IDS components, deployed across *different administrative domains*, exchange security alerts and contextual information to collectively detect sophisticated, multi-stage attacks that span organisational boundaries. (1.5 marks)

It differs from a Distributed IDS in **three key aspects**:
1. **Scope of cooperation** — DIDS operates within a *single* administrative domain; CIDS operates across *multiple* administrative domains. (0.5 mark)
2. **Trust model** — DIDS assumes trusted components by default; CIDS requires explicit, dynamic trust and reputation mechanisms to handle untrusted peers. (0.5 mark)
3. **Response** — CIDS enables cross-organisational coordinated response (e.g., shared blocklists, joint threat intelligence), whereas DIDS typically coordinates only local response within one organisation. (0.5 mark)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`**
*List any three challenges faced in deploying Cooperative IDS and briefly explain each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Trust Management (1 mark):** In a cooperative setting, sensors may belong to different organisations with conflicting interests. A malicious or compromised sensor can inject false alerts (false positives) or suppress real ones (false negatives), degrading the global detection capability. Robust trust and reputation models are required.

2. **Data Privacy and Confidentiality (1 mark):** Sharing raw security logs or packet traces may leak sensitive business information, customer data, or personally identifiable information (PII). Compliance with regulations (GDPR, HIPAA) limits what can be exchanged, forcing coarse-grained sharing that reduces detection accuracy.

3. **Scalability and Latency (1 mark):** As the number of cooperative nodes grows to thousands (e.g., Internet-scale botnet detection), the communication, storage, and correlation overhead grows super-linearly. Overlay-network design, hierarchical clustering, and efficient gossip protocols are needed to maintain timely decisions.

*(Other valid answers: alert normalisation heterogeneity, message authentication, collusion attacks, legal/jurisdictional barriers.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule Reminder:** Answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the three architectural models of Cooperative IDS in detail. Compare their advantages and disadvantages with respect to scalability, fault-tolerance, and privacy.*

**Model Solution:**

The three architectural models of Cooperative IDS are:

**1. Centralized Cooperative Architecture (2 marks)**

A single, logically central **fusion center** receives alerts from all member sensors. The center performs global correlation, aggregation, and decision-making; results are then pushed back to the nodes.

- *Advantages:* Simple to design and audit; globally consistent view; easy policy enforcement; minimal inter-node communication (all go through center).
- *Disadvantages:* **Single point of failure** — if the center crashes, the entire cooperative detection collapses; **scalability bottleneck** — alert volume from thousands of sensors saturates the center; **privacy risk** — all raw data is exposed to one party.

**2. Hierarchical Cooperative Architecture (2 marks)**

Sensors are grouped into **clusters** with **local fusion nodes**, which roll up into higher-level regional and finally global fusion nodes. Mirrors a military command hierarchy.

- *Advantages:* **Scalable** — local clusters pre-correlate and only summary alerts traverse upward; **fault-tolerant within a tier** — one local cluster's failure doesn't disrupt others; **privacy-preserving at the leaf level** — raw data stays local.
- *Disadvantages:* Intermediate fusion nodes become **high-value targets** for attackers; **trust must be established at every tier**; **latency** for global decisions increases with hierarchy depth.

**3. Decentralized / Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture (2 marks)**

No central authority. Each node is both producer and consumer of alerts. Communication is via **DHT-based overlays (Chord, Pastry)**, **gossip protocols**, or **publish-subscribe brokers**.

- *Advantages:* **Highly resilient** — no single point of failure; **naturally scales** to Internet dimensions; **strong privacy** — data can be shared selectively with trusted neighbours only.
- *Disadvantages:* **Eventual consistency** — global views lag; **complex trust bootstrapping** for new nodes; **harder to enforce global policies**; **higher per-node communication cost** in unstructured overlays.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Centralized | Hierarchical | P2P |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scalability | Low | High | Very High |
| Fault Tolerance | Low | Medium | High |
| Privacy | Low | Medium | High |
| Global Consistency | Strong | Medium | Weak (eventual) |
| Implementation Complexity | Low | Medium | High |

**(b) [7 marks]** *Describe the IETF IDMEF alert format and explain how trust and reputation are maintained in a Cooperative IDS. Use the Beta reputation system in your answer.*

**Model Solution:**

**IETF IDMEF (Intrusion Detection Message Exchange Format) (3.5 marks)**

The IETF, through the **Intrusion Detection Working Group (IDWG)**, standardised the **IDMEF** in **RFC 4765** to provide a common data model and XML-based representation for exchanging alerts between heterogeneous IDS components.

**Hierarchical structure of an IDMEF message:**
- **Alert** (root) — contains one or more *Classifications*, *Sources*, *Targets*, *Assessments*, and *AdditionalData*.
- **Classification** — categorises the alert (e.g., "SSH brute-force", mapped to CVE).
- **Source / Target** — describe the originator and victim of the suspicious activity (IP, port, MAC, service).
- **Assessment** — severity, impact, confidence, and action to be taken.
- **Time** — detect-time, analyzer-time, create-time.

**Why IDMEF matters for Cooperative IDS:**
1. **Interoperability** — a Snort alert from Domain A and a Suricata alert from Domain B can be correlated because they share a common schema. (1 mark)
2. **Transport** — IDMEF messages are transported over the **IDXP** (Intrusion Detection Exchange Protocol) which uses **BEEP (Blocks Extensible Exchange Protocol)** as the framing layer. (0.5 mark)
3. **Extensibility** — XML namespaces allow vendors to add proprietary fields without breaking standard parsers. (0.5 mark)
4. **Machine-readability** — enables automated correlation engines to consume alerts at scale. (0.5 mark)

**Trust and Reputation in Cooperative IDS (3.5 marks)**

In an open cooperative setting, sensors may be compromised, malicious, or simply unreliable. A static "trust everyone" assumption is unsafe.

**The Beta Reputation System** is a well-known dynamic trust model used in cooperative IDS literature. It is based on the **Beta probability density function** $Beta(\alpha, \beta)$, which models the distribution of a binary random variable (alert was *correct* or *incorrect*).

For each peer $i$, two counters are maintained over time:
- $\alpha_i$ = number of alerts from $i$ that were confirmed correct (e.g., a downstream alert corroborated the same attack).
- $\beta_i$ = number of alerts from $i$ that were false or contradicted by others.

The **reputation score** of peer $i$ is then the **expected value of the Beta distribution**:

$$
\text{Rep}(i) = E[\text{Beta}(\alpha_i, \beta_i)] = \frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_i + \beta_i}
$$

**Update rule** after each cooperative decision:

$$
\alpha_i(t+1) = \alpha_i(t) + \Delta\alpha_i, \quad \beta_i(t+1) = \beta_i(t) + \Delta\beta_i
$$

where $\Delta\alpha_i = +1$ if peer $i$'s alert agreed with the global verdict, and $\Delta\beta_i = +1$ if it disagreed.

**Key properties of the Beta reputation model:**
1. **Inertia** — a peer needs many observations to shift its score, providing resistance to single-event manipulation. (0.5 mark)
2. **Forgetting** — older observations can be decayed exponentially to allow recovery from past mistakes. (0.5 mark)
3. **Uncertainty modelling** — the Beta variance shrinks as $\alpha_i + \beta_i$ grows, capturing increasing confidence in the score. (0.5 mark)
4. **Defence against collusion** — when combined with subjective weighting, the system down-weights groups of nodes that consistently agree with each other but disagree with the global consensus. (0.5 mark)

**Conclusion (included for full marks):** IDMEF provides the *syntactic interoperability* required for cooperation, while the Beta reputation system provides the *semantic trust* required to make cooperation *safe*. Together they form the operational backbone of a deployable cooperative IDS.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 — Internal Choice]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *With a neat diagram, describe the functional architecture of a Cooperative IDS. Explain the role of each component.*

**Model Solution:**

A Cooperative IDS comprises **five primary functional components** deployed across a hierarchy of nodes.

**1. Local Detection Engine (1.5 marks)**
- Runs at each sensor node (host-based, network-based, or hybrid).
- Generates raw alerts using signature matching (Snort rules), anomaly detection (statistical profiles, ML models), or stateful protocol analysis.
- *Role:* Provides the *initial* evidence; the cooperative system is only as good as the local detectors.

**2. Alert Pre-processor / Local Correlator (1.5 marks)**
- Performs **deduplication**, **normalisation** to IDMEF, **enrichment** (geo-IP, threat intel lookup), and **local clustering** of related alerts.
- *Role:* Reduces alert *volume* and *noise* before sharing — minimises bandwidth and protects privacy.

**3. Trust & Reputation Manager (1.5 marks)**
- Maintains per-peer trust scores (e.g., Beta reputation).
- Decides whether to accept, weight, or discard alerts from each peer.
- *Role:* Provides the *trust fabric* that makes cooperation safe against malicious peers.

**4. Communication Module (1 mark)**
- Handles **secure message exchange** over the cooperative overlay — TLS/SSL tunnels, signed IDMEF messages, replay protection via nonces, rate limiting.
- Implements the IDXP / BEEP transport or a publish-subscribe broker (e.g., Kafka, MQTT).
- *Role:* Provides the *secure transport* for inter-node evidence sharing.

**5. Global Fusion / Decision Engine (1.5 marks)**
- Combines alerts from all trusted peers using voting, Bayesian, or Dempster–Shafer fusion.
- Applies decision thresholds to produce the global verdict (ATTACK / BENIGN / UNCERTAIN).
- Triggers the **Cooperative Response Engine** on ATTACK.
- *Role:* Provides the *global situational awareness* that defines cooperation.

**Diagram (included for full marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Node[Cooperative IDS Node]
        LDE[Local Detection Engine]
        APC[Alert Pre Processor and Local Correlator]
        TRM[Trust and Reputation Manager]
        CM[Communication Module]
        GFE[Global Fusion and Decision Engine]
    end
    EXT[External Peers and Fusion Centers]
    CRE[Cooperative Response Engine]
    DB[(Shared Alert Knowledge Base)]

    LDE --> APC --> TRM
    TRM <--> GFE
    APC --> CM
    CM <-.secure IDMEF.-> EXT
    GFE --> CRE
    GFE <--> DB
```

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain any two evidence fusion techniques used in cooperative intrusion detection. Compare their strengths and weaknesses.*

**Model Solution:**

**Technique 1 — Weighted Majority Voting (3.5 marks)**

**Concept:** Each cooperative sensor casts a binary vote (ATTACK or BENIGN). The vote of sensor $i$ is weighted by its current trust score $T_i$. The verdict with the larger weighted sum wins.

**Mathematical form:**

$$
V_{\text{final}} = \arg\max_{v \in \{A, B\}} \sum_{i \,:\, \text{vote}_i = v} T_i
$$

**Strengths:**
- **Computationally trivial** — O(N) for N sensors. (0.5 mark)
- **Intuitive and explainable** — easy for analysts to understand. (0.5 mark)
- **Naturally integrates trust** — low-trust sensors have minimal influence. (0.5 mark)
- **Robust to single noisy sensor** if trust is dynamic. (0.5 mark)

**Weaknesses:**
- **Information loss** — collapses each sensor's nuanced belief into a single bit, discarding confidence levels. (0.5 mark)
- **No uncertainty modelling** — cannot express "I don't know". (0.5 mark)
- **Vulnerable to collusion** — a group of high-trust colluders can dominate the vote. (0.5 mark)

---

**Technique 2 — Dempster–Shafer Theory (DST) of Evidence (3.5 marks)**

**Concept:** DST is a generalisation of Bayesian inference that explicitly distinguishes **uncertainty** from **ignorance**. Each piece of evidence is represented by a **mass function** $m: 2^{\Theta} \to [0,1]$ over the frame of discernment $\Theta = \{A, \bar{A}\}$, where $m(A)$ is the mass assigned to "Attack", $m(\bar{A})$ to "Benign", and $m(\Theta) = 1 - m(A) - m(\bar{A})$ is the mass assigned to "I don't know" (ignorance).

**Combination rule (Dempster's rule):**

$$
(m_1 \oplus m_2)(C) = \frac{1}{1 - K} \sum_{B \cap D = C} m_1(B) m_2(D)
$$

where $K = \sum_{B \cap D = \emptyset} m_1(B) m_2(D)$ is the conflict between the two evidence sources.

**Belief and Plausibility** for any hypothesis $X$:

$$
\text{Bel}(X) = \sum_{Y \subseteq X} m(Y), \quad \text{Pl}(X) = 1 - \text{Bel}(\bar{X})
$$

**Strengths:**
- **Explicit uncertainty modelling** via $m(\Theta)$ — DST can say "I have evidence but I cannot yet commit". (0.5 mark)
- **No prior required** — unlike Bayesian fusion, DST does not need $P(A)$. (0.5 mark)
- **Handles conflicting evidence gracefully** via the normalisation factor $1-K$. (0.5 mark)
- **Rich evidence representation** — supports second-order uncertainty. (0.5 mark)

**Weaknesses:**
- **Computational cost** — exponential in the frame size for general $\Theta$. (0.5 mark)
- **Counter-intuitive behaviour in high-conflict** scenarios (Zadeh's paradox). (0.5 mark)
- **Harder to interpret** than voting; requires trained analysts. (0.5 mark)
- **Combining many sources** is associative only under specific conditions. (0.5 mark)

**Comparative Summary:**

| Aspect | Weighted Voting | Dempster–Shafer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Computational cost | Very low | Moderate–High |
| Handles uncertainty | No | Yes |
| Requires prior | No | No |
| Interpretability | High | Medium |
| Robustness to conflict | Low | Medium (with caveats) |
| Best use case | Real-time, large N | Small N, nuanced verdicts |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Distributed IDS with Cooperative IDS** — *distributed* means many sensors in one domain; *cooperative* means many sensors in *multiple* domains with explicit trust. (–2 marks if inter-domain aspect is missing.)
> 2. **Forgetting to mention IDMEF** — almost every CIDS exam answer must reference the IETF IDMEF (RFC 4765) and IDXP transport. (–1 mark.)
> 3. **Omitting the trust/reputation mechanism** — cooperation without trust is a textbook red flag. (–2 marks.)
> 4. **Writing only definitions without architecture** — in 14-mark questions, examiners expect a *diagram* and *component-wise explanation*. (–2 to –3 marks.)
> 5. **Numerical errors in Bayesian fusion** — sign errors in the product-of-likelihoods formula are extremely common. Practise the worked example in Section 3.3.
> 6. **Skipping the privacy trade-off** — the L0→L4 sharing spectrum is a KTU-favoured sub-question; ignoring it loses marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cooperative IDS = Distributed IDS + inter-domain trust + coordinated response.** Memorise this one-line definition.
- **Three architectures:** Centralized, Hierarchical, P2P — know the trade-offs (scalability vs consistency vs fault-tolerance).
- **Five functional components:** Local Detection → Pre-processor → Trust Manager → Communication → Global Fusion.
- **IETF IDMEF (RFC 4765)** is the standard alert schema; **IDXP/BEEP** is its transport — write these acronyms in full at least once in any answer.
- **Sharing Granularity L0–L4:** More sharing = more detection power but more privacy leakage.
- **Trust models:** Static trust is simple but inflexible; **Beta Reputation** is the KTU-favoured dynamic model — know the update rule $T_i = \alpha_i / (\alpha_i + \beta_i)$.
- **Fusion methods (know at least two):** Weighted Majority Voting, Bayesian Fusion (Naïve Bayes independence), Dempster–Shafer Theory. Bayesian fusion is the most exam-friendly.
- **Standard Bayes numerical format:** $P(A \mid E_1 \ldots E_n) = \dfrac{P(A)\prod P(E_i \mid A)}{P(A)\prod P(E_i \mid A) + P(\bar{A})\prod P(E_i \mid \bar{A})}$.
- **Real-world examples:** DShield, Shadowserver, ISACs, STIX/TAXII, MISP — name-dropping these adds value.
- **Threats to cooperation:** False-alert injection, collusion, privacy leakage, scalability collapse, single-point-of-failure in centralized models.
- **P2P/Overlay protocols for CIDS:** DHT (Chord, Pastry), Gossip, Publish-Subscribe.
- **Exam mantra:** Always close your cooperative IDS answer with a sentence on *coordinated response* — that is the operational "so what" examiners want.
- **DIKW pyramid link:** CIDS takes *Data* (raw events) → *Information* (alerts) → *Knowledge* (correlated incidents) → *Wisdom* (coordinated action). State this for bonus marks.
